## Arthroplasty-cervical

## ID: AGS26/237

## P0001: Sex-based differences in outcomes of cervical disc arthroplasty: a propensity-matched cohort study of a large national database

Navya Dandu

1

, Simran Shamith

1,2

, Praveer Vyas

1

, Boyle Cheng

1,2

, Nestor Tomycz

1,2

, Ryan Sauber

1,2

, Daniel Altman

1,2

1

Allegheny Health Network, Pittsburgh, United States

2

Drexel University College of Medicine, Pittsburgh, United States

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) is increasingly used to treat cervical disc disease, yet sex-based differences in postoperative outcomes remain underexplored. This study sought to long-term outcomes in males and females undergoing CDA using a propensity score-matched analysis. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed using the TriNetX US Collaborative Network, including 4,381 female and 4,381 male patients undergoing CDA. Propensity score matching was conducted matched by age (46.9 ± 10.4 vs 47 ± 10.6 years, p = 0.8) cervical pathology (e.g. myelopathy, radiculopathy), and baseline chronic comorbidities (e.g. diabetes mellitus, obesity). Outcomes included additional cervical fusion or disc arthroplasty at any level following the index procedure, opioid use, epidural/trigger point injections, repeat advanced imaging, physical therapy evaluations, and CDA removal or revision. All outcomes were assessed from 1-day post-index surgery. Opioid use, PT/OT, and injections were measured up to 2 years after the index surgery. Bonferroni-adjusted significant level was utilized due to multiple comparisons (p < 0.008). To explore the relative contributions of demographic variables and comorbidities, a post hoc multivariable Cox proportional hazards analysis was performed. Results: Females were more likely to undergo CDA removal or revision (1.2% versus 0.6%; Odds Ratio 1.98 [95% CI: 1.2 to 3.1], p = 0.003). Within 2 years after surgery, females demonstrated higher rates of opioid use (75.7% vs. 71.1%, Odds Ratio 1.27 [95% CI: 1.2 to 1.4], p < 0.001), and non-significant difference in PT/OT evaluations (30.9% vs. 28.3%, p = 0.009). There were no significant differences in rates of pain management injections, repeat imaging, additional fusion or CDA after the index procedure. Female sex was independently associated with a significantly higher hazard of revision or removal compared to male sex (HR 1.66 [95% CI: 1.1 to 2.6], p = 0.03). Osteoporosis was found to be the strongest independent predictor (HR 5.11 [95% CI: 2.0 - 13.1], p = 0.001) of revision or removal of CDA. Conclusion: In this large, age-matched cohort, female patients demonstrated more CDA removal or revision and more prevalent use of postoperative opioid prescriptions. In post hoc analysis, osteoporosis was identified to be the strongest predictor, which may suggest that although it is being recognized preoperatively (given the ICD diagnosis), it may still affect outcomes after CDA. Future studies should investigate the current epidemiology and efficacy of osteoporosis treatment prior to arthroplasty. It is also important to recognize inherent limitations given the nature of the database utilized (including low granularity of data and dependence on ICD/CPT coding).

## ID: AGS26/250

## P0002: Imaging of cervical spine motion: a review of current modalities and metrics

Amirzeb Aurangzeb

1

, Hasjmy Mohamad

1

, Qing Hang Tan

1

, Raj Kumar Sockalingam

1

, Leong Dalun

1

, Dinesh Shree Kumar

1

, Michael Janssen

2

, Terry Teo

3

, Zhihong Chew

1

1

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

2

Center for Spine and Ortho, Denver, Colorado, United States, Colorado, United States

3

SportsIn Orthopaedic Clinic, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Accurate assessment of cervical spine motion is critical for understanding spinal biomechanics, diagnosing instability, evaluating surgical outcomes, and selecting or monitoring motion-preserving interventions such as cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA). Traditional flexion-extension radiographs remain the most widely used technique due to their accessibility and low cost, yet they provide only gross angular measurements, are prone to variability, and offer little information on intervertebral motion quality. Advances in imaging technology now allow for higher-precision, multi-planar, and even three-dimensional motion tracking, promising deeper biomechanical insight and potentially earlier detection of pathological changes. However, these modalities differ markedly in accuracy, radiation exposure, cost, and clinical feasibility. A clear synthesis of their relative strengths, limitations, and ideal use cases is needed to guide both research and clinical adoption. Material and Methods: A literature search was conducted in PubMed using key terms addressing techniques of cervical spine motion analysis. Only peer-reviewed English-language studies involving human or cadaveric models and published between 2000 and 2025 were included. Results: Seventeen studies were included, spanning static radiography, AI-enhanced radiography, dynamic and model-based fluoroscopy, quasi-static MRI, ultrasound, and radiostereometric analysis (RSA). Static radiographs offer basic end-range assessment but fail to capture true intervertebral motion. AI tools improve reproducibility by automating landmark detection. Dynamic fluoroscopy and biplanar model-based tracking provided high-fidelity motion analysis with millimetric accuracy. RSA enabled precise implant micromotion tracking but required invasive markers. Kinematic MRI integrated soft tissue evaluation with upright posture imaging while ultrasound enables radiation-free outpatient monitoring. Conclusion: While advanced motion analysis tools provide more precise insights into cervical biomechanics than static imaging, their routine use will require standardized protocols and proven cost-effectiveness. Advancing the field will depend on methodological harmonization and close collaboration across disciplines.

## ID: AGS26/409

## P0003: Regression of anterior cervical disc-osteophyte complex following artificial disc replacement

Brian Chin

1

, Dennis Hey Hwee Weng

1

1

National University Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) has gained popularity as a suitable alternative to ACDF for treatment of anterior cervical compressive pathologies. Regression of anterior cervical disc osteophyte complex after CDA is a less commonly recognized and understood radiological finding, one which was previously only described following fusion. The aim of this study is to describe the regression of anterior cervical disc osteophyte complex following CDA, and discuss its potential clinical significance. Material and Methods: A retrospective review of 50 patients (122 levels) who underwent CDA with a minimum of 2-year follow up was conducted. Baseline characteristics, clinical presentation, perioperative details, and postoperative details were collected. Radiological parameters included pre and postoperative global cervical alignment, segmental range of motion, protrusion of cervical disc-osteophyte complex at each vertebral level. Presence of implant subsidence, peri-implant fractures, and heterotopic ossification were also recorded. Results: Regression of anterior cervical disc osteophytes was observed in 92 levels (75%) at 3-month postoperatively, 75 levels (61%) at 1-year postoperatively, and 40 levels (33%) at 2-year postoperatively. Mean osteophyte size significantly decreased from immediate postoperative period up to 1-year follow-up (p < 0.005), with no significant difference in osteophyte size between 1 and 2-year follow-up. Range of motion of C2-7 and operated functional spinal unit improved throughout all 3 follow-up timepoints. Heterotopic ossification was observed up to 35 levels (28%) at 2-year follow-up. Conclusion: Regression of anterior cervical disc-osteophyte complex following CDA suggests stress distribution and restoration of movement in the cervical spine, and may be a useful predictor of satisfactory functional and radiological outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/736

## P0004: Three-or-more-level cervical disc arthroplasty versus multilevel ACDF: a systematic review and meta-analysis of clinical, radiological, and safety outcomes

Chinedu Egu

1

, Hussein Akil

1

, Neel Badhe

1

, Michael Woodmass

1

, Rebecca Hakim

2

, Joel Triance

1

, Samuel Akintunde

2

, Edidiong Essiet

2

, Palaniappan Lakshmanan

1

1

South Tyneside and Sunderland Royal Hospital NHS Trust, Sunderland, United Kingdom

2

Centre for Spinal Studies and Surgery, Queens Medical Centre, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: The comparative effectiveness of cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) versus anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) for three-or-more-level cervical degenerative disease remains unclear. CDA offers motion preservation, but data at ≥ 3 levels are limited. The aim of this study was therefore to systematically review and critically appraise the literature comparing multilevel CDA with ACDF in degenerative cervical spine disease. By assessing clinical, radiological, and perioperative outcomes, this review seeks to clarify whether CDA may offer a viable alternative to fusion in this complex subgroup of patients. Material and Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were performed following PRISMA guidelines. PubMed, Embase, CENTRAL, Scopus, and Web of Science were searched for studies comparing ≥ 3-level CDA and ACDF. Outcomes included NDI, mJOA, VAS neck/arm, segmental and global ROM, cervical lordosis (CL), sagittal vertical axis (SVA), T1 slope, perioperative metrics, complications, and revisions. Random-effects models generated pooled mean differences (MD), risk ratios (RR), and 95% confidence intervals (CI). Results: Five retrospective studies (n = 17,529; CDA 3,025; ACDF 14,504) were included. Both groups improved similarly in NDI (MD 1.68, 95% CI -1.48 to 4.83; p = 0.30), mJOA (MD 0.40, 95% CI -0.77 to 1.50; p = 0.49), and VAS neck/arm (p > 0.1). Segmental ROM was better preserved with CDA (MD 10.22°, 95% CI -3.28 to 23.73; p = 0.14). Operated-level lordosis increased (CDA MD -5.55°, p < 0.0001; ACDF MD -5.57°, p = 0.0018), and global CL improved (CDA MD -5.04°, p = 0.03; ACDF MD -4.25°, p = 0.024). SVA and T1 slope were unchanged (all p > 0.05). Operative time was similar; blood loss was slightly higher for CDA (MD 23.2 mL, 95% CI 6.9-39.41; p = 0.005). Overall complications favoured CDA (RR 0.67, 95% CI 0.54-0.84), with lower rates of dysphagia (p = 0.0005), wound complications (p = 0.007), infection (p = 0.03), instrumentation failure (p = 0.01), ED visits (p < 0.0001), readmissions (p = 0.0005), and revisions (p = 0.01); posterior revisions were more frequent after ACDF (0.9% vs 0.5%, p = 0.04). Conclusion: In ≥ 3-level cervical surgery, CDA and ACDF achieve comparable symptom relief and alignment. CDA preserves motion and reduces early complications and revisions, supporting its selective use in multilevel degenerative disease.

## ID: AGS26/880

## P0005: Biomechanical principles of cervical disc arthroplasty: a systematic review of implant design and clinical implications

Qing Hang Tan

1

, Amirzeb Aurangzeb

1

, Zhihong Chew

1

1

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) has emerged as a motion-preserving alternative to fusion for degenerative disc disease. While implant designs vary substantially in their biomechanical principles, a comprehensive analysis of how these design features influence clinical outcomes remains lacking. Material and Methods: We systematically evaluated 15 clinical and biomechanical studies (2010-2023) comparing CDA devices across three themes: (1) Kinematics & Constraint, (2) Materials & Wear, and (3) Load distribution patterns. Data included range of motion (ROM), wear rates and presence of adjacent segment disease (ASD). Results: Constrained designs reduce facet strain but increase adjacent-segment stress, while unconstrained devices replicate native motion at the cost of higher facet loads. Viscoelastic implants offer load dampening but lack long-term osseointegration data. Key trade-offs include material durability (metal-on-polymer vs. metal-on-metal) and fixation methods (keel vs. spike). Conclusion: Current CDA designs present fundamental biomechanical trade-offs between motion preservation, facet protection, and adjacent segment unloading. No single implant optimizes all parameters, highlighting the need for: (1) patient-specific design selection based on individual anatomy and biomechanics, (2) advanced materials to address wear and fixation challenges, and (3) long-term studies of next-generation viscoelastic devices. These findings provide a framework for surgeons to evaluate implant designs based on their biomechanical principles.
Keywords: cervical disc arthroplasty, implant biomechanics, adjacent segment disease, motion preservation

## ID: AGS26/940

## P0006: Long-term clinical and radiological outcomes of multilevel cervical disc arthroplasty

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2 3

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Alexander Kuharev

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, K. Daniel Riew

5 6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, USA, New York, United States

6

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, USA, New York, United States

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) has been recognized as a popular option for cervical radiculopathy or myelopathy. Single-level CDA has shown high clinical and radiological efficacy. However, the results of multi-level CDA are contradictory. The purpose of the study is to report our minimum 5-year clinical and radiographic outcomes, as well as complications of the multi-level CDA with cervical disk herniation. Material and Methods: Sixty-eight cases underwent CDA between 2012 and 2020. Of those, sixty-one patients (89.7%) had surpassed the minimum 5-year follow-up and were included in the retrospective study (n = 33 two-level and n = 28 three-level). Clinical and radiological data were obtained preoperatively, at 1-year and 78 (65;89) months post-operatively. Outcome measures collected and included neck and arm pain, quality of life, intraoperative and postoperative complications and revision rates. Additionally, cervical lordosis, segmental mobility, heterotopic ossification, index and adjacent segment degeneration were analyzed. Results: VAS, NDI and SF-36 scores showed significant improvement compared to baseline. From preoperatively to minimum 5 years postoperatively, mean NDI decreased from 64.8 to 11.9, mean neck pain decreased from 65.1 to 2.7, mean arm pain decreased from 72.2 to 1.3, mean PCS increased from 21.7 to 43.6, and mean MCS increased from 28.3 to 45.6. Patient satisfaction exceeded 89% 5 years after surgery. The average ROM of the operated, upper and lower adjacent segments increased significantly at the final FU. The IDH (intervertebral disc height) of the index level had a tendency to significantly increase. The IDH of adjacent levels were not significantly affected by the surgery. Mean cervical lordosis was increased at the final follow-up. Three patients after two-level CDA and n = 28 five patients after three-level CDA demonstrated minimal radiographic adjacent segment degeneration (ASD), but no patient had symptomatic ASD requiring reoperation. Heterotopic ossification was detected in 73/150 (48.7%) operative segments. Four (6.5%) patients (three patients after two-level CDA and one patient after three-level CDA) underwent secondary surgery. Conclusion: This study suggests that multi-level CDA is a safe and effective treatment for patients with cervical disk herniation at a minimum 5 years postoperatively.

## ID: AGS26/1464

## P0007: Deviation of spinous processes in the adult cervical spine

Kevin Liu

1

, Jacob Wechsler

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: During arthroplasties, where midline placement of the artificial disc is crucial for proper biomechanics, some surgeons may use the spinous processes (SPs) as directly visualized intraoperative guides or as confirmation for accurate midline disc placement through fluoroscopy. However, previous studies and clinical practice have shown that the SPs of the cervical spine can have varying degrees of offset from true midline, although none have directly evaluated this in detail. This study aims to investigate the lateral and angular deviation of cervical SPs to determine their utility as midline markers in spine surgery. Materials and Methods: Adult patients who visited a university-affiliated academic medical center between December 2020 and February 2024 were included if they had available preoperative CT scans of the cervical spine prior to disc surgery with hardware placement. Patients were excluded if they had previous hardware, underwent prior surgery that involved trauma to or removal of any SP, or had CT evidence of ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament. The angle of SP deviation was measured by determining the angle between an anteroposteriorly drawn midline through the vertebral body and a line drawn from the anterior canal to the tip of the SP. For lateral deviation, the distance between the vertebral midline and a parallel line connected to the SP tip was measured. The uncinate processes were used to adjust for any rotation in the image. The two-sample t-test was used to compare results between relevant demographic groups. This study was approved by an institutional review board (HS-23-00758). Results: Of 62 patients who met inclusion criteria, the average age was 60.7 years, with 64.5% males. The average angular deviation from midline for each cervical level was 2.94˚ for C3, 3.52˚ for C4, 3.50˚ for C5, 4.31˚ for C6, and 3.53˚ for C7. The average lateral deviation from midline for each level was 1.38 mm for C3, 1.56 mm for C4, 1.67 mm for C5, 2.37 mm for C6, and 2.51 mm for C7. C7 was found to have the largest variation between individuals both for angular deviation (SD = 2.86˚) and for lateral deviation (SD = 2.52 mm), and C3 showed the smallest variation in both angular deviation (SD = 2.06˚) and in lateral deviation (SD = 1.03 mm). There were no significant differences at any level for angular deviation or lateral deviation when results were compared between sexes (p > 0.05 for all). Conclusion: In adults, there is marked variability in the orientation and position of the cervical SPs both within and between patients, with C3 having the smallest variability and C7 having the greatest variability between individuals. As such, the cervical SPs, especially C6 and C7, are not reliable indicators of vertebral midline from an operative standpoint. In conclusion, in spine procedures such as disc arthroplasty, the SPs should not be considered as reliable indicators of vertebral midline when gauging proper artificial disc placement and positioning.

## ID: AGS26/1484

## P0008: Rising adoption of off-label three-level cervical disc replacement: trends and predictors

Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Dil Patel

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) remains the standard of care for multilevel cervical pathology but carries increased risk of pseudarthrosis and adjacent segment disease, which may necessitate reoperation. Cervical disc replacement (CDR) with its motion-preserving properties has demonstrated advantages over ACDF for one- and two-level procedures, yet three-level CDR remains off-label and understudied. This study aims to evaluate national utilization trends and identify predictors of three-level CDR. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing three-level CDR or ACDF from 2015 to April 2023 were identified in the PearlDiver national database via CPT codes. Patients with trauma, malignancy, or infection were excluded. Primary outcomes included annual utilization trends for three-level CDR and ACDF. Percent change in utilization was calculated annually by comparing to the previous year and by comparing each year to 2015. Secondary outcomes compared demographic and procedural characteristics between cohorts. Multivariable logistic regression was performed to identify independent predictors of undergoing three-level CDR, adjusting for age, gender, and Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI). Results: Among 82,458 included patients, 3,986 (4.8%) underwent three-level CDR and 78,472 (95.2%) underwent three-level ACDF. CDR patients were younger (50.0 vs 58.7 years, p < 0.001), more likely male (47.5% vs 45.8%, p-0.028), had fewer comorbidities (ECI 3.29 vs 4.73, p < 0.001), and higher household incomes ($84,959 vs $78,521, p < 0.001). Three-level CDR utilization increased 197.9% from 2015 to 2022, with an average annual growth rate of 18.8%. In 2015, three-level CDR accounted for 1.84% of all three-level anterior cervical procedures, rising to 6.13% by 2022. In contrast, three-level ACDF utilization declined from 2015 to 2018 and plateaued thereafter. Multivariable analysis identified independent predictors of three-level CDR, including age < 40 years (aOR 3.47), male gender (aOR 1.14), cervical radiculopathy diagnosis (aOR 1.27), outpatient surgery (aOR 3.71), Midwest (aOR 1.19) or West region (aOR 2.32), higher education level (aOR 1.46), surgery by an orthopaedic surgeon (aOR 1.75), commercial insurance (aOR 1.65), and household income > $75,000 (aOR 1.42) (all p < 0.001). Conclusion: Off label three-level CDR utilization has grown markedly since 2015. Its use is associated with younger, healthier, and socioeconomically advantaged patients, while older patients and those with public insurance are more likely to undergo ACDF. These findings highlight emerging disparities in access to this motion-preserving surgery, which may provide added benefit in younger patients. The rapid growth and socioeconomic disparities underscore the urgent need for prospective studies and regulatory review to guide coverage policies and ensure equitable access to this evolving surgical option.

## ID: AGS26/1700

## P0009: The outpatient migration of cervical disc arthroplasty: national trends in medicare volume and payments between 2016 and 2022

Mitchell Ng

1

, Paul Mastrokostas

2,3

, Aaron Lavi

3

, Luke Schwartz

3

, Leonidas Mastrokostas

2,3

, Yasmine Eichbaum

1

, Yulia Lee

1

, Morgan Hitchner

1

, William Green

1

, Gregorio Baek

1

, Joshua Mathew

1

, Jonathan Dalton

1

, Afshin Razi

3

, Alan Hilibrand

1

, Alex Vaccaro

1

, Christopher Kepler

1

, Gregory Schroeder

1

1

Rothman Orthopaedic Institute, Philadelphia, United States

2

SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University, Maimonides Medical Center, Brooklyn, United States

3

Maimonides Medical Center, Brooklyn, United States

Background Context: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) has gained popularity as a motion-preserving alternative to anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) for treating cervical degenerative disc disease. As outpatient spine surgery expands, little is known about how Medicare reimbursement for CDA varies across settings, provider types, and geographic regions. The purpose of our study is to evaluate national trends in Medicare utilization and reimbursement for CDA, comparing inpatient and outpatient settings, provider types, and geographic regions. Methods: This retrospective study utilized CMS Medicare Part A and Part B claims filed from 2016 and 2022 for Medicare beneficiaries undergoing single or multilevel CDA. Procedures were identified using Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) and Medicare Severity Diagnosis Related Group (MS-DRG) codes. In Medicare B datasets, CPT code 22856 was used to identify single level and 22858 for multilevel add-ons. In Medicare part A, MS-DRG code 581 was to use to identify inpatient claims. Primary outcomes were annual procedural volume and mean inflation-adjusted Medicare reimbursement by setting, provider type, region, and rural-urban classification. Inflation-adjusted payments and procedure counts were analyzed using descriptive statistics, linear regression, ANOVA, and t-tests. Subgroup comparisons included region, provider type, and core-based statistical area (CBSA) classification. Statistical significance was set at the P < 0.05 level. Results: Outpatient single-level CDA volume increased by 1,016% from 2016 to 2022, while inpatient CDA volume decreased by 90.3%. Outpatient reimbursement for single-level CDA rose from $1,459 to $3,143 (p = 0.011) and multilevel add-ons declined from $470 in 2017 to $397 in 2022 (15% decrease; p = 0.029). On the other hand, inpatient reimbursement increased from $24,956 to $32,679 (p = 0.025). Regional and rural-urban variation was observed, with the West consistently demonstrating higher reimbursement and volume for both outpatient and inpatient CDA. Reimbursement was significantly higher in ASCs than surgeon-only claims ($11,869.58 vs. $1,219.28; p < 0.001), and in inpatient settings compared to surgeons ($28, 132.00 vs. $1,219.28; p < 0.001). Conclusion: CDA procedure is more commonly being performed the outpatient setting, a pattern that matches general spine surgery trends. This outpatient shift is multifactorial and involves the increase in safety, policy changes, and decrease in overall operational costs in ambulatory surgical centers compared to those in hospitals. However, there is marked differences in Medicare reimbursement across provider types and regions. This matches the disparities associated with other spine procedures. Low reimbursement rates in more rural communities may dissuade CDA-capable outpatient facilities from offering it and thus provide a barrier to patients. These findings underscore the importance of setting-specific reimbursement policy in promoting access and value in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1963

## P0009.1: Morbi-mortality of prevascular anterior cervical spine approach in multilevel disc replacement: a retrospective analysis of 3-4 level vs. 1-2 level surgeries in an academic cohort.

Jean-Baptiste Odent

1

, Baptiste Bozzi

1

, Arnaud Delafontaine

1

, Mehdi Miladi

1

, Thierry Bégué

1

, Jean-Charles Aurégan

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery and Traumatology, Antoine Béclère Hospital, APHP, Paris Saclay University, Clamart, France, CIAMS, University Paris-Saclay, 91405, Orsay, France, B3OA, University Paris Cité, 75010, Paris, France, Clamart, France

Introduction: The prevascular anterior cervical approach via cervicotomy is a well-established technique for the treatment of cervical spine disorders. While 1- to 2-level anterior cervical disc replacement is well-documented, some patients with multi-level discopathies may require 3- to 4-level disc surgeries. The objective of the present study is to compare the epidemiology of complications associated with 1-2 level versus 3-4 level anterior cervical spine surgeries. Our hypothesis was that the morbi-mortality of prevascular anterior cervical spine approach in multilevel disc replacement 3-4 Level was not higher than 1-2 level surgeries. Material and Methods: In December 2024, we conducted a retrospective monocentric study on patients included in an academic cohort who underwent cervical total disc replacement (cTDR) surgery for cervical discopathy using the prevascular anterior approach and with a minimum follow-up of one year. Intraoperative parameters recorded were the number of discectomies performed, type of cTDR used, total time of surgery, total operative time, intraoperative blood loss, and need for transfusion. Perioperative complications, including vascular, esophageal, or laryngeal injuries, as well as neurological symptoms, were also documented. Postoperative assessment included length of hospital stay, hematologic parameters (hemoglobin and hematocrit levels at postoperative days 0, 1, and discharge), need for transfusion, hemoglobin drop, and estimated blood loss. Additionally, rehospitalization and approach-related complications persisting at one year were evaluated. Results: Demographic characteristics of both groups were statistically comparable. The 3- or 4-level group had a significantly longer operative duration (112.8 ± 27.1 min vs. 72.9 ± 19.9 min, p < 0.001) and operating room occupancy time (213.3 ± 31.6 min vs. 162.2 ± 31.8 min, p < 0.001). No intraoperative complications or blood transfusions were recorded in either group. Hemoglobin decreases during hospitalization did not significantly differ between groups (2.3 ± 6.8 g/dl vs. 1 ± 1.04 g/dl, p = 0.28). Postoperative complications were rare and comparable. Conclusion: Apart from the longer operative duration, no significant differences were observed between the two groups. Anterior cervical surgery at 3–4 levels appears to be a reliable and effective approach, with rare complications comparable to those observed in 1–2 level surgeries.

## ID: AGS26/2286

## P0009.2: Mid-Term Clinical and Radiological Outcomes of Multi-Level Cervical Total Disc Replacement Using Prodisc-C Vivo: A Single-Center Retrospective Cohort Study.

Jean-Baptiste Odent

1

, Ying Tang

2

, Arnaud Delafontaine

1

, Morad Bensidhoum

2

, Thierry Hoc

2

, Thierry Bégué

1

Jean-Charles Aurégan

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery and Traumatology, Antoine Béclère Hospital, APHP, Paris Saclay University, Clamart, France, CIAMS, University Paris-Saclay, 91405, Orsay, France, B3OA, University Paris Cité, 75010, Paris, France, Clamart, France

Introduction: Cervical degenerative disc disease (cDDD) is a progressive condition influenced by aging and mechanical stress, presenting as axial neck pain, radiculopathy, myelopathy, or a combination thereof. It is a leading cause of disability worldwide. Management follows a stepwise approach, beginning with conservative treatment—education, activity modification, medications, and interventional pain management. When non-surgical interventions fail, surgery is indicated to alleviate symptoms, prevent further structural deterioration, and improve quality of life. Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) has been the gold standard for decades; however, total disc replacement (TDR) has emerged as a promising alternative. The Prodisc-C Vivo (Centinel Spine, USA) has shown favorable safety and efficacy profiles in treating symptomatic cDDD. Nonetheless, concerns remain regarding heterotopic ossification (HO) and adjacent segment disease (ASD). We hypothesized that strict surgical protocol and patient selection would result in favorable outcomes with multi-level cervical TDR (cTDR) using the Prodisc-C Vivo. Material and Methods: In August 2025, we conducted a retrospective single-center study of patients undergoing cTDR with Prodisc-C Vivo via a cervical anterior pre-vascular approach. Inclusion criteria included cervicobrachial neuralgia due to cDDD, refractory to ≥6 months of conservative treatment, and ≥1 year of postoperative follow-up. Clinical and radiological data were collected preoperatively and at 1, 2, and 5 years postoperatively. Evaluated outcomes included VAS-Neck, VAS-Arm, Neck Disability Index (NDI), segmental lordosis, range of motion, incidence of HO, ASD, and complication/reoperation rates. Results: A total of 57 patients were included (29 female [51%], 28 males [49%]; mean age 50 ± 8.7 years; mean BMI 25.2 ± 3.7 kg/m2). Procedures included: 13 one-level, 27 two-level, 13 three-level, and 4 four-level cTDR. VAS-Neck improved from 6.28 ±1.5 pre-op to 1.6 ±1.2 post-op, 0.14 ±0.5 at 1 year, 0.25 ±7.5 at 2 years, and 1.6 ±1.8 at 5 years (p< 0.05). VAS-Arm improved from 6.61 ±1.2 pre-op to 0.5 ±1 post-op, 0.3 ±0.9 at 1 year, 0.35 ±0.9 at 2 years, and 1.5 ±2.8 at 5 years (p< 0.05). NDI improved from 23.04 ±5.3 pre-op to 11.06 ±3 post-op, 2.76 ±2.5 at 1 year, 2.86 ±3 at 2 years, and 6.8 ±8.8 at 5 years (p< 0.05). Segmental lordosis improved from -3.2° pre-op to 15.9° at 1 year, 11.4° at 2 years, and 9.1° at 5 years (p< 0.05). Range of motion decreased from 43.7° pre-op to 36.9° at 1 year, 41° at 2 years, and 39° at 5 years. Cranial ASD was 0% at 1 year, 14% at 2 years, and 30% at 5 years. HO incidence at final follow-up was 25%, with high-grade (III-IV) HO in 10%. There were no vascular, neurological, or implant-related complications. One patient underwent reoperation for adjacent segment syndrome at 4 years postoperatively. Conclusion: This study confirms the hypothesis that multi-level cTDR using Prodisc-C Vivo provides favorable mid-term clinical and radiological outcomes, with significant improvements in pain and function, low complication and reoperation rates, and acceptable rates of HO and ASD up to 5 years. Strict surgical technique and careful patient selection are key factors in achieving successful outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2296

## P0010: Five-year prospective analysis of cervical viscoelastic total disc replacement: clinical and radiographic outcomes in a Jamaican population

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Sukanya Chebrolu

1

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

1

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States,

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States,

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Cervical degenerative disc disease leads to axial pain, radiculopathy, and myelopathy when conservative care fails. Fusion is effective but sacrifices motion and can accelerate adjacent segment degeneration. Viscoelastic total disc replacement was designed to preserve motion through elastic load sharing and to reproduce near-physiologic kinematics. Evidence from Caribbean health systems is limited. This prospective series reports medium term clinical and radiographic outcomes after viscoelastic cervical disc replacement in a Jamaican cohort, with attention to pain relief, disability, segmental motion, fixation stability, and heterotopic ossification observed on delayed imaging. Materials and Methods: Consecutive patients with symptomatic degenerative cervical disc disease and imaging correlation underwent single or multilevel viscoelastic cervical disc replacement at a single center. Inclusion required failure of nonoperative management. Exclusion included instability, osteoporosis, or prior fusion at the index level. Clinical endpoints were Neck Disability Index and Visual Analogue Scale for neck and arm pain. Radiographic review assessed implant position, migration, subsidence, segmental range of motion on flexion extension radiographs, and heterotopic ossification grading on follow up imaging. Patients were followed at standard intervals through two and five years, with extended radiographic review available to eight years in selected cases. Descriptive statistics summarized outcomes over time. Results: Six patients underwent fourteen level arthroplasty across C3 to C7. Five were female and one was male, mean age 53 years. Mean intraoperative blood loss was 41.7 mL. All patients were discharged within forty-eight hours. Neck Disability Index improved from a preoperative mean of 67.3 to 16 at two years, with durable improvement at five years. Visual Analogue Scale for pain improved from 9.5 preoperatively to 1.8 at two years and remained improved at five years. No revision procedures were required. One patient experienced a traumatic event with anterior translation that did not require surgery and resolved without loss of fixation. Delayed imaging showed heterotopic ossification in some treated levels, including high grade changes in selected segments. Inventory limitations at the time led to occasional size mismatch and anterior overhang; these placement issues did not compromise implant stability or clinical recovery in this series. Conclusion: Viscoelastic cervical disc replacement provided sustained pain relief, disability reduction, and segmental motion preservation in this Jamaican cohort at five years. Implants remained stable without migration, and no revision procedures were necessary. Heterotopic ossification was observed on late imaging but did not adversely affect fixation or the favorable clinical course. These outcomes support viscoelastic arthroplasty as a motion preserving option where fusion is undesirable and provide early regional data relevant to practice in resource constrained settings.

## P0011: Outcome of three levels prefilled cervical interbody fusion without plates after anterior cervical discectomy

Ameer Majeed Kareem

1

, Salim Mardan Omer

2

, Haider Ali Hamood AL-Ogaily

3

, Ali Qais Abdulkafi

4

, Tareq Jawad Kadem Al-Rubayee

5

1

Kurdistan Ministry of Health, Erbil Health Department, Erbil Teaching Hospital, Erbil, Iraq

2

Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research, Kirkuk University \ College of Medicine, Kirkuk, Iraq

3

Iraqi Ministry of Health, Baghdad Al-Russafa Health Directorate, AL-Kindy Teaching Hospital, Baghdad, Iraq

4

Iraqi Ministry of Health, Kirkuk Health Department, Kirkuk Teaching Hospital, Kirkuk, Iraq

Backgrounds: Prefilled Cervical interbody fusion by synthetic bone without plate Facilitates the retention of physiological lordosis and cervical spine stability in patients who have undergone cervical discectomy by anterior approach, a secure and efficacious treatment for disc herniation or spondylosis. Several spine specialists advocate the incorporation of a plate system to boost outcomes. According to literature findings, surgery involving three levels results in reduced fusion rates. Patient & Methods: Sixty-three person with cervical radicular pain and nine with myelopathy underwent anterior discectomy and fusion with prefilled or (Impregnated) cervical cages by anterior approach between July 2012 and June 2022 were included in the study without using plate systems. There was a total of 72 individuals, comprising of 48 males and 24 females, whose average age amounted to 54.33 years, falling within a range of 33 to 73 years, In variable hospitals of Erbil city. All cases took (an MRI), and 35 among them also took a C-Scan for preoperative assessment. Results: All cases exhibited improvement in radiculopathy following surgery. However, myelopathy persisted in only three patients. After 12 months, 96% of patients demonstrated the fusion of surgically treated discs, as confirmed by an X-ray of the cervical spine region. Cervical lordosis was reinstated in nearly all patients who had lost it prior to the operation. No complications stemming from cage extrusion were reported and there were no instances of symptomatic pseudarthrosis. Conclusions: The inclusion of impregnated interbody fusions serves to impart a load-sharing capability and enhance spinal stability, thereby resulting in augmented segmental stiffness. This, in turn, leads to similar fusion rates as those elicited by bone grafts, even in cases of cervical degenerative disease spanning three levels.
Keywords: Cervical disc, Anterior discectomy, cervical intervertebral body cage fusion

## ID: AGS26/2559

## P0012: Cervical disc replacement in cervical myelopathy: clinical insights from a retrospective study

Arvind G. Kulkarni

1

1

Mumbai Spine Scoliosis & Disc Replacement Center, Bombay Hospital, Breach Candy Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: The role of cervical disc replacement in radiculopathy is well established. However, its application in management of cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM) remains unclear. The objective of this study was to evaluate the neurological and functional outcomes of cervical disc replacement in patients with CSM. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective observational study of 15 consecutive patients (mean age 48.1 ± 9.4 years; 11 males, 4 females) who underwent single or two-level cervical disc replacement for CSM between January 2010 and January 2024. Diagnosis was based on clinical findings and MRI evidence of cord compression. Outcomes were assessed using the modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) score, Neck Disability Index (NDI), and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for neck-pain at baseline, 6 weeks, 6 months, 1 year post-operatively. Results: Out of 15 patients, 14 patients underwent single level CDR, and 1 patient underwent a two-level CDR. Mean mJOA improved from 11.7 ± 1.0 to 15.7 ± 1.0 at 12 months (p < 0.05). NDI scores decreased from 25.0 ± 1.8 at baseline to 7.8 ± 0.6 at 12 months. VAS-neck pain improved from 5 to 2. No neurological deficit or complications were observed. All patients demonstrated significant improvement in neurological and functional outcomes. Conclusion: Cervical disc replacement is a safe, effective option in selected CSM patients, providing neurological recovery, pain relief, functional improvement, and motion preservation.

## P0013: Feasibility of one to four level cervical viscoelastic total disc replacement in an emerging country: a pilot study

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Sukanya Chebrolu

1

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

1

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Cervical total disc replacement aims to preserve motion and reduce the burden of adjacent segment degeneration associated with fusion. Traditional articulating devices can develop heterotopic ossification and non-physiologic kinematics. Viscoelastic total disc replacement seeks to address these limitations by incorporating an elastic core that shares load and allows controlled motion. Evidence for multilevel use in emerging health systems remains limited. This pilot study reports feasibility and two-year outcomes for one to four level viscoelastic cervical arthroplasty. Materials and Methods: Six consecutive patients with symptomatic degenerative disc disease underwent viscoelastic total disc replacement at one to four levels between C3 and C7. Outcomes included Neck Disability Index, Visual Analogue Scale for neck and arm pain, and segmental range of motion on flexion extension radiographs. Radiographic review assessed implant position, stability, and the presence of heterotopic ossification. Patients were evaluated at standard intervals to twenty four months. Nonparametric statistics assessed change from baseline with significance at p < 0.05. Results: Fourteen implants were placed across the cohort of six patients (n = 6). Mean intraoperative blood loss was 41.7 mL. All patients were discharged within forty-eight hours and reported early symptom relief. No intraoperative or postoperative complications occurred, and no revision procedures were required during follow-up. At two years, Neck Disability Index improved from 67.3% to 16% (p < 0.001), and Visual Analogue Scale improved from 9.5 to 1.83 (p = 0.013). Segmental motion was preserved at all treated levels. Radiographs showed stable implant positioning without heterotopic ossification, osteolysis, or migration. Conclusion: Single and multilevel viscoelastic cervical arthroplasty was feasible and effective in this pilot series, with significant improvement in pain and disability, preserved motion, and no adverse radiographic findings through two years. These results justify larger studies and longer follow up to define durability and comparative effectiveness against fusion in multilevel disease.

## ID: AGS26/2772

## P0014: Non-tobacco nicotine dependence is associated with higher rates of hospitalizations following cervical disc arthroplasty surgery

Manaav Mehta

1

, Abhinav Sharma

2

, Ryan Beyer

2

, Vivian Chen

2

, Michael Kim

2

, Paramveer Birring

2

, Hao-Hua Wu

2

, Sohaib Hashmi

2

, Yu-po Lee

2

, Nitin Bhatia

2

, Don Park

2

1

University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, United States

2

University of California Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Symptomatic cervical radiculopathy and myelopathy have historically been treated with the anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) procedure, which has reliably achieved decompression and stabilization of the cervical spine. However, pseudoarthrosis and iatrogenic adjacent segment disease associated with fusion procedures remain significant complications, resulting in increasing interest in motion-preserving procedures, including cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA). The association between non-tobacco nicotine dependence (NTND) and complications following CDA has yet to be explored. The goal of this study is to examine the association between NTND and the incidence of adjacent segment disease, heterotopic ossification, implant failure, revision surgery, dysphagia, post-operative infections, and other perioperative complications in patients undergoing CDA. Material and Methods: TriNetX, a global health research network, was queried from 2004-2024 for patients undergoing single or multi-level CDA (CPT 22856, 22858) and who had documented pre-operative nicotine dependence (ICD10 F17) excluding cigarette, chewing tobacco, and other tobacco product dependence (ICD10 F17.21, F17.22, F17.29). Analysis included chi-squared tests with a control cohort propensity score matched for age, sex, race, smoking, diabetes, BMI, and uremia. The control cohort had coded negative history of nicotine use or dependence (ICD10 Z87.981, F17). Outcomes of interest were DVT, emergency department (ED) visits, inpatient hospitalization, opioid abuse, PE, sepsis, surgical site infection, and wound complications within 90 days, and pseudoarthrosis, adjacent segment disease, dysphagia, heterotopic ossification, implant failure, and revision within 2 years post-operation. Results: A total of 514 patients with NTND were compared to propensity matched controls. After matching, patients were 45.6 ± 10 years old, 50% male, 77% white, 12% diabetic, and 38% obese. Pre-operative NTND was significantly associated with increased odds of inpatient hospitalization (OR 1.70, CI 1.02-2.83) within 90 days, and pseudoarthrosis (OR 3.34, CI 1.68-6.66). Odds of DVT, PE, post-operative SSI, wound complications, ED visits, inpatient hospitalization, sepsis, adjacent segment disease, implant failure, dysphagia, heterotopic ossification, revision surgery, and opioid abuse within 2 years were not significant. Conclusion: This cohort study of patients undergoing CDA found that those with a history of NTND had higher rates of pseudoarthrosis and inpatient hospitalizations compared to those with no history of nicotine use. These findings suggest that NTND may significantly impact rates of postoperative complications leading to hospitalization and pseudoarthrosis following CDA, and further prospective research is needed to confirm these results and explore the underlying mechanisms.

## ID: AGS26/2807

## P0015: Beyond fusion: cervical disc arthroplasty for traumatic cervical disc herniation - A case series

Fabianto Santoso

1

, Muhamad Aulia Rahman

1

, Muhammad Andika Wibisono

1

, Arief Purwoditho

1

, Paul Atmaja

1

1

RISE and SPINE, Jakarta, Indonesia

Introduction: Traumatic disc herniations often result from acute injuries, such as motor vehicle accidents, falls, or sports-related trauma, leading to sudden displacement of disc material that compresses neural structures. The traditional gold standard for surgical management is anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF). Cervical disc replacement (CDR), also known as cervical arthroplasty, has emerged as a motion-preserving alternative to fusion, which is designed to maintain segmental mobility while addressing neural compression. Theoretically, CDR can minimize the potential for future adjacent-segment diseases. Currently, CDR is primarily used for degenerative diseases. Herein, we report two cases of traumatic cervical disc herniation treated with CDR. Material and Methods: This study presents two case reports of patients who underwent cervical disc replacement (CDR) for traumatic cervical disc herniation. The cases were selected from a single institution's surgical database, Primaya Hospital Bekasi Timur, Indonesia. All surgical procedures were performed by two spine surgeons. We used CDR ROTAIO® in both patients. We evaluated the alignment and range of motion of the patients. Results: The first case is a 32-year-old female patient was injured in a traffic accident with cervical disc herniation and facet tear at level C5-C6. The patient complained of axial pain with a visual analog scale (VAS) 7-8. There was no radiculopathy or myelopathy. The alignment before surgery was kyphotic with C2-C7 alignment using Harrison’s method of 5.608°-C5-C6 alignment of 5.742°. We used a CDR ROTAIO® with a height of 5 mm, width of 15 mm, and depth of 13 mm. Post surgery C2-C7 alignment improve to 7.29o and C5-C6 to 3.31°. The postoperative range of motion (ROM) was 46.91°. Postoperatively, there was no pain or complications. The mJOA score improved from 16 to 17, and the NDI score improved from eight to zero. The second case was a 31-year-old male patient who was injured while playing basketball and presented with cervical disc herniation at C4-5 and C5-6. The patient complained of axial pain with a VAS 6-7. There was radiculopathy with dermatomal C5 on both sides. The alignment before surgery was normal with a C2-C7 alignment of 16.8°. We used a CDR ROTAIO® with a height of 5 mm, width of 15 mm, and depth of 13 mm for C4-5, and a height of 5 mm, width of 17 mm, and depth of 13 mm for C5-6. Postoperatively, there was no pain or complications. The mJOA score improved from 15 to 18, and the NDI from nine to zero. Conclusion: Cervical disc replacement is a viable option for traumatic cervical disc herniation with intact facets. In this report, there was an improvement in the clinical and radiological findings. Further studies and long-term follow-up are required.

## ID: AGS26/2820

## P0016: Revision cervical disc arthroplasty and cutibacterium acnes infection: a systematic review

Pranav Shah

1

, Riaz Mohammed

2

1

Namaha Specialised Healthcare Hospital, Mumbai, India

2

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) is commonly performed as an alternative to fusion in selected patients. While outcomes are generally favourable, concerns regarding implant longevity and revision surgery are emerging. Though the indications for such revisions is multifactorial, the concern of deep prosthetic infection always is at the forefront. Evidence suggests Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes) as overwhelmingly the most common single organism cultured in revision spine surgery. This slow growing, gram-positive anaerobic bacillus is low-virulence cutaneous commensal and has been commonly implicated in shoulder and spine infections. However, its prevalence in revision CDA specifically is not well documented. This study aims to review the current literature to understand the clinical relevance of C. acnes infection in revision CDA. Methods: PubMed database search was undertaken to identify all articles till date in English language on human populace assessing revision CDA cases associated with C. acnes infection with positive tissue cultures. References mentioned in these articles were also studies to identify additional relevant publications. Study characteristics, clinical presentation, intra-op findings and management strategies were analysed. Results: 8 relevant studies (3 case series and 5 case reports) depicted 19 patients collectively where CDA revisions demonstrated C. acnes positive tissue cultures. Majority of the revisions involved the M6-C (Orthofix, Lewisville, Texas) disc replacement prostheses. Presentation ranged from early postoperative pain and radiculopathy to more commonly pain, dysphagia or implant loosening several years after implantation. Intraoperative findings included periprosthetic collections, osteolysis, granulomatous inflammation, and frank purulence in some cases. Microbial diagnosis required prolonged tissue cultures and sometimes confirmation with 16S ribosomal RNA (rRNA) molecular sequencing. Management generally consisted of implant removal and conversion to anterior cervical decompression and fusion, supplemented with prolonged (12 week) postoperative poly-antibiotic therapy. Outcomes were favourable, with resolution of presenting symptoms in most cases. However, the distinction between true infection, low-grade contamination, and inflammatory/foreign body reaction remains unclear. Conclusion: Positive tissue cultures for C. acnes are an increasingly concerning finding in revision CDA, often presenting with a slow, subtle course, absence of acute inflammatory features and associated with significant bone loss. Surgeons should maintain a high index of suspicion for this indolent anaerobic microbial infection in cases of delayed CDA failure. Multicentre prospective studies are needed to better understand the under-recognised burden of C. acnes infection in revision CDA.

## ID: AGS26/2912

## P0017: Hybrid cervical reconstruction with artificial disc replacement and ACDF: a retrospective review

Jerzy Gregorczyk

1

, Cooper Gardner

2

, Jack Petros

2

, Negin Fani

1

, Jared Ament

2 3 4

, Amir Vokshoor

2 3 5

1

Medical University of Warsaw, Warsaw, Poland

2

Institute of Neuro Innovation, Santa Monica, United States

3

Neurosurgery and Spine Group, Santa Monica, United States

4

Cedars Sinai Medical Center, Santa Monica, United States

5

Saint John's Health Center, Santa Monica, United States

Introduction: Artificial disc replacement (ADR) is an innovative procedure typically reserved for discogenic pain with preserved range of motion, whereas anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) is favored for instability or deformity. The function of ADR in correcting subluxation and reducing spinal cord compression is controversial. Data comparing functional outcomes and sagittal alignment after hybrid constructs remains limited. The objective of this review is to evaluate clinical outcomes and radiographic alignment following hybrid cervical reconstruction compared with traditional ACDF. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who underwent hybrid reconstruction (ADR + ACDF) at 3 institutions. Collected data included patient-reported outcomes (visual analog scale [VAS] for pain, Neck Disability Index [NDI]) and radiographic measures. Global cervical lordosis (C2-C7 Cobb angle) was measured on neutral lateral radiographs pre- and postoperatively. In a subset, segmental Cobb angles were measured on flexion-extension radiographs to assess the range of motion (ROM). Paired t-tests were used to compare pre- and postoperative outcomes. Results: We included 14 patients with complete clinical and radiographic data (CT, MRI, and X-ray). Mean VAS neck scores improved from 5.4 to 1.86 (p < 0.01), and mean NDI decreased from 45.43 to 21.43 (p < 0.01). Global cervical lordosis improved significantly from 0.84° ± 12.09° preoperatively to 14.2° ± 6.6° postoperatively (p < 0.05). Segmental analysis demonstrated preserved motion at ADR levels (5-8° ROM) with expected loss of mobility at ACDF levels (< 2°). No major complications or reoperations were observed. Conclusion: Hybrid cervical constructs provide significant improvement in pain, disability, and cervical lordosis, while preserving motion at ADR levels. Segmental analysis demonstrates predictable loss of mobility at fused levels with redistribution of motion across the construct. Our results support the role of hybrid surgery in multilevel cervical reconstruction and call for further studies with larger cohorts and long-term follow-up. Limitations include a small sample size and heterogeneity in follow-up duration.

## ID: AGS26/3360

## P0018: Mechanical retrieval findings of simplify ceramic-on-polymer cervical disc arthroplasty device: early analysis of six explants

Philip Paschal

1

, Fedan Avrumova

1

, Gregory Paschal

1

, Alexandra Satanovsky

1

, Deven Bhatnagar

1

, Sydney Warwick

1

, Darren R. Lebl

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) has been introduced as an alternative to anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), with the intent of preserving motion and reducing adjacent segment disease. A newer design incorporating PEEK endplates with titanium plasma-spray coating and a zirconia-toughened alumina ceramic core was developed to minimize imaging artifact and metal wear. Short term clinical results have been positive. To date, there have been few reports from retrieval analysis. The purpose of this study was to characterize the early mechanical retrieval findings of this ceramic-on-polymer CDA device. Material and Methods: Six CDA devices were explanted during revision surgery in three patients within a seven-month period. All explants were subjected to gross inspection, microbiological swabbing and culture with pathological reports, and light microscopy was used to categorize surface wear. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was further employed to assess high-resolution surface morphology, coating separation, polymer phase striations, and the presence of embedded particulate debris. Results: The average time from index surgery to explant was 23 ± 5 months. Across the six devices retrieved demonstrated polishing and linear surface scratching of the ceramic core (6/6,100%), consistent with repetitive articulation in vivo. Delamination of the titanium plasma spray coating from the PEEK endplates was present in all devices (6/6, 100%), ranging from focal flaking to broad areas of separation at the bone implant interface. Furthermore, core microcracking was identified in a single device (1/6, 16.7%), and visible fine subsurface fissures were confirmed with microscopy. Gross ceramic fractures were observed in multiple devices, with discrete fracture lines traversing load-bearing regions of the core. Upon further inspection, SEM revealed additional wear phenomena including areas of partial bony incorporation on endplate surfaces, phase striations within the polymer consistent with stretch-induced deformation, and striated wear tracks across the articulation. Lastly, evidence of third-body wear was present on all surfaces for all devices, with metallic debris embedded in the PEEK and fine ceramic particles distributed across the articulation zone. Conclusion: Overall, this retrieval analysis of CDA was unique as it demonstrated early and consistent mechanical compromise. When compared traditional CDAs, these findings were not consistent with prior retrievals, instead its unique hydrophobic behavior and tarnishing phenomena specific to this design. Therefore, further studies are required to clarify such mechanisms and for future designs for CDA devices.

## ID: AGS26/3375

## P0019: Thirteen in vivo failure mechanisms of the M6-C unconstrained viscoelastic cervical disc arthroplasty

Philip Paschal

1

, Aarti Shenoy

1

, Joseph Babrowicz III

1

, Gregory Paschal

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Rudolph Bertagnoli

2

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Bogen Klinik, Bogen, Germany

Introduction: The M6-C cervical disc (Orthofix Medical Inc.) is an unconstrained viscoelastic cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA) implant designed to replicate the morphology and biomechanics of the natural disc, with the goal of preserving motion and reducing the risk of adjacent segment degeneration. Despite these theoretical advantages, long-term durability in clinical use has been reported with variable success. This study analyzed retrieved M6-C devices through microscopic examination, with emphasis on surface wear, fixation integrity, and previously unreported modes of degradation. Material and Methods: Six patients undergoing revision surgery primarily for unresolved pain had thirteen M6-C devices explanted. The cohort included three patients with single-level, two with two-level, and one with four-level CDA. Explants were catalogued and examined using light microscopy and high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Surfaces were analyzed for fixation stability, metallic debris, burnishing, scratching, annulus fiber disruption, intergranular corrosion, pitting, and cracking. Results: The mean age at revision surgery was 48.0 ± 8.8 years. All 13 explants (100%) exhibited mechanical wear, with burnishing and linear scratching most pronounced along the articulating core and peripheral sheath. Polymeric sheath damage was present in 11 devices (84.6%), ranging from localized thinning to complete rupture, while annulus fiber disruption was identified in 8 (61.5%). Fixation fin deformation was seen in 7 devices (53.8%), and endplate separation in 6 (46.2%), often accompanied by loosening at the implant-bone interface. Delamination occurred in 5 devices (38.5%), frequently associated with metallic debris deposition (38.5%). Pitting and intergranular corrosion were observed in 3 devices (23.1%), affecting the metallic fixation elements. Several explants demonstrated multimodal failure, with combinations of sheath rupture, fin deformation, and particulate debris distributed across multiple surfaces. Conclusion: M6-C retrievals demonstrated consistent surface wear, sheath disruption, and fixation compromise, with additional novel evidence of intergranular corrosion. These findings suggest unique vulnerabilities of unconstrained viscoelastic CDA and underscore the importance of continued retrieval surveillance to inform future design improvements.

## ID: AGS26/156

## P0019.1: Surface carburized 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V biomimetic porous motion-preserving artificial cervical vertebra and its initial clinical application

Wenlong Yang

1

, Chaoyuan Ge

1

1

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, China

Introduction: The general surgical treatment of cervical spondylotic myelopathy is anterior decompression and fusion, which has significant effect. But the non physiological compensatory movement after surgery may accelerate the degeneration of adjacent segments. Our team successfully developed a 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V biomimetic porous motion-preserving artificial cervical vertebra implant. The prothesis would not only restores the anatomical stability of the operation area, but also retained the physiological and motion functions of the corresponding segments, which can effectively avoid the risk of potential secondary operation. However, the inherent wear resistance of Ti-6Al-4V material is poor, and the prosthesis will fail due to excessive wear in a short time after implantation. Material and Methods: The object of this study was 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V movable artificial cervical vertebra. (1) The surface ceramic modification of 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V was carried out by using glow plasma hydrogen-free carburizing technology, to optimize the process by characterize and analyze the properties of the samples treated with different parameters. (2) Using spine and joint motion simulator to carry out friction experiment of the prothesis, the surface ceramicized titanium alloy was used as the joint ball, and the joint sockets made of the same material and ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene were respectively matched. (3) Suitable patient were chosen to implant the artificial cervical vertebra, evaluating the short-term efficacy and in-vivo service performance of the prosthesis. Results: (1) A layer of TiC ceramics was formed on the surface of the samples after carburizing, and the surface hardness was nearly 3 times higher than that before treatment. (2) After 5 million cycles, for the self-matching joint, the total wear rate of the ball and socket (0.160 ± 0.004 mm3/Mc) was an order of magnitude lower than that of commercial artificial cervical intervertebral discs, for the titanium-polyethylene pair, the wear rate (8.00 + 0.06 mg/Mc) was 8 times larger than that of commercial artificial cervical disc CoCrMo/UHMWPE. (3) Clinical case of anterior cervical subtotal resection + 3D printing movable artificial cervical vertebra implantation had been completed. The clinical manifestations were disappeared after operation. Imaging examination showed that the position of the prosthesis was normal and the motion of the surgical area was preserved. No serious complications occurred. Conclusion: Carburizing technology can improve wear resistance of the 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V movable artificial cervical vertebra without changing the biocompatibility. (2) The tribological study of the joint machine showed that, for the titanium alloy self-matching pair, the total wear rate was much lower than that of commercial artificial cervical intervertebral discs. When paired with polyethylene, the joint socket was damaged by the hard TiC ceramic layer, and severe wear and plastic deformation occurred. (3) Clinical application showed that anterior decompression + surface ceramic movable artificial cervical vertebra implantation achieved significant short-term curative effect while retaining the physiological motion function of the operating area, which was a useful innovation and exploration for the surgical treatment of single-segment cervical vertebral diseases.

## ID: AGS26/360

## P0019.2: A Self-developed 3D Printed Anatomical Biomimetic Cervical Artificial Disc Prosthesis for Single-level Cervical Spondylosis

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi’an Honghui Hospital, Xi’an, China

Introduction: To investigate the safety and clinical efficacy of a new cervical artificial disc prosthesis in the treatment of single‐level cervical spondylosis. Material and Methods: The clinical data of 40 patients with single‐level cervical spondylosis who underwent 3D printed anatomical bionic cervical disc replacement at our hospital from January 2022 to July 2024 were analyzed retrospectively. There were 16 males and 24 females, aged (45±8) years old (range: 28 to 58 years). The surgical segment was located at C3-4 level in 6 cases, C4-5 level in 10 cases, C5-6 level in 20 cases, and C6-7 level in 4 cases. The clinical and radiographic outcomes were recorded and compared before surgery, 3 months after surgery, and 12 months after surgery. The clinical assessments included JOA, NDI and VAS. Imaging assessments included range of motion (ROM) of cervical spine, subsidence and migration of the prosthesis. Results: All patients underwent the operation successfully and were followed up for more than 12 months. Compared with preoperative score, the JOA score, NDI and VAS were significantly improved after surgery (all P< 0.05). There was no statistically significant difference in the ROM of the surgical segment compared with that before surgery and at 1 month and 12 months after surgery. The prosthesis subsidence was (0.26± 0.13) mm (range: 0.18 to 0.50 mm) at 12‐month postoperatively. The migration of prosthesis at 12‐months postoperatively were (0.71±0.20) mm (range: 0.44 to 1.08 mm). Conclusion: The 3D printed anatomical biomimetic cervical artificial disc replacement has a good early clinical effect in the treatment of one-level cervical spondylosis, which can maintain the mobility of the cervical spine while achieving good stability.

## ID: AGS26/1261

## P0019.3: Surface carburized 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V biomimetic porous motion-preserving artificial cervical vertebra and its initial clinical application.

Wenlong Yang

1

, Chaoyuan Ge

2

1

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xian, Xi’an, China

2

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, China

Introduction: The general surgical treatment of cervical spondylotic myelopathy is anterior decompression and fusion, which has significant effect. But the non physiological compensatory movement after surgery may accelerate the degeneration of adjacent segments. Our team successfully developed a 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V biomimetic porous motion-preserving artificial cervical vertebra implant. The prothesis would not only restores the anatomical stability of the operation area, but also retained the physiological and motion functions of the corresponding segments, which can effectively avoid the risk of potential secondary operation. However, the inherent wear resistance of Ti-6Al-4V material is poor, and the prosthesis will fail due to excessive wear in a short time after implantation. Material and Methods: The object of this study was 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V movable artificial cervical vertebra. (1) The surface ceramic modification of 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V was carried out by using glow plasma hydrogen-free carburizing technology, to optimize the process by characterize and analyze the properties of the samples treated with different parameters. (2) Using spine and joint motion simulator to carry out friction experiment of the prothesis, the surface ceramicized titanium alloy was used as the joint ball, and the joint sockets made of the same material and ultra-high molecular weight polyethylene were respectively matched. (3) Suitable patient were chosen to implant the artificial cervical vertebra, evaluating the short-term efficacy and in-vivo service performance of the prosthesis. Results: (1) A layer of TiC ceramics was formed on the surface of the samples after carburizing, and the surface hardness was nearly 3 times higher than that before treatment. (2) After 5 million cycles, for the self-matching joint, the total wear rate of the ball and socket (0.160 ± 0.004 mm3/Mc) was an order of magnitude lower than that of commercial artificial cervical intervertebral discs, for the titanium-polyethylene pair, the wear rate (8.00 ± 0.06 mg/Mc) was 8 times larger than that of commercial artificial cervical disc CoCrMo/UHMWPE. (3) Clinical case of anterior cervical subtotal resection + 3D printing movable artificial cervical vertebra implantation had been completed. The clinical manifestations were disappeared after operation. Imaging examination showed that the position of the prosthesis was normal and the motion of the surgical area was preserved. No serious complications occurred. Conclusion: (1) Carburizing technology can improve wear resistance of the 3D printed Ti-6Al-4V movable artificial cervical vertebra without changing the biocompatibility. (2) The tribological study of the joint machine showed that, for the titanium alloy self-matching pair, the total wear rate was much lower than that of commercial artificial cervical intervertebral discs. When paired with polyethylene, the joint socket was damaged by the hard TiC ceramic layer, and severe wear and plastic deformation occurred. (3) Clinical application showed that anterior decompression + surface ceramic movable artificial cervical vertebra implantation achieved significant short-term curative effect while retaining the physiological motion function of the operating area, which was a useful innovation and exploration for the surgical treatment of single-segment cervical vertebral diseases.

## ID: AGS26/1728

## P0019.4: Scheme selection and curative effect analysis of cervical spine revision surgery

Wenlong Yang

1

, Chaoyuan Ge

1

1

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, China

Introduction: To summarize the indications, methods, curative effects and complications of revision surgery for cervical spine, and to provide some basis for surgical treatment. Material and Methods: A total of 27 patients who underwent cervical revision surgery in our hospital from 2020 to 2023 were reviewed, and the epidemiological data, the type of the first surgery, the way of revision surgery and the complications were summarized and analyzed. JOA score was used to evaluate postoperative pain and nerve function recovery. The indications for cervical revision surgery were classified into four categories: adjacent segment disease, infection, endoplant failure, and non-infectious complications. According to the first surgical approach, the patients were divided into three groups: anterior approach group, posterior approach group, anterior and posterior combination group. Results: The mean age of patients was 48 years (52% were male). Adjacent segment disease was observed in 59% of patients (16 cases), secondary surgery due to infection was performed in 19% of patients (5 cases), and endoplant failure occurred in 3 cases (11%). 63% of patients in the anterior and 80% in the posterior groups were treated with the same approach as in the initial surgery. In the combined anterior and posterior group, 67% of revision procedures were performed via the posterior approach. The early complication rate was 3%. Six patients (22%) had excellent outcomes, 11 patients (40%) had good outcomes, 8 patients (30%) had satisfactory outcomes, and 2 patients (7%) had poor outcomes. No patients were lost to follow-up. No radiographic failure of the implant after revision or the need for re-surgical revision was found. Conclusion: Cervical spine revision surgery requires careful preoperative planning and requires high professional level of the surgeon. In this study, more than half of the patients obtained good surgical results, and nearly 1/3 of the patients were satisfied with the surgical results. The incidence of surgery-related complications was lower than expected. For some complex cases, it is recommended to refer them to our hospital or other medical institutions of the same level for diagnosis and treatment.
