## ID: AGS26/489

## P0020: Clinical and radiographic outcomes after one-level lumbar total disc arthroplasty: single surgeon’s experience

Jason Garber

1

, Sophia Vassallo

1

1

Las Vegas Neurosurgical Institute for Spine and Brain Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

Introduction: Lumbar Total Disc Arthroplasty (TDA) has shown satisfactory clinical outcomes with low complications and reoperations rates and provides a better surgical alternative to fusion. Objectives: To evaluate the clinical and radiographic outcomes of one-level TDA for lumbar degenerative disc diseases (DDD) in patients with a minimum follow-up of 6 months. Material and Methods: A total of 81 patients who reported at least 6 months of follow-up results after one-level lumbar TDA were included. Data was collected and analyzed from patients who received TDA for symptomatic lumbar DDD between January of 2018 and July of 2024. A total of 81 patients (38male, 43female) with mean age of 43.8 ± 10.6 years (range 19-64) were included in this report. The follow-up period ranged from 6 months to 6 years (average 13.2 months). 2 patients had 1-level TDA at the L3-L4 level, 16 at the L4-L5 level, and 63 at the L5-S1 level. Recorded data: patient demographics, Visual Analogue Scale, Oswestry Disability Index, complications, reoperations, blood loss and time of procedure. Radiographic measurements included a segmental range of motion, device migration, subsidence and Heterotopic Ossification (HO). Patients were examined preoperatively and at 6,12,24-months postoperatively, then yearly. The last follow-up data was used for analysis. Results: Postoperatively, all outcome measure scores (VAS and ODI) immediately improved and these improvements were maintained with statistical significance at all follow-up periods including the last follow-up visits, reaching maximal improvement 2 years postoperative. Patients demonstrated significant improvements in VAS scores from 6.7 to 1.8 and from 44.2% to 10.2% for ODI scores at the last follow-up. The percentage of improvement was documented with an average of 74.9%. The reoperation rate was 1.2% (1/81) with one reoperation at the L5-S1 level due to a non-spontaneous subsidence at the L5 inferior endplate. We did not observe any device migration or severe HO (grades 3 or 4). The final segmental range of motion was well-maintained in monosegmental TDA patients. The average blood loss was 37 cc (range of blood loss 15-125 cc), and the average time of procedure was 39 minutes (range 26-67 minutes). Conclusion: This study reveals that lumbar TDA semiconstrained prosthesis is a safe and effective treatment for chronic back pain caused by DDD. It effectively results in pain relief and an improvement in quality of life and showed successful outcomes. Patients had significant improvement and maintained reduction in patient-reported outcome over time, and low rates of index revision and reoperation.

## ID: AGS26/525

## P0021: Lumbar disc arthroplasty and implant migration/dislocation: a systematic review of patterns, risk factors, and surgical outcomes

Sumanth Gera

1

, Gennie Lim

2

, Zhihong Chew

3

1

National University Health System, Singapore, Singapore

2

Changi General Hospital, Ministry of Health Holding (Mohh), Singapore, Singapore

3

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Lumbar disc arthroplasty (LDA) preserves spinal motion and may reduce adjacent segment degeneration compared with fusion. Despite advances in design and techniques, complications such as implant migration, subsidence, and dislocation do remain, and it could threaten vascular and neurologic structures. This review summarizes patterns, risk factors, and surgical outcomes of LDA migration/dislocation. Materials and Methods: A systematic search of MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane Central was performed. Studies reporting migration or dislocation after LDA were included; non-English reports, cadaveric studies, and studies without event-specific outcomes were excluded. Data extracted included device type, level, timing/direction of migration, risk factors, management, and outcomes. Results: Twenty studies (case reports, small series, cohort analyses) were included, reporting various devices. Though the reported migration/dislocation rates ranged between 3-6% but they were clinically significant. Early complication (< 3 months) were anterior dislocations often due to anterior placement or undersizing; polyethylene inlay escape was also reported. Late complications included subsidence and posterior migration associated with endplate violation. Risk factors included malposition, undersizing, high mechanical shear at L5 -S1, poor bone quality, inadequate endplate preparation, and trauma. Treatment involved fusion with or without explantation. Fusion-only strategies yielded comparable outcomes in selected cases, while explantation carried higher perioperative morbidity, particularly due to vascular risk from the anterior approach. Conclusion: Implant migration/dislocation after LDA is rare but clinically significant. Prevention requires meticulous technique, appropriate sizing, and endplate preparation. Revision should be individualized: fusion alone is often sufficient for contained events, whereas anterior escape with vascular threat necessitates urgent explant and stabilization.

## ID: AGS26/574

## P0022: Lumbar disc arthroplasty periprosthetic fractures: systematic review and a proposed classification system

Zhi Chen Kim Koh

1

, Hasjmy Mohamad

1

, Michael Janssen

2

, Zhihong Chew

1

1
Changi General Hospital, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Singapore, Singapore, 
2
Center for Spine and Orthopaedics, Denver, United States
Introduction: Total disc arthroplasty (TDA) of the lumbar spine is an advanced surgical option for symptomatic lumbar discogenic pain, with clinical outcomes comparing favorably to arthrodesis. With any form of arthroplasty, periprosthetic fractures are a potentially catastrophic complication. This study aims to systematically review existing literature on cases of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA, and propose a novel classification system to guide management of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA. Material and Methods: A systematic review of cases of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA in existing literature (PubMed, Google Scholar) was conducted as according to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta Analyses (PRISMA) recommendations. The included articles must have reported on a periprosthetic fracture from a lumbar TDA. Results: In total, 7 articles were identified with 9 cases of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA found. Out of the 9, 2 cases occurred intra-operatively while the rest were post-operative complications. Sudden pain was the primary presenting complaint. All fractures involved the vertebrae adjacently above or below the artificial disc, except for one instance of a L3 burst fracture when the TDA was performed at the L4-L5 level. All fractures involved were exclusively in either the pedicles, or the vertebral body. Time of discovery of the fractures ranged from intra-operatively, to a year post-operatively. Conclusion: We propose a classification system of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA that range in terms of stability and determines management. Type A fractures include incomplete pedicle fractures, and endplate subsidence without implant migration. Type B fractures are a progression of Type A fractures, and involve endplate fractures resulting in implant migration. They can be unstable and will require fusion with or without implant removal. Type C fractures are vertical sagittal plane fractures, most commonly related to keel cuts. They are potentially unstable without good keel fixation. Type D fractures are coronal plate fractures of the vertebral body, pedicles or pars. These are unstable and will require fusion. Further validation of our proposed classification system with larger patient samples is warranted to evaluate further its effectiveness at guiding management of periprosthetic fractures in lumbar TDA.

## ID: AGS26/918

## P0023: Long-term clinical and radiological effectiveness of the M6-L total disc replacement: a retrospective single-center study

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2 3

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Yurii Pestryakov

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, K. Daniel Riew

5 6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, USA, New York, United States

6

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, USA, New York, United States

Introduction: Previous studies have confirmed the successful long-term clinical and radiological outcomes of lumbar total disk replacement (TDR). However, there are no long-term follow-up reports for the M6-L TDR. The purpose of the study is to report our minimum 7-year clinical and radiographic outcomes, as well as complications of the M6-L disk prostheses in railway workers with lumbar disk herniation causing back and radicular pain (LDH). Material and Methods: Ninety-seven cases underwent TDR using the M-6 L artificial disc between 2012 and 2017. Of those, eighty-six patients (88.6%) had surpassed the minimum 7-year follow-up and were included in the retrospective study. Clinical and radiological data were obtained preoperatively, at 1-year and 106 (90;139) months post-operatively. Outcome measures collected included back and leg pain, quality of life, intraoperative and postoperative complications and revision rates. Additionally, lumbar lordosis, segmental mobility, heterotopic ossification, index and adjacent segment degeneration were analyzed. Results: VAS, ODI and SF-36 scores showed significant improvement compared to baseline. 78 patients (90.7%) had a successful outcome, as defined by the FDA. The average ROM of the operated, upper and lower adjacent segments increased significantly at the final FU. The IDH (intervertebral disc height) of the index level had a tendency to significantly increase. The IDH of adjacent levels were not significantly affected by the surgery. Mean lumbar lordosis was increased at the final follow-up. Intra-operatively, a vena cava tear occurred in 3 patients. Of all the male patients, four cases of retrograde ejaculation were observed. At the last follow-up, 3 patients complained of low back pain (VAS greater than 50 mm), leg pain (VAS greater than 20 mm) and the ODI score greater than 20 points. There were no cases that required revision surgery due to implant failure. Five patients demonstrated minimal radiographic adjacent segment degeneration (ASD), but no patient had symptomatic ASD requiring reoperation. Heterotopic ossification was detected in 35/86 (40.7%) operative segments, including 19/86 (22.1%) segments with class I HO, 12/86 segments (13.9%) with class II, 3/86 (3.5%) segments with class III, and 1/86 (1.2%) with a class IV (fused segment). At the last follow-up period, 75/86 (87.2%) patients went back to their original work, and 8/86 (9.3%) patients changed jobs, with 3/86 (3.5%) patients unable to return to work. Conclusion: This study suggests that lumbar TDR using M-6 L is a safe and effective treatment for radicular and back pain caused by LDH at a minimum 7 years postoperatively. We found that the prosthesis retained its functionality in the long-term postoperative period and was associated with good clinical and radiological results with a minimal incidence of complications.

## ID: AGS26/1011

## P0024: Robot-assisted total posterior facet arthroplasty for lumbar spinal stenosis: a single-center experience

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Kevin Wojcik

1

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Lumbar spinal canal stenosis often results in pain and functional impairment. Conventional surgical management has relied on decompression alone or combined with fusion, both of which carry long-term risks such as postoperative instability and adjacent segment disease (ASD). Posterior lumbar arthroplasty with facet replacement offers a motion-preserving alternative, aiming to mitigate ASD risk and reduce revision rates. The Total Posterior Spine (TOPS) system is a novel facet replacement prosthesis that provides dynamic, multiaxial stabilization across all three spinal columns while maintaining physiologic motion. Although prior studies have demonstrated the safety and efficacy of the TOPS device implantation, evidence regarding robotic assistance in this procedure remains limited. Robotic assistance may enhance the accuracy of pedicle screw placement, optimize device positioning, and shorten operative time. This study aimed to report preliminary clinical experience with robot-assisted TOPS system implantation, focusing on perioperative metrics, patient outcomes, and the safety of robotic integration. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed all patients who underwent robot-assisted TOPS device implantation between April 2024 and August 2025 at a single center. The primary outcome was implant-related complications. Secondary outcomes included operative duration, estimated blood loss, symptomatic improvement, reoperations, and new or worsening neurological deficit. Results: Eight patients with degenerative lumbar stenosis and facet arthropathy were included. All patients presented with low back pain and radiculopathy. Median age was 69.5 years (interquartile range [IQR]: 65.2-72.5), and four patients (50%) were female. One patient (12.5%) was treated at L1-L2, one (12.5%) at L2-L3, four (50%) at L3-L4, and two (25%) at L4-L5. Median operative time was 160 minutes (IQR: 126.7 -176), with a median estimated blood loss of 175 mL (IQR: 150 -200). No intraoperative complications occurred, including screw misplacement or the need for repositioning. Median hospital stay was 3 days (IQR: 2.2 -3.7). At a median follow-up of 7 months (IQR: 3 -11), all but one patient (87.5%) reported symptomatic improvement. No patient required reoperation or experienced implant-related complications such as device malfunction, migration, or screw loosening. One patient developed a new postoperative unilateral lower extremity sensorimotor deficit, and another had an asymptomatic seroma detected on follow-up MRI. Neither complication was attributed to robotic assistance or the TOPS implant. Conclusion: Robot-assisted implantation of the TOPS device appears safe, technically feasible, and efficacious for patients with degenerative lumbar stenosis. Robot assistance enabled accurate screw placement without the need for intraoperative adjustments, eliminated the need for conversion to fusion, and was associated with low morbidity and favorable clinical and radiographic outcomes. Future prospective studies with larger cohorts and longer follow-up are warranted to validate these findings and assess the long-term durability of outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1037

## P0025: Lumbar disc arthroplasty is associated with favourable economic and perioperative outcomes versus lumbar fusion for single-level degenerative disc disease: a systematic review and economic analysis

Alqasim Elnaggar

1

, David Abdelnour

1

, Michael Brown

2

, Ali Mehaidli

2

, Magd Boutany

1

, Ryan Berger

2

1

Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

2

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Henry Ford Health System, Detroit, United States

Introduction: The purpose of this study was to compare the economic and perioperative outcomes of lumbar disc arthroplasty (LDA) versus lumbar fusion for the surgical management of single-level degenerative disc disease (DDD). Additionally, we evaluated the methodological quality of existing literature using the Quality of Health Economic Studies (QHES) instrument to assess the rigor of economic reporting in spine surgery. Material and Methods: We conducted a systematic review in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. A comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane CENTRAL identified comparative studies published between 2005 and 2025 reporting cost-related outcomes for one-level LDA and lumbar fusion. Inclusion criteria required full-text English articles reporting direct or indirect hospital costs, payer costs, reimbursements, or relative value units (RVUs). Data extraction included revision, readmission, complication rates, operative time, blood loss, transfusion rates, and length of stay (LOS). Methodological quality was assessed using the 16-item QHES instrument. Results: Nine studies encompassing over 850,000 patients met inclusion, though economic outcomes were reported inconsistently across them. Of the five studies evaluating direct hospital costs, four demonstrated significantly lower costs for LDA. All studies reporting hospital charges found LDA to be less expensive, with savings ranging from 15% to over 50%. Studies assessing reimbursement and RVUs showed lower physician payments for LDA, but these differences did not offset overall system-level savings. Perioperatively, LDA was associated with shorter LOS (2.9 vs. 5.1 days in the largest dataset), fewer complications (3.6% vs. 8.0%), and reduced transfusion rates. Readmission and revision burdens were variable, with some studies favoring LDA and others showing no difference. Methodological quality was moderate, with QHES scores ranging from 31.3% to 62.5%; common deficiencies included lack of sensitivity analyses, unclear time horizons, and absence of formal cost-effectiveness modeling. Conclusion: For single-level DDD, LDA is associated with significant perioperative and economic benefits compared to lumbar fusion, including lower direct hospital costs, shorter hospital stays, and fewer complications. Despite lower physician reimbursement, system-level savings favor LDA. Adoption, however, remains constrained by patient selection criteria, surgical complexity, and implant longevity concerns. Current evidence is limited by heterogeneity in cost reporting and moderate methodological rigor, underscoring the need for prospective, standardized cost-effectiveness studies incorporating quality-adjusted life years and long-term follow-up.

## ID: AGS26/1146

## P0026: Lumbar disc replacement: motion preservation and hybrid surgery in contemporary anterior lumbar spine practice

Stone Sima

1

, Ashish Diwan

1,2

, Matthew Rackham

3

1

Spine Labs, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George and Sutherland Clinical Campuses, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

2

Spine Service, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George Hospital, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

3

Spinal Unit, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Royal Adelaide Hospital, University of Adelaide, Adelaide, Australia

Introduction: Surgical management of symptomatic lumbar degenerative disc disease (DDD) and low-grade spondylolisthesis recalcitrant to appropriate non-operative treatment, includes both fusion and motion-preserving options. While anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is widely used, lumbar total disc replacement (TDR) remains a motion-preserving surgery for select cases and select lumbar segments that is well established in the literature. Hybrid constructs combining TDR and fusion is considered in multilevel disease, particularly when pathology and treatment goals vary across segments. This study presents real-world prospectively collected outcomes of all patients undergoing ALIF, TDR, or hybrid anterior lumbar surgery from a single surgeon’s practice. Materials and Methods: Eighty-one patients underwent anterior-based surgery for either isolated DDD or DDD with associated spondylolisthesis. Procedures included ALIF (n = 40), TDR (n = 21), and hybrid surgery (n = 20). TDR was only performed in lumbar segments with pure DDD and no radiographic instability. Hybrid surgery was used predominantly in cases with mixed pathology - typically DDD at one level and low-grade spondylolisthesis at another. ALIF was performed in both DDD and spondylolisthesis cases. Clinical outcomes were assessed using VAS (back and leg), ODI, and RMDQ scores up to 24 months. Operative time and blood loss were also recorded. Data was primarily inputted in REDCap and analysis was done on SPSS. Results: TDR patients were younger (mean age 37.8 vs. 48.4 for ALIF, p < 0.01), and procedures were associated with shorter operative times (97 ± 23 min) and less blood loss (96 ± 118 mL) compared to ALIF (p < 0.05). The heterogeneity in follow-up duration (longest:shortest = x:y months) reflects real world assessment. All three groups showed significant improvements across all outcome measures at each follow-up point (p < 0.001). Hybrid surgery achieved similar improvements in pain and function, though with higher operative demands (170 ± 78 min, 213 ± 173 mL EBL), reflecting the complexity of multilevel intervention. Complication rates were low and similar between groups. Discussion: A single surgeon practise removes confounders associated with surgical techniques and system issues related to multi-centric studies. ALIF, TDR, and hybrid approaches all led to meaningful clinical improvement in patients with lumbar DDD and/or low-grade spondylolisthesis. Hybrid constructs offer a flexible solution in cases with mixed pathology, enabling a pragmatic approach to treatment that matches the pathology and treatment goals. A baseline audit system for complex spinal reconstruction has been established to track the same surgeon’s practise temporally, with capacity for data-transfer to larger registry’s or global databases with appropriate approvals.

## ID: AGS26/1526

## P0027: A recent increasing national trend in lumbar disc arthroplasty

Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Mirbahador Athari

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Lumbar fusion has long been the gold standard for operative treatment of lumbar degenerative disc disease. However, lumbar fusion inherently limits spinal motion, thereby increasing risk of adjacent segment disease and pseudarthrosis. Mirroring the development of cervical disc arthroplasty (CDA), lumbar disc arthroplasty was introduced to preserve spinal motion, decreasing the risk of adjacent segment disease and eliminating the risk of pseudarthrosis. Prior studies have demonstrated decreasing LDA utilization trends until 2017. This study aims to investigate more recent yearly national trends of LDA utilization. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing LDA from 2010 to April 2023 were identified in the PearlDiver national database via ICD-9/10 and CPT codes. Primary outcome included annual utilization trends of LDA, with CDA as a comparator. Temporal trends were analyzed using annual percent change from the prior year and percent change from 2010. Secondary outcomes compared demographic characteristics between two LDA cohorts from 2010 to 2016 and 2017 to 2023. Continuous variables were compared using Welch’s t-tests and discrete variables using Chi-square tests. Results: From 2010 to April 2023, 4388 patients underwent LDA while 42,773 patients underwent CDA. In 2010, there were 471 LDA cases. Initially, LDA utilization decreased by 60% from 2010 to 2014 and then plateaued between 2014 and 2017, reaching a nadir of 188 cases in 2017. After 2017, LDA utilization steadily increased to 517 cases in 2022, an overall increase of 9.8% from 2010. In contrast, there were 1100 CDA cases in 2010 with steady increase until 2019 followed by a plateau to 2022, with an overall increase of 316% over time. When compared to 2010-2016, patients who underwent LDA in 2017-2023 were older (43.5 vs 41.8 years), had more comorbidities (ECI 3.24 vs 1.84), had higher education rates (16.3% vs 8.2%), and shorter length of stay (2.17 vs 2.71 days) (all p < 0.001). The percentage of patients paying with commercial insurance increased (87.0% vs 89.1%, p = 0.04) and more procedures were performed as outpatient surgeries (13.4% vs 30.7%, p < 0.001). Conclusion: Lumbar disc arthroplasty utilization reached a nadir in 2017 with subsequent steady increases to 2022, demonstrating an overall increase of 10%. Notably, LDA is increasingly performed in patients with more comorbidities, while hospital length of stay has decreased and outpatient adoption has increased. This highlights advances in perioperative management and surgical safety and emphasizes the now expanding role of this motion-preserving lumbar surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3173

## P0028: Polyethylene core luxation in the Prodisc-L lumbar prosthesis: diagnostic and therapeutic challenges

Jean-Baptiste Odent

1

, Thierry Bégué

1,2,3

, Jean-Charles Aurégan

1,2,3,4

1

Department of Orthopedic, Traumatological and Reconstructive Surgery, Antoine Béclère Hospital, APHP, University Paris Saclay, Clamart, France

2

CIAMS, University Paris-Saclay, Orsay, France

3

CIAMS, University of Orléans, Orléans, France

4

B3OA, University Paris Cité, Paris, France

Introduction: Lumbar total disc replacement (TDR) with devices such as Prodisc-L is an established treatment of lumbar disc disease aiming to restore disc height and biomechanics while preserving segmental mobility. Although overall complication rates are low, polyethylene insert dislocation represents a rare but serious event that may cause disabling pain and necessitate complex revision surgery. Its mechanisms and risk factors remain incompletely understood. The aim of this work is to discuss the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges of this complication, illustrated by four clinical cases. Methods: We retrospectively reviewed three patients who developed polyethylene insert dislocation following Prodisc-L implantation. Clinical presentation, radiological features, surgical management, and outcomes were analyzed. Results: All patients presented with recurrent lumboradicular pain several years after the index procedure. Diagnosis was established by radiographic signs including malalignment of the polyethylene marker, prosthetic plate asymmetry, and iatrogenic spondylolisthesis. With the exception of one case, all patients required revision surgery. Due to severe facet arthropathy or anatomic constraints, conversion to fusion was performed rather than revision arthroplasty. Discussion: Polyethylene insert dislocation appears to be multifactorial. Potential contributors include long-term polyethylene wear or creep, traumatic events, excessive posterior loading during extension, and sagittal imbalance (e.g., high sacral slope, Roussouly type 4). Insufficient prosthetic lordosis, particularly with low-angle implants at L5 -S1, may further predispose to posterior shear forces. Preventive strategies rely on careful patient selection, optimization of implant alignment, and the use of prostheses with adequate lordosis in patients with high sacral slope. Conclusion: Polyethylene insert dislocation after lumbar TDR is a rare but distinctive complication, recognizable through characteristic clinical and radiographic findings. Surgical revision -most often conversion to fusion -remains the standard management. Awareness of risk factors and meticulous preoperative planning are essential to improve prevention, ensure timely diagnosis, and optimize surgical outcomes.
Keywords: Degenerative disc disease (DDD), Lumbar Total disc replacement (lTDR), Lumbar Disc Replacement Dislocation, Lumbar Disc Revision, ProDisc-L.

## ID: AGS26/62

## P0029: Technical insights and implications of thoracolumbar spine and neural tissue harvesting in recently deceased organ donors: a direct anterior approach integrated into multi-organ procurement protocols

Paolo Brigato

1

, Federico Cardahi

2

, Kai Sheng

2

, Hosni Cherif

2

, Li Li

2

, Christopher Coluni

2

, Kirby Upshaw

2

, Jean Ouellet

2

, Lisbet Haglund

2

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

McGill University, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: High-quality human spinal tissue is essential for biomechanical, cellular, and translational spine research. However, standardized harvesting protocols that ensure both anatomical integrity and compatibility with routine multi-organ procurement procedures are lacking. This study describes a novel anterior approach for thoracolumbar spine harvesting that addresses these limitations. Materials and Methods: A direct anterior retroperitoneal technique was used to harvest thoracolumbar spinal columns including spinal cord, dorsal root ganglia (DRGs), and posterior elements en bloc within 1-2 hours after aortic cross-clamping. Targeted osteotomies were performed, and anatomical integrity was verified radiographically. Specimens underwent same-day processing to preserve biological viability of intervertebral discs, facet joints, and native cell populations. Results: The technique was applied in over 340 cadaveric donors, with a mean harvesting time of approximately 30 minutes. All specimens retained gross anatomical integrity, and radiographic analysis confirmed alignment and completeness. High cellular viability was consistently observed, supporting use in multiple experimental contexts. Conclusion: This anterior spinal harvesting method is safe, reproducible, and efficient. It can be seamlessly integrated into standard multi-organ donation workflows without disrupting reconstruction or logistics, offering a valuable protocol for obtaining viable and anatomically intact human spinal tissue for research purposes.

## ID: AGS26/399

## P0030: Trabecular and cortical bone mineral density in human vertebrae: comparison of frozen and thawed states with qCT

Titus Kuehlein

1

, Adrian Cavalcanti Kußmaul

1

1

Musculoskeletal University Center Munich (MUM), LMU University Hospital, Munich, Germany

Introduction: Bone mineral density (BMD) is a key determinant of skeletal strength, relevant in many disciplines. Freezing is widely used to preserve cadaveric bone, but its effect on BMD remains controversal. This study assessed whether a vBMD is altered in frozen versus thawed state of human vertebral bodies measured by quantitative computed tomography (qCT). Material and Methods: Thirty-three human vertebrae (14 C7, 9 Th1, 10 Th2) were stored at -20 °C. Trabecular and cortical vBMD were measured in the frozen state (-15 to -20 °C) using qCT with a calibration phantom. After thawing overnight at room temperature, identical scans were performed. Paired t-tests and Pearson correlation coefficients compared frozen and thawed values. Results: One trabecular specimen was excluded as an outlier. Mean trabecular volumetric bone mineral density (vBMD) was 154.68 mg Ca-HA/ml (frozen) vs. 148.93 mg Ca-HA/ml (thawed), mean difference -5.74 mg Ca-HA/ml (p = 0.164). Cortical vBMD was 219.56 mg Ca-HA/ml (frozen) vs. 219.90 mg Ca-HA/ml (thawed), mean difference 0.34 mg Ca-HA/ml (p = 0.948). Correlations between frozen and thawed values were strong (r = 0.89 trabecular; r = 0.94 cortical). Conclusion: A single freeze-thaw cycle at -20 °C does not significantly alter cortical or trabecular vBMD in human vertebral bodies measured by qCT. These results support the measurement of BMD of frozen specimens, minimizing handling and avoiding unnecessary thawing in research and forensic settings.

## ID: AGS26/435

## P0031: Pre-surgical plasma proteomics improve prediction of post-surgical outcomes for degenerative cervical myelopathy

Aditya Vedantam

1

, Anjishu Banerjee

1

, Amulya Setlur

1

, Matthew Budde

1

, Timothy Meier

1

1

Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Plasma proteins are promising targets for biomarker discovery in DCM due to the ease of obtaining biospecimens, the potential for repeated sampling, and objective quantification. Plasma protein biomarkers have shown strong potential for prognostication in spinal cord disorders such as traumatic spinal cord injury and multiple sclerosis. There are limited data on the role of plasma proteomics for prognostication in degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM). The objective of this study was to determine if plasma protein biomarkers can independently prognosticate outcomes for DCM and whether these biomarkers improve model performance when added to existing clinical and imaging prognosticators. Material and Methods: Patients clinically diagnosed with DCM and scheduled to undergo decompressive surgery were prospectively enrolled from July 2022 to February 2025. Plasma neuronal and inflammatory proteins (n = 126) were quantified before surgery using ultrasensitive NULISA immunoassay. Outcome at three months post-surgery was categorized as achievement of minimum clinically important difference (MCID) for modified Japanese Orthopedic Association (mJOA) and Short Form 36 Physical Component Summary (SF36 PCS) score. Pre-surgical clinical (age, sex, duration of symptoms, baseline disability) and imaging (T2-weighted intramedullary hyperintensity) were also collected. Predictive models were constructed using three feature sets: (1) biomarkers only, (2) clinical and imaging only, and (3) a combined biomarker-clinical-imaging panel. Data-driven feature selection was performed using Elastic Net logistic regression to rank biomarkers by absolute coefficients. The optimal number of features was determined using a 1 - standard error rule for each model. Logistic regression model performance was assessed with out-of-fold predictions to compute AUC, and 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Results: A total of 58 patients (mean age 61.9 ± 11.2 years, 60.3% (n = 35) male) with follow-up MCID data were included. Mean duration of preoperative symptoms was 41.9 months ± 61.9 months. Based on baseline mJOA severity, 25.9% were classified as mild (n = 15), 48.3% as moderate (n = 28), and 25.8% as severe (n = 15). Pre-surgical MRI data was available in 53 patients, of whom 15 (28.3%) had intramedullary T2-weighted hyperintensity. MCID was achieved for mJOA and SF36 PCS in 33 (56.9%) and 28 (48.3%) patients respectively. For predicting patients achieving the MCID for mJOA after surgery, plasma proteomic biomarker panels significantly outperformed clinical-imaging models (AUC 0.77 vs AUC 0.52, +49.2%, p = 0.009). Addition of plasma biomarkers to the clinical-imaging model significantly improved prognostic accuracy (AUC 0.9 vs AUC 0.52, +74.3%, p < 0.001). For predicting patients achieving the MCID for SF 36 PCS after surgery, plasma proteomic biomarker panels outperformed clinical-imaging models (AUC 0.83 vs AUC 0.66, +24.8%, p = 0.08). Addition of plasma biomarkers to the clinical-imaging model significantly improved prognostic accuracy by (AUC 0.85 vs AUC 0.66, +28.6%, p = 0.009). Conclusion: Pre-surgical plasma protein biomarkers accurately predict clinically meaningful improvement in function and quality of life at three months after surgery. Blood-based biomarkers significantly improve existing clinical-imaging prognostic models for DCM and show potential as novel prognostic tools for DCM.

## ID: AGS26/522

## P0032: POSTN/TGF-β1 positive feedback loop promotes mechanical stress-mediated hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum via the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway

Minye He

1

1

Liang Wang, Guangzhou, China

Introduction: Lumbar spinal canal stenosis (LSCS) is a common degenerative spinal disorder often caused by hypertrophy of the ligamentum flavum (LFH). Thickening of the ligamentum flavum leads to compression of the spinal cord and nerve roots, resulting in pain and functional impairment. Although mechanical stress is considered a key factor in ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, its molecular mechanisms remain incompletely understood. This study aims to investigate the role of periostin (POSTN) in LFH, particularly how the POSTN/TGF-β1 positive feedback loop regulates the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, with the goal of providing new theoretical insights for the monitoring, prevention, and treatment of this condition. Material and Methods: This study began with proteomic analysis and single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of ligamentum flavum samples collected from patients undergoing decompressive surgery to identify differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) associated with LFH. Subsequently, in vitro experiments involved applying mechanical stretch to ligamentum flavum cells to simulate in vivo conditions, examining the expression changes of POSTN and TGF-β1 under mechanical stress, and assessing their activation of the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. Techniques such as qRT-PCR, Western blot, and immunofluorescence were used to evaluate the expression levels of fibrosis markers (e.g., COL1A2, COL3, and α-SMA). Finally, in vivo experiments utilizing a bipedal standing mouse model were conducted to validate the role of POSTN in LFH. Results: Proteomic and scRNA-seq analyses revealed significantly elevated POSTN expression in the LFH group compared to the non-LFH group. In vitro experiments demonstrated that mechanical stress upregulated the expression of POSTN and TGF-β1 in ligamentum flavum cells, subsequently activating the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway and increasing the expression of fibrosis markers (COL1A2, COL3, and α-SMA). Additionally, it was confirmed that POSTN and TGF-β1 form a positive feedback loop in ligamentum flavum cells, with POSTN amplifying the pro-fibrotic effects of TGF-β1. Further in vivo experiments validated the critical role of POSTN in LFH: mice injected with recombinant POSTN protein exhibited significant characteristics of ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, whereas inhibition of POSTN markedly slowed the fibrotic process. Conclusion: This study is the first to reveal the important role of POSTN in ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, particularly through the POSTN/TGF-β1 positive feedback loop promoting fibrosis via the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway. This finding provides new insights into the molecular mechanisms of LFH and suggests that POSTN may serve as a novel target for monitoring and treating lumbar spinal canal stenosis. Future research should further explore the potential of POSTN-specific inhibitors in clinical applications and evaluate their efficacy in preventing and treating ligamentum flavum hypertrophy.

## ID: AGS26/538

## P0033: Spinal cord injuries and acute myeloid leukemia share convergent transcriptomic pathways

Josh Callaway

1

, Hania Shahzad

1

, Audrey Zhao

1

, Brian Vasquez

1

, Muhammad Padela

1

, Wyatt Vander Voort

1

, Rolando Roberto

1

, Safdar Khan

1

, Yashar Javidan

1

, Eric Klinerberg

1

, Allan Martin

1

, Hai Le

1

1

UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord injury (SCI) triggers complex cellular and molecular responses that critically influence recovery, yet the underlying mechanisms remain incompletely characterized. Understanding these pathways is essential for developing predictive biomarkers and therapeutic strategies. Comparative omics approaches have the potential to uncover conserved molecular programs across distinct disease contexts, revealing novel insights into injury response and opportunities for drug repurposing. Acute myeloid leukemia (AML), a hematologic malignancy with well-characterized proliferative and stress-response programs, may share overlapping pathways with SCI, particularly in cellular stress, DNA repair, and immune signaling. Leveraging similarities between these conditions could accelerate therapeutic discovery for SCI by drawing on existing oncology drug pipelines. Material and Methods: Transcriptomic data from peripheral blood and injury-site samples collected from a cohort of SCI patients (n = 38) were analyzed, identifying differentially expressed genes (DEGs) relative to healthy controls. To account for generalized trauma effects, non-SCI trauma controls were also included. SCI transcriptomic signatures were then compared with publicly available AML datasets, including 27 primary patient samples and 46 AML cell lines (three of which had duplicate experiments), to identify shared molecular features. Analyses encompassed differential expression testing, pathway enrichment, gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA), and network analysis to pinpoint key hub genes and potential therapeutic targets. Hub gene druggability was further evaluated using publicly available databases, highlighting candidates with existing or investigational therapeutics. Results: We identified 53 genes significantly upregulated in both SCI and AML, after controlling for healthy and non-SCI trauma controls. These included critical cell cycle regulators (BUB1, CCNB1, TOP2A), DNA repair proteins (POLQ, FANCB), and immune modulators (CD6, LCK). Pathway enrichment highlighted convergent biological processes, including mitotic regulation, DNA damage response, and immune signaling. Network analysis revealed hub genes (BUB1, CCNB1, KIF14) with high connectivity and potential druggability. Several of these hubs overlap with AML signatures, suggesting conserved cellular stress and proliferation programs between SCI and AML. Importantly, these shared hubs indicate the potential for cross-disease drug repurposing, whereby therapeutics developed for hematologic malignancies could be evaluated as modulators of SCI pathology, potentially improving neurological outcomes and recovery trajectories. Conclusion: This integrative omics study identifies shared transcriptomic networks between SCI and AML, revealing conserved mechanisms of cellular stress, proliferation, and immune regulation. By highlighting hub genes with translational potential, our findings provide a framework for prognostic biomarker development and targeted therapeutic interventions. Importantly, cross-disease analyses suggest that existing AML-targeted therapeutics could be repurposed for SCI, offering a novel approach to accelerate translational research in trauma care. Overall, these results advance our mechanistic understanding of SCI pathophysiology, establish a comparative omics framework for investigating shared disease biology, and point toward actionable strategies to improve patient outcomes through targeted molecular interventions.

## ID: AGS26/566

## P0034: Evaluating frailty and epigenetic tests for predicting complications following ASD surgery

Steven Zhang

1,2

, Christopher Diaz

1

, Lane McCoy

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Chronologic age (CA) is a traditional risk factor for adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery, but emerging data suggest frailty and epigenetic age (EA) may better reflect biological vulnerability. This study evaluates various aging metrics as predictors of perioperative complications. Material and Methods: ASD patients undergoing ≥ 7-level fusion or 3-column osteotomy were enrolled and monitored for 30-day complications. Blood was collected on surgery day to assess DNA methylation in peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMC). EA was calculated using Horvath’s DNAmAge, Levine’s DNAmAge (LevD), and Levine’s PhenotypicAge (LevP). Frailty was measured using the Edmonton Frailty Index (EFI). The predictive value of each metric - including the differences between CA and EA - was assessed using univariate and multivariate models. Results: 40 surgical ASD patients were enrolled. 23 (57.5%) were revisions. 29 patients (72.5%) received all-posterior and 11 (27.5%) underwent anterior-posterior surgery. 10 (25%) received a three-column osteotomy and average levels fused was 11.5. Complications were pulmonary emboli (N = 2), death (N = 1), reoperation for dehiscence (N = 1), altered mental status (N = 6), and acute kidney injury (N = 5). There were no 30-day readmissions. Univariate analysis revealed that CA, EFI, DNAmAge, LevP, LevD did not predict complication rates (all p-values > 0.1). However, CA-DNAmAge (p = 0.015), CA-LevP (p = 0.033), and CA-LevD (p = 0.045) all demonstrated a predictive value. The multivariate model revealed that CA-DNAmAge was the only significant predictor of complication rate (p = 0.029). Odds ratio (OR) of CA- DNAmAge was 1.03, meaning a 3% increase in complication rate for every year that DNAmAge was greater than CA. Conclusion: Frailty indices and age metrics, including chronological and epigenetic age, do not predict postoperative complications in ASD patients. However, the gap between chronological and epigenetic age (DNAmAge) may aid risk assessment. Future studies should use larger datasets and advanced models to refine these findings and improve patient risk stratification.

## P0035: Impact of prognostic nutritional index in neuromuscular vs early-onset scoliosis surgery

Sezgin Tekin

1

, Hüseyin Coşkun

2

1

Medicalpoint Hospital Gaziantep, Gaziantep, Turkey

2

Ondokuz Mayıs University Hospital Department of Orthopedics, Samsun, Turkey

Introduction: Early-onset scoliosis (EOS) and neuromuscular scoliosis (NMS) represent distinct clinical entities with different perioperative challenges. Nutritional and immunological status, reflected by the prognostic nutritional index (PNI), may influence postoperative outcomes in these patients. Our aim in this study is to compare perioperative characteristics and outcomes between EOS and NMS patients undergoing scoliosis surgery, and to evaluate the association of PNI with hospital stay. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 49 patients who underwent surgery for scoliosis (27 NMS, 22 EOS). Demographic, radiological, and perioperative variables were recorded, including PNI, blood loss, hospital stay, complications, and transfusion requirement. Group comparisons were performed using t-test, Mann-Whitney U, and chi-square tests. Correlations were assessed with Spearman’s test. Results: The NMS group was significantly older (13.7 ± 2.9 vs. 8.4 ± 2.6 years, p < 0.001) and had higher preoperative Cobb angle (75.1° vs. 58.3°, p < 0.001). Hospital stay was longer in NMS patients (13.4 vs. 6.2 days, p < 0.001). Pelvic tilt (63.0% vs. 22.7%, p = 0.005), complications (37.0% vs. 9.1%, p = 0.024), and transfusion requirement (63.0% vs. 31.8%, p = 0.030) were also more frequent in the NMS group. PNI did not differ significantly between groups (44.6 vs. 43.1, p = 0.174). In the overall cohort, no correlation was found between PNI and hospital stay (r = -0.011, p = 0.940). However, in the NMS subgroup, PNI showed a significant negative correlation with length of stay (r = -0.415, p = 0.031). Conclusion: Compared with EOS, NMS patients undergoing scoliosis surgery exhibited higher morbidity, longer hospitalization, and greater transfusion requirements. While overall PNI levels were similar, lower PNI was associated with prolonged hospital stay in NMS patients, suggesting its potential role as a prognostic factor in this subgroup.

## ID: AGS26/827

## P0036: Impact of rod diameter, material, and surface texture on screw-rod gripping capacity in spinopelvic fixation: a biomechanical study

Cale Hendricks

1

, Samuel Hendricks

1

, David Polly

1

, Alec Thingvold

1

, Arin Ellingson

1

1

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

Introduction: Spinopelvic fixation is critical in adult spinal deformity surgery, yet mechanical failure rates range from 4.5-38%. Failure often involves the screw-tulip-rod interface, where rod diameter, composition, and surface texture may affect fixation strength. However, the combined biomechanical effects of these variables remain understudied. This study aimed to evaluate the influence of rod diameter, material, and surface texture on axial gripping capacity (AGC) of the screw-rod interface. Methods: Axial gripping capacity tests were performed in accordance with ASTM F1798-21 using porous fusion/fixation screws (iFuse Granite, SI-Bone). Rods of 5.5 mm and 6.0 mm diameter were tested in cobalt-chrome (CoCr) and titanium (Ti) with three finishes: grit blast, bead blast, and anodized bead blast. Maximum AGC was defined as the peak load within 1.5 mm of displacement; ultimate AGC (UAGC) within 5 mm. Ten trials per condition were performed. A two-way ANOVA with Bonferroni correction evaluated effects of diameter and material/surface combinations (α = 0.05). Results: Each tested variable significantly affected AGC (p < 0.001). Independent of material or finish, 6.0 mm rods had significantly higher AGC than 5.5 mm (2518.0 ± 30.9 N vs. 2091.1 ± 30.9 N; p < 0.001). Among material/finish groups, bead-blasted Ti rods produced the highest AGC (2776.0 ± 61.9 N for 5.5 mm; 2910.3 ± 61.9 N for 6.0 mm), while anodized bead-blast Ti rods yielded the lowest (1656.6 ± 61.9 N and 2076.8 ± 61.9 N, respectively). CoCr grit-blast rods outperformed bead-blast CoCr. UAGC trends mirrored AGC, with minor differences in select comparisons. Pairwise comparisons are in Figures. Discussion: Rod diameter, material, and surface finish each significantly influenced gripping capacity. Larger diameters consistently improved fixation, but surface finish strongly modulated outcomes within each material. Bead-blasted Ti rods provided the highest fixation strength, while anodized bead-blast Ti rods performed worst. Among CoCr rods, grit-blast outperformed bead-blast, reinforcing that surface texture can alter performance even within a single material class. These findings emphasize that rod selection should consider both size and finish, as optimization at the screw-rod interface may reduce mechanical failure in spinopelvic constructs. These findings demonstrate that implant design choices-particularly rod diameter and surface finish-impact the screw-rod interface. Optimizing rod properties may reduce mechanical failure and improve outcomes for patients undergoing spinopelvic fixation.

## ID: AGS26/835

## P0037: Evaluating the role of preoperative coagulation testing before posterior spinal fusion in AIS: is laboratory measurement still necessary?

Ahmad Hussien

1

, Marcus De Matas

1

, Sudarshan Munigangaiah

1

1

Alder Hey Children Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom

Introduction: Preoperative laboratory testing, including coagulation profiles such as prothrombin time (PT), is routinely conducted before posterior spinal fusion (PSF) for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). However, the clinical relevance of mild abnormalities in PT remains uncertain. This study aims to evaluate the impact of elevated PT values on intraoperative bleeding, need for transfusion in AIS patients undergoing PSF. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on AIS patients undergoing PSF in Alder Hey Children's hospital, categorized into two groups based on preoperative PT values: Group 1 (Elevated PT) and Group 2 (Normal PT). Comparative analysis was performed to assess differences in blood loss (BLEEDING), transfusion requirements, Mann-Whitney U and chi-square tests were used due to non-normal distribution of most variables. Results: A total of 76 patients were analyzed (38 per group). Group 1 had significantly higher PT values (mean 12.20 vs. 10.89, p < 0.0001). Despite this, no significant differences were observed in intraoperative blood loss (mean 217.7 ml vs. 200.1 ml, p = 0.97) or transfusion rates (p = 1.0). Conclusion: Routine preoperative PT testing may not be predictive of bleeding risk or transfusion need in AIS patients undergoing PSF. The findings suggest that mildly elevated PT values are not associated with adverse surgical outcomes. Prospective studies are needed to refine preoperative assessment protocols and avoid unnecessary delays or concerns over mild coagulation abnormalities.

## P0038: Real-time AI object detection in spine endoscopy: development and validation using spine scope videos

Yoon Jae Cho

1

, Jung Sub Lee

1

, Tae Sik Goh

1

, Hansol Kim

1

1

Pusan National University Hospital, Busan, South Korea

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery relies on precise identification and differentiation of anatomical structures. To support this, we developed an AI-based object detection model capable of accurately distinguishing structures in spine scope videos. Material and Methods: This multicenter study included 112 patients undergoing spine endoscopic surgery. Videos were analyzed by extracting one frame per 300 frames, resulting in a diverse dataset. Patients were divided into training and validation cohorts in a 4:1 ratio. Key anatomical structures were annotated in the extracted frames to create a labeled dataset for training. Model performance was evaluated using Intersection over Union (IoU) to measure the overlap between predicted and ground-truth regions, along with Mean Average Precision (mAP) for classification within detected regions. Results: The model achieved an average IoU of 0.753 and mAP of 0.857 across the validation dataset, indicating its ability to accurately localize and classify key anatomical structures. Multicenter data enhanced the model's generalizability across diverse imaging conditions. Conclusion: The proposed AI model successfully distinguishes anatomical structures in spine scope videos with high accuracy. By focusing on region-specific classification and precise localization, the model demonstrates its potential for enhancing surgical navigation and reducing intraoperative errors. Further research will explore real-time deployment in clinical settings.

## ID: AGS26/1170

## P0039: Development and impact of an AI-assisted model for spinal radiograph interpretation: enhancing accuracy and efficiency of mSASSS assessment

Yoon Jae Cho

1

, Jung Sub Lee

1

, Tae Sik Goh

1

, Kihun Kim

1

, Jae Young Kim

1

, Hansol Kim

1

1

Pusan National University Hospital, Busan, South Korea

Introduction: The modified Stoke Ankylosing Spondylitis Spinal Score (mSASSS) is the gold standard for assessing structural progression in ankylosing spondylitis (AS), but manual scoring is time-consuming and subject to inter-observer variability. We developed and externally validated an end-to-end deep learning model for automated mSASSS scoring and evaluated its clinical utility in a paired crossover reader study. Material and Methods: A convolutional neural network with a ResNet-UPerNet backbone was trained on 1,159 radiographs (877 cervical, 282 lumbar) comprising 13,908 annotated vertebral corners from 823 AS patients. External validation was performed on 100 independent radiographs. Automated scoring performance was evaluated using accuracy, F1-score, Cohen’s κ, and intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC). To assess clinical utility, two orthopedic surgeons scored the same 100 cases under two conditions (human-only vs AI-assisted hybrid) in a randomized crossover design. Primary endpoints were mean absolute error (MAE) reduction and reading time difference. Results: The segmentation network achieved mean IoU of 93.7% (cervical) and 96.7% (lumbar). Automated mSASSS scoring reached 83.5% exact grade-match accuracy, κ = 0.72, and ICC = 0.963 with a mean absolute error of 2.4 ± 3.1 points. In the reader study, hybrid scoring significantly reduced per-case MAE (Δ = 0.36 points, 95% CI 0.22-0.50, p < 0.001, dz = 0.55) and shortened reading time by (1.63 minutes, 95% CI 1.44-1.82, p < 0.001, dz = 1.65), with higher corner-level accuracy (84.7% vs 79.2%, p < 0.001). Conclusion: This end-to-end AI model enables automated, accurate, and reproducible mSASSS scoring. When integrated into a hybrid reading workflow, it improves observer agreement, enhances per-corner accuracy, and significantly reduces interpretation time, supporting its potential for routine clinical implementation.
