## P0040: Estimation of spinal curvature using Chest X-ray and generative models: a deep learning approach to Cobb angle prediction

Yoon Jae Cho

1

, Jung Sub Lee

1

, Tae Sik Goh

1

, Hansol Kim

1

, Kihun Kim

1

, Minjun Choi

1

1

Pusan National University Hospital, Busan, South Korea

Introduction: Whole-spine radiographs are not routinely obtained in all clinical settings, and spinal deformities are often detected incidentally on chest X-rays. If spinal curvature could be estimated from chest radiographs, it would serve as a useful screening tool. This study aimed to estimate the Cobb angle using chest X-ray images and generative deep learning models. Material and Methods: A total of 534 single-channel chest X-ray images (160×128 resolution) were collected and preprocessed via standard augmentation techniques (rescaling, cropping, contrast/brightness tuning, and flipping). A Variational Autoencoder (VAE) was first trained in a self-supervised manner using masked autoencoding. Its encoder and decoder weights were then transferred to initialize a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) model for outpainting tasks. The GAN generated extended images for scoliosis visualization, and Cobb angles were measured and compared to ground truth annotations.. Results: The self-supervised VAE outperformed the vanilla VAE with a lower FID score (240.06 vs. 251.31) and higher Inception Score (1.67 vs. 1.54), suggesting improved generation quality. The GAN model achieved a FID score of 130.91 for outpainted chest X-ray images. The average difference in Cobb angle estimation between real and generated images was 2.25°, indicating high accuracy and clinical feasibility. Conclusion: Chest X-rays augmented with generative models can be used to approximate spinal curvature with clinically acceptable error. This approach has potential to support scoliosis screening or follow-up in settings where full-spine imaging is unavailable.

## ID: AGS26/1300

## P0041: Evaluation of students perception between using live or cadaveric animal model for endoscopic surgery training: a cross-sectional study

Rafael Lindi Sugino

1

, Weby Mizael

2

, Luciana Cintra

1

, Guilherme Gregores

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

3

, Gabriel Pokorny

3

1

Einstein Hospital Israelita, São Paulo, Brazil

2

Weby Mizael, São Paulo, Brazil

3

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Simulation on living tissue bridges the gap between inert/virtual trainers and cadaver labs by exposing trainees to bleeding, haemostasis and real-time complication management-skills considered essential for modern endoscopic spine surgery. Porcine and ovine columns are particularly valued for their anatomical compatibility, yet ethical, logistic and anatomical caveats persist. This study explored how postgraduate endoscopy students perceive the educational value, realism and limitations of animal models within a competency-based curriculum. Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional, anonymised survey of postgraduate spine-surgery students after institutional ethics approval. The 12-item questionnaire captured prior exposure, frequency of use, perceived usefulness, realism, challenges and confidence gains for both in-vivo and cadaveric models. Categorical responses were summarised as counts and percentages in R 4.2. Results: Thirty-four students completed the survey (response rate 100 %). Although 73 % had some previous contact with animal models, only 30 % had practised more than twice. In-vivo models were judged “useful” or “very useful” by 90 % of respondents, and 91 % felt they adequately simulate human endoscopic procedures; the chief difficulties were controlling the surgical environment and managing animal reactivity (both 21 %). Cadaveric specimens were also rated useful/very useful by 79 %, but half lamented the absence of physiological response and 33 % cited difficulty executing precise movements. When forced to choose a single platform, two-thirds (66 %) favoured in-vivo models for greater realism. Notably, every participant reported that either model increased their confidence, and 64 % said it “greatly” did so. Conclusion: Animal laboratories remain a highly valued, confidence-building component of endoscopic-spine training programmes. Trainees perceive in-vivo models as more realistic, yet acknowledge cadaveric advantages in anatomical visualisation and animal-welfare considerations. Integrating both platforms - while addressing technical and ethical challenges - appears essential for a balanced, high-fidelity curriculum.

## ID: AGS26/1307

## P0042: Nutritional optimization with vitamins D3 and K2 as a strategy to prevent implant-associated complications

Oleksii Nekhlopochyn

1

, Vadim Verbov

1

, Milan Vorodi

1

, Ievgen Cheshuk

1

1

Romodanov Neurosurgery Institute, Kyiv, Ukraine

Introduction: The widespread use of implants in spinal surgery, particularly interbody devices and pedicle screw systems, has significantly improved treatment outcomes. However, implant-related complications such as screw loosening, cage migration, and pseudarthrosis remain clinically important challenges. Bone mineral density (BMD) and vitamin D status are key determinants of implant stability, yet deficiency of vitamin D is highly prevalent in Eastern Europe. Combined vitamin D3 and K2 supplementation may provide additional benefits for bone quality and fusion stability, but clinical evidence in spinal surgery remains limited. The aim was to evaluate the relationship between vitamin D status, bone mineral density, and the risk of implant-associated complications in patients undergoing spinal fusion, and to assess the role of postoperative vitamin D3 and K2 supplementation in improving outcomes. Materials and Methods: A retrospective single-center cohort study was conducted at the Romodanov Neurosurgery Institute (Kyiv, Ukraine) from January 2023 to December 2024. A total of 250 patients (18-81 years) who underwent thoracolumbar stabilization with pedicle screws and/or interbody cages were analyzed. Baseline assessments included demographics, BMI, serum 25-(OH)D3, and BMD (measured via CT, HU scale at L1). Patients were stratified into three groups: (1) normal vitamin D levels requiring no correction (NNC), (2) patients with deficiency receiving vitamin D3 and K2 supplementation (SRD), and (3) patients with deficiency but untreated (NNT). Implant-related complications were classified using original radiological grading systems for screw loosening and cage migration. Statistical analyses included regression modeling and correlation testing. Results: Vitamin D deficiency (< 20 ng/ml) or insufficiency (20-29 ng/ml) was identified in more than 70% of patients, with a strong correlation between serum 25-(OH)D3 and BMD (r = 0.68). Younger patients with normal vitamin D demonstrated significantly higher BMD and lower complication rates. After four months of supplementation, all patients in the SRD group achieved normal 25-(OH)D3 levels. BMD showed a modest but consistent increase in the SRD group, while it declined in untreated patients. At six months, the risk of pedicle screw loosening was reduced by 69.8% (OR 0.30), and cage migration by 56.2% (OR 0.44) in the SRD group compared to untreated patients. At 12 months, reductions reached 85.1% for screw loosening (OR 0.15) and 64.7% for cage migration (OR 0.35). Most complications in untreated patients required revision surgery, while those in supplemented patients were mild, non-progressive, and did not necessitate reoperation. Conclusions: Postoperative correction of vitamin D deficiency with combined D3 and K2 supplementation significantly improves bone quality dynamics, reduces implant-associated complications, and enhances fusion stability. These results suggest that the adaptive response of bone to implantation, supported by nutritional optimization, is more important than absolute BMD values alone. Combined vitamin D3 and K2 therapy should be considered an effective adjunct to spinal fusion surgery, particularly in populations with endemic vitamin D deficiency.

## ID: AGS26/1325

## P0043: What exactly are we looking at? A validation study for abul cranial shift phenomenon

Muhammet Kocaoğlu

1

, Mustafa Kavasoğlu

1

, Osman Ceylan

1

, Ali Öner

1

1

Baltalimanı Kemik Hastalıkları Hastanesi, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: The Abul Cranial Shift (ACS) phenomenon was first described by Abul et al. in 2023 as a novel entity observed in patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). This phenomenon refers to the association between the thoracolumbar transitional vertebra and L5 sacralization in the context of AIS. In the present study, we investigated the prevalence of this phenomenon in individuals with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis as well as in the healthy population. Material and Methods: Radiographic data were retrospectively analyzed from three cohorts: 493 patients who had undergone surgical treatment for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), 493 control subjects, and 328 patients who were either conservatively managed or are currently under follow-up for scoliosis or kyphosis. Standard full-spine standing radiographs (orthoradiograms) were independently evaluated by two observers, each performing two separate assessments with a six-week interval between sessions. The following parameters were examined: presence of thoracolumbar transitional vertebrae, presence of a C7 transverse process, presence or absence of rib remnants at T12, rib count, and the presence of sacralized lumbar vertebrae. Intraobserver and interobserver correlation coefficients were calculated to assess reliability. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: The prevalence of Abul Cranial Shift (ACS) was found to be 17% (84/493) in the surgically treated AIS group and 14% (73/493) in the control group. Intraobserver agreement coefficients ranged from 0.501 to 0.735, whereas interobserver agreement coefficients ranged from 0.576 to 0.595. Conclusion: Although the Abul Cranial Shift (ACS) has been described in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), it is evident that variations in spinal alignment also exist within the normal population. Recognition of ACS is important in the preoperative planning of AIS surgery in order to prevent potential level selection errors during the procedure. Furthermore, given its presence in the normal population, the potential associations of ACS with other spinal deformities remain an open field for future investigation.

## ID: AGS26/2408

## P0044: CALM1 polymorphism in degenerative cervical myelopathy of the Indian cohort

Sarvdeep Dhatt

1

1

PGIMER, Chandigarh, India

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM)- often termed cervical spondylotic myelopathy- is the most com-mon cause of spinal cord injury in adults, arising from age-related degenerative changes in the spine. Genetic susceptibility may modulate which individuals develop myelopathy under these mechanical stresses. Calmodulin-1 (CALM1) is a gene encoding calmodulin, a key calcium-binding protein involved in cartilage maintenance and neural signalling, and CALM1 variants have been implicated in degenerative joint disease. We hypothesized that a CALM1 polymorphism could influence DCM risk. Material and Methods: In this pilot case-control study, 48 DCM patients (with imaging-confirmed cervical spondylotic cord compression) {males (n = 42, 84.36%), females (n = 6, 12.76%)}with cervical disc anterolisthesis (degenerative), chronic neck pain (> 3 months duration), and/or quadriparesis with positive bilateral Babinski’s sign, and 57 controls (with imaging- no significant degenerative changes in the disc) {males (n = 47, 82.45%), females (n = 10, 17.54%)} with acute neck pain (< 3months dura-tion), cervical spondylosis or acute traumatic spine injuries were recruited from an Indian population. This design aimed to distinguish genetic factors specific to degenerative pathology. All participants underwent Sanger sequencing of the CALM1 gene to identify polymorphisms. The primary variant analyzed was a common single-nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) in CALM1’s regulatory region. Genotype and allele frequencies were compared between DCM cases and controls using Fish-er’s exact test, and odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated. No formal sample size calculation was performed due to limited prior data, as this study was exploratory. Results: The cases group primarily exhibited three new intronic variations (NG_013338.1: g.11552G > A, NG_013338.1:g.5361T > C, and NG_013338.1:g.11731G > A of the CALM1 gene). Among these, NG_013338.1:g.11731G > A was significantly associated with DCM, with an odds ratio of 0.43 (p-value = 0.0117). In silico (i.e., using computer-based prediction models) analysis revealed that these intronic variations changed the splicing regulatory elements, which could impact gene expression. The CALM1 risk-allele frequency was similar in DCM patients (27.1%) and controls (14.0%, OR = 0.43, 95% CI 0.2-1.35; p = 0.012). No other CALM1 coding variants showed significant case-control association. Rou-tine laboratory parameters (e.g., calcium profiling, serum Vitamin B12 levels) were comparable between groups, suggesting no metabolic confounders. Conclusion: In this Indian cohort, CALM1 polymorphism was not significantly associated with degenerative cervical myelopathy. The variant’s effect, if any, appears to be of low pathogenicity. These findings, while preliminary, provide novel population-specific data and highlight the importance of further research. This pilot study lays the groundwork for larger studies to elucidate genetic contributions to DCM, underscoring that CALM1 and similar variants merit continued investiga-tion as hypothesis-generating candidates.

## ID: AGS26/2504

## P0045: A new self-assessment score for simulation in spine surgery at low cost with learning curve at the operating room

Marc Prod'homme

1

, Arun Thangavelu

2

, Jonas Müller

3

, Sadat Mazreku

4

, Lionel Helfer

5

, Blaise-Dominique Zambaz

1

, Alexandre Simonin

6

1

Clinic CIC, Clarens, Switzerland

2

EUROSPINE Center of Excellence, Brig, Switzerland

3

Institute of Trauma and Orthopaedics of Swiss Leman (ITOLS), Lausanne, Switzerland

4

Intercantonal Hospital of the Broye (HIB), Payerne, Switzerland

5

Medical Center West Lausanne (CMLO), Prilly, Switzerland

6

Sion Cantonal Hospital, Sion, Switzerland

Introduction: Surgical simulation improves surgical skills through repetitive standardized exercises. Cadaver labs or computerized simulators used for surgical training are costly and there is a lack of access for most of the spine surgeons. The goal of this pilot study was to define a new self-assessment score evaluating spine surgery procedures in simulation, and to correlate it with the learning curve at the operating room. Material and Methods: Simulation (S) and operative (O) groups were defined. Data of one spine fellow were prospectively collected during 9 months. 5 basic spine procedures (microdiscectomy, freehand pedicle screw insertion, decompressive laminectomy, dural repair and cervical anterior discectomy) were assessed. A mark /6 for each following topic regarding anatomy recognition, accuracy of the procedure, estimated speed, ability to self-criticize and correct, and procedure time with its quartile’s mark, lead to a topic score /30. Self-confidence and self-progression were assessed on /10 each. Thus, with doubling and adding all these scores, a final score was obtained /100. Learning curve cumulative sum (LC-CUSUM), comparison and correlation studies were performed. Average run length (ARL) was estimated when the scores were upon a mean value. Cumulative costs in United States Dollars (USD) were also collected. Results: 50 procedures were performed, 25 in each group. For groups S and O, mean topic scores were 19.6/30 vs. 20.2/30 respectively, p = 0.57. Average self-assessment scores were 11.0/20 vs. 11.5/20 respectively, p = 0.51. The mean S and O final scores were 61.0/100 vs. 63.3/100 respectively, p = 0.52. There was no correlation between S and O overall and detailed scores, p > 0.05, except for the time mark (p < 0.01). A learning curve effect was observed after an ARL of 14 procedures in simulation and of 17 in operative conditions, at 6 months, with a strong correlation in LC-CUSUM between the groups (p < 0.01). Overall costs of the simulation training were 250 USD. Conclusion: The described self-assessment score of spine surgery simulation showed good preliminary results with a strong correlation of the learning curve at the operating room. Costs were low and the model adequate for spine fellowship training. Further studies on larger samples are needed to confirm these results.

## ID: AGS26/3054

## P0046: Enhanced fusion and safety profile of a novel rhBMP-2-containing biphasic ceramic putty in a sheep lumbar interbody fusion model

Jumi Han

1

, Hyun Jung Park

2

, Hyun Seung Ryu

2

, Mi Young Ryu

2

1

CG USA, Irvine, United States

2

CGBio Co., Ltd., Seongnam-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea

Introduction: This pivotal ovine lumbar interbody fusion study evaluated the safety and efficacy of an injectable ceramic-based bone graft containing rhBMP-2, compared with iliac crest autologous bone graft (ICBG). Spinal fusion is commonly performed to treat various pathological spinal conditions. An ideal graft should possess both osteoinductive and osteoconductive properties. The ceramic-based bone graft consists of rhBMP-2 bound within the micropores of a β-TCP/HA composite, enabling slower and more gradual release than rhBMP-2 delivered on an absorbable collagen sponge (ACS). Material and Methods: Ninety-six skeletally mature female sheep underwent single-level lumbar interbody fusion at L3-L4 using PEEK cages. Each animal received either ICBG or the rhBMP-2/ceramic composite graft at 0.2, 0.4, or 2.0 mg/cc. At 30, 90, 180, and 365 days postoperatively, radiographic (uCT/CT), histomorphometric, biomechanical (manual palpation), histopathological, and clinical pathology evaluations were conducted. Local and systemic safety was qualitatively and quantitatively assessed according to ISO 10993-6 and ISO 10993-11. Results: uCT analyses showed rhBMP-2 dose-dependent improvements in fusion, with the high-dose group achieving 100% complete fusion at both 180 and 365 days. Low- to mid-dose groups showed comparable fusion rates to ICBG (50-66.7%) at 365 days. Histomorphometry revealed progressive new bone formation in all groups without significant intergroup differences at 180 or 365 days. Histopathology showed more mature trabecular bone in the rhBMP-2 groups from day 180 onward, and manual palpation confirmed fusion in most animals by day 180. Local reactions were mild, and no systemic toxicity was observed. Residual graft material degraded markedly within 90 days and remained stable (steady state) at the local site thereafter. Conclusion: The injectable ceramic-based bone graft containing rhBMP-2 demonstrated fusion outcomes comparable or superior to ICBG while enhancing bone quality and bridging in a dose-dependent manner and maintaining a favorable safety profile. These findings support this graft as a safe and effective alternative to autograft bone for spinal fusion and provide a basis for future clinical studies.

## ID: AGS26/3310

## P0047: Assessment of osteogenesis on three-dimensionally printed titanium implants with complex surface topography

Gregory Paschal

1

, Philip Paschal

1

, Fedan Avrumova

1

, Sydney Warwick

1

, Deven Bhatnagar

1

, Gbolabo Sokunbi

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: With current advancements in additive manufacturing technology, implant surface modifications can aid in osseointegration. Surface characteristics in the macro, micro and nanoscale ranges allow for texturized surfaces designed to guide bone formation through the cellular response thereby optimizing osteoconductivity. Osteoblastic differentiation favors a micro-roughness while cellular adhesion is enhanced by nano-roughness. Investigation of surface roughness complexity in vitro can predict bony on-growth in vivo. The purpose of this study is to evaluate the response of mesenchymal stem cells to titanium implants with different surface morphology (roughness). We hypothesize that there is an ideal range of roughness that will best promote both cellular adhesion and proliferation towards bony ongrowth. Material and Methods: Titanium coupons were three dimensionally printed with varying surface roughness (smooth, very fine, fine, medium, rough, and very rough) and cultured with human mesenchymal stem cells for a total of 7 days. Six surfaces of increasing roughness were selected for this study. Cell lysate and media were collected and assayed for viability and via ELISA for cell signaling indicators of progression toward osteocyte differentiation such as osteocalcin, interleukins (IL) 1b, 6, 8, and 10, BMPs 2, 4, and 7, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEG-F). Fluorescent microscopy and DNA quantification were used to determine cell viability. Cell morphology and attachment to the titanium surface were imaged using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Results: Cell viability tests showed that cells were able to grow on all surfaces, yet performed best on surfaces with mid-range roughnesses. Electron microscopy showed cellular attachment and communication on all samples except smooth, and at a greater amount in the medium to rough range. Osteocalcin expression was measured on all surfaces, suggesting cellular differentiation towards bone formation. BMP2, BMP4, and BMP7 were all measured for all surfaces, suggesting angiogenesis. Live/dead immunofluorescence imaging showed high viability on all surfaces. Conclusion: Cellular attachment and biological response are necessary for osteoblast differentiation and bone formation. Current 3D printing technology allows for the production of implants with surface morphology that optimizes an osteopromotive biological response. The results of our study can hopefully further our understanding of the specific characteristics that optimize bone formation for implants.

## ID: AGS26/422

## P0047.1: Outweighting Spine Surgery: the Impact of Bariatric Surgery on Spinal Health.

Luis López Urda

1

, Renaud Lafage

2

, Juan José Correa Gámiz

1

, María Isabel Almagro Gil

1

, Adoración Garzón Alfaro

3

, Rafael Carlos Gómez Sánchez

1

, Jesús García Rubio

1

, Cristina Carvajal Pedrosa

1

, Pedro Hernández Cortés

1

, Nicolas Plais

1

1

Hospital Universitario Clínico San Cecilio, Granada, Spain

2

Lennox Hill Hospital, Northwell, New York, United States

3

Hospital Joan XXIII, Tarragona, Spain

Introduction: Low back pain and reduced mobility are common in patients with morbid obesity. Meta-analyses have demonstrated worse surgical outcomes and higher complication rates in obese individuals undergoing spinal surgery. This study aims to evaluate the impact of weight loss through bariatric surgery on spinal health, assessing both radiological and clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: Fifty patients with morbid obesity (mean BMI: 44.8 kg/m2 ± 4.6 ranging from 35.9 to 54.2) underwent bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy or gastric bypass) between October 2020 and April 2023 were enrolled. Clinical assessment included properative and 1-year postoperative lumbar and leg pain using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS), as well as disability using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), along with long cassette and flexion-extension X-rays to measure pelvic, lumbar, and global sagittal alignment parameters. Change in health-related quality of life (RQoL) and sagittal alignment were analyzed using paired statistical tests (t-test or Wilcoxon, as appropiated). Results: The mean age at the time of the bariatric surgery was 40 ± 10 year-old, with 65.3% of patients being female. The average weight loss was 36.6 ± 14.9 kg (ranging from 11 to 75 kg). Clinical Outcomes Preoperatively, the median ODI was 16 (IQR 3 to 40) with 57.1 % of patients reporting minimal disability (ODI <20) and 35% reporting severe disability (ODI >40). In term of pain, 33% reported elevated back pain (VAS >7) and 37% reported elevated leg pain, with 16% reporting both. At one-year postoperatively, ODI improved significantly overall (median ΔODI= -8 IQR -3 to -23) as well as within each group of disability (all p<0.05), with the greatest reductions in those with higher baseline ODI. Notably, only 18.2% of patients with preoperative ODI >40 remained above this threshold after surgery, while 45.5% improved to a level similar to the general population (ODI: 8.8 ± 6). Back and leg pain also showed significant reductions (both p<0.001). Among patients with elevated pain preoperative, respectively 31% and 44% reported minimal or no back and leg pain (VAS<3) post-operatively. Weight loss was significantly correlated with improvements in back and leg pain (r=0.35 and 0.41 respectively; both p<0.05). Radiological Findings At baseline, degenerative spinal changes were common: 46% had disc degeneration, 24% spondylolisthesis (isthmic or degenerative), and 14% presented degenerative scoliosis. Spinal alignment demonstrated minimal deviation from asymptomatic population (PI-LL: -1.2 ± 9.1; TPA: 12.1° ± 7.1). Preoperative pelvic tilt (17.0° ± 7) was slightly elevated compared to normal populations, though not statistically significant. Postoperatively, kyphosis increased significantly, suggesting global spinal relaxation. Conclusion: Obesity is not synonym of back pain or disability, but patients with severe back pain and high disability present significant improvement of their quality of life following bariatric surgery. Weight loss via bariatric surgery (or GLP-1 receptor agonists) may significantly reduce the need for spinal surgery in obese individuals. While sagittal alignment remains largely unchanged, obesity seems to accelerate degenerative processes associated with the aging cascade. Obesity is not synonym of back pain or disability, but patients with severe back pain and high disability present significant improvement of their quality of life following bariatric surgery. Weight loss via bariatric surgery (or GLP-1 receptor agonists) may significantly reduce the need for spinal surgery in obese individuals. While sagittal alignment remains largely unchanged, obesity seems to accelerate degenerative processes associated with the aging cascade.

## ID: AGS26/116

## P0048: Relationship between postoperative variation of adjacent segment three-dimensional kinematics and postoperative PI-LL in patients with degenerative lumbar scoliosis

Fei Xu

1

, Weishi Li

1

1

Orthopaedic Department, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: This study investigated the variation in pre- and post-operative range of motion (ROM) in adjacent segments in patients with degenerative lumbar scoliosis (DLS) following posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF). Material and Methods: Eight patients with symptomatic DLS were analysed using a biplane radiographic imaging system while adopting different postures. Synchronised biplane radiographs from L1 to S1 and the motions of each vertebra were acquired pre- and post-operatively. Six degrees-of-freedom (DOF) of kinematic data were compared between different postoperative pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis (PI-LL) groups (group A: PI-LL=−10°∼10°; group B: PI-LL=10°∼20°). Results: After surgery, the axial rotational movement (primary rotation) during flexiong-extension, bending and torsion in the first adjacent segment at L3-4 decreased significantly in three patients (8.14 ± 2.78 vs 15.13 ± 6.71; 8.36 ± 5.59 vs 9.08 ± 3.57; 5.07 ± 0.56 vs 9.25 ± 5.06). At this level, the torsion around crania-caudal (CC) axis during bending (1.48 ± 1.01 vs 7.05 ± 5.84, p < 0.05) and flexion around mediolateral (ML) axis and bending rotation around anterioposterior (AP) axis during torsion decreased postoperatively (6.37 ± 6.01 vs 13.83 ± 8.12, 4.53 ± 1.97 vs 13.06 ± 6.65; p < 0.05, p < 0.05). After surgery, in L1-2 segment, translation along the ML direction decreased during bending (3.69 ± 2.12 vs 14.76 ± 7.99, p < 0.05). In the adjacent L5-S1 segment, primary flexed rotation around ML axis increased in group B postoperatively during flexion-extension, but decreased in group A (6.08 ± 1.17 vs −13.41 ± 2.99, p < 0.05). Coupled flexed rotation around ML axis decreased showed the opposite trend during bending (−10.76 ± 5.51 vs 18.12 ± 39.83, p < 0.05). Conclusion: Postoperative coupled motion at the adjacent segment decreased, which indicates an improved balance of the spinal order compared to before the surgery. However, primary motion changed according to the location of the upper instrumented vertebrae. Our results indicate postoperative PI-LL values between 10° and 20° were associated with lower coupled motion and higher primary motion at L5-S1.

## ID: AGS26/325

## P0049: The skiving phenomenon in spinal bone drilling: influence of drill parameters on accuracy and clinical implications

Raffael Cintean

1

, Florian Gebhard

1

, Alexander Eickhoff

1

1

Department of Orthopedic Trauma, University Medical Center Ulm, Ulm, Germany

Introduction: Spinal interbody fusion is performed in more than 250,000 cases annually in the United States, with pedicle screw fixation considered the most stable dorsal anchorage. Precise placement of implants is essential, as misplacement can result in vascular or neurological injury. A major challenge during bone drilling is the skiving phenomenon, where the drill bit slips along the curved cortical surface before penetration, leading to malpositioning, thermal damage, or even revision surgery. This study systematically investigates the influence of drilling parameters on skiving to derive recommendations for clinical practice and autonomous drilling systems. Material and Methods: Drilling trials were performed on standardized artificial bone blocks (Sawbones®, Sweden) using a CNC milling/drilling machine (FP4A, DECKEL AG, Germany). Parameters tested included rotational speed (1000, 2000 rpm), feed rate (6-120 mm/min), and entrance angle (90°, 75°, 60°, 45°). Three drill bit designs were evaluated: a standard surgical drill, a centering drill, and a milling cutter. Each drilling attempt was recorded from two perspectives and analyzed using OpenCV-based tracking algorithms and MATLAB to quantify deviations from the desired entry point. Skiving was defined as a deviation > 0.5 mm. Results: In total, 65 drillings were performed, of which 61 were analyzable. Skiving occurred in 58% of cases, with a mean deviation of -1.11 ± 1.24 mm (x-direction) and 0.44 ± 0.36 mm (y-direction). At 90° entry, no significant drift was observed, while reduced entrance angles markedly increased skiving. At 75°, 75% of trials showed drift, and at 60° all trials demonstrated significant deviation (maximum −5.04 mm, 1.65 mm). At 45°, standard drills failed to penetrate the cortex. Higher feed rates correlated with increased drift, while higher rotational speed (2000 rpm) slightly reduced downward but not lateral skiving. Among drill types, the centering drill performed best at moderate angles, while the milling cutter proved unsuitable. Conclusion: Bone drilling accuracy is strongly influenced by entrance angle, feed rate, and drill bit design. Perpendicular drilling minimizes skiving, while oblique entry angles significantly increase deviation. Lower feed rates improve accuracy but prolong drilling times. Although higher rotational speeds provide more stable trajectories, they must be balanced against risks of thermal osteonecrosis. For clinical practice, perpendicular entry, use of pilot indentations, and guidance sleeves are recommended to minimize skiving and improve pedicle screw placement accuracy. These findings also provide a foundation for developing control algorithms in autonomous bone drilling systems

## ID: AGS26/455

## P0050: Navigated vs. non-navigated thoracolumbar pedicle screws: implications on biomechanical stability including the costovertebral joint

Jula Gierse

1

, Joel Stöter

1

, Benno Bullert

1

, Paul Grützner

1

, Sven Vetter

1

1

BG Unfallklinik Ludwigshafen, Ludwigshafen, Germany

Introduction: Instabilities and injuries of the thoracolumbar spine often require surgical stabilization. A standard procedure is dorsal instrumentation using pedicle screws and rigid rods as a fixateur interne. The placement of the screws is a challenging procedure due to the proximity to neurovascular structures. Navigation systems are gaining popularity and are used more frequently to minimize the risk of injury and increase the screw accuracy. Pedicle screws show high biomechanical stability. However, this stability varies due to different factors such as screw-pedicle-ratio, bone density and distance to pedicle cortex. The aim of this study was to analyze the biomechanical stability of navigated and non-navigated thoracolumbar pedicle screws depending on screw accuracy. Material and Methods: Four fresh-frozen human specimens underwent percutaneous dorsal instrumentation from T6 to L3 using pedicle screws. A 3D-navigation system (NAV) was used unilaterally, and fluoroscopy (2D) was used contralaterally. Screw accuracy was evaluated according to the Gertzbein and Robbins classification (Grades A-E). Screws graded A and B (perforations < 2 mm) were considered accurate. Additionally, screws located in the costovertebral joint with a screw-pedicle ratio > 1.0 were considered as accurate (Grade CV). The spine was removed from the specimens, and the individual vertebral bodies were prepared to the bone. The vertebral bodies were placed into a 3D-printed mole and fixed using a polyol-isocyanate solution (ratio 1:1). Subsequently, force controlled cyclic loading and unloading were applied. Cyclic loading of the screws started at a bending moment of 0.5 Nm and was increased up to 2.5 Nm in 0.5 Nm increments. The screws were subjected to 100 cycles at each loading level at approximately 0.1-0.2 Hz, resulting in a total of 500 cycles per screw. Afterwards the screws underwent testing of pullout force. Results: A total of 52 thoracic and 23 lumbar pedicle screws (n = 75) were tested regarding pullout force. Five screws were excluded from testing due to mechanical failure of the pedicles. Of the 75 screws, 67 (89%) were rated as accurate (Grade CV or higher) and eight as inaccurate (Grade C or lower). Statistical analysis of screw accuracy showed a significant difference between navigation and fluoroscopy (p = 0.005*). There was no statistical significance in the overall comparison of optimally (NAV: median = 791.5 N; 2D: median = 798.5 N; Overall: median = 798.5 N) and suboptimally rated screws (NAV: median = 290.5 N; 2D: median = 238.0 N; Overall: median = 238.0 N; p = 0.06). The pullout force of thoracic screws placed intrapedicularly (918.19 ± 576.59 N) was significantly higher compared with screws placed in the costovertebral joint (515.95 ± 461.72 N; p = 0.008*). Conclusion: This study demonstrated differences in pullout force regarding pedicle screw position. In terms of biomechanical stability, screws placed in the costovertebral joint showed significantly lower pullout forces than fully intrapedicular screws, regardless of perforation. Regarding biomechanical stability screw placement in the costovertebral joint should be avoided.

## ID: AGS26/979

## P0051: Biomechanical evaluation of titanium versus carbon fiber-reinforced polyetheretherketone composite implants for spine tumor surgery: a finite element study

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Balaji Harinathan

1

, Karthik Devaraj

1

, Abdul Mounnem Yassin Kassab

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Narayan Yoganandan

1

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: The management of spinal tumors remains surgically complex, often necessitating durable instrumentation to achieve stability. Traditionally, titanium alloys have been the predominant material used in spinal oncology implants. However, carbon fiber-reinforced composites have recently gained attention as a promising alternative. These implants provide several potential advantages, including radiolucency that minimizes postoperative imaging artifacts and facilitates radiation planning and delivery. Despite these benefits, comparative data on the biomechanical performance of different implant materials in spinal oncology remain limited. Finite element (FE) modeling is a widely recognized engineering simulation method that facilitates the ‘in silico’ modeling of complex structures, offering a reliable and validated method to simulate complex spinal biomechanics under both healthy and diseased conditions. This study aimed to compare the biomechanical properties of titanium, continuous carbon fiber-reinforced polyetheretherketone (CFR-PEEK), and chopped CFR-PEEK implants using FE analysis in a lumbar spine model with an L3 vertebral tumor. Material and Methods: A validated lumbar spine FE model was employed to simulate an L3 vertebral tumor by reducing cancellous bone properties to 10% of baseline values. Instrumentation constructs spanned L1-L5 with bilateral pedicle screws placed at all levels except L3. Screws measured 50 mm in length and 5.5 mm in diameter, and rods were 6 mm in diameter. Three material types for rods and screws were tested: titanium, continuous CFR-PEEK, and chopped CFR-PEEK. Surgical simulation included L2-L5 laminectomy with removal of the ligamentum flavum and bilateral complete facetectomies at L2/3 and L3/4. Biomechanical parameters analyzed included range of motion (ROM), intervertebral disc pressure, rod stress and strain, and the bone-screw contact force, which is a measure of resistance to screw pull-out. Results: Continuous CFR-PEEK constructs showed reduced total ROM in flexion and extension (16.74° and 27.09°, respectively) compared with titanium (17.32° and 27.39°, respectively) and chopped CFR-PEEK implants (22.37° and 35.06°, respectively). Average intervertebral disc pressures were lowest with continuous CFR-PEEK (0.05 MPa flexion, < 0.01 MPa extension) versus titanium (0.06 MPa flexion, < 0.01 MPa extension) and chopped CFR-PEEK (0.14 MPa flexion, < 0.01 MPa extension). Chopped CFR-PEEK rods demonstrated lower stress but higher strain (vonMises stress 12.2 MPa flexion, 17.76 MPa extension; effective plastic strain 0.17% flexion, 0.7% extension) compared with continuous CFR-PEEK (vonMises stress 156.21 MPa flexion, 206.8 MPa extension; effective plastic strain 0.03% flexion, 0.04% extension) and titanium (vonMises stress 157.07 MPa flexion, 203.72 MPa extension; effective plastic strain 0.05% flexion, 0.07% extension). The bone-screw contact force was higher in the continuous CFR-PEEK implants (32.8 N flexion, 59.8 N extension) compared to titanium (23.8 N flexion, 43.3 N extension) and chopped CFR-PEEK (0.5 N flexion, 1.1 N extension). Conclusion: Continuous CFR-PEEK implants provided biomechanical stability in flexion and extension and excellent resistance to screw pullout, comparable to titanium and markedly superior to chopped CFR-PEEK implants. The lower strain observed in continuous CFR-PEEK implants suggests a potential advantage in reducing the risk of implant-related complications. Despite these benefits, the higher stress concentrations observed in continuous CFR-PEEK implants warrant further investigation to assess implications for long-term durability.

## ID: AGS26/1020

## P0052: Measuring mechanical exposure in traumatic spine injuries

Oleksii Nekhlopochyn

1

, Michael Karpinsky

2

, Vadim Verbov

1

1

Romodanov Neurosurgery Institute, Kyiv, Ukraine

2

Sytenko Institute of Spine and Joint Pathology, Kharkiv, Ukraine

Introduction: Spinal injuries result from a wide spectrum of mechanical exposures, yet current classifications (AIS, AO Spine) focus on morphology rather than quantifying external force intensity. Mechanisms are often reduced to binary categories (“low- vs. high-energy”), overlooking the continuous nature of exposure and limiting comparability. To address this gap, we developed a multilevel scale providing a standardized, clinically interpretable measure of event severity. The aim was to develop and conduct primary validation of a multilevel (0-10 points) scale for quantitative assessment of external mechanical exposure intensity in spinal trauma. Materials and Methods: The study followed COSMIN principles. A literature review (PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, 1990-2025) defined threshold values and modifying factors (patient mass, impulse transfer coefficient Tland, effective deceleration distance sland). Validation was based on two datasets: 40 standardized clinical vignettes and 52 real cases of thoracolumbar junction trauma (Th11-L2) with mandatory CT/MRI verification. Evaluated parameters included construct and criterion validity, inter-rater reliability (ICC, κ), absolute reliability (SEM, MDC95), diagnostic accuracy (ROC analysis), agreement (Bland-Altman), and threshold stability. Results: The analysis confirmed the feasibility of using Equivalent Height (HEQ) as a universal criterion for quantifying mechanical exposure. On this basis, a 0-10 point scale was constructed, along with a derivative metric, the Effective Spinal Height (ESH), aimed at adjusting HEQ for spine-specific biomechanical modifiers. Inter-rater reliability was high: ICC(2,1) reached 0.84 for HEQ and 0.79 for ESH, while pooled ICC(2,k) values were even higher (0.95 and 0.92, respectively), indicating excellent reproducibility across experts. Weighted κ values confirmed substantial agreement. Absolute measurement error was acceptable, with SEM ranging 0.80-0.95 and MDC95 between 2.2-2.6 scale points. Bland-Altman analysis demonstrated narrow limits of agreement and absence of systematic bias, confirming internal consistency of the scale. Threshold stability testing showed that disagreements beyond ± 1 level occurred in fewer than 7% of cases, underscoring robustness of cut-off values. The derivative metric ESH showed significant correlations with clinically relevant morphological features: vertebral body wedge deformity (r = 0.58), canal compromise (r = 0.49), and ordinal severity according to AO Spine classification (ρ = 0.62; p < 0.001). These associations support both construct validity (the measure behaves as theoretically expected) and criterion validity (alignment with established classification systems). Logistic regression demonstrated that each 1-m increment in ESH nearly doubled the odds of sustaining injuries of type ≥A3 (OR = 1.85; 95% CI 1.45-2.38), indicating strong predictive capability. Diagnostic accuracy was further supported by ROC analysis: AUC values reached 0.82 for fracture detection, with an optimal cut-off around 1.3 m (sensitivity 0.76, specificity 0.72). This suggests that the scale can effectively discriminate clinically significant injuries and guide risk stratification. Conclusions: The proposed scale provides a quantitative, mass-neutral, and clinically interpretable description of event severity. It complements morphological classifications, improves risk stratification, and can be applied for triage, diagnostic planning, and multicenter studies.

## ID: AGS26/1086

## P0053: Graft bolt outperforms center screw fixation in single-level anterior lumbar interbody fusion: a biomechanical study

Christopher Mikhail

1

, Dave Huang

1

, Alexander Turner

2

, Nora Galoustian

1

, Hirofumi Kusumoto

1

, David Skaggs

1

, Melodie Metzger

1

1

Cedars Sinai Medical Center Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Los Angeles, United States

2

ATEC Spine, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is a common procedure for lumbar pathology. Failures in standalone ALIFs often result from micromotion and lack of supplemental fixation. Studies highlight the importance of implant material and design in promoting bony ingrowth and fixation. The IdentiTi-ALIF Standalone System introduces a fenestrated, Hollow Graft Bolt designed to enhance screw fixation. While fenestrated screws show biomechanical advantages in other contexts, limited data exist on their use in ALIFs, indicating a need for further biomechanical and clinical evaluation. Material and Methods: Six cadavers were dissected into 24 motion segments (L2-L5). ALIF cages were placed on the superior endplate of the caudal lumbar vertebrae of each motion segment with a single distal screw, randomized to either GraftBolt or standard center lock screw. Instrumented segments were potted and mounted on a biaxial testing machine with a custom device to apply an anterior shear load to the cage. 400 N of axial compression was applied using a pulley system while specimens were subjected to 1,000 cycles of shear loading (20-140 N at 1 Hz), followed by shear load-to failure 5 mm/min. Failure was defined as a 30% drop from peak load. Cyclic displacement, ultimate load, displacement at failure, stiffness, and toughness, were compared using students t-test. Failure method of each specimen was visually examined. Results: During cyclic shear loading, graft-bolt constructs recorded significantly less anterior displacement than centerscrew constructs at all measured intervals (300-1000 cycles, p < 0.05). By the 1000th cycle, displacement was 0.358 ± 0.063 mm for graft bolts versus 0.435 ± 0.122 mm for center screws. When loaded to failure, graft bolts withstood higher maximum force (597.1 ± 186.8 N vs. 471.5 ± 170.7 N, p < 0.05). Stiffness, toughness, and displacement at failure were not significantly different (p > 0.05). All failures involved shear-induced bone stripping at the screw-bone interface, with no loosening or disengagement of hardware. Conclusion: This study compared the immediate post-operative biomechanical performance of a novel graft bolt versus a conventional center screw in single-level ALIF constructs. The graft bolt significantly reduced implant translation during cyclic loading and demonstrated a higher ultimate load to failure, suggesting enhanced early postoperative stability. These preliminary mechanical findings suggest GraftBolt’s hollow design may reduce micromotion and improve fixation in lumbar ALIF constructs.

## ID: AGS26/1145

## P0054: Bone quality in spinopelvic stabilization: a morphometric analysis of 672 algorithm-optimized S2AI corridors

Jan Gewiess

1

, Dominique Ressnig

1

, Sudeesh Dass

2

, Philippe Zysset

2

, Johannes Bastian

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery and Traumatology, Bern University Hospital, University of Bern, Bern, Switzerland

2

ARTORG Center for Biomedical Engineering Research, University of Bern, Bern, Switzerland

Introduction: Sacral alar-iliac (S2AI) screws, placed between S1 and S2, are considered the standard of spinopelvic fixation. However, there is a gap of knowledge on how bone quality within the S2AI corridor changes with age. The objective of this study was to characterize bone volume fraction (BV/TV) and the proportion of the screw corridor located outside the bone in S2AI trajectories (7-10 mm diameter) using a forensic quantitative CT (QCT) dataset. Material and Methods: Pelvic bones were automatically segmented from QCT data. An algorithm-optimized iterative approach was developed to optimize screw trajectories within manually defined entry and exit regions. Each screw corridor was subdivided into 16 sections. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, t-tests, Friedman, Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon tests, and biphasic nonlinear regression. Results: A total of 336 pelves (mean age 52 ± 18 years, range 15-94) and 672 S2AI screw corridors (mean length 137 ± 11 mm) were analyzed. BV/TV followed a characteristic distribution: low values in sections 1-3, an increase in 4-7, a plateau in 7-11, a decline in 12-14, and a second increase in 15-16. The highest mean BV/TV was observed in 10 mm corridors, followed by 9, 8, and 7 mm. In individuals aged ≥ 65 years (n = 81), BV/TV was significantly lower across all diameters (p < 0.0001), particularly in sections 6-15. The inflection point was identified at 48 years (7 mm) and 47 years (10 mm). Depending on diameter, 6-7% of the total corridor volume extended beyond the bone, mainly in sections 2-9, with a peak in sections 5 and 6. No significant differences were observed between diameters regarding the proportion outside the bone. Conclusion: Bone volume fraction within the S2AI corridor decreases significantly with age. In geriatric patients, larger screw diameters should be preferred. Ten-millimeter screws can be safely placed in most cases. To reach regions of highest bone density, S2AI screws should be at least 90 mm in length.

## ID: AGS26/1159

## P0055: Relationship of posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS) morphology and ideal s1 pedicle screw trajectory: a cross-sectional study of closed vs open PSIS morphology

Preston Jenkins

1

, Bryan Yu

1

, Chase Walton

1

, Robert Ferdon

2

, Jason Silvestre

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

2

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

Introduction: S1 pedicle screws are commonly used in spine surgery. As these screws are often the most caudal part of the construct, it is essential to have excellent fixation via the ideal pedicle screw placement of a medialized end point in the sacral promontory, yielding tricortical fixation. This trajectory is often precluded by the presence of a closed posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS) morphology, leading to use of alternative, potentially less optimal fixation options. The aim of this study was to define the prevalence of closed PSIS morphology. Materials and Methods: Computed tomography (CT) scans of the pelvis from 133 patients at a single institution were reviewed to determine the ideal S1 screw trajectory. PSIS morphology was classified as either open or closed, based on whether a line extending from the ideal S1 pedicle screw trajectory intersected the PSIS. Statistical analyses included descriptive statistics (with 95% confidence intervals), group comparisons (one-sample proportion tests, chi-square tests, t-tests, and Mann-Whitney U tests), correlation analyses, and logistic regression to identify predictors of PSIS morphology. Effect sizes (Cohen’s d, Cramer’s V) were used to assess clinical significance in addition to statistical significance. Results: 81 patients (60.9%) demonstrated closed PSIS morphology. Gender was the only significant predictor of closed PSIS morphology, with males being 79% less likely to have closed PSIS morphology. Conclusion: Pre-operative CT imaging should be carefully reviewed and scrutinized to assess for closed PSIS morphology, and if closed PSIS morphology is present, plans for alternative fixation methods should be considered and available at the time of surgery.

## P0056: Reconceptualizing non-traumatic spine pain: a tensegrity-based review of biomechanical and inflammatory mechanisms

Khalid Mukhter

1

, Yaman Khamis

1

, Abdul Moeen Baco

1

, Anas Albasha

1

, Alaa Zakout

1

, Isam Moghamis

1

, Saeed Qaimkhani

1

, Omar Alnori

1

1

Hamad Medical Corporation, Doha, Qatar

Introduction: Non-traumatic spine pain remains a prevalent clinical issue, often inadequately explained by disc-centric or segmental degeneration models. A more holistic understanding is needed to explain symptom clustering at spinal transition zones and the chronicity of fascial and muscular dysfunction. This conceptual review aims to structure a more integrative and fascia-aware approach to non-traumatic spine pain, drawing on biomechanics, muscle physiology, and inflammation science. We explore the tensegrity model as a unifying framework to explain regional load accumulation, muscular fatigue, the inflammatory process and fascial adhesions.
Material and Methods: Drawing from human anatomy, myofascial research, and analogies in robotics and bioengineering, the tensegrity paradigm is applied to the spine to explain patterns of muscle deconditioning, fiber-type shifts, and collagen densification. Current literature on fascial biomechanics, neuromuscular adaptation, and pain generation is synthesized.
Results: We identify structural weak points at the regional junctions; cervicothoracic, thoracolumbar, and lumbosacral, as common sites of dysfunction due to load concentration and architectural transitions. Proposed treatment strategies include inflammatory modulation, in addition to isometric and eccentric loading, fascia-targeted manual therapies, and ultrasound-guided trigger point interventions for refractory myofascial nodes.
Conclusion: Reframing spinal pain through the lens of tensegrity enables a systems-level understanding of posture failure, muscle imbalance, and fascial pathology, offering more targeted and durable therapeutic pathways.

## ID: AGS26/1379

## P0058: Preoperative hip stiffness effect on spinopelvic compensation following lumbar fusion

William Karakash

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Matthew Gallo

1

, Chimere Ezuma

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Spinopelvic sagittal alignment is thought to play a critical role in physical function and clinical outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery. Patients with stiff hips may have a reduced ability to adapt to postural changes following surgery. This study evaluated differences in spinopelvic alignment changes from standing to sitting after lumbar fusion between patients with preoperatively identified stiff hips and controls. Material and Methods: Adult patients undergoing 1 or 2 level lumbar fusion by a single surgeon with preoperative and 1-year postoperative lateral radiographs in standing and sitting positions were identified. Exclusion criteria included previous lumbar fusion surgery, revision within 1 year postoperatively, and >2 levels fused. Spinopelvic parameters analyzed included pelvic femoral angle (PFA), pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), lumbar lordosis (LL), lumbar tilt (LT), and sagittal angle (SA). LT was defined as the angle between a line drawn from the posterior edge of the superior L1 endplate to the center of the femoral head and a vertical line. SA is the angle between a line drawn from the posterior aspect of the superior L1 endplate to the center of the femoral head and a line parallel to the femoral shaft. Patients were classified as having stiff hips preoperatively if ΔPFA from standing to sitting was less than -55°. Preoperative and 1-year postoperative sitting and standing spinopelvic measurements were compared using independent samples t-tests between groups. Results: Fifty-four patients met inclusion criteria. The average age was 64.9 ± 12.3 years, and the group included 32 females (55.2%). The average BMI was 29.3 ± 5.5, with 27 patients identified as having stiff hips preoperatively. Following lumbar fusion, patients with stiff hips showed greater decreases from standing to sitting in SS (-14.89° vs -7.44°) and LL (-18.48° vs -6.73°), greater increases in LT (5.83° vs 8.70°) and PT (16.07° vs 8.30°), and smaller decreases in SA (-64.15° vs -74.15°) and PFA (-58.59° vs -69.15°). Preoperative trends followed similar trends. From preoperative to postoperative, stiff-hip patients had larger decreases in ΔPFA (-17.37° vs -2.26°) and ΔLT (-2.52° vs -0.96°). Conclusion: Patients with stiff hips demonstrated increased pelvic retroversion (larger increase in ΔPT, larger decrease in ΔSS) and larger decrease in lumbar lordosis from standing to sitting. These differences in spinopelvic compensation may impact long-term outcomes following lumbar fusion, as there is likely a redistribution of forces into vertebral segments adjacent to the lumbar fusion construct. Patients with preoperative hip stiffness exhibit distinct compensatory spinopelvic adaptations after lumbar fusion surgery, which may alter load distribution across adjacent segments. Increased awareness of how spinopelvic parameters change postoperatively in patients with stiff hips may help guide preoperative planning.

## ID: AGS26/1415

## P0059: Biomechanics in treatment of spondylolysis with listhesis. Smile technique

Carlos Mariano Nirino

1

1

Hospital Santamarina, Buenos Aires, Argentina

Introduction: Our aim is to describe the biomechanics and the results obtained in 15 patients operated in the last 5 years with spondylolysis with listhesis grade 1 and 2 according to Meyerding classification. The natural history of spondylolisthesis has been extensively studied but the different etiopathogenic theories and the diverse criteria for surgical treatment are still inconclusive. Some studies have evaluated the anatomical and biomechanical capabilities of the lumbosacral spine and its relation with external factors. Under normal conditions and in the upright position the anterior spine supports 80% of the total axial loads, whereas the posterior column bears only the remaining 20%. The biomechanical loads in the lumbosacral disk include those originating from gravity and muscular action as a vector of combined forces directed forward and downward. Besides the compression of the disc, perpendicular to the vertebral endplates, there is a shearing force directed forward through the intervertebral disc, controlled by the intact fibrous ring and the posterior structures, fundamentally the facets, their capsules and ligaments. Therefore the normal spinal functional unit converts the forces acting in the lumbar region into a load bearing system (discs, vertebral bodies, and posterior arches) and of tensile forces at the level of the posterior ligaments (mainly intralaminar and intraspinous) and finally the spinal extensor muscles which work against gravity. The integrity of the “pars” is fundamental for antero-posterior stability. Our goal is its reconstruction. Material and Methods: 15 patients were evaluated, 9 men and 6 women. In all cases the complaint was low back pain or sciatica not relieved through orthopedic and physiotherapy treatment. All patients were studied with Rx, CT scan and MRI. All lysis and listhesis were found at L4-L5 (5 cases) and L5-S1 (10 cases) only grades 1 and 2 were included in this study. Surgical technique: Posterior approach at the level of the affected vertebrae. Sharp dissection of the lamina, localization of the lysis and stripping, leaving two bone surfaces facing each other. Placement of two transpedicular screws in the lytic vertebrae and placement of a “U” bar under the spinous process. Reduction of the listhesis with an approximator which grasps the bar under the spinous and the tulip of the transpedicular screw, after the placing of bone graft taken from the spinous of the overlying vertebra. Fixation of the bar in position (“smile technique”). We prefer the use of monoaxial screws, placing them at 70° and fixing them at 90° (between the screw and the bar) exert a significant reduction force over the listhesis. Results: In no case was discectomy performed, all patients returned to their usual physical activities, even those who played sports. The patients did not present postoperative complications. Everyone referred complete or partial improvement of their symptoms. Conclusion: This method (“smile”) is usually used in our department for the treatment of spondylolysis with listhesis grade 1 and 2. For years we operated this pathology in this way achieving very good results. In most cases there was a total remission of symptoms.

## ID: AGS26/1419

## P0060: Biomechanical impact of splint rods in posterior cervicothoracic fixation: a finite element analysis

Ian Polyzois

1,2

, Jaskaran Singh

2

, Sara Gustafson

1,2

, Trevor Gascoyne

1,2

, Michael Goytan

2,3

1

Orthopaedic Innovation Centre, Winnipeg, Canada

2

Department of Surgery, Max Rady College of Medicine, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

3

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

Introduction: Posterior stabilization of the cervicothoracic spine is challenging as constructs must bridge the mobile cervical spine with the rigid thoracic spine. High stresses across this transition zone predispose to hardware complications such as screw loosening or rod fracture. Strategies to increase stiffness include larger rods, cobalt chrome alloys, and larger screws; however, these may transmit higher stresses to the bone-screw interface. Splint rods, or accessory rods, may augment load sharing and reduce primary rod strain, but their biomechanical role in cervicothoracic fixation is unclear. This study used finite element analysis (FEA) to evaluate the impact of splint rods, rod diameter, rod alloy, and lateral mass screw size on construct load bearing capacity. Material and Methods: An FEA model simulated a C7 vertebrectomy construct with pedicle screws at T1-T2 and lateral mass screws at C5-C6, replicating ASTM F1717/ISO 12189 conditions. Eight construct variations were analyzed. Variables included primary rod alloy (titanium [Ti] vs cobalt-chrome [CoCr]), presence or absence of splint rods, splint rod diameter (3.5 mm vs 4.5 mm), and lateral mass screw size (3.5 mm vs 4.0 mm). Vertebral bodies were modeled using grade 10 polyurethane foam, and discs were simulated with hyperelastic properties. Each construct underwent ramped cranial-caudal displacement of 2 cm until failure. Outcomes included yield load, displacement, stiffness, and stress distributions. Results: The primary failure mode was shear at the lateral mass screw-bone interface, simulating screw loosening. A secondary failure mode was rod notching at T1 from pedicle tulip impingement. The best configuration was 3.5 mm Ti primary and splint rods, yielding 107 N load capacity and 29.8 N/mm stiffness. The worst configuration was a single 3.5 mm Ti rod without splint rods, with 66 N load capacity and 20.3 N/mm stiffness. Increasing splint rod diameter from 3.5 mm to 4.5 mm raised stiffness but reduced load capacity due to earlier screw loosening. Larger 4.0 mm lateral mass screws improved capacity in single-rod constructs but reduced it when combined with splint rods. Switching from Ti to CoCr improved capacity in single-rod constructs but reduced stiffness with splint rods, suggesting altered load sharing. Conclusion: Splint rods consistently improved load bearing capacity in cervicothoracic constructs. However, greater stiffness from larger rods, screws, or stiffer alloys did not uniformly increase capacity and sometimes predisposed to earlier screw-bone failure. Construct optimization requires balancing stiffness and load sharing rather than maximizing rigidity. Splint rods appear effective for enhancing construct capacity, but material and dimensional choices are critical. Further studies with dynamic loading are needed to clarify fatigue performance and clinical implications.

## ID: AGS26/1430

## P0061: Biomechanical comparison of seven occipital-cervical fixation constructs using finite element analysis

Jaskaran Singh

1

, Ian Polyzois

1,2

, Sara Gustafson

1,2

, Trevor Gascoyne

1,2

, Michael Goytan

1,3

1

Department of Surgery, Max Rady College of Medicine, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

2

Orthopaedic Innovation Centre, Winnipeg, Canada

3

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

Introduction: The occipitocervical junction (OCJ) is a complex region requiring stabilization when rendered unstable or when neurological compression occurs. Fixation is challenging due to limited occipital bone purchase, proximity to critical neurovascular structures, and the need to resist multi-axis biomechanical forces. Although multiple occipital fixation strategies exist, there is no consensus on the optimal construct design. This study used finite element analysis (FEA) to compare seven commonly used occipitocervical (OC) fixation constructs to determine differences in stiffness, screw pullout forces, and overall biomechanical performance. Material and Methods: Seven bilateral OC fixation constructs spanning the occiput to C3 were modeled per ASTM F2706 guidelines. Variations included bilateral rods, keel plates, and one- or two-hole clamp systems, with screws ranging from 4.5 to 5.0 mm in diameter. Vertebral segments were represented by rigid polyurethane blocks, and intervertebral discs were modeled with compression springs to replicate physiological stiffness. Screws were modeled as smooth cylinders bonded to the bone blocks. Each construct was subjected to a ramping flexion torque of 6.12 Nm at the occipital hinge. Outcomes included bending stiffness, screw pullout forces, stress distribution, and a combined performance ranking using the Multi-Attribute Utility Theory (MAUT) score. Results: Bending stiffness varied substantially between constructs, ranging from 509 to 820 N·mm/deg. Construct E, composed of medially fixed one-hole and two-hole clamps, achieved the highest stiffness (820 N·mm/deg) and the greatest overall MAUT score (0.79), indicating an optimal balance between stiffness and reduced screw pullout forces. Construct C, a wide keel plate, demonstrated the lowest performance (MAUT 0.19), with high localized pullout forces and reduced stiffness. Constructs employing keel plates concentrated stress centrally, while rod-and-clamp constructs distributed forces more evenly. Increasing screw diameter from 4.5 mm to 5.0 mm provided minimal benefit, with only a 2 N reduction in pullout force. Stress analysis showed all constructs localized peak stresses at implant-rod interfaces, predicting rod notching as a potential failure mode under fatigue loading. Despite these stresses, none of the screw pullout forces exceeded theoretical maximum pullout strength in bone. Conclusion: This FEA study demonstrates that construct design significantly influences the biomechanical stability of OC fixation. Medially aligned clamp-based constructs provided superior stiffness and balanced load distribution compared to keel plates, which concentrated forces and risked higher pullout. Larger screw diameter had minimal impact on stability. Stress analysis suggests rod notching at implant interfaces as a possible long-term failure mechanism. Optimal OC fixation requires careful selection of construct type to maximize stability while minimizing screw pullout risk. These findings provide biomechanical insight into construct selection and may guide future clinical decision-making and implant design.

## ID: AGS26/1542

## P0062: Mapping fusion zones to motion resistance: finite element insights into return-to-activity after interbody fusion

Lionel Yan Jin Lee

1

, Namith Rangaswamy

2

, Joshua K

2

, Si Jian Hui

2

, Jiong Hao Tan

2

, Naresh Kumar

1,2

1

National University of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore

2

National University Health System, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Lumbar interbody fusion is widely used for degenerative lumbar pathologies with TLIF and PLIF common due to technical reliability. While approach influences perioperative outcomes, long-term success hinges on achieving solid interbody fusion that restores stability and alignment as well as reduces undue biomechanical stress. Radiographic assessment treats fusion as global, yet the spatial distribution of bone growth around the cage may differentially affect biomechanics under direction-specific loads. We used finite element analysis to examine how flexion/extension, lateral bending, and axial rotation interact with anterior, posterior, and lateral fusion zones. We aim to inform surgical planning and guide safe and progressive postoperative rehabilitation by linking localized fusion distribution to segmental resistance. Material and Methods: We used finite element analysis to examine how flexion/extension, lateral bending, and axial rotation interact with anterior, posterior, and lateral fusion zones in an L4-L5 lumbar interbody fusion model. The disc space was partitioned into four fusion zones (anterior, right, posterior, left) plus a central cage. Two constructs were analyzed: cage-only and cage-screw-rod, with tie constraints at all interfaces to simulate full osseointegration. The L5 inferior surface was fixed while pure moments were applied to L4 to compute angular motion at a superior-center reference point. To reflect construct stiffness, moments were 300/300/400/200 Nm (extension/flexion/lateral bending/torsion) for cage-screw-rod and 100/100/100/50 Nm for cage-only. The vertebrae were modelled as cancellous bone (0.35 GPa), screw and rods as titanium alloy (110 GPa), and cages in both PEEK (2 GPa) and titanium. Fusion-zone stiffness followed power-law scaling according to fusion quality. Scenarios examined zone presence/absence (including asymmetries) and heterogeneous early (50% vs 25%) and mid-term (80% vs 50%) fusion, yielding moduli of 224, 87.5, and 21.9 MPa. Results: Construct stiffness increased monotonically with more fusion zones and higher fusion quality. Using a ≤5° ROM criterion, many 3-zone patterns were close to 4-zone performances, but lateral bending strongly depended on the presence and quality of both lateral zones. A 2-zone lateral pattern could outperform some 3-zone patterns in lateral bending and torsion due to the larger fused contact area. Critical loads were higher with instrumentation at 144.1-334.9 Nm versus 74.1-147.7 Nm for cage-only. Instrumentation also had the greatest effect when no fusion was present and showed diminishing benefit once lateral zones were included. With instrumentation, removed zones lowered thresholds to 69.0-80.5% of full fusion, while cage-only thresholds were highly quality-dependent and could drop to 7.8%. With screw-rod fixation, three-fusion zones at 80% quality could achieve thresholds within 5% of full fusion, particularly for anterior-posterior loading. However, lateral and torsional responses continue to hinge on the lateral zone quality. Titanium cages provided minimal added stiffness when instrumented (0.5-3.0%) and modest gains in cage-only scenarios (2.1-16.1%). Conclusion: Three fusion zones can often achieve 4-zone-equivalent stability for ≤5° ROM, especially in anterior-posterior motions with instrumentation. However, lateral bending uniquely requires both lateral zones and torsional resistance scales with fused contact area. Cage-only constructs are highly sensitive to fusion presence and quality while instrumentation blunts this sensitivity for anterior-posterior motions.

## ID: AGS26/1580

## P0063: Morphometric measurement of pedicles and feasibility of cortical bone trajectory (CBT) for thoracic spine stabilisation utilising CT-scan

Ahmad Mustaqim Mat Zain

1

, Sudhir Kumar Sri Kumar

1

, Fattah Rahiman Ghazali

2

, Mohd Imran Yusof

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, University Sains Malaysia, Kubang Kerian, Malaysia

2

Department of Radiology, Universiti Sains Malaysia, Kubang Kerian, Malaysia

Introduction: Conventional pedicle screw (CPS) fixation for spinal stabilisation is often compromised by screw loosening, especially in poor bone quality, with loosening rates reported as high as 54.6% in certain cases. The Cortical Bone Trajectory (CBT) technique has emerged as an alternative to traditional pedicle screw techniques where it maximises engagement with cortical bone, demonstrating superior pullout strength and insertional torque. While extensively studied for the lumbar spine, there is limited data on its application in the thoracic region, which presents unique anatomical challenges like smaller pedicles and proximity to vital structures. This research will address the current gap in the literature regarding thoracic CBT morphometry in the region and the data gathered will provide an essential reference for surgeons, aiding in preoperative planning and potentially improving the safety and efficacy of thoracic spinal fixation in the Malaysian population. Material and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted using Computed Tomography (CT) images of thoracic vertebrae from Malaysian adults aged 18-60 without spinal deformities. Morphometric parameters relevant to the CBT technique - including pedicle width, pedicle height, cephalad angle, lateral angle, screw width, and screw length. The data was collected from the Picture Archiving & Communication System (PACS) radiological data at the University Sains Malaysia Specialist Hospital in Kelantan, Malaysia between August 2019 to July 2024. Results: This study analysed 756 thoracic vertebrae from 63 Malaysian adults (42 males, 21 females). The results showed that pedicle height and feasible screw length increased progressively from T1-T12, while pedicle width & the corresponding screw diameter were narrowest in the critical mid-thoracic region (T4-T5). In terms of trajectory, the cephalad angle remained relatively consistent (15°-20°) across all levels, but the lateral angle was unmeasurable in the upper thoracic spine (T1-T4) due to medial pedicle orientation, increasing steadily from T5 downwards. Significant sex-based differences were noted, with males consistently demonstrating larger pedicle dimensions, lateral angles, and feasible screw sizes compared to females at nearly all levels; however, no significant difference was observed for the cephalad angle. These findings align with other international studies however underscores a critical safety concern as the risk of lateral wall fracture is higher in females. Conclusion: This study highlights the potential benefits & the feasibility of thoracic spine CBT technique in the Malaysian population. Based on these anatomical constraints, the study concludes that CBT is most feasible at T6 and below for males & T9 and below for females. Although CBT offers a key safety advantage by directing screws away from the spinal canal (reducing neurological risk), complication rates like screw misplacement & pedicle fracture are comparable to traditional techniques. However, meticulous preoperative CT imaging is essential, for better outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1760

## P0064: Assessment of the angular measurements of the spine. Are we doing our best?

Adimilson dos Santos Delgado

1

, Bruna Souza Morais

1

, Paulo Pereira

2,3

, Helton Defino

4

, Arlindo Neto Montagnoli

5

1

Interunit Graduate Program in Bioengineering, University of São Paulo, São Carlos, Brazil

2

Faculty of Medicine, Porto University, Porto, Portugal

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Centro Hospitalar Universitário São João, Porto, Portugal

4

Department of Orthopedics and Anesthesiology, Ribeirão Preto Medical School, University of São Paulo, Ribeirão Preto, Brazil

5

Electrical Engineering Department, Federal University of São Carlos, São Carlos, Brazil

Introduction: The Cobb angle method, although widely adopted in clinical and research contexts for spinal assessment, presents intrinsic limitations. By restricting the measurement to the angular relationship between segmental endpoints, it fails to provide an accurate representation of the curvature distribution along the entire spine. Consequently, important morphological variations and localized alterations may remain undetected. To address these limitations, alternative strategies have been proposed to refine the quantification of sagittal alignment and improve the reliability of curvature analysis. Material and Methods: We developed a methodology based on mathematical modeling of the spine, designed to enable continuous and segmental analysis of spinal curvature. Three independent observers annotated the vertebral body vertices from C3 to S1, as well as the femoral heads, in 26 standing lateral spine radiographs. Each examination was repeated twice, with a minimum interval of one week between sessions. From these landmarks, vertebral centroids were calculated as the intersection of diagonals connecting opposite vertices. The centroids were then interpolated to generate a mathematical model of the spine. Segmental intervertebral angles were subsequently computed by measuring the difference of the inclination of each vertebral body relative to the normal of the model. Inter and intraobserver reliability were assessed using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs). Results: The proposed method demonstrated superior reproducibility and accuracy compared with the traditional endplate-based approach. Intraobserver reliability was consistently high across vertebral levels, and interobserver agreement was equally robust. Specifically, mean ICC values for the endplate method were 0.49 (interobserver) and 0.55 (intraobserver), whereas the centroid method reached 0.92 (interobserver) and 0.94 (intraobserver). These results confirm the method’s capacity to provide a reliable, segment-by-segment characterization of spinal curvature. By enabling individualized and reproducible assessment, the approach captures subtle morphological features frequently overlooked by conventional techniques. Conclusion: The intervertebral angle profile proved to be a precise and reliable method for comprehensive evaluation of curvature pattern. Unlike the Cobb method, which offers only a global estimate, this methodology allows continuous and individualized analysis of curvature distribution at each vertebral level. The findings support its potential as an advanced alternative for the investigation and monitoring of structural spinal alterations, overcoming the limitations inherent to conventional approaches.

## ID: AGS26/1872

## P0065: Prospective analysis of functional outcomes using 3D-printed trabeculated titanium interbody cages in minimally invasive TLIF

Shrey Binyala

1

, Vishal Peshattiwar

1

1

Kokilaben Dhirubhai Ambani Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-TLIF) has emerged as an effective technique for managing degenerative lumbar pathologies. Interbody cage design significantly influences osseointegration, stability, and long-term outcomes. Three-dimensional (3D) printed titanium cages, with their porous architecture mimicking cancellous bone, promise improved biomechanical integration compared to conventional cages. However, clinical evidence on their functional efficacy remains limited. Material and Methods: This prospective study included 35 patients undergoing single-level MIS-TLIF using 3D-printed titanium interbody cages between February 2025 and May 2025. Demographic data, perioperative parameters, and radiological findings were recorded. Functional outcomes were assessed preoperatively and at follow-up (3, 6, and 12 months) using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for back and leg pain. Fusion status was evaluated using plain radiographs and CT scans where indicated. Results: The mean age of the cohort was 46.2 years, with a slight female predominance. Operative time averaged 152 minutes, and mean blood loss was 112 ml. All patients demonstrated significant improvement in ODI and VAS scores at the 6- and 12-month follow-up (p < 0.05). Radiological assessment suggested early signs of osseointegration and bridging trabeculae, with 93% achieving Grade I or II fusion by 12 months. No cage subsidence, migration, or major complications were observed.
