Conclusion: This preliminary prospective analysis indicates that 3D-printed titanium cages in MIS-TLIF provide favorable short-term functional and radiological outcomes, supporting their potential role in enhancing spinal fusion. Larger, long-term comparative studies are warranted.

## ID: AGS26/1955

## P0066: Quantitative CT is superior to MRI-based bone quality for predicting vertebral compression strength

Anna-Maria Mielke

1

, Soji Tani

2

, Koji Ishikawa

3

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Bruno Verna

1

, Ali Guven

1

, Ek Tsoon Tan

1

, John Carrino

1

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Jennifer Shue

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Alexander Hughes

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Showa University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan

3

Duke University, Durham, United States

Introduction: Determining vertebral strength is critical in planning lumbar spine surgery, since poor bone quality increases the risk of screw loosening, cage subsidence, and perioperative fractures. Several imaging-derived markers of bone health have been suggested. Volumetric bone mineral density (vBMD) derived from quantitative CT (qCT) and MRI-based vertebral bone quality (VBQ) have been proposed as surrogate markers of bone integrity. Abdominal aortic calcification (AAC) assessed on radiographs reflects systemic vascular disease and has been linked to skeletal fragility. Yet, their relative importance in predicting mechanical strength remains uncertain. Finite element analysis (FEA) yields individualized estimates of vertebral compression strength (CS) but is not widely available in clinical settings. We sought to determine which imaging parameters best predict FEA-derived vertebral CS, hypothesizing that CT-based vBMD would be the strongest predictor. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients with degenerative spinal disorders who underwent lumbar fusion at a single specialized orthopedic center (2014-2020). IRB approval was obtained. Inclusion criteria required preoperative lumbar CT and MRI within 12 months; patients with prior fusion, fracture, tumor, infection, or poor imaging quality were excluded. Seventy-six patients met criteria. CT-derived vBMD at L1-L2 was calculated using scanner-specific calibration of Hounsfield units. VBQL1/2 was determined on T1-weighted MRI as vertebral body signal intensity normalized to cerebrospinal fluid. AAC was scored on lateral lumbar radiographs using the Kauppila method. Vertebral CS was calculated by FEA from CT-based three-dimensional models with incrementally applied compressive displacement until failure. Spearman correlations assessed associations between CS and imaging parameters. Multivariable linear regression adjusted for age, sex, and BMI, with collinearity checks (VIF > 2.5). Results: Of the 76 patients (54% female; mean age 61.1 ± 10.6 years; BMI 29.6 ± 6.2 kg/m2), vBMD strongly correlated with CS (ρ = 0.75, p < 0.001). VBQ (ρ = -0.20, p = 0.086) and AAC (ρ = -0.22, p = 0.062) were not significant. In regression models, vBMD was the only independent imaging predictor (β = 64.9, p < 0.001), with male sex also contributing (β = 2252, p < 0.001). VBQ did not predict CS (p = 0.606; adjusted R2 = 0.09). Adding VBQ or AAC to vBMD did not improve explanatory power (adjusted R2 = 0.77 vs. 0.76-0.78). Conclusion: qCT-derived vBMD was the only imaging biomarker that reliably predicted vertebral CS, underscoring trabecular density as the key determinant of load capacity. In contrast, MRI-based VBQ and AAC did not improve prediction, likely reflecting marrow composition or systemic processes rather than intrinsic vertebral strength. CT-based bone quality assessment therefore remains the most robust and practical approach for preoperative risk stratification in patients undergoing lumbar fusion. Future studies should validate these findings in larger, diverse populations and investigate complementary roles of MRI- and vascular-based biomarkers in comprehensive skeletal health assessment.

## ID: AGS26/1959

## P0067: The effect of four different surgical methods on preventing junctional kyphosis in a sheep spine model: a biomechanical study

Yekta Furkan Altin

1

, Murat Korkmaz

1

, Turgut Akgül

1

, Hayati Durmaz

1

1

Istanbul Faculty Of Medicine,Orthopedics and Traumatology Departmant, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) is a problem with rigid systems made with pedicle screws owing to improved instrument systems and understanding of spinal deformities. Proximal junctional kyphosis is defined as the development of a kyphotic deformity of more than 10 °between the instrumented vertebra and the proximal level. PJK is an important problem because it requires revision surgery and there is no consensus in the literature on its prevention. The aim of our study was to determine which of the different materials placed proximal to the instrument is more durable with loading and to help reduce the incidence of PJK. In our study, we aimed to evaluate the effect of a transverse hook, mersilene tape, and a dual-band system with and without cutting the interspinous ligament in preventing proximal junction kyphosis. Material and Methods: In our study, 23 sheep spines of 3-4 years old Karaman sheep breed, in which paraspinal soft tissues were preserved between C7-T10 levels, were included. The facet joints and interspinous and supraspinous ligaments of the spines included in the study were preserved. The first group was divided into four different groups by placing 5 level bilateral pedicle screws between T2-7. The first group was the control group(A0) with five sheep spines, the second group was the mersilene tape (A1) with six sheep, the third group was the transverse hook (A2) with six sheep, and the fourth group was the dual band (A3) system with six sheep. In the second stage, the interspinous ligament was cut at the level proximal to the pedicle screw level in the spine of 17 sheep, and mersilene tape(B1), transverse hook(B2), and dual band(B3) system were placed in two sheep in the control group (B0) and five sheep each proximally. Results: In the first phase of the study, where the interspinous ligament was intact, the control group(A0) had an average of 335.88 ± 53.46 Newton, A1 group had 473.97 ± 89.13 Newton, A2 group had 486.57 ± 122.6 Newton, and A3 group had 436.00 ± 86.16 Newton. No significant difference was observed between the Newton values obtained (p > 0.05). In the second phase, where the interspinous ligament was cut, the control group(B0) had 254.35 ± 0.35 Newton, B1 group had 266.14 ± 7.10 Newton, B2 group had 276.38 ± 29.42 Newton, and B3 group had 346.82 ± 33.75 Newton. Newton values showed a statistically significant difference between the groups (p = 0.010; p < 0.05). Pairwise comparisons revealed that Newton values in the control group were significantly lower than those in the dual-band group (p = 0.012; p < 0.05). Comparing the experiments with and without cutting the interspinous ligament, the Newton values of the A2 group, where the interspinous ligament was intact, were significantly higher than those of the group with the cut interspinous ligament(B2) (p = 0.004; p < 0.01). Similarly, the Newton values of the A1 and A3 groups with intact interspinous ligaments were significantly higher than those of the groups with cut interspinous ligaments (B1-B3) (p = 0.004, p < 0.01, and p = 0.030; p < 0.05, respectively). Conclusion: PJK is one of the most important problems in spinal surgery. In addition to ensuring sagittal alignment, the use of surgical techniques and materials has also made important contributions. In our study, the importance of preserving the interspinous ligaments re-emerged, and the use of dual bands increased the durability of the junction area.

## ID: AGS26/2064

## P0068: Sagittal imbalance after posterior fossa tumor surgery in children: an underestimated problem

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction / Objectives: Posterior fossa tumors are among the most common pediatric brain neoplasms. With improved survival, long-term complications gain importance. Postoperative sagittal spinal imbalance remains underrecognized. This study evaluates its incidence, mechanisms, and clinical impact in children treated for posterior fossa tumors. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed pediatric patients operated on for posterior fossa tumors between 2015 and 2020. Clinical data, imaging, and follow-up were analyzed. Sagittal balance was assessed using standing spine radiographs (sagittal vertical axis, pelvic parameters, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis). Functional outcomes were evaluated using pediatric quality-of-life scales. Results: Of 42 children, 7 (16.6%) developed progressive sagittal imbalance within 2-4 years post-surgery. Contributing factors included postoperative truncal hypotonia, cerebellar mutism syndrome, and radiation-induced myopathy. Radiographs showed global sagittal malalignment with compensatory pelvic retroversion. Clinically, affected children had postural difficulties, gait disturbances, and reduced participation in daily activities. Early rehabilitation was insufficient to halt deformity progression; 2 patients required spinal fusion. Conclusion: Sagittal imbalance is a significant but underreported long-term complication after posterior fossa tumor surgery in children. Systematic spinal follow-up allows early detection and timely intervention with physiotherapy, orthoses, or preventive surgical strategies. Incorporating spinal assessment into multidisciplinary survivorship programs is essential to optimize long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2411

## P0069: Biomechanical and clinical evaluation of PSO, modified PSO and VCR treating OVCF kyphosis: a finite element analysis

Junyu Li

1

, Lizhi Xu

1

, Haotian Wang

1

, Yinhao Liu

1

, Hanwen Zhang

2

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Yan Zeng

1

1
Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China, 
2
Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China
Introduction: In the context of an aging population, osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture (OVCF) has become one of the major complications of osteoporosis. For OVCF patients with severe kyphosis, osteotomies may be necessary to restore sagittal balance. Several osteotomy techniques are currently used in clinical practice. As OVCF patients are often elderly and severely osteoporotic, perioperative risks are high and postoperative mechanical complications are common, making it difficult to select the optimal osteotomy strategy. In recent years, some scholars have proposed a modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy (mPSO) to correct kyphosis. Although this technique has demonstrated favorable clinical outcomes and prognosis in some patients, its biomechanical rationale remains unclear. Material and Methods: Clinical Study: A retrospective study was conducted involving 33 consecutive patients with no significant differences in risk factors for mechanical complications. Sagittal parameters were measured on preoperative, postoperative, and follow-up lateral spinal radiographs. Biomechanical Study: A finite element (FE) model was developed using CT scan data from a female OVCF volunteer with kyphosis only. The model was used to simulate pedicle subtraction osteotomy (PSO), vertebral column resection (VCR), and modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy (mPSO). Each postoperative model was tested under six loading conditions (flexion, extension, left/right lateral bending, left/right rotation). Stress distribution and deformation of the finite element models were measured. Results: Clinical Study: No significant differences were observed in preoperative sagittal parameters. mPSO showed superior outcomes in multiple postoperative and follow-up sagittal parameters compared to PSO and VCR, with shorter operative time and less intraoperative blood loss. There were no significant differences in postoperative mechanical complications. Biomechanical Study: Under most loading conditions, the displacement in mPSO was similar to that in PSO and greater than in VCR. The maximum equivalent stress in all vertebral bodies remained within a safe range, with stress mainly distributed at the T10 vertebra and the fixed L2 vertebra. The stress in VCR was higher than in mPSO and PSO. Under all loading conditions, VCR exhibited the highest intervertebral disc pressure, followed by PSO, and then mPSO. The maximum pressure was located at the T10-T11 disc. Conclusion: Finite element analysis indicates that mPSO provides spinal stability comparable to PSO and may offer a better environment for bone fusion while preventing adjacent segment degeneration. Combined with its lower surgical risk, we believe that modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy can be a suitable surgical option for indicated OVCF patients.

## ID: AGS26/2422

## P0070: Spinopelvic compensation in sitting and standing postures following lumbar fusion

William Karakash

1

, Matthew Gallo

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Kevin Liu

1

, Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Spinopelvic sagittal alignment is thought to play a critical role in physical function and clinical outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery. While sagittal alignment is typically assessed in the standing position, sitting is the most common posture in daily life. Lumbar fusion may induce changes in spinopelvic adaptation, potentially impairing the body’s ability to achieve optimal alignment during postural transitions from standing to sitting. This study aimed to describe the normative changes in spinopelvic sagittal alignment after lumbar fusion in both the standing and sitting positions, and to evaluate the magnitude of the changes in spinopelvic compensatory mechanisms when transitioning from standing to sitting after surgery. Material and Methods: Adult patients undergoing 1 or 2 level lumbar fusion by a single surgeon with preoperative and 1-year postoperative lateral radiographs in standing and sitting positions were identified. Exclusion criteria included previous lumbar fusion surgery, revision within 1 year postoperatively, and > 2 levels fused. Spinopelvic parameters analyzed included pelvic femoral angle (PFA), pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), lumbar lordosis (LL), lumbar tilt (LT), modified lumbar tilt (mLT), and sagittal angle (SA). LT was defined as the angle between a line drawn from the posterior edge of the superior L1 endplate to the center of the femoral head and a vertical line; mLT was similar, but with the line drawn from the posterior edge of the superior L3 endplate. SA is the angle between a line drawn from the posterior aspect of the superior L1 endplate to the center of the femoral head and a line parallel to the femoral shaft. Preoperative and 1-year postoperative sitting and standing spinopelvic measurements were compared using paired samples t-tests. The magnitude of change in spinopelvic parameters from standing to sitting posture between preoperative and postoperative radiographs was compared using paired samples t-test. Results: Fifty-four patients met inclusion criteria. The average age was 64.9 ± 12.3 years, and the group included 32 females (55.2%). The average BMI was 29.3 ± 5.5, and 43 patients (79.6%) underwent one-level fusions. In standing, postoperative increases were observed in SS (33.7° to 35.7°) and PFA (192.8° to 196.2°). In sitting, SS increased (19.3° to 24.5°), PFA decreased (138.8° to 132.3°), PT decreased (37.0° to 33.7°), and LL increased postoperatively (32.4° to 37.3°) (all p < 0.05). Comparing standing-to-sitting transitions, postoperative changes included a larger ΔPFA (-54.1° to -63.9°) and ΔSA (-65.0° to -69.1°), and a smaller ΔSS (-14.3° to -9.8°), ΔLL (-16.8° to -12.7°), ΔPT (15.6° to 12.2°) (all p < 0.05). Conclusion: Postoperative changes were more pronounced in the sitting position, highlighting the importance of evaluating both postures during surgical planning and postoperative follow-up. Lumbar fusion reduced spinopelvic compensatory capacity during transitions, as reflected by limited pelvic retroversion (decreased ΔSS and ΔPT) partially offset by increased hip flexion (greater ΔPFA). Understanding how lumbar fusion alters spinopelvic alignment in both standing and sitting positions provides insight into compensatory limitations during postural transitions. These findings may help guide surgical planning and postoperative care to optimize functional outcomes and reduce mechanical complications.

## ID: AGS26/2489

## P0071: Real-time cage-endplate pressure mapping in circumferential (360°) lumbar fusion: effects of staged decompression and controlled screw compression

Christoph Lorenzen

1

, Nicolas H. von der Höh

1

, Robin Heilmann

1

1

Leipzig University Hospital, ZESBO, Leipzig, Germany

Introduction: Circumferential (“360°”) lumbar fusion achieves high fusion rates, yet time- and space-resolved pressures at the cage-endplate interface across a complete surgical sequence remain insufficiently characterized. Prior in-vitro and in-silico studies often report single-point forces or static load steps and rarely use standard implants, limiting transferability. We establish a reproducible, implant-agnostic protocol for continuous endplate pressure mapping during a standardized 360° fusion workflow and report feasibility signals from an initial specimen; full accrual and analysis will be completed before presentation. Material and Methods: Fresh-frozen thoracolumbar spines undergo pre-test CT with a hydroxyapatite phantom to derive specimen-specific trabecular vBMD (covariate). CT-based segmentation informs pedicle-trajectory planning and fabrication of vertebra-conforming guides to standardize screw placement. At the index level, bilateral pedicle screws/rods are placed; after standardized discectomy, ultrathin (0.1 mm) pressure films are positioned cranially and caudally so that each film lies between cage and endplate, followed by central cage insertion. A calibrated compression clamp applies quasi-static plateaus of 0/50/100 N while pressure maps are sampled at 100 Hz during sequential configurations: unilateral facetectomy→ bilateral facetectomy → extended laminectomy. Primary outcomes: peak/mean interface pressure, active contact area, apparent axial stiffness. A prespecified linear mixed-effects model includes configuration and load as fixed effects, vBMD as covariate, and specimen-level random intercepts. Sensitivity analyses address variance structures and influential observations. Results: Feasibility (pilot n = 1 FSU, L3-4): Continuous acquisition was stable and artifact-free across all three configurations and load plateaus in a single test sequence. No device malfunctions occurred, and no macroscopic endplate or posterior-element damage was observed on post-test inspection. Time-resolved maps qualitatively captured early pressure relaxation within each plateau. These pilot signals confirm the practicality of repeated, configuration-wise testing with controlled screw compression and complete cage-footprint coverage in one specimen. Conclusion: This standardized, design-agnostic workflow yields continuous, spatially resolved pressure maps throughout a full 360° fusion sequence using standard implants and controlled preload. Integrating CT-derived vBMD and guide-assisted instrumentation enhances reproducibility and enables quantification of configuration- and bone-quality-dependent changes in peak pressure, contact area, and stiffness. Final results are intended to define practical bounds for intraoperative compression and provide high-fidelity inputs for finite-element calibration; completed data will be presented at the Global Spine Congress.

## ID: AGS26/2515

## P0072: Comparison of pull-out strength of different screwing techniques in cases with pedicle fracture or requiring pediculectomy: study on a porcine cadaver spine model

Bekir Ay

1

, Inan Uzunoglu

1

, Ceren Kizmazoglu

2

, Fatma Erzurumlu

1

, Elif Perdeci

3

, Yunus Uslan

4

, Teyfik Demir

4

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Faculty of Medicine, Dokuz Eylül University, İzmir, Turkey

3

Department of Biomedical Engineering, TOBB University of Economics and Technology, Ankara, Turkey

4

Department of Mechanical Engineering, TOBB University of Economics and Technology, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: In modern spine surgery, the primary goals include achieving stabilization, correcting deformities, and preserving neurological structures. However, intraoperative pedicle injury, pedicle fractures associated with trauma, or pedicle involvement in oncological cases may compromise pedicle integrity. In such situations, stabilization systems are often extended to one or more adjacent segments to reduce the risk of transpedicular screw loosening. Spinal fusion, however, may increase the range of motion (ROM) and intradiscal pressure at adjacent motion segments, thereby accelerating degenerative changes. Consequently, current literature emphasizes that the shortest possible fusion should be preferred in order to minimize the risk of adjacent segment degeneration and preserve spinal mobility. The aim of this study was to investigate the biomechanical effects of different screw placement techniques on pull-out strength. Material and Methods: In this study, seven cadaveric porcine spines from six-month-old pigs weighing 100-110 kg were used. Each level was selected through blinded randomization, and 35 vertebral segments were divided into seven different groups. Thus, all vertebral levels from L1 to L5 were equally represented. The groups were arranged as follows:
1. Group: Monocortical transpedicular screw placement into an intact vertebra
2. Group: Bicortical transpedicular screw placement into an intact vertebra
3. Group: Bicortical cement-augmented transpedicular screw placement into an intact vertebra
4. Group: Monocortical transpedicular screw placement into a pedicle-trimmed vertebra
5. Group: Monocortical cement-augmented transpedicular screw placement into a pedicle-trimmed vertebra
6. Group: Bicortical transpedicular screw placement into a pedicle-trimmed vertebra
7. Group: Bicortical cement-augmented transpedicular screw placement into a pedicle-trimmed vertebra
All screws used were 4.5 mm in diameter and varied in length (35, 40 and 50 mm). Biomechanical pull-out tests were conducted at a rate of 5 mm/min. Results: The highest mean pullout strength was observed in G7, followed by G6, G4, G3, G5, G1, and G2 respectively. One-way ANOVA with post hoc (LSD) pairwise comparisons was performed. It showed that G7 had significantly higher pullout strength than all other groups (p < 0.05), except G6, where the difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.085). Comparisons among the remaining groups revealed that G4 was significantly higher than G1 (p < 0.05), which indicated that bicortical fixation was superior to monocortical fixation. Cement augmentation (G3, G6) consistently increased pullout strength compared to their respective non-cement counterparts (G1, G4). Pedicle shaving alone (G2, G5) did not provide a statistically significant benefit compared to the G1 group (monocortical). Conclusion: The literature emphasizes that the shortest possible fusion should be preferred in order to prevent adjacent segment degeneration and preserve spinal mobility. The findings of this study demonstrate that, in cases requiring pediculectomy or with pedicle compromise, stabilization can be achieved with an effective and safe technique that reduces the need to extend fusion to adjacent segments. This approach provides significant advantages such as shorter operative time, reduced complication risk, and preservation of motion segments, thereby maintaining spinal mobility. Moreover, in the group with resected pedicles, bicortical and cement-augmented screws yielded superior outcomes compared to monocortical screws placed in intact vertebrae, offering an advantage in managing intraoperative complications.

## ID: AGS26/2606

## P0073: Bone density of traditional pedicle screw, cortical bone pedicle screw, and modified cortical bone pedicle screw trajectories - a CT-based morphometric analysis of 1750 vertebrae

Sathish Muthu

1,2

, Vibhu Krishnan Viswanathan

1,3

, Kavya Priyadharshini Natarajan

4

, Barath Kumar Sivakumar

1

, Dhibin Vikash Kolarpatti Ponnusamy

1

1
Department of Spine Surgery, Orthopaedic Research Group, Coimbatore, India, 
2
Department of Orthopaedics, Government Medical College,, Karur, India, 
3
Department of Spine Surgery, Devadoss Multispeciality Hospital, Madurai, India, 
4
Department of Radiodiagnosis, Government Medical College, Karur, India
Introduction: Pedicle screw fixation is a cornerstone of spinal stabilization, yet screw loosening remains a significant complication, particularly in osteoporotic bone. Alternative trajectories such as the cortical bone trajectory (CBT) and modified cortical bone trajectory (MCBT) have been proposed to enhance fixation strength by engaging denser cortical bone. However, comparative morphometric and bone density data across these trajectories remain limited. We aim to evaluate and compare bone density along the traditional pedicle trajectory (TPT), CBT, and MCBT using computed tomography (CT)-derived Hounsfield unit (HU) measurements. Methods: CT scans of the lumbar spine (L1-L5) of adult patients who presented for non-spinal ailments were retrospectively analyzed. Three pedicle screw trajectories were virtually simulated: TPT, CBT, and MCBT. For each trajectory, CTHU values were measured in sagittal section at four anatomical points along the screw path: posterior cortex, mid-pedicle, mid-vertebral body, and anterior vertebral body cortex. Mean CTHU values, maximum screw lengths were compared across trajectories, and subgroup analyses were performed. Statistical comparisons were conducted using one-way ANOVA with post hoc testing, linear regression, and independent-sample t-tests, with significance set at p < 0.05. Results: A total of 350 patients (1,750 vertebrae) were analyzed. Mean CTHU values differed significantly among trajectories: CBT (538.2 ± 73.1 HU) > MCBT (472.6 ± 87.9 HU) > traditional PS (362.8 ± 68.4 HU) (p<0.001). At the posterior cortex, density was highest for CBT (1,128.9 ± 147.6 HU), followed by MCBT (962.4 ± 192.7 HU) and TPT (582.1 ± 162.3 HU). Across mid-pedicle, mid-body, and anterior cortex, CBT and MCBT showed comparable values, both significantly greater than TPT. Age negatively correlated with CTHU across all trajectories, most pronounced in TPT (r = -0.36, R2 = 0.13). Patients ≥ 60 years exhibited a 17.8% reduction in TPT density versus 6.9% and 4.2% for MCBT and CBT, respectively. Gender differences were significant only for TPT (p<0.05). MCBT showed significantly increased length of screw compared to CBT and TPT (p < 0.001). Conclusion: Cortical bone-based trajectories traverse denser bone than the traditional pedicle path, with CBT showing the highest density and MCBT offering the advantage of longer screw length. Both cortical-based approaches were less affected by age-related bone loss, supporting their clinical utility in optimizing pedicle screw fixation, particularly in elderly or osteoporotic patients.

## ID: AGS26/2644

## P0074: Fulcrum-induced extension bending of fusion rods during flexion relaxation enables smart implant detection

Mohsen Khodaee

1,2

, Alexandros Tsolakidis

3

, Tobias Goetschi

1

, Taekwang Jang

2

, Mazda Farshad

3

, Jonas Widmer

1

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Spine Biomechanics, Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

2

Department of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering, ETH Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

3

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

Introduction: Sensor-equipped implants provide an opportunity to monitor mechanical loading in spinal fusion constructs. While axial compression is the dominant load on the spine and generally produces only small, non-critical rod forces, anterior bending moments are far more harmful. These may occur at end range anterior flexion with inadequate muscular contraction due to fatigue, sudden movements, or postoperative muscle degeneration. The objective of this study was to reproduce such conditions, referred to as flexion relaxation, in cadaveric lumbar segments with intact discs, PLIF and TLIF cages, and to assess whether strain-gauged rods can detect the resulting critical loading patterns. Material and Methods: Twelve fresh-frozen human lumbar motion segments were dissected while preserving intervertebral discs and bony elements. A test setup simulated flexion relaxation by applying a 100 N axial force transformed into an 18.6 N·m sagittal bending moment via the spinous process. Each specimen was equipped with four pedicle screws and two strain-gauged rods. Four specimens received a PLIF cage, four a TLIF cage, both with embedded force sensors, and four had no cage. In the no-cage group, sequential decompression (laminotomy, nucleotomy, facetectomy) was performed. In cage groups, rod and cage loads were recorded before and after simulated cage subsidence and cage removal. Results: Under flexion relaxation, fixation rods in all specimens consistently exhibited extension bending, as confirmed by strain-gauge outputs. This occurred because the disc or cage acted as a central fulcrum, generating distraction forces on the rods. The resulting tensile loading produced a counter-moment that placed them in extension despite the externally applied flexion load. This counter-moment, detected by the rod sensors when the segments were in flexion, could be considered an indicator of harmful loading on the construct. At 18.6 N·m bending moment in flexion relaxation, median normalized rod strain decreased by 35.6% after endplate disruption and by 41.9% after cage removal (p = .009 and .003), while cage load declined by 50% following endplate disruption (p = .001). Progressive decompression increased rod strain by 6.6-10.2% (p = .090 to < .001), whereas complete nucleotomy reduced it by 45.7% (p = .004). The reductions observed after cage subsidence and complete nucleotomy further confirmed the fulcrum effect, indicating loss of the mechanism that previously amplified loading on the fixation rods. Notably, cage configuration also influenced rod loading, with rod strain 18.9% lower in the PLIF group and 38.5% higher in the TLIF group compared to no-cage controls. Conclusion: Our results demonstrate that critical loading can be detected across different fusion constructs using sensor-equipped fixation rods. Recognition of the fulcrum effect and the resulting extension bending on the rods is central to this detection. These findings support the potential of future smart implants to identify harmful loading conditions occuring specifically during flexion-relaxation in spinal fusion.

## ID: AGS26/2683

## P0074.1: Biomechanics of Lumbar Lateral Interbody Fixation Augmented with Pedicle Screws, Facet Screws, or Spinous Process Plate

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Sukanya Chebrolu

1

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

1

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Posterior stabilization is commonly used to augment lateral interbody fusion by limiting motion and protecting against implant migration and subsidence. Among available options are bilateral pedicle screws, bilateral transfacet screws, and spinous process plates. The relative contribution of these constructs when paired with a standalone lateral interbody device has not been fully quantified under physiologic loading. This cadaveric study compared the stabilizing effects of these constructs across primary motion planes. Materials and Methods: Fourteen human lumbar spine specimens (n = 14) from L3 to S1 were tested at the L4 to L5 motion segment using a non-constraining pure moment protocol to 7.5 newton meters in flexion extension, lateral bending, and axial rotation. Specimens were evaluated sequentially in the intact state, lateral interbody alone, lateral interbody with spinous process plate, and lateral interbody with unilateral or bilateral fixation using pedicle screws or transfacet screws. Motion was tracked with an optoelectronic system. Range of motion was the primary outcome. Repeated measures analyses with post hoc testing compared constructs, with significance at p < 0.05. Pullout strength and displacement variance were also assessed. Results: All posterior constructs significantly reduced range of motion compared with lateral interbody alone in all planes. Bilateral pedicle screw and bilateral transfacet screw constructs provided the largest reductions and approached intact-spine stability, especially in axial rotation. In flexion extension, there were no significant differences among constructs. In lateral bending, the lateral interbody plus spinous process plate exhibited greater motion than other constructs except lateral interbody with spinous process plate and unilateral pedicle screw fixation (p = 0.073). Pullout strength variance was not significant across constructs, while displacement variance demonstrated significance for pullout strength but not for displacement. Conclusion: Augmentation of lateral interbody fusion with posterior fixation improves mechanical stability. Bilateral constructs provide the greatest benefit, and transfacet screw constructs perform comparably to pedicle screws in key planes. These data can inform selection of posterior augmentation tailored to desired stability while considering invasiveness.

## ID: AGS26/2821

## P0076: Biomechanical changes in kyphotic cervical spine after anterior cervical discectomy and fusion with different degrees of correction

Hongyu Chen

1

, Xu Ma

2

, Yu Sun

1

, Feifei Zhou

1

1

Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

2

Beihang University, Beijing, China

Introduction: A finite element simulation study exploring the appropriate correction angle of cervical kyphosis based on patient imaging data from a mechanical perspective. Material and Methods: We utilized CT data from three patients with severe cervical kyphosis of different etiologies (idiopathic, iatrogenic, degenerative) and employed finite element simulation to compare the mechanical environment differences of the cervical spine under varying correction angles following multilevel anterior cervical discectomy and fusion. Results: All patient-specific models demonstrated consistent biomechanical behavior across varying orthopedic angles. Adjacent C2-C3 segments exhibited greater flexion mobility under low correction angles and increased extension under high angles, while axial rotation showed irregular patterns. Stress distribution maps revealed that extreme correction angles caused localized and asymmetric stress concentrations, whereas intermediate angles (10°-20°) promoted more uniform stress across intervertebral discs, facet joints, and implants. Quantitative analysis of Von-Mises stress showed a clear U-shaped trend across key structures. In Patient A, cage stress decreased from 5.06 MPa at 40° to 1.50 MPa at 20°, then increased to 7.51 MPa at −10°; interface stress followed a similar pattern, dropping to 1.00 MPa at 20° and rising to 5.62 MPa at −10°. Titanium plate and screw stresses also minimized at moderate angles before increasing again at extreme corrections. These results indicate that moderate Cobb angles reduce mechanical burden and may lower the risk of postoperative complications. Conclusion: As the correction degree increases, the adjacent segment ROM demonstrates varied trends, remaining relatively balanced at moderate correction levels. The stress on adjacent intervertebral discs, cages, facet joints, vertebral surfaces, plates and screws initially decreases and then rises with increasing correction degree. The correlation between these biomechanical data and the occurrence of complications following cervical kyphosis correction underscores the presence of an appropriate range of correction angles.

## ID: AGS26/3003

## P0077: Biomechanical effects of facet joint, tension band, and spinous process violation in TLIF surgery

Alina Jacob

1

, Ivan Zderic

2

, Daniel Haschtmann

3

, Tamas Fekete

3

, Hans Georg Kesseler

1

, Rainer Wirtz

1

, Geoff Richards

2

, Boyko Gueorguiev

2

, Markus Loibl

3

1

University hospital of Ulm, Ulm, Germany

2

AO Institute Davos, Davos, Switzerland

3

Schulthess Clinic, Zurich, Switzerland

Introduction: Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) is a well-established procedure for treating degenerative pathologies of the lumbar spine. This study aims to investigate the impact of breaches during TLIF surgery on ROM of the instrumented and adjacent segment. Material and Methods: Seven human cadaveric spines (L1-L5) underwent quasi-static non-destructive biomechanical testing in flexion-extension (Flex-Ext), lateral bending (LB), and axial rotation (AR). Motion was evaluated native, after TLIF L3-4, and a three-step sequential damage protocol was applied: superior-segment facet joint capsule incision at L2/3 (step 1), posterior tension band disruption at L2-3 (step 2), and spinous process fracture L3 (step 3). Primary outcome was Range of motion (ROM) and neutral zone (NZ) at the cranial adjacent level. To analyze relative changes, ROM and NZ were normalized to the native state. Results: At the cranial adjacent level L2-3, step 1 increased relative ROM significantly in AR (172%, p = 0.041). Step 2 significantly increased relative ROM in Flex (131%, p = 0.019). Step 3 did not significantly change relative ROM (p ≥ 0.058). At L2-3, mean NZ increased for all loading directions without statistical significance. At L3/4 TLIF instrumentation decreased L3-4 ROM significantly in Flex (-2.05 ± 1.05°, p=0.007), Ext (1.44 ± 0.81°, p = 0.003), right LB (-2.0 ± 1.59°, p = 0.032), and left LB (1.35 ± 0.57°, p = 0.027), with a trend in left AR (-0.69 ± 0.74°, p = 0.071) and right AR (0.95 ± 0.91°, p=0.079). Steps 1-3 did not cause significant change of the absolute and relative ROM at L3-4 (p ≥ 0.079). Conclusion: In vitro testing of human lumbar specimens showed that minor instabilities become more pronounced when they occur adjacent to a fused segment. The investigated surgical breaches should be avoided wherever possible.

## ID: AGS26/3030

## P0078: The impact of technical factors on pelvic parameters and pseudarthrosis in long-segment spinal instrumentation

Fatma Hediye Erzurumlu

1

, Bekir Ay

1

, Berfin Aknar

1

, Inan Uzunoglu

1

1

Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Objective: Long-segment spinal instrumentation is a complex surgical procedure performed in patients with advanced spinal deformities or degenerative pathology. Restoration and preservation of sagittal balance are essential for favorable outcomes. Technical details such as dual-rod constructs, domino connectors, and iliac fixation are believed to influence both radiographic alignment and the risk of pseudarthrosis. This study aimed to evaluate the relationship between these surgical techniques, spinopelvic parameters, and pseudarthrosis, with specific consideration of pelvic incidence score (PIS) stratification. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed patients who underwent long-segment spinal stabilization between 2022 and 2024. Only patients in whom the construct extended inferiorly to the pelvis and superiorly to at least L1 were included. Demographic characteristics, surgical details (single vs. dual rods, presence of domino connectors, iliac domino fixation), and spinopelvic parameters were collected. Radiographic parameters (PI, PT, SS, LL, SVA, GT) were evaluated both pre- and postoperatively. Patients were classified according to PIS: grade 1 (< 45°), grade 2 (45-60°), and grade 3 (> 60°). Pseudarthrosis was assessed at the 6th postoperative month based on radiographic and clinical criteria. Statistical analysis was performed to determine significant associations.

 

POST OP AP AND LATERAL GRAPHY
Results: The cohort consisted of patients with a mean age of 60 ± 12 years, with a predominance of female cases. Dual-rod constructs were associated with more stable sagittal balance and reduced loss of lumbar lordosis compared with single-rod constructs (p < 0.05). Domino connectors contributed significantly to lordosis preservation in high PIS patients (p = 0.041). Iliac domino fixation was associated with a markedly reduced rate of pseudarthrosis (p = 0.032). Stratification by PIS demonstrated that grade 3 patients (> 60°) had the greatest challenge in restoring sagittal alignment and exhibited the highest rate of pseudarthrosis, despite technical reinforcement (p < 0.05). In contrast, grade 1-2 patients showed significantly lower pseudarthrosis rates and more favorable radiological outcomes. Conclusion: Technical factors play a crucial role in the success of long-segment spinal instrumentation. Dual-rod constructs, domino connectors, and iliac fixation provide biomechanical advantages that support sagittal balance and lower the risk of pseudarthrosis, particularly in high PIS patients. Pelvic incidence stratification should be considered during preoperative planning, as higher PIS values are predictive of greater biomechanical demand and increased risk of nonunion. Tailoring the construct according to pelvic morphology may optimize long-term stability and improve patient outcomes. Limitations: The main limitations of this study include the relatively small number of patients and the follow-up limited to six months, which may restrict the generalizability of the findings. Larger prospective series with longer follow-up are required to validate these results.

## ID: AGS26/3255

## P0079: Anatomical and biomechanical considerations of posterior fixation at the cervicothoracic junction: literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

1

, Elouni Emna

2

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: The cervicothoracic junction (CTJ) represents a biomechanically complex region due to the transition from the flexible cervical to the rigid thoracic spine. Variations in vertebral morphology and pedicle size increase the risk of screw misplacement and implant failure during posterior fixation. Understanding these anatomical and biomechanical features is essential for safe and effective stabilization. Material and Methods: A systematic review of PubMed and Google Scholar was performed using the keywords: “cervicothoracic junction,” “posterior fixation,” “pedicle screw,” “biomechanics,” and “anatomy.” Studies reporting vertebral measurements, pedicle dimensions, screw placement techniques, and biomechanical outcomes were included. Data on clinical outcomes, complication rates, and construct stability were also analyzed. Results:
- Pedicle dimensions vary significantly from C7 to T2, influencing screw trajectory and diameter selection.
- Biomechanical analyses show that short-segment constructs across the CTJ may experience higher stress at the junction, whereas long constructs improve stability but may increase adjacent segment motion.
- Intraoperative navigation and imaging significantly enhance screw placement accuracy and reduce neurovascular injury.
- Clinical series highlight the importance of preoperative planning based on CTJ anatomy and biomechanics to optimize construct stability and minimize complications.
Conclusion: Posterior fixation at the CTJ requires meticulous preoperative planning informed by anatomical and biomechanical data. The use of intraoperative navigation and imaging improves safety, and construct length should be tailored to balance stability with preservation of adjacent segments. Further in vivo biomechanical studies are warranted to refine surgical strategies and improve patient outcomes in this challenging anatomical region.

## ID: AGS26/3262

## P0080: Contribution of biomechanical modeling of the trunk in lower back pain

Ahmed Adel Bezzaouia

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, oussama zaibet

1

, Yahia Aissaoui

1

, Khalil Habboubi

1

, Hassen Makhlouf

1

1

Mohamed Kassab Institute of Orthopedics, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: The spine is supported by the core muscles, which ensure balance and stability during movement. Disorders of the core muscles are a common cause of spinal pain. Analysis of spinal loads and surrounding muscle forces provides insight into and may even predict the progression of painful lumbar disorders. The aim of this project was to determine the muscle activities that maintain spinal balance during daily physical activities, as well as the potential compensatory mechanisms in the presence of chronic low back pain. Musculoskeletal modeling is used to estimate trunk muscle loads, reactions, and joint moments during various postures and loads. Material and Methods: Two healthy subjects and two patients suffering from chronic non-specific low back pain, aged between 32 and 69, participated in this study. Videos of load-bearing movements were recorded. A biomechanical analysis involving mathematical estimates of trunk muscle strength, trunk segment kinematics, and moment calculations was performed. In this project, the spine model, developed using OPENSIM musculoskeletal modeling software, consisted of the entire thoracolumbar spine and rib cage. The subjects performed flexion movements with and without a 12 kg load. Comparisons between the groups were made on the average model of the complete cycle of all biomechanical variables for the different postures studied. Results: Biomechanical modeling revealed significant differences between the groups for certain muscles (lumbar and thoracic erector spinae). In the trunk extension posture, we clearly observe that the L5-S1 intervertebral joint bears the most load, with an articular moment intensity five times higher than the other intervertebral joints. In healthy subjects, we found that in the L5-S1 joint, the flexion moment increased from 19.06 Nm in the standing position with load to 222.84 Nm in the 90° flexed position with load, which represents an increase of 203.78 Nm. Despite a wide variability in the responses reported in the literature, in this project, a smaller range of motion in flexion was observed in patients with chronic low back pain. Thus, the stiffening of the trunk due to increased muscle activity, which is often reported as a protective mechanism in people with chronic low back pain, leads to limited movement. Conclusion: Biomechanical analysis of healthy and pathological trunks during standardized physical activity has revealed the impact of movements and external forces on the forces applied by the trunk muscles and joint reactions. Musculoskeletal modeling was used to analyze the effects of spinal flexion and extension on its various bone and muscle components. This work is a preliminary study to test the contributions of this biomechanical analysis to the study of low back pain, opening up interesting and more in-depth perspectives on this topic.

## ID: AGS26/3274

## P0081: GAP score as a functional parameter in outpatient low back pain assessment

Oguz Kagan Demirtas

1

, Fatma Hediye Erzurumlu

1

1

Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: The Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score was designed to predict mechanical complications after surgery for adult spinal deformity. While its role in surgical planning has been well studied, little is known about its clinical relevance in patients who have not undergone surgery. Understanding whether sagittal imbalance measured by GAP is related to pain and disability in routine outpatient settings may provide new insight into its broader utility. This study aimed to investigate the association between GAP score and patient-reported outcomes, including the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), back pain Visual Analog Scale (VAS), and Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire (RMDQ), in a cohort of nonoperatively managed patients with low back pain. Material and Methods: This cross-sectional study included 107 consecutive patients who visited our outpatient clinic with low back pain. None of the patients had previous spinal surgery. Standard standing radiographs were used to measure sagittal parameters, and the Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score was calculated for each patient. Patient-reported outcomes were collected on the same day, including the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), back pain Visual Analog Scale (VAS), and Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire (RMDQ). Patients were grouped as proportioned, moderately disproportioned, or severely disproportioned according to GAP. Statistical analysis included Spearman correlation between GAP and each outcome, and group comparisons using Kruskal-Wallis tests. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Results: A total of 107 patients were evaluated. The Kruskal-Wallis test showed a significant difference in Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores among GAP categories (χ2 = 7.59, p = 0.023). Post-hoc analysis revealed that patients in the moderate and severe disproportion groups had higher disability scores compared with the aligned group. In contrast, no significant differences were observed for back pain Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores (χ2 = 1.52, p = 0.467) or Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire (RMDQ) scores (χ2 = 3.10, p = 0.212). Although ODI increased with worsening GAP category, pain intensity and RMDQ scores did not follow the same pattern. These findings suggest that GAP is more closely associated with functional disability than with pain severity. Conclusion: This study showed that the Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score is associated with disability, but not directly with pain, in patients with low back pain who have not undergone surgery. These findings suggest that GAP may provide useful information about functional limitation beyond traditional pain scores. In this way, GAP has the potential to serve as a simple and practical parameter in the routine outpatient evaluation of patients with low back pain. Given the cross-sectional design and sample size, these results should be interpreted cautiously, and further prospective studies are needed to confirm whether GAP can be integrated as a standard clinical measure.

## ID: AGS26/3485

## P0082: Systematic review of lordotic cage angles in posterior/lateral lumbar fusion: higher angles yield greater segmental correction but inconsistent total improvement

Grace Kim

1

, Evan Dunn

2

, Justin Chung

3

, Rowen Lin

3

, Kevin Mo

2

, William Fang

2

, Shanthan Challa

2

, Daniel Lee

2

1

Western University of Health Science, Pomona, United States

2

Valley Hospital Medical Center, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

3

Touro University Nevada, College of Medicine, Henderson, United States

Introduction: Interchange lordosis is important to restoring lumbar sagittal alignment in spinal fusion. Cage angles can range from neutral to hyperlordotic in order to optimize radiographic and clinical outcomes. This systematic review evaluates radiologic outcomes across different lordotic cage angles in posterior/lateral lumbar fusion. Methods: This systematic review was performed adhering to PRISMA Guidelines to compare the different angles of the lordotic cage. PubMed and Google Scholar were queried for studies comparing varying cage angles (0°, 4-5°, 6-8°, 10-12°, and 15-20°) in PLIF or LLIF. Studies that were non-English and pediatric were excluded. Lumbar lordosis and segmental lordosis were recorded. Mean differences, ranges, and p-values were extrapolated to analyze by grouped angles (0°, 4°-8°, 10°-12°, 15°-20°). Results: Our final review included nine studies with 940 patients in which 197 had non-lordotic cages (0°), 255 received low-angle cages (4°-5°), 239 had medium-angle cages (6°-8°), 174 had high-angle cages (10°-12°), and 75 received extreme-angle cages (15°-20°) when undergoing posterior/lateral lumbar interbody fusion. When comparing to non-lordotic cages, the mean difference between low-angle cages was 8° total and the segmental was 3° (p < 0.05); the mean differences for medium-angle cages were -1.99° total and 3° segmental (p > 0.05); for high-angle cages were -0.8° total and -2.6° segmental (p > 0.05). For medium angles, compared to low-angle cages mena differences ranged from -1.4° to +2.4° total and +0.6° to +1.5° segmental indicated mixed statistical significance; compared to high-angle cages had a +2.3° total and -0.2° segmental difference, (p > 0.05); extreme-angle cages had -1.4° total and -2.9° segmental differences, (p < 0.05). When comparing high angles and medium angles, mean differences ranged from -2.3° to -5.6° total (p > 0.05) and the segmental differences ranged from +0.61° to +10.2° segmental (p < 0.05); extreme angles showed -1.4° total and -2.9° segmental differences, (p < 0.05). Conclusion: Lordotic cage angle is positively correlated with segmental lordosis restoration. Higher angles (≥ 10°) show superior segmental correction but do not consistently improve global lumbar alignment or pelvic parameters. These findings suggest segmental alignment may benefit from higher angles. Only nine studies have been performed so far and further research is necessary to establish their impact on global alignment and patient-reported outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1281

## P0082.1: Mechanical Properties of 3D-Printed Porous Spinal Implants: Structural Optimization and Effects of Surface Ceramization

Wenlong Yang

1

, Chaoyuan Ge

1

1

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, China

Introduction: To reduce the elastic modulus of TC4, a porous design was applied to the implant without compromising its strength. By removing “redundant portions,” mechanical matching at the implant-bone interface was achieved. Material and Methods: The CAD method was selected for the optimized design of the porous structure. Using UG NX 12.0 software, a rhombic dodecahedron was chosen as the unit cell, with the porosity fixed at 65%. Four types of porous spinal implant components with pore sizes of 650 μm, 650 μm, 850 μm, and 1100 μm were designed. The Gibson-Ashby model was used to predict the mechanical properties before and after surface ceramization. These predictions were then validated through quasi-static compression experiments. Considering the manufacturing precision of the selective laser melting (SLM) process, porous samples highly compatible with the bone tissue repair and mechanical properties of different spinal motion segment regions were selected, achieving structural optimization of the porous spinal implant. Results: (1) SEM Surface Quality Observation: The SLM-formed porous samples exhibited a regular structure with neatly arranged struts and relatively uniform dimensions. No severe defects such as pores or cracks were observed on the surface. Pores were clearly visible without fused pores or irregular pores caused by strut overlapping. Samples of the same type showed good consistency in forming. No significant difference in surface quality was observed between carburized and non-carburized samples, indicating that surface ceramization did not alter the surface morphology. However, partially unmelted spherical powder particles and “step-like” structures were observed on the strut surfaces, resulting in a relatively rough microscopic morphology and slightly larger formed dimensions compared to the design model. (2) Compared to the design model, the structural dimensions of the formed parts showed slight deviations. A one-way ANOVA comparing the model design parameters with the formed parameters measured by Micro-CT revealed that all P-values were greater than 0.05, indicating no statistically significant differences between the designed and measured values of pore size, strut diameter, and porosity for the different porous structures. This phenomenon is related to the “rapid melting and solidification” characteristics of the SLM process. Unmelted metal powder adhering to the strut surfaces and the layer-by-layer powder stacking during inclined surface formation increased strut diameter, reduced pore size, and consequently decreased porosity. Statistical analysis demonstrated that the selected SLM process parameters ensured good consistency and high forming quality for the porous structures designed in this experiment. (3) Mechanical Properties: The stress-strain curves of the four types of porous structures before and after carburization showed that, in the initial stage of the compression test, the stress-strain curve exhibited nonlinear behavior due to slight mismatching between the compression plates and the porous samples, resulting in incomplete parallelism of the assembly end faces with the horizontal plane. Subsequently, the samples quickly entered the elastic deformation stage and remained in a linear elastic deformation state under the given load conditions without undergoing plastic failure. The stress-strain curves of the four types of porous structures before and after carburization were plotted. The elastic modulus of the samples, represented by the slope of the curve, was uniformly determined at a compressive strain of 0.015% to represent the mechanical properties of the samples. The results demonstrated that the porous design significantly reduced the elastic modulus of TC4, avoiding the “stress shielding” effect. (4) Bone Ingrowth Experiment: The porous structure with a pore size of 850 μm showed the highest amount of bone tissue ingrowth. Conclusion: The porous spinal implant with a rhombic dodecahedron unit cell, 65% porosity, and 850 μm pore size exhibited an elastic modulus and mechanical strength close to those of spinal vertebral bone tissue, along with excellent osseointegration capability. It can serve as a foundational design unit for porous spinal implants.

## Deformity-cervical

## ID: AGS26/59

## P0083: Third occipital condyle: as rare as it is

Alexandrina Nikova

1

, Dimitrios Papoutsakis

1

, Athena Papachristou

1

, Savvas Lipiridis

1

, Anastasia Pana

1

, George Makrigiannakis

1

, Christos Gogos

1

1

Asclipieio Voulas Hospital, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Third occipital condyle or condylus tertius or the median occipital condyle is congenital anatomical variation due to an embryological remnant of the proatlas sclerotome. On imaging or anatomical studies, it appears attached to the skull base and often articulates with the anterior arch of the atlas and the odontoid apex. In some cases, it could be assessed as occipital condyle fracture. The incidence of the third condyle is extremely rare, but it could lead to significant instability and neurovascular compression. In the literature until now are presented 14 cases with third condyle with variable symptomatology. In the present study, we aim to report a case of third occipital condyle. Case Description: A 36-years old male was presented to the emergency department due to reported bar fight in the last 48 hours. The patient was fully responsive with 15/15 Glasgow Coma Scale, without any neurological deficits. The only reported symptoms were neck pain and vertigo. There were no additional injuries or symptoms. The patient obtained an x-ray of the cervical spine and skull. The report was insufficient for the presence of any pathology in the craniovertebral junction, or the skull, therefore the patient obtained a brain and cervical computer tomograph scan (CAT). The results showed the presence of third condyle without any additional injuries. Additionally, the patient was evaluated by ENT specialist for the vertigo and conservative therapy was prescribed for several days. Conclusions: Third occipital condyle is very rare entity that could be easily confused with occipital condyle fracture. The importance of such cases is to raise awareness of the presence of such anomalies that could change completely the therapeutic approaches.
