## P0084: Pott's disease of the cervical spine: a case report of a rare tubercular localization

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Balkis Hajji

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Tuberculosis remains a healthcare problem in many underdeveloped countries. Tuberculosis of the central nervous system accounts for approximately 1% of all cases of tuberculosis, among which 50% involve the spine. Tuberculosis (TB) of the spine, also known as Pott’s disease, is the most common form of skeletal TB. Lumbar and thoracic spines are the most frequent localizations of this infection. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 19 years-old female patient with no medical background. She presented for the onset of cervical pain evolving since 2 months with no improvement after medical treatment. Neurological examination didn’t show any motor or sensitive deficit, or any abnormalities in reflexes. Standard Radiography and cervical CT scan showed a lytic lesion of C2 with an evident compression on the spinal cord. The MRI confirmed the diagnosis showing a complete destruction associated to an epiduritis at the level of C1 and C2. A surgical biopsy was realized, and pathological examination concluded to a tubercular localization of the cervical spine. The patient was reoperated. An osteosynthesis of the cervical Spine performed, followed by 12 months of anti tuberculosis treatment with a good outcome. After 3 years of clinical and radiological follow up, the patient had neither recurrence of the pain, nor neurological signs. Imaging showed a total removal of the lytic lesions. Results: Spinal Tuberculosis represents approximately half of all cases of skeletal TB. The most common involved site is the mid thoracic vertebrae along with lumbar spine. The involvement of the cervical spine remains less common. Diagnosis of spinal TB is made essentially made by a combination of imaging and culture of Mycobacterium tuberculosis from biopsy samples. In addition to that, spinal lesions are often accompanied by the presence of a large para-spinal abscess. Currently, medical treatment with four-drug therapy is considered to be a first-line treatment for spinal tuberculosis. Surgery for these kind of lesions is risky, it has limited indications including neurological sequelae, spinal instability, significant kyphosis, refractory pain, or failure of medical treatment. It is important in these patients to provide close neurological follow-up with appropriate TB medications and aggressive pain control for successful medical management. Conclusion: Prognosis for cervical Pott’s disease has been improved by early diagnosis and rapid intervention. A high degree of clinical suspicion is required if patients present with chronic pain, even in the absence of neurologic symptoms and signs. Medical treatment is generally effective. Surgical intervention is necessary in cases with marked bony involvement, abscess formation, or neurological deficit.

## ID: AGS26/347

## P0085: Imaging-based evaluation and surgical decision-making in retro-odontoid pseudotumor: proposal of a novel scoring system

Yoshihiro Ishihama

1

, Terumasa Ikeda

1

, Shunki Iemura

1

, Kensuke Toriumi

1

, Koji Goto

1

1

Kindai University Hospital, Osakasayama, Japan

Introduction: Retro-odontoid pseudotumor (ROP) can cause cervical myelopathy due to spinal cord compression, requiring precise clinical judgment and imaging-based assessment. While ROP is frequently associated with rheumatoid arthritis, non-rheumatoid cases are increasingly recognized, and optimal criteria for surgical decision-making remain unclear. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 38 consecutive patients (mean age, 75.8 ± 8.0 years; 65.8% male) with non-rheumatoid ROP who underwent cervical spine surgery at our institution between 2017 and April 2025. Eighteen patients underwent surgery for ROP (surgical group, S group), and 20 were managed conservatively (conservative group, C group). Radiographic parameters included atlantoaxial instability, atlantodental interval (ADI), C1/2 osteoarthritis, C2-7 sagittal vertical axis (SVA), T1 slope, O-C1 angle, C1-2 angle, C2-7 angle, and ranges of motion (ROM) at O-C1, C1-2, and C2-7. MRI variables included disc degeneration (C2/3-C7/T1), retro-odontoid soft tissue thickness (t-ROP), spinal canal anteroposterior diameter (APD), and the t-ROP/APD ratio. Results: Age, sex, and C1/2 osteoarthritis did not differ between groups. A tendency toward atlantoaxial instability was observed in the S group (p = 0.08). Compared with the C group, the S group demonstrated greater C2-7 SVA (50.0 vs. 33.9 mm, p < 0.05), smaller O-C1 angle (0.78° vs. -11.4°, p < 0.001), smaller C2-7 angle (5.8° vs. 15.3°, p = 0.05), decreased ROM at O-C1 (7.1° vs. 11.8°, p < 0.05) and C2-7 (20.5° vs. 36.5°, p < 0.001), increased t-ROP thickness (11.4 vs. 7.8 mm, p < 0.001), reduced APD (8.1 vs. 12.7 mm, p < 0.001), and higher t-ROP/APD ratio (1.65 vs. 0.63, p < 0.001). Significant disc degeneration at C4/5 was also noted. Based on ROC-derived cutoffs, we constructed a scoring system incorporating four variables: O-C1 angle ≤ -4° (1 point), ROM(O-C1) ≤ 8° (1 point), ROM(C2-7) ≤ 30° (2 points), and t-ROP/APD ratio ≥ 1.0 (2 points), with a maximum score of 6. A score ≥ 4 identified 94.1% of surgical cases, while no conservative cases exceeded this threshold. The score correlated strongly with surgical indication (p < 0.001), with an AUC of 0.98, indicating excellent discriminative ability. Conclusion: Decreased mobility of the occipito-atlantal and subaxial cervical segments appears to promote compensatory atlantoaxial instability and ROP formation. We propose a novel exploratory scoring system integrating dynamic radiographic and MRI indices, which demonstrated strong discriminatory performance in identifying surgical candidates. Although limited by small sample size and single-center design, this system may serve as a practical guide for surgical decision-making in non-rheumatoid ROP. External validation in larger multicenter cohorts is warranted.

## ID: AGS26/381

## P0086: Single-stage circumferential correction of rigid post-traumatic cervical kyphosis in a patient with DISH and OPLL: a rare case report

Ricardo Navarro-Navarro

1,2

, Tito Fernadez-Varela

1

, Arturo Montesdeoca-Ara

1

, Jose Augusto Lorenzo-Rivero

1

, Laura Pastor-Martin

1

, Brian Melchiorsen

1

1

Hospital Universitario de Gran Canaria Dr Negrin, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

2

University of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria (ULPGC), Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

Introduction: Rigid post-traumatic cervical kyphosis in patients with Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis (DISH) and Ossification of the Posterior Longitudinal Ligament (OPLL) is uncommon. These deformities cause severe mechanical pain and functional limitation, and their surgical correction carries respiratory and neurological risks, requiring mandatory neurophysiological monitoring. Few reports describe single-stage circumferential correction in this setting. Material and Methods: A 67-year-old woman with aspirin allergy and prior gynecological and bariatric surgery sustained a fall with occipital trauma, resulting in a C5-C6 fracture. After 5 months she developed rigid kyphotic deformity, mechanical pain, and severe functional limitation (vertical chin-brow angle [vCBA] of 40°) without neurological deficits. MRI and CT showed chronic C5-C6 wedge fractures with bone fragility, DISH and cOPLL, C3-7 canal stenosis (most severe at C3-C4), and kyphosis with C2-C7 angle 27° and C4-C7 angle 31°. Results: A single-stage surgery was performed under multimodal neurophysiological monitoring. First, posterior release and C4-C7 facets osteotomy were done, followed by anterior approach with C5-C6 corpectomy and C4-C7 anterior fusion with an expandable titanium spacer and plate. At 10 months follow-up, bone fusion was achieved, correction of C2-C7 kyphosis to 8° and C4-C7 kyphosis to 5°, and reduction of vCBA to 10°, with marked functional improvement and no neurological deficits. Conclusion: This case represents a rare example of rigid posttraumatic cervical kyphosis with combined DISH and OPLL, without neurological impairment. The single-stage circumferential posterior- anterior approach restored cervical alignment, improved function, and minimized neurological risk through neurophysiological monitoring. The radiological angle correction aligns with favorable outcomes reported in limited literature, although this combination is rare. Careful planning, posterior release, and stable anterior reconstruction are essential for satisfactory clinical and radiological results.

## ID: AGS26/528

## P0087: Cervical muscle morphology and cervical alignment changes following lumbar pedicle subtraction osteotomy

Bruno Verna

1

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Ali Guven

1

, Anna-Maria Mielke

1,2

, Johnathan Slawitsky

3

, Thomas Caffard1

4

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Jennifer Shue

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Alexander Hughes

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Centrum für Muskuloskeletale Chirurgie, Charité, Berlin, Germany

3

Georgetown University School of Medicine, Washington DC, United States

4

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, University of Ulm, Ulm, Germany

Introduction: Sagittal alignment correction in adult spinal deformity is achieved through various osteotomy techniques, including pedicle subtraction osteotomy (PSO). Prior research has traditionally focused on sagittal alignment, degree of lumbar correction, and curve rigidity to optimize surgical outcomes. However, the paraspinal soft tissue envelope is increasingly recognized as an important factor. This study evaluates the impact of preoperative cervical muscle morphology on cervical alignment changes following PSO. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on patients who underwent lumbar PSO with available preoperative and postoperative standing whole-spine lateral radiographs. Cervical alignment changes were assessed preoperatively and at two postoperative time points (PO1: 1-30 days and PO2: 1-2 years). Alignment parameters included cervical lordosis, C2-7 sagittal vertical axis (cSVA), C0-2 angle, T1 slope, C7 slope, cervical tilt, cranial tilt, neck tilt, and thoracic inlet angle (TIA). Cervical muscles from C3 to C7 were segmented as five groups: sternocleidomastoid (SCM), trapezius (TP), anterior group (AG), posteromedial (PM), and posterolateral (PL). Functional cross-sectional area (fCSA) and fat infiltration (FI) were measured. Spearman correlation assessed relationships between preoperative muscle parameters and both immediate (Preoperative to PO1) and long-term (PO1 to PO2) cervical alignment changes. Results: Twenty-two patients (54% female, median age 61, BMI 26.6 kg/m2) were included. Spearman analysis revealed several significant correlations (all p < 0.05). In the immediate analysis, higher AG fCSA at C3 correlated with smaller decreases in cSVA, while higher PL fCSA at C3 and C7 correlated with smaller decreases in C7 slope and cranial tilt. In contrast, higher PM fCSA at C6-C7 correlated with larger decreases in cranial tilt. TP fCSA showed mixed effects: at C3 it correlated with larger decreases in cSVA, whereas at C4 it correlated with smaller decreases in cervical tilt and C7 slope. Regarding FI, higher trapezius FI correlated with smaller decreases in cSVA (C3) and larger decreases in cranial tilt (C5), while higher PM FI correlated with smaller decreases in C7 slope (C4) and cranial tilt (C4-C6). In the long-term analysis, higher PM fCSA at C3-C4 correlated with greater increases in cSVA and T1 slope, while higher AG fCSA at C5 correlated with greater increases in the C0-2 angle. PL fCSA correlated with greater increases in TIA and C0-2 angle. In contrast, TP fCSA at C5 correlated with smaller increases in C0-2 angle. Higher FI across multiple levels correlated with smaller increases in TIA, T1 slope, C7 slope, cranial tilt, C0-2 angle, and cSVA. By contrast, higher TP FI at C3 and C5 correlated with greater increases in T1 slope and C7 slope. Conclusion: Preoperative cervical muscle morphology had an impact on both immediate and long-term cervical alignment changes after PSO. Higher fCSA was generally linked to smaller early changes, suggesting short-term stabilization, and to larger long-term increases, consistent with compensatory adaptation. In contrast, higher FI was associated with smaller changes both immediately and over time, pointing to short-term mechanical stiffness and long-term reduced adaptability. These findings highlight the role of cervical muscles in modulating reciprocal cervical alignment after lumbar correction and support its consideration in surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/623

## P0088: Vertebral anomalies in MURCS association: case-based evidence and systematic review of reported cases

Halit Alioglu

1

, Omar Alomari

2

, Mert Yavuz

1

, Serife Altunbay

3

, Hediye Pınar Gunbey

3

, Baris Ozoner

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Dr. Lutfi Kirdar Kartal Training and Research Hospital, University of HealthSciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Hamidiye International School of Medicine, University of HealthSciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Department of Radiology, Dr. Lutfi Kirdar Kartal Training and Research Hospital, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser (MRKH) syndrome is a rare congenital disorder in 46, XX females, typically presenting with uterine and vaginal agenesis. The MURCS association, a subtype of MRKH, additionally involves renal malformations and cervicothoracic somite anomalies. Spinal malformations, although common and clinically significant, remain underreported. This study presents a rare case of MURCS with complex vertebral anomalies and provides a systematic review of reported spinal abnormalities in the literature. Material and Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic search was performed across PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Cochrane Library (1979-2025). Studies reporting spinal anomalies in MURCS were included, while reviews without primary data and duplicates were excluded. Descriptive statistics were used to summarize clinical features, radiological findings, and management strategies. Additionally, a 35-year-old female patient presenting with cervical scoliosis, block vertebrae, and an omovertebral bone was evaluated with multimodal imaging, clinical assessment, and genetic confirmation. Results: Forty studies involving 41 patients were included. Publications spanned 1979-2025, with India, the USA, and Turkey being the most frequent contributors. The mean age was 20.5 ± 11.2 years; females accounted for 87.8%. Common presenting complaints were amenorrhea (41.5%), infertility (17.1%), neck pain (12.2%), and abdominal pain (9.8%). Short stature and short neck were noted in 53.7% each, while scoliosis (36.6%), Sprengel’s deformity (19.5%), and torticollis (14.6%) were frequent clinical findings. Cervical vertebral fusion was the predominant anomaly (53.7%), followed by Klippel-Feil syndrome (26.8%), hemivertebrae (12.2%), and cervical ribs (14.6%). Non-cervical anomalies included rib malformations (12.2%), spina bifida (7.3%), and spondylolisthesis (4.9%). Renal anomalies were nearly universal (92.7%), while hearing loss (24.4%) and cardiac defects (17.1%) represented key systemic associations. Despite the high prevalence of structural deformities, only 7.3% underwent surgical correction, with most managed conservatively. Conclusion: MURCS syndrome represents a multisystemic disorder with high prevalence of spinal anomalies, particularly cervical fusion and scoliosis, often accompanied by renal malformations. Early recognition through comprehensive imaging is essential to guide management and prevent long-term complications. Multidisciplinary care, including gynecology, urology, orthopedics, and genetics, is critical for optimizing outcomes in affected individuals.

## ID: AGS26/713

## P0089: Beyond the incision: does release extent matter in congenital muscular torticollis? A systematic review and meta-analysis

Elie Najjar

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Study Design: Systematic review and meta-analysis. Objectives: To compare outcomes after unipolar versus bipolar sternocleidomastoid (SCM) release for congenital muscular torticollis. (CMT). Methods: Following PRISMA, 17 observational studies were included (total 382 procedures: unipolar 228, bipolar 154). The primary endpoint was a good-excellent global result (Lee or modified Cheng-Tang). Secondary endpoints were recurrence and any complication. For comparative synthesis, random-effects meta-analysis (log-odds ratio, Haldane-Anscombe correction) was restricted to the 5 studies reporting both techniques (n = 133; unipolar 92, bipolar 41). Technique-specific context used random-effects single-arm pooling of proportions (logit transform). Heterogeneity was summarized with. I2. Results: Comparative meta-analysis showed no difference in good-excellent outcomes between unipolar and bipolar (OR 1.08, 95% CI 0.22-5.27, p = 0.920; k = 4, I2 = 21.6%). Recurrence favored bipolar but was not significant (OR 2.07, 95% CI 0.37-11.50, p = 0.406; k = 3, I2 = 18.1%). Across all studies, pooled success was high for both techniques-unipolar 0.85 (95% CI 0.75-0.92; k = 4) and bipolar 0.76 (95% CI 0.61-0.87; k = 4). Pooled recurrence was 8.1% (95% CI 1.8-30.1%; k = 6, I2 = 73.7%) after unipolar (sensitivity excluding an early small outlier: 4.1%, 0.7-21.6%) and 9.5% (95% CI 5.0-17.5%; k = 10, I2 = 0%) after bipolar; pooled complications were 5.0% (95% CI 1.6-14.7%; k = 4, I2 = 21.1%) vs 9.1% (95% CI 4.8-16.6%; k = 9, I2 = 0%), respectively. Angle/ROM data (where available) improved markedly after both procedures; in the adult neglected cohort, CMA correction was similar between techniques while minor complications occurred only in bipolar. Conclusion: Unipolar and bipolar SCM release yield comparable global outcomes with low recurrence and complication rates. Differences are more likely driven by age at surgery and postoperative rehabilitation intensity than release extent. Comparative certainty is limited by small head-to-head samples and incomplete arm-level reporting.

## ID: AGS26/960

## P0090: Distal junctional kyphosis at the cervicothoracic junction following surgery for adult cervical deformity: a systematic review

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Mohamad Bakhaidar

1,2

, Randall Treffy

1

, Tyler Williamson

3

, Abdul Mounnem Yassin Kassab

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Peter Passias

4

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

2

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Surgery, Faculty of Medicine, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

3

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, University of Texas Health San Antonio, San Antonio, United States

4

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Duke University Medical Center, Durham, United States

Introduction: Distal junctional kyphosis (DJK) is a significant postoperative complication in adult cervical spine deformity surgery. DJK at the cervicothoracic junction presents challenging complications that can significantly impact patients' quality of life. This systematic review aims to summarize the current literature on DJK at the cervicothoracic junction, identify knowledge gaps, and suggest directions for future research. Material and Methods: We conducted a systematic review following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines to include studies that discussed DJK at the cervicothoracic junction. Our systematic review focused on risk factors, management, and prevention strategies for DJK at the cervicothoracic junction. Results: Sixty-five articles were included in this systematic review. The identified risk factors for DJK at the cervicothoracic junction included: (1) Patient-related factors, such as preoperative neurological symptoms (e.g., presence of myelopathy), patient comorbidities (e.g., frailty), and compromised bone quality; (2) Radiographic factors, including increased preoperative C2-7 sagittal vertical axis, elevated T1 slope, reduced cervical lordosis, and thoracic hypokyphosis; And (3) surgical factors, as performing combined anterior/posterior approaches, performing Smith-Peterson or pedicle subtraction osteotomies, and choosing lower instrumented vertebra (LIV) at thoracic apex. The clinical presentation of DJK can range from asymptomatic radiographic changes to severe symptomatic conditions, significantly impacting patients' quality of life. Symptoms include neck pain, weakness, sensory impairment, symptoms of myelopathy, and radiculopathy. Radiographic evaluation should be the initial step in assessing patients with concerns about DJK. Scoliosis films (36-inch standing films) are essential for assessing global spinal alignment. Computed tomography (CT) and single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) scans can serve as valuable adjunctive tools for evaluating patients with suspected pseudarthrosis, adjacent segment degeneration, and hardware failure. Furthermore, an MRI should be obtained for patients presenting with any new neurological deficits, and it can also be beneficial in preoperative planning. To prevent DJK, it is essential to achieve optimal correction and proper cervical alignment, judiciously select the LIV with sufficient bone health, and preserve the posterior ligament complex in the distal segment. The management of DJK represents a wide range of options, with variations arising from the diversity of surgical approaches and underlying pathologies. Asymptomatic cases are typically treated non-surgically, whereas symptomatic cases often require surgical intervention. In cases where scoliosis is present in the thoracic spine, consideration should be given to extending the fusion to a sagittally stable vertebra. Conclusion: A comprehensive preoperative risk assessment and strategic surgical planning are critical for achieving optimal correction and preventing DJK at the cervicothoracic junction. Future research should prioritize high-level clinical studies that explore patient-specific, age-adjusted deformity parameters and leverage artificial intelligence to improve the prediction of DJK at the cervicothoracic junction, thereby enhancing surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1089

## P0091: Occipitalization of atlas with basilar invagination treated by occipitocervical fixation: a rare case report

Yusuf Brilliant

1

1

Kanudjoso Public Hospital Balikpapan, Balikpapan, Indonesia

Introduction: Occipitalization of atlas is an osseous anomaly of the craniovertebral junction which occurs at the base of the skull in the region of the foramen magnum. The incidence rate of occipitalization has been reported 1.42.5/1000 children. Most of the patients are asymptomatic initially. Clinical features usually appear after 3rd decade depending on the level and degree of spinal cord compression with surrounding vital structures such as vertebral artery involvement. Hence, early definitive diagnosis and subsequent successful treatment are necessary in such cases to prevent life-threatening complications. Methods: a 46 year old female come with progressive tetraparesis since a year ago with limited neck movement. Cervical spine CT scan shown the absence of atlas. MRI revealed the odontoid process above the Chamberlains line suggestive basilar invagination. Occipital cervical fixation was planned also with foramen magnum decompression. Results: Patient had motoric improvement after post operative and able to walk without assisted. Conclusions: The knowledge of atlanto-occipital assimilation and its clinical significance can help in sensitizing the patient about potential existing/future neurological implications to seek medical advice and intervention. Treatment modality completely depends on neurological symptoms and instability. Although, different surgical options are available but decompression of the spinal cord with occipitocervical fusion is the most superior surgical option for occipitalization of atlas with instability.

## ID: AGS26/1127

## P0092: Surgical strategy for dropped head deformity based on the surgicaloutcomes of 51 cases

Yoshifmi Kudo

1

, Soji Tani

1

, Ryo Yamamura

1

, Chikara Hayakawa

1

, Koki Tsuchiya

1

, Yusuke Oshita

2

, Ichiro Okano

1

, Yushi Hoshino

3

1

Showa Medical University Hospital, Tokyo, Japan

2

Showa Medical University Northern Yokohama Hospital, Yokohama, Japan

3

Asahi University Hospital, Gifu, Japan

Introduction: Dropped head syndrome (DHS) is characterized by chin-on-chest deformity with horizontal gaze difficulty and neck pain. Surgical strategy for DHS remains unestablished. Since 2019, we have been applying thoracolumbar corrective surgery to the DHS patients with significant thoracolumbar deformity factors (TL group) such as SVA > 50 mm, PI- LL > 10°, local kyphosis due to OVF > 30°. The purpose of this study is to evaluate the impact of thoracolumbar spinal alignment on surgical outcomes of the DHS patients. Material and Methods: We included DHS patients who underwent corrective surgery in our facility since 2014. Patients were divided in two groups, TL group and cervical (C) group (others). The surgical outcome of each group was compared. We investigated the spinal sagittal alignment parameters (C2-7A, CSVA, T1slope, SVA, TK, LL, PI, PT), surgical procedures, improvement of horizontal gaze difficulty and complications such as junctional failure and revision surgery. Results: This study included 51 cases (13 males, 38 females, mean age 74.9 years, F/U period 22.4 months). 22 cases in the TL group showed significantly higher preoperative SVA, T1S, PI-LL, and PT compared to 29 cases in the C group. All cases in the C group underwent cervical corrective surgery. In comparisons of pre- and post-operative spinal parameters, significant improvement was seen in C2-7A, CSVA, SVA, PT, and PI-LL. 28 cases (96.5%) showed symptom improvement whereas only one case had poor outcomes (DJF, required extension of fixation). In the TL group, 4 cases out of 7 with cervical surgeries (57.1%) had poor outcomes (2 DJF, 2 symptom recurrence due to lumbar kyphosis), with 3 cases requiring reoperation (additional surgery). In contrast, among 15 cases of TL group with thoracolumbar corrective surgeries, 12 cases (80%) showed significant improvement of the symptoms, whereas 3 cases (residual symptoms, recurrence, PJF) required reoperation (additional surgery). Significant improvements were seen in C2-7A, CSVA, SVA, T1S, PT, and PI-LL in comparisons of pre- and post-thoracolumbar corrective surgery. Finally, symptom improvement was achieved in 43 cases (84.3%), with reoperation in 7 (13.7%) cases. The ratio of poor outcomes was higher in the TL group (7/22 cases, 31.8%) than in the C group (1/28 cases, 3.6%). Conclusion: Surgical outcomes of DHS were generally favorable. Although the TL group patients with cervical corrective surgery showed poor results, improvement were achieved in 80% of cases with thoracolumbar corrective surgery. These findings suggest that DHS may occur secondarily due to anterior displacement of the weight-bearing axis associated with thoracolumbar kyphosis and inadequate compensation. Therefore, to solve this pathology, it is considered crucial to improve the underlying thoracolumbar deformity factors. It is considered that treatment selection based on assessment of whole spinal alignment is important for improving surgical outcomes in DHS.

## ID: AGS26/1186

## P0093: Treatment of muscular torticollis in adults. Clinical observational study

Arkadii Kazmin

1

, Vladimir Pereverzev

1

, Sergei Kolesov

1

1

N. N. Priorov National Medical Research Center, Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: Congenital torticollis results from contracture or fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, leading to homolateral tilt and contralateral rotation of the face and chin. The etiology of congenital torticollis remains unknown. The main method of treatment is conservative in children under 1-4 years of age, in case of unsatisfactory results of treatment, surgical treatment is used. However, in some cases, patients do not receive treatment until adulthood. Surgical treatment of adult patients is controversial. The aim of our study was to evaluate the results of surgical treatment of patients with congenital muscular torticollis older than 18 years. Material and Methods: Prospective observational study was conducted from 2010 to 2023 years and involved 14 patients with congenital muscular torticollis, who were not operated in childhood, 5 male and 9 female. One patient was excluded from the study due to previous surgical treatment for congenital muscular torticollis. The average age was 23.7 years (18-37 years). All patients treated conservatively in childhood without significant effect. For various reasons, surgical treatment was not performed. In terms of preoperative preparation, all patients underwent examination to confirm the muscular etiology of torticollis - X-ray, CT, ultrasound. Surgical treatment was carried out according to the clinical protocol. Under general anesthesia, a halo ring was applied, followed by dissection of the altered strand of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, followed by the removal of the head into the hypercorrection position in the Halo apparatus for 6-8 weeks. Results: The average wearing time of the Halo device was 6.9 weeks. The mean angle of lateral bending restriction was improved by 17.9° (20.3° PreOp vs 2.4° PostOp), and the mean angle of rotation restriction was improved by 6.1° (8.7° PreoOp vs. 2.6° PostOp). The CMA was 18.5° (9.1°-33°) PreOp and 2.1° (0°-7.6°) PostOp. There were no complications that required revision interventions. In 1 patient at week 5, inflammation of the skin of the head around the pin was detected, which resolved conservatively. Cosmetic and functional satisfaction was in 11 patients, good results in 2 patients. Conclusion: The use of Halo device with hypercorrection in the postoperative period can significantly improve cosmetic and functional results in adult patients with congenital muscular torticollis.

## ID: AGS26/1311

## P0094: Combined surgical management of basilar invagination with platybasia and atlanto-occipital assimilation: endoscopic transnasal odontoidectomy and occipitoaxial fixation

Edgar Higuera Gonzalez

1

, Alan Ferrufino Mejia

1

1

Centro Medico Nacional Siglo XXI, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Basilar invagination (BI) is a complex malformation of the craniovertebral junction, characterized by the upward migration of the odontoid into the foramen magnum, leading to compression of the brainstem and upper spinal cord. This anomaly may be congenital or acquired, and is frequently associated with other osseous abnormalities such as platybasia (a decrease in the basal angle of the skull) and atlas assimilation (fusion of the posterior arch of C1 with the occipital bone), which together constitute a clinical and radiological scenario of remarkable diagnostic and therapeutic complexity. Historically considered a radiological curiosity, basilar invagination has been redefined following the recognition of its origin in atlantoaxial instability rather than in a fixed, irreducible bony fusion. This understanding has led to a paradigm shift from classic decompression techniques (transoral or posterior approaches) toward surgical strategies centered on reduction, realignment, and stabilization of the atlantoaxial joint. The clinical presentation of these patients may include occipital headache, cervical pain, motor and sensory deficits, ataxia, dysphagia, and bulbar dysfunction. In advanced cases, brainstem compression can be life-threatening. Radiological diagnosis relies on reference lines such as Chamberlain, McGregor, and McRae, as well as morphometric measurements of the clivus and cranio-cervical axis, which help quantify the degree of invagination and guide the surgical approach. The combination of basilar invagination, platybasia, and atlas assimilation represents a particularly severe variant of craniovertebral malformations. Far from being incidental findings, these alterations reflect a chronic adaptive response to structural instability at the C0-C2 segment. Consequently, surgical management must address not only neural decompression but also biomechanically stable reconstruction of the craniovertebral complex. In recent years, combined approaches (such as endoscopic endonasal decompression followed by occipitocervical fusion) have been successfully described, allowing effective and minimally invasive treatment in selected cases. This clinical case is presented within this context to illustrate the diagnostic, anatomical, and surgical challenges involved in treating a patient with basilar invagination associated with platybasia and atlas assimilation, emphasizing the importance of a multimodal approach to evaluation and surgical planning. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 39-year-old male patient from Mexico City with a clinical and radiological diagnosis of basilar invagination associated with platybasia and atlas assimilation. He was initially evaluated in April 2025 due to a three-month history of progressive symptoms, including cervical and lumbar pain, paresthesias in all four limbs, gait disturbance, and foot numbness during prolonged walking, without improvement under conventional medical treatment. Physical examination revealed reduced cervical mobility, pain on palpation and lumbar movement, generalized hyperreflexia (++++) in all extremities, bilateral clonus, preserved strength and sensation, and no sphincter dysfunction. Imaging studies included CT and MRI of the cervical spine, which confirmed basilar invagination with odontoid migration above Chamberlain’s line, platybasia, and congenital atlas assimilation to the occiput. Ventral brainstem compression and cervical myelopathy were noted on T2-weighted sequences. Surgical treatment was performed on June 24, 2025, using a single-stage combined approach. The first stage consisted of an endoscopic transnasal odontoidectomy assisted by endoscopy and navigation, achieving direct resection of the odontoid and brainstem decompression. During the same session, a posterior approach was carried out with occipito-axial cranio-cervical fixation using an occipital plate with two screws (3.5 × 8 mm), bilateral C2 translaminar screws (20 and 24 mm × 3.5 mm), two longitudinal rods, and a cross-link. Results: The surgery was completed in a single stage with sequential anterior and posterior approaches, without intraoperative complications. Complete resection of the odontoid process was achieved by endoscopic transnasal odontoidectomy, followed by cranio-cervical stabilization with occipito-axial instrumentation. The system included bilateral C2 translaminar screws (20 and 24 mm × 3.5 mm), an occipital plate with two screws (3.5 × 8 mm), bilateral rods, and a central cross-link. In the immediate postoperative period, the patient evolved favorably, with resolution of cervical pain, recovery of gait, spontaneous voiding, and tolerance of oral intake, without evidence of new neurological deficits. The surgical wound remained clean, and postoperative imaging confirmed correct hardware placement and adequate decompression of the cranio-medullary junction. The patient was discharged 48 hours later with a favorable prognosis for neurological function and quality of life. Conclusion: Basilar invagination associated with platybasia and atlas assimilation is a complex craniovertebral anomaly requiring individualized surgical planning based on precise clinical, radiological, and anatomical criteria. The combination of endoscopic transnasal odontoidectomy with posterior occipito-axial cranio-cervical fixation is a safe and effective strategy to achieve ventral brainstem decompression and biomechanical stabilization of the C0-C2 complex. This case demonstrates that, even in patients with congenital osseous malformations and significant brainstem compression, functional neurological recovery and stable structural outcomes can be obtained through a well-planned minimally invasive combined approach. Proper technical execution and careful clinical and imaging follow-up are essential to ensure long-term durability and minimize complications. The described surgical approach should be considered a first-line option in selected cases, particularly when anatomical conditions are favorable and the necessary infrastructure for endoscopic, image-guided surgery is available.

## ID: AGS26/1771

## P0095: Platysma muscle splitting incision enhances early cosmetic outcomes following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion: a prospective randomized trial

Tony Tannoury

1

, Rehan Khan

1

, Jeongeun Kim

1

, Nader El-Hajj

2

, Monica Abou-Ezzi

1

, Matthew Kim

1

, Neil Shah

1

, Chadi Tannoury

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Boston University School of Medicine, Boston, United States

2

University of Colorado Anschutz School of Medicine, Aurora, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Boston University School of Medicine, Boston, Aurora, United States

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) typically employs a transverse skin incision to optimize cosmesis by aligning with natural skin creases. However, whether the underlying platysma is split vertically (in line with its fibers orientation) or cut transversely (in line with the skin incision) influences incisional cosmetic healing is not known. Material and Methods: This is a prospective, randomized, single-center trial, and aims for evaluating if platysma incision orientation influences scar cosmesis. Among 127 enrolled patients undergoing elective ACDF, 103 subjects who had standardized follow-up data were included. All patients received a standardized transverse skin incision. Platysma incision was randomized to either vertical (muscle splitting, in line with muscle fibers orientation) or transverse (muscle cutting, in line with skin incision) orientation using block randomization stratified by surgeons. Demographic, comorbidity, and operative variables were collected. Standardized clinical photographs were obtained at 2 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months and each was graded independently by two blinded reviewers using the validated, modified Hollander Wound Evaluation Scale (0 - 6, higher = better). The mean score at each time point was analyzed. Potential predictors were first assessed individually with univariate analysis, followed by a multivariate logistic regression to identify predictors of achieving a perfect Hollander grade (6/6) at each timepoint. Inter-rater reliability between graders was measured with intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC). Results: Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. At 2 weeks, vertical platysma splitting was a predictor of better cosmetic healing (OR 120.3, 95% CI 15.0-964.6; p < 0.001). The association between vertical platysma splitting as a predictor of cosmetic healing persisted at 3 months (OR 73.8, 95% CI 8.1-668.4; p < 0.001). Hyperlipidemia reduced the odds of healing at 3 months only (OR 0.192, 95% CI 0.045-0.828; p = 0.027). At 6 and 12 months follow up, there were no significant predictors of achieving a Hollander grade of 6/6. By 6 months, 86% of all patients achieved a Hollander grade of 6/6 and 87.2 % by 12 months. At 12 months, 100% of the vertical group and 75% of the transverse group had perfect scores. Inter-rater reliability was excellent across all timepoints (ICC 0.973 at 2 weeks, 0.956 at 3 months, 0.976 at 6 months), with perfect agreement at 12 months (ICC = 1.000). Conclusion: Following a transverse skin incision for ACDF access, a vertical platysma splitting rather than a transverse muscle cutting incision, yields superior cosmetic surgical wound healing, especially in the first three months and sustained cosmetic advantage at 12 months follow-up. Overlapping transverse skin and transverse platysma incisions may potentiate surgical site inflammation and scarring in the early stages of wound healing. These findings help with patient counseling and can be employed to improve patient satisfaction with wound cosmesis.

## ID: AGS26/2249

## P0096: Retrospective study on conventional and extended transoral approach for the treatment of craniocervical junction pathologies. surgeries performed between 2000 and 2018 at a specialized spine center in Brazil

Luis Eduardo Carelli Texeira da Silva

1

, Fernando Ribeiro

2

, Alderico Girão Campos de Barros

1

, Jackson Daniel

1

, Renato Tavares

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedics Jamil Haddad (INTO), Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

2

Federal University of Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

Introduction: Diseases affecting the craniovertebral junction present a challenge to surgeons planning to approach them. The intricate cervical anatomy, with so many vital and delicate structures, makes access to the upper cervical spine and skull base difficult. In recent decades, with the development of revolutionary posterior techniques such as C1-C2 facet distraction and arthrodesis, conditions that previously required an anterior approach as an absolute indication could be treated from the back. However, transoral approaches still remain highly useful in certain situations and hold their due importance both in the present and the future of cervical spine surgery. Therefore, our objective is to review surgical cases of patients with high cervical spine diseases treated via transoral (TO), extended access (glossotomy-labio-mandibular - GLM), and percutaneous approaches, comparing with relevant literature. Material and Methods: Review of surgical cases approached via the transoral route between 2000 and 2018 at the same specialized Brazilian spine surgery center. Patients were followed clinically and radiologically for a minimum period of one year. The evaluated parameters included: patient age at the time of surgery, underlying pathology, type of surgical procedure performed (classic, extended, or percutaneous transoral approach), whether or not a velotomy was performed, type of pharyngeal access, preoperative Frankel classification, year of surgery, length of follow-up, whether posterior fusion was performed as a complementary procedure, and the complications encountered. Complications were classified as either major or minor. Results: Among 23 patients with transoral approaches, two were reoperated, totaling 26 procedures. Fourteen were TO, four GLM, and six percutaneous. Approach was suitable for all. Extended exposure required GLMM. For proximal approaches including skull base, velotomy was effective. Complications included four velopharyngeal insufficiencies (mainly nasal voice), two incidental duratomies repaired intraoperatively, and one polymethylmethacrylate extravasation needing transoral removal. One GLM patient had superficial wound infection, delayed pharyngeal dehiscence, and pseudoarthrosis. Conclusion: Transoral approaches efficiently treated each disease, providing good exposure, acceptable neurovascular safety, and favorable outcomes with few, mostly manageable, complications.

## ID: AGS26/2348

## P0097: The impact of workers’ compensation claims on outcomes of cervical spine surgery: a meta-analysis

Ralph Maroun

1

, Mohammad Daher

2

, Ethan Cottrill

2

, Richard Hurley

3

, William Lavalle

4

, Peter Passias

2

, Amer Sebaaly

5

, Alan Daniels

6

1

Lebanese University, Beirut, Lebanon

2

Duke University, Durham, United States

3

San Antonio Military Medical Center, San Antonio, United States

4

SUNY Upstate, New York, United States

5

Hotel Dieu de France, Beirut, Lebanon

6

Brown University, Providence, United States

Introduction: Work-related musculoskeletal disorders can lead to significant disability, impaired productivity, and quality of life. WC status in patients has been linked to worse outcomes following orthopedic surgeries, including cervical spine procedures. Studies analyzing the influence of WC claims on different cervical spine surgeries report contradicting results. This meta-analysis aims to clarify the impact of Workers’ compensation (WC) status on cervical spine surgery outcomes. Material and Methods: Following the PRISMA guidelines, PubMed, Cochrane, and Google Scholar (pages 1-20) were accessed and explored until May 2025. The extracted data consisted of overall complications, number of reoperations, surgery-related outcomes (estimated blood loss (EBL), operative time, and length of stay (LOS)), the improvement in patient reported outcome measures (PROMS) at 1 year post-operatively, number of patients returning to work, and mean time to return to work. Results: Eight retrospective studies met the inclusion criteria, including 801 patients in the WC group and 1102 in the NWC group. The patients in the WC group had a shorter operative time (Mean difference = -2.83 minutes; 95% CI: -5.03 - -0.64, p = 0.01), and LOS (Mean difference = -2.62 hours; 95% CI: -4.61- -0.63, p = 0.01). On the other hand, they had a worse improvement in neck pain (Standardized mean difference = -0.32; 95% CI: -0.45 - -0.19, p < .001), and arm pain (Standardized mean difference = -0.26; 95% CI: -0.39 - -0.13, p < .001), as well as a longer time to return to work (Mean difference = 4.53 weeks; 95% CI: 2.70 - 6.36, p < .001). Conclusion: Patients with WC claims had worse improvement in arm and neck pain and needed more time to return to work. These results highlight the impact of WC claims have on cervical spine surgery outcomes, making compensation status an essential factor to consider in clinical evaluation and patient counseling.

## ID: AGS26/2363

## P0098: Decision making and use of outcome analysis of staged surgery in pathologies of cervical spine

Sudhir Srivastava

1

1

K.J. Somaiya Medical College and Research Centre, Somaiya Ayurvihar, Sion (E), Mumbai, India

Introduction: Due to anatomical peculiarities and specific nature of pathology, many a times cervical spine requires staged surgeries. Sometimes it poses a challenge for treating surgeon in decision making. We present long term outcome in patients of cervical spine affected by different pathologies requiring staged procedure (Anterior, Posterior or combined) under same anesthesia or after interval of few days to few weeks. The algorithm used in decision making proved extremely beneficial in terms of neural safety, neurological recovery and biomechanically aligned sound column. Material and Methods: In a tertiary referral institute we treated 84 patients suffering from different pathologies of cervical spine where staged surgery was done. Age ranged from 3yrs to 70 years. There were 51 males and 33 females .74 patients had neurological deficit. We had a minimal follow up of 5 years. Outcome analysis was done in terms of neural recovery, bio-mechanical column alignment and complications. Pathologies were grouped according to its etiologies. These were as follows - Congenital (20), Post operative kyphosis (10) Degenerative cervical myelopathy (35), Infection - 5, Trauma- 14. We grouped these patients in 4 different categories according to procedure and stages used. A. Only anterior soft tissue release and then turning the patient prone to perform fixation, column alignment and fusion. B. Anterior bony excision (anomalous bony mass / vertebral body) then turn the patient prone under same anesthesia or after a gap of few days to weeks. The interval being utilized for post-operative traction for deformity correction or observing the neural recovery and further optimizing the general condition of patients. Anterior bony excision / Posterior (± decompression) fixation and column alignment / Revisit anterior and do sound reconstruction {so Anterior - Posterior - Anterior} C. First stage Posterior decompression / alignment and fixation then turn the patient (either same anesthesia or after a gap of few days to weeks time then do anterior decompression and reconstruction.
All these patients were followed up for 5 years and were assessed for neural recovery, correction of deformity, maintenance of achieved column alignment and any complications. Results: Except traumatic etiology patients, all other patients of different etiologies improved neurologically. Three patients deteriorated neurologically in follow up due to implant failure and reappearance of deformity. Fixation was revised and re-fusion was done. These 3 patients regained their motor power. Two patients had CSF leak and were managed conservatively. In the beginning 3 patients had superficial occipital sore and were managed conservatively. Later on we started keeping the head on soft foam during lying down / sleeping. 4 patients had superficial surgical site infection which could be managed with dressing. Conclusion: Understanding the pathology and changed biomechanics of cervical spine in different etiologies is extremely important. Even with unique neurovascular orientation and adjacent vital structures, cervical pathologies can be tackled meticulously and successfully by following an algorithm of staged procedure. It helps in decision making in terms of neural safety, neural recovery and providing normally aligned sound cervical column.

## ID: AGS26/2594

## P0099: Efficacy of minimally invasive surgery for adult congenital muscular torticollis: a real-world retrospective study

Haolin Sun

1

, Lei Yue

1

1

Peking University First Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: Congenital muscular torticollis (CMT) is a common musculoskeletal anomaly caused by unilateral sternocleidomastoid (SCM) contracture. Minimally invasive surgery (MIS) has gained popularity for its smaller incisions and lower complication rates. This study was performed to evaluate the efficacy and radiographic outcomes of MIS in adults with CMT and to introduce a novel SCM tension-based classification (Type S: sternal head-dominant; Type C: clavicular head-dominant) for optimizing surgical management. Material and Methods: Design This was a retrospective real-world study conducted at a tertiary hospital in China. Patient Sample The study included 90 adults with CMT who underwent MIS between March 2022 and April 2025, with a follow-up of 3 months. Outcome Measures Radiographic outcomes included parameters of the head, neck, and overall postural balance. Clinical outcomes included cosmetic appearance, functional scores, operative details, and complications. Results: The mean age of the cohort was 29.33 ± 8.70 years. At baseline, Type S patients had greater head tilt (7.44° ± 4.20° vs. 5.66° ± 2.83°, p = 0.022) and torticollis angle (9.21° ± 4.48° vs. 7.17° ± 4.07°, p = 0.027) than Type C patients. Postoperatively, all patients showed significant improvements in head shift, head tilt, torticollis angle, Cobb angle, thoracic kyphosis, cervical sagittal vertical axis, and coronal balance deviation (all p < 0.05). Type S patients demonstrated a significant increase in cervical lordosis (14.82° ± 10.93° to 19.53° ± 9.54°, p = 0.006), while Type C patients showed marked improvement in shoulder tilt (3.25° ± 2.07° to 1.89° ± 1.55°, p < 0.001). The overall prevalence of scoliosis decreased from 34.44% at baseline to 20.00% at 3 months. EQ-VAS scores improved from 83.15 ± 8.96 to 95.10 ± 5.98 (p < 0.001). The most common complications were effusion (10%) and hematoma (3.33%), and none required reoperation. Conclusion: MIS is effective for treating CMT in adults, leading to improvements in head and neck alignment as well as overall spinal balance. The proposed SCM tension-based classification offers a practical framework for surgical planning and may enhance the precision and outcomes of treatment.

 

## ID: AGS26/2596

## P0100: A novel bone screw implantation strategy in isolated C1-2 instability: a framework for evolving high cervical spine surgery

Chih-Ju Chang

1

, Jing-Jing Fang

2

1

Cathay General Hospital, Department of Medicine, School of Medicine, Fu Jen Catholic University, Taipei, Taiwan

2

Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan

Introduction: We evaluated the safety and accuracy of C1-2 pedicle screw insertion using our-developed planning software and 3D-printed guiding templates, comparing outcomes with freehand techniques to identify an optimal strategy for atlantoaxial fixation. Material and Methods: A 3D planning method associated with guiding template techniques was present to pedicle screws in high cervical vertebra fixation. Thirteen patients (Group 1) received freehand screw placement, while 17 (Group 2) were treated using the planning and guiding system. We analyzed demographics, complications, and screw placement precision. Results: Demographics, operative time, and blood loss were compared. Complications occurred in Group 1 patients; none were reported in Group 2. Most Group 2 patients underwent the C1PD + C2PD approach, which showed significantly higher screw placement safety. Conclusion: C1PD + C2PD is preferable to occipitocervical fusion when feasible. The planning strategy and its associated guiding system improves the implanted screws precision and safety of pedicle screw placement, particularly in cases with high-riding vertebral arteries. When the C2 pedicle diameter exceeds 3 mm, C2PD can be safely performed. This method supports more precise preoperative planning for atlantoaxial instability.

## ID: AGS26/2655

## P0101: Tomographic evaluation of the upper cervical spine in patients with cleft lip and palate and Class III malocclusion

Ricardo Correa da Costa Dias, Maria Noel Marzano Rodrigues, Ivy Kiemle Trindade-Suedam, Sergio Henrique Kiemle Trindade

Objective: To perform a morphometric analysis of the upper cervical spine (UCS) by means of cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) for the diagnosis of malformations or craniocervical instabilities in patients with cleft lip and palate (CLP) and class III malocclusion. Materials and Methods: A total of 72 CBCTs from adult patients (48 male and 24 female) with Angle Class III malocclusion were divided into three groups: 1) Unilateral cleft lip and palate (UCLP) (n = 29; male = 65.5%; age = 24, 2 ± 4.2 years); 2) Bilateral cleft lip and palate (BCLP) (n = 18; male = 83.3%; age = 26.4 ± 6.0 years); 3) Control group (CON) (n = 25; male = 56.0%; age = 27.8 ± 9.3 years). The version 11.7 of the Dolphin® software (Chatsworth, California, USA) was used to evaluate the morphometric measurements and anomalies of the UCS. Data were analyzed by descriptive and inferential statistics (p ≤ 0.05). Results: For the UCLP, BCLP and CON groups, respectively, the measures were: atlantodental interval (2.1 ± 0.5; 2.1 ± 0.4; 2.0 ± 0.3 mm), basion-opisthion (35.9 ± 3.2; 36.4 ± 3.0; 34.7 ± 1.9 mm), hyoid-C3 (34.5 ± 3.7; 34.5 ± 5.2; 35.3 ± 4.5 mm), and hyoid-sella (108.1 ± 9.8; 111.3 ± 9.2; 109.7 ± 10 mm); clivus-canal angle (152.3 ± 13; 150.3 ± 10; 150.7 ± 10°) and Torg-Pavlov index (1.0 ± 0.2; 1.0 ± 0.1; 1.1 ± 0.2). Potentially unstable anomalies and malformations were more prevalent in the UCLP group (34.4%). Conclusion: Subjects with UCLP presented compressive or unstable anomalies on upper cervical spine, more frequently than con- trols and BCLP, despite the lack of statistically significant differences among groups. Future studies could increase the safety of patients and healthcare professionals specialized in craniofacial anomalies.
Keywords: cleft lip and palate, computerized tomography, craniofacial morphology, imaging, orthopedic treatment, skeletal morphology

## ID: AGS26/2875

## P0102: Instability at the craniovertebral junction in children

Anastas Kanev

1

, Vladimir Nakov

1

, Christo Tzekov

1

, Asen Cekov

1

, Toma Spiriev

1

, Milko Milev

1

, Ivan Tarev

1

1

Acibadem City Clinic Tokuda University Hospital, Sofia, Bulgaria

Introduction: The craniovertebral junction (CVJ), also known as the occipitoatlantoaxial complex, is a stable, interconnected unit that forms the transition between the skull and the spine. The presence of multiple synovial joints and the complex mobility of this region make it susceptible to trauma and various pathologies. Instability at the CVJ is a significant clinical concern, particularly in pediatric patients, due to the elevated risk of neurological complications, even after minimal trauma. Materials and Methods: Between 2024 and 2025, we treated four pediatric patients (aged 5-13 years) diagnosed with craniovertebral junction instability. Two of the patients had Down syndrome. The etiology of instability was as follows: trauma in one patient (who also had Down syndrome), infection (Grisel syndrome) in two patients, and congenital abnormalities in one patient due to Down syndrome. Surgical interventions were tailored to the type of instability, using either anterior or posterior instrumentation. Results: All patients showed significant improvement in neurological function, overall status, and quality of life following treatment. Of the two patients with Grisel syndrome, one required surgical intervention due to persistent instability despite initial conservative management. The surgical procedures of choice in all three cases was C1-C2 fusion or occipital cervical fusion. The patients with Down syndrome required a staged surgical approach. Conclusion: The cases presented illustrate a variety of etiological factors and therapeutic strategies, highlighting the importance of early diagnosis, meticulous preoperative preparation, and a multidisciplinary approach in managing CVJ instability. Close postoperative monitoring and vigilance following conservative treatment are essential due to the potential for disease progression, which may necessitate further intervention.
