## P0103: Bilateral Sprengel’s shoulder in an 11yr old girl

Kabir Abubakar

1

, Afeez Oderinde

2

1

National Orthopaedic Hospital, Ansar Orthospinal Hospital, Kano, Nigeria

2

National Orthopaedic Hospital, Kano, Nigeria

Introduction: Symptomatic Sprengel’s shoulder patients rarely present for treatment in our society. Many just accept the deformity and carry on in spite of any limitations in upper limb movement. Those that seek treatment usually present late when they become symptomatic. Bilateral cases are even more rare with a typical bilateral shoulder elevation and difficulty abducting both upper limbs. Material and Methods: We present an 11yr old girl who presented with difficulty moving her neck sideways and inability to raise both upper limbs above her head of 9years duration. Her parents noticed these at 2years of age. Examination revealed a reduced range of motion (ROM) of the neck, shoulder and hypoplastic scapula. CT-Scan revealed a bilateral omovertebral body connecting superomedial scapula to the cervical spine with hypoplastic scapula. She had modified Woodward procedure and soft tissue release with a satisfactory outcome. Results: She had an excellent outcome with full ROM over the affected parts. She has commenced physiotherapy and both the patient and her parents are very happy with the outcome. Conclusion: Early and appropriate surgical intervention for Sprengel’s deformity gives a satisfactory outcome with prognosis higher in early patient presentation and intervention.

## ID: AGS26/3332

## P0104: Cervical neurofibroma with progressive neurological deficit: case report and surgical management

Mohamed Amine Dhifallah

1

, Habboubi Khalil

1

, oussama zaibet

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Hachicha Hassan

1

, ben brahim safouen

1

, Mondher Mestiri

1

1

Institut Med Taieb Kassab, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal neurofibromas are rare benign tumors arising from peripheral nerve sheaths, most frequently in the cervical region. Despite their benign nature, they may cause significant neurological deficits through local mass effect. We report a case of cervical neurofibroma managed surgically and discuss the therapeutic strategy. Material and Methods: A 61-year-old woman, with a history of hypertension and diabetes, presented with chronic cervical pain, dysphagia, and left upper limb paresthesias. Clinical examination revealed cervical tenderness without initial neurological deficit. Imaging (CT and MRI) demonstrated a cervical mass extending from C3 to C5, suggestive of a nerve sheath tumor. An initial posterior decompression with arthrodesis C3-C7 was performed, followed by adjuvant radiotherapy (40 Gy in 20 fractions). Two months later, the patient presented with progressive spastic tetraparesis. Results: MRI confirmed residual tumor compression. A second-stage anterior approach with C3-C5 corporectomy, subtotal tumor resection (R2), and anterior arthrodesis with fibular graft was performed. Postoperative course was uneventful, with stable neurological status. Histopathological analysis confirmed a neurofibroma. Conclusion: This case highlights the therapeutic challenges of cervical neurofibromas, especially when neurological deterioration occurs after initial management. Multidisciplinary treatment combining staged surgery and radiotherapy may provide tumor control and prevent further deterioration. Early diagnosis and complete resection remain crucial for optimal neurological outcome.

## Deformity-thoracolumbar (Adolescent)

## ID: AGS26/61

## P0105: Thoracoplasty does not improve patient-reported outcome measures in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis patients undergoing posterior spinal fusion: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Paolo Brigato

1

, Jean Ouellet

2

, Sergio De Salvatore

3

, Leonardo Oggiano

3

, Davide Palombi

4

, Federico Cardahi

2

, Neil Saran

2

, Pier Francesco Costici

3

, Gianluca Vadalà

1

, Rocco Papalia

1

, Vincenzo Denaro

1

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

McGill University, Montreal, Canada

3

Bambino Gesù Children's Hospital, Palidoro, Italy

4

Fondazione Policlinico Universitario Agostino Gemelli IRCCS, Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore, Rome, Italy

Purpose: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is commonly associated with rib hump (RH) deformity, which poses a significant cosmetic concern for patients. Traditionally, thoracoplasty (TH) has been the preferred treatment to address this issue. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluates the impact of combining TH with posterior spinal fusion (PSF) compared to PSF alone in AIS surgery. It focuses on patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), particularly self-image, to assess the true cosmetic benefit of incorporating TH into PSF procedures. Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic review was conducted from inception to March 2025 using Medline, Scopus, Embase, and Cochrane Library. Search terms included “thoracoplasty”, “scoliosis”, “rib hump”, “Scoliosis Research Society Score” and “self-image scores”. Outcomes assessed included surgical and radiological metrics, functional outcomes and complications. A meta-analysis was conducted to compare PROMs between patients treated with TH+PSF and those treated with PSF alone. The risk of bias was assessed using the MINORS score. Results: The 5 included studies had a level of evidence of either 2 or 3, with a total of 599 patients analyzed. Patients who underwent TH+PSF (n = 270) did not exhibit statistically significant differences in functional outcomes, including SRS questionnaire scores for pain, function, mental health, satisfaction, self-image, and overall score, compared to those who underwent PSF alone (n = 329) (p > 0.05). Additionally, the TH+PSF group experienced an higher rate of postoperative complications than the PSF alone group. Conclusion: The current meta-analysis indicates that TH did not result in significant improvements across any PROMs domains when added to PSF in AIS patients. Further well-conducted randomized controlled trials are needed to refine clinical decision-making by identifying the degree of deformity that may benefit from the addition of TH.

## ID: AGS26/96

## P0105.1: Evaluation of a Novel Osteoinductive Synthetic Bone Substitute in Spinal Deformity Surgery: A Retrospective Case Review

Andrew Bowey

1

, Ahmed Fadulemola

1

, Luuk van Dijk

2

, John Andrews

1

, Katherine Sage

3

1

Newcastle Hospitals, Newcastle, United Kingdom

2

Kuros Biosciences, Bilthoven, Netherlands

3

Kuros Biosciences, Atlanta, United States

Introduction: Synthetic calcium phosphates with submicron surface features have been demonstrated to have osteoinductive capacity. This retrospective case review was performed to evaluate the performance of a novel synthetic bone graft, a biphasic calcium phosphate with submicron surface topography, in patients undergoing spinal deformity surgery with fusion within the period from November 1, 2021, to May 24, 2023 at the Orthopedics department of the Royal Victoria Infirmary, Newcastle. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted of a cohort comprising 44 spinal deformity patients who received corrective surgery with the synthetic bone graft, of which 31 patients have reached 6 months follow-up, and 21 patients have reached 12 months follow-up. For all patients, routine Cobb angle measurement was performed pre- and post-operative, and fusion was assessed using CT at 6 months and 12 months follow-up. Results: The cohort exhibited various cases of kyphosis (n=9) and scoliosis (n=36), including but not limited to adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (n=23). Mean patient age was 31 ± 20.69 years, with a median of 18 years, and a of range 5-73 years. Mean Cobb angle pre-surgery was 60.00° ± 21.03 and 17.22° ± 10.49 after surgery, corresponding to a mean correction of 39.36° ± 18.79 and a correction rate of 71.94% ± 14.26. The fusion rate as determined by CT at 6 months post-surgery was 96.77% (30/31), and 100% (21/21) at 12 months follow-up. One pseudoarthrosis with a broken rod was identified at 6 months follow-up in a 52-year old patient after a revision surgery of a complex deformity correction. Conclusion: This retrospective case review demonstrates the favorable performance of a novel synthetic calcium phosphate in spinal deformity surgery among a diverse group of patients with spinal pathology. The data highlights the high fusion rates and correction rates achieved, substantiating the suitability of this bone substitute for spinal deformity correction.

## ID: AGS26/114

## P0106: Selective fusion in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis lenke type 1B and 1C: a study of 102 cases

Jorge Saisi Brugues

1

, Ricardo Saisi

2

1

PSSJ, Sant Boi, Spain

2

Practica Privada, Rosario, Argentina

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is a complex three-dimensional deformity of the spine. The primary objectives of surgical treatment are to stop the progression of the spinal curve, achieve and maintain deformity correction, preserve both coronal and sagittal alignment, minimize the incidence of perioperative and long-term complications, and, whenever possible, retain spinal mobility. Selective thoracic fusion, a surgical strategy in which only the vertebrae strictly necessary for deformity correction are fused, has become increasingly accepted, particularly in patients with Lenke type 1B and 1C curves. By limiting the distal extension of the fusion, this technique aims to maintain lumbar motion segments, thereby improving long-term function and quality of life. This study builds upon an earlier investigation of 25 cases and extends the sample to 102 patients treated between 2007 and 2017. The larger cohort and longer follow-up provide stronger evidence regarding the effectiveness and safety of selective thoracic fusion in AIS. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted including 102 consecutive patients with AIS Lenke 1B and 1C who underwent selective thoracic fusion at Hospital Provincial del Centenario between 2007 and 2017. The minimum follow-up was 5 years. Clinical and radiographic variables were collected: age at surgery, sex, Risser grade, lumbar modifier, Cobb angles (thoracic and lumbar), thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, apical vertebral translation (AVT), coronal and sagittal balance, and perioperative or long-term complications. Results: Clinical characteristics: The mean age at surgery was 14.6 years; the female:male ratio was 11.5:1, and the mean Risser grade was 3.7. Radiographic outcomes: - Thoracic curve: reduced from a mean preoperative Cobb angle of 62° to 30.1° at final follow-up, reflecting a 59.5% correction. - Lumbar curve: decreased from 42° preoperatively to 12.4° at follow-up, representing a 72.4% correction. - AVT: thoracic AVT improved from 57 mm to 24 mm; lumbar AVT from 45 mm to 20 mm. - Coronal balance: improved from 14 mm preoperatively to 10.3 mm at follow-up. - Sagittal balance: remained stable, with no significant alterations. Complications: The overall complication rate was 16%. Superficial infection occurred in 12%, deep infection in 4%, and transient radicular deficit in 4%. One patient presented mild lumbar decompensation, successfully managed with revision surgery. These results confirm that selective thoracic fusion provides substantial correction of both the structural thoracic curve and the compensatory lumbar curve while preserving lumbar mobility and sagittal alignment. Conclusion: Selective thoracic fusion in AIS Lenke 1B and 1C patients demonstrates long-term effectiveness and safety. In this cohort of 102 patients with a minimum follow-up of 5 years, the technique achieved: - Durable correction of both thoracic and lumbar curves, as well as AVT. - Preservation of lumbar motion segments, ensuring better flexibility and long-term function. - Improvement of coronal balance without negatively affecting sagittal alignment. - A complication profile comparable to that reported in international literature. Taken together, these findings support selective thoracic fusion as a reliable treatment option in carefully selected AIS patients, offering lasting structural correction, maintenance of spinal mobility, and improved quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/151

## P0107: Magnetically controlled growing rod in early onset scoliosis. A case series with early experience

Jin Tatt Gan

1

, Faizal Manan

1

, Dzulkarnain Amir

1

, Fazir Mohamad

1

1

General Hospital Kuala Lumpur, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

Introduction: The aim of this study is to report our early experience of magnetically controlled growing rod system (MAGEC) in early onset scoliosis. Material and Methods: A total of six patients with early onset scoliosis treated with MAGEC in a tertiary hospital in Malaysia. The medical records were reviewed to obtained pre-operative, post-operative and follow-up Cobb angles. The T1 to S1 length was measured on whole spine X-ray in standing, antero-posterior view. All patients were followed-up for at least one-year duration (12- 36 months). The serial lengthening was done in clinic every six month. Results: All six patients were female with three congenital, two neuromuscular and one idiopathic cause of early onset scoliosis. A total of three patients had dual rod constructs implanted and three patients received single MAGEC rod constructs. All six patients had MAGEC rods as primary procedure. The patients mean age at the surgery of growing rod insertion was 10.2 years (range: 6.9 - 11.9). The mean pre-operative Cobb angle was 82.2 degree (range: 50- 110), with immediate post-operative Cobb angle of 62.2 degree (range: 40-83.7). The mean correction immediate post- operation was 24.3 %. The mean Cobb angle during final follow-up was 60.2 degree (range: 42- 80.3). The mean pre-operative T1 to S1 length measured was 350.8mm (range: 230-355). The mean T1 to S1 length during final follow-up was 376.0mm (245-365). The mean percentage of lengthening achieved in this series was 7.2% (range: 2.8-10.2) One of the case experienced proximal thoracic screw pulled out underwent revision surgery 6 months post surgery. We do not experience loss of magnetic distraction in current series. Conclusion: MAGEC growing rod system is effective in control early onset scoliosis. The complications of this rod systems include rod breakage, screw pulled out and loss of magnetic distraction are not uncommon. However, MAGEC growing rod system stands a point in avoidance of multiple lengthening surgeries. This is especially important in the group of patients with multiple comorbidities.

## ID: AGS26/584

## P0108: An Obstetrical and Gynaecological Insight to Measure the Gender Specific Issues Including Menstrual Disorders in Patients with Scoliosis – A Genuine Step Towards Empowering Women’s Inclusive Health

Tejaswin Jha

1

, Bhavuk Garg

1

, Nishank Mehta

1

, Buddhadev Chowdhury

1

, J.B Sharma

1

, Smita Manchanda

1

1

All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, India

Introduction: Scoliosis, a three-dimensional spinal deformity, has systemic implications beyond musculoskeletal changes. Its potential impact on hormonal, menstrual, reproductive, and obstetrical health in females remains underexplored. The objective of this study is to evaluate and compare the menstrual, reproductive, and obstetrical health of females with different subtypes of scoliosis and to identify associated gynaecological abnormalities. Material and Methods: An ambispective observational study was conducted at a tertiary care centre between January 2021 and November 2022. A total of 292 female scoliosis patients (192 retrospective, 100 prospective) and 50 age-matched controls were enrolled. Participants were assessed through clinical evaluation, questionnaires, and ultrasonographic examination. Parameters studied included age at menarche, cycle regularity, bleeding characteristics, physical/psychological symptoms, and ultrasonographic findings (endometrial thickness, ovarian morphology). Obstetric outcomes were recorded where applicable. Statistical tests included Fisher’s exact test and Bartlett’s test for variance. Results: Mean age at menarche was delayed in scoliosis patients, especially in congenital scoliosis (12.5 ± 0.77 years) compared to controls (12.04 ± 0.75 years, p < 0.01). Menstrual irregularities, menorrhagia, and dysmenorrhea were significantly higher in congenital and syndromic scoliosis groups. Physical complaints were significantly more common in scoliosis groups than controls (p = 0.003), but psychological symptoms showed no statistical difference. Endometrial thickness and incidence of follicular cysts/bulky ovaries were significantly elevated in congenital and syndromic scoliosis groups. Of 15 patients who conceived, 13 had unplanned caesarean sections; no cases of spontaneous abortion or infertility were recorded. Conclusion: Scoliosis, particularly of congenital and syndromic origin, is associated with delayed menarche, menstrual irregularities, and gynaecological abnormalities. Although obstetrical data were limited, trends suggest potential complications that merit further investigation.

## ID: AGS26/330

## P0109: Bridging the gaps in scoliosis care: experiences of patients, families and providers during transition

Daisy Lu

1,2

, Jesse Shen

1,2

, Zhi Wang

1,2

1

Centre Hospitalier de l'Universite de Montreal, Montreal, Canada

2

University of Montreal, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: The transition from pediatric to adult healthcare poses significant challenges for patients with chronic conditions, including scoliosis. This period is often marked by gaps in continuity of care, differing treatment approaches, and varying levels of readiness among patients, families and providers. Despite the recognized importance of structured transition processes, little is known about the lived experiences and perspectives of those directly involved in scoliosis care. Understanding these viewpoints is critical to informing patient-centered strategies that improve long-term outcomes and satisfaction. Material and Methods: A prospective qualitative study was conducted, involving semi-structured interviews with scoliosis patients ages 18 to 25 who had recently transferred from pediatric to adult care, as well as their family members and healthcare providers. Interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed verbatim, and analyzed using thematic analysis to identify common experiences, challenges and facilitators in the transition process. Results: Preliminary findings indicate that patients and families often experience uncertainty regarding expectations in adult care, with information gaps emerging as a major barrier to smooth transition. Differences in care models between pediatric and adult systems were highlighted, particularly regarding family involvement and psychosocial support. Patients and families often report increased anxiety and worry during the transition period. Key facilitators included prompt adult follow-up and sufficient information about scoliosis management and long-term health expectations. All patients and families interviewed supported the implementation of a dedicated transition coordinator, noting that such a role would facilitate timely follow-up updates, promote continuity of care, reduce stress and provide a reliable point of contact to address emerging questions of concerns. Participants also consistently highlighted the importance of online resources that can give accurate information on disease condition, self-management and appropriate care. Conclusion: Transitioning from pediatric to adult scoliosis care is a complex process shaped by the perspectives of patients, families, and providers. Our findings underscore the need for structured, multidisciplinary transition programs that foster communication, autonomy, and sustained support. Incorporating these stakeholder insights may help bridge current gaps and optimize long-term outcomes for individuals living with scoliosis.

## ID: AGS26/357

## P0110: Patient-specific pre-bent rods in spinal deformity surgery. Clinical impact and predictive value

Klaus Schnake

1

, Zimo Lu

2

, Olga Cheremina

1

, Denis Rappert

1

, Alexander Hammer

1

1

Center for Spinal and Scoliosis Surgery, Malteser Waldkrankenhaus St. Marien, Erlangen, Germany

2

The Collaborative Innovation Center of Tissue Damage Repair and Regeneration Medicine, Zunyi Medical University, Zunyi, China

Introduction: Pre-bent, patient-specific rods (PSRs) have been developed to optimize surgical outcomes for sagittal deformity. This prospective study aimed to evaluate the accuracy of PSR alignment parameters achieved during surgery, analyze the learning curve, investigate the correlation between alignment correction and complication rates, and explore the predictive ability of preoperative parameters for surgical outcomes. Material and Methods: We enrolled 22 consecutive patients undergoing spinal deformity correction using the UNiD™ rod system. Standardized full-spine radiographs were obtained pre- and postoperatively and at the one-year follow-up. The following parameters were measured: pelvic incidence (PI), lumbar lordosis (LL), T1 pelvic angle (TPA), pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS) and sagittal vertical axis (SVA). Two novel metrics were developed to evaluate surgical accuracy: The Correction Achievement Ratio (CAR), where 100% represents perfect achievement, and the Correction Maintenance Index (CMI), where 0% represents perfect maintenance. A learning curve analysis was conducted using chronological case analysis, cumulative summation analysis and case complexity stratification. Logistic regression analysis was used to examine the association between postoperative complications and correction accuracy. Results: Twenty patients could be evaluated (15 females with an average age of 66.5 ± 6.3 years). Significant improvements in sagittal alignment parameters were achieved from preoperative to immediate postoperative measurements. Closest to the planned targets were PI-LL mismatch (CAR = 102.8%) and TPA (CAR = 91.9%). PT (CAR = 149.2%) and SS (CAR = 123.5%) tended to overcorrect. Most parameters exhibited low stability over time (CMI > 20%), except lumbar lordosis, which exhibited moderate stability (CMI = 12.5%). Learning curve analysis revealed a significant turning point around case 8. After this point, the variance of correction decreased significantly. Overcorrection of PT (CAR > 120%, OR = 3.41, p = 0.025) and PI-LL mismatch (CAR > 120%, OR = 4.25, p = 0.007) were significantly associated with an increased risk of complications, particularly proximal junctional kyphosis. Conclusion: Initial sagittal alignment parameters can significantly be improved by PSR technology, but maintenance of correction is difficult. Surgical accuracy improves significantly after the initial learning curve. Overcorrection significantly increases the risk of complications, so accurate preoperative planning is crucial. This technology shows potential in reducing mechanical complications.

## ID: AGS26/438

## P0111: Scoliosis in chest wall deformities: coincidence or a unique pattern?

Eren Akin

1

, Selahaddin Aydemir

2

, Omer Akcalı

1

1

Dokuz Eylul University, School of Medicine, İzmir, Turkey

2

Kastamonu State Hospital, Kastamonu, Turkey

Introduction: Pectus excavatum (PE) and pectus carinatum (PC) are the most common congenital chest wall deformities. Although these deformities primarily affect the anterior thoracic structure, their relationship with spinal deformities and especially with scoliosis is attracting more and more attention. The aim of this study was to determine the prevalence and predictors of scoliosis in patients with PE and PC. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on a total of 1,352 patients diagnosed with PE or PC in our centre. Demographic data (age, gender) of the patients were recorded. The presence and pattern of scoliosis were divided into four subgroups based on radiographic findings: proximal thoracic (PT), main thoracic (MT), thoracolumbar (TL) and lumbar (L). The direction of curvature was also evaluated as right or left. Results: Of a total of 1,352 patients, 803 (59.4%) were diagnosed as pectus excavatum (PE) and 549 (40.6%) as pectus carinatum (PC). The mean age was 15.69 ± 4.92 years in PE patients and 15.42 ± 7.21 years in PC patients. Male gender was predominant in both groups; 78.5% in the PE group and 78.9% in the PC group. The overall prevalence of scoliosis was 22.9% in the PE group and 8.4% in the PC group. When scoliosis subtypes were evaluated, proximal thoracic (PT) scoliosis was found in 16.2%, main thoracic (MT) in 7.6%, thoracolumbar (TL) in 5.1% and lumbar (L) in 0.5% in the PE group. In the PC group, PT scoliosis was observed in 6.4%, MT scoliosis in 3.3%, TL scoliosis in 2.2%, and L scoliosis was not detected. Conclusion: This study shows that the prevalence of scoliosis is significantly increased in patients with chest wall deformity, especially in individuals with PE. The findings suggest that the scoliosis pattern in chest wall deformities tends to increase especially in the proximal thoracic region.

## ID: AGS26/502

## P0112: Features of spinal deformities after treatment of open neural tube defects

Alexandr Burtcev

1

, Olga Sergeenko

1

, Dmitriy Savin

1

1

Ilizarov Center, Kurgan, Russian Federation

Introduction: Open neural tube defects (ONTD), including myelomeningocele and myeloschisis, are frequently associated with the development of severe spinal deformities. These deformities range from rigid angular kyphosis with trophic disorders to paralytic scoliosis with pelvic obliquity. The aim of this study was to analyze the specific features of spinal deformities developing after surgical closure of ONTD. Material and Methods: A total of 65 patients who underwent closure of ONTD followed by surgical correction of spinal deformity between 2010 and 2023 were included. Patients were divided into two groups: group K (n = 38) with kyphosis/kyphoscoliosis, and group S (n = 27) with paralytic scoliosis and pelvic obliquity. Radiographic parameters, neurological status, and functional presentation were analyzed. Results: Group K patients demonstrated rigid angular kyphosis located mainly at the thoracolumbar junction, often accompanied by skin trophic ulcers over the apex. Group S patients presented with long, progressive paralytic scoliosis, frequently associated with pelvic obliquity and hip dislocation. In both groups, progressive deformity significantly impaired sitting balance and ambulation. Conclusion: Spinal deformities after myelomeningocele repair present with distinct features depending on the predominant pathology. Kyphosis is usually rigid and ulcerated, while scoliosis is progressive and combined with pelvic imbalance. Recognition of these patterns is essential for planning surgical correction.

## ID: AGS26/613

## P0113: Intraoperative fluoroscopic assessment of coronal balance in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis using rod aligned along the plumb line

Hesham El Saghir

1,2

, Mabrouk Harbia

1

, Aya Abdelgelil

3

, Tarek Fiky

1

, Alaa Elbanna

1

1

Alexandria University, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Spine Surgery Unit, Alexandria, Egypt

2

Spine Surgery Department, Zentralklinik, Bad Berka, Germany

3

Department of Radiology, Alexandria Faculty of Medicine, Alexandria, Egypt

Introduction: Achieving and maintaining coronal balance (CB) is a crucial goal in correction of AIS. The target alignment should be within a range between -2 cm to +2 cm from the CSVL. Long-cassette AP radiograph is a known method for assessment of intraoperative CB but requires the availability of a radiolucent table and is associated with increased radiation exposure. This paper introduces a fluoroscopic method in intraoperative assessment of CB. Material and Methods: A prospective study was conducted on 50 patients with AIS who were corrected using transpedicular screw rod system in the period between March 2021 and March 2023 in our institute. After correction of the deformity a rod was applied externally on the skin at the cervicothoracic junction along the plumb line (PL) starting at the spinous processes of C7 and T1 under fluoroscopic control. AP view was done at the lower part of the lumbar spine. Deviation of the PL from the CVL was then measured in mm using the PACS system. Results: CB was achieved without the need for additional correction in 37 patients (74%). In the remaining 13 patients (26%) further correction was added to achieve an accepted CB. The deviation of the PL from CSVL at the end of the operation ranged from -20 mm to +21 mm (mean 13 mm, SD: 5.5 mm). The mean PL-CSVL deviation at the immediate postoperative period was 18 mm. Five patients (10%) developed coronal imbalance and three of them (6%) retained this imbalance after one year from the operation. Conclusions: The rod-aligned plumb line method is a simple but adequate measurement of intraoperative CB of the spine. It can be implemented in any operative room. The real-time feedback allows timely adjustments to the spinal instrumentation before closing the wound. The introduced method is inexpensive and is associated with low exposure to radiation compared to using long-cassette films. However, an achieved intraoperative CB is not always a guarantee against the development of coronal imbalance at the postoperative period.

## ID: AGS26/616

## P0114: Preoperative paraspinal muscle size and fat infiltration as predictors of postoperative recovery in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis patients: a pilot study

Paolo Brigato

1

, Sergio De Salvatore

1

, Davide Palombi

2

, Camilla Ravaioli

3

, Sergio Sessa

4

, Lorenzo Gregori

4

, GianMario Sangiovanni

5

, Andrea Magistrelli

4

, Leonardo Oggiano

4

, Pier Francesco Costici

4

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

Gemelli University Hospital, Rome, Italy

3

Gemelli University Hospital, Rome, Italy

4

Bambino Gesù Children's Hospital, Rome, Italy

5

Sapienza University, Rome, Italy

Introduction: The iliopsoas and other paraspinal muscles play a crucial role in spinal stability and postoperative mobility. In adults undergoing lumbar spinal surgery, preoperative cross-sectional area (CSA) of paraspinal muscles has been shown to influence recovery. To date, no studies have specifically evaluated these parameters in pediatric populations, including patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). This study aimed to evaluate preoperative CSA and muscle quality of the iliopsoas, erector spinae, and multifidus in AIS patients, and their relationship with key perioperative parameters, including blood loss, operative time, time to first ambulation, and length of hospital stay (LOS). Material and Methods: This retrospective study included 81 consecutively treated AIS patients undergoing posterior spinal fusion from September 2021 to August 2023 at a single institution. Preoperative MRI was used to measure paraspinal muscle CSA and Goutallier grade at the L3 level. Measurements were independently performed in a blinded fashion by two trained radiologists, and the mean values were used for analysis. Inter-observer reliability was assessed to ensure consistency between the observers. Postoperative outcomes recorded included intraoperative blood loss, surgical duration, time to first ambulation, and LOS. Correlation analyses and Kruskal-Wallis tests were performed to assess associations between muscle parameters and outcomes (p < 0.05). Results: Patients (mean age 14.8 ± 1.7 years) were divided by Lenke type: 1-2 (Group 1, N = 26), 5 (Group 2, N = 18, lumbar curves), and 3-4-6 (Group 3, N = 37). Mean CSA and Goutallier scores of paraspinal muscles were similar across groups (p > 0.05). No significant correlations were observed between CSA or muscle quality and intraoperative blood loss or surgical duration in any group. In Group 2 (lumbar curves), an higher fat infiltration of erector spinae muscles and total paraspinal muscles correlated with longer LOS (p = 0.042 and p = 0.026, respectively). In Group 3 (double or triple curves), lower total paraspinal CSA also correlated with longer LOS (p = 0.039). Group 1 (thoracic curves) showed no significant associations. Conclusion: Preoperative paraspinal muscle size and quality appear to have minimal influence on intraoperative parameters in AIS surgery. Nonetheless, increased fat infiltration and reduced CSA-particularly in the erector spinae and overall paraspinal muscles-were associated with longer LOS in patients with lumbar-dominant curves (Lenke 5) and related subtypes (Lenke 3-4-6). These findings highlight the potential value of preoperative muscle assessment in anticipating early postoperative recovery. Further prospective studies are needed to confirm these results and to investigate targeted prehabilitation strategies.

## P0115: Enhanced recovery after surgery for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis: a revised systematic review and meta-analysis

Paolo Brigato

1

, Sergio De Salvatore

1

, Leonardo Oggiano

2

, Davide Palombi

3

, GianMario Sangiovanni

4

, Gianmichele Di Cosimo

5

, Sergio Sessa

2

, Laura Ruzzini

2

, Pier Francesco Costici

2

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

Bambino Gesù Children's Hospital, Rome, Italy

3

Gemelli University Hospital, Rome, Italy

4

Sapienza University, Rome, Italy

5

ba, Rome, Italy

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis surgery (AIS) is often associated with high costs and significant recovery challenges. Enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) protocols aim to improve outcomes, reducing hospital stays and complications compared to traditional (TD) pathways. This study evaluates the impact of ERAS protocols on AIS treatment. Material and Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, a systematic review identified 30 studies (n = 15,954 patients). Search terms included “ERAS,” “adolescent idiopathic scoliosis,” and “posterior spinal fusion.” Outcomes assessed included surgical metrics, recovery milestones, complications, and pain. A meta-analysis was performed, and the risk of bias was evaluated using the MINORS score. Results: ERAS patients (n = 5582) had shorter surgical durations (- 23 min, p = 0.08) and reduced blood loss (- 126 mL, p = 0.033) compared to TD patients (n = 7916). Recovery milestones improved, including earlier ambulation (- 37.4 h, p < 0.0001), patient-controlled analgesia discontinuation (- 1.1 days, p < 0.0001), catheter removal (- 0.75 days, p < 0.001), and shorter hospital stays (- 1.7 days, p < 0.0001). Complications were lower in the ERAS group (4% vs. 8%, p = 0.0074), while wound-related complications and 30-day readmission rates were comparable. Pain scores were significantly reduced from surgery day to postoperative day 2 (p < 0.0181). Conclusion: ERAS protocols improve recovery and reduce complications in AIS surgery, with shorter hospital stays and enhanced patient outcomes. These findings support broader implementation and further randomized trials to evaluate long-term benefits and patient satisfaction.

## ID: AGS26/620

## P0116: All-pedicle screw posterior spinal fusion after magnetically controlled growing rods in walking early-onset scoliosis patients: radiographic and surgical outcomes

Sergio De Salvatore

1

, Paolo Brigato

2

, Leonardo Oggiano

1

, Davide Palombi

3

, GianMario Sangiovanni

4

, Edoardo Costici

5

, Sergio Sessa

1

, Pier Francesco Costici

1

1

Bambino Gesù Children's Hospital, Rome, Italy

2

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

3

Gemelli University Hospital, Rome, Italy

4

Sapienza University, Rome, Italy

5

Saint Camillus International University of Health and Medical Sciences, Rome, Italy

Introduction: Early-onset scoliosis (EOS) significantly affects lung development and life expectancy. Magnetically Controlled Growing Rods (MCGR) effectively promote thoracic growth while managing curvature progression. This study aims to assess the impact of definitive spinal fusion on residual deformity and complications in EOS patients treated with MCGRs. Material and Methods: The study included 27 EOS patients who underwent final fusion surgery between January 2017 and September 2022. The primary outcome was the evaluation of coronal and sagittal radiographic parameters postoperatively and at a minimum of 2 years of follow-up (FUP). Secondary outcomes included major complications (≥ IIIB, according to Clavien-Dindo Classification), surgery duration, blood loss and length of hospital stay (LOS). Results: Major curve (45.5° and 33.5°, p = 0.003, Brunner-Munzel Test Statistic = - 3.869254) and Thoracic Kyphosis (TK) (19.8° and 13.5°, p = 0.002, Brunner-Munzel Test Statistic = - 2.258845) were statistically impacted by arthrodesis, and maintained at the final follow-up. The surgeries had an average duration of 254.2 ± 39.5 min (range: 195-336 min) and an average blood loss of 574.1 ± 255.1 mL (range: 200-1300 mL). The mean LOS was 8.5 ± 1.8 days (range: 6-13 days). Following the final fusion, 5 patients (18.5%) developed a complication, of which one was classified as major. Conclusion: This study demonstrated substantial improvements in coronal and sagittal alignment following graduation surgery, with results maintained at the two-year FUP. Further research with larger sample sizes is needed to provide a more comprehensive assessment of radiographic and surgical outcomes and to establish guidelines for implant density in MCGR graduation surgery.

## ID: AGS26/621

## P0117: Final fusion strategies in early-onset scoliosis: does implant density make a difference after magnetically controlled growing rod treatment?

Paolo Brigato

1

, Leonardo Oggiano

2

, Sergio De Salvatore

1

, Davide Palombi

3

, Sergio Sessa

2

, Umile Giuseppe Longo

1

, Pier Francesco Costici

2

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

Bambino Gesù Children's Hospital, Rome, Italy

3

Gemelli University Hospital, Rome, Italy

Background / Objectives: Early-onset scoliosis (EOS) frequently requires growth-friendly interventions, such as magnetically controlled growing rods (MCGRs), followed by definitive spinal fusion upon skeletal maturity. The optimal implant density (ID) for final posterior spinal fusion in these patients remains controversial. This study aimed to compare the radiographic, surgical, and economic outcomes associated with high-density (HD) versus low-density (LD) screw constructs in EOS patients previously treated with MCGRs undergoing definitive fusion. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included 27 EOS patients who underwent definitive posterior spinal fusion between January 2017 and September 2022. Participants were categorized into two groups: HD (n = 13) and LD (n = 14). Primary outcomes included coronal and sagittal radiographic parameters assessed at early postoperative and final follow-up visits (minimum of 2 years). The secondary outcomes analyzed were major postoperative complications (grade ≥ IIIB according to Clavien-Dindo-Sink Classification [CDSC]), operative time, blood loss, hospital stay length, and total implant costs. Results: Baseline characteristics between the HD and LD groups were comparable. Early postoperative radiographic assessment demonstrated significantly greater thoracic kyphosis (16.3 ± 7.6° vs. 10.9 ± 14.4°, p = 0.021) and T1-S1 spinal height (43.3 ± 6.7 mm vs. 39.1 ± 4.3 mm, p = 0.039) in the HD group. At final follow-up, only T1-S1 spinal height remained significantly higher in the HD group (45.4 ± 7 mm vs. 39.7 ± 5.1 mm, p = 0.021). Implant costs were significantly higher in the HD group (EUR 6046.5 ± 1146.9 vs. EUR 4376.4 ± 999.4, p < 0.001), while operative time, blood loss, and hospital stay length showed no significant differences. HD constructs had three major complications requiring surgical revision, whereas LD constructs reported no perioperative complications but experienced three late-onset complications also necessitating revision surgery. Conclusion: LD constructs provided comparable long-term radiographic and clinical outcomes to HD constructs, with significantly lower implant-related costs. Despite initial superior kyphosis correction in HD constructs, this benefit diminished by the final follow-up. These findings support a selective, lower-density screw placement strategy to minimize costs and surgical complexity without compromising patient outcomes in EOS undergoing definitive spinal fusion.

## P0118: Clinical applicability of AI-based photo analysis in scoliosis follow-up and comparison with radiographic measurements (artificial intelligence assisted scoliosis examination-prospective randomized controlled trial)

Ahmet Hamdi Olçar

1

, İsmail Tüter

1

, Yusuf Altuntaş

2

, Hubeyb Ünal

1

, Ömer Gençosmanoğlu

1

, Tuhan Kurtulmuş

1

, Özgür Karakoyun

3

1

Sancaktepe Prof. Dr. Ilhan Varank Training and Research Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Seyrantepe Hamidiye Etfal Training and Research Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Doç.Dr.Özgür Karakoyun Private Practice, Tekirdağ, Turkey

Introduction: The aim of this study was to evaluate the effectiveness and accuracy of a photo-based artificial intelligence (AI) measurement method that can reduce radiation exposure in the diagnosis and follow-up processes of scoliosis patients. Material and Methods: The prospectively designed study included 17 patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis and 15 healthy controls. Markers were placed at specific vertebral points on the back of the participants and clinical photographs were taken under standardized conditions. The YOLOv8 model was trained on the obtained photographs and spine curvature angles were calculated automatically. The results obtained with AI were compared with the gold standard radiographic Cobb angles. Results: High correlation (r = 0.93, p < 0.0001) was found between radiographic and AI-based measurements. The mean measurement difference was 3.71° (± 5.85°) and statistically significant (p = 0.019). Conclusion: Artificial intelligence-based photo analysis method provides radiation-free, reliable and reproducible results in scoliosis follow-up. This method has the potential to reduce the need for radiography, especially in routine follow-up. It can be integrated into clinical practice and improve the quality of orthopedic care with larger-scale studies in the future.

## P0119: Active apex correction in early onset scoliosis patients with neurofibromatosis

Abdullah Shah

1

, Ahmad Hammad

2

, Alaa Azmi Ahmad

3

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahor, Pakistan

2

American University of Beirut, Beirut, Lebanon

3

Palestine Polytechnic University, Hebron, Palestine

Introduction: Active apex correction (APC) is a posterior tethering technique for correction of children with Early Onset Scoliosis (EOS). APC has been increasingly used for treatment of EOS in patients with neurofibromatosis. The aim of the study was to assess the outcomes of APC on EOS with neurofibromatosis patients in terms of curve correction, spine length, complications, and unplanned surgeries. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective multicenter study including 11 neurofibromatosis patients with EOS treated by APC which involves posterior tethering in adjunct with guided growth allowing modulation of the apex according to the Hueter-Volkmann law. Excluded patients with other etiologies, follow-up < 1 year and in whom APC was not the primary surgical intervention. Results: Mean age 8.8 ± 1.9 years, mean follow-up post-surgery 39.4 ± 22 months. At final follow up, there was a statistically significant improvement in major Cobb angle (Δ = 25.7%, p = 0.0009), minor Cobb angle (Δ = 21.7%, 0.0014), spinal length T1-L5 (Δ = 18.9%, p = 0.0012) but not apical vertebral translation (AVT) albeit clinical improvement (Δ = 6.8%, p = 0.15). 3 Complications (27%) including 1 proximal screws pull-out, 1 broken rod and 1 distal hook pull-out and 1 unplanned surgery (9%) for broken rod change. Conclusion: APC is a corrective modality for scoliotic deformities in neurofibromatosis patients with promising results in terms of clinical improvement, complication rates and need for unplanned reoperation.

## ID: AGS26/703

## P0120: Determining the optimal screw density in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis surgery: a breakpoint analysis reveals a clinical threshold at 1.94 screws/level

Sezgin Tekin

1

, Hüseyin Coşkun

2

1

Medicalpoint Hospital Gaziantep, Gaziantep, Turkey

2

Ondokuz Mayıs University Hospital Department of Orthopedics, Samsun, Turkey

Introduction: High screw density is commonly assumed to improve curve correction in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) surgery, but the optimal threshold remains unclear. Our aim in this study is to determine a clinically relevant cut-off for screw density (screws per fused level) and its relationship with curve correction and complications. Material and Methods: This retrospective single-center study included 49 AIS patients with complete screw count and fusion length data. Screw density was calculated as total screws / fused vertebral levels. Primary outcome was major curve correction percentage: (Preop Cobb - Postop Cobb) / Preop Cobb × 100. A segmented (piecewise) regression model identified the breakpoint. Groups were compared below and above the cut-off using Welch t-test and Fisher’s exact test. Results: The optimal threshold was identified as 1.94 screws/level. Patients below the threshold had higher mean correction (73.6% ± 12.7) compared to those above (67.8% ± 12.6, p = 0.022), despite having higher preoperative Cobb angles (60.9° vs 51.9°) and longer fusions (12.84 vs 10.71 levels). Complication rates were lower above the cut-off (7.7% vs 12.0%, p = 0.35). Conclusion: Screw density above ∼2.0 screws/level does not yield additional curve correction and may represent a point of diminishing returns. This finding supports a more cost-effective approach without compromising outcomes. Prospective multicenter validation is warranted.

## ID: AGS26/718

## P0121: Curved narratives: cultural representation of spinal deformity in children’s animated films and its psychosocial implications

Elie Najjar

1

, Reem Najjar

1

, Rodrigo Muscogliati

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Background: Spinal deformities, particularly adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), are associated with impaired self-image. While psychosocial consequences are well-documented in clinical literature, little is known about how such conditions are represented in children's media or how these portrayals may contribute to stigma and self-perception. Objective or Hypothesis: This study aimed to systematically evaluate how spinal deformities are portrayed in Disney and Pixar animated films and to assess whether recurring visual and narrative stereotypes reflect psychosocial challenges reported by adolescents with AIS. Methods: A systematic content analysis was conducted of all full-length Disney and Pixar films released from 1989 to 2025. Characters were included if they exhibited consistent anatomical features suggestive of kyphosis, scoliosis, or lordosis. Each character was assessed for physical, social, and moral traits using a standardized checklist. Clinical plausibility was confirmed by an FRCS-trained spine surgeon. Descriptive statistics were used to analyze prevalence and trait distribution. Results: Forty-eight characters met inclusion criteria, most showing kyphosis (79%). The majority were secondary (42%) or peripheral (35%) figures. Common portrayals included clumsiness (60%), frailty (42%), and frightening demeanor (33%). Only 27% were heroes and 19% were leaders. Female characters were underrepresented (27%). Conclusions: Spinal deformity is frequently depicted in children’s animation through lenses of physical and social inferiority. These portrayals may contribute to internalized stigma and identity challenges in adolescents with AIS. Clinicians should consider these cultural narratives when discussing appearance-related treatment options.

## ID: AGS26/719

## P0122: Beyond the curve: sexual and psychological health after adolescent idiopathic scoliosis treatment

Elie Najjar

1

, Rodrigo Muscogliati

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Background: While the primary goal of treating Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis (AIS) is curve correction, long-term psychological and sexual outcomes are rarely examined. This systematic review evaluates whether bracing or surgical treatment affects sexual health, body image, and psychological well-being compared to healthy controls. Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. PubMed, Embase, and CENTRAL were searched up to November 2024 for studies assessing sexual and psychological outcomes in AIS patients treated with bracing or surgery. Inclusion criteria encompassed observational and interventional studies with validated outcome tools. Risk of bias was assessed using the ROBINS-I tool. Results: Nine studies involving 3,231 participants were included. Among 1,085 AIS patients (average age 15.6 years; 18F:1M), 498 underwent bracing and 587 surgery. Compared to healthy controls, AIS patients reported higher rates of sexual distress, body image dissatisfaction, and reduced self-esteem. Surgically treated patients showed marginally better sexual satisfaction scores than braced patients, though differences in psychological or intimacy measures were inconsistent. Physical limitations and pain-related sexual dysfunction were noted across both treatment groups. Conclusion: AIS treatment may influence long-term sexual and psychological health, particularly among brace-treated patients. While surgical intervention may offer modest benefits, evidence remains limited. These outcomes should be incorporated into AIS follow-up and future research, emphasizing patient-centred care.

## ID: AGS26/726

## P0123: The Lenke paradox: substantial agreement or methodological mirage?

Elie Najjar

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Study Design: Systematic review and meta-analysis. Introduction: The Lenke system is widely used in AIS surgical planning. While initial reports showed excellent reproducibility, subsequent studies have yielded inconsistent results, particularly for lumbar and sagittal modifiers. We aimed to evaluate inter- and intra-observer reliability of the Lenke classification for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) and identify methodological factors influencing agreement. Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines, five databases (PubMed, Embase, Cochrane CENTRAL, Scopus, and Web of Science) were searched to August 2025. Data on observer characteristics, imaging protocols, measurement methods, and statistical indices (κ, ICC) were extracted. Study quality was assessed with QAREL. Descriptive synthesis and random-effects meta-analysis were performed. Results: Eleven studies (528 radiographs) met inclusion. Inter-observer reliability averaged κ = 0.76 (range 0.50-0.96); lumbar κ = 0.75 (0.53-0.92); sagittal κ = 0.79 (0.41-1.0). Pooled analysis of two studies with confidence intervals yielded κ = 0.69 (95% CI 0.56-0.82; I2 = 21%). Intra-observer reliability was higher (κ ≈ 0.82). Reliability improved with pre-measured radiographs, experienced observers, computer assistance, and short repeat intervals; it was lower with non-premeasured films, mixed cohorts, and longer intervals. Lumbar modifiers were relatively robust, whereas sagittal reproducibility was most vulnerable to methodological bias. One study illustrated the κ paradox, with high agreement but κ = 0.41 due to skewed distribution. Conclusions: The Lenke classification is reliable under controlled conditions but less consistent in everyday practice. Reported κ values should be interpreted within their methodological context. Standardised protocols, transparent reporting, and digital tools may improve reproducibility.
Keywords: adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, Lenke classification, reliability, reproducibility, systematic review, meta-analysis

## ID: AGS26/789

## P0124: Efficacy of intraoperative taping technique in the reduction of deformity in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis surgery and its impact on surgical outcomes

Panyajarn Laohapornsvan

1,2

, Pittavat Leelapattana

2

1

Nopparat Rajathanee Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Ramathibodi Hospital, Faculty of Medicine, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Surgery for idiopathic scoliosis, especially with severe abnormalities, is still very problematic. Pedicle screw fixation has been the traditional practice in surgery though this may pose some challenge under severe conditions. Intraoperative traction has proven to be an effective method in reducing the deformity and facilitate instrumentation. This study outlines a new less invasive skin taping method aimed at reducing the severity of scoliosis before insertion of pedicle screws. The objective of this study is to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of a novel skin taping technique used during patient positioning in scoliosis surgery. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study analyzed the outcomes for AIS patients who underwent derotation surgery from 2017 to 2023. Patients were grouped among those who received skin taping and those who did not. The study excluded patients with syndromic scoliosis, severe scoliosis curve exceeding 100 degrees, congenital scoliosis, revision surgery, or no available data of more than 1 year’s follow-up time. Results: The study analyzed 52 patients, with no significant difference in gender distribution and average age between the taping and non-taping groups. However, the major curve size for taping was higher than that of non-taping patients. After surgery, there was significant improvement for both major and secondary curvature among the two groups after surgery, with the taping group showing a higher percentage of correction. The taping group also had a higher incidence of skin redness, while the non-taping group had a slightly higher incidence of screw complications. Conclusion: The study concludes that the use of a skin taping method can contribute to a reduction in the major curve during patient positioning in scoliosis surgery. This technique may allow for safer insertion of pedicle screws and less manipulation during reduction maneuvers. Despite some limitations due to the retrospective nature of the study, the results suggest that the taping technique may enhance the correction of deformity. However, further studies are needed to fully demonstrate the effect of this technique in scoliotic curve reduction.

## ID: AGS26/826

## P0125: Surgical treatment in late diagnosed kyphoscoliotic Ehlers-Danlos syndrome - Case report

Vlad Pencea

1

, Bianca Tanase

1,2

, Ana Manu

1

, Doria Crismaruc

1

, Stefan Gavriliu

1,2

1“

Maria Sklodowska Curie” Children's Emergency Hospital, Bucharest, Romania

2“

Carol Davila” University of Medicine and Pharmacology, Bucharest, Romania

Introduction: Kyphoscoliotic Ehlers Danlos syndrome (kEDS) is a severe and rare genetic condition affecting collagen production causing hyperlaxity, tissue fragility and hypermobility. One of the orthopaedic issues is represented by kyphoscoliosis. The exact incidence is unknown with estimates of about 1:100.000 births. The literature mentions a series of severe perioperative complications which is why the surgical treatment of kyphoscoliosis should be cautious and individualized. The aim of this paper is to present the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges in a severe case of kEDS to add data to the already presented cases in the literature. Material and Methods: Our patient is a premenarchal 12-years-old female with severe thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis. The deformity was first noticed 1 year after birth. Initial treatment consisted of a brace and kinesiotherapy. The curve progressed despite adequate conservative treatment reaching a severe U-shaped lumbar kyphoscoliosis. Clinical examination revealed the thoracic hypokyphosis and a prominent left lumbar gibbus resulting in a severe sagittal imbalance. No neurologic issues were noticed. Other specific features of this patient include arachnodactyly, generalized ligamentous hyperlaxity and hyperpigmentation overlying the patellae. The patient was severely underweight with a body mass index of 11.3 (23kg and 143cm) raising concerns of spinal implant coverage. Preoperative X-rays highlighted the severity of the deformity with a L1-L5 kyphosis of 100° and thoracic hypokyphosis. No associated spinal cord anomaly was observed on MRI. The surgical treatment consisted of a single stage posterior approach with T4-pelvic fixation associated with multilevel Ponte osteotomies. Intraoperative correction was achieved progressively using multiple rods, applied consecutively. Results: No major intra and postoperative complications occurred. A brace was used postoperatively, and assisted walking was possible 6 days after surgery. The patient gained 7 cm in height immediately after surgery. Postoperative X-rays and clinical exam reasonable spatial correction of the deformity. The 1-year clinical and X-rays follow-up showed minimal correction loss without any other complications. The latest BMI is 13.4 (31kg and 152 cm). Conclusion: Kyphoscoliotic Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge due to comorbidities and spinal curve pattern. Posterior-only approach is suitable to reasonably correct the spatial balance of the spine.

## ID: AGS26/831

## P0126: Free hand vs navigation guided pedicle screw insertion in scoliosis - An analysis of 5000 screws using intra operative low dose CT scan

Ahmad Hussien

1

, Marcus De Matas

1

, Sudarshan Munigangaiah

1

1

Alder Hey Children Hospital, Liverpool, United Kingdom

Introduction: Pedicle screw placement accuracy is critical in scoliosis correction surgery. Previous studies have shown up to 3.6 % rate of malposition despite the use of navigation. Malposition can lead to a return to theatre up to 2 % in free hand technique. Our Aim is to evaluate whether there is a significant difference in revision rates between pedicle screws inserted, using the freehand technique versus navigation-guided placement, assessed utilizing intraoperative low-dose CT for assessment. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of all consecutive operative cases of scoliosis between 2019 to 2022 at Alder Hey was undertaken. 275 patients and a total of 5,006 implanted pedicle screws were included. Of these, 4,449 were inserted freehand and 557 using navigation. Screw positioning was assessed intraoperatively with low-dose CT, and intra operative revisions were recorded. Statistical analyses included the Wilcoxon test, Kruskal-Wallis test, and chi-square test as appropriate. Results: Of the 5,006 screws, 4,803 (95.9%) were deemed to have been in acceptable position, while 203 (4.1%) were unsatisfactory and required intra operative revision. Counter intuitively the revision rates were similar between the freehand and navigation groups, with no statistically significant difference. No association was found between revision rates and gender, vertebral level, instrumentation length, Cobb angle, or operation time. The median Cobb angle was 75° (IQR: 63-90), median operation time was 300 minutes (IQR: 240-360), and median instrumentation length was 12 levels (IQR: 10-13). Two patients returned to theatre, one for PJK and the second for rod breakage. No patient returned to theatre for screw malposition. Conclusion: The revision rates for pedicle screws inserted freehand and navigation guided in scoliosis surgery in a high-volume specialist unit are comparable. Intraoperative low-dose CT is an effective tool for real-time assessment after screw placement. It allows for intra-operative revision and eliminates the risk of return to theatre.

## ID: AGS26/878

## P0127: Results of surgical treatment of high-grade spondylolisthesis

Andriy Mezentsev

1

, Dmytro Petrenko

1

, Dmytro Demchenko

1

1

Rudnev Multidisciplinary Clinical Hospital, Dnipro, Ukraine

Introduction: Spondylolisthesis of Grade III-IV according to the Meyerding classification, referred in the literature as “high-grade spondylolisthesis,” involves a significant degree of vertebral displacement and leads to substantial impairment of spinal function, gait disturbances, and early disability in patients. Despite the fact that clinical symptoms, classifications, and diagnostic methods have been known and established for quite some time, the issue of surgical treatment remains a subject of debate to this day. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on the surgical outcomes of 24 patients with significant vertebral displacement who underwent intraoperative traction and transpedicular fixation using a “spine-pelvis” system. Pre- and postoperative radiographic measurements included the slip angle (SA), pelvic incidence (PI), lumbar lordosis (LL), sacral slope (SS), pelvic tilt (PT), as well as evaluation of sagittal vertical axis (SVA) alignment and pelvic tilt angle (PT). Results: All patients demonstrated a reduction in slip angle (SA) and restoration of sagittal vertical axis (SVA) alignment to within normal limits. Other parameters of spinopelvic balance were also moderately improved, thereby bringing sagittal spinal alignment closer to normal values and enhancing the biomechanical conditions for spinal function. Eight patient had gait disturbance postoperatively that resolved 3-6 month after the surgery. Rod breakage observed in 3 patients after 1, 2 and 5 years correspondingly. Conclusion: The use of intraoperative traction via ligamentotaxis allowed for repositioning of the displaced vertebra and facilitated the placement of transpedicular screws. The combination of intraoperative spinal traction and pulling on transpedicular screws using reduction devices enabled correction of the displaced vertebra to Grade.

## P0128: Preoperative pain distribution and mental health in Lenke type 1-2 AIS: a prospective cross-sectional study

Paolo Brigato

1

, Ekin celtikcioglu

2

, Christopher Coluni

2

, Kai Sheng

2

, Ojasvi Sharma

2

, Neil Saran

2

, Lisbet Haglund

2

, Jean Ouellet

2

1

University Hospital Campus Bio-Medico of Rome, Rome, Italy

2

McGill University, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: Pain is common in adolescents with idiopathic scoliosis (AIS), yet its distribution is heterogeneous and mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Beyond structural factors, emerging evidence suggests a psychosocial component with links to mental health. This study investigated associations between preoperative pain distribution and mental health in Lenke type 1-2 AIS undergoing posterior spinal fusion. Material and Methods: In this prospective single-center cohort (July 2023-July 2025), patients completed SRS-22 and a standardized pain diagram (10 regions). Coronal/sagittal parameters were measured on EOS imaging. Univariable linear regressions examined SRS-22 Mental Health vs pain regions and radiographic variables; models were run unadjusted and adjusted for age, sex, and primary Cobb (α < .05). Results: Forty-two patients (31 female; mean age 16.3 ± 2.3 years) were included, with a mean SRS-22 Mental Health score of 3.7 ± 0.66. Neck region pain (R1) was reported by 21.4% of patients and was significantly associated with lower Mental Health scores (β = -0.54, p = .029) in both unadjusted and adjusted models. No other pain regions or radiographic parameters demonstrated significant associations. Conclusion: Preoperative axial neck pain was independently associated with poorer mental health, whereas radiographic features were not. These exploratory findings support a biopsychosocial perspective in AIS and motivate routine preoperative screening for pain and mental health to identify at-risk patients. Confirmation in larger, multicenter longitudinal cohorts with predefined multiplicity control is warranted.

## P0129: A comparative study of surgical correction of idiopathic scoliosis with spinal pedicle screw in children

Nurbek Nadirov

1

, Bolat Nagymanov

1

, Marat Dzhetpisov

1

, Olzhas Zhunusov

2

, Nurislam Magauiya

1

, Ulykbek Zhaipan

1

, Dauren Turalbaev

1

, Nurbek Seidazimov

1

, Sarkyt Bigash

1

1

University Medical Center Corporate Fund, Astana, Kazakhstan

2

Multidisciplinary City Children's Hospital №2, Astana, Kazakhstan

Introduction: Surgical treatment of severe forms of thoracic idiopathic scoliosis remains an urgent and not fully resolved problem. When correcting idiopathic scoliosis, it is important to restore the frontal and sagittal profiles of the deformed spine, rotate the apical vertebra and maintain this result in the postoperative period. Material and Methods: the work is based on the analysis of the results of surgical treatment of 80 children diagnosed with thoracic idiopathic scoliosis grade 3-4: 12 male patients and 68 female patients aged 14 to 17 years. All patients (80 patients) were of the first type (Lenke 1); the sagittal contour in most of them was marked as normokyphosis. Patients were divided into 4 groups depending on the type of surgical correction. The choice of the technique used to correct spinal deformity in children with idiopathic thoracic scoliosis was determined by the initial size of the main curvature, its mobility, and the anatomo-anthropometric features of the vertebral arch roots comprising the curvature arch. Results: When comparing the effectiveness of surgical correction between the first and second groups, the correction of scoliotic deformity of the spine in patients of the first group (92.1 ± 7.1%) was greater than in the second group (81.8 ± 6.3%). TAt the same time, it should be emphasized that the magnitude of derotation in the first group (65.9 ± 9.5%) is significantly higher compared to patients in the second group (23.2 ± 4.3%). The magnitude of the correction of kyphosis and lordosis in both groups was the same, and clinically and radiographically improvement or complete recovery was noted. Following mobilizing interventions on the anterior parts of the vertebral bodies of the main deformity curve from the anterolateral approach and dorsal correction with a metal structure with transpedicular supporting elements, it was noted that the magnitude of the correction of scoliotic deformity of the spine in the third group of patients (78.1 ± 7.7%) was greater than in the fourth group (71.7 ± 17.4%), which could be explained by the fact that installed transpedicular screws on both sides of the deformed area increase the effect on the spinal column. It should also be emphasized that the percentage of derotation of the apical vertebra in patients of the third group (46.3 ± 11.9%) was significantly higher than in the fourth group (22.7 ± 8.8%). Conclusion: Using different levels and lengths for installation of transpedicular elements, the sequence of corrective manipulations as well as the use of the VCM system made it possible to individualize the approach and to correct all components of the deformity in three planes. A conducted study on surgical treatment in patients with idiopathic scoliosis showed that using transpedicular support elements through the whole curvature arch with VCM system, was significantly better in correction of the deformity (scoliotic arch and rotation of the apical vertebra), compared to those in whom pedicle screws were not installed over two or more vertebrae from the concave side of the curvature.
