## P0130: Vertebral body tethering (VBT) vs brace treatment in patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Arkadii Kazmin

1

, Vladimir Pereverzev

1

, Darya Tesakova

1

, Sergei Kolesov

1

1

N. N. Priorov National Medical Research Center, Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: Idiopathic scoliosis is always old and always young orthopedic problem. International standards include brace conservative treatment with specialized exercise therapy for growing spine. Dorsal surgery with instrumentation and fusion is recommended for bone growth ended patients. Mobile correction technologies as VBT has been actively developed inspite of large number of complications and reoperations (up to 60%). Material and Methods: Prospective observational study included 62 patients aged 16 to 21 who underwent anterior scoliosis correction (ASC) in the period from 2018 to 2022. The ASC technology included deformity arc stabilization by two cords with two screws into the vertebral body implantation and nucleotomy at the top of the deformity for a greater derotation. These particularities differ ASC from VBT. Lenke I was in all patients from this group (right-sided thoracic spinal deformity). All deformities in group were mobile according to functional radiographs, Risser was 4-5. The male to female ratio was 1:4. Literature data were used for comparative analyziz. Postural radiographs of the spine in two projections, traction test, right/left tilts were done for all patients. Measurement of the Cobb angle, assessment of bone age according to Risser, MRI of the entire spine, SRS-22 questionnaire, postoperative complications registration performed. Results: In 1 year after surgery the evaluation of the results was carried out. The PreOp deformity Cobb angle was 45.5° (33-58°), and PostOp Cobb was 24.5° (11-38°) respectively, and was assessed as a satisfactory correction. According to the SRS-22 questionnaire, 52 patients would agree to undergo such surgical treatment again, eight could not answer, one would refuse. Among postoperative complications, pneumothorax occurred in 7 patients, chylothorax in 1 patient. The total number of complications was 8 (12.9%). No cord breaks, deformity hypercorrection, or loss of correction occured. Conclusion: ASC technology can be alternative surgery for patients with failed brace treatment during growth with maintaining the spine mobility in the PostOp life. It can provide higher satisfaction with the treatment, confirmed by the SRS-22 questionnaire. At the same time, the complications associated with the spine growth could be also eliminated with ASC technology comparable with VBT. The authors invite interested parties to join the study for growing patients undergoing brace treatment and using ASC technology for patients with Risser 4-5 while maintaining indications for surgical treatment, but minimizing the risks associated with spinal growth.

## ID: AGS26/1216

## P0131: Refining diagnostic criteria for atypical Scheuermann

Gabriel Souza

1

, Lucas Yuya

1

, Alexandre Portelinha

1

, Mariana Pontes

1

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

1

1

University of Sao Paulo, Ribeirao Preto, Brazil

Introduction: Scheuermann’s kyphosis is the most common cause of juvenile hyperkyphosis, typically involving the thoracic spine. An atypical variant, however, affects the thoracolumbar junction and may present with distinctive clinical and radiographic features, such as more severe back pain, neurological manifestations, and significant vertebral wedging. Although Sorensen’s criteria remain the standard for diagnosis, the literature on atypical Scheuermann’s disease is limited, with uncertainties regarding its characterization and prevalence. This study aims to assess the diagnostic approaches currently adopted by spine surgery specialists and to propose refined criteria to enhance early recognition and management of this underexplored condition. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional survey was distributed to members of the Brazilian Society of Spine Surgery. The questionnaire included 12 items assessing surgeons’ knowledge, experience, and diagnostic preferences regarding both typical and atypical Scheuermann’s disease. Results: A total of 36 spine surgeons participated (88.9% orthopedic surgeons, 11.1% neurosurgeons), with a mean of 8.9 years of experience. Most respondents (97.2%) had treated patients with Scheuermann’s disease, and 83.3% identified the affected spinal segment as the main distinction between typical and atypical forms. Atypical Scheuermann’s disease was perceived to present with more severe symptoms by 72.2% of participants. The most commonly used diagnostic imaging was standard lateral spine radiographs, with anterior wedging of ≥ 5° in three consecutive vertebrae as the most cited criterion. Conclusion: The current exploration of diagnostic criteria for atypical Scheuermann's disease underscores a critical need for standardization. While the established parameters for typical Scheuermann's kyphosis, as defined by Sorensen, provide a solid foundation for diagnosis, the lack of clarity and uniformity regarding the evaluation of atypical cases presents significant challenges for spine surgeons.

## ID: AGS26/1230

## P0132: Use of posterior apical dynamic distraction device in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis: 2 years follow-up

Karen Weissmann

1

, Marcelo Perez

2

, Francoise Descazeaux

3

, Gianella Benvenuto

4

1

Clinica MEDS, patagonia Spine Foundation, Santiago, Chile

2

Clinica MEDS, Santiago, Chile

3

Clinica MEDS, Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Universidad de Santiago de Chile, Santiago, Chile

4

Universidad de Santiago de Chile, Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: The Posterior Apical Dynamic Distraction Device (PADD) is a novel surgical technique that corrects scoliosis without spinal fusion, thereby preserving mobility. Materials and Methods: We conducted a cohort study of patients operated between July 2022 and December 2024 using a PADD. Clinical and radiographic parameters were assessed preoperatively, immediately postoperatively, and at 3, 12, and 24 months. Statistical analysis included t-test and ANOVA (p < 0.05). Results: Sixteen patients (13 females) were included, with a mean age of 14.4 years; 62.5% practiced sports. Nine patients had Lenke type 1 curves and seven Lenke type 5, with Risser 3-4 and Sanders 5-6. Mean follow-up was 16 months. Average surgical time was 77.8 minutes with blood loss of 80 cc. Patients were discharged at 48 hours, returned to school at 18 days, and resumed sports at 33 days. The main curve decreased from 46.3° to 22° postoperatively (p < 0.001) and remained stable at 1 year (22.4°; p < 0.001) and 2 years (27.45°; p < 0.001). The secondary curve decreased from 30.9° to 17.4° (p < 0.001) and remained stable at follow-up. T1-T12 height increased from 25.4 mm to 28.7 mm (p = 0.22). Kyphosis showed no significant variation. Lordosis decreased slightly postoperatively, then increased in Lenke 1 (52.4° to 62°) and Lenke 5 (45.8° to 53.7°; p = 0.83). Device expansion was from 26.6 mm to 39.6 mm at 1 year (p < 0.001), reaching 38 mm at 2 years (p < 0.001). Conclusion: The PADD technique achieves scoliosis correction while maintaining sagittal balance at 1- and 2-year follow-up. It represents a valid alternative for flexible Lenke 1 and 5 curves measuring less than 60°.

## ID: AGS26/1240

## P0133: Use of posterior apical dynamic distraction device in patients with scoliosis and lytic spondylolisthesis: report of three cases

Karen Weissmann

1,2

, Francoise Descazeaux

1,3,4

, Gianella Benvenuto

3,4

1

Clinica MEDS, patagonia Spine Foundation, Santiago, Chile

2

Patagonis Spine Foundation, Santiago, Chile

3

Universidad de Santiago de Chile, Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Santiago, Chile

4

Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Approximately 6.2% of patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) present with concomitant lytic spondylolisthesis (LS). The coexistence of these diagnoses complicates the choice of fusion levels, since long fusions generate biomechanical overload in the adjacent unfused segments. In such cases, posterior apical dynamic distraction devices (PADD) emerge as an innovative alternative to preserve mobility and reduce overload. Materials and Methods: We present three cases of adolescents with combined diagnosis of AIS and L5-S1 LS: two with Lenke 5-like curves and one with Lenke 1. All were treated with PADD. Included patients had grade I and III lytic spondylolisthesis, curves < 30°, reducible, and Risser 3-4. Results: Mean age was 14.3 years. One patient had Ehlers-Danlos syndrome and another had Opitz G/BBB syndrome. The main curve decreased from 45.3° to 18.2°, while the secondary curve decreased from 31.5° to 19.4°. T1-T12 height increased from 24.9 cm to 26 cm. Two patients required additional surgery for LS: in one high-grade case, L4-S1 fusion with sacral osteotomy was performed, showing improvement in pelvic incidence (65.7° to 74.2°), kyphosis (5.3° to 27.5°), and lordosis (42.6° to 67.8°). In the low-grade LS case (L5-S1 fusion) and in the conservatively managed case, spinopelvic parameters showed no relevant changes. None presented sagittal imbalance. Discussion: In this series, PADD achieved significant correction of the Cobb angle, while maintaining sagittal parameters except in the high-grade LS case. Moreover, it allowed rapid functional recovery without complications. In patients with ligamentous laxity or syndromic diagnoses, this approach may offer biomechanical advantages by avoiding rigid constructs. Conclusion: PADD is a valid alternative for patients with AIS and LS, particularly in flexible curves and in those with comorbidities who may benefit from a less rigid approach.

## ID: AGS26/497

## P0134: Extending the Indications of Minimally Invasive Scoliosis Surgery in Idiopathic Scoliosis: Feasibility in Very Severe Curves Exceeding 80°

Joungwoo Shin

1

, Hong Jin Kim

1

, Ppuri Bak

1

, JunHyeok Kwon

1

, Sanghyun Park

1

, Jae Hyuk Yang

2

, Yunjin Nam

1

, Seung Woo Suh

1

1

Korea University Guro Hospital, Seoul, Korea, Rep. of South

2

Korea University Anam Hospital, Seoul, Korea, Rep. of South

Introduction: Minimally invasive scoliosis surgery (MISS) through a posterior approach has been shown to achieve comparable radiological outcomes and superior perioperative metrics when compared to conventional open scoliosis surgery in patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). Despite these advantages, the application of MISS has remained largely restricted to cases with moderate to severe curve magnitudes. Its feasibility and efficacy in treating very severe curves exceeding 80° remain unestablished. Material and Methods: A total of 588 patients with IS who underwent MISS between 2015 and 2023 were included, each with a minimum follow-up period of two years. Included cases were divided into the three groups based on the main Cobb’s angle: M group (n = 335, 45° ≤ Main Cobb’s angle < 60°), S group (n = 216, 60° ≤ Main Cobb’s angle < 75°), and VS group (n = 35, Main Cobb’s angle ≥ 80°). Radiological parameters, operative data, and complications were compared across groups at the preoperative, postoperative, and final follow-up. Results: The mean operative time was significantly shorter in the M group (279.0 minutes) compared to the S (311.2 minutes) and VS (319.9 minutes) groups (P < 0.001). Estimated blood loss was also significantly lower in the M group (813.1 mL) compared to the S (986.6 mL) and VS (1000.0 mL) groups (P = 0.006), though there were no significant differences in postoperative transfusion rates. Radiologically, the mean preoperative Cobb angles were 52.9° (M), 66.2° (S), and 82.3° (VS) (P < 0.001). No significant differences were found among groups regarding correction rate, correction loss, coronal balance, sagittal vertical axis, thoracic kyphosis, or lumbar lordosis. Pleural effusion was the most common complication, associated with thoracoplasty, but incidence did not differ significantly across groups. Conclusion: MISS via a posterior approach demonstrated acceptable radiological and operative outcomes, even in patients with idiopathic scoliosis presenting with Cobb angles exceeding 80°. These findings support the feasibility of MISS as a viable surgical option in more severe cases, thereby suggesting an expanded indication for its application in the treatment of idiopathic scoliosis.

## ID: AGS26/1350

## P0135: Less for more or less for less? Preserving lumbar mobility by choosing LIV on L3 on patients that met criteria for L4 fusion in adolescent idiopathic scholiosis: our clinical experience

Doria Crismaruc

1

, Vlad Pencea

1

1

Emergency Clinical Hospital for Children Marie Curie, Bucharest, Romania

Introduction: Treatment of AIS has always been a matter of concern among surgeons, especially regarding the limits of distal instrumentation, keeping in mind trunk balance, aesthethics and mobile lumbar segments. In this study we tried to follow up what happened with the adding on curve in patients with 3C, 4C, 5C and 6C Lenke that had criteria for LIV at L4 but were fused to L3 to preserve as much as possible lumbar mobillity and avoid disk degeneration without imbalancing the spine. Material and Methods: Radiological data of 12 patients who met the inclusion criteria were documented. We included patients with Adolescent Idiopathic Scholiosis (3C, 4C, 5C and 6C Lenke curve), based on the relationship of L3 to CSVL on AP and right bending XRay and rotation of L3 on left bending. We included the patients that met the following criteria: L3 does not cross the CSVL on right bending, L3 had more than II Nash Moe rotation on left bending or L3 was barely touched by CSVL or not touched on AP Xray. We chose L3 as LIV, preserving lumbar mobility. We excluded patients with only thoracic curve, patients with other types of scholiosis and also patients with intramedullary pathologies on MRI. Results: To the patients analysed with a medium preoperative Cobb angle of 66 degree, during a mean follow up of 2.3 years, we observed a mean wedging of 6 degree (N < 5 degree) at LIV and a mean translation of 13 mm at LIV+1 from CSVL (N < 10 mm) and also an increasing postoperative lumbar curve from 23 degree to 29 degree. Conclusion: Even thought we had some decompensation, the balance of the spine was kept and we managed to preserve lumbar mobility in patients that met criteria for L4 instrumentation. We did not have any revision surgery to extend the implant at the analysed patients.

## ID: AGS26/1362

## P0136: Transposition of the spinal cord to rescue neurological function in severe spinal deformities

Ahmed Shawky Abdelgawaad

1

, Mohammad El-Sharkawi

2

1

Helios Hospitals Erfurt - Germany, Assiut University Hospitals - Egypt, Erfurt, Germany

2

Assiut University Hospitals, Assiut, Egypt

Introduction: Severe kyphoscoliosis deformities are challenging conditions that may lead to progressive neurological deficits. Surgical correction is sometimes hindered by the patient’s medical condition or the availability of infrastructure. Surgical correction further puts the cord at risk of neurological deterioration. Transposition of the spinal cord is a safe, reproducible procedure that saves cord function in selected cases. Material and Methods: Four patients with severe kyphoscoliosis deformities experienced deterioration of their neurological function perioperatively. Transposition of the spinal cord was done to rescue neurological function. The procedure involves resection of the concave side pedicles, ligation, and resection of the dorsal nerve roots (at least 4 nerve roots). The spinal cord is then mobilized and relocated outside the spinal canal lateral to the angular deformity. Results: All patients improved neurologically. At three months postoperative, the 4 patients were AIS E. The average operative time was 130 minutes. The average blood loss was 400 ml. Three patients had severe congenital kyphoscoliosis, and one patient had neglected early-onset deformity. Two patients had preoperative neurological deficits (ASIA Impairment Score) C, and two experienced intraoperative loss of SSEPs and MEPs with immediate postoperative AIS B. Conclusion: Transposition of the spinal cord is a safe, reproducible surgical procedure that rescues neurological function in selected severe angular kyphoscoliosis cases.

## ID: AGS26/1458

## P0137: Traditional dual growing rods treatment for early onset scoliosis in a pediatric high complexity Chilean hospital. Are we meeting the objectives?

Ignacio Parra Morales

1

, Diego Alvarez

2

, Francoise Descazeaux

1

, Jorge Saavedra Gallardo

2

, Carlos Huaiquilaf

3

1

Hospital Exequiel González Cortés, MEDS, Santiago, Chile

2

Hospital Exequiel González Cortés, Clínica Bicentenario, Santiago, Chile

3

Hospital Exequiel González Cortés, MEDS, Clínica Bicentenario, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Traditional growing rods treatment is a commonly used surgical growth sparing strategy for patients with early onset scoliosis. The objectives are to control the progression of the spinal deformity and maintain longitudinal spine and thoracic growth in children who have not reached skeletal maturity. This study will show the results of traditional dual growing rods treatment in a Pediatric High Complexity Chilean Hospital. Material and Methods: A retrospective observational descriptive study was conducted in our health center, including all the patients who underwent growing rods surgery between the years 2019 and 2025. The inclusion criteria were: two or more years of patient follow-up and a minimum of one lengthening surgery. The exclusion criteria were: incomplete records, non-traditional (magnetic) growing rods system or unilateral rod. Some of the variables included were the following: Age at the beginning and the end of the treatment (or up to date), sex, scoliosis etiology, number of lengthenings, interval between index surgery - first lengthening and between lengthenings (in months), average measure of the distraction procedure (in millimeters), Major curve Cobb angle (initial, initial with traction, after first surgery and final), thoracic height and T1-S1 measure in millimeters (initial, initial with traction, after first surgery and final), proximal and distal anchorage levels and complications during follow up. In all patients included in the study, the technique was dual growing rods construct using a parallel wedding band two-hole domino. For digital measurements Surgimap and local XERO Viewer software were used. Results: A total of 9 patients fulfill the study criteria. The average age at index surgery was 7.6 years (4-10), and 11 years (8-14) at the end of the treatment. 55.6% of the patients were women and 44.4% men. Regarding etiology; 4 patients had idiopathic scoliosis, 3 neuromuscular and 2 syndromic. The number of lengthenings was in average 2.6 (1-4), the interval between index surgery and first lengthening was 9.6 months (5-16) and between lengthenings was 14 months (9-25). The average distraction per procedure was 13 mm (11-15). The initial major cobb angle was in average 58° (36-99) and the average reduction in cobb angle at final follow up was 21° (7-44, SD 13.6). The increment in thoracic height was average 10.4 mm (8-13, SD 1.8) by year, and the difference between the final thoracic height and immediately post-index surgery was in average 10.7 mm (1-28, SD 8.6). The increment in T1-S1 distance was average 16.6 mm (12-22, SD 3) by year, and the difference between final T1-S1 distance and post-index surgery was in average 17.6 mm (8-32, SD 8). We should mention that 66% of the patients had some type of complication. Conclusion: This study shows that the objectives of growing rods treatment are fulfilled, despite the wide range in number of lengthenings and interval between them. None of the patients progressed in their scoliosis degree and the gainings in thoracic height and T1-S1 distance by year are more than historically describe by Dimeglio et al. for the range of 5-10 years (majority of the patients).

## ID: AGS26/1462

## P0138: Comparative evaluation of postoperative outcomes between the Rod-link system and conventional methods in the correction of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis deformity

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Rodolfo Páez González

1,2

, David Flórez Torres

1,2

, Carlos Castellanos Cordero

1,2

, Catalina Sarmiento Gómez

1,2

1

Hospital Infantil Universitario de San José, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Fundación Universitaria De Ciencias De La Salud, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) affects 1-3% of individuals aged 10-18 years, particularly females, is characterized by coronal curvature and vertebral rotation with significant clinical and functional implications. Conventional surgical correction using rods and screws, while effective, carries neurovascular risks and is associated with prolonged recovery. The Rod-link system was developed to enhance the adaptability and efficacy of surgical correction. This study compares postoperative outcomes of the Rod-link system with traditional techniques in adolescents with AIS, providing local evidence that may inform clinical decision-making and optimize treatment protocols. Material and Methods: An analytical observational study was conducted in adolescents with AIS treated either with the Rod-link system or conventional techniques. A convenience sampling was applied between 2015 and 2024 at a fourth-level complexity hospital. Clinical, radiographic, and postoperative outcome variables were recorded using a structured data collection form, managed in Excel 365, and statistically analyzed with R version 4.3.1. Descriptive and comparative analyses were performed, applying parametric or nonparametric tests according to data distribution, with a significance level set at p < 0.05. Results: Forty-six patients (82.6% women) were included, with a median age of 14 years (IQR 13-15.8). Patients in the Rod-Link group were younger at surgery (13.7 vs. 15.2 years; p = 0.049), had a larger preoperative Cobb angle (68.4° vs. 49.1°; p = 0.006), and had a shorter surgical duration (378 vs. 430 minutes; p = 0.035) compared with conventional techniques. No differences were observed in intraoperative bleeding, postoperative hemoglobin, or hospital stay (p > 0.05). The use of hooks was more frequent in conventional techniques (37.5% vs. 4.5%; p = 0.012), with no differences in postoperative complications or neuromonitoring alarms. Conclusion: The Rod-Link system was applied in patients with more severe curves and was associated with a reduction in surgical time compared with conventional techniques. No differences were observed in bleeding, transfusion, postoperative hemoglobin, or hospital stay; postoperative complications were infrequent and similar between groups. However, the retrospective design, sample size, and convenience sampling restrict the generalizability of the findings. Prospective, multicenter studies with larger numbers of patients are needed to confirm these results and evaluate long-term clinical and functional outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1575

## P0139: Surgical treatment of severe and rigid Scheuermann’s kyphosis with simultaneous gradual anterior column lengthening and posterior compression following PVCR: case series

Burak Abay

1

, Sepehr Asadollahmonfared

1

, Hamisi Mraja

1

, Mehmet Zamanoglu

1

, Ilyas Dolas

1

, Selhan Karadereler

1

, Meric Enercan

2

, Azmi Hamzaoglu

1

1

Scoliosis and Spine Center - Istanbul, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Demiroglu Bilim University, Istanbul, Turkey

Hypothesis: Gradual anterior column lengthening and simultaneous posterior compression technique after PVCR achieves ideal restoration of regional and global sagittal alignment, prevents dural buckling and iatrogenic neurological deficit and eliminates the need for anterior surgery. Study Design: Case series. Introduction: Combined anterior-posterior surgery is considered the gold standard for surgical treatment for severe and rigid Scheuermann’s kyphosis. Recently there has been a trend towards posterior-only surgery for Scheuermann’s kyphosis. Among 3CO’s only PVCR remains a valid option for correcting the severe and rigid kyphotic deformity. PVCR provides flexibility for angular deformity correction and circumferential decompression of the spinal cord. Gradual anterior column lengthening corrects the angular and global deformity while preventing dural buckling and iatrogenic neurological deficit. This study evaluates clinical and radiologic outcomes of PVCR in the surgical treatment of severe and rigid Scheuermann’s kyphosis. Methods: Pts with Scheuermann’s kyphosis treated with PVCR were included. Following PVCR, the correction technique involved gradual anterior column lengthening with simultaneous posterior compression manuevers followed by placement of an expandable cage to the osteotomy site. Preop, postop and final f/up x-rays were evaluated for local kyphosis angle (LKA) and sagittal parameters. Health related quality of life was assessed by SRS-22r. Results: 10 pts with Scheuermann’s kyphosis, with a mean age 22.4 (15-36) yrs and f/up 4.9 (2-11) yrs were included. Osteotomies performed at T8 (2pts), T9 (2pts), T10 (3pts), T11 (2pts), and T12 (1pts). Preop LKA of 64.6° (55-76) restored to 10.5°(83.8% Correction Rate) and preop thoracic kyphosis of 86.7° (79-102) corrected to 45.8° (47.1%). Preop LL of 74.6° (56-90) restored to 49.6° (47.1%). Preop 47.4° of kyphosis between T10-L2 levels restored to 4.2°. None of the pts had postop neurological defisit. Solid fusion was achieved in all pts without significant loss of correction in the sagittal plane at the final f/up. SRS-22r total scores improved from a mean of 3.58 to 4.64. Conclusion: Simultaneous gradual anterior column lengthening and spinal shortening provided by posterior compressions following PVCR allows ideal correction of thoracic and thoracolumbar kyphotic deformity, restores global sagittal alignment, prevents dural buckling and iatrogenic neurologic deficit and eliminates anterior surgery for the severe and rigid Scheuermann’s kyphosis.

 

## ID: AGS26/1608

## P0140: Do less is more: achieving optimal outcomes with selective instrumentation and fusion of strategic vertebrae in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Md. Shah Alam

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedic Rehabilitation, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Scoliosis surgery is a complex and highly specialized procedure aimed at correcting abnormal curvature of the spine. This condition, known as scoliosis, can range from mild to severe and can significantly impact a person's quality of life. The purpose of this study is to provide a concise overview of scoliosis surgery, including its indications, surgical techniques, potential risks and complications, and post-operative care with minimum instrumentation. Material and Methods: From February 2009-July 2025, 181 cases underwent surgery. 108 were female and 73 were male and age ranged 14-38 years. The pedicle is a power nucleus of the vertebra and offers a secure grip of all 3 columns. Pedicle screw instrumentation ensures rigid fixation with improved 3D correction. Accurate placement of screws is important to reduce possible irreversible complication. All cases were corrected by transpedicular screws and rods with minimum instrumentation addressing strategic vertebre. 172 cases were managed by only posterior approach and 9 cases required both anterior releases, costoplasty, posterior stabilization as well. Fusion was done in selected segments. Total follow up time was about 10 years (6 months-7 yrs). All patients were assessed by deformity correction, cosmesis, and functional outcome. Use of Neuromonitor is an excellent & effective method for prevention of post operative neuro deficit. Results: 40% patients had average coronal plane cobb’s angle measuring about 700 pre-operatively and 17.120 immediate post-operative period. 2% patients developed neurodeficit. 3 regained completely but other regaining her neurological deficit very slowly. There were malposition of screws in 13%, painful prominence of screws in 10%, superficial infection in 4%. 80% patients improved cosmetically. Conclusion: Scoliosis surgery is a valuable treatment option for individuals with significant spinal curvature. While it carries certain risks, advancements in surgical techniques and post-operative care have improved patient outcomes and overall success rates.

## ID: AGS26/1610

## P0141: Balancing motion and correction: mid-term outcomes of hybrid fixation in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Feyzi Kilic

1

, Altug Yucekul

2

, Gokhan Ergene

3

, Sahin Senay

4

, Elif Gizem Carus

1

, Tais Zulemyan

1

, Caglar Yilgor

5

, Ahmet Alanay

2

1

Comprehensive Spine Center, Acibadem Maslak Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, istanbul, Turkey

3

Department of Operating Room Services, Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University Vocational School of Health Sciences, Istanbul, Turkey

4

Department of Cardiovascular Surgery, Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

5

Koc University Hospital Spine Center, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Vertebral body tethering (VBT) provides growth-modulated correction for scoliosis while preserving segmental motion. In patients with double major curves or thoracic deformities accompanied by thoracolumbar/lumbar curves, selective thoracic fusion alone may not provide sufficient control. Extending fusion into the lumbar spine ensures correction but at the cost of motion loss. To balance correction and mobility, a hybrid approach-thoracic posterior fusion combined with thoracolumbar/lumbar VBT-has recently gained popularity as a motion-preserving alternative. However, comprehensive series reporting mid-term outcomes of this technique remain limited. This study reports radiographic, patient-reported, pulmonary, and implant-survival outcomes of a consecutive hybridx fixation cohort. Materials and Methods: Prospectively maintained data were retrospectively analyzed at a single center. Outcomes included Cobb angles for the upper thoracic (UT), main thoracic (MT), and thoracolumbar/lumbar (TLL) curves at preoperative, first-erect, and latest follow-up. Patient-reported outcomes comprised SRS-22 Function, Pain, Self-image, and Mental Health domains from preoperative to latest follow-up. The minimal clinically important difference (MCID) was defined as 0.5× the preoperative SD. Pulmonary function tests (PFT) included percent-predicted FVC and FEV1. Complications, readmissions/reoperations, and time to first tether break were captured. Results: Twenty-five patients were analyzed (mean age 13.9 ± 2.5 years; 84% female). Preoperatively, median Sanders, CVM, and Risser were 5.5 [IQR 3-7], 4.0 [4-5], and 1.0 [0-3], respectively. Median follow-up was 25.3 months [24.2-29.2]. Lenke types were: Type 1: 43.5%, Type 2: 17.4%, Type 4: 4.3%, Type 5: 26.1%, and Type 6: 8.7%; lumbar modifiers: A: 4.3%, B: 26.1%, C: 69.6%. Preoperative flexibility was 48.8 ± 20.5% for UT, 56.8 ± 20.5% for MT, and 80.7 ± 18.9% for TLL. UT, MT, and TLL Cobb angles were 36.0 ± 11.1°, 61.8 ± 17.3°, and 50.9 ± 13.9° preoperatively; improved to 8.1 ± 5.0°, 7.8 ± 5.0°, and 12.8 ± 6.6° at first erect; and measured 8.8 ± 6.3°, 11.0 ± 8.7°, and 12.3 ± 8.3° at latest follow-up. SRS-22 self-image improved significantly (Δ +0.84, MCID achievement 67%, p < 0.001), while Function (Δ -0.16), Pain (Δ -0.16), and Mental Health (Δ +0.02) remained unchanged (all p > 0.05). PFT percent-predicted changes were -4.25 for FVC% and +0.21 for FEV1% (p > 0.05). Two mechanical complication occurred (tether break in 8%); no readmissions or reoperations were recorded. Conclusion: In this consecutive hybrid fixation cohort with median follow-up exceeding two years, UT, MT, and TLL Cobb angles improved substantially and were maintained. Patient self-image improved with preservation of pulmonary function. The mid-term implant profile was favorable, with low mechanical complication rates and survival without tether break >95% at one year and >80% at two years. These results highlight hybrid fixation as a promising motion-preserving alternative, warranting confirmation in larger cohorts with longer follow-up.

## ID: AGS26/1646

## P0142: No rod breakage occur by cMIS anterior posterior correction fusion from thoracic level

Takashi Moriwaki

1

1

Osaka Gyoumeikan Hospital, Osaka, Japan

Introduction: Rod breakage can be prevented of cMIS correction and fusion by anterolateral access interbody fusion for the anterior spine from the thoracic level to the lumbar and sacral vertebrae, including the thoracic-lumbar junction.To evaluate the outcome of changing from L5/SPLIF to OLIF51 in cMIS deformity correction and fusion surgery by Mini-Open retropleural, OLIF25, mainly anterolateral interbody correction and fusion from the thoracic to lumbosacral spine. Material and Methods: 23 cased cMIS for ASD by Mini-Open retropleural, OLIF25, and OLIF51 or bil.PLIF from the thoracic to lumbosacral level, all PPS, iliac screw cantilever rod rotation correction were perfomed. Of 23 cMIS cases (M/F; 8/15, mean age 74.0y, mean follow up 23.4M) ,7 cases of cMIS (by Mini-Open retopleural, OLIF25, OLIF51 and all PPS, iliac screw, single rod cantilever technique correction) (M/F; 2/5, age 71.5y, f/u 7.0M) and 16 cases of cMIS (by Mini-Open retopleural, OLIF25, bilateral PLIF and all PPS, iliac screw, single rod cantilever technique correction) (M/F; 10/6, age 75.1y, f/u 28.8M) were compared for changes in standing global alignment before and after surgery, and changes in GLL, LLL, and LDI related to lumbar lordosis formation. Results: In both PLIF and OLIF51, there were zero rod breakages in any of the cases. Conclusion: Rod breakage does not occur with cMIS deformity correction surgery performed across the thoracic junction, which is considered a biomechanical disadvantage. By switching to OLIF51, there was a tendency for a difference in LL.

## ID: AGS26/1678

## P0143: Correction of congenital kyphosis in skeletally mature patients not treated during childhood. Results and complications. Prospective study

Alfonso González-Menocal

1

, Rosa M Egea-Gámez

2

, Rafael González-Díaz

2

1

Hospital Universitario Infanta Elena, Madrid, Spain

2

Hospital Infantil Universitario Niño Jesús, Madrid, Spain

Introduction: Among congenital spinal deformities, congenital kyphosis is the leading cause of non-traumatic paraplegia in the adult population in the United States. Early management during childhood is therefore essential. In cases where patients reach skeletal maturity, surgical treatment becomes more aggressive and complex. The aim of the present study was to analyze the clinical and radiological outcomes of adolescents with congenital kyphosis treated by posterior vertebral column resection (PVCR) at a pediatric referral center for congenital spinal malformations. Material and Methods: All patients with congenital kyphosis undergoing surgical treatment between 2017 and 2020 by the same surgical team were included. Patients with an open triradiate cartilage or with follow-up of less than one year were excluded. Variables collected included type of spinal anomaly, surgical details, presence of intraoperative neurophysiological events, complications, clinical outcomes, and radiological results assessed on pre- and postoperative full-spine radiographs. Results: A total of 10 patients were analyzed, with a mean age at surgery of 14.99 years (range: 13.62-15.25), 60% of whom were female. Statistically significant improvements were observed in the following radiological parameters pre- vs. post-PVCR: Cobb angle 19.8° (12.4-52.8) vs. 7.1° (1.4-17.23); thoracolumbar junction T10-L2 46.2° (35-53.13) vs. 2.95° (0-8.93); segmental kyphosis 60.85° (59.4-71.6) vs. 8° (7.3-19.15); pelvic tilt 9.35° (2.5-18.58) vs. 2.4° (-6.6-6.93); and sacral slope 32° (30-33.33) vs. 34.7° (34-37.63). Regarding complications, two patients (20%) presented intraoperative neurophysiological changes with clinical repercussions: one recovered completely, while the other improved partially to an ASIA C classification. Conclusion: PVCR is effective in restoring sagittal spinal alignment in adolescent patients with rigid deformity secondary to congenital kyphosis. However, it is a complex procedure with a risk of severe complications. To minimize risks, surgical correction should ideally be performed during childhood. In skeletally mature patients, we recommend thorough preoperative planning and that surgery be undertaken by experienced spine surgeons.

## P0144: Scoliosis in patients with autism spectrum disorder: comparative clinical, radiographic, and surgical analysis versus adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Diego Alvarez

1

, Francoise Descazeaux

1

, Gianella Benvenuto

1

, Carlos Huaiquilaf

2

, Karen Weissmann

3

, Ignacio Parra Morales

1

1

Hospital Dr Exequiel Gonzales Cortes, Santiago, Chile

2
Clinica Redsalud Santiago, Santiago, Chile, 
3
Clinica MEDS, Santiago, Chile
Introduction: Scoliosis associated with Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) remains poorly characterized, despite clinical features resembling neuromuscular scoliosis (NMS) more than adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). Axial hypotonia, ligamentous hyperlaxity, and congenital vertebral anomalies suggest a distinct musculoskeletal phenotype. However, curve patterns and surgical strategies for this population are not clearly defined. Objective: To compare clinical, radiographic, and surgical outcomes of posterior instrumented fusion in patients with severe scoliosis and ASD versus a matched AIS cohort. Material and Methods: A retrospective comparative cohort study included 8 ASD patients with severe scoliosis who underwent posterior instrumented fusion between 2021 and 2025. A matched cohort of 8 AIS patients was selected based on age, sex, and radiographic characteristics. Statistical analysis included descriptive measures and independent-sample t-tests, with significance set at p < 0.05. Results: Both groups were comparable in age (14.6 ± 1.2 years ASD vs. 15.0 ± 1.5 AIS; p = 0.624) and sex distribution (50% female in both). Preoperative Cobb angles showed no significant differences: main thoracic 69.4° ± 12.1 (ASD) vs. 70.2° ± 11.7 (AIS; p = 0.956); proximal thoracic 35.8° ± 9.5 vs. 39.1° ± 8.8 (p = 0.376); thoracolumbar 44.6° ± 10.7 vs. 47.2° ± 9.9 (p = 0.527). Kyphosis was 46.2° ± 8.3 vs. 44.9° ± 7.7 (p = 0.558). Intraoperative variables were also comparable: operative time 270 ± 38 min vs. 262 ± 41 min (p = 0.697); blood loss 980 ± 240 ml vs. 940 ± 220 ml (p = 0.621), corresponding to 22.3% ± 5.8 vs. 21.7% ± 6.1 of blood volume (p = 0.891). Instrumented levels were 11.2 ± 1.7 vs. 10.9 ± 1.9 (p = 0.373), with invasiveness index 12.1 ± 3.2 vs. 11.8 ± 2.9 (p = 0.919). One major osteotomy and one neuromonitoring alert were recorded in the ASD group (both p = 0.302). Postoperatively, mean hospital stay was 7.1 ± 1.8 days (ASD) vs. 6.5 ± 1.5 (AIS; p = 0.222). A clinical complication occurred in 1 ASD patient (12.5%) vs. none in AIS (p = 0.302). Radiographic outcomes showed comparable corrections: main thoracic Cobb 25.3° ± 6.9 vs. 24.7° ± 6.1 (p = 0.433), with correction rates of 63.5% ± 9.1 vs. 64.7% ± 8.7 (p = 0.424). Thoracolumbar Cobb was corrected to 18.6° ± 5.2 vs. 17.9° ± 4.8 (p = 0.368), with relative correction 58.3% ± 7.5 vs. 62.1% ± 6.8 (p = 0.016). Conclusion: In patients with ASD and severe scoliosis undergoing posterior instrumented fusion, clinical, intraoperative, and radiographic outcomes were largely comparable to AIS. A trend toward lower thoracolumbar curve correction was observed in ASD. These findings suggest conventional AIS surgical strategies may be applicable to ASD patients, although larger studies are required to validate these results and assess potential tailored perioperative adaptations.

## ID: AGS26/1698

## P0145: Scoliosis in patients with Down syndrome. Correlation between curve magnitude and functional ambulation level

Rosa M Egea-Gámez

1

, Alfonso González-Menocal

2

, Rafael González-Díaz

1

1
Hospital Infantil Universitario Niño Jesús, Madrid, Spain, 
2
Hospital Universitario Infanta Elena, Madrid, Spain
Introduction: Patients with Down syndrome (DS) have a higher incidence of scoliosis compared to the general population; however, it remains unclear whether gross motor function (GMF) influences the characteristics and severity of the deformity. Functional walking abilities, described by the Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS), allow stratification of patients into five levels from greater to lesser functional ability. The aim of this study was to evaluate whether there is an association between GMF in patients with DS and spinal deformity. Material and Methods: This prospective case series included all patients with a diagnosis of DS, yielding a final sample of 649, of whom 46 were identified with scoliosis (60.42% female; mean age 14.6 ± 2.33 years). Based on their functional walking abilities, patients were classified according to the GMFCS, and various radiological parameters in the coronal and sagittal planes were measured. The treatment modality for scoliosis (observation, bracing, or surgery) was recorded. A multivariate associative analysis was performed between radiological parameters and GMFCS level. Results: Twenty-two patients were classified as GMFCS level I, 23 as level II, and 1 as level III. Curve location was thoracic in 12 cases, thoracolumbar in 10, and lumbar in 24. A significant association was found between GMFCS level and curve magnitude: GMFCS I 18.92° (6.6-79), GMFCS II 36.65° (20.7), GMFCS III 42.2° (p = 0.004, 1-ß = 0.879). A cutoff point of 22.55° was identified (AUC = 0.915, p < 0.005, sensitivity = 0.91, specificity = 0.82). The relationship between patients requiring surgery and GMFCS level was also significant (p = 0.036). No significant associations were found between functional level and coronal imbalance (17.39 mm [13.48] vs. 17.01 mm [12.76] vs. 49 mm, p = 0.07) or sagittal imbalance (32.36 mm [21.49] vs. 36.04 mm [33.14] vs. 24.9 mm, p = 0.91), nor with other radiological parameters including CL C2-C7 (p = 0.76), TK T5-T12 (p = 0.68), TL T10-L2 (p = 0.48), LL L1-S1 (p = 0.85), PI (p = 0.06), PT (p = 0.15), and SS (p = 0.51). Conclusion: In this study, patients with poorer GMF presented with greater scoliosis curve magnitude and higher risk of requiring surgery. No associations were found between functional level and other radiological parameters or curve location.

## ID: AGS26/1702

## P0146: Comparative analysis of thoracic transverse diameter of spinal canal and pedicle width: implications for pedicle screw insertion in pediatric patient

Dimas Rahman Setiawan

1

, Jungwook Lim

2

, Mubashar Bajwa

3

, Mohammad Kasim Aldawood

4

, Yunjin Nam

2

, Seung Woo Suh

2

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Cipto Mangunkusumo National General Hospital, Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Indonesia, Jakarta, Indonesia

2

Department of Orthopedic, Korea University Guro Hospital, College of Medicine, Korea University, Seoul, South Korea

3

Department of Orthopedic, Rai Medical College and Teaching Hospital, College of Medicine, Punjab, Pakistan

4

Department of Orthopedic, College of Medicine, Imam Abdulrahman bin Faisal University, Dammam, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Pedicle screw insertion is a routine procedure during scoliosis surgery. Various modalities facilitate the insertion of pedicle screws, such as robotic, navigation, C-arm guided, and freehand techniques. The freehand technique is widely used because it is cost-effective and more familiar to surgeon. However, this technique still has the potential for complications, especially in pediatric patient, due to anatomical size differences compared to adults’ patients. This difference could have an impact on determine entry point for pedicle screw insertion at the thoracic level in pediatric patient. Material and Methods: We retrospectively evaluated the imaging data of 60 study subjects, consisting of 30 pediatric and 30 adults, who underwent thoracic computed tomography (CT). We measured the ratio of transverse diameters of spinal canal width (SCW) and bilateral pedicle width (PW) compared to total diameter of SCW plus bilateral PW from 1st thoracic vertebra to the 12th thoracic vertebra by using patient’s CT. Results: Statistically, the results showed that the SCW ratio at all thoracic levels in pediatric population is wider than that in adults’ population (p < 0.05; t-test), with the mean differences varying from 0.044 to 0.096. Additionally, the PW ratio at all thoracic levels in pediatric is smaller than adults’ population (p < 0.05; t-test). Conclusion: Determining the entry point for pedicle screw insertion at the thoracic level in pediatric children needs to consider the wider SCW ratio and the smaller PW ratio compared to adults. We recommend that the entry point for pedicle screw insertion at the thoracic level in pediatric should be more lateral than that in adults.

## ID: AGS26/1747

## P0147: Three-column osteotomy versus halo-gravity traction combined with posterior column osteotomy in the treatment of dystrophic neurofibromatosis type 1 kyphoscoliosis: a retrospective comparative cohort study

Yuan-Shun Lo

1,2

, Erh-Ti Lin

1,2

, Yi-Hsun Huang

1,2

, Benlong Shi

3

, Yong Qiu

3

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, China Medical University Beigang Hospital, China Medical University, Yunlin, Taiwan

2

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, China Medical University Hospital, China Medical University, Taichung, Taiwan

3

Division of Spine Surgery, Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Medical School, Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

Introduction: Dystrophic neurofibromatosis type I (NF1) kyphoscoliosis presents unique challenges for corrective spinal surgery due to anatomical abnormalities. To compare the radiographic and clinical outcomes of dystrophic neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) kyphoscoliosis patients undergoing three-column osteotomy (3CO), halo-gravity traction (HGT), or posterior column osteotomy (PCO) and to evaluate their efficacy and safety in this cohort, different treatment strategies and their associated complication rates warrant further comprehensive investigation. Material and Methods: Dystrophic NF1 kyphoscoliosis were divided into 3CO, HGT, and PCO groups based on the surgical strategy. Radiographic parameters were measured preoperatively, postoperatively, and at each follow-up. Intraoperative and postoperative complications were recorded for each patient, and patient-reported outcomes were assessed using the Scoliosis Research Society-22 (SRS-22) questionnaire. Differences among the three groups were analyzed. Results: A total of 9 patients were included in the 3CO, 22 in HGT group, 95 in PCO groups, respectively. Significant differences among the three groups were found in terms of operation time (p = 0.011), estimated blood loss (p = 0.003), and number of satellite rod techniques (p = 0.013). At pre-operation, the Cobb angles of main curves were 84.3 ± 24.6° in 3CO group, 99.1 ± 24.3° in HGT group, 60.0 ± 16.8° in PCO group. At post-operation, significant post-operative improvements were found in the Cobb angles of the main curves, apical vertebral translation (AVT), segmental kyphosis (SK), and deformity angular ratio (DAR) in all three groups (p < 0.001). No significant correction loss was observed during the follow-up. Six complications were found in the 3CO group, 13 in the HGT group, and 40 in the PCO group. Conclusion: PCO, 3CO, and HGT could be applied to dystrophic NF1 patients. The 3CO is also associated with increased perioperative complications.

## ID: AGS26/1811

## P0148: Visual and vestibular anomalies in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis: a case-control study

Rocio Garcia

1,2

, Imma Vilalta Vidal

1,2

, Victor Martin-Gorgojo

1,2

, Alejandro Peiró García

1,2

1
SJD Barcelona Children's Hospital, Barcelona, Spain, 
2
Institut de Recerca Sant Joan de Déu (IRSJD), Barcelona, Spain
Introduction: Clinical observations in our unit have noted trunk decompensation and, in some cases, visual deviations in patients with adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). Previous studies have reported both visual anomalies, such as increased back surface abnormalities in children with visual deficiencies (Catanzariti, Jean F et al.,Spine, 2001), and vestibular anomalies, including asymmetric vestibular perception in AIS patients (Woo, Emma J et al., Front Neurol, 2019). Genetic analyses have also identified rare OTOP1 variants linked to subtle inner ear anomalies in AIS, and mouse models show that OTOP1 loss causes vestibular defects, suggesting a potential role of vestibular dysfunction in scoliosis development (Sumalde, Angelo Augusto M et al., 2023, Genes; Tian, Lifeng et al., 2022, Nat Commun). Despite these findings, no studies have simultaneously assessed visual, vestibular, and postural factors in AIS. This study aims to fill that gap by analyzing their prevalence and potential correlations compared to a control group. Material and Methods: Prospective case-control study conducted in a tertiary pediatric hospital. Demographic data, family history of scoliosis and other clinical variables were collected. The vestibular evaluation included the Fukuda-Unterberger test and the Barany test. Visual function was analyzed using the cover test, cyclotorsion retinography, crossed ocular laterality, and MAIA microcampimetry. The KINDL questionnaire was also used to assess quality of life. Results: A total of 38 patients were included in the AIS group; the sex ratio was 34:4 (girls: boys) and the mean age 14.8 ± 1.32 years old. In the control group, 33 patients were included, with a sex ratio 30:3 and mean age of 14.2 ± 1.56 years old. Significant differences were found in family history of AIS (p < 0.01) and dyslexia (p = 0.055). Regarding the vestibular system, we did not find statistically significant differences in the Fakuda-Unterberger test (p = 0.48), but we did observe almost significant results in the Barany test (p = 0.059). In the visual system assessment, we found differences in the use of glasses (55.3% of AIS patients vs. 21.2% in the control group; p = 0.003). Regarding the cyclotorsion, in the AIS more patients presented incyclotorsion (26.3% vs. 12.5%) and excyclotorsion (21.1% vs. 6.2%) of the left eye (p = 0.039). However, only significant difference were found in the right eye macular integrity with the MAIA (p = 0.04). AIS patients reported lower KINDL (p < 0.01), specifically in well-being (p = 0.01), self-esteem (p = 0.03), and family (p = 0.02) domains. Conclusion: AIS patients exhibit a higher prevalence of visual and vestibular impairments, which can affect their posture and contribute to a reduced quality of life. These findings offer new insights into AIS etiopathology and suggest potential avenues for intervention.

## ID: AGS26/1885

## P0149: Re-evaluating the role of preoperative SSEP monitoring in low-risk idiopathic scoliosis patients

Youssef Sadek

1

, Mostafa Elmaghraby

2,3

, Julia Loughran

4

, Alaa Morsi

5

, Chuck Lam

1

, Neha Narayanan

1

, Sai Bhargava Reddy Maddireddy

2

, David Marks

2

, Duggineni Kumar

2

, Morgan Jones

2

, George Mckay

2

1

University of Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom

2

Royal Orthopaedic Hospital, Birmingham, United Kingdom

3

Faculty of Medicine, Al Azhar University, Cairo, Egypt

4

Queen Elizabeth Hospital, Birmingham, United Kingdom

5

NHS Birmingham and Solihull,, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Corrective surgery for idiopathic scoliosis routinely incorporates the use of intraoperative neuromonitoring as standard practice to promptly detect and mitigate neurological deficits. Elective preoperative somatosensory-evoked potential (SSEP) testing in an outpatient setting prior to surgery is often conducted to establish baseline neurological function, which may be of particular benefit in high-risk patients. However, their value in low-risk patients with a normal MRI and clinical findings remains uncertain and may potentially introduce unnecessary costs and procedural delays. The aim of this study was therefore to evaluate the necessity of preoperative SSEP testing in low-risk idiopathic scoliosis patients, in order to optimise preoperative protocols, efficiency, and resource utilisation. Material and Methods: This single-centre retrospective cohort study analysed idiopathic scoliosis patients undergoing corrective surgery between September 2019 and September 2024. All patients underwent a clinical examination, MRI and preoperative SSEP testing in an outpatient clinic setting. During surgery, intraoperative SSEP and motor-evoked potential (MEP) monitoring were utilised for every patient. Patients with abnormal or incomplete MRI scans or missing MRI reports were excluded. Correlations between preoperative SSEP findings and postoperative neurological outcomes were evaluated. Results: Of the 556 idiopathic scoliosis patients reviewed, 280 (50.4%, mean age: 17.4 ± 3.22 years) met the inclusion criteria. Exclusions were made due to unavailable MRI scans (n = 109), missing MRI reports (n = 77), and abnormal MRI findings (n = 89). One patient lacked preoperative SSEP data and was therefore excluded. Among the 280 included patients, 260 (92.9%) demonstrated reliable elective preoperative SSEP responses, while 22 (7.9%) had unreliable readings, comprising 17 with mild sensory responses and 5 with negative responses. Despite these unreliable readings, no postoperative neurological deficits were identified in any patient. Additionally, all 280 exhibited normal MEP following anaesthetization of the patient in the anaesthetic room. Conclusion: Based on the findings of this study, routine preoperative SSEP testing may be unnecessary in low-risk idiopathic scoliosis patients with normal neurological and MRI findings. In this cohort, no postoperative deficits were observed. Additionally, potential issues may be effectively identified through intraoperative monitoring. Hence, eliminating routine elective preoperative SSEP testing in these patients could streamline preoperative protocols and reduce costs without compromising safety.

## ID: AGS26/1956

## P0150: Novel insights in congenital scoliosis from the Paediatric Hospital “Aghia Sofia”

Aris Sopilidis

1

, Platon Papageorgiou

1

, Anastasios Sinnis

1

, Ioannis Giannadakis

1

, Anastasia Pilichou

1

, Panagiotis Krallis

1

1

Aghia Sophia Pediatric General Hospital, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Congenital scoliosis is a rare but significant spinal deformity caused by anomalies in vertebral formation or segmentation during embryonic development. It affects approximately 1 in 10,000 children and presents unique management challenges due to its progressive nature and potential for associated anomalies. The primary causes include hemivertebrae (wedge-shaped vertebrae) and block vertebrae (fused vertebrae), resulting in spinal asymmetry and curvature. The severity of scoliosis is determined by the number, location, and type of vertebral anomalies, as well as their growth potential. Treatment strategies are individualized according to the progression of deformity, ranging from observation for mild and non-progressive curves to surgical interventions including in situ fusion, hemivertebra excision, or growth-friendly instrumentation for progressive or severe deformities. Early diagnosis and a multidisciplinary approach are crucial for effective management of congenital scoliosis, highlighting the importance of awareness and timely intervention in affected children. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of patients with congenital scoliosis treated at the “Aghia Sofia” Children's Hospital in Athens between 2017 and 2024. Medical records were reviewed for patient demographics, anatomical locations of anomalies, types of congenital vertebral malformations, surgical procedures performed, and clinical outcomes. All patients underwent comprehensive preoperative evaluation including radiographic assessment, MRI studies, and screening for associated anomalies. Surgical indications included progressive curves, significant cosmetic deformity, and risk of neurological compromise. Treatment strategies included posterior spinal fusion, hemivertebra excision, and growth-friendly instrumentation when appropriate. Results: Fourteen patients with complete data were identified (mean age 10.2 years, range 5.7-14.3 years) with equal gender distribution (50% female, 50% male). Hemivertebrae represented the most common anomaly (64.3%, n = 9), followed by congenital kyphosis (28.6%, n = 4). The thoracolumbar junction was the predominant location affected (92.9%, n = 13), with one case involving the isolated lumbar spine. Surgical management included posterior spinal fusion in all cases (100%), with hemivertebra excision performed in 50% of cases (n = 7). Growth-friendly instrumentation was utilized in one patient (7.1%) requiring staged management. One patient required revision surgery (7.1%) for extension of fusion. The most commonly affected levels for hemivertebrae were the thoracolumbar junction (T9-L5), with both thoracic and lumbar involvement observed. Associated congenital kyphosis was present in 28.6% of cases, predominantly at the thoracolumbar junction. No major perioperative complications were recorded, and all patients demonstrated satisfactory correction of spinal deformity at final follow-up. Conclusion: This series from a tertiary pediatric center demonstrates that congenital scoliosis predominantly affects the thoracolumbar junction, with hemivertebrae being the most common vertebral anomaly. Equal gender distribution was observed, contrasting with some literature reports of female predominance. Hemivertebra excision combined with posterior spinal fusion provided effective treatment in half of the cases, with excellent safety profiles and satisfactory deformity correction. The low revision rate and absence of major complications support the efficacy of individualized surgical management in pediatric congenital spine deformity. Early recognition and timely surgical intervention in progressive cases can achieve excellent clinical outcomes while preserving spinal growth and function. Our experience emphasizes the importance of specialized pediatric spine centers in managing these complex deformities and the need for long-term follow-up to monitor for potential complications and ensure optimal functional outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2313

## P0151: Assessment of the use of tranexamic acid in the postoperative period in patients with scoliosis undergoing posterior spinal fusion

Alexandry Carvalho

1

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

2

1

Ortomed Clinic, Teresina, Brazil

2

Faculdade de Medicina de São Paulo, USP, Ribeirão Preto, Ribeirão Preto, Brazil

Introduction: The posterior spinal fusion is the most commonly used surgical technique for the treatment of scoliosis in children and adolescents. However, the associated bleeding continues to be a challenge as intra or postoperative transfusions are linked to increased morbidity and mortality. Tranexamic acid has been employed intraoperatively in various surgeries, including scoliosis correction procedures. Therefore, the study aimed to evaluate the use of tranexamic acid in the postoperative period to reduce blood loss in patients undergoing surgical treatment for scoliosis correction. Material and Methods: A prospective, randomized, double-blind study was conducted on a cohort of 28 patients undergoing posterior spinal fusion for scoliosis treatment. The patients were divided into 2 groups based on the use or non-use of TXA (tranexamic acid) in the postoperative period: the control or SAT (without TXA) and the experimental or CAT (with TXA). Both groups received intravenous infusion of tranexamic acid at a dosage of 30 mg/kg during the surgical procedure. However, patients in the CAT group also received an additional dose of 10 mg/kg/h until removal of the suction drain, 48 hours post-procedure. The variables studied included demographic data (age, gender, weight, body mass index, and etiology of scoliosis), surgical factors (preoperative hemoglobin levels (g/dl), total surgery time (minutes), and amount of transfused blood products (ml)), and postoperative factors (total hospitalization time (days), amount of blood loss in suction drain per day (ml), total number of transfused blood bags, hemoglobin values (g/dl) on the 1st and 3rd days, and occurrence of postoperative complications). Statistical analysis was performed using R software version 4.02, and the comparison of means between groups was done using the Student’s t-test or Wilcoxon test. The Chi-square test was used for categorical variables. Results: The results did not show a statistically significant difference regarding age, gender, etiology of scoliosis, Cobb angle value of the main scoliotic curve pre and post-operation, body weight, body mass index (BMI) between the SAT and CAT groups. Preoperative hemoglobin (Hb) ranged from 10.7 g/dl to 14.7 g/dl in the SAT group and from 11.1 g/dl to 16.2 g/dl in the CAT group. The total surgery time (minutes) was slightly longer in the CAT group (mean of 220.4 min) compared to the SAT group (mean of 182.5 min). However, the comparison of the results obtained was not statistically significant (p = 0.1028). Ultimately, the number of blood bags transfused in patients in each group did not show a statistically significant result (p > 0.05). Total blood loss (ml), total drain output (ml), total hospitalization time (days), Hb value on the first and third postoperative days, and the occurrence of complications also did not show a statistically significant difference between the groups. Conclusion: The intravenous administration of TXA in the 48 hours postoperative period of posterior spinal fusion surgeries for scoliosis correction in children and adolescents did not demonstrate a significant impact in reducing intraoperative bleeding rates and the need for blood transfusions.

## ID: AGS26/2360

## P0152: Examining the relationship between sleep quality, insomnia severity, and quality of life in adolescents with idiopathic scoliosis: a pilot study

Cansu Demir

1

, Muhammet Kocaoğlu

2

, Tuğçe Yavuz

2

, Akif Albayrak

3

, Gamze Başkent

4

1

Istanbul Rumeli University, Faculty of Health Sciences, Department of Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Bahçeşehir University, Graduate School of Education, Doctoral Program in Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Central Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Istanbul Rumeli University, Faculty of Health Sciences, Department of Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Central Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

4

Istanbul Rumeli University, Faculty of Health Sciences, Department of Physical Therapy and Rehabilitation, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is a three-dimensional structural deformity of the spine. AIS, one of the most common chronic musculoskeletal problems during adolescence, is significant in terms of its effects on sleep and quality of life. Sleep is a fundamental biological process that replenishes energy stores and facilitates physical and mental rest; chronic musculoskeletal disorders can negatively affect quality of life by leading to sleep deprivation. This pilot study aims to examine the relationships between sleep quality, insomnia severity, and quality of life in individuals with AIS. Material and Methods: This study included 12 individuals diagnosed with AIS (4 females, 33.3%; 8 males, 66.7%) with a Cobb angle of 10-45° who presented to the Central Hospital. The average age of participants was 16.00 ± 2.34 years, and the average body mass index (BMI) was 18.57 ± 2.24 kg/m2. Quality of life was assessed using the Scoliosis Research Society-22 (SRS-22) questionnaire (function: SRS-1; pain: SRS-2; self-image: SRS-3; mental health: SRS-4; satisfaction with treatment: SRS-5). Insomnia severity was measured using the Insomnia Severity Index (ISI), and sleep quality was measured using the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI); the PSQI subscales are as follows: subjective sleep quality (PSQI-1), sleep latency (PSQI-2), sleep duration (PSQI-3), sleep efficiency (PSQI-4), sleep disturbance (PSQI-5), sleep medication use (PSQI-6), and daytime dysfunction (PSQI-7). Statistical analyses were performed using IBM SPSS 27.0 software. The relationships between the SRS-22, ISI, and PSQI scales were examined using bivariate correlation analysis with Spearman correlation coefficients. A significance level of p < 0.05 was accepted for all tests. Results: A strong and positive significant correlation was found between the ISI and PSQI total scores (r = 0.78; p < 0.05). At the subscale level, significant positive relationships were also found between ISI and PSQI-1 (r = 0.60), PSQI-4 (r = 0.66), and PSQI-5 (r = 0.77) (p < 0.05). On the other hand, a strong and negative significant correlation was observed between insomnia severity and the SRS total score (r = -0.82; p < 0.05). In the subscale analyses, significant negative correlations were determined between ISI and SRS-3 (r = -0.78), SRS-4 (r = -0.84), and SRS-5 (r = -0.80) (p < 0.05). Significant negative relationships were found between PSQI total and subscale scores and SRS-22 subscale and total scores (r = -0.57 to -0.75; p < 0.05). Conclusion: The findings indicate that an increase in insomnia severity is associated with a decrease in sleep quality. Specifically, as subjective sleep quality, sleep efficiency, and sleep disturbance parameters deteriorate, insomnia severity also increases. Increased insomnia severity is associated with a decline in individuals' quality of life; their self-image weakens, their mental health deteriorates, and their satisfaction with treatment decreases. Furthermore, while increases in PSQI scores in some subscales correspond to decreases in SRS scores, increases in SRS scores are associated with decreases in PSQI scores. In conclusion, increasing insomnia severity shows inverse relationships between PSQI and SRS scores, along with deteriorations in sleep quality and declines in quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/2412

## P0153: Restoration of thoracic kyphosis after AIS surgery

Ayrat Syundyukov

1

1

Federal State Budgetary Institution Federal Center of Traumatology, Orthopedics and Endoprosthesis Replacement of Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation (Cheboksary), Cheboksary, Russian Federation

Introduction: Restoration of the sagittal profile is a key task for achieving a stable result and improving the quality of life of patients with idiopathic scoliosis. Objective: To evaluate the achievement of target indicators of thoracic kyphosis in AIS surgery. Material and Methods: Retrospective analysis of AIS Lenke 1a (n = 42). Two groups of patients. Group 1: correction of deformity by standard rotation of the rod with enblock derotation. (n = 22). Group 2: correction by fixing the rod in the end screws and gradual translation on the rod. (n = 20). Lumbar lordosis, thoracic kyphosis before and after surgery and approaching the target indicators were assessed. Results: Thoracic kyphosis preop in group 1 was 14.5 [10.1-18.9], postop 14.5 [11.1-17.9], target 29 [25.4-32.6]. In the second group, respectively, preop 19 [14.1-23.9], postop 27.5 [25.5-29.5], target 31.5 [28.9-34.1]. Linear correlation showed that in the surgery performed in group 1, the thoracic kyphosis angle did not approach the target, while in group 2, the kyphosis angle indicators approached them. (Group 1 R2 8.418e-006; Y = 0.003034*X+30.46; p = 0.9898. Group 2 R2 0.5764; Y=0.5846*X+7.787; p = 0.0001). Conclusion: The use of some non-standard corrective maneuvers made it possible to bring the sagittal profile parameters closer to the target ones.

## ID: AGS26/2484

## P0154: Impact of curve severity and location on self-image and mental health in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Panapol Varakornpipat

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Faculty of Medicine, Naresuan University, Thapho, Thailand, Phitsanulok, Thailand

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is a three-dimensional spinal deformity that can negatively affect physical and psychosocial well-being. The degree of spinal curvature, measured by the Cobb angle, may be associated with patient-reported quality of life. This study aimed to investigate the relationship between Cobb angle and Scoliosis Research Society-22 (SRS-22) questionnaire domains in non-surgical AIS patients. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional study was conducted among 120 AIS patients aged 10-18 years who had not undergone surgical treatment. Cobb angles were measured from standing posteroanterior spine radiographs by two independent spine specialists. Each patient completed the validated Thai version of the SRS-22 questionnaire, which assesses five domains: Function, Pain, Self-image, Mental Health, and Satisfaction. Pearson’s correlation coefficients were used to examine the relationship between Cobb angle and SRS-22 domain scores. Results: The mean age of participants was 14.6 ± 2.1 years, with a mean Cobb angle of 34.2° ± 10.8°. The mean overall SRS-22 score was 3.8 ± 0.6. Cobb angle showed a significant negative correlation with the Self-image domain (r = -0.48, p < 0.001) and the Mental Health domain (r = -0.31, p = 0.002). Subgroup analysis revealed that patients with proximal thoracic curves reported the lowest scores in Self-image and Satisfaction (mean 3.2 ± 0.5), followed by those with thoracolumbar curves (mean 3.5 ± 0.6). Patients with main thoracic curves reported comparatively higher Self-image scores (mean 3.9 ± 0.5), indicating the least impact on appearance. No significant correlation was observed between Cobb angle and Function (r = -0.12, p = 0.18) or Pain (r = -0.09, p = 0.26). Conclusion: Greater curve severity in AIS, as measured by Cobb angle, is associated with poorer self-image and mental health. Curve location also influences patient perception of appearance: proximal thoracic curves were associated with the greatest dissatisfaction, thoracolumbar curves with moderate dissatisfaction, and main thoracic curves with the least impact. These findings emphasize the importance of considering both radiographic severity and curve pattern when evaluating quality of life and counseling AIS patients.

## ID: AGS26/2645

## P0155: Precision without navigation: an innovative, squaring vertebra technique for safe pedicle screw instrumentation in scoliosis

Sarwar Jahan

1

, Shah Shah Alam

2

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopedic Rehabilitation (NITOR), Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: The placement of pedicle screws in scoliosis correction relies heavily on either fluoroscopic navigation or complex intraoperative 3D navigation systems to mitigate the risks associated with altered spinal anatomy. While effective, these technologies present significant barriers, including high cost, increased radiation exposure, procedural complexity, and limited availability in resource-constrained settings. This study presents and evaluates the outcomes of a novel, structured technique designed to achieve a high degree of precision and safety in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) without the use of any intraoperative navigation. Material and Methods: A prospective cohort study was conducted on 55 consecutive AIS patients who underwent posterior spinal fusion using the novel non-navigated technique. The technique is based on a meticulous step-by-step protocol utilizing consistent anatomical landmarks, specific entry points tailored to the deformed vertebrae, and a unique “graded probing” tactile feedback system to navigate dysmorphic pedicles. All screws were inserted using C-arm by making the each vertebra squared method. Postoperative precision was assessed objectively using a low-dose CT scan, with screw positions graded according to the established Gertzbein-Robbins classification system (Grade 0: within pedicle; Grade 3: >6mm breach). Major breaches (≥ Grade 2, >2mm) were considered clinically significant. Operative time, estimated blood loss, and any intraoperative or postoperative complications were also recorded. Results: On an average 16 pedicle screws were placed across each patients. Postoperative CT analysis revealed a High Percentage about 95.8% acceptable placement rate (Gertzbein-Robbins Grade 0 or 1). The rate of clinically significant breaches (Grade 2 or 3) was very Low Percentage about 2.1%, with no breaches resulting in neurological or vascular complications. The mean operative time was 240 minutes, and mean estimated blood loss was 150 ml. Conclusion: This innovative, structured C-arm based squaring vertebra technique demonstrates that a high degree of precision and safety in pedicle screw instrumentation for scoliosis surgery can be achieved reliably without navigation. The technique offers a viable, cost-effective alternative to navigated systems, potentially democratizing access to high-quality scoliosis care. Its reproducibility suggests it could serve as a valuable standard, particularly in settings where advanced technology is not available. Further comparative studies are warranted to validate these findings against current navigated standards.
Keywords: Scoliosis Surgery, Non-navigated, Cost-Effective, Squaring Vertebra.

## ID: AGS26/2667

## P0156: Halo-pelvic distraction as a bridge to definitive surgery in patients with severe kypho-scoliosis: experience from a lowresource setting

Jigi Naol

1

, Yohannis Kebede

2

, Stefano Bolongaro

2

, Timothy Nunn

2

, Scott Nelson

3

, Wai Yoon

4

, Federico Canavese

5

1

Soddo Christian Hospital, Soddo, Ethiopia

2

Cure International Hospital, Addis Ababa, Ethiopia

3

Loma Linda University, Loma Linda, United States

4
Queens Medical Centre, University Hospitals of Leicester NHS Trust, Nottingham, United Kingdom, 
5
University of Genoa, Genoa, Italy
Introduction: Halo-pelvic distraction (HPD) is a potential strategy for progressively reducing the magnitude of curves in patients with severe kypho-scoliosis (KS) and for optimizing care for patients waiting for definitive surgery. This study evaluates the effectiveness of HPD as a bridging intervention for patients with severe KS in environments with limited resources. Methods: A prospective study was conducted on 15 consecutive patients with severe, progressive KS who underwent preoperative HPD between 01/2024 and 08/2025. Cobb angle (CA), thoracic kyphosis (ThK), and T1-T12 height, were measured from standardized pre- and post-distraction radiographs. Clinical parameters, including weight and complications, were recorded. Paired t-tests were used for statistical analysis. Results: The study included 11 females and 4 males with severe, rigid KS and a mean age of 10 years 10 months (range, 9 - 14). Over a mean of 7 weeks (range, 37 - 79 days) of HPD, the mean thoracic CA decreased by 37% (from 97° to 61°, p < 0.001) and ThK decreased by 34% (from 68° to 45°, p < 0.001). The T1-T12 height increased by an average of 5.0 cm (range 2.1 - 7.9). During HPD patients gained an average of 3 kg (range 1.5 - 7.5 kg), indicating improved nutritional status. Complications occurred in 3 out of 15 patients (20%): one case of transient sixth cranial nerve palsy and two pin site infections, all of which resolved conservatively. All patients were able to mobilize and care for themselves independently. Following HPD, 9 patients underwent posterior spinal fusion, and 6 underwent a growing rod construct. Conclusion: HPD appears to be an effective bridging strategy because it reduces curve magnitude and optimizes care for patients with severe KS who are awaiting definitive surgery. Additionally, the technique carries a low risk of complications, making it appealing in settings with limited resources.
Keywords: severe scoliosis, halo-pelvic distraction, delayed surgical care, resource limited settings, bridging therapy spinal deformity, waiting list management.

## ID: AGS26/2710

## P0157: Scoliosis development after neurosurgical surgery for chiari malformation type I or tethered cord syndrome: a retrospective study

Maritha Kumlin

1

1

Karolinska Institutet, Department of Clinical Science, Intervention and Technology, Karolinska University Hospital, Stockholm, Sweden

Introduction: The effectiveness of neurosurgical intervention to prevent scoliosis curve progression in patients with Chiari Malformation Type I (CM I) or tethered cord has been unclear. This study aims to increase knowledge of scoliosis development after neurosurgical intervention for CM I and tethered cord syndrome, with or without syringomyelia. Material and Methods: Retrospective single-center cohort study of 240 consecutive patients aged from 1 to 17 years, who underwent neurosurgery from 2014 and 2024. The main outcome was progress > 6 degrees, de novo scoliosis or the need for spinal fusion. Wilcoxon rank sum test was used to evaluate differences between treatments. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: Two-hundred forty patients were identified, 57.5% were girls. The mean age at neurosurgery was 7.1 years. Seventy-seven patients were decompressed for CM I (32.1%) and 162 patients (67.5%) underwent detethering. Out of 240 patients, 10 (4.2%) had both CM I and tethered cord. Syringomyelia was presented in 71 patients (29.7%). Scoliosis was present in 58 patients (24.2%) prior to neurosurgical intervention, the majority (62.7%) being girls with a mean age of 10 years. Mean Cobb angle was 34.50 (10-870). After neurosurgical intervention, mean Cobb angle was 33.70 (4-700), (p = 0.937). Seven patients developed de novo scoliosis (2.9%) with a majority being boys (57.1%), where mean Cobb angle at follow-up was 16.10 (6-380). Seventeen patients (7.1%) underwent posterior spinal surgery after neurosurgery where 52.7% were girls. Mean Cobb prior neurosurgery was 49.80 (24-870) and reduced to 40.90 (16-700) after neurosurgery, however not significantly (p = 0.201). Conclusion: Our results indicate that scoliosis progression and de novo scoliosis after prophylactic neurosurgical intervention for CM 1 and tethered cord syndrome are prevalent. Prospective studies are warranted to further elucidate the prophylactic effect of neurosurgical intervention on scoliosis development.
