## P0158: Impact of scoliosis surgery on breast asymmetry and trunk aesthetics in female patients

Gauthier Lejeune

1

, Sofía Frank

1

, Julie Joncas

1

, Soraya Barchi

1

, Brassard Felix

1

, Jean-Marc Mac-Thiong

1,2

, Stefan Parent

1

1

CHU Sainte-Justine, Montreal, Canada

2

Hôpital Sacré-Coeur, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: Surgical correction of Idiopathic Scoliosis affects body image, and there is growing evidence linking physical appearance to psychological well-being. The Truncal Anterior Asymmetry Scoliosis Questionnaire (TAASQ) was developed to document female patients’ perceptions of their anterior trunk. This study aims to determine whether scoliosis surgery improves the appearance of the breasts and trunk asymmetry, as assessed by TAASQ, and identify factors that predict postoperative outcomes. We hypothesized that Surgical correction of scoliosis in female patients improves breasts symmetry and anterior trunk. Specific factors are predictive of satisfaction. Material and Methods: TAASQ responses from female IS patients who underwent surgery were reviewed. Mean Curve type, angular value, and hump measurements were analyzed. Mean pre-operative and 2 years post-operative differences in domain-specific and overall TAASQ scores were assessed using t-tests. Results: We reviewed the records of 218 patients, 148 of whom completed the TAASQ. The mean age at surgery was 15.2 ± 2.2 years. Mean curve values were 51° ± 15.1° (main thoracic), 49.2° ± 14.5° (thoracolumbar), and 35.1° ± 19.9° (proximal thoracic). The preoperative thoracic hump averaged 12.3° ± 5.5°, which improved to 6.5° ± 4.3° postoperatively, with a statistically significant difference. Preoperative overall TAASQ scores averaged 3.64 ± 0.68, rising to 4.13 ± 0.71 postoperatively (p = 0.00). Overall score being the sum of main domains divide by their number (3). Statistically significant improvements were observed across all TAASQ domains. Conclusion: Surgical correction of scoliosis significantly improves the appearance of the breasts and anterior trunk in female IS patients, as assessed by the TAASQ. Pre- and postoperative factors can predict the likelihood of positive or negative outcomes, aiding in patient expectation management.

## P0159: Assessing the role of large language models in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis care: a comparison between ChatGPT and Google Gemini

Semih Yaş

1

, Dilek Yapar

2

, Aliekber Yapar

3

, Tayfun Ozel

4

, Mehmet Tokgoz

5

, Alim Baymurat

5

, Alpaslan Senkoylu

5

1

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Turkish Ministry of Health, Dr. Abdurrahman Yurtaslan Ankara Oncology Training and Research Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

2

Department of Public Health, Turkish Ministry of Health, Muratpasa District Health Directorate, Antalya, Turkey

3

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Antalya Training and Research Hospital, Antalya, Turkey

4

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Ankara Training and Research Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

5

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Gazi University Faculty of Medicine, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: The objective of this study is to evaluate the accuracy, applicability, comprehensiveness, and communication quality of responses generated by ChatGPT® and Google Gemini® in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS)-related scenarios, with the aim of assessing their potential utility as tools in patient management. Material and Methods: Six case-based questions reflecting common patient concerns related to adolescent idiopathic scoliosis were developed by orthopedic specialists. Responses generated by ChatGPT and Google Gemini were independently evaluated by 61 orthopedic surgeons using a standardized rubric assessing accuracy, applicability, comprehensiveness, and communication clarity, each rated on a 1-5 Likert scale. Comparative analyses between platforms were performed using the Mann-Whitney U and Wilcoxon Signed Rank tests. Additionally, open-ended feedback was collected to explore participants’ perspectives on the potential and limitations of AI-based consultations. Results: ChatGPT outperformed Google Gemini in terms of accuracy (p = 0.013) in post-operative care scenarios. The results for applicability (p = 0.119), comprehensiveness (p = 0.619), and communication (p = 0.240) were not statistically significant. Orthopedic specialists rated both AI models significantly higher than residents in accuracy, applicability, and comprehensiveness. Most evaluators acknowledged the potential of AI to reduce physician workload and support patient guidance; however, concerns were raised regarding reliability, ethical implications, and the current limitations of AI in ensuring patient safety. Conclusion: ChatGPT® and Google Gemini® demonstrated moderate accuracy and communication quality in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis-related scenarios, with ChatGPT showing a modest advantage. Although both models show promising results as supportive tools for patient education and preliminary consultations, their current limitations in accuracy and comprehensiveness restrict their clinical reliability. Multidisciplinary collaboration is crucial to ensure effective applications of AI in orthopedic practice.

## ID: AGS26/2797

## P0160: Cardiopulmonary consequences of scoliosis and the role of VO2 max in surgical recovery: a systematic review

Suhani Sharma

1

, Aura Ilovan

1

, Arun Ramnarine

1

, Karim Fouad

1

, Samuel Kirby

1

, Shreya Sankar

1

, Michael O'Connor

1

, George-Paul O'Byrne

2

1
Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland, Dublin, Ireland, 
2
University Hospital Limerick, Limerick, Ireland
Introduction: Scoliosis alters thoracic anatomy, potentially impairing respiratory and exercise physiology. While pulmonary function tests (PFTs) are commonly assessed, they may not fully reflect the physiological burden of deformity. A range of parameters, including VO2 max, ventilatory efficiency, lung volumes, and submaximal exercise tolerance, have been studied, but their trajectories with scoliosis progression remain unclear. Materials and Methods: We systematically searched PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane for studies reporting cardiopulmonary outcomes in paediatric scoliosis. Eligible studies included patients ≤ 18 years assessed with CPET, PFTs, or functional exercise testing. Outcomes extracted included VO2 max, lung volumes, flow measures, and exercise tolerance. Due to heterogeneity in methods and outcomes, results were synthesised narratively following PRISMA guidance. Results: Across cohorts of the included studies, scoliosis severity correlated with declines in multiple functional domains. VO2 max was reduced to as low as 28-34% of predicted despite preserved spirometry, with congenital scoliosis showing an inverse relationship between thoracic kyphosis and VO2 max. Lung volumes and flows declined incrementally with curve progression, with meta-regression showing ∼1% reduction in pulmonary function for every 2.6-2.9° increase in Cobb angle. Functional tests such as the six-minute walk correlated strongly with CPET-derived VO2 max (r = 0.431, p < 0.001). Adolescents with scoliosis also demonstrated higher oxygen cost and lower ventilatory reserve during exercise compared with controls. Long-term follow-up after posterior spinal fusion revealed little evidence of sustained pulmonary recovery. Conclusion: A spectrum of cardiopulmonary parameters deteriorates with scoliosis progression. VO2 max may provide the most integrative marker of physiological burden, but prospective longitudinal data are required to establish its role in recovery after surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2818

## P0161: Impact of surgical waiting time on scoliosis severity and postoperative outcomes

Antonio Ramírez

1

, Jorge Cuellar

1

, Mauricio Campos Daziano

1

, Hugo Demandes

1

, Francisco Fernandez Schlein

1

, Benjamín Muñoz

1

1

Orthopaedic Surgery Department, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: The GES (Explicit Health Guarantees) system in Chile mandates timely treatment for scoliosis within 270 days; if unmet, patients must be referred to a secondary provider. Despite this coordinated approach, the public healthcare system-responsible for over three-quarters of the population-faces resource constraints, resulting in persistent waiting lists. In practice, there are often significant delays between GES activation and surgical intervention. Limited data exist on the impact of these delays on scoliosis severity and postoperative complications, and their clinical consequences have not yet been evaluated in our setting. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective observational study of patients who underwent surgery between 2016 and 2025 after being referred to our center as a secondary provider under FONASA (Chile’s national public health insurance). Demographic data, notification and surgery dates, perioperative details, and complications were recorded. Preoperative and postoperative radiographic data were also analyzed. Results: A total of 203 patients were included (74.9% female), with a median age of 15 years (range: 4-26). The median waiting time was 413 days, with a maximum of 4,533 days (∼12 years). The median preoperative curve magnitude was 56.2° (range: 8.0°-141.3°), with 14.3% of patients presenting with curves > 80°. Patients with curves > 80° experienced significantly longer waiting times (546 vs. 411 days; p = 0.0431). Male patients also had longer delays (551 vs. 401.5 days; p = 0.0118). The overall rate of unplanned reoperations was 8.8%, with no significant difference between those who waited longer than the median (p = 0.0629). Conclusion: Prolonged waiting times were associated with more severe spinal deformities, especially in patients with curves exceeding 80°. This may reflect either disease progression during the delay or greater barriers to surgical access for complex cases. Although complications such as reoperations did not significantly differ based on wait time, the trend suggests a potential clinical impact. These findings highlight that delayed surgical access under the GES framework may contribute to deformity progression. Reducing waiting times could improve clinical outcomes and reduce surgical complexity.

## P0162: Giant neurofibroma associated with severe kyphoscoliosis in a type I NF-I adolescent patient, surgical management: case report

Mario Herrera Méndez

1,2,3

, Madeline Bilbao

2

, Herman Freund

2,4

, Francisco Linares

3,4

1

Instituto Ortopedia Infantil Roosevelt, Bogota, Colombia

2

Clinica Universitaria Colombia, Fundación Universitaria Sanitas, Bogota, Colombia

3
Hospital San Ignacio, Bogota, Colombia, 
4
Clinica Reina Sofia, Bogota, Colombia
Introduction: Neurofibromatosis can be classified as type I (NF-I) and type II according to its clinical. Type I is associated with severe Kyphoscoliosis, which severe deformities, wedging, rotation, vertebral subluxation, penciling of ribs, enlargement of nerve root canal and pedicle defects as well of gigantic neurofibromas is a very challenging for the surgical spine team. Treatment involves a multidisciplinary team, staged surgeries with high risk neurological complications. Material and Methods: Case report in a fifteen-year old male, diagnosed with NF-I with a giant intramedullary neurofibroma associated with severe kyphoscoliosis in two-stage surgery. First stage surgery, previous surgery, selective embolectomy was performed to reduce bleeding. Careful dissection and posterior removal of the giant neurofibroma, transpedicular screw placement and an internal distraction rod. Second-stage kyphoscoliosis definitive correction via type 2 osteotomies. Posterior management of a postoperative deep wound infection with drainage, removal of bone grafts, and negative pressure systems were used to control E. Coli infection. IV antibiotic therapy was given and a cervicothoracic orthosis was used for protection. Results: A two-stage surgical procedure for the giant neurofibroma associated with kyphoscoliosis was performed. No neurological deficit was found in either procedure. Despite deep wound infection, removal of bone graft, adequate arthrodesis was achieved after 6 months and no recurrence of the neurofibroma. Conclusion: Severe spine deformities such as kyphoscoliosis in NF-I is still a challenge for spine surgeons. Giant neurofibromas can make it even difficult to approach, the use of embolization, temporary distraction rod and spine osteotomies are a safe way to achieve greater correction an minimize neurological impairment.

## ID: AGS26/2904

## P0163: The role of COL11A2 in pathogenesis and diagnosis of scoliosis: case report

Alim Baymurat

1

, Mehmet Ali Ergün

2

, Ali Eren

3

, Yusuf Bahap

2

, Alpaslan Senkoylu

1

1

Orthopedic Surgery Department

2

Medical Genetics Department, Gazi University, Ankara, Turkey

3

Orthopedic Surgery Department, Karadeniz Technical University, Trabzon, Turkey

Introduction: Scoliosis is a complex spinal deformity with multifactorial etiology involving genetic, environmental, and biomechanical factors. Increasing evidence highlights the role of collagen-related genes, particularly COL11A2, which encodes type XI collagen, crucial for extracellular matrix integrity. Mutations in COL11A2 have been implicated in skeletal dysplasias and may contribute to scoliosis pathogenesis. This study aimed to investigate potential genetic variants in two pairs of twins from different families who underwent different treatment strategies. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included two families, each consisting of twin sisters with scoliosis. Clinical diagnoses were established through physical and radiological examination. In Family 1, both twins underwent posterior instrumented spinal fusion. In Family 2, one twin underwent surgery, while the other was treated conservatively with a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO). All participants were subsequently referred for genetic evaluation. Genomic DNA was extracted from peripheral venous blood samples and sequenced using Twist Exome 2.0 (Twist Bioscience, USA) kits on a NextSeq1500 platform (Ilumina, USA). Reads were processed with Ilyome (Ilyome, Turkey), aligned to GRCh38, and variants called to VCF files. Variant classification followed ACMG and ACGS guidelines. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants. Results: In Family 1, both affected twin sisters were identified to carry a heterozygous COL11A2:c.4705C>G variant, classified as a variant of uncertain significance (VUS) with criteria PM2 and PP3. Both underwent posterior spinal fusion for scoliosis. In Family 2, two affected twin sisters carried a heterozygous COL11A2:c.2464A>T variant, also classified as a VUS (PM2). One twin underwent surgery, while the other was managed conservatively. No other pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants relevant to scoliosis were detected. In both families, the variants were consistently present in affected individuals. Conclusion:
COL11A2 is a gene associated with the extracellular matrix and plays a physiological role in spine formation. Besides the multifactorial and polygenic background of scoliosis, previous studies have highlighted that musculoskeletal collagen genes, such as COL11A2, occupy a critical place within this polygenic framework. In line with the literature reporting that rare variants in COL11A2 may be implicated in the etiology of scoliosis, our study identified rare variants in this gene in four affected individuals from two unrelated families. These results may suggest the potential value of larger-scale genetic studies to clarify the role of collagen-related genes in the complex molecular architecture of scoliosis. Integration of genetic screening into clinical practice may enable earlier diagnosis, risk stratification, and personalized management strategies for scoliosis patients.

## ID: AGS26/2946

## P0164: Determining the lowest instrumented vertebra (LIV) in selective thoracic fusion: a comparative analysis of radiographic criteria to prevent coronal decompensation

Grouz Khalil

1

, Ahmed Mohsni

1

, Mohamed Zairi

1

1

Hopital d'Enfant Bechir Hamza De Tunis, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Selective thoracic fusion for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) aims to correct the main curve while preserving the maximum number of lumbar mobile segments. The primary challenge lies in selecting the lowest instrumented vertebra (LIV) to avoid postoperative coronal decompensation. This study evaluates the impact of LIV selection based on different radiographic criteria on mid-term coronal balance. Materials and Methods: This multicenter retrospective study included 150 patients with Lenke 1 AIS who underwent selective thoracic fusion with a minimum 3-year follow-up. Patients were divided into two groups based on the LIV selection criterion: Group A (n = 78), where the LIV was the stable vertebra, and Group B (n = 72), where the LIV was the end vertebra. Analysis focused on curve correction, spontaneous lumbar curve correction, and coronal balance (C7-CSVL distance). Coronal decompensation was defined as a C7-CSVL distance > 2 cm. Results: Thoracic curve correction was comparable between the two groups (68% vs 70%, p = 0.67). Spontaneous lumbar curve correction was also similar. However, the rate of coronal decompensation at 3 years was significantly lower in Group A (LIV = Stable Vertebra) than in Group B (LIV = End Vertebra): 3.8% versus 19.4% (p = 0.002). An average of 0.8 fewer lumbar levels were instrumented in Group B, but this difference did not have a significant impact on SRS-22 functional scores (p = 0.31). Conclusion: In selective thoracic fusion for Lenke 1 AIS, selecting the stable vertebra as the lowest instrumented vertebra drastically reduces the risk of coronal decompensation at mid-term follow-up, without compromising overall functional outcomes. Although this strategy may involve extending the fusion by one level, it ensures better postoperative alignment and should be considered the gold standard to minimize balance-related complications.

## ID: AGS26/2988

## P0165: Comprehensive review of surgical kyphectomy techniques for gibbus deformity in myelomeningocele: a systematic review

Izzet Akosman

1

, Sree Vemu

1

, Austin Nguyen

1

, Connor Roper

2

1
Houston Methodist Hospital, Houston, United States, 
2
Texas A&M University Naresh K. Vashisht College of Medicine, Bryan, United States
Introduction: Gibbus deformity describes a significantly pronounced structural kyphosis of the spine resulting from the anterior collapse of vertebrae. While the indication for surgery is typically kyphosis > 70°, in severe cases of myelomeningocele, the apex of deformity may reach nearly 180°. Due to the severity of curvature and poor prognosis without surgery, multiple techniques have been proposed in the literature to tackle extreme curve correction in young children and adolescents. The goal of this systematic review is to describe various kyphectomy techniques, including vertebral column fixation, pelvic fixation, and growth-friendly construct implementation. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was performed on studies reporting surgical details in the treatment of Gibbus deformity (Cobb > 70°) with kyphectomy and instrumentation in patients with myelomeningocele. Surgical techniques were categorized as vertebral column fixation, pelvic fixation, or growth-friendly constructs. Studies reporting only cases with traditional methods, including posterior instrumentation with pedicle screws, sublaminar wires, hooks, and sacroiliac screws, were excluded. Indications, clinical reasoning, and notable surgical details associated with each method were summarized. Additional data on the number of study participants, the ages of patients, and the country of origin were collected. Results: A total of 37 studies meeting the inclusion criteria were identified, representing 18 countries. Patients’ age ranged between 23 months and 16 years and study size ranged from 1 to 59 patients. Fifteen studies reported alternative vertebral column fixation techniques. These included combined anterior + posterior fixation (3 studies), titanium cage placement (2), lag screw fixation (2), plating vertebra with ACDF plates (2) or dynamic compression plates (1), Illizarov constructs (1), Steinmann nails (1), and tibial strut grafting (1). Alternative pelvic fixation methods were described in 17 studies. The Warner-Fackler method (curved rod passed anteriorly through the S1 foramen) was most common (11 studies), followed by the Dunn-McCarthy technique (curved rod over the sacral ala; 6), Luque-Galveston technique (curved rod impacted laterally into the iliac wings; 3), and trans-lumbosacral rod impaction (2). Mayfield technique (double square-holed hooks placed on sacral ala), Roger-Jackson intrasacral distal rod insertion, and use of an “ACM plate” (curved hook impacted through three vertebral bodies and secured proximally with pedicle screws) were each described in 1 study. Eleven studies focused on growth-preserving fixation strategies for skeletally immature patients. Sliding growing rods with pedicle screws were the most common approach, featured in 9 studies. Dual rib-to-iliac growing rods were used in 2 studies. Both the Luque trolley system and magnetically controlled growing rods were described in a single study each. Conclusion: Patients with Gibbus deformity present multiple surgical challenges with respect to methods of construct fixation and stabilization in the setting of severe kyphosis, particularly in skeletally immature patients. No standard of care has been established, and many surgeons have adopted novel and alternative techniques in the setting of extreme curve correction. This review provides a comprehensive summary of surgical kyphectomy techniques for Gibbus deformity in myelomeningocele.

## ID: AGS26/2999

## P0166: Building a VBT team & performing the first case

Han Sim Lim

1

1

Penang General Hospital, Georgetown, Penang, Malaysia

Introduction: Vertebral body tethering (VBT) offers a non-fusion, motion-preserving surgical option for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS). While widely practiced in select international centers, VBT had not been established in Malaysia due to regulatory, institutional, and team-related challenges. This study describes the stepwise journey of developing a local VBT program, adapting a surgeon-led model, and successfully performing the country’s first VBT case. Material and Methods: Following fellowship training and exposure to mature VBT teams abroad, a systematic strategy was undertaken to build local capacity. Steps included regulatory approval, collaboration with implant providers, participation in regional training (Singapore National University Hospital), and structured team education using simulations and sawbone models. A streamlined workflow was adapted for Malaysia, emphasizing a spine-surgeon-led approach without dedicated thoracic or vascular surgeons. The first AIS patient was carefully selected (Lenke 1, Risser 0, flexible curve). Surgical planning, intraoperative workflow, and postoperative protocols were standardized. Results: The inaugural case was performed on November 30, 2024. Key intraoperative strategies included thoracoscopic access, controlled tether tensioning, and optimized fluoroscopy use. Postoperative recovery was uneventful, with early mobilization and chest tube removal by day 2. At 6 months follow-up, correction was maintained (40° to 16°). To date, 11 VBT cases have been successfully completed nationwide, including 2 double-sided and 1 hybrid VBT, supported by a traveling multidisciplinary team. Conclusion: The successful establishment of Malaysia’s first VBT program demonstrates that with structured planning, targeted training, and adaptive team models, it is feasible to overcome regulatory and institutional barriers. A surgeon-led approach provides a practical framework for centers without thoracic or vascular surgery support. This milestone paves the way for broader regional adoption of motion-preserving scoliosis surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3024

## P0167: S1 vertebral bone quality score independently predicts proximal junctional kyphosis after posterior hemivertebra resection with transpedicular instrumentation

Youwei Ai

1

, Hong Ding

1

, Yongdi Wang

1

, Ce Zhu

1

, Limin Liu

1

, Yueming Song

1

1

West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China

Introduction: Proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) is a prevalent postoperative complication after posterior hemivertebra (HV) resection. Previous research has demonstrated a strong correlation between the development of PJK and reduced bone mineral density. The vertebral bone quality (VBQ) score, derived from magnetic resonance imaging, is considered a predictive and evaluative tool for bone mineral density. However, obtaining accurate magnetic resonance imaging signal intensity at the L1-L4 vertebrae is challenging in patients with HV. Consequently, a novel S1 VBQ score has been proposed, which exhibits high accuracy. This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy of S1 VBQ scores in predicting the incidence of PJK in these patients. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of patients diagnosed with HV who underwent posterior HV resection with transpedicular instrumentation at our department from 2010 to 2020. Comprehensive demographic and radiographic data were collected. To identify potential preoperative risk factors for the incidence of PJK, we utilized the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator method. Subsequently, a multivariate logistic regression model was constructed to evaluate the risk factors identified through the least absolute shrinkage and selection operator analysis. Cutoff values were determined through receiver operating characteristic analysis to assess the predictive value of the S1 VBQ score for PJK. Results: A total of 88 patients met the inclusion criteria, with 14 patients (15.9%) developing PJK. Five potential risk factors were selected, including S1 VBQ scores, Risser sign, thoracic kyphosis, pelvic tilt, and proximal junctional angle. The multivariate logistic regression model demonstrated that the S1 VBQ score is an independent risk factor for predicting PJK. The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (area under the curve) for S1 VBQ scores was 0.770 with an optimal threshold of 2.793 (sensitivity: 78.6%, specificity: 67.6%). Conclusion: A higher S1 VBQ score is an independent risk factor for PJK, with a diagnostic accuracy of 77%. The S1 VBQ threshold of 2.793 was found to be effective in identifying PJK.

## ID: AGS26/3060

## P0168: Staged correction of severe kyphosis using modified halopelvic Ilizarov assembly and definitive surgery

Muhammad Saad Ilyas

1

, Uruj Zehra

2

, Amer Aziz

1

1
Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan, 
2
University of Health Sciences, Lahore, Pakistan
Introduction: Severe kyphotic deformities of spine pose significant surgical and functional challenges to orthopedic surgeons due to compromised pulmonary function, neurological risk, and technical difficulty during surgery. The halopelvic device, though historically underutilized, offers a powerful means of gradual, controlled preoperative correction by applying sustained distraction forces across the deformity. While we have previously reported successful outcomes in patients with kyphoscoliosis managed with our modified Ilizarov halopelvic distraction device presurgical, limited evidence exists regarding its role in isolated severe kyphotic deformities. The current study presents series of our patients with severe thoracic kyphosis who underwent corrections prior to definitive surgery through our modified Ilizarov halopelvic assembly demonstrating significant radiological and functional improvements. Material and Methods: Patients with severe thoracic kyphosis were managed preoperatively using a modified halopelvic Ilizarov distraction assembly. Detailed clinical evaluation, including pulmonary function testing, was performed, and full-length scoliosis series radiographs were obtained to assess thoracic kyphosis along with spinopelvic parameters prior to assembly application and during two-year follow-up. The modified Ilizarov construct consisted of a pelvic component and a halo ring, with distraction applied at a rate of 2-3 mm/day over 6-12 weeks. Radiographic measurements of kyphotic angle and spinopelvic parameters were compared before distraction, after distraction, and following definitive surgical correction. Results: Total of 30 patients with average age & BMI of 14.3 ± 8.5 years & 20.5 ± 8.5kg/m2 were included in the study after informed consent and ethical approval of the institution. Mean ± SD of kyphosis was 97.7 ± 25.4. Correction obtained through modified halopelvic assembly before surgery was almost 32% (p = 0.001) with improvement in height (p = 0.003). Apical vertebral translation was also improved significantly (p < 0.05). Further improvement in all the parameters was obtained after definitive surgery with improvement in FEV1 & FVC. Conclusion: The modified halopelvic assembly provided significant preoperative correction of severe kyphosis, improving both sagittal alignment and patient height, with additional enhancement of apical vertebral translation. Definitive surgery further augmented these corrections and was associated with measurable improvement in pulmonary function (FEV1 and FVC). These findings highlight the effectiveness of a staged approach using halopelvic distraction followed by surgery in achieving substantial deformity correction and functional gains in patients with severe kyphotic deformities.

## ID: AGS26/638

## P0169: Posterior angular correction method with limited apical fusion and fusionless posterolateral vertebral tethering can reduce the number of levels fused and preserve lumbar range of motion in posterior pediatric scoliosis surgery

Saechin Kim

1

1

Massachusetts General Hospital/Mass General Brigham, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Harvard Medical School, Boston, United States

Introduction: In standard posterior pediatric scoliosis surgery, spinal fusion is usually done with fixation placed above, through and below the structural curve(s) resulting in the fusion of disc levels outside of the structural curves. i.e., non-structural disc levels (nSDL), that are flexible and often correct completely with side bending (SB). A posterior angular correction (PAC) surgical method with limited apical fusion and fusionless posterolateral vertebral tethering (PLVT) of nSDL can reduce the number of levels fused and save range of motion (ROM), especially in the lumbar spine. Materials and Methods: The PAC method is based on the observation that posterolateral compression of rods placed transversely into the pedicle screw tulips of the same vertebra in adjoining vertebrae can change the adjoining disc angle. Tulips of a dual tulip iliac connector line up longitudinally when the connector is placed transversely in the pedicle screw tulips of the same vertebra and capture the angular correction for fusion. The angular correction can also be captured with polyethylene terephthalate or sublaminar bands placed through the closed heads of iliac connectors placed transversely for PLVT. After IRB approval, we prospectively collected the results for the first 31 patients treated using the PAC method (avg 42.3 mo., min. 36). The number of levels fused for each patient is compared to matched historical standard posterior fusion control (MHC). Radiographic measurements from 3-mo. and final f/u assess for adding on phenomenon (AO) and SB xrays at pre-op and at f/u determine the T12L5 SBROM. SRS 22r was scored pre-op and at each f/u. Statistical analysis done with the t-test. Results: Three patients were lost to follow up, leaving 28 patients (2 Lenke 1A, 1 1B, 3 1C, 4 2A, 1 2B, 2 2C, 3 3C, 2 4C, 6 5C and 4 6C curves). Avg age was 15.9 +/- 2.3 yrs, pre-op main curve was 56o+/-10o, and post-op main curve was 19.9o+/-12o for avg correction of 64%. The number of levels fused (avg 5.1+/-1.9) was less than in the MHC (avg 11.1+/-1.9) (p<.0001). Pre-op (32.5 o +/- 11.6 o) and post-op (36.8 o +/- 13.4 o) SB ROM were similar (p=.25). Lowest fused vertebra (LFV) was usually the superior vertebra of the nSDL as determined by SB xrays. Lowest instrumented vertebra (LIV) was generally the last substantially touched vertebra by the central sacral vertical line and the disc levels between LFV and LIV were treated with fusionless PLVT. There were two complications. In one, bilateral pedicle screws at the LFV were found to be broken with intact screws below. At 4-year f/u, the deformity correction did not change significantly, and revision surgery has been deferred. In the second, AO was seen when LFV was one level proximal to the superior vertebra of the nSDL. SRS-22r score at final f/u was 4.5/5 and pre-op was 4.0/5 (p<.001). Conclusion: The PAC method with limited apical fusion of SDL and PLVT of nSDL appears to be safe and results in significantly fewer levels fused compared to MHC and preserves lumbar SB ROM in AIS with diverse Lenke classifications.

## ID: AGS26/3088

## P0170: From high risk to safer outcomes: halo-pelvic traction vs. direct fusion in rigid kyphoscoliosis

Muhammad Saad Ilyas

1

, Amer Aziz

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Severe & rigid kyphoscoliotic deformities present major surgical challenges due to their stiffness, pulmonary compromise, and high risk of neurologic injury. Halo-pelvic traction (HPT) offers a gradual, staged correction strategy that can reduce curve rigidity, improve sagittal and coronal alignment, and potentially enhance surgical safety. Our modified halopelvic Ilizarov distraction device has achieved promising results in correcting severe spinal deformities without significant risk to neurology with fewer complications and good patient compliance. The current study compares radiological, surgical, and clinical outcomes between staged HPT followed by definitive fusion and direct fusion-only surgery in adolescents with severe kyphoscoliosis. Material and Methods: Patients diagnosed with severe (> 85° Cobb angle) adolescent idiopathic kyphoscoliosis were included in the study after institution ethical approval. Patients were divided into two groups: HPT Group underwent 6 weeks of HPT followed by final fusion, while non-HPT Group underwent direct fusion without modified halopelvic traction. Radiological parameters including AP cobb and lateral angles, fulcrum view angles, osteotomy types, surgical time, intraoperative neuromonitoring signals, and postoperative complications were analyzed. Results: A total of 68 patients were included (36 HPT, 32 non-HPT). Mean age was higher in the HPT group (15.58 ± 1.13 vs. 14.34 ± 1.04 years, p < 0.001), with 50% males compared to 31.2% in non-HPT group. Severe curves (Cobb’s > 100°) were more frequent in HPT group (75% vs. 18.8%). Preoperative deformity was greater in HPT group (AP Cobb 112.19° vs. 92.69°, p < 0.001; lateral Cobb 82.22° vs. 48.44°, p < 0.001; fulcrum angle 89.11° vs. 65.84°, p < 0.001). Postoperatively, AP Cobb correction was 75.2% (29.2°) in HPT group vs. 66.2% (38.0°) in non-HPT group, while sagittal plane correction was significantly superior in HPT group (69.97% vs. 49.97%, p = 0.002). Coronal correction was comparable (67.13% vs. 72.56%, p = 0.054). In curves < 100°, both groups achieved similar correction. No neuromonitoring signal loss occurred in HPT group, whereas three events occurred in non-HPT group (two recovered intraoperatively). One postoperative mortality due to pulmonary complications was reported in non-HPT group. Mean surgical time was shorter in HPT group (366 vs. 414 minutes). Osteotomy distribution showed fewer high-grade osteotomies in HPT group (Type 3-4 in 2 patients) versus non-HPT group (4 patients), despite greater deformity severity. Conclusion: For large, rigid adolescent kyphoscoliotic deformities, a staged approach with halo-pelvic traction prior to fusion achieves superior sagittal plane correction, reduces the need for high-grade osteotomies, ensures safer neuromonitoring, and facilitates more efficient surgery even in more severe curves. Fusion-only surgery, by contrast, carries higher neurologic risks and demands more invasive corrective techniques.

## ID: AGS26/3116

## P0171: Return to sport after posterior spinal fusion in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis: a retrospective analysis and literature review

Maria Celeste Zamora

1

, Leonardo Benolol

1

, Juan Jesus Mazzeo

1

, Ricardo Caldara

1

1

Hospital Aleman de Buenos Aires, Buenos Aires, Argentina

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is the most common structural spinal deformity in young patients. Surgical treatment with posterior spinal fusion using pedicle screws allows deformity correction while preserving as much motion as possible. One of the main concerns of patients and their families is the return to physical activity after surgery. However, there are no clear guidelines and limited evidence regarding the appropriate timing and the expected level of participation after surgery. Material and Methods: A retrospective, cross-sectional observational study was conducted. Patients diagnosed with AIS who underwent posterior spinal fusion between 2014 and 2023, with a minimum follow-up of 2 years, were included. Clinical and surgical data, as well as return to physical and school activities, were analyzed through surveys and medical record review. Results: A total of 25 patients were included, with a mean age of 14 years. All underwent surgery with pedicle screws. Preoperatively, 84% engaged in physical activity, and 96% resumed sports after surgery. The mean time to return was 9.28 months. Fifty-six percent changed discipline, mostly by personal choice. No significant differences were found in frequency or competitive level before and after surgery. No associations were found between fusion levels, upper or lower instrumented vertebra, and return to sports. All patients resumed school/work activities, with a mean time of 6.16 weeks. Conclusion: Return to physical and school activities after posterior spinal fusion for AIS is high. No surgical factors were identified as determinants. Larger studies with longer follow-up are required.

## ID: AGS26/3189

## P0172: Recurrent dorsal spinal arachnoid cyst in a 13-year-old patient: case report and surgical management

Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal arachnoid cysts are rare lesions, particularly in the pediatric population. They may cause progressive neurological deficits due to spinal cord compression. Recurrence after surgical excision is uncommon but poses a significant therapeutic challenge. We report the case of a 13-year-old boy with a recurrent dorsal arachnoid cyst successfully managed by re-operation. Material and Methods: A 13-year-old male with no past medical history presented with progressive gait disturbance and bilateral lower limb weakness evolving over 2 months. Neurological examination revealed paraparesis (3-/5 on the left, 3+/5 on the right), pyramidal signs, but no sensory level or sphincter dysfunction. Spinal MRI demonstrated a posterior dorsal extradural cystic lesion extending from D5 to T12, isointense to cerebrospinal fluid and without contrast enhancement, displacing the spinal cord anteriorly. The patient underwent laminectomy and complete excision of the arachnoid cyst, confirmed histologically. Results: Postoperatively, the patient experienced partial neurological improvement but persistent lower limb pain and left L5 weakness (2/5). At 14-month follow-up, gait had improved but new urinary leakage episodes were reported. Neurological examination revealed spastic paraparesis with brisk reflexes, bilateral Babinski sign, and no sensory level. MRI confirmed recurrence of the cyst at the same location. The patient underwent re-operation via the previous dorsal midline approach. Intraoperatively, a large cystic cavity was found with two dural defects (median and left lateral). Both foramina were closed with 4-0 sutures reinforced with aponeurotic grafts, achieving watertight closure. Postoperative recovery was uneventful, and at 1-month follow-up the patient was asymptomatic with complete resolution of symptoms. Conclusion: Spinal arachnoid cysts in children are rare but should be considered in cases of progressive spastic paraparesis. While surgical excision usually provides good outcomes, recurrence may occur due to dural defects. Meticulous identification and closure of dural communications are essential to prevent recurrence. Our case highlights the importance of long-term follow-up and tailored surgical strategy in managing pediatric spinal arachnoid cysts.

## ID: AGS26/3235

## P0173: Surgical management of diastematomyelia: a bicentric retrospective study of 13 cases over 10 years

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Roua Latrach

1

, Mohamed Ali Kharrat

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Diastematomyelia is a very rare malformation, accounting for 3.8% of congenital spinal anomalies, with a prevalence of approximately 1 in 5,000 live births (0.02%). Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective bicentric study including all patients who underwent surgical treatment for diastematomyelia over a 10-year period in two neurosurgery departments in Tunisia. Clinical, radiological, surgical, and postoperative data were analyzed. Results: Thirteen patients were identified, including 9 children and 4 adults, with a mean age of 6 years in children and 58 years in adults. The sex ratio was 0.44. Clinical manifestations included scoliosis (7 cases), motor deficits (7), cutaneous stigmata such as a hair tuft (6, including 4 with hair tuft), urinary disturbances (4), and spinal or radicular pain (4). MRI and CT imaging were performed in all cases, revealing 10 type I and 3 type II diastematomyelia. Associated dysraphic anomalies were present in 10 patients. Postoperative complications were observed in 4 cases: intraoperative hemorrhage with anemia (1), cerebrospinal fluid leak (1), wound infection (1), and transient motor deficit (1). Neurological outcomes were favorable, with motor deficits and pain improving in all patients; two patients achieved complete motor recovery, while sphincter dysfunction improved in only one case. Conclusion: Diastematomyelia, or split cord malformation, is a rare form of occult spinal dysraphism. Diagnosis is often suggested by cutaneous markers and neuro-orthopedic manifestations. Surgery remains the treatment of choice, aiming to prevent irreversible neurological sequelae and improve functional outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3456

## P0174: Case report and scoping review in the management of congenital scoliosis and derived surgical complications

Rodolfo Paez Gonzalez

1,2

, David Florez

3

, Nicolas Niño Latorre

4

, Catalina Sarmiento Gómez

1 5

, Andrés Felipe Pereira Rosero

1

1

Hospital Infantil Universitario de San Jose, Hospital Universitario de San Jose, Bogota, Colombia

2

Hospital de San Jose, Bogota, Colombia

3

Hospital Militar Central, Hospital Infantil Universitario de San José, Bogota, Colombia

4

Fundación Santa Fe de Bogotá, Bogota, Colombia

5

Hospital Universitario de San Jose, Bogota, Colombia

Introduction: The surgical management of congenital scoliosis includes a wide variety of procedures depending on the subtype, age, and characteristics of the deformity. Associated complications can range from infection of the surgical site, mechanical complications, material complications to neurological complications, and others. They can occur in combination or in isolation, with varying degrees of severity, or can be either perioperative or postoperative. The literature covering this topic is usually reserved to individual experiences managing these complications. Currently, the management of these patients poses a challenge for the spine surgeon. Material and Methods: This is a case presentation of a 13-year-old patient. Previously diagnosed with congenital scoliosis. She had a history of multiple surgical and non-surgical procedures, including cast application, bracing, lengthening system application, and subsequent associated surgical procedures. Posterior fixation resulted in infection as an associated complication requiring hardware removal and follow-up, developing a severe kyphotic deformity. Due to the challenges experienced with this case, a scoping review was done trying to summarize what the literature offers to the spine surgeon regarding the different management approaches to resolve this type of complications (centered around congenital scoliosis). Results: The patient underwent multilevel posterior instrumentation associated whit osteotomies and vertebrectomy (for severe kyphoscoliosis) in two surgical stages. The patient has been followed up with clinical and radiographic examinations demonstrating satisfactory progress. Conclusion: Infection management as a complication of deformity correction surgery in congenital scoliosis deformities usually follows a similar timeline: Hardware removal, transient management either with a varying type of orthosis, cervical traction or follow up. In many cases, leading to a progression in the severity of the deformity. Once infection management is complete, the resulting deformity may require extensive definitive management, such as multi-level posterior instrumentation associated with multiple osteotomies and/or vertebrectomies.

## ID: AGS26/47

## P0174.1: Factors Affecting Radiological Improvement in Brace Treatment of Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis Patients

Tunahan Aka

1

, Murat Songür

1

, Ahmet Bayar

1

, Selçuk Keser

1

, Ercan Şahin

1

, Sancar Alp Ovalı

1

1

Zonguldak Bülent Ecevit University Faculty of Medicine, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Medical Specialty Thesis, Zonguldak, Türkiye

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is a complex three-dimensional spinal deformity occurring between ages 10 and 18. Bracing is the main conservative treatment for Cobb angles between 20° and 45°, but patient-related factors, especially joint laxity, may affect outcomes. This study evaluates the impact of joint laxity and other patient factors on radiological improvement after bracing. Material and Methods: A retrospective study of 52 AIS patients treated with bracing from 2017 to 2024 was conducted. Demographics, radiographs, SRS-22r scores, and Beighton scores were analyzed. Cobb angle changes were measured at 6, 12, and 18 months. The effects of brace wear time and exercise adherence on radiological outcomes were assessed. Results: 67.3% of patients were female, mean age 15.96 years. Average daily brace wear was 17.8 hours. Mean Cobb angle decreased significantly from 26.7° to 17.8° at 18 months (p < 0.001). 59.6% achieved ≥8° correction, with higher brace compliance and quality of life scores (p < 0.001). Additional symptoms, Lenke classification, and brace wear duration affected success (p < 0.05). No significant relation was found between Beighton score, exercise duration, Risser stage, initial Cobb angle, and correction. Conclusion: While our performance was found to be significant in terms of preserving the change amount of the large Cobb angle in patients with no additional complaints and an SRS score of >4.5, it showed a significant difference in percentage, although not significant, in terms of separation status in patients with a Beighton score of ≥4 and a Risser stage of ≥2. Conservative treatment in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is accepted as an effective and safe intervention method. However, this effectiveness reaches its maximum level when combined with patient motivation, quality of life, social support and individualized approaches, not only with physical interventions. This study has made an original and holistic contribution to both clinical practice and literature by summarizing many factors that give direction to treatment. Conservative performance is possible not only by giving a brace to the disease; but also by reading it correctly, supporting it in a readable and holistic way.

## ID: AGS26/138

## P0175: Large intrathoracic meningocele: a case report

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Wièm Mansour

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Intrathoracic meningocele is a saccular protrusion of the meninges through a dilated intervertebral foramen or a bony defect of the vertebral column. They usually remain asymptomatic, but back or radicular intercostals pain, cough, and dyspnoea are possible manifestations. Kyphoscoliotic deformity of the thoracic spine is often associated. Seventy per cent of the intrathoracic meningoceles reported in the literature occurred in patients with neurofibromatosis. Giant intrathoracic meningoceles associated with neurofibromatosis are rare, and standard treatment in these cases remain controversial. The purpose of this case is to emphasize this entity via a review of the literature. Material and Methods: A 23-year-old female, with a history of anemia since early age, presented with exertional dyspnoea. Thisdyspnoea began 2 months before admission, and it slowly increased in severity. Physical examination found multiple “cafe au lait” spots on the back and on the abdomen, cutaneous neurofibromas, and a right kyphoscoliosis. The neurological examination showed a pyramidal syndrome on the two inferior limbs. The Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) showed a large right Intrathoracic meningocele at the level of of D3-D4. The patient was operated through a drainage of the CSF contained in the meningocele via a thoracoperitoneal shunt. Postoperative course was uneventful. A radiological control through CT scan confirms the right emplacement of the catheter. Clinical symptoms had progressively disappeared after surgery. Results: Congenital meningocele is rare and usually associated with generalized mesenchymal dysplasia, such as NF-1 or Marfan syndrome. In the thorax, lateral meningoceles are more frequent because the paravertebral muscles are relatively weak and the pressure gradient between cerebrospinal fluid and thorax is high. Kyphoscoliosis in NF-1 is characterised by the predominance of kyphosis associated with scoliosis; acute posterior angulation is a typical sign as it was in the case reported here. The vertebral bodies are frequently so deformed and attenuated at the apex that it may be impossible to identify them on routine radiographs, and severe angulation of the spine is responsible for paraplegia. Meningoceles should be differentiated from tumors, especially those that commonly arise from the posterior mediastinum, such as neurofibroma, neuroblastoma, and ganglioneuroma. Conclusion: Intrathoracic meningoceles are rare. They are often associated to a neurofibromatosis, but this association is seldom within giant forms. Kyphoscoliosis associated tomeningoceles occuring on the convex side of the scoliosis are allways associated during these malformities. Surgery is recommended in symptomatic cases (neurological expression or dyspnea…). Athoracoperitoneal shunt is often proposed, with satisfactory clinical results.

## P0176: Intradural Schwannoma associated to a degenerative lumbar stenosis: what to do?

Emna Mzoughi

1

, seif amara

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Schwannoma is a common intradural slow-growing, benign and encapsulated tumor that originates from the myelin sheaths of the nerve fibers. However, a lumbar schwannoma complicating the symptoms of spinal stenosis is an extremely rare association. Material and Methods: We describe the case of a woman presenting a lumbar schwannoma in association with spinal stenosis, and report our approach and experience regarding it. Results: A 53 year-old woman presented due to the progressive onset of a lumbar pain that was irradiating to both inferior legs, associated to neurogenic claudications and genitosphincteric disorders. Neurological examination did not show motor or sensitive deficit, but rectal examination found a perianal anesthesia. Magnetic resonance imaging showed lumbar spinal stenosis due to flavum ligament hypertrophy and disc herniations, as well as an intracanalar expansive lesion with homogeneous contrast enhancement regarding the L2 pedicle. The patient underwent surgery. Peroperative, we proceeded through a posterior screw instrumentation from L3 to L5, followed by a decompression of the stenotic segments. Afterwards, we performed a L2 laminectomye in order to remove the tumor. Postoperative course was uneventful. Pathologic examination defined the tumor as a schwannoma. After a 1 year follow up, the patient reported a total regression of her pain, and recovered a nearly full control of her sphincteric functions. Conclusion: The present study highlights that lumbar schwannoma is a possible etiology complicating the symptoms of patients with previous lumbar spinal stenosis. It is important to treat both pathologies to improve the patients’ symptoms

## ID: AGS26/161

## P0177: Comparison of hook and vertebroplasty for the prevention of proximal junctional failure: hook may be the preferable option

Sung Tan Cho

1

, Jae Hwan Cho

2

, Dong-Ho Lee

2

, Chang Ju Hwang

2

, Sehan Park

2

1

Seoul Seonam Hospital, Seoul, South Korea

2

Asan Medical Center, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Proximal junctional failure (PJF) is a severe and increasingly recognized complication following long-segment fusion for degenerative sagittal imbalance (DSI), associated with substantial morbidity and compromised functional outcomes. The pathogenesis of PJF is multifactorial, involving mechanical, biological, and patient-specific factors. Adjunctive strategies aimed at reinforcing the proximal junction, such as vertebroplasty (VP) and hook fixation at the uppermost instrumented vertebra (UIV), have been proposed to mitigate PJF risk. However, comparative data on their clinical effectiveness in preventing PJF remain limited. This study aims to compare the effectiveness of vertebroplasty and hook fixation in preventing PJF in patients undergoing long-segment fusion for DSI. Material and Methods: A total of 139 consecutive patients with DSI who underwent posterior spinal fusion from T9/T10 to the pelvis at a single institution between 2016 and 2022 were included. Only patients with a minimum of 2 years of postoperative follow-up were analyzed. Patients were categorized into three groups based on proximal junctional intervention: vertebroplasty (n = 21), hook (n = 47), and control (no proximal intervention, n = 71). The primary outcome was the incidence of PJF. Secondary outcomes included the incidence of proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK), distal junctional failure, rod breakage, and radiographic changes in global sagittal alignment parameters, including pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis mismatch, thoracic kyphosis, pelvic tilt, and C7 sagittal vertical axis. Surgical procedures included long-segment posterior spinal fusion from T9/T10 to the pelvis. Interbody fusion from L2 to S1 was performed using posterior lumbar interbody fusion or oblique lumbar interbody fusion techniques, based on anatomical considerations and surgeon preference. Proximal junctional interventions were performed at the discretion of the attending surgeon and consisted of either vertebroplasty or hook fixation applied at the UIV. Patient demographics, surgical variables, and radiographic outcomes were compared across the three groups. Multivariate logistic regression analysis was conducted to identify independent risk factors for PJF, adjusting for age, sex, BMI, osteoporosis status, number of rods, instrumentation type at the UIV, and sagittal alignment parameters. All statistical analyses were performed using appropriate parametric or non-parametric tests, with a significance threshold of p < 0.05. Results: The overall incidence of PJF was significantly lower in the intervention group (vertebroplasty or hook) compared to the control group (5.9% vs. 21.1%, p = 0.009). Among the intervention subgroups, hook fixation demonstrated a numerically lower PJF rate (4.3%) compared to vertebroplasty (9.5%), although this difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.544). Multivariate analysis revealed that hook fixation exerted the strongest protective effect against PJF (OR 0.036, p = 0.015), followed by vertebroplasty (OR 0.077, p = 0.033). The incidence of PJK did not significantly differ across groups (p = 0.640). Conclusion: Both vertebroplasty and hook fixation effectively reduced PJF incidence following long-segment fusion for DSI, with hook fixation demonstrating a trend toward superior efficacy. These findings highlight the importance of optimizing proximal construct dynamics and suggest that hook fixation may be a preferable adjunctive strategy for mitigating PJF risk

## ID: AGS26/192

## P0178: Optimization of spinal alignment strategies in low pelvic incidence patients: a computational modeling approach

Devender Singh

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Sagittal balance is a key determinant of spinal health, particularly in patients with low pelvic incidence (PI < 45°). Surgical planning for these patients requires optimizing spinopelvic parameters to achieve biomechanically favorable postural alignment. However, current alignment strategies may not consistently achieve ideal sagittal profiles, leading to potential compensatory mechanisms or postoperative complications. This study aims to evaluate the effectiveness of three alignment strategies in low PI patients based on compliance with key sagittal alignment criteria. Material and Methods: A cohort of 20 patients with PI < 45° underwent computational modeling of three sagittal balance strategies: PI-LL = 10° (less_curve), PI-LL = 0° (ideal_curve), and PI-LL = -10° (more_curve). Each strategy was evaluated using three alignment goals: C2PL = 0 mm, T1PA = 10°, and T1PA = 15°. Plans were assessed based on compliance with three key criteria: T1 slope between 15°-35°, T1 pelvic angle (T1PA) < 20°, and C2PL between +9 mm and -33 mm. Compliance rates were calculated for each condition and strategy. Results: The analysis revealed that compliance rates varied significantly across strategies. The highest compliance was achieved in the C2PL = 0 mm strategy within the PI-LL = -10 subgroup, with a compliance rate of 61.90%. The lowest compliance was observed in the T1PA = 10° strategy for the PI-LL = -10 subgroup, with a compliance rate of only 4.76%.
The T1PA = 10° and 15° strategies struggled to achieve the required T1 slope and C2PL alignment simultaneously. The most challenging criterion to satisfy was T1 slope (15°-35°), which failed in up to 71.20% of cases, particularly in T1PA strategies. Conclusion: The findings suggest that current alignment strategies may not fully accommodate the sagittal alignment needs of low PI patients. The T1 slope parameter was the most difficult to optimize, indicating potential gaps in thoracic compensation or spinal alignment adjustments. The C2PL constraint was also a major limiting factor in T1PA-based strategies, suggesting that global sagittal balance needs further refinement. Future studies should explore the role of additional compensatory mechanisms such as thoracic kyphosis and pelvic tilt to improve alignment predictability.

## ID: AGS26/193

## P0179: The middle ground mystery: unraveling spinal alignment challenges in average pelvic incidence

Devender Singh

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Sagittal balance is critical for spinal health, particularly in patients with average pelvic incidence (PI = 45°-60°). Surgical strategies aim to optimize spinopelvic parameters to achieve biomechanically favorable postural alignment. However, alignment goals may not always be met due to unaccounted compensatory mechanisms, leading to suboptimal surgical outcomes. This study evaluates the effectiveness of three alignment strategies in patients with average PI, focusing on compliance with key sagittal alignment criteria. We hypothesize that none of the three alignment strategies (PI-LL = 10, PI-LL = 0, PI-LL = -10) will achieve full compliance with all sagittal alignment criteria, and that some strategies will be more prone to failure in specific parameters, particularly T1 slope and C2PL. Material and Methods: A cohort of 20 patients with PI between 45° and 60° underwent computational modeling of three sagittal balance strategies: PI-LL = 10° (less_curve), PI-LL = 0° (ideal_curve), and PI-LL = -10° (more_curve). Each strategy was tested using three alignment goals: C2PL = 0 mm, T1PA = 10°, and T1PA = 15°. Plans were analyzed based on three criteria: T1 slope between 15°-35°, T1 pelvic angle (T1PA < 20°), and C2PL between +9 mm and -33 mm. Results: The analysis revealed that compliance rates varied significantly across strategies. The highest compliance was observed in the C2PL = 0 mm strategy, which achieved 53.66% compliance, whereas the lowest compliance was found in the T1PA = 10° strategy, at only 2.44%. The most frequently failed parameter was T1 Slope 15°-35°, with failure rates exceeding 85% in some cases. Patients with average PI showed difficulty in maintaining sagittal alignment when relying on T1PA-based strategies, leading to suboptimal T1 slope and improper C2PL positioning. The inability to achieve correct alignment increases the risk of compensatory mechanisms, which may contribute to postoperative lumbar strain, early degeneration, and adjacent segment disease. Conclusion: These findings highlight that current alignment strategies may not fully address sagittal balance in patients with average PI. The challenges in achieving ideal alignment suggest that additional compensatory mechanisms, such as thoracic kyphosis and lumbar adjustments, should be integrated into spinal realignment strategies. Future research should explore multi-segmental compensations to enhance patient-specific surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/206

## P0180: The suction sign: an intraoperative audible marker for adequate intradiscal release and improved radiographic correction

Robert Ricotti

1

, Matthew Parry

1

, Alex Tang

1

, Michael Pheasant

1

, Mina Girgis

1

, Aidan Papalia

1

, Tan Chen

2

1

Geisinger Northeast Orthopaedic Residency, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

2

Geisinger Musculoskeletal institute, Division of Orthopaedic Spine Surgery, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-TLIF) has been described as a less morbid alternative compared to traditional open approaches for degenerative lumbar pathology. One challenge associated with MIS-TLIF is reliable assessment of intraoperative disc release to achieve surgical correction and address segmental deformity. The suction sign is a confirmatory intraoperative auditory phenomenon that can be produced by toggling a blunt instrument in the disc space once sufficient annular release and segmental mobility has been achieved. This audible feedback may provide a novel method to predict adequate intraoperative annular release and subsequent postoperative radiographic corrections. The objective of this study was to characterize the intra- and postoperative corrections in anterior disc height (ADH), posterior disc height (PDH), segmental lordosis (SL), and slip angle (SA) among suction sign positive patients. Material and Methods: From January 2023 to September 2024, clinical and radiographic data of suction sign positive patients were retrospectively collected through chart review and analyzed. The suction sign was documented intraoperatively in each case. Pre-, intra-, and postoperative radiographic measurements of ADH (mm), PDH (mm), SL (degrees), and SA (degrees) were performed by two trained, independent readers. Descriptive and inferential statistics were performed. Results: Of the 25 suction sign positive patients, 15 (60%) underwent MIS-TLIF and 10 (40%) underwent mini-open TLIF. Fifteen (60%) patients were male, mean age was 52.1 ± 14.8 years, mean body mass index (BMI) was 29.1 ± 5.2, and mean follow up was 5.8 ± 4.4 months (range: 0.4-14.7 months). Inter-reader agreement was considered substantial (k > 0.75) for all radiographic measurement categories. Mean ADH and PDH significantly increased intra- (ADH: 14.0 ± 2.3, p < 0.0001; PDH: 8.8 ± 1.4, p < 0.0001) and postoperatively (13.0 ± 2.6, p < 0.0001; PDH: 8.2 ± 1.3, p < 0.0001) compared to preoperatively (ADH: 7.8 ± 3.7, PDH: 5.1 ± 1.8). Mean ADH significantly decreased postoperatively (13.0 ± 2.6, p = 0.0001) compared to intraoperatively (14.0 ± 2.3). There were no differences in PDH intraoperatively vs. postoperatively (8.8 ± 1.4 vs. 8.2 ± 1.3, p = 0.005). SL and SA significantly improved intra- (SL: 19.5 ± 9.5, p = 0.0005; SA: 12.5 ± 6.3, p < 0.0001) and postoperatively (SL: 18.1 ± 8.0, p = 0.0005; SA: 11.7 ± 4.7, p < 0.0001) compared to preoperatively (SL: 13.3 ± 8.0; SA: 5.3 ± 5.7). Correction was maintained in SL and SA intraoperatively vs. postoperatively (SL: 19.5 ± 9.5 vs. 18.1 ± 8.0, p = 0.05; SA: 12.5 ± 6.3 vs. 11.7 ± 4.7, p = 0.18). Conclusion: Patients with a positive suction sign demonstrated significant radiographic improvements in ADH, PDH, SL, and SA intra- and postoperatively following TLIF compared to preoperatively. These findings may offer reassuring evidence that the suction sign can be employed as a useful and novel audible marker in determining adequate intraoperative disc release prior to instrumentation and deformity correction.

## ID: AGS26/261

## P0181: The effectiveness and safety of high and low dosages of intraoperative intravenous tranexamic acid during surgical treatment of adult spinal deformity: a network meta-analysis

Anthony Baumann

1

, Omkar Anaspure

2

, Aryan Anaspure

3

, Mark Villers

4

, Keegan Conry

1

, Jacob Hoffmann

1

1

Cleveland Clinic Akron General, Akron, United States

2

Perelman School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, United States

3
Rutgers University, New Brunswick, United States, 
4
Northeast Ohio Medical College, Rootstown, United States
Introduction: Intravenous tranexamic acid (TXA) is increasingly used during adult spinal deformity (ASD) correction to reduce blood loss, yet optimal intraoperative dosing and comprehensive safety remain uncertain. Prior syntheses pooled heterogeneous regimens and emphasized venous thromboembolism alone, limiting dose-response and broader adverse-event insights. We therefore conducted an ASD-specific network meta-analysis comparing high-dose, low-dose, and no-TXA strategies to clarify efficacy and safety and inform perioperative decision-making. Material and Methods: We performed a Bayesian network meta-analysis of studies from PubMed, Embase, and CINAHL comparing high- (30-50 mg/kg loading; 3-10 mg/kg/h), low- (10-20 mg/kg loading; 0.5-2 mg/kg/h), and no-TXA regimens in ASD surgery, reporting mean difference (MD) or risk ratio (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI). Results: Eight articles (three randomized studies and five observational studies) were included. Patients (n = 1,016; mean age = 59 years; mean operated levels = 12.2; mean operative time = 384 minutes) were divided into the High TXA group (n = 223), Low TXA group (n = 539), or No TXA group (n = 254) based on TXA dosage with sufficient transitivity. For effectiveness, the High TXA group had a high probability of being the best treatment (> 90%) and the No TXA group had a high probability of being the worst treatment (> 90%) for minimizing total intraoperative EBL and EBL per vertebral level. Compared to the No TXA group, there was a statistically significant decrease in EBL per vertebral level and total EBL for the High TXA group (MD = -60.9 mL and -602.0 mL) with no statistically significant difference for the Low TXA group (MD = -31.3 mL and -387.0 mL). There was no statistically significant difference in EBL per vertebral level or total EBL between the High TXA group and the Low TXA group. For safety, none of the groups had a high probability of the best or worst treatment for minimizing TXA-related complications with no statistically significance difference in risk between groups. The total TXA-related complication rate was 10.8% for the High TXA group, 8.6% for the Low TXA group, and 12.7% for the No TXA group, suggesting similar risk. Rates for venous thromboembolism (2.4%-3.6%), stroke (0.4%-0.5%), and cardiac complications (4.2%-6.7%) were similar between groups. Certainty of evidence was very low. Conclusion: Intraoperative high dose IV TXA during surgery for ASD may be associated with greater effectiveness in reducing total EBL (MD = -602.0 mL) and EBL per level (MD = -60.9 mL) without a concomitant increase in total TXA-related complications compared to no TXA, but not compared to low dose IV TXA, based on very low certainty of evidence derived from eight comparative studies. Additionally, this study found that specific complication rates for VTE (2.4%-3.6%), stroke (0.4%-0.5%), and cardiac complications (4.2%-6.7%) were similar between groups, potentially indicating similar risk profiles between high-dose TXA, low-dose TXA, and no TXA during surgery for ASD. Surgeons should cautiously incorporate this evidence on a case-by-case basis while considering the unique risk-benefit profile for each patient. These results for ASD appear consistent with the wider literature for all-type spine surgery for high-dose TXA.

## P0182: Does a diagnosis of osteoporosis increase the incidence of mechanical and junctional failures in older patients undergoing ASD surgery?

Vanessa Vashishth

1

, Brett Rocos

2

, Stephen Lewis

3

, Marinus De Kleuver

4

, Yong Qiu

5

, Yukihiro Matsuyama

6

, Ahmet Alanay

7

, Ferran Pellisé

8

, Kenneth Cheung

9

, David Polly

10

, Jonathan N. Sembrano

11

, Christopher Shaffrey

2

, Justin Smith

12

, Michael Kelly

13

, Benny Dahl

14

, Sigurd Berven

15

, Lawrence Lenke

16

1

University Health Network, Toronto, Canada

2

Duke University, Durham, United States

3

Toronto Western Hospital, Toronto, Canada

4

Radboud UMC, Nijmegen, The Netherlands

5

Department of Spine Surgery, Drum Tower Hospital, Medical School of Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

6

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka, Japan

7

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

8

Hospital Vall D´Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

9

The University of Hong Kong Shenzhen Hospital, Shenzen, China

10

University of Minnesota, Minnesota, United States

11

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, Minnesota, United States

12

University of Virgina, Charlottesville, United States

13

Rady Children's Hospital, San Diego, United States

14

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Rigshospitalet, University of Copenhagen, Copenhagen, Denmark

15

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

16

Columbia University, New York, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity surgery (ASD) is associated with a nearly 50% risk of mechanical complications. It has long been established that the majority of patients are female, and with the higher prevalence of osteoporosis within this demographic, it may be that there is a greater incidence of mechanical complication within the same group. The purpose of this review is to analyze the medical, surgical and implant related adverse events in osteoporotic patients undergoing multilevel ASD surgery. Material and Methods: Patients > 60 years of age undergoing ASD surgery > 5 levels were prospectively reviewed from 12 international centers. Bone health, medical and surgical management was at the discretion of the treating surgeon. Baseline bone mineral density (BMD) was performed using femoral neck DXA studies, with results categorized according to the WHO criteria. Patients were divided into 3 groups based on their pre-operative BMD and analyzed for peri-operative adverse events. Results: 219 patients were analyzed for review. 80.4% were female and 143 underwent pre-op femoral neck BMD testing with 52 having normal bone density, 68 osteopenia and 23 osteoporosis. Of the patients with osteoporosis, 82.6% were female with a rate of 60.9% in the Asian patients, 34.8% in the North American patients, and 4.3% in European patients. Patients with osteoporosis had a significantly lower BMI (22.8 ± 3.8 kg/m2) than osteopenic (24.6 ± 4.8 kg/m2) and normal BMD (27.3 ± 6.0 kg/m2) p < 0.001. Surgical features were similar in each group except for levels fused which greater in the osteoporotic group (11.3 ± 5.5 vs 9.7 ± 4.8 vs 7.2 ± 4.4 levels, p = 0.001). At up to 5 years post-op, the rod fracture rate was 3.8%, 11.8% and 4.3% in each group, although the difference was not significant. The PJK rate was 34.8%, 29.4% and 23.1% in each group, a non-significant difference. Medical and surgical adverse events rate was similar in each group. Conclusion: Patients with osteoporosis aged over 60 years undergoing > 5 level fusions for ASD had a lower BMI, longer constructs and a higher rate of PJK. Rod fracture and implant related complications were similar in each group.

## P0183: Assessing pain as a primary outcome measure in the surgical treatment of adult spinal deformity in patients over 60 years of age with 2 and 5 year outcome data from the prospective evaluation of elderly deformity (PEEDS) study

Colby Oitment

, Maarten Spruit

Duke University Medical Center, Department of Neurosurgery, Durham, North Carolina, United States

The University of Hong Kong Shenzhen Hospital, Shenzhen, China

10

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

11

Department of Spine Surgery, Drum Tower Hospital, Medical School of Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

12

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Japan,

13

Hospital Vall D´Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

14

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

15

Spine Unit, Department of Ortopaedic Surgery, Rigshospitalet, University of Copenhagen, Copenhagen, Denmark

16

Hospital for Special Surgery (HSS), New York, United States

17

Radboud University Medical Centre (Radboudumc), Nijmegen, The Netherlands

18

Sint Maartenskliniek, Nijmegen, The Netherlands
