Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

20

University of Virginia, Virginia, United States

Introduction: Back and leg pain are key motivators for surgery in patients with Adult Spinal Deformity (ASD), yet they are infrequently assessed as a primary outcome measures. This study evaluates pain outcomes in patients over 60 undergoing multilevel fusion for ASD, using multiple validated Patient Reported Outcome Measures (PROMs) with 5-year data. Material and Methods: A prospective, international, multi-center study enrolled patients ≥ 60 years undergoing spinal deformity surgery with ≥ 5-level instrumented fusion. Patients were evaluated preoperatively and at 10 weeks, 1 year, and 2 and 5 years using the Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) for back and leg pain, and pain-related subscores from the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Scoliosis Research Society-22r (SRS-22r), and EQ-5D. Results: 219 patients met inclusion criteria (mean age 67.5 years, 80.4% female). Baseline pain was predominantly back-dominant (mean NRS back: 6.1; leg: 4.3). At 2 years, significant pain reduction was observed: mean NRS back 2.7, NRS leg 2.3. AT 5 years, the pain reduction was largely maintained with NRS back 2.9, NRS leg 2.6. Over 80% showed improvement in NRS back pain, 76.7% on SRS-22r pain, and 70.3% on ODI pain. Despite this, 15.1% experienced worse leg pain, and 5.7% reported worsened back pain. At 5 years, 53.3% of patients had improvement in NRS back pain, 46.6 on SRS-22r Pain and 48.6% in ODI pain. Patients with higher baseline pain had the greatest improvement, while those with low initial scores were more likely to deteriorate. At 2 years, 64.9% achieved an acceptable symptom state (PASS), compared to 18.1% preoperatively. There were no significant differences in outcomes across age groups. Conclusion: Surgical treatment for ASD in patients over 60 results in meaningful and durable improvements in back and leg pain at 2 and 5 years, with most patients reporting only mild or no pain.

## ID: AGS26/429

## P0184: Prediction of L1 pelvic angle can be improved by using both morphologic and positional parameters

Taryn Ludwig1, Chien Kow1, Farbod Moghaddam1, Remi Pelletier-Roy1, Mina Aminghafari1, Michael Kelly2, Fred Nicholls1

1
University of Calgary, Calgary, Canada, 
2
Rady Children's Hospital, San Diego, United States
Introduction: Pelvic incidence (PI) - lumbar lordosis (LL) mismatch is widely used as a marker of sagittal balance, but lordosis frequently extends above L1 and is poorly predicted by PI alone. Assessment of the true arc of the lordotic segment of the lumbar spine and association to PI is challenging and unreliable. The first goal of this study is assessment of the true arc of the lordotic segment of the lumbar spine and association with PI. The L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) is a positional property that can be used to help qualify sagittal alignment, is easily measured intraoperatively, is directly affected by lordosis distribution and becomes a fixed property when the lumbar spine is fused. PI has also been shown to more reliably predict L1PA than it predicts LL. The second goal of this study is predicting L1PA in young, healthy, asymptomatic individuals from spinopelvic and femoroacetabular positional and morphologic parameters. Material and Methods: Healthy volunteers with no hip or spine pathology were recruited (N = 500). Full body EOS scans and 3D reconstructions of the pelvis were obtained. Femoroacetabular parameters (acetabular anteversion (AA), acetabular inclination (AI), femoral version (FV)) and spinopelvic parameters (PI, LL, L1PA, pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS)) were calculated and recorded. Lumbar lordosis from L1 to S1 (L1S1) and from the cranial end of the lumbar lordotic segment to S1 (trueLL, calculated from raw coordinates) were obtained and compared to PI by linear regression. Associations between L1PA and spinopelvic and femoroacetabular parameters were tested using linear regression analyses. Results: 377 volunteers (age 28.90 ± 5.1, 57% F, BMI 24.8 ± 4.2) were included. The cranial end of LL was above L1 for 57% of participants. PI and LL-L1S1 were statistically significantly associated by linear regression (R2 = 0.361, p < 0.001), as were PI and trueLL (R2 = 0.339, p < 0.001). Polynomial regression did not provide a better fit. LL was poorly predicted by PI alone (R2 = 0.34). Using stepwise regression, L1PA could be reasonably predicted by PI alone (R2 = 0.62, L1PA = 0.47PI-18, Mdn PI = 48.6, IQR = 13.5), and improved with inclusion of AA in the model (R2 = 0.67, L1PA = 0.44PI+0.34AA-22, Mdn AA = 14.4, IQR = 4.9). Substituting PT and SS for PI further improved predictability (R2 = 0.79, L1PA = 0.25SS+0.75PT-12, Mdn SS = 39.4, IQR = 10.2, Mdn PT = 9.6, IQR = 8.8). Conclusion: The conventional L1-S1 measurement does not accurately capture the lordotic curvature in the majority of healthy, asymptomatic volunteers. We furthermore identified strong relationships between morphologic spinopelvic and femoroacetabular parameters in defining sagittal balance of the young healthy spine via L1PA. The inclusion of positional parameters (PT and SS) resulted in much improved prediction of L1PA. Future work should focus on improving understanding of factors affecting sacropelvic positioning. Our study provides a foundation for studying changes in alignment with aging and associated compensatory mechanisms. This can ultimately help guide restoration of not only individualized spinopelvic alignment but acetabular orientation in THA as well, potentially reducing the need for revision surgery.

## ID: AGS26/481

## P0185: Predictive value of the global alignment and proportion (GAP) score for mechanical complications after adult spinal deformity surgery: a systematic review

Vinicius Ricieri Ferraz

1

, Guilherme Santos Piedade

2

, Carlos Goulart

3

, Maria Ricieri Ferraz Franco de Souza

4

, Marcelo Ochoa Coelho de Souza Furlan

5

, Philippe A. Mercier

6

, Tobias Mattei

6

1

Dr. José de Carvalho Florence Hospital, The Regional Hospital of São José dos Campos - Dr. Rubens Savastano, São José dos Campos, São Paulo, Brazil

2

Department of Neurosurgery, University of Utah, Salt Lake City, Utah, United States

3

Division of Neurological Surgery, Essentia Health, Duluth, Minessota, United States

4

Division of Neurology, Irmandade da Santa Casa de Misericordia de Santos, Santos, São Paulo, Brazil

5

Pontifical Catholic University of Paraná, Curitiba, Brazil

6

Division of Neurological Surgery, Saint Louis University School of Medicine, Saint Louis, Missouri, United States

Introduction: Mechanical complications (MCs) frequently occur following adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. The Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score was developed to personalize sagittal alignment targets based on pelvic incidence. However, the predictive accuracy of the GAP score remains a topic of debate. This article systematically reviews the evidence regarding the performance of the GAP score, its modifications, and related factors. Material and Methods: Following the PRISMA guidelines, a comprehensive search was conducted across PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus, and Cochrane databases for studies published from January 2017 to July 2025. The inclusion criteria included studies involving adult patients with Adult Spinal Disorder (ASD) that reported GAP scores, along with outcomes related to mechanical complications-such as proximal junctional kyphosis/failure, rod fracture, and implant failure-and/or patient-reported outcomes (PROs). Data on demographics, surgical techniques, GAP categories, rates of mechanical complications, and predictive metrics were systematically collected and qualitatively analyzed. Additionally, findings from existing meta-analyses were integrated into the review. Results: Thirty-two studies involving over 5,700 patients met the inclusion criteria. The reported incidence of MC ranged from 17.6% to 61%. The predictive accuracy of the GAP score was inconsistent, with AUC values ranging from 0.53 to 0.86. Some cohorts showed no significant relationship between GAP categories and MCs, while others demonstrated a clear gradient. A meta-analysis of 1,617 patients revealed a significantly higher risk of MCs for disproportioned patients, with an odds ratio (OR) of approximately 2.6-2.8. The GAP score did not predict MCs in circumferential MIS cohorts, but its predictive value improved in open construct cohorts. The modified GAPB, which includes BMI and BMD, showed better accuracy (AUC approximately 0.885 versus 0.798). Machine-learning models that integrated GAPB variables achieved prediction accuracies over 73%. Bone quality, indicated by low HU at UIV/UIV+1, and sarcopenia emerged as independent predictors even in proportioned patients. The associations between these factors and PROs were mixed, indicating a possible trade-off between radiographic perfection and surgical morbidity. Conclusion: The GAP score has limited, context-dependent predictive value for mechanical complications after ASD surgery. It should not be used as a sole algorithm. Multifactorial models that incorporate radiographic alignment (GAP), bone quality, muscle condition, BMI, and surgical approach provide better risk stratification. Future research should focus on large prospective studies and advanced predictive analytics to support personalized surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/562

## P0186: Establishing the timeline for pelvic tilt stabilization after adult spinal deformity surgery

Lane McCoy

1

, Christopher Diaz

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Pelvic tilt (PT) plays a crucial role in sagittal alignment and functional outcomes after adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. However, the timeline for PT stabilization postoperatively remains unclear. This study aims to determine the time point at which PT stabilizes and whether other sagittal alignment parameters follow similar trends, providing insight into postoperative alignment progression. Material and Methods: ASD patients who underwent a fusion from the upper thoracic region (T1-T5) to the pelvis and met their targeted L1 pelvic angle (PA) (L1PA = 0.5PI - 19 ± 2) goal immediate postoperative mark were evaluated. Radiographic parameters were measured at immediate postop (iPO), 6 weeks (6W), 6 months (6M), 1 year (1Y), and 2 years (2Y) postoperatively. The changes in PT, C7 sagittal vertebral axis (SVA) and C2 SVA over these time points were assessed. Statistical significance was determined via paired t-tests. Results: 43 patients met our inclusion criteria. PT significantly increased from iPO to 6W (15.58 vs 19.65 [p = 0.0373]). There was no other significant increase between measured time intervals (6W:19.65, 6M: 19.33, 1Y: 21.03, 2Y: 19.63). Similarly, C7 SVA and C2 SVA exhibited a decrease from iPO to 6W (C7 SVA: 52.25 vs 23.85mm, p = 0.001, C2 SVA: 89.88 vs 49.23mm, p = 0.0003), after which they stabilized with no significant change occurring between time intervals. Conclusion: This study demonstrates that PT, C7 SVA, and C2 SVA stabilize at the 6-week postoperative point and remain consistent through 2 years in ASD patients aligned to L1PA criteria. This has significant implications for postoperative monitoring and long-term alignment expectations. Future studies should explore whether other early postoperative sagittal alignment changes could predict long-term clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/563

## P0187: Predictors of functional recovery after ASD surgery: an evaluation using SRS function PASS+ criteria

Christopher Diaz

1

, Lane McCoy

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Functional recovery after adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery varies, with some patients recovering faster than others. The Scoliosis Research Society physical function (SRS-PF) score combined with PASS+ criteria marks clinically meaningful recovery. This study aims to identify factors predicting both recovery likelihood and timing in ASD patients. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing fusion of ≥ 7 levels for ASD with pelvic fixation and at least 2 years of follow-up were included. Demographic, clinical, radiographic, and SRS outcome scores were collected at 6 weeks, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively. The cohort was divided into PASS+ (score > 3.3) and PASS- groups. Variables were compared using parametric tests. To study recovery timing, the PASS+ group was subdivided into those achieving PASS+ by 6 months and those at 1 or 2 years, identifying factors linked to faster recovery. Results: 143 patients were included in this study with 67% reaching PASS+ by 2 years. On univariate analysis, male sex (p = 0.04), higher Modified 5 frailty index (MFI5) (p = 0.0058), higher sagittal vertebral axis (SVA) (p < 0.01), lower lumbar lordosis (p = 0.04), and less Cobb angle correction (p = 0.01) were all associated with being SRS-PF PASS-. However, multivariate analysis showed no significant predictors for PASS+ versus PASS- status. In the PASS+ cohort, 49% patients became PASS+ by 6 months and 51% at 1 year or 2 years. Univariate analysis showed ASD patients with shorter length of stay (p = 0.03), lower estimated blood loss (p = 0.01), shorter operative time (p = 0.034), lower MFI5 (p = 0.02), and lower C2 SVA (p = 0.04) were more likely to reach PASS+ in 6 months. Multivariate analysis showed MFI5 was the strongest predictor of recovery time, with higher frailty indicating delayed recovery (p < 0.01). Conclusion: Patient-specific and radiographic factors may influence whether ASD patients achieve PASS+ physical function. Among those who recover, frailty significantly affects the timing, highlighting its importance in predicting functional recovery speed.

## P0188: Staged OLIF combined with posterior column osteotomy and posterior internal fixation for the correction of adult spinal deformity: a retrospective study focusing on intervertebral derotation and lateral slippage

Dechun Wang

, Cunxin Zhang

2

, Longwei Chen

1

1

Qingdao Municipal Hospital, Qingdao, China

2

Jining No. 1 People's Hospital, Jining, China

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is usually rigid and requires a combined anterior-posterior approach for deformity correction. The objective of this research is to evaluate the effects of OLIF and posterior column osteotomy(PCO) combined with posterior internal fixation for intervertebral derotation and lateral listhesis correction. Material and Methods: In this retrospective study, 36 consecutive patients diagnosed with ADS were included, with a mean age 67.14 years, who underwent two stages surgery: Stage1.OLIF ; Stage 2. PCO combined with posterior internal fixation between 2019 and 2024. The follow-up time were at least 18monthes.The full-spine anteroposterior and lateral X-rays, as well as lumbar CT and MRI were obtained to measure the Cobb angle, lateral slip distance, vertebral rotation, spinopelvic parameters, sagittal vertical axis (SVA), the distance between the C7 plumb line and the central sacral vertical line (C7PL-CSVL) preoperatively, postoperatively(stage1, stage2)and at the final follow-up. Results: Significant three-dimensional correction was observed following the first-stage OLIF and the second-stage PCO combined with posterior internal fixation. The lumbar lordosis(LL) increased from 27.72° to 36.94°; 37.48°;37.39° (p < 0.001). The lateral slip distance decreased from 9.12mm to 5.35 mm; 3.73 mm; 3.44 mm (p < 0.001). Conclusion: This study demonstrated that: First-stage OLIF was efficient for partially vertebral derotation and lateral listhesis correction as well as improving the sagittal spinopelvic parameters simultaneously. PCO may be needed to obtain sufficient correction of coronal and sagittal deformity in ASD with severe coronal and sagittal imbalance.

## ID: AGS26/624

## P0189: Lumbar lordosis distribution in adult spinal deformity: targets based on pelvic morphology

Peter Passias

1

, Francesca Totis

1

, Kyriakos Chatzis

1

, Mohammad Daher

1

, Anthony Baumann

2

, Peter Tretiakov

1

, Tobi Onafowokan

1

, Max Fisher

1

, Ethan Cottrill

1

1

Duke University, Durham, United States

2

Cleveland Clinic, Ohio, United States

Hypothesis: Thresholds for realignment of the lumbar lordosis distribution index (LDI) based on variations in pelvic morphology will lead to better outcomes. Study Design: Retrospective. Introduction: The LDI is an important constituent of achieving adequate correction of adult spinal deformity (ASD). While previous literature has evaluated LDI, targets can vary based on pelvic morphology that remains to be evaluated in the ASD population. Methods: Operative ASD patients fused from at least L1 and proximal to the pelvis with two-year follow-up were included. Patients were stratified into three categories based on pelvic incidence (PI) [P1: PI < 45°, P2 PI 45-60°, P3 PI > 60°]. LDI was assessed based on: L4-S1 lordosis/L1/S1 lordosis. LDI based on Lafage et al and GAP were evaluated. Thresholds were determined by conditional inference tree (CIT) analysis. Results: 334 patients met the inclusion criteria. Overall, 49% had mechanical complications, with 32% considered major and 22% undergoing reoperation for mechanical complications. 54% had PJK, while 13% developed PJF. No difference in mechanical complication rate when meeting Lafage LDI (48 vs 49%, p = .8) or GAP LDI targets (46 vs 51%, p = .4). CIT analysis determined thresholds based on PI (all p < .05). PJK rates were reduced in P1 (low PI) if LDI was corrected to > 0.6. While those in P2 (mid PI), with PT < 23° had reduced rates of early PJF when LDI was corrected to > 0.4. In P3 (high PI) with preoperative PT < 48°, reduced rates of PJF if postoperative LDI was < 85%, while reduced major mech comp with LDI < 68. Conclusions: Correction of lumbar lordosis distribution based on pelvic morphology reduced rates of PJK and PJF. Reduced rates of PJK were seen with thresholds for LDI correction of at least 60% for low PI, and lower than 85% in those with high IP.

## ID: AGS26/626

## P0190: The economic and clinical burden of poor bone health in adult spinal deformity surgery

Peter Passias

1

, Kyriakos Chatzis

1

, Mohammad Daher

1

, Francesca Totis

1

, Peter Tretiakov

1

, Anthony Baumann

2

, Tobi Onafowokan

1

, Max Fisher

1

, Ethan Cottrill

1

1

Duke University, Durham, United States

2

Cleveland Clinic, Ohio, United States

Introduction: Osteoporosis is commonly comorbid with adult spinal deformity (ASD), with a reported a prevalence of 14.4-28.6%. The cyclical relationship between ASD and poor bone health poses a significant challenge to healthcare systems. Methods: This is a retrospective analysis of a prospectively enrolled clinical trial. Operative ASD patients ≥18Y with complete BL and 2Y data included. Bone quality categorized per the American Association of Clinical Endocrinology for DEXA and clinical features. Cohorts: O (untreated osteopenic/osteoporotic) and NO (treated or normal). DRG reimbursements calculated using established data. QALYs calculated using ODI converted to SF-6D and employed an annual 3% discount rate to calculate surgical cost-effectiveness with linear regression extrapolating QALY data out to 5Y. Results: Among the 1142 (21.5% O; 78.5% NO) included (62.5 ± 14.7Y; 67%F; BMI 27.6 ± 5.5 kg/m2; CCI 1.1 ± 1.7), 16.6% had a formal diagnosis of osteoporosis, but 17.1% of osteoporotics and 76.1% of osteopenics were undiagnosed. 193 patients were treated 54.4% with PTH, 20.2% bisphosphonates, 19.2% Denosumab, and 4.1% Romosozumab. Surgical details: OR time 403 ± 143mins, LOS 7.3 ± 5.1d, EBL 1278 ± 1177 mL, Levels Fused 12 ± 4. O had higher revision rates (9.0 v 6.7%). O was also 2x more likely to develop radiographic PJK (2.0, [1.1, 3.8], p = .029) and 3.4x as likely to require revision for PJK (3.35, 1.4, 8.1], p = .007). Similarly, O was 2.5x more likely to develop a radiographic VBF (2.5, [1.2, 5.1], p = .017) and 3.8x more likely to require revision for VBF (3.8, [1.6, 9.3], p = .003). Cost calculations located in . DRG reimbursement for index surgeries averaged $94,442.93, while revision surgeries averaged an additional $95,117.73 per case. The added cost burden for O was $2,097,334.92 for revisions alone. The cost avoidance totaled $3,675.66 per patient in the NO cohort, with an aggregate saving of $3,297,066.27. In the extrapolated dataset, NO had higher surgical cost-effectiveness at Y3 (22,202.39 v $23,777.64, p = .008), Y4 (14,994.04 v $16,057.86, p = .008), and Y5 (11,319.11 v $12,122.20, p = .008). Conclusion: Our findings highlight the clinical and economic burden of poor bone quality in ASD surgery. Proactive management can potentially to mitigate complications, reduce revisions, and lower healthcare costs.
 

## ID: AGS26/627

## P0191: A tale of two planes: the impact of coronal deformity on sagittal alignment in adult spinal deformity

Peter Passias

1

, Mohammad Daher

1

, Kyriakos Chatzis

1

, Francesca Totis

1

, Peter Tretiakov

1

, Anthony Baumann

2

, Tobi Onafowokan

1

, Max Fisher

1

, Ethan Cottrill

1

1

Duke University, Durham, United States

2

Cleveland Clinic, Ohio, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) impacts both sagittal and coronal alignment, yet the role of coronal deformity in achieving sagittal alignment and its link to postoperative complications is underexplored. This study examines how the severity of coronal deformity affects postoperative alignment and complication rates. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective analysis of a prospectively enrolled database of 899 ASD Patients. ASD Patients ≥18Y with complete baseline (BL) and up to 2-year (2Y) follow-up data included. Sagittal parameters: SAAS criteria, sagittal vertical axis (SVA), and vertebral-pelvic angles. Coronal parameters: maximum Cobb angle (MCA), pelvic obliquity (PO), sacral obliquity (SO), C7 plumb line (PL), leg length discrepancy (LLD), and coronal inclination (Inc). Patients were stratified into three groups for each coronal parameter: Low (L): 0-25th, Moderate (M): 25th-75th, and Severe (S): 75th-100th percentiles. Multivariate analysis (MVA) was used to control for BL sagittal deformity and demographic factors. Results: 800 included (60.9 ± 14.6y, 75%F, 27.4 ± 5.7kg/m2, CCI 1.7 ± 1.7). Surgical characteristics: OR time 346mins, LOS 7.6days, EBL 1299mL, levels fused 10.6. BL Radiographics: MCA 38.2 ± 21.7°, PO 2.5 ± 2.0°, SO 4.6 ± 3.6°, PL 34.9 ± 32,9mm, LLD 8.4 ± 7.0mm, and Inc 3.7 ± 3.6°. Differences in coronal parameters are detailed in . At BL, 29.9% of patients met PT, 13.1% met PI-LL, and 22.1% met SVA goals. At 6W postoperatively, 26.2% met PT, 22.8% met PI-LL, and 29.7% met SVA goals. MVA demonstrated that patients in S for MCA were 1.7x less likely to achieve PI-LL alignment at 6W than L (OR: 1.71, [1.1, 2.8], p = 0.39). At 1Y, those in M and S MCA cohorts were 1.7x (OR: 1.68, [1.1, 2.6], p = 0.024) and 1.8x (OR: 1.75, [1.1, 3.0], p = 0.041) less likely, respectively, to meet SVA goals compared to L. Similarly, those in the M PL group were 1.8x less likely to achieve PT alignment at 2Y (OR: 1.82, 95% CI: [1.1, 3.0], p = 0.023). Patients in the S MCA group were 1.9x more likely to experience a major complication (OR: 1.93, [1.2, 3.2], p = 0.011) and 1.8x more likely to experience a minor complication (OR: 1.78, [1.1, 2.8], p = 0.11) compared to L. Additionally, those in S for PO had a 1.7x increased likelihood of a major complication compared to L (OR: 1.74, [1.1, 2.8], p = 0.024). Conclusion: Severe coronal deformity decreases the likelihood of achieving sagittal alignment and increases the risk of complications. These findings emphasize the need for targeted preoperative strategies to address coronal deformity and optimize surgical outcomes.
 

## ID: AGS26/628

## P0192: Impact of obesity on alignment in adult spinal deformity: a 3-year category-based analysis

Peter Passias

1

, Mohammad Daher

1

, Kyriakos Chatzis

1

, Francesca Totis

1

, Peter Tretiakov

1

, Anthony Baumann

2

, Tobi Onafowokan

1

, Max Fisher

1

, Ethan Cottrill

1

1

Duke University, Durham, United States

2

Cleveland Clinic, Ohio, United States

Introduction: The impact of obesity on global spinopelvic alignment is poorly understood. This study investigated the effect of body mass index on achieving alignment targets and compensation mechanisms after corrective surgery for adult spinal deformity (ASD). Material and Methods: This is a Retrospective cohort study of 899 consecutive ASD patients. Inclusion criteria were operative ASD patients (coronal Cobb angle ≥ 20°, SVA ≥ 50 mm, PT ≥ 25°, and/or thoracic kyphosis > 60°) who underwent ASD corrective surgery, > 18 yrs with complete baseline (BL) and 3-year (3Y) radiographic and HRQL data. Patients were stratified by body mass index into normal BMI, overweight, and obese groups (< 25, 25-30, and > 30). Postoperative alignment was compared with age-adjusted ideal values. Prevalence of patients who matched ideals and unmatched (undercorrected/overcorrected) was assessed. Means comparison tests assessed differences in baseline demographics, surgical details, and perioperative outcomes between normal BMI, overweight, and obese patients. Additionally, multivariable logistic regression and ANCOVA assessed differences in outcomes while accounting for age, CCI, frailty, baseline deformity, and invasiveness. Results: 340 ASD patients met inclusion criteria (Age: 59.0 ± 15.0; F = 78%; CCI: 1.6 ± 1.7). 91 patient were obese. 126 were overweight. 123 has a normal BMI. In terms of demographics, O has significantly higher CCI (2.1 vs 1.7 vs 1.2; p < 0.001), m-ASD-FI (11.4 vs 5.5 vs 4.2; p < 0.001), age (63.2 vs. 58.5 vs. 56.3; p = 0.004), less female (69% vs 74% vs 89%; p < 0.001). In term of surgical details, overweight significantly higher decompression rate (66% vs Obese: 59% vs N:61%; p = 0.013). No significant difference in osteo rate, number of level of fusion, ISSG invasiveness, EBL, Op time, SICU admission rate, and LOS. In terms of radiographic, obese patient shows a loss of correction PI-LL over time (6.3 at 3Y vs 2.5 at 6W), whereas normal BMI (-0.8 at 3Y vs -3.0 at 6W) and overweight (3.7 at 3Y vs 2.3 at 6W) held. obese patient shows a loss of correction PT over time (23.2 at 3Y vs 20.1 at 6W), whereas normal BMI (18.1 at 3Y vs 15.7 at 6W) and overweight (21.8 at 3Y vs 19.3 at 6W) held. At 3 years, obese patients exhibited the most pronounced thoracic kyphosis in the unfused region compared to the overweight group and normal BMI respectively (-27° vs -24° vs -19°; p = 0.009). They also experienced the greatest loss of correction in unfused thoracic kyphosis over 3 years (-9.1° vs -7.7° vs -6.2°; p = 0.045). At 3 years, the obesity group demonstrated the highest prevalence of mismatched pelvic tilt (PT) sagittal modifier (60.4% vs. 57.1% vs. 43.1%; p = 0.021) and mismatched sagittal vertical axis (SVA) modifier (52.7% vs. 40.0% vs. 26.8%; p < 0.001), compared to the other categories. In term of global alignment, obesity has the lowest Roussely match rate at 1Y (37.2% vs 52.1% vs 56.3%; p = 0.023), 2Y (37.5% vs 39.6% vs 55.1%; p = 0.35). The multivariant analysis demonstrated obesity has lower odd of achieving SVA matching at 3Y (OR: 0.48; 95% CI: 0.27-0.82; p = 0.008), PT matching at 3Y (OR: 0.69; 95%CI: 0.53-0.91; p = 0.009). Conclusion: Obesity significantly impacts both spinopelvic alignment in adult spinal deformity correction. At 3 years postoperatively, obese patients demonstrated greater loss of alignment correction, particularly in pelvic tilt and sagittal vertical axis modifiers, and more pronounced thoracic kyphosis compared to normal and overweight patients. These findings highlight the challenges in achieving and maintaining optimal alignment in obese patients undergoing ASD corrective surgery, underscoring the need for tailored surgical strategies and intensive postoperative management in this population ( ).
 

## ID: AGS26/636

## P0193: Spino-pelvic fixation using the dual sacral alar iliac screw technique for geriatric patients with spinal deformity: preventing distal instrumentation failure

Akihito Wada

1

, Katsunori Fukutake

1

, Daisuke Kamakura

1

, Takashi Dezawa

1

, Akinori Tani

1

, Hiroshi Takahashi

1

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Toho University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: We introduced the dual sacral alar iliac (S1AI + S2AI: DAI) screw technique as a preventive measure against S2 alar iliac (S2AI) screw failure following spino-pelvic fixation (SPF) for adult spinal deformity in geriatric patients, and we evaluated its efficacy. Background context: Japan, which is facing an unprecedented super-aged society, is experiencing a continuous increase in surgical treatment opportunities for elderly patients with spinal deformities. The S2AI screw provides a stable pelvic anchor for spino-pelvic correction and fusion. However, the lumbosacral junction poses a high risk of nonunion or pseudarthrosis in elderly patients with poor bone quality. Postoperative complications may include rod fracture and S2AI screw failure. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective comparative analysis of distal mechanical failure in two groups of geriatric spinal deformity patients (aged over 65) who had undergone corrective surgery and had a minimum follow-up period of two years. Twenty-eight patients in the S2AI-only group served as historical controls, and we compared them with 29 patients in the DAI group. The S2AI-only group included 23 female patients with a mean age of 73 years and a mean follow-up period of 92 months. The DAI group included 24 female patients with a mean age of 74 years and a mean follow-up period of 44 months. Both groups underwent thoracolumbar-pelvic fixation with multiple rods and sacral alar iliac screws. All cases involved fusion from T8 or T9 to the sacrum/pelvis. Results: Preoperative spino-pelvic radiographic parameters showed no significant difference in deformity severity: PI-LL (S2AI: 37°, DAI: 34°), PT (S2AI: 31°, DAI: 29°), and SVA (S2AI: 119 mm, DAI: 122 mm). Immediately after surgery, both groups showed significant improvements in PI-LL, PT, and SVA: S2AI: 0°, 14°, and 10 mm, respectively; DAI: 7°, 19°, and 32 mm, respectively. There was no statistical difference between the groups. However, at the final follow-up, the PI-LL (S2AI: 11°, DAI: 8°) and PT (S2AI: 30°, DAI: 22°) in the DAI group maintained the immediate postoperative correction. Meanwhile, the S2AI group showed statistically significant deterioration. In the S2AI group, seven cases of screw failure were identified: five cases of S2AI screw neck fracture and two cases of set screw pop-up. These failures occurred, on average, one year and five months after surgery. Two cases required additional surgery due to buttock pain caused by L5/S pseudarthrosis. In contrast, the DAI group had one case of a set screw popping up, but there were no clinical symptoms. Conclusion: While the multiple rod technique is useful for preventing rod fractures in geriatric patients undergoing SPF for thoracolumbar kyphoscoliosis, we experienced multiple cases of instrumentation failure with S2AI screws alone. Consequently, we introduced the DAI screw technique for geriatric patients with spinal deformity, which has achieved favorable middle-term postoperative outcomes. The DAI screw method effectively maintains postoperative correction and prevents distal instrumentation failure.

## ID: AGS26/651

## P0194: Machine-learning models for the prediction of ideal surgical outcomes in patients with adult spinal deformity

Xiaolong Chen

1

, Dongfan Wang

1

, Shibao Lu

1

1

Xuanwu Hospital Capital Medical University, Beijing, China

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery can reduce pain and disability. However, the actual surgical efficacy of ASD in doing so is far from desirable, with frequent complications and limited improvement in quality of life. The accurate prediction of surgical outcome is crucial to the process of clinical decision-making. Consequently, the aim of this study was to develop and validate a model for predicting an ideal surgical outcome (ISO) two years after ASD surgery. Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of 458 consecutive patients who had undergone spinal fusion surgery for ASD between January 2016 and June 2022. The outcome of interest was achievement of the ISO, defined as an improvement in patient-reported outcomes exceeding the minimal clinically important difference, with no postoperative complications. Three machine-learning (ML) algorithms - LASSO, RFE, and Boruta - were used to identify key variables from the collected data. The dataset was randomly split into training (60%) and test (40%) sets. Five different ML models were trained, including logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and multilayer perceptron. The primary model evaluation metric was area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC). Results: The analysis included 208 patients (mean age 64.62 years (SD 8.21); 48 male (23.1%), 160 female (76.9%)). Overall, 42.8% of patients (89/208) achieved the ideal surgical outcome. Eight features were identified as key variables affecting prognosis: depression, osteoporosis, frailty, failure of pelvic compensation, relative functional cross-sectional area of the paraspinal muscles, postoperative sacral slope, pelvic tilt match, and sagittal age-adjusted score match. The best prediction model was LightGBM, achieving the following performance metrics: AUROC 0.888 (95% CI 0.810 to 0.966); accuracy 0.843; sensitivity 0.829; specificity 0.854; positive predictive value 0.806; and negative predictive value 0.872. Conclusion: In this prognostic study, we developed a machine-learning model that accurately predicted outcome after surgery for ASD. The model is built on routinely modifiable indicators, thereby facilitating its integration into clinical practice to promote optimized decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/660

## P0195: What degree of malalignment justifies performance of three column osteotomy in revision lumbar spinal fusion?

Peter Passias

1

, Francesca Totis

1

, Kyriakos Chatzis

1

, Mohammad Daher

1

, Anthony Baumann

2

, Peter Tretiakov

1

, Tobi Onafowokan

1

, Max Fisher

1

, Ethan Cottrill

1

1
Duke University, Durham, United States, 
2
Cleveland Clinic, Ohio, United States
Hypothesis: The extent of malalignment in revision surgeries for complex spinal deformity can justify the use of 3CO.
Study Design: Retrospective cohort multicenter study. Introduction: While the use of three column osteotomy (3CO) in complex spinal deformity is associated with improved outcomes and acceptable complication rates, we have yet to compare outcomes after 3CO based on varied degrees of preoperative malalignment and determine when 3CO is warranted. Methods: Revision complex spinal deformity patients who were fused from at least L1 and proximal to S1 and below with available baseline and 1 year (1Y) follow-up were isolated. Patients were stratified by whether they had 3CO (3CO+) and those that did not (3CO-). 3CO- cohort was further assessed based on development of complications and regional failure. Analysis controlled for age, gender, BMI, CCI, frailty, baseline deformity (PT, PI-LL, TPA, C7-PL), and levels fused. Multivariable regression determined which sagittal parameters were predictive of good outcomes. Conditional inference tree (CIT) was used to determine thresholds. Results: 132 CADS patients met inclusion, with no significant difference in age, gender, BMI, CCI, and frailty. Deformity at baseline was significantly worse in 3CO+. Despite fewer levels fused in 3CO+ (10.7 vs 13.3, p < .05) cohort, there was higher EBL (2.2L vs 1.2L, p < 0.001), increased rates of massive blood loss (13% vs 1%, p = 0.008), LOS (9.9 vs 6.6 days, p < 0.001), SICU admission (87 ± 35% vs 60 ± 49%, p = 0.007), discharge to SNF or rehab. Differences amongst cohorts in 1Y radiographic alignment, with 3CO- cohort having worse PT (24.2° vs 19.6°, p = 0.008) and TPA (21.5° vs 16.3°, p = 0.01). Parameters predictive of mechanical complications were: increasing PT (OR: 1.325 [1.003-1.750], p = .048) and increasing TPA (OR: 1.064 [1.012-1.119], p = .016). Thresholds values for baseline deformity associated with favorable outcomes in 3CO+ were: PT < 15.3° (p = 0.047), PI-LL < 47.5° (p = 0.026), TPA < 33.2° (p = 0.047). Conclusion: The use of three-column osteotomy in revision complex spinal deformity surgery is associated with improved alignment by 1 year relative to those that did not. Threshold values for radiographic parameters were found to be PT < 15.3°, PI-LL < 47.5°, and TPA < 33.2° being associated with favorable outcomes when using a 3CO.

## ID: AGS26/697

## P0196: Outcomes of adult spinal deformity surgery in octogenarians - How old is too old?

So Kato

1

, Yuki Taniguchi

1

, Naohiro Kawamura

2

, Yusuke Sato

2

, Yuki Onishi

2

, Yuta Nakayama

2

, Hideki Nakamoto

1

, Kosei Nagata

1

, Hiroyuki Nakarai

1

, Sakae Tanaka

1

, Yasushi Oshima

1

1

The University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan

2

The Japanese Red Cross Medical Center, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: The safety and efficacy of spinal deformity surgery in elderly patients have not been well established. This study aims to compare outcomes of adult spinal deformity surgery in patients aged 80 and older with those aged 65 and younger. Material and Methods: Patients who underwent fusion surgery involving ≥ 5 spinal levels for spinal deformity between 2017 and 2023 at two institutions were included. Revision surgeries following previous fusions ≥ 4 levels were excluded. Patients were divided into two groups: elderly (≥ 80 years) and younger (50-65 years). Background factors, surgical details (fusion level, duration, blood loss), pre- and one-year post-operative radiographic parameters (PI, LL, PT, SVA), and patient-reported outcome measures (PROs: NRS for back and leg pain, ODI, EQ-5D), satisfaction at two years, 30-day complications, and rates of proximal junctional failure (PJF), rod breakage, and reoperation within two years. Results: Forty-four elderly patients and 29 younger patients were included. Significant differences were found in smoking history (0% vs. 9%, p = 0.04), ASA classification (2.4 vs. 2.0, p = 0.001), and osteoporosis treatment history (82% vs. 34%, p < 0.001). Surgical extent and operative time were similar, but blood loss was lower in the elderly group (883 vs. 1355 mL, p = 0.005). Preoperative PI-LL (46° vs. 32°, p = 0.007), PT (36° vs. 28°, p = 0.002), and SVA (147 vs. 92 mm, p < 0.001) were significantly worse in the elderly group. Postoperatively, PI-LL was similar (19° vs. 10°, p = 0.11), but PT (29° vs. 23°, p = 0.005) and SVA (84 vs. 53 mm, p = 0.03) remained higher in the elderly group. Only leg pain was significantly worse preoperatively in the elderly group (4.7 vs. 2.7, p = 0.02), but all PROs and satisfaction at two years showed no significant differences. PJF occurred more frequently cpncc in the elderly group (31% vs. 10%, p = 0.04), while rod breakage (5% vs. 3%, p = 0.72) and reoperation rates (21% vs. 13%, p = 0.44) were similar. Conclusion: Despite more severe preoperative deformity and higher PJF risk, patients aged 80 and above achieved comparable surgical outcomes and satisfaction to younger patients. Careful patient selection and thorough preoperative counseling are essential for safe and effective surgical intervention in this population.

## ID: AGS26/717

## P0197: Social media as a window into patient experience: sentiment analysis of 14,596 posts in spinal deformity forums

Mudassir Khan

1

, Hailey Hulse

1

, Reme Arhewoh

2

, Nathan Rotramel

1

, Rakesh Patel

2

, Ilyas Aleem

2

, Joshua Piche

2

1

University of Illinois College of Medicine - Rockford, Rockford, United States

2

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Spinal deformities span pediatric and adult populations and are associated with pain, disability, and progressive dysfunction. Although tools such as the SRS-22 and ODI are used to measure patient-reported outcomes, they may not fully reflect long-term psychosocial burden or treatment dissatisfaction. Social media platforms such as Reddit offer a unique, anonymous space for patients and caregivers to share their experiences with spinal deformities. With this broad repository of data and advances in artificial intelligence, we aimed to analyze sentiment and engagement in Reddit posts about pediatric and adult spinal deformities to explore how social media may supplement traditional patient-reported outcomes. Material and Methods: We performed a retrospective, cross-sectional mixed-methods analysis of ∼69,000 Reddit posts from 2014-2024 across 36 spine and health-related forums. Posts containing spinal deformity keywords were extracted and categorized into four groups: surgery-related, pain management, physical therapy and rehabilitation, and general discussion. Sentiment was classified as negative vs positive/neutral using NLP-based analysis. Post metadata (length, date, upvotes, and comments) was also collected and associations with sentiment were explored. Results: A total of 14,596 posts were analyzed. Overall, 82.0% of posts were positive/neutral and 18.0% were negative. Pain management and surgery-related posts had the highest negative sentiment (26.0% and 23.6%). Posts longer than 351 words were negative 30.9% of the time, and highly upvoted posts were negative 24.9% of the time. Negative posts received more comments over time, showing people increasingly engaged with difficult experiences. Post types remained stable over the decade, while post volume increased 47% annually after 2018, with 5,961 new posts in 2024 alone. Recent years showed a decline in negative sentiment in pain-related posts but a rise in surgery-related discussions. Conclusion: This is the first large-scale sentiment analysis of spinal deformity discussions on social media. While most posts were positive or neutral, surgery and pain-related content showed higher negative sentiment, often reflecting unmet expectations and emotional burdens. Findings highlight a potential disconnect between published outcomes and patient experiences. Social media offers valuable insights that may enhance counseling, set realistic expectations, and support patient-centered care for spinal deformities.

## ID: AGS26/754

## P0198: Staged anterior and posterior reconstruction for post-traumatic L5-S1 spondyloptosis with paravertebral abscess and preserved neurologic status: a case report

Aries Hidayat

1

, Lukas Widhiyanto

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic and Traumatology, Dr. Soetomo General Academic Hospital, Surabaya, Indonesia

Introduction: High-grade spondylolisthesis (spondyloptosis) with concurrent infection presents a complex surgical challenge. This report details the management of a case of L5-S1 spondyloptosis with a large paravertebral abscess following remote trauma in a neurologically intact young male. Material and Methods: A 20-year-old male presented with acute-on-chronic debilitating low back pain and immobility, following a motorcycle accident five months prior. Notably, his neurologic examination was intact throughout. Examination revealed a fixed lumbar deformity, tenderness at L5-S1, a palpable left iliac fossa mass, and associated anemia. Imaging confirmed near-complete L5-S1 spondyloptosis with large paravertebral abscesses. Results: Management involved a two-stage procedure: a left pararectal retroperitoneal approach for abscess debridement, L5 corpectomy, and anterior interbody cage placement, followed by posterior instrumented fusion from L4 to S1. Microbiological analysis of the intraoperative biopsy confirmed Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and appropriate anti-tuberculous therapy was initiated. The patient’s neurologic status remained intact post-operatively. His recovery was excellent: he was able to sit independently at 2 months and had returned to normal, unassisted walking by the 3-month post-operative mark. Conclusion: This case highlights that severe spinal destruction from tuberculosis, even to the degree of spondyloptosis, can occur without neurologic compromise. A combined surgical approach for radical debridement and 360-degree fusion, coupled with targeted antimicrobial therapy, can achieve outstanding functional results, preserving and facilitating a full neurologic and mechanical recovery.

## ID: AGS26/795

## P0199: Neuromonitoring changes in three-column osteotomies (3CO) for spinal deformity correction surgery were associated with a high rate of denovo neurological deficits

Stephen Lewis

1

, Juan Pablo Sardi

2

, Christopher J. Nielsen

1

, Sigurd Berven

3

, Zeeshan Sardar

4

, Lawrence Lenke

4

, Yong Qiu

5

, Ferran Pellise

6

, Ahmet Alanay

7

, Justin Smith

2

, Nasir Quraishi

8

, Randolph Gray

9

, Saumyajit Basu

10

, Go Yoshida

11

, Amer Aziz

12

, AO Spine Knowledge Forum Deformity

13

1

University Health Network, Toronto, Canada

2

University of Virginia, Charlottesville, United States

3

University of California San Francisco, San Fransisco, United States

4

Columbia University, New York, United States

5

Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

6

Hospital Universitari de la Vall d’Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

7

Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

8

Nottingham University Hospitals NHS Trust, Nottingham, United Kingdom

9

The Children`s Hospital Westmead, St. Leonards, Australia

10

Kothari Medical Centre, Kolkata, India

11

Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Japan

12

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

13

AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: 3CO are associated with high rate of neurological complications. Intraoperative Neuromonitoring (IONM) provides real-time feedback on nerve function and has been shown to increase safety of complex spinal deformity surgeries. Anticipating high- risk steps and responding appropriately to IONM is crucial for ensuring patient safety. Material and Methods: The study is a post hoc analysis of data from a prospective multicenter international observational study (Spine Deformity Intraoperative neuromonitoring: SDIM), where 20 international centers prospectively documented IONM (EMG, SSEP and MEP) alerts, demographics, radiographic findings, and surgical events of patients (10-80 years) undergoing spinal deformity surgery. The results included univariate analysis of baseline and radiological characteristics, incidence of IONM alerts and postop neurological status. Results: 20 out of 146 (13.7%) patients undergoing 3CO had an alert during surgery and 12 (60%) developed postop de novo motor deficit. The risk factors for alerts in 3CO patients include revision surgery (p < 0.05), higher coronal Cobb (p < 0.05), anterior spinal fusion (p < 0.05). Pts with alerts had longer duration of surgery (p < 0.05) and less sagittal corrections (p < 0.05) likely in attempts to resolve the IONM changes. Risk factors for motor deficit at postop were presence of baseline motor deficit (p < 0.05), revision surgery (p < 0.05) and longer duration of surgery (p < 0.05). Patients with alerts had decreased baseline MEP and SSEP signal, and were more likely to have postop neurological (p < 0.001) deficits. All pts had loss of MEP, and the event preceding the alert being osteotomy/release (71.4%) and correction/rod placement (28.6%). The most frequent response in these patients was to elevate blood pressure, give blood and further decompression. 71.4% of alerts recovered to baseline compared to 92.9% of alerts in patients without postop motor deficit. Conclusion: 60% of 3CO pts sustaining IONM alerts had new neurological deficits. Revision surgery, higher coronal Cobb and anterior surgery had a higher likelihood of IONM alerts in patients undergoing 3CO. Osteotomy/release and correction/rod placement were most often associated with MEP alerts in performing 3CO. Patients with IONM alerts during 3CO had lesser sagittal corrections and increased operative time in response to these alerts. The high rate of neurological deficits likely results from a mechanical mechanism of the neurological injury.

## ID: AGS26/799

## P0200: Does adult spinal deformity surgery accelerate hip osteoarthritis?

David Polly

1

, Kari Odland

1

, Lawrence Lenke

2

, Christopher Shaffrey

3

, Kenneth Cheung

4

, Sigurd Berven

5

, Yong Qiu

6

, Yukihiro Matsuyama

7

, Ferran Pellise

8

, Jonathan N. Sembrano

1

, Benny Dahl

9

, Ahmet Alanay

10

, Stephen Lewis

11

, AO Spine Knowledge Forum Deformity

12

1

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

2

Columbia University, New York, United States

3

Duke University Medical Center, Durham, United States

4

The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, Hong Kong

5

University of California San Francisco, San Fransisco, United States

6

Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

7

Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Japan

8

Hospital Universitari de la Vall d’Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

9

Rigshospitalet, University of Copenhagen, Copenhagen, Denmark

10

Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

11

University Health Network, Toronto, Canada

12

AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: Hip-spine syndrome is widely acknowledged, but the impact of extensive spinal fusion on the hip joints remains debatable. It is uncertain whether this issue arises from preexisting biological factors or altered biomechanics. Furthermore, there is a lack of high-quality, international prospective cohort studies to thoroughly evaluate this concern. Material and Methods: Retrospective data analysis from international multicenter prospective cohort study (PEEDS) of patients > 60 years old indicated for > 5 level spinal fusion. All patients had baseline radiographs and patient reported outcomes. Preop, 2 yr and 5 yr imaging was obtained and analysed for presence of hip arthroplasty. Results: Global preop prevalence was 16/187 (8.5%), 2 yr incidence 2/153 (1.3%), 5 yr 5/88 (5.6%). Europe preop prevalence was 2/30 (6.7%), 2yr incidence 0/24, 5 yr 1/8 (12.5%); Asia preop prevalence was 3/64 (4.7%), 2 yr incidence was 2/63 (3.2%), 5 yr 1/49 (2%); North America preop prevalence was 11/93 (11.8%), 2 yr incidence 0/66, 5 yrs 3/31 (9.7%). There were noted gender differences in prevalence, preop prevalence for women was 14/148, (9.5%), preop prevalence for men was 2/39, (5.1%); incidence at 2 yr for women was 1/122, (0.8%), for men was 1/31 (3.2%); 5 yr incidence for women was 5/77 (6.5%), no new THA was recorded for men at 5 years. Previous ASD studies have reported varied prevalence of THA (Sing: 3.35%, Weinstein: 4.2%, Bedard: 4.6%, Kress: 16.4%, Buckland: 20.2%) The overall prevalence rate in the US was reported by Kremers as 0.83%. In this study, the preop prevalence is higher than the general US population based on Kremers data. Conclusion: In this retrospective analysis of international prospective multicenter study data, the preop prevalence of THA is on par with other reported studies and higher than general prevalence of the US population, suggesting a potential role of ASD in hip osteoarthritis prior to spinal fusion.

## P0201: Evaluation of multimodal analgesia with specialist nurses as the main body in adult patients with degenerative scoliosis during perioperative period-A multicenter prospective controlled study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Li Huo

1

, Qian Cheng

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: To explore the analgesic effect of multi-modal analgesia program with specialist nurses as the main body on adult degenerative scoliosis patients during perioperative period, and to observe the applicability of multi-modal analgesia program, so as to provide scientific guidance for clinical nursing and provide high-quality nursing services for patients. To explore the role and significance of specialist nurses in pain management system. Material and Methods: A prospective, non-randomized controlled study was conducted in 69 patients with adult degenerative scoliosis diagnosed preoperatively in three hospitals between January 2016 and June 2018. According to the exclusion criteria, 15 patients were excluded from the study. Fifty-four patients underwent posterior long-segment fixed orthopedics were selected, including 30 patients in the experimental group, 13 males and 17 females, aged 54-70 years, with an average of aa+bb years. In the control group, 24 patients were treated with conventional on-demand analgesia, 11 males and 13 females, aged 55-68 years, with an average of aa+bb years. The VAS score, Ramsay sedation score (4, 8, 12, 24, 48 hours after operation), S-AI state anxiety scale score (36 hours before and after operation), patient satisfaction rate (satisfaction rate = (satisfaction + general)/total number of cases (100%) and recovery after operation (first functional forging) were compared between the two groups. To evaluate the effect of multimodal analgesia with nurses as the main body on adult patients with degenerative scoliosis during perioperative period. Results: Visual analogue score (VAS) and sedation score of multimodal analgesia group were significantly lower than those of routine nursing group at each time point after operation (p < 0.05). The satisfaction rate of postoperative analgesia in experimental group was significantly better than that in control group. There was no significant difference in S-AI state anxiety scale score between experimental group and control group before operation, and there was a score of 36 hours after operation. Significant difference (p < 0.05). For patients with multimodal analgesia, the anxiety score of 36 hours after operation was significantly lower than that before operation (p < 0.05); there was no significant difference between 36 hours after operation and that before operation in the routine nursing group (p > 0.05). The time of first functional exercise in the experimental group was significantly earlier than that in the control group (p < 0.05), and the length of hospitalization was shorter than that in the control group (p < 0.05). The incidence of adverse reactions in the experimental group was 1.7%, and that in the control group was 8.4%. There was a significant difference between the two groups (p < 0.05). Conclusion: Nurse-based multi-modal analgesia can significantly relieve acute pain, improve patient satisfaction, reduce the incidence of adverse reactions, and optimize the quality of medical and nursing services in adult patients with degenerative scoliosis during perioperative period. It can also alleviate anxiety and psychological pressure of patients and shorten hospitalization. Time, speed up rehabilitation exercise, prevent bedridden complications, and promote rapid recovery of patients.

## ID: AGS26/834

## P0202: Adult spinal deformity correction durability: does the lifespan of the construct vary among different osteotomy techniques?

Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Schahin Salmanian

2

, Dana Saleh

1

, Adrian Urdaneta

1

, Melissa Touadi BS

2

, Vito Evola BS

3

, Srujan Kopparapu

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

2

USF Health Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

3

Dr. Kiran C. Patel College of Osteopathic Medicine, Fort Lauderdale, United States

Introduction: Osteotomies play a critical role in correcting adult spinal deformity (ASD). However, as surgeons perform more aggressive osteotomies, the constructs used must withstand greater biomechanical loads, which can increase the risk of complications such as rod or screw fracture, pseudoarthrosis, and screw pull-out. Moreover, long and rigid constructs predispose patients to proximal junctional kyphosis/failure (PJK/PJF) and other mechanical complications, many of which may ultimately require revision surgery. This study evaluates a cohort of ASD patients treated with four different osteotomy techniques and compares their time to revision. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 146 patients who underwent posterior fusions for ASD correction between 2016 and 2022, considering only patients with ≥ 1 year of follow-up. Patients were stratified based on the osteotomy that contributed most to sagittal alignment correction: interbody disc osteotomy (IDO), pedicle subtraction osteotomy (PSO), anterior column realignment (ACR), or Ponte osteotomy. Revision-free survival time was analyzed using Kaplan-Meier curves, with follow-up extending from the index surgery to either revision or last available follow-up. Group differences were evaluated using the log-rank test. Results: A total of 146 patients underwent ASD correction with IDO (n = 23), PSO (n = 21), ACR (n = 32), or Ponte (n = 70) osteotomies. Groups were comparable in age, BMI, preoperative disability, and preoperative spinopelvic parameters. Kaplan-Meier analysis of revision-free survival up to 1,500 days (50 months) after surgery demonstrated no significant differences among the four osteotomy groups (Log-rank [Mantel-Cox] χ2 = 2.483, df = 3, p = 0.478). The IDO group maintained the most stable curve, with the highest probability of remaining revision-free during the first 18 months postoperatively. The first reoperations occurred in the Ponte group, followed sequentially by the ACR and PSO groups. Within the first year, 1 reoperation (4.8%) was recorded in the PSO group, 2 (6.3%) in ACR, and 6 (8.6%) in Ponte (p = .516). Conclusion: Although differences did not reach statistical significance, IDO demonstrated the highest early revision-free survival, most likely due to the more harmonious correction achieved, while Ponte osteotomies showed the earliest failures, in accordance with the lowest corrections achieved by such techniques, followed by ACR and PSO. These findings highlight the potential benefits of IDO in maintaining alignment stability and reducing the risk of early reoperations. Larger, long-term studies are needed to validate these observations and guide osteotomy selection for optimizing construct durability and patient outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/845

## P0203: Prediction of positional spinopelvic parameters in a young, healthy population is improved using the sacro-acetabular angle

Chien Yew Kow

1,2,3

, Farbod Moghaddam

1,4,5

, May Choi

1,5

, Fred Nicholls

1,2,5

1

Cumming School of Medicine, University of Calgary, Calgary, Alberta, Canada

2

University of Calgary Spine Program, Calgary, Alberta, Canada

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Auckland City Hospital, Auckland, New Zealand

4

Department of Obstetrics and Gynaecology, University of British Columbia

5

McCaig Institute of Bone and Joint Health, Calgary, Alberta, Canada

Introduction: With increasing awareness of the importance of normal spinal alignment and its restoration in spine surgery, an increasing number of spinopelvic parameters have been identified, with variable importance. Many of these parameters are positional and involved in compensatory posture. While restoration of these angles is crucial to achieving good outcomes in the correction of spinal deformity, morphologic properties have failed to reliably predict positional properties either intra or post operatively. One of the most important morphologic parameters remains pelvic incidence (PI), which has been demonstrated to be predictive of expected lumbar lordosis (LL) and L1 pelvic angle (L1PA). The morphology leading to rotation of the pelvis around the femoral heads however, remains incompletely defined. The sacro-acetabular angle (SAA) is a morphologic parameter defined by the angle subtended by a line extending along the S1 superior endplate and a line connecting the anterior and posterior walls of the acetabulum. Version and inclination of the acetabulum define the safe arc of motion of the hip joint, necessary for both upright posture and mobilization. The version of the pelvis is limited by this orientation of the acetabulum and its articulation with the femoral head. We postulate that SAA can be used to help predict the preferred position and tilt of the pelvis, which can in turn be used to predict other positional parameters. Material and Methods: Full length EOS images of healthy, asymptomatic adult volunteers between 20 and 40 years old from the community were performed and segmented using the Keops® online platform. Subjects’ demographic and radiographic parameters were measured and recorded. Clinical characteristics included age, gender, BMI and ethnicity. Radiolographic spinopelvic parameters measured include SAA, PI, L1PA, pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), LL, and Roussouly morphotype. SAA was determined using the sagittal image, and calculated as an average of bilateral SAA. Correlation between SAA and subjects’ demographics and spinopelvic parameters were analyzed using linear regression analyses. Results: One hundred and eighty-seven subjects met initial inclusion criteria. Mean for SAA was 73.6 ± 8.9. There is significant correlation between SAA and SS (SS = 0.71*SAA -12.5; r2 = 0.64) (Figure 1). Using stepwise linear regression, prediction of SS is improved when SAA and PI were both used as continuous variables (SS = 0.31*PI +0.46*SAA -9.2; r2 = 0.71). Conclusion: This initial analysis shows promise that SAA can be utilized in conjunction with PI to predict normative sacral slope. This is the first step to better predict expected positional parameters using morphological parameters, and improve our understanding of overall spinal alignment as a continuous rather than categorical transformation. The significance of restoration to expected positional parameters still needs further study.

 

## ID: AGS26/939

## P0204: Deformity correction through minimally invasive anterior column release vs conventional posterior osteotomies: a study examining length of stay, disability, and discharge outcomes in a cohort with over one year of follow-up

Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Schahin Salmanian

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Ahmad Alkadri

2

, Jade Vanderpool

2

, Jillian Baader

2

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Patrick Kim

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

2

USF Health Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Corrective spinal deformity procedures often require resection of posterior or middle column elements, leading to significant surgical morbidity, prolonged hospitalization, and increased complication risks. Anterior column release (ACR), performed via a retroperitoneal approach, has been proposed as a less invasive alternative that may reduce hospital length of stay (LOS). However, evidence supporting this potential advantage remains limited. This study evaluates a large cohort of adult spinal deformity (ASD) patients to compare LOS and functional outcomes between ACR and traditional posterior approaches. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 146 patients who underwent posterior spinal fusion extending from L2 or above to the pelvis for ASD correction between 2016 and 2022, including only those with ≥ 1 year of follow-up. Demographic data, LOS, and disability scores were collected. Comparisons were first made between the ACR group and the overall non-ACR cohort. To better contextualize ACR’s performance relative to other osteotomy techniques, subgroup analyses were performed based on the highest-order osteotomy performed during correction. Results: Of 146 patients, 32 underwent ACR and 114 underwent non-ACR approaches. Baseline characteristics, including age, BMI, and preoperative Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), were similar between groups. ACR patients had a higher proportion of males and significantly shorter instrumented constructs compared with non-ACR patients (6.97 ± 2.41 vs. 9.04 ± 2.86 levels, p < 0.001). LOS was comparable between the ACR and non-ACR groups (9.88 ± 4.93 vs. 8.87 ± 4.27 days, p = 0.400). Further subgroup analyses by osteotomy type confirmed these findings; LOS for ACR remained slightly longer (9.88 ± 4.93 days) compared with other osteotomy groups, but the differences were not statistically significant (p = 0.680). Postoperative ODI scores and ODI changes were similar between groups (p = 0.300 and p = 0.294, respectively). Most patients in both groups were discharged home (p = 0.725). Conclusion: ACR is an effective alternative to traditional posterior approaches for correcting thoracolumbar deformities that require fusion from the pelvis to L2 or above. While minimally invasive strategies are typically associated with shorter LOS, our analysis demonstrates that this benefit does not extend to patients undergoing ACR, likely due to the higher frequency of staged procedures in this population. At one-year follow-up, functional outcomes were comparable between ACR and non-ACR cohorts, suggesting similar overall recovery despite differences in surgical technique.

## ID: AGS26/962

## P0205: Paraspinal muscle quality as a predictor of proximal junctional kyphosis

Puya Alikhani

1

, Dana Saleh

2

, Daven Mejica

3

, Ahmad Alkadri

2

, John Lingam

2

, Jillian Baader

3

, Diego Soto Rubio

3

, Mohsen Rostami

2

1

university of south florida, tampa, United States

2
University of South Florida, Tampa, United States, 
3
University of South Florida, Tampa, United States
Introduction: Proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) remains a significant complication following adult spinal deformity correction, with rates ranging from 20-40%. The role of paraspinal muscle quality in PJK development has been understudied despite its potential importance in maintaining spinal alignment. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study comparing 22 patients who developed PJK with 28 patients who did not develop PJK following four levels fusion surgery for adult spine deformity. Key measures using pre-operative MRI included the Sarcopenia Index (total psoas area / vertebral body area) at the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) and L4 levels, and Goutallier classification (grades 0-4) to quantify fat infiltration in the erector spinae and multifidus muscles at the UIV, L4/5, and L5/S1 levels. Statistical comparisons were made using the Mann-Whitney U test. Results: No statistically significant differences were found between cases (n = 22) and controls (n = 28) for any measured variable. The Sarcopenia Index at L4 was nearly identical between groups (Cases: 1.78 ± 0.65 vs. Controls: 1.80 ± 0.53, p = 0.938). Similarly, comparisons of psoas muscle cross-sectional area at the UIV and L4 levels showed no significant differences (all p-values > 0.18). Analysis of Goutallier grades revealed no significant differences in fat infiltration at any spinal level or muscle group (all p-values > 0.18), though both groups demonstrated the expected anatomical pattern of increasing fat infiltration in the lower lumbar levels. Conclusion: These findings suggest that other biomechanical, surgical, or patient-specific factors may play a more dominant role in the pathogenesis of this complication. The findings indicate that routine screening for sarcopenia alone may not be sufficient for PJK/PJF risk stratification.

## ID: AGS26/971

## P0206: Can postoperative changes in pelvic incidence occur after adult spine deformity surgery? When do they occur, and what factors influence them?

Marco Manzetti

1,2

, Alberto Ruffilli

1,2

, Giovanni Viroli

1,2

, Matteo Traversari

1

, Marco Ialuna

1

, Cesare Faldini

1,2

1

Istituto Ortopedico Rizzoli, Clinica Ortopedica e Traumatologica I, Bologna, Italy

2

Department of Biomedical and Neuromotor Science - DIBINEM, Bologna, Italy

Introduction: Restoring appropriate sagittal balance is a fundamental goal in adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. Pelvic incidence (PI) forms the anatomical basis for sagittal realignment and has traditionally been considered a fixed anatomical parameter. However, recent evidence suggests that PI may undergo postoperative changes, potentially affecting the achieved sagittal alignment. This study aimed to review the literature on postoperative changes in PI following ASD surgery and to identify potential radiological and surgical predictors of such modifications. Material and Methods: Studies reporting postoperative PI changes in ASD patients were systematically reviewed. Inclusion criteria required use of standing full-spine or full-body X-rays for spinopelvic measurements. The primary outcome was the prevalence of significant PI changes (> 5°). Subgroup and metanalyses were performed to identify radiological and surgical predictors, including sacropelvic fixation, fusion length, preoperative PI value, and sagittal vertical axis (SVA). Results: Thirteen studies (1055 patients) met the inclusion criteria. All included studies assessed spinopelvic parameters using standing full-spine X-rays, except for one that used full-body standing X-rays, demonstrating moderate\high reproducibility in PI measurement (ICC: 0.71-0.96). While the mean absolute PI value remained stable postoperatively, 46.3% of patients experienced significant changes (> 5°). Factors associated with significant PI variations included preoperative lumbar lordosis (p  =  0.02 I2  =  96.2%) preoperative PI-LL mismatch (p  =  0.005 I2  =  95.1%), and preoperative SVA (p  =  0.03 I2  =  97.6%). Sacropelvic fixation reduced PI instability, whereas prolonged anterior imbalance and compensatory pelvic retroversion increased the likelihood of PI shifts. Interestingly, patients with extreme PI values-whether high or low-were prone to postoperative changes in PI, with an increase in low PI or decrease in high PI. Long-term follow-ups indicated that PI changes could not recover or increase in patients without sacropelvic fixation and in patients with higher preoperative PI values if they experience a decrease in PI postoperatively. Conclusion: The current evidence challenges the traditional view of pelvic incidence as a fixed parameter in ASD surgery, especially in patients with marked sagittal imbalance or extreme PI values. Recognizing that PI may change under specific preoperative or surgical conditions could help refine alignment targets and optimize postoperative realignment in selected subgroups of patients.

## ID: AGS26/1209

## P0207: Updating the coronal imbalance classification for degenerative scoliosis: a relook into the type A subtype

Yong Qiu

1

, Yanjie Xu

1

, Zhen Liu

1

, Zezhang Zhu

1

, Benlong Shi

1

, Li Jie Li Jie

1

1

Division of Spine Surgery, Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Affiliated Hospital of Medical School, Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

Introduction: The purpose of this study is to propose a refined coronal imbalance classification with subgroup analysis of degenerative scoliosis (DS) patients with Type A coronal pattern and to ascertain its implications on postoperative coronal imbalance (CIB). Material and Methods: A total of 239 DS patients who underwent spinal correction surgery were recruited in this study. Patients were divided into Type A, B, and C based on the Nanjing coronal imbalance classification system. Patients with Type A coronal imbalance were further divided into three subtypes according to the coronal balance distance (CBD) and the tendency of trunk inclination: Type Aa, CBD ≤ 1 cm; Type Ab, CBD > 1 cm, and C7 Plumb Line (C7PL) shifted to the concave side of the curve; Type Ac, CBD > 1 cm and C7PL shifted to the convex side. The Scoliosis Research Society (SRS)-22 questionnaire scores were analyzed, and the incidence of postoperative CIB was compared across groups. Results: The incidence of postoperative CIB is 23% (32/139) in the Type A group, 18% (11/60) in the Type B group, and 58% (23/40) in the Type C group (p < 0.001). Among patients with Type A coronal alignment, 54 patients were Type Aa, 46 patients were Type Ab, and 39 patients were Type Ac. After surgery, 32 patients had a postoperative coronal imbalance, with 5 patients in Type Aa (9%), 5 patients in Type Ab (11%), and 22 patients in Type Ac (56%). Postoperative coronal malalignment was found to be more prevalent in Type Ac patients (p < 0.001). Conclusion: Patients with Type Ac coronal imbalance are at greater risk of postoperative CIB following surgery compared with patients with Type Aa and Type Ab alignment. The modified coronal imbalance classification highlights the high risk of CIB in Type Ac, similar to the Type C coronal imbalance pattern. These findings provide a more comprehensive delineation of coronal alignment phenotypes and introduce a refined system for stratifying the risk of postoperative CIB.

## ID: AGS26/1212

## P0208: Modified intradiscal osteotomy: a versatile technique in adult spinal deformity surgery

Eduardo Quiroz Ruiz

1

, Alberto Perez

1

, Diana Chávez

1

1

Hospital Ángeles, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity is a complex entity which can involve sagittal and coronal malalignment. An increase in ASD prevalence has been reported, being > 60% in population > 60 years old. Both sagittal and coronal malalignment had been associated with less health-related quality of life. ASD surgery stills a challenge, multiple improvements in osteotomy techniques have been described mainly for sagittal balance correction. Here we describe a modification to the intradiscal osteotomy technique described by Ramey, through a combined posterior and lateral approach aiming a non-structural intersomatic fusion, achieving a partial correction of the main curve avoiding postoperative malalignment and also increasing segmental lordosis. Material and Methods: It is a technical report in which we present a modification to the intradiscal osteotomy technique. The objective of this modification is to optimize the coronal alignment through a non-structural intersomatic fusion, with a less invasive technique and also gaining lordosis in the intervertebral segment. Results: A midline incision is made and the muscular plane is dissected until the facet joints of the targeted level are located. A total facetectomy is then performed. Subsequently, using de fluoroscopy, the intervertebral disc of the target segment is located in lateral projection. The anterior and posterior borders of the vertebral bodies and the disc space are marked. A 2 cm transverse incision is made, the external oblique fascia is incised and blunt dissection is used to dissect the external oblique, internal oblique and transverse muscle until reaching the fascia transversalis which we also incised to enter the retroperitoneum. The initial dilator is placed on a previously planned trajectory according to patient anatomy, vertebral level and Moro classification of zones. Electrostimulation is performed through the dilator to verify that we are not close to or over any lumbar plexus branch. Once in a safe area, under fluoroscopic control, the initial dilator is impacted to reach the disc space. Then, the working sleeve is placed and the initial dilator is removed. A drill, disc rongeur and disc shaver are used to perform discectomy and preparation of end plates. Finally, the allograft or autograft selected according to the patient's characteristics is placed though the working sleeve with and impactor to achieve the non-structural intersomatic fusion without horizontalizing the endplates of the intervertebral segment. Conclusion: This modified technique is useful in adult spinal deformity surgery, in the coronal plane it allows us to perform a partial correction of the main curve without horizontaling completely the vertebral endplates of the segment. This is especially useful in the superior arch of the main curve in coronal curves where complete horizontalization of L4 coronal tilt is not achieved and we need to make a match between the fractional and the main curve to avoid postoperative over correction of the main curve and coronal malalignment. In the sagittal plane combining it with a Ponte or Smith-Peterson osteotomy we can achieve up to 5-10° of lordosis in the segment.

## ID: AGS26/1220

## P0209: Comparison of transverse process hooks versus pedicle screws at the upper instrumented vertebra in adult spinal deformity surgery involving three-column osteotomy

Puya Alikhani

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Anjali Pradhan

1

, Eric Cohen

2

, Patrick Kim

1

, Albert Brotgandel

2

, Dana Saleh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

2

USF Health Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: In adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery involving three-column osteotomy and pelvic fixation, the fixation technique at the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) may influence junctional integrity and long-term outcomes. This study compares transverse process hook (TPH) versus pedicle screw (PS) fixation at the UIV. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on 132 ASD patients who underwent three-column osteotomy and pelvic fixation with a minimum 2-year follow-up. Patients were categorized by UIV fixation method: TPH or PS. Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) including Visual Analog Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and SF-36 were recorded preoperatively and at 6, 12, and 24 months postoperatively. Rates of proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK), proximal junctional failure (PJF), and reoperation were compared. Results: PROMs were comparable between the TPH and PS groups at all time points. PJK was observed in 25.7% of patients in the TPH group and 31.1% in the PS group (p < 0.05). PJF occurred in 12.0% (TPH) versus 17.0% (PS) (p = 0.21). Reoperation rates were high in both groups, 31.8% for TPH and 37.0% for PS, with no statistically significant difference (p = 0.38). Conclusion: While PROMs were similar between groups, the use of pedicle screws at the UIV was associated with a significantly higher rate of PJK compared to hooks. These findings suggest that transverse process hooks may help reduce junctional complications. However, this did not translate into a lower reoperation rate, highlighting the multifactorial nature of surgical failure in complex ASD reconstructions.

## ID: AGS26/1233

## P0210: Comparison of the predictive efficacy of the C - GAP score for postoperative mechanical complications in patients with degenerative and idiopathic adult spinal deformity

Chunxiao Chen

1

, Zhen Liu

1

1

Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing, China

Introduction: Compare the predictive efficacy of the C-GAP (Chinese ethnicity-tailored GAP, C-GAP) score for postoperative mechanical complications in patients with degenerative and idiopathic adult spine deformity (ASD).
