Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 98 patients with adult spinal deformity (ASD) who underwent posterior spinal orthopedic internal fixation in our hospital from January 2017 to December 2022 and were followed up for more than 1.5 years. Among them, there were 16 males and 82 females, with an average age of 43.47 ± 14.73 years and an average follow - up time of 21.47 ± 9.62 months. According to the patients’ past medical history and imaging features, the ASD patients were divided into the idiopathic adult spinal deformity group (IS group, 47 cases) and the degenerative adult spinal deformity group (DS group, 51 cases). The immediate postoperative sacral slope (SS), pelvic tilt (PT), pelvic incidence (PI), lumbar lordosis (LL), distal lumbar lordosis (DLL), global tilt (GT), sagittal vertical axis (SVA), and thoracic kyphosis (TK) of the patients were measured by standing full - spine anteroposterior and lateral x - ray films, and the C - GAP score was calculated. The IS group and the DS group were further sub - grouped according to the C - GAP score: the coordinated group P (C - GAP score 0 - 2 points), the moderately uncoordinated group MD (C - GAP score 3 - 6 points), and the severely uncoordinated group SD (C - GAP score 7 - 12 points). The occurrence of mechanical complications (MC) at the last follow - up was recorded, including proximal/distal junction kyphosis (PJK/DJK), proximal/distal junction failure (PJF/DJF), and internal fixation failure. The predictive efficacy of the C - GAP score for the IS and DS groups was evaluated by the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC) (area under the curve, AUC). Results: Among the 98 ASD patients, a total of 25 cases (28.4%) developed postoperative mechanical complications, including 21 cases of PJK, 2 cases of PJF, and 2 cases of DJF. In the IS group with 47 patients, the P, MD, and SD subgroups included 27, 20, and 0 patients respectively, and the incidence rates of MC were 22.2% and 15.0% respectively (p = 0.713). The C - GAP score had no predictive ability for postoperative complications in the IS group (AUC = 0.443, p = 0.598). In the DS group with 51 patients, the P, MD, and SD subgroups included 18, 24, and 9 patients respectively, and the incidence rates of MC were 11.1%, 37.5%, and 55.6% respectively (p = 0.043). The C - GAP score had good predictive ability for postoperative complications in the DS group (AUC = 0.701, p = 0.022). Conclusion: Compared with idiopathic adult spinal deformity, the C-GAP score is more applicable to the prediction of postoperative mechanical complications in degenerative adult spinal deformity. The prediction of postoperative MC in patients with idiopathic ASD using the C-GAP score needs further improvement.

## ID: AGS26/1244

## P0211: Paraspinal sarcopenia in patients with degenerative spinal deformity: implication in clinical outcomes

Abdukahar Kiram

1

, Ming Wang

1

, Li Jie Li Jie

1

, Yong Qiu

1

, Zezhang Zhu

1

, Zhen Liu

1

1

Division of Spine Surgery, Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, The Affiliated Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing, China

Introduction: Recent studies suggest that paraspinal sarcopenia is distinct from systemic sarcopenia and influences the clinical manifestations of patients with degenerative lumbar stenosis (DLS). It was found that systemic sarcopenia is more prevalent and paraspinal muscle degeneration was more severe in patients with degenerative spinal deformity (DSD). However, the prevalence and clinical impact of paraspinal sarcopenia and its correlation with systemic sarcopenia remains unclear. Material and Methods: A total of 106 patients with DSD and 157 patients with degenerative lumbar stenosis were prospectively included in our intuition. Systemic sarcopenia was diagnosed according to diagnostic criteria recommended by the Asian Working Group for Sarcopenia (AWGS). hand grip strength, 6-meter walk test and 5-time chair stand test. Paraspinal muscle fat infiltration rate (FI%) and cross-sectional area were measured in MRI scans. The paraspinal muscle extensor endurance test (ET) was performed at baseline according to Ito test. The paraspinal muscle maximal exert (MVE) was measured using hand-held dynamometry. One-quarter of patients with significant multifidus muscle atrophy were defined as paraspinal sarcopenia in DSD patients. Degenerative characteristics of DSD including facet joint degeneration, Clinical measures of HRQoL included the SRS-22 and visual analog scale scores (VAS) for back pain and leg pain. The Spearman's correlation coefficient was used to examine the correlation between the paraspinal muscle measurements and sarcopenic values or clinical outcome scores. Results: A total of 263 patients enrolled in this study. The average age (61.8 ± 6.4 vs. 59.6 ± 5.3, p > 0.05) did not differ between the DSD group and the DLS group. A total of 21.7% of DLS and 51.9% of DSD were diagnosed as generalized sarcopenia. The correlation strength between sarcopenia measurements (Grip strength) and paraspinal muscle parameters including MF FI% and ET (rho = 0.31, p < 0.05), MVE (rho = 0.32, p < 0.05) low in DSD patients. The multifidus muscle fat infiltration rate (MF FI%) of DSD patients was 34.8 ± 18.6%, while 22.6 ± 12.2% in DLS patients. One-quarter of DSD patients demonstrated approximately 40% fat infiltration in MF muscle. MF FI% showed a negative correlation with VAS-back and SRS-22 scores in DSD patients. DSD patients with paraspinal sarcopenia had lower endurance time (11.8 vs. 21.5s) and MVE (76.5 vs. 88.0N) and poor HRQoL (SRS-22, 2.4 ± 0.9 vs. 2.6 ± 0.8, p = 0.035) compared to non-paraspinal sarcopenia patients. Conclusion: Our findings offer substantial evidence for site-specific muscle loss of paraspinal muscle that is different from systemic sarcopenia in patients with DSD. DSD patients with paraspinal sarcopenia were characterized by loss of paraspinal function, impaired HRQoL. These findings provide new insights into the unique pathophysiology and screening DSD patients with poor HRQoL, as well as help to develop personalized muscle augmentation strategies.

## ID: AGS26/1383

## P0212: Endovascular management of pedicle screw-related aortic injuries: a case series and literature review

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Santiago Rosales

1

, Juan Ramon

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Aortic injuries in spine surgery are rare but potentially life-threatening complications. This study presents our experience managing aortic injuries caused by pedicle screw placement in spinal surgery. Case Presentation: Case 1: A 29-year-old woman underwent T3-L4 instrumentation for scoliosis at an external institution. Six months postoperatively, she presented to our institution with a lumbar surgical site infection, which was managed appropriately. One month after admission, she developed dyspnea, chest pain, and hypoxia. Imaging studies revealed pulmonary thromboembolism and, incidentally, a left T7 pedicle screw in contact with the descending aorta. Intravascular ultrasound confirmed a 5 mm deformation of the aortic wall without perforation. A multidisciplinary team decided to proceed with endovascular stent placement and pedicle screw removal. The procedure was successful, and the patient had an uneventful recovery. Case 2: A 37-year-old man sustained multiple vertebral fractures following a fall from a height of more than five meters. He underwent T7-T10 instrumentation at another institution. Four months later, he presented to our hospital with dyspnea and progressive functional decline. Imaging studies revealed a T7 pedicle screw eroding the aortic wall, with no other findings related to his initial symptoms. Intravascular ultrasound confirmed the diagnosis. The patient underwent endovascular stent placement and pedicle screw removal, following the same protocol as the first case. Results: In both cases, the affected pedicle screws were successfully removed and repositioned, and endovascular stents were placed without complications. Both patients recovered without complications and continue under routine follow-up, with no reported symptoms to date. Conclusions: Although rare, postoperative vascular complications from pedicle screws can lead to significant morbidity and may present a wide range of symptoms. Endovascular treatment, combined with pedicle screw removal or repositioning, is the preferred management strategy. Based on our experience, intravascular ultrasound is a valuable tool for confirming diagnosis and guiding treatment.

## ID: AGS26/1405

## P0213: Risk factors for rod fracture in adult spinal deformity: emphasis on postoperative spinopelvic parameters

Mirbahador Athari

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is associated with pain, disability, and diminished quality of life. Corrective surgery has improved outcomes, yet implant-related complications such as rod fracture remain a significant challenge, often necessitating revision surgery. While prior studies have identified risk factors including body mass index (BMI), osteotomy, and implant characteristics, the influence of postoperative spinopelvic alignment on rod durability remains unclear. This study aimed to identify demographic, surgical, and particularly postoperative radiographic predictors of rod fracture. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study of ASD patients undergoing posterior spinal fusion across ≥ 5 instrumented levels at a single academic center (2013-2023). Patients with tumor, infection, trauma, or pathologic fractures were excluded. Demographic, operative, and radiographic data were collected from a prospectively maintained database. Radiographs were analyzed preoperatively, postoperatively, and at 1 year. Rod fracture was defined as radiographic evidence of rod discontinuity. Patient-reported outcomes were assessed with the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Logistic regression was performed to identify independent predictors, with model calibration and discrimination evaluated using the Hosmer-Lemeshow test and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). Results: A total of 138 patients were included, with 41 (29.7%) developing rod fracture during follow-up. Demographics, including age, sex, BMI, ASA class, and smoking status, were not independently associated with fracture. Preoperative radiographic measures showed no significant associations. Postoperative spinopelvic parameters, however, were predictive. Greater lumbar lordosis (OR 1.12, p = 0.034), increased pelvic tilt (OR 1.17, p = 0.028), and larger PI-LL mismatch (OR 1.09, p = 0.050) were significantly associated with increased fracture risk. Larger rod diameter was protective (OR 0.0093, p = 0.014). Reduction in modified pelvic femoral angle showed a trend toward decreased risk (p = 0.069). The model demonstrated good calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.64) and acceptable discrimination (AUC = 0.77). Conclusion: Rod fracture occurred in nearly one-third of ASD patients, a higher rate than previously reported. Unlike prior studies emphasizing demographic and operative factors, this analysis highlights the critical role of postoperative sagittal alignment in influencing rod durability. Increased lumbar lordosis, pelvic tilt, and PI-LL mismatch were independent predictors of fracture, while larger rod diameter conferred protection. These findings underscore the biomechanical contribution of sagittal balance to rod stress and suggest that optimizing postoperative alignment, alongside robust construct selection, may mitigate fracture risk.

## ID: AGS26/1447

## P0214: Evaluation of segmental lordosis following lumbar PSO and its impact on proximal junction failure

Lane McCoy

1

, Christopher Diaz

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1
Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States, 
2
Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States
Introduction: Proximal Junctional Failure (PJF) is a common long-term complication in patients undergoing ASD surgery. Various factors have been identified as potential risk factors for PJF such as obesity, inadequate postoperative sagittal balance, advanced age, and osteoporosis. This study aims to evaluate the impact of sagittal alignment parameters and segmental lordosis distribution on the risk of PJF in upper thoracic fusion patients instrumented to the pelvis who underwent a PSO. Material and Methods: The cohort consisted of 67 ASD patients who underwent upper thoracic fusion (T1-T8) to the sacrum with PSO. Patients were grouped based on if they underwent revision surgery for PJK (PJF). Preoperative and postoperative sagittal parameters, including thoracic kyphosis (TK), pelvic tilt (PT), lumbar lordosis (LL), C7 sagittal vertical axis (SVA), C2 SVA, L1 pelvic angle (L1PA), alignment using L1PA and T4PA, segmental lordosis of each lumbar level, and Roussouly types, were analyzed. To determine segmental lordosis, we measured from the upper endplate of each vertebra. Statistical correlations were made to identify significant differences in these parameters between patients with and without PJF. Results: Of the 67 ASD patients, 30 had 2-year follow up. The percentage of patients who developed PJF within 2 years was 13.4%. Patients with L3 PSOs who had less change in LL from preoperative to postoperative (17.4 vs 39.8, p = 0.006), and had less postoperative LL (38.5° vs 59.2°, p < 0.001) were more likely to develop PJF. Increasing segmental lordosis at L2-L3 increased the risk of developing PJF after a PSO (PJF:7.25, No PJF 2.19, p = 0.0395). Assessment of other sagittal parameters, including TK, PT, C7 SVA, C2 SVA, L1PA, L1PA alignment (aligned vs malaligned), L1PA + T4PA alignment, Roussouly types, or segmental lordosis at other levels; these factors did not show any significant correlation with PJF occurrence. Conclusion: In patients undergoing ASD surgery extending to the upper thoracic region, inadequate correction of lumbar lordosis following a PSO increases the risk of revision surgery for PJK. However, restoring this lumbar lordosis more proximal to the PSO within the lumbar spine may also elevate the risk of PJF.

## ID: AGS26/1475

## P0215: Intensive care unit admission after three column osteotomy for adult spinal deformity is associated with inferior perioperative course and negates patient-reported outcome measure improvements

Christopher Lai

1

, Bradley Hammoor

1

, Joshua Coan

1

, Benjamin Varieur

1

, Jordan Gasho

1

, Theresa Chua

1

, Marcos Gonzalez

1

, Stuart Hershman

1

1

Massachusetts General Hospital Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Boston, United States

Introduction: Three column osteotomies (3CO) are useful in correcting stiff deformities in adult spinal deformity (ASD). However, they increase surgical risk, and patients are often admitted to the ICU postoperatively. The effect of ICU admission after 3CO on perioperative course and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) remains unclear. We evaluate 1) perioperative outcomes and 2) changes in PROMs between patients admitted to ICU versus floor following 3CO. Material and Methods: ASD patients undergoing 3CO from 2016-2023 with 2-year follow-up were stratified by postoperative admission to ICU vs. Floor. 1) Multivariable logistic/linear regressions models assessed the impact of ICU admission on inpatient complications, length of stay (LOS), discharge disposition (facility vs home), and late ambulation (ambulation after postoperative day 2). 2) Mixed-effects models compared changes in PROMIS domain scores within and between ICU and floor groups across baseline, 1-year, and 2-years. Significance level was set at p < 0.05. Results: 196 patients met criteria and were grouped into postoperative ICU (N = 64) and Floor (N = 132). There were no significant differences in demographics and comorbidities between groups. 1) ICU patients had higher rates of any inpatient complication [86.4% vs. 51.5%, p < 0.001], including non-surgical site infection, cardiac/pulmonary event, respiratory failure, anemia, hypotension/tachycardia, and renal injury (all p < 0.05). LOS [9(6.3) vs. 6(4) days, p < 0.001], discharge to a facility [73.4% vs. 56.8%, p = 0.04], late ambulation [83.6% vs. 49.2%, p < 0.001], and transfusion rate [71.2% vs 50.8%, p = 0.010] were higher in ICU patients. Readmission and reoperation rates were not significantly different at 90-days and 2-years. On multivariable regression, ICU [β 3.2 (1.5-5.0), p < 0.001] and inpatient complication [β 1.7 (0.08-3.3), p = 0.041)] significantly predicted increased LOS. ICU [OR 5.05 (2.22-12.6), p < 0.001] and EBL [OR 2.32 (1.17-4.67), p = 0.016] significantly predicted inpatient complications. Finally, ICU [OR 3.55 (1.6-8.40), p = 0.003] was a significant predictor for late ambulation. 2) Baseline PROMIS were similar between groups. Global Physical score was higher in Floor at 1-year [37.9(9.3) vs. 33.4(6.8), p = 0.01]; Global Mental score [42.2(9.8) vs. 37.4(10.1), p = 0.04] and Physical Function score [34.8(9.1) vs. 30.1(7.2), p = 0.01] were higher in Floor at 2-years. Mixed effects modeling examined changes in PROMIS over time. Within ICU, PROMIS did not significantly improve across any time interval (p > 0.05). Within Floor, Global Physical scores improved from baseline to 1-year [4.14(1.83-6.46), p < 0.001] and baseline to 2-years [3.71(1.24-6.18), p < 0.001]. Global Mental improved from baseline to 1-year [2.86(0.38-5.35), p = 0.015]. Physical Function scores improved from baseline to 1-year [2.93(0.88-4.98), p = 0.001] and baseline to 2-years [2.81(0.060-5.01), p = 0.005]. When comparing ICU and Floor groups over these intervals, the Floor group demonstrated significantly greater improvements in Global Physical scores from baseline to 1-year. There were no significant differences in the amount of PROM improvement between the ICU and No ICU groups at other timepoints. Conclusion: 1) ICU admission after 3CO is associated with increased inpatient complication, longer hospitalization, and delayed mobilization. Routine ICU placement should be avoided if not medically necessary to optimize hospital course. 2) Patients without ICU admission experience significant PROM improvements for up to 2-years while those with ICU admission fail to experience meaningful PROM improvements over time.

## ID: AGS26/1477

## P0216: Pelvic incidence determines spinal alignment in the sitting position

Atahan Durbas

1

, Han Jo Kim

1

, Robert Uzzo

1

, Gabrielle Dykhouse

1

, Tejas Subramanian

1

, Andrea Pezzi

1

, Luis Felipe Colon

1

, Farah Musharbash

1

, Jung Kee Mok

1

, Austin Kaidi

1

, Donghua Huang

1

, Zhan Wang

1

, Mihir Dekhne

1

, Michael Mazzucco

1

, Sereen Halayqeh

1

, Quante Singleton

1

, Kasra Araghi

1

, Gregory Kazarian

1

, Michael Kelly

2

, Jeffrey Hills

3

, Matthew Cunningham

1

, Francis Lovecchio

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Rady Children's Hospital, San Diego, United States

3

University of Texas Health Science Center at San Antonio, San Antonio, United States

Introduction: Current alignment guidelines are derived from standing radiographs, which may not accurately reflect daily activities such as sitting. The T4-L1-Hip axis has been established as a reliable tool for defining normative sagittal alignment in the standing position using vertebral pelvic angles (VPA). However, sitting alignment parameters are still not well understood. Understanding normal sitting alignment is clinically important, especially for non-ambulatory patients. This study aimed to establish normative spinal alignment in the sitting position by developing pelvic incidence (PI)-based formulas. The goal was to describe VPAs and vertebral tilts (VTs) among asymptomatic adults in the sitting position. Material and Methods: This retrospective study examined lateral standing and sitting radiographs from 1,428 adults being considered for hip replacement. Exclusion criteria included prior spinal surgery, disc degeneration visible on imaging (such as osteophytes, endplate sclerosis, disc space narrowing), spondylolisthesis, or a coronal Cobb angle (CA) greater than 10°. The measured spinal and pelvic parameters included PI, pelvic tilt (PT), L1-S1 CA, VPAs, and VTs from L5 to T10, which was the most proximal vertebra visible on sitting radiographs. Relationships between PI and other spinopelvic parameters were assessed using Spearman’s correlation (ρ), and linear regression was used to model sitting alignment. Results: A total of 553 patients (278 females, 255 males) met the inclusion criteria, with a mean age of 56.91 ± 11.49 years, BMI of 28.44 ± 5.46 kg/m2, and sitting PI of 55.45° ± 10.78. Sitting VTs were: L5VT -25.49° ± 10.76, L4VT -18.48° ± 9.61, L3VT -15.38° ± 8.67, L2VT -14.28° ± 7.85, L1VT -13.91° ± 7.16, T12VT -13.69° ± 6.54, T11VT -13.45° ± 6.13, and T10VT -13.36° ± 5.86. VT variability progressively decreased, and distributions became more symmetrical at proximal levels. Thoracic VTs (T10-T12) were weakly correlated with PI (ρ = -0.14, -0.19, -0.17; all p < 0.001), whereas lumbar VTs (L1-L5) had stronger correlations (ρ = -0.18 to -0.50, all p < 0.001). PT demonstrated a strong correlation with PI (ρ = 0.61, p < 0.001). Linear regression showed PI explained 4.6% (R2, p < 0.001) of the variance in L1-S1. Established formulas for normal sitting VPAs included: T10PA = 0.57×PI-10.08 (R2 = 0.36), L1PA = 0.55×PI-11.01 (R2 = 0.52), and L4PA = 0.35×PI-4.15 (R2 = 0.64) (all p < 0.001). T10PA and L1PA were nearly perfectly correlated (r = 0.94, R2 = 0.88), with differences of ≤ 8.4° in 80% of cases; L1PA and L4PA also showed a strong correlation (r = 0.84). Conclusion: PI is a strong determinant of thoracolumbar alignment while sitting, similar to its role in standing. This study provides PI-based formulas for sitting spinal alignment that could serve as normative targets for non-ambulatory patients. The relationship between T10PA and L1PA suggests they can be used interchangeably for clinical assessment in the sitting position. High PI is associated with increased pelvic motion during sitting, emphasizing the need for personalized alignment goals.

## ID: AGS26/1488

## P0217: Do males and females have similar patient-reported outcomes in ASD surgery?

Ganesh Swamy

1

, Chien Yew Kow

1

, Yukihiro Matsuyama

2

, Kenneth Cheung

3

, Lawrence Lenke

4

, Ferran Pellisé

5

, David Polly

6

, Jonathan N. Sembrano

7

, Christopher Shaffrey

8

, Justin Smith

9

, Michael Kelly

10

, Christopher J. Nielsen

11

, So Kato

12

, Yong Qiu

13

, Ahmet Alanay

14

, Sigurd Berven

15

, Benny Dahl

16

, Stephen Lewis

11

1

University of Calgary, Calgary, Canada

2

Hamamatsu University, Hamamatsu, Japan

3

University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, China

4

Columbia University, New York, United States

5

Barcelona Spine Institute, Barcelona, Spain

6

University of Minnesota, Mineapolis, United States

7

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

8

Duke University, Durham, United States

9

University of Virginia, Charlottesville, United States

10

Rady Children's Hospital, San Diego, United States

11

University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

12

University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan

13

Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

14

Acibadem University School of Medicine, Acibadem, Turkey

15

University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

16

University of Copenhagen, Copenhagen, Denmark

Introduction: ASD cohorts are largely female (80-85%), but the outcome difference between males and females is incompletely studied. Bumpass et al. (2017) suggested no outcome differences between sexes in an ASD cohort, and there are no other reports. There is much interest in sexual dimorphism in patient-reported outcomes in surgery. In total knee replacement, female patients generally report worse pain and function pre-op, and post-operative outcomes are worse in females. In 80% of reports involving sex differences in lumbar degenerative surgeries, female patients had at least one category of worse outcome scores. Therefore, we sought to examine sex differences in ASD surgery outcomes. Material and Methods: In this post-hoc analysis from prospectively collected data, elderly ASD (> 60 years) patients from the international, multicentre Prospective Evaluation of Elderly Deformity Surgery (PEEDS) study were divided into male and female cohorts. Radiographic and outcomes data were collected at 2- and 5-years post-operative. Multivariate linear regression adjusted for age, BMI, and Charlson Comorbidity Index, and unadjusted linear mixed-effects regression models were constructed for patient-reported outcomes (SRS, ODI, EQ5D) and radiologic parameters, with interaction terms for time and gender. Two-sided Wald tests were performed to test for significant differences (α = 0.05). Results: Of 219 patients at inception of the cohort (176 females and 43 males), there were 144 females (81.2%) and 33 males (76.7%) with 2-year follow-up and 94 (53.4%) and 22 (51.2%) at 5-year follow-up. There were no differences in pre-operative demographics or comorbidities between sexes, except for a significantly increased rate of osteopenia (51% vs 17%) in females (p = 0.006); however, osteoporosis rates were not different (11% females vs 13% males). The only significant sex difference in pre-operative radiographic measurements was a higher SVA in males (p = 0.024). The only significant postoperative measurement was the PJK angle at the UIV+1, which was not different at 2-years, but significantly different at 5-years (15 deg female vs 20 deg male). There were no significant sex differences between pre- and postoperative SRS, ODI or EQ-5D at 2-years follow-up, but SRS-mental health subscore became different between groups at 5-years (4.1 male vs 3.7 female) (p = 0.04). With linear regression adjusted for age, BMI and comorbidity, there were no differences between sexes at 2-years postoperative. By 5-years postoperative, improvements for men were higher in SRS-total score (p = 0.015), mental health (p = 0.005) and self-image (0.022), and there was an increase in thoracic kyphosis in women at 5-years. In addition, depression rates were significantly higher in women at 5-years (OR 4.0 [95% CI 1.0-15.7]; p = 0.049), whereas there were no differences between groups at baseline and 2-years postoperatively. With mixed-effects linear regression adjusted for age, BMI and comorbidity, with the addition of an interaction term for time and gender, there were significant differences in favor of men for SRS-total score (p = 0.038), pain (p = 0.026), self-image (p = 0.035) and ODI (p = 0.04). Conclusion: While there are large differences in incidence of ASD between sexes, there are no apparent differences in pre- and post-operative radiographic measures or patient-reported outcomes at baseline (except for higher SVA in males) and at 2-years after ASD surgery. By 5-years post-operatively, women have significantly higher PJK angle than males and less thoracic kyphosis. Perhaps accordingly, there was no continued improvement in total SRS scores. In addition, women had 4X more significant depression by 5-years post op. These results are tempered by a low follow-up rate. Gender differences at 5-years postoperative in ASD surgery, particularly the association with higher PJK angles in women and evolving rates of depression, will deserve further exploration.

## ID: AGS26/1506

## P0218: Prone lateral anterior column realignment versus pedicle subtraction osteotomy for fracture-related adult spinal deformity: a matched cohort analysis

Yu-Cheng Yeh

1

, Yung-Hsueh Hu

1

, Tsung-Ting Tsai

1

, Po-Liang Lai

1

1

Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taoyuan City, Taiwan

Introduction: Prone lateral spinal surgery enables simultaneous lateral and posterior access, improving operative efficiency. With advances in minimally invasive techniques, anterior column realignment (ACR) has emerged as a less invasive alternative to pedicle subtraction osteotomy (PSO) for adult spinal deformity (ASD). This study compared the radiographic and clinical outcomes of prone lateral ACR versus PSO. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed ASD patients with vertebral fracture-related deformities who underwent prone lateral ACR between April 2019 and April 2023. Patients who underwent PSO for similar etiologies were matched by age, body mass index, and Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI). A modified ACR classification was applied to stratify procedures by complexity, subdividing high-grade ACR into Grades 5A, 5B, 6A, and 6B based on single versus multilevel corpectomy and the use of posterior osteotomies. Demographics, perioperative variables, complications, radiographic parameters, and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) were evaluated with a minimum 2-year follow-up. Results: Forty patients were included (20 ACR, 20 PSO). Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. Both techniques achieved significant improvements in lumbar lordosis, pelvic tilt, and global sagittal alignment, with no difference in local Cobb angle correction. Compared with PSO, ACR required fewer fusion levels (4.8 ± 2.0 vs. 6.9 ± 1.4, p < 0.001) and resulted in reduced blood loss (1411 ± 1023 vs. 2074 ± 1227 mL, p = 0.045). The modified ACR classification demonstrated a predictable gradient of segmental correction from 17° in low-grade cases to > 70° in multilevel corpectomy with posterior osteotomy. Conclusion: Prone lateral ACR achieves radiographic correction equivalent to PSO with fewer fusion levels and lower blood loss, supporting its role as a less invasive alternative for ASD correction. The modified ACR classification provides a framework for estimating achievable correction and guiding surgical planning. Further prospective studies are warranted to confirm long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1522

## P0219: Prospective cohort comparison of long construct durability in adults between combined or single posterior approach

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) requiring long-segment thoracolumbar fusion with pelvic fixation represents a particularly high-risk population in which sustained radiographic correction and construct durability are paramount. Posterior-only fusion remains the most employed technique, whereas combined anterior-posterior fusion theoretically augments anterior column support but entails greater operative complexity and morbidity. Comparative evidence in large prospective cohorts with extensive constructs and long-term follow-up is scarce. This study prospectively investigates radiographic, clinical, and mechanical outcomes of posterior-only versus combined anterior-posterior fusion in patients undergoing long-segment ASD correction with pelvic fixation. Methods: This prospective cohort included consecutive ASD patients undergoing instrumented thoracolumbar fusion spanning ≥7 levels with pelvic fixation (2016-2023). Patients were treated with posterior-only or combined anterior-posterior fusion. Selection of the approach was determined by rigidity and need for a 3-column osteotomy. Outcomes included radiographic correction, patient-reported outcomes (ODI, SRS-22r), and mechanical complications, with Kaplan-Meier survival analysis for mechanical complications; rod fracture and proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK). Results: A total of 207 patients were analyzed (posterior-only, n = 114; combined anterior-posterior, n = 93). Baseline demographics and comorbidities were similar between groups, with no differences in age (62.7 vs. 61.3 years, p = 0.40), sex distribution (78% vs. 77% female, p = 1.0), or major comorbidities. Posterior-only patients more frequently underwent pedicle subtraction osteotomy (31% vs. 9%, p < 0.001). Operative characteristics differed: combined procedures had longer operative times (671 vs. 535 minutes, p < 0.001) and longer hospital stays (10.1 vs. 8.1 days, p < 0.001), but lower blood loss (1283 vs. 1651 mL, p = 0.012). Both approaches achieved durable correction. Lumbar lordosis increased by 20.7° in posterior-only and 17.7° in combined fusion (p = 0.23). Pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis mismatch improved by 18.3° versus 12.5° (p = 0.012), and sagittal vertical axis decreased by 43.4 mm versus 30.4 mm (p = 0.08). Pelvic tilt improved in both groups (-5.5° vs. -3.0°), with a modest between-group difference (p = 0.046). GAP scores decreased significantly from baseline (posterior: 9.2 to 5.2; combined: 8.8 to 5.0), without between-group difference. T4-L1 pelvic angle mismatch also improved substantially (posterior: 10.4° to 2.4°; combined: 9.0° to 2.6°), again with no between-group difference. Patient-reported outcomes improved comparably across groups. ODI decreased by 19.8 points in posterior-only and 18.9 points in combined fusion (p = 0.73). All SRS-22r domains (pain, function, self-image, mental health, satisfaction) improved significantly within groups, with no between-group differences. MCID achievement rates were similar for ODI (65% vs. 64%) and all SRS-22r domains. Mechanical complications differed: rod fractures occurred in 14.9% of posterior-only versus 3.2% of combined cases (p = 0.01). Kaplan-Meier analysis confirmed superior rod fracture-free survival (log-rank p = 0.0099) and composite complication-free survival (p = 0.0199) in the combined cohort. Proximal junctional kyphosis was less frequent with combined fusion (10.8% vs. 19.3%), though not statistically significant (p = 0.17). Conclusions: Both posterior-only and combined anterior-posterior approaches provide durable radiographic correction and meaningful clinical improvement in ASD. However, combined fusion reduces rod fracture risk and enhances mechanical complication-free survival, supporting its role in long-segment ASD surgery improving the construct durability.

## ID: AGS26/1523

## P0220: Impact of GLP-1 agonist use on short-and long-term outcomes following adult spinal deformity surgery: a propensity score-matched analysis

Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Mirbahador Athari

1

, Natalie Kistler

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Raymond Hah

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is associated with a high risk of postoperative complications. Recently, there has been an increase in research evaluating the effects of Semaglutide and Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists on postoperative outcomes due to its increasing popularity in treatment of diabetes and obesity. Prior research remains conflicted regarding GLP-1 agonist use on rates of postoperative complications, reoperation, and pseudarthrosis in spine surgery; however, the impact of GLP-1 agonist use on ASD surgery remained unknown. This study aims to evaluate short- and long-term outcomes in ASD surgery using a national database. Material and Methods: Adult patients who underwent spinal deformity surgery between 2010 and 2023 were identified using the PearlDiver Mariner database using multi-level spinal fusion procedure CPT codes along a diagnosis of ASD. Patients were grouped based on GLP-1 use within 1 year preoperatively. Exclusion criteria included patients under 18 years old, those with less than 2 years of postoperative follow-up, or those undergoing surgery for trauma, malignancy, or infection. A 1:1 nearest-neighbor propensity score matching for age, Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI), gender, number of instrumented levels, insulin use, obesity, diabetes, and smoking status was performed. Primary outcomes included 30 day and 90 day complications and readmissions. Secondary outcomes included complications at 2 years, including pseudarthrosis, hardware removal, and secondary decompression or fusion. Continuous variables were compared using Welch’s t-tests and discrete variables were compared using Chi-square tests. Univariate analysis was performed for associations between GLP-1 agonist use and rates of complications, readmissions, pseudarthrosis, and secondary surgery. Results: After propensity score matching, cohorts who underwent ASD surgery were identified with preoperative GLP-1 agonist use (N = 86) and without preoperative GLP-1 agonist use (N = 86). Cohorts were similar in age (64.2 vs 62.7 years), comorbidities (ECI: 7.94 vs 7.62), number of levels fused (7-12 levels: 28 vs 37, 13+ levels: 28 vs 28) (p > 0.05). Between cohorts, there was no difference in 30 day or 90 day complication rates of cerebrovascular accident, pneumonia, respiratory failure, myocardial infarction, acute kidney injury, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, postoperative anemia, sepsis, aspiration pneumonitis, urinary tract infection, hematoma, wound dehiscence, surgical site infection, hardware infection, or mechanical complications (all p > 0.05). GLP-1 use was associated with increased 90 day readmission rates (OR: 2.303, p = 0.048). At 2 years, GLP-1 agonist use was associated with increased risk of hardware removal (OR: 0.042, p = 0.042). Rates of pseudarthrosis and secondary decompression or fusion were similar among cohorts at 2 years (p > 0.05). Conclusion: Preoperative GLP-1 agonist use was not associated with increased short-term complication rates after ASD surgery, but was linked to higher 90-day readmission rates and greater risk of hardware removal at 2 years. These findings suggest that while GLP-1 therapy may be safe in the immediate postoperative setting, it may influence long-term implant-related outcomes. Surgeons should be aware of these potential risks during patient counseling and preoperative management, and consider closer follow-up in patients receiving GLP-1 therapy.

## ID: AGS26/1529

## P0221: L1 slope and combined procedure as determinants of rod fracture in adult spinal deformity surgery

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Rod fracture is one of the common mechanical complications after long instrumented spinal fusion for adult spinal deformity (ASD). Despite its prevalence, the independent risk factors remain debated. This study aimed to identify predictors of rod fracture using a prospective cohort of patients with long fusions to the pelvis and ≥ 2 years follow-up. Methods: A prospective cohort of 207 ASD patients who underwent thoracic-to-pelvis fusion with ≥ 7 instrumented levels and pelvic fixation, all with ≥ 2 years of follow-up was analyzed. The primary outcome was rod fracture, defined radiographically and clinically, occurring at any time after surgery. Baseline demographic and clinical variables included age, sex, body mass index (BMI), ASA class, comorbidity count, osteoporosis, and osteopenia. Operative variables included estimated blood loss, operative time, upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) level (≤ T6 vs ≥ T7), rod size (5.5 or 6.35), rod material (cobalt-chromium, titanium, stainless steel), multi-rod construct (accessory and satellite), combined anterior-posterior versus posterior-only approach, pedicle subtraction osteotomy (PSO), vertebral column resection (VCR), and bone graft characteristics (dose per level). Radiographic parameters were measured 6 weeks postoperatively and included lumbar lordosis (LL), pelvic incidence-LL mismatch (PI-LL), thoracic kyphosis (TK), pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), L1 slope, L1 pelvic angle (L1PA), T9 and T4 pelvic angles, and regional mismatch angles (T4-L1PA, T1-T9PA, L1PA mismatches). GAP category was also recorded as proportioned and disproportioned. Univariate logistic regression was performed for each variable with rod fracture as the outcome. Predictors with p < 0.25 were entered into a multivariable logistic regression with forward stepwise selection based on the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). Multicollinearity was assessed with variance inflation factors (VIF). Model discrimination was assessed with receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis. Results: Among 207 patients, 20 (9.7%) experienced rod fracture during follow-up, only 12 (5.8%) were revised. On univariate logistic regression, several variables were associated with rod fracture. Decreased risk: combined procedure (p = 0.010), BMP dose per level 3.2 mg (ave./level) (p = 0.065), and older age (p = 0.093). Increased risk: L1 slope (p = 0.002), multi-rod constructs (p = 0.043), T1-T9 pelvic angle mismatch (p = 0.139), sacral slope (p = 0.154), T4-L1 pelvic angle mismatch (p = 0.200), UIV category (p = 0.212). All other candidate predictors, including LL, PI-LL, and thoracic kyphosis, had p > 0.25. Stepwise forward regression identified two independent determinants of rod fracture: as a risk-increasing factor L1 slope (Estimate -0.12; OR 0.89 per degree, 95% CI 0.82-0.96; p = 0.002); and as a protective factor combined procedure (Estimate -1.74; OR 0.17, 95% CI 0.04-0.56; p = 0.008). The final model demonstrated fair discrimination (AUC = 0.77). Conclusions: Rod fracture occurred in 9.7% of patients with only 5.8% revision rate, lower than most reports of long fusions to the sacrum in adults. Independent predictor with increased risk was greater L1 slope, while combined anterior-posterior procedure was protective. These findings highlight the role of early sagittal alignment and construct strategy in mitigating rod fracture risk and may inform surgical planning and patient counseling in ASD surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1532

## P0222: Rod diameter provides greater protection against fracture than multi-rod augmentation in adult spinal deformity surgery

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Rod fracture (RF) is a common late complication after long-segment fusion for adult spinal deformity (ASD). Multi-rod strategies, including accessory or satellite constructs, are often used to reduce fracture risk. The comparative effectiveness of these strategies versus upsizing to a larger-diameter rod remains uncertain. Methods: We analyzed a prospective cohort of 207 ASD patients who underwent thoracic-to-pelvis fusion with at least seven instrumented levels and ≥2 years of follow-up. Constructs were classified by rod size (5.5 vs 6.35 mm), configuration (2-rod, accessory 3-rod, satellite 4-rod), and material (cobalt-chrome, titanium, stainless steel). Patient variables included age, sex, body mass index (BMI), ASA class, osteoporosis or osteopenia, and revision status. Operative factors included posterior-only versus combined anterior-posterior approach, osteotomy type, and upper instrumented vertebra. Biologic support with bone morphogenetic protein (BMP) and allograft per level, as well as preoperative alignment and 6-week correction, were assessed. The primary outcome was RF. Results: RF occurred in 20 of 207 patients (9.7%), including 12 (5.7%) who required revision at a mean follow-up of 46 months. In 132 patients with 5.5mm cobalt-chrome rods, 15 fractures occurred (11.4%). In 75 patients with 6.35mm rods, all titanium or stainless steel, 5 fractures occurred (6.7%). Despite being implanted in heavier patients (BMI 31.8 ± 5.9 vs 28.3 ± 5.4, p < 0.001), 6.35 mm rods fractured less often. In 5.5mm cobalt-chrome constructs, the combined approach had less rod fractures than posterior-only constructs: RF was 1 of 24 with accessory rods (4.2%) and 1 of 30 with two rods (3.3%) in the combined group, compared with 9 of 30 (30.0%) and 1 of 25 (4.0%), respectively, in the posterior-only group. In 6.35 mm constructs, RF remained low regardless of 2-rod versus 4-rod configuration, and no accessory rods were used. By configuration, RF occurred in 6 of 108 2-rod constructs (5.6%), 10/54 accessory 3-rod constructs (18.5%), and 4/45 satellite 4-rod constructs (8.9%, p = 0.034). Satellite constructs were applied at osteotomy sites in 45 cases (43 PSO, 2 VCR), with all 4 rod fractures occurred at PSO levels. Rod material tracked with diameter: all 5.5 mm rods were cobalt-chrome (15 fractures, 11.3%), while 6.35 mm rods were titanium (4 fractures, 8.5%) or stainless steel (1 fracture, 3.7%), with no overall difference by material (p = 0.54). Preoperative PI-LL mismatch averaged 25°, lumbar lordosis 42°, thoracic kyphosis 33°, pelvic tilt 24°, GAP score 6.2, and T4-L1 pelvic angle mismatch 19°, with no group differences. Corrections at six weeks averaged -21° for PI-LL, +21° for lumbar lordosis, and +12° for thoracic kyphosis, again without differences. BMP averaged 3.2 ± 1.0 mg/level and allograft 16.6 ± 4.3 cc/level across groups, with no significant difference. Conclusions: 6.35 mm rods, all titanium or stainless steel and used in heavier patients, consistently demonstrated lower fracture rates without additional rods. Accessory 3-rod constructs with 5.5 mm cobalt-chrome rods did not reliably prevent fracture, and the apparent higher risk of accessory constructs reflected selective use in posterior-only cases. Rod diameter, rather than rod multiplicity or material, was the dominant determinant of construct durability.

## ID: AGS26/1537

## P0223: Upper thoracic fusion is protective against PJK: a survival analysis in 207 ASD patients

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) is a common complication after adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery, particularly in long thoracic-to-pelvis fusions. The choice of the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) remains an area of debate. Stopping in the thoracolumbar region may preserve mobility and reduce operative morbidity but has been associated with higher rates of junctional complications. Extending to the upper thoracic spine may reduce mechanical failures but requires longer constructs and greater surgical exposure. Given this ongoing controversy, we evaluated whether upper thoracic UIVs provide longer PJK-free survival compared to lower thoracic UIVs. Materials and Methods: We prospectively analyzed 207 ASD patients who underwent long thoracic-to-pelvis fusion with pelvic fixation and ≥ 7 instrumented levels. All patients had a minimum 2-year follow-up. PJK was defined as a ≥ 20° increase in proximal junctional angle from baseline at any follow-up, or if the patient underwent revision surgery for a proximal junctional kyphosis complication. Patients were stratified by UIV location: upper thoracic (≤ T6) versus lower thoracic (≥ T7). Kaplan-Meier methods evaluated PJK-free survival, with log-rank testing for group comparisons. Cox proportional hazards models were used to adjust for age, sex, BMI, osteotomy use, and radiographic alignment parameters. Results: A total of 207 patients were included, of whom 32 (15.5%) developed PJK over a mean follow-up of 46 months. Baseline demographic and surgical characteristics are summarized in Table 1. The median estimated PJK-free survival for the entire cohort was 89.7 months (95% CI: 83.0-96.4). When stratified by UIV level, patients with upper thoracic UIVs (≤ T6) demonstrated a lower incidence of PJK (12.6%, 19/151) compared with those fused to the lower thoracic spine (≥ T7) (23.2%, 13/56), although this difference did not reach statistical significance on chi-square testing (p = 0.10). Kaplan-Meier analysis, however, confirmed significantly longer survival in the upper thoracic group, with 5-year PJK-free survival of 82% versus 68%, respectively (log-rank χ2 = 3.98, p = 0.046). On unadjusted survival estimates, the mean PJK-free survival time was 91.8 months (95% CI: 83.7-99.9) for the upper thoracic group and 70.7 months (95% CI: 61.1-80.4) for the lower thoracic group. Median survival was not reached within the follow-up window for either group due to censoring. In multivariate Cox regression adjusting for demographic variables (age, sex, BMI), osteotomy use, and radiographic alignment parameters, lower thoracic UIV location independently increased the risk of PJK (HR = 1.92, 95% CI: 1.04-3.56, p = 0.038). No other demographic or radiographic factors demonstrated independent association after accounting for UIV level. Conclusion: Upper thoracic long fusions demonstrate significantly longer PJK-free survival compared with lower thoracic long fusions. UIV location was an independent predictor of junctional durability, with lower thoracic constructs nearly doubling the hazard of PJK. These findings highlight the importance of UIV selection in ASD surgery, supporting the use of upper thoracic fixation to mitigate complications and extend construct longevity.

## ID: AGS26/1545

## P0224: Redefining reciprocal changes: postoperative T4-L1PA mismatch in lower thoracic fusions

Atahan Durbas

1

, Han Jo Kim

1

, Andrea Pezzi

1

, Robert Uzzo

1

, Gabrielle Dykhouse

1

, Tejas Subramanian

1

, Stephane Owusu-Sarpong

1

, Mihir Dekhne

1

, Jung Kee Mok

1

, Kasra Araghi

1

, Quante Singleton

1

, Farah Musharbash

1

, Luis Felipe Colon

1

, Matthew Cunningham

1

, Francis Lovecchio

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: The T4-L1 pelvic angle (T4-L1PA) mismatch has been validated as a personalized alignment target in upper thoracic (UT) to pelvis fusions for adult spinal deformity (ASD), while its role in lower thoracic (LT) to pelvis fusions is an active area of research. Thoracic flexibility (TF) may help predict postoperative mismatch in this setting. This study aimed to evaluate the relationship between TF and T4-L1PA mismatch in patients undergoing LT to pelvis fusions and to assess how postoperative alignment targets affect mechanical outcomes. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study included ASD patients undergoing fusion from T9-T12 to the pelvis, fused ≥ 5 levels, excluding those with prior thoracic fusions. All patients had preoperative standing and supine radiographs and discharge imaging. TF was defined as the ratio of the supine-to-standing T4-T12 Cobb angle (CA). The L1PA offset was calculated as the difference between the measured and PI-based expected L1PA value, which equals PI × 0.5 - (19° ± 2°). The ideal range for T4-L1PA mismatch was determined using published values by Hills et al. (2024) that have been shown to minimize mechanical complications after UT to pelvic fusions. Patients were then categorized as pre- and postoperative “matched” (T4-L1PA within range) [M] and “mismatched” (T4-L1PA out of range) [MM]. TF was defined as the preoperative supine-to-standing T4-T12 ratio (lower ratio indicates higher flexibility) and was analyzed for associations with T4-L1PA alignment between groups. Postoperative outcomes included mismatch status and proximal junctional complications: PJK (PJA ≥ 10° or ΔPJA ≥ 10°) and PJF (PJA ≥ 28° or ΔPJA ≥ 22°, or proximal fusion extension). Logistic regression was used to identify the relationship between radiographic parameters and postoperative mismatch status. Results: 87 patients (61 females, 26 males) with a mean age of 65.61 (± 8.13) years, a BMI of 26.73 (± 5.47) kg/m2, PI of 52.71°, and a follow-up period of 1.9 years were included. Baseline: 11.5% M, 88.5% MM; post-op: 14.9% M, 85.1% MM. Of the 10 patients who were preoperatively M, 8 (80%) became MM. Transitioned patients had lower BMI (p = 0.04), osteoporosis (p = 0.04), and lower preoperative PI-LL mismatch (p = 0.02). Univariate analysis linked TF (p = 0.02), preoperative T4-L1PA mismatch (p = 0.03), and postoperative thoracic kyphosis (p = 0.02) to mismatch status. In multivariate regression, only TF (p < 0.05) and osteoporosis (p = 0.01) were associated with postoperative mismatch status, while preoperative mismatch status and postoperative TK were not. Mechanical complications were predominantly observed in MM. PJF was seen exclusively in patients who were preoperatively mismatched and remained mismatched after surgery. Conclusion: Increased TF is associated with pre- and postoperative T4-L1PA mismatch, suggesting that UT-derived thresholds may not be valid for LT constructs. While baseline mismatch status was not predictive, persistent mismatch was associated with higher rates of proximal junctional complications, including PJF. These findings emphasize the need for LT-specific alignment targets and highlight the importance of including TF and systemic patient factors in preoperative planning and risk stratification.

## ID: AGS26/1550

## P0225: Was it just laminectomy? Drivers of clinical improvement after sagittal realignment in adult spinal deformity

Atahan Durbas

1

, Han Jo Kim

1

, Andrea Pezzi

1

, Gabrielle Dykhouse

1

, Tejas Subramanian

1

, Robert Uzzo

1

, Luis Felipe Colon

1

, Stephane Owusu-Sarpong

1

, Mihir Dekhne

1

, Jung Kee Mok

1

, Kasra Araghi

1

, Quante Singleton

1

, Farah Musharbash

1

, Matthew Cunningham

1

, Francis Lovecchio

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) frequently presents with coexisting lumbar spinal stenosis and global sagittal malalignment, both causing significant disability and reducing the quality of life. It remains unclear whether improvements in function and pain after ASD surgery are mainly due to laminectomy or realignment. This study aimed to assess whether ASD patients with lumbar stenosis gain more postoperative benefits than those without, and to identify the relative contributions of laminectomy and sagittal realignment in determining clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed ASD patients who underwent instrumented fusion of four or more spinal levels, with a minimum of two years of follow-up. Patients with previous spinal surgery or thoracic stenosis were excluded. Patients were then divided into groups based on whether they underwent lumbar laminectomy for stenosis concurrent with sagittal correction (Lam) or only underwent sagittal realignment (NoLam). The primary outcomes were PROMs: Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Visual Analog Scale-back (VAS-Back), Visual Analog Scale-leg (VAS-Leg), the Short Form 12-item Health Survey (SF12), and the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS). These surveys were collected preoperatively and at 6 weeks, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively. Preoperative and postoperative spinopelvic and coronal parameters-including pelvic incidence (PI), pelvic tilt (PT), PI-LL mismatch, L4-S1 lordosis, L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) offset, T4-L1 pelvic angle (T4L1PA) mismatch, C7 plumb line (C7PL), and the maximum Cobb angle (CA)-were compared between groups at the same time points. Variables were tested for normality with the Shapiro-Wilk test, and either parametric or nonparametric tests were applied accordingly. Results: A total of 143 patients (mean age 61.2 ± 13.6 years; 59 males, 84 females; mean BMI 27 ± 5.7 kg/m2; mean PI 53.8° ± 12.9) were included in the study, with 73 (51%) undergoing lumbar laminectomy. Radiographic parameters and the extent of sagittal correction showed no significant differences between groups, either before or after surgery. The Lam group presented with worse preoperative disability (ODI 44.2 vs. 37.1, p = 0.015), higher leg pain (VAS-Leg 5.5 vs. 3.5, p < 0.001), and lower physical function (SF-12 PCS 23.4 vs. 31.3, p = 0.004) compared to the NoLam group. Both groups experienced significant improvements in all PROMs after surgery. Patients in the Lam group reported greater early relief of leg pain at 6 months (ΔVAS-Leg: 2.9 vs. 1.3, p = 0.03) and 1 year (3.4 vs. 1.4, p = 0.02). However, by two years, all PROMs converged, with no significant differences between groups. Conclusion: Patients undergoing ASD correction achieve similar long-term improvements in pain and function regardless of whether laminectomy is performed. The findings suggest that, while decompression may accelerate early symptom relief, it combines the benefits of neural decompression and comprehensive sagittal realignment.

## ID: AGS26/1552

## P0226: The sacral base as the key to coronal balance: clinical and geometric perspectives

Buse Sarigul

1

, Nejat Dalbayrak

2

, Mesut Yilmaz

3

, Sedat Dalbayrak

3

1

Umraniye Research and Training Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Munich Technical University, Munich, Germany

3

Medicana Atasehir Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Norospinal Akademi, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Optimally there is no curvature of the spine in the coronal plane and this is maintained with a sacrum and pelvis parallel to the ground. Leg-length discrepancy, lumbosacral junctional deviations and sacral asymmetry may predispose sacral and pelvic obliquity, thus producing compensatory scoliotic curves to establish the coronal alignment. This study investigates the correlation between the asymmetric sacropelvic base and the compensatory scoliosis. Material and Methods: Patients in whom lumbar scoliosis was detected in the scoliosis X-rays were included in the study. Study population included 32 patients who were diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis and had non-structural lumbar scoliosis with sacropelvic asymmetry and 36 patients who admitted with low back pain and diagnosed with lumbar scoliosis. Standing and supine AP/lateral scoliosis X-rays, lateral bending X-rays, orthorontgenograms, CT with scanograms, MRI were obtained to evaluate the sacropelvic obliquity and its underlying causes. The correlation between the lumbosacral angulation and compensatory spinal curve was assessed and formulized. Progressive deformity and persistent symptoms and/or degenerative pathologies secondary to asymmetric loading were treated with surgical intervention. Results: 78 patients were included in the study. 66 (84.6%) were female and 12 (15.4%) were male, the mean age was 28.6 ± 9.2 years (range: 12-48). 16 (%20.5) patients had pelvic obliquity secondary to leg-length discrepancy. 62 patients had pelvic asymmetry due to anatomical abnormalities including lumbosacral junction abnormalities (n:42; 53.8%), sacral asymmetry (n:17; 21.8%) and pelvic obliquity (n:3;3.8%). The osseous anatomical anomalies were analyzed via three-dimensional CT reconstruction images. A geometric model was established to quantify coronal plane deviation, demonstrating that center deviation (D) equals patient height (L) multiplied by the tangent of the sacral obliquity angle (α) (D = L × tan α). The minimum compensatory lumbar curvature required for balance was derived as Cobb = 2α. The cohort demonstrated a mean sacral obliquity of 8.2° ± 3.1 (range: 3°-15°), with corresponding mean lumbar Cobb angle of 19.4° ± 5.6 (Cobb/α ratio: 2.34). Surgical intervention was performed in 15 (19.2%) patients (6 with curve progression, 9 with symptomatic degeneration) using TLIF cages and short-segment stabilization. This achieved significant correction, reducing mean Cobb angles from 28.2° ± 6.8 preoperatively to 7.8° ± 2.4 postoperatively, with no perioperative complications or loss of correction at 1-year follow-up. Conclusion: Preserving coronal spinal alignment fundamentally requires the superior surface of S1 to remain parallel with the horizontal plane. Any tilt of this base inevitably induces compensatory scoliosis. Asymmetric mechanical loading accelerates early degeneration of the intervertebral discs and facet joints, leading to earlier onset of symptoms. With time, such curves evolve into structural deformities and progressive imbalance. In conclusion, when idiopathic scoliosis is suspected-especially in patients with non-structural lumbar curves-sacral base orientation must be carefully assessed. If leg-length discrepancy is present, it should be corrected. In patients with sacral obliquity and compensatory scoliosis but without limb-length inequality, early correction of the base and short-segment stabilization is advisable before the curve becomes structural. Such an approach can halt deformity progression and improve long-term clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1583

## P0227: The impact of rod insertion on intraoperative neuromonitoring alerts in spinal deformity surgery

Zeeshan Sardar

1

, Justin Reyes

1

, Lawrence Lenke

1

, Yong Qiu

2

, Ferran Pellise

3

, Ahmet Alanay

4

, Justin Smith

5

, Nasir Quraishi

6

, Randolph Gray

7

, Saumyajit Basu

8

, Go Yoshida

9

, Amer Aziz

10

, Stephen Lewis

11

, AO Spine Knowledge Forum Deformity

12

1

Columbia University, New York, United States

2

Nanjing University, Nanjing, China

3

Hospital Universitari de la Vall d’Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

4

Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

5

University of Virginia, Charlottesville, United States

6

Nottingham University Hospitals NHS Trust, Nottingham, United Kingdom

7

The Children`s Hospital Westmead, St. Leonards, Australia

8

Kothari Medical Centre, Kolkata, India

9

Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Japan

10

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

11

University Health Network, Toronto, Canada

12

AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: Intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) plays a critical role in detecting potential neurological compromise during spinal deformity correction. The impact of rod placement as a preceding event remains incompletely understood. This study explores factors associated with rod placement as the preceding event of IONM alerts. Material and Methods: The study is a post hoc analysis of data from a prospective multicenter international observational study, where 20 international centers prospectively documented IONM (EMG, SSEP and MEP) alerts, demographics, radiographic findings, and surgical events of patients (10-80 years) undergoing spinal deformity surgery. Patients included had a major Cobb > 80° or underwent a Schwab type 2-6 osteotomy. The data from alerts that occurred following rod placement were reviewed. The primary outcomes included the modality, and laterality of IONM alerts, as well as associated corrective measures and neurological outcomes. Results: Of the 543 patients analyzed, 17 (3.1%) experienced 25 IONM alerts directly following rod placement while 59 (10.9%) sustained non-rod related IONM alerts. Those with rod-related alerts had a median coronal Cobb angle of 84.5° (vs. 68°), coronal deformity angular ratio (DAR) of 13.5° (vs. 10°), sagittal DAR of 11.2 (vs. 7.4), and 35.3% had undergone intraoperative traction (vs. 9.5%). MEP changes were the most common alert modality (96% in rod related vs. 90.9% in non-rod), with bilateral alerts occurring in 66.7% of cases. SSEP changes occurred in 8% of rod related alerts vs. 20.8% in non-rod related alerts. There were 16 bilateral MEP alerts and 8 unilateral MEP.2 had associated SSEP changes. The most common surgical responses for bilateral MEP alerts included systemic responses (13/16, 81.3%), elevating BP (12/16, 75%), and rod removal (7/16, 43.8%). The most common surgical responses for unilateral MEP without SSEP included systemic responses (3/6 50%), elevating BP (2/6, 33%) and rod removal (2/6, 33%). For unilateral alerts with SSEP, all responses (2/2) were systemic, included rod removal and steroids. In bilateral MEP groups, all alerts recovered at least partially (13/16, 81.2% full recovery, 3/16, 18.8% partial), whereas in unilateral MEP group only 4/8 (50%) of alerts recovered fully, and 1 alert recovered partially, and all 2/2 alerts recovered in unilateral MEP with SSEP group. Overall, for alerts including rod placement, responses that included giving blood, removing rod or lessening correction resulted in higher full recovery of signal (14/17, 82.4% vs 4/8, 50%). Overall, 2/15 (13.3%) patients with rod placement as preceding event had de novo neurological deficits at discharge. Conclusion: Patients with rod placement had predominantly MEP alerts. Responses that included giving blood, removing rod or lessening correction resulted in high full recovery of signal. IONM alerts occurring following rod placement are a frequent occurrence and can be fully reversible in a large number of cases if the appropriate maneuvers are performed. The surgical team should be extra vigilant during this important and high risk step in the procedure.

## ID: AGS26/1617

## P0228: Evaluation of the effects of anesthetics and surgery on sleep quality in patients undergoing posterior spinal instrumentation surgery: a prospective randomised clinical trial

Selcan Akesen

1

, Gökay Eken

2

, Yücel Bilgin

2

, Saltuk Buğra Güler

3

, Elifgül Ulutaş

1

1

Bursa Uludag University, School of Medicine, Department of Anesthesiology and Reanimation, Bursa, Turkey

2

Bursa Uludag University, School of Medicine, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Bursa, Turkey

3

Mamak State Hospital, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: We aimed to evaluate the impact of the anesthesia method and surgical procedure on the sleep patterns and sleep quality of patients undergoing posterior spinal instrumentation using the Pittsburgh Insomnia Rating Scale-20 (PIRS-20). Material and Methods: A total of 40 patients ASA (American Society of Anesthesiology) I-III aged 18 and over who underwent elective spinal posterior instrumentation was included. The patients were divided into two groups randomly - those with sevoflurane and remifentanil anesthesia and those with total intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) - using the closed envelope method. Patients were evaluated before and after the surgery with the PIRS-20 (surgery one month previously and postoperative 7th day) for sleep quality, VAS (Visual Analogue Scale; recorded preoperative night and postoperative first hour), pain, and State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI) for anxiety (recorded preoperative night and postoperative 7th day) scores. Results: When pre-operative and postoperative PIRS-20, VAS, and STAI scores were compared, no significant difference was observed between Group T and Group S (p > 0.05). The VAS values in Group S and Group T decreased significantly over time (p < 0.001, p = 0.001, respectively). The STAI scores decreased significantly over time (p = 0.001, p = 0.001, respectively). The PIRS-20 values remained unchanged in Groups S and T (p = 0.132, p = 0.828, respectively). Conclusion: The results of the study showed that, while the type of anesthesia did not affect the quality of sleep in the group of patients receiving posterior instrumentation, the surgical procedure did affect pain reduction in both anesthesia methods.

## P0229: The Hsu-Lace: a novel technique to prevent PJF

Jennyfer Paulla Galdino Chaves

1

, Brian Hsu

1

, Bhisham Singh

1

, Stone Sima

2

, Ashish Diwan

2

1

NSW Spine Specialist, Sydney, Australia

2

University of New South Wales, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: This retrospective study assesses the efficacy of Hsu-Lace technique in creating a “soft landing” for patients submitted to thoraco-lumbar-pelvic reconstruction intending to reduce proximal junction failure and reoperation rates. The present study aims to show a new technique in Adult Spine Deformity (ASD) surgery looking into the effects of the artificial interspinous Nylon and Tendon soft stabilization on proximal junction kyphosis/failure (PJK/PJF) and extension of fusion rates. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on 53 patients who underwent thoraco-lumbar-pelvis fusion for ASD since 2019. Conclusion: We believe the Hsu-lace technique is valid and changed our practice reducing our PJF and extension of fusion rates.

## P0230: Anatomical iliac screw fixation: early outcomes and technical description of a sacroiliac-sparing, connector-free approach

Mohannad W. Awwad

1

, Zyad Al Dosari

2

, Hisham Al Hathloul

2

, Abdulmajeed Al Zakri

2

, Osama Al Rehaili

2

, Nizar Al Garni

2

, Waleed Awwad

2

1

Medical Student, College of Medicine, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

2

Orthopedic Surgery Department, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Background: Iliac fixation is fundamental for long lumbosacral constructs, yet conventional strategies such as S2-alar-iliac (S2AI) and classic iliac screws carry risks of wound complications, hardware prominence, and construct failure. S2AI fixation also traverses the sacroiliac joint and requires domino connectors, which may increase biomechanical stress and construct complexity. We describe a morphology-guided anatomic iliac fixation technique that spares the sacroiliac joint and avoids domino connectors, and report early outcomes in comparison with other fixation strategies. Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who underwent pelvic fixation at a tertiary center between 2018-2023. Patients were grouped as anatomic iliac fixation, classic iliac, or S2AI fixation. Data collected included demographics, operative time, blood loss, transfusion requirements, wound complications, and construct failure. The anatomic iliac fixation technique is detailed, highlighting consistent pelvic morphology landmarks that allow safe, reproducible placement while preserving sacroiliac integrity. Results: Thirty-two patients underwent pelvic fixation: 10 with anatomic iliac fixation, 10 with classic iliac, and 12 with S2AI screws. Mean age was 43.1 years, 78% female, with a mean BMI of 28.7. Operative time was shortest in anatomic iliac fixation (mean 5.5 h) compared with classic iliac (5.9 h) and S2AI (7.3 h). Mean blood loss was lower in anatomic iliac fixation (402 ml) compared with classic iliac (455 ml) and S2AI (1,208 ml). Transfusion was required in 66% of S2AI patients, 40% of anatomic iliac fixation, and none of the classic iliac cases. Wound complications were lowest in anatomic iliac fixation (20%) and classic iliac (20%) when compared to S2AI (33%). Construct failure with revision was observed in one classic iliac and one S2AI case, but none in anatomic iliac fixation. No anatomic iliac fixation patients experienced neurological, thromboembolic, or systemic complications. Conclusion: The morphology-guided anatomic iliac fixation technique provides a sacroiliac-sparing, connector-free alternative for pelvic fixation. Early outcomes suggest reduced wound morbidity, lower blood loss, and absence of construct failure compared with conventional iliac fixation methods. Importantly, anatomic iliac fixation demonstrates no inferiority to widely used techniques, positioning it as a practical and reproducible strategy that warrants further prospective validation.

## ID: AGS26/1704

## P0231: Why thoracolumbar junction matters: dynamic motion predicts mechanical complications in adult spinal deformity surgery

Sun Joon Yoo

1

, Kyung Hyun Kim

2

1

Yong-in Severance Hospital, Yonsei University College of Medicine, Yongin-si, Gyeonggi-do, South Korea

2

Gangnam Severance hospital, Yonsei University College of Medicine, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Mechanical complications and revision surgery after long instrumented fusion for adult spinal deformity (ASD) are well-recognized problems that adversely affect clinical outcomes. When the thoracolumbar junction (TLJ) is selected as the uppermost instrumented vertebra (UIV), these complications must be carefully considered due to the unique transitional biomechanics between the rigid thoracic and the mobile lumbar spine. Although several studies have analyzed proximal junctional problems related to TLJ as the UIV, little is known about the impact of dynamic motion at this segment. The purpose of this study was to investigate the risk factors for mechanical complications and revision surgery in posterior fusion constructs stopping at the TLJ, with particular focus on the role of dynamic motion. Material and Methods: Between January 2012 and December 2019, we retrospectively reviewed patients with ASD who underwent long-segment posterior instrumented fusion terminating at the TLJ (UIV at L1 or T12). All patients achieved fusion to the sacrum and were followed for a minimum of 2 years. Demographic variables (age, sex, body mass index [BMI], bone mineral density [BMD], smoking status), surgical factors (fusion length, additional iliac fixation), and occurrence of mechanical complications were evaluated. Radiographic parameters, including pre- and postoperative alignment as well as flexion-extension segmental angle changes at the TLJ, were analyzed to assess the impact of dynamic motion on complication risk. Results: A total of 126 patients were included in the study (97 with UIV at L1 and 29 with UIV at T12). The mean age was 67.7 years (range, 49 to 83 years), and the mean duration of follow-up was 28.3 months (range, 24 to 90 months). Mechanical complications occurred in 42 of 97 patients at L1 (43.3%) and in 12 of 29 patients at T12 (41.4%), with no significant difference between the two groups. Multivariate regression analysis revealed that low bone mineral density (OR 0.206, p = 0.039), the use of iliac fixation (OR 6.069, p = 0.036), higher preoperative pelvic tilt (OR 1.121, p = 0.017), greater preoperative flexion-extension difference at the UIV-1/UIV segmental angle (OR 1.436, P = 0.039), and greater flexion-extension difference at the proximal junctional angle (OR 1.193, P = 0.044) were significant risk factors for mechanical complications. Conclusion: Low bone mineral density, the use of iliac fixation, higher preoperative pelvic tilt, and greater flexion-extension motion at both the UIV-1/UIV segmental angle and the proximal junctional angle were identified as significant risk factors for mechanical complications after long-segment fusion stopping at the thoracolumbar junction. These findings suggest that complication risk is determined not by whether the UIV is located at L1 or T12, but rather by whether the UIV involves a dynamic motion segment. Careful evaluation of preoperative motion dynamics at the thoracolumbar junction is therefore essential when selecting the UIV in adult spinal deformity surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1748

## P0232: Evaluation of reciprocal thoracic kyphosis changes after staged corrective surgery in adult spinal deformity

Erh-Ti Lin

1

, Yi-Hsun Huang

1

, Yi Chin Fong

1

, Yuan-Shun Lo

1

1

China Medical University Beigang Hospital, Yunlin County, Taiwan

Introduction: Reciprocal changes (RCs) in unfused spinal segments can significantly affect the global alignment after corrective surgery. Identifying radiographic thresholds for guiding surgical strategies is critical for optimizing the outcomes. Material and Methods: Ninety-eight ASD patients who underwent staged surgeries, including lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) and posterior spinal fusion (PSF), were analyzed. According to the final follow-up image, the patients were classified into balanced (BG) and imbalanced (IG) groups, with IG further stratified into proximal junctional kyphosis/failure (IG-PJK/PJF) and non-PJK/PJF (IG-NPJK/PJF). Radiographic and clinical data were collected preoperatively, postoperatively, and at 2-year follow-up. Results: The IG exhibited greater RCs in thoracic kyphosis (TK) and PI-LL mismatch from postoperative to follow-up than the BG (p = .030, p = .008). Significant predictors included Age > 65.5 y/o (AUC: .672) and Post-PT > 17.5° (AUC: .852) for imbalance and Post-TK > 34.5° (AUC: .755) for IG-PJK/PJF. IG-PJK/PJF showed poorer ODI scores than BG and IG-NPJK/PJF (p = .021 and p = .022, respectively). IG-PJK/PJF showed poorer total SRS-22 scores than IG-NPJK/PJF (p = .021). Conclusion: Increased RCs in TK was associated with adverse outcomes. Extending the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) to the upper thoracic spine and addressing excessive TK (> 34.5°) may improve alignment and reduce complications. Radiographic thresholds can provide actionable guidance in surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/1751

## P0233: A cost-effective irrigation technique in thoracolumbar spine fractures

Halil Gok

1

1

Ankara Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: To evaluate the effect of standardization of irrigation volume, a cost-effective intraoperative modification, on surgical site infection (SSI) rates in patients undergoing posterior spinal fusion for thoracolumbar (TL) vertebral fractures. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on 241 patients who underwent posterior spinal fusion for TL fractures by a single surgeon between 2001 and 2022. Patients were divided into two groups: Group 1 (2001-2011) underwent conventional syringe-based irrigation with non-standardized saline volumes, while Group 2 (2011-2022) received irrigation via an arthroscopic system using a standardized 3 L saline volume. Demographics, clinical characteristics, operative parameters, and SSI rates were compared between groups. Results: A total of 207 patients met the inclusion criteria (Group 1: n = 98; Group 2: n = 109). The average irrigation volume was 1,343 mL in Group 1 and 3,000 mL in Group 2 (p < 0.001). SSIs occurred in 5 patients (5.1%) in Group 1 and in 1 patient (0.92%) in Group 2 (p = 0.019). Pathogens isolated in Group 1 included Staphylococcus epidermidis (2), MRSA (1), MSSA (1), and Escherichia coli (1). In Group 2, only MSSA was isolated. No significant differences were observed in age, sex, BMI, blood loss, hospital stay, smoking status, or diabetes. Conclusion: Irrigation with a standardized 3 L saline volume using an arthroscopic irrigation system was associated with a lower SSI rate compared with non-standardized syringe irrigation. Considering the morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs related to SSIs, this simple and low-cost technique may be considered a useful adjunct in the surgical treatment of thoracolumbar fractures, although prospective multicenter studies are warranted.
