## P0234: Types of pelvic fixation and their influence on pelvic incidence change after surgery for adult spinal deformity: a retrospective analysis

Mohammad Daher

1

, Guillaume Riouallon

2

, Louis Boissiere

3

, Youssef Jaballah

2

, Ibrahim Obeid

3

, Pawel Jankowski

4

, Raymond Hah

5

, Peter Passias

1

, Alan Daniels

6

, Pierre Roussouly

7

, Amer Sebaaly

8

1

Duke University, Durham, United States

2

Groupe Hospitalier Paris Saint Joseph, Paris, France

3

Clinique du Dos-Bordeaux Terrefort, Bordeaux, France

4

HOAG, Irvine, United States

5

USC, LA, United States

6

Brown University, Providence, United States

7

Centre médico-chirurgical de réadaptation des Massues, Lyon, France

8

Hotel Dieu de France, Beirut, Lebanon

Introduction: In Adult spinal deformity (ASD), restoring sagittal spinal alignment which is usually based on the measurement of pelvic incidence (PI) can positively modify the quality of life in patients post-operatively. While which was postulated to be a constant value specific to each person, recent literature reported that the latter can change with pelvic fixation after ASD surgery. This study will compare post-operative PI modification by types of pelvic fixation. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective multicenter study of 423 patients who have undergone ASD surgery between 2012 and 2022. These patients were divided into four groups, based on the type of pelvic fixation they received being either S2-Alar-Iliac screws (S2AI), Iliac screws (IS), Iliosacral screws (ISS), and no pelvic fixation. Post-operative PI change was defined by an absolute difference of ≥ 6° between pre- and post-operative values. Furthermore, patients were divided into 3 groups pre-operatively based on their PI: low (< 40°), medium (40°-60°), and high (> 60°). Results: Of the patients who had pelvic fixation, 45.9% had a change in PI compared to 12.8% in the group without pelvic fixation. Patients with S2AI had the higher rate of post-operative PI change (80%) followed by patients with ISS (39.6%) and patients with IS (15.3%).). In addition, patients with a low pre-operative PI tended to have an increase in post-operative PI (33.3° pre-operatively to 42.1° post-operatively) while patients with a high pre-operative PI tended to have a lower post-operative PI (69.9° pre-operatively to 66.5° post-operatively). Furthermore, a logistic regression model controlling for gender, pre-operative PI groups, the change in lumbar lordosis, pelvic tilt, sacral slope, and pelvic fixation showed that only the latter predicted the post-operative change in PI with an adjusted odd-ratio of 5.7. However, pelvic fixation was not a predictor of PI modification when we selected only patients with IS. Conclusion: Pelvic fixation was found to be an independent risk factor for PI change with an adjusted OR of 5.7. When stratifying by pelvic fixation type, S2AI screws had the greatest impact on post-operative PI change (ORa = 26.3) followed by ISS (ORa = 4.6) with IS having no impact on post-operative PI change.

## P0235: Impact of sarcopenia on surgical outcomes in adult spinal deformity: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Kento Yamanouchi

1,2

, Rafael Garcia de Oliveira

1

, Iyan Younus

1

, Kenneth Nguyen

1

, Aiyush Bansal

1

, Venu Nemani

1

, Jean Christophe Leveque

1

, Philip Louie

1

1

Virginia Mason Medical Center, Seattle, United States

2

Keio University, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is a complex condition frequently associated with high surgical risk and incidence of postoperative complications. Aging-related loss of skeletal muscle volume, strength, and quality defined as sarcopenia may have an adverse impact of ASD surgery. While reduced psoas and paraspinal muscle volume has been associated with complications in spine surgery, its specific impact in ASD remains unclear. This systematic review aimed to evaluate the association between sarcopenia and surgical outcomes in patients undergoing ASD surgery. Materials and Methods: A systematic search of PubMed and EMBASE was performed on May 5, 2025, to identify studies evaluating sarcopenia in ASD surgery. Eligible studies included adult patients (≥ 18 years) undergoing thoracolumbar corrective surgery, with sarcopenia diagnosed using validated methods. Outcomes of interest were postoperative complications, reoperation, readmission, length of stay (LOS), and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs). Data were extracted on study design, patient demographics, surgical characteristics, and follow-up. Meta-analysis was conducted using Review Manager 5.4, with odds ratios (ORs) for dichotomous outcomes and mean differences for continuous outcomes. Results: Fifteen studies comprising 1567 patients met the eligibility criteria in this review. Meta-analysis showed no significant difference in reoperation rates (OR = 1.43 [95% CI 0.79- 2.59]) or readmission rates (OR = 1.51 [95% CI 0.88- 2.59]). Patients with proximal junctional kyphosis had significantly lower muscle volume (MD = -174.83 [95% CI -258.92, -90.75]). Length of stay was significantly longer in sarcopenia patients (MD = 1.00 [95% CI 0.65, 1.36]). No statistically significant difference was observed in wound complications (OR = 2.00 [95% CI 0.58, 6.86]). Additionally, patients with higher CSA demonstrated smaller T4-L1PA mismatch after ASD surgery. Conclusions: This systematic review and meta-analysis clarified the impact of sarcopenia on surgical outcome in ASD patients. Our findings indicate that sarcopenia is a significant factor contributing to poorer surgical outcomes in ASD. Preoperative evaluation of trunk muscle volume may facilitate risk stratification, prevention of postoperative complications, and inform the development of appropriate perioperative interventions such as prehabilitation and nutritional support.

## P0236: A comparison of spinal radiographic parameters and 30-day complications with freehand or robotic-assisted dual S1AI and S2AI in adult spinal deformity

Sultan Baz

1

, Arthur Cowman

1

, Hana Hanks

1

, Brandon Lehman

1

, Ryan Hoang

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Jason Horowitz

1

, Alexander Nazareth

1

, Alan Santiago

1

, Gregory Fasani-Feldberg

2

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Don Park

1

, Isador Lieberman

2

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of California, Irvine, School of Medicine, Orange, United States

2

Scoliosis and Spine Tumor Center, Texas Back Institute, Plano, United States

Introduction: Both long and short segment fusions in ASD surgery mandate rigid spinopelvic fixation to prevent mechanical spinopelvic failure. Despite technical and hardware improvements, spinopelvic pseudoarthrosis and early hardware failure remain common following ASD. Recent literature suggests that the DSAI technique decreases these complications. Considering the growing role of robotics in ASD, we sought to evaluate the differences in spinopelvic parameters, complications, operative duration, and estimated blood loss between RA and FH for DSAI. We hypothesize spinal radiographic parameters following lumbosacral pelvic fixation using the dual S1 alar-iliac (AI) and S2AI (DSAI) technique in adult spinal deformity (ASD) would be similar between the robotic-assisted (RA) and freehand (RH) groups. Material and Methods: A multicenter retrospective chart review was conducted on clinical records at two institutions for patients who underwent primary DSAI for ASD. We collected clinical data including demographics, operative data, perioperative outcomes, and postoperative complications. Results: 38 patients were included (n = 24 RA, n = 14 FH). When comparing the RA and FH groups, preoperative demographic variables were comparable between groups age (67.3 vs 68.5, p = 0.65) and BMI (27.8 vs. 28.95, p = 0.67), etc. Preoperative radiographic values for L4-S1, Lumbar Lordosis (LL), Pelvic Incidence (PI), PI-LL, and pelvic tilt (PT) were not statistically different between cohorts. The only intraoperative complication was durotomy, which occurred in 16.6% RA vs 14.3% FH, p = 0.85. Operative duration was shorter in the RA group (392 mins RA vs. 542 mins FH, p < .01). EBL was less in the RA group (p = 0.03). LOS was comparable between groups (8.38 days RA vs. 9.64 days FH, p = 0.30). There was no difference in SSI (p = 0.28). Conclusion: Both RA and FH DSAI techniques confer similar changes to spinopelvic parameters in ASD. While there may be a trend of greater spinopelvic change with FH, further data is required to evaluate significance. We did not find a statistically significant difference in intraoperative or perioperative complications. We observed that RA DSAI may decrease blood loss and require shorter operative times.

## P0237: Study on adjacent vertebral fracture and degenerative scoliosis after percutaneous kyphoplasty in patients with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures

Sachin Sharma

1

, Harvinder Singh Chhabra

1

1

SRI Balaji Action Medical Institute, Delhi, India

Introduction: To investigate the impact of degenerative scoliosis on the incidence of adjacent vertebral fractures after percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP) in patients with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCFs). Methods: A retrospective review was performed on 269 patients who underwent PVP for OVCFs between January 2014 and October 2022, all with ≥ 24 months of follow-up. Among them, 121 patients had degenerative scoliosis. Patients were grouped by the presence and severity of scoliosis, and adjacent vertebral fracture incidence was compared. Cox proportional hazards regression and Kaplan-Meier survival analysis were applied to identify risk factors and recurrence times. Results: Of the 121 patients with degenerative scoliosis, 28 (23.1%) experienced adjacent vertebral fractures during follow-up, significantly higher than the rate in patients without scoliosis. Independent predictors of refracture included a T-score < -3.0 and the presence of degenerative scoliosis (both p < 0.001). When the scoliotic curve and index fracture were localized within T10-L4, a postoperative Cobb angle ≥ 20° (p = 0.002) and progressive angle increase (p = 0.001) markedly increased refracture risk. The mean recurrence interval was 17 months. Conclusion: Degenerative scoliosis is a significant risk factor for adjacent vertebral fractures after PKP in OVCF patients. Curve severity within the thoracolumbar region, particularly Cobb angle ≥ 20° or progressive deformity, further predisposes to refracture. Close postoperative surveillance and preventive measures are strongly recommended in this high-risk group.

## ID: AGS26/2837

## P0238: C2PA alone is insufficient: prediction of pelvic tilt correction requires consideration of lower-limb compensation

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Pelvic tilt (PT) correction is vital in adult spinal deformity surgery. The C2-pelvic angle (C2PA) is a proposed geometric predictor of PT change, yet its accuracy may be altered by compensatory lower-limb (bent-knee) posture. Whether ΔC2PA maintains predictive efficiency when such compensation is present remains unclear. Methods: We analyzed a prospectively collected cohort of adults undergoing long spinal fusion (≥ 7 levels) for deformity correction. Among 207 eligible patients, 184 had complete preoperative and 6-week postoperative standing radiographs including knee flexion angle (KFA) measurements. Patients were stratified based on preoperative knee flexion angle into Low (≤ 5°, n = 63), Mid (5-12.7°, n = 60), and High (> 12.7°, n = 61) groups. Demographic and radiographic variables were analyzed, focusing on PT, PI-LL mismatch, lumbar lordosis (LL), thoracic kyphosis (TK), thoracolumbar alignment (TL), T4-L1 pelvic angle (T4L1PA), and Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score. Preoperative to 6-week changes were compared between groups using ANOVA with Tukey post-hoc tests. Multivariable regression tested the contributions of ΔC2PA and preoperative C2 tilt to PT change (ΔPT = ΔC2PA+Preoperative C2 Tilt), with simple slope analysis quantifying the modifying effect of knee flexion. Results: Overall, ΔC2PA and preoperative C2 tilt explained PT change with high accuracy (adjusted R2 = 0.81). Stratified analysis showed the strongest fit in the Low flexion group (R2 = 0.86), intermediate in Mid (R2 = 0.79), and lowest in High (R2 = 0.75). Simple slope analysis revealed that the efficiency of ΔC2PA in translating to PT correction decreased progressively with knee flexion: from 0.82 PT/°C2PA at 0° flexion to 0.74 PT/°C2PA at 15°. This equates to roughly a 10% greater C2PA correction required in the presence of 15° knee flexion to achieve the same PT change. Prediction error of the geometric model also increased linearly (+0.063 per-degree of flexion, p = 0.003). At 6-week, patients with greater preoperative knee flexion achieved larger absolute corrections across several spinopelvic parameters. PT decreased by -3.0° in the Low, -5.0° in the Mid, and -8.2° in the High group (High vs Low: -5.2°, p = 0.002). PI-LL mismatch improved by -14.3° (Low), -19.1° (Mid), and -30.9° (High), with significantly greater correction in the High group compared with both Low (-16.6°, p < 0.001) and Mid (-9.8°, p = 0.005). Lumbar lordosis increased by +15.1° (Low), +22.0° (Mid), and +32.4° (High), with the High group showing the largest gain (+17.3° vs Low, p < 0.001). GAP score improved by -3.6 (Low), -4.1 (Mid), and -5.8 (High), with greater improvement in the High group compared with Low (-2.2, p = 0.004). For TK, TL, T4L1PA, preoperative values were worse in patients with greater knee flexion, but the magnitude of change from baseline to 6-week did not differ significantly between groups. Conclusions: C2PA alone is insufficient for predicting pelvic tilt correction without accounting for compensatory knee flexion. Patients with bent-knee posture have worse baseline spinopelvic parameters and achieve larger absolute corrections in PT, PI-LL mismatch, LL, and GAP score after surgery. Our study shows reduced efficiency of ΔC2PA in driving PT correction, with ∼10% more correction required at 15° of flexion. Surgical planning should explicitly incorporate knee compensation to optimize sagittal balance.

## ID: AGS26/2841

## P0239: C2 pelvic angle as a surrogate for global alignment and proportion: comparative validation against GAP and T4L1PA in long-fusion ASD surgery

Nuri Demirci

1

, Yasemin Yavuz

2

, Munish Gupta

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Washington University in St. Louis, Saint Louis, United States

2

Department of Biostatistics, Faculty of Medicine, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: The Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score is widely used for preoperative planning and mechanical complication risk assessment in adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery, but its intraoperative application is limited by reliance on Cobb angles. Vertebral-pelvic angles (VPAs) are reliable intraoperative metrics, though they lack segmental planning utility and their preoperative applicability remains restricted. C2 Pelvic Angle (C2PA) has been proposed as a parameter to guide pelvic tilt (PT) correction, yet its relationship to global alignment is not fully established. To examine the relationship between GAP score and C2PA in long-fusion ASD surgery, thereby assessing the validity of C2PA as a preoperative planning parameter and comparing its predictive value with T4L1PA. Methods: A prospective cohort of 207 surgical patients undergoing long-segment fusion (≥ 7 levels), all with pelvic fixation, was analyzed. Normative C2PA values were derived using the formula PI × 0.4 - 13. The primary outcome was C2PA mismatch > 13°, as proposed in prior literature derived from community-dwelling adults. Predictors included GAP score (continuous, categorical, binary (< 7 vs ≥ 7)) and T4L1PA (continuous, categorical, binary). For T4L1PA categorical analysis, values < -1° were classified as overcorrection, -1° to +3° as in target, and > +3° as under correction, consistent with published thresholds. For the binary analysis, a 4° cutoff was applied, with > 4° defined as high risk and ≤ 4° as low risk. Discrimination was assessed using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis with DeLong comparisons. Logistic regression estimated odds ratios for C2PA mismatch per unit increase in GAP. Patient-reported outcomes (PROs), including SRS-22 subscores/total and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), were evaluated at 1-year follow-up, the point at which improvements typically plateau. Results: GAP showed excellent discrimination for C2PA mismatch > 13° (AUC 0.93, 95% CI 0.89-0.96). Each 1-point increase in GAP doubled the odds of mismatch (OR 2.07, 95% CI 1.73-2.57), while a 5-point increase conferred OR 37.7 (95% CI 14.1-100.5). GAP continuous significantly outperformed T4L1PA continuous (AUC 0.93 vs. 0.86, p = 0.032). T4L1PA continuous demonstrated an AUC of 0.78 when tested against GAP severity. For categorical analyses, GAP performed strongly (AUC 0.87) and significantly outperformed T4L1PA categories (AUC 0.79, p = 0.023). Agreement between GAP and T4L1PA categorical groupings was negligible (κ ≈ 0). For binary analyses, GAP and T4L1PA demonstrated similar discrimination (AUC 0.76 vs. 0.77, p = 0.80), with GAP favoring specificity and T4L1PA favoring sensitivity. Patient-reported outcomes were consistently more favorable in patients with C2PA mismatch ≤ 13°, though none reached statistical significance. ODI showed a nonsignificant trend toward improvement in the ≤ 13° group. Conclusion: C2PA demonstrates strong potential as a practical surrogate for the GAP score in preoperative planning, offering proportional targets linked to global alignment. In contrast, T4L1PA is valuable intraoperatively but does not adequately capture overall alignment. Used together, C2PA and T4L1PA provide complementary guidance: C2PA for preoperative planning of proportional correction and T4L1PA for intraoperative verification.

## ID: AGS26/2868

## P0241: Perioperative recovery kinetics and narcotic use following CMIS surgery for adult spinal deformity correction of 6 or more levels

Neel Anand

1

, Nikita Iayer

1

, Anita Anand

1

, Gurmit Singh

1

, Tomi Lanre-Amos

1

, Nicole Nishime

1

, Babak Khandehroo

1

, Cheri Phillips

1

1

Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Circumferential minimal invasive surgery (cMIS) for adult spinal deformity (ASD) avoids extensive muscle dissections and osteotomies to achieve optimal alignment. Therefore, it is conceivable that patients would ambulate early after surgery and would need fewer narcotic medications. It is important to objectively quantify these parameters to better understand cMIS outcomes. We conducted this study to evaluate patient mobility after 2 stage cMIS and use of narcotic pain medications in early postoperative period and follow-up up to 3 months. Methods: We analyzed retrospectively a prospectively maintained registry of patients undergoing 6 or more levels thoracolumbar 2 staged cMIS for ASD from Jan 2011 to Dec 2023. Total number of steps ambulated in postop period and the morphine equivalent (MME) of narcotics administered were calculated in postoperative period and up to 3 months after surgery. Results: A total of 133 patients were identified. No patient went to the ICU126 patients (95%) were able to stand sometime between the 2 stages of cMIS. On postop day 1 {(POD 1), days denoted after second stage} 71 patients (53.3 %) were able to stand upright, and 22 patients (16 %) walked at least 25 feet. By POD 4,102 patients (77%) had walked at least 25 feet, of which 43 patients (32%) walked 100 feet or more. 52 patients were taking narcotic pain meds preoperatively. The patients needing narcotics decreased from 124 (93%) (average MME 89.7) on POD 1 to 36 patients (27%) on POD 7 (average 47.3 MME). At 2 week follow up, 57 patients (43 %) were completely off narcotic medications and the rest 76 patients had narcotics use decreased to 13.6 MME on average. At the 3 month follow up 94 patients (71%) were completely off narcotics, and the rest of 39 patients (29%) had a decreased use to an average of 4.8 MME (95 % decrease from POD 1). Conclusion: Utilizing cMIS for ASD probably helps in early mobility and use of significantly less narcotic pain medications post-surgery. These advantages of cMIS could minimize complications associated with hospital stay and could improve functional outcomes in the early postoperative phase. This possibly makes cMIS a more favorable option for elderly population and patients with comorbidities. Further studies directly comparing data from open surgical techniques may be warranted to delineate the differences with cMIS.

 

## ID: AGS26/2872

## P0242: Translating research to clinical practice: understanding the research needs and learning methods of surgeons to help guide future spine deformity research: a survey of AO Spine members

Marta Morawska

1

, Nicole Lewis

2

, Stephen Lewis

2

, AO Spine Knowledge Forum Deformity

1

1

AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

2

University Health Network, Toronto, Canada

Introduction: Outcomes research through prospective and retrospective studies have helped provide guidance in the management of patients. How this research is disseminated to reach the target audience is an evolving process especially with the rapid advances in technology and social media. Projects that we have undertaken to date have had variable uptake by the spine surgery community. Controversy exists whether the incorporation of our work was influenced by the relevance of the research or the method of dissemination. Before investing in future studies, a survey was carried out to determine the needs of our target spine surgeons, assess the impact of our past work, and understand the modalities used to acquire new knowledge. Material and Methods: An anonymous English language survey was developed on Survey Monkey and consisted of 40 questions, including knowledge gaps and knowledge translation. The survey was distributed to members of AO Spine via email with a link to the questionnaire between March 20th and April 14th, 2025. Data analysis was performed with Excel. Results: A total of 399 responses were received from AO Spine members practicing across 60 countries. 28% of responses were from Asia Pacific region, 27% from Europe South Africa, 18% from Latin America, 16% from North America, and 11% from the Middle East and Northern Africa. 80% of responders were orthopedic surgeons. 279 respondents reported a focus on deformity surgery. 75%vof responders who performed deformity surgery indicated they use classification tools when evaluating patients and 80% routinely apply clinical guidelines in treatment planning, while 91% stated that findings from multicenter studies influence their clinical decision-making. 52-62% of responders were not aware of our previously published tools, revealing significant gaps in dissemination and adoption across clinical settings. The most cited sources of exposure included publications, conferences, and AO Spine educational courses, while key barriers to implementation were difficulties incorporating additional resources into existing workflows and administrative limitations. About 50% of responders were familiar with Scoli-RISK study, while 45% reported being unaware of our other cited studies. The most common channels through which respondents became aware of these studies included conferences and meetings, peer-reviewed journals, and AO Spine courses. The highest-rated research priorities identified by the respondents included the development of diagnostic and treatment guidelines (71%) and prediction tools or decision algorithms (69%). The most critical knowledge gaps in the field of spinal deformity identified by 259 participants were proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) prevention and treatment (69%), spinal alignment guidelines (54%), guidance for treatment of osteoporotic patients (52%), better tools for risk stratification for patients undergoing surgery (46%), and pseudarthrosis and rod fracture/instrumentation failure (41%). Conclusion: PJK, spinal alignment and osteoporosis were the leading knowledge gaps identified by the spinal deformity surgeons responding to the survey. The highest-rated research priorities were development of diagnostic and treatment guidelines and prediction tools/ decision algorithms. The dissemination of research outcomes should be improved to aid the adoption in clinical settings. The results of this survey will help in our planning future research projects and knowledge translation activities.

## ID: AGS26/2887

## P0243: High pelvic incidence and limited PI-LL correction increase risk of rod fracture

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Ryan Screven

1

, Srujan Kopparapu

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

University of South Florida, Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Rod fracture (RF) is a frequent mechanical complication of adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. Spinopelvic alignment is central to sagittal balance and inadequate alignment may predispose to construct failure. This study evaluated the impact of preoperative parameters, postoperative correction, and ROC-derived thresholds on RF occurrence. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed the charts of 140 ASD patients who underwent long-segment posterior fusion (≥ 7 levels) between 2015 and 2023. Radiographic analysis included pelvic incidence (PI), pelvic tilt (PT), lumbar lordosis (LL), and PI-LL mismatch (PILL). Preoperative values, postoperative outcomes, and correction (ΔLL, ΔPT, ΔPILL) were compared between patients with and without RF. ROC analysis was performed to identify optimal predictive thresholds. Results: RF occurred in 35.7% of patients at a median of 761 days postoperatively. Patients with RF had higher preoperative PI (60.5° vs. 56.5°, p = 0.037) and PT (28.7° vs. 25.8°, p = 0.042). Preoperative LL (p = 0.183) and PILL (p = 0.573) were not predictive. Postoperative PT (21.6° vs. 19.8°, p = 0.229), LL (50.4° vs. 48.3°, p = 0.273), and PILL (11.9° vs. 9.7°, p = 0.264) did not significantly differ between groups. However, ΔPILL was smaller in the RF group (-15.3° vs. -19.9°, p = 0.042), indicating that insufficient mismatch correction increased fracture rates. ΔLL (p = 0.696) and ΔPT (p = 0.453) were not associated. ROC analysis demonstrated modest discriminative ability. PI ≥ 52.5° predicted RF with sensitivity 0.84 and specificity 0.37 (AUC = 0.60). PT ≥ 25.5° showed sensitivity 0.74 and specificity 0.47 (AUC = 0.61). ΔPILL ≥ -26.3° yielded sensitivity 0.88 and specificity 0.31 (AUC = 0.59). Conclusion: Elevated preoperative PI and PT, together with insufficient PILL correction, were associated with higher RF rates. ROC analysis identified sensitive thresholds for PI and ΔPILL, though overall predictive accuracy was limited. These findings highlight the importance of careful preoperative alignment assessment and optimization of sagittal correction to reduce mechanical failure risk in ASD surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2986

## P0244: Can ideal L1PA and T4PA-L1PA mismatch predict mechanical failure in circumferential minimally invasive adult spine deformity correction?

Neel Anand

1

, Nikita Iayer

1

, Anita Anand

1

, Gurmit Singh

1

, Tomi Lanre-Amos

1

, Nicole Nishime

1

, Babak Khandehroo

1

, Cheri Phillips

1

1

Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Role of sagittal balance in improving patient clinical outcomes has been well established in adult spine deformity (ASD) correction. More recently, L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) and L1PA-T4 pelvic angle (T4PA) mismatch have been shown as predictors of post-operative mechanical failures in long construct deformity correction. We conducted this study to evaluate if realignment of ideal L1PA and T4PA-L1PA mismatch in ASD patients undergoing circumferential minimally invasive spine fusion decreases postoperative mechanical complications. Method: Single surgeon adult spinal deformity (ASD) database of patients who underwent circumferential minimally invasive spine surgery (cMISS) was retrospectively examined from 2011 to 2022. Inclusion criteria were ASD (Cobb > 20°, SVA > 50mm, PI-LL > 10°), fusion 6 levels, minimum 2-year follow-up, upper instrumented level (UIV) T10-T12 and lowest instrumented vertebrae (LIV) of L5 or below, with having pre-operative and post-operative long standing X-rays. Pre-operative and post-operative long standing X-rays were measured for L1PA, T4PA, sacral vertical axis (SVA), pelvic incidence (PI), lumbar lordosis (LL), PI-LL mismatch for each patient. Ideal L1PA was defined as (0.5 x PI) - 21 ± 5°. The ideal T4PA-L1PA mismatch was defined as -4° to 4°. Patient charts were reviewed for any mechanical complications. A mechanical complication was defined as proximal junctional kyphosis, proximal junctional failure, rod fracture, reoperation due to hardware failure, pseudoarthrosis, screw loosening. Mechanical complications rates and revisions rates were compared between patients who attained an ideal L1PA and L1PA-T4PA mismatch versus those did not. Results: 109 patients met the inclusion criteria with an average age of 66.8 years (22-84, SD 9.6). The average pre-operative L1PA, T4PA-L1PA mismatch, SVA, PI-LL mismatch was 14.8°, 8.2°, 69.7mm, and 20.5° respectively. The average post-operative L1PA, T4PA- L1PA mismatch, SVA, PI-LL mismatch was 12.2°, 6.5°, 40.1mm, and 10.2°, respectively (p < 0.01). 68 (62.4%) patients achieved an ideal L1PA post-operatively. 44 (40.4%) patients achieved an ideal T4PA-L1PA mismatch. Mechanical complication rate between patients who achieved an ideal L1PA versus those who did not was 29.3% and 8.8%, respectively (p < 0.01). Mechanical complication requiring revisions surgery in patients who achieved an ideal L1PA versus those who did not was 12.2% and 5.9%, respectively (p = 0.29). Mechanical complication rate between patients who had an ideal post-operative T4PA- L1PA mismatch versus those who did not was 4.5% and 24.6%, respectively (p < 0.05). Mechanical complication requiring revisions surgery in patients who achieved an ideal T4PA- L1PA mismatch versus those who did not was 2.3% and 12.3%, respectively (p < 0.05). Proximal junctional failure requiring revision surgery was seen in only 1 patient who did not achieve an ideal L1PA and in 2 patients who had an ideal L1PA post-operatively. Conclusion: L1PA and T4PA- L1PA mismatch may be predictive of mechanical failure in patients undergoing circumferential minimally invasive spine fusion for spinal deformity. T4PA- L1PA mismatch may be predictive of need for revision surgery. However, L1PA and T4PA- L1PA mismatch were not predictive of proximal junctional kyphosis or proximal junctional failure.

 

 

## ID: AGS26/2993

## P0245: Long segment 6 Plus levels circumferential minimal invasive surgery (cMIS) for adult spinal deformity: 2-year outcomes analysis

Neel Anand

1

, Nikita Iayer

1

, Anita Anand

1

, Gurmit Singh

1

, Tomi Lanre-Amos

1

, Nicole Nishime

1

, Babak Khandehroo

1

, Cheri Phillips

1

1

Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Deformity correction with circumferential minimal invasive surgery (cMIS) involving longer segments (6+ levels) spanning the thoracolumbar spine is technically more challenging than fusions limited to lumbar spine. Rod fixation and reduction of deformity over more vertebral levels in a minimally invasive subfascial manner contributes to the technical difficulty. Radiological and clinical outcomes specific to fusions involving these long segments for adult spinal deformity (ASD) have not been delineated previously. We conducted this study to evaluate radiological spinal parameters and clinical outcomes at 2 years postoperatively in the 6+ levels of cMIS deformity correction patient group. Methods: A prospectively collected database of all patients who underwent CMIS correction of ASD from Jan 2011 to December 2021 was studied. Inclusion criteria was instrumentation of 6 + levels with upper instrumented vertebra (UIV): T12 and above, lower instrumented vertebra (LIV):S1 and below in patients with ASD [Cobb > 20, SVA > 50 mm, pelvic incidence PI- lumbar lordosis LL > 10, or pelvic tilt (PT) > 20], and at least 2year follow-up. Pre-operative, post-operative, and ≥ 2 years radiographic parameters (Cobb, CVA, SVA, LL, PI, PT, SS, PI-LL mismatch), clinical outcomes (VAS, ODI, SRS-22), complications and readmissions were analyzed. Results: A total of 105 patients were identified of which four patients had passed away from natural causes and seven were lost to follow-up. Thirty patients had a follow up between 1-2 years and 64 patients had ≥ 2 year complete radiological and clinical data and were included for this study (Mean = 59 month (24-130, SD 26.6). The clinical outcomes scores VAS (p < 0.001), TIS (p < 0.001), ODI (p = 0.01), SRS22 (p = 0.01) were all significantly improved both at 3-month and 2-year follow ups. The sagittal radiological measure of alignment including SVA, PI-LL mismatch, lumbar lordosis and thoracic kyphosis improved significantly compared to preop, whereas correction in coronal alignment/CVA was not statistically significant (p = 0.06). Three patients had proximal junctional failure, and underwent hardware revision (2 weeks, 15 and 21 months postop). Pseudoarthrosis with hardware failure were noted in three patients at thoracolumbar junctions and underwent reinstrumentation. Two patients had hardware prominence with associated localized pain posteriorly and underwent hardware removal. Four patients had asymptomatic hardware failure noted past 13 months that did not need any intervention. Two patients had superficial wound dehiscence and underwent debridement and closure. Conclusion: In the treatment of ASD, cMIS involving long segment fusions is a safe and effective option both for deformity correction and improving patient outcomes significantly. There were no catastrophic complications. The complication profile and the rates of hardware failure and proximal junctional failure are noted to be lower compared to reported outcomes with open deformity correction techniques.

## ID: AGS26/2994

## P0246: Rod fracture after adult spinal deformity surgery: a surgical rather than patient-driven complication

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Ryan Screven

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Srujan Kopparapu

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) correction is a complex surgical undertaking that often requires extensive instrumentation and carries a risk of mechanical failure, including rod fracture (RF). While technical factors have been linked to RF, ambiguous evidence regarding patient-derived variables such as age, sex, BMI, or baseline frailty and disability has contributed to hesitancy in offering surgical treatment to vulnerable populations. This study investigates the relationship between patient characteristics and the incidence of RF to evaluate whether certain populations are indeed more susceptible to this complication. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed the charts of ASD patients who underwent posterior spinal fusion with ≥ 7 instrumented levels between 2015 and 2023 at a single academic center. Follow-up was a minimum of 1 year. RF was confirmed by radiographic imaging. Patient variables included age, sex, BMI, sarcopenia (measured via MRI-based psoas index), preoperative opioid use, frailty score, and baseline Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Statistical associations between these variables and RF were assessed using Spearman’s correlation and chi-square testing. Results: Among 140 patients, RF occurred in 35.7%. RF rates were not influenced by baseline patient characteristics such as age (rho = -0.016, p = 0.851), sex (χ2 = 0.001, p = 0.980), or BMI (rho = -0.046, p = 0.592). Similarly, patient health indicators including sarcopenia (rho = 0.072, p = 0.502), frailty score (rho = 0.078, p = 0.361), and ODI (rho = -0.151, p = 0.089) showed no association with RF. Preoperative opioid use, often a surrogate for complex chronic conditions, also did not differ between groups (p = 0.390). Conclusion: In our cohort, RF rates were not influenced by baseline patient factors including age, BMI, sex, frailty, sarcopenia, or preoperative disability. These findings suggest that the risk of RF is shaped primarily by surgical and biomechanical factors rather than by variations in patient health or baseline characteristics. They also reveal that it may be safe to implement standard reinforcement strategies across a broad and diverse ASD population, regardless of preoperative health status.

## ID: AGS26/3002

## P0247: Precision computer-assisted surgical planning for double-level modified PSO in the thoracolumbar region for severe kyphotic deformity in ankylosing spondylitis

Huiren Tao

1

, Zhe Wang

1

1

Hong Kong University-Shenzhen Hospital, Shenzhen, China

Objective: This study aimed to evaluate the accuracy of a computer-assisted surgical planning approach integrating automated image stitching, apical vertebra identification, and a machine learning algorithm for double-level modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy (mPSO) in severe kyphotic deformity secondary to ankylosing spondylitis (AS). We hypothesized that this solution would overcome the limitations of traditional radiographic assessment and improve the precision of osteotomy planning. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted on 26 AS patients with severe kyphosis who underwent double-level mPSO (including both single- and double-level osteotomy cases in a 2:1 ratio). A custom-developed module within the 3D Slicer platform was employed, utilizing a three-feature-point matching algorithm for full-spine image stitching. Spinal curvature analysis was used for apical vertebra identification and osteotomy simulation. A machine learning model was trained on preoperative parameters (Sagittal Vertical Axis - SVA, Global Kyphosis - GK, Chin-Brow Vertical Angle - CBVA) to predict the osteotomy strategy (1-level or 2-level). Results: The planning platform achieved an 80% success rate in full-spine image stitching and 85% accuracy in apical vertebra identification. The machine learning model demonstrated 85% agreement with the actual surgical decisions regarding the number of osteotomy levels. Postoperative assessment revealed significant improvement in sagittal alignment: SVA decreased by 20.79 ± 1.60 cm, the kyphotic angle decreased by 47.23 ± 4.14°, and the pelvic incidence minus lumbar lordosis (PI-LL) mismatch improved from 79.65 ± 4.25° to 30.52 ± 4.20°. T12 and L3 were the most frequently selected osteotomy levels. The mean operative time was 511.1 minutes with an average blood loss of 2159 mL. Conclusion: Computer-assisted planning incorporating advanced image analysis and machine learning effectively supports surgical decision-making for severe AS-related kyphotic deformity. This approach enables accurate spinal assessment, precise osteotomy planning, and contributes to personalized surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3009

## P0248: Efficacy and safety of modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy for kyphotic deformity in ankylosing spondylitis

Huiren Tao

1

, Zhe Wang

1

1

Hong Kong University-Shenzhen Hospital, Shenzhen, China

To summarize our experience with modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy (mPSO) for kyphotic deformity in ankylosing spondylitis (AS), focusing on its efficacy and safety. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 149 patients who underwent mPSO between January 2018 and September 2025, all performed by a single surgeon. Single-level osteotomy: 121 patients; double-level osteotomy: 26 patients. Patient demographics, radiographic correction, clinical outcomes, and perioperative complications were analyzed. Results: The mean follow-up duration was 35.17 ± 9.98 months. Both single- and double-level osteotomy groups achieved significant improvement in sagittal alignment. SVA improved from 766.5 ± 176.4 mm to 101.4 ± 65.03 mm (single-level) and from 1044.0 ± 198.6 mm to 139.1 ± 75.3 mm (double-level). Maximum thoracic kyphosis decreased from 42.00 ± 12.71° to 22.02 ± 14.92° (single-level) and from 104.40 ± 19.86° to 47.23 ± 20.69° (double-level). PT improved from 37.02 ± 13.30° to 28.56 ± 11.25 (single-level) and from 32.56 ± 18.33° to 28.77 ± 13.10° (double-level). PI-LL decreased from 13.56 ± 8.32° to 7.85 ± 8.95° (single-level) and from 79.65 ± 21.26° to 30.52 ± 21.02° (double-level). LL increased from -3.33 ± 2.62 to 29.80 ± 2.25 (single-level) and from -24.42 ± 17.73° to 21.50 ± 17.58° in the single- and double-level groups, respectively. The overall complication rate was 20.0% for single-level osteotomy and 26.9% for double-level osteotomy, with the most common events being dural tears/CSF leakage, neurological deficits, and systemic or wound-related complications. Severe complications, such as intraoperative neurophysiological signal loss and postoperative motor deficits, were rare, occurring in 2.7% of cases. Conclusion: mPSO provides effective radiographic correction of kyphotic deformity in AS, with overall satisfactory safety. However, 2 cases of severe intraoperative complications occurred, highlighting the need for careful surgical planning and meticulous intraoperative technique.

## ID: AGS26/3093

## P0249: Preoperative radiographic and CT evaluation imprinted on plain paper: a cost-effective, accurate alternative to navigation and robot-assisted correction of severe, rigid, and neglected spinal deformities in resource-limited settings

Sharif Jonayed

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopaedic Rehabilitation (NITOR), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Navigation and robotic systems enhance precision in spinal deformity correction but remain inaccessible in many resource-limited settings due to cost and infrastructure demands. This study evaluates a low-cost, paper-based preoperative planning method using serial radiographs and CT scans to reconstruct pedicle anatomy, curvature patterns, screw trajectories, and osteotomy strategies-imprinted on plain paper-for severe, rigid, and neglected spinal deformities. Material and Methods: Thirty-two patients (mean age: 18.9 ± 5.4 years) underwent corrective surgery planned with the imprinted paper-based technique. Preoperative imaging was manually analyzed to reconstruct spinal anatomy and simulate instrumentation. Surgical and radiographic outcomes were compared with published navigation-assisted benchmarks. Results: Preoperative Cobb angles ranged from 81.6° to 110.2° (mean 94.8° ± 7.3°) and improved postoperatively to 23.8°-40.6° (mean 31.2° ± 4.6°), yielding correction rates of 61.9%-76.5% (mean 68.4% ± 4.1%). Screw placement accuracy was 89.8%-97.0% (mean 93.9% ± 2.2%), with malposition rates of 2.0%-6.1% (mean 3.7% ± 1.1%). Operative times ranged from 6.1 to 7.8 hours (mean 6.9 ± 0.5 hours), comparable to complex navigation-assisted surgeries. Neurological complications occurred in four patients (12.5%), all transient and resolved without permanent deficit. Estimated cost savings were 82%-88% compared to navigation-equipped procedures. At 15-26 months follow-up (mean 20.4 ± 2.6 months), all patients maintained stable correction and solid fusion. Conclusion: Preoperative radiographic and CT evaluation imprinted on plain paper is a scientifically robust, cost-effective, and accurate alternative to navigation- or robot-assisted techniques for severe spinal deformity correction. Comparable correction rates, screw accuracy, and operative times, coupled with manageable complication rates, make this method a practical solution for delivering high-quality spine care in resource-limited environments.

## ID: AGS26/3102

## P0250: Single-stage posterior vertebral column resection (PVCR) for the correction of severe and rigid spinal deformities: a prospective analysis of 31 cases

Sharif Jonayed

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopaedic Rehabilitation (NITOR), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: The management of severe and rigid spinal deformities remains one of the greatest challenges in spine surgery. Posterior vertebral column resection (PVCR) has evolved into a powerful technique that allows three-column correction through a single posterior approach. Despite its technical demands and potential complications, PVCR offers the possibility of meaningful correction and functional improvement in carefully selected patients. Material and Method: This prospective case series included 31 patients with severe (Cobb angle > 60°) or rigid kyphotic deformities who underwent single-stage PVCR between July 2016 and June 2023. Demographic data, operative parameters, radiological correction, functional outcomes, and complications were analyzed. Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for pain. Results: The mean age of patients was 30.7 ± 9.8 years (range 15-52), with a mean follow-up of 27.6 ± 8.4 months. The average operative time was 422 ± 95 minutes (range 320-760), and mean intraoperative blood loss was 760 ± 210 mL (range 560-1380). Postoperative kyphotic Cobb angle correction ranged from 31.2° to 56.4°, representing substantial deformity reduction. Functional outcomes improved significantly: mean ODI decreased from 58.6 ± 6.5 preoperatively to 11.2 ± 3.5 at final follow-up, and mean VAS improved from 6.7 ± 0.5 to 1.8 ± 0.7. Complications included dural tears in 3 patients (9.6%), transient thoracic root pain in 1 patient (3.2%), and neurological deterioration in 4 patients (12.9%)-three of which were transient with full recovery within 3 months, and one late-onset deficit with partial recovery. Surgical site infection occurred in 1 patient (3.2%). No mortality was observed. Conclusion: Single-stage PVCR is a safe and effective option for the correction of severe and rigid spinal deformities when performed in experienced hands. Despite its complexity and potential complications, PVCR can achieve substantial radiological correction, significant pain reduction, and marked functional improvement, making it a valuable tool in the armamentarium of deformity surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3347

## P0251: Clinical and radiological results for dual anchorage (SI-S2AI) system using patient specific templates in long construc instrumentations

Geert Mahieu

1

1

Orthoca Spine Surgery Department, Antwerp, Belgium

Introduction: In the context of long-segment instrumentations, extension of fixation to the sacroiliac joint (SIJ) is often required to ensure mechanical stability. At the same time, the SIJ is increasingly recognized as a potential source of chronic pain after long-segment instrumentations. Therefore, simultaneous posterior sacroiliac fusion could be a critical, yet technically demanding step during these compex surgeries. This study aims to evaluate sacroiliac joint fusion and screw placement accuracy while also documenting complication and revision rates in a cohort of patients treated with this novel patient-matched technology. Material and Methods: A dual-anchorage system with both an SI- as well as an S2AI screw was used bilaterally in 20 patients. Each patient underwent a preoperative CT scan, which is used to perform surgical planning for the customization of the patient-specific guide. All surgeries are performed by a single surgeon via a posterior only approach. Follow-up evaluations are conducted at 3 and 6 months postoperatively including collection of sarco-iliac pain through VAS score and provocative tests. Subjects underwent high-resolution CT at 6 months after SI joint fusion. An independent radiologist analysed CT scans for the following endpoints: 1) Degree of apposition of bone to the implant within the sacrum (rated as 0-30%, 30-50%, 50-90%, > 90%). 2) Bridging bone within the treated joint (and, if present, location of bridging [adjacent, distant, both] and extent of bridging (< 5%, 5-15%, 15-30%, 30-100% of SI joint volume). 3) evidence of positive bone remodeling, defined as presence of increased bone density/sclerosis adjacent to the implant that appears to be due to new bone formation and/or an increase in mechanical demand at or distant from the bone-implant interface. A superimposition analysis was performed comparing the planned and actual screw trajectories. Results: 20 patients were operated by one single surgeon. 40 SI and 40 S2AI screws were placed. 3/40 (7.5 %) S2AI screws and 1/40 2.5 %) SI screw were placed in a direction that was significantly deviated from the preoperative planning. One complication related to malplacement of the screws was noted (Sacral fracture). None of the screws were revised. 40 SIJ’s were instrumented. In 2/20 (10 %) patients, we could not confirm fusion of the SI joint. In a third patient an infection led to removal of the implanted screws. Postoperatively 60 % of patients were satisfied with their overall clinical result. 1 out of 20 (10 %) patients had pain related to the SI joint. Conclusion: Using a patient specific guide for both SI and S2AI screw placement is suggestive to be accurate and safe. SI fusion can be achieved using this dual anchorage system in the vast majority of cases. Additional clinical benefit of less postoperative SI joint pain after long segment fusion was noted. Further research with larger cohorts is necessary to confirm these findings.

## A ID: AGS26/3349

## P0251.1: Association between sagittal malalignment and proximal junctional compression in lumbar fusion patients

Christopher J. Nielsen

1

, Fatemeh Alavi

1

, Stephen Lewis

1

1

University of Toronto, University Health Network (UHN), Toronto, Canada

Introduction: Sagittal malalignment is a well-established risk factor for mechanical complications following adult spine deformity correction surgery. In particular, increased biomechanical loading at the proximal junction has been linked to proximal junctional kyphosis and failure. The Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score was developed to assess the adequacy of spinopelvic realignment, yet its relationship with proximal junctional loading remains insufficiently defined. This study aims to investigate the association between GAP score and compressive force at the proximal junction in patients with lumbar fusion to the pelvis. Methods: For 41 patients who underwent lumbar fusion to the pelvis, 246 sagittal alignments were simulated using each patient’s preoperative alignment and five suggested realignment strategies: Age-adjusted, GAP, Segmental, Roussouly, and T4–L1-axis. Musculoskeletal (MSK) models were generated by adjusting sex-matched generic templates to various spinal alignments. The models were fused from L2 to the pelvis. MSK analysis was performed under standing posture and spinal compression at the proximal junction (UIV/UIV+1) were calculated. The association between proximal junctional compression and GAP score was analyzed. Because the data were not normally distributed, Spearman’s rank correlation was used to assess associations, and GAP categories (proportioned, moderately disproportioned, severely disproportioned) were compared using the Kruskal–Wallis test and Post-hoc Dunn’s test. Results: Spearman’s correlation demonstrated a significant positive association between GAP score and proximal junctional compression (ρ = 0.63, p < 0.001). Kruskal–Wallis testing confirmed significant differences in compression across GAP categories (H = 77.8, p < 0.001). Post-hoc Dunn’s test revealed stepwise increases, with compression forces significantly higher in moderately and severely disproportioned patients compared with proportioned alignment, and highest in the severely disproportioned group (all p < 0.001). Conclusion: Proximal junctional compression is strongly associated with GAP score in patients undergoing lumbar fusion to the pelvis. Higher GAP scores, indicating greater sagittal malalignment, correspond to significantly increased biomechanical load at the proximal junction. These findings suggest that restoring proportional spinopelvic alignment may reduce mechanical stress and potentially lower the risk of proximal junctional complications.

## ID: AGS26/3394

## P0252: Should we try to classify the etiology of adult spinal deformity? An analysis of images from the PEEDS study

Ganesh Swamy

1

, So Kato

2

, Brian Neuman

3

, Robert Ravinsky

4

, Stephen Lewis

5

1

University of Calgary, Calgary, Canada

2

University of Tokyo, Tokyo, Japan

3

Washington University, St. Louis, United States

4
Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States, 
5
University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada
Introduction: Adult spinal deformity (ASD) is a broad term, encompassing patients with a variety of disorders which lead to radiographic spinal deformity. Babu et al. reported a consensus Delphi-based study to categorize ASD patients, which resulted in a classification system encompassing 4 broad metrics to classify the General Health Status, Spine-Specific Status encompassing many common HRQOL sets, Radiographic Measurements and Type of Deformity. Type of Deformity is derived from the Aebi classification, which classifies ASD into three categories: de novo degenerative lumbar scoliosis (DNDLS), progressive idiopathic scoliosis in adulthood (Adult Idiopathic Scoliosis (AdIS)) and Secondary Scoliosis (e.g. pelvic obliquity due to leg-length inequality, neuromuscular or congenital disorders). The Delphi panel felt with almost unanimity that the classification should be included. In theory, the Aebi classification represents different disease processes which may have effect on outcomes. It was felt that patients with DNDLS may have different curve patterns and worse sagittal plane parameters which may lead to different surgical patterns than in AdIS. However, the discriminant ability and reliability of the Aebi classification have not been well-tested. Guler et al. tested the classification reliability of 179 x-rays (103 AdIS and 76 DNDLS) amongst 5 surgeons and found modest interrater reliability. Unanimity of classification was achieved in 42/103 AdIS patients and in only 17/76 DNDLS patients and Kappa coefficients ranged from 0.04 to 0.6. In this study, we sought to measure reliability of the Aebi classification in a set of images from the PEEDS study. Material and Methods: In this post-hoc analysis from prospectively collected data, elderly ASD (> 60 years) images patients from the international, multicenter Prospective Evaluation of Elderly Deformity Surgery (PEEDS) study were anonymized and 4 experienced spine surgeons (GS, RR, SK and SL) classified the images, with 3 surgeons classifying them twice. Images were examined in person in the first round, and then again in person 6 months later. We used Microsoft Forms for data collection, with separate terms for Classification (Likely DNDLS, Likely AdIS, Congenital and Other). There were a maximum 7 classifications per image. 50 images were examined, with AP and lateral 36” images being reviewed. Frequencies of case classifications over 80%, between 60-80% and below 60% were measured. For each case, surgeons were asked a descriptive field leading to their decision, including Cobb angle, lateral rotatory olisthesis, sagittal plane imbalance, Schwab classification (D, N or L) and were able to enter free text. Frequencies of these descriptors were tallied. Results: There were 21 cases with 7 classifications, 28 cases with 6 classifications, and one case with only 4 classifications. Of 50 cases, only 5 (10%) received unanimous classification as DNDLS. A further 11 cases had only 1 instance of misclassification (22%) and were mostly classified as DNDLS. Therefore, 16/50 or 32% of cases had high levels of agreement. In 11 (or 22%), there was 60-80% agreement. In 23 cases (or 46%), there was less than 60% agreement. Some descriptors were strongly associated with classification. When there was sagittal plane imbalance, 96% of the cases were classified as DNDLS. Similarly, a thoracolumbar Cobb apex resulted in a AdIS classification 90% of the time. Lateral rotatory olisthesis was alternately classified as DNDLS (63%) or AdIS (37%). Conclusion: While the Aebi classification is deemed important by spine surgeons in a Delphi process, the accuracy of classification is poor. Only 32% of cases had high concordance of agreement, and these were highly more likely to be DNDLS cases. As such, without further development of classification systems, based on a single visualized image, surgeons cannot achieve concordance on the underlying diagnosis of ASD. The only other investigation on classification reliability for etiology of ASD was by Guler et al., who also found only fair agreement between observers. In both studies, sagittal plane imbalance was rated to be highly associated with DNDLS. While AdIS and DNDLS may be distinct diagnostic groups and may respond to treatment differently, it is impossible to accurately classify at this time. Future investigations could investigate serial images over a long time-range (> 20 years) to understand the evolution of ASD cases.

## ID: AGS26/3429

## P0253: Social determinants of health predict patient-reported pain outcomes 60 months following surgery for grade 2 lumbar spondylolisthesis: a QOD study

Vardhaan Ambati

1

, Niall Buckley

1

, Jay Kumar

1

, Kai-Ming Fu

1

, Timothy Yee

2

, Andrew Chan

3

, Jay Turner

4

, Juan Uribe

4

, Anthony Asher

5

, Domagoj Coric

5

, Michael Virk

6

, Christopher Shaffrey

7

, Oren Gottfried

7

, Eric Potts

8

, Mohamad Bydon

9

, Michael Wang

10

, Paul Park

11

, Steven Glassman

12

, Kevin Foley

11

, Cheerag Upadhyaya

13

, Dean Chou

3

, Chun-Po Yen

14

, Erica Bisson

15

, Anthony DiGiorgio

1

, Praveen Mummaneni

1

1

University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

2

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

3

Columbia University, New York, United States

4

Barrow, Phoenix, United States

5

CNSA, Charlotte, United States

6

Cornell University, New York, United States

7

Duke University, Durham, United States

8

Goodman Campbell, Indianapolis, United States

9

University of Chicago, Chicago, United States

10

University of Miami, Miami, United States

11

Semmes-Murphey, Memphis, United States

12

Norton Leatherman Spine Center, Louisville, United States

13

University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, United States

14

University of Virginia, Charlottesville, United States

15

University of Utah, Salt Lake City, United States

Introduction: We aim to investigate whether social determinants of health (SDOH) affect patient-reported outcomes (PROs) 2 years after surgery for Meyerding grade 2 lumbar spondylolisthesis. Methods: Patients with grade 2 spondylolisthesis prospectively enrolled at the 14 SpineCORe study group sites of the Quality Outcomes Database (QOD) were identified. Separate multivariate linear regression models were trained to identify predictors of 60-month postoperative PROs, including numerical rating scale (NRS) leg pain (NRS-LP), back pain (NRS-BP). Each model was trained using the following SDOH: insurance payor, education level (at least college degree vs none), and preoperative employment status. The models also controlled for age, gender, ASA grade, BMI, and preoperative value of each PRO. Results: In total, 328 patients who underwent surgery for grade 2 lumbar spondylolisthesis were identified. Twenty-five patients expired prior to the 60-month follow-up point, yielding a cohort of 303 patients. The 60-month follow-up rate for this cohort of 303 patients was 81%. Usage of Medicaid versus private insurance was an independent predictor of worse NRS-LP (β-coefficient: 2.564, p = 0.007) and NRS-BP (β-coefficient: 2.2, p = 0.027) at 60-month follow-up. Medicaid insurance status was associated with a worse baseline NRS-BP compared to private insurance (Medicaid: 8.8 ± 1.5 vs. Private: 6.9 ± 2.2). Patients with Medicaid insurance compared to private insurance had worse absolute NRS leg (Medicaid: 4.9 ± 2.2 vs. Private: 2.3 ± 2.8, p = 0.004) and back (Medicaid: 5.4 ± 3.2 vs. Private: 2.5 ± 2.7, p = 0.026) pain scores at 60-month follow-up; however, they experienced similar degrees of preoperative to postoperative (delta) leg (Medicaid: -1.8 ± 3.0 vs. Private: -4.0 ± 3.8, p = 0.052) and back (Medicaid: -3.4 ± 2.8 vs. Private: -4.3 ± 3.3, p = 0.387) pain improvements. Conclusion: In patients with grade II spondylolisthesis, Medicaid insurance status was associated with worse baseline low back pain. After surgery for grade II spondylolisthesis, Medicaid insurance status was an independent predictor of worse leg pain and significantly associated with worse absolute leg and back pain, Patients with grade II spondylolisthesis experienced similar improvements in back and leg pain following surgery regardless of insurance status.

## P0254: What factors predict return to work after surgery for grade 1 lumbar spondylolisthesis? A 5-year spine CORe QOD analysis

Timothy Yee

1

, Vardhaan Ambati

, Jay Kumar

, Arati Patel

, Anthony DiGiorgio

2

, Catherine Ravikumar

2

, Paul Kim

3

, Anthony Asher

, Eric Potts

4

, Erica Bisson

5

, John Knightly

6

, Kai-Ming Fu

7

, Michael Virk

7

, Kevin Foley

8

, Paul Park

8

, Chun-Po Yen

9

, Mohamad Bydon

10

, Dean Chou

11

, Andrew Chan

11

, Christopher Shaffrey

12

, Jonathan Slotkin

13

, Michael Wang

14

, Regis Haid

15

, Steven Glassman

16

, Praveen Mummaneni

2

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

2

University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

3

CNSA, Charlotte, United States

4

Goodman Campbell, Indianapolis, United States

5

University of Utah, Salt Lake City, United States

6

Maxim Spine, Morristown, United States

7

Cornell University, New York, United States

8

Semmes-Murphey, Memphis, United States

9

University of Virginia, Charlottesville, United States

10

University of Chicago, Chicago, United States

11

Columbia University, New York, United States

12

Duke University, Durham, United States

13

Geisinger Neuroscience Institute, Danville, United States

14

University of Miami, Miami, United States

15

Atlanta Brain and Spine Care, Atlanta, United States

16

Norton Leatherman Spine Center, Louisville, United States

Introduction: Unemployment following surgery incurs significant societal costs. We aim to identify predictors of return to work following surgery for grade 1 lumbar spondylolisthesis. Methods: Patients in the prospective Quality Outcomes Database Spine CORe Grade 1 Lumbar Spondylolisthesis module were divided into two groups: employed preoperatively and unemployed preoperatively. Univariate and multivariate instruments were used to identify predictors of return to work/employment within 5 years postoperatively. Results: Across the 12 highest enrolling QOD sites (Spine CORe Group), 608 patients were enrolled. Of these 608 patients, 604 patients had baseline employment status recorded. 275 were employed preoperatively, of which 249 had return to work follow-up data. Of the 329 patients unemployed preoperatively, 218 had return-to-work follow-up data. By 5 years postoperatively, 87.1% (n = 217) of those employed preoperatively and 22.0% (n = 48) of those unemployed preoperatively returned to work. In each cohort, there were no differences in age, gender, BMI, ASA grade, or ethnicity between those who did and those who did not return to work. On multivariate analysis for the preoperatively employed cohort, college degree (OR: 5.1, CI: 1.6-20.3) and active employment (OR: 6.4, CI: 1.8-23.7) remained independent predictors of returning to work. For those preoperatively unemployed, a college degree (OR: 2.6, CI: 1.2-5.9) independently predicted return to work. Conclusions: Approximately 87% of patients employed preoperatively return to work, and 22% of patients unemployed preoperatively return to the workforce within 60 months after surgery for grade 1 spondylolisthesis. College-level education independently predicted return to work for both preoperatively employed and preoperatively unemployed patients.

## P0255: Anterior cervical trapezoid corpectomy and fusion (ACTCF): a novel technique for cervical spondylotic myelopathy

Jixuan Huang

1

, Jincai Yang

1

, Honghao Yang

1

, Yong Hai

1

1

Beijing Chao-Yang Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: To describe a novel technique, namely, anterior cervical trapezoid corpectomy and fusion (ACTCF), and to research its initial clinical efficacy in the treatment of cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM). Material and Methods: A total of 30 patients with CSM who were treated with the ACTCF between May and December 2021 were retrospectively reviewed. The main surgical procedures include discectomy, trapezoidal osteotomy, pruning and replanting of autogenous bone, and implantation of cage. Relevant data, including patient sex, age, follow-up time, operative duration, postoperative complications, operative level, and osteotomy segment, were collected. Visual analog scale (VAS), Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) scores and Neck Disability Index (NDI) before surgery, immediately, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months after surgery were collected. The decompression and fusion outcomes were evaluated by X-ray and CT. Disc height of involved segment, C2-C7 cervical lordosis (C2-C7 CL) and sagittal segmental alignment (SSA) before surgery, immediately, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months after surgery were measured. The correlated data were analyzed using t test, with significance defined as p < 0.05. Results: All patients in this study had favorable outcomes and experienced no postoperative complications. The limbs and neurological symptoms of 30 patients improved significantly after the operation. The mean follow-up was 13.4 months. The VAS, JOA scores and NDI significantly improved at immediately, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months after surgery (p < 0.05). Disc height of involved segment, C2-C7 CL and SSA improved at immediately, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months after surgery (p < 0.05). Radiographic review revealed no significant back plant settlement, and good fusion was observed at an early stage. Conclusion: For the treatment of CSM with cervical spinal stenosis, ACTCF may be a good alternative to traditional ACCF which can reduce complications, maintain intervertebral height, and accelerate fusion time.

## ID: AGS26/88

## P0256: Novel lever-up laminoplasty for treating multilevel cervical spondylotic myelopathy: a prospective study of clinical and radiologic outcomes

Jixuan Huang

1

, Jincai Yang

1

, Honghao Yang

1

, Yong Hai

1

1

Beijing Chao-Yang Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: To assess the clinical and radiological outcomes of lever-up laminoplasty (LLP) using innovative hinged titanium plates for treating multilevel cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM). Material and Methods: In this prospective study, 25 patients with multilevel CSM underwent LLP from June to October 2022. Intraoperative metrics (operative time, blood loss, transfusion volume, and complications) were recorded. Postoperative imaging evaluated spinal cord decompression by measuring the midsagittal diameter, transverse area, and osseous canal volume. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using the Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) scores preoperatively, and axial symptoms (AS) were quantified using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS). Results: All procedures were successfully performed, with follow-up periods of 24 months. Postoperative imaging confirmed complete neural decompression in the treated segments, without any signs of cervical instability. Significant increases in the mid-sagittal diameter (C3-C7) and cross-sectional area of the spinal canal were observed compared to preoperative values (p < 0.01). The osseous spinal canal volume increased by an average of 61.82% ± 18.50%. Both the JOA and VAS scores demonstrated significant improvements postoperatively (p < 0.01). One patient experienced transient C5 nerve root palsy, which was resolved with conservative treatment. No other complications or significant AS were reported. Conclusion: LLP using hinged titanium plates is a safe and effective surgical approach for the treatment of multilevel CSM. This technique results in significant expansion of the spinal canal, thorough neural decompression, and a reduced incidence of postoperative AS, thereby presenting a promising alternative to traditional laminoplasty methods.

## ID: AGS26/167

## P0257: Cervical paraspinal muscle atrophy is associated with internal carotid artery stenosis

Bruno Verna

1

, Ali Guven

1

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Anna-Maria Mielke

1,2

, Erika Chiapparelli

1

, Thomas Caffard1

1,3

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Jennifer Shue

1

, John Carrino

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Alexander Hughes

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Centrum für Muskuloskeletale Chirurgie, Charité, Berlin, Germany

3

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, University of Ulm, Ulm, Germany

Introduction: Paraspinal muscle degeneration, characterized by fatty infiltration (FI) and reduced functional cross-sectional area (fCSA), contributes to spine instability, chronic pain, and disc degeneration. The association between abdominal aortic calcification (AAC) and lumbar muscle atrophy is well-established, highlighting the interplay between vascular health and muscle degeneration. However, similar vascular-muscle associations in the cervical spine are unexplored. Carotid stenosis, primarily recognized as a biomarker of cardiovascular risk, may also reflect vascular impairment to cervical paraspinal muscles. The objective of our study is to assess the relationship between carotid artery stenosis (CAS) and muscle morphology and understand the association between vascular health and musculoskeletal degeneration. Material and Methods: A retrospective cross-sectional study. Muscle measurements were conducted on T2-weighted axial MRIs at the C3 mid-vertebral level, categorized and segmented into five bilateral functional groups: 1) the sternocleidomastoid group (SCM), 2) the anterior group (AG), 3) the posteromedial group (PM) , 4) the posterolateral group (PL), and 5) the trapezius group (TP). The fCSA and FI for each muscle group were calculated. Carotid analysis was performed on CTA images using specialized software, which semi-automatically calculated mean and maximum stenosis. Correlations between muscle parameters and mean and maximum stenosis were analyzed using Spearman’s correlation coefficient. Multivariable linear regression analysis was conducted to further examine the relationship between stenosis measurements and muscle measurements with adjustments for age, BMI, and sex. Results: Sixty-five patients undergoing primary cervical spine surgery between 2009 and 2018 with preoperative cervical MRI and CTA exams (≤ 12 months apart) were included. After adjusting for age, sex, and BMI, regression analyses showed that more severe stenosis was associated with higher FI. Mean stenosis was significantly associated with FI in all muscle groups, including SCM (p = 0.001), anterior (p = 0.022), PL (p = 0.031), PM (p = 0.003), and TP (p = 0.001). Maximum stenosis was associated with reduced fCSA in SCM (p = 0.032) and anterior (= 0.032), as well as FI in SCM (p = 0.005), TP (p = 0.006), and PM (p = 0.001). Conclusion: This study is the first to demonstrate a significant association between CAS and cervical paraspinal muscle atrophy. These findings suggest that vascular health, particularly carotid stenosis, may contribute to muscle degeneration through impaired perfusion and metabolic changes. Future research should investigate the causal relationship between vascular health and muscle degeneration to enhance clinical management, given the link between muscle atrophy and spinal instability, chronic pain, and poor postoperative outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/196

## P0258: Thoracic inlet angle as a predictor of recovery in cervical spine surgery: a cluster analysis of demographic interactions on radiographic and functional outcomes

Devender Singh

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Thoracic Inlet Angle (TIA) is a critical factor in understanding cervical alignment and predicting recovery outcomes following cervical spine surgery. We analyzed the correlations between TIA and T1Slope, CL, and cSVA at multiple time points, perform cluster analysis based on PreopΔ, and assess how demographic interactions impact outcomes within each cluster. Material and Methods: In this multi-center study of 157 cervical spine surgery patients, correlations between TIA and T1Slope, CL, and cSVA were evaluated preoperatively and at 1 month, 1 year, and 2 years postop. Clusters based on PreopΔ divided patients into Cluster 0 (high PreopΔ), Cluster 1 (moderate PreopΔ), and Cluster 2 (low PreopΔ). Results: TIA showed strong correlations with T1Slope (r = 0.76, p < 0.001) and CL (r = 0.63, p < 0.001), confirming TIA’s alignment impact. While Cluster 0, with the highest PreopΔ, experienced persistent alignment challenges, Cluster 2 - despite lower PreopΔ - exhibited delayed functional recovery and significantly higher ODI scores at 2 years, particularly among smokers (p < 0.001). This finding diverges from expectations, suggesting that lower preoperative misalignment alone does not ensure better outcomes. Cluster 1 outcomes were heavily influenced by BMI and age, with older and high-BMI patients showing worse TIA and CL recovery. The interaction effects reveal that demographic factors like smoking, age, and BMI may sometimes outweigh alignment severity in determining functional recovery. Conclusion: High PreopΔ was linked to alignment difficulties, as expected, but functional recovery was notably poorer in Cluster 2 smokers, showing that demographic risks can heavily influence outcomes. Cluster 1 patients with high BMI and age showed exacerbated alignment and pain, suggesting these patients require weight management and targeted postoperative alignment support.
