## P0259: Cervical spinal canal stenosis: a rare cause of syringomyelia

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Syringomyelia is commonly associated with Chiari malformations, spinal trauma, arachnoiditis or tumors. Cervical canal stenosis is rarely implicated in this intramedullary cavitation. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 60 year-old man with disabling paresthesia and impaired pain in upper limbs. Physical examination revealed spastic tetraparesis and syringomyelic syndrome. X-ray of the cervical spine demonstrated stenosis of the cervical canal and degenerative changes from C3 to C6 levels. Magnetic resonance imaging showed also a syrinx located in the cervical and thoracic regions from C1 to T3. There were no contrast enhancement or cranio-vertebral junction abnormalities. After cervical laminectomy, symptoms improved and the syrinx has almost disappeared. Results: Magnetic resonance imaging showed also a syrinx located in the cervical and thoracic regions from C1 to T3. There were no contrast enhancement or cranio-vertebral junction abnormalities. After cervical laminectomy, symptoms improved and the syrinx has almost disappeared. Conclusion: Syringomyelic cavity may be developed due to craniospinal pressure dissociation caused by spinal cord compression. Laminectomy could be sufficient to induce regression of the syringomyelia.

## ID: AGS26/439

## P0260: Diagnostics and surgical correction of spondylogenic dysphagia in cervical spondylosis

Alexander Trashin

1

, Vitaly Stepanenko

2

, Vladimir Shamanin

2

, Eugeny Belov

2

1

North-Western State Medical University named after I.I. Mechnikov, Saint-Petersburg, Russia, Department of Neurosurgery, City Multifield Hospital #2, Saint-Petersburg, Russia

2

City Multifield Hospital #2, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Spondylogenic dysphagia occurs in patients over 60 years of age, according to various authors, with a frequency of up to 10.6% and often remains undiagnosed. Cervical deforming spondylosis and Forestier's disease occupy a leading place among the causes of this syndrome. Material and Methods: The results of diagnostics and surgical treatment of five patients with spondylogenic dysphagia are presented. All patients underwent CT of the cervical spine and barium esophagography before surgery. All patients were assessed before and after surgery (after 2 weeks and 6 months) using the following scales: 10-point numerical rating scale (NRS) for pain syndrome and the functional outcome of swallowing scale for the degree of dysphagia (FOSS scale). Results: Before surgery, 2 patients had FOSS III dysphagia, and 3 patients had FOSS IV dysphagia. The average pain level according to the NRS was 7.8. All patients underwent osteophytectomy via anterior transcervical access. In the early and late postoperative period, gradual regression of swallowing disorders to FOSS I was noted, as well as regression of pain syndrome to 2 according to the NRS. The follow-up period was 2 years. Conclusion: Microsurgical transcervical osteophytectomy provides stable regression of dysphagia and pain syndrome in the absence of relapses.

## ID: AGS26/464

## P0261: Handgrip strength reflects frailty, gait, and dexterity in patients with degenerative cervical myelopathy

Hirokazu Inoue

1

1

Jichi Medical University, Shimotsuke, Japan

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) is a progressive neurological disorder caused by compression of the spinal cord within the cervical canal, leading to neurological deficits such as gait and hand dexterity impairments. Handgrip strength is not merely an indicator of muscle weakness; it is also used as a predictor for various outcomes, including length of hospital stay and prognosis. This study aimed to evaluate whether handgrip strength affects frailty, gait status, and dexterity in patients with DCM. Material and Methods: This cross-sectional observational study included 94 patients (29 females, 65 males; mean age 66.9 years) who underwent surgery for DCM. Preoperatively, we measured handgrip strength, lower extremity extension power (LEP), pinch strength, 10-meter walk test, counter test, height, weight, body mass index (BMI), the Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) score, the geriatric nutritional risk index (GNRI), and the 5-item modified frailty index (mFI-5). The mFI-5 was used to classify patients into a healthy group (0 points), a prefrail group (1 point), and a frail group (≥ 2 points). Results: Handgrip strength was significantly correlated with age (r = -0.468, p < 0.001), height (r = 0.601, p < 0.001), weight (r = 0.459, p < 0.001), LEP (r = 0.675, p < 0.001), walking speed (r = 0.617, p < 0.001), counter test (r = 0.659, p < 0.001), pinch strength (r = 0.723, p < 0.001), total JOA score (r = 0.468, p = 0.001), upper extremity motor function (r = 0.616, p < 0.001), lower extremity motor function (r = 0.658, p < 0.001), mFI-5 (r = -0.369, p = 0.001), and GNRI (r = 0.207, p = 0.046). No significant correlation was found with BMI, upper extremity sensory function, lower extremity sensory function, trunk sensory function, or bladder function. The mean handgrip strength was 19.5 ± 9.7 kg in the frail group, 25.2 ± 7.1 kg in the prefrail group, and 30.0 ± 7.5 kg in the healthy group. Handgrip strength in the frail group was significantly lower than in both the prefrail and healthy groups. Conclusion: In preoperative DCM patients, handgrip strength was positively correlated with height, weight, lower extremity extension power, walking speed, pinch strength, and counter test, and negatively correlated with age. Our findings suggest that handgrip strength can serve as an indicator of walking ability, manual dexterity, and frailty status in preoperative DCM patients. This highlights the potential utility of handgrip strength in the preoperative assessment of patients with DCM.

## ID: AGS26/526

## P0262: Understanding the role of vascular health in cervical muscle atrophy and disc degeneration

Bruno Verna

1

, Ali Guven

1

, Anna-Maria Mielke

1,2

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Johnathan Slawitsky

3

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Jennifer Shue

1

, John Carrino

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Alexander Hughes

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2
Centrum für Muskuloskeletale Chirurgie, Charité, Berlin, Germany, 
3
Georgetown University School of Medicine, Washington DC, United States
Introduction: Degeneration of paraspinal muscles and intervertebral discs has been associated with spinal instability, chronic pain, and suboptimal surgical outcomes. While the impact of aortic calcification on lumbar spine structures has been previously explored, similar vascular-degenerative associations in the cervical spine remain poorly understood. The objective of this study is to evaluate the relationship between carotid artery stenosis (CAS) and cervical paraspinal muscle morphology as well as its potential association with intervertebral disc integrity. Material and Methods: A retrospective cross-sectional study of patients undergoing primary cervical spine surgery between 2009 and 2018 with preoperative cervical MRI and CTA exams (≤ 12 months apart) was performed. T2-weighted axial MRIs from C3 to C7 were used to measure fat infiltration (FI) and functional cross-sectional area (fCSA) in six bilateral muscle groups: sternocleidomastoid (SCM), anterior group (AG), scalenus (SN), posteromedial (PM), posterolateral (PL), and trapezius (TP). Disc degeneration was evaluated using a validated Disc Signal Intensity (DSI2) index across the same cervical levels. CAS was measured on CTA using semi-automated software, yielding both maximum stenosis (single most narrowed point) and mean stenosis (average narrowing throughout the vessel). Correlations between muscle and disc parameters and carotid artery stenosis were analyzed using Spearman’s correlation and multivariable regression adjusted for age, sex, and BMI. Results: Sixty-five patients (58% female, median age of 67 years and BMI of 26.5 kg/m2) were included. Spearman analyses demonstrated that higher mean and maximum CAS were significantly correlated with increased FI and reduced fCSA across multiple muscle groups, particularly at the C3 and C4 levels (all p < 0.05). In multivariable linear regression models, higher mean stenosis was significantly associated with increased FI in the SCM (p = 0.008), PL (p = 0.024), PM (p = 0.001), TP (p = 0.003), and AG (p = 0.026) at C3, as well as the PM (p = 0.023) at C4 and SCM (p = 0.032) at C5. Higher mean stenosis was also associated with reduced fCSA in the PM (p = 0.049) and SCM (p = 0.034) at C4. Similarly, increased maximum stenosis was significantly associated with increased FI in the SCM (p = 0.008) and TP (p = 0.012) at C3, and the PL (p = 0.019) at C4, along with reduced fCSA in the SCM (p = 0.035) at C4. No significant associations were observed between CAS and disc degeneration, as assessed by the DSI2 index. Conclusion: CAS was linked to cervical muscle atrophy, particularly at the C3 level, pointing toward a potential role of vascular health in muscle degeneration. In contrast, disc degeneration as measured by the DSI2 index showed no association with CAS, suggesting that muscle atrophy and disc degeneration may follow distinct degenerative or metabolic pathways.

## ID: AGS26/551

## P0263: Postoperative surgical site infections (SSI) in cervical spine surgery from preceding epidural steroid injection: a systematic review and metanalysis

Riaz Mohammed

1

, Pranav Shah

2

1

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

2

Namaha Specialised Healthcare Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: When elective spine surgery is planned on the background of recent epidural steroid injections (ESI), the risk of infection is of foremost relevance. This is more so of concern when these treatment episodes are in proximity. The immunosuppressive effects of corticosteroids coupled with the long half-life of these medications is of clinical significance. Though evidence exists for lumbar spine surgery on this topic, there is paucity of evidence of cervical spine surgery (CSS). This systematic review assesses risk of post operative infections following cervical spine surgery within 3 months of an ESI for a degenerative spinal pathology. The three month interval duration was deemed to be clinically relevant for patient-care and also practical period for analyses, based on existing literature in lumbar spine surgery. Method: A systematic review in accordance with Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic review and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines was conducted for English literature assessing infection as an outcome in cervical spine surgery following cervical ESI. Studies with interval greater than 3 months between ESI and cervical spine surgery, case reports, review articles were excluded. From a cumulative 271 studies identified from the PubMed database, three retrospective cohort studies were included for the systematic review and meta-analysis. Results: One study assessed outcomes of anterior and posterior CSS separately while the remaining two included both anterior and posterior CSS. All three papers analysed outcomes from retrospective American health insurance-based clinical databases. A total of 22,445 patients received an ESI within the preceding 3 months of CSS as against 355,002 patients in the control group undergoing CSS without preceding ESI. Post-operative infection was found in 155 patients (0.69%) receiving preceding ESI as against 2950 patients (0.83%) in the control group. Analysis performed using random effects model with Mantel-Haenszel method found no statistical difference between the two cohorts with risk ratio of 1.21 (95% confidence interval 0.92-1.6). Conclusion: Our meta-analysis confirms no increased risk of SSI in patients who have had steroid injections within three months preceding their cervical spine surgery compared to the group that did not. This information is clinically relevant for shared decision making for patients with degenerative cervical spinal pathology. Larger prospective trials to may shed stronger evidence for clinical practise to overcome the limitations of the retrospective insurance-based database studies used for this analysis.

## ID: AGS26/593

## P0264: Transient skeletal muscle loss after cervical decompression for myelopathy

Isaiah Gritters

1

, Reagan Grieser-Yoder

1

, Natalie Glass

1

, Bradley Hindman

1

, Andrea Strayer

1

, Matthew Howard

1

, Catherine Olinger

1

1

University of Iowa, Iowa City, United States

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) is a prevalent spinal cord disorder in older adults that causes neurologic symptoms such as gait instability and disruption of fine motor control. The goal of surgical decompression for DCM is to halt or reverse neurologic symptoms as measured by the modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) scale, but factors influencing postoperative recovery remain incompletely understood. Sarcopenia, defined as abnormally low skeletal muscle mass and/or strength, is common in older adults and is associated with accelerated functional decline, increased mortality, and may negatively affect outcomes in cervical spine procedures. Bioelectrical impedance analysis (BIA) is a guideline-accepted, noninvasive method for quantifying skeletal muscle mass, and can be used to calculate the appendicular skeletal muscle index (ASMI, kg/m2) and detect sarcopenia at presurgical baseline and postoperative changes over time. This study was designed to determine the preoperative prevalence of sarcopenia and characterize postoperative longitudinal changes in ASMI in patients undergoing surgical decompression for DCM. Material and Methods: In this prospective study, patients between 18 to 100 years of age scheduled to undergo non-emergent cervical decompression for DCM were enrolled after providing written informed consent. Exclusion criteria were cardiac electronic devices and inability to stand unsupported for 60 seconds. ASMI was measured using BIA preoperatively and at 3, 6, 13, and 26 weeks postoperatively. Functional status was assessed at the same time points using mJOA scores calculated from questionnaires. Repeated measures generalized linear models were used to model longitudinal change in ASMI following surgical decompression using follow-up week as the independent variable. Estimated marginal means for each follow-up time-point were compared with baseline and simulation adjustment was used to account for multiple comparisons. Analyses were repeated using sex, mJOA score, and surgical invasiveness as model covariates. Surgical invasiveness was defined as the number of vertebral levels fused (1, 2-3, or ≥ 4 levels). Results: Data were analyzed from 31 patients. Preoperative characteristics included age, body mass index (BMI), BMI group, obesity status, sex, ASMI, mJOA score, sarcopenia status (yes/no), osteoporosis status, and the number of vertebral levels fused. Preoperatively, 6.5% (n = 2) of patients met the criteria for sarcopenia. Postoperatively, ASMI was significantly different (less) from baseline at weeks 3, 6, and 13 (p = 0.002, 0.001, and 0.008, respectively) but did not differ from preoperative values by week 26 (p = 0.71). Males had a significantly greater ASMI than females (p = 0.001), but the interaction between sex and postoperative week was not statistically significant (p = 0.78), indicating post-operative change in ASMI did not differ by sex. mJOA score was not associated with ASMI (p = 0.66). Conclusion: ASMI decreased significantly during the first 13 weeks after cervical decompression but appeared to recover to presurgical baseline levels by week 26. The early ASMI decrease is consistent with expected decreases in postoperative activity and lifting restrictions and suggests transient, rather than persistent, postoperative muscle loss. Continued postoperative follow-up to one and two years may reveal any long-term changes in ASMI or sarcopenia prevalence as a result of decompression.

## ID: AGS26/684

## P0265: Post-operative Horner’s syndrome (HS): a case report on a rare complication after anterior cervical dissectomy and fusion (ACDF) surgery

Jiawen Fong

1

, Walter-Soon-Yaw Wong

1

, Yilun Huang

1

1

Sengkang General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Cervical degenerative disc disease (CDDD) is common in older individuals, typically presenting with pain and neurological symptoms. The gold standard for correction is Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF), a safe, effective procedure. Horner’s Syndrome (HS) is an extremely rare complication immediately post-ACDF with approximately 0.06% incidence, due to impairment of the sympathetic trunk. We aim to present, to our knowledge, the first case of delayed-onset HS post-ACDF. Material and Methods: The patient, a 48-year-old woman, with CDDD-related pain and radiculopathy, was counselled and consented for C5/6 and C6/7 ACDF. ACDF was performed by standard anterior left-sided incision at appropriate levels identified with II and Kasper pins. Contrasting with our usual practice, deeper-toothed retractors were employed for better exposure. Two cages were inserted at C5/6 and C6/7. Standard ACDF procedural time was taken at 180 minutes without intra-operative complications. Results: The patient experienced significant relief from CDDD symptoms immediately post-ACDF, however, developed sudden onset left-sided HS, with ptosis and anhidrosis approximately 48-hours post-ACDF. A prednisolone course was initiated, and she was followed-up at 2, 6, 12, and 16 weeks postoperatively. By 2-weeks, she exhibited clinical improvement, and by 12-weeks, her HS had completely resolved, with no residual effects. Conclusion: This case study is the first documented instance of delayed-onset HS post-ACDF to our knowledge. It may possibly be associated with the type of retractors used, resulting in neuropraxia or strain on the sympathetic chain ganglia. We recommend adequate exposure while minimizing excessive retraction of the longus colli muscle to reduce irritation to the sympathetic trunk.

## ID: AGS26/747

## P0266: Short and long-term outcomes in multiple sclerosis patients undergoing cervical spine surgery: a retrospective database review

Abdel Diab

1

, Reme Arhewoh

1

, Omar Diab

2

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

, Joshua Piche

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

2

University of Texas Medical Branch, Galveston, United States

Introduction: Cervical spinal myelopathy (CSM) in patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) presents a unique clinical challenge due to the interplay of demyelination and spinal degeneration, which exacerbates neurological deficits and complicates surgical outcomes. While cervical decompression and stabilization procedures are standard for symptom relief, current data is limited by small sample size leading to unclear short-term medical complications and long-term mechanical outcomes in MS patients. Methods: Using the TriNetX database, a retrospective cohort study was conducted across 104 healthcare organizations. Patients undergoing cervical decompression or stabilization procedures were categorized into two cohorts: MS patients diagnosed ≥ 1 year before surgery and non-MS patients were propensity score matched (1:1) with balanced demographic and comorbidities. Outcomes included 30-day medical complications (e.g., hospital readmissions) and 2-year mechanical complications (e.g., hardware failure, adjacent segment disease, revision surgeries). Statistical analyses included risk differences, risk ratios, and p-values. Results: 840 patients were included in each cohort. The matched cohorts were predominantly female (67.4% MS vs 64.9% non-MS), white (74.6% MS vs 75.4% non-MS), and non-Hispanic (74.3% MS vs 74.5% non-MS), with mean ages of 57.4 ± 10.6 and 58.1 ± 11.1 years respectively. MS patients had significantly higher rates of hospital readmissions within 30 days compared to non-MS patients (18.2% vs. 13.6%; risk difference = 0.046; 95% CI: 0.012-0.081; p = 0.009). No significant differences were observed in 2-year mechanical complications, including hardware failure, adjacent segment disease, or revision surgeries, between the two cohorts. Discussion: This study highlights an increased risk of 30-day hospital readmissions in MS patients undergoing cervical spine procedures, emphasizing the need for tailored perioperative care in this population. However, comparable 2-year mechanical outcomes suggest that MS alone should not contraindicate cervical spine surgery. These findings provide clinicians with valuable insights to guide surgical decision-making and optimize postoperative outcomes for MS patients.

## ID: AGS26/851

## P0268: Timing of subsidence in segmental plate fixation for anterior cervical discectomy and fusion

Michael Mousselli

1

, Ezra Levy

1

, Jacob Rumley

1

, Graal Diaz

2

1

Center for Spine and Orthopedics, Thornton, CO, United States

2

Community Memorial Health System, Ventura, CA, United States

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) has become a gold standard in treatment of degenerative cervical myelopathy and radiculopathy. While this is an effective treatment strategy when successful, complications such as pseudoarthrosis and cage subsidence can affect outcomes. Anterior plate fixation can decrease these complications but can be difficult to apply, particularly in multi-level procedures. Segmental anterior plate application has been proposed to decrease difficulty in plate application and enhance biomechanical stability which can decrease operative time and potential patient complications. While subsidence rates are still being elucidated, the time from surgery to subsidence is equally important for the surgeon's knowledge. This study aims to determine the time from surgery to subsidence for ACDF with segmental anterior plate fixation as well as identify risk factors that may lead to subsidence. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective review of 86 patients who underwent ACDF with segmental anterior plate fixation from January 2020 to January 2022. Patient demographics and clinical data were collected. Radiographs were taken during the time of surgery and at subsequent patient follow-up visits. Subsidence was defined as > 3 mm of height loss across the fusion level on subsequent radiographs. Average time from surgery to subsidence was calculated and the Welch t-test was used to determine what patient characteristics were associated with subsidence. Results: 86 patients were included in the study. 11 patients had subsidence of the cage-plate construct. The average time from surgery to subsidence was 130 days (SD 102.6 days). There were no statistically significant differences for categorical or continuous variables in those who subsided and those who did not. However, there was a large positive effect for body mass index (BMI) (Cohen’s d = 0.95) and weight (Cohens’ d = 0.85) for those with higher BMI trending towards higher rates of subsidence. Smoking showed a non-significant increase in odds of subsidence, Odds ratio [95% confidence interval] (3.686 [0.747, 18.193]). Conclusion: The average time from surgery to subsidence was 130 days. Subsidence can cause issues such as an increase in patient’s pain and potential need for revision surgery. Knowing when these implants subside can help surgeons understand at what time points follow-up is important and when to get post-operative radiographs. Understanding risk factors for subsidence can also help surgeons to optimize patients pre-operatively to decrease these complications.

## ID: AGS26/868

## P0269: What factors on initial patient presentation are associated with patient satisfaction following cervical spine surgery? Insights from a prospectively collected outcomes database
Reme Arhewoh1, Erin Nicholas1, Elinor Stern1, Mihir Surapaneni1, Joshua Piche1, Rakesh Patel1, Ilyas Aleem1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Patient satisfaction following cervical spine surgery is multifactorial, but the precise contributors remain incompletely understood. Prior research suggests that demographic characteristics, medical comorbidities, and psychosocial factors can influence postoperative outcomes. Identifying which factors are important provides avenues for improving patient selection and intervening to improve outcomes. This study examined which patient factors at initial presentation were associated with satisfaction at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years after cervical spine surgery. Material and Methods: A prospective multicenter database was queried for patients undergoing cervical spine surgery at a tertiary care institution from 2013-2023. Patients presenting emergently or lacking two-year follow-up satisfaction data were excluded. Satisfaction, assessed at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively, was dichotomized into “satisfied” (met expectations and improved, would repeat) and “not satisfied” (improved, but would not repeat and did not meet expectations). Baseline symptoms, prior interventions, medical history, education and employment plans were evaluated as predictors of satisfaction. T-tests and Chi-square tests were used for continuous and categorical variables respectively. Multivariable logistic regression was employed to identify predictors. Results: A total of 515 patients (53% male, 47% female; 89% White, 11% non-White) were included. Overall satisfaction (categorized as “Met expectations was 85% at 90 days, 82% at 1 year, and 78% at 2 years. Race was a consistent predictor of satisfaction (p < 0.001 at 90 days and 1 year, p = 0.024 at 2 years), with White patients reporting higher satisfaction. Higher education level was significantly associated with greater satisfaction at 90 days (p = 0.018) and at 2 years (p = 0.023). Patients who planned to return to work reported significantly higher satisfaction at 1 year (p = 0.009) and 2 years (p = 0.002). Early satisfaction at 90 days was lower among patients with prior cervical surgery (p < 0.001), atrial fibrillation (p = 0.037), congestive heart failure (p < 0.001), anxiety (p = 0.001), or adjacent segment disease (p = 0.028). At 2 years, a longer interval since previous spine surgery (p = 0.003) and the absence of chiropractic treatment (p = 0.034) were associated with higher satisfaction. Logistic regression was limited due to number of patients with complete data. Conclusion: Patient satisfaction following cervical spine surgery is influenced by multiple factors, with race, education level, and return-to-work plans showing the most consistent associations across time points. Medical comorbidities including atrial fibrillation, congestive heart failure, and anxiety significantly impact early satisfaction while previous treatments influence long-term outcomes. These findings may inform patient selection, pre-operative counseling, and targeted interventions to optimize satisfaction outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/871

## P0270: Impact of perioperative anticoagulation on outcomes following cervical spine fusion surgery: a propensityscore-matched analysis

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Jack Eichman

1

, Aayush Unadkat

1

, Abdel Diab

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Anticoagulation therapy in spine surgery is an often-debated topic due to the competing risks of deep vein thrombosis and epidural hematoma. These risks are further compounded by differing patient comorbidities and surgical complexity. Recent literature has begun to better shed light on the necessity of anticoagulation with some studies showing benefits and others with detrimental effects. However, a broader look at specific postoperative complications and long term satisfaction following cervical fusion surgery has not been undertaken. This study takes advantage of dichotomous prescribing habits between two services and rigorous propensity score matching to analyze the influence of anticoagulation therapy on clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: A multicenter, prospectively collected database was retrospectively reviewed to identify all patients who underwent cervical fusion at a tertiary healthcare system between 2014 and 2024. A comprehensive review of patient charts was conducted to ensure adherence to the prescribing patterns of both orthopedics and neurosurgery services. Patients with documented histories of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or hypercoagulable conditions were excluded. Patients were stratified based on anticoagulation status (anticoagulated: n = 581; control: n = 675). Propensity score matching was performed using age, gender, BMI, race, smoking status, diabetes, and ASA classification, resulting in 374 matched pairs (n = 748). Composite outcomes for all complications and neurologic complications. T-tests and Chi-square tests were used for continuous and categorical variables respectively. Binary logistic regression was employed with Firth correction used for rare events. Results: Pre-matching analysis revealed the anticoagulation group was significantly older (61.6 vs. 56.0 years, p < 0.001), had higher prevalence of CAD (p = 0.007), hypertension (p < 0.001), and differences in racial composition (p = 0.006). Post-matching, all baseline characteristics were balanced (p > 0.05), with matched groups having a mean age of approximately 59.7 years, BMI around 31.0, and similar distribution of gender, race, smoking status, diabetes, ASA class and other cardiovascular comorbidities. Postoperatively, the anticoagulation group experienced significantly higher rates of lower extremity weakness (20.1% vs. 11.8%, p = 0.002). Although myelopathy (1.1% vs. 0%) and bowel/bladder dysfunction (1.1% vs. 0%) were more common in the anticoagulated group, these differences were not statistically significant (p = 0.124 for both). Rates of DVT (0.8% vs. 0.5%, p = 1.000), pulmonary embolism, CSF leak, wound dehiscence, and hematoma (0% vs. 1.1%, p = 0.124) were also not statistically significant. The anticoagulated group had significantly longer hospital stays (mean LOS: 3.87 vs. 1.83 days, p < 0.001) and were more frequently discharged to non-home facilities such as skilled nursing, rehabilitation, or acute care settings (15.0% vs. 4.5%, p < 0.001). Readmission and reoperation rates within 90 days were not significantly different. Patient satisfaction at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years were not significantly different between groups (all p > 0.05). Across cervical fusion regression models, longer hospital stay consistently predicted worse outcomes-new weakness (OR 1.14/day, p < .001), any complication (OR 1.22/day, p < .001), neurologic complications (OR 1.17/day, p < .001), and hematoma (OR 1.20/day, p < .001). Anticoagulant use was not protective for DVT (removed from model; p = .871), PE (OR 3.76, p = .347), or hematoma (removed; p = .612), but it predicted neurologic complications (OR 1.61, p = .042) and new weakness (OR 1.70, p = .024). Diabetes predicted wound dehiscence (OR 12.82, p = .021); osteoporosis increased risk of any complication (OR 2.00, p < .001), neurologic complications (OR 1.71, p = .031), and hematoma (OR 7.24, p = .009) but was inversely associated with new weakness (OR 0.57, p = .022). Race effects were notable: Asian patients had higher odds of any complication (OR 4.63, p = .014) and neurologic complications (OR 6.25, p = .002); Black (OR 6.11, p = .002) and Asian (OR 3.40, p = .033) patients had higher odds of new weakness; Hispanic patients showed elevated odds for neurologic complications (OR 3.47, p = .032). Conclusion: This propensity-matched analysis demonstrates that anticoagulation therapy following cervical fusion surgery is associated with increased postoperative morbidity without providing demonstrable clinical benefits. Anticoagulation was associated with significantly higher rates of lower extremity weakness, neurological complications, along with substantially longer hospital stays and increased discharge to institutional care facilities. In regression modeling, anticoagulation use also predicted neurologic outcomes. Despite these interventions aimed at preventing thromboembolic events, anticoagulation provided no protective effect against DVT or pulmonary embolism. The substantial increase in healthcare resource utilization, combined with the lack of protective benefits and increased risk of neurological complications, challenges the routine use of anticoagulation in cervical fusion patients. These findings support a more selective, individualized approach to anticoagulation decisions and suggest that routine prophylaxis may not be warranted in the majority of cervical fusion cases.

## ID: AGS26/914

## P0271: Single-level ACDF outcomes in hypertensive patients: a propensity score-matched comparison of ARBs and ACE inhibitors

Matthew Kim

1

, Seungjun Lee

2

, Graham Goh

1

, K. Daniel Riew

3,4

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Boston University Medical Center, Boston, United States

2

Boston University Chobanian and Avedisian School of Medicine, Boston, United States

3

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill-Cornell Medical Center, New York, United States

4

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Columbia University Medical Center, New York, United States

Introduction: Activation of Renin-angiotensin system (RAS) receptors in bone has been shown to promote osteoclastic activity, whereas RAS blockers (commonly prescribed for hypertension) decreases osteoclastic and increases osteoblastic activity. Clinical data suggest potential associations between RAS blocker use and reduced fracture risk, yet evidence on their role in spinal fusion outcomes remains limited. Importantly, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs) and angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEIs) act through distinct mechanisms, which may translate into differential effects on fusion and perioperative outcomes. This study aimed to compare perioperative complications, revision rates, and healthcare utilization in hypertensive patients undergoing single-level (SL) ACDF treated with ARBs versus ACEIs. Material and Methods: The TriNetX Global Collaborative Network was queried to identify hypertensive patients undergoing SL ACDF using CPT and ICD-10 codes. Two cohorts were defined: ARB users with hypertension and ACEI users with hypertension. ARB and ACEI users respectively had prescriptions within 6 months before and 6 months after surgery. Propensity score matching (1:1) was performed for age, sex, BMI, Charlson Comorbidity Index, smoking status, osteoporosis. Outcomes assessed included medical complications at 90 days, as well as surgical complications, revision rates, and resource utilization at 90 days, 6 months, and 1 year. Results: After matching, 4,551 patients were included in both the ACEi and ARB cohorts. ARB use was associated with a significantly lower risk of deep infection after ACDF at 90 days (OR 0.453, p = 0.034). Pseudarthrosis rates were reduced in the ARB group at 90 days (OR 0.844, p = 0.008), 6 months (OR 0.838, p = 0.004), and 1 year (OR 0.837, p = 0.003). Conversely, myocardial infarction (MI) risk was significantly increased in ARB patients at 90 days (OR 1.531, p = 0.028). Resource utilization was lower in the ARB group, with decreased readmissions at 90 days (OR 0.880, p = 0.048), 6 months (OR 0.871, p = 0.023), and 1 year (OR 0.883, p = 0.025). Conclusion: ARB use was associated with lower risks of pseudarthrosis, deep infection, and hospital readmission compared to ACEI use. These findings suggest a potential protective effect of ARBs on fusion integrity and perioperative outcomes. ARB use was associated with higher MI rates at 90 days follow-up, which has been reported in other trials. Larger prospective studies are needed to validate these associations, but in the meantime, our study suggests avoiding ACEi, if possible, in the early post-operative period following ACDF.

## ID: AGS26/945

## P0272: Multicenter analysis of clinical results of the algorithmic approach to the choice of anterior decompressive-stabilizing intervention in two-segment degenerative diseases of the cervical spine

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2,3

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Alexander Kuharev

1

, Marat Aliyev

4

, K. Daniel Riew

5,6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Neurosurgery, Kazakhstan-Russian Medical University, Almaty, Kazakhstan

5

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, United States

6

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, United States

Introduction: Currently, there are no substantiated clinical and radiological indications in the specialized literature for the differentiated use of discectomy with a two-level ACDF and one-level ACCF in the treatment of patients with two-segment cervical degenerative diseases. Purpose of the study was prospective evaluation of the long-term clinical results of the algorithmic approach to the choice of ACDF and ACCF for two-segment degenerative diseases of the cervical spine. Material and Methods: A comparative analysis of the treatment of patients with two-segment cervical degenerative diseases was performed among a prospective cohort of patients (n = 95) operated on according to the preoperative clinical and morphological algorithm in three neurosurgical departments of Irkutsk and Krasnoyarsk (Russia) and Almaty (Kazakhstan) in the period 2021-2023 and a retrospective control group (n = 186) operated on at the Irkutsk from 2009 to 2020 using the same surgical techniques depending on the surgeon's preference. The results were compared between the subgroups of ACDF and ACCF according to preoperative and remote clinical data (neurological symptoms, level of pain syndrome in the cervical spine and upper extremities, functional status according to ODI and mJOA, quality of life according to the SF-36 scale, the result of the operation according to the Macnab scale) and the presence of complications according to the Dindo-Clavien classification. Results: After ACCF, the prospective group demonstrated better dynamics: recovery of neurological functions (p < 0.05), regression of pseudoradicular symptoms (p < 0.05), improvement of NDI (p = 0.032) and mJOA (p = 0.041), physical (p = 0.015) and psychological (p = 0.013) health components according to SF-36, and better indicators according to the Macnab scale (p = 0.006). The incidence of complications in prospective subgroups was significantly lower compared to retrospective ones: after ACDF (p = 0.025) and ACCF (p = 0.032). Conclusion: An algorithmic anterior approach to differentiated choice of ACDF and ACCF in two-segment cervical degenerative diseases led to significant improvement of clinical outcomes and reduction of postoperative complications with a minimum 2-year postoperative follow-up period.

## ID: AGS26/964

## P0273: Cervical anterior osteophytes: case series, management, and clinical results

Ali Imran Ozmarasali

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Bursa City Hospital, Bursa, Turkey

Cervical anterior osteophytes and Diffuse Idiopathic Skeletal Hyperostosis (DISH) are non-inflammatory conditions frequently observed in the elderly. While often asymptomatic, they can rarely lead to serious clinical manifestations, including dysphagia, dysphonia, respiratory distress, and increased vulnerability to spinal cord injury following mild trauma due to altered spinal biomechanics. This study aims to retrospectively review cases diagnosed at Bursa City Hospital Neurosurgery Clinic between November 2023 and August 2025 and share findings regarding their management. Six male patients (mean age 69.3 ± 8.7 years) were evaluated. The primary presenting complaints included neck and arm pain (66.6%), gait disturbance and weakness (50%), and dysphagia/dysphonia (33.3%). Preoperative neurological examinations varied from normal motor strength with severe movement limitation to spastic tetraparesis and tetraplegia. Radiological findings identified anterior osteophyte involvement predominantly at C4-7 and massive involvement from C2-T1. One patient was followed conservatively after declining surgery. Another patient with massive DISH achieved complete resolution of symptoms three months after an anterior osteophytectomy. The remaining cases required combined anterior and posterior surgical approaches due to concomitant cervical degenerative disease and spinal cord lesions. Notably, histopathological examination in two patients revealed osteochondromas, a rare finding in the anterior column that carries risks of recurrence and malignant transformation. Surgical management of these conditions is challenging as they often coexist with other degenerative pathologies. Technical nuances are critical, including the use of a rigid nasogastric catheter for esophageal identification, the potential use of a chisel for excision, and intraoperative neuromonitoring to protect the recurrent laryngeal nerve. In conclusion, while many cases of DISH and cervical osteophytes can be managed conservatively, symptomatic and progressive cases require surgical intervention. Clinicians should remain vigilant regarding the potential for underlying malignancies or rare tumors like osteochondromas, making histopathological evaluation recommended whenever possible.

## P0274: Impact of frailty on surgical outcomes in degenerative cervical myelopathy: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Mohsen Rostami

1

, Sadegh Bagherzadeh

2

, Ahmad Alkadri

3

, Eric Cohen

3

, Shrinit Babel

3

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Melissa Touadi BS

3

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

2

Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

3

USF Health Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Frailty is increasingly recognized as a key predictor of surgical risk, yet its impact on outcomes in degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) remains poorly defined. Although frail patients often present with worse baseline function, it is unclear whether they achieve similar postoperative gains or experience disproportionate perioperative risk. Objective: To systematically evaluate the effect of frailty-measured by the modified Frailty Index (mFI) or Hospital Frailty Risk Score (HFRS)-on functional recovery and complication rates after surgical intervention for DCM. Material and Methods: A systematic search of PubMed, Embase, Scopus, and Web of Science was performed through July 2025. Studies were included if they reported frailty status and at least one clinical outcome in adults undergoing surgery for DCM. Meta-analyses were conducted using standardized mean differences (SMD) and odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI). Risk of bias was assessed using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale, and publication bias was evaluated with Egger’s test. Results: Eleven retrospective cohort studies comprising 105,166 patients met inclusion criteria. High-frailty patients demonstrated worse preoperative status, including lower mJOA scores (10.83 vs. 12.31), higher Nurick grades (3.97 vs. 3.05), and poorer SF-36 PCS and NDI scores (all p < 0.05). Despite these baseline differences, postoperative functional improvements were comparable between high- and low-frailty groups across all metrics (e.g., ΔmJOA: p = 0.466; ΔNDI: p = 0.794). Frailty was, however, strongly associated with increased perioperative risk, including prolonged hospital stay (SMD 2.25), readmission (OR 2.09), reoperation (OR 4.00), and mortality (OR 11.95). Intraoperative parameters, such as blood loss and operative time, did not differ significantly between groups. Conclusion: Frailty is a strong predictor of perioperative complications in DCM surgery but does not reduce the potential for meaningful functional recovery. Incorporating frailty assessments into preoperative evaluation can improve risk stratification, guide patient counseling, and support perioperative optimization-without excluding appropriately selected frail patients from surgical treatment.

## ID: AGS26/968

## P0275: Comparing complication profiles and patient factors for C2 versus C3 as the upper instrumented vertebra in cervical fusion

Puya Alikhani

1

, Ahmad Alkadri

1

, Eric Cohen

1

, Shrinit Babel

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, John Lingam

1

1

Univeristy of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Extending a cervical fusion construct to the C2 vertebra is a more complex procedure than stopping at C3. This study aims to identify the patient demographics and complication rates associated with choosing C2 versus C3 as the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV). Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study of 200 patients undergoing multi-level posterior cervical fusion was conducted. Patients were grouped by UIV: C2 (n = 103) or C3 (n = 77). Demographics, comorbidities, and complication rates were compared using chi-square and t-tests. Results: The two cohorts were well-matched for age, sex, BMI, smoking status, and prevalence of diabetes or inflammatory arthritis. However, the revision surgery rate was significantly higher in the C3 cohort (17.1% vs. 4.2%, p = 0.005). Furthermore, the C3 cohort had a significantly higher rate of wound complications (9.2% vs. 0.0%, p = 0.002). There was a non-significant trend towards a higher number of laminectomy levels in the C3 group (4.55 vs. 4.09, p = 0.034) and a higher rate of other neurologic deficits (23.7% vs. 13.5%, p = 0.086). Rates of C5 palsy, SSI, PJK, PJF, DJK, and DJF were not significantly. Conclusion: While baseline patient factors are similar, choosing C3 as the UIV is associated with a significantly higher risk of revision surgery and wound healing complications compared to constructs incorporating C2. This may be due to the increased mechanical strain on a construct that ends at the more mobile C3 segment versus the inherently stable C2. Surgeons must weigh the potential radiographic advantages of stopping at C3 against its significantly higher complication profile.

## ID: AGS26/995

## P0276: Clinical signs and symptoms for degenerative cervical myelopathy: a scoping review of case-control studies to facilitate early diagnosis among healthcare professionals with stakeholder engagement

Suhani Sharma

1

, Alisha Sial

2

, Stone Sima

2

, Ashish Diwan

2

1

Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland, Dublin, Ireland

2

University of New South Wales, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) is a leading cause of chronic spinal cord dysfunction, with diverse clinical presentations that complicate diagnosis. Therefore, it is important to identify the signs and symptoms of DCM that demonstrate high diagnostic accuracy. This review aims to evaluate the sensitivity and specificity of signs and symptoms in diagnosing DCM. Material and Methods: Articles up to June 2024 were retrieved from PubMed, EMBASE, and Cochrane databases using search terms like “degenerative cervical myelopathy”, “cervical spondylotic myelopathy”, “sensitivity”, “specificity”, and related signs and symptoms. Studies were screened based on selection criteria assessing the sensitivity and specificity of signs or symptoms using an appropriate control group. Results: Sixteen studies were included. The most sensitive signs were Tromner sign (93-97%) and hyperreflexia (15-85%). Specific signs included the Babinski sign (93-100%), Tromner sign (79-100%), clonus (96-99%), and inverted supinator sign (78-99%). Neck pain had a sensitivity of 76-94% and specificity of 11-73%. Hand incoordination showed 52% sensitivity and 92% specificity. Altered hand sensation had 76% sensitivity and 90% specificity. Upper extremity weakness had 51-75% sensitivity and 18-95% specificity. Gait imbalance exhibited 56-63% sensitivity and 52-95% specificity. Conclusion: Sensitive signs like the Tromner sign and hyperreflexia are useful for screening, while specific signs such as Babinski, clonus, and the inverted supinator sign aid in confirmation of DCM. Symptoms like neck pain, hand incoordination, and altered hand sensation should heighten suspicion and guide differential diagnosis. Early and accurate diagnosis using these indicators can improve patient outcomes and reduce diagnostic delays.

## ID: AGS26/1000

## P0277: The effect of body mass index and metabolic syndrome on postoperative outcomes after cervical laminoplasty

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Abdul Mounnem Yassin Kassab

1

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Obesity and metabolic syndrome have become common comorbidities among patients undergoing spine surgery. While previous studies have explored the effects of body mass index (BMI) and metabolic syndrome on various spine procedures, large-scale database analyses specifically focused on cervical laminoplasty remain lacking. This study aimed to evaluate 30-day postoperative outcomes following cervical laminoplasty, stratified by BMI categories and the presence of metabolic syndrome. Material and Methods: This study utilized data from the American College of Surgeons National Surgical Quality Improvement Program database from 2007 to 2022. Patients who underwent cervical laminoplasty were identified and categorized into underweight, normal weight, pre-obese, and class I-III obese groups according to the World Health Organization guidelines, as well as according to the presence or absence of metabolic syndrome. The primary outcome was the 30-day occurrence of at least one complication (excluding blood transfusions). Secondary outcomes included rates of major and minor complications, readmissions, reoperations, hospital length of stay (LOS), and total operative time. Results: A total of 2,261 patients were analyzed, including 21 underweight, 510 normal-weight, 782 pre-obese, 565 class I obese, 249 class II obese, and 134 class III obese individuals. The cohort consisted of 244 individuals with metabolic syndrome and 2,017 without. There were no significant differences in the 30-day rates of overall complications (excluding blood transfusions), major or minor complications, readmissions, reoperations, or hospital LOS across non-normal-weight BMI groups compared to normal-weight patients. However, after adjusting for baseline variables, class II obesity (coefficient 23.585, 95% confidence interval [CI] 10.815-36.355; p < 0.001) and class III obesity (coefficient 20.096, 95% CI 4.712-35.479; p = 0.011) were independently associated with longer operative time. Patients with metabolic syndrome had a significantly higher likelihood of experiencing at least one postoperative complication, excluding blood transfusion (11.1% versus 5.9%, p = 0.003), and at least one major complication (7% versus 3%, p = 0.003). Specifically, they exhibited significantly increased rates of deep incisional surgical site infection (SSI; 2% versus 0.5%, p = 0.017), postoperative pneumonia (2.9% versus 0.7%, p = 0.005), and postoperative dialysis (0.4% versus < 0.1%, p = 0.032). Additionally, patients with metabolic syndrome had significantly longer hospital stays (4.6 ± 4.4 days versus 3.7 ± 4.0 days, p = 0.003) and were significantly more likely to experience prolonged hospitalization (27% versus 14.7%, p < 0.001). After adjusting for confounding variables, metabolic syndrome remained an independent risk factor for deep incisional SSI (odds ratio [OR] 3.25, 95% CI 1.07-9.80, p = 0.037), postoperative pneumonia (OR 4.17, 95% CI 1.63-10.63, P = 0.003), and prolonged hospitalization (OR 1.68, 95% CI 1.17-2.41, p = 0.005). Conclusion: Patients with metabolic syndrome face a significantly increased risk of adverse perioperative outcomes after cervical laminoplasty. Implementing preoperative interventions such as rigorous serum glucose control and effective hypertension management may help mitigate surgical complications and reduce associated costs in these patients. Furthermore, preoperative weight loss in class II-III obese patients may help decrease operative time, potentially minimizing surgical risks and associated costs.

## ID: AGS26/1008

## P0278: Racial and ethnic disparities in posterior cervical spine surgery: an analysis of 19,994 cases

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Kevin Wojcik

1

, Peter Palmer

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Although racial disparities have been studied in the context of anterior cervical spine surgery, limited literature exists on outcomes following posterior cervical spine procedures, which differ in surgical techniques, indications, and recovery. This study aimed to evaluate the extent of racial and ethnic disparities in perioperative outcomes after posterior cervical spine surgeries. Material and Methods: Using data from the 2006-2023 American College of Surgeons National Surgical Quality Improvement Program database, we conducted a retrospective cohort study of patients who underwent posterior cervical fusion or cervical laminoplasty, identified through Current Procedural Terminology codes 63050, 63051, 22590, 22595, and 22600. Patients were stratified by race (White, Black or African American, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander, American Indian or Alaska Native) and Hispanic ethnicity. Individuals of the White race served as the reference group in all analyses. The primary outcome was the incidence of any postoperative complication (excluding blood transfusion) within 30 days. Secondary outcomes included major, minor, medical, and surgical complications, total operative time, hospital length of stay, and discharge disposition. Results: A total of 19,994 patients were included in the study. The majority were White (n = 14,276, 71.3%), followed by Black or African American (n = 3,473, 17.4%), Asian (n = 822, 4.1%), American Indian or Alaska Native (n = 218, 1.1%), Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander (n = 114, 0.6%), and Hispanic (n = 1,091, 5.5%). After adjusting for covariates, Black or African American race was independently correlated with higher risk of experiencing at least one major complication (odds ratio [OR] 1.264, 95% confidence interval [CI] 1.07-1.488; p = 0.005), a medical complication (OR 1.234, 95% CI 1.057-1.436; p = 0.007), cardiac arrest requiring cardiopulmonary resuscitation (OR 2.631, 95% CI 1.427-4.718; p = 0.001), pulmonary embolism (OR 1.88, 95% CI 1.262-2.755; p = 0.001), extended hospital stay (OR 1.921, 95% CI 1.742-2.117; p < 0.001), and non-home discharge (OR 1.801, 95% CI 1.645-1.972; p < 0.001), in addition to longer total hospital stay (coefficient 1.114, 95% CI 0.969-1.258; p < 0.001). Asian race was independently correlated with longer operative times (coefficient 17.621, 95% CI 12.249-22.994; p < 0.001) and longer hospital stay (coefficient 0.77, 95% CI 0.498-1.042; p < 0.001). Native Hawaiian or Pacific Islander race was independently correlated with longer hospital stays (coefficient 1.938, 95% CI 1.253-2.623; p < 0.001) and higher odds of prolonged hospitalization (OR 2.396, 95% CI 1.535-3.668; p < 0.001). Similarly, Hispanic ethnicity was independently associated with increased hospital stay duration (coefficient 0.597, 95% CI 0.36-0.833; p < 0.001) and higher risk of extended hospitalization (OR 1.309, 95% CI 1.109-1.54; p = 0.001). Conclusion: Racial and ethnic minority patients were significantly more likely to experience unfavorable outcomes within 30 days following posterior cervical spine surgery in comparison to White patients. These disparities underscore the need for comprehensive, interdisciplinary approaches to address inequities in surgical care. Future research should incorporate social determinants of health to further explore the root causes of these racial and ethnic outcome differences.

## ID: AGS26/1040

## P0279: Prevalence of congenital cervical spinal stenosis in the Brazilian population

Larissa Tozetti

1

, Denise Silva

1

, Mariana Pontes

1

, Talis Freitas

1

, Thabata Soeira

1

, Vitoria Perez

1

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

1

1

University of Sao Paulo, Ribeirao Preto, Brazil

Introduction: Cervical spinal canal stenosis is a multifactorial condition characterized by narrowing of the canal, most commonly affecting individuals over 50 years of age. Although more frequent in the lumbar region, about 25% of cases involve the cervical spine, where it may cause nerve root and spinal cord compression, leading to cervical spondylotic myelopathy. Etiology includes degenerative changes and congenital factors, with congenital narrowing representing a relevant risk in younger individuals. Surgical treatment may be required depending on symptoms. Knowledge of physiological parameters of the cervical canal is essential for evaluating inflammatory, traumatic, and degenerative conditions; however, normative values and prevalence of congenital stenosis remain poorly described in the Brazilian population. This study aimed to establish the prevalence of congenital cervical spinal canal stenosis in a Brazilian cohort using magnetic resonance imaging. Material and Methods: A total of 600 cervical spine MRIs were analyzed retrospectively, equally distributed by sex and age group (20-80 years). Sagittal canal diameter and canal area were measured from C3 to C7. Interobserver reliability was assessed using the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC). Results: A total of 600 cervical spine MRIs were analyzed. Mean sagittal canal diameter ranged from 11.3 to 13.5 mm, and canal area ranged from 210 to 264 mm2. Men had larger canals than women. Overall, 86% of individuals had sagittal diameters below 14 mm, and 52% below 12 mm, suggesting a high prevalence of potential congenital stenosis. Interobserver reliability was generally good to excellent for diameter and moderate to excellent for canal area. Conclusion: The Brazilian population exhibits smaller cervical canal dimensions, suggesting a potential predisposition to congenital stenosis and an increased risk for myelopathy.

## ID: AGS26/1248

## P0280: Standalone cage for the treatment of symptomatic adjacent segment stenosis in the degenerative cervical spine

Hamdan Abdelrahman

1,2

, Seyed-Emadaldin Sadat

1

, Ahmed Shawky Abdelgawaad

1,2

, Ali Ezzati

1

1

Helios Clinic Erfurt, Erfurt, Germany

2

Asiut University Hospitals, Asiut, Egypt

Introduction: ACDF with cage is considered to be the standard technique in the treatment of degenerative cervical stenosis. Cervical adjacent segment disease (ASD) is not a common pathology like in the lumbar spine. In case of ASD treated with interbody fusion, the stresses expected on the implant make the use of standalone cages debatable. Data dealing with this subject is few in the literature. Material and Methods: A retrospective study of prospectively collected patients undergoing ACDF for ASD using standalone cage (1/2013 and 1/2022). Total of 101 levels in 87 patients (48 m. and 39 f., mean age: 57.3 years). The data were analysed with emphasis on the sagittal profile, cage subsidence and fusion rate at the final follow-up (FU). Results: ASD occurred after monosegmental fusion in 30 cases, bisegmental fusion in 42, and trisegmental fusion in 15 patients. ASD was cranial to the fusion in the majority of cases (71%) and affected mostly a single disc. Mean FU period of two years with minimum one year. The analysis revealed an improvement of the mean SL of adjacent disc from 4.2° preop. to 7.5° postop. At the final FU, the mean SL remained more or less unaffected with a value of 7.2°. Cage subsidence occurred in 12 patients (13.8%). A patient was re-operated due to postoperative hematoma. Temporary unilateral recurrent laryngeal palsy occurred in 3 patients (recovered). CL improved from 15.1° to 21.2° at the final FU. Fusion rate in one-year postop, full radiologic fusion was achieved in 85 patients with a pseudoarthrosis rate of 3.4% (three patients). These 3 patients were asymptomatic. Neither the number of fused segments at the index surgery nor the location of the ASD did have a significant effect on the final results. Conclusion: Standalone cage for ACDF in case of ASD is a safe procedure yielding good results in terms of cervical sagittal profile and fusion rate, regardless the number of fused segments at the index surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1263

## P0281: Is cervicothoracic OPLL a distinct clinico-radiological variant? A critical analysis of neurological recovery predictors

Ajoy P. Shetty

1

, Karthik Ramachandran

1

, Praveen Iyer

1

, S. Rajasekaran

1

1

Ganga Medical Centre and Hospital, Coimbatore, India

Introduction: Ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL) involving the cervicothoracic junction (CTJ) represents a distinct and under-characterised variant of spinal pathology. While sub-axial cervical OPLL has been extensively studied, CT-OPLL poses unique biomechanical and neurological challenges, with limited literature addressing its clinico-radiological features and prognostic indicators. The aim of the study is to evaluate the clinical and radiological predictors of neurological recovery in patients with CT-OPLL undergoing posterior instrumented decompression and fusion. Material and Methods: This retrospective study analysed 53 patients with CT-OPLL treated surgically between 2017 and 2023. Preoperative imaging included CT-based axial morphology classification (hill, mushroom, plateau), canal occupancy ratio measurement, and MRI evaluation of cord signal changes and morphology. Clinical parameters such as mJOA score, Nurick grade, hand myelopathy signs, and bladder dysfunction were recorded. Recovery rate (RR) was calculated using Hirabayashi’s formula, with RR ≥ 50% considered good recovery. Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses were performed to identify independent predictors of poor recovery. Results: Hill-type axial morphology and a canal occupancy ratio (COR) greater than 65% were significantly associated with poor neurological recovery (p < 0.006). ROC analysis yielded an AUC of 0.777 for the occupancy ratio, with 68% specificity and 60.7% sensitivity. Patients with plateau and mushroom morphologies showed better postoperative outcomes. Notably, signs of hand myelopathy were absent in 30% of patients, particularly those with maximum stenosis below C5, and 31.4% experienced bladder dysfunction, with limited postoperative improvement. MRI cord signal changes and sagittal alignment parameters did not significantly correlate with recovery. Conclusion: CT-OPLL is a distinct clinico-radiological entity with poorer neurological outcomes compared to sub-axial OPLL. COR and axial morphology are strong predictors of recovery in patients with CT-OPLL. These findings highlight the importance of detailed preoperative imaging and morphology-based risk stratification to guide surgical planning and patient counselling.

## ID: AGS26/1446

## P0282: Use of tranexamic acid in posterior cervical decompression and fusion: safety and efficacy

Sebastian Fras

1

, Antony Palmer

2

, Rajkishen Narayanan

1

, Alex Vaccaro

1

, Chris Kepler

1

, Gregory Schroeder

1

1

Thomas Jefferson University, Philadelphia, United States

2

Oxford University, Oxford, United Kingdom

Introduction: The purpose of this study was to investigate the safety and efficacy of intravenous tranexamic acid in posterior cervical spine surgeries at a single academic institution. Various measures of blood loss, such as transfusion rate, are recognized to be critical in influencing patient outcomes in surgical procedures. The role of tranexamic acid, an antifibrinolytic agent, has not been investigated in cervical spine surgeries, specifically in posterior cervical spine fusions. This study aims to investigate the safety and efficacy of tranexamic acid use in posterior cervical instrumented fusion operations at a single center, through a retrospective chart review. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort analysis of 555 patients undergoing posterior cervical fusion was conducted, examining whether tranexamic acid administration reduced intraoperative blood loss, the need for transfusions, and postoperative complications. Patients were divided into two cohorts: those who received and those who did not receive tranexamic acid. Statistical analyses were performed to compare estimated blood loss, transfusion rates, postoperative hemoglobin and hematocrit levels, complication rates, and 90-day readmission rates. Results: The results did not find an association between tranexamic acid and a reduced need for blood transfusions, or a lower estimated blood loss. Tranexamic acid administration was associated with a higher incidence of 90-day complications and readmission rates. There was no significant difference in venous thromboembolic events between the cohorts. Conclusion: Tranexamic acid was not associated with a reduction in clinically significant outcomes of blood loss or transfusion rates in posterior cervical fusions. The increased complication and readmission seen in patients administered with tranexamic acid warrants further investigation through prospective studies, and a tempering of expectations by spine surgeons as to the efficacy and safety of the drug in posterior cervical spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1459

## P0283: Pain relief outperforms functional improvement for patient satisfaction two years after cervical spine surgery

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Jordan Haber

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Patient satisfaction is an important metric for evaluating outcomes in value-based cervical spine care. PROMIS scores in particular have become widespread and broadly accepted as a tool to track patient progress and measure surgical success. Yet, neck pain and arm pain remain vital patient reported symptoms during clinical visits. Although both functional and symptomatic outcomes are routinely collected, their relative impact on long-term satisfaction remains uncertain. This study investigated whether improvements in physical function (PROMIS-PF) or reductions in pain more strongly predict satisfaction two years after cervical spine surgery. Material and Methods: A multicenter, prospective database was used to identify of all patients who underwent cervical spine surgery between 2013 and 2023 at a tertiary care institution. Inclusion criteria was the presence of 2 year satisfaction data. Satisfaction at two years was dichotomized as “satisfied” (met expectations or would repeat surgery) versus “unsatisfied” (did not meet expectations or would not repeat). PROMIS-PF, neck pain, and arm pain scores were collected at baseline, 90days, 1 year and two years. MCID per the registry was defined as a ≥ 4.5-point increase for PROMIS-PF and a ≥ 2-point reduction for pain scores. Statistical analysis included univariate testing and multivariate logistic regression with backward stepwise elimination. Results: The study cohort contained 515 patients with a mean age of 58.4 ± 11.7 years, BMI of 30.9 ± 6.4, and was 54.8% male. The majority were White (89%). Overall satisfaction rate at two years was 76.4%. In univariate analysis, all outcome measures at 2 years were significantly associated with satisfaction (p < 0.001), including PROMIS-PF scores, PROMIS-PF change from baseline, and MCID achievement. Similar associations were found for both neck and arm pain scores and their respective MCID status. The largest effect sizes were observed for meeting MCID at 2 years in neck pain (χ2 = 61.3), followed by arm pain (χ2 = 39.7). PROMIS-PF change scores remained consistently significant across all timepoints. The final logistic regression model (Nagelkerke R2 = 0.323) identified four independent predictors of satisfaction: lower arm pain at two years (OR 0.786, 95% CI 0.692-0.893, p < 0.001), achievement of neck pain MCID at two years (OR 3.011, 95% CI 1.414-6.413, p = 0.004), higher PROMIS-PF score at two years (OR 1.048, 95% CI 0.996-1.102, p = 0.070), and older age (OR 1.037 per year, 95% CI 1.006-1.069, p = 0.021). Conclusion: Contrary to our hypothesis, pain-related outcomes-specifically persistent arm pain and clinically meaningful improvement in neck pain-were stronger predictors of two-year satisfaction than physical function. Although PROMIS-PF trended toward significance, pain relief emerged as the dominant factor. These findings suggest that long-term satisfaction after cervical spine surgery is more tightly linked to symptom resolution than to functional gains, underscoring the importance of targeted pain management strategies in surgical planning and follow-up.

## ID: AGS26/1468

## P0284: Fusion, function, and risk factors after ACDF: A > 1,000 case consecutive single-surgeon cohort from Tasmania

Naquiya Murtaza Mohsin

1

, Ting Yoon Kwan

1

, Arvind Dubey

1,2

, Aryan Dubey

1,2

, Anjali Menon

1

, Nicholas Holona

1

, Lucas Chu

1

, Kawya Jayasinghe

1

1

Royal Hobart Hospital, Hobart, Australia

2

Calvary Lenah Valley Hospital, Hobart, Australia

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) is one of the most frequently performed procedures in spine surgery. While outcomes for single- and two-level constructs are well established, the role of multilevel ACDF remains controversial due to concerns regarding pseudarthrosis, subsidence, and dysphagia. Most published series are small, heterogeneous, or derived from specialist referral centers, leaving uncertainty about real-world performance in broader health systems. We report one of the largest consecutive single-surgeon ACDF cohorts worldwide, spanning both public and private practice across Tasmania. This study directly compares single/2-level procedures with extended constructs (3-5 levels), with specific analysis of smoking status, providing a unique benchmark for contemporary practice. Materials and Methods: This was a retrospective consecutive cohort study including all patients undergoing ACDF by a single neurosurgeon between January 2017 and January 2025. Patients were stratified as single/2-level or multilevel (3-5). Baseline variables collected included demographics, comorbidities, smoking status (current, former, never), surgical indication, and preoperative Neck Disability Index (NDI). The primary outcome was radiographic fusion at 6 and 12 months, assessed by plain radiographs or CT. Secondary outcomes were functional improvement (absolute NDI, change in NDI, and achievement of the minimal clinically important difference [MCID, ≥ 10 points]) and perioperative complications. Sensitivity analyses excluded early imaging (< 20 weeks for the 6-month window; < 10 months for the 12-month window). Subgroup analyses were performed by construct length (1-2 vs 3-5 levels), age group, and smoking status. Results: Over 1,000 patients were included: > 500 single/2-level and > 500 multilevel ACDFs, with constructs up to 5 levels. Indications were predominantly neck pain with radiculopathy (80-90%), with myelopathy in 5-10%. Fusion rates exceeded 98% for single/2-level constructs, 91% for 3-level, 89% for 4-level, and > 85% for 5-level constructs. Smoking was associated with a modestly reduced fusion rate at 12 months, but the majority of smokers (> 85%) still achieved union. Functional recovery was substantial across all groups, with mean NDI improvement of 18-22 points and > 70% achieving MCID. Complications were infrequent: 9 postoperative hematomas requiring evacuation, 4 recurrent laryngeal nerve palsies requiring injection, 6 posterior fixations for symptomatic subsidence, 3 infections requiring drainage, and 1 Horner’s syndrome. Transient dysphagia (∼8%) and dysphonia (∼1%) resolved without intervention. Importantly, complication rates did not significantly differ between smokers and non-smokers, although delayed fusion was more common in smokers. Conclusion: This > 1,000 case Tasmanian cohort represents one of the largest single-surgeon ACDF experiences worldwide. Across both public and private systems, ACDF achieved high fusion rates, substantial and durable functional recovery, and a low incidence of major complications. Extended constructs up to 5 levels performed comparably to single- and two-level procedures, directly challenging the perception that multilevel ACDF carries prohibitive risk. Smoking was identified as a potential but not prohibitive risk factor for delayed fusion. These findings provide robust, real-world evidence that long-segment ACDF can be performed safely and effectively, setting new international benchmarks and informing surgical decision-making. Further detailed comorbidity analysis, including postoperative smoking impact, will be presented in the full manuscript.

## ID: AGS26/1628

## P0285: Does implant choice matter? Clinical and radiological outcomes of three-level ACDF with standalone anchored cage versus plate-cage system

Namith Rangaswamy

1

, Joshua K

1

, Gabriel Leow

1

, Si Jian Hui

1

, Wong Hee Kit

1

, Dinesh Shree Kumar

2

, Naresh Kumar

1

1

National University Health Systems, Singapore, Singapore

2

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) is a well-established procedure for cervical degenerative disc disease (DDD). While the efficacy of stand-alone anchored cage (SAAC) systems has been demonstrated in single- and two-level fusions, evidence remains sparse regarding their use in three-level disease. This study compared clinical and radiological outcomes of three-level contiguous ACDF using SAAC versus traditional plate-cage (PC) constructs. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients undergoing three-level contiguous ACDF (C3-C7) between January 2017 and December 2022 with a minimum follow-up of 24 months. Clinical outcomes recorded include Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for pain, Neck Disability Index (NDI), and modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) scores. Radiological outcomes assessed segmental height, local alignment, radiological fusion and C2-C7 Cobb angle at multiple time-points. Perioperative variables (operative duration, blood loss, and drain output) and complications were documented. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: Forty-five patients were included (PC: 25; SAAC: 20) in this study. Patients in the SAAC group were significantly older (68.7 ± 7.8 vs. 55.8 ± 9.8 years, p < 0.001). Mean blood loss (108.8 vs. 167.2 ml, p < 0.001) and operative duration (281.0 vs. 310.0 minutes, p = 0.014) were lower in the SAAC cohort. Both groups demonstrated significant improvement in VAS, NDI, and mJOA scores at two years, without intergroup differences. Radiologically, both constructs improved segmental height and lordosis; the SAAC group showed greater immediate segmental correction (15.7° vs. 10.8°, p = 0.020). Both groups had demonstrated radiological fusion by 24 months. Complication rates were low and comparable, though transient hoarseness was more common in SAAC. One SAAC patient required re-exploration for hematoma. Conclusion: Three-level ACDF using SAAC provides comparable clinical and radiological outcomes to PC constructs, with the advantages of reduced operative time and blood loss. SAAC may represent a safe and effective alternative, performing well even in older cohort.
