## P0312: Influence of metabolic bone status and nutritional status on patient reported outcomes: a prospective study of lumbar spinal fusion patients with 12-month follow-up

Gaston Camino-Willhuber

1

, Haffer Henryk

2

, Erika Chiapparelli

3

, Maximilian Muellner

2

, Lukas Schönnagel

2

, Paul Köhli

2

, Jennifer Shue

3

, William Zelenty

3

, Gbolabo Sokunbi

3

, Federico Girardi

3

, Frank Cammisa

3

, Andrew Sama

3

, Alexander Hughes

3

1

Orthopaedic Department, Policlinica Gipuzkoa, San Sebastian, Spain

2

Center for Musculoskeletal Surgery, Charité - Universitätsmedizin Berlin, corporate member of Freie Universität Berlin and Humboldt-Universität zu Berlin, Berlin, Germany

3

Orthopaedic Surgery, Spine Care Institute, Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: The impact of preoperative metabolic and nutritional parameters on patient reported outcomes (PROs) after spine surgery is unclear. The objective of this study was to examine the relationship between nutritional and metabolic parameters to PROs and after lumbar fusion in patients undergoing open posterior lumbar fusion surgery due to degenerative spinal conditions between 2014-2021. Material and Methods: Prospectively collected data of patients undergoing open posterior lumbar fusion surgery due to degenerative spinal conditions between 2014-2021 was analyzed. Preoperative volumetric bone mineral density (vBMD), metabolic and nutritional parameters such as 25-hydroxyvitamin D (VitD), Urinary N-telopeptide (uNTX), serum N-telopeptide (sNTX), bone alkaline phosphatase (BAP), parathyroid hormone (PTH), Procollagen type I N-terminal Propeptide (P1NP) and albumin were analyzed. PROs included the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), numeric rating scale for leg (NRS-L) and back (NRS-B) pain and the 12-item Short Form Health Survey (SF-12) were collected at baseline before and 6 weeks (6W-FU), 3 months (3M-FU), 6 month (6M-FU) and 12 months (12M-FU) after surgery. Spearman correlation and multivariable regression analyses (confounder: age, BMI, sex, baseline PROs) were performed. Results: 125 patients with a median age of 62.1 (IQR 54.2-69.5) years and a median BMI of 29.1 (IQR 26.1-33.0) kg/m2 were analyzed. The univariate analysis showed that lower 25-hydroxyvitamin D was inversely associated with ODI (3M-FU, 12M-FU), NRS back pain (3M-FU) and leg pain (12M-FU). Lower preoperative vBMD was associated with more leg pain at the 12M-FU. The univariate analysis demonstrated no significant associations between uNTX, BAP, sNTX, PTH and PROs, besides a negative correlation between sNTX and PCS-12 at the 3M-FU. The multivariable regression analysis showed that lower Vitamin D levels were associated with worse ODI outcomes at the 3M-FU, 6M-FU and 12M-FU and worse SF-12 physical component scores (3M-FU, 6M-FU and 12M-FU). Conclusion: This prospective investigation demonstrated that lower preoperative Vitamin D levels were associated with worse ODI and SF-12 scores after lumbar fusion. This might support the routine assessment of Vitamin D as a preoperative biomarker in fusion surgery. Vitamin D supplementation might be a therapeutic intervention that can improve PROs in elective spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/90

## P0313: Expandable lumbar transforaminal interbody cages: do they modify radiological parameters?

Joan Toribio

1

, Laura Perelló

1

1

Hospital Universitari Son Llatzer, Palma de Mallorca, Spain

Introduction: The use of transforaminal expandable interbody cages is controversial, and there is no evidence that they modify radiological parameters in the lumbar spine, for which they were designed. The objective of this study was to retrospectively assess whether expandable interbody cages modify the following radiological parameters: local and regional lumbar lordosis, anterior and posterior disc height, and foraminal height. Material and Methods: A total of 18 expandable cages implanted in 2022 were reviewed in 17 patients: 10 cases in the L5-S1 segment, 7 cases in L4-L5, and one at the L3-L4 dual level. The patients ranged in age from 35 to 64 years, 10 women and 7 men. The cages implanted on 15° lordotic implants, and 3 cages had a 10° lordosis. Radiological assessments: the following distances were measured on lateral lumbar spine X-rays: anterior disc heigh, posterior disc height, segmental lordosis at the treated level, lumbar lordosis, foraminal height. These measurements were taken on the preoperative and immediate postoperative radiographs. Both radiographs were compared to assess whether there was a significant change in the radiological parameters. Results: The preoperative and postoperative foraminal height, although it has increased, has not shown a statistically significant improvement, going from 1.4 cm in preoperative x-ray to 1.6 cm in postoperative. The anterior disc height has obtained an improvement or statistically significant increase, going from 0.6 cm to 1.1 cm. The posterior disc height has also recorded statistically significant changes going from 0.4 cm to 0.9 cm. The global lumbar lordosis has gone from 38° to 40°, these data not being statistically significant. Conclusion: In our hands, expandable intersomatic lumbar cages have not achieved a statistically significant modification of the radiological parameters assessed.

## ID: AGS26/194

## P0314: Long-term efficacy and safety of basivertebral nerve ablation for chronic low back pain: a single-center 5-year prospective study

Devender Singh

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Chronic low back pain (CLBP) is a significant global health issue with profound physical, emotional, and socioeconomic impacts. Conventional treatment options, including medications, physical therapy, and surgical interventions, often provide limited long-term relief. Basivertebral nerve ablation (BVNA) is an emerging minimally invasive treatment aimed at addressing vertebrogenic back pain by targeting the basivertebral nerve. The primary aim of this study is to evaluate the long-term clinical outcomes of patients undergoing BVNA for CLBP. Specifically, we aim to assess improvements in pain and disability, the achievement of clinically significant improvements, the use of adjunctive treatments, and patient-reported functional recovery. We hypothesize that BVNA leads to clinically and statistically significant improvements in pain (VAS) and disability (ODI) scores at long-term follow-ups, with a majority of patients experiencing sustained functional recovery. Material and Methods: This prospective, single-arm follow-up study enrolled 15 patients (6 male and 9 female) with CLBP who underwent BVNA at a single center. Baseline data included demographic characteristics, Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores for pain, and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores. Follow-up assessments were conducted at 3, 4, and 5 years, capturing VAS, ODI, medication and injection usage, and patient-reported outcomes. Clinically significant improvements were defined as patients achieving a ≥ 15-point reduction in ODI and a ≥ 2-point reduction in VAS. Results: The mean age of our cohort was 55 years (± 8.2 yeras). At baseline, the mean VAS and ODI scores were 6.7 ± 1.2 and 47.8 ± 9.3, respectively. At 3, 4, and 5 years, the mean % improvements in VAS were 60.8%, 53.6%, and 67.8%, respectively, while ODI improvements were 72.3%, 77.6%, and 74.1%. Clinically significant improvements in both ODI and VAS scores were achieved by 93.3% of patients at 3 years, 80.0% at 4 years, and 86.7% at 5 years. Medication usage was reported by 13.3% of patients at 3 and 4 years, while injection usage remained consistent at 6.7% across all follow-ups. Functional recovery, defined as resuming pre-back-pain activity levels, was reported by 80.0%, 66.7%, and 86.7% of patients at 3, 4, and 5 years, respectively. Importantly, no serious device- or procedure-related adverse events were reported throughout the study. Additionally, 100% of enrolled patients completed the 5-year follow-up, ensuring robust data collection and analysis. Conclusion: The findings of this study demonstrate that BVNA is an effective long-term treatment for CLBP, resulting in significant reductions in pain and disability. A high percentage of patients achieved clinically significant improvements in both ODI and VAS scores, with sustained functional recovery observed over 5 years. The minimal need for adjunctive treatments and the absence of serious adverse events highlights the safety and efficacy of BVNA.

## ID: AGS26/195

## P0315: Sustained satisfaction and reduced dependency: a single center 5-year evaluation of basivertebral nerve ablation for chronic low back pain

Devender Singh

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

, John Stokes

1

, Matthew Geck

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Chronic low back pain (CLBP) is a prevalent condition that significantly affects quality of life. Basivertebral nerve ablation (BVNA) is a novel treatment for vertebrogenic CLBP, offering a minimally invasive approach. This study provides a detailed analysis of patient satisfaction, the relationship between baseline scores and improvement, medication reduction, and adverse events over a 5-year follow-up period. We aim to evaluate the long-term effectiveness and safety of BVNA, with a focus on patient-reported satisfaction, baseline predictors of improvement, medication dependency, and adverse event rates. Our hypothesis was that BVNA will result in clinically and statistically significant long-term reductions in pain (VAS) and disability (ODI) scores, accompanied by high levels of patient satisfaction, minimal reliance on adjunctive treatments, and no serious adverse events. Patients with higher baseline scores are expected to experience greater percentage improvements. Material and Methods: This single-center, prospective study followed 15 patients (6 males, 9 females; mean age 55 ± 8.2 years) treated with BVNA. Outcomes were assessed at 3-, 4-, and 5-year follow-ups. Measures included VAS and ODI scores, patient satisfaction (whether they would undergo the procedure again), correlation between baseline scores and improvement, and trends in medication use. Clinically significant improvement was defined as a ≥ 15-point reduction in ODI and a ≥ 2-point reduction in VAS. Results: Patient satisfaction was consistently high across all follow-ups; with the majority (88.9%) reporting they would undergo BVNA again for the same condition. A weak negative correlation was observed between baseline scores and percentage improvement in VAS (-0.10) and ODI (-0.23), indicating that improvement was not strongly influenced by baseline severity. Medication usage significantly declined over time; only 13.3% of patients reported taking pain medications at 3 and 4 years, with limited data available for 5 years. Injection reliance was minimal, with just 6.7% of patients requiring injections across follow-up visits. Importantly, no serious device- or procedure-related adverse events were reported during the 5-year study. Conclusion: BVNA provides sustained improvements in pain and disability, coupled with high levels of patient satisfaction and minimal reliance on adjunctive treatments. The lack of serious adverse events reinforces its safety and long-term effectiveness as a treatment option for vertebrogenic CLBP. These findings highlight the procedure’s broad applicability, independent of baseline symptom severity.

## ID: AGS26/233

## P0316: The role of pelvic incidence in predicting functional outcomes after lumbar fusion: a retrospective cohort study

Devender Singh

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

, Eric Mayer

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Spinopelvic alignment plays a crucial role in spinal pathology and surgical outcomes. Pelvic Incidence (PI) and Pelvic Tilt (PT) are key radiographic parameters influencing postoperative function and disability. Understanding their relationship with patient-reported outcomes can optimize surgical planning and improve clinical outcomes for patients undergoing lumbar fusion for degenerative disc disease. This study does not address PI-LL mismatch or global sagittal balance issues; all patients had PI-LL values less than 10°, indicating adequate spinopelvic harmony across the cohort. This study aims to assess the predictive value of Pelvic Incidence and Pelvic Tilt on patient-reported outcomes, including PROMIS Physical Function (PF), Pain Interference (PI), Social Role (SR), and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), in patients who underwent one or two-level lumbar fusion for degenerative disc disease. A subgroup analysis was performed to evaluate differences between low and high PI groups. We hypothesized that Pelvic Incidence is a stronger predictor of patient-reported outcomes than Pelvic Tilt and that patients with high PI may have worse functional outcomes postoperatively compared to those with low PI. Material and Methods: This was a retrospective cohort study of patients who underwent one or two-level lumbar fusion for degenerative disc disease. Demographic data, radiographic parameters (PI and PT), and patient-reported outcomes (PROMIS-PF, PROMIS-PI, PROMIS-SR, and ODI) were collected. Linear regression analysis was performed to evaluate the predictive value of PI and PT for each outcome measure. Subgroup analysis was conducted by stratifying patients into Low PI (< 55°) and High PI (≥ 55°) groups. Results: A total of 140 patients were analyzed (Low PI: 76; High PI: 64). The mean age was 67.8 ± 8.0 years for the Low PI group and 65.4 ± 11.6 years for the High PI group. Females comprised 54.7% of the Low PI group and 51.3% of the High PI group. Regression analysis demonstrated that PI had a moderate correlation with PROMIS-PF (r = -0.50, R2 = 0.25), PROMIS-PI (r = 0.40, R2 = 0.16), and PROMIS-SR (r = 0.44, R2 = 0.19), whereas PT showed weak correlation with all outcomes (r ≤ -0.04, R2 ≈ 0.00-0.09). The subgroup analysis revealed that patients with High PI had significantly lower PROMIS-PF scores and higher ODI scores than those with Low PI. The Chow Test confirmed a significant difference in the predictive strength of PI between Low and High PI groups for PROMIS-PF (p = 0.05). Conclusion: This study highlights the clinical relevance of Pelvic Incidence as a predictor of patient-reported outcomes in lumbar fusion patients. PI demonstrated a stronger predictive relationship with functional and disability scores compared to PT. Patients with High PI had worse postoperative functional outcomes, reinforcing the need for individualized surgical planning based on spinopelvic parameters. These findings support the consideration of PI in preoperative assessments to optimize patient selection and postoperative rehabilitation strategies.

## ID: AGS26/258

## P0317: Independent risk factors for sacral fracture after lumbosacral fusion in adults: a Cox proportional hazards retrospective analysis

Anthony Baumann

1

, Robjert Trager

2

, Omkar Anaspure

3

, Tyler Metcalf

1

, Davin Gong

4

, Ethan Cottrill

5

, Peter Passias

5

, Gordon Preston

1

1

Cleveland Clinic Akron General, Akron, United States

2

Department of Rehabilitation Services, University Hospitals, Cleveland, United States

3

Perelman School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, United States

4

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

5

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Duke University, Durham, United States

Introduction: Sacral fractures are an uncommon complication after lumbosacral fusion, with recent research suggesting that osteoporosis is an independent risk factor. However, there is a paucity of data regarding other potential risk factors, potentially hindering risk stratification. The purpose of this study is to determine the independent risk factors for sacral fracture after lumbosacral fusion in adults to optimize patient care. Material and Methods: This pre-registered retrospective database study (TriNetX) included adults (≥18 years) who underwent first-time lumbosacral fusion between 2005-2023, divided into two cohorts based on osteoporosis diagnosis: the osteoporosis cohort and the no osteoporosis cohort. Patients with history of spinal fusion/instrumentation, sacral fracture, spinal neoplasm, and pelvic instrumentation were excluded. The primary outcome was the incidence of sacral fracture after lumbosacral fusion through two years, estimated using Kaplan-Meier analysis, with hazard ratios (HRs) estimated for each covariate using a multivariable Cox proportional hazards model (p < 0.05). Covariates included those associated with the sacral fracture risk based on existing literature. A forest plot was created to graphically display HRs. Results: There were 592 sacral fractures (2.3%) among 25,598 adults who underwent lumbosacral fusion with 470 cases (2.8%) in the osteoporosis cohort and 122 cases (1.3%) in the no osteoporosis cohort. Independent risk factors (p < 0.05) for sacral fracture after lumbosacral fusion included previous pelvic radiation [95% CI] (HR: 3.04 [1.26, 7.36]), history of falls (HR: 2.65 [2.21, 3.18]), osteoporosis (HR: 2.02 [1.64, 2.49]), long-term systemic corticosteroid use (HR: 1.47 [1.10, 1.98]), age at index lumbosacral fusion (HR: 1.01 [1.00, 1.02] per year), while protective factors included overweight and/or obesity status (HR: 0.79 [0.65, 0.97]), and male sex (HR: 0.64 [0.48, 0.85]). Variables determined not to be independent risk factors (p > 0.05) in our model included nicotine dependence, history of total hip arthroplasty, anterior surgical approach, posterior surgical approach, long lumbosacral spinal fusion with instrumentation (7-12 vertebral levels), diabetes mellitus, bone density medications, inflammatory polyarthropathies, pelvic fixation, vitamin D prescription, chronic kidney disease, as well as glucagon-like peptide-1 analogues. Conclusion: Based on a retrospective study with the largest sample size of sacral fractures after lumbosacral fusion to date, clinically meaningful independent risk factors for sacral fractures after lumbosacral fusion include pelvic radiation, prior falls, osteoporosis, and long-term systemic corticosteroid use, while protective factors include male sex, and being obese/overweight. Despite being plausible risk factors, variables such as nicotine dependence, history of THA, surgical approach, long lumbosacral spinal fusion with instrumentation, diabetes mellitus, bone density medications, inflammatory polyarthropathies, pelvic fixation, vitamin D prescription, chronic kidney disease, and GLP-1 analogues were not independently associated with sacral fractures after lumbosacral fusion. However, these results should be taken with caution due to the retrospective study design. Clinicians should integrate the knowledge of these independent variables into surgical planning, education, and postoperative detection to optimize care.

## ID: AGS26/308

## P0318: The guiding value of ultrasound-guided selective nerve root block in the surgical treatment of multilevel lumbar degeneration

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: At present, the surgical options for multi-level lumbar degeneration remain controversial. The aim of this study is to explore the guiding value of ultrasound-guided selective nerve root block (SNRB) in surgical treatment decision making in patients with multi-level lumbar degenerative diseases. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on 90 patients with multi-level lumbar degenerative disease who were surgically treated in our hospital from January 2020 to December 2023. The patients were divided into SNRB group and non-SNRB group according to whether or not they underwent SNRB before surgery. There were 48 patients in the SNRB group. Their responsible segments were identified by ultrasound-guided SNRB, combined with symptoms and imaging examination before surgery. Then decompression was performed on the responsible segments. A total of 42 patients in the non-SNRB group, and conventional multi-level lumbar spinal canal decompression was performed because their responsible segments were not clear. The operative time, intraoperative blood loss, the VAS score and JOA score of lumbar and leg pain before surgery, the 3rd day after surgery, 6 months after surgery, and the last follow-up were recorded and compared between the two groups, and the occurrence of intra-operative complications were also recorded. Results: The operation was successfully completed in both groups, and no serious complications occurred during surgery. One case of dural tear occurred in the non-SNRB group and was repaired intraoperatively. The operative time and blood loss in SNRB group were significantly less than those in non-SNRB group (p < 0.05). The VAS scores and JOA scores of low back pain and leg pain in SNRB group were better than those in the non-SNRB group on the 3rd day after surgery, 6 months after surgery and at the last follow-up, with statistical significance (p < 0.05). No loosening, or displacement of the instrumentation were found in the two groups, and the bone grafting healed well. Conclusion: This study showed that preoperative ultrasound-guided SNRB could identify the responsible segments and provide surgical guiding value for patients with multi-level lumbar degeneration.

## ID: AGS26/316

## P0319: High-resolution characterization of quality-of-life recovery trajectories after degenerative lumbar spine surgery using an integrated digital health platform: a prospective feasibility study

Lenga Pavlina

1,2

, Sebastian Ille

1

, Martin Dugas

2

, Sandro Krieg

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Heidelberg University Hospital, Heidelberg, Germany

2

Institute of Medical Informatics, Heidelberg, Germany

Introduction: Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) are crucial for evaluating recovery following lumbar spine surgery, but traditional assessment schedules frequently miss critical early postoperative changes. This creates an early “black box,” limiting timely, patient-centred interventions. High-frequency, digitally integrated PROMs initiated immediately post-surgery can capture detailed, real-time recovery trajectories, potentially improving clinical responsiveness, precision, and patient outcomes. We aimed to implement and assess a novel electronic high-frequency PROM system directly integrated into clinical workflows. Material and Methods: From February 1 to May 15, 2025, we prospectively enrolled 73 patients undergoing surgery for lumbar disc herniation (LDH, n = 25) or spinal canal stenosis (SCS, n = 48). Surgical indications were confirmed via multidisciplinary neurosurgical discussions integrating clinical and imaging data. PROMs (overall health status, wound, leg pain, and sensory disturbances) were recorded every second day over four weeks using the secure, web-based MyEDC platform integrated into the hospital's electronic health record. Results: Significant demographic differences existed between groups; SCS patients were older (67·0 vs 51·9 years; p < 0.001) and had higher comorbidity burden (ASA 2·2 vs 1·8; p = 0.01). High-frequency monitoring revealed distinct recovery trajectories: SCS patients initially had lower overall health status but surpassed LDH patients at four weeks (74·1% vs 64.5%; p < 0.0001). Initial wound pain was greater in the SCS group but resolved similarly by four weeks. Sensory disturbances were significantly worse in LDH patients (p < 0.0001), while leg pain was slightly higher in SCS patients (p = 0.035). Younger LDH patients (< 40 years) reported significantly lower postoperative QoL (p = 0.0003). Elevated baseline depressive symptoms negatively correlated with postoperative health status (LDH: r = -0.15, p = 0.034; SCS: r = -0.33, p = 0.045). Electronic assessment response rates reached approximately 95% within two weeks. Conclusion: High-frequency electronic PROM collection immediately after lumbar spine surgery uncovers distinct, clinically relevant recovery trajectories, significantly influenced by psychological factors. By addressing the early postoperative “black box,” this approach enables personalized care, timely interventions, and establishes a new benchmark for precision medicine in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/353

## P0320: Postoperative radiculitis in interlaminar endoscopic discectomy secondary to decompensated diabetes mellitus: case report and literature review

Diego Prado

1,2

, Andres Chahín

1

, Cristian Ricardo Correa Valencia

1

, Álvaro Soteres

1

, Sofia Chahin

1

, Javiera Babarovich

1

1

Clinica Las Condes, Instituto de Neurocirugía Doctor Asenjo, Santiago, Chile

2

Instituto de Neurocirugía Dr. Asenjo, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) is a major global public health issue and remains one of the leading causes of morbidity and mortality worldwide. Beyond its systemic complications, T2DM has been recognized as an important perioperative risk factor, particularly in spine surgery. Diabetic patients are more prone to postoperative infections, delayed wound healing, accelerated disc degeneration, and peripheral nerve injury. Although radiculitis is a known complication of lumbar discectomy, the association between poorly controlled diabetes and postoperative radicular pain has been infrequently reported. We present the case of a patient who developed severe radiculitis after interlaminar endoscopic discectomy, in the context of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, and we review the relevant literature. Material and Methods: Case Report: A 59-year-old male with a history of hypertension, coronary artery disease, and pre-diabetes managed with metformin 850 mg/day was admitted through the Chilean GES program for surgical treatment of a left L5-S1 lumbar disc herniation (LDH). Preoperative evaluation revealed a fasting glucose of 142 mg/dL, attributed to prior intramuscular betamethasone. An interlaminar endoscopic discectomy was performed without intraoperative complications, and the patient was discharged 12 hours postoperatively in stable condition. Seven days after surgery, he developed severe left-sided sciatica, more intense than his preoperative symptoms. Initial outpatient management with high-dose analgesics and pregabalin for seven days failed to provide relief; corticosteroids were not administered. Due to persistent symptoms, he was readmitted for pain management and further evaluation. Magnetic resonance imaging excluded recurrent herniation and revealed only expected postoperative edema. A transforaminal epidural block was planned. However, pre-procedure laboratory tests demonstrated marked hyperglycemia (642 mg/dL) and an HbA1c of 10.1%, indicating uncontrolled diabetes. The procedure was canceled, and the patient was evaluated by internal medicine. Insulin therapy was initiated, and he was discharged with optimized analgesia and a structured insulin regimen. Results: During follow-up, the patient experienced a progressive reduction in radicular pain. At his most recent outpatient visit, HbA1c had improved to 7%, and he remained asymptomatic with no recurrence of neurological symptoms. Conclusion: This case highlights the potential impact of uncontrolled diabetes on the development of postoperative radiculitis following spine surgery. Several mechanisms may explain this relationship, including microvascular compromise of the nerve roots, metabolic stress, and impaired healing in the setting of hyperglycemia. Prior studies have reported an increased risk of neuropathic pain in diabetic patients undergoing lumbar procedures, although specific reports linking decompensated diabetes to acute postoperative radiculitis remain scarce. We emphasize the importance of preoperative metabolic optimization in patients with diabetes or pre-diabetes undergoing elective lumbar discectomy. Comprehensive evaluation of glycemic status, including HbA1c measurement, should be mandatory to reduce the risk of complications. Early involvement of internal medicine in perioperative management may help prevent adverse outcomes and improve surgical recovery. Decompensated diabetes mellitus can act as a contributing factor in the development of postoperative radiculitis after endoscopic lumbar discectomy. Careful preoperative metabolic assessment and optimization are essential, even in patients with pre-diabetes or mild disease, to ensure safe surgical outcomes

## ID: AGS26/432

## P0321: Regional variety in severity of indication and outcomes of patients with discectomy for lumbar disc herniation: the Finspine Register Study

Eetu Suominen

1

, Antti Saarinen

1

, Antti Malmivaara

2

, Liisa Pekkanen

3

, Jukka Huttunen

4

, Henri Salo

2

, Jussi Repo

5

1

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Turku University Hospital, Turku, Finland

2

Finnish Institute for Health and Welfare, Helsinki, Finland

3

Hospital Nova of Central Finland, Jyväskylä, Finland

4

Kuopio University Hospital and Institute of Clinical Medicine, University of Eastern Finland, Kuopio, Finland

5

Tampere University Hospital and University of Tampere, Tampere, Finland

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation treated with lumbar discectomy is a common surgical procedure. Finland’s public healthcare system aims to provide equitable access for treatment but regional differences in patient characteristics and surgical outcomes may exist. The aim of the present study is to evaluate preoperative factors and postoperative outcomes after lumbar discectomy across Finland’s five university hospital catchment areas. Material and Methods: We analyzed data from 18,954 patients undergoing primary lumbar discectomy between 2017 and 2024 from the Finnish National Spine Register (FinSpine). Preoperative demographics and patient-reported outcomes measures (PROs) - Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for pain, and EQ-5D-5L quality of life instrument - were compared across regions. Follow-up assessments occurred at 3 months, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively. Results: Baseline disability (median ODI 42-48 points), pain, BMI, smoking status, and employment varied significantly between regions (p < 0.001). Pain duration also differed, with some regions reporting longer preoperative symptoms. Postoperatively, all regions demonstrated marked improvements: median ODI scores decreased to 10-15, and over 50% of patients reported at least a 50% reduction in pain at 2 years. EQ-5D scores improved significantly (median increase from 0.47-0.51 to 0.65-0.73 points). Patient satisfaction was consistently high (75%-88% across regions), with a majority reporting symptom improvement. Differences in preoperative PROs diminished by 2 years, indicating convergence of outcomes nationwide. Conclusion: Despite regional variations in baseline health and socioeconomic factors, lumbar discectomy outcomes are uniformly positive across Finland, supporting equitable surgical care. Further research should address referral and waiting time disparities to improve preoperative health equity.

## ID: AGS26/442

## P0322: Comorbidity prevalence of degenerative lumbar scoliosis in patients with degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis: age distribution pattern and clinical characteristics

Siyu Zhou

1,2,3

, Junjie Ma

4

, Wenyuan Xin

4

, Zhuoran Sun

1,2,3

, Weishi Li

1,2,3

1

Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

2

Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Beijing, China

3

Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

4

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: With the deepening of population aging, the incidence of degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis (DLSS) is increasing year by year, while, due to the overall degeneration of the spine, the comorbidities in elderly DLSS patients have become more complex. Among these, preoperative degenerative lumbar scoliosis (DLS) is one of the most common conditions, which significantly affects the surgical strategy. Although the incidence of DLS in the elderly population has been reported, the comorbidity prevalence of DLS in patients with DLSS hasn't been well demonstrated, and there still lack the Large-scale comprehensive studies on the comorbidity of DLS in patients with LSS. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective cross-sectional study on 1689 patients hospitalized for DLSS at our institution between January 2018 and September 2020. The inclusion criteria of DLS was defined as a coronal curvature (main lumbar curve) ≥ 10° measured using the Cobb method and older than 45 years without history of scoliosis. The preoperative radiograhic and clinical data of these patients including Visual Analogue Score (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), C7 plumb line-center sacral vertical line (C7PL-CSVL), Cobb angle, sagittal vertical axis (SVA), pelvic incidence minus lumbar lordosis (PI-LL) and pelvic tilt (PT) is reviewed. Pearson correlation analysis is used to explore the relationship between coronal deformity and sagittal deformity, and to demonstrate the association between radiographic parameters and clinical symptoms. Results: Among the patients, 443 (157 males and 286 females, mean age 65.54 ± 7.49 years, mean Cobb angle 17.95 ± 7.93°) met the criteria for scoliosis (Cobb angle ≥ 10°), resulting in a combined prevalence of 26.2%. In LSS patients aged 45-49, 50-54, 55-59, 60-64, 65-69, 70-74, and 75 years and older, the DLS comorbidity rates were 12.0%, 18.3%, 21.0%, 26.6%, 28.4%, 30.6%, and 35.6%, respectively. In comorbid patients with C7PL-CSVL ≤ 20 mm, sagittal imbalance rate was 37.3%; in comorbid patients with C7PL-CSVL between 20mm and 40 mm, the rate was 47.2%; and in comorbid patients with C7PL-CSVL ≥ 40 mm, the rate was 51.1%. VAS low back pain was significantly correlated with Cobb angle (0.140, p = 0.003), PI-LL (0.147, p = 0.002) and SVA (0.097, p = 0.04). ODI was significantly correlated with C7PL-CSVL (0.126, p = 0.008), PT (0.129, p = 0.007) and SVA (0.249, p < 0.001). Conclusion: The comorbidity prevalence of DLS in patients with DLSS is 26.2%, which increases from 12.0% to 35.6% with age from 45 to 75 years older. Generally, the sagittal imbalance deteriorates along with the aggravating coronal deformity, which reminds us of the three-dimensional characteristic in spinal deformity. During the management of DLSS patients combined with DLS, the evaluation of sagittal balance should also be noticed. Preoperative sagittal and coronal imbalance is significantly associated with patient's clinical symptom. In view of the high comorbidity rate of DLS in elderly patients with DLSS and its close relationship with preoperative clinical symptom, surgical planning should be prudent and comprehensive.

## P0323: Elaboration of predictive algorithms to help decison-making in lumbar degenerative pathologies: categorical variables

Igor Barreira

1

, Luiz Pimenta

2

, Murilo Daher

3

, Daniel Arnoni

4

, Fernando Melo

5

, Raphael Pratali

6

, Ricardo Acacio

7

, Carlos Romeiro

8

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Thiego Pedro

9

, Flavio Porto

10

, Alexandry Carvalho

11

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

12

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

2

Atec Spine, San Diego, United States

3

Centro de Reabilitação e Readaptação Dr. Henrique Santillo (CRER), Goias, Brazil

4

Daniel Arnoni, São Paulo, Brazil

5

Centro Integrado Neurologia de Campinas, São Paulo, Brazil

6

Hospital do Servidor Público Estadual, São Paulo, Brazil

7

Hospital Infantil de Joinville, Santa Catarina, Brazil

8

Instituto de Coluna e Ortopedia de Recife (InCore), Recife, Brazil

9

Hospital Sírio Libanês, Brasília, Brazil

10

Hospital Regional de Presidente Prudente, Presidente Prudente, Brazil

11

Ortomed, Teresina, Brazil

12

Universidade de São Paulo (USP), Faculdade de Medicina de Ribeirão Preto, São Paulo, Brazil

Background: The ability to predict categorical outcomes, such as the need for intensive care or significant clinical improvement, is crucial for managing patient expectations and resources in degenerative lumbar spine surgery. This study developed machine learning models to predict key binary and multinomial outcomes in a Brazilian population. Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted using the Brazilian Spine Study Group database on adult patients undergoing surgery for degenerative lumbar conditions. A suite of classification models, including Logistic Regression, SVM, XGBoost, and Neural Networks, was developed to predict: hospital stay > 2 days, need for ICU admission, and significant clinical improvement based on the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) at 1-3 and 6-12 months. Models were evaluated using balanced accuracy, F1-score, and Area Under the Curve (ROC). Results: For predicting hospital stay > 2 days, the Logistic Elastic Net model demonstrated the best overall performance. The most important predictor was the amount of blood loss. For ICU admission, a Rule-Based model was superior, with blood loss and surgical time being the most influential variables. For ODI improvement at 1-3 months, a Simple Tree model performed best, with the baseline ODI score and patient age as key predictors. For ODI improvement at 6-12 months, the LGBM model was the most accurate. Conclusion: Machine learning algorithms can effectively predict important categorical outcomes after lumbar spine surgery. Different models excelled at predicting specific outcomes, highlighting the importance of selecting the appropriate algorithm for each clinical question.

## P0324: GLP-1 receptor agonist use reduces risk of pseudoarthrosis in non-diabetic patients undergoing spinal fusion: a propensity-matched comparative cohort study

Hanna von Riegen

1

, Alireza Mirahmadi

1

, Peter Ahorukomeye

2

, Jad Lawand

3

, Kaveh Momenzadeh

2

, Ara Nazarian

1

, Jason Pittman

1

, Sapan Gandhi

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Harvard Medical School in Boston, Boston, United States

2

Harvard Combined Orthopedic Residency Program, Harvard Medical School, Boston, United States

3

UT Medical Branch Galveston, Galveston, TX, USA, Galvestone, United States

Introduction: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) have emerged as effective treatments for obesity in non-diabetic populations. While obesity is associated with impaired bone healing and higher complication rates following spinal fusion, it remains unknown whether GLP-1 RA therapy improves postoperative outcomes in this group. This study evaluated the impact of perioperative GLP-1 receptor agonist (RA) use on one-year outcomes following cervical and lumbar spinal fusion in non-diabetic patients. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study using the TriNetX US Collaborative Network. Adult obese patients undergoing cervical or lumbar spinal fusion between 2004 and 2024 were identified. Diabetic patients were excluded. The treatment group included patients prescribed GLP-1 RAs within 6 months before or after surgery (N = 1,084), and the control group included those with no GLP-1 exposure (N = 171,151). After 1:1 propensity score matching, 851 patients remained in each group; subgroup analyses included 377 cervical and 447 lumbar fusion pairs. The primary outcome was diagnosis of pseudoarthrosis at one year. Secondary outcomes included mechanical loosening, revision surgery, wound complications, infection, and readmission. Results: GLP-1 RA use was associated with a significantly lower rate of pseudoarthrosis at one year in the pooled cohort (10.0% vs. 16.0%; OR, 0.586; p = 0.001), consistent across cervical (8.2% vs. 17.0%; OR, 0.486; p < 0.001) and lumbar (11.1% vs. 16.0%; OR, 0.657; p = 0.045) subgroups. Kaplan-Meier analysis confirmed improved pseudoarthrosis-free survival (p < 0.05 for all). Readmission rates were lower in cervical patients (9.5% vs. 14.9%; p = 0.026). ED visits (22.1% vs. 17.4%; p = 0.015) and infection (3.5% vs. 2.1%; p = 0.035) were more frequent in GLP-1 users. Conclusion: In obese, non-diabetic patients undergoing spinal fusion, perioperative GLP-1 RA use was associated with significantly lower pseudoarthrosis rates. These benefits were observed despite persistent obesity, suggesting bone- or healing-related effects independent of weight loss.

## ID: AGS26/635

## P0325: Unilateral biportal endoscopy versus posterior lumbar interbody fusion for single-level lumbar spinal stenosis with Grade I spondylolisthesis: a retrospective cohort study

Hu Dong

1

1

Beijing Tsinghua Changgung Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: The optimal surgical strategy for lumbar spinal stenosis with Grade I spondylolisthesis remains debated, particularly regarding decompression alone versus decompression with fusion. Unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) allows adequate decompression while preserving motion, but its safety and efficacy in patients with Grade I spondylolisthesis-specifically, whether it may exacerbate instability-are uncertain. This study aimed to compare perioperative outcomes, clinical efficacy, and radiographic results between UBE decompression and fusion surgery. Material and Methods: A total of 168 patients with single-level lumbar spinal stenosis and Grade I degenerative spondylolisthesis treated between June 2021 and August 2024 were retrospectively reviewed. They were divided into a UBE group (n = 66) and a fusion group (n = 102). Perioperative parameters were recorded. Clinical outcomes were assessed using Visual Analog Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) scores. Radiographic evaluations included lumbar MRI to measure central sagittal diameter and cross-sectional area (CSA) of the spinal canal, and dynamic lumbar radiographs to assess spondylolisthesis percentage and intervertebral angle changes. Stability was defined as <3% increase in slippage and < 5° angular change. Complications were documented. Results: Preoperative baseline characteristics showed no significant differences. The UBE group had significantly less intraoperative blood loss (45.8 ± 20.4 mL vs. 120.5 ± 45.1 mL, p < 0.01) and shorter postoperative hospital stay (2.5 ± 1.3 days vs. 6.2 ± 2.1 days, p < 0.01). Operation time was shorter in the UBE group (108.7 ± 28.9 min vs. 142.3 ± 25.6 min, p < 0.05). At 12 months postoperatively, both groups showed significant improvements in VAS, ODI, and JOA scores (p < 0.01), with no significant differences between groups. Radiographic parameters improved significantly in both groups, with no intergroup differences. All UBE patients met stability criteria at final follow-up, with no progression of spondylolisthesis or need for revision fusion. Complications in the fusion group included 2 dural tears and 1 surgical site infection; the UBE group had 1 minor intraoperative dural tear (managed conservatively with good recovery) and no nerve root injuries. Conclusion: Both UBE decompression and fusion surgery yield excellent clinical and radiographic outcomes in patients with single-level lumbar spinal stenosis and Grade I spondylolisthesis. UBE offers advantages including less bleeding, shorter hospitalization, and faster recovery, while avoiding internal fixation and fusion. This study demonstrates that in selected patients with Grade I spondylolisthesis, UBE did not lead to radiographic instability or progression at 1-year follow-up. Larger studies with longer follow-up are needed to confirm long-term stability.

## ID: AGS26/650

## P0326: The association between low virulence organisms in lumbar spine and intervertebral disc structural failure a prospectice study

Xiaolong Chen

1

, Shibao Lu

1

1

Xuanwu Hospital Capital Medical University, Beijing, China

Introduction: Many factors may trigger intervertebral disc (IVD) structural failure (intervertebral disc degeneration (IDD) and endplate changes), including inflammation, infection, dysbiosis, and the downstream effects of chemical factors. Of these, microbiome dysbiosis has been considered as one of the potential reasons for disc structural failure. The exact relationships between microbial colonisation and IVD structural failure are not well understood. This prospective study aimed to investigate the impact of microbial colonisation and its location (such as skin, IVD, muscle, soft tissues, and blood) on IVD structural failure and corresponding low back pain (LBP) if any. Material and Methods: First, we searched four online databases for potential studies. The potential relationships between microbial colonisation in different sample sources (such as skin, IVD, muscle, soft tissues, and blood) and IDD and endplate change were considered as primary outcomes. Odds ratio (OR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) for direct comparisons were reported. Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) scale was used to assess the quality of evidence. Second, this study recruited 66 patients who underwent lumbar discectomy surgery for testing of gut microbiota, fecal metabolites, and IVD. 16S rRNA gene sequencing was used to detect the composition of gut bacteria, while liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was used to analyze metabolites in feces and intervertebral discs. Results: Twenty-five cohort studies met the selection criteria. Overall pooled prevalence of microbial colonisation in 2419 patients with LBP was 33.2% (23.6%-43.6). The pooled prevalence of microbial colonisation in 2901 samples was 29.6% (21.0%-38.9%). Compared with the patients without endplate change, the patients with endplate changes had higher rates of microbial colonisation of disc (OR = 2.83; 95% CI = 1.93-4.14; I2 = 37.6%; p = 0.108). The primary pathogen was Cutibacterium acnes which was present in 22.2% of cases (95% CI = 13.3%-32.5%; I2 = 96.6%; p = 0.000). 16S rRNA gene sequencing analysis revealed significant changes in the alpha and beta diversity of gut microbiota in patients with endplate alterations. Endplate degeneration group (Modic II type) is enriched with tumor bacteria. Metabolomics analysis revealed significant changes in tissue metabolites such as branched chain amino acid (BCAA) levels and their corresponding signaling pathways. Conclusion: This study found low-quality grade evidence for an association between microbial colonisation of disc with endplate changes. The primary pathogen was Cutibacterium acnes. Due to lack of enough high-quality studies and methodological limitations of this review, further studies are required to improve our understanding of the potential relationships and mechanisms of microbiota and IVD structural failure.

## P0327: Physician-artificial intelligence comparison in the radiological interpretation of spondylolisthesis: a retrospective single center study

Ahmet Hamdi Olçar

, İsmail Tüter

, Osman Yüksel Yavuz

Sancaktepe Prof. Dr. Ilhan Varank Training and Research Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Spondylolisthesis is a common clinical condition in orthopedic practice that can be difficult to diagnose. Artificial intelligence is expected to assist in the diagnosis and treatment process. The evaluation of X-rays by orthopedic specialists and artificial intelligence, and the integration of artificial intelligence into the diagnosis and treatment process in clinical practice are planned.
