## P0328: Association between MRI changes of degenerative lumbar spine and Oswestry Disability Index in patients with chronic low back pain: a cross-sectional study

Dipak Shrestha

1

, Jagadish Thapa

1

, Bikash Parajuli

1

1

Dhulikhel Hospital, Kathmandu University Hospital, Dhulikhel, Nepal

Introduction: Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, such as disc degeneration, facet joint osteoarthritis, and spinal stenosis, are commonly observed on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) in patients with chronic low back pain (CLBP). While some studies suggest a link between MRI-detected degenerative changes and higher Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores and Numerical Pain Rating Scale (NPRS), others report weak or inconsistent associations. Understanding this relationship is crucial for clinical decision-making in patients with CLBP. This cross-sectional observational study aims to evaluate the correlation between MRI-identified degenerative changes of the lumbar spine, e.g., lumbar disc degeneration (LDD), lumbar disc herniation (LDH), facet joint arthritis (FJA), and Schmorl’s nodes, and ODI in patients with CLBP. Material and Methods: This cross-sectional study included 100 participants (mean age: 39.9 ± 10.82, Male: Female = 51:49) with chronic low back pain and had MRI analysis of 300 discs at the level of L3/L4, L4/L5, and L5/S1. These patients completed questionnaires for the Oswestry Disability Index and the Numerical Pain Rating Scale to assess back pain. The ODI was categorized into two groups for logistic regression analysis to identify predictors among demographic features and MRI findings of degenerative changes for disability. Results: The most common finding was disc herniation (63%), followed by disc degeneration (Pfirmann's grade ≥ 3) (47.67%), foraminal stenosis (36%), facet joint arthritis (32%), lumbar canal stenosis (19.67%), and Schmorl’s nodes (10.33%). Facet joint arthritis at L5/S1 significantly correlated with ODI (ρ = 0.355, p < 0.001), as did the Schmorl’s nodes (ρ = 0.297, p = 0.003). Severity of disc herniation at the L5/S1 level correlated with both higher NPRS scores (R = 0.260, p = 0.009) and higher ODI scores (R = 0.211, p = 0.035). In contrast, no significant correlation was between MRI degenerative findings at L3/L4 and L4/L5 levels with respect to ODI scores and NPRS scores. Logistic regression analysis showed age (p = 0.02, OR:0.94, CI: 0.89-0.99), female gender, (p = 0.001, OR:6.09, CI: 2.06-17.94) presence of Schmorl's nodes in L5/S1 (p = 0.03, OR:9.60, CI: 1.28-71.54), and L5/S1 facet joint arthritis (p = 0.001, OR:3.38, CI: 1.6-7.15) significantly predicted the ODI. Conclusion: Facet joint arthritis, severity of disc herniation and Schmorl’s nodes at L5/S1 significantly correlated with ODI. Severity of disc herniation at the L5/S1 also correlated with NPRS. Though disc herniation and disc degeneration are the most frequently observed MRI findings in patients with CLBP, they do not correlate with ODI. Age, female gender, the presence of Schmorl's nodes at L5/S1, and L5/S1 facet joint arthritis are the significant predictors of disability due to CLBP.

## ID: AGS26/690

## P0329: Interspinous process device as a screwless fusion technique for lumbar spondylolisthesis

Mei-Cheng Hsiao

1

, Meng-Yin Yang

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Neurological Institute, Taichung Veterans General Hospital, Taichung, Taiwan

Introduction: Interspinous process devices (IPD) have been developed as an alternative to pedicle screw constructs for lumbar stabilization. By anchoring between spinous processes, they provide segmental rigidity while preserving the facet joints, thereby potentially lowering the incidence of adjacent segment disease. When combined with posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) or transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF), or used as supplemental fixation after anterior or lateral interbody fusion, IPDs offer a less invasive option for selected patients. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 50 patients with degenerative lumbar pathology who underwent PLIF or TLIF procedures with adjunctive IPD implantation. Postoperative fusion status, clinical improvement, and perioperative parameters were evaluated. Radiographic evidence of fusion was confirmed in 47 patients, corresponding to a 94% success rate, which parallels outcomes commonly reported with pedicle screw fixation. Results: Implantation of IPDs achieved stable fixation and facilitated solid arthrodesis. Patient-reported outcome measures demonstrated meaningful improvement, although restoration of lateral bending was limited. The technique was associated with shorter operative time, reduced intraoperative blood loss, and decreased fluoroscopic exposure compared with conventional pedicle screw instrumentation. Conclusion: IPD-assisted lumbar fusion appears to be a reliable alternative to screw-based fixation in well-selected cases. The procedure is technically straightforward, requires a relatively short learning curve, and reduces surgical invasiveness without compromising fusion rates. Optimal results depend on proper device placement and sufficient bone quality. In one- to two-level fusions, particularly when combined with anterior column support, IPDs can achieve outcomes comparable to traditional pedicle screw systems.

## ID: AGS26/694

## P0330: Influence on physical activities of short segmental lumbar surgery

Ryota Kimura

1

, Michio Hongo

1

, Yuji Kasukawa

1

, Daisuke Kudo

1

, Yuichi Ono

1

, Naohisa Miyakoshi

1

1

Akita University Graduate School of Medicine, Akita, Japan

Introduction: Lumbar short-interbody fusion surgery is reported to improve quality of life (QOL) without causing limitations in activities of daily living (ADL). However, few reports exist on postoperative return to physical activities, including sports. Furthermore, comparisons between pre- and postoperative states, as well as between fusion and decompression procedures, are lacking. We compared exercise habits and QOL before and after lumbar short-interbody fusion and decompression surgery. Material and Methods: Self-administered questionnaires were completed postoperatively for degenerative disease after lumbar short-level (1-2 levels) surgery. The study included 103 responding patients (54 in the fusion group, 49 in the decompression group). For exercise habits, physical activity intensity was classified into three levels using METs (light: 0-2.9, moderate: 3.0-5.9, vigorous: 6.0 or higher). The maximum activity intensity performed during the year before surgery and the year after surgery was investigated. Additionally, RDQ and SF-36 summary scores (PCS, MCS, RCS) were assessed preoperatively and one year postoperatively. Statistical analysis was performed, with p < 0.05 considered significant. Results: No significant differences were observed between groups for age, gender, or number of operated intervertebral levels. Preoperative scores for all evaluation items also showed no significant differences between groups. Exercise habits showed a slight tendency toward decrease compared to preoperative levels, but no significant difference was found (Fusion: light 35%→32%, moderate 52%→59%, vigorous 13%→9%; Decompression: light 39%→43%, moderate 37%→39%, vigorous 24%→18%). Cases showing an increase in METs postoperatively were also observed in both groups. Weight training, Judo, and other activities clearly placing high loads on the spine were not performed postoperatively. QOL showed significant improvement in both groups for RDQ (Fusion: 10.8→6.8, Decompression: 11.0→6.8), PCS (Fusion: 26→34, Decompression: 26→32), and MCS (Fusion: 51→54, Decompression: 47→54). RCS improved significantly only in the decompression group (35→41). No significant differences were observed between groups postoperatively. Conclusion: Lumbar short-interbody fusion surgery improved QOL without changing exercise habits. Patients in the fusion group also performed exercise equivalent to the decompression group postoperatively, indicating no physical activity restrictions due to fusion. However, since some sports may become impossible postoperatively, suggesting modifications to exercise content can provide useful information for maintaining motor function and improving QOL.

## ID: AGS26/709

## P0331: Dynamic screw-based instrumentation with non-parallel screws trajectory angulation

Feras Rawagah

1,2,3

, Gonzalo Moros

2

, Thomas Marx

2

, Ehab Shiban

2

1

Mutah Universitz, Karak, Jordan

2

Medizinische Universität Lausitz - Carl Thiem, Cottbus, Germany

3

Arbeitsgemeinschaft für Osteosynthesefragen (AO Spine), Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: The Dynamic screws fixation system (COSMIC, Ulrich medical, Ulm Germany) allows for 8 degrees flexion-extension only. Initial recommendation for screws insertion was to insert the screws always parallel. However, parallel insertion is often not feasible due to anatomical and technical limits. We aim in this study to check the outcome of doing the COSMIC dynamic fixation with non-parallel screw trajectory. Material and Methods: A Prospective observational study of patients undergoing one segment dynamic screw fixation between July 2024 to August 2025 was performed. We measured by computerized tomography the angle between the right and left screws in each instrumented vertebra. The Inter-Screws Angle Difference (ISAD) was defined as the absolute difference in this angle between the upper instrumented vertebra and the lower instrumented vertebra. ISAD value of zero indicates parallel screws placement, and the higher the value the less parallel the screws are. We recorded the complications, the postoperative course, and the time to ambulation. Results: 23 Patients were analyzed. Patients were 9 males and 14 females, age 25-87 years (average 64.9 years). All patients had degenerative lumbar diseases with symptoms including radiculopathy, claudication, or axial pain. Operated Level L3-L4 (1 patient), L4-L5 (17 patients), L5-S1 (5 patients). Spondylolisthesis preoperatively ranged from 2.4 mm retrolisthesis to 9.8 mm anterolisthesis with an average of 4.5 mm. All patients except 4 underwent some sort of decompression ranging from foraminotomy to full laminectomy. 17 patients had open surgery, and 6 patients had minimally invasive techniques. ISAD values ranged from (0.6 degrees) to (24.4 degrees) with an average of (7.2 degrees) and standard deviation of (5.6 degrees). Time to ambulation ranged from 1 day to 5 days with an average of 1.7 days. Complications: 1 screw misplacement, 2 unintentional durotomies, 1 postoperative hemorrhage, 1 wound leak, 2 needed reoperation for further decompression, 1 wound healing issue. Apart from the one screw misplacement, presumably none of the mentioned complication may be related to the angle of screw placement. The correlation coefficient between the ISAD and the time to ambulation was 0.370 ± 0.397 with a P-value 0.083, which implies insignificance. Conclusion: Insertion of dynamic screws non-parallel with various angles is practical with no recorded adverse results at short term follow up. This makes the screws insertion more considering for the anatomy and presumably reduces the complications related to screw insertion as trying to force the screw trajectory to be parallel against the anatomical consideration presumably leads to more pedicular breach. Further study is needed for the long-term results of those patients after adequate follow-up time elapses.

## ID: AGS26/745

## P0332: Lower social economic status impacts early recovery but not long-term outcomes after sacroilliac joint fusion

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Krupa Patel

1

, Kirsten Mansfield

1

, Aayush Unadkat

1

, Elias Schwartz

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Sacroiliac joint (SI-J) fusion procedures continue to increase as diagnostic and treatment approaches gain broader acceptance. While socioeconomic disparities are known to influence surgical outcomes across spine procedures, their impact on SI-J fusion recovery remains poorly characterized. The Area Deprivation Index (ADI), a validated composite measure of neighborhood socioeconomic disadvantage, provides a quantifiable approach to investigating this relationship. We examined how socioeconomic status affects recovery trajectories following SI-J fusion, focusing on patient-reported outcomes and satisfaction rates. Material and Methods: A retrospective review identified 135 isolated SI-J fusions performed at a single tertiary center between 2016 and 2024. Exclusion criteria included fusions done as part of complex lumbosacral surgeries, trauma and insufficiency. State-decile ADI scores classified patients into low (1-3), medium (4-7), or high (8-10) deprivation cohorts. PROMIS-Physical Function (PROMIS-PF) and PROMIS-Pain Interference, were collected pre-operatively and at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 12 months, and 24 months when available. Binary satisfaction data was collected at 1 year follow-up. Two-year outcomes were limited due to the conclusion of care for most patients by one year. Normally distributed variables were compared with t-tests or one-way ANOVA; non-normal data with Mann-Whitney U or Kruskal-Wallis tests; categorical data with χ2 or Fisher’s exact tests (α = 0.05). Caaes were excluded on a pair-wise basis. Results: A total of 135 isolated SI-joint fusions were analysed (low-ADI = 50, medium = 64, high = 21). High-ADI patients were younger than both medium- and low-ADI cohorts (45.8 ± 12.8 yr vs 54.9 ± 12.7 yr and 61.0 ± 11.6 yr, respectively; p < 0.001) and were more frequently female (86 % vs 56 % vs 38 %; p = 0.001). Body-mass index, smoking status, osteoporosis/osteopenia, and other baseline comorbidities were comparable across groups; diabetes differed only modestly (0 % high vs 23 % medium vs 16 % low; p = 0.045). Baseline PROMIS-Pain was higher in the medium/high strata than in the low-ADI group (median 68-70 vs 67; p = 0.042), while pre-operative PROMIS-PF was similar (p = 0.436). At 2 weeks, pain remained greater in medium/high ADI patients (median 75) than in low ADI (64; p = 0.030); PROMIS-PF did not differ (p = 0.505). By 6 weeks-and at 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years-no statistically significant differences were detected in pain or function (all p ≥ 0.07).Satisfaction at last follow-up was 77.6 % in low-ADI, 85.9 % in medium-ADI, and 61.9---% in high-ADI patients, demonstrating a non-significant trend toward lower satisfaction with greater deprivation (p = 0.060). Post-operative complication rates (15 %) and subsequent lumbosacral surgery rates (30 %) were not associated with ADI stratum (p > 0.1 for both). Conclusion: Patients from areas of higher socioeconomic deprivation are younger and worse off at time of surgery and show poorer early postoperative outcomes following SI-J fusion, as reflected in higher pain levels. However, these disparities dissipate by as early as 3 months out, with long-term outcomes, complications and satisfaction rates remaining comparable across socioeconomic strata. Notably, a nonsignificant trend toward lower satisfaction persists in the most deprived cohort. These findings support offering SI-J fusion surgery regardless of socioeconomic status, while emphasizing the potential benefit of tailored early postoperative support and careful monitoring of long term outcomes for patients from higher deprivation areas.

## ID: AGS26/752

## P0333: Misdiagnosis between coccygeal dislocation fracture and lumbosacral disc herniation

Gilberto Gardea Ramirez

1

, Gilberto Gardea Loera

2

1

Unidad Médica de Alta Especialidad (UMAE) Hospital de Especialidades No. 1, Centro Médico Nacional del Bajío (CMNB), Leon Guanajuato, Mexico

2

Hospital Vistas Del Sol, Delicias Chihuahua, Mexico

Introduction: Coccydynia, first described by Simpson in 1859, is incapacitating pain in the coccyx that is usually caused by sitting or moving from a sitting to a standing position. This pain in the coccyx can radiate to the sacrum or lumbar spine or laterally to the buttocks. In rare cases, patients may experience rectal pain or associated radicular symptoms. One-third of patients have associated back pain, which contributes to misdiagnosis. Due to a lack of awareness of this condition among spine specialists, many patients may suffer for years without adequate treatment. Diagnosis is based on history and physical examination, supplemented by radiological imaging findings. Coccydynia is a common cause of pain that is often misdiagnosed. Coccydynia is usually caused by a coccygeal fracture-dislocation and causes symptoms that resemble lumbosacral pathology. Many patients undergo surgery for lumbosacral pathology without improvement. Diagnosis is straightforward, as all patients experience pain on digital palpation of the coccyx, ideally with the index finger, and most show coccyx instability on dynamic X-rays. Surgery is simple, and all patients experience pain relief. The aim of this study is to show how poor examination and overestimation of lumbosacral data can confuse a coccyx disease, as well as to identify patients diagnosed with lumbosacral pathology who will improve with coccyx surgery. Material and Methods: Forty patients, 36 women and 4 men, with an average age of 41.75 years, underwent coccygectomy. All patients suffered from coccydynia diagnosed by coccygeal palpation and dynamic radiographs: the patient standing and a lateral radiograph taken, then the patient sitting with the back in moderate extension. The duration of symptoms and signs ranged from 0.5 to 96 months, with a mean of 24.3. The causes were idiopathic in 21 patients, postpartum in 6, and trauma in 13. All patients presented with coccydynia, 30 reported lumbar pain radiating to the pelvic limbs, 13 were diagnosed with lumbar pathology, 10 patients had undergone spinal surgery without improvement, and in 6 we performed coccygectomy and spinal surgery in a single procedure. Results: The results 15 days after surgery on the VAS scale showed a significant change from a preoperative score of 9.63 to 1.6, with a p < 0.001. Likewise, 4 months later there was an additional improvement on the VAS scale from 1.6 to 0.50. On the MACNAB scale, the results were excellent in 35 patients, good in 3, fair in 1, and poor in 1. There were reports of infection, which improved completely with antibiotics and daily wound cleaning in 3 patients; 9 had superficial and partial wound dehiscence. Conclusion: This study presents 40 cases, of which 32.5% were confused with lumbosacral pathology, and of these, 25% underwent lumbosacral spine surgery despite having coccygeal pathology. It has been forgotten that a coccyx disease can produce symptoms of low back pain or lumbar sciatica. To improve this confusion, two components can make a difference: a) Clinical examination, finding, above all, pain caused by digital palpation of the coccyx, as a pathognomonic sign of the disease, in our opinion. b) Dynamic X-rays of the coccyx: lateral standing and lateral sitting. The data from this study show a statistically significant reduction (p < 0.001) in pain reduction on the VAS scale (Delta -8.03). Coccygeal fracture-dislocation is a cause of pain that is confused with lumbosacral pathology and is rarely diagnosed if there is no history of direct trauma to the coccyx. Many patients undergo lumbosacral spine surgery, most for herniated discs or some for spondylolisthesis, even on several occasions, and obviously do not improve because the pathology is coccygeal. Therefore, as this study shows, in cases of low back pain or lumbar sciatica, it is advisable to examine the coccyx, both clinically, by provoking pain on coccygeal palpation, and by dynamic X-rays, since in most cases these show instability of the coccyx. Surgery significantly improves patients' condition, as shown by the MACNAB scale and 8 to 9 points on the visual analog scale (VAS). Although it is a small sample of patients, this study seeks to emphasize the clinical importance of making a differential diagnosis in lumbosacral pathology and coccygeal pathology. Other authors have reported coccyx surgeries, especially in traumatic pathology or coccygectomies in pelvic tumors, sacrum, or metastases. Others have reported coccyx surgery to prevent or improve bedsores, but there are few studies that make a differential diagnosis between spinal pathology and coccygeal pathology. The authors of this study suggest examining the coccyx in all patients with lumbosacral pathology.

## ID: AGS26/790

## P0334: Lumbar disc arthroplasty versus anterior lumbar interbody fusion: a propensity-matched cohort analysis

Jason Silvestre

1

, Robert Ferdon

2

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1
Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States, 
2
University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States
Introduction: There is a paucity of long-term data investigating the purported benefits of lumbar disc arthroplasty (LDA). This study aims to compare the short- and long-term complication profiles following LDA and anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF). Materials and Methods: A propensity-matched retrospective cohort study was conducted on a multi-institutional electronic health record database for patients undergoing single-level LDA or ALIF. Exclusion criteria included prior lumbar surgery, <18 years old, and a history of spine neoplasms, trauma, inflammatory, or infectious bone conditions. Patients at a mean age of 41 years (n = 1,156) were evenly divided into the LDA and ALIF cohorts (n = 578). Comparisons were made with independent sample t-tests. Results: At thirty days, there were similar rates of medical complications between patients undergoing single-level LDA and ALIF. Patients undergoing ALIF had higher odds of emergency room admissions at thirty days compared to LDA (OR: 2.6, 95% CI: 1.3-4.9, p = 0.004). At five-years, patients undergoing single-level ALIF demonstrated a higher risk of pseudoarthrosis (OR: 3.3, 95% CI: 2.3-4.9; p < 0.001) and subsequent lumbar spine fusion surgery (OR: 3.1, 95% CI: 1.7-5.6; p < 0.001) than patients undergoing single-level LDA. There were similar rates of adjacent segment disease at five-years between the two cohorts (OR: 1.4, 95% CI: 1.0-2.0; p = 0.088). Conclusion: LDA was associated with lower rates of pseudoarthrosis and subsequent rates of reoperation at five years when compared to ALIF. Future prospective studies are warranted to confirm these associations.

## ID: AGS26/813

## P0335: Therapeutic effect of Biocage on single-level lumbar degenerative diseases in non-fusion high-risk patients - A prospective controlled study based on at least two years of follow-up

Wenjie Wu

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: To evaluate the clinical application of biocage in single lumbar degenerative diseases and to observe the value of biocage in lumbar fusion. Material and Methods: A prospective, non-randomized controlled study was conducted in 183 patients with single-segment degenerative lumbar spine disease diagnosed before operation from January 2013 to December 2016. They were divided into Bio according to the choice of fusion cage according to the high risk factors of non-fusion (osteoporosis, age > 65 years old, smoking > 20 branches per day and > 5 years, diabetes mellitus). The cage group (102 cases) and Peek group (81 cases) were followed up for 24-48 months, with an average of 36 months. The operation time, intraoperative bleeding volume, hospitalization time, intervertebral space height, intervertebral foramen area, fusion time, fusion rate, failure rate of internal fixation, VAS score and ODI index were compared between two different fusion cages. Results: All 183 patients successfully completed the operation. There were no significant differences in gender, age, clinical diagnosis, lesion segment, operation time, bleeding volume, VAS score and ODI index between the two groups. Although there was no significant difference in fusion rate and fusion time, the fusion rate of Biocage group was higher than that of PPEK group at the same time point, and the fusion time was shorter. During follow-up, the height of intervertebral space in Biocage group recovered significantly compared with that in PPEK group (p < 0.05), and the height and width of intervertebral foramen recovered significantly different between the two groups. The recovery effect of Biocage group was better than that of PPEK group. Conclusion: Biocage has good clinical efficacy and safety in patients with non-fusion, high-risk, single-level lumbar degenerative diseases. However, for patients with high risk factors such as osteoporosis, there is still a risk of non-fusion, and further research is needed to analyze the impact of different factors on bone fusion.

## ID: AGS26/848

## P0336: Predicting clinically important difference in lumbar decompression surgery: the influence of demographic, clinical and radiographic characteristics

Ayham Yasein

1

, Sanam Tavakkoli Oskouei

2

, Aaron Buckland

2

1

Faculty of Medicine, Nursing and Health Sciences, Monash University, Spine & Scoliosis Research Associates Australia, Melbourne, Australia

2

Spine and Scoliosis Research Associates Australia, Windsor, Australia

Introduction: Lumbar decompression is a common intervention for spinal stenosis and disc herniation, yet many patients fail to achieve a Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID) in disability. Identifying predictors of MCID may optimize patient selection and improve surgical outcomes. The purpose of this study is to identify demographic, clinical, and radiographic predictors of MCID achievement one year after lumbar decompression, and to develop a preoperative risk stratification tool. Material and Methods: This was a Prospective cohort study using a single-center spine registry. 190 patients undergoing 1-4 level lumbar decompression (laminectomy, microdiscectomy, or laminotomy) from 2020-2023. Outcome Measures were Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) improvement meeting MCID, defined as ≥ 12.8-point improvement or ≥ 50% improvement if baseline ODI ≤ 26, per validated thresholds. Patients were grouped by MCID status (MCID+ vs. MCID-). Comparative statistics, ROC analysis, and stepwise logistic regression were used to identify independent preoperative predictors. Results: Mean age was 60.2 years; 64% were male. At one year, mean ODI improved from 41.9 to 16.3 (p < 0.001). Predictors of successful outcomes (MCID+) included: Demographic (age < 62.2yrs, CCI < 1.5, Clinical (symptom duration < 6 months, ODI > 41%) and Radiographic (segmental lordosis > 14°, pelvic tilt < 20.9°, pelvic incidence < 54°). Symptom duration < 6 months was the strongest individual predictor of meeting MCID+. A 3-point risk score was developed using the three strongest independent predictors: symptom duration < 6 months, ODI > 41, and pelvic tilt < 20.9°. MCID achievement ranged from 33% (0 predictors) to 92% (3 predictors). Conclusion: Shorter symptom duration, greater baseline disability, and favorable pelvic alignment were independently associated with MCID achievement. The derived 3-point risk score is a practical tool for individualized preoperative counseling and surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/857

## P0337: Multimodal intervention targeting sarcopenia-osteoporosis complex improves functional outcomes and reduces complications in elderly patients undergoing spinal surgery: a prospective propensity-matched cohort study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Qian Cheng

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

1

The People’s Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: The coexistence of sarcopenia and osteoporosis in elderly patients undergoing spinal surgery is associated with increased postoperative disability and complications. However, evidence-based, resource-efficient perioperative management strategies remain scarce, particularly in resource-limited settings. To evaluate the efficacy of a multimodal intervention (nutrition, exercise, and pharmacotherapy) targeting the muscle-bone axis in improving functional recovery and reducing complications in sarcopenic-osteoporotic patients undergoing spinal surgery. Material and Methods: A prospective propensity-matched cohort study was conducted from January 2023 to December 2024. A total of 160 patients aged ≥50 years with sarcopenia (EWGSOP2 criteria) and osteoporosis (T-score ≤ -2.5) undergoing lumbar/thoracic surgery (TLIF/PLIF/vertebroplasty) were enrolled. After propensity score matching (1:1 for age, sex, diabetes, fracture type), 80 patients were allocated to the intervention group and 80 to the control group. • Intervention group: Received whey protein (30 g/day), vitamin D3 (2000 IU/day), calcium (1200 mg/day), structured elastic band resistance training (pre- and postoperatively), and denosumab or zoledronic acid. • Control group: Received routine care with calcium supplementation as needed. Primary outcomes included ODI success (≥ 30% improvement at 12 months) and fusion success (Bridwell grade I-II on CT). Secondary outcomes included VAS pain scores, instrumentation failure, and serum biomarkers (IL-6, IGF-1, CTX, PINP). Results: All 160 patients completed the 12-month follow-up. The intervention group showed significantly better outcomes: • ODI success: 58% (46/80) vs. 28% (22/80) in controls (aOR = 2.8, 95% CI: 1.5-5.3, p = 0.002). • Fusion success: 83% (66/80) vs. 65% (52/80) in controls (absolute increase 18%, NNT = 5.6, p = 0.01). • Complications: 7.5% (6/80) vs. 15.6% (12/80) in controls (HR = 0.48, 95% CI: 0.25-0.91, p = 0.02). • Biomarkers: Reduction in IL-6 correlated with ODI improvement (r = -0.41, p < 0.001). No serious adverse events related to the intervention were observed. Conclusions: A low-cost, multimodal intervention targeting sarcopenia and osteoporosis significantly improves functional recovery, fusion rates, and reduces complications in elderly spinal surgery patients. This protocol offers a scalable and practical model for resource-limited healthcare settings.
Keywords: Sarcopenia; Osteoporosis; Spinal Surgery; Multimodal Intervention; Functional Outcomes; Propensity-Matched Cohort

## ID: AGS26/864

## P0338: Modified MIS-TLIF technique in primary hospitals: clinical efficacy and cost-effectiveness analysis - A prospective cohort study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Li Huo

1

, Qian Cheng

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-TLIF) offers advantages such as reduced tissue injury and faster recovery in the treatment of lumbar degenerative diseases. However, its application in resource-limited primary hospitals remains challenging due to high equipment costs and a steep learning curve. This study introduces a modified MIS-TLIF technique using conventional instruments combined with a low-cost access system and evaluates its clinical value in a county-level hospital setting. To evaluate the clinical efficacy, safety, and cost-effectiveness of the modified MIS-TLIF technique for single-level lumbar degenerative disease. Material and Methods: A prospective cohort study was conducted from January 2023 to December 2024, including 60 patients with single-level lumbar degenerative disease. Participants were divided into a modified MIS-TLIF group (n = 30) and an open TLIF group (n = 30). The modified technique featured: (1) conventional instruments with a low-cost tubular access system, (2) unilateral biportal endoscopy-assisted decompression, and (3) a simplified bone grafting protocol. Perioperative indicators, VAS, ODI, and JOA scores were recorded. Fusion was assessed using Brantigan criteria, and cost-effectiveness analysis was performed. Results: The modified group had longer operative time (142.3 ± 15.6 min vs. 118.7 ± 12.4 min, p < 0.05) but significantly less blood loss (145.6 ± 18.3 ml vs. 325.4 ± 26.8 ml, p < 0.01). Drain removal time (2.1 ± 0.6 days vs. 4.3 ± 0.9 days) and hospital stay (5.8 ± 1.2 days vs. 9.6 ± 2.1 days) were significantly shorter in the modified group (p < 0.01). At 12 months, ODI scores were superior in the modified group (18.3 ± 4.2 vs. 23.7 ± 5.1, p < 0.05), while VAS scores showed no significant difference. Fusion rates (93.3% vs. 90.0%) and complication rates (6.7% vs. 10.0%) were comparable. The modified technique also demonstrated lower direct surgical costs ($5,243 ± $423 vs. $6,385 ± $587, p < 0.05) and a better cost-effectiveness ratio (373.9 vs. 433.3). Conclusion: The modified MIS-TLIF technique is a safe, effective, and cost-efficient surgical option for single-level lumbar degenerative disease in resource-limited settings. Despite longer operative times, it offers advantages such as reduced blood loss, faster recovery, and shorter hospital stays, making it a feasible minimally invasive alternative in primary hospitals.
Keywords: minimally invasive TLIF, lumbar degenerative disease, county hospital, cost-effectiveness

## ID: AGS26/885

## P0339: Multilevel lumbar spine stenosis in patients with Parkinson’s disease

Arkadii Kazmin

1

, Vladimir Pereverzev

1

, Sergei Kolesov

1

1

N.N. Priorov National Medical Research Center, Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: Parkinson’s disease belongs to the group of neurodegenerative diseases and has still unknown causes. Although PD its clinical manifestations are largely related to the musculoskeletal system. In addition to poor bone quality and an increased risk of fractures, muscle dysfunction is another important clinical manifestation. Scoliosis, thoracic kyphosis, and other deformities are more common in patients with PD. Severe and involuntary forward flexion of the thoracolumbar spine is a disabling process in the course of PD. Material and Methods: Comparative prospective study was performed in 2013-2020 yy. in Priorov Central Institution. 36 patients with PD and lumbar spine stenosis were enrolled and divided in two groups according to type of surgery. The results of treatment of 36 patients with degenerative pathology of the lumbar spine and PD were analyzed. Spinal fusion and decompression surgery using transpedicular constructions was performed in 21 patients (group I), only decompression at the most significant level of stenosis was performed in 15 patients (group II). Evaluation criteria preOP: mean duration of PD, age, gender, prePO visual analog pain scale (VAS) for low back pain, comorbidities. PostOP evaluated: VAS, the presence/absence of complications, and X-ray control. Results: Clinical results were satisfactory in 2 patients in group I, the remaining patients noted dissatisfaction with surgical treatment. The mean postoperative VAS at the time of the last follow-up was higher than the mean before surgery; painkillers were taken by all patients after surgery. The mean postoperative VAS was 6.4, while the mean preoperative VAS was 5.9. (p = 0.19). All patients in this group continued to take levodopa. During the first 12 months after the operation, 17 out of 21 patients underwent revision surgery due to instability of the structure (n = 7) or fractures of the hardware elements (n = 10). In 24 months after the surgery complete removal of the instrumentation was performed in 8 patients in this group. Group II patients in the early postoperative period showed greater satisfaction with the surgical intervention. Thus, 12 patients were fully satisfied, despite the fact that VAS before surgery and after surgery was equivalent to the indicators of group I: 6.1 before surgery and 5.8 after surgery. Revision interventions were not performed in this group. Conclusion: The outcomes of surgical treatment of lumbar spine stenosis in patients with Parkinson’s disease (PD) are controversial and might be improved by minimization of surgical activity. Given the progressive natural course of Parkinson's disease, a poor prognosis for surgical treatment is inevitable. The mainstay of treatment for patients with PD should be medical or surgical treatment of PD itself, thereby also improving the outcome of spinal surgery. Minimization of surgical activity for patients with PD is the only possible treatment option for degenerative spinal pathology with severe clinical manifestations.

## ID: AGS26/904

## P0340: Indirect decompression by lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF): the impact of facet joint fluid

Fernando Melo Filho

1

, Luiz Pimenta

2

, Rodrigo Amaral

2

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

1

1
University of Sao Paulo, Ribeirao Preto, Brazil, 
2
Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil
Introduction: Lumbar spinal canal stenosis (LSCS) is a common degenerative condition, particularly in older adults, characterized by compression of neural and vascular elements and frequently leading to neurogenic claudication. Surgical treatment, such as lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF), has shown efficacy in achieving indirect decompression by restoring disc height and tensioning the posterior ligamentous complex. However, precise predictors of indirect decompression success remain elusive. A sparsely explored factor is T2 hyperintensity within the facet joints on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which may indicate segmental instability and greater potential for ligamentotaxis. This study investigates the correlation between facet joint fluid and the effectiveness of indirect decompression after LLIF. The study objective was to evaluate whether T2-weighted hyperintensity in lumbar facet joints on preoperative MRI can be considered an indicator of greater effectiveness of indirect decompression of the lumbar canal in patients undergoing LLIF. Material and Methods: We conducted a single-center, retrospective, comparative, nonrandomized study including 42 patients (mean age 52 years, 56% female) who underwent LLIF for lumbar stenosis between 2011 and 2024, totaling 38 operated levels (five patients had two levels treated, and one patient had two staged procedures). Inclusion criteria required preoperative MRI within six months and postoperative MRI between nine and twelve months, acquired on 1.5T or 3T scanners. Axial T2-weighted images were assessed for canal dimensions using a modified Schizas grading (A-D). The spinal canal area (CV) and vertebral body area (VB) were measured in arbitrary units in RadiAnt DICOM Viewer with a correction factor for standardization. Statistical analyses were performed in R (v4.2). Canal decompression across groups-fluid in both facets, in one facet, or absent-was compared with Welch’s t test with bootstrap. Changes in Schizas grades were evaluated with Fisher’s test. A generalized linear mixed-effects regression was used to explore the relationship between facet fluid and canal decompression. Results: Across 38 levels, the postoperative CV/VB ratio increased significantly (p < 0.001) compared with preoperative values: postoperative median 0.111 (IQR 0.084-0.157), mean 0.126 (SD 0.060); preoperative median 0.067 (IQR 0.037-0.095), mean 0.070 (SD 0.044). This represents an average increase of approximately 5% and a median increase of 4% in canal size. Operated levels were L2-L3 (18.4%), L3-L4 (34.2%), and L4-L5 (47.3%). Facet fluid was bilateral in 28.9%, unilateral in 23.6%, and absent in 47.3%. Improvement in Schizas grade from pre to postoperative was observed more frequently in cases with bilateral facet hyperintensity than with unilateral or absent hyperintensity. Nonetheless, multivariable linear regression did not show a significant impact of facet fluid on the delta change in canal decompression (p = 0.7 for two facets, p = 0.4 for no fluid, p = 0.3 for one facet). Conclusion: Bilateral T2 hyperintensity may serve as a clinical marker of greater effectiveness of LLIF-mediated indirect decompression of the lumbar canal, potentially refining patient selection and optimizing surgical outcomes. Limitations include the single-center design, modest sample size, and MRI quality variability, supporting the need for multicenter, standardized future studies.

## ID: AGS26/924

## P0341: Prognostic modeling of the long-term clinical outcome of minimally invasive lumbar interbody fusion based on complex clinical, radiological and neuroimaging analysis in railway workers

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2,3

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Yurii Pestryakov

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, Dmitriy Hozeev

1

, K. Daniel Riew

5,6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, United States

6

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, United States

Introduction: The incidence of unsatisfactory surgical outcomes in degenerative lumbosacral spine diseases varies depending on the surgical technique used and is difficult to predict due to heterogeneity of clinical and instrumental characteristics of patient groups. Development and use of predictive models of clinical outcomes can assist the surgeon in decision making and improve surgical outcomes. The aim of the study was to develop and test the effectiveness of a multivariate prognostic model for assessing the risks and benefits of minimally invasive decompressive and stabilizing interventions in the treatment of railway workers with degenerative diseases of the lumbar spine. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of a prospectively collected database of 457 patients operated on between 2020 and 2023 using minimally invasive decompression and stabilization techniques was performed. Comprehensive clinical, radiological and neuroimaging assessments were performed before surgery and 24 months after the intervention. Regression analysis was used to create prognostic models of long-term clinical outcomes (y0 - ODI; y1 - SF-36 PCS (physical component score); y2 - SF-36 MCS (mental component score); y3 - Macnab scale). Results: All analyzed clinical parameters (ODI, VAS, SF-36) significantly improved after surgery compared to preoperative values (p < 0.05). Within 90 days after surgery, the reoperation rate was 5.7%, and the number of readmissions during the observation period was 8.3%. 87.3% of railway workers returned to their previous work activities. Clinical deterioration was observed in 51 patients (11.2%). It was found that preoperative variables allow predicting long-term clinical outcome, playing a different role and dominance depending on the developed prognostic model. Conclusion: The study confirmed acceptable errors in the approximation of regression models for y1 (SF-36 PCS), y2 (SF-36 MCS) and y3 (Macnab) - 13.9%, 15% and 17.3%, respectively, which confirms the possibility of their clinical use. The y0 (ODI) model has significantly lower predictive capabilities of 39.2%, but it can be effectively used as an additional tool for identifying factors influencing the output variable and quantitatively assessing the degree of this influence. Overall success in the study population, defined as postoperative back pain and lower extremity pain VAS less than 20 mm, ODI less than 20 points, and freedom from reoperation or readmissions, was 88.8%.

## ID: AGS26/931

## P0342: Comparative analysis of the effectiveness and safety of anesthetic care during neurosurgical interventions on the lumbar spine in patients with high-risk factors

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2,3

, Victoria Goloborodko

1

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Yurii Pestryakov

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, K. Daniel Riew

5,6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, United States

6

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, United States

Introduction: Provision of anesthetic care during neurosurgical treatment of patients with degenerative diseases of the lumbar spine, having high-risk factors for anesthesia and surgery, is associated with an increase in the number of perioperative complications, an extension of the duration of inpatient treatment and an increase in financial costs. The aim of the study is to conduct a comparative analysis of the effectiveness and safety of providing anesthetic care during neurosurgical interventions on the lumbar spine in patients with high-risk factors. Material and Methods: The prospective comparative study included patients who had a high risk of anesthesia and surgery. All patients underwent open posterior decompressive and stabilizing interventions in the period from 2021 to 2023. Depending on the type of anesthesia and the method of postoperative analgesia, 3 groups of patients were identified: in group 1 (n = 41), intravenous anesthesia based on propofol and fentanyl was used; in group II (n = 40), prolonged epidural analgesia with ropivacaine was additionally performed for 3 days after surgery; In group III (n = 43), multimodal analgesia was combined with preoperative intramuscular administration of ketoprofen, infiltration of the paraspinal muscles, subcutaneous fat and skin with ropivacaine before performing the approach, intraoperative dexmedetomidine, and postoperative paracetamol. Comparative analysis included an assessment of perioperative hemodynamic parameters, postoperative pain intensity, perioperative use of opioids, the dynamics of recovery of psychomotor functions, side effects of anesthesia and postoperative surgical complications. Results: Compared with patients in groups I and II, in group III there were no significant changes in hemodynamics, better results were obtained in the rate of recovery of psychomotor functions and a smaller amount of opioid drugs was noted. In groups II and III, a minimal level of postoperative local pain syndrome and a lower need for analgesics were recorded. The number of side effects of anesthesia in group I was 29.3%, in group II - 27.5%, in group III - 9.3% (p = 0.01). The number of postoperative surgical complications was comparable: in group I - 7.3%, in group II - 7.5%, in group III - 4.7% (p = 0.56). Conclusion: The use of complex perioperative analgesia, compared with traditional anesthesia, including prolonged epidural analgesia, has the advantages of no depression of perioperative hemodynamic parameters, fewer side effects of anesthesia and postoperative surgical complications.

## ID: AGS26/950

## P0343: Factors associated with immediate postoperative pain control after elective lumbar fusion surgery

Napat Chantarapitak

1

, Piya Chavalparit

1

1

Orthopedic Department, Faculty of Medicine Vajira Hospital, Navamindradhiraj University, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Postoperative pain management is a pivotal component of recovery after elective lumbar fusion surgery, with inadequately controlled pain contributing to patient dissatisfaction and suboptimal outcomes. Identifying preoperative factors associated with severe immediate postoperative pain may enable personalized care pathways and improve overall recovery. Material and Methods: This ambidirectional cohort study was conducted between 2022 and 2025, included patients undergoing elective lumbar spine fusion. Poorly controlled postoperative pain was defined as severe pain (VAS ≥ 4) within the immediate postoperative period. Exclusions were intrathecal or epidural analgesia, revision or trauma surgeries, concurrent thoracolumbar procedures, multiple same-day surgeries, tumors, infections, tandem stenosis, or refusal. Demographic, surgical, and perioperative data were collected. Univariable and multivariable logistic regression were performed on 25 candidate variables identified by systematic review and expert consensus. Results: A cohort of 124 elective lumbar spine surgery patients, 52% experienced poorly controlled pain in the immediate postoperative period. Univariable analyses (p < 0.05) revealed nine variables were significantly associated with this outcome, including principal pathology, any nicotine use, surgical approach, involvement of ≥ 3 motion segments, fusion level, decompression level, estimated blood loss, and operative time. Age, sex, BMI, preoperative medications, preoperative back pain, and history of epidural steroid injection were not significantly associated with immediate postoperative pain outcomes in the current study. Conclusion: Poor pain control after spinal fusion surgery is a common problem. In this study, nine factors were found to be linked with worse outcomes. These results highlight the importance of careful preoperative assessment and tailored pain management, especially for patients having posterior fusion with associated disc herniation, a history of smoking, or multilevel surgery, to improve recovery and lower the risk of complications.
Keywords: lumbar fusion surgery, post-operative pain, immediate post-operative, pain, fusion surgery, spine, fusion, elective surgery

## ID: AGS26/999

## P0344: Investigating the relationship between glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist use and incidence of vertebral fractures in female patients aged > 50 years with osteoporosis: a propensity-matched analysis

Kyle Stump

1

, Sean Kelleher

1

, Stephan Aynaszyan

2

, Samuel Ricci

1

, Theresa Pazionis

2

1

Lewis Katz School of Medicine at Temple University, Philadelphia, United States

2

Department of Orthopaedics and Sports Medicine, Temple University Hospital, Philadelphia, United States

Introduction: Vertebral fractures are relatively common in patients with osteoporosis and frequently result in substantial morbidity, such as persistent pain and functional deficit. Although primarily indicated for the management of Type 2 diabetes mellitus and obesity, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) have demonstrated positive effects on bone mineral density (BMD) in the lumbar spine. However, it remains unknown if this effect translates to positive clinical outcomes for patients with osteoporosis. The purpose of this study is to investigate the relationship between GLP-1 receptor agonist exposure and the incidence of vertebral fracture and surgical intervention for these injuries in patients with osteoporosis. Material and Methods: This study utilized the TriNetX database to identify female patients aged > 50 years diagnosed with osteoporosis without a current pathological fracture within the 10-year period ended January 1, 2025. The study group included patients with a history of GLP-1 RA use after osteoporosis diagnosis compared to a control group with no GLP-1 RA exposure. Cohorts were propensity-matched based on baseline demographic characteristics, BMI, HbA1c, eGFR, medical comorbidities, osteoporotic medication use, and serum calcium, phosphate, and Vitamin D levels. Outcomes assessed included lifetime incidence of vertebral collapse and kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty. Results: There were 56,142 matched pairs. The rate of vertebral fracture in the experimental group was approximately 1.9% compared with 4.3% in the control group (RR 0.434, 95% CI 0.404 - 0.467, p < 0.001). Additionally, GLP-1 RA exposure was associated with lower incidence of kyphoplasty or vertebroplasty (RR 0.453, 95% CI 0.381 -0.538, p < 0.001). Conclusion: Glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonist use is associated with lower incidence of vertebral fracture and osteoporotic fracture intervention in female patients aged 50+ with osteoporosis. These findings underscore the potential protective effects of GLP-1 receptor agonists in patients with poor bone quality.
