## P0345: Is Anchored Stand-Alone ALIF Effective and Safe for the Treatment of Low-Grade L5-S1 Isthmic Spondylolisthesis? A Comparative Study With Posterior Lumbar Interbody Fusion

Marco Ajello

1

, Stefano Colonna

1

, Ayoub Saaid

1

, Enrico Lobue

1

, Alberto Morello

1

, Ludovico Maria Comite

1

, Luca Ceroni

2

, Diego Garbossa

1

, fabio cofano

1

, Nicola Marengo

1

1

Neurosurgery Unit, Department of Neuroscience “Rita Levi-Montalcini”, University of Turin, Turin, Italy

2

Department of Psychology, University of Turin, Turin, Italy

Introduction: Optimal surgical treatment for low-grade L5-S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis (IS) is still subject of debate. While various surgical approaches exist, minimally invasive anchored stand-alone (SA) ALIF has emerged as a promising technique, offering distinct advantages in terms of biomechanical stability and potential for neural decompression. This study aimed to compare the efficacy, as well as the clinical and radiological outcomes of anchored SA-ALIF and posterior lumbar interbody fusion in the management of low-grade L5-S1 IS. Material and Methods: A total of 53 patients, 26 from the anterior group and 27 from the posterior group, met the inclusion criteria. Intraoperative blood loss, operative time, radiation exposure and postoperative length of hospitalization were retrospectively evaluated. Clinical outcomes were assessed using the ODI and VAS scales. Upright lumbosacral X-ray and lumbosacral CT scan were used to evaluate spinopelvic parameters and intersomatic fusion according to Brantigan-Steffee-Fraser (BSF) scale, respectively. Results: The mean postoperative follow-up was 39 months. Intraoperative blood loss, radiation exposure, operative time, and postoperative length of hospitalization were significantly lower in anchored SA-ALIF group. Effective ODI and VAS improvement was achieved in both anterior and posterior groups. No significant differences were observed between the two groups in postoperative spinopelvic parameters assessment. Effective spinal fusion was achieved in 23 patients (88.4%) in the anchored SA-ALIF group, and in 21 patients (77.8%) in the posterior group. Conclusion: While both techniques effectively achieve spinal fusion and symptom relief, minimally invasive anchored SA-ALIF offers significant advantages over posterior fusion techniques in terms of intraoperative blood loss, radiation exposure, operative time, and postoperative length of hospitalization.

## ID: AGS26/1051

## P0346: Fragility of randomized trials comparing decompression alone and decompression with fusion for lumbar spinal stenosis

Michael Brown

1

, Andy Ton

2

, Alqasim Elnaggar

3

, Ali Mehaidli

1

, Gary George

1

, Matthew Easton

1

, Kevin Taliaferro

1

, Ryan Berger

1

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Henry Ford Health System, Detroit, United States

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of California Irvine, Irvine, United States

3

Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

Background: Degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis (DLS) is a common condition that increases with age. While many patients improve with conservative care, severe disease often requires surgical treatment. Lumbar decompression is the standard option, with fusion added when instability is present, though fusion carries risks such as hardware failure, pseudarthrosis, and adjacent segment disease. Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) have shown similar outcomes for decompression alone compared to decompression with fusion in patients without instability, yet fusion remains widely used. Fragility analysis, which evaluates the robustness of statistically significant findings through measures such as the fragility index (FI) and fragility quotient (FQ), has been applied in other orthopaedic subspecialties but rarely in spine surgery. No prior study has assessed the fragility of RCTs comparing decompression alone and decompression with fusion in DLS, highlighting the need for this analysis. Materials and Methods: We conducted a meta-analysis in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. A specific search strategy was applied to three online databases (PubMed, Embase and CENTRAL) to identify all trials comparing outcomes between decompression and decompression with fusion for treating LSS. A predetermine set of incision criteria was used to screen articles for appropriateness. All fragility calculations were performed on IBM SPSS Statistics software. Results: Seven articles were included in our analysis. The fragility index (FI) for all the dichotomous outcomes was 6 (IQR: 5-11), with a corresponding fragility quotient (FQ) 0.046 (IQR: 0.024-0.061). This indicates that only 6 events or 4.6% of events, is sufficient to reverse the aggregated significance of these trials. Sub-analysis found significant events to be more fragile than non-significant ones (FI: 4 versus 8). Radiographic findings and minimal clinical important different (MCID) were the most and least fragile outcomes (FI: 4 vs 15), respectively. Conclusion: The Fragility Index (FI) provides useful insight into the robustness of trial outcomes, with our analysis showing a median FI of 6 (FQ: 0.046), indicating that few event changes can reverse statistical significance. Significant outcomes were generally more fragile than non-significant ones, with radiographic findings being the most fragile. However, this review is limited by its focus on dichotomous outcomes, a small number of included studies, and the FI’s inability to assess study bias or methodological quality. These findings highlight the importance of using FI alongside traditional statistical measures to better interpret clinical evidence.

## ID: AGS26/1123

## P0347: Morphometric variations in oblique lumbar interbody fusion corridors in degenerative lumbar scoliosis: a comparative study of the apex direction

Ji Uk Choi

1

, Jae Hwan Cho

2

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Incheon St. Mary's Hospital, College of Medicine, The Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, Incheon, South Korea

2

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Asan Medical Center, University of Ulsan College of Medicine, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Degenerative lumbar scoliosis (DLS) alters spinal anatomy, impacting the feasibility and dimensions of surgical corridors for oblique lumbar interbody fusion (OLIF). This study aimed to compare the morphometric characteristics of OLIF corridors between patients with left and right curves, focusing on corridor dimensions, psoas muscle asymmetry, and segmental artery positioning. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of 80 patients with DLS (left apex: n = 43; right apex: n = 37) was conducted. Corridor angles and distances, psoas muscle cross-sectional areas, and segmental artery locations were measured at L2-3, L3-4, and L4-5 using T2-weighted MRI. Multivariate regression analysis identified key anatomical predictors of corridor variation at L2-3. Results: Regardless of the apex direction, the left-sided corridor was consistently larger across all levels. Corridor angles were significantly greater on the left side in both groups (left apex: L2-3: 39.7° vs. 13.5°, L3-4: 38.3° vs. 11.9°, L4-5: 38.6° vs. 6.9°; right apex: L2-3: 53.7° vs. 18.1°, L3-4: 43.1° vs. 18.5°, L4-5: 28.6° vs. 11.9°). Psoas muscle areas were larger on the concave side of the curve (left apex: 125.7 mm2 vs. 67.9 mm2; right apex: 125.4 mm2 vs. 77.4 mm2). Segmental artery positioning exhibited curve-dependent asymmetry at L2-3 (left apex: 8.8 mm vs. 7.9 mm; right apex: 9.6 mm vs. 8.0 mm). Multivariate regression analysis revealed that in the left apex group, the left psoas area (β = -0.132) and segmental artery distances were significant predictors of corridor angle variation (R2 = 0.517). In the right apex group, psoas asymmetry (β = 0.123) and sagittal alignment (β = -0.851) were associated with corridor differences. Conclusion: OLIF corridors in DLS patients demonstrate consistent left-sided dominance, regardless of apex direction. Psoas muscle asymmetry and segmental artery positioning contribute to corridor variations. While the left-sided approach remains standard, right-sided access may be feasible in select cases, particularly at L4-5. These findings provide insights for optimizing preoperative planning and surgical decision-making in DLS patients.

## ID: AGS26/1136

## P0348: Comparison of clinical and radiological results of polyetheretherketone cage and cage-shaped allograft use in lumbar interbody fusion surgery

Sefa Altun

1

, Yücel Bilgin

2

, Selcan Akesen

3

, Saltuk Buğra Güler

4

, Burak Akesen

2

, Rıfat Özpar

5

, Ayşenur Küçükata Tekin

5

1

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Bucak State Hospital, Burdur, Turkey

2

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, School of Medicine, Bursa Uludag University, Bursa, Turkey

3

Department of Anesthesiology and Reanimation, School of Medicine, Bursa Uludag University, Bursa, Turkey

4

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Mamak State Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

5

Department of Radiology, School of Medicine, Bursa Uludag University, Bursa, Turkey

Introduction: This study compared the clinical and radiological results of polyetheretherketone(PEEK) cage and cage-shaped allograft use in lumbar interbody fusion surgery. Material and Methods: In the study, the results of 30 patients who underwent lumbar interbody fusion surgery and used a PEEK cage for fusion were compared with those of patients who used a cage-shaped allograft. The Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) were used in the clinical evaluation of the patients. The ‘Brantigan classification’ was used in the computerized tomography scans taken in the first postoperative year to evaluate the interbody fusion radiologically. Results: While the preoperative VAS scores of patients in whom cage-shaped allografts were used were 9.1, it was observed that they decreased to 2.87 at the 1st year controls. Again, it was observed that the preoperative ODI score, which was 48.87 in the allograft group, reduced to 18.53 in the 1st year. In the PEEK cage group, the preoperative VAS score decreased from 8.8 to 3.97, and the ODI score decreased from 42.13 to 24.87. The change in VAS and ODI scores was better in the allograft group (p = 0.002, p = 0.012). According to the Brantigan classification, no statistically significant difference was observed in the intervertebral fusion evaluation between the groups (p = 0.301). Conclusion: In lumbar interbody fusion surgery, using PEEK cages and cage-shaped allografts provides similar radiological results in fusion. However, allografts provided better clinical results in the first postoperative year.

## ID: AGS26/1148

## P0349: Microbiome impacts surgical outcomes - Comparison of gut microbiome dysbiosis in patients undergoing lumbar microdiscectomy: results from the MISO Pilot Study

Stone Sima

1

, Thomas Jeffries

2

, Alisha Sial

1,3,4

, Suhani Sharma

1

, Neha Chopra

1,4

, Saeed Kohan

5

, Peter Khong

5

, Lauren Barber

6

, Victor Chen

1

, Ashish Diwan

1,3,4

1

Spine Labs, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George and Sutherland Clinical Campuses, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

2

School of Science, Western Sydney University Hawkesbury Campus, Richmond, New South Wales, Sydney, Australia

3

Spinal Unit, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Royal Adelaide Hospital, University of Adelaide, Adelaide, Australia

4

Spine Service, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George Hospital, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

5

Department of Neurosurgery, St George Hospital, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

6

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond, VA, Richmond, United States

Introduction: More than 10% of patients undergoing lumbar microdiscectomy for symptomatic lumbar disc herniation (LDH) develop failed back surgery syndrome (FBSS) often without clear cause. Recent findings of pathobiont gut bacteria in degenerative intervertebral discs (IVDs) suggest a potential “gut-disc axis,” where microbiome-mediated inflammation and disc colonization might undermine surgical outcomes. This novel pilot study aims to determine whether changes in gut microbial composition (dysbiosis) can predict FBSS after one level lumbar microdiscectomy for symptomatic LDH. Materials and Methods: 20 adult patients undergoing one-level lumbar microdiscectomy for symptomatic LDH were prospectively studied. Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores were collected preoperatively and at 3 months and 6 months postoperatively. Achievement of minimum clinically important difference (MCID) was defined as a > 30% improvement in NRS and ODI score. Preoperative Stool samples were analyzed using 16S rRNA sequencing. Alpha diversity and beta diversity were assessed to determine microbial diversity differences. Taxonomic composition was compared at the phylum and genus levels. Results: Of the 20 patients, 16 achieved MCID, while 4 developed FBSS at 3 and 6 months. There were no significant differences in age, sex, BMI, race, level of surgery, and pre-operative NRS and ODI between groups. Alpha diversity was similar (p = 0.437), but beta diversity showed significant differences in microbial community structure (p < 0.05). Differential abundance analysis identified patients who experienced FBSS had higher levels of pro-inflammatory Prevotella (U = 2, p = 0.002) and lower levels of commensal Bacteroides (U = 61, p = 0.003)) and Faecalibacterium (U = 53, p = 0.047). Conclusion: This study is the first to identify a Prevotella-dominant enterotype, along with a reduction in the anti-inflammatory genera Bacteroides and Faecalibacterium, as potential contributors to an increased risk of FBSS. Studies of higher power are required to estimate the effect sizes of these associations, investigate causation, and consider the viability of synbiotic therapy to improve spine surgery outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1151

## P0350: Effect of age on sagittal alignment characteristics in patients with symptomatic lumbar spinal stenosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Sifang Li

1

, Veysel Cara

1

, Corina Nüesch

1,2

, Florian Halbeisen

3

, Christian Appenzeller-Herzog

4

, Stefan Schären

1,2

, Cordula Netzer

1,2

1

Department of Spine Surgery, University Hospital Basel, Basel, Switzerland

2

Department of Biomedical Engineering

3

Surgical Outcome Research Center

4

University Medical Library, University of Basel, Basel, Switzerland

Introduction: Symptomatic lumbar spinal stenosis (sLSS) is considered a degenerative disease. However, few people have investigated whether the sagittal alignment of patients with sLSS and their symptoms change with age. Sagittal alignment plays a crucial role in maintaining an efficient posture, balanced load distribution, and optimal spinal function and is changed in patients with sLSS. In a meta-analysis of healthy persons, sagittal alignment was associated with age. In addition, the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) is a questionnaire used to assess patient symptoms and is widely used for patients with LSS. We systematically reviewed the literature to investigate the influence of age on sagittal alignment parameters and ODI in patients with sLSS. Material and Methods: A systematic literature search on sagittal spinal alignment in adult patients with degenerative sLSS was performed in Embase, Medline, and the Web of Science Core Collection. Peer-reviewed studies were included if measurements were obtained from standing radiography. We excluded conference abstracts, case reports, systematic reviews, and studies with postoperative data only, missing patient age, adolescent-only populations, or non-standing measurements. Associations between age and sagittal alignment parameters and ODI were assessed using meta-regression in R using the package “meta”. Results: From 1,593 articles screened, 214 met our inclusion criteria (163 Asian, 51 non-Asian; total n = 17,339). The average age of the patient in the included study ranged between 42-85 years. Hundert-seventy-eight articles reported lumbar lordosis (LL) measurements, 90 sagittal vertical axis (SVA), 105 sacral slope (SS), 120 pelvic incidence (PI), 116 pelvic tilt (PT), 12 T1 Pelvic Angle (TPA), 6 cervical lordosis (CL), 51 thoracic kyphosis (TK) and 85 ODI. The meta-regression analysis showed that age was associated with LL, SVA, PT and TPA. Specifically, LL (β = -0.3 degree per year; 95% confidence interval (CI) [-0.6,-0.1], p = 0.002, R2 = 4.92%) decreased with age, while SVA (β = 2.6mm per year; 95%CI [1.8,3.1], p < 0.001, R2 = 32.27%), PT (β = 0.2 degree per year; 95%CI [0.1,0.3], p < 0.001, R2 = 9.51%) and TPA (β = 0.8 degree per year; 95%CI [0.0,1.6], p = 0.0429, R2 = 23.33%) increased with age. Age was not associated with ODI and other sagittal alignment parameters (SS, PI, SSA, TK and CL). Conclusion: In patients with sLSS, LL, PT, SVA and TPA but not ODI were associated with age. Two-thirds of the included studies were performed in Asia. Hence, an influence of ethnicity on the observed relationships between age and sagittal alignment cannot be excluded and warrants further investigation.

## ID: AGS26/1156

## P0351: Side of approach does not affect outcomes in direct lateral lumbar interbody fusion. A non-inferiority retrospective cohort study

Dean Biddau

1

, Gregory Malham

2

1

Queensland University of Technology, Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Brisbane, Australia

2

Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Melbourne, Australia

Introduction: Lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) is a minimally invasive surgical technique with various clinical benefits. However, there is no consensus on the optimal side of approach. This study investigates clinical and radiographic outcomes associated with surgical laterality, where the approach side was chosen based on operating room theatre flow rather than anatomy. Material and Methods: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data from 233 patients (2016-2021) who underwent LLIF by two surgeons, each consistently using a specific side due to operating room setup. Outcomes included Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Visual Analog Scale (VAS), SF-12, and radiographic fusion. Statistical comparisons included predefined non-inferiority margins for operative time (15 min) and fusion (10%). Results: Left-sided (n = 116) and right-sided (n = 117) approaches showed no significant differences in operative time (p = 0.39), fusion rates at 12 months (p = 0.66), VAS, ODI, or complication rates. Fusion at 12 months was 94.9% vs. 93.2% respectively. Conclusion: LLIF outcomes did not differ by side of approach. While anatomical considerations may apply in selected cases (e.g., coronal deformity), theatre flow factors can guide side selection without compromising outcomes. This study is limited by surgeon-side bias and absence of randomization.

## ID: AGS26/1173

## P0352: The prevention of infectious complications in degenerative spine surgery with sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione use

Arkadii Kazmin

1

, Vladimir Pereverzev

1

, Roman Ryzhenko

1

, Sergei Kolesov

1

1

N. N. Priorov National Medical Research Center, Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: A surgical site infection (SSI) is defined as an infection that occurs after surgery in the part of the body where the operation was performed. SSIs lead to increased costs, repeated hospitalizations, longer hospital stays and, in some cases, death of patients. At the same time, according to a number of authors, from 40% to 60% of these infections can be prevented. Modern immunomodulation is also becoming important in spinal surgery. Purpose: To evaluate the immunological status of patients operated on for degenerative diseases of the lumbar spine and how it could be effected by sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione. Material and Methods: A prospective, comparative, non-randomized, single-center study. The study included 60 patients, 30 in the experimental group, 30 in the control group. Each patient signed informed consent. When the patient was included in the study, a laboratory examination was performed: T-lymphocytes (CD3+); T-helpers (CD3+CD4+); T-cytotoxic lymphocytes (CD3+CD8+); immunoregulatory index (IRI); B-lymphocytes (CD19+); Natural killers (NK cells, CD3-CD16+/CD56+); T-natural killers (CD3+CD16+CD56+); indicators of phagocytosis of granulocytes and monocytes; circulating immune complexes (CIC), immunoglobulins A (IgA), M (IgM), G (IgG), E (IgE); leukocyte formula with the determination of the proportion (in%) and concentration (thousand cells / μl) of neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils. The described laboratory examination was repeated in patients on the 10th day PostOp after the course of the 200 mg sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione 1 time per day. Results: It was found that the percentage of patients with positive dynamics of immunological indicators was significantly (p < 0.05) higher after the course of sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione (T-, B-cell immunity, IgG level, indicators in the CBC - the level of lymphocytes, neutrophils), thus this chemical compound potentiates, enhances the positive effect on the main links of immunity in a much larger number of patients with this pathology. Sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione demonstrated a pronounced immunomodulatory activity in patients with degenerative diseases of the spine, acting as a true immunomodulator, potentiating anti-infective protection in a significantly larger percentage of individuals compared to the control group (p < 0.05). Conclusion: Sodium aminodihydrophthalazinedione significantly effected on cellular immunity, thereby reduced the possible risks of infectious complications in the surgical area in patients with degenerative diseases of the lumbar spine. Further studies, including multicenter ones, will make it possible to more clearly formulate the indications for the use of this chemical compound in the orthopedic patients treatment.

## ID: AGS26/1286

## P0353: The psychological effect of using back orthoses post one/two level lumbar fusion

Chante Van Der Merwe

1

, Jaytesh Pillay

1

1

Mediclinic Kloof Hospital, Pretoria, South Africa

Introduction: Back orthoses have evolved over decades, and the effect of different types of braces have been well established over time. Despite substantial evidence indicating that bracing does not improve post-operative outcomes in one- or two-level lumbar fusion, many patients continue to prefer wearing a brace. Patients often report feeling vulnerable without it, fearing they may cause further damage to their spine. This perception leads to increased caution in movement when not wearing a brace. While bracing does not provide a measurable improvement in clinical outcomes, it appears to offer a sense of stability and protection. Existing literature indicates that the use of back orthoses post-lumbar fusion does not result in significant differences in post-operative pain relief, recovery time, complication rates, or fusion success compared to cases where no orthosis is used. Conversely, studies highlight potential complications associated with bracing. Despite clear evidence against its effectiveness, bracing remains controversial, largely due to patient preference. This raises the question of whether the continued use of back braces is influenced by a placebo effect, providing psychological comfort rather than physiological benefit. Unfortunately, there is a notable gap in research investigating the psychological impact of orthoses post-lumbar fusion and how this affects patient management and recovery. This study aims to assess the psychological impact of brace use following one- or two-level lumbar fusion. The primary objective is to determine whether bracing continues to play a significant role in post-operative care, primarily as a psychological support mechanism rather than a tool for physical recovery. Material and Methods: This study was conducted at a single facility, with all surgical procedures performed by the same surgeon. Eligible participants included patients undergoing a one- or two-level open posterior interbody lumbar fusion. Exclusion criteria included revision surgeries, trauma-related procedures, and patients with an elevated BMI. Pre-operatively, patients were randomized to either receive a Miami Lumbar orthosis or no brace. Postoperatively, patients completed the Pain Anxiety Symptom Scale (PASS) along with additional questionnaires addressing brace usage, perceived support, and overall satisfaction. Results: A total of 30 patients were included in this study and were followed up at 2 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months post-operatively. Our findings indicate that brace use did not provide an advantage in terms of comfort, mobility, sitting tolerance, or overall clinical outcomes. The only significant difference observed was a reduction in patient anxiety among those who wore a brace. Conclusion: The use of a lumbar brace in the post-operative period following a one- or two-level lumbar fusion does not appear to contribute meaningfully to clinical recovery, serving primarily as a psychological support mechanism. Based on these findings, we recommend considering brace use selectively for patients who present with high levels of pre-operative anxiety.

## ID: AGS26/1295

## P0354: Anterior and transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion at L5-S1 level: clinical outcomes and factors predicting failure

Ana Vilela

1

, Rita Teles

2

, Joana de Azevedo

1

, David Ferreira

1

, Nuno Oliveira

1

, Paulo Gil Ribeiro

1

, Pedro Domingues

1

, Emanuel Novais

3

, Rui Cerqueira

4

, Bruno Direito Santos

1 5

, Pedro Varanda

1 5

1
Unidade Local de Saúde de Braga, Braga, Portugal, 
2
Escola de Medicina da Universidade do Minho, Braga, Portugal

3

Unidade Local de Saúde do Litoral Alentejano, Santiago do Cacém, Portugal

4

Unidade Local de Saúde do Alto Ave, Guimarães, Portugal

5

Life and Health Science Research Institute, Universidade do Minho, Braga, Portugal

Introduction: Lumbar interbody fusion at the L5-S1 level is a common surgical procedure for degenerative lumbar conditions. Among the various approaches, anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) and transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) are frequently employed. ALIF provides direct access to the disc space, theoretically offering superior restoration of disc and foraminal height, as well as correction of lumbar lordosis. TLIF, on the other hand, involves less soft tissue dissection and is typically favored when posterior access is preferable. Although both techniques have shown efficacy, direct comparisons of clinical and radiographic outcomes, fusion rates, and complication profiles remain limited. This study aimed to evaluate and compare the outcomes of ALIF and TLIF at L5-S1 in patients with degenerative lumbar conditions. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study included patients who underwent either ALIF or TLIF at the L5-S1 level. A total of 44 patients were included: 9 (20.5%) underwent ALIF and 35 (79.5%) TLIF. Data collected included demographics, comorbidities, surgical parameters (operative time, blood loss, hospital stay), and radiographic measurements (anterior and posterior disc height, lumbar lordosis). Fusion status was assessed at 1-year follow-up, and complications were documented. Statistical analyses were performed to evaluate differences between groups. Results: The mean age was 50.34 ± 11.02 years, with a predominance of female patients (56.8%), particularly in the ALIF group (100% women). No significant demographic differences were found between groups, aside from sex distribution. Mean operative time was slightly longer for ALIF (214.3 vs. 188.8 minutes, p = 0.150). Blood loss data were unavailable for ALIF but varied widely in TLIF (50-600 mL). Hospital stay was similar (median 3 days, p = 0.549). At 1-year follow-up, fusion rates were 100% for ALIF and 88.6% for TLIF. Radiographically, ALIF achieved superior restoration of anterior and posterior disc height (p < 0.01 and p = 0.003, respectively), whereas no significant differences were observed in lumbar lordosis correction. Complications occurred at similar rates (22.2% in ALIF vs. 22.9% in TLIF, p = 0.672), with foraminal compression predominating in ALIF and radicular pain or implant-related issues in TLIF. Conclusion: Both ALIF and TLIF demonstrated high effectiveness in the management of degenerative lumbar disease at L5-S1, with comparable fusion rates and complication profiles. ALIF offered superior disc height restoration, while TLIF was equally effective in restoring lumbar lordosis. Operative time, hospital stay, and complications did not significantly differ between groups. Patient-specific factors such as age, comorbidities, and obesity influenced surgical outcomes and should be considered in preoperative planning. Despite the limitations of sample size and retrospective design, this study contributes valuable data supporting the use of both techniques. Larger prospective studies are warranted to confirm these findings.

## ID: AGS26/1369

## P0355: Functional recovery and pain reduction after surgery for lumbar spinal stenosis - A retrospective analysis using the Oswestry Disability Index

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Luis Vásquez

1

, Carlos Acosta

1

, Daniel Zambrano Lizcano

1

, Pedro Barrera

1

, María Constanza Bedoya

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) is a clinical syndrome characterized by reduced space for neurovascular structures in the lumbar spinal canal. This study aimed to describe and analyze recovery outcomes related to disability and functionality following LSS surgery, using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted on 230 patients who underwent LSS surgery between January 2016 and December 2022. The ODI was assessed preoperatively, at six months, and twelve months postoperatively. Statistical analyses included Wilcoxon, Kruskal-Wallis, and Spearman correlation tests, with a significance threshold of p < 0.05. Results: The mean patient age was 69.05 years, and 62.3% were female. Preoperatively, the median ODI score was 48.88 (IQR 37.7-62), indicating severe disability. At six months, the ODI significantly improved to 10 (IQR 0-26), p < 0.05, with no further significant changes at twelve months (10 [IQR 0-26.6], p = 0.938). Pain severity on the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) decreased from 8.39 preoperatively to 1.74 at six months and 1.53 at twelve months (p < 0.017). Correlation analysis demonstrated a statistically significant negative relationship between preoperative ODI scores and postoperative improvements (r = -0.21 at six months, p < 0.05; r = -0.14 at twelve months, p < 0.02). Conclusion: Surgical intervention for LSS improved functional outcomes and pain scores, with almost all the recovery occurring within the first six months. These findings highlight the role of ODI as a critical measure in postoperative assessment and support early surgical intervention in patients with severe functional impairment. Future studies should assess long-term ODI trends beyond twelve months to better understand sustained functional recovery.

## ID: AGS26/1376

## P0356: Radiographic and clinical outcomes of indirect decompression via extreme lateral interbody fusion (XLIF): a retrospective case series of 89 patients (2019-2022)

Luke Pearson

1

, Vashisht Sekar

1

, Aboubakr Amer

1

, Ulas Cikla

1

, Dunbar Alcindor

1

, Daryoush Tavanaiepour

1

, Kourosh Tavanaiepour

1

1

University of Florida Jacksonville, Jacksonville, United States

Introduction: Extreme Lateral Interbody Fusion (XLIF) is a minimally invasive lumbar fusion technique that uses a lateral transpsoas approach to achieve interbody fusion and indirect decompression. By restoring disc height, foraminal area, and spinal alignment via ligamentotaxis, XLIF relieves nerve root compression without direct neural manipulation, potentially reducing morbidity compared to posterior approaches. This is particularly relevant for degenerative lumbar conditions, including severe stenosis. Evidence on XLIF’s indirect decompression efficacy in moderately sized cohorts is limited. This retrospective review evaluates radiographic and clinical outcomes of indirect decompression via XLIF in 89 patients at a single center from January 2019 to December 2022, focusing on disc height, foraminal area, and symptom relief. Material and Methods: We analyzed 89 consecutive patients (aged 18-80 years) undergoing XLIF for degenerative disc disease, low-grade spondylolisthesis (Meyerding I-II), or adjacent segment degeneration with radiculopathy/claudication, confirmed by MRI/CT. Exclusion criteria included prior lumbar fusion, active infection, or severe osteoporosis (T-score <-3.0). Three fellowship-trained surgeons performed XLIF using a standardized protocol: lateral decubitus positioning, transpsoas access, neuromonitoring, and PEEK/allograft cage implantation (10-12 mm height, 18-22 mm width). Posterior fixation supplemented 67% of cases. Data included preoperative and postoperative (6, 12, 24 months) radiographic metrics (disc height, foraminal area, lordosis via X-rays/CT) and clinical outcomes (Visual Analog Scale [VAS] for leg/back pain, Oswestry Disability Index [ODI]). Indirect decompression was quantified by foraminal area and disc height increases. Perioperative data included operative time, blood loss (EBL), length of stay (LOS), and complications. Statistical analysis (SPSS v26) used paired t-tests, Pearson correlation, and multivariate regression (p < 0.05). Institutional Review Board approval was obtained. Results: The cohort included 89 patients (mean age 60.2 ± 11.0 years; 58% female) undergoing 1-3 level XLIF (mean 1.5 levels; L3-L5 predominant). Operative time was 135 ± 32 minutes per level, EBL 72 ± 38 mL, and LOS 2.2 ± 1.1 days. Indirect decompression increased disc height by 4.3 ± 1.7 mm (67%, p < 0.001) and foraminal area by 23.8 ± 7.9% (p < 0.001) at 6 months, sustained at 24 months (n = 76; 85% follow-up). Lordosis improved by 6.5° ± 3.0° (p < 0.001). VAS-leg decreased from 7.0 ± 1.8 to 2.5 ± 1.5 (p < 0.001), VAS-back from 7.3 ± 1.6 to 3.2 ± 1.7 (p < 0.001), and ODI from 50.9 ± 14.2 to 24.3 ± 10.8 (p < 0.001); 75% achieved ODI > 15 points. Foraminal area correlated with VAS-leg improvement (r = 0.49, p < 0.01). Complications (16%) included transient dysesthesia (10%), subsidence (3%), and infection (1%). Severe stenosis (p = 0.02) and larger cages (p = 0.01) predicted better decompression; obesity increased neuropraxia risk (OR 2.0, p = 0.03). Conclusion: XLIF achieves effective indirect decompression, with significant disc height and foraminal area restoration correlating with pain and disability improvements. Indirect decompression via XLIF is valid even for severe lumbar stenosis, as disc heightening, foraminal restoration, and ligamentotaxis often eliminate the need for direct decompression. Its low morbidity supports its utility, though neuropraxia is a concern in obese patients. Retrospective design is a limitation; prospective studies are needed. XLIF is a reliable option for selected patients.

## ID: AGS26/1377

## P0357: Transforaminal endoscopic lumbar discectomy versus microdiscectomy for surgical management of lumbar disc herniation: experience from a single tertiary center

Nikolaos Gkantsinikoudis

1

, Constantinos Chaniotakis

1

, Mikail Chatzivasiliadis

1

, Stylianos Kapetanakis

1,2

1

Spine Department and Deformities, Interbalkan European Medical Center, Thessaloniki, Greece

2

Academic Orthopaedic Department, Aristotle University of Thessaloniki, Thessaloniki, Greece

Introduction: Transforaminal Endoscopic Lumbar Discectomy (TELD) represents a minimally invasive and full-endoscopic method for surgical treatment of Lumbar Disc Herniation (LDH). This procedure is associated with minimal skin incision and intraoperative hemorrhage, preservation of dorsal musculature and relative spine elements, minimal epidural scarring as well as rapid postoperative mobilization with return to daily activities. However, conventional microdiscectomy (MD) remains the gold standard method for surgical management of LDH to date, whilst existent data from high quality studies remain scarce. Hence, aim of this study is to present our single-center experience regarding clinical outcomes of TELD versus MD in patients with surgically treated LDH. Material and Methods: Patients with LDH fulfilling current established indications for surgical management and operated from 2013-2019 in the single tertiary center and from the same operating surgeon were considered eligible for participation in this study. Finally, eight hundred and eighty five (885) patients were included and retrospectively evaluated. Data for defined outcome measures were retrieved from hospital registry. Except for standard demographic parameters, length of incision, operative time, postoperative mobilization time and hospitalization duration were considered primary outcome measures. Furthermore, established patient-reported outcome measures as Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Short-Form 36 Medical Health Survey Questionnaire and functional status - according to modified McNab criteria- and type and rate of recorded complications were secondary outcome measures. Available data were derived from preoperative assessment as well as from standard evaluation in outpatient clinics at 6 weeks and 3, 6, 12, 24 months and 5 years postoperatively. Results: Enrolled individuals were successfully subjected to TLED or MD with no significant perioperative complications. Demographic parameters were standardized between two groups, featuring no statistically significant differentiation. Regarding primary outcome measures, all parameters were defined to be statistically significantly ameliorated in TELD group, advocating for its minimal invasiveness (p value < 0.05). Contrariwise, self-reported secondary outcome measures were not demonstrated to differ significantly between groups in recorded follow-up checkpoints, featuring, however, statistically significant amelioration in each follow-up interval in comparison with previous in each group. Moreover, type and frequency of recorded complications were similar between two groups. Conclusion: Results from our single large-series analysis demonstrate that TELD is non-inferior with MD in terms of clinical effectiveness, exhibiting also superiority in specific parameters as operation duration, skin incision length and postoperative mobilization and hospitalization duration. However, selection of TELD should be thoughtfully conducted on a case-by-cases basis, considering meticulously patient- as well as surgeon-related parameters, taking also into account the established steep learning curve of this technique.

## ID: AGS26/1400

## P0358: Risk factors for reherniation following lumbar microdiscectomy: an institutional retrospective analysis

Mirbahador Athari

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Jessica Porras

1

, Ryan Chang

1

, Joshua Davood

1

, Ji Chin

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation is a leading cause of radiculopathy, and microdiscectomy remains the most common surgical treatment. Despite its effectiveness, reherniation occurs in up to 20% of patients and often necessitates revision surgery. Prior studies have examined both clinical and imaging predictors of recurrence, but risk stratification remains challenging. The purpose of this study was to evaluate clinical and MRI-based predictors of reherniation after single-level microdiscectomy at a single institution. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who underwent primary single-level lumbar microdiscectomy between 2012 and 2022. Exclusion criteria included prior lumbar surgery, trauma, tumor, or infection. Clinical variables included age, sex, body mass index (BMI), hypertension (HTN), diabetes mellitus (DM), obesity, and smoking status. Preoperative MRI parameters included sagittal disc size, anterior-posterior (AP) and mediolateral (ML) herniation dimensions, and the ratio of disc herniation to canal area (Disc/Canal ROI ratio). The primary outcome was symptomatic reherniation confirmed by MRI and managed surgically. Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses were performed to identify independent predictors. Results: A total of 491 patients were included (mean age 41 ± 13 years; 72% male). Reherniation occurred in 102 patients (20.8%). On univariate analysis, higher BMI (p < 0.001), smoking (p = 0.026), and HTN (p = 0.003) were significantly associated with reherniation. MRI-based parameters including sagittal disc size, AP size, ML size, and disc height were not significantly different between groups. The Disc/Canal ROI ratio showed a trend toward significance (p = 0.13). In the multivariate model, smoking (OR 1.85, 95% CI 1.00-3.45; p = 0.049) and HTN (OR 1.94, 95% CI 1.14-3.33; p = 0.016) remained independent predictors of reherniation. BMI demonstrated a nonsignificant trend (p = 0.097). Conclusion: In this large institutional cohort, reherniation occurred in approximately one-fifth of patients following lumbar microdiscectomy. Clinical factors, particularly smoking and hypertension, were significant independent predictors of recurrence, whereas MRI-based measurements were not independently predictive. These findings highlight the importance of addressing modifiable clinical risk factors when counseling patients and planning perioperative care. Future studies may clarify the role of advanced imaging metrics in refining risk stratification.

## ID: AGS26/1453

## P0359: PROMIS physical function scores more strongly predict two-year satisfaction than pain outcomes following lumbar spine surgery

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Elizabeth Kappler

1

, Erin Nicholas

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Patient-reported outcomes (PROMIS) increasingly influence healthcare quality metrics and reimbursement. Although both PROMIS Physical Function (PF) and subjective pain scores are routinely collected, their relative ability to predict patient satisfaction remains unclear. We hypothesized that improvements in PROMIS-PF scores would show stronger associations with satisfaction compared to changes in back and leg pain scores. Material and Methods: The Michigan Spine Surgery Improvement Collaborative (MSSIC) data was queried for patients undergoing lumbar spine surgery at University of Michigan from 2013-2023. Patient satisfaction was evaluated at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively using a 4-point scale PROMIS-PF and pain scores were obtained at baseline, 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years. MCID was defined as a decrease of ≥ 2 points for pain scores and an increase of ≥ 4.5 points for PROMIS-PF. Analysis included chi-square, Kruskal-Wallis tests and multivariate logistic regression. Results: The study cohort included 1,161 patients with a mean age 60.4 ± 14.1 years, 56% mal. At 2-year follow-up, 83.4% of patients reported improvement, with 59.7% reporting met expectations and 23.7% indicating they would repeat the surgery despite not meeting all expectations. MCID achievement at 2 years varied: 55.5% for PROMIS-PF, 66.6% for back pain, and 75.1% for leg pain. PROMIS-PF scores at 2 years showed the strongest association with satisfaction (χ2 = 303.706), followed by back pain (χ2 = 287.723) and leg pain (χ2 = 186.951; all p < 0.001). Baseline-to-2-year score changes demonstrated similar but weaker associations (PROMIS-PF: χ2 = 227.092; back pain: χ2 = 172.066; leg pain: χ2 = 143.525; all p < 0.001). Multiple regression analysis confirmed PROMIS-PF at 2 years as a significant independent predictor of satisfaction (p = 0.019), with the overall model demonstrating robust predictive value (Nagelkerke R2 = 0.562). Notably, failure to achieve MCID in back pain remained independently significant (p = 0.018) as a negative predictor, suggesting a complementary role alongside PROMIS-PF in predicting satisfaction. Conclusion: While PROMIS-PF demonstrated lower MCID achievement rates than pain measures, it showed superior association with satisfaction in both absolute and change scores. This suggests that PROMIS-PF may be a more stringent but more meaningful measure of surgical success. The stronger association of absolute scores versus change scores indicates that final functional status may be more important than degree of improvement in determining patient satisfaction. These findings reinforce the value of PROMIS-PF in outcomes assessment for spine surgery and suggest the need to reevaluate MCID thresholds for different metrics.

## P0360: Adult-onset acute lumbar spondylolysis in athletes: case-series

Guillermo Ricciardi

1

, Santiago Formaggin

1

, Ignacio Gabriel Garfinkel

1

, Daniel Oscar Ricciardi

1

, Gabriel Genaro Carrioli

1

1

Hospital General de Agudos Dr. Teodoro Álvarez, Buenos Aires, Argentina

Introduction: Acute spondylolysis is typically observed in skeletally immature patients. However, a few small case series of adult-onset acute spondylolysis have been reported. We aim to present three cases of adult athletes who developed acute spondylolysis. Methods: This is a descriptive case series of adult patients (≥ 18 years) with documented acute lumbar spondylolysis. Demographic data, sport activity, treatment, and clinical outcomes were recorded. Results: Three male patients were included, with a mean age of 19.3 years (range, 18-22). Two were soccer players and one a volleyball player, all presenting with acute L5 spondylolysis. Diagnosis was confirmed using CT and MRI, showing bone marrow edema on STIR sequences. All patients were treated non-operatively with activity cessation, immobilization, and physiotherapy. The mean time to return to sport was 5 months. Conclusion: We report three cases of adult-onset acute spondylolysis successfully treated non-operatively, resulting in good clinical outcomes and full return to sport activity.

## P0361: Symptomatic retroperitoneal fluid collection following anterior lumbar interbody fusion: a single institution study

Henry Avetisian

1

, Joshua Davood

1

, Camille Flynn

1

, Rahki Banerjee

1

, Vivek Satish

1

, Marco Napolitano

1

, Joshua Khorsandi

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, William Karakash

1

, Matthew Gallo

1

, Gregory Magee

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Retroperitoneal fluid collection (RFC), including hematomas and seromas, is a known complication of anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF), contributing to patient morbidity due to extended hospital stays and drainage procedures. Identifying risk factors is essential to mitigate its occurrence. This study evaluates the incidence of and risk factors for symptomatic RFC following ALIF. Materials and Methods: We conducted a retrospective review of consecutive ALIF cases (2014-2023) at a single-center academic hospital. Patients were stratified based on the development of symptomatic RFC, confirmed through advanced imaging (CT/MRI/Ultrasound). Exclusion criteria included patients under 18 years old, asymptomatic RFCs, and those with surgical indications for trauma, malignancy, or infection. Chi-squared and Student’s t-tests compared patient demographics and surgical variables. Univariable logistic regression analyses were performed to identify predictors of RFC. Results: Of 554 included ALIF cases, 44 (7.94%) developed a symptomatic RFC, with 28 (63.64%) requiring drainage. There were no significant differences between the RFC and non-RFC cohorts in terms of age (62.16 ± 10.34 years vs. 58.87 ± 12.44 years), BMI (28.20 ± 5.19 kg/m2 vs. 28.95 ± 15.05 kg/m2) sex distribution (Males: 43.18% vs. 47.25%), tobacco use (29.55% vs. 29.41%), or other recorded comorbidities (all p > 0.05). However, American Society of Anesthesiologists (ASA) score (2.65 vs. 2.41, p = 0.005) was significantly higher in the RFC cohort. Comorbidities such as hypertension (56.82% vs. 51.18%), diabetes (15.91% vs. 13.33%), and coronary artery disease (11.36% vs. 7.45%) were more prevalent, but non-significantly (all p > 0.05). Intraoperative transfusion (59.09% vs. 23.92%, p < 0.001) and venous injury (31.82% vs. 15.29%, p < 0.001) were significantly more prevalent in the RFC cohort. Additionally, estimated blood loss (1,657 ± 2,066 mL vs. 633.29 ± 1,135 mL, p < 0.001), operative time (8.38 ± 3.04 hours vs. 6.57 ± 2.82 hours, p < 0.001), and number of ALIF levels (≥ 2 levels 75.0% vs 39.4%, p < 0.001) were all found to be significantly higher in the RFC cohort. Finally, univariable logistic regression identified several risk factors: intraoperative venous injury (OR: 2.51, [1.27-4.94], p = 0.008), multi-level ALIF (OR: 2.11, [1.36-3.29], p < 0.001), and intraoperative transfusion (OR: 4.59, [2.44-8.66], p < 0.001). Conclusion: This study found a 7.94% incidence of symptomatic RFC following ALIF and identified multiple risk factors. Spine surgeons should remain vigilant-particularly in cases with venous injury, multi-level ALIF, or those requiring intraoperative transfusions-and may benefit from use of risk-stratified perioperative planning, explicit preoperative counseling, and a lower threshold for early postoperative imaging with timely drainage.

## P0362: Bucks fixation - for spondylolysis & low grade spondylolisthesis in young adolescent’s

Akhilesh Khobragade

1

, Mohammed Faizan

2

1

Max Hospital, Nagpur, India

2

MAX Superspeciality Hospital, Nagpur, India

Introduction: Thoracolumbar and lumbar fractures are among the most common spinal injuries and pose significant challenges in surgical stabilization. Traditional short-segment posterior fixation has been associated with higher failure rates compared with long-segment fixation. To overcome this, inclusion of the fractured vertebra with index-level screws has been proposed as a biomechanically advantageous strategy, aiming to preserve motion segments while enhancing stability. Aim of this study is to signify the role of index-level pedicle screws in thoracolumbar fracture fixation, comparing them with conventional short- and long-segment constructs. Material and Methods: Those cases who had a thoracolumbar and lumbar fractures, coming to the health facility were enllored for this study from the year 2024 to 2025. A total of 20 cases were recruited for this study. Results: Cases showed favourable radiological correction and functional recovery, with significant improvements in VAS and ODI scores at 2 years. Conclusion: Evidence consistently supports the biomechanical and clinical advantage of including index-level pedicle screws in thoracolumbar fracture fixation. This approach offers comparable correction and pain relief to long-segment constructs while preserving motion segments, reducing implant failure, and facilitating minimally invasive techniques. Long-segment fixation remains preferable for highly unstable fractures, but fracture-level fixation represents a reliable, motion-preserving alternative for selected cases.

## P0363: Efficacy of multi-level interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy and discectomy in restoration of segmental lordosis and lumbar sagittal balance in adult spinal deformity surgery

Hamisi Mraja

1

, Burak Abay

1

, Sepehr Asadollahmonfared

1

, Sinan Kahraman

2

, Ilyas Dolas

1

, Meric Enercan

2

, Selhan Karadereler

1

, Azmi Hamzaoglu

1

1

Scoliosis and Spine Center - Istanbul, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Demiroglu Bilim University, Istanbul, Turkey

Hypothesis: Multi-level interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy and discectomy will improve segmental and overall lumbar lordosis restoration in surgical treatment of ASD. Introduction: The main goal of the surgical treatment in adult spinal deformity is to restore of the ideal lumbar lordosis (LL) and sagittal balance. In the presence of open and mobile disc, complete bilateral facetectomy and discectomy provide additional flexibility for sagittal plane restoration at the thoracolumbar and lumbar spine. The aim of this study is to compare the efficacy of multi-level interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy and discectomy (BFD) procedure in restoring segmental lordosis and lumbar sagittal alignment. Methods: 46 pts (27F,19M), mean age 63 (36-77) years in whom instrumentation started from L1 level or above, down to sacrum and underwent a multi-level interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy and discectomy were included. Coronal and sagittal parameters were measured on preop and f/up x-rays. Clinical assessment was done with ODI and VAS. Results: Mean f/up was 43 (26-63) months. Mean interbody fusion levels were 2.9 (2-5). PI-LL mismatch was mean 23°. Preop lumbar lordosis of 27.5° was restored to 48.3° (75%) with BFD. The mean improvement in LL was 20° (13°-45°). Segmental lordosis and overall LL improved significantly in all pts (p < 0.05). The mean segmental lordosis increase per interbody fusion level was 7.44°. The contribution of segmental lordosis was greater in L2-L3, L3-L4 and L4-5 than other levels (7.55°, 7.57°, 10.4°). ODI and VAS scores improved significantly in both groups. Conclusion: In the presence of open disc in lumbar spine, multi-level interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy and discectomy provide improvement in both segmental lordosis (7.44° per level) and overall lumbar lordosis (p < 0.01). When interbody fusion with bilateral facetectomy is performed at 3 or more levels, it can serve as an alternative technique to 3-Column Osteotomy for the restoration of thoracolumbar and lumbar sagittal alignment in adult spinal deformity.

## P0364: Clinical advantages of minimally invasive TLIF over open TLIF: lower incidence of adjacent segment disease and reoperations: meta-analysis

Anton Denisov

1

, Nikita Zaborovskii

2

, Amgad Elshoeibi

3

, Waeel Hamouda

4

, Eduardo Tornero

1 5

, Ahmad Alzahrani

6

, Mohamed Ali

7

1

University Hospital Mollet, University of Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain

2

Vreden Ortopedic Institute, Saint Petersburg University, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

3

Hamad General Hospital, Hamad Medical Corporation, Doha, Qatar

4

Security Forces Hospital Dammam, Dammam, Saudi Arabia

5

Hospital Clinic Barcelona, Barcelona, Spain

6

Security Forces Hospital Riyadh, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

7

Prince Mohamed Ben Abdelaziz Hospital, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Open TLIF is effective for lumbar fusion but is associated with greater soft tissue disruption and prolonged recovery. MIS-TLIF minimizes morbidity, but its impact on ASD and long-term outcomes remains unclear. Objective: To compare minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-TLIF) with open TLIF regarding adjacent segment disease (ASD), pseudarthrosis, reoperation, and perioperative outcomes. Material and Methods: PubMed, Cochrane Library, Scopus, Embase, Web of Science, and Google Scholar were searched (2000-2025) for comparative studies in adults with degenerative lumbar disease or isthmic spondylolisthesis. Primary outcomes included ASD, pseudarthrosis, and reoperation. Random-effects meta-analyses with logit transformation for rare events were performed. Multivariate meta-regression evaluated age, BMI, fusion level, follow-up, and smoking. Results: Fourteen studies (1,827 patients) were included. MIS-TLIF reduced odds of ASD (OR 0.51, 95% CI 0.37-0.69; p < 0.001) and reoperation (OR 0.53, 95% CI 0.31-0.90; p = 0.018) with low heterogeneity. Pseudarthrosis rates were similar (OR 0.96, 95% CI 0.38-2.48; p = 0.94). Multivariate meta-regression identified multiple-level fusion as increasing ASD risk (p = 0.002) and older age as protective (p = 0.002). BMI, smoking, and follow-up duration were not significant predictors. No publication bias was detected. Conclusion: MIS-TLIF offers lower risk of ASD and reoperation compared to open TLIF, with comparable fusion outcomes. Fusion level and patient age are important predictors of ASD. MIS-TLIF is an effective technique for degenerative lumbar spine disorders with minimized adjacent segment complications.

## ID: AGS26/1668

## P0365: Correlation between preoperative sarcopenia and postoperative complications in adult scoliosis surgery

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement, Sahloul, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Adult scoliosis surgery carries substantial morbidity. Sarcopenia reduced skeletal muscle mass and function has been linked to adverse outcomes in various surgical fields, but its impact on adult spinal deformity surgery is underexplored. This study evaluates the correlation between preoperative sarcopenia and postoperative complications. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed adult patients (≥ 18 years) undergoing elective scoliosis correction between 2018 and 2023. Preoperative sarcopenia was assessed using CT or MRI measurements of paraspinal muscle cross-sectional area normalized for height. Postoperative complications within 90 days-including wound infection, neurological deficit, pulmonary complications, and reoperation-were recorded. Multivariate logistic regression analyzed the association between sarcopenia and complications. Results: Sixty patients were included; 35% had sarcopenia. Overall complication rate was 25%. Patients with sarcopenia had a higher complication rate than non-sarcopenic patients (30% vs 15%, p = 0.01). Multivariate analysis identified sarcopenia as an independent predictor of postoperative complications. Conclusion: Preoperative sarcopenia is significantly associated with increased postoperative complications in adult scoliosis surgery. Early identification of sarcopenic patients may allow targeted prehabilitation and perioperative optimization, potentially improving surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1687

## P0366: Post spinal surgery syndrome in patients with pre-existing neurological co-morbidities: a 5-year-old retrospective study of a single center

Maria Eleni Sakellaridou

1

, John Charalampous

1

, Athanasios Katsikas

1

, Georgios Rados

1

, Panagiotis Papadopoulos-Manolarakis

1

, Konstantinos Barkas

1

, Moshos Fratzoglou

1

, Panagiotis Papanikolaou

1

, Theofilos Paleologos

1

1

Neurosurgical Department, General Hospital of Nikea-Piraeus, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Post spinal surgery syndrome (PSSS), formerly known as failed back surgery syndrome, is a complex condition characterized by persistent pain and neurological symptoms after spinal surgery. With the ageing population and the continuously increasing incidence of neurological disorders, the patients with neurological co-morbidities have become a special sub-group of neurosurgical patients prone to post-operative complications. The aim of this study is to estimate the prevalence of PSSS in this specific patient sub-group and to identify possible prognostic factors. Material-Methods: This is a 5-year retrospective review of our department's database (01/2020-01/2025). 1564 patients that underwent elective spinal surgery at our department were included in the study. The patients’ sample was furthermore separated into two sub-groups: a group of 572 patients with pre-existing neurological co-morbidities (stroke, multiple sclerosis, spinal cord injury, dementia etc.) (Group A) and a group of 992 patients with no known pre-existing neurological history (Group B). The existence of PSSS was determined at the 1-month, 3-months and 6-months post-operative follow-up. Results: The overall PSSS prevalence was estimated at 13.6% (213 patients). 122 of the PSSS patients belonged to Group A, estimating the prevalence of PSSS of this sub-group at 21.3% (versus a 9.2% prevalence of Group B). Most common symptoms were persistent neuropathic pain and numbness. The most common predisposing factors were smoking, age over 63, the use of instrumentation and pre-existing spinal cord injury. Conclusion: PSSS is a worldwide complication that can affect the patients’ quality of life, especially in a population susceptible to further neurological deterioration such as patients with pre-existing neurological disorders. The careful patients’ selection, the meticulous pre-surgical planning and the elimination of predisposing factors such as smoking are advised to possibly improve the outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1690

## P0367: Post-operative calcification of cauda equina: a case report of a rare neurosurgical entity

Maria Eleni Sakellaridou

1

, Vasiliki Georgilaki

1

, Eftichia Simoglaki

1

, Tiffany Georgiou

1

, Angelos Leventis

1

1

Neurosurgical Department, General Hospital of Nikea-Piraeus, Athens, Greece

Introduction: The calcification of the cauda equina refers to the deposition of calcium in the nerve roots below the conus medullaris. It can lead to neurological deficits and even cauda equina syndrome. It is a very rare condition, and its prevalence is difficult to be estimated. The aim of this study is to showcase this uncommon cause of cauda equina syndrome and to highlight the importance of a prompt diagnosis. Case presentation: A 70-year-old male patient presented to the neurosurgical department due to paraparesis, sensory deficits and erectile dysfunction. His symptomatology started 6 years ago after a failed lumbar spinal fusion surgery, followed by post-operative infection and removal of the instrumentation. His symptoms worsened gradually. His clinical examination showed paresis of bilateral quadriceps (3/5) and 4-/5 of the rest of muscle groups of the lower extremities, severe sensory deficits, erectile dysfunction and incontinence, hyporeflexia of the lower extremities and lumbar pain. A CT and MRI of the lumbar region were performed revealing calcification of the cauda equina at L5 up to S2 level. Surgical decompression was performed with mild improvement of his neurological status. Discussion: The pathogenesis of the calcification of cauda equina is unclear. It is believed to be secondary to acute trauma and surgical scarring and the subsequent local lesions. Calcified cauda equina has been reported post-traumatically, post-operatively, in association with spinal tumors and spinal infections. The literature is very scarce and there is no indicated treatment. Decompression and removal of the ossifications when possible have been reported with various results. Conclusion: The calcified cauda equina is a very uncommon condition that, in many cases, needs surgical treatment. Due to its rarity, there is a need for further study of the pathophysiology of this entity.

## ID: AGS26/1692

## P0368: Does fat matter? Comparison of adjacent level paraspinal musculatures in patients who undergo extension fusion with those that do not in the lumbar spine

Jennyfer Paulla Galdino Chaves

1

, Brian Hsu

1

, Stone Sima

2

, Bhisham Singh

1

, Ashish Diwan

2

1

NSW Spine Specialist, Sydney, Australia

2

University of New South Wales, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: Fat infiltration of the paraspinal muscles reflect muscle atrophy and degeneration and has been known to lead to dysfunction of the low back. However, it is unclear if fat infiltration affects spinal surgical outcomes. This study aims to determine if post-operative degeneration of the paraspinal musculature is associated with the need for a subsequent extension of fusion in the lumbar spine. Material and Methods: This study was conducted as a retrospective cohort study of adult patients who underwent primary multi-level lumbar fusion that included the sacrum with or without further extension of fusion and had pre-operative and post-operative MRI scans conducted. Fat infiltration of the paraspinal muscles was measured at the superior adjacent segment level using the Goutallier T1W, T2W and Four Grade Fat classification. Patients were divided into extension of fusion (EOF) and non-extension of fusion (NOF) groups. Results: Of the 88 patients included in the study, 41 underwent extension of fusion. The age of the extension of fusion group was 11% higher than the NoF group (p < 0.05). The level of paraspinal fat infiltration in the adjacent segment following lumbar fusion as measured by the Goutallier T1W, Goutallier T2W and Four Grade Fat Classification was more severe in the MRI of patients in the EOF group compared to patients in the NOF group. Conclusion: Patients who required an extension of fusion following initial surgery had a higher level of fat infiltration in the paraspinal musculature of the adjacent segment level compared to patients who did not require extension. Therefore, we posit that muscle loss should be considered as one of the risks for adjacent segment disease and extension of fusion, necessitating the need for paraspinal muscle therapy post fusion.

## ID: AGS26/1716

## P0369: ‘Rebound phenomenon’ - A cause of early cage back-out in transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion surgery: insights from case series of 1545 patients

Sathish Muthu

1,2

, Guna Pradeep

1,3

, Jvaghar Jill

4

1

Department of Spine Surgery, Orthopaedic Research Group, Coimbatore, India

2

Department of Orthopaedics, Government Medical College, Karur, India

3

Department of Orthopaedics - Spine Division, Meenakshi Mission Hospital, Madurai, India

4

Institute of Orthopaedics & Traumatology, Rajiv Gandhi Government General Hospital, Madras Medical College, Chennai, India

Introduction: Factors like elderly age, low body mass index, placement of undersized cages, bony endplate damage and improper cage placement are several risk factors for cage back-out following TLIF surgeries. Despite this, lacunae remains regarding the other factors contributing to cage migration. We aim to assess the various possible causative factors of cage retropulsion and the management strategies to be utilized for cage back-out following open transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion(TLIF) surgery in degenerative lumbar spine pathology. Materials and Methods: We performed a retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data from patients who underwent TLIF surgery for degenerative disc disease with stenosis or instability between 2011 and 2024. We identified patients who presented with cage back-out and analysed the characteristics of these patients to elucidate the possible causes of cage retropulsion. Results: Among 1545 patients who underwent TLIF surgery, 16 patients (1%) experienced cage back-out. This included 11 single-level fusions and five two-level fusions, predominantly at the L4-L5 level. Eight patients had early cage back-out within 3 months, while four had back-out after 1 year. Eleven cases involved posterior migration, one case each for postero-lateral and anterolateral migration, respectively. Twelve patients were managed conservatively, whereas four required revision surgery. We identified inadequate disc space preparation and “rebound phenomenon” as significant risk factors for early back-out, necessitating revision for optimal outcomes. Late back-out may benefit from conservative management due to the potential for partial union, with surgery reserved for refractory cases or those with neurological symptoms. Conclusions: Cage migration is a recognized and severe complication in TLIF surgery, potentially impacting patient outcomes. Our study found that intraoperative rebound phenomenon and inadequate discectomy or endplate preparation were significant risk factors for cage migration. Surgeons should consider these factors and patient-specific variables to optimize clinical outcomes in TLIF patients.

## P0370: Radiographic measures of central obesity predict mechanical complications following posterior spinal fusion

Alex Hernandez

1

, Gabriel Gonzalez

1

, Manjot Singh

1

, Jinseong Kim

2

, Nicolas Carayannopoulos

1

, Zvipo Chisango

1

, Catherine Hurley

1

, Joseph Nassar

1

, Eren Kuris

2

, Alan Daniels

2

, Bassel Diebo

2

1

The Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University, Providence, United States

2

Brown University, Providence, United States

Introduction: Body mass index (BMI) is widely used to assess obesity in surgical patients; however, it fails to differentiate between fat and lean mass or account for fat distribution. In spine surgery, recent studies have investigated other radiographic markers to better capture central adiposity, which can more directly influence postoperative complications than BMI alone. This study investigates the predictive value of radiographically derived abdominal circumference (AC) and abdominal circumference to pelvis circumference (ACPC) ratio for adverse outcomes in orthopedic surgery. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort analysis was conducted on adults who underwent posterior spinal fusion between January 2022 and December 2023, and had computed tomography imaging and two-year follow-up data. AC and ACPC ratio were calculated using axial cross-sections at the L3 vertebral level. Postoperative complications were assessed, including 90-day wound-related events and 2-year mechanical complications (proximal junctional kyphosis [PJK], adjacent segment disease, instrumentation failure, and revision surgery). Receiver operating characteristic curve analyses were performed to evaluate the predictive performance of these measures. Results: Among 100 patients (mean age 64.6 ± 11.9 years; 64.0% female), wound-related complications occurred in 13.0%, and mechanical complications were observed in up to 12.1% of patients. BMI significantly predicted wound-related complications (AUC = 0.669, p = 0.017). In contrast, AC and ACPC ratio significantly predicted PJK (AUC = 0.776 and 0.765, respectively; p = 0.004), adjacent segment disease (AUC = 0.745 and 0.717, respectively; p ≤ 0.012), and revision surgery (AUC = 0.809 and 0.810, respectively; p ≤ 0.002). Optimal cutoff values associated with elevated revision risk were 118.3cm for abdominal circumference and 1.4 for the ACPC ratio. Conclusion: Radiographically-derived measures of central obesity, particularly AC and ACPC ratio, outperform BMI in predicting several mechanical complications following spinal fusion. These findings support the integration of CT-based adiposity assessments into preoperative planning, and more prospective studies are needed to better stratify surgical risk and improve postoperative outcomes

## ID: AGS26/1743

## P0371: Pain tolerance perceptions and postoperative opioid use in lumbar fusion: physician estimates outperform patient self-assessment

Marc Abdou

1

, William Karakash

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Hannah Licari

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: The opioid crisis in the United States has prompted reevaluation of postoperative pain management strategies, particularly in spine surgery where patients often present with chronic pain. Predicting which patients will have high opioid needs or severe postoperative pain remains a challenge. This study evaluated the relationship between patient self-reported pain tolerance, physician assessments, and postoperative outcomes following lumbar fusion. Material and Methods: This prospective cohort study included 30 patients undergoing single- or multi-level lumbar spine fusion at a single academic center. Exclusions were age < 18 years and surgery for infection, trauma, or malignancy. Prior to surgery, patients rated their own pain tolerance (0-10) and compared themselves to peers (0-4). Surgeons rated patient pain tolerance (0-10) and estimated postoperative opioid requirements (0-10). Outcomes included hospital length of stay, total opioid consumption, and cumulative pain scores over postoperative days 1-14. Correlations were assessed using Spearman’s rank; multivariate regression evaluated demographic and clinical predictors. Results: Mean age was 67 ± 13.7 years, 57% were female, and mean BMI was 30.4 ± 6.7. Physicians rated patient pain tolerance significantly lower than patients rated themselves (5.37 ± 2.34 vs. 6.67 ± 2.09; p = 0.027). Neither patient- nor physician-reported pain tolerance predicted opioid use, pain scores, or hospital stay. However, physician-estimated opioid needs correlated with actual opioid consumption (r = 0.484; p = 0.008) and cumulative pain scores (r = 0.442; p = 0.016). Patients who rated themselves as having higher pain tolerance than peers had longer hospital stays (r = 0.356; p = 0.047). No demographic or clinical variables were associated with opioid use or pain scores. Conclusion: Patient self-reported pain tolerance did not predict postoperative pain or opioid requirements after lumbar fusion. In contrast, physician-estimated opioid needs correlated with both analgesic use and pain scores, suggesting surgeons may have meaningful insight into postoperative pain trajectories. Incorporating physician judgment into preoperative planning may improve individualized opioid prescribing and optimize pain management strategies. This ongoing study will expand the cohort to validate these preliminary findings.

## ID: AGS26/1744

## P0372: Preoperative GLP-1 receptor agonist use and short term outcomes after lumbar decompression: a matched cohort study

Abdel Diab

1

, Omar Diab

1

, Reme Arhewoh

1

, Elinor Stern

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

, Joshua Piche

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Lumbar decompression is a common surgical intervention for symptomatic spinal stenosis and related conditions, aimed at alleviating pain and improving mobility. GLP-1 receptor agonists, known for their glucose-lowering, weight-reducing, and anti-inflammatory properties, are increasingly prescribed to patients who may also undergo surgery. However, their potential impact on surgical outcomes remains underexplored. This study investigates the association between GLP-1 receptor agonist use and surgical outcomes following lumbar decompression, focusing on short-term complications (45-day and 90-day outcomes). Material and Methods: Utilizing the TriNetX database spanning 101 healthcare organizations, we conducted a retrospective cohort analysis. Patients undergoing lumbar decompression (identified by CPT codes) between 2014 and 2023 were stratified into GLP-1 users and matched controls through propensity score matching (1:1). Matching controlled for demographics and comorbidities, including diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease. Outcome assessments focused on short-term complications at 45 and 90 days, including medical complications (sepsis, renal failure, MI, readmission) and mechanical complications (reoperation, wound disruption). Statistical analyses included risk ratios, risk differences, and p-values. Results: 7,002 patients were included in the study; 3,501 GLP-1 users and matched controls. Baseline characteristics were well-balanced between matched cohorts. At 45 days, GLP-1 users exhibited a lower rate of sepsis compared to controls (0.9% vs 1.5%; RR = 0.566, p = 0.011) and renal failure (2.5% vs 3.3%; RR = 0.748, p = 0.038). At 90 days, GLP-1 users had decreased risks of sepsis (1.1% vs 2.1%; RR = 0.541, p = 0.001), renal failure (3.1% vs 4.4%; RR = 0.712, p = 0.006), myocardial infarction (0.6% vs 1.0%; RR = 0.583, p = 0.046), and hospital readmissions (9.8% vs 11.3%; RR = 0.868, p = 0.043). Conclusion: Our findings suggest that preoperative GLP-1 receptor agonist use in lumbar decompression patients is associated with a reduced risk of several short-term medical complications. The decreased rates of sepsis, renal failure, myocardial infarction, and hospital readmissions suggest potential perioperative benefits. These results warrant further investigation into the broader impact of GLP-1 RAs on surgical outcomes and may inform perioperative management strategies for patients on these medications undergoing lumbar decompression.

## ID: AGS26/1765

## P0373: Correlation between lumbar disc herniation and facet joint inflammation: a comprehensive MRI-based cohort study of 303 patients

Halil Gok

1

, Alim Baymurat

2

, Abdulsamet Emet

1

, Hamisi Mraja

3

, Mustafa Taşar

4

1

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Ankara Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

2

Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Gazi University Medical Faculty, Ankara, Turkey

3

Scoliosis-Spine Center Istanbul, Istanbul Florence Nightingale Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

4

Ministry of Health University Gülhane Training and Research Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Low back pain (LBP) is a leading cause of disability worldwide, often arising from complex interactions within the spinal three-joint complex. Lumbar disc herniation (LDH) and facet joint (FJ) inflammation are common pain generators that frequently present with overlapping symptoms, complicating diagnosis and management. Although both entities have been extensively studied, the correlation between LDH severity and FJ inflammation remains poorly defined. This study aimed to investigate the relationship between LDH severity and FJ inflammatory changes using a large MRI-based cohort. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort of 303 patients (161 females, 142 males; mean age 45.5 ± 12.3 years) who underwent lumbar MRI for LBP between 2023 and 2024 was analyzed. LDH severity was graded using the Michigan State University (MSU) classification, which stratifies herniations by size and location. FJ inflammation was scored on a 0-3 scale incorporating joint effusion, bone marrow edema, and soft tissue edema. Two blinded radiologists independently evaluated all images. Level and side of LDH and FJ inflammation were recorded. Patients were stratified into two age groups: < 40 years (n = 140; age range 20-39) and ≥ 40 years (n = 163; age range 40-79) for subgroup analyses. Correlation analyses were performed using Spearman’s rho. Results: The most frequently affected levels were L4-L5 (44.6%) and L5-S1 (41.2%). LDH severity demonstrated a significant positive correlation with FJ inflammation score (ρ = 0.314, p < 0.001) and with the side of FJ inflammation (ρ = 0.276, p < 0.001). FJ inflammation score was strongly correlated with the side of inflammation (ρ = 0.945, p < 0.001). Edema was most frequently ipsilateral to the herniated disc, consistent with the observed correlation between mechanical compression and local inflammatory response. Age group analysis revealed no significant differences in LDH or FJ scores; however, weak negative correlations were observed between age and herniation level (ρ = -0.180, p = 0.002) as well as age and side of herniation (ρ = -0.120, p = 0.036). Conclusion: This study demonstrates that higher LDH severity is associated with greater ipsilateral FJ inflammation, supporting the hypothesis of biomechanical and inflammatory interaction between these structures.
