The findings highlight the importance of considering concomitant FJ pathology in patients with LDH to improve diagnostic accuracy and guide individualized management strategies for LBP.

## ID: AGS26/1843

## P0374: Exploring lumbar-hip muscle interactions with MRI-based quantification

Anna-Maria Mielke

1

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Bruno Verna

1

, Ali Guven

1

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Jennifer Shue

1

, Peter Sculco

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Alexander Hughes

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Hip and spine disorders commonly coexist and negatively affect patient function and surgical outcomes. While degeneration of paraspinal muscles has been extensively studied, considerably less is known about hip musculature and its contribution to spinal pathology. Currently, no standardized terminology exists to describe hip muscle quality. Establishing such a framework is clinically relevant, given the role of periarticular muscles not only in hip arthroplasty, and degenerative joint disease but also in spinal alignment and postoperative recovery. We hypothesized that hip muscle degeneration reflects region-specific and compensatory adaptations within the hip-spine axis, rather than a generalized systemic sarcopenic process. To evaluate this, we examined correlations between lumbar and hip muscle composition using MRI-based quantitative techniques, adapting established methods from paraspinal to hip musculature. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included patients who underwent spinal fusion at a single tertiary orthopedic center between 2017 and 2023. Institutional Review Board approval was obtained. Patients were eligible if both lumbar and pelvic MRI scans were available within one year preoperatively. Exclusion criteria included previous fusion, total hip arthroplasty, or inadequate imaging quality. Muscle quality was assessed using lumbar T2-weighted and hip proton density-weighted axial MRI. At L4, psoas, multifidus, and erector spinae were segmented. At the greater trochanter level, six hip muscles (gluteus minimus, medius, maximus, tensor fasciae latae, sartorius, rectus femoris) were analyzed. Measurements included cross-sectional area (CSA), functional CSA (fCSA), and fatty infiltration (FI). Bilateral values were summed. Spearman’s correlations were applied, with multiple comparisons adjusted via the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. Results: Of 143 screened patients, 31 met inclusion criteria (71% female; mean age 63.5 ± 10.0 years; BMI 25.9 ± 4.6). Moderate correlations were observed between lumbar and hip muscle degeneration. Erector spinae FI correlated negatively with sartorius FI (ρ = -0.508, p = 0.005) and rectus femoris FI (ρ = -0.483, p = 0.008). Multifidus FI was inversely related to tensor fasciae latae FI (ρ = -0.396, p = 0.019). In contrast, positive correlations were found between erector spinae and multifidus fCSA and gluteus minimus FI (ρ = 0.626, p < 0.05). However, after correction for multiple testing, none of these associations remained statistically significant. Conclusion: While no associations remained statistically significant after adjustment, directional trends suggest that lumbar and hip muscle degeneration may be interdependent rather than parallel. Negative correlations between paraspinal and hip FI may reflect compensatory recruitment, whereas positive associations between lumbar fCSA and hip FI point to regional load redistribution. These findings support the emerging concept of Hip-Specific Sarcopenia, emphasizing region-specific adaptations rather than generalized sarcopenia. Adapting MRI-based methods from paraspinal to hip musculature proved feasible and underscores the need for a standardized vocabulary to define hip muscle health. Future studies with larger cohorts should establish normative reference values, integrate alignment and gait analyses, and expand phenotyping across the hip-knee-spine axis. Such a framework may improve individualized surgical and rehabilitation strategies for patients with concurrent hip and spine disorders.

## ID: AGS26/1876

## P0375: Reconsideration of hyperintensity zones - Classification, distribution and their association with MRI phenotypes - A retrospective study

S. Rajasekaran

1

, Suhas Aradhya

1

, Ajoy P Shetty

1

, Rishi Kanna

2

, Sri Vijay Anand KS

1

1

Ganga Medical Centre and Hospital, Coimbatore, India

2

Ganga Hospital and Medical Centre, Coimbatore, India

Introduction: Hyperintensity zone (HIZ) are characterized as focal T2-weighted hyperintensities in the posterior annulus of the disc. Lumbar HIZ is related to a concordant pain response on discography and it is a significant Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) biomarker for the diagnosis of low back pain. Symptomatic HIZs are associated with a signal intensity of ≥ 50% of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). Histologically these zones are either due to presence of vascularized granulation tissue and resulting oedema or due to Mucoid fluids containing fat. There are two distinct types of HIZ’s one, Traditional T2-hyperintense and T1-hypointense known as “single-HIZ”, which indicate annular disruption and painful in presentation, two, T1- and T2-intense called “dual-HIZ” related to calcified or ossified tissue and non-painful in nature. Currently no standardized classification system for researchers to classify morphological/topographical traits of HIZ and most descriptions are based solely upon T2 weighted MRI. Coupling of T2W, T1W and FLASH MRI sequences useful to elaborate upon various HIZ phenotypes, and its correlation to endplate changes, which helps to shed light upon their clinical relevance and decision-making. Purpose of study is to find out distribution and specific patterns of HIZ. Methods: Ninety-five subjects presenting with HIZ in MRI were included in this study (58 male, 37 females; mean age 31 ± 10.30 years). The demographic profile of the eligible patients will be noted in a proforma including age, sex, BMI, and occupation. The MRI showing hyperintensity zones noted based on location as anterior, posterior, superior or inferior end plate, intra-protrusion, marginal. Level of involvement and whether involved in disease segment, end plate changes, Type based on MRI sequence documented. Any correlation in above parameters were statistically analysed. Results: The prevalence of HIZ in the overall lumbar spine were 47.3% and commonly found at L4/5 (32.5%) and L5/S1 (47.0%). T2W high -intensity type of HIZ was most prevalent (71.8%), followed by T1W low intensity (6.8%). Of all discs, round types were most prevalent (37.7%) followed by rim type (10.6%). At all affected levels, there was a significant association between HIZ and disc degeneration, disc bulge/protrusion and Modic type II (p < 0.01). Posterior HIZ and T2W high-intensity type of HIZ were significantly associate with disc bulge/protrusion and disc degeneration (p < 0.01). Conclusion: This study will bring out the advantage of coupling T1, T2 & FLASH weighted sequence which poorly discussed in the literature. Specific HIZ sub-phenotypes were found in specific MRI degenerative changes. The study data will influence the decisions in standardising the consideration of HIZ in fusion of lumbar levels.

## ID: AGS26/1899

## P0376: Outcomes of single and multilevel direct lumbar interbody fusion (DLIF) in a single Australian tertiary centre over 10 years

Ting Yoon Kwan

1

, Naquiya Murtaza Mohsin

1

, Arvind Dubey

1

, Arjun Dubey

1

, Nicholas Holona

1

, Lucas Chu

1

, Anjali Menon

1

, Kawya Jayasinghe

1

1

Royal Hobart Hospital, Hobart, Australia

Introduction: DLIF is a well-established surgical intervention for managing degenerative diseases of the lumbar spine. This approach is increasingly preferred due to its ability to insert large interbody cages while avoiding injury to critical blood vessels, minimising muscle disruption, and requiring only a small incision. Despite its growing popularity, long-term outcomes and effectiveness remain underexplored in the literature. This study aims to address this gap by presenting the outcomes of patients who underwent single-level and multilevel DLIF at a tertiary centre in Tasmania over the past 10 years. Method: This is a retrospective cohort study that includes all adult elective DLIF cases done from July 2014 to September 2024 at the Royal Hobart Hospital by a single neurosurgeon. Demographic data (age, gender, BMI), comorbidities(smoking status, diabetes), operative details(number of levels fused, indication and the use of self-locking mechanism), and outcomes were extracted from clinical records. Radiographical fusion status was determined by reviewing XRs or CTs of the lumbar spine that were taken at least 6 months after the operation. Early (< 30 days) and late (> 30 days) post-operative complications were recorded. Fisher’s exact test was used to calculate the odds ratio (OR) for fusion according to smoking, diabetes and single- vs multi-level surgery. Logistic regression was attempted to assess the independent predictors of the fusion. Confidence intervals (95% CI) for proportions were calculated using Wald methods. Result: The mean age of the cohort was 63.7 years, with a mean BMI of 28.7. The majority of the patients were female (71.4%). Smokers and patients with diabetes were present in 28.6% and 20% of the cohort. Single-level procedures accounted for 30% of the cases, with the remainder undergoing multilevel DLIFs. Overall, fusion was achieved in 91.4% of the patients. Fusion rates in non-smokers are higher than in smokers (93% vs. 86%), but the difference is not statistically significant (OR 0.26, p = 0.48). The fusion rates for single-level vs. multilevel DLIFs did not show any significant difference. Logistic regression did not identify any independent predictors of fusion rates. Early complications (< 30 days) occurred in 20% of the cohort and are most commonly wound infection. 10% of the cohort developed late complications (> 30 days) such as an instrumental complication. Smoking status and level of operation did not cause any significant difference in complication rate. Also, the fusion rates and complication rates of self-locking vs non-self-locking groups did not have statistically significant differences. Conclusion: DLIF remains a promising option for managing degenerative lumbar spine disease in Tasmania, as demonstrated by high radiographic fusion rates and complication rates consistent with global data. While no independent risk factors for fusion success were identified due to sample size limitations, the results highlight the procedure’s overall effectiveness. Given these limitations, further studies with larger sample sizes are needed to identify potential risk factors for fusion and post-operative complications, helping to optimise patient selection and improve DLIF outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1917

## P0377: Elevated lumbar subcutaneous fat tissue thickness and Schmorl’s nodes in patients with total hip arthroplasty

Bahadır Balkanlı

1

, Ali Ekrem Adiyaman

2

, Murat Ekşi

3

1

Sisli Hamidiye Etfal Training and Research Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

2

University of Health Sciences, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Istinye University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Total hip arthroplasty (THA) is a standard treatment for advanced hip osteoarthritis, and patients requiring THA often present with coexisting degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. The interdependence of hip and spine pathology (sometimes described as a hip-spine syndrome) suggests that altered biomechanics or shared risk factors (e.g. obesity) could link these conditions. However, it remains unclear whether individuals with THA exhibit distinct lumbar degenerative features compared to those without THA. This study aimed to compare lumbar spine degenerative changes and tissue characteristics between THA patients and matched controls without THA. Material and Methods: We retrospectively compared 131 patients with THA to 128 age- and sex-matched individuals without THA. All subjects had lumbosacral spine magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) available for analysis. MRI scans were assessed for subcutaneous fat tissue thickness at each intervertebral level from L1-L2 through L5-S1, the presence of Schmorl’s nodes at these levels, Modic changes, intervertebral disc degeneration grades, and paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration scores (multifidus, erector spinae, and psoas muscles). Group differences were analyzed using appropriate statistical tests (non-parametric tests for continuous variables; chi-square/Fisher’s exact tests for categorical variables). Multiple comparisons across levels were adjusted with a false discovery rate correction. Results: The THA and control groups were similar in age and sex distribution. Patients with THA demonstrated significantly greater subcutaneous fat tissue thickness at all lumbar levels compared to controls (mean thickness range 22.7-39.0 mm in THA patients vs 9.07-24.1 mm in controls across L1-S1; adjusted p < 0.001 at each level). Schmorl’s nodes were also more prevalent in the THA group, with significantly higher rates of Schmorl’s nodes at the L1-2, L2-3, L3-4, L4-5 and L5-S1 levels in THA patients than in controls (all adjusted p < 0.05). By contrast, there were no statistically significant differences between groups in the presence of Modic changes at any lumbar level, the grades of intervertebral disc degeneration, or the paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration scores. Conclusion: THA patients exhibited greater lumbar subcutaneous fat tissue thickness and more Schmorl’s nodes than matched controls, with no differences in Modic changes, disc degeneration, or paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration. These associations may reflect shared systemic factors (adiposity/metabolic dysregulation, reduced bone quality) or long-standing spinopelvic/gait adaptations that increase endplate loading. Recognizing these differences may help inform comprehensive management of spine health in patients undergoing hip arthroplasty.

## ID: AGS26/2141

## P0378: Surgical management of symptomatic Bertolotti syndrome: operative experience and clinical follow-up of three patients

Álvaro Silva

1

, Joaquin Zuniga Soria

1,2

, Andrea Marre

2

, Manuel Valencia

1

, Nicolas Bifani

2

1

Clínica Alemana de Santiago, Santiago, Chile

2

Facultad de Medicina Universidad del Desarrollo-Clínica Alemana, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Bertolotti syndrome (BS) is an uncommon but clinically relevant cause of chronic low back pain, associated with a lumbosacral transitional vertebra (LTV) in which the transverse process of L5 articulates with the sacrum or ilium. Diagnosis requires correlation of clinical and radiological findings, and is confirmed by selective injection of local anesthetic with corticosteroids into the transitional joint. For patients refractory to conservative treatment, surgical resection of the symptomatic mega-transverse process has been described as a therapeutic option. Objective: To report a series of three patients with Castellvi type IIa BS managed surgically with resection of the symptomatic transverse process, including operative details and clinical outcomes. Methods: All patients presented with symptomatic BS, confirmed by diagnostic injection and absence of significant disc degeneration or adjacent osteoarthritis. Resection of the symptomatic transverse process was performed via a Wiltse-type approach, with intraoperative navigation in two cases. Surgical complications, pain relief, functional outcomes, and return to baseline activities were evaluated with a minimum follow-up of six months. Results: No intraoperative or postoperative complications occurred. All three patients experienced complete pain resolution, functional recovery, and return to previous activities, with no recurrence during follow-up. Discussion and Conclusion: Our findings support surgical resection as an effective treatment for carefully selected patients with symptomatic BS, particularly in the absence of disc degeneration, adjacent osteoarthritis, or Castellvi type III fusion. Proper case selection and use of intraoperative navigation enable precise resection with low morbidity. Larger prospective studies with long-term follow-up are warranted to further define the role of surgery in BS management.

## ID: AGS26/2213

## P0379: Pelvic morphotype influence on surgical outcomes of TLIF vs PLF in degenerative spondylolisthesis: toward a personalized approach

Hadj Mohamed Slim

1

, Achref Abdennadher

1

, Youssef Mallat

1

, Ayari Rabie

1

, Khaled Khlil0

1

, Elyes Zoghlami

1

, Znagui Talel

1

, Khalil Amri

1

1

Military Hospital Tunis, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinopelvic parameters-namely pelvic incidence (PI), pelvic tilt (PT), and sacral slope (SS)-play a critical role in sagittal balance and influence surgical strategy in degenerative spondylolisthesis. Pelvic morphotype, defined by the dynamic relationship among these parameters (anteverted vs retroverted pelvis), may affect outcomes depending on the fusion technique employed. This study investigates whether pelvic morphotype correlates with differential outcomes following transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) versus postero-lateral fusion (PLF). Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on patients operated for single-level degenerative spondylolisthesis (L4-L5 or L5-S1) between 2017and 2023. Patients were stratified by surgical technique (TLIF vs PLF) and preoperative pelvic morphotype (anteverted: PI < 45°, SS > 35°, PT < 15°; retroverted: PI > 60°, SS < 30°, PT > 20°). Clinical outcomes were assessed using Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores at 12 months postoperatively. Radiological parameters included segmental lordosis gain and fusion rate. Multivariate regression was used to adjust for potential confounders. Results: A total of 70 patients (36 TLIF, 34 PLF) were included. In the retroverted subgroup, TLIF demonstrated superior outcomes compared to PLF in terms of ODI improvement (-18.7 vs -17points, p < 0.0001), segmental lordosis restoration (+3.2° vs +00°, p < 0.001), and fusion rate (91.7% vs 85.3%, p < 0.05). In anteverted morphotypes, clinical and radiological outcomes did not significantly differ between techniques. Higher preoperative PT was a strong predictor of improved sagittal alignment with TLIF (β = 52.2, p < 0.01). Conclusion: Pelvic morphotype significantly influences the biomechanical and clinical outcomes of fusion techniques in degenerative spondylolisthesis. TLIF provides greater sagittal correction and clinical benefit in patients with a retroverted pelvis. These findings support a morphotype-driven surgical planning paradigm to optimize technique selection and long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2223

## P0380: Degenerative spondylolisthesis grade 1: is fixation necessary?

Hadj Mohamed Slim

1

, Achref Abdennadher

1

, Ayari Rabie

1

, Khaled Khlil0

1

, Elyes Zoghlami

1

, Youssef Mallat

1

, Znagui Talel

1

, Khalil Amri

1

1

Military Hospital Tunis, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Degenerative spondylolisthesis is a common condition in the elderly, often presenting with chronic low back pain, with or without radiculopathy. The therapeutic approach, especially the indication for spinal fixation in stage 1, remains debated. This study aims to evaluate the necessity of fixation in such cases. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective comparative study on patients operated for grade 1 degenerative spondylolisthesis between 2017 and 2022. Outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for pain, the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) for function, and postoperative complications at 6 and 18 months. Results: A total of 27 patients (mean age: 64.6 years; mean follow-up: 2.6 years) were included. Isolated low back pain was present in 14.8% of cases, while 85.2% had associated radicular symptoms. Patients were divided into two groups: Group A (n = 18) underwent spinal fixation. Group B (n = 9) underwent decompression only. At 18 months, both groups showed significant clinical improvement. VAS scores improved from 7.05 to 2.88 (Group A) and from 7.55 to 3.55 (Group B) (p = 0.82). ODI scores improved from 18.38 to 7.83 (Group A) and from 17.88 to 8.00 (Group B) (p = 0.71). There was no statistically significant difference between the groups. Symptom recurrence was higher in Group B (22.2%) than in Group A (16.6%), but this difference was also not significant (p = 1). Conclusion: While fixation in grade 1 degenerative spondylolisthesis may offer better functional outcomes and a lower recurrence rate, the differences are not statistically significant. A tailored approach based on patient-specific factors remains crucial. Further prospective studies are needed to clarify the optimal indications for fixation in this setting.

## ID: AGS26/2316

## P0381: Utility of the L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) for assessment of sagittal alignment of the lumbar spine following short segment fusion: comparison to segmental measures

Zvipo Chisango

1

, Catherine Hurley

1

, Gabriel Gonzalez

1

, Michael Farias

1

, Nicolas Carayannopoulos

1

, Joseph P.

1

, Manjot Singh

1

, Jinseong Kim

2

, Bryce Basques

2

, Eren Kuris

2

, Bassel Diebo

2

, Alan Daniels

2

1

The Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University, Providence, United States

2

Brown University, Providence, United States

Introduction: The Lumbar Pelvic Angle (L1PA) was introduced as a convenient intraoperative and postoperative measure of sagittal alignment. However, the utility of L1PA for short segment lumbar fusions remains incompletely understood. This study investigates the relationship between L1PA and segmental parameters of spinal alignment for short segment lumbar fusions. Material and Methods: This retrospective analysis was conducted on adult patients with degenerative conditions undergoing primary spinal fusion surgery between L4 and S1. Patients with < 5° change in L1PA from pre- to postoperative measurements were stratified into two groups: those (1) achieving > 35° of regional lordosis at L4-S1 postoperatively and (2) those with > 5% change in Lumbar Distribution Index (LDI). Spinopelvic parameters were compared between preoperative and postoperative measurements. Results: The study cohort included 539 patients, of which 360 (66.8%) had an L1PA change less than < 5° pre- to postoperatively. Patients in both Group 1 and 2 had significant increases in the L5-S1 segmental angle (both p < 0.001) without a significant change at the L4-L5 segment. In Group 2, there was a significant increase in PI-LL mismatch (p < 0.001). In both groups there was a significant increase in L4-S1 lordosis, a relaxation in L1-L4 lordosis (both p < 0.001), and no significant change in total LL. Conclusion: This investigation assessed L1PA in over 500 patients undergoing lumbar fusion between L4 and S1 and found that despite value to the measure, important changes to spinal shape and segmental alignment may occur even in the face of Δ L1PA < 5°. As such, L1PA serves as a potentially useful adjunct to clinical assessment and radiographic measurement in lumbar degenerative fusion but should not serve as a stand-alone measurement.

## ID: AGS26/2320

## P0382: Effect of transforaminal lumbar interbody cage position for patients with coronal disc asymmetry

Daniel Alsoof

1

, Nicolas Carayannopoulos

2

, Gabriel Gonzalez

2

, Manjot Singh

2

, Zvipo Chisango

2

, Michael Farias

2

, Catherine Hurley

2

, Alex Hernandez

2

, Joseph Nassar

2

, Jinseong Kim

1

, Bryce Basques

1

, Bassel Diebo

1

, Eren Kuris

1

, Alan Daniels

1

1

Brown University, Providence, United States

2

The Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University, Providence, United States

Introduction: Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) can maintain intervertebral height and lordosis while providing disc space bony fusion. Despite its strengths, the standard placement of TLIF cages in the center of the intervertebral disc has been found to lead to worse radiological outcomes in patients with greater severity of coronal imbalance. The aim of this study is to investigate the effect of a lateralized TLIF placement, in the lateral third of the disc space, as a method to achieve coronal plane correction. Material and Methods: Adults undergoing TLIF at a large academic institution were identified. Segmental, L1-S1 cobb angle, and L1-S1 lordosis was measured at baseline and on follow up. Cage placement was classified as lateralized (placement of cage in the central third of disc space) or standard (placement in the lateral third of disc space) by assessing AP radiographs at 6-weeks. Multivariate linear regression was used to investigate the association between lateralized vs standard lumbar cage placement and postoperative cobb angle correction, to perform sensitivity analysis on patients who received single level TLIF at L4-L5, to investigate the effect of cage placement on L4-L5 cobb angle and L1-S1 cobb angle. Additionally, the association between symmetry of cage placement and correction of L1-S1 lordosis was assessed. Effect of symmetric vs asymmetric cage placement on incidence of cage subsidence and rate of pseudoarthrosis was evaluated using chi-square analysis. Results: 501 patients with single-level lumbar fusion were included with mean age 61.2 years, mean CCI 2.69, and 55.0% female. Preoperatively, mean L1-S1 cobb angle was 4.4°, and postoperative cobb angle was 3.8°. Preoperative mean L1-S1 lordosis was 49.46°, and postoperative lordosis had a mean of 48.9°. In this cohort, 108 (21.6%) patients received lateralized cages. Multivariable linear regression model controlling for age, gender, BMI, and CCI revealed that lateralized cage placement was associated with greater correction of L1-S1 Cobb angle (3.93° vs 3.15°, p = 0.014). Assessment of cobb angle correction only in patients fused at L4-L5 showed a correlation between lateralized cage placement and increased postoperative L1-S1 Cobb angle correction (3.77° vs 2.75°, p = 0.025). Assessment of association between cage placement location and L4-L5 Cobb angle correction showed that lateralized cage placement was associated with increased cobb angle correction (2.8° vs 2.08°, p = 0.033). Assessment of lordosis correction showed no significant association with cage location (- 0.99° in the lateralized group vs. - 0.73° in the standard group, p = 0.819). Chi square analysis of cage placement and incidence of subsidence in L4-L5 fusion patients found a higher rate of subsidence in lateralized placed cages (9.5% asymmetric vs 2.1% symmetric, p = 0.05). There was no statistically significant difference in the rate of pseudoarthrosis between groups (5.2% lateralized vs 5.3% standard; p = 0.98). Conclusion: Lateralized TLIF cage placement may provide greater coronal plane correction in patients with segmental disc asymmetry when compared to standard cage placement and appears to be achieved without compromising sagittal correction. These results may assist with optimized cage placement in patients with asymmetric lumbar disc collapse.

## ID: AGS26/2329

## P0383: Radiographic and clinical outcomes following L5-S1 anterior lumbar interbody fusion: the ALIF learning curve

Maxwell Sahhar

1

, Manjot Singh

1

, Derrick Kang

1

, Jinseong Kim

2

, Rhea Rasquinha

1

, Joseph Nassar

1

, Michael Farias

1

, Zvipo Chisango

1

, Nicolas Carayannopoulos

1

, Eren Kuris

2

, Todd Stafford

2

, Bassel Diebo

2

, Alan Daniels

2

1

The Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University, Providence, United States

2

Brown University, Providence, United States

Introduction: Over the course of their careers, spine surgeons refine patient selection, surgical planning, and intraoperative workflow. Radiographically, they may also achieve greater disc height and segmental lordosis correction, improving global sagittal alignment. However, whether this is the case in degenerative lumbar disc disease patients undergoing L5-S1 anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) has not previously been studied. Material and Methods: Adult patients who underwent L5-S1 ALIF with or without posterior fixation for degenerative disc disease between June 2017 and December 2024 by a single surgeon were included. Patients were stratified into Early (from licensing to December 2020), Middle (January 2021 to December 2022), and Recent (January 2023 to December 2024). Demographics, radiographic alignment, in-hospital outcomes, and 2-year complication and reoperation rates were compared by career stage. Multivariate logistic and linear regression adjusted for age, sex, BMI, comorbidities, prior fusion, and posterior instrumentation was conducted to assess the effect of continuous surgeon experience. Results: A total of 203 ALIFs were performed (mean age 57.6 years, 50.7% female, mean Charlson Comorbidity Index 2.1). Recent cases showed greater PT reduction (Early = 0.9°, Middle = -1.5°, Recent = -2.2°, p = 0.039), improved PI-LL mismatch correction (-0.4°, -4.8°, -5.4°, p = 0.007), higher L5-S1 lordotic correction (6.7°, 8.4°, 11.4°, p = 0.003), lower blood loss (21.9 mL, 13.8 mL, 10.0mL, p = 0.006), shorter OR time (107.4 min, 86.6 min, 75.2 min, p < 0.001), and fewer mechanical complications (39.3% vs 13.7%, p < 0.001) and reoperations (10.7% vs 2.1%, p = 0.023). Regression showed that each additional year of experience predicted improved alignment, lower blood loss and OR time, and reduced odds of complications (OR = 0.54, p < 0.001) and reoperations (OR = 0.49, p = 0.015). Conclusion: Surgeon experience was associated with improved sagittal alignment, in-hospital outcomes, and reduced odds of complication and reoperation following L5-S1 ALIF.

## ID: AGS26/2332

## P0384: Cage subsidence after L4-L5 TLIF: effect of overcorrecting segmental lordosis

Nicolas Carayannopoulos

1

, Manjot Singh

1

, Gabriel Gonzalez

1

, Joseph Nassar

1

, Michael Farias

1

, Zvipo Chisango

1

, Lachlan Kirby

1

, Caden Moenning

1

, Catherine Hurley

1

, Jinseong Kim

2

, John Czerwein

2

, Eren Kuris

2

, Bryce Basques

2

, Renaud Lafage

3

, Alan Daniels

2

, Bassel Diebo

2

1

The Warren Alpert Medical School of Brown University, Providence, United States

2

Brown University, Providence, United States

3

Lenox Hill Hospital, New York City, United States

Introduction: Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine can contribute to loss of segmental and regional lordosis, which may be associated with low back pain, radiculopathy, fatigue, and muscle imbalances from postural instability. Proper restoration of lordotic curvature is a primary goal of surgical intervention through fusion approaches such as transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF). This study evaluates the impact of lordotic correction achieved by TLIF on postoperative radiographic and clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: Adults who underwent TLIF at the L4-L5 level were identified. L4-L5 lordosis and disc heights were measured at baseline and at the 6-week, 1-year, and 2-year follow-ups. Cage subsidence was defined as loss of disc height of 2mm or more. Association between surgical lordotic correction and postoperative loss of disc height was evaluated using linear regression analysis. Association between changes in L4-L5 lordosis angles and presence of radiographic postoperative cage subsidence at each timepoint was evaluated using repeated-measures ANOVA. Impact of preoperative lordosis angle and degree of surgical lordotic correction on postoperative subsidence was evaluated using binary logistic regression. Results: In total, 142 patients with 2-year follow-up were included with a mean age of 60.0 years, mean CCI of 2.5, and 63.3% of female sex. Preoperatively, mean L4-L5 lordosis was 13.1° and mean L4-L5 disc height was 10.9 mm. Postoperatively, mean L4-L5 lordosis was 14.9° and mean L4-L5 disc height was 13.4 mm at 6 weeks, 14.2° and 12.7 mm at 1 year, and 13.6° and 11.9 mm at 2 years. In this cohort, 39 (27%) patients developed cage subsidence. Multivariable linear regression model controlling for age, BMI, and sex revealed that greater L4-L5 lordosis restoration was associated with greater disc height loss at 1 year (β = 0.091, p < 0.001). Repeated-measures ANOVA showed a visit-dependent correlation between L4-L5 lordotic changes and the presence of radiographic subsidence (p < 0.001). Mean preoperative L4-L5 lordosis was 12.7° in subsided patients and 13.4° in non-subsided patients. Non-subsided patients showed stable or slightly increased L4-L5 lordosis after surgery (6-week = 14.0°, 1-year = 15.2°, 2-year = 14.9°), while subsided patients showed notable postoperative loss of lordosis (6-week = 15.9°, 1-year = 13.1°, 2-year = 12.4°). Binary logistic regression showed that preoperative lordosis angle was not correlated with the development of postoperative subsidence ((p = 0.279) but degree of surgical lordotic correction was correlated (p = 0.012). Conclusion: Overcorrection of segmental lumbar during L4-L5 TLIF predisposes patients to disc height loss and cage subsidence. Further investigation is warranted to determine how long-term alignment may be optimized during interbody fusion procedures.

## ID: AGS26/2347

## P0385: Neuromodulation for Postoperative Low Back Pain After Lumbar Spine Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with Trial Sequential Analysis and Meta-Regression

Plamen Penchev

1

, Daniela Milanova-Ilieva

2

, Lyubomir Gaydarski

3

, Dobromir Dimitrov

4,5

, Boris Tablov

6

, Noor Husain

7

, Remzi Hyusein

8

,Cristian Vladimirov

8

, Petar-Preslav Petrov

9

, Andrey Velev

10

, Pavel Stanchev

11

, Nikolay Ramadanov

12,13

1

Faculty of Medicine, Medical University of Plovdiv, Plovdiv, Bulgaria

2

Department of Pediatrics, St.George’s University Hospital, Plovdiv, Bulgaria

3

Department of Anatomy, Histology and Embryology, Medical University of Sofia, Sofia, Bulgaria

4

Department of Surgical Propedeutics, Faculty of Medicine, Medical Univesity of Pleven, Pleven, Bulgaria

5

Center of competence in personalized medicine, 3D and telemedicine, robotoic assisted and minimally invasive surgery – “Leonardo da Vinci”, Pleven, Bulgaria

6

Department of Anesthesiology and Intensive Care, Multiprofile Hospital for Active Treatment “Life Hospital”, Burgas, Bulgaria

7

Department of Pharmacology, Indira Gandhi Institute of Medical Sciences, Patna, India

8

Faculty of Medicine, Medical University of Sofia, Sofia, Bulgaria

9

Department of Anatomy, Histology and Embryology, Medical University of Plovdiv, Plovdiv, Bulgaria

10

Hisar Military Sanatorium, Military Medical Academy, Bulgaria

11

Clinic of Endocrinology and Metabolic Diseases, St. George’s University Hospital, Medical University of Plovdiv

12

Center of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Brandenburg Medical School, University Hospital Brandenburg, Brandenburg an der Havel, Germany

13

Faculty of Health Science Brandenburg, Brandenburg Medical School Theodor Fontane, Brandenburg an der Havel, Germany

Introduction: Acute and chronic postoperative low back pain are frequent complications after lumbar spine surgery, contributing to long-term disability and increased analgesic use. Neuromodulation techniques-both invasive and non-invasive-have emerged as promising non-pharmacological interventions. However, current evidence remains fragmented. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of neuromodulation in managing acute and chronic postoperative low back pain. Materials and Methods: We systematically searched PubMed, Scopus, and Cochrane databases up to 30 June 2025 for randomized controlled trials comparing neuromodulation to sham modulation in patients with postoperative low back pain following lumbar spine surgery. Primary outcomes included pain intensity reduction, analgesic consumption, functional disability, and adverse effects. Standardized mean differences (SMDs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for continuous outcomes were calculated using a frequentist random-effects model. Proportions and 95% CIs for binary outcomes, including adverse events and sex distribution, were computed using the Freeman-Tukey double arcsine transformation to stabilize variance. Heterogeneity was assessed using the I2 statistic and Cochran’s Q test. Results: Twelve RCTs involving 413 patients (mean age 53 years; 55% female) were included, with 207 (50%) receiving neuromodulation. In the pooled analysis, neuromodulation significantly reduced pain intensity (SMD -0.82; 95% CI [-1.13, -0.50]; p < 0.00001; I2 = 53%) and analgesic consumption (SMD -0.91; 95% CI [-1.63, -0.20]; p = 0.01; I2 = 81%) compared to sham. No significant difference was found in functional disability (SMD -0.31; 95% CI [-0.85, 0.24]; p = 0.28; I2 = 44%). The pooled female proportion was 55% (95% CI [42.99, 66.95]; I2 = 73%), and the prevalence of adverse effects was 30.99% (95% CI [11.97, 53.80]; I2 = 77%), with higher rates observed in patients with chronic pain. Conclusion: This meta-analysis demonstrates that neuromodulation significantly reduces postoperative pain and opioid consumption after lumbar spine surgery, although it does not improve functional disability. Given the growing need for non-pharmacological pain management strategies, neuromodulation represents a promising evidence-based adjunct in postoperative care. Future high-quality RCTs should refine optimal protocols and evaluate long-term functional outcomes.
Keywords: neuromodulation, postoperative low back pain, FBSS, lumbar spine surgery

## ID: AGS26/2378

## P0386: Clinical outcomes after emergency surgical decompression for cauda equina syndrome (CES): is obesity a determining factor?

Riaz Mohammed

1

, Rhiannon Frostick

1

, Hui-Ling Kerr

1

, Alex Torrie

1

1

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

Background: An elevated body mass index (BMI) has been shown as an associated risk factor for the spinal emergency Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES). However not much evidence exists for association between outcome of CES surgery and obesity. High BMI is linked to higher complications from lumbar spine surgery, and clinical outcomes can be affected by these adverse events. Our retrospective cohort study compared patient related outcome measures (PROMs) between obese (BMI > 30 kg/m2) and non-obese patients after surgical decompression for CES. Methods: Review of 32 consecutive patients undergoing surgery for CES in a single spinal unit between October 2021 and May 2024 were included. Baseline demographics, length of stay and perioperative complication data were collected from the National Health Service (NHS) Hospital Episode Statistics (HES) data. The British Spine Registry (BSR) provided prospective PROMs data including both EQ-5D and ODI scores. SPSS 29 was used to analyse data with significance accepted at p < 0.05. Results: 19 Female:13 Male patients were included with a median age of 61.5 (29-83) years. 13 (41%) patients were obese (8F:5M). Obese patients presenting with CES were younger than non-obese patients (p = 0.027). Multiple medical comorbidities correlated with advancing age (p = 0.03) in our study group. Significant residual post-operative pain presented in 13 (41%) patients and was associated with younger age (p = 0.018) but not obesity. At 6-month follow-up, the mean EQ-5D and ODI in the obese were worse than the non-obese, but not statistically significant (p = 0.61 and p = 0.61 respectively). There was no significant difference in 30-day complication rates (p = 0.54) or operative length (p = 0.59) in the obese vs non-obese groups. Conclusions: Obese patients presenting with CES at a younger age tended to have reduced length of stays, but poorer clinical outcomes as observed by higher residual ODI and lower EQ-5D scores at both 6-months and 1 year when compared to non-obese patients. Complication rates or length of operation do not significantly differ between obese and non-obese patients. Surgeons and patients can utilise these findings in shared decision-making process and determining realistic expectations of surgical outcomes in CES.

## ID: AGS26/2406

## P0387: Association of GLP-1 receptor agonist use with risk of lumbar degenerative disease and spine surgery in patients with type 2 diabetes

Yu Chang

1

, Hong-Min Lin

2

1
National Cheng Kung University Hospital, Tainan, Taiwan, 
2
Chi-Mei Medical Center, Tainan, Taiwan
Introduction: This study aimed to evaluate whether treatment with glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs) is associated with a reduced risk of lumbar degenerative disease (LDD) and lumbar spine surgery in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). Materials and Methods: Data were obtained from the TriNetX Global Collaborative Network. Adult patients with T2DM who initiated GLP-1 RA therapy were matched 1:1 with non-users based on demographics, comorbidities, and concomitant medication use. Patients with pre-existing LDD or prior lumbar spine surgery were excluded. The primary outcome was the incidence of LDD, and the secondary outcome was lumbar spine surgery. Outcomes were assessed at 3- and 5-year intervals. Odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated. Results: A total of 196,435 matched pairs were analyzed. At 3 years, GLP-1 RA users had a lower incidence of LDD compared with non-users (11.9% vs. 13.3%; OR 0.89, 95% CI 0.87-0.91). At 5 years, the difference was more pronounced (14.6% vs. 18.7%; OR 0.74, 95% CI 0.73-0.74). Lumbar spine surgery rates were similar at 3 years (0.4% in both groups; OR 1.03, 95% CI 0.93-1.14), but significantly lower among GLP-1 RA users at 5 years (0.5% vs. 0.6%; OR 0.86, 95% CI 0.78-0.92). Conclusions: Use of GLP-1 RAs was associated with a reduced long-term risk of developing LDD and requiring lumbar spine surgery in patients with T2DM. These findings suggest that GLP-1 RAs may have potential as disease-modifying agents for spinal health.

## ID: AGS26/2415

## P0388: Frailty Index as a predictor of outcomes in lumbar fusion surgery

Mustafa Kavcar

1

, Baran Can Alpergin

1

1

Etlik City Hospital Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Frailty is increasingly recognized as a predictor of poor surgical outcomes, yet its impact in elective lumbar fusion remains underexplored. This study evaluates frailty index as a determinant of perioperative risk and long-term recovery in lumbar fusion patients. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 126 patients undergoing posterior lumbar fusion (2022-2025). Frailty index was calculated using a 40-item deficit accumulation model. Outcomes included perioperative complications, length of stay, and postoperative ODI and SF-36 scores. Subgroup analysis stratified patients as robust, pre-frail, and frail. Results: Frailty strongly correlated with complications (frail: 38% vs robust: 12%, p < 0.001) and prolonged hospital stay (mean 9 vs 5 days, p = 0.002). At 1 year, robust patients achieved greater ODI and SF-36 improvements (p < 0.01). Pre-frail patients demonstrated intermediate outcomes, highlighting a gradient effect. Frailty remained an independent predictor after multivariate adjustment. Conclusion: Frailty index is a valuable predictor of outcomes in lumbar fusion surgery. Routine frailty screening can enhance risk stratification and guide perioperative optimization, improving recovery trajectories in vulnerable patients.

## ID: AGS26/2429

## P0389: Bone resorption around the annular closure deviceduring a postoperative follow-up of 8 years

Abdugafur Sanginov

1

, Alexey Peleganchuk

1

1

Novosibirsk Research Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedics named after Y.L.Tsivyan, Novosibirsk, Russian Federation

Study Design: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data. Objective: To identify the characteristics of bone resorption around the ACD. Summary of Background Data: ACD implantation is considered to be an effective means of preventing reherniation after microdiscectomy; however, there is an issue: bone may resorb around the ACD. The causes of vertebral bone resorption remain unexplored; no cases have been assessed the dynamics of changes in bone resorption around the ACD and it characteristics. Methods: 133 patients underwent ACD implantation after microdiscectomy, 61 of them were followed up for 8 years after surgery (Oswestry, VAS). Lumbar CT scans helped characterize the bone resorption area around the ACD. Results: The median of follow-up was 85 [74; 93] months (from 73 to 105 months). The prevalence of bone resorption around the ACD was up to 63.5%, mainly around the polymer mesh of the ACD (70.0%). The resorbed bone volume increased with time and reached its maximum of 5.2 cm3 (12% of the vertebral body volume) once a sclerotic rim developed around the bone resorption area. No differences in VAS pain intensity or in Oswestry Disability Index were found between patients with and without resorption (p > 0.05). The volume of the intervertebral disc before surgery is a predictor of bone resorption (OR = 0.79, p = 0.009): if it is less than 13.2 cm3, the risk of bone resorption significantly increases (p < 0.05). Conclusion: ACD implantation is an effective way to reduce reherniation rates after microdiscectomy. The majority of patients (up to 63.5%) with implanted the ACD have vertebral bone resorption around it. The bone resorption area around the ACD mesh increases with time to up to 12% of the vertebral body volume - with no clinical symptoms or signs, though. The formation of a sclerotic rim prevents the bone resorption area from further growth. If the volume of the intervertebral disc before surgery is less than 13.2 cm3, the risk of bone resorption significantly increases.

## ID: AGS26/2431

## P0390: The predictive factors for resorptionof lumbar disc herniation

Abdugafur Sanginov

1

, Alexey Peleganchuk

1

1

Novosibirsk Research Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedics named after Y.L.Tsivyan, Novosibirsk, Russian Federation

Purpose of the study: The aim of this study is to determine the term and identify the predictors of resorption of herniated lumbar disc herniation. Material and methods: This study is retrospective and is devoted to the study of two groups of patients - group I (herniated disc resorption), and group II - absence of resorption. All patients underwent MRI of the lumbar spine at the onset of first symptoms. A second study was conducted during the second visit, and based on MRI results, patients were divided into two groups. According to MRI findings, the following parameters were assessed: the degree of intervertebral disc degeneration according to Pfirmann, the degree of arthrosis of facet joints according to Grogan, type of disc herniation, degree of migration of hernial material according to Komori, Modic changes, state of closure plates according to Rajasekaran, presence of retrolisthesis and presence of dynamic resorption of herniated fragment. The Syngo.via workstation was used to measure the volume of the herniated fragment. On each section where a hernia was visible, it was traced in the sagittal plane. Then, the Syngo software isolated the hernia and calculated its volume. Resorption of a disc herniation was considered to be a decrease in its volume by more than 50% of the initial value, as well as mandatory relief of radicular pain syndrome. Identification of predictors for resorption was performed by building logistic regression models. Single-factor models were used to identify individual predictors associated with the target event. ROC analysis revealed maximum threshold values for continuous indicators according to the Youden’s index. Results: The first group, in which spontaneous resorption was observed, included 141 patients. The second group, where no resorption occurred (group II), consisted of 93 participants. Statistically significant differences were found in several parameters: the average age of the patients in the second group was two years younger, the interval between MRIs was on average two months longer, and there were more hernias in the second group than in the first group. Gender, body mass index (BMI), retrolisthesis, and smoking habits did not differ significantly between the groups. Regarding the level of herniation, there were more cases at the L5-S1 level in group II. This, we believe, is associated with the higher incidence of hernias at this level. Using logistic regression models, we identified hernia size, Komori type of herniation, BMI, and Modic changes as significant factors in resorption. The volume of the herniated disc is more than 1.1 cm3, and the lack of Modic changes of type II and type III, as determined by Komori, and a BMI below 30.24, increase the chances of herniated disc resorption. Conclusion: The average time for the resorption of a herniated lumbar disc is 5.5 months. Factors that predict the resorption of the hernia include type II and III according Komori classification (correspond to sequestered herniation). Other factors include the absence of Modic changes, a volume of disc herniation larger than 1.1 cm3, and a body mass index (BMI) less than 30.24
Key words: lumbar disc herniation, resorption of the disc herniation.

## ID: AGS26/2454

## P0391: Lumbar fusion vs lumbar disc replacement for degenerative disc disease: spin bias in comparative systematic reviews and meta-analyses

Kevin Mathew

1

, Connor Buchanan

1

, Ryan Chang

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Lumbar fusion (LF) has been historically recognized as the standard operative treatment for degenerative disc disease (DDD). However, lumbar disc replacement (LDR) has emerged as a promising alternative to LF. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses provide evidence-based insight to determine which procedure should be selected. One factor potentially undermining the credibility of systematic reviews and meta-analyses is spin bias, in which reporting of results is distorted, intentionally or unintentionally, to favor one intervention over the other. This may mislead clinicians to prioritize interventions based on misconstrued results. This study aims to assess the presence and amount of spin bias in systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing the outcomes of LF and LDR and find potential predictors of spin bias. Material and Methods: This study adhered to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic reviews and Meta-analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. A search query using keywords was performed using PubMed, EMBASE, and Web of Science databases. Articles were first screened by title and abstract, and selected articles subsequently underwent full-text screening. Each step was performed by two independent authors. Each included study was assessed for the nine most severe types of spin bias as defined by Yavchitz et al in 2016. Each article was assessed using A Measurement Tool to Assess Systematic Reviews 2 (AMSTAR-2) criteria for methodological quality categorizing each study as critically low, low, moderate, or high. Logistic and Poisson regression modeling were used to determine whether AMSTAR-2 rating, journal impact factor, or citations per year were predictive of the presence or number of types of spin. Results: 1,180 studies were imported for screening: 438 duplicates were removed, 742 studies were screened, and 22 met inclusion criteria. 8 (36.4%) studies were found to have at least one type of spin bias. Upon AMSTAR-2 assessment, 1 study was rated high, 5 were rated low, and 16 were rated critically low. The average impact factor of the publishing journal was 2.47, ranging from 0.71 to 3.7. The average citations per year was 4.53, ranging from 1.18 to 15.9. Binomial regression found that AMSTAR-2 rating above critically low (OR: 0.805, p = 0.85), impact factor (OR: 1.45, p = 0.58), and citations per year (OR: 0.765, p = 0.212) were not predictive of spin presence. Poisson regression found that AMSTAR-2 rating above critically low (IRR: 0.66, p = 0.60), impact factor (IRR: 1.36, p = 0.50), and citations per year (IRR: 0.84, p = 0.21) were not predictive of the number of types of spin present. Conclusion: Spin bias was identified in 36.4% of the systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing LF and LDR for DDD. Spin bias must be combated to ensure spine surgeons are receiving accurate interpretations of evidence to guide clinical practice. The integrity of literature would likely benefit from publishing journals implementing systems to recognize and combat spin bias when reviewing articles for publication.

## ID: AGS26/2551

## P0392: Evaluating PLIF with cage and bone graft for degenerative spondylolisthesis: insights into clinical and radiological findings

Mahabub Alam

1

1

Micare Health & Research, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) is an effective and standard surgical procedure for patients with Lumbar canal stenosis & spondylolisthesis. The surgery involves a posterior approach to remove a damaged intervertebral disc, decompress neural structures, and facilitate a fusion between adjacent vertebrae. Modern PLIF technique involves the use of interbody cages, most commonly constructed from titanium that are strategically inserted into the disc space previously occupied by the damaged disc. Although the efficacy and outcomes of PLIF using interbody cages have been extensively reported in developed countries, there is a scarcity of published data from Bangladesh, where spine surgery is an evolving subspecialty. Materials and Methods: A prospective interventional study was conducted among 28 patients in the department of Orthopedic Surgery at Dhaka Medical College Hospital (DMCH) from January 2022 to December 2022. All the data were analyzed statistically by using Statistical Package for Social Science (SPSS). Results are presented as percentages and mean ± standard deviation (SD). A p-value of less than 0.05 was considered for statistical significance. Result: The study population had a mean age of about 51 years, with a female-to-male ratio of 1.6:1. Single-level involvement was identified in 60.72% of cases. At the final follow-up, a significant reduction in pain scores was observed. The mean Visual Analog Scale (VAS) score for back pain decreased from 6.55 ± 0.66 to 2.29 ± 0.46, and for leg pain from 6.59 ± 0.65 to 1.21 ± 0.42. Similarly, the mean Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) score improved markedly, declining from 56.71 ± 3.09 to 15.82 ± 3.46. Superficial wound infection was observed in only two cases (7.14%) as a perioperative complication. Radiological evaluation demonstrated a Grade-I fusion rate of 89.28%. Based on the Modified Macnab criteria, overall functional outcomes were rated as excellent in 89.29% of patients. Conclusion: Posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) with cages and bone graft is a highly effective treatment for degenerative lumbar canal stenosis, providing excellent functional and neurological recovery and high rates of solid arthrodesis, though results may be limited by purposive sampling, short follow-up, and lack of advanced operative facilities.
Keywords: Lumbar canal stenosis, Spondylolisthesis, PLIF, Interbody fusion.

## ID: AGS26/2553

## P0393: Machine learning approach for pelvic incidence-dependent clustering of sagittal spinal alignment in asymptomatic middle-aged and elderly adults

Xiaolong Chen

1

, Qijun Wang

1

, Shibao Lu

1

1

Xuanwu Hospital Capital Medical University, Beijing, China

Introduction: Restoration of sagittal alignment is the key to preventing mechanical complications and achieving good clinical outcomes in ASD surgery. However, high variations in the reported incidence of mechanical complications and clinical outcomes under current ASD realignment strategies have severely impeded the decision-making process for the optimal surgical plan. This study aimed to refine the sagittal morphological classification of the spine in asymptomatic middle-aged and elderly adult populations using the unsupervised machine learning (ML) techniques and, by leveraging these findings, to propose and validate a surgical correction reference for adult spinal deformity (ASD) patients across different morphological subtypes. Methods: This study cross-sectionally enrolled asymptomatic middle-aged and elderly Chinese adults. Sagittal spinal morphology clusters and pelvic incidence-based correction criteria for ASD realignment surgery were derived from whole spine radiographs using unsupervised ML algorithms. To externally validate the realignment strategy identified in asymptomatic adults, a consecutive cohort of ASD patients with sagittal deformity who underwent realignment surgery was examined for postoperative mechanical complications, unplanned reoperation, unplanned readmission, and clinical outcomes during follow-up. Results: A total of 635 asymptomatic adults were enrolled for morphological stratification, and 103 ASD patients with sagittal deformity were included for validation. The unsupervised ML algorithm successfully stratified spinal morphology into four clusters. The pelvic incidence-based surgical correction criteria computed by the regression algorithm demonstrated plausible clinical relevance, evidenced by the significantly lower incidence of postoperative mechanical complications, unplanned reoperation, unplanned readmission, and superior patient-reported outcomes in the restored group (conforming to the correction criteria) during follow-up. Conclusion: In this study, unsupervised ML algorithm effectively partitioned asymptomatic sagittal spinal morphology into four distinct clusters. Using the pelvic incidence-based proportional correction criteria, ASD patients can anticipate a reduced incidence of mechanical complications and improved clinical outcomes following spinal realignment surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2555

## P0394: Longitudinal changes in Psoas-Vertebral Index and its association with timing of adjacent segment disease following lumbar fusion surgery: a pilot study

Shawn Seah

1

, Mark Hao Xuan Yeo

1

, Joy Tan

1

, Joseph Lai

1

, Youheng Ou Yang

1

, Reuben Soh

1

, Yee Gen Lim

1

, Michael Shen

1

, Christian Heng

1

, Lei Jiang

1

1

Singapore General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Adjacent segment disease (ASD) is a common long-term complication after lumbar fusion, contributing to pain, disability, and revision surgery. Established risk factors include age, sagittal malalignment, fusion length, and pre-existing adjacent-level degeneration. Recently, sarcopenia-characterized by reduced muscle mass and quality-has emerged as a potential risk factor. In spine surgery, the psoas-vertebral index (PVI) provides a reproducible MRI-based surrogate for regional sarcopenia. Prior studies suggest that patients with lower baseline psoas size or greater paraspinal fatty infiltration are more likely to develop ASD. However, most literature has focused on cross-sectional or baseline muscle measurements. There is limited evidence on whether longitudinal changes in muscle mass influence the timing of ASD onset. If progressive muscle decline accelerates ASD development, targeted rehabilitation to preserve paraspinal and core muscle mass may represent a modifiable strategy to reduce this complication. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analysed 20 patients who underwent surgery for ASD after prior lumbar fusion between 2013 and 2023. PVI was measured on axial MRI at the L4 level before the index fusion and at the time of ASD diagnosis. Three variables were examined: baseline PVI, PVI at ASD presentation, and change in PVI between these time points. Baseline and ASD PVI values were compared, and all three variables were correlated with time from index fusion to ASD development. Results: The mean age of the cohort was 76.5 ± 7.6 years, with 14 females (70%). The mean time to ASD was 105 ± 59.9 months. Mean baseline PVI, PVI at ASD, and change in PVI were 1.49 ± 0.54, 1.36 ± 0.44, and -0.13 ± 0.29, respectively. No significant difference was observed between baseline PVI and PVI at ASD (p = 0.068). Baseline PVI showed no significant correlation with time to ASD (ρ = 0.136), and change in PVI similarly demonstrated no association (ρ = 0.747). Conclusion: In this pilot cohort, neither baseline PVI nor longitudinal change in PVI correlated with the timing of ASD onset. While the reduction in PVI following index fusion surgery did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.068), the effect size suggests a possible association. To our knowledge, this is among the first studies to examine longitudinal muscle changes in relation to ASD progression. Although no significant associations were identified, the study is limited by its small sample size and potential type II error. Larger cohorts or propensity-matched comparative studies including patients without ASD are needed to determine whether accelerated muscle loss predisposes to earlier ASD. If confirmed, these findings would support greater emphasis on peri-operative rehabilitation and muscle-preserving strategies as a means of reducing ASD risk after lumbar fusion.

## ID: AGS26/2558

## P0395: Outcome of chemonucleolysis with condoliase for the treatment of recurrent lumbar disc herniation

Takahiro Ushimaki

1

, Shogo Tahata

1

, Takafumi Inoue

1

, Toru Fujimoto

1

, Seiichiro Naruo

1

1

Naruo Orthopedic Hospital, Kumamoto, Japan

Introduction: Condoliase, a chondroitin sulfate ABC endolyase, was approved in 2018 in Japan as a novel, minimally invasive treatment for lumbar disc herniation. It is considered an intermediate option between conservative care and surgical discectomy. By degrading glycosaminoglycans within the nucleus pulposus, intradiscal injection decreases intradiscal pressure, thereby reducing nerve root compression and alleviating pain. Previous clinical studies in primary lumbar disc herniation have demonstrated favorable safety and efficacy, with symptom improvement reported in approximately 80% of patients. In contrast, treatment options for recurrent lumbar disc herniation following surgery remain limited. Revision discectomy is technically more challenging due to epidural scarring and adhesions, and extensive bone removal is sometimes required, leading to spinal instability and necessitating fusion procedures. Given these limitations, less invasive alternatives such as condoliase injection may represent an attractive option. However, evidence regarding its clinical effectiveness in the postoperative recurrent setting is scarce. The present study aimed to investigate the short-term outcomes of intradiscal condoliase injection for patients with recurrent lumbar disc herniation after prior surgery. Material and Methods: From March 2021 to February 2025, 11 patients (8 males, 3 females; mean age 49.8 years) with recurrent lumbar disc herniation after previous discectomy underwent intradiscal injection of condoliase at our institution. The treated levels were L2/3 in 1 case, L4/5 in 4 cases, and L5/S1 in 6 cases. All patients had persistent radicular symptoms refractory to conservative treatment. Clinical outcomes were evaluated using the Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) for leg and low back pain at baseline, 2 weeks, 1 month, and 3 months post-injection. Treatment efficacy was defined as ≥ 50% reduction in leg pain NRS compared with baseline. MRI was performed to assess disc degeneration using the Pfirrmann classification. Results: The mean leg pain NRS improved significantly from 6.6 at baseline to 3.5 at 2 weeks, 3.1 at 1 month, and 1.9 at 3 months. Six patients (54.5%) achieved ≥ 50% improvement at both 2 weeks and 1 month, and 9 patients (81.8%) at 3 months. Low back pain NRS also decreased from 6.1 at baseline to 3.1, 3.5, and 2.3 at the respective time points. Two patient (18.2%) demonstrated insufficient improvement and subsequently required surgical intervention. MRI follow-up revealed progression of disc degeneration by at least one Pfirrmann grade in 5 patients. No severe adverse events or neurological complications were observed. Conclusion: Intradiscal condoliase injection demonstrated meaningful short-term efficacy for recurrent lumbar disc herniation after prior surgery, providing rapid and sustained symptom relief in the majority of patients. Its minimally invasive nature allows avoidance of technically demanding revision discectomy or fusion surgery in selected cases. Although disc degeneration progression was noted in some patients, no major safety concerns emerged. Clinical relevance: Condoliase may represent a valuable therapeutic option for recurrent lumbar disc herniation, especially in patients at risk of surgical morbidity. Broader, multicenter prospective studies with longer follow-up are warranted to further validate efficacy and clarify the long-term structural consequences.

## ID: AGS26/2562

## P0396: Correlation between preoperative slip angle and postoperative functional outcomes in lumbar spondylolisthesis

Mustafa Kavcar

1

, Semih Bal

1

, Çağrı Elbir

1

1

Etlik City Hospital Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Lumbar spondylolisthesis remains one of the most common degenerative spinal pathologies requiring surgical treatment. Preoperative radiological parameters, including slip angle, disc height, and foraminal dimensions, may influence postoperative functional recovery. This study aimed to evaluate the correlation between preoperative slip angle and postoperative clinical outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery. Materials and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 82 patients operated for lumbar spondylolisthesis between 2022-2025. Preoperative radiological measurements included slip angle, disc height, and foraminal area assessed on MRI and plain radiographs. Postoperative functional outcomes were evaluated using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores at 6 and 12 months. Correlation analysis was performed between radiological parameters and functional outcomes. Results: Patients with lower preoperative slip angles (< 15°) demonstrated significantly better improvement in ODI scores (p = 0.021) compared to those with higher slip angles. Reduction in slip angle correlated positively with postoperative foraminal area restoration (r = 0.48, p < 0.05). Disc height restoration was not found to be an independent predictor of clinical improvement. Conclusion: Preoperative slip angle is a significant predictor of postoperative functional outcomes in lumbar spondylolisthesis. Patients with lower preoperative slip angles achieved superior recovery, suggesting that radiological evaluation should be integrated into surgical decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/2574

## P0397: Efficacy of indirect neural decompression achieved during lumbar fusion: a 227-level analysis

Hardeep Singh

1

, Michael Mancini

1

, Lisa Tamburini

1

, Justin King

1

, Scott Mallozzi

1

, Isaac Moss

1

1

University of Connecticut, Farmington, United States

Introduction: Indirect decompression via interbody distraction during lumbar fusion is widely utilized to treat central canal and foraminal stenosis without direct posterior decompression. While clinical adoption is increasing, quantitative radiographic and MRI evidence of its effectiveness remains limited. Materials and Methods: A retrospective review of 148 patient (227 levels) who underwent lumbar fusion from 2018-2023 was performed. Sub-analyses included single-level cases (n = 100) and patients presenting with lumbar radiculopathy or claudication (n = 87; 137 levels). Pre- and postoperative MRI were graded for central (Guen 2011), paracentral (Bartynsky 2003), and foraminal stenosis (Lee 2009). Grades 0-1 were considered low-grade, and grades 2-3 were high-grade. Radiographic parameters included posterior disc height, segmental lordosis, and global lordosis. Operative records and follow-up identified revision procedures and need for subsequent direct decompression. Paired comparisons were performed with significant set at p < 0.05. Results: Indirect decompression resulted statistically significant improvements in MRI stenosis scores: mean central stenosis improved by 1.24 ± 1.06 points (p < 0.001); right and left paracentral stenosis improved by 1.4-1.7 points (p < 0.001); and foraminal stenosis improved bilaterally (p ≤ 0.002). Posterior disc height increased by +4.97 ± 1.96 mm (p < 0.001). Segmental lordosis increased by +2.8° (p < 0.001), while global lordosis decreased slightly by -2.43° (p = 0.010). Clinical durability was reflected by low reoperation rates: 11 total returns to the OR, with only 3 same-level revisions and 2 subsequent direct decompressions. Findings were consistent in both single-level and radiculopathy-only cohorts. Conclusions: Lumbar interbody fusion with indirect decompression significantly reduces MRI stenosis scores and restores posterior disc height, with low revision and direct decompression rates. These results support indirect decompression as an effective treatment strategy for lumbar stenosis in appropriately selected patients. Prospective studies with long-term clinical outcomes are warranted to further validate these findings.

## ID: AGS26/2576

## P0398: Outcome of transforaminal endoscopic lumbar discectomy in treatment of recurrent disc herniation

Lotfy Shwitter

1

1

Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, Egypt

Introduction: Recurrent herniation remains a major concern due to the risks of scarring and disc degeneration, which can worsen outcomes after standard posterior surgery. TELD offers the advantage of bypassing scarred areas and can be done under local anesthesia, though it requires considerable surgical expertise to learn. Material and Methods: This prospective cohort study included 20 consecutive patients who underwent transformational endoscopic discectomy for recurrent lumbar disc herniation after a previous discectomy. Twenty patients with mostly radicular issues had transforaminal endoscopic lumbar discectomy (TELD) for a recurring herniated disc between January 2021 and November 2023. 50.4 years old was the average age. The female/male ratio was 4/16 (20%/80%). Results: At the 2-year follow-up, 95% of patients reported that the procedure had a good or very good outcome. Five percent rated the outcome as fair, and none reported a poor or negative experience. On the VAS scale (1-10), the average improvement in leg pain was 6-7 points, and the average improvement in back pain was about 6 points. 65% of the patients reported feeling completely regenerated, 30% reported feeling somewhat restricted in their functional capacity, 5% reported feeling notably constrained, and 0% reported feeling unchanged or worse, based on the McNab criterion. There was one instance of nerve root irritation among the 5% total complication rate. Neither infection nor discitis were reported. Conclusion: With few side effects and a high patient satisfaction rate, TELD appears to be a successful treatment for recurrent disc herniation.
Keywords: Endoscopic discectomy, Lumbar disc herniation, McNab criteria, Recurrent herniation, Transforaminal approach

## ID: AGS26/2602

## P0399: Development of simple predictive scoring system for clinical outcome after lumbar spinal fusion using prospective study

Baejae Kim

1

, Doohee Han

1

, Joohyung Shin

1

, Jong Hyeok Park

1

, Jae-Tack Hong

2

1

Bundang Jesaeng General Hospital, Daejin Medical Center, Seongnam-si, South Korea

2

Eun Pyeong St. Mary's Hospital, The Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Lumbar fusion surgery is widely performed for various lumbar disorders, but accurately predicting postoperative clinical outcomes (COs) remains difficult due to the complexity of influencing factors. Although several predictive tools exist, such as FIRRST, Modified Frailty Index and other scores, such scores are complex and have significant limitations in practical clinical application. This study aims to develop a scoring system that can predict the prognosis after lumbar fusion surgery using simple clinical information. Material and Methods: This study retrospectively analyzed the records of 600 patients over 18years old who underwent lumbar fusion surgery (L2-S1) between January 2010 and December 2023. Patients with ≥ 5 levels fused, thoracolumbar junction surgeries, infection, tumor, metastatic disease, incomplete records, or prior lumbar surgery were excluded. Clinical data including age, sex, BMI, BMD, comorbidities, fusion levels, and functional status were collected. Outcomes were measured using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) recovery rate, with > 40% improvement defined as clinically successful. Based on this data, a simple predicting scoring system was developed. Its accuracy was prospectively validated in a cohort of 50 patients from January to June 2025 at Bundang Jeseung Hospital. Results: After univariate analysis of 600 retrospective cohorts, women, age, BMD, fusion level, preoperative symptoms, ambulation, DM, cerebrovascular disease, chronic kidney disease, American Society of Anesthesiologist classification were significant. The rate of successful clinical outcomes among lumbar fusion patients was 63.3%. After multivariate regression analysis, significant outcome predictors included age, BMD, fusion level, preoperative ambulation, and ASA grade. Simple scoring system was made of 5 key factors. The simple predictive scoring model demonstrated accuracy of 72%, sensitivity of 76%, and specificity of 57% in identifying patients who achieved favorable outcomes. Conclusion: Predicting clinical outcomes after lumbar fusion remains challenging due to diverse, often unquantifiable patient characteristics. Nonetheless, this simple scoring system using 5 key factors offers acceptable predictive accuracy and could enhance patient counseling and management for lumbar fusion surgery

## ID: AGS26/2617

## P0400: Association between facet tropism and adjacent-level disease: proposal of a novel classification system

Abdullah Eissa

1

1

HMG, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: The Zygapophyseal or facet joint is synovial, diarthrodial and paired in each vertebral level. This joint lies between the articular processes of two adjacent vertebrae. Due to its unique orientation, the facet joint plays a major role in axial loading and provides stability for each vertebral level by limiting the rotation and flexion. More than 50% of the elderly develop degenerative changes, which may lead to instability and foraminal stenosis. Several authors studied the facet joint and facet tropism, and they found that they are associated with the degenerative changes in the lumbar spine, disc herniation and spondylolisthesis. Facet orientation refers to the angle of the facets in the transverse plane relative to the sagittal plane. While the facet tropism refers to asymmetry of the facet joints with respect to each other in the sagittal plane. Furthermore, due to the influence of the joint orientation and tropism on the biomechanics of the vertebral segment, it can be one of the risk factors that is associated with the adjacent level disease (ALD). ALD refers to the pathologic changes and deterioration of the disc or facet joint adjacent to a fusion segment with or without clinical symptoms after a spinal fusion surgery. The aim of the study is to find out the association between the facet tropism and adjacent level disease, and propose a novel classification system. Material and Methods: Patients having undergone posterior lumbar decompression and fusion and diagnosed with adjacent level disease between January 2014 - January 2024 with a follow-up of at least one year will be identified in the HMG Spinal Surgical Database. Exclusion criteria are < 18 years, tumors, trauma, and deformity. Analysis will be performed with the Corel Viewer, and spreadsheet software such as Microsoft Excel, and statistical software (e.g. Stata). Qualitative variables will be presented as frequency (n) and percentages. Quantitative variables will be given as mean ± standard deviation (STD). The association between the categorical variables will be analyzed by using a Pearson's Chi-square. The statistical tests for continuous outcome variables will be analyzed by the t-test. We will propose a novel classification of facet tropism that stratifies patients by degree of asymmetry linked to high risk of adjacent-level disease, and assess its inter- and intra-observer reliability. Results: The study is ongoing, and final results are not yet available. Conclusion: Completion is expected soon, and the study will provide a novel facet tropism classification linked to adjacent-level disease risk.

## ID: AGS26/2638

## P0401: Multifidus muscle volume reduction and endurance impairment following rigid lumbar fixation: pilot study

Mustafa Arık

1

, Ukbe Sırayder

2

, Merdan Orunoğlu

3

, Oğuzhan Yılmaz

2

1

AO Spine, Davos, Switzerland

2

Nuh Naci Yazgan University, Kayseri, Turkey

3

Kayseri Public Hospital, Kayseri, Turkey

Introduction: The long-term effects of surgical techniques for lumbar degenerative pathologies on paraspinal muscle function remain controversial. This pilot study aimed to compare multifidus muscle volume, trunk muscle endurance, and surface electromyography (sEMG) findings following rigid pedicle screw-rod fixation and non-instrumented surgery. Material and Methods: A total of 20 patients who had undergone lumbar surgery between 2019 and 2020 with at least 5 years of follow-up were randomly selected: 10 with rigid fixation (ISG) and 10 with non-instrumented surgery (NISG). Multifidus muscle volumes were calculated using standardized MRI-based measurements. Trunk endurance was assessed with standardized core endurance tests, and paraspinal muscle activity was examined with sEMG. Results: The mean multifidus muscle volume was 23.08 ± 4.29 cm3 in the ISG and 26.09 ± 3.41 cm3 in the NISG, with a statistically significant difference between groups (p < 0.05, Cohen’s d = 0.77). In addition, the ISG demonstrated reduced trunk endurance, lower activation capacity, and higher resting activity in sEMG recordings compared with the NISG (p < 0.05). Importantly, a positive relationship was observed between multifidus muscle volume and trunk endurance (r = 0.387, p < 0.05). Conclusion: Pilot data suggest that rigid fixation not only reduces multifidus muscle volume compared to non-instrumented surgery but also negatively impacts endurance and alters sEMG activity patterns. The observed association between muscle volume reduction and decreased endurance highlights the potential influence of surgical technique on long-term muscle function. These findings emphasize the importance of muscle-preserving surgical approaches and structured postoperative rehabilitation. Ongoing studies with larger samples are expected to confirm and expand upon these preliminary results.

## ID: AGS26/2679

## P0402: Impact of facet joint degeneration on radiological outcomes after OLIF surgery

Hee-Woong Chung

1

, Han-Dong Lee

1

, Sumin Lim

1

, Myungsub Lee

1

, Seok-Jun Hong

1

, Nam-Su Chung

1

1

Ajou University School of Medicine, Suwon, South Korea
