Introduction: Oblique lateral interbody fusion (OLIF) is widely utilized in the treatment of degenerative lumbar conditions, as its minimally invasive approach facilitates the restoration of disc angle and height. However, severe facet joint degeneration may cause posterior ankylosis, limiting segmental mobility and hindering the insertion of larger interbody cages. Nevertheless, few studies have investigated the relationship between the degree of facet degeneration and radiological outcomes following OLIF. Therefore, the present study aimed to evaluate the impact of facet joint degeneration on radiological outcomes following OLIF in patients with degenerative lumbar spine disease. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 292 patients (442 disc levels) who underwent OLIF for degenerative lumbar disease with at least 1 year of follow-up. Patients were categorized into four groups according to the severity of facet joint degeneration. Radiological outcomes including changes in disc angle, presence of endplate injury, cage subsidence, and fusion rate were compared across groups. Results: There were no significant differences in demographic characteristics (except diagnosis), cage angle or height, or operated levels among the four groups. Although differences in disc angle were observed at final follow-up, the amount of disc angle change was not significantly different. The incidence of endplate injury was significantly higher in groups with severe facet degeneration (p = 0.024). However, there were no statistically significant differences in cage subsidence or fusion rate between groups. Conclusion: OLIF demonstrated favorable radiological outcomes irrespective of the degree of facet joint degeneration. However, severe facet degeneration may increase the risk of endplate injury during cage insertion; thus, careful attention is warranted during disc space distraction and endplate preparation in such cases.

## ID: AGS26/2698

## P0403: Radiological features and postoperative outcomes in patients of degenerative lumbar scoliosis with pelvic obliquity: the application of an novel classification

Junyu Li

1

, Bowen Xie

1

, Yihao Liu

2

, Hanwen Zhang

2

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Weishi Li

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: Degenerative lumbar scoliosis (DLS) is a prevalent spinal condition in the elderly, with an incidence of approximately 2%-68 %. Pelvic obliquity (PO), a sign of coronal imbalance, is frequently observed in patients. PO can cause joint deterioration and aberrant gait, which can have a significant negative impact on the patient's quality of life. Systematic studies and reports on the radiographic characteristics of DLS patients with PO and the outcome of PO following orthopedic surgery are still lacking. The radiological characteristics of DLS patients with PO were collated in this study, and we found that there are two types of patients with PO depending on the orientation of the pelvic iliac spine on the higher side. As each subtype of PO has distinct clinical traits, each subtype should be treated using a different surgical strategy. The objectives of the study are to propose a novel classification of preoperative PO in DLS patients and discuss how this PO classifications could guide scoliosis corrective surgery. Material and Methods: Patients with PO (POA ≥ 3°) were divided into type I (n = 48) and type II patients (n = 42). The higher iliac spine of the pelvis was congruent with the direction of the C7 plumb line(C7PL) offset in Type I, whereas in Type II, the higher iliac spine was opposite to the direction of the C7PL offset. A comparative analysis was performed between pre- and postoperative radiological parameters and patient-reported outcomes of various patient’s types. Results: 90 patients (31%) had PO, of whom 48 were type I and 42 were type II. Compared to patients who had persistent postoperative PO, type I patients who recovered from PO had lower Apical vertebral translation (AVT) (13.27 vs. 20.34, p = 0.025), while type II recovered patients had lower postoperative Sacral Obliquity Angle (SOA) (1.62 vs. 3.89, p = 0.015). The percentage of intraoperative fixation to the sacrum was lower in type II patients with follow-up-recurrent-PO (15.8% vs. 60%, p = 0.014). Conclusion: Patients can be categorized into type I and II based on the classification of PO that we proposed and should adopt different surgical strategies accordingly. Intraoperative SOA correction and fixation to the sacrum in Type II patients are crucial for restoring long-term pelvic balance while improvement in Cobb angle and AVT during surgery are more important for correcting Type I PO.

## ID: AGS26/2714

## P0404: Global sagittal imbalance in degenerative spine disease: identification of key predictors and a novel scoring tool

Vivek Yadav

1

, Sachin Borkar

1

1

All India Institute of Medical Sciences, New Delhi, India

Introduction: Degenerative spine disease is increasingly recognized as a significant contributor to chronic disability. Global sagittal malalignment is a key biomechanical parameter influencing clinical outcomes. However, full-length standing radiographs are not always feasible in routine practice, particularly in resource-constrained settings. Beyond cost and accessibility, a greater challenge lies in anticipating which patients are at risk. Identifying reliable clinical and regional radiographic predictors of sagittal imbalance can enable early recognition, improve surgical planning, and support the development of pragmatic diagnostic tools. Material and Methods: In this cross-sectional analytical study, 200 adult patients with degenerative cervical and/or lumbar spine disease were prospectively evaluated. Clinical variables, comorbidities, and regional radiographic parameters were recorded. Global sagittal alignment was assessed using whole-spine standing lateral radiographs, with C2 and C7 sagittal vertical axis (SVA) as primary metrics. Multivariate logistic regression identified independent predictors, which were incorporated into a scoring system. Results: Global sagittal malalignment was observed in 22.5% (C2 SVA) and 17.5% (C7 SVA) of patients. Significant predictors included age, parity > 2 in females, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, cervical myelopathy, lumbar spondylolisthesis, high T1 slope, increased cSVA, high pelvic tilt and incidence, and reduced lumbar lordosis. A clinically applicable scoring system was derived using these predictors, demonstrating good discriminatory ability. Conclusion: Global sagittal malalignment is common in degenerative spine disease and can be anticipated using a combination of clinical and regional radiological parameters. The proposed scoring system offers a simple and effective tool to identify at-risk patients in routine practice, particularly where access to whole-spine imaging is limited.

## ID: AGS26/2719

## P0405: Outcome of unilateral biportal endoscopy in treatment of degenerative spinal canal stenosis associated with scoliosis

Lotfy Shwitter

1

1

Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, Egypt

Introduction: Unilateral biportal endoscopic (UBE) as a minimally invasive (MI) approach can be used to treat degenerative lumbar canal stenosis (DLCS) that associated with degenerative scoliosis. Beside the advantages of minimal invasive approach such as small wound, less postoperative pain and short hospital stay; UBE technique do not affect posterior structures which have important rule in maintaining spinal stability. Patient and Methods: This is prospective cohort study of 12 patients with degenerative lumbar canal stenosis (DLCS) associated with degenerative scoliosis treated between October 2023 and April 2025 using UBE technique. They were 8 males and 4 females with an average age of 62.5. Results: At the end of follow up, the mean VAS for low back pain was improved from 5.3 ± 3.0 to 1.8 ± 1.0 and the VAS for leg symptoms was improved from 8.2 ± 2.1to 1.2 ± 0.9. The mean ODI score was significantly improved from 57.6 ± 17.9 to 15.8 ± 12.6, respectively. Modified Macnab criteria were excellent in 7 patients (58.3%), good in 3 (25%), fair in 2 (16.3%). The average hospital stay was 3.6 ± 2.4 days. Complications included no dural tears, 1 transient motor weakness, 1 inadequate decompression, and 1 epidural hematoma. Conclusions: UBE techniques allow effective and safe decompression for DLCS that associated with scoliosis. The soft tissue and facet joint destruction are minimal so we can avoid spinal fusion and preserve the segmental stability.
Keywords: Minimally invasive surgery, biportal endoscopic spine surgery, lumbar canal stenosis, scoliosis

## ID: AGS26/2737

## P0406: Comparison of canal diameter and foraminal height changes after short- and long-segment fixation in lumbar spinal stenosis

Mustafa Kavcar

1

, Semih Bal

1

, Çağrı Elbir

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Surgical fixation strategies for lumbar spinal stenosis vary between short-segment and long-segment stabilization. The choice of method may directly impact postoperative canal diameter and foraminal height, which are crucial for decompression efficacy. This study aimed to compare radiological outcomes between short- and long-segment fixation techniques. Materials and Methods: A total of 96 patients with degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis operated between 2022-2025 were included. Patients were divided into two groups: short-segment fixation (n = 52) and long-segment fixation (n = 44). Radiological parameters including canal diameter, foraminal height, and segmental lordosis were measured preoperatively and postoperatively using CT and MRI scans. Results: Both groups showed significant postoperative improvement in canal diameter (p < 0.001). Long-segment fixation resulted in greater increases in canal diameter (mean +28%) compared to short-segment fixation (+19%). Foraminal height restoration was superior in the short-segment group (mean +15% vs. +9%, p = 0.037). Segmental lordosis correction was significantly better in the long-segment group. Conclusion: While long-segment fixation provides superior canal diameter restoration and sagittal alignment, short-segment fixation offers better foraminal height preservation. Surgical planning should be tailored according to the predominant pathology and the patient’s sagittal balance requirements.

## ID: AGS26/2751

## P0407: Sacroiliac pain after lumbosacral arthrodesis

Elyes Zoghlami

1

, Khaled Khlil0

1

, Ayari Rabie

1

, Youssef Mallat

1

, Dhiaeddine Bouzrara

1

, Khalil Amri

1

1

Tunis Military Hospital, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Sacroiliac pain syndrome (SIPS) of biomechanical etiology constitutes a frequent complication following lumbosacral arthrodesis (LSA), resulting from the transfer of mechanical stress to the adjacent sacroiliac joint. This iatrogenic pathology represents a major cause of persistent or recurrent low back pain in the post-surgical spinal population. Diagnosis relies on a clinical-radiological approach combining specific provocation tests and dedicated imaging. The objective of this study was to analyze the incidence, clinical characteristics, and evolution of SIPS in patients who underwent lumbosacral arthrodesis. Material and Methods: This was a retrospective single-center study conducted at the Principal Military Teaching Hospital of Tunis, including 27 patients (January 2017 - December 2024) who developed SIPS after lumbosacral arthrodesis with a minimum follow-up of 2 years. The population comprised 6 men and 21 women with a mean age of 59 years (27-88 years). Medical history revealed an average of 3 prior lumbar interventions per patient (1-7). The diagnosis of SIPS was established based on clinical criteria (positive provocation tests) and radiological criteria (degenerative changes of the sacroiliac joint). Evaluation criteria included lumbar and radicular pain intensity (NRS 0-10), functional scores (Oswestry Disability Index, Short Form-12), and analysis of predictive factors for SIPS occurrence. Results: The incidence of SIPS following lumbosacral arthrodesis was significant in our cohort. Lumbar pain presented a mean intensity of 8.4 (7-10) and radicular pain of 5.1 (2-10). The Oswestry score revealed severe functional disability with a mean of 69.4 (52-86). Symptom onset occurred on average 18 months after initial surgery. Factors associated with SIPS occurrence included arthrodesis extension to the sacrum, advanced age, and history of multiple surgeries. Bilateral localization of sacroiliac pain was observed in 55.6% of patients (15/27). Conclusion: Sacroiliac pain syndrome constitutes a frequent and disabling biomechanical complication following lumbosacral arthrodesis. This study emphasizes the importance of differential diagnosis of persistent post-surgical low back pain and the necessity for appropriate clinical surveillance of this at-risk population.

## ID: AGS26/2768

## P0408: Patients receiving GLP-1 agonists have reduced odds of pseudoarthrosis following lumbar decompression and fusion

Manaav Mehta

1

, Abhinav Sharma

2

, Ryan Beyer

2

, Vivian Chen

2

, Michael Kim

2

, Paramveer Birring

2

, Hao-Hua Wu

2

, Sohaib Hashmi

2

, Yu-po Lee

2

, Nitin Bhatia

2

, Don Park

2

1

University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, United States

2

University of California Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: In the past two decades, the frequency of lumbar spine decompression and fusion surgeries has risen exponentially. This increase has coincided with a rise in postoperative complications, notably pseudoarthrosis-a failure in spinal fusion manifesting as recurring pain and mechanical instability. The association between glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) agonist use and complications following lumbar decompression and fusion has yet to be explored. The goal of this study is to examine the association between GLP-1 agonist usage and the incidence of pseudoarthrosis, adjacent segment disease, revision surgery, and other perioperative complications following lumbar decompression and fusion surgery. Material and Methods: TriNetX, a global health research network, was queried from 2004-2024 for patients undergoing open lumbar fusion using a posterior/transforaminal (CPT 22633, 22630, 22612), anterior (CPT 22558), or lateral approach (CPT 22533) with a decompression lumbar laminectomy or laminotomy (CPT 63047, 63030) and who had documented GLP-1 agonist usage within 1-year post-operation. Analysis included chi-squared tests with a control cohort propensity score matched for age, sex, race, smoking, diabetes, BMI, and uremia who did not have post-operative GLP-1 agonist usage. Outcomes of interest were DVT, emergency department (ED) visits, inpatient hospitalization, opioid abuse, PE, sepsis, surgical site infection, wound complications within 90 days, adjacent segment disease, implant failure, pseudoarthrosis, and revision within 2 years. Subgroup analysis based on surgical approach was conducted. Results: A total of 1,266 patients who received GLP-1 were compared to propensity matched controls. After matching, patients were 62.7 ± 10 years old, 39% male, 70% white, 85% diabetic, and 67% obese. GLP-1 agonist use within 1-year post-operation was significantly associated with reduced odds of ED visits (OR 0.78, CI 0.62-0.97), inpatient hospitalization (OR 0.29, CI 0.24-0.34), sepsis (OR 0.49, CI 0.27-0.89), post-operative SSI (OR 0.42, CI 0.27-0.65), wound complications (OR 0.50, CI 0.33-0.77) within 90 days, and pseudoarthrosis (OR 0.46, CI 0.38-0.56) within 2 years post-operation. In the posterior approach subcohort, GLP-1 agonist use was significantly associated with reduced odds of ED visits (OR 0.78, CI 0.62-0.98), inpatient hospitalization (OR 0.27, CI 0.23-0.32), post-operative SSI (OR 0.43, CI 0.27-0.66), wound complications (OR 0.60, CI 0.39-0.93) within 90 days, and pseudoarthrosis (OR 0.51, CI 0.42-0.62) within 2 years post-operation. In the anterior approach subcohort, GLP-1 agonist use was significantly associated with reduced odds of inpatient hospitalization (OR 0.30, CI 0.17-0.53) within 90 days post-operation. The study population was insufficient to assess lateral approaches independently. Conclusion: Lumbar decompression and fusion surgery patients prescribed GLP-1 agonists in the post-operative period experienced lower rates of pseudoarthrosis, postoperative infections, and overall hospitalizations compared to those who did not receive GLP-1 agonists. GLP-1 agonists may offer a therapeutic benefit in improving outcomes in this patient population, and further prospective research is needed to validate these findings and investigate the underlying mechanisms.

## ID: AGS26/2775

## P0409: Long-term reoperation rates after minimally invasive lumbar decompression versus open laminectomy: a national claims database analysis

Makenzie Tomihiro

1

, Pirooz Fereydouni

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Arthur Cowman

1

, Ryan Hoang

1

, Briyana Tillman

1

, Alekos Theologis

2

, Yu-po Lee

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, The University of California, Irvine, Orange, CA, United States

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, The University of California, San Francisco, San Francisco, United States

Introduction: Surgical intervention is typically reserved for patients with lumbar spinal stenosis or disc herniation who fail conservative management. Two common surgical options are minimally invasive lumbar decompression (MILD) and open laminectomy. MILD offers shorter recovery, reduced hospital stays, less blood loss, and lower perioperative pain, whereas open laminectomy is more invasive and carries greater postoperative risks. However, the long-term durability of MILD relative to open laminectomy remains unclear, as prior studies are often limited by small sample sizes and short follow-up. This study compares longitudinal reoperation rates between MILD and open laminectomy using a nationally representative, high-powered database. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study was conducted using the PearlDiver Mariner database to identify patients who underwent lumbar decompression between 2010 and 2022. Cohorts were defined by CPT codes: MILD (0275T) and open laminectomy (63030, 63047). The first decompression claim was designated as the index procedure within the study window. The primary outcome was reoperation, defined as repeat decompression (0275T, 63030, 63047), assessed at 0-90 days, 90 days-1 year, 1-2 years, 2-5 years, and cumulatively through 1, 2, and 5 years. Covariates included demographics, insurance type, service location, and comorbidities. Cohorts were compared using chi-square tests. Propensity-score bucket matching was performed to adjust for baseline differences. Analyses were conducted in R within PearlDiver, with significance set at p < 0.05. Results: A total of 13,698 MILD patients and 1,169,645 open laminectomy patients were identified. Within the unmatched cohort, MILD patients were older (70.9 ± 9.9 vs 58.9 ± 14.3 years, p < 0.001), had greater comorbidity burden, including COPD (34.8% vs 25.8%) and hypertension (89.2% vs 75.6%), and more often underwent surgery in ambulatory centers (98.3% vs 58.3%). Reoperation was higher after MILD compared to open laminectomy: 0-90 days (1.8% vs 1.5%), 90 days-1 year (4.2% vs 1.9%), 1-2 years (2.2% vs 1.4%), and 2-5 years (2.1% vs 2.0%) (all p < 0.001). Cumulative reoperation rates after MILD and open laminectomy were 5.9% vs 3.4% at 1 year, 8.0% vs 4.8% at 2 years, and 10.0% vs 6.7% at 5 years (all p < 0.001). After 1:1 propensity-score matching, 13,698 balanced pairs were analyzed. Reoperation rates remained higher for MILD compared to open laminectomy: 0-90 days (1.8% vs 1.1%), 90 days-1 year (4.1% vs 1.9%), 1-2 years (2.1% vs 1.3%), and 2-5 years (2.0% vs 1.5%) (all p < 0.05). Cumulative reoperation rates after MILD and open laminectomy were 5.9% vs 3.0% at 1 year, 8.0% vs 4.3% at 2 years, and 10.0% vs 5.8% at 5 years (all p < 0.001). Conclusion: In this large retrospective cohort, MILD was associated with consistently higher reoperation rates than open laminectomy through 5 years. While MILD offers perioperative advantages, these must be weighed against its reduced long-term durability. This study utilized a large, nationally representative cohort with extended follow-up, enabling robust comparison of long-term outcomes. Future prospective trials incorporating patient-reported outcomes are needed to clarify long-term effectiveness and refine patient selection.

## ID: AGS26/2792

## P0410: Comparative outcomes of awake spine surgery under spinal versus general anesthesia in patients over 80 years old with spinal lumbar stenosis: a pilot study

Valerija Teodosic

1

, Milos Vasic

1

, Uros Novakovic

1

, Jovana Grupkovic

1

, Miloš Mladenović

1

, Ivan Tulić

1

, Slavisa Zagorac

1

1

Clinic for Orthopaedic Surgery and Traumatology, University Clinical Center of Serbia, Medical Faculty, University of Belgrade, Belgrade, Serbia

Introduction: When it comes to spine surgery, traditionally it is performed under general anesthesia. There has been much controversy about awake surgery in recent years, but it has not yet become mainstream in many countries. Awake spine surgeries found in the literature includes laminectomies and discectomies, anterior cervical discectomy and fusions (ACDF), lumbar fusions, and dorsal column (DC) stimulator placement. The aim of this study was to compare the early postoperative outcomes of elderly patients undergoing awake spine surgery with those undergoing surgery under general anesthesia. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective matched case-controlled study conducted in tertiary care institution, where awake spine surgery is performed for the first time. Ten patients over 80 years old with lumbar spinal stenosis were matched by age, sex, spinal stenosis grade (it is used Schizas grade system) and type of surgical procedure. Results: In case group the average time of postoperative recovery and hospital stay is statistically significant shorter. There were none intraoperative complications in case group, nor postoperative delirium. Postoperative pain were better regulated with fewer medications (average VAS score was lower) in case group. Conclusion: In our pilot study we have shown that awake spine surgery is more safer method for elderly patients than surgery under general anesthesia, with lower rate of postoperative complications and lower use of postoperative pain drugs.

## ID: AGS26/2808

## P0411: Predictive value of preoperative nutritional risk index for screw loosening after lumbar interbody fusion in elderly patients with lumbar spine diseases

Youwei Ai

1

, Qian Chen

2

, Hong Ding

1

, Limin Liu

1

, Yueming Song

1

1

West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China

2

Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College, Nanchong, China

Introduction: Pedicle screw loosening is one of the common complications in elderly patients undergoing transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) for lumbar spine disease. Malnutrition, prevalent among elderly patients, has been shown to be associated with increased complications. The Geriatric Nutritional Risk Index (GNRI) serves as a simple indicator of nutritional status. However, the relationship between malnutrition, particularly GNRI, and pedicle screw loosening has not been adequately investigated. This study aims to investigate the relationship between GNRI and pedicle screw loosening following TLIF to guide the perioperative nutritional management of patients and prevent postoperative complications. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on clinical data from patients who underwent single-level TLIF between 2014 and 2022. Data collection encompassed patient demographics, preoperative laboratory parameters, surgery-related data, perioperative radiographic data, and patient-reported outcomes were comprehensively documented. All patients were followed up for a minimum of 12 months. The relationship between GNRI and pedicle screw loosening was evaluated by univariate and multivariate Cox regression analysis, restricted cubic spline (RCS) analysis, receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis, and Kaplan-Meier survival analysis. Results: A total of 426 patients were included in the study. The rate of pedicle screw loosening rate was 16.4% at a minimum follow-up of 12 months. Patients with pedicle screw loosening exhibited significantly lower GNRI (89.0 ± 8.0 vs. 99.2 ± 9.3, p < 0.001) and volumetric bone mineral density measured by quantitative computed tomography (QCT-vBMD) (84.2 [interquartile range (IQR) 79.6-92.2] vs. 104.0 [IQR 88.2-126.0] mg/cm3, p < 0.001) compared to those in the non-loosening group. Multivariate Cox regression analysis identified sex (hazard ratio [HR] 1.433, 95% confidence interval [CI] 0.714-2.876, p = 0.027), age (HR 1.062, 95% CI 1.014-1.113, p = 0.012), GNRI (HR 0.841, 95% CI 0.711-0.994, p = 0.043), and QCT-vBMD (HR 0.982, 95% CI 0.967-0.997, p = 0.019) as independent risk factors for screw loosening. RCS analysis showed that GNRI was negatively correlated with screw loosening (p < 0.0001). The area under the curve (AUC) for the GNRI in predicting pedicle screw loosening was 0.794, with a cut-off value of 95.590 (sensitivity, 85.7%; specificity 65.2%). Kaplan-Meier survival analysis identified that the lower-level GNRI group exhibited a higher cumulative incidence of screw loosening (log-rank test, p < 0.0001). Conclusion: The GNRI was an independent risk factor for postoperative screw loosening in elderly patients undergoing TLIF for lumbar spine disease. Preoperative GNRI may potentially serve as a valuable tool in predicting postoperative screw loosening in elderly patients undergoing TLIF.

## ID: AGS26/2871

## P0412: Lumbar plexus intraoperative neuromonitoring during lateral interbody fusion: preliminary findings

Joaquim Cruz Teixeira

1

, João Levy Melancia

1

, José Hipólito Reis

1

, Nuno Faria

1

, João Leote

1

1

Hospital CUF Tejo, Lisboa, Portugal

Introduction: Intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) is an online technique that evaluates the integrity of neural pathways in real time. Selecting appropriate modalities to maximize patient safety while maintaining surgical workflow remains a challenge. The transpsoas lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) technique is an effective alternative to traditional anterior and posterior approaches. We report our preliminary findings using a multimodal IONM protocol specifically focused on lumbar plexus monitoring during LLIF. Material and Methods: Monitoring included saphenous nerve somatosensory evoked potentials (snSSEP), motor evoked potentials (MEP), and electromyography (EMG) with psoas, quadriceps, and tibialis anterior recordings. Transpsoas lumbar plexus mapping was performed with monopolar stimulation (500 µs, biphasic pulse) applied through the dilator to the ipsilateral anterior iliac fossa, using at least three stimuli at 180° angles. The minimum current eliciting a compound muscle action potential (CMAP) was recorded. Results: Seven patients (4 female; median age 57 years) underwent LLIF at L3-L4 (n = 3) or L4-L5 (n = 4). Median psoas retraction time was 18 minutes. Lumbar plexus mapping was feasible in all patients, with a median threshold of 12 mA. In one case, quadriceps CMAP was obtained at 5 mA, leading to revision of the approach. In another case, CMAPs were consistently obtained between 5-6 mA and the LLIF was aborted. Monitoring findings included: one transient EMG denervation episode (200 ms) during fusion, one 40% MEP amplitude decrease in psoas, and one 30% snSSEP amplitude decrease after psoas retraction. None of these changes correlated with permanent postoperative neurological deficits. Conclusion: Multimodal IONM with MEPs, SSEPs, and EMG appears valuable in reducing neurologic risk during LLIF. The minimum safe threshold for transpsoas lumbar plexus mapping remains uncertain and requires further investigation.

## ID: AGS26/2900

## P0413: Patient-reported outcomes measures in degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis surgery: a systematic review

Waeel Hamouda

1

, Sathish Muthu

2

, Yabin Wu

3

, Ashish Diwan

4

, Amit Jain

5

, Samuel Cho

6

, S. Tim Yoon

7

, Zorica Buser

8

, AO Spine Knowledge Forum Degenerative

9

1

Neurosurgery department - Security Forces Hospital Dammam - Saudi Arabia, Neurosurgery department - Faculty of Medicine - Cairo University - Egypt, Dammam, Saudi Arabia

2

Department of Spine Surgery, Orthopaedic Research Group, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, India, Central Research Laboratory, Meenakshi Medical College Hospital and Research Institute, Meenakshi Academy of Higher Education and Research, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

3

Research Department, AO Spine, AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

4

Spine Labs, St. George and Sutherland Clinical School, Faculty of Medicine, University of New South Wales, Spine Service, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St. George Hospital, Kogarah, New South Wales, Australia

5

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, USA, Baltimore, United States

6

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, New York, United States

7

Department of Orthopaedics, Emory University, Atlanta, United States

8

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, NYU Grossman School of Medicine, NY Orthopedics PC, New York, United States

9

AO Spine International, AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: Multiple studies proved effectiveness of surgery in treating degenerative spondylolisthesis (DSL) symptoms. However, a great deal of heterogeneity exists in the patient reported outcomes (PROMs) utilized in these published studies, underscoring the need for a better understanding of the selection and implementation of appropriate PROMs. This is the first systematic literature review to analyze the usage of different PROMs in published studies reporting on the diverse types of surgical management of DLS. Material and Methods: PubMed and 5 other databases were searched through January 1st, 2025, for studies published in 2000 or later in English language. Data from included articles including publication year, first author, study country, study design, registry used if any, number of total and male cases, number of level(s) operated, type(s) of surgical intervention, number of cases per intervention, and PROM used for each reported intervention were analyzed. The primary outcome of interest was finding the commonly used PROMs as well as investigating the trends of PROMs utilization in relation to other collected data variables. Results: 170 studies from 24 countries where 30 different PROMs (either alone or more commonly in combination) were utilized to evaluate outcome of 29 different surgical interventions combinations were performed on 23,575 patients have been analyzed. Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) was the most common PROM utilized in 74% (n = 126), then numerical rating scale (NRS) / Visual analogue scale (VAS) for back pain in 72% (n = 123), followed by NRS/VAS for leg pain in 56% (n = 96). The short-form 36 (SF-36) was the most used generic non-spine-specific PROM in 25% (n = 43). PROMs utilization increased over time (p < 0.001), from 24 PROMs utilized in 14 studies (2000-2005) up to 218 PROMs in 63 studies (2016-2020). Singapore & Switzerland showed the highest utilization rate of almost 4 PROMs per article. Studies describing endoscopic approach (3.9 PROMs/study) and pedicle screw fixation with fusion (3.7 PROMs/study) had significantly higher PROM usage per study (p < 0.001). NRS/VAS back pain was more commonly used in retrospective studies (81%, 60/74) while ODI was the most used in prospective studies (78%, 75/96). In studies reporting on surgeries for 1 (n = 94) or 2 (n = 37) spinal levels, NRS/VAS back pain was the most used in 77% (n = 102). In studies reporting on 3 or more levels, ODI was the most used PROM, 77% (n = 14). Seven different spine registries were used in 31 articles where the most used PROMs were NRS/VAS for back pain and NRS/VAS for leg pain (n = 6), followed by ODI (n = 5), and EQ-5D (n = 4) with an average of 5 different PROMs used per registry. Conclusion: There inconsistency and heterogeneity in the utilization of PROMs as an outcome evaluation tool after surgical interventions for the management of DSL which jeopardizes the objective comparison of interventions. A study evaluating the clinimetric properties of the commonly used PROMs identified in this systematic review is warranted to understand their relevance to DSL and aid in reaching a consensus on the most appropriate one.

## ID: AGS26/2918

## P0414: Effects on segmental lumbar sagittal alignment after anterior hyperlordotic reconstruction of the lumbarsacral spine

Samir Smajic

1

, Anel Dracic

1

1

University Witten-Herdecke, Witten, Germany

Introduction: Hyperlordotic ALIF aims to restore lumbar sagittal alignment, but its impact on segmental correction, normative alignment targets, and clinical outcomes remains under investigation. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 28 patients undergoing L4-L5 and/or L5-S1 ALIF with hyperlordotic cages. Radiographic measurements (lordosis angles, Baastrup grades, Roussouly type) and clinical outcomes (VAS, ODI) were assessed pre-op and at 12 months. Mixed-effects models (frequentist and Bayesian) evaluated the influence of cage lordosis (CageLL) on segmental alignment. Alignment was compared to published normative L4-S1 targets. Results: Significant segmental lordosis changes occurred post-op, with CageLL modulating the distribution between L4-L5 and L5-S1. Each degree of CageLL increased L5-S1 lordosis (+2.1°) and reduced L4-L5 correction (-1.25°). All patients fused; 28.6 % developed ASD. Pain and function improved markedly (ΔVAS = 7.0; ΔODI = 28.3), with > 90% responder rates. Normative L4-S1 target deviation did not predict outcomes. Roussouly-type change was associated with L5-S1 correction. Diabetes modestly reduced pain relief; other comorbidities had no clear effect. Conclusions: Hyperlordotic ALIF effectively restores segmental alignment and improves clinical outcomes. Cage angle influences segmental correction distribution but not overall patient benefit. Normative targets may have limited predictive value; individualized alignment goals are essential.

## ID: AGS26/2960

## P0415: Low rate of hardware failure and revision surgery in patients treated with lateral lumbar interbody fusion and unilateral pedicle screw rod fixation

Kaveh Khajavi

1

, Cristiano Menezes

2

, Pedro Berjano

3

, Juan Cárdenas

2

, William Smith

4

1

Georgia Spine & Neurosurgery Center, Southeastern Neurosurgical Specialists / Northside Hospital-Atlanta, Georgia, United States

2

Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG), Columna Institute, Belo Horizonte, Brazil

3

IRCCS Ospedale Galeazzi-Sant’Ambrogio, Milan, Italy

4

Western Regional Center for Brain & Spine Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

Introduction: Lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) is increasingly used for degenerative lumbar disease. Bilateral pedicle screw fixation (BPSF) provides rigid stabilization but may increase operative time, blood loss, and costs. Unilateral pedicle screw fixation (UPSF) has been proposed as a less invasive alternative, though evidence in LLIF remains limited. To assess radiographic and clinical outcomes of LLIF with UPSF, including fusion, subsidence, migration, and revision surgery rates. Material and Methods: This multicenter retrospective study included consecutive adults undergoing one- or two-level LLIF with UPSF for degenerative lumbar conditions. Demographics, surgical details, and radiographic follow-up ≥ 6 months were reviewed. Fusion was defined as absence of lucency, hardware failure, or motion on flexion-extension radiographs. Results: A total of 216 patients (129 females, 87 males; mean age 63.8 ± 10.9 years) were analyzed. Twenty-four (11.1%) were smokers and 52 (24.1%) had prior lumbar surgery. Single-level LLIF was performed in 153 patients (70.8%), most commonly at L4-L5 (76.5%). Mean follow-up was 24.8 months. Fusion was achieved in 196 patients (90.7%). Subsidence occurred in 11 (5.1%), with three requiring reoperation. Migration was observed in five (2.3%), one requiring revision. Pseudoarthrosis developed in eight (3.7%), with one revision. Adjacent segment degeneration was noted in eight (3.7%), with five reoperations. Overall reoperation rate for cage-related or hardware failure was 1.9%. Conclusion: LLIF with UPSF achieved high fusion rates and low reoperation rates, with acceptable subsidence and migration. This technique appears safe and effective in selected patients, representing a less invasive and potentially more cost-effective alternative to BPSF.

## ID: AGS26/3016

## P0416: Single- vs double-level ALIF (L4-L5-S1): comparison of complications and clinical-radiological outcomes

Francesco Lolli

1

, Gennaro Salierno

1

, Nicolò Regge Gianas

1

, Andrea Baioni

1

, Marco Cancedda

1

, Ilaria Barni

1

, Riccardo Draghi

1

, Roberto Ricca

1

, Andrea Messina

1

, Carmela Altruda

1

, Ignazio Borghesi

1

1

Maria Cecilia Hospital, Cotignola (Ravenna), Italy

Introduction: The anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is an increasingly adopted technique in degenerative spine surgery, both as a primary and revision procedure. However, comparative data on complications and clinical-radiological outcomes in patients undergoing single-level (L5-S1 or L4-L5) vs. double-level (L4-S1) ALIF are still limited. The primary aim of this study was to assess the incidence of intra- and perioperative complications in patients treated with ALIF L4-L5-S1, comparing the different groups according to the treated level(s). Secondary objectives included the analysis of clinical and radiological outcomes at ≥ 6 months of follow-up. Materials and Methods: A single-center retrospective analysis was conducted on 470 patients who underwent ALIF between 2013 and 2025. Patients were divided into three groups: ALIF L5-S1 (n = 280), ALIF L4-L5 (n = 100), ALIF L4-S1 (n = 90). Patients treated with anterior-only or combined anterior-posterior approaches were included, provided that posterior fusion did not extend above L2. Exclusion criteria were age <18 years, stabilizations above L2, or incomplete clinical-radiological data. Collected data included clinical (VAS, ODI, RMDQ), radiological (pelvic parameters, lumbar lordosis, disc height), and intraoperative variables (complications, blood loss, operative time, ICU requirement). Results: No statistically significant differences were observed in the incidence of intra- and perioperative complications among the three groups. Operative times were longer in the double-level groups, but without a significant increase in morbidity. Postoperative radiological parameters showed comparable improvements across groups, with a good recovery of segmental lordosis and disc height. Clinical outcomes (VAS, ODI, RMDQ) demonstrated significant improvement compared with baseline in all groups, with no clinically relevant differences among them. Conclusions: In our center, double-level ALIF is not associated with a substantial increase in complications compared to single-level procedures. The anterior approach proves to be an effective and safe strategy for the treatment of lumbar degenerative disease, even in complex or revision cases, ensuring favorable clinical and radiological outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3035

## P0417: MRI-based L1 vertebral bone quality scores predict cage subsidence following transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion similar to L1 CT Hounsfield units

Youwei Ai

1

, Yongdi Wang

1

, Ce Zhu

1

, Qian Chen

2

, Hong Ding

1

, Limin Liu

1

1
West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China, 
2
Affiliated Hospital of North Sichuan Medical College, Nanchong, China
Introduction: Cage subsidence is one of the most common complications after transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) and correlates with inferior bone quality. Studies have reported L1 vertebral bone quality score (VBQ) based on MRI to be a promising alternative to evaluating preoperative bone quality. However, to the knowledge of the authors, no study has examined the correlation between L1 VBQ scores and cage subsidence after TLIF. The purpose of the study was (1) to assess the interrelation between the L1 VBQ score and cage subsidence after TLIF; and (2) to compare L1 VBQ and L1 CT Hounsfield Unit (HU) values in predicting cage subsidence after TLIF. Material and Methods: We reviewed patients who had undergone TLIF at one institution between 2012 to 2021. Cage subsidence was measured using postoperative lumbar CT based on cage protrusion through the endplates at more than 2 mm. The L1 VBQ score was calculated by dividing mean L1 signal intensity (SI) by mean SI of the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) at L1. The L1 HU value representing bone mineral density (BMD) was measured using computed tomography. We then performed Student's t -test for independent samples and logistic regression analyses for statistical analysis. We also conducted receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis to assess the predictive ability of the L1 VBQ score and L1 CT HU. Results: Of 233 participants, cage subsidence was observed in 41 patients (17.6%). Comparison between the characteristics of patients between the group with subsidence and the group without subsidence revealed significant differences in the age, VBQ score, and L1 CT HU. Multivariate logistic regression showed that higher L1 VBQ score (OR = 2.499, 95% CI: 1.205-5.180, p = 0.014) and lower L1 CT HU (OR = 0.960, 95% CI: 0.933-0.987, p = 0.005) were associated with an increased rate of cage subsidence. Area under the curve (AUC) analysis of the L1 VBQ score returned 0.735 (95% CI: 0.620-0.850) and the suitable threshold was 3.424 (sensitivity: 82.9%, specificity: 70.7%). The AUC of L1 CT HU was 0.747 (95% CI: 0.642-0.852) and the suitable threshold was 136.5 (sensitivity: 85.4%, specificity: 56.1%). Conclusion: The present study demonstrates that L1 VBQ score and L1 CT HU are reliable predictors with similar performance for cage subsidence after TLIF.

## ID: AGS26/3040

## P0418: MRI-based endplate bone quality scores outperform vertebral bone quality scores in predicting adjacent segment disease following transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion

Youwei Ai

1

, Yongdi Wang

1

, Ce Zhu

1

, Hong Ding

1

, Zhuojie Xiao

1

, Limin Liu

1

1

West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China

Introduction: Adjacent-segment disease (ASD) complicates up to one-third of single-level TLIFs and drives 10 % of revisions; low bone mineral density (BMD) is the principal modifiable risk factor. While DEXA and QCT are reference standards, they are under-used because of degenerative overestimation, extra radiation and limited access. MRI-based vertebral bone quality (VBQ) correlates with DEXA and predicts ASD, yet site-specific BMD may be more informative. We therefore compared the novel endplate bone quality (EBQ) score-quantifying subchondral fat infiltration at the adjacent segment-with global VBQ to determine whether EBQ is the stronger independent predictor of ASD after single-level TLIF. Material and Methods: A single-center retrospective analysis was conducted of patients undergoing single-level TLIF for degenerative spinal disease from 2014 to 2020. Demographic, surgery, and radiographic data were collected. Logistic regression was used to identify independent risk factors for ASD. Furthermore, a receiver operating curve (ROC) analysis was conducted to evaluate the predictive efficacy of the EBQ score and VBQ score. Results: The rate of ASD was 16.4% at a minimum 24-month follow-up. Significant risk factors for ASD were higher VBQ score (OR = 3.418, 95%CI: 1.297-9.008, p = 0.013), higher EBQ score (OR = 2.469, 95%CI: 1.085-5.621, p = 0.031), and higher adjacent segment Pfirrmann grade (OR  =  2.866, 95%CI: 1.765-4.653, p < 0.001). The diagnostic accuracy of VBQ and EBQ for distinguishing ASD were 0.806 (95%CI: 0.728-0.883) and 0.835 (95%CI: 0.757-0.912). The optimal threshold of VBQ scores was 2.926 (sensitivity: 90.6%, specificity: 62.0%) and of EBQ was 3.511 (sensitivity: 90.6%, specificity: 71.8%). Conclusion: Higher VBQ and EBQ scores are both independent risk factors of ASD after single-segment TLIF surgery, and EBQ scores perform better in predicting ASD. When EBQ > 3.511, there is a considerable risk of ASD.

## ID: AGS26/3043

## P0419: Prediction of adjacent segment disease after transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion: a predictive model combining multiple radiology variables

Youwei Ai

1

, Yongdi Wang

1

, Hong Ding

1

, Ce Zhu

1

, Zhuojie Xiao

1

, Limin Liu

1

1

West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China

Introduction: Adjacent-segment disease (ASD) develops in 15-37 % of TLIF patients and prompts revision in 11%; low bone mineral density (BMD), pedicle-screw position, paraspinal-fat infiltration and other radiological traits have been individually implicated, yet single-factor models lack clinical power. We therefore fused site-specific MRI metrics-modified vertebral bone quality (MVBQ) and endplate bone quality (EBQ)-with demographic and technical variables to build and validate a practical nomogram that quantifies individual ASD risk after single-level TLIF. Material and Methods: This study included patients undergoing single-level TLIF at our center from 2014 to 2020. Basic information and radiology parameters of patients were collected. The least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) method was used to select risk factors and a nomogram was established. The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve was used to assess predictive accuracy of ASD by single hazard variables and the joint predictive model. Results: 228 patients were included in this study and 46 patients (20.2%) developed ASD. Patients with ASD had higher PS tip-VE distance (p = 0.023), higher adjacent segment disk Pfirrmann grade (p<0.001), lower TCSA (p = 0.037), lower FCSA (p = 0.037), higher FI (p<0.001), higher VBQ scores (p<0.001), higher MVBQ scores (p<0.001), and higher EBQ scores (p<0.001). After LASSO regression analysis, six potential risk factors were selected. The ROC analyze showed the joint predictive model had excellent predictive accuracy (AUC = 0.95, 95%CI: 0.920-0.980, higher than the AUC of single risk factors. Conclusion: Pfirrmann grade, FCSA, FI, PS tip-VE distance, MVBQ scores and EBQ scores are all risk factors of ASD. The joint prediction model developed based on the above risk factors has excellent predictive accuracy for ASD, which is a promising tool for clinical application.

## ID: AGS26/3049

## P0420: Simplified S1 vertebral bone quality score predicts screw loosening in patients with lumbar spondylolisthesis

Youwei Ai

1

, Yongdi Wang

1

, Hong Ding

1

, Ce Zhu

1

, Zhuojie Xiao

1

, Limin Liu

1

1

West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu, China

Introduction: Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) for spondylolisthesis is frequently complicated by S1-pedicle-screw loosening (up to 41 %), especially in patients with low bone mineral density (BMD). While dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry can mis-classify BMD in degenerative spines and quantitative CT (QCT) is rarely available, MRI-based vertebral bone quality (VBQ) scores are emerging alternatives. We compared the traditional L1-4 VBQ score with a newly proposed single-level S1 VBQ score against QCT to determine which better predicts S1 screw loosening after single-level TLIF. Material and Methods: We reviewed 165 patients undergoing single-segment TLIF for lumbar spondylolisthesis at our center from 2012 to 2021. Demographic and radiographic data were collected. To determine the independent factors affecting pedicle screw loosening, univariate analysis and multivariate logistic regression were conducted. Receiver operating characteristic analysis was carried out to assess the predictive ability. Results: 16.3% of patients experienced screw loosening at a minimum of 24 months of follow-up. Statistically significant differences in the 2 groups were observed in surgery level (L5-S1), traditional VBQ, S1 VBQ, and QCT values. QCT values, traditional VBQ score, and the S1 VBQ score were independent risk factors for screw loosening. The receiver operating characteristic analysis showed that area under the curve (AUC) of QCT was 0.839 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.771-0.907) and the cut-off value was 119 (sensitivity: 85.2% and specificity: 76.8%); AUC of VBQ scores was 0.787 (95% CI: 0.699-0.875) and the cut-off value was 3.585 (sensitivity: 88.9% and specificity: 69.6%), while AUC of S1 VBQ score was 0.823 (95% CI: 0.739-0.908) and the cut-off value was 3.72 (sensitivity: 81.5% and specificity: 76.8%). Conclusion: The elevated S1 VBQ score was identified as an independent risk factor for screw loosening and exhibits greater predictive capability compared to the traditional VBQ score in forecasting screw loosening following TLIF for lumbar spondylolisthesis.

## ID: AGS26/3050

## P0421: Correlation of Modic changes with interventional procedures in lumbar spine pain

Ivan Bošnjak

1

, Mia Smoljan

1

, Dario Josip Zivkovic

1

, Ivan Radic

1

, Antonio Klemencic

1

1

Clinical Hospital Center Sisters of Mercy, Zagreb, Croatia

Introduction: Low back pain (LBP) represents a major public health concern, being a principal driver of occupational absenteeism and a significant factor in diminishing patients' quality of life. The anatomical complexity of the lumbar spine (LS) often complicates the precise determination of the pain source. Furthermore, the intricate neural innervation patterns mean that nociception originating from a single intervertebral segment can easily manifest as pain referred to adjacent cranial or caudal segments. Materials and Methods: This retrospective analysis included 70 patients who underwent invasive interventional procedures for chronic low back pain over a one-year period. The interventions comprised either Radiofrequency Ablation (RFA) or Epidural Steroid Injection (ESI). The primary objective was to investigate the correlation between the presence of Modic changes (M1 and M2), as determined by Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and the indication for these invasive procedures. Specifically, we aimed to compare the prevalence of Modic changes in the patient subset receiving ESI versus the subset receiving RFA. Results: In the Epidural Steroid Injection (ESI) group, 30 patients presented with Modic 1 (M1) and/or Modic 2 (M2) changes, while 12 patients showed no evidence of Modic changes. Within the ESI group, M1 changes were observed to be more prevalent than M2 changes. Conversely, in the Radiofrequency Ablation (RFA) group, 18 patients exhibited M1 and/or M2 Modic changes, and 10 patients had no Modic changes. Interestingly, the RFA group demonstrated a higher frequency of M2 changes compared to M1 changes. Conclusion: The findings indicate a correlation between the presence of Modic changes and the severity of patient back symptoms leading to the need for invasive treatment. Patients who presented with Modic changes were more frequently treated with interventional procedures (ESI or RFA) than those without. Given the differential prevalence of M1 and M2 changes between the ESI and RFA groups, further comprehensive studies are warranted to explore additional factors that correlate with specific Modic change subtypes and their prognostic value regarding treatment response.

## ID: AGS26/3096

## P0422: Short term outcome analysis of the first 35 cases of interlaminar lumbar decompression using uniportal endoscopy

Sharif Jonayed

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopaedic Rehabilitation(NITOR), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Uni-portal endoscopic interlaminar lumbar decompression is increasingly adopted for lumbar disc herniation and canal stenosis. However, outcome data during the early learning curve with at least one-year follow-up remain limited. This study evaluates the clinical and radiological outcomes of the first 35 consecutive cases performed over a one-year period, providing practical insights for new endoscopic surgeons. Material and Method: Between July 2023 and June 2024, 35 patients (23 lumbar disc herniation, 12 lumbar canal stenosis) underwent uni-portal endoscopic interlaminar decompression. Demographics, operative time, complications, and clinical outcomes were prospectively recorded. Pain was assessed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for leg and back pain, and functional outcomes were evaluated using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Minimum follow-up was 12 months (mean 13.6 ± 1.2 months). Results: The mean age was 49.6 ± 11.2 years (20 males, 15 females). The mean operative time for the first 10 cases was 180 ± 22 minutes, which decreased to 108 ± 15 minutes in the subsequent 25 cases, reflecting a steep learning curve. At one-year follow-up, mean VAS scores for leg pain improved from 7.8 ± 0.9 preoperatively to 2.0 ± 0.8, while back pain improved from 6.2 ± 1.1 to 2.3 ± 0.9 (p < 0.001 for both). ODI scores improved from 58.4 ± 7.6% to 18.9 ± 5.4% (p < 0.001). Dysesthesia occurred in 7 patients (20%), all transient and resolving within 6-8 weeks. One patient (2.8%) with a broad-based herniation required revision due to incomplete decompression. No neurological deterioration, dural tears, infections, or hematomas were observed. The mean hospital stay was 1.3 ± 0.6 days, and return to routine activities occurred at 3.8 ± 1.2 weeks. Clinical improvements were maintained at one-year follow-up. Conclusion: Uni-portal endoscopic interlaminar lumbar decompression provides significant and durable pain relief with functional improvement at one year, even during the initial learning curve. Operative efficiency improves rapidly, supporting the safety, efficacy, and reproducibility of this minimally invasive technique for both lumbar disc herniation and canal stenosis.
Keywords: Uni-portal endoscopy; Interlaminar lumbar decompression; Lumbar disc herniation; Lumbar canal stenosis; Learning curve; Minimally invasive spine surgery

## ID: AGS26/3152

## P0423: A spontaneous anterior fusion of lumbar spine after posterolateral lumbar fusion with pedicle screw-plate system

Gun Keorochana

1

, Chaiwat Kraiwattanapong

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, Ramathibodi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Lumbar posterolateral fusion (PLF) with Pedicle Screw-Plate (PSP) fixation without interbody fusion found spontaneous anterior fusion (SAF). No study has reported the SAF of lumbar spine in patients who underwent PLF. This study reports the rate of SAF of lumbar spine after PLF with PSP system compared with the rate of SAF after PLF with pedicle screw-rod (PSR) system. Material and Methods: Retrospective reviews of charts and radiographs were performed in 111 patients who underwent PLF with PSP system and PSR system for treatment of degenerative lumbar spine. Demographic data, diagnosis, Myerding grading, level of fusion, Pfirmann grading, disc height, pedicle screw depth, follow up time and SAF were compared between PSP and PSR groups. Results: Fifty-five patients in the PSP group and 56 patients in the PSR group were included with similar baseline characteristics. There were more degenerative spondylolisthesis patients in the PSP group compared with patients in the PSR group. However, there was no significant difference of Pfirmann grading in both groups. Fifteen patients (27.3%) in the PSP group developed SAF while no patient in the PSR group had SAF. When patients in the PSP group were analyzed, the mean follow-up time in patients with SAF was significantly longer than the no SAF group. Conclusion: This study has reported SAF of lumbar spine in patients who underwent PLF with the PSP system. The SAF increasingly occurred with longer follow-up time. In this study, no SAF was found in patients who underwent PLF with the PSR system.

## ID: AGS26/3171

## P0424: Pelvic incidence-lumbar lordosis mismatch correction in lumbar fusion: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Aazad Abbas

1

, Daniel Bour

2

, Kevin Wang

2

, Jaskaran Singh

3

, Gurjovan Sahi

2

, Ali Eren

2

, Nischal Ghimire

3

, Stephen Lewis

4

, Jay Toor

3

1

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, Department of Surgery, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

2

Temerty Faculty of Medicine, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

3

Division of Spine Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

4

Toronto Western Hospital, Division of Spine Surgery, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

Introduction: Sagittal balance is a critical goal in lumbar fusion surgery, with mismatch between pelvic incidence and lumbar lordosis (PI-LL) shown to correlate with disability and clinical outcomes. A PI-LL mismatch of ≤ 10° is widely considered optimal, yet the comparative benefits of achieving full correction (≤ 10°) versus partial correction (> 10°) remain unclear. This study aimed to systematically review and synthesize available evidence on clinical, radiographic, and patient-reported outcomes associated with full versus partial PI-LL correction in adults undergoing lumbar fusion for degenerative disease. Material and Methods: A systematic search of MEDLINE, Cochrane, Scopus, Embase, and Web of Science was performed to identify studies stratifying postoperative PI-LL and reporting patient-reported and/or radiographic outcomes. Study designs included fixed threshold comparisons (≤ 10° vs > 10°), three-group classifications, and age-adjusted targets. Patient-reported outcomes were predominantly assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and visual analog scale (VAS) for pain. Meta-analyses were performed where sufficient data were available. Pooled mean differences (MD) and odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated using random-effects models. Heterogeneity was assessed using the I2 statistic. Statistical significance was set at p = 0.05. Results: Twenty-two studies involving 2994 patients met inclusion criteria. Full correction (PI-LL ≤ 10°) was associated with better ODI scores in 43% of studies and worse in 7%, while VAS pain scores improved in 56% of studies with no reported worsening. Pooled analysis of eight studies demonstrated no significant difference in the mean change in VAS low back pain between full and partial correction (MD [full-partial] -2.8, 95% CI -12.3 to 6.7; I2 = 88%). However, full correction was associated with significantly lower odds of adjacent segment disease in seven studies (OR 0.23, 95% CI 0.07-0.81, p = 0.028). Conclusion: Achieving postoperative PI-LL within 10° in lumbar fusion for degenerative disease is associated with improved patient-reported outcomes and reduced risk of adjacent segment disease. Nonetheless, heterogeneous exposure definitions and retrospective designs limit the certainty of available evidence.

## ID: AGS26/3172

## P0425: Decompression alone or with fusion: do outcomes really differ in lumbar spinal stenosis?

Abdus Salam

1

, Omar Aziz

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis (DLSS) is a frequent cause of pain and disability in the aging population. The role of adjunctive fusion with instrumentation in addition to decompression remains debated. To compare functional and perioperative outcomes of decompression alone versus decompression with transpedicular screw fixation in patients with DLSS. Materials and Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted at a Level I trauma center in Pakistan between March 2020 and June 2025. Patients with radiologically confirmed spinal trauma were included. Data collected included age, sex, mechanism of injury, anatomical site, neurological status (ASIA scale), associated injuries, management strategy, ICU admission, length of stay (LOS), and outcomes. Logistic regression was applied to identify independent predictors of mortality. This retrospective study included 95 patients treated at the Department of Orthopaedic & Spine Surgery, Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, between January 2022 and December 2022. Patients were divided into two groups: decompression alone (n = 37, 38.9%) and decompression with fixation (n = 58, 61.1%). Outcomes were assessed using the Swiss Spinal Stenosis (SSS) score, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for back and leg pain at baseline, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years. Operative time, blood loss, hospital stay, and complications were also recorded. Results: Both groups showed significant improvement in SSS, ODI, and VAS scores at 2 years (p < 0.001). No meaningful difference in long-term functional outcomes was observed between groups (p > 0.05). The fixation group had longer mean operative time, greater blood loss, and longer hospital stay (6.3 vs 4.5 days). Complication and reoperation rates were similar. Radiological analysis showed solid fusion in fixation cases and no significant postoperative instability in the decompression-alone cohort. Conclusion: Decompression alone and decompression with fixation provide comparable functional improvement. Decompression alone offers the benefits of shorter surgery, less blood loss, and faster recovery, while fixation may be reserved for patients with instability or multilevel disease.

## ID: AGS26/3224

## P0426: OLIF with antero lateral stabilisation in degenerative disorders of lumbar spine - Is posterior surgery necessary?

Kannan Karthik Kailash

1

1

Sri Ramachandra Higher Institute for Research For Research and Education, Indian Institute of Technology Madras India, Chennai, India

Introduction: Indirect neurological decompression in form of increased foraminal opening and central canal opening has been the central concept in lateral surgeries of spine. These minimal invasive procedure also preserve both the ALL and PLL providing efficient reduction of vertebral body through ligamentotaxis. Purpose: To assess clinical and radiological outcomes of oblique lumbar Inter body fusion technique in single stage single anterior approach with anterolateral screw without going posterior (OLIF-AL). Material and Methods: Observational type study of 3 years duration. Inclusion criteria: 1. Degenerative spinal diseases. 2. Failed conservative management for over 2 months. 3. Revision surgery following failed posterior surgery. Exclusion criteria: 1. L5-S1 level involvement. 2. Higher iliac crest. 3. More than 2 level involvement. 4. Presence of congenital deformities. Clinical assessment was done using Numeric Rating scale score (NRS) and Oswestry disability Index score. Operating time and blood loss was also monitored. Radiological assessment of disc height, foraminal height and foraminal area on standing lateral radiography. Segmental lordotic angle. Fusion status analysed using modified Birdwell fusion criteria. Results: 55 patients in our study Mean age was 54.5 yrs. Mean preoperative NRS score for back pain and leg pain was 4.6 and 5.9 will was reduced to 2.3 and 1.8 respectively at final follow up. ODI score decreased from 48.8% to 24.0%. In Radiological outcome Birdwell fusion criteria at 1 yr and final follow up was 70% and 90%. Disc height increased from mean of 8.6 mm to 13.4 mm. Average increase in foraminal area was 30%. Segmental lordotic angle increased from mean of 10.1 degree to 15.2 degree. We observed about 30% in operation time and significant reduction in blood loss. Complication in 3 patients, which was approach related injury to psoas muscle (hip flexor weakness) which improved completely over the period of 3 months. Conclusion: Single stage single anterior approach OLIF for degenerative lumbar diseases showed favourable clinical and radiological outcome. It helps avoids various complications of second procedure in standard OLIF. Overall OLIF with anterolateral screw is safer faster and efficient.

## ID: AGS26/3252

## P0427: Posterolumbar interbody fusion with laminoplasty in the treatment of lumbar isthmic spondylolisthesis. A prospective clinical study

Hamdan Abdelrahman

1

, Seyed-Emadaldin Sadat

2

, Ahmed Shawky Abdelgawaad

1

, Ali Ezzati

2

1

Asiut University Hospitals, Asiut, Egypt

2

Helios Clinic Erfurt- Germany, Asiut University Hospitals- Egypt, Erfurt, Germany

Introduction: The technique for surgical treatment of isthmic spondylolisthesis (ISL) is still controversial. Different opinions have been expressed by various authors on timing, type of surgery, type of grafts that are used as well as on whether reduction should be applied or not and the value of reduction. In light of these data we describe our technique in the treatment of ISL and its outcomes. Material and Methods: we examined 70 consecutive patients that had been operated in our clinic due to ISL. The patients have been assessed clinically and radiologically. The same posterior surgical technique was used in all patients (interbody fusion using bone substitute and cage, reduction of the olisthesis and reinsertion of the lamina). Surgical Technique: In prone position, after exposure of the spine posteriorly and transpedicular screw fixation of the affected segment, the lamina of the affected level was removed en-bloc through the facet joint and site of lysis on both sides. Decompression of the nerve roots on both sides followed by discectomy and preparation of the endplates for bone substitute and cage was performed. Partial reduction of the olisthesis could be achieved through the discectomy and additional reduction through the application of lordotic rods. Finally, after preparation of the lamina and removal of the cartilage on the joint surfaces the lamina was placed and fixed through small screws 2.7mm to the facet joint on both sides. Results: The mean age of the 44 females and 26 males was 53.2 years (18-78). Main complaint at the time of presentation was low back pain (mean VAS 5.9/10) in 59 patients with mean duration of 11.9 months. Leg pain was found in 57 cases; L5 radicular pain in 46 of them. Four patients had foot weakness due to foraminal disc herniation. Olisthesis grade I was seen in 36 patients, grade II in 24 and grade III in 10 cases. The technique was performed in 57 patients for L5/S1, in 10 patients for 4/5, in 3 patients for L4-S1. Fusion of adjacent degenerative level was done in 11 cases. Mean operative time was 182 min. with a blood loss of 630 ml. complete reposition could be obtained in 60 patients. Three patients had dural tears intraoperatively, one patient had foot weakness postop. that improved completely within one week. Out of 2 Patients with superficial wound infections one needed revision surgery. Solid fusion was found in 65 patients at the last FU. Three patients developed symptomatic screw loosening of L5 which had to be re-operated. VAS reduced to 3/10 before discharge and to 1.2 at the last FU (mean of 20.5 months). Conclusion: Removal of the lamina facilitates decompression of the nerve roots and enables reduction of Olisthesis. The use of bone substitute prevents donor site complications. The laminoplasty preserves the osseous structures of the posterior column and prevents scar formation. It also facilitates performing the revision surgeries.

## ID: AGS26/3256

## P0428: Power-drill fluoroscopy-controlled freehand vs. navigation-assisted pedicle screw placement: a propensity score-matched retrospective cohort study

Felix Corr

1

, Faizan Kareem

1

, Silvio Heinig

1

, Benedict Caspari

2

, Karl Kapahnke

3

, Simon Behringer

1

, Erik Schulz

1

, Yesim Yildiz

1

, Nader Hejrati

1

, Oliver Bozinov

1

, Benjamin Martens

4

, Martin Stienen

1

, Stefan Motov

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery & Interdisciplinary Spine Center, HOCH Health Ostschweiz, Cantonal Hospital of St. Gallen, St. Gallen, Switzerland

2

Neurosurgery, University Hospital Düsseldorf, Düsseldorf, Germany

3

Neurosurgery, University Hospital Greifswald, Greifswald, Germany

4

Orthopedics, HOCH Health Ostschweiz, Kantonsspital St. Gallen, St. Gallen, Switzerland

Introduction: Precision in spinal instrumentation is critical in preventing neurological injury and hardware failure. While freehand (FH) and navigation-assisted (NA) techniques are well-studied, power drill-assisted methods remain underreported. This study compares the accuracy of power-drill fluoroscopy-controlled FH (PFH) versus power-drill fluoroscopy NA-assisted pedicle screw placement. Material and Methods: This propensity score-matched retrospective cohort study evaluated PFH versus NA pedicle screw insertion in degenerative thoracolumbar spinal pathologies. Only patients with postoperative CT scan were included. Pediatric, cervical, and non-degenerative cases were excluded. Matching was based on demographic variables. Accuracy was assessed and dichotomized using CT-based Gertzbein-Robbins classification (A+B = satisfactory, C-E = unsatisfactory), comparing complication rates and surgical parameters. A generalized linear mixed model was utilized for within-patient clustering to estimate accuracy determinants. Results: Among 1,102 screened cases, 528 had follow-up CT imaging. A total of 49 patients (314 screws) in the PFH and 61 patients (262 screws) in the NA were identified. Thirty-five patients per group have been matched in the final analysis (224 vs. 154 screws). PFH demonstrated significantly higher rates of satisfactory screw placement (GRS A + B: 98.1% vs. 94.3% pre-matching; 98.2% vs. 93.5% post-matching; p = 0.02) and a 66.7% lower likelihood of revision surgery at 12-month follow-up (5.71% vs. 17.14%, p = 0.26). Radiation dose (mGy·cm2) was 65% lower in PFH compared to NA (8,378.43 ± 5,302.08 vs. 23,793.02 ± 13,161.81, p < 0.0001). Pedicle screws were found to be longer in the PFH group (5.08 ± 0.54 vs. 4.9 ± 0.44; p < 0.001), while osteoporosis was associated with lower screw accuracy (OR 0.43, 95%CI 0.2-0.92; p = 0.02). Conclusion: PFH pedicle screw placement demonstrates higher overall accuracy compared to NA-assisted techniques with less radiation exposure in our cohort. PFH seems to be a viable, effective alternative to NA with reduced dependency on advanced imaging systems.

## ID: AGS26/3265

## P0429: Predictive factors of recurent lumbar disc herniation

Oussama Zaibet

1

, Mzid Ahmed

1

, Mohamed Amine Dhifallah

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Ahmed Adel Bezzaouia

1

, Khalil Habboubi

1

, Hassen Makhlouf

1

1

Institut Mohamed Kassab, Faculty of Medicine Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation surgery, while generally successful, can be complicated by recurrent disc herniation often requiring reoperation. The causes are multifactorial, potentially involving patient traits, spinal biomechanics, and surgical technique. However, identified risk factors remain inconsistent across studies and populations. This study aimed to evaluate the predictive factors within a specific Tunisian cohort. Identifying these factors is essential for improving surgical outcomes through better patient selection, tailored techniques, and targeted postoperative management. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 120 patients operated for single-level lumbar disc herniation at the Institut Kassab of Orthopedics between 2014 and 2024. Surgical techniques included standard discectomy with laminotomy, microdiscectomy, hemilaminectomy, total laminectomy, and discectomy with posterolateral non-instrumented fusion. Patients were divided into a recurrent group (n = 18, 15%) and a non-recurrent group (n = 102). Demographic variables (age, sex, BMI, smoking, diabetes, occupational factors), clinical parameters (history of trauma, pain characteristics), and radiological features (type of herniation, disc height index, sagittal range of motion, facet orientation/tropism) were analyzed. Results: The recurrence rate was 15%. Univariate analysis showed significant associations between recurrence and younger age, male gender, obesity, smoking, diabetes, traumatic events, heavy occupational lifting, transligamentous extrusion type, and surgical technique (total laminectomy vs. microdiscectomy). Radiological predictors included increased disc height index, greater sagittal range of motion, and augmented facet tropism, decreased facet orientation. Conclusion: In this Tunisian series, recurrent lumbar disc herniation was associated with both patient-related and biomechanical factors. Attention to modifiable risks such as smoking cessation, weight control, and occupational strain reduction, combined with careful surgical technique, may help decrease recurrence rates. Patients with high-risk profiles require closer follow-up and tailored postoperative rehabilitation.

## ID: AGS26/3266

## P0430: Is facet orientation the key to success in non surgical treatment of lumbar facet cysts ?

Nrupam Mehta

1

, Priyank Patel

1

, Abhay Nene

1

1

We are Spine, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Lumbar facet cysts are a common cause of radiculopathy. Percutaneous facet cyst rupture (PFCR) offers a minimally invasive, cost-effective alternative to surgery. This study evaluates PFCR outcomes in patients with favorable versus unfavorable facet orientations, with emphasis on symptom relief and recurrence. Methodology: Sixty-seven patients (32 men, 35 women) with acute radiculopathy (< 3 months) from lumbar facet cysts were included. Patients were grouped by facet orientation: 28 favorable and 39 unfavorable. Exclusion criteria were prior lumbar surgery, chronic symptoms, or other spinal disorders. Follow-up was conducted at 3, 6, and 12 months post-PFCR. Recruitment is ongoing to expand the cohort to 100-150 patients for broader analysis. Results: In the favorable orientation group, 27 of 28 patients achieved complete pain relief without recurrence at 12 months; one required surgery. In the unfavorable group, 27 of 39 reported complete initial relief, though 15 developed recurrent back pain. Among the remaining 12, two were lost to follow-up, six underwent surgery for persistent stenotic symptoms, and four had cyst recurrence requiring repeat PFCR. Conclusion: PFCR is highly effective in patients with favorable facet orientation, yielding sustained pain relief and minimal recurrence. In those with unfavorable orientation, initial outcomes are encouraging, but recurrence of pain and cyst regrowth are more frequent. Facet orientation appears to be an important predictor of long-term PFCR success. Expansion of the cohort will help refine efficacy and complication profiles.

## ID: AGS26/3285

## P0431: Lumbar interbody fusion for degenerative spondylolisthesis: clinical and radiological outcomes in a Tunisian cohort of 100 patients

Mohamed Amine Dhifallah

1

, Habboubi Khalil

1

, Oussama Zaibet

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Ben Brahim Safouen

1

, Hachicha Hassan

1

1

Institut Med Taieb Kassab, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Degenerative spondylolisthesis is a frequent cause of low back pain and radiculopathy in elderly patients. Lumbar interbody fusion is widely performed to restore spinal stability, decompress neural elements, and improve functional outcomes. However, data from North African populations remain scarce. This study aimed to evaluate the clinical and radiological results of lumbar interbody fusion for degenerative spondylolisthesis in a Tunisian cohort. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 100 patients (72 women, 28 men) operated between 2010 and 2022 for single- or two-level degenerative spondylolisthesis. The mean age was 59 years (range 42-75). All patients underwent posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) or transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) with pedicle screw fixation. Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for back and leg pain. Radiological fusion was evaluated by CT scan at 12 months. Complications and reoperation rates were recorded. Results: At a mean follow-up of 36 months, 85% of patients reported significant improvement in back and leg pain (mean VAS improvement: 5.2 points, p < 0.001). ODI scores improved from a mean of 62% preoperatively to 24% at final follow-up. Radiological fusion was achieved in 92% of cases. Early complications occurred in 12 patients (12%), including dural tears (5 cases) and superficial infection (4 cases). Late complications included adjacent segment disease in 6 patients (6%). The reoperation rate was 8%. Conclusion: Lumbar interbody fusion provides satisfactory clinical and radiological outcomes for degenerative spondylolisthesis in the Tunisian population. Despite a moderate complication rate, this technique remains a safe and effective option for restoring spinal stability and improving patient quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/3324

## P0432: Incidental durotomy in lumbar surgery: does it really change the outcome?

Nrupam Mehta

1

, Priyank Patel

1

, Abhay Nene

1

1

We are Spine, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Incidental durotomy is a common complication of lumbar spine surgery, with reported rates between 1% and 17%. Concerns include cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage, infection, pseudomeningocele, and delayed wound healing, often leading to conservative measures such as bed rest, acetazolamide, or prolonged drains. When the tear is irreparable intraoperatively, management becomes more challenging. However, it remains unclear whether such durotomies truly alter postoperative outcomes when managed with standardized protocols. This study aimed to assess the clinical impact of irreparable durotomies in lumbar decompression. Materials and Methods: One hundred patients undergoing lumbar decompression between 2022 and 2024 were prospectively followed for 12 months. All were managed with a uniform protocol: mobilization within 24 hours, no acetazolamide or head-low positioning, and uncharged drains maintained for up to 72 hours before removal. Results: Most patients (78.9%) recovered without headaches or prolonged stays. Complications were limited to wound CSF leakage requiring re-exploration (n = 2), postoperative meningitis (n = 1), delayed wound healing (n = 4), and pseudomeningocele without communication (n = 3). Conclusions: Accidental intraoperative durotomies that cannot be primarily repaired do not appear to significantly worsen outcomes when managed with consistent postoperative care. Most patients recovered without prolonged hospitalization, delayed mobilization, or escalated therapy. Although complications such as pseudomeningocele, infection, or wound issues can occur, their incidence remains low and manageable. These findings suggest that with proper protocols, irreparable durotomies should not be considered a major determinant of adverse recovery in lumbar decompression surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3357

## P0433: Reshaping the spine: sacral dome osteotomy for high-grade L5-S1 spondylolisthesis-safety, radiographic correction, and clinical outcomes

Ashish Patel

1

, Alfred-John Bayaton

2

, Kyle Dann

1

, Michael Coffin

3

1

The Spine Center at Duly Health and Care, Naperville IL, United States

2
