Kettering Health Grandview Hospital Orthopedic Surgery Residency Program, Dayton OH, United States

3

University of Cincinnati Department of Neurosurgery, Cincinnati OH, United States

Introduction: High-grade L5-S1 spondylolisthesis (Meyerding Grade III or higher) presents complex challenges in alignment correction, particularly in restoring spinopelvic parameters and achieving safe reduction. Conventional reduction techniques risk neural injury and may inadequately address segmental alignment. Sacral dome osteotomy (SDO) facilitates posterior translation of L5 by resecting the anterior dome of the sacrum, allowing anatomical reduction while preserving neural integrity. This study aims to evaluate the safety, radiographic correction, and clinical outcomes of SDO in the treatment of high-grade L5-S1 spondylolisthesis. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on patients undergoing SDO and L4-S1 posterior spinal fusion (PSF), with or without lateral interbody fusion (LIF), at a single institution. Demographic, perioperative, and radiographic data were collected. Outcome measures included estimated blood loss (EBL), length of stay (LOS), Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores, fusion rates, and revision surgery. Pre- and postoperative sagittal parameters were assessed, including lumbar lordosis (LL), L5-S1 and L4-5 segmental lordosis, pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), and PI-LL mismatch. Results: Five patients met inclusion criteria (mean age 39.2 ± 17 years; BMI 25 ± 6 kg/m2). Mean EBL was 1280 ± 766 mL; mean LOS was 7 ± 4.5 days. VAS improved from 6.4 ± 2 preoperatively to 0.8 ± 1.3 at final follow-up (mean 815 ± 344 days). LL improved from 72° ± 6.5 to 75° ± 16; PI-LL mismatch decreased from 11° ± 10.6 to 6.5° ± 14. Segmental lordosis at L5-S1 increased from 13.3° ± 9 to 21.3° ± 10, and at L4-5 from 27.2° ± 5 to 28.3° ± 9.5. L4-S1 lordosis improved from 35° ± 13 to 37.5° ± 12.8. PT increased slightly (23.7° ± 4 to 25.9° ± 9), and SS decreased marginally (58.3° ± 6.5 to 56° ± 8). One patient underwent hardware removal at 52 weeks. No cases of pseudoarthrosis, sacral fracture, or permanent neurological deficits were observed. Conclusion: SDO offers a safe and effective strategy for achieving bony reduction in high-grade L5-S1 spondylolisthesis. In this small cohort, it enabled substantial radiographic correction and pain relief with minimal complications or need for revision. Larger studies are warranted to validate these findings and define optimal indications.

5

Neurosurgery Department, Cairo University, Cairo, Egypt, Neurosurgery Department, University at buffalo, Buffalo, NY, United States

6

Orthopaedics and Trauma Surgery Università Campus Bio-Medico di Roma, Fondazione Policlinico Universitario Campus Bio-Medico, Rome, Italy

7

Department of Traumatology, University Hospital Centre Sestre ilosrdnice, Zagreb, Croatia

8

Department of Orthopaedic and Trauma Surgery Campus Bio-Medico University of Rome, Rome, Italy

9

Department of Neurosurgery, Bergmannstrost Hospital, Halle, Germany

10

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, New York, United States

11

Department of Orthopaedics, Emory University, Atlanta, United States

12

USC Spine Centre, Department of Neurological Surgery and Orthopaedics, Keck School of Medicine, University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

13

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, United States

14

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, NYU Grossman School of Medicine, New York, United States

15

AO Spine International, AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

Introduction: Bracing after lumbar discectomy surgery is a controversial topic with paucity of related scientific literature. Previous surveys on spine surgeons’ preferences were limited both in geographical coverage and number of respondents. Cross sectional observational study. The aim of this study is to fill this gap in literature. Material and Methods: An international online survey among AO spine members regarding the postoperative recommendations for bracing, activity restriction, and associated factors, was performed. Results: A total of 703 spine surgeons participated in the survey of which 34% recommended postoperative bracing, with half of them reported usage for four weeks. Main influencing variables were respondents from Europe/South Africa region, greater extent of bony decompression, and larger amount of excised disc material. Seventy-nine percent of the respondents recommended postoperative activity restrictions. Prolonged standing, prolonged sitting, and driving were usually restricted for two weeks. Bending, twisting, lifting, and low intensity physical activities were restricted for 3 months, while high intensity physical activities and direct contact sports for 6 months were restricted. Conclusion: Despite recent insights into the literature showing a lack of efficacy, one third of a large cohort of international spine surgeons practice bracing after lumbar discectomy for variable duration. A more extensive bony decompression, and a more aggressive disc material debulking seems to significantly influence the surgeons’ decision to brace. More high-quality studies are warranted on this topic to reach an evidence-based recommendation.

## P0435: Lateral lumbar interbody fusion (XLIF) for the management of lumbar pseudarthrosis: technical note and case series results

Sebastian Cabello

1

, sebastian blanco gruebler

2

, Gabriel Fuenzalida

2

, Juan Peña

2

, Matias Blanco

3

, Facundo Álvarez

1

, Jorge Garrido

1

, Lyonel Beaulieu

1

, José Fleiderman

4

, Ignacio Cirillo

4

, Samuel Pantoja

1

, Roberto Postigo

1

1

Clinica Universidad de Los Andes, Santiago, Chile

2

Universidad de Los Andes, Santiago, Chile

3

Universidad San Sebastian, Concepcion, Chile

4

Clinica Las Condes, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: The number of lumbar fusions performed for degenerative pathology has increased, leading to more revision surgeries, frequently due to pseudarthrosis, defined as the absence of bony consolidation one year after the index surgery. Its incidence ranges from 5% to 35% and is associated with both surgical- and patient-related risk factors. Pseudarthrosis is linked to morbidity, reoperation, and increased costs. The most common clinical presentation is mechanical axial pain, and diagnosis should combine imaging and clinical symptoms. Revision procedures must address both mechanical and biological factors, with possible strategies including interbody fusion and correction of risk factors. Posterior revision surgery poses significant challenges, such as fibrosis, adhesions, potential neurological complications, and high recurrence and failure rates. Lateral approaches, such as extreme lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF), may offer advantages by avoiding prior surgical scars, reducing operative time, blood loss, and enhancing recovery. The main objective is to describe clinical outcomes of patients undergoing revision surgery for lumbar pseudarthrosis using the LLIF approach. Material and Methods: A retrospective descriptive study was conducted including patients who underwent revision surgery for lumbar pseudarthrosis using LLIF between July 2019 and July 2024 at Clinica Universidad de los Andes. Results: Our case series included 9 patients who underwent exclusive lateral approach for pseudarthrosis management. There were 7 men and 2 women, with a mean age of 67.6 years (range 49-78, SD 10.1) and mean BMI of 27.0 kg/m2. The mean postoperative interval from the index surgery was 566.9 days. Fusion was achieved in 8 of 9 patients; one patient later required reoperation due to adjacent segment disease. Titanium cages were used in some cases, while the rest were PEEK. The mean operative time was 134 minutes. Mean immediate postoperative VAS was 2.88, decreasing to 1.41 at 24 hours. The mean ODI was 8%. Four cases of left-sided cruralgia and three cases of left psoas paresis related to the approach were reported; all symptoms were transient, resolving within 5 to 15 days. Conclusion: This case series demonstrates short-term results of LLIF as an alternative to traditional posterior revision for lumbar pseudarthrosis. Outcomes suggest that this minimally invasive approach achieves adequate fusion with low complication rates. Further studies with larger cohorts, longer follow-up, and direct comparison with the standard posterior technique are warranted.

## P0436: Influence of sagittal spinal balance on mid-term functional outcome after discectomy

Bousrih Anis

1

, Houssem Eddine Chahed

1

, HIchem Abid

1

, yousef sbai

1

, Ramzi Bouzidi

1

, Mouadh Nefiss

1

1

Mongi Slim Hospital, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Discectomy is the reference treatment for lumbar disc herniation resistant to conservative therapy or presenting with neurological signs. However, functional outcomes vary between studies and may depend on several factors, including sagittal balance. The aim of this work was to assess the impact of spinal sagittal alignment on clinical and functional outcomes after discectomy. Material and Methods: This retrospective study covered a 6-year period (2015-2021) with a minimum follow-up of 36 months. All patients who underwent lumbar discectomy were included. Collected data comprised demographics and spinopelvic parameters-pelvic incidence, sacral slope, and lumbar lordosis-measured pre- and postoperatively. Postoperative outcomes were evaluated at final follow-up using residual pain on a Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Results: The series included 65 patients with a mean age of 45.4 years and a sex ratio of 0.7. Thirteen percent of patients had Roussouly type-1 spines, 46% type-2, and 41% type-3 or 4. The mean ODI improved from 30.9 preoperatively to 14.8 at final follow-up. Mean VAS pain scores decreased from 7.1 to 3.87. Outcomes were better in patients with type-1 and type-2 spines, but comparison across Roussouly types showed no statistically significant difference in functional results. Conclusion: Sagittal balance did not significantly influence mid-term functional outcomes after lumbar discectomy. Nevertheless, assessing sagittal alignment remains an important step in preoperative evaluation.

## ID: AGS26/3392

## P0437: Anterior to posterior docking: a feasibility study of lumbar plexus displacement in lateral lumbar interbody fusion

Ashish Patel

1

, Alfred-John Bayaton

2

, Michael McDermott

2

, Kyle Dann

1

, Mihir Chandra

1

1

The Spine Center at Duly Health and Care, Naperville IL, United States

2

Kettering Health Grandview Hospital Orthopedic Surgery Residency Program, Dayton OH, United States

Introduction: Transpsoas lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) at L4-5 navigates a constrained safe zone due to anterior migration of the lumbar plexus and the ventral position of the great vessels. This study evaluated whether initial docking at the anterior one-third, followed by gradual posterior advancement under intraoperative neuromonitoring, could displace the plexus posteriorly and expand the anterior corridor without compromising motor function. This study will assess the feasibility of posterior lumbar plexus displacement using anterior-first docking at L4-5. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was performed on 74 consecutive patients who underwent prone LLIF at the L4-5 segment with institution accessible pre-operative MRI and post-op CT scans. Preoperative axial T2-weighted MRI were used to localize the left femoral nerve. A horizontal reference line was drawn at the posterior margin of the disc space, just anterior to the spinal canal. From this reference, two perpendicular measurements were obtained: one to the anterior-most point of the disc, defining the anteroposterior (AP) disc diameter, and one to the anterior-most point of the femoral nerve. Nerve position was expressed as a percentage of the total AP disc depth. Postoperative CT scans were reviewed using the same reference line to measure the perpendicular distance to the posterior edge of the implant. Femoral nerve movement was calculated as the absolute difference in millimeters between the preoperative nerve location and the final implant position. Results: The mean AP disc diameter was 41.8 ± 4.9 mm. The femoral nerve lay 16.3 ± 5.1 mm from the posterior margin, corresponding to 38.8 ± 10.8% of disc depth. The posterior edge of the implant measured 8.7 ± 3.7 mm, corresponding to 20.9 ± 8.7% of disc depth. Femoral nerve movement averaged 7.6 ± 4.6 mm posteriorly. No patient exhibited intraoperative neuromonitoring alerts indicative of neural injury, nor demonstrated ≤ 3/5 knee extension strength immediately postoperatively or at the two-week follow-up. Conclusion: Final implant position consistently measured posterior to the preoperative femoral nerve location, suggesting transient plexus displacement during surgery. As preoperative MRI was obtained supine, the degree to which this shift reflects prone positioning versus anterior-to-posterior dilator advancement remains uncertain. Nonetheless, the absence of postoperative motor deficits supports the feasibility of this docking strategy to safely expand the anterior corridor at L4-5.

## P0438: Effect of Body Mass Index on perioperative outcomes in minimally invasive oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion versus open fusions: a multivariant analysis

Amir Sharif

1,2

, Hamid Reza Abbasi

2

1

Sana Regio Klinikum Pinneberg, Pinneberg, Germany

2

Inspired Spine, Burnsville, United States

Introduction: Oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion (OLLIF) is a minimally invasive lumbar surgery. Differences in resource consumption between open spinal surgeries, transformational lumbar interbody fusions (TLIF) and OLLIF, are not documented. We monetize quantifiable differences in resource utilization between the two procedures. A retrospective review of 124 surgeries was performed (OLLIF = 69, TLIF = 55). Standard conversion factors were used and values reported based on the levels (1-4) addressed at surgery. One level surgery time (OLLIF 62.9 vs. TLIF 134.9 minutes) and surgical expense (OLLIF $5,253 vs. TLIF $11,264) were reduced in the OLLIF population. Inpatient costs (OLLIF $5,712 vs. TLIF $9,271) and length of stay (LOS) were also reduced (OLLIF 2.6 vs. TLIF 4.2 days). Per case, reduced resource consumption suggests lower total hospital costs. Reduced surgical time and LOS can result in greater patient throughput per operating room and patient bed for OLLIF patients in hospitals that have resourced constrained environments. Material and Methods: We monetize quantifiable differences in resource utilization between the two procedures. A retrospective review of 124 surgeries was performed (OLLIF = 69, TLIF = 55). Standard conversion factors were used and values reported based on the levels (1-4) addressed at surgery. Results: One level surgery time (OLLIF 62.9 vs. TLIF 134.9 minutes) and surgical expense (OLLIF $5,253 vs. TLIF $11,264) were reduced in the OLLIF population. Inpatient costs (OLLIF $5,712 vs. TLIF $9,271) and length of stay (LOS) were also reduced (OLLIF 2.6 vs. TLIF 4.2 days). Conclusion: Per case, reduced resource consumption suggests lower total hospital costs. Reduced surgical time and LOS can result in greater patient throughput per operating room and patient bed for OLLIF patients in hospitals that have resourced constrained environments.

## ID: AGS26/3418

## P0439: Multicenter comparative outcomes of endoscopic transforaminal vs interlaminar lumbar discectomy

Paula Krause

1

, Jannik Leyendecker

2

, Jan Bredow

2

, Tobias Prasse

3

, Pia Rückels

2

, Christoph Hofstetter

1

1

Department of Neurological Surgery, University of Washington, Seattle, United States

2

Krankenhaus Porz am Rhein, Klinik für Orthopädie, Unfallchirurgie und Wirbelsäulenchirurgie, Cologne, Germany

3

Orthopädie und Unfallchirurgie Uniklinik Köln, Cologne, Germany

Background: Lumbar disc herniation (LDH) is among the most common spinal disorders and a leading indication for surgical intervention in degenerative spine care. Over the past two decades, full-endoscopic spine surgery (FESS) has become a widely adopted minimally invasive alternative, offering precise neural decompression with minimal collateral tissue disruption. Two principal surgical corridors are used: the transforaminal approach (TELD), best suited for foraminal and upper lumbar pathology, and the interlaminar approach (IELD), advantageous at L5/S1 and for central or paracentral herniations. Despite their widespread adoption, prospective comparative evidence between TELD and IELD remains scarce. Leveraging high-frequency, app-based patient-reported outcomes, this multicenter study provides a temporally resolved analysis of recovery trajectories, offering new insight into how procedural geometry influences clinical recovery. Methods: In this prospective, multicenter cohort study, 573 patients underwent ambulatory full-endoscopic single-level lumbar discectomy (TELD: n = 351; IELD: n = 222). Perioperative data were documented via a digital health platform (SPINEHealthie). Patient-reported outcome measures (back pain, leg pain, Oswestry Disability Index [ODI]) were collected daily during the first postoperative week and at 2 weeks, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months. Given baseline imbalances in age, BMI, herniation morphology, and L5/S1 distribution, adjusted linear mixed-effects models were applied to account for these covariates. Results: Both TELD and IELD resulted in marked and sustained improvement in pain and disability. IELD was associated with superior early outcomes. Back pain scores were significantly lower from 2 weeks to 6 months (2 weeks: -0.77 [95% CI -1.38 to -0.16], p = 0.014; 3 months: -0.87 [-1.54 to -0.21], p = 0.010; 6 months: -1.27 [-1.98 to -0.56], p < 0.001), converging by 12 months. Leg pain improved more rapidly after IELD, with significant differences from days 1-3 through 6 months (days 1-3: -0.87 [-1.52 to -0.22], p = 0.009; days 4-7: -0.98 [-1.64 to -0.32], p = 0.004; 2 weeks: -1.15 [-1.81 to -0.48], p < 0.001; 6 months: -1.55 [-2.32 to -0.79], p < 0.001), converging by 12 months. ODI trajectories likewise favored IELD in the intermediate phase (2 weeks: -2.50 [-4.91 to -0.10], p = 0.042; 3 months: -4.00 [-6.58 to -1.43], p = 0.002; 6 months: -6.14 [-8.89 to -3.40], p < 0.001), with no significant differences at 12 months. Conclusions: Both TELD and IELD are effective, tissue-preserving techniques for lumbar discectomy with durable one-year outcomes. IELD was associated with faster and more pronounced improvements in pain and function, whereas TELD remains valuable particularly for foraminal pathology and non-L5/S1 levels. These findings highlight the importance of anatomy-guided, individualized selection of surgical approach in endoscopic lumbar discectomy.

## ID: AGS26/3465

## P0440: Association of lumbar spine fusion with increased long-term risk of hip, knee, and ankle arthroplasty: a matched cohort study of adjacent joint degeneration

Joshua Wang

1

, Allen Wang

1

, Pouya Alijanipour

1

1

University of Texas Medical Branch, Galveston, United States

Introduction: Spinal fusion surgery is known to alter spinal biomechanics, but its long-term impact on adjacent lower extremity joints remains unclear. We hypothesized that lumbar spinal fusion accelerates degenerative changes in adjacent joints, increasing the risk of hip, knee, and ankle arthroplasty. Spinal fusion surgery is known to alter spinal biomechanics, but its long-term impact on adjacent lower extremity joints remains unclear. We hypothesized that lumbar spinal fusion accelerates degenerative changes in adjacent joints, increasing the risk of hip, knee, and ankle arthroplasty. Material and Methods: A retrospective matched cohort study was conducted using a large electronic health record database. Adult patients who underwent lumbar were compared to matched controls without spinal fusion. Matching was performed 1:1 for age, sex, ethnicity, body mass index, and comorbidities including osteoporosis, chronic kidney disease, liver disease, type 2 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis. Primary outcomes were rates of total hip arthroplasty, total knee arthroplasty, and ankle arthroplasty over 1-, 3-, and 5-year follow-up periods. Hazard ratios (HR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. Results: At one year, lumbar fusion patients had significantly higher rates of THA (HR 1.59, 95% CI 1.29-1.95, p < 0.001), while rates of TKA and ankle arthroplasty were not significantly different. At 3 years, the fusion group demonstrated increased risk of both THA (HR 1.83, 95% CI 1.55-2.15, p < 0.001), and TKA (HR 1.28, 95% CI 1.12-1.47, p < 0.001), with no significant difference in ankle arthroplasty. By 5 years, lumbar fusion was associated with elevated risks of THA (HR 1.78, 95% CI 1.54-2.05, p < 0.001), TKA (HR 1.45, 95% CI 1.29-1.63, p < 0.001), and ankle arthroplasty (HR 2.90, 95% CI 1.15-7.29, p = 0.018). Conclusion: Lumbar spinal fusion was associated with a significantly increased long-term risk of total hip, knee, and ankle arthroplasty in this cohort, suggesting that the original spinal pathology requiring lumbar fusion or the altered biomechanics post-fusion may contribute to accelerated degeneration in the weight-bearing joints of lower extremities advancing from proximal to distal. These findings may underscore the importance of counseling patients undergoing lumbar fusion on potentially subsequent joint consequences.

## ID: AGS26/3474

## P0441: Fully endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion with an expandable cage to treat grade II L5/S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis: an observational cohort study with more than 5 year follow up

Jian Shen

1

1

The Endoscopic Spine Institute of New York, New York, United States

Introduction: Fully endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) surgery potentially offers better endplate preparation (increased fusion rate), less complications and quick recovery, than traditional open or tubular TLIF surgeries. Material and Methods: 36 patients with symptomatic grade II L5/S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis were treated with uniportal endoscopic TLIF from January, 2018 through January, 2024. In this consecutive observational cohort study, pre- and postoperative VAS scores of back pain and leg pain, ODI, as well as clinical fusion rate were analyzed at a final follow-up of more than 5 years. Results: Intraoperative data and clinical outcomes were analyzed and reported. For all the 36 patients, intra-operative blood loss was average 63ml. There were no infections or wound complications. All 36 patients had successful reduction of spondylolisthesis and placement of a large size expandable interbody cage. At the final 5-year follow-up, the postoperative VAS scores of back and leg pain, ODI score were all significantly improved comparing with the preoperative scores. Clinical fusion rate was 100%, but 2 patients developed lateral recess and/or foraminal heterotopic ossification which required re-operation. Conclusion: Uniportal fully endoscopic TLIF is a new and effective technique with potential advantages of better end plate preparation, safe placement of large interbody fusion device, faster recovery and better fusion rate, hence good post-operative clinical outcome for patients with grade II L5/S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis.

## ID: AGS26/1772

## P0442: Development and Validation of a Magnetic Resonance Imaging-Based Predictive Model for Recurrence after Simple Nucleotomy in Lumbar Disc Herniation

Wenlong Yang
1
, Chaoyuan Ge
1

1

Honghui Hospital, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, China

Introduction: This study aims to explore the development and validation of a predictive model for postoperative recurrence in patients with lumbar disc herniation (LDH) undergoing simple nucleotomy, based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on clinical and MRI data of LDH patients who underwent simple nucleotomy between January 2017 and December 2023. Multidimensional indicators were extracted, including demographic characteristics, conventional MRI sequence features, enhanced MRI parameters, cartilaginous endplate integrity, and degree of disc degeneration. Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) regression and multivariate logistic regression analyses were employed to identify independent predictors and develop a combined predictive model. The model's discriminative ability, calibration accuracy, and clinical utility were evaluated using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, calibration curves, and decision curve analysis (DCA). Results: The results demonstrate that the predictive model, constructed based on MRI radiomic features combined with clinical risk factors, exhibits high predictive efficacy for postoperative recurrence of LDH. Conclusion: For patients after simple nucleotomy in LDH, the MRI-based predictive model can provide valuable support for individualized recurrence risk stratification and clinical decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/153

## P0443: Effect of lumbosacral transitional vertebra on bone density in the lumbar spine

Ömer Erşen

1

, Harun Tüzün

2

, Uğur Yüzügüldü

3

, Uğur Bozlar

4

1

Gülhane School of Medicine, Health Sciences University, Ankara, Turkey

2

Gülhane Education and Research Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

3

Balıkesir City Hospital, Balıkesir, Turkey

4

Gülhane School of Medicine, Department of Radiology, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: This study aimed to compare HU measurements in lumbar vertebrae between patients with LSTV and an age- and sex-matched group without LSTV using existing abdominal CT images. Additionally, lumbar bone mineral density differences of LSTV types according to Castellvi’s classification were also assessed. Material and Methods: We reviewed all abdominal CT scans of patients in our hospital’s PACS between July 2024 and June 2025 to cover a full period of one year. While data were recorded for patients with LSTV in the scans, patients without LSTV were also recorded to create an age- and sex-matched control group. HU values were measured on CT images of the lumbar spine obtained through the PACS software by one experienced radiologist and one experienced spine surgeon separately. The average of these two measurements was used in the study. Vertebral bodies were located in the mid-sagittal images, and an elliptical range of interest (ROI) was placed at the interpedicular section of the selected vertebral body and enlarged as much as possible without touching cortical bone. To achieve meticulous measurement, the cross-sectional area was measured. Since the greatest load distribution is at the lumbosacral junction, after measuring HU at all levels, the ratio of L5 and S1 measurements to the average of L1-4 measurements was calculated to reduce the effect of individual differences of the patients. Measurements of patients with and without LSTV were compared. Results were also evaluated separately based on LSTV type. Results: A total of 344 LSTVs (142 female, 202 male) were detected in abdominal CT scans. Castellvi type I was found in 238 studies, type II in 58 studies, type III in 34 studies, and type IV in 14 studies. Age and gender distribution according to LSTV type was not found to be statistically significant. HU values were significantly lower in the LSTV group than in the control group at all levels except L5. The ratio of the L5 measurement to the L1-L4 mean value was higher in the LSTV group, while the ratio of the S1 measurement to the L1-L4 mean value was similar between the groups. When the HU values of LSTV types were compared, the HU value at the L5 level was found to be higher in patients with type 3 anomaly than in other types (p = 0.008). When types 3 and 4, which include anomalies in the form of fusion to the sacrum, were compared with types 1 and 2, it was revealed that the HU value at the L5 level was higher in the fused types (p = 0.039) and the S1 value was lower in the fused types (p = 0.018). Conclusion: This study demonstrated that LSTV abnormality causes a decrease in bone density in the lumbar spine, except at the L5 level. The load distribution due to LSTV caused an increase in bone density at the L5 level compared to the average of the other lumbar vertebrae. Also, types with transverse processes fused to the sacrum resulted in changes in the L5 and S1 levels.

## ID: AGS26/203

## P0444: Pre-albumin as a predictor of patient reported outcomes following lumber interbody fusion

Brendan Fitzgerald

1

, Alex Tang

2

, Allison Kelliher

2

, Tan Chen

3

1

Geisinger Commonwealth School of Medicine, Scranton, United States

2

Geisinger Northeast Orthopaedic Residency, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

3

Division of Orthopaedic Spine Surgery, Geisinger Musculoskeletal Institute, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

Introduction: Lumbar fusion is a common surgical technique indicated for degenerative, deforming, and traumatic pathologies. Low prealbumin level is considered a biomarker of malnutrition and sarcopenia and has been shown to increase the risk of complications after spine surgery. However, studies assessing the impact of prealbumin on patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) after spine surgery are lacking in orthopedic literature. The purpose of this study is to determine if pre-op prealbumin level is a reliable predictor of patient-reported outcomes following lumbar interbody fusion. Material and Methods: Operative information, pre-op prealbumin level, and patient-specific information were collected from electronic medical records. The Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and PROMIS-10 were collected pre-op, 12 weeks, 6 months, and 1-year post-op. Inclusion criteria consisted of patients undergoing elective 1-2 level open or minimally invasive TLIF/PLIF at a single academic center for degenerative pathology. ROC analysis determined a prealbumin cutoff for achieving established minimal clinically important difference (MCID) values for each PROM. The impact of prealbumin on changes in PROM scores was assessed using univariate and multivariate linear and logistic regression. Mixed effects models were used to evaluate differences in PROMs between groups over time. Results: The optimal ROC curve identified a prealbumin cutoff of 20.15 mg/dL for achieving MCID on the ODI at 1 year (Sensitivity 84.2%, Specificity 100%, AUC 0.882). Using a prealbumin cutoff of 20.15, individuals with sarcopenia (< 20.15) were significantly older than individuals without sarcopenia. Prealbumin did not predict changes in PROMs from baseline to any time point postoperatively. Sarcopenia did not impact the odds of achieving MCID for any PROM. All PROMs improved significantly for both groups postoperatively and no significant differences between groups were found. Conclusion: Although patients with prealbumin < 20.15 were older than patients with prealbumin > 20.15, both groups had similar clinical improvements over time. Prealbumin may not be a reliable predictor of postoperative PROMs following lumbar interbody fusion.

## ID: AGS26/205

## P0445: The correlation between scoliosis curvature rigidity and spinal cord abnormalities: a retrospective clinical study

Mohamad Firas Wahbeh

1

1

Ibn Al-Nafis Hospital, Damascus, Syria, Arab Rep.

Introduction: Idiopathic scoliosis (IS), characterized by a lateral spinal curvature exceeding a 10° Cobb angle with vertebra rotation, affects approximately 2-5% of adolescents globally. Despite decades of research, the complex relationship between scoliosis and spinal cord abnormalities continues to challenge our understanding of spinal deformities. While the Cobb angle remains the standard measurement for curve severity, we've observed that curve rigidity the spine's resistance to corrective forces may offer deeper insights, particularly in cases with neural involvement. Our team conducted this retrospective clinical investigation to explore the nuanced association between scoliosis curve rigidity and specific spinal cord abnormalities through a biomechanical lens, comparing results with a control group of scoliosis patients with normal MRI findings. Material and Methods: reviewed 50 cases comprehensive data from 12 patients with scoliosis and documented spinal cord abnormalities (Group 1), and a comparison group of 38 patients with scoliosis and normal MRI findings (Group 2). All patients underwent thorough clinical assessment, detailed radiographic evaluation including flexibility measurements, and high-resolution magnetic resonance imaging. We quantified curve rigidity by calculating correction percentages from bending or traction radiographs, defining curves with less than 30% flexibility as significantly rigid. Results: analysis revealed tethered cord syndrome as the predominant abnormality in Group 1 (58.3% of cases either isolated or combined), followed closely by thoracic Syringomyelia (50.0%). Less frequently, we encountered Diastematomyelia and grade 3 spondylolisthesis (8.3% each). Progression patterns varied markedly by abnormality type patients with Diastematomyelia showed strikingly aggressive curve progression (-64.29%), while those with isolated tethered cord demonstrated more modest progression (-27.86%). Pain was remarkably common (66.7% of patients in Group 1) and correlated positively with curve magnitude. When comparing the two groups, we found statistically significant differences in final Cobb angles and correction rates, with Group 1 (spinal cord abnormalities) showing significantly larger final angles and worse correction rates. A significant difference in rigidity distribution was also observed, with all Group 1 patients exhibiting severe rigidity, while Group 2 showed varying degrees of flexibility ranging from flexible to moderate. Pain was more prevalent in Group 1 (66.7%) compared to Group 2 (26.3%). Conclusion: These findings lend substantial support to the “asynchronous neuro-osseous growth” theory, wherein a fundamental mismatch between vertebral column and spinal cord growth rates creates a tethering effect that constrains normal vertebral development. This constraint triggers a cascade of biomechanical adaptations rotational lordosis, vertebral rotation around the spinal cord axis, and progressive deformity driven by asymmetric growth plate loading (Hueter-Volkmann principle). Beyond theoretical significance, our work has practical implications for clinical practice, potentially reshaping MRI screening protocols, improving prognostication based on specific neural abnormality patterns, and informing more targeted treatment approaches for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis with neural involvement.

## ID: AGS26/208

## P0446: 12th rib anomalies and the development of idiopathic scoliosis: a theoretical framework for anatomical and biomechanical mechanisms

Mohamad Firas Wahbeh

1

1

Ibn Al-Nafis Hospital, Damascus, Syria, Arab Rep.

Introduction: Idiopathic scoliosis (IS), defined as a lateral spinal curvature exceeding a Cobb angle of 10 degrees without a clear structural cause, affects 2-5% of adolescents, particularly during periods of rapid growth. The quadratus lumborum (QL) muscle, anchored to the twelfth rib, plays a critical role in stabilizing the lumbar spine. Congenital anomalies of the twelfth rib such as agenesis, hypoplasia, or asymmetry may disrupt QL function, potentially exacerbating IS progression through biomechanical imbalances. This study proposes a theoretical framework linking these anomalies to IS development, integrating anatomical, biomechanical, and genetic perspectives. Material and Methods: A systematic review was conducted of studies published between January 2022 and June 2025 across PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and Embase. From 420 articles screened using PRISMA guidelines and quality-assessed via the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale, 40 relevant studies were identified. A meta-analysis estimated the prevalence of twelfth rib anomalies in IS patients, with sensitivity analysis to assess result stability. An FEA model, developed using ANSYS 2023 R1, simulated lumbar spine dynamics, incorporating interactions of multiple muscles (QL, psoas major, obliques) and sensitivity analyses for muscle stiffness variations (±20%) and rib length changes (±30%). Anatomical and clinical correlations were reviewed to support the proposed mechanisms. Results: The meta-analysis revealed a 12% prevalence of twelfth rib anomalies in IS patients (95% CI: 8-16%) compared to 4% in controls (95% CI: 2-6%), with an odds ratio of 3.2 (95% CI: 2.1-4.8, p < 0.001, I2 = 40%). Sensitivity analysis confirmed result stability (OR = 3.0, 95% CI: 1.9-4.6). FEA simulations demonstrated increased lateral spinal loading (160 N in agenesis, 140 N in hypoplasia, 150 N in asymmetry, compared to 100 N in normal conditions) due to ineffective compensation by adjacent muscles (e.g., psoas major, obliques), elevating vertebral stress by 5-10%. Twelfth rib anomalies reduced QL force by 40% in agenesis and 20-30% in hypoplasia, supporting a “vicious cycle” hypothesis for IS progression. Proposed mechanisms suggest that asymmetric loading, genetic interactions (e.g., BNC2), and disrupted respiratory mechanics contribute to curve exacerbation. Conclusion: Twelfth rib anomalies represent a modifiable factor in IS progression by disrupting QL function and increasing lateral spinal loading. Findings from the meta-analysis and FEA modeling support this association, highlighting the need for screening rib anomalies using low-dose CT imaging and targeted physiotherapy. The study recommends longitudinal research and genetic analyses to confirm these mechanisms.

## ID: AGS26/209

## P0447: Atypical left-sided thoracolumbar scoliosis (Type 3C-L) with T9-T11 apical vertebra: a case study proposing a novel classification framework and tailored therapeutic strategies

Mohamad Firas Wahbeh

1

, Silvio Selfo

2

1

Ibn Al-Nafis Hospita., Damascus, Syria, Arab Rep.

2

Department of Physiotherapy, University Hospital of Trauma, Tirana, Albania

Introduction: Left-sided thoracolumbar scoliosis with an apical vertebra between T9 and T11 is a rare presentation within the Type 3C classification of the Physiotherapeutic Scoliosis-Specific Exercises (PSSE) Schroth Method. Existing classification systems (King, Lenke, Schroth) are predominantly designed for right-sided curves, limiting their applicability to atypical patterns. This study aims to document a rare case, evaluate its alignment with established classifications, and propose a refined descriptive framework. Case Presentation: A 14-year-old female presented with a left-sided thoracolumbar scoliosis (apex at T11, spanning T9 to L2) identified during routine school screening. Radiographic assessment revealed a Cobb angle of 56ز8 degrees, a coronal imbalance (CSVL-C7PL) of 75 mm, and an Apical Vertebra Transition Angle (AVTA) of 31 degrees. The L3 vertebra tilted toward the convex (right) side, with no structural proximal curve. The curve was classified as Type 3C per PSSE Schroth and A1 per Rigo-Weiss criteria. MRI confirmed the absence of underlying pathologies, and the patient was diagnosed with idiopathic scoliosis, referred for PSSE physiotherapy with bracing considered. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, targeting left-sided thoracolumbar scoliosis, Type 3C patterns, and T9-T11 apex cases (1990-June 2025). The case was analyzed using King, Lenke, and Schroth systems, with a proposed Type 3C-L framework to address taxonomic gaps. Due to the early stage of intervention, follow-up data are not yet available; however, a follow-up evaluation is planned at 6-month intervals over 12 months to assess Cobb angle, coronal imbalance, and quality of life (SRS-22). Therapeutic recommendations were developed based on biomechanical considerations. Results: The case aligned with King Type IV and Lenke Type 5C but deviated due to its left-sided direction, revealing limitations in existing classifications. The Schroth Type 3C framework was structurally applicable but required adjustment for the left-sided orientation and T9-T11 apex. Literature review confirmed the rarity of this presentation, with a 16% prevalence of left-sided curves at T11. The Type 3C-L subtype was proposed, incorporating left-sided curve direction, T9-T11 apex, and tailored PSSE exercises with adjusted bracing. Conclusion: This case highlights the need for refined classification systems to address atypical scoliosis patterns. The proposed Type 3C-L framework provides a structured approach for documentation and management. Tailored PSSE exercises and adjusted bracing are recommended, supported by a planned prospective cohort study (50 patients, 12 months) to validate the subtype, assess prevalence, and evaluate long-term outcomes. A multidisciplinary approach involving scoliosis specialists, physiotherapists, and Orthotists is emphasized to advance treatment strategies.

## ID: AGS26/212

## P0448: Apical vertebra translation angle (AVTA) in scoliosis: a theoretical framework for assessing spinal deformity severity and progression, pilot study

Mohamad Firas Wahbeh

1

1

Ibn Al-Nafis Hospital, Damascus, Syria, Arab Rep.

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) represents a multifaceted three-dimensional spinal disorder impacting 1-3% of young individuals, marked by lateral curvature surpassing 10 degrees alongside vertebral rotation. Although the Cobb angle serves as the primary diagnostic benchmark, its emphasis on the coronal plane overlooks crucial axial and sagittal dimensions that drive disease advancement. This work introduces the Apical Vertebra Translation Angle (AVTA) as an innovative measure, merging the apical vertebra's translational offset from the central sacral vertical line (CSVL) with the Cobb angle, refined by biomechanical adjustments based on vertebral position. The conceptual model seeks to deliver a more integrated evaluation of deformity intensity, focusing on vertebral stability and degenerative hazards, thereby paving the way for applications in non-invasive interventions like targeted physiotherapy and orthotic support. Material and Methods: The investigation employed a conceptual paradigm drawing on simulated parameters from existing scholarship, including apical vertebral translation (AVT) ranging from 4-6 cm, trunk width of 25-30 cm, and Cobb angles between 20-40 degrees. AVTA computation involves two key stages: first, deriving a normalized ratio of AVT to trunk width for scale invariance; second, modulating the Cobb angle via a coefficient (k) reflecting regional biomechanics (0.95 for thoracic, 1.0 for lumbar). Severity tiers are delineated as mild (< 20 degrees), moderate (20-30 degrees), and severe (>30 degrees). A theoretical biomechanical evaluation probed AVTA's effects on spinal loading, complemented by correlation modeling with Cobb angle and axial rotation informed by three-dimensional imaging research. Results: Simulated computations yielded a projected severity distribution: 25% mild, 55% moderate, 20% severe, exemplified by AVTA values such as 19 degrees for a 20-degree Cobb angle (mild). Robust associations emerged with the Cobb angle (r = 0.80-0.85) and moderate links to vertebral rotation (r = 0.60-0.70), with a 11.8-degree threshold forecasting rotatory instability. AVTA exceeding 30 degrees correlated with amplified facet joint loading by 25% and disc compression by 15-20%, heightening risks of degeneration and ligamentous damage, alongside a potential 2.5-fold elevation in chronic complications. Furthermore, elevated AVTA aligned with diminished quality-of-life metrics, notably in pain and self-perception domains. Conclusion: AVTA emerges as a pioneering conceptual index that enhances scoliosis appraisal by fusing translational and angular facets across planes, surpassing the Cobb angle in comprehensiveness. It bolsters guidance for conservative therapies and degenerative risk identification, yet necessitates prospective validation to affirm clinical relevance. This framework establishes a basis for advancing diagnostic instruments in scoliosis management.

## ID: AGS26/274

## P0449: Which vacuum phenomenon is more predictive for future junctional disorders? Intradiscal or intrafacet?

Emel Özcan-Ekşi

1

, Murat Ekşi

2

1

Private Practice, Istanbul, Turkey

2

İstinye University, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Recent spine studies focused on identifying whether intradiscal vacuum phenomenon (VP) was associated with spinal instability. However, none of them reported a direct association between VP and spinal instability following fusion for degenerative lumbar spine disorders (DSDs), namely junctional disorders. In the present study, we aimed to evaluate whether the VP was predictive for junctional disorders in patients who underwent short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion for DSDs at a tertiary spine center. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed prospectively collected database of patients who underwent short-segment decompression and fusion for DSDs. Pre-operative sagittal and axial computed tomography (CT) scans were evaluated in terms of intradiscal and intrafacet VP at all lumbar levels, respectively. Each VP was scored as 1 point. Then, the total VP score was calculated as the sum of intradiscal VP score and intrafacet VP score. Then, we analyzed the possible predictivity of VP for junctional disorders at final follow-ups of the patients operated for short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion. Results: Patients with junctional disorders had significantly higher total and intrafacet VP scores compared to those without junctional disorders. Total VP score had an OR of 1.217 (p = 0.014) and intrafacet VP score had an OR of 1.465 (p = 0.008). The ROC analysis depicted that the cut-offs value for total and intrafacet VP scores to predict junctional disorders following short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion were 1.5 points and 0.5 point, respectively. Conclusion: Vacuum phenomenon could be associated with junctional disorders in patients who underwent short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion for DSD. Intrafacet VP was more important than intradiscal VP in predicting junctional disorders. Proper surgical planning including the evaluation of both intrafacet and intradiscal VP at all lumbar levels is crucial to decrease the likelihood of junctional disorders.

## ID: AGS26/275

## P0450: Erector spinae could be the game changer in surgical decision-making in patients with lumbar spondylolisthesis: a cross-sectional analysis of an age-, sex-, subtype-, level-matched patients with similar spinopelvic parameters received surgical or conservative management

Murat Ekşi

1

, Emel Özcan-Ekşi

2

1
İstinye University, Istanbul, Turkey, 
2
Private Practice, Istanbul, Turkey
Introduction: In this study we aimed to identify whether paraspinal muscle morphology could play a role in surgical decision-making in patients with lumbar spondylolisthesis. Material and Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional analysis of a prospectively collected database between January 2013 and May 2023. Consecutive women and men, who visited our outpatient clinics with chronic LBP, neurogenic claudication, and had lumbar spine magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) for their complaints were included into the preliminary dataset. We compared the patients who had conservative management (conservative group) or underwent surgery for lumbar spondylo-listhesis (surgical group) in terms of intervertebral disc degeneration, end-plate changes, fatty infiltration in the paraspinal muscles and spinopelvic parameters. Results: Conservative and surgical groups were similar in terms of severe IVDD and Modic changes at any lumbar level. Surgical group had significantly fattier erector spinae compared to the conservative group. Regression analysis and ROC analysis revealed an OR of 1.088 and a cut-off value of 17 points for fatty infiltration in the erector spinae to predict which patient could undergo surgery for lumbar spondylolisthesis. Conclusion: Each 1-point increment in fatty infiltration in the erector spinae at any lumbar level increased the likelihood of surgery by 8%. Lumbar spondylolisthesis patients with fatty infiltration score for erector spinae at or above 17 were more likely to have surgery. We recommend clinicians to focus on improving erector spinae muscles in patients with lumbar spondylolisthesis.

## ID: AGS26/326

## P0451: Lumbar spine motion analysis - Current concepts

Hasjmy Mohamad

1

, Amirzeb Aurangzeb

1

, Zhihong Chew

1

1

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Lower back pain affects 49-80% of the population globally, with degenerative lumbar disc disease representing a significant portion of cases requiring surgical intervention. Accurate assessment of lumbar spine range of motion (ROM) remains critical for clinical outcomes, patient selection, and device evaluation. Current assessment methods vary significantly in accuracy, invasiveness, and clinical applicability. This review synthesizes and consolidates current concepts in lumbar spine motion analysis, evaluating available methodologies for measuring segmental and global lumbar ROM. Material and Methods: A comprehensive literature search was conducted on PubMed using the terms “lumbar spine” AND (“motion analysis” OR “motion evaluation” OR “motion assessment”), limited to peer-reviewed English language articles published between 2000-2025. Studies were systematically reviewed and categorized by methodology and clinical application. Results: The review identified multiple approaches for lumbar motion analysis, ranging from traditional static radiographic techniques to advanced dynamic assessment methods. Static flexion-extension radiographs remain the clinical standard despite significant inter- and intraobserver variability. Radiostereometric analysis offers high precision with submillimetre accuracy, however does require invasive marker implantation. Dynamic techniques including fluoroscopic kinematic analysis, wearable insertional measurements untis, marker-based and markerless optical motion capture systems provide real-time assessment capabilities with varying degrees of accuracy and clinical feasibility. Conclusion: Despite a plethora of motion analysis methods have been proposed, developed and tested over the years, static radiographs remain clinically ubiquitous in the assessment of lumbar spine ROM. No single method universally excels in accuracy, feasibility, and clinical applicability for lumbar motion analysis. These new techniques may potentially offer superior precision and dynamic assessment capabilities. Future research should focus on standardizing imaging protocols and validating emerging technologies against established benchmarks.

## ID: AGS26/349

## P0452: The impact of 12th rib length on lumber nerve morphology and cranial-caudal symptom shift

Juri Teramoto

1,2

, Hidetoshi Nojiri

1,2

, Shota Tamagawa

1,2

, Daiki Fukazawa

1,2

, Kazuki Nakai

1

, Yuta Sugawara

1,2

, Hisashi Ishibashi

1

, Hiromitsu Takano

1

, Muneaki Ishijima

1,2

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Juntendo University Faculty of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan

2

Department of Medicine for Orthopaedics and Motor Organ, Juntendo University Graduate School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Discrepancies between physical and imaging findings complicate accurate level diagnosis in spinal disorders. Anatomical studies suggested that 12th rib length correlates with cranial-caudal shifts of the lumbosacral plexus, but this remains speculative because the plexus has not been visualized in patients. This study investigated whether the conus medullaris and L4 morphology-particularly the branch to the lumbosacral trunk (LST)-could be visualized on MRI, and whether these findings correlate with 12th rib length and cranial-caudal symptom shifts. Material and Methods: This was a retrospective observational study on prospectively collected data, combined with cadaveric analysis. We included 160 consecutive patients who underwent surgery for lumbar disc herniation with preoperative coronal MRI between July 2015 and July 2025. Exclusion criteria were cauda equina syndrome, idiopathic scoliosis, double-level herniation, and cranial or caudal migration. MRI and radiographs were used to assess the conus medullaris level, LST branch visibility, and their relationship with 12th rib length and cranial-caudal symptom shifts. The straight-line distance to the conus was measured using the inferior endplate of T12 as a reference line; distances caudal to the line were recorded as positive, cranial as negative. The 12th rib length and conus distance were normalized to height (mm/m) to correct for body size. In the cadaveric study, 25 cadavers were evaluated for relationships among LST thickness, 12th rib length, and plexus deviation. LST thickness was defined as the LST branch/L4 ratio, calculated by dividing branch thickness by L4 root width. Results: A total of 127 patients (81 men and 46 women; mean age, 44.8 ± 17.4 years) met the inclusion criteria. A discrepancy between imaging and physical findings was observed in 39 cases (30.7%). Patients with caudally shifted symptoms had a more cranially located conus medullaris (16.7 ± 8.8 mm vs. 3.7 ± 6.6 mm, p < 0.001), shorter 12th ribs (78.4 ± 7.3 mm/m vs. 43.0 ± 22.3 mm/m, p < 0.001), and higher MRI visibility of the LST branch (17.6% vs. 77.2%, p < 0.001). The conus medullaris level correlated positively with 12th rib length (r = 0.36, p < 0.001). Cadaveric analysis demonstrated a strong negative correlation between 12th rib length and the LST/L4 ratio (r = -0.86, p < 0.001). Smaller ratios were consistently associated with caudal plexus shifts (p < 0.01). Conclusion: The 12th rib length was strongly correlated with positional shifts of the conus medullaris and lumbosacral plexus, explaining cranial-caudal deviations of symptoms. MRI evaluation of L4 morphology, particularly visualization of the LST branch, further reflected these shifts. These findings indicate a close link between bone morphology and neural anatomy. Incorporating 12th rib length and L4 morphology into preoperative assessment may improve diagnostic accuracy, resolve discrepancies between physical and imaging findings, and guide surgical level selection. In conclusion, the 12th rib may serve as a practical, readily available radiographic marker to optimize surgical planning and improve outcomes in patients with lumbar disorders.

## ID: AGS26/401

## P0453: Intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) alerts in severe spinal deformity corrections with baseline neurodeficit - Are they different from those without - results from international multicenter prospective Spinal Deformity Intraoperative Monitoring (SDIM) study

Saumyajit Basu

1

, Stephen Lewis

2

, Lawrence Lenke

3

, Justin Smith

4

, Ferran Pellisé

5

, Yong Qiu

6

, Ahmet Alanay

7

, Nasir Quraishi

8

, Randolph Gray

9

, Go Yoshida

10

, Amer Aziz

11

1

Kothari Medical Centre, Kolkata, India

2

Toronto Western Hospital, Toronto, Canada

3

Columbia University, Columbia University, New York, United States

4

University of Virginia Health System, Charlottesville, Virginia, Canada

5

Hospital Vall D´Hebron, Barcelona, Spain

6

Drum Tower Hospital of Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing, China

7

Acibadem Mehmet Ali Aydinlar University School of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

8

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals NHS Trust, Nottingham, United Kingdom

9

The Children`s Hospital Westmead, St. Leonards, Australia

10

Hamamatsu University School of Medicine, Hamamatsu, Japan

11

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: The characteristics of IONM alerts in spinal deformity surgery are different in patients with or without a baseline neurodeficit. Preexisting neurological deficit can be a risk factor in deformity surgery, however detailed analysis of this patient population regarding intraoperative neural monitoring alerts has not been reported earlier. Material and Methods: 20 international centers prospectively documented the demographics, radiographic findings, and surgical events of patients undergoing spinal deformity correction (major Cobb > 80°) with EMG, SSEP and MEP monitoring. Detailed neurological examination was performed at baseline, immediately post-op and prior to discharge from hospital. IONM alert was defined as a loss of amplitude of > 50% in SSEP or MEP from baseline or sustained EMG activity that lasts > 10 seconds. Post-hoc analysis of patients with baseline neurologic deficit vs patients with normal neurological status was performed. Results: 546 patients were analysed - 477 had no deficit (ND) or an isolated sensory deficit and 69 had motor deficits (D)of which 45 were isolated motor and 24 combined motor and sensory. There was significant difference (p < 0.001) in baseline MEPs and SSEPs, which was decreased or absent in 3.1% and 2.1% in ND and 18.8% and 14.1% in D patients. 66 (13.9%) of patients in ND had a total of 90 alerts during surgery, and 12 (17.4%) in D had 15alerts. LEMS score from baseline to postop worsened in 8.4% in ND versus 21.7% in D (p < 0.01). Decline in neurological status from baseline to discharge was recorded in 5.9% in ND versus 23.2% in D (p < 0.001). Surgical events were identified as potential causes in 68 alerts out of 90 in ND and 10 out of 15 in D (p = 0.4) with osteotomy and rod insertion being the most frequent cause in both groups. In D patients, 11 out of 15alerts fully recovered to baseline, 3 did not and 1 recovered only partially. Conclusion: Patients with preoperative motor deficits had a higher rate of decreased or absent baseline MEP and SSEP signal and a higher rate of neurological deterioration after surgery. The difference in the rate of alerts was not significant with osteotomy and rod insertion being the most frequent surgical cause of alerts in both groups.

## ID: AGS26/541

## P0454: Development of a comprehensive treatment algorithm for tandem spinal stenosis - Decision making and surgical strategy

Rishi Kanna

1

, Praveen Iyer

1

, Ajoy P Shetty

1

, S. Rajasekaran

1

1

Ganga Hospital, Coimbatore, India

Introduction: Tandem spinal stenosis (TSS) can present with a constellation of myriad neurological symptoms and signs. Whole spine MRI has improved the diagnosis but does not aid therapeutic decision-making. While most studies have focused on cervico-lumbar TSS, there is inadequate literature on TSS of other regions. Material and Methods: 238 patients surgically treated for TSS with a minimum follow-up of 2 year were studied to develop a clinico-radiological treatment algorithm for TSS. The patients were classified based on the areas of stenosis namely, cervico-lumbar (Type A -186), cervico-thoracic (Type B -11), thoracolumbar (Type C -24), and cervical, thoracic & lumbar (Type D -17). The criteria for diagnosis of TSS were cord compression and T2 signal intensity changes in cervical/ thoracic regions, and/or Schiza’s grade ≥ C lumbar canal stenosis (LCS). In the sequence of surgical algorithm, clinical severity was taken as the prime factor followed by cord signal changes. The outcomes were evaluated through Nurick’s grade (NG) and ODI. Results: Type A cervico-lumbar TSS was divided into four sub-types. Type A1-patients with clinical myelopathy without symptoms of LCS underwent cervical decompression alone (n = 58). NG improved from 3.8 ± 0.98 to 1.74 ± 0.74 (p < 0.05). Only 11 (18%) required lumbar decompression later. Patients with LCS (Type A2) without clinical myelopathy underwent lumbar decompression alone (n = 97). Four (4.1%) developed myelopathy during follow-up requiring a cervical surgery. Patients with predominant LCS symptoms with only signs of myelopathy (Type A3, n = 7) underwent lumbar decompression initially but all required cervical surgery within 2 years. Type A4 (n = 24) with both myelopathy and claudication symptoms were treated by combined staged decompressions procedures. Type B (n = 11) had patients with myelopathy due to compression at cervical and thoracic regions. They were sub-divided based on upper limb myelopathy. In patients with both upper and lower limb myelopathy (Type B1, n = 1 1), cervical decompression was done first (n = 6) followed by staged thoracic decompression. The NG improved from 4.6 ± 0.5 to 1.8 ± 0.3 (p < 0.05). In patients with predominant lower limb myelopathy (Type B2, n = 5), only thoracic surgery was performed. Only one required cervical surgery later. In Type C thoracic & lumbar TSS (n = 24), 20 were treated by thoracic decompression based on signs of myelopathy. The mean NG improved from 4.1 ± 0.7 to 1.9 ± 0.9 (p < 0.05). Six cases required lumbar level surgery later. Type D with stenosis at all three regions presented variedly, and underwent staged decompression (n = 17). Conclusion: Based on clinical features of myelopathy and claudication, along with specific diagnostic MRI criteria, the present study comprehensively classified all possible types of TSS. The algorithm provides specific surgical strategies for each subtype, based on a large cohort of patients. More than MRI evidence of stenosis and signal changes, clinical symptoms and signs were helpful in formulating treatment.

## ID: AGS26/586

## P0455: Validation of MRI-based vertebral bone quality (VBQ) score for opportunistic osteoporosis screening in patients with chronic low back pain: a retrospective comparative study

Shahzaib Riaz Baloch

1

, Raghad Barri

1,2

, Mohammad Sohail Rafi

1

1

Dr. Ziauddin Hospital, Clifton Campus, Ziauddin University, Karachi, Pakistan

2

Dr. Soliman Fakeeh Hospital Riyadh, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Opportunistic screening for osteoporosis using available imaging is crucial for early intervention. The MRI-based Vertebral Bone Quality (VBQ) score offers a non-ionizing method to assess bone mineral density (BMD) in patients already undergoing MRI for chronic low back pain (LBP). The objective of this study is to validate the VBQ score against the established standards of CT Hounsfield Units (HU) and DEXA T-scores in a cohort of patients with degenerative LBP. Material and Methods: This retrospective study, approved by the institutional ethical review committee, analyzed electronic medical records (EMR) of a tertiary care hospital from January 2020 to December 2024. We included 200 patients with chronic LBP who had undergone lumbar spine MRI, plus either an abdominal/pelvic CT or a DEXA scan within a 6-month period. VBQ scores were calculated from T1-weighted MRIs. Pearson correlation and ROC analysis were used to evaluate the relationship between VBQ, L1 CT HU, and DEXA T-scores. Results: A strong negative correlation was found between VBQ scores and both L1 CT HU (r = -0.72, p < 0.001) and lumbar spine DEXA T-scores (r = -0.68, p < 0.001). VBQ scores were significantly higher in patients diagnosed with osteoporosis via DEXA (3.48 ± 0.31) compared to those with osteopenia (2.95 ± 0.22) and normal BMD (2.51 ± 0.18) (p < 0.001). A VBQ score cut-off of 3.0 demonstrated a sensitivity of 88% and specificity of 92% for identifying osteoporosis (AUC = 0.94). Conclusion: The VBQ score is a highly effective, opportunistic tool strongly correlated with both CT and DEXA measures of BMD. Its integration into routine MRI reporting for LBP can significantly improve osteoporosis screening rates, enabling earlier diagnosis and fracture prevention in a high-risk population.

## ID: AGS26/606

## P0456: MRI based whole spine sagittal parameters are equivalent to standing whole spine radiograph measurements

Rishi Kanna

1

, Swathikaa Ponnuraj

1

, Ajoy P. Shetty

1

, S. Rajasekaran

1

1

Ganga Hospital, Coimbatore, India

Background: The importance of whole spine sagittal balance in several spinal diseases has been extensively studied. The current assessment is based on whole spine radiographs, which involves significant radiation risks, infrastructure and cost. The aim of this study is to evaluate the utility and comparability of sagittal spinal parameters measured in the mid-sagittal MRI of the whole spine. Methodology: 53 patients (disc prolapse, n=36, degenerative listhesis, n = 10 and degenerative disc disease, n=7) underwent standing whole spine radiograph and supine whole spine MRI. The T2 mid-sagittal whole spine section and the corresponding coronal T2 image were used to fix the femoral head centre and measure the different sagittal parameters. The parameters measured in both images were the C2 plumbline, C7 plumbline, C0-C2 Cobb angle, C3-C7 lordosis, T5-T12 kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, pelvic incidence, sacral slope and pelvic tilt and the Roussouly classification. The mean and median was calculated and the statistical tools used to compare were independent t-test and Wilcoxon rank sum test. Results: The mean age of the study population was 44.2 ± 10.7 years. The male:female ratio was 34:19. There were no significant differences between the whole spine X-ray and the MRI for C2 plumbline (p = 0.446), C7 plumbline (p = 0.536), pelvic incidence (p = 0.295), sacral slope (p = 0.809), pelvic tilt (p = 0.091), C3-C7 Cobb angle (p = 0.538) and lumbar lordosis (p = 0.125). There were significant differences between the X-ray and MRI findings of C0-C2 Cobb angle (p ≤ 0.001) and T5-T12 kyphosis (p ≤ 0.001). 75.4% patients (n = 40) had the same Roussouly type in both X-ray and MRI. On comparing the agreement for Roussouly type between X-ray and MRI using kappa statistics, agreement was substantial (0.68, 95% CI: 0.53-0.84). Conclusion: Supine whole spine MRI sequence can be used as an alternative to standing whole spine radiograph for measuring sagittal spine parameters, and classify the spine based on Roussouly classification. This can avoid radiation exposure and extra imaging cost in a patient. For specific analysis of upper cervical spine and thoracic kyphosis standing radiographs are more accurate.

## ID: AGS26/862

## P0457: Feasibility and accuracy evaluation of microfluidic chip for rapid detection of drug-resistant genes in tuberculosis clinical isolates

Wenjie Wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Qian Cheng

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: The objective of this study was to assess clinical isolates of Mycobacterium tuberculosis using a microfluidic chip, simultaneously detecting eight drug-resistant genes associated with first- and second-line anti-tuberculosis drugs. These findings were compared with phenotypic drug susceptibility results to evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of the microfluidic chip for diagnosing drug-resistant tuberculosis. Focusing specifically on clinical samples of spinal tuberculosis, the microfluidic chip was employed for species identification of Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex and detection of drug-resistant genes, aiming to offer guidance for personalized chemotherapy regimens. Material and Methods: A total of 544 clinical isolates of Mycobacterium tuberculosis with documented drug susceptibility results were gathered from the hospital's strain repository. This cohort comprised 500 drug-resistant strains and 44 susceptible strains. The 544 clinical isolates underwent testing using the microfluidic chip, with results compared against phenotypic drug susceptibility testing to assess the chip's feasibility and accuracy in detecting resistance to eight anti-tuberculosis drugs. Additionally, 136 patients diagnosed with spinal tuberculosis via pathological and radiological assessments were included. Tissue specimens (pus, caseous materials, or granulation tissue) were procured through lesion clearance or CT-guided puncture. These specimens were evenly divided, with one portion undergoing rapid culture using the BACTEC MGIT960 liquid culture system, followed by modified Roche absolute concentration method for drug susceptibility testing on positive samples. The other portion underwent species identification of Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex and detection of mutations associated with resistance to first- and second-line anti-tuberculosis drugs using the microfluidic chip. Phenotypic drug susceptibility testing served as the gold standard to evaluate sensitivity and specificity of the microfluidic chip in detecting clinical samples of spinal tuberculosis. Results: Among the 544 clinical isolates of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, there were 197 strains resistant to INH and 303 sensitive strains, 219 strains resistant to RIF and 281 sensitive strains, 97 strains resistant to EMB and 403 sensitive strains, 133 strains resistant to SM and 367 sensitive strains, 82 strains resistant to FQS and 418 sensitive strains, 102 strains resistant to AMK and 398 sensitive strains, 70 strains resistant to KAN and 430 sensitive strains, and 49 strains resistant to CPM and 451 sensitive strains. The sensitivity and specificity of the microfluidic chip for detecting INH resistance were 89.34% and 97.36%, respectively; for RIF resistance, 91.78% and 95.73%; for EMB resistance, 76.29% and 95.29%; for SM resistance, 87.97% and 97.00%; for FQS resistance, 95.12% and 96.65%; for AMK resistance, 88.24% and 97.74%; for KAN resistance, 84.29% and 97.21%; and for CPM resistance, 89.80% and 96.90%, respectively. Using a microfluidic chip, identification of Mycobacterium tuberculosis species was performed on 136 clinical samples of spinal tuberculosis, with a positivity rate of 87.50%. Among culture-positive samples, the sensitivity of Mycobacterium tuberculosis species identification by the microfluidic chip was 95.45%. The sensitivity for samples positive on both smear and culture was 95.80%, while for samples negative on smear but positive on culture, the sensitivity was 31.32%. Regarding isoniazid (INH) resistance detection, the microfluidic chip exhibited a sensitivity of 80.95% and a specificity of 91.04%. For rifampicin (RIF) resistance detection, the sensitivity of the microfluidic chip was 88.24%, with a specificity of 90.74%. The sensitivity for streptomycin (SM) resistance detection was 81.82%, with a specificity of 92.21%. For fluoroquinolones (FQS) resistance detection, the sensitivity was 88.89%, and the specificity was 97.47%. The sensitivity for ethambutol (EMB) resistance detection was 71.43%, with a specificity of 96.30%. Kanamycin (KAN) resistance detection showed a sensitivity of 75.00% and a specificity of 95.24%. Capreomycin (CPM) resistance detection had a sensitivity of 100% and a specificity of 98.84%. The average time required for Mycobacterium tuberculosis species identification and detection of drug-resistant mutations using the microfluidic chip was 6 (range 5-9) hours. Conclusion: The application of the chip in detecting Mycobacterium tuberculosis strains demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity in identifying common first- and second-line anti-tuberculosis drug-resistant mutation genes. The process is simple to operate and time-efficient. In the clinical testing of spinal tuberculosis samples, this microfluidic chip exhibits high sensitivity. Compared to traditional chips, it shows higher sensitivity and specificity, particularly in detecting ethambutol (EMB) and fluoroquinolones, thus providing a promising tool for tuberculosis diagnosis and drug resistance surveillance.

## ID: AGS26/1064

## P0458: Smartphone-based computer vision of finger kinematics for screening in degenerative cervical myelopathy

Viprav Raju

1

, Ramesh Arnest

1

, Huy Truong

1

, Anjishu Banerjee

1

, Yin Li

2

, Aditya Vedantam

1

1

Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

2

University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, United States

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) is a progressive spinal cord disorder that remains frequently underdiagnosed, with diagnostic delays averaging 1-4 years. Current assessments, including the 10-second grip-and-release test, rely on grip counts but are limited in accuracy, particularly in older adults with comorbid hand conditions such as osteoarthritis or peripheral neuropathy. There is a need for objective, scalable, and accessible screening tools. In this study, we evaluated a smartphone-based computer vision tool to quantify finger kinematics during the grip-and-release test, with the goal of improving diagnostic accuracy for DCM and correlating video-derived features with spinal cord compression on MRI. Materials and Methods: Smartphone videos were collected from 123 participants: 58 patients with DCM and 65 age-matched controls (healthy individuals and those with non-DCM hand dysfunction). The control group included three subgroups: (1) individuals with impaired hand dexterity due to various other conditions such as arthritis, peripheral neuropathy, or prior hand injury or surgery (n = 18); (2) individuals with symptomatic lumbar stenosis (n = 22); and (3) healthy individuals without hand dysfunction (n = 25). Finger landmarks were extracted using MediaPipe, and 250 kinematic features per finger were computed across both hands. Feature selection was performed using ANOVA (p < 0.05) and mutual information scores (> 0.01). A CatBoost classifier was trained on selected features using an 80/20 train-test split with five-fold cross-validation. For comparison, a logistic regression model was trained using grip count alone. Model performance was assessed using AUC, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity. Linear regression models examined associations between video-derived features and MRI-derived measures of spinal cord compression in 56 DCM participants. Results: Mean grip count was significantly lower in the DCM group (7.92 ± 3.27) compared to controls (10.26 ± 3.78; p < 0.001). Among control subgroups, the hand dysfunction group averaged 7.09 grips, the lumbar stenosis group averaged 10.65 grips, and healthy controls achieved 12.13 grips. The CatBoost model trained on 66 selected kinematic features achieved superior performance (AUC 0.90, F1-score 0.83, sensitivity 83.3%, specificity 84.7%) compared to the grip count-only model (AUC 0.69, F1-score 0.67, sensitivity 75.0%, specificity 46.2%). Among the top 20 SHAP features the thumb contributed the most (∼34%) followed by the Index and the Middle to classification decisions. Video-derived kinematic features were significantly associated with MRI measures of spinal cord compression, including sagittal diameter (R2 = 0.45, p = 0.001), transverse diameter (R2 = 0.43, p = 0.002), compression ratio (R2 = 0.42, p = 0.003), and maximum spinal cord compression (R2 = 0.36, p = 0.018). Conclusion: A smartphone-based computer vision tool analyzing finger kinematics significantly augments the conventional grip and release test in detecting hand motor impairment specific to DCM. Video-derived features demonstrated strong diagnostic accuracy and were associated with objective measures of spinal cord compression on MRI. Smartphone-based computer vision holds promise as an objective, scalable screening tool for DCM in both clinical and community settings.

## ID: AGS26/1079

## P0459: Comparative evaluation of DEXA and opportunistic quantitative CT for lumbar spine bone mineral density assessment in patients undergoing thoracolumbar spine surgery

Suhail AlAssiri

1

, Firas Alsebayel

1

, Dana Aldughiman

2

, Majed Abaalkhail

1

, Fahad Alhelal

1

, Nalia Ashraf

3

, Musaad Bin Dukhi

2

, Sultan Alotaibi

2

, Abdulazeez Alzailaie

2

, Mohammad Almosa

2

, Sami Aleissa

1

1

King Abdulaziz Medical City, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

2

College of Medicine, King Saud bin Abdulaziz University for Health Sciences, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

3

King Abdullah International Medical Research Center, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Osteoporosis is one of the most common diseases leading to generalized bone frailty. It is characterized by reduced bone quality and density, which increases susceptibility to fractures. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) is the most widely used technique for assessing bone mineral density (BMD). While it is accessible, involves relatively low radiation, and requires minimal operator training, DEXA has important limitations. As it only areal BMD (aBMD), which may fail to capture bone fragility in a significant proportion of patients. Quantitative computed tomography (QCT), in contrast, provides volumetric BMD (vBMD) measurements using standard CT scans. The aim of this study is to evaluate the diagnostic performance of quantitative computed tomography (QCT) in assessing bone mineral density. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study was conducted at King Abdulaziz Medical City, Riyadh. The study included all patients who underwent thoracolumbar spine fusion surgery between 2018 and 2025 with available preoperative CT and DEXA scans. Variables included demographics, clinical data, surgical details, and radiological data. QCT measurements were performed by a single blinded musculoskeletal radiologist. The categorical variables were reported by using frequency and percent. The categorical variables were compared by groups using chi-square/ fisher exact as applicable. The ROC analysis was generated by using logistic regression models. Analyses was conducted by using SAS software, version 9.4. Results: In total 160 subjects were recruited, 80 cases and 80 controls. The DEXA T-scores lumbar attenuation was 57% sensitive and 70% specific. The DEXA T-scores hip attenuation was 74% sensitive and 63% specific. The DEXA T-score lumbar screening (n = 149) shows BMD categories as osteoporotic 18(12%), osteopenic 45(30.2%), and normal 86(57.7%). There was a minimal agreement observed (0.21) between DEXA T-scores lumbar and hip compared to average CT scores. Conclusion: This study demonstrates that DEXA has limited sensitivity and specificity in detecting osteoporosis, particularly in patients with spinal degeneration. Opportunistic QCT derived from routine CT scans provides a more accurate assessment of lumbar spine BMD and identifies cases of osteoporosis that may be missed by DEXA.

 

## ID: AGS26/1093

## P0460: Motor evoked potentials and contact heat evoked potentials in degenerative cervical myelopathy: a retrospective cross-sectional analysis

Aleksandar Zuzul

1

, Markus Hupp

1

, Mazda Farshad

1

, Paulina Scheuren

2

, Michele Hubli

1

, Jan Rosner

3

, Patrick Freund

1

, Armin Curt

1

, Carl Zipser

1

1

Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

2
UBC, Vancouver, Canada, 
3
Aarhus University, Aarhus, Denmark
Introduction: Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy (DCM) is the leading cause of non-traumatic spinal cord injury. Decompressive surgery is the mainstay for moderate to severe cases of DCM. Diagnosis is currently based upon clinical examination and cervical cord MRI. The diagnostic workup can be complemented by neurophysiological examination to detect spinal cord damage, ruling out differential diagnoses, and monitoring the disease course. Contact heat evoked potentials (CHEPs) are considered a sensitive method for detecting spinal cord conduction deficits in mild DCM, superior to somatosensory evoked potentials (SEPs). Motor evoked potentials (MEPs) facilitate quantification of corticospinal conduction in DCM and to monitor the disease course.
