However, a comparison between MEPs and CHEPs has not yet been performed and may elaborate upon the complimentary utility of MEPs within the diagnostic workup of DCM. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional retrospective analysis of a prospective cohort study in 171 patients with DCM (NCT02170155) was performed. DCM was diagnosed through presence of one clinical sign or symptom of myelopathy and supportive findings on MRI. DCM severity was scored with the modified Japanese Orthopedic Association Scale (mJOA). CHEPs from cervical dermatomes C6, C8, T4 were obtained routinely in all patients, while MEPs of lower, and in one case upper extremity were performed as an additional exam upon individual clinical judgement. The patient record was searched for participants with DCM that were examined with both MEPs and CHEPs within a 30-day interval without surgical intervention or clinical deterioration between the examinations. The grading of MEPs and CHEPs was binarized to either “pathological” or “normal” and a contingency table was created for comparison. Results: From the original study cohort of 171 participants, a subset of 29 patients met the criteria for the final analysis. Exclusion criteria comprised of subjects who lacked both MEPs and CHEPs (n = 103), or too of an interval between MEPs and CHEPs (n = 39). The mean mJOA of the subset was 14.8 points as compared to 15.6 in the full cohort. From the subset, 58.6% had spasticity. With regards to neurophysiology, 58.6% (n = 17) had concordant grading in both modalities: 55.2% (n = 16) graded both pathological, 3.4% (n = 1) graded both as normal. Conversely, 41.4% (n = 12) patients had discordant grading: 27.6% (n = 8) graded as normal MEPs, but pathological CHEPs, 13.8% (n = 4) graded as pathological MEPs, but normal CHEPs. Conclusion: Our findings indicate that CHEPs and MEPs provide complementary information in a considerable number of DCM patients. In this subset of a cohort with mild DCM, CHEPs were more sensitive to detect spinal cord damage compared to MEPs. A significant proportion with concordant pathology in MEPs and CHEPs may be due to the anatomic proximity of the spinothalamic and corticospinal tract to the lesion. Interestingly, several patients showed pathologic MEPs while CHEPs were normal. This analysis is limited by its retrospective design and selection bias of patients that underwent additional MEP recording. Future investigations may elaborate if concordant grading is more frequent in patients with extended spinal lesions.

## ID: AGS26/1095

## P0461: The role of electrophysiological workup in lumbar spinal canal stenosis (LSCS): a systematic review

Guichande Duarte

1

, Markus Hupp

1

, José Spirig

1

, Mazda Farshad

1

, Martin Schubert

1

, Armin Curt

1

, Patrick Freund

1

, Carl Zipser

1

1

Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

Introduction: LSCS is a highly prevalent degenerative spine condition characterized by neurogenic claudication, radicular pain, and muscle weakness. While lumbar spine MRI is the imaging modality for detecting spinal canal narrowing, it correlates poorly with clinical symptoms. Electrophysiological methods, including electromyography (EMG), nerve conduction studies (NCS), and evoked potentials (MEP and SEP), may provide complementary information on neural dysfunction. Current guidelines support paraspinal electromyography (EMG) mapping for symptomatic patients with imaging confirmed stenosis (grade B). In contrast, the diagnostic value of other electrophysiologic tests in lumbar spinal canal stenosis (LSCS) remains uncertain. Material and Methods: A systematic literature search was conducted in Medline and Embase for original studies on LSCS between 2020 and 2024. Two independent reviewers screened studies for inclusion. Extracted data was synthesized qualitatively. Study quality was assessed using the Robins-V2 tool. PROSPERO registration (CRD42024622427). Results: Thirteen studies met the inclusion criteria; study quality was moderate. Needle EMG of the limbs was evaluated in 23% of studies to detect denervation as a sign of radiculopathy. 23% of the studies examined tibial nerve SEP or cauda equina MEP conduction time for lesion localization, with varying findings and utility for diagnosing LSCS. Surface EMG was investigated in 31% of studies and revealed significantly altered muscle activation patterns and compensatory gait adaptations in LSCS. Conclusion: There is an increasing number of studies combining surface EMG with gait assessments and tasks. This approach is interesting for being non-invasive with clinical utility to be further determined. Based on previous guidelines, paraspinal mapping is considered the gold-standard electrophysiological diagnostic tool. Interestingly, there were no recent studies on paraspinal mapping, indicating a shift to alternative methods.

## ID: AGS26/1149

## P0462: Does preoperative psoas muscle cross sectional area predict post-operative outcomes following lumbar spine fusion surgery? A systematic review

Dean Biddau

1

, Gregory Malham

2

1

Queensland University of Technology, Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Brisbane, Australia

2

Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Melbourne, Australia

Introduction: Low back pain remains a leading cause of global disability, with lumbar fusion surgeries increasingly utilized despite variable outcomes. Preoperative psoas muscle morphology has been proposed as a potential predictor of postoperative success. However, evidence supporting a link between psoas morphology and outcomes post fusion surgery is growing yet remains unclear. The purpose was to systematically evaluate whether preoperative psoas muscle characteristics predict clinical and radiographic outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery. Material and Methods: Five databases were searched from inception to June 2025. Included studies assessed preoperative psoas morphology (cross-sectional area, functional cross-sectional area, or volume) and reported postoperative clinical (pain, disability, quality of life) or radiographic outcomes (fusion rates, alignment, complications). Risk of bias was assessed using QUIPS, and evidence evaluated using GRADE. Results: Sixteen retrospective cohort studies encompassing 2,250 patients were included. Overall, data indicates that psoas morphology is not a consistent predictor of outcomes following fusion procedures. A small number of studies, however, suggests that the surgical approach may influence this relationship. Posterior fusion techniques showed some association where a larger psoas was linked to early improvements in pain and function, while lateral and anterior approaches demonstrated no relationship. However, findings were based on a limited number of low-quality studies and there remains a paucity of primary research. Conclusion: Current evidence does not support preoperative psoas assessment for predicting lumbar fusion outcomes. Higher-quality prospective studies with standardized measurement protocols are needed to clarify the prognostic value of psoas morphology in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1211

## P0463: A comparison between deep learning-based artificial intelligence and human performance in detecting osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures

Sungkyu Kim

1

, Sohyun Moon

2

1

Chonnam National University Medical School and Hospital, Gwangju, South Korea

2

Department of Nursing, Chosun University, Gwangju, South Korea

Introduction: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCFs) present significant health challenges, particularly as the population ages, and osteoporosis-related fractures become more common. Rapid and accurate diagnosis of an OVCF is crucial for appropriate treatment. However, in some cases, timely diagnosis and treatment are hindered by shortages of medical personnel and testing equipment. Deep learning-based artificial intelligence (AI) can solve such situations. This study aimed to assess the efficacy of deep learning-based AI in detecting OVCF and calculating compression rates on X-ray images. Material and Methods: Between January 2019 and December 2023, 100 patients with suspected vertebral compression fractures, comprising 780 vertebral bodies, were enrolled. Patients underwent anteroposterior and lateral spine X-rays, followed by CT or MRI within 5 weeks. BMD was assessed within a year of the X-ray. Each patient's plain radiograph was assigned a unique identification number, and diagnoses and compression rate calculations were based solely on these X-rays. To evaluate the AI model (version 3.0.0, DS-CF-01, DEEP NOID, Korea) in diagnosing osteoporotic vertebral fractures and calculating compression ratios, results were compared with those of two orthopedic surgeons (with 1 and 14 years of experience), one orthopedic resident, and one general physician. Results: AI demonstrated a diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity of 92.9%, 66.3%, and 96.6%, respectively, while the orthopedic surgeon with 14 years of experience achieved 96.8%, 86.3% and 98.3%, respectively. The surgeon with 1 year of experience achieved 95.9%, 82.1% and 97.8%, respectively. The orthopedic resident achieved 95.5%, 80% and 97.7%, respectively. The general physicians achieved 94.6%, 75.8%, and 97.2%, respectively. Especially, the AI's sensitivity was significantly lower than that of all physician groups (p < 0.01), but its specificity was as high as that of most physician groups. Pearson correlation coefficients for compression rates showed strong positive correlations between AI and each group of physicians (p < 0.01). Conclusion: The current morphometric assessment AI model has not yet achieved the diagnostic performance of a general physician. However, it has statistically useful specificity in large datasets. Further algorithm advancements are needed for primary use in clinical settings.

## ID: AGS26/1395

## P0464: Intraoperative neuromonitoring in diabetic spine surgery patients: a multivariate model for predicting neurological risk

Sydney Rucker

1

, Chase Walton

1

, Lakota Stitt

1

, Jessica Barley

1

, Robert Ferdon

2

, John Glaser

1

, Charles Reitman

1

, James Lawrence

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

2

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

Introduction: Intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) is a cornerstone of spinal surgery safety, designed to detect and prevent neurological complications in real time. Diabetes mellitus (DM), a prevalent comorbidity in spine surgery patients, is thought to interfere with baseline monitorability and increase perioperative neurological risk, though evidence remains inconsistent. Current literature considers isolated risk factors but rarely examines the cumulative effect of comorbidities within multivariate models. While several clinical characteristics have been associated with increased risk of IONM alert, the interplay of these characteristics with high-risk comorbidities such as DM remains understudied. Addressing this gap could enable more precise risk stratification and improve perioperative management. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed IONM data from 102 patients undergoing thoracolumbar or lumbar decompression and fusion between March 2022-March 2024. Demographic and clinical risk factors were collected via chart review for a total of 15 variables, including age, sex, BMI, ASA score, A1C, DM status, and relevant comorbidities. IONM alerts without postoperative deficit, as identified by postoperative neurological exams, were classified as false positives. Separate stepwise logistic regression models were constructed to identify significant predictors of three outcomes: (1) IONM alert, (2) postoperative neurological deficit, (3) false-positive alert. Full models included all 15 variables, and simplified models were generated by iteratively removing non-significant predictors to optimize fit. Model performance was assessed using -2 Log Likelihood (-2LL), Nagelkerke’s R2, Hosmer-Lemeshow test, and area under the ROC curve (AUC). Results: Model performance varied across outcomes, with full models providing the best overall fit and all models showing higher specificity than sensitivity. For IONM alerts, the simplified three-variable model (BMI, DM status, total monitoring time) demonstrated fair discrimination and good calibration (AUC = 0.72, R2 = 0.23, Hosmer-Lemeshow p = 0.696). Elevated BMI and longer total monitoring time were significant predictors of both IONM alert and postoperative neurological deficit (p < 0.05). Presence of DM was not a significant predictor of alert (p = 0.082, OR = 2.874) or false-positive alert (p = 0.08, OR = 2.437), but consistently demonstrated an elevated odds ratio suggesting a clinically relevant trend toward increased risk. Conclusion: Our findings suggest that readily available clinical factors-including BMI, monitoring time, and DM status-may inform perioperative risk modeling for patients undergoing spine surgery with IONM. While current model performance is modest, the observed effect size of DM highlights a trend likely limited by sample size. This study is ongoing, with a planned enrollment of 500 patients over multiple years to refine predictive accuracy and incorporate advanced modeling techniques. Such efforts may ultimately provide a robust, clinically actionable tool for perioperative decision-making in high-risk populations undergoing spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1414

## P0465: Occupational risks in spinal surgery: narrative review of the literature and perspectives for prevention

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

1

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

, Irene Lengua Haddad

2

, Juanita Hurtado Castro

2

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Universidad de Los Andes, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Spine surgery is a highly complex subspecialty, characterized by prolonged procedures and exposure to multiple occupational hazards. Several studies have reported high rates of musculoskeletal disorders, chronic ionizing radiation exposure, and psychological distress, including stress, anxiety, and burnout (1,2). These conditions negatively affect surgeon health, surgical safety, and professional productivity (3,4). International literature highlights that, compared with other surgical specialties, spine surgery entails particularly high occupational risks due to unfavorable ergonomic factors, intensive fluoroscopy use, and prolonged static postures (5,6). In Latin America, however, available evidence remains limited and largely descriptive. This review examines occupational risks among spine surgeons and the preventive measures reported in the literature. Material and Methods: We conducted a structured narrative literature review to synthesize evidence on occupational hazards in spine surgeons. Searches were performed in international databases (PubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, Web of Science) and regional sources (SciELO, LILACS) using keywords such as “spine surgeon,” “occupational hazards,” “musculoskeletal disorders,” “radiation exposure,” “burnout,” “ergonomics,” “sharp injuries,” and “occupational health.” Literature published between 2015-2025 was prioritized, though earlier conceptually relevant studies were also included. Eligible publications comprised original articles, reviews, surveys, and cross-sectional studies, as well as neurosurgery and orthopedic research with extrapolable findings. Anecdotal reports and documents lacking explicit methodology were excluded. Findings were organized into three categories: ergonomic and musculoskeletal risks, radiation exposure and environmental factors, and psychological and well-being outcomes. Results: Studies meeting inclusion criteria focused on spine surgeons or related specialties, with primarily descriptive and cross-sectional designs (1, 2, 7-11). Radiation exposure was the most frequently investigated risk, with reports of ocular, thyroid, and skin alterations, as well as neoplasms, often associated with inadequate protection and absence of real-time dosimetry (1, 7, 12, 13). Musculoskeletal disorders were also highly prevalent, exceeding 80% in international series, particularly affecting the neck, back, and shoulders, and linked to prolonged static postures and high ergonomic strain (8, 14-16). Sharps injuries were commonly reported but significantly underreported (17, 18). Psychosocial factors, including burnout, depressive symptoms, and occupational stress, were described as highly prevalent, with both individual and organizational determinants, and scarce evidence on effective interventions (2, 5, 6,1 9-22). No spine-specific guidelines were identified; most recommendations were extrapolated from orthopedics and general safety protocols (13, 16, 23-26). Conclusion: Spine surgery poses a substantial occupational burden, encompassing ergonomic, radiological, and psychosocial risks that compromise both surgeon health and patient safety. The available evidence, though limited, underscores the urgent need for integrated preventive strategies and methodologically robust studies to quantify risks, validate interventions, and inform institutional policies that ensure the sustainability of spine surgical practice.

## ID: AGS26/1651

## P0467: Spine biopsy using percutaneous vertebroplasty cannula under fluoroscopy; case series

Halil Ibrahim Süner

1,4

, Abdullah Revaha Yildirim

1

, Baran Babayigit

1

, Sibel Catalca

2,4

, Belmal Hasbay

3,4

, Ozgür Kardes

1,4

, Emre Durdag

1,4

, Soner Civi

1,4

, Kadir Tufan

1,4

1

Department of Neurosurgery

2

Department of Anestesiology and Reanimation

3

Department of Pathology, Baskent University School of Medicine Adana, Turkey

4

Adana Dr. Turgut Noyan Application and Research Center, Adana, Turkey

Introduction: Spinal lesions can occur due to a primary disease or the result of pathological processes elsewhere in the body. Biopsy and histological analysis are required for definitive diagnosis. In recent years, tomography-guided fine needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB) has gained popularity due to its minimally invasive nature, but it may not be sufficient for diagnosis in cases of infection or some malignancies. Therefore, we aimed to present the results of percutaneous vertebral biopsy using a fluoroscopy-guided vertebroplasty cannula due to it may allow for more accurate diagnosis by obtaining more sample material. Material and Methods: Thirty-seven patients were performed percutaneous transpedicular vertebral biopsy in the operating room by using fluoroscopy, vertebroplasty cannula, and pituitary rongeur under sedoanalgesia in our Neurosurgery department between 2021 and 2024. Results: Thirty-four patients were included in the study. The average age of the patients was 63.9%. Sixteen of the patients were male and 18 were female. The most commonly affected vertebraes were L2 and L4 (6 each). The most common presenting complaint was back pain. The most common systemic disease was diabetes mellitus. Histopathologically positive results were obtained in 22 patients (64.7%), and a pathogen was detected from cultures of 13 of 21 samples (61.9%) examined in the microbiology laboratory. Nine of the patients in the study had a history of previous malignancy, but 3 patients were diagnosed with malignancy for the first time. Conclusion: In cases of spondylitis and spondylodiscitis, bacterial detection is often absent in microbiology cultures, particularly during intensive antibiotic therapy and due to limited CT-guided FNAB material. Therefore, diagnosis can be challenging. Histological diagnosis of some systemic malignant diseases, such as lymphoma and myeloma can be difficult. We believe that in such cases, fluoroscopy guided percutaneous biopsy by using vertebroplasty cannulas and rongeur in the operating room may be superior to FNAB in making the diagnosis.

## ID: AGS26/1727

## P0468: Cervical angina: an often-overlooked cause of non-cardiac chest pain

Jonathan Japa

1

, Philip Zitser

2

, Mark Ehioghae

1

, Kevin Yoon

1

, Addisu Mesfin

3

1

MedStar Washington Hospital Center, Washington, DC, United States

2

NYIT College of Osteopathic Medicine, Old Westbury, NY, United States

3

Orthopaedic Institute, MedStar Washginton Hospital Center, Washington, DC, United States

Introduction: Chest pain is the second most common chief complaint in all emergency department visits, with a broad differential diagnosis that includes life-threatening conditions. Therefore, immediate management of chest pain involves ruling out life-threatening conditions such as acute coronary syndrome, which accounts for approximately 31% of chest pain cases. Once a cardiac cause of chest pain is ruled out, non-cardiac causes are investigated, with the most common being gastroesophageal reflux disease and musculoskeletal issues. However, cervical angina, a less common and less well-known cause of noncardiac chest pain, may be omitted from most clinicians’ differential diagnoses. Material and Methods: We conducted a literature review using MEDLINE. Search terms included “cervical angina”; “cervical angina management”; “non-cardiac chest pain AND cervical spine”; “cervical radiculopathy AND chest pain”; “cervical sympathetic chain AND chest pain”; “degenerative cervical disease AND chest pain”; “pseudoangina AND cervical spine”; and “spondylosis”. Results: We identified 15 articles that met our inclusion criteria. Of these, 7 articles included reports of cervical angina incidence, with incidence ranging from 1.4% to 32%. The most widely discussed etiology of cervical angina was cervical radiculopathy of the C6-C7 cervical spine. Diagnosis involves ruling out cardiac and common non-cardiac causes of chest pain, followed by collecting a focused history and conducting a physical examination. Imaging and ancillary tests can be performed to confirm the diagnosis. Literature review suggests both non-operative and operative interventions are effective in treating cervical angina. Conclusion: Cervical angina is an important non-cardiac cause of chest pain that mimics true angina pectoris. Because of these similarities, some patients might receive unnecessary cardiac procedures, which can raise their risk of complications from cardiac catheterization. Properly ruling out cardiac issues as the cause of chest pain and initiating spinal assessment are essential for a timely diagnosis. This is especially important because, although cervical angina often remains underdiagnosed, it has effective surgical and non-surgical treatment options. All physicians, especially those in emergency and primary care, should be aware of this potential cause of chest pain because many patients will initially go to them. They can then work with other specialists, such as those in orthopedics, to provide effective treatment for these patients.

## ID: AGS26/1916

## P0469: Global variation in fusion assessment following ACDF: a call for standardization

Krystell Ceballos

1

, Elyette Lugo

1

, K. Daniel Riew

2

, Sydney Thai

1

, Jessica Siragusa

1

, S. Tim Yoon

3

, Christopher Martin

4

, Samuel Cho

5

, Zorica Buser

6

, Amit Jain

1

1

The Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, MD, United States

2

Cornell University, Ithaca, NY, United States

3

Emory University, Atlanta, GA, United States

4

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, MN, United States

5

Mount Sinai, New york, NY, United States

6

New York University, New york, NY, United States

Introduction: Despite the widespread use of anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), no consensus exists on a gold standard for defining radiographic fusion. Variability in assessment methods leads to discrepancies in reported fusion rates, which in turn affect clinical decision-making and comparative research. This study used the Delphi method to explore expert perspectives on standardized fusion assessment criteria. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional, modified 30-item survey was distributed to global experts in cervical spine, selected based on their research impact. Items addressed surgeon demographics, preferred fixation and interbody techniques, bracing protocols, imaging strategies, and perceived strength of evidence for various fusion assessment criteria. Responses were analyzed descriptively. Results: Forty-four surgeons responded (40% response rate), predominantly orthopedic spine surgeons and neurosurgeons from North America. Substantial variation was observed in imaging preferences and fusion criteria. Most surgeons preferred plate fixation for both 1-2 level ACDF (mean 71%) and 3-4 level ACDF (mean 92%), making it the most commonly chosen fixation method across both groups. Similarly, metallic cages were the most frequently used interbody devices, with mean preferences of 40% for 1-2 levels and 42% for 3-4 levels. Follow-up was most often discontinued one year after confirmed fusion. Fusion status and patient satisfaction were the primary determinants of follow-up duration. Flexion-extension radiographs and CT were the most frequently used modalities. The most strongly endorsed criteria for fusion were < 1 mm interspinous motion, < 5° angular motion, and graft bridging bone ≥ 2 mm on CT, though up to 20% of surgeons still rated these measures as moderate or poor evidence. While 77.8% endorsed the need for standardized criteria, only half were aware of existing guidelines. Conclusion: This international survey of experts highlights significant variability in the assessment of fusion following ACDF, reflecting inconsistencies in imaging use and confidence in radiographic criteria. These findings underscore the urgent need for a universally accepted, evidence-based standard to improve reliability in research and consistency in clinical practice.

## ID: AGS26/2318

## P0470: Spinal arachnoid cysts after intradural surgery: iatrogenic or natural history?

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Background: Spinal arachnoid cysts are rare, typically congenital or idiopathic. They may also appear after intradural surgery, raising questions about whether they are iatrogenic or a natural postoperative development. Objective: To analyze spinal arachnoid cysts occurring after intradural tumor or vascular surgery and discuss their pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who developed arachnoid cysts following intradural spinal procedures. Clinical presentation, imaging, surgical history, and treatment outcomes were assessed. Literature was reviewed to contextualize findings. Results: Patients presented months to years post-surgery with myelopathy or radiculopathy. MRI revealed cystic collections compressing the spinal cord, sometimes communicating with the subarachnoid space. Management included cyst fenestration, resection, or CSF diversion. Outcomes varied: some patients improved significantly, others only partially recovered. Conclusion: Postoperative spinal arachnoid cysts may represent a spectrum from iatrogenic arachnoid adhesions to natural arachnoid remodeling. Awareness is key for early diagnosis and management. Further studies are needed to determine whether these cysts are preventable sequelae or inevitable in certain cases.

## ID: AGS26/2398

## P0471: Assessment of online information quality on lumbar disc herniation

Caner Sarikaya

1

, Buse Sarigul

2

, Levent Aydin

3

, Cumhur Yaltirik

4

1

Maltepe University Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Umraniye Research and Training Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Medicana Beylikdüzü International Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

4

Clemenceau Medical Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: The internet is a widely used source of health-related information, but the quality and reliability of online content vary significantly. This study aimed to evaluate the quality of web-based information on lumbar disc herniation using the DISCERN instrument, JAMA benchmarks, and a custom attachment criteria scale. Material and Methods: A Google search using the term ‘lumbar disc herniation’ was conducted on May 9, 2025. The first 100 search results were screened. Websites were excluded if they were video-only, duplicates, promotional/product-focused, or file-based (PDF/Word). A total of 100 unique websites were included. Each website was evaluated using the DISCERN instrument (16 questions, score range: 1-5 per item), JAMA criteria (authorship, attribution, disclosure, currency; score range: 0-4), and categorized based on content producer and information type. Additionally, the proportion of AI-generated or AI-assisted text was estimated. Results: Among the 100 websites, 45% were affiliated with hospitals or clinics, 20% with individual medical professionals, 15% with physiotherapists, and 20% with general health platforms or blogs. The mean DISCERN score was 3.73 ± 0.32 and the mean JAMA score was 1.87 ± 0.84. While many websites provided basic information on symptoms and treatment, only 12% offered cited, up-to-date, and transparent content. An estimated 78% of the websites included partially or fully AI-assisted text, particularly among blogs and commercial health sites. Conclusion: The quality of online information on lumbar disc herniation is variable and generally moderate. Most websites lack comprehensive sourcing, transparency, and regularly updated content. Healthcare professionals should be aware of these limitations and guide patients toward more reliable resources.

## ID: AGS26/2511

## P0472: Extraspinal sciatica mimicking lumbar radiculopathy: a case report

Micaela Besse

1,2

, Claudio Bulos

2

, Jose Rosado Pardo

2

, Anibal Sarotto

1,2

1

Hospital General de Agudos Carlos G. Durand, Sanatorio Vitorrio Francin, Buenos Aires, Argentina

2

Sanatorio Franchin, Buenos Aires, Argentina

Introduction: Sciatica is defined as low back or gluteal pain radiating down the posterior aspect of the lower limb, reaching the foot, with a reported incidence of 13-40%. True radiculopathy of spinal origin is considerably less frequent, with a reported incidence of 3-5%, most commonly caused by disc herniation. The objective of the following case report is to highlight the importance of the physical examination given the overlap of pain distribution areas. Materials and Methods: A 32-year-old female patient, athlete, with no relevant medical history, was referred by her primary care physician for “left-sided lumbosciatica.” She reported pain along the posterior aspect of the left lower limb (from buttock to foot) with a 6-month history. On physical examination, FADIR and FABER tests were positive, mild low back pain was present, and the Lasègue test was negative. Complementary studies were requested. Results: MRI showed no significant discopathy; however, MR arthrography revealed an anterosuperior labral tear of the left hip, with a positive lidocaine test. Discussion: The overlap in pain distribution between lumbar and hip joint pathology may complicate diagnosis. In 1983, the “hip-spine syndrome” was described within this diagnostic challenge. This overlap often leads to misdiagnosis between true sciatica and pseudosciatica, which can be caused by differential diagnoses such as piriformis syndrome, paralabral cysts, labral tears, and sacroiliitis. Although not specific, clinical maneuvers may be suggestive and guide the appropriate evaluation of the patient, particularly when root traction tests are negative or when the pain does not reproduce the typical features of spinal radiculopathy. Conclusion: The physical examination of patients presenting with spinal symptoms should include a hip evaluation, as symptom overlap is frequent. In young patients, the hip-spine syndrome encompasses labral tears, paralabral cysts, and piriformis syndrome.

## ID: AGS26/2542

## P0473: A global survey on intra-operative neuromonitoring (IONM) practices, attitudes towards its use, and barriers among spine surgeons

Sameh Abolfotouh

1

, Mason Al Nouri

2

, Krishna Kumar

3

, Mostafa Abolfotouh

4

1

MEDCARE Hospitals, Dubai, United Arab Emirates

2

AL Jalila Children Hospital, Dubai, United Arab Emirates

3

Institute of Spine and Scoliosis Surgery, Kochi, India

4

King Abdullah International Medical Research Center, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Intra-operative neuromonitoring (IONM) is a critical tool for minimizing neurological risk during spine surgery. This study aims to provide an up-to-date, global perspective on the current practices, attitudes, and perceived barriers related to IONM among spine surgeons. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional survey was conducted among 529 spine surgeons from various AO spine regions, including Asia Pacific (31.3%), Europe & Southern Africa (26.4%), and North America (15.0%). The respondents, predominantly male (90.7%) orthopaedic surgeons (63.7%), primarily work in academic or university hospitals (42.5%). The survey collected data on demographics, IONM access and models used, actions taken during an unexpected loss of intraoperative potentials, attitudes towards its use, and perceived limitations. Results: Access to IONM was reported as “always” available by 55.5% of surgeons, with 35.7% having occasional access and 8.8% reporting no access. The most common model used was an external company (59.5%), followed by in-house services (40.5%). IONM data interpretation was primarily handled by a neuromonitoring technician (63.6%), while surgeons themselves interpreted the data in 26.1% of cases. A majority of surgeons (76.3%) reported using a checklist in the event of an unexpected loss of potentials, whereas fewer would perform a wake-up test (17.7%) or abort the surgery (9.1%). A strong majority of surgeons agreed that IONM is crucial for enhancing outcomes (89.9%), important for medicolegal protection (91.2%), and provides surgeon reassurance (87.8%). Half of all surgeons (50.5%) reported a positive attitude towards the use of IONM, and only 5.3% reported negative attitude. The main limitations to IONM use were identified as economic (38.2%), logistical (27.4%), and a lack of training (25.6%). Conclusion: This global survey reveals that a majority of spine surgeons have regular access to and hold overwhelmingly positive attitudes towards IONM, particularly regarding its impact on patient outcomes and medicolegal protection. However, significant barriers, especially economic and logistical challenges, remain, highlighting the need for continued efforts to improve the accessibility and implementation of this technology worldwide.

## ID: AGS26/2573

## P0474: Spinal cord injury due to vertebral destruction syndrome from thoracic aorta aneurysm: report of 1 case

Ramon Rincon Valencia

1

, Juan Ramon Perez Perez

1

, Jose Alfonso Corona de la Torre

1,2

, Jorge Quiroz Williams

1

, Ruben Amaya Zepeda

3

1

High Specialty Medical Unit, Traumatology and Orthopedics Hospital, Manuel Avila Camacho, Mexican Institute of Social Security Puebla, Puebla, Mexico

2

Hospital Angeles de Puebla, Puebla, Mexico

3

High Specialty Medical Unit, Orthopedics Hospital, Magdalena de las Salinas, Mexican Institute of Social Security, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Thoracic aortic aneurysms (TAA) are pathological dilatations that often remain silent until advanced or complicated stages. They are less prevalent than abdominal aortic aneurysms, occur more frequently in men over 60 years of age, and are associated with hypertension, atherosclerosis, and connective-tissue disorders. A rare but clinically significant complication of giant TAA is erosion of adjacent bone-most notably the vertebral bodies-driven by chronic pulsatile compression, contained rupture with periaortic hematoma, or direct invasion in mycotic/inflammatory settings. Materials and Methods: We report the case of a 73-year-old woman with remote vertebral tuberculosis (Pott’s disease) involving T6, treated medically ∼30 years earlier. She subsequently underwent spinal surgery with bone graft and stabilization bars for a paravertebral mass that was ultimately proven to be a lipoma, with good clinical recovery. At 62 years of age, she developed progressive thoracic pain and a left subscapular pulsatile mass, followed by worsening motor and sensory deficits that culminated in a complete spinal cord injury at and below the T7 level. Results: Contrast-enhanced CT demonstrated a large thoracic aortic aneurysm and a destructive lytic lesion of the T6 vertebral body-the same level previously affected by tuberculosis. The paravertebral mass was resected and confirmed histologically as a lipoma; the prior hardware was removed. Given the aneurysm’s morphology and the patient’s hemodynamic stability, conservative management with a beta-blocker and an angiotensin II receptor blocker plus imaging surveillance was chosen. The aneurysm appeared as a partially thrombosed saccular lesion (approximately 3-5.5 cm in maximal dimension). Although earlier comparable imaging was unavailable, the vertebral lysis has remained radiographically stable over ∼12 years, consistent with a chronic, partially thrombosed aneurysm exerting low-grade mechanical and/or inflammatory effects. Conclusion: This case highlights a rare but important complication of TAA: progressive vertebral erosion at a site of prior infection/surgery. In patients with known or suspected TAA, persistent localized pain or new neurological findings should prompt targeted imaging to assess for osseous involvement. Early multidisciplinary evaluation can align vascular and spine strategies to balance aneurysm control, mechanical stability, and neurological preservation.
Keywords: spinal cord injury; Frankel A; sequelae; aneurysm; vertebral lysis; bone tumor-like lesion.

## ID: AGS26/2584

## P0475: Prevalence of osteoporosis and osteopenia diagnosed using DEXA and quantitative CT in patients undergoing thoracolumbar fusion surgery

Suhail AlAssiri

1,2,3

, Firas Alsebayel

2

, Dana Aldughiman

1

, Majed Abaalkhail

1,2,3

, Fahad Alhelal

1,2,3

, Naila Ashraf

3

, Musaad Bin Dukhi

1

, Sultan Alotaibi

1

, Abdulazeez Alzailaie

1

, Mohammad Almosa

1

, Sami Aleissa

1,2,3

1

King Saud bin Abdulaziz University for Health Sciences, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

2

Orthopedics Surgery Department, Ministry of the National Guard-Health affairs, King Abdulaziz Medical City, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

3

King Abdullah International Medical Research Centre, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Osteoporosis and osteopenia are common in patients undergoing spine surgery and are associated with hardware loosening, implant failure, and non-union. Preoperative identification of low bone mineral density (BMD) is therefore essential. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) remains the standard diagnostic tool but has limitations in the spine, whereas quantitative computed tomography (QCT) may provide more accurate assessment. Material and Methods: This case-control study included 80 patients undergoing thoracolumbar fusion and 80 matched controls. All subjects underwent DEXA and QCT. BMD categories were defined using standard thresholds. Prevalence was reported with 95% confidence intervals (CI). Logistic regression was performed to identify risk factors for osteoporosis, with results expressed as odds ratios (OR) and corresponding 95% CI. Postoperative complications were also recorded. Results: The prevalence of osteopenia and osteoporosis was high in both surgical patients and controls, with no significant differences between groups. Females had a significantly higher prevalence of osteopenia compared with males (p = 0.031). Logistic regression identified age (p = 0.021) and female sex (OR 3.7; p = 0.011) as independent predictors of osteoporosis. No significant differences were observed between DEXA- and QCT-based BMD classifications. Postoperative complications were reported in 26 patients (16%), including screw loosening (12%), implant failure (3%), and proximal junctional kyphosis (4%). Conclusion: Osteoporosis and osteopenia are highly prevalent among thoracolumbar fusion patients, particularly in older women. Given the comparable diagnostic performance of DEXA and QCT, and the fact that most patients already undergo preoperative CT, opportunistic QCT screening may serve as a practical approach to detect osteoporosis before surgery. Routine bone health assessment and early intervention could help reduce underdiagnosis and lower the risk of hardware-related complications.

## ID: AGS26/2659

## P0476: Optimization of patients for spinal instrumentation surgery: defining the magnitude of BMD overestimation with DEXA

Arvind G. Kulkarni

1

1

Mumbai Spine Scoliosis & Disc Replacement Center, Bombay Hospital, Breach Candy Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Bone mineral density (BMD) assessment is critical in patients with degenerative spine disease undergoing surgery. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) is widely used, but its two-dimensional measurements are confounded by osteophytes, sclerosis, and vascular calcifications, leading to underestimation of osteoporosis. Quantitative computed tomography (QCT) provides volumetric BMD (vBMD) and site-specific analysis. However, direct comparative studies of QCT and DEXA performed in the same surgical cohort remain scarce in the literature. This study compared lumbar BMD and T-scores from DEXA and QCT in the same patient. Material and Methods: An observational study was conducted on 125 consecutive patients, including post-menopausal women over 50 and men over 70 years, all requiring surgery for degenerative spine disease. Lumbar BMD and T-scores were measured using both DEXA and QCT. Mean values with standard deviations (SD) were calculated and compared. Results: The average age of the patients was 70.5 ± 8.6 (range:54-79). QCT demonstrated a lumbar T-score mean of -3.62 ± 1.10, significantly lower than DEXA (-1.95 ± 1.72). Among 125 patients with osteoporosis confirmed by QCT, DEXA mis-classified 67 patients (53.6%) as non-osteoporotic (T-score > -2.5), highlighting a substantial underestimation of bone loss. Conclusion: DEXA underestimates osteoporosis and is unreliable for medical management of osteoporosis and operative planning. QCT, by accurately capturing both T-scores and vBMD, offers superior assessment of spinal bone quality, making it the preferred modality for surgical decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/2661

## P0477: Clinical characterstics and surgical treatment results of double crush syndrome by spinal degenerative disease and peripheral nerve entrapment disease

Ryuya Maejima

1,2

, Masahito Hara

2

, Yu Yamamoto

1,2

, Tadashi Watanabe

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery

2

Department of Spine Surgery, Aichi Medical University, Nagakute, Japan

Introduction: Double crush syndrome (DCS) is a clinical condition involving spinal degenerative disease (SDD) and peripheral nerve entrapment disease (PNED). The clinical features of DCS remain unclear. In this study, we report our DCS cases with a literature review. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed the medical records of 711 surgical cases of SDD in our hospital between April 2019 and March 2023 (312 cervical spine cases, 399 thoracolumbar cases). Among these, surgical cases of PNED were subjected to this study. Clinical characteristics and surgical treatment results by NRS and McNab scale were evaluated at the first visit and 3-months postoperative and the end of follow-up. Results: The subjects included 11 patients (1.5% of the total, 6 men, 5 women, mean age 67.2 years); 2 cases of cervical myelopathy with carpal tunnel syndrome, 2 cases of cervical myelopathy with cubital tunnel syndrome, 1 case of cervical myelopathy with tarsal tunnel syndrome, 3 cases of cervical radiculopathy with cubital tunnel syndrome, 1 case of lumbar radiculopathy with piriformis syndrome and peroneal nerve disorder and 3 cases of lumbar radiculopathy with peroneal nerve disorder. The diagnosis was based on clinical symptoms and physical examination with clinical imaging for SDD and nerve conduction study for PNED. One-stage surgery was performed in 3 patients (27.2%) and staged surgery was performed in 8 patients (72.7%). In staged surgery cases, the average interval between surgeries was 16.6 months. 7 case (87.5%) of staged surgery cases underwent surgery for SDD at first. 7 patients of all (63.6%) have a negative Tinel-like sign particular to the PNED upon physical examination at first visit. In three of staged surgery cases, a Tinel-like sign changed from negative to positive at after spinal surgery. 3 patients (27.2%) showed satisfactory outcome (good or excellent by McNab scale) at 3-months postoperative, but 9 patients (81.8%) at the end of follow-up. Conclusion: Surgical DCS is a relatively rare condition. Although the diagnosis of DCS are challenging, the surgical treatment are effective. In DCS patients, Tinel-like sign characteristic of the PNED tend to be a negative and there is a possibility of the positive conversion after spine surgery. It is important carefully follow up the patient to detect changes in his or her condition. The surgical results for DCS diagnosed by doing this are good at the end without immediate effect. There was no difference between upper and lower limbs unless no detailed reports about DCS of lower limbs have been published.

## ID: AGS26/2669

## P0478: Enhancing clinical documentation and management of cauda equina syndrome: the development and impact of a structured assessment proforma

Mustafa Al Jaafar

1

, Omar Al-Doori

1

1

Aneurin Bevan University Health Board, Newport, United Kingdom

Background: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a neurosurgical emergency associated with potentially devastating neurological sequelae and significant medico-legal implications. Timely surgical decompression is critical; however, effective management relies on precise documentation of clinical findings. In many settings, documentation of key neurovascular assessments remains suboptimal. This study aimed to evaluate and improve the quality of clinical documentation in suspected CES cases by implementing a structured assessment proforma, thereby facilitating timely and accurate clinical evaluation, expediting neurosurgical referral, and mitigating legal risks. Methods: A retrospective review was conducted to compare documentation practices in 50 patients with suspected CES before and 50 patients after the introduction of a custom-designed proforma in the Trauma and Orthopaedic Department. Documentation was evaluated for the following nine key parameters: clinical history, motor examination, sensory examination, bladder function, bowel function, reflexes, perianal pinprick test, sexual dysfunction assessment, and radicular pain. Referral times for urgent surgical intervention were also recorded. Results: Implementation of the proforma significantly improved documentation rates. Perianal pinprick testing increased from 12 (24%) cases pre-proforma to 44 (88%) cases post-proforma. Bladder function documentation improved from 31 (62%) cases to 49 (98%) cases, and sexual history documentation increased from six (12%) cases to 46 (92%) cases. Among patients requiring urgent surgical intervention, the time from referral to definitive management was reduced by an estimated seven hours. Eight urgent cases were identified pre-proforma, compared to five post-proforma. Conclusions: The structured assessment proforma significantly enhanced the completeness of neurovascular documentation and streamlined the management of suspected CES cases, resulting in a notable reduction in referral delays for urgent intervention. Adoption of such a proforma may improve diagnostic accuracy, expedite neurosurgical referrals, and reduce medico-legal risk.

## ID: AGS26/2758

## P0479: Spontaneous intracranial hypotension with acquired tonsillar descent: diagnostic pitfalls and surgical repair of a thoracic ventral dural defect

Alexandru Budu

1

, Anna Chan

2

1

University Hospitals Birmingham - Queen Elizabeth Hospital, Birmingham, United Kingdom

2

University of Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Spontaneous intracranial hypotension (SIH) is an under-recognised yet increasingly diagnosed cause of orthostatic headaches. The condition arises most commonly from spinal cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaks, which may result from dural defects, meningeal diverticula, or CSF-venous fistulae. The consequent reduction in intracranial CSF volume and pressure can produce characteristic imaging findings, including diffuse pachymeningeal enhancement, venous engorgement, and acquired cerebellar tonsillar descent, often mimicking Chiari I malformation. Distinguishing SIH from other causes of hindbrain herniation is critical, as timely recognition and targeted treatment of the spinal CSF leak can lead to resolution of symptoms and reversal of radiological abnormalities. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 55-year-old man with a 10-year history of postural headaches. Despite two epidural blood patches, his symptoms persisted and later progressed to experiencing back pain, gait imbalance, sensory loss in the C2-C6 dermatomes, and behavioural changes consistent with a frontal syndrome, thought to reflect glymphatic system dysfunction. Follow-up imaging was initially labelled as Chiari malformation; however, detailed review demonstrated a subtle ventral epidural fluid collection extending along the thoracic and cervical spine. Localising the exact site of the CSF leak proved challenging: CT myelography failed to identify the defect, highlighting the limitations of conventional imaging in this context. Digital subtraction myelography (DSM), although not universally reliable in pinpointing small or ventral leaks, suggested a probable site at T3-T4. A hybrid approach was adopted, whereby intraoperative DSM was performed under the same anaesthetic immediately prior to surgical exploration. This confirmed the level of the leak, allowing T3-T4 laminectomy and intradural repair of the ventral dural defect with patch and suture closure. Results: Patient recovered with complete resolution of symptoms with no neurological deficits or weakness. Two-month post-operative MRI scans showed a full resolution of cervical cord oedema, near complete resolution of syrinx and major improvement of the displacement of brainstem and cerebellar tonsils and other features of intracranial hypotension. Conclusion: SIH-related persistent headaches and symptoms can present as a troublesome condition for patients to live with. Identifying the pathological defect on imaging can be challenging, which may delay treatment. Our case report illustrates that appropriate surgical intervention has the capability of near-complete resolving of pathological deformities including Chiari malformation, syrinx formation and oedema, and other associated changes due to intracranial hypotension. This in turn allows a complete resolution of symptoms and minimal disruption upon return to normality for patients.

## ID: AGS26/2895

## P0480: Physical exam of the SI joint: what is the variation in force application?

Elizabeth Lord

1

, Benjamin D. Elder

2

, Christopher Kleck

3

, Robert Eastlack

4

, Taylor Lawson

5

, David Polly

6

1

UCLA, Los Angeles, United States

2

Mayo Clinic, Rochester, United States

3

University of Colorado, Anschutz Medical Center, Denver, United States

4

Scripps Clinic, La Jolla, United States

5

SI-Bone, Santa Clara, United States

6

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

Introduction: Sacroiliac (SI) joint pain is implicated in up to 30% of low back pain cases, but diagnosis remains challenging due to symptom overlap with lumbar and hip pathology and the absence of a definitive imaging test. Physical examination maneuvers are widely used to provoke and assess SI joint pain, yet there is little evidence guiding the optimal force application or assessing inter-clinician variability. Our objective herein was to quantify the variation in force applied by experienced spine surgeons during standardized SI joint provocative maneuvers. Material and Methods: Sixteen spine surgeons performed common SI joint physical exam maneuvers (palpation, distraction, thigh thrust, and compression) on a volunteer lying on a force plate, blinded to force readouts. Palpation measurements were repeated to determine intra-examiner reliability. Surgeon demographics, specialty, years of practice, and relevant surgical experience were collected. Force data were analyzed for range, intra-examiner reliability, and correlation with surgeon background. Results: Considerable force variation was observed across all maneuvers: palpation (69-236N), palpation repeat (49-292N), distraction (231-545N), compression (214-532N), and thigh thrust (299-697N). Palpation exhibited the highest intra-examiner reliability (Pearson r = 0.95). No significant relationships were detected between applied force and surgeon specialty, years in practice, or surgical volume. Despite moderate clustering around mean forces, substantial outliers existed. Conclusion: Examiners had a high intra-observer reliability for force applied during palpation. There was substantial inter-examiner variability in the force applied during SI joint physical examination maneuvers which was independent of surgeon specialty or experience. This variation may contribute to inconsistent diagnostic yield reported in the literature, and standardization of applied force may enhance the consistency of SI joint pain diagnosis. Future studies should explore optimal force thresholds and develop training protocols to improve clinical reproducibility.

## ID: AGS26/2936

## P0481: Management of recurrent dorsal spinal arachnoid cyst: a case report and review of the litterature

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Mohamed Ali Kharrat

1

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Roua Latrach

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal arachnoid cysts (SACs) are rare intradural lesions that can cause radicular or myelopathic symptoms due to spinal cord compression. Surgical cyst wall resection is the standard treatment, and recurrence after complete resection is exceedingly rare. Material and Methods: We present a 43-year-old female with a recurrent dorsal spinal arachnoid cyst. She underwent two prior resections, followed by a subarachnoid cyst shunt after a third recurrence, achieving 15 years of symptom-free status. She later developed progressive paraparesis due to a fourth compressive cystic recurrence. Results: For this recurrence, a cystoperitoneal shunt was performed, resulting in complete recovery of her paraparesis on follow-up. Conclusion: This case highlights the rare potential for long-term recurrence of dorsal SACs despite multiple surgical interventions. It underscores the importance of long-term follow-up in patients with spinal arachnoid cysts, as timely intervention can prevent neurological deterioration and ensure sustained recovery.

## ID: AGS26/2969

## P0482: Genetic testing informs surgical decision-making and reduces complications in spine surgery

Kexin Xu

1,2,3

, Di Liu

1,2,3

, Jialuo Han

1,2,3

, Xiangjie Yin

1,2,3

, Sen Zhao

1,2,3

, Jianguo Zhang

1,2,3

, Nan Wu

1,2,3

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, Peking Union Medical College and Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Bejing, China

2

Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Innovation and Application for Skeletal Health Medical Care, Bejing, China

3

Key Laboratory of Big Data for Skeletal Deformities, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, China

Introduction: Genetic testing reveals disease features that can be overlooked during standard clinical assessment. The impact of genetic testing on surgical decision-making in spine surgery requires investigation. We aimed to evaluate whether genetic testing informs surgical decision-making and reduces complications in spinal surgery. Material and Methods: Patients with a surgical indication for spinal deformity and a definitive genetic diagnosis were included. Patients who underwent surgery had a minimum of two years of postoperative follow-up. Propensity score matching was performed to match patients who received preoperative genetic testing (Pilot Group) to those who received postoperative genetic testing (Control Group). We identified genetic testing-informed refinements of perioperative plans and compared major and minor surgical complications between the two groups. Results: The study included 86 patients (30 in the Pilot Group, 56 in the Control Group). Genetic testing informed four alterations to perioperative plans in the Pilot Group and redirected treatment pathways. The rationale for these changes included the identification of altered spinal biomechanics and multisystemic involvement. These features were well-recognized as being associated with increased surgical risk but were often undetected during standard preoperative clinical assessments. The refined selection of the lowest instrumented vertebra and osteotomy approach can be generalized to hereditary connective tissue disorders, considering the characteristic spinal hypermobility due to connective tissue fragility. The clinical impact of preoperative genetic testing was demonstrated by a statistically significant decrease in the number of late postoperative complications among patients in the Pilot Group, without inter-group differences in baseline or surgical characteristics. Moreover, 4 out of 12 major complications in the Control Group could have been prevented with preoperative genetic testing. We further proposed a framework for dynamic assessment of surgical appropriateness that incorporates the understanding of genetic etiology, emphasizing its fundamental role in enhancing patient outcomes. Conclusion: Genetic testing informs surgical decision-making and improves surgical outcomes. Incorporating the understanding of genetic etiology enhances surgical appropriateness, paving the way for precision spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3237

## P0484: Spinal epidural venous angioma: a case report

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal vascular malformations are rare and usually intradural. Purely extradural venous angiomas are extremely uncommon, with only five cases reported to date. We present a case of spinal epidural venous angioma in a patient with a prior malignancy, emphasizing the importance of radiological suspicion in atypical presentations. Material and Methods: A 42-year-old right-handed woman presented with a 3-week history of back pain, progressively associated with gait difficulties and sphincter disturbances. Her past medical history was significant for ovarian cancer treated by radical surgery two years earlier. Neurological examination revealed paraparesis (motor strength 3/5) with hyperactive deep tendon reflexes. Spinal MRI showed a T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense epidural mass at T2-T6 with strong contrast enhancement, causing significant spinal cord compression. Metastatic epiduritis was initially suspected. The patient underwent emergency T2-T6 laminectomy. Intraoperatively, a highly vascular grayish mass was observed and gross total resection was performed. Results: Histopathology demonstrated cystically dilated vessels coalescing into larger channels, confirming a spinal epidural venous angioma. Postoperatively, the patient recovered fully, with no neurological deficits or radiological evidence of recurrence at two-year follow-up. Conclusion: Although exceedingly rare, spinal epidural venous angiomas should be included in the differential diagnosis of epidural masses, even in patients with a history of malignancy. Careful preoperative planning and meticulous surgical technique allow for gross total resection and excellent clinical outcomes. Early recognition is critical to prevent misdiagnosis and optimize neurological recovery.

## ID: AGS26/3251

## P0485: Posterior fixation of the cervicothoracic junction: technical challenges in a series of four cases

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

, Elouni Emna

2

1
Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, 
2
Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia
Introduction: The cervicothoracic junction (CTJ) is a biomechanically complex transition between the mobile cervical spine and rigid thoracic spine. Pathologies such as trauma, degeneration, and tumors often require surgical stabilization. Posterior fixation remains technically challenging due to narrow pedicles, complex anatomy, and neurovascular risks. We report our preliminary experience with four patients undergoing posterior CTJ fixation. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed four consecutive patients treated between 2022 and 2024. Indications included traumatic instability (n = 2), degenerative disease (n = 1), and metastatic lesion (n = 1). Posterior instrumentation involved pedicle screws at C7-T2, with or without lateral mass screws, tailored to anatomical feasibility. Operative data, radiological outcomes, complications, and clinical evolution were analyzed. Results: Posterior fixation was successfully achieved in all cases with correct screw placement on postoperative imaging. Mean operative time was 180 minutes and average blood loss 450 ml. No intraoperative neurovascular injuries occurred. One patient developed a superficial wound infection, managed conservatively. At a mean follow-up of 10 months, fixation remained stable without hardware failure. Neurological status improved in two patients and was stable in the others. Pain relief was achieved in three patients, and all regained the ability to perform daily activities. Conclusion: Posterior CTJ fixation is a safe and effective procedure when meticulous anatomical planning is observed. Despite technical challenges, pedicle screw constructs provided stable stabilization and favorable short-term clinical outcomes. Larger studies are warranted to validate these preliminary results and refine surgical strategies in this anatomically complex region.

## P0486: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas: a series of six cases

, Mohamed Badri

Introduction: Perimedullary venous drainage spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas are rare. They represent the most common type of spinal vascular malformations. While their clinical presentation and imaging findings are now well understood, the optimal treatment approach remains a matter of debate. Material and Methods: The clinical presentation was dominated by a spinal cord compression syndrome in all patients. The diagnosis was confirmed by spinal angiography. Results: The diagnosis was established through spinal angiography. Two patients underwent complete endovascular embolization, while four were treated surgically. ## P0487: Emergency MRI in acute spinal cord syndromes: diagnostic yield and impact on patient triage - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Acute spinal cord syndromes (ASCS) are medical emergencies that require rapid assessment to prevent irreversible neurological deficits. MRI is the imaging modality of choice, but its real-time impact on emergency triage and decision-making remains under evaluation. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed and Google Scholar with the keywords: “acute spinal cord syndrome,” “emergency MRI,” “triage,” “diagnostic yield,” and “management impact.” Studies assessing MRI findings, diagnostic accuracy, changes in patient management, and time to intervention were included. Results: • Emergency MRI demonstrated a high diagnostic yield, detecting compressive, inflammatory, ischemic, and hemorrhagic lesions. • In 70-85% of cases, MRI findings directly influenced immediate clinical management, including surgical decompression or medical therapy initiation. • MRI facilitated prioritization of patients for neurosurgical intervention, particularly in cases with subtle clinical signs. • Early MRI reduced time to definitive treatment and improved risk stratification, enhancing patient safety and optimizing resource allocation in emergency settings. • Limitations include availability, scan duration, and need for patient stability during imaging. Conclusion: Emergency MRI provides critical diagnostic information in acute spinal cord syndromes, significantly impacting patient triage and management. Its integration into early assessment protocols ensures timely intervention, improves clinical decision-making, and enhances outcomes. Wider availability and streamlined protocols are recommended to maximize its clinical benefit in urgent care settings.
