## ID: AGS26/40

## P0489: Does disc consistency matter? A comparative series on calcified and soft thoracic disc herniations

Chinedu Egu

1

, R.M Prasanna Ratnayake

1

, Ibrahim Alrabi

1

, Nalinda Jayalath

1

, Neel Badhe

1

, Eduardo Muscogliati

1

, Weronika Nocun

1

, Nasir Quraishi

1

, Elie Najjar

1

1

Centre for Spinal Studies and Surgery, Queens Medical Centre, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Though rare, thoracic disc herniation (TDH) can lead to debilitating myelopathy and poses considerable surgical challenges. The consistency of the herniated disc - soft versus calcified - may significantly influence surgical planning and perioperative outcomes. This retrospective study compares clinical features, surgical parameters, and neurological outcomes in patients with calcified versus soft TDH. Material and Methods: Thirty-six patients with thoracic myelopathy due to TDH were treated at a tertiary spine centre between 2018 and 2024. Patients were stratified into calcified (n = 20) and soft (n = 16) disc cohorts based on imaging and intraoperative findings. Data included demographics, imaging, operative details, and neurological outcomes. A p-value < 0.05 was considered significant. Results: Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. The T10-T11 level was the most commonly affected. All soft disc cases were managed via a posterior approach, while calcified cases required more varied surgical approaches. Calcified TDH was associated with significantly greater intraoperative blood loss (450 mL vs 275 mL, p = 0.027) and more frequent intraoperative neuromonitoring alerts (p = 0.02). Immediate postoperative neurological deficits and non-neurological complications were more frequent in the calcified group but did not reach statistical significance. At a mean follow-up of two years, neurological outcomes were similar across both groups. Conclusion: Calcified TDHs are associated with greater surgical complexity and risk. However, with appropriate planning, long-term outcomes match those of soft discs. Disc morphology should inform intraoperative strategy but does not necessarily predict prognosis.

## ID: AGS26/356

## P0490: Intradural lumbar disc herniation: a case report and literature review

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: Clinically, most lumbar herniated nucleus pulposi are located epidurally, outside or under the posterior longitudinal ligament. Lumbar disc herniation into the dural space is a very rare phenomenon of degenerative lumbar lesions, accounts for only 0.04-0.33% of all lumbar disc herniations. Material and Methods: The pathogenesis and natural course of intradural disc herniation remain unclear. In this case study, we describe a patient with intradural disc herniation and review the literature to explore its potential pathogenesis, natural course, diagnosis, and treatment. Results: We describe a rare case of intradural lumbar disc herniation. A 68-year-old man presented with progressive lower back pain and radiating pain and numbness in both legs for 3 years. Magnetic resonance imaging revealed a large herniated disc at L4-L5. Posterior discectomy and fusion of the L4-L5 was performed after conservative treatment failed. Intraoperatively, only minimal disc fragments in the epidural space were found after meticulous probing following laminectomy of the L4-L5 vertebrae. The dorsal dura mater was saturated, tense, and bulged at the L4-L5 levels; additionally, an intradural mass was palpable and confirmed by intraoperative ultrasonography. Subsequently, dorsal middle durotomy was performed. Upon opening the dural sac, a large cauliflower-like mass similar to nucleus pulposus tissue was found near the arachnoid membrane. The mass was dissociative and could be completely resected. The dorsal dural incisions were closed after careful exploration, followed by fixation and fusion of the L4-L5 levels. Pathological examination revealed disc tissue with central balloon-type cystic degenerative changes. The patient's lower back pain and radiating pain and numbness of both legs improved remarkably postoperatively, and he became asymptomatic at 3 months postoperatively. Conclusion: The pathogenesis and natural course of intradural disc herniation is still unclear. Several hypotheses have been used to explain it, including the “acute pressure” hypothesis, the “chronic compression” hypothesis and the “adhesions” hypothesis. In terms of the preoperative diagnosis of IDDH, the clinical manifestation of patients and preoperative imaging including radiographs and computed tomography often lack specificity. Intradural lumbar disc herniation should be highly suspected when intraoperative findings are incompatible with findings from the preoperative imaging examination, and it could be further confirmed via intraoperative ultrasonography and pathological examination of the resected tissue from the dural space. Prompt surgery is recommended, and surgical results are usually favorable.

## ID: AGS26/572

## P0491: A systematic review of the natural history of degenerative thoracic myelopathy

Edwin Koubeh

1

, Sebastian Möbus

1

, Tanzil Rujeedwa

1

, Oliver Mowforth

1

, Mark Kotter

1

1

Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom

Introduction: Degenerative thoracic myelopathy (DTM), a recently proposed term, encompasses a spectrum of degenerative spine conditions that cause thoracic spinal cord compression and progressive neurological dysfunction. The natural history of DTM is poorly characterised, hindering prompt diagnosis and management of this debilitating condition. This systematic review aimed to assess the initial symptoms, progression, and prognostic factors to aid clinicians to manage DTM. Studying DTM as a unified condition provides a larger body of evidence to draw conclusions from, analogous to progress achieved following the use of the term Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy. Material and Methods: A systematic literature search was performed in Embase and MEDLINE up to 15th December 2023 to identify papers assessing DTM, with relevant outcomes including initial symptoms, symptom progression over time, duration of disease and risk factors for progression. This review followed PRISMA guidelines, and risk of bias assessment was completed for each included study. Results: Sixteen studies met the inclusion criteria. Sensory disturbances were more common than motor symptoms in the early stages of DTM, with all 8 studies reporting initial symptoms finding that numbness, tingling or pain were most common. Gait disturbances and motor symptoms such as weakness were also commonly reported, although this tended to be later in disease progression. The prevalence of both sensory and motor disturbances increased over time. Symptom progression was highly variable in both severity and pace, with a mean duration from symptom onset to surgery of 25.5 months. Risk factors for progression include the underlying aetiology, age and the presence of T2 hyperintensity on spinal MRI. Conclusion: Despite a paucity of literature on the natural history of DTM, this review identifies several patterns which provide actionable guidance for clinicians. Recognising common early symptoms may reduce diagnostic delay, prognostic features can inform risk-stratified surveillance and understanding progression may inform treatment thresholds. Importantly, diagnostic delay is associated with poorer post-operative outcomes in DTM, highlighting the need for improved awareness of this under-recognised condition.

## ID: AGS26/1317

## P0493: Analysis of risk factors of intervertebral discs adjacent to thoracolumbar junction fractures treated by posterior fixation

Mouldi Lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Mohamed Hassine

1

, Abdenasser Ben Salem

1

, Hamza Daly

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Fractures of the thoracolumbar junction are among the most common spinal injuries. Posterior fixation is the gold standard treatment. However, the disc above or below a spinal fixation appears to be exposed to accelerated degeneration. Identifying risk factors is essential for surgical indication and long-term follow-up. The objective of our work is to determine the predictive factors of adjacent segment degeneration after treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included 120 patients surgically treated for thoracolumbar spine fractures between January 2010 and January 2020. The fractures were classified according to the Magerl classification. The intervertebral disc degeneration (IDD) was studied before surgery and at the last follow-up according to the UCLA classification. All patients underwent posterior fixation. At the final radiological follow-up, patients were divided into two groups: Group A: patients without IDD (n = 88). Group B: patients with IDD (n = 32). The following factors were analyzed: age, sex, body mass index (obesity), diabetes, tobacco use, neurological deficit, vertebral level (T12 or L1), posterior wall involvement, intra-canal fragment, pedicular fracture, articular fracture, fracture-dislocation, AO classification (types A2, A3, B, C), fixation type (short, long, mixed), laminectomy, grafting, nonunion, material failure, infection, and hardware removal. Odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) were calculated, with p < 0.05 considered significant. Results: Our series included 120 patients with a mean age of 48.39 years. The sex ratio was 1.6. The body mass index (BMI) was 28 kg/m2 with extremes of 20.7 kg/m2 and 38.21 kg/m2. According to the Magerl classification, type A3 was the most frequent. 54.2% of the patients had a long fixation. 33 of our patients had a mixed fixation. The short fixation was used with 22 patients. The average follow-up was 57 months. IDD (UCLA 3 or 4) were observed in 32 patients (26.7%). The predictive factors for IDD were: age over 60 years (OR = 33.36, CI [11.04-100.78], p < 0.001), male sex (OR = 3.24, CI [1.40-7.51], p = 0.05), diabetes (OR = 2.97, CI [1.74-5.04], p = 0.001), obesity (OR = 4.17, CI [1.06-16.40], p = 0.01), short-segment fixation (OR = 3.00, CI [1.77-5.13], p < 0.001), and nonunion (OR = 4.10, CI [2.97-5.66], p = 0.005). Furthermore, long-segment assembly is a protector factors for IDD (p = 0.02, OR= 2.47, CI [1.07-5.70]. However, other parameters, including vertebral level, posterior wall involvement, intra-canal fragment, pedicular fracture, articular fracture, AO classification, mixed segment assembly, laminectomy, grafting, infection, material failure, and hardware removal, did not reach statistical significance. Conclusion: Advanced age, male sex, diabetes, obesity, nonunion, and short-segment fixation were identified as significant risk factors for degenerative changes after posterior fixation for surgical treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Short-segment fixation was strongly associated with higher rates of IDD, while long fixation appeared to have a protective effect. These findings emphasize the need for individualized surgical strategies and careful long-term follow-up in patients with high-risk profiles.

## ID: AGS26/1629

## P0494: Cervical disc disease as a novel etiology of dropped head syndrome: targeted ACDF as a less invasive alternative to long-segment fusion

Satoshi Takamoto

1

, Koichiro Ono

1

, Yuka Yamami

1

, Kazuma Miura

2

, Yushi Yamaguchi

2

, Yuki Kasuga

2

, Hiroshi Kawagushi

2

, Makoto Hirao

2

1

Orthopedic Department, Honkawagoe Hospital, Orthopedic Department, Nippon Medical School Hospital, Saitama, Japan

2

Orthopedic Department, Nippon Medical School Hospital, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Dropped Head Syndrome (DHS) is an uncommon but highly disabling condition characterized by cervical kyphosis and inability to maintain horizontal gaze. The disorder has traditionally been attributed to neuromuscular causes such as myopathy or motor neuron disease, and surgical correction usually requires long-segment cervicothoracic fusion. While this approach can restore alignment, it carries substantial morbidity, loss of mobility, and non-negligible failure rates, with cervical-only procedures historically considered unreliable. To date, cervical disc disease has not been recognized as a primary etiology of DHS. We present what we believe to be the first case series establishing disc pathology as a distinct, surgically correctable cause of DHS, and we demonstrate that targeted anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) can provide durable symptom relief without extensive constructs. Material and Methods: Four consecutive patients (3 males, 1 female; mean age 66.3 ± 5.2 years) presenting with DHS and severe forward gaze impairment were prospectively evaluated. All complained of intractable cervical pain (VAS ≥ 80 mm) and exhibited temporary improvement in alignment after diagnostic cervical disc block, confirming discogenic origin. Exclusion criteria included neuromuscular disease, widespread muscle atrophy on MRI, or significant cervical myelopathy. Patients underwent one- or two-level ACDF tailored to the symptomatic segment. Primary endpoints were pain reduction and maintenance of horizontal gaze. Secondary outcomes included operative time, hospital stay, perioperative complications, and radiographic bone fusion assessed by CT at one year. Clinical follow-up extended to a mean of 38.5 months (range 36-42). Results: All four patients experienced dramatic improvement in pain, with mean VAS decreasing from 92.5 ± 9.6 mm to 7.5 ± 5.0 mm (91.9% reduction, p < 0.001), surpassing established MCID thresholds. Horizontal gaze was restored in every case and maintained throughout follow-up. Mean operative time was 114 minutes (range 62-143), and hospital stay averaged 7.3 days. No intraoperative or perioperative complications occurred. At one year, CT scans confirmed 100% fusion. Importantly, none required revision surgery or subsequent extension to cervicothoracic fusion. This contrasts sharply with previously reported high failure rates of cervical-only approaches for DHS, which in some series exceed 60-70%. Conclusion: This case series highlights cervical disc disease as a previously underrecognized etiology of DHS and introduces a targeted, less invasive treatment strategy. Disc blockade proved valuable in identifying discogenic DHS, serving both as a diagnostic and predictive tool for surgical success. Unlike neuromuscular etiologies, which demand extensive mechanical support or rehabilitation, disc-related DHS responds to focused pain relief via ACDF. Our findings suggest that in patients with severe axial pain, a positive response to a diagnostic block, and the absence of diffuse muscle atrophy, limited ACDF can replace traditional long-segment fusion, preserving mobility and minimizing morbidity. While the small sample size warrants cautious interpretation, these results provide a strong rationale for larger, multicenter studies that stratify DHS by etiology. Ultimately, recognition of disc-related DHS could significantly expand treatment algorithms, offering surgeons a new, evidence-based pathway for carefully selected patients.

## ID: AGS26/2568

## P0496: Clinical-functional outcomes of patients with lumbar disc herniation when comparing different modalities of surgical treatment performed in a tertiary hospital in Mexico

Ramon Rincon Valencia

1

, Jorge Quiroz Williams

1

, Jose Luis Garcia Navarro

1,2

, Juan Ramon Perez Perez

1

1

High Specialty Medical Unit, Traumatology and Orthopedics Hospital, Manuel Avila Camacho, Mexican Institute of Social Security Puebla, Puebla, Mexico

2

Hospital Angeles de Puebla, Puebla, Mexico

Introduction: Herniated discs (HD) occur more frequently in the lumbar spine, because it is the segment with the greatest mobility and is subjected to the highest axial load, with the L5-S1 segment accounting for 43%, followed by L4-L5 at 37%. They represent 85% of the causes of sciatica. In Mexico, the prevalence is 21.11%. It is a pathology that mainly affects the population aged 30 to 50 years, with a ratio of 2:1 in males. There are different treatment modalities, both conservative and surgical. Surgical indications for lumbar disc herniation (LDH) are carried out when there is the presence of cauda equina syndrome, progressive motor neurological deficit, following a period of 6 to 8 weeks of conservative treatment without improvement. Objective: To analyze the clinical-functional results of patients with lumbar disc herniation (LDH) by comparing the surgical treatment modalities performed at high specialty medical unit, trauma and orthopedics, Manuel Avila Camacho, spine surgery department in Puebla, Puebla Mexico. Material and Methods: A quasi-experimental study was conducted on patients with a diagnosis of LDH confirmed by MRI, at a single level herniated disc, aged 20 to 75 years, who attended outpatient follow-up consultations of the spine department from March 1 to July 31, 2024. Pain will be analyzed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and functionality with the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), before, one month, and two months after surgery, and only for VAS 24 hours from the surgery. The statistical analysis will involve the determination of means, standard deviation of the mean, as well as the Wilcoxon test as a hypothesis-testing method. The value considered statistically significant will be p < 0.05. Results: 56 patients, 62.5% male and 37.5% female, average age of 51.1 years. 53.6% with hernia at L5S1 and 5.7% at L4L5. Treated with T-LIF in 69.6% and Discectomy (DCM) in 30.4%. Complications in 7.1%. At one month, DCM with axial and lower limb pain VAS of 2, and ODI 20 vs. TLIF (Transforaminal Lumbar Interbody Fusion) of 2 for axial pain and 1 for lower limbs, and ODI 20. At two months, DCM with axial and lower limb pain VAS of 2 and ODI 15 vs. TLIF of 2 for axial pain and 1 for lower limbs, and ODI 17 with no significant difference. Conclusion: There are significant differences in the improvement outcomes of pain and function before and after surgical treatment when comparing both surgical modalities, but there are no significant clinical-functional differences measured by means of axial VAS, lower limbs, and ODI in postoperative patients with discectomy vs TLIF for patients with lumbar disc herniation.
Key words: Intervertebral Disc Protrusion; Intervertebral disc displacement; Discectomy; Laminectomy; Orthopedic procedures; Outcome assessment.

## ID: AGS26/2879

## P0497: Microbiology of lumbar disc herniations in the absence of underlying degenerative disease

William Lambert

1

, Thomas Pieters

1

1

University of Massachusetts, Worcester, United States

Introduction: When the contents of the nucleus pulposus are exposed to the host immune system during intervertebral disc degeneration autoimmune reactions have been observed, suggesting that the nucleus pulposus is an immune privileged site. Therefore, it is hypothesized that occult infection of the intervertebral disc may contribute to the pathophysiology of symptomatic disc herniations. Occult infections of the disc space have been previously studied in patients with significant degenerative spondylosis, however assessment for occult infections in acutely herniated discs in young patients in the absence of a background of degenerative disc disease represents a knowledge gap and the focus of this study. Material and Methods: In this single center prospective case series patients were eligible for inclusion if they were age 18-40 years, presenting with an acute/subacute operative lumbar disc herniation in the absence of moderate to severe degenerative changes to the lumbar spine as noted on MRI. At the time of surgery cultures swabs were sterilely collected and sent for gram stain and aerobic/anaerobic culture analysis. Results: There patients met study inclusion criteria. Gram stain resulted negative in all three cases. No patient had documented systemic antibiotic use within 6 months of surgical intervention. All patients experienced clinical improvement following surgery. A 31-year-old male presenting with radiculopathy and a left L5-S1 central/subarticular disc herniation final cultures came back negative. A 31-year-old female presenting with radiculopathy and a left subarticular disc herniation at L4/L5 had final aerobic cultures with scant growth of staphylococcus capitis. A 32-year-old female presenting with cauda equina from a large central L5-S1 disc herniation had final aerobic cultures with scant growth of Corynebacterium species and staphylococcus aureus. Conclusion: Multiple studies have documented that when swabbed and sent for microbiological analysis and culture, patients with significant degenerative disc disease grow organisms of low virulence suggesting indolent infection. This report is the first to document that these indolent infections may be present prior to significant disc degeneration, and may contribute to the inflammatory cascade that ultimately leads to degenerative lumbar disc disease.

## ID: AGS26/2896

## P0498: Heavy metal exposure induces ROS, senescence and inflammation in the disc resulting in allodynia

Michael Grant

1

, Rowa Bakadlag

1

, Laura Epure

1

, Koren Mann

1

, John Antoniou

1

, Fackson Mwale

1

1

McGill University, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: The heavy metal tungsten has been increasing in demand for use in manufacturing and recently, medical devices, as it imparts flexibility, strength, and conductance of metal alloys. Given the surge in tungsten use, our population may be subjected to elevated exposures. We recently determined that when mice are exposed to tungsten [15 ppm] in their drinking water, it bioaccumulates in the intervertebral disc (IVD) and vertebrae tissues leading to disc degeneration, fibrosis and increases in markers on inflammation and pain. This study was performed to determine the mechanism and physiological effects following tungsten exposure in the IVD. Material and Methods: Bovine nucleus pulposus (hNP) were cultured in medium supplemented with tungsten in the form of sodium tungstate [0, 1, 5 and 15 ug/mL] or H2O2 [100 μm]. QPCR was performed to measure changes in the expression of matrix, inflammatory and pain markers. Assessment of oxidative stress was performed using H2DCFDA to measure ROS cellular levels. DNA damage was measured using γH2AX fluorescence. Cellular senescence was determined by measuring changes in β-galactosidase activity. IHC for MOMA staining was performed IVDs from C57BL/6 mice on water supplemented with 15 ppm tungsten for 4 weeks. Von Frey performed on mice following 6 months of tungsten exposure. Results: Tungsten and H2O2 significantly increased the expression of IL1B, TNFA, NGF and COLII in NP cells (p < 0.01, n = 4). On the contrary, the matrix protein aggrecan (ACAN) was downregulated suggesting a degenerative phenotype (p < 0.01, n = 4). Oxidative stress was found to be increased in a dose-dependent manner in NP cells (p < 0.05; n = 4). Interestingly, there were no changes in DNA damage; however, we did observe an induction of cellular senescence following tungsten exposure. Tungsten significantly increased the infiltration of macrophages at lumbar IVD sites (p < 0.05, n = 4). Mice exposed to tungsten for 6 months in their drinking water showed significant changes in mechanical allodynia (p < 0.05, n = 5). Conclusion: We provide evidence that chronic tungsten exposure induces disc degeneration and pain behaviour in mice and may play a role in disc degeneration disease.

## ID: AGS26/3022

## P0499: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) for recurrent lumbar disc herniation or post-discectomy degeneration: a single-center retrospective study

Francesco Lolli

1

, Gennaro Salierno

1

, Nicolò Regge Gianas

1

, Marco Cancedda

1

, Ilaria Barni

1

, Riccardo Draghi

1

, Roberto Ricca

1

, Andrea Baioni

1

, Andrea Messina

1

, Ignazio Borghesi

1

1

Maria Cecilia Hospital, Cotignola (Ravenna), Italy

Introduction: Lumbar microdiscectomy is the standard treatment for disc herniation, but recurrence and post-discectomy disc degeneration remain common clinical challenges. Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) has been proposed as an alternative surgical strategy in these cases, but comparative evidence remains limited. The aim of this study was to evaluate the safety and efficacy of ALIF in patients previously operated on with microdiscectomy who later developed recurrent herniation or associated disc degeneration. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 108 patients treated with ALIF at our institution between January 2017 and April 2025. Evaluated parameters included intra- and perioperative complications, length of hospital stay, clinical outcomes (VAS, ODI, RMDQ), radiological parameters (pelvic indices, lumbar lordosis, disc height) before and after surgery, fusion rate, recurrence rate, patient satisfaction, and return to work at 6 months. Results were compared with literature data on patients undergoing repeat microdiscectomy for recurrence. Results: ALIF demonstrated significant clinical improvements in terms of VAS, ODI, and RMDQ scores, along with radiological improvements in lumbar lordosis and disc height. The fusion rate was high, with low recurrence and complication rates. Compared with the literature on re-microdiscectomy, ALIF showed no increase in morbidity and a reduced risk of re-recurrence, while maintaining favorable patient-reported satisfaction and return-to-work rates at 6 months. Conclusions: ALIF represents a safe and effective surgical option for patients with recurrent lumbar disc herniation or post-discectomy degeneration, offering significant clinical and radiological benefits without increasing morbidity compared to repeat microdiscectomy.

## ID: AGS26/3034

## P0500: Intervertebral disc degeneration: a risk factor for metastatic vertebral fractures

Margherita Pasini

1

, Giulia Cavazzoni

1

, Gaia Youssef

1

, Luca Cristofolini

1

, Enrico Dall'Ara

2,3

, Marco Palanca

1

1

Department of Industrial Engineering, Alma Mater Studiorum - University of Bologna, Bologna, Italy

2

Division of Clinical Medicine, The University of Sheffield, Sheffield, United Kingdom

3

INSIGNEO Institute, The University of Sheffield, Sheffield, United Kingdom

Introduction: Vertebral metastases have become a major concern for the healthcare system over the past decade. They compromise vertebral microstructure, reducing load-bearing capacity and increasing the risk of fracture. Among the biomechanical parameters used to stratify metastatic patients and assess the vertebral risk of fracture [1], intervertebral disc (IVD) conditions are overlooked. However, evidence suggests that age-related degeneration alters IVD mechanical properties and load-sharing function, and may significantly influence the mechanical behaviour of the adjacent vertebrae [2,3]. This study aimed to assess whether IVD degeneration affects the failure mode of metastatic vertebrae. Material and Methods: Thirteen thoracic spinal segments, with one lytic and one radiologically healthy vertebra, were harvested from human metastatic donors (Prot.113043). 1.5T MRI scans (T2-weighted sequence) were acquired to characterise the IVDs’ degeneration level [4]. Five specimens were injected in the Nucleus Pulposus (NP) of the central IVD with a solution of Collagenase type II (GibcoTM, 125 U/mg) and Phosphate-Buffered-Solution [2mg/ml] [3] to induce a severe artificial degeneration. In situ mechanical tests were performed in a custom jig placed within a μCT scanner (VivaCT80, Scanco). Each specimen was μCT scanned twice: unloaded and at failure. Compressive deformations were measured in healthy and metastatic vertebrae through Digital Volume Correlation (BoneDVC) [5]. The failure location was identified by examining the deformation maps and overlaying the microCT scans acquired in unloaded condition and after failure. Results from the non-degenerated specimens were compared with the degenerated specimens. Results: Compressive strains in the non-degenerated specimens reached -8557 με in lytic vertebrae and -3560 με in control vertebrae, whereas in degenerated specimens they were -8927 με and -8716 με, respectively. The non-degenerated specimens failed at or near the vertebral endplates; in particular, the collapse usually occurred between the endplate and metastatic lesions. The degenerated specimens, instead, failed at mid-height of the metastatic and/or control vertebral body; failure involved both trabecular and cortical bone, while the endplates remained intact. Conclusion: IVD degeneration was found to be a key factor in determining the failure mode and location not only of metastatic vertebrae, but also of healthy ones. In non-degenerated specimens, the NP bears the majority of the load, resulting in endplate collapse and damage of the underlying trabecular bone[5]. Conversely, in degenerated IVDs, the load is shifted from the NP towards the Annulus Fibrosus (AF), increasing the load transferred to the cortical shell of adjacent vertebrae. This altered load distribution leads to cortical shell failure at the mid-height of the vertebral body, resulting in transverse fractures. Therefore, if the metastatic lesions are confined to the trabecular region, IVD degeneration may act as a protective factor for the cortical bone shell, whereas if the lesion involves the cortical shell, failure is more likely to occur. These results highlight the clinical relevance of introducing MRI-based IVD degeneration assessment in the clinical routine to improve vertebral fracture risk evaluation and metastatic patient stratification.

References

1. Fisher et al. Spine 2010.
2. Pasini et al. JMBBM 2025.
3. Cavazzoni et al. Acta Biomaterialia 2025.
4. Pfirrmann et al. Spine 2001.
5. Palanca et al. Bone 2023.
Acknowledgements: HEU-Funded METASTRA project (grant 101080135)

## ID: AGS26/3234

## P0501: Thoracic disc herniations: a series of 25 cases

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracic disc herniations are rare, with an estimated annual incidence of 1 per 1,000,000 inhabitants. Clinical presentation is often non-specific, making systematic assessment of sublesional syndromes essential. MRI is the key diagnostic tool, complemented by electrophysiological studies to evaluate functional impact. Surgical treatment is generally indicated for symptomatic cases. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 25 patients (15 males, 10 females; mean age 51 years, range 21-73) who underwent surgery for thoracic disc herniation. Clinical presentation, imaging, surgical approach, complications, and postoperative outcomes were analyzed. The diagnostic delay ranged from 3 weeks to 8 years (mean 15 months). Six patients had a history of spinal trauma. Results: Presenting symptoms included pain (72%), motor or bladder dysfunction (52%), and sensory disturbances (52%). Neurological examination revealed pyramidal signs in all patients, paraparesis in 72%, and sensory deficits in 52%. Standard radiographs were positive in 10 patients, with calcifications in 3 and Scheuermann disease in 4. CT was positive in 17 patients. MRI localized herniations mainly at D10-D11 and D11-D12 levels. Eleven patients underwent preoperative arteriography. Surgical approaches included posterior (n = 9), posterolateral transpedicular (n = 4), lateral costotransversectomy (n = 6), and anterolateral thoracotomy (n = 6). One patient required reoperation due to wrong level identification. Postoperatively, no immediate surgical complications occurred. At follow-up (11 months-6 years), 40% of patients reported persistent sensory symptoms (paresthesia/electric shocks), and 28% experienced resolution of bladder dysfunction. Conclusion: Thoracic disc herniation is a rare but potentially debilitating condition. MRI remains the diagnostic gold standard, and individualized surgical approaches provide good decompression and neurological improvement. Long-term follow-up is essential due to residual sensory deficits and variable recovery of bladder function.

## ID: AGS26/3250

## P0502: Cauda equina syndrome on disc herniation: about our experience and review of the literature

Ahmed Adel Bezzaouia

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Houssem Mars

1

, Safouen Ben Brahim

1

, Mzid Ahmed

1

, mondher mestiri

1

, Hassen Makhlouf

1

1

Mohamed Kassab Institute of Orthopedics, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Cauda equina syndrome is the clinical manifestation of compression of the conus medullaris. Herniated discs are the predominant cause. The severity of the sequelae of this relatively rare spinal condition encourages the development of a better understanding aimed at codifying the management of this condition, which remains a subject of controversy. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective study including seven cases treated in our department between May 2003 and September 2018 involving disc herniation associated with bladder-sphincteric disorders. Results: Our series includes six women and one man. The average age is 47, ranging from 29 to 62. All patients presented with symptoms of spinal syndrome with unilateral or bilateral polyradiculopathy in four cases and three cases, respectively. The bladder-sphincteric disorders were incontinence, urinary retention with overflow leakage, and sexual dysfunction. Four patients had saddle anesthesia and loss of anal reflex, but none of the patients had a gaping anus on admission. All patients had associated motor and/or sensory deficits. Radiological examination was performed by lumbar spine X-ray for all seven patients, MRI for five, and CT scan for the other two patients. The time to surgery was six hours for one patient, 12 hours for another, and more than 48 hours for the rest. In terms of treatment, three patients underwent laminectomy, discectomy, and PLIF, one patient underwent laminectomy, dissection, and TLIF. Two patients underwent laminectomy with discectomy without instrumentation, and the last patient underwent recalibration with posterior instrumented arthrodesis. The complications that occurred were a dura mater breach in one case and an infection at the surgical site in another. At a mean follow-up of 57 months, three patients had made a full recovery, while two patients had persistent perineal paresthesia with sensory-motor recovery. Two patients are still recovering (short follow-up). Conclusion: The lack of consensus in terms of definition and treatment makes cauda equina syndrome a real challenge for any practitioner treating spinal disorders.

## ID: AGS26/3286

## P0504: Posterior decompression alone in giant thoracic disc herniation: a case report and review of surgical controversies

Luis Vasquez-Viana

1

, Juan Sierra

2

, Isabella Negrete

3

1

Fundación Cardioinfantil - La Cardio Clinic, Bogota, Colombia

2

Fundación Cardioinfantil - La cardio Clinic, Bogota, Colombia

3

Fundación Cardioinfantil - La cardio Clinic, El Rosario University, Bogota, Colombia

Introduction: Thoracic disc herniations (TDHs) are rare, accounting for only 0.25-1.8% of all herniated disc cases, with an estimated incidence of 1 per 1,000,000 individuals. A subset, known as giant thoracic disc herniations (GTDHs), involves compression of more than 40% of the anteroposterior diameter of the spinal canal. These lesions may result in myelopathy or radiculopathy, often necessitating surgical intervention. However, there is no consensus regarding the optimal surgical approach, with entire resection osteotomy vs posterior decompression alone. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 41-year-old male who developed progressive thoracic myelopathy secondary to a massive GTDH. MRI and CT imaging confirmed a large, calcified disc herniation at the thoracic level, significantly compressing the spinal cord. Given the patient's clinical status and surgical risk profile, a posterior decompression alone was performed, avoiding the need for complete calcified disc resection with interesting results. We made a review of the available evidence to summarize recommendations to determine surgical options for these cases. Results: The patient showed significant neurological improvement after surgery, with sustained neurological recovery in follow-up. There were no major complications, and imaging confirmed adequate decompression of the spinal cord. The clinical outcome suggests that decompression without full resection may suffice in select cases, particularly when the disc is calcified or adherent to the dura. Posterior decompression alone may offer a safer and effective alternative in selected patients, especially those with comorbidities, older age, or surgically challenging disc anatomy. The case also underscores the importance of individualized surgical planning and highlights key areas of controversy in GTDH management. Conclusion: Posterior decompression alone may be a viable treatment option for select patients with GTDH, challenging the traditional paradigm of complete disc removal. Further studies are warranted to better define criteria for patient selection and to compare outcomes across different surgical approaches.

## ID: AGS26/3291

## P0505: Surgical techniques and outcomes in giant calcified thoracic disc herniation: a scoping review

Luis Vasquez-Viana

1

, Juan Sierra

1

, Isabella Negrete

1

1

Fundación Cardioinfantil - La Cardio Clinic, Bogota, Colombia

Introduction: Massive calcified thoracic disc herniations (MCTDH) are a rare yet clinically significant pathology, often leading to spinal cord compression and myelopathy. Surgical management is considered the primary treatment in symptomatic cases; however, the presence of dense calcification and complex anatomy poses significant technical challenges with no clear management consensus. The objective of this scoping review is to understand the extent and type of evidence regarding the surgical techniques used for the management of massive calcified thoracic disc herniations, with a particular focus on bleeding, surgical time, dural tears, intra and postoperative complications and neurological outcomes and functional recovery. Material and Methods: This scoping review was conducted following the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) methodology. A systematic literature search was performed in Medline, Embase, Central, and LILACS. The search will include publications from 2010 to 2025 and be limited to articles in English and Spanish. This review included studies involving adult patients diagnosed with giant or massive calcified thoracic disc herniations treated surgically. Studies reported on the surgical approach, decision making and associated clinical outcomes, particularly neurological complications. All analytical and descriptive observational studies with more than 10 patients, as well as systematic reviews, were considered. Single case reports, qualitative studies, and expert opinion articles were excluded. Results: A total of 4,300 articles were initially identified through the database and grey literature search. After title and abstract screening, followed by full-text review and eligibility assessment, 18 studies met the inclusion criteria. Across these studies, surgical intervention remains the primary treatment for symptomatic giant calcified thoracic disc herniation (GTDH), particularly in the presence of neurological deficits. However, the unique anatomical features of the thoracic spine, including its narrow spinal canal, complex vascular anatomy, and proximity to vital thoracic structures, make surgical management significantly more challenging than in the lumbar region. Multiple surgical approaches have been reported, ranging from traditional anterior thoracotomy and posterolateral costotransversectomy to more recent minimally invasive techniques. These include the use of high-speed drills, ultrasonic aspiration devices, and other advanced surgical tools to reduce morbidity. A recurring theme in the literature is the ongoing debate regarding the necessity of complete disc resection, especially in calcified herniations. Historically, total excision was considered the gold standard, particularly for large, central lesions. However, several recent studies have investigated posterior decompression alone as a potentially safer and equally effective alternative in select cases-particularly in older patients, those with significant comorbidities, or when the herniated disc is densely adherent to the dura mater. Notably, these studies report comparable neurological outcomes between complete resection and decompression-only strategies in carefully selected patients. Conclusion: Surgical management of giant calcified thoracic disc herniation remains complex, with no clear consensus on the optimal approach. While complete resection is traditionally favored, recent evidence supports posterior decompression alone as a safe and effective option in selected patients. Further studies are needed to guide surgical decision-making and standardize treatment strategies.

## ID: AGS26/3333

## P0512: Recurrence of operated lumbar disc herniations: a study of risk factors

Elouni Emna

1

, Nessrine Nessib

1

, Khaled Radhouene

2

, Sameh Achoura

2

, Ahmed Harbaoui

2

, Ridha Chkili

2

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Hôpital Militaire Principal d'Instruction de Tunis, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation is the most common degenerative condition of the spine. Lumbar discectomy is the surgical procedure that ensures radicular decompression after the failure of a well-conducted conservative treatment. However, this surgical procedure carries the risk of hernia recurrence. These recurrences are influenced by certain factors. Therefore, the aim of our study was to identify the risk factors for recurrent lumbar disc herniation after surgery. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study on patients who underwent lumbar discectomy for lumbar disc herniation at the Department of Neurosurgery of the Main Military Instruction Hospital in Tunis between 2013 and 2021. Our study included 180 patients divided into two groups: the first group comprised 90 patients who underwent surgery for lumbar disc herniation without recurrence, and the second group included 90 patients who underwent surgery for lumbar disc herniation and were subsequently reoperated due to recurrence. Results: The prevalence of recurrences of operated lumbar disc herniation in our study was 21.4%. The risk factors identified in our work included prolonged standing occupation (p = 0.003), belonging to the military population (p = 0.002), the type of herniation: subligamentous (p = 0.04), Pfirmann stage III (p = 0.012), MODIC type I changes (p < 10-3), partial discectomy (p < 10-3), and the absence of postoperative rehabilitation (p < 10-3). Among these factors, we identified independent factors correlated with a higher rate of hernia recurrence. In this study, partial discectomy (p = 0.001; OR = 0.123) and the absence of postoperative rehabilitation (p = 0.002; OR = 56.5) were independent risk factors for the recurrence of operated lumbar disc herniation. Conclusion: It is essential to identify the risk factors for recurrent lumbar disc herniations. This in-depth study has enabled the establishment of recommendations regarding surgery for lumbar disc herniations, as well as the determination of specific criteria for surgical indications and techniques, with the aim of preventing recurrences.

## ID: AGS26/3337

## P0513: Recurrent lumbar disc herniation in the Tunisian population: surgical outcomes and risk of spinal instability (2000-2025)

Mohamed Amine Dhifallah

1

, Habboubi Khalil

1

, Oussama Zaibet

1

, Ben Brahim Safouen

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Hachicha Hassan

1

, Mondher Mestiri

1

1

Institut Med Taieb Kassab, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Recurrent lumbar disc herniation (rLDH) remains a common cause of persistent pain and disability following lumbar discectomy, with recurrence rates ranging from 5% to 15%. The optimal surgical strategy remains debated, particularly regarding the prevention of postoperative instability. This study aimed to assess surgical outcomes and the risk of spinal instability in Tunisian patients operated for rLDH. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 124 patients operated for rLDH between 2000 and 2025 at the Mohamed Taieb Kassab Institute. Inclusion required MRI-confirmed recurrence at the same level and side, after ≥ 6 months symptom-free. Patients underwent either revision microdiscectomy (74%) or discectomy with fusion (26%). Clinical outcomes were assessed using VAS and ODI scores. Postoperative stability was evaluated radiologically. Mean follow-up: 42 months. Results: The cohort included 64% males, mean age 46 years. At last follow-up, 78% of patients achieved good/excellent outcomes, with mean ODI improvement from 58% to 24% (p < 0.001) and VAS reduction of 5.6 points. Complications occurred in 11% of cases (dural tears, superficial infection). Postoperative instability developed in 14% of patients after revision discectomy versus 6% after fusion. Re-recurrence occurred in 8% of cases, predominantly after discectomy alone. Conclusion: This 25-year experience demonstrates that surgery for rLDH provides significant functional improvement in Tunisian patients. However, the risk of spinal instability is not negligible after isolated revision discectomy. Fusion procedures, although more invasive, reduced instability and re-recurrence in selected patients. Individualized surgical strategy remains essential to optimize long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3358

## P0514: A systematic review of the spectrum of magnetic resonance imaging features of degenerative thoracic myelopathy: a global perspective

Eleanor White

1

, Amir Rafati-Fard

1

, Jay Wardropper

1

, Renuka Chintapalli

1

, Edwin Koubeh

1

, Hariesha Pathmaraj

1

, Sruthi Ranganathan

1

, Tanzil Rujeedawa

1

, Chandan Sekhon

1

, Benjamin Davies

1

, Mark Kotter

1

, Oliver Mowforth

1

1

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom

Introduction: Degenerative thoracic myelopathy (DTM) is an umbrella term encompassing a range of degenerative pathologies leading to symptomatic compression of the thoracic spinal cord. Diagnosis is achieved through clinical history, examination, and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). A comprehensive review of the pathological findings seen on MRI and their clinical significance is currently lacking in DTM. The objective was therefore to synthesise current evidence on MRI features of DTM. Materials and Methods: A systematic review was registered on PROSPERO (CRD42024518673) and performed adhering to PRISMA guidelines. MEDLINE and Embase were searched from conception to February 2025. Screening, data extraction and risk of bias assessments were performed in duplicate. Studies reporting on MRI imaging for adult patients with degenerative thoracic myelopathy met inclusion criteria. A qualitative synthesis of the literature was conducted, following the Synthesis Without Meta-Analysis (SWiM) reporting guidelines. Results: Of 7937 screening records, 59 papers satisfied inclusion criteria, including one cross-sectional and 58 cohort studies. A total of 2940 patients were included. DTM was most commonly localised to the lower thoracic spine (62.5%, 880/1409), with the remaining cases split evenly between the upper (18.2%, 256/1409) and middle regions (19.4%, 273/1409). Ossification of the ligamentum flavum (OLF) was the most common pathology (71.4%, 2065/2893), followed by ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL) (19.7%, 569/2893) and disc herniation (20.1%, 582/2893). Measurements of spinal canal compromise were described in 55.5% of patients (1631/2940) and hyperintensity of the spinal cord was reported in 30.0% (881/2940). High grade spinal cord hyperintensity, spinal canal compromise, OLF, OPLL and multilevel disease were all predictive of greater severity of myelopathy and poorer functional recovery. Conclusion: In the published literature, ligamentous pathologies are significantly more prevalent than disc related pathologies in DTM. DTM is most common in the lower thoracic spine. T2 signal change and the extent of spinal canal compromise correlate with clinical outcomes. Standardisation of the terminology used to describe imaging features, in addition to a more consistent approach to study design may help to improve future research.

## ID: AGS26/26

## P0515: Crosstalk between osteoporosis and fat-infiltrated psoas at the upper lumbar levels

Murat Ekşi

1

, Emel Özcan-Ekşi

2

1

İstinye University, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Private Practice, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Osteoporosis is a chronic metabolic bone disease characterized by reduced bone mass, microarchitectural deterioration, and an increased risk of fractures. Alongside osteoporosis, sarcopenia, the age-related decline in skeletal muscle mass and strength, is commonly observed in the elderly, especially among women. Prior studies have linked vertebral compression fractures with impaired spinal mobility, muscle atrophy, fatty degeneration of paraspinal muscles, and balance deficits. However, the relationship between osteoporosis in the absence of vertebral fractures and the condition of paraspinal muscles remains unclear. This study aimed to investigate whether osteoporosis is associated with fatty infiltration of the paraspinal muscles in patients experiencing chronic low back pain. Material and Methods: Age- and sex-matched patients with and without osteoporosis, all presenting with chronic low back pain, were retrospectively assessed using lumbar magnetic resonance imaging. Evaluations included the degree of intervertebral disc degeneration (IVDD), Modic changes, and fat infiltration in the multifidus, erector spinae, and psoas muscles at all lumbar levels. Results: No significant differences were found between osteoporotic and non-osteoporotic groups regarding the severity of IVDD or the presence of Modic changes. However, patients with lumbar spine osteoporosis exhibited significantly greater fatty infiltration in the psoas muscle at the L1-L2 (odds ratio [OR] = 6.191; p = 0.015), L2-L3 (OR = 3.205; p = 0.028), and L5-S1 (OR = 1.564; p = 0.040) levels. Additionally, those with femoral neck osteoporosis demonstrated greater fat infiltration in the psoas muscle specifically at L1-L2 (OR = 4.487; p = 0.043). Conclusion: Osteoporosis appears to be associated with increased fatty infiltration of the psoas muscle, particularly at the upper lumbar levels. Therefore, detailed evaluation of the psoas muscle should be considered in osteoporotic patients. Conversely, the presence of fat-infiltrated psoas muscles at upper lumbar levels may warrant clinical suspicion for underlying osteoporosis. Management strategies for osteoporosis should incorporate interventions aimed at enhancing the quality and strength of the psoas muscle to help prevent vertebral compression fractures.

## ID: AGS26/477

## P0517: Spinal injuries in athletes: an 18-year retrospective analysis at a university hospital

Marcos Vaz de Lima

1

, Pedro Baches Jorge

1

1

Santa Casa de São Paulo, Spine AI, São Paulo, Brazil

Objective: To analyze the prevalence, segment distribution, and etiological diagnosis of spinal injuries in athletes treated over an 18-year period in a Sports Traumatology outpatient clinic. Methods: A retrospective review of 550 athlete medical records from 2006 to 2023 was performed. Inclusion criteria were regular sports practice and confirmed diagnosis of spinal pathology. Statistical analysis included Chi-Square and Two-Proportion Z-tests (significance p < 0.05). Results: Lumbar involvement was predominant (90.5%), followed by cervical (10.4%) and thoracic (1.6%). The most common diagnoses were lumbar disc disease (32%), spondylolisthesis (8.2%), and scoliosis (4.2%). Notably, 25% of cases were managed conservatively without imaging. Males accounted for 54.9% of the sample. Conclusion: Spinal injuries are prevalent among athletes, with clear predominance of lumbar involvement. Clinical evaluation and conservative treatment were effective in many cases.
Keywords: Spine injuries; athletes; epidemiology; low back pain; conservative treatment.

## ID: AGS26/727

## P0519: Predictors of long-term patient satisfaction after lumbar spine surgery: a two-year prospective analysis

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Elinor Stern

1

, Mihir Surapaneni

1

, Roberto Gonzalez

1

, Abdel Diab

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Rising rates of lumbar spine surgery have heightened focus on patient satisfaction as a key metric of surgical success. While studies have identified demographic factors affecting immediate outcomes, the impact of baseline characteristics on long-term satisfaction remains unclear. This study examines how initial patient presentation predicts satisfaction trajectories, aiming to enhance preoperative counseling and optimize outcomes. Material and Methods: A prospective multicenter database was queried for patients undergoing lumbar spine surgery at a tertiary care institution from 2013-2023. Patients presenting emergently or lacking two-year follow-up satisfaction data were excluded. Satisfaction, assessed at 90 days, 1 year, and 2 years postoperatively, was dichotomized into “satisfied” (met expectations/improved, would repeat) and “not satisfied” (improved, but would not repeat/did not meet expectations). Baseline symptoms, prior interventions, medical history, education and employment plans were evaluated as predictors of satisfaction. T-tests and Chi-square tests were used for continuous and categorical variables respectively. Multivariable logistic regression was employed to identify predictors. Results: 1161 patients were included in final analysis. The cohort had a mean age of 60.4 ± 14.1 years, was predominantly white (92.6%) and male (56.0%), with 38.0% retired and 33.8% employed. Most patients presented with radicular symptoms (92.9%) and axial back pain (88.7%), and 42.5% had prior spine surgery. Satisfaction rates declined from 86.1% at 90 days to 82.0% at 2 years. Variables associated with satisfaction at 2yrs included: Education (post-college vs. high school: 66.0% vs. 47.0% at 2 years, p < 0.001), COPD status (no-COPD vs. COPD: 83.5% vs. 67.3%, p < 0.001), Smoking status (never vs. current: 84.8% vs. 79.4%, p < 0.001), Previous spine surgery (no previous vs. previous: 86.2% vs. 76.6%, p < 0.001). From 90d to 2yrs, satisfaction gaps widened over time for education (13.0% to 19.0%), COPD (13.0% to 16.2%), and previous surgery (6.1% to 9.6%), while remaining stable for smoking status (∼5.4-6.5%). In multivariable logistic regression (n = 532 with complete data), independent predictors of greater satisfaction at 2 years included:
• Male gender (OR 1.75, 95% CI 1.06-2.87, p = .027).
• Lower BMI (OR 0.94 per unit increase, 95% CI 0.91-0.98, p = .001).
• Absence of COPD (OR 0.37, 95% CI 0.16-0.87, p = .023).
• No prior spine surgery (OR 0.48, 95% CI 0.30-0.77, p = .002).
• Being retired (vs unemployed) (OR 2.96, 95% CI 1.38-6.34, p = .005).
• Planning to return to work (OR 4.12, 95% CI 1.73-9.80, p = .001).
Other employment categories (disability, part-time, full-time) were not significant. Education was significant in earlier steps but did not remain in the final model after stepwise selection. Conclusion: While overall satisfaction remains above 80% at two years, specific patient factors-including sex, BMI, COPD, surgical history, and employment outlook-strongly predict long-term outcomes. The widening satisfaction gaps over time, particularly for education level (13.0% to 19.0%) and COPD status (13.0% to 16.2%), suggest these factors have compounding effects on outcomes. These findings identify key targets for intervention: tailored preoperative education, optimization of pulmonary function, and comprehensive counseling on smoking cessation, weight management, and return-to-work planning to improve patient satisfaction trajectories following lumbar spine surgery.

 

 

 

## ID: AGS26/891

## P0520: Different pathoanatomy of central stenosis in lumbar spondylolytic spondylolisthesis: an intersegmental stenosis

Hyuk-joon Sohn

1

, Eun-seok Son

1

, Min-gyu Lee

1

1

Keimyung University, Dongsan Medical Center, Daegu, South Korea

Introduction: Spondylolytic spondylolisthesis (SS) has classically been described as a condition that primarily induces foraminal stenosis, while central stenosis has been regarded as a rare finding in standard textbooks. However, in clinical practice, central stenosis is frequently encountered in patients with SS and may significantly contribute to neurological symptoms. Despite this, the morphologic basis of central stenosis in SS has not been clearly characterized. This study aimed to investigate the incidence and contributing factors of central stenosis in SS and to elucidate its unique pathophysiology, distinct from degenerative spondylolisthesis. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 280 patients diagnosed with SS using T2-weighted mid-sagittal magnetic resonance imaging. Quantitative parameters included dural sac anterior-posterior diameter (DSAPD), disc height, anterior translation, pseudo-bulging of the disc, and ligamentum flavum hypertrophy. Central stenosis was graded as mild (7-10 mm), moderate (5-7 mm), or severe (< 5 mm) according to DSAPD. Correlations between each factor and the presence of central stenosis were statistically examined, and characteristic morphologic patterns were described. Results: Patients with central stenosis demonstrated significantly reduced disc height (5.19 mm vs. 7.67 mm, p < 0.001), greater anterior translation (6.92 mm vs. 4.35 mm, p < 0.001), more pronounced pseudo-bulging (6.25 mm vs. 5.51 mm, p = 0.001), and marked ligamentum flavum hypertrophy (8.03 mm vs. 4.16 mm, p < 0.001). In contrast to degenerative spondylolisthesis, where stenosis occurs mainly at the slipped level, SS revealed a distinct mechanism of “intersegmental stenosis,” produced by the pseudo-bulging disc of the spondylolytic vertebra and the buckled, hypertrophied ligamentum flavum of the upper vertebra. Conclusion: Central stenosis in SS is strongly correlated with disc height loss, anterior translation, pseudo-disc bulging, and ligamentum flavum hypertrophy. The characteristic morphology is best described as intersegmental stenosis, representing a novel pathophysiologic concept distinct from degenerative disease. These findings underscore the importance of targeted imaging assessment and may influence surgical decision-making in this unique patient population.

## ID: AGS26/1035

## P0521: The impact of COVID-19 on spine surgery: temporal trends in complications and patient-reported outcomes across a statewide cohort

Ali Mehaidli

1

, Richard Easton

2

, Ahmad Almaat

3

, Gary George

1

, Alqasim Elnaggar

3

, Victor Chang

4

, Doris Tong

5

, Jad Khalil

6

, Kevin Taliaferro

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Henry Ford Hospital, Detroit, United States

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Corewell Health Hospital, Troy, United States

3

Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

4

Department of Neurosurgery, Henry Ford Hospital, Detroit, United States

5

Department of Neurosurgery, Henry Ford Providence Hospital, Southfield, United States

6

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Oakland University William Beaumont School of Medicine, Auburn Hills, United States

Introduction: The COVID-19 pandemic caused major disruptions in elective surgical care, including spine surgery, due to widespread hospital restructuring and resource limitations. Although previous studies have examined surgical volumes and safety during the pandemic, limited data exist on how outcomes varied across multiple phases of the pandemic, especially in real-world, multicenter spine surgery populations. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study used data from the Michigan Spine Surgery Improvement Collaborative (MSSIC) to compare outcomes in patients undergoing lumbar or cervical spine surgery across four COVID-19 timeframes: pre-COVID (1/2019-2/2020), onset-COVID (3/2020-9/2020), active-COVID (10/2020-2/2022), and post-COVID (3/2022-12/2022). Patient demographics, surgical characteristics, 30- and 90-day adverse events, and patient-reported outcomes (PROs) were compared across cohorts using Chi-squared or Fisher’s exact tests for categorical variables and ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis tests for continuous variables. Results: Among 47,831 patients (33,532 lumbar; 14,299 cervical), demographics were consistent across phases. For lumbar surgery, onset-COVID was marked by higher durotomy, urinary retention, and 90-day return-to-OR, while CSF leak and 30-day readmissions declined during active-COVID. Cervical surgery showed increased dysphagia during active-COVID and higher 90-day return-to-OR and readmissions during onset-COVID, with urinary retention rates declining over time. Patient-reported outcomes were generally stable, however, lumbar patients were more likely to achieve back pain MCID during onset-COVID. Notably, cervical patients reported higher one-year satisfaction during onset-, active-, and post-COVID compared with pre-COVID. Functional recovery showed different patterns: lumbar return-to-work dropped at onset but later stabilized, whereas cervical return-to-work stayed consistent across phases and improved post-COVID. Readmission causes also shifted: pulmonary complications increased at onset then decreased, while electrolyte, pharmacologic, and stroke-related readmissions declined over time. Conclusion: Spine surgery outcomes remained largely stable throughout the COVID-19 pandemic. Although certain complications temporarily increased during the onset phase, outcomes normalized as healthcare systems adapted. Long-term patient-reported outcomes were not adversely impacted and, in some cases, improved, suggesting resilience in surgical care delivery despite pandemic-related challenges.

## ID: AGS26/1195

## P0522: Trident approach to chronic low back pain classification: a comprehensive cohort study of patients with nociceptive and neuropathic pain assessed by the PainDETECT Questionnaire

Stone Sima

1

, Samuel Lapkin

2

, Victor Chen

1

, Lauren Barber

3

, Ashish Diwan

1,4,5

1

Spine Labs, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George and Sutherland Clinical Campuses, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

2

School of Nursing and Midwifery, Griffith University, Gold Coast, Australia

3

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Virginia Commonwealth University, Richmond, VA, Richmond, United States

4

Spine Service, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George Hospital, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

5

Spinal Unit, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Royal Adelaide Hospital, University of Adelaide, Adelaide, Australia

Introduction: Low back pain (LBP) is the leading cause of disability world-wide with more than 500 million people at one time significantly limited in their ability to undertake activities of daily living. The nature of LBP is varied at each individual level and complex at a systems level. At an individual level, it is hypothesized that pain is either generated via mechanical compression or chemical irritation via inflammatory biomarkers. Pain has been traditionally studied using a scalar measurement for its intensity, however, the nature of pain incorporates various descriptors. Nociceptive LBP (NoP) results from the activation of nociceptors which innervates many structures in the lumbar spine. Neuropathic LBP (NeP) is caused by a ‘lesion or disease of the somatosensory nervous system’. Traditionally, NeP and NoP have been regarded as discrete entities, primarily attributed to patho-anatomical abnormalities within the lumbar spine. However, it is increasingly evident that the nature of both pain classifications is multifaceted, encompassing a range of physical, biomechanical, chemical, and psychosocial factors. As such this study aims to address current shortcomings in the literature regarding LBP classification by (1) investigating whether there is a difference in pain severity, disability, quality of life, and sociodemographic factors between patients with NeP and NoP, (2) exploring associations between radiological findings on MRI and X-ray and NeP and NoP, and (3) elucidate associations between non-spinal comorbid conditions and NeP and NoP. Methods: This study received approval from the Human Research Ethics Committee of the University of Wollongong (No. 2020/329) and the University of New South Wales (No. HC210096). The developer of the PainDETECT Questionnaire (PDQ) provided written approval for its use in this study, a score of ≤ 12 indicated NoP and ≥ 19 indicated NeP. A retrospective analysis of a prospectively collected database involving a cohort of adult patients (> 18 years) presenting with chronic LBP (> 3 months) to a tertiary spine clinic was conducted. Patients were excluded if they had a history of spinal surgery, were diagnosed with specific spinal pathologies (fracture, malignancy, infection, axial spondylarthritis, cauda equina syndrome), or were diagnosed with non-spinal conditions that present with neuropathic symptoms (diabetic neuropathy, complex regional pain syndrome, central nervous system disorders, peripheral neuralgia). Patient demographic, radiology, and comorbidity analysis is outlined in. Independent t-test and Mann-Whitney U was used to analyze the difference in continuous and ordinal parameters, respectively. Pearson Chi-square was used to analyze the independence of association. A binary logistic regression model was used to analyze the confounding status of comorbid medical conditions to determine independent relationships between specific conditions and neuropathic pain. Results: A total of 707 patients completed the PainDETECT questionnaire and were included in the study. 512 met the inclusion and exclusion criteria and were included in the analysis, of which 279 had completed MRI and X-ray images and 400 had completed comorbidity questionnaire information. The NeP group had a lower mean age than the NoP group (55 ± 15.6 vs. 59 ± 17.8, p < 0.05), there was no difference in gender between the groups. The NeP group had higher pain severity (8.0 ± 1.5 vs. 5.8 ± 2.3, p < 0.001), higher disability (48.7 ± 21.4 vs. 25.7 ± 19.4, p < 0.001), and lower quality of life (0.562 ± 0.26 vs. 0.278 ± 0.25, p < 0.001) compared to the NoP group. Smokers and patients with no partner marital status were 2.4 (OR 2.373, 95%C.I. [1.319-4.266] and OR 2.384, 95%C.I. [1.390-4.092], respectively, p < 0.01) times more likely to have NeP compared to NoP. Additionally, the NeP group were of lower income class ( =-2.425, p < 0.05). In the radiology study, patients with NeP had higher foraminal (U = 18.962, p = 0.002) and spinal stenosis (U = 14.481, p = 0.005) severity. A subgroup analysis of patients without stenosis also revealed that the NeP group had higher prevalence of disk bulge (96% vs. 78%, p = 0.002), high-intensity zones (51% vs. 19%, p < 0.001) and Pfirmann grade (U = 11.321, p = 0.020). In the comorbidity analysis, Patients with depression, rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and gastrointestinal disorders were 2.844 (Exp(B) = 2.844, 95%C.I. [1.426-5.670], p < 0.01), 2.726 (Exp(B) = 2.726, 95%C.I. [1.183-6.283], p < 0.05) and 2.847 (Exp(B) = 2.847, 95%C.I. [1.473-5.503], p < 0.05) times more likely to suffer NeP than NoP, respectively. Discussion: NeP is associated with higher levels of disability and lower quality of life. Smokers, individuals with a no partner marital status, and individuals with a lower income class were more likely to suffer NeP rather than NoP. The study also reveals that NeP is associated with more severe neural compression, increased presence of discogenic disease and inflammatory disk severity. Additionally, NeP correlates with gastrointestinal conditions, rheumatoid arthritis, and depression, highlighting the potential involvement of the gut microbiome as a common factor linking non-spinal comorbidities. These findings emphasize the importance of individualized management plans tailored to the specific pain and medical profiles of patients, rather than relying on a one-size-fits-all approach. Furthermore, the detrimental impact of NeP on patient well-being underscores the need to represent pain on a nociceptive-neuropathic continuum to achieve more accurate differentiation of pain components and reduce unnecessary pharmacotherapy, imaging, and non-targeted surgical interventions.

## ID: AGS26/1226

## P0523: Prevalence study of atypical Scheuermann's kyphosis using computed tomography scans

Gabriel Souza

1

, Lucas Yuya

1

, Alexandre Portelinha

1

, Mariana Pontes

1

, Carlos Fernando Herrero

1

1

University of Sao Paulo, Ribeirao Preto, Brazil

Introduction: Scheuermann’s disease (SD) is a structural spinal disorder classically characterized by thoracic hyperkyphosis, anterior vertebral wedging, and associated radiographic changes. While the typical form predominantly affects the thoracic spine, the atypical variant involves the thoracolumbar region, presenting with distinct clinical and imaging features. Despite its relevance, epidemiological data on atypical SD remain scarce, as most prevalence studies do not differentiate between the two patterns. This study aims to determine the prevalence of atypical Scheuermann’s disease in young adults using abdominal computed tomography as a screening tool. Material and Methods: This cross-sectional observational study involved 1287 computed tomography scans from patients aged 18 to 40 years of both genders. Criteria for diagnosing Atypical Scheuermann's Disease included wedging of 5° in three consecutive vertebrae, combined with a total Cobb angle of 10° or more within the thoracolumbar interval from T8 to L2. Positive cases were assessed for kyphosis severity, Schmorl's nodes presence, and scoliosis. Prevalence estimation and correlation analysis with age and sex were performed. Results: The study identified 28 cases of Atypical Scheuermann's Kyphosis, indicating a prevalence of 2.8%. Conclusion: This research, utilizing abdominal tomography, offers valuable insights into the prevalence of Scheuermann's disease in its atypical form within the sampled population.

## ID: AGS26/1297

## P0524: Risk of hip fracture and mortality significantly increases following lumbar fracture in older adults across high-risk comorbidity subgroups

Ali Mehaidli

1

, Ahmad Almaat

2

, Alqasim Elnaggar

2

, Muaaz Wajahath

3

, Ryan Berger

1

, Kevin Taliaferro

1

, Erickson Andrews

4

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Henry Ford Hospital, Detroit, United States

2

Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

3

Michigan State University College of Human Medicine, East Lansing, United States

4

Darnall Army Medical Center, Fort Hood, United States

Introduction: The extent to which comorbidities compound the risk of hip fracture and mortality following lumbar fracture remains unclear. This study evaluated whether high-risk comorbidities amplify the risk of subsequent hip fracture and mortality among older adults with lumbar fracture, independent of osteoporosis and other bone density disorders. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study using the TriNetX Research Network, a large, nationally representative federated electronic health record database. Adults aged ≥65 years with a diagnosis of lumbar fracture between 2005 and 2025 were identified. Two analyses were performed: a single-cohort analysis to estimate the overall five-year risk of hip fracture (subtypes and composite) and all-cause mortality, and matched two-cohort analyses comparing patients with lumbar fracture plus a comorbidity (rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes mellitus, type 2 diabetes mellitus, or abnormal gait) against those with lumbar fracture alone. Propensity score matching accounted for demographics, clinical variables, corticosteroid use, and disorders of bone density. Patients with previous hip fracture were excluded from all hip fracture outcome analyses. Outcomes were assessed as relative risks (RR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI). Results: In the single-cohort analysis of 280,916 patients, fracture of the head and neck of the femur occurred in 8,064 (2.87%) patients, intertrochanteric fracture in 4,679 (1.67%) patients, subtrochanteric fracture in 918 (0.33%) patients, acetabular fracture in 1,257 (0.45%) patients, and the composite “all hip fracture” outcome occurred in 10,157 (3.62%) patients. All-cause mortality occurred in 47,666 of 280,916 (16.97%) patients without previous hip fracture and 51,930 of 299,035 (17.37%) overall. In the two-cohort analyses, comorbidity presence significantly increased hip fracture risk: rheumatoid arthritis (5.3% vs 4.5%; RR 1.175, 95% CI 1.055-1.308; p = 0.003), type 1 diabetes (5.8% vs 4.3%; RR 1.342, 95% CI 1.170-1.540; p < 0.001), type 2 diabetes (4.5% vs 3.9%; RR 1.146, 95% CI 1.083-1.212; p < 0.001), and abnormal gait (6.6% vs 4.7%; RR 1.398, 95% CI 1.324-1.476; p < 0.001). Mortality was significantly elevated in all subgroups except abnormal gait. Conclusion: Lumbar fracture combined with high-risk comorbidities independent of osteoporosis confers an elevated risk of subsequent hip fracture and, in most subgroups, increased mortality.

## ID: AGS26/1370

## P0525: Prevalence, risk factors, and impact of back pain on physical function and psychosocial health in a multi-ethnic Asian population in Singapore: a cross-sectional study

Shilin Wang

1

, Jiong Hao Tan

1

, Shuna Khoo

2

, Reshma Merchant

3,4

, Lile Jia

2

, Li Feng Tan

5,6

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, National University Hospital, National University Health System, Singapore, Singapore

2

Department of Psychology, Faculty of Arts and Social Sciences, National University of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore

3

Division of Geriatric Medicine, National University Hospital, National University Health System, Singapore 119074, Singapore, Singapore, Singapore

4

Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore

5

Healthy Ageing Programme, Alexandra Hospital, National University Health System, Singapore, Singapore

6

Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Back pain is a major cause of disability worldwide. Current epidemiological studies have focused largely on Caucasian populations, and Asian ethnicities are under-represented. Ethnic, cultural and social disparities may significantly impact the efficacy of public health interventions. This study aims to characterise the prevalence of back pain across all ages in a multi-ethnic, Asian population, identify associated epidemiological factors, and examine its impact on physical function and psychosocial health. Materials and Methods: This is a prospective, population-based, cross-sectional study conducted in the format of a survey administered to a multi-ethnic residential cohort in a major housing estate of Singapore, conducted between September 2023 and May 2024. Demographic information was obtained from the Department of Statistics to determine a representative sample. All Singapore citizens/Permanent Residents aged 21 years or older were eligible to participate. Clinical, functional, mental health, and subjective quality-of-life measures were compared between participants with back pain and those without. Results: This study included 4274 participants. Age prevalence of back pain showed a bimodal distribution, with peaks in the age groups of 41-50 and > 90 years. Younger age, higher education levels, employment status, multimorbidity, smoking, and alcohol intake were factors associated with back pain (p < 0.05). Individuals with back pain were more likely to be physically inactive, impaired in their instrumental activities of daily living, and frail. Psychosomatic/depressive symptoms, social isolation, and lower quality of life were also more prevalent in individuals with back pain. Conclusion: Public health interventions for back pain should be tailored to specific cultural and demographic contexts, and focus on increasing outreach to older adults from less privileged socioeconomic strata, so as to maximise their scope and impact. A multidisciplinary approach should be adopted for the management of back pain.

## ID: AGS26/1371

## P0526: High rates of spin bias in systematic reviews regarding anterior cervical discectomy and fusion vs posterior cervical foraminotomy

Daniel Rusu

1

, Jadesola Olurin

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Niels Mohty

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Cervical degenerative disc disease often presents with debilitating neck pain and radiculopathy or myelopathy. Initial treatment is conservative, followed by surgical management for refractory symptoms. Options for surgical management include anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF - the gold standard) and posterior cervical foraminotomy (PCF); however, current literature remains conflicted over the superiority of one procedure. Systematic reviews and meta-analyses allow for comprehensive synthesis of literature but are susceptible to spin bias that may skew results and affect clinical decision making. This study aimed to evaluate the presence and number of spin bias in systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing ACDF and PCF. Material and Methods: A systematic search was conducted on PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, and Scopus for systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing ACDF and PCF in June 2025. Included articles were assessed by 2 independent reviewers for the 9 most severe types of spin bias established by Yavchitz et al. Articles were also graded on methodological quality using the AMSTAR-2 tool. Any disagreements were resolved through discussion. Associations between AMSTAR-2 rating, number of citations, citations per year, impact factor of publishing journal, reported funding, adherence to PRISMA guidelines, performance of a meta-analysis and total types of spin bias or presence/absence of spin were assessed using Poisson regression modeling and a Firth’s penalized likelihood regression model, respectively. Results: Twelve studies met inclusion criteria. Spin bias was found in 11 of 12 (92%) included articles, with number of different types of bias ranging from 0 to 2. Type 9 was the most common type of spin in 5 of 12 (42%) articles. Type 5 spin was found in 4 (33%) articles, Type 3 in 3 (25%) articles, Type 1 in 2 (16%) articles, and Types 2 and 4 in 1 (8%) article each.
