AMSTAR-2 quality assessments rated 7 of 12 (58%) articles as “critically low”, and 5 of 12 (42%) as “low”; none received a “high” quality rating. AMSTAR-2 rating, number of citations, citations per year, impact factor of publishing journal, reported funding, adherence to PRISMA guidelines, and performance of a meta-analysis were not able to significantly predict the quantity or presence of spin bias (all p > 0.05). Conclusion: Spin bias is highly prevalent (92%) in systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing ACDF and PCF, with a maximum of 2 types of spin per article. All studies received either “critically low” or “low” quality ratings according to AMSTAR-2 criteria, emphasizing the need for improving methodological quality in orthopaedic research comparing these interventions. Notably, all studies rated as “critically low” quality had at least 1 type of spin bias. Spine surgeons should exercise caution when reading systematic reviews comparing ACDF and PCF to inform clinical decision making, due to high prevalence of spin bias. Efforts and strategies to mitigate spin bias in orthopaedic literature are imperative to collectively enhance published literature.

## ID: AGS26/1378

## P0527: The national burden of vertebral body fractures in Germany: a population-based analysis of statutory health insurance claims (2015-2022)

Jonas Krueckel

1

, Dominik Szymski

1

, Franz Hilber

2

, Christian Heiss

3

, Volker Alt

1

, Siegmund Lang

1

, Markus Rupp

3

1

University Hospital Regensburg, Department of Trauma Surgery, Regensburg, Germany

2

Sporthopaedicum, Regensburg, Germany

3

Department of Trauma, Hand and Reconstructive Surgery, University Hospital Giessen, Giessen, Germany

Introduction: Vertebral body fractures impose a major clinical and socioeconomic burden worldwide. They arise after high energy trauma and increasingly from osteoporosis related fragility, with sequelae that include pain, kyphotic deformity, functional decline, and reduced quality of life. In Germany, demographic ageing is expected to increase their incidence, with implications for planning and resource allocation. Yet nationwide estimates across the full care continuum remain scarce. Hospital based reporting captures surgically treated cases, whereas many conservatively managed ambulatory cases are incompletely ascertained. We therefore analysed German statutory health insurance claims, assuming that almost every vertebral fracture generates at least one ambulatory contact and thus permits comprehensive case ascertainment. The aims were to provide a nationwide overview of vertebral fracture incidence from 2015 to 2022, to describe temporal trends by age and sex, and to characterise anatomical distribution across cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions. Material and Methods: Retrospective population based analysis of German outpatient claims from the Central Institute for Statutory Health Insuranc, January 2015 to December 2022. Incident vertebral fractures were identified by ICD-10-GM S12.0, S12.1, S12.2, S22.0, and S32.0. To limit duplicate events, identical diagnoses for the same individual within the four subsequent quarters after the index quarter were excluded. Annual incidence per 100,000 by age, sex, and anatomical region was calculated using population denominators from the Federal Statistical Office. Results: Across 2015-2022, vertebral body fracture counts rose significantly from 153,636 to 180,824 (+17.7%; overall IRR 1.14, 95% CI 1.136-1.151). By location, lumbar fractures predominated (2022: 107,776; 59.4%), thoracic fractures accounted for 65,006 (35.8%), and cervical fractures, including atlas and axis, for 8,729 (4.8%). Incidence increased in all regions and these rises were significant: cervical from 7.6 to 10.3 per 100,000 (+36.3%; IRR 1.36, 95% CI 1.319-1.408), thoracic from 66.5 to 77.1 per 100,000 (+15.9%; IRR 1.16, 95% CI 1.146-1.172), and lumbar from 114.1 to 127.8 per 100,000 (+12.0%; IRR 1.12, 95% CI 1.110-1.130). A modest pandemic related dip in 2020 was evident for thoracic and lumbar fractures with rebound thereafter; both dips were significant (thoracic 2020 vs 2019 IRR 0.973, 95% CI 0.962-0.984; lumbar 0.986, 95% CI 0.977-0.994), whereas cervical fractures increased steadily throughout. Overall incidence rose from 188.1 to 215.2 per 100,000 (+14.4%). Adults ≥ 65 years accounted for 71.0% of cases in 2015 and 72.6% in 2022. Female incidence exceeded male throughout, reaching 285.6 versus 142.6 per 100,000 in 2022. Conclusion: Germany’s vertebral body fracture burden increased substantially between 2015 and 2022, driven by ageing demographics and a persistently higher incidence in women, with lumbar and thoracic regions comprising the vast majority of cases.

## ID: AGS26/1387

## P0528: Assessment of the top authors in spine

Natalie Kistler

1

, Sarah Bergren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Chimere Ezuma

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Bibliometric analyses help define the intellectual architecture of spine research by quantifying publication trends, citation patterns, and authorship networks. Understanding the influence of individual contributors is particularly important in shaping evidence-based practice and guiding future inquiry. The present study aimed to conduct a focused bibliometric analysis of Spine to identify the most frequently cited authors to better understand the trajectory of academic influence in spine surgery and the evolving role of Spine as a cornerstone of the field. Material and Methods: The Web of Science (WoS) Core Collection database was queried for all articles and reviews published in Spine from January 1, 1976, to December 31, 2024. The number of citations for each author published in Spine was determined. The top 20 most cited authors in (1) first author position, (2) any author position, and (3) last author position were identified and listed separately. Citation number was confirmed for each author through a manual search of the author’s name in the WoS database. Per previously reported protocol, the MEDLINE database was utilized to find the number of published articles in Spine associated with each author’s name. WoS was used to confirm each author’s total publications in Spine. Relative citation index (RCI), a metric used to evaluate how an author's citation count compares to others in the field, was calculated by dividing each author’s total citations by the number of articles published in Spine. Level of training and current institution were determined for each author. Results: The WoS search yielded 17,610 publications. The author group with the highest average number of citations was the authors in any authorship position cohort (5,207), followed by authors in last position (3,708), and authors in first position (3,438). Similarly, the top 20 authors in any authorship position demonstrated the highest RCI, with 1,265 citations per publication (range, 137-4,559). Alternatively, the top 20 authors in first position had the highest average number of articles published in Spine, at 74, followed by authors in first author position, 54, and authors in any authorship position, 13. The top ranked first author, D.E. Beaton, had only two first author publications in Spine, yet achieved the highest citation count amongst all authors. Seven authors were ranked as both a top 20 first author and a top cited author in any authorship position. Further, six of the top cited last authors were a top cited author in first position or any authorship position. Conclusion: As a highly regarded journal in the field of orthopaedics and spine research, Spine’s top authors have significantly influenced spine research and its clinical applications. The average number of articles published for the top last authors was close to one and a half times what it was for first authors which may reflect last authors’ ability to be involved in a greater number of projects. Our findings demonstrated a core group of researchers, highlighting the need for continued vigilance regarding repeat authorship based on citation metrics and visibility.

## ID: AGS26/1507

## P0529: Top cited articles in Global Spine Journal

Sarah Bergren

1

, Hannah Shelby

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Founded in 2011, Global Spine Journal (GSJ) has quickly established itself as an influential multidisciplinary journal covering a wide range of surgical, nonsurgical and emerging topics in spine surgery. The journal’s impact factor has been steadily increasing over time with a current impact factor of 3.0, ranking in the first quartile of orthopaedic research. GSJ is AO Spine International’s official journal, highlighting its worldwide reach and role in shaping macroscopic trends in spine research. Because of its broad visibility and growing popularity, highly cited articles and their authors published within GSJ drive international scientific discussion and investigation within this field. In this study, we examined the most cited articles within GSJ to better understand the journal's current standing as well as the state and evolving priorities of spine research worldwide. Material and Methods: Web of Science (WoS) Core Collection was searched for all articles and reviews published in GSJ from 2011 to 2025. The top 20 articles by citation count were identified and exported for further analysis. Title, authors, citation count, citation count per year since publication, article type, topic and level of evidence (LOE) were collected for each top article. LOE was determined following the Oxford 2011 Levels of Evidence guidelines. Results: The WOS search identified 2,324 publications. The average number of citations was 204 citations (range: 112-434). The average number of citations per year was 28 (range: 12-104). The majority of publications were literature reviews (55%), followed by systematic reviews/meta-analyses (30%). Most articles (60%) were LOE IV and V. There were a variety of topics covered across the top articles with cord compression/decompression (15%) and surgical complications (15%) being the most common. Several authors appeared multiple times within the top cited articles. Dr. Michael G. Fehlings was first author on three of the top articles and Dr. Keith L. Jackson was first author on two top articles. Conclusion: The young nature of GSJ is reflected in the relatively lower citation count of its top articles. Further, many of the top articles were literature reviews, a study type considered to be of lower quality. This is reflected in the majority of articles having a lower level of evidence (IV and V). Despite this, a considerable portion of the top articles were systematic reviews/meta-analyses, studies type considered to be of higher quality. The combination of having a majority of literature reviews and systematic reviews/meta-analyses reflects the maturation of GSJ and its growing influence. Notably, Dr. Micheal G. Fehling, the most frequent first author, focuses on spinal cord injuries, aligning with the prominence of spinal cord compression/decompression related topics. Similarly, Dr. Keith L. Jackson’s work on patient risk factors underscores the multidisciplinary nature of GSJ . Examining the most cited articles within GSJ highlights its developing nature as it continues to publish higher impact studies and advance the understanding of key clinical challenges, such as spinal cord compression, related surgeries and surgical complications in general.

## ID: AGS26/1802

## P0530: Most impactful articles in Spine: comparing past and current research

Sarah Bergren

1

, Hannah Shelby

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction:
Spine is one of the top journals in spine research and therefore Spine’s most cited publications demonstrate this field’s current landscape and future directions. However, when looking at citation count as a proxy for influence, older articles are overemphasized while recent articles are underrepresented due to limited time for citation accumulation. In order to provide a more balanced perspective on both emerging trends and historical impact, this study aimed to identify the top articles since Spine’s inception and compare them to the top articles in the last 10 years. Material and Methods: All publications in Spine from 1976 to 2024 were identified using the Web of Sciences (WoS) Core Collection. Based on citation count, the most cited articles were separated into top 20 cited articles from 1976 to 2024 and top 20 cited articles from 2014 to 2024, to represent the most recent complete 10-year period. For each article, title, authors’ names, publication date, citation count, article type, subject, and level of evidence (LoE) were collected. LoE was assigned based on the stated LOE within the abstract. If no LOE was listed, the Oxford 2011 Levels of Evidence were used to assign a level. Article type, subject and LoE were verified by two independent reviewers. Results: The WOS search yielded 17,610 publications. Among the top articles from 1976 to 2024 and 2014 to 2024, the average number of citations was 2,240 and 308 respectively, while the average number of citations per year was 90 and 43, respectively. For top articles from 1976 to 2024, the most common article types were methodological studies (30%) and case studies (25%), the most common subjects were back pain (25%), disability (15%), and surgical procedures (15%) and the most common LoE were levels V (45%) and IV (30%). For top articles from 2014 to 2024, the most common article types were cohort studies (30%) and database studies (20%), the most common subject was surgical procedures (35%) and adult spinal deformity (20%) and the most common LoE was level III (60%). Conclusion: Surgical procedures have become a greater focus in recent years, and Spine appears to be publishing studies with overall higher LOE. The top articles from 1976 to 2024 reflected a broad exploration into back pain, while recent publications have shifted toward understanding different etiologies of back pain such as adult spinal deformity. Further, studies utilizing databases have become increasingly common, allowing for larger datasets, which is reflected in a higher LoE. None of the overall top cited articles within Spine were published in the last 15 years, indicating these articles do not provide an accurate representation of the current direction of spine surgery and the challenges current patients and providers face in clinical practice. Looking at the most cited articles across multiple time points in Spine, a top ranked and influential journal, allows for a broad understanding of both the foundations and the trajectory of spine research.

## ID: AGS26/1822

## P0531: Most prolific articles in Spine comparing past and current research

Hannah Shelby

1

, Sarah Bergren

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction:
Spine is one of the most influential journals and leading platforms contributing to the advancement of spine surgery. Its publications are largely responsible for curating the academic and surgical field that exists today. This study aims to identify the most prolific authors in this journal. Furthermore, this study aims to analyze patterns of authorship characteristics to better understand the individuals largely responsible for this journal’s impact. Material and Methods: This study searched Spine for all articles and reviews, excluding letters, editorials and corrections. Authors were sorted by first, last and any position; solo authors were excluded from first and last iterations. In order to further analyze authorship, this study utilized the Shannon diversity index and the Gini coefficient. The Shannon diversity index was applied to authorship positions (first, middle, last) to assess the balance of roles among contributors. Values were normalized on 0-1 scale, 0 indicating an author’s contributions were limited to a single position and 1 indicating equal distribution across all three positions. The Gini coefficient was calculated from the Lorenz curve of cumulative authorship contributions, ranging from 0 (perfectly equal distribution) to 1 (all output concentrated in a single position), thereby quantifying skew to a certain authorship position. Additional authorship characteristics were collected, including total citation count at the corresponding authorship position, gender, academic degree, H-index as a measure of scholarly impact, institutional affiliation, country of origin, and keywords of associated publications. Results: 17,396 publications were identified. Considering publications in any author position, the most prolific author was Lawrence Lenke (304 publications, H-index 105, 27,773 citations), followed by Alexander Vaccaro (248 publications, H-index 85, 11,169 citations) and Keith Bridwell (223 publications, H-index 102, 30,524 citations). Shannon diversity index ranged from 0.176 to 0.577, while Gini coefficients ranged from 0.17 to 0.435, indicating heterogeneity in authorship role distribution. Limiting the search to most prolific authors with first-author publications, Peter Passias (38 publications, H-index 41), Manohar Panjabi (37 publications, H-index 82), and Alexander Vaccaro (36 publications, H-index 85) were the most prolific. These authors demonstrated higher Shannon diversity index (0.522-0.650) and lower Gini coefficients (0.07-0.27). For last author publications, Jonathan Grauer (69 publications, H-index 59), James Weinstein (64 publications, H-index 77), and Kern Singh (62 publications, H-index 40) were the leading contributors. Last author leaders generally demonstrated lower Shannon diversity index (0.295-0.454) and higher Gini coefficients (up to 0.39). Additionally, top authors in all categories had “complications” as one of their top 5 keywords. Conclusion: This study highlights those shaping the field of orthopaedics and who are responsible for some of its most impactful advancements. Gaining a better understanding of Spine’s authorship community and its characteristics provides insight into the distribution of academic influence and the roles these individuals play as collaborators and leaders within the scholarly community.

## ID: AGS26/1838

## P0532: Demographic evolution of spinal fractures in a workers compensation hospital: a comparative analysis between 2015 and 2025

Juan José Zamorano

1

, Esteban Peña

1

, José Gengler Prieto

1

, Hugo Zanetta

1

, Iris Castillo

1

, Ignacio Farías

1

, Ignacio Cirillo

1

, Carlos Tapia

1

, Marcos Gimbernat

1

1

Hospital del Trabajador, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Spinal fractures (SF) represent a significant public health issue globally, with a notable increase in incidence, particularly among the elderly. Although age-standardized rates have decreased, the overall burden of SF continues to rise, driven by an aging population. This study aims to analyze how the demographics of patients with SF have changed in a workers' compensation hospital between 2015 and 2025, highlighting variations in patient profiles and the mechanisms of injury involved. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of medical records of patients with SF treated in a workers' compensation hospital was conducted. Data were collected from 90 patients in 2015 and 102 in 2025. Demographic variables such as age, sex, and type of work-related accident were examined. Average age and sex distribution were calculated and compared between the two years. Descriptive analysis was employed to evaluate the observed trends. Results: A total of 90 patients were recorded in 2015 and 102 in 2025 with diagnoses of SF. The average age of patients in 2015 was 46.01 years, increasing to 48.46 years in 2025. Regarding gender, 16.6% (15) of patients in 2015 were female, compared to 24.5% (25) in 2025. Furthermore, 45.6% (41) of cases in 2015 were due to commuting accidents, whereas this figure was 41.2% (42) in 2025. These data suggest that the patient profile is becoming older and that female representation is increasing. Conclusion: The analysis of these data indicates a significant demographic evolution among patients with SF in a workers' compensation hospital. An increase in average age and a higher incidence of injuries in women are observed, implying a shift in the demographics of this working-age population. Recognizing these trends is crucial for developing public health policies and intervention programs tailored to the specific needs of these patients, especially in a context where the population is aging. These changes also raise new questions regarding the current situation of working-age patients: Is the total number of SF increasing? Is there a higher proportion of cases caused by low-energy mechanisms due to workforce aging? Is there a decline in bone density among active workers? Future research should focus on better understanding the mechanisms of injury and implementing prevention programs aimed at the most vulnerable populations.

## ID: AGS26/1842

## P0533: Race and insurance disparities in discharge disposition following cervical spine trauma

William Lambert

1

, Thomas Pieters

1

, Benjamin George

2

1

University of Massachusetts, Worcester, United States

2

University of Rochester Medical Center, Rochester, United States

Introduction: Previous evidence describes racial and socioeconomic disparities in the treatment and outcomes of patients who have suffered cervical spine trauma. Although acute care and surgical intervention for cervical spine trauma are important, the ability of a patient to obtain sufficient rehabilitation following discharge from acute care is crucial in optimizing functional outcomes. The goal of this study is to investigate racial and socioeconomic disparities in the disposition of patients with cervical spine trauma. Material and Methods: We surveyed the National Inpatient Sample database (NIS) from 2016-2020 to identify all patients with a diagnosis of cervical spine trauma. Data were analyzed using standard descriptive methods and multinomial logistic regression to assess race- and insurance-based differences in discharge disposition after cervical spine trauma, controlling for patient factors. Patients with cervical spine trauma aged 18-64 years with complete data on race and insurance status, discharged alive from inpatient care, without significant injuries to other organ systems were eligible for study. Results: Of 3,530 patients who met eligibility criteria for the study 60% were White, 23% Black, 11% Hispanic, 1% Asian and 5% Other. Most patients had insurance, and highest levels of uninsured patients were seen in Hispanic patients (23%), Black patients (22%) and Other patient (21%). Compared to patients with government insurance, privately insured patients were more likely to be discharged to facility than home (RRR 1.2; 95% CI 1.03 - 1.45) and uninsured patients were less likely to be discharged to facility than home (RRR 0.55; 95% CI 0.45 - 0.67). When compared to White patients, there were no statistically significant differences in disposition home vs facility for any other race/ethnic group. Conclusion: Insurance status serves as a strong predictor of discharge to facility following hospitalization for patients with cervical spine trauma. Although prior literature suggests that there are racial disparities in discharge disposition following traumatic injuries to various other organ systems, our study does not recapitulate these results in this subset of patients with cervical spine trauma.

## ID: AGS26/2377

## P0534: A scoping review: knowledge translation interventions, contextual factors, and their impact on surgical practice change

Elizabeth Manafo

1

, Elyette Lugo

2

, Amit Jain

2

, Lisa Petermann

1

, Benjamin Davies

3

, Olesja Hazenbiller

4

, Janneke Loomans

4

, Charles Fisher

5

1

EXEP Consulting Inc, Calgary, Canada

2

John Hopkins School of Medicine, Baltimore, United States

3

University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom

4

AO Foundation, Davos, Switzerland

5

Department of Orthopaedics, Division of Spine, University of British Columbia and Vancouver General Hospital, Vancouver, Canada

Introduction: Knowledge translation (KT) interventions are essential for implementing evidence-based practices in healthcare However, despite their proven effectiveness in addressing global health challenges, KT strategies in surgery remain challenging to apply. This scoping review examines KT strategies in surgery, their effectiveness, and key barriers and enablers to their implementation. Material and Methods: This scoping review followed the Arksey and O’Malley and Levac et al. frameworks, integrating the RE-AIM (Reach, Effectiveness, Adoption, Implementation, Maintenance) and the PRISM (Practical Robust Implementation Sustainability Model) models to evaluate the effectiveness of knowledge translation interventions in surgical practice change and associated contextual barriers and facilitators. A systematic search was conducted across MEDLINE (PubMed, OVID), CINAHL (EBSCO), and PsycINFO (ProQuest). Articles were screened using predefined selection criteria, emphasizing experimental and quasi-experimental studies. Data extraction categorized KT interventions: knowledge diffusion, dissemination, and implementation approaches with regional specific adaptation. Results: A total of 34 studies met the inclusion criteria. Most were hospital-based (88%) and focused on guideline adherence. The review identified three primary KT strategies: i) educational materials and educational outreach, ii) reminders and prompts, and iii) audit and feedback systems. The most effective KT strategies used a combination of these interventions to maximize impact. Barriers included physician resistance, limited leadership support, financial constraints, and workflow disruptions, while enablers included institutional leadership, structured training programs, financial incentives, and interdisciplinary collaboration. A notable finding was the lack of standardized validation processes for adopting changes in the surgical setting, which often burdens individual surgeons and their institutions, thereby constraining both capacity and motivation for practice change. Conclusion: The integrated application of RE-AIM and PRISM frameworks proved valuable in assessing the interventions' sustainability and real-world effectiveness. Findings suggest that layered, interdisciplinary KT strategies are the most effective for driving surgical practice change and overcoming institutional barriers. This comprehensive analysis contributes to the growing body of knowledge on effective implementation strategies in surgical settings and provides a foundation for future practice improvement initiatives. Future research should focus on refining KT methodologies, expanding implementation frameworks, and addressing barriers to sustainability across diverse surgical settings.

## ID: AGS26/2388

## P0535: The prevalence of cervical ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL) in the Māori and Non-Māori population in the Bay of Plenty, New Zealand

Tiffany Huang

1

1

Orthopaedics Department, Rotorua Hospital, Rotorua, New Zealand

Introduction: The primary objective of this study is to determine the prevalence of cervical ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL) among Māori compared to non-Māori populations in the Bay of Plenty (BOP) region of New Zealand. Material and Methods: Cervical spine and trauma computed tomography (CT) scans performed between 2021 and 2025 in the Bay of Plenty, New Zealand were retrospectively reviewed for evidence of cervical OPLL. Patients aged ≥40 years with available ethnicity data and adequate imaging were included. Cases with a prior diagnosis of OPLL were excluded to reduce selection bias. Results: A total of 423 CT scans were from the BOP region, comprising 224 Māori and 199 non-Māori patients. Cervical OPLL was identified in 15 Māori patients, with one case excluded due to prior diagnosis. This yielded a statistically significant OPLL prevalence of 6.4% (14/223, p = 0.0133) among Māori in BOP. Māori in BOP were 6.6 times more likely to have cervical OPLL compared to non-Māori [OR 6.60, 95% CI 1.48-29.4]. In the non-Māori BOP cohort, two cases of cervical OPLL were identified, resulting in a prevalence of 1.0% (2/199). Conclusion: This study demonstrates a significantly higher prevalence of cervical OPLL in the Māori population of the Bay of Plenty compared to non-Māori.

## ID: AGS26/2432

## P0536: Vertebral artery injury in cervical spine fracture dislocation: our observation in Indian Spinal Injuries center

Ziaul Hasan

1

, Shah Alam

2

, Iftekharul Shibli

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedic Rehabilitation, NITOR, Dhaka, Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital. BSOH, Dhaka, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Incidence of traumatic vertebral artery injury (VAI) associated with cervical spine trauma varies widely in published trauma series. The purpose of this study was to determine the frequency of traumatic vertebral artery injury in patients with cervical spine trauma by using routine Magnetic Resonance (MR) imaging and identify associations with Age, Sex, Mechanism of injury, level of injury and severity of neurologic injury. Vertebral Artery Injury is mostly asymptomatic but serious complication like Cerebro vascular event or even death can occur when it occurs in dominant side or bilateral. Material and Methods: A retrospective review of 96 patients with cervical spine fracture dislocation with or without an associated spinal cord injury (SCI) who were admitted in Indian Spinal Injuries Center in last 10 years was performed. Cervical MR imaging was used to diagnose vertebral artery injury. Patients age, sex, cervical injury level, mechanism of injury, neurologic level of injury, and American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) grade and clinical sequel of vertebral artery injury were recorded. Results: In this study, 96 patients met the inclusion criteria, 18 (18%) of whom had VAI on the admission MR imaging. All of VAI patients had an associated SCI. VAI was significantly more common in motor-complete patients (ASIA A 61% and B 22%) where no VAT in neurologically intact cervical spine injured patients. VAI incidence was significantly high in Flexion distraction type of injury (12, 66%). Most common level associated with VAI was C4/5 and C6/7 (22%). 27% of VAI was associated with Foraminal fracture, 72% of VAI was associated with Facet dislocation among the 44% bi-facetal and 28% uni facetal dislocation. Conclusion: Incidence of traumatic vertebral artery is not very Uncommon and need careful and meticulous screening and management. Otherwise, complications can happen. Mostly it is associated with high velocity injury and higher neurologically injure patient. MRI can be used as a good screening tool which can be confirmed by CT angiogram or Digital subtraction angiography (DSA). Proper pre operative evaluation of vascular injury in cervical spine fracture dislocation is very much important for patient management as well as patient counselling.

## ID: AGS26/2452

## P0537: Preoperative bone health optimization in lumbar fusion surgery: a missed opportunity

Henry Avetisian

1

, William Karakash

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Many older adults undergoing lumbar fusion have underlying osteoporosis, a modifiable risk factor for mechanical complications and reoperation. Preoperative management with anabolic or antiresorptive medications can mitigate these risks, yet there is limited research on osteoporosis screening and treatment in this population. This study examined trends in the proportion of patients meeting osteoporosis screening criteria, undergoing preoperative dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scans, and receiving preoperative medical management for osteoporosis. Material and Methods: The PearlDiver Mariner database was queried for patients undergoing one- to five-level lumbar fusion between 2012 and 2022. Patients under 18 years old, those with less than two years of preoperative follow-up, or indications related to trauma, malignancy, or infection were excluded. DEXA screening eligibility was determined using the International Society for Clinical Densitometry (ISCD) guidelines: females ≥ 65, males ≥ 70, or younger individuals with one or more established risk factors for osteoporosis. Temporal trends were assessed by comparing data from two periods: 2012-2016 and 2017-2022. Results: Among 460,334 patients undergoing lumbar fusion, those meeting osteoporosis screening criteria increased from 61.4% (2012-2016) to 70.1% (2017-2022) (p < 0.001). Despite this increase, preoperative DEXA utilization in the two years before surgery remained low, rising only marginally from 12.36% to 13.09% (p < 0.001). Among patients under the age threshold for screening, 45.58% met criteria due to other risk factors. These included tobacco use (27.77%), complicated diabetes (9.76%), bone-affecting medical conditions (9.43%), and high-risk medication use (8.52%). Of those meeting screening criteria, women were more likely than men to undergo preoperative DEXA. (18.60% vs. 3.58%, p < 0.001). The prevalence of diagnosed osteoporosis increased from 9.31% to 12.29% (p < 0.001), but rates of preoperative medical management declined slightly from 13.85% to 12.70% (p < 0.001). Conclusion: Although awareness of osteoporosis’s impact on surgical outcomes has increased, significant gaps in screening and management remain among lumbar fusion patients. As the population ages, optimizing preoperative bone health is critical. It is important for spine surgeons to recognize the high prevalence of undiagnosed and untreated osteoporosis in this population and prioritize standardized protocols for screening and intervention to improve surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2663

## P0538: Prevalence of vertebral compression fractures associated with bone fragility secondary to osteoporosis: a decade-long study in a tertiary care center

Marcos Felipe Contreras Zuñiga

1

, A. González Moga

1

, M. Fuentes Rivera

1

, JC Verde Becerra

1,2

, H. Santos Benítez

1

, I. Santos Uc

1

, J. Trejo Miranda

1

, R. Vázquez

1

1

ISSEMYM Medical Center, Ecatepec, Mexico, Mexico

2

Spine Surgery, Ruber Hospital, Madrid, Spain

Introduction: Vertebral compression fractures (VCFs) are the most common osteoporotic fractures and constitute a major clinical and public health challenge worldwide. (3) Data from the World Health Organization (WHO) indicate that 500 million people suffer from this disease; as a result, nine million fragility fractures occur annually. It is estimated that osteoporosis affects 10 million people in Mexico; a fragility fracture occurs every three seconds, and the risk of a second fracture increases by 86 percent. (4) It is estimated that 70 percent of patients with spinal fractures in Mexico do not require hospital treatment, due, among other reasons, to the fact that recovery is possible with outpatient medical treatment. (4). Objective: To determine the prevalence of vertebral compression fractures associated with bone fragility in patients treated at a tertiary referral medical center. Material and methods: An observational, descriptive, analytical, retrospective and cross-sectional study was carried out over a period of 10 years, with a total sample of 736 patients with osteoporosis, of which 174 patients met the inclusion criteria. The Chi square (X2) test was used to establish the dependence between polytomous categorical variables. The Student t test was used to compare numerical variables between groups. A p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. In the case of dichotomous variables, the Odds Ratio (OR) was calculated with its corresponding 95% confidence interval (95% CI) as a measure of risk association. Results: 174 patients presented vertebral compression fractures associated with bone fragility, representing a prevalence of 23.6%. The patients were treated between February 2015 and July 2025, highlighting the high clinical burden of this type of fracture in this population. Discussion: In this study, we found that almost one in four patients with osteoporosis had a vertebral compression fracture associated with bone fragility, equivalent to a prevalence of 23.6%. This finding is consistent with what has been reported in other international studies, where these fractures affect between 20% and 25% of patients with osteoporosis, especially in older adults. Conclusion: The findings of this study can serve as a basis for future prospective studies evaluating the effectiveness of prevention strategies in high-risk populations, as well as for research exploring the relationship between bone mineral density and fracture recurrence in older adults. The results of this study lead to the conclusion that vertebral compression fractures associated with bone fragility represent a common clinical problem in patients with osteoporosis, with a prevalence of 23.6% in the population treated at our tertiary center.

## ID: AGS26/2724

## P0539: First experiences with the implementation of a fast-track concept in spine surgery

Bastian Stemmer

1

1

Departement of Neurosurgery, Universityhospital Augsburg, Augsburg, Germany

Introduction: ERAS (Enhanced Recovery After Surgery) concepts were established in the 1990s, particularly in abdominal surgery. Such programs have only been discovered in spinal surgery in recent years. The goal of the Fast Track approach is to accelerate patient recovery by reducing surgical stress and postoperative pain. This is achieved through preoperative patient optimization and training, multimodal anesthesia, and a reduction in surgical trauma and rapid mobilization. The rapid recovery of patients also leads to faster discharge from inpatient care, which also benefits our healthcare system. Material and Methods: A FastTrack concept for spinal surgery is currently being established at our hospital. Initially, all patients undergoing surgery via a dorsal approach to the thoracic and lumbar spine (decompression and fusion surgeries) will be included. Patients included in this program are screened preoperatively for anemia and nutritional status, are educated on the postoperative surgical procedure, and receive anesthesiological and analgesic treatment using a multimodal treatment pathway. Patients are mobilized on the day of surgery as part of a coordinated physiotherapy program. A key focus is also the ongoing motivation and support of patients by a FastTrack assistant. To capture initial changes resulting from the implementation of the FastTrack program, 18 patients before the implementation of the program were compared with the first 18 patients treated using the FastTrack method. For this purpose, the postoperative length of hospital stay and postoperative pain levels were comparatively evaluated. All data were collected prospectively. Results: Eighteen patients each were included in the FastTrack and Non-FastTrack groups (44% male, 56% female vs. 61% male, 39% female). The median age in the Non-FastTrack group was 67.5 years (vs. 73 years), and the median BMI in this group was 30.1 (vs. 31.7). The postoperative median length of hospital stay was 10 days in the Non-FastTrack group (mean 14.28 days) and 4 days in the FastTrack group (mean 5 days). The median VAS in the non-FastTrack group was 6.5 on surgery day, 5 on the first post-operative day, 5 on the second post-operative day, and 4 on the third post-operative day (vs. FastTrack group: surgery day 5.5; 1 post-operative day 4.0; 2 post-operative day 3.0; 3 post-operative day 2.5). Conclusion: In summary, a significant reduction in postoperative hospital stay and postoperative pain reduction could be demonstrated within the patient cohort through a FastTrack concept in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2741

## P0540: Predisposing factors for pseudarthrosis in spinal fusion: analysis of 107 patients

Mustafa Kavcar

1

, Semih Bal

1

, Çağrı Elbir

1

1

Etlik City Hospital Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Pseudarthrosis remains a major late complication after spinal fusion surgery, often resulting in persistent pain, functional limitation, and the need for revision procedures. While surgical technique and implant selection are important, patient-related systemic comorbidities also play a critical role in bone healing and fusion outcomes. The aim of this study was to analyze predisposing factors for pseudarthrosis in a cohort of 107 patients, with particular emphasis on diabetes mellitus, hypertension, osteoporosis, and rheumatologic diseases. Materials and Methods: This retrospective study included 107 patients diagnosed with pseudarthrosis after spinal fusion surgery between November 2022 and September 2025. Diagnosis was based on clinical symptoms and confirmed radiologically using dynamic radiographs, computed tomography (CT), and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Demographic variables (age, sex, body mass index), surgical characteristics (fusion length, use of instrumentation, graft type), and systemic comorbidities were recorded. Patients with diabetes, hypertension, osteoporosis, and rheumatologic disorders were analyzed separately. Statistical analysis included chi-square and logistic regression to evaluate associations between comorbidities and pseudarthrosis outcomes. Results: Of the 107 patients, 62 were female (57.9%) and 45 male (42.1%), with a mean age of 61.4 years (range: 38-79). Diabetes mellitus was present in 34 patients (31.7%), and these patients demonstrated significantly lower fusion rates compared with non-diabetics (p = 0.018). Hypertension was identified in 50 patients (46.7%), and although it did not emerge as an independent predictor of pseudarthrosis, the coexistence of hypertension with diabetes markedly increased the risk of non-union (p = 0.037). Osteoporosis was diagnosed in 31 patients (28.9%), and this condition was strongly associated with higher pseudarthrosis rates, particularly in those undergoing long-segment fusion (p = 0.012). Rheumatologic disease was found in 16 patients (14.9%), and these individuals required revision surgery more frequently than those without rheumatologic disease (62% vs. 34%, p = 0.041). Multivariate regression confirmed diabetes and osteoporosis as independent predictors of pseudarthrosis, while rheumatologic disease showed a strong association with the need for revision surgery. Conclusion: This study demonstrates that systemic comorbidities substantially influence fusion success in spinal surgery. Diabetes and osteoporosis emerged as the strongest independent risk factors for pseudarthrosis, while rheumatologic disease significantly increased the likelihood of revision surgery. Although hypertension alone was not an independent risk factor, its coexistence with diabetes amplified the risk of non-union. Careful preoperative optimization and individualized surgical planning are essential to minimize complications in high-risk patients.

## ID: AGS26/2845

## P0541: A serial cross-sectional analysis of patient demographics in image-guided spinal fusion surgery

Connor Burke

1

, Matthew Blackwell

2

, Andrew Brumett

2

, Anoop Chinthala

2

, Arshjeet Singh

2

, Raj Lavadi

2

, Gordon Mao

2

1

Shanghai Medical College of Fudan University, Shanghai, China

2

Indiana University School of Medicine, Indianapolis, United States

Introduction: Intraoperative imaging systems in spinal fusion surgery allow surgeons greater accuracy in pedicle screw placement. Despite the benefits that may exist, adoption of a new technology is rarely equal across demographic groups. Analysis of past surgeries can allow for disparities in uptake of this surgical technique to be identified. This analysis can guide future decisions on how and where to implement this technology. The present study aims to retrospectively identify trends in race, age group, ZIP code income quartile, payer type, and urbanicity of patients that underwent image-guided spinal fusion surgery. Material and Methods: Using the National Inpatient Sample (NIS), a serial, cross-sectional analysis of spinal fusion cases from 2010 to 2021 was conducted, considering race, age group, ZIP code income quartile, payer type, and urbanicity to assess patterns across periods. The significance and magnitude of demographic shifts were determined using survey-weighted Poisson regression, difference-in-differences (DiD) risk ratios, and Cohen’s h. Results: Relative to free-hand, image-guided spinal fusion case share declined in central metropolitan areas (DiD RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.85-0.95, p < 0.001) with increased uptake in micropolitan counties (RR 1.09, 1.00-1.18, p = 0.049). Payer mix had less decline among private insurance payers (RR 1.04, 1.01-1.08, p = 0.013), increased growth among Medicare payers (RR 1.03, 1.00-1.06, p = 0.045), and decline in self-pay cases (RR 0.69, 0.54-0.88, p = 0.003). The highest income quartile saw decreases (RR 0.94, 0.89-0.99, p = 0.018) while the middle-upper quartile saw increases (RR 1.10, 1.04-1.17, p < 0.001). Patients 65 and over experienced slightly lower growth rates (RR 0.97, 0.94-1.00, p = 0.038). Native American patients displayed growth at substantially lower rates (RR 0.30, 0.21-0.43, p < 0.001). Conclusion: From 2010 to 2021, image-guided spinal fusion surgery shifted in demographic composition - use became less concentrated in urban, wealthy, and privately funded patients and diffused across more diverse geographies and financial contexts. Despite this trend, future expansion efforts in image-guided spinal fusion surgery should ensure that Native American patients are adequately supported.

## ID: AGS26/2881

## P0542: Prevalence and patterns of multi-system comorbidities among non-vertebral deformity scoliosis: a cohort study of 1,826 patients

Xinyu Yang

1,2,3

, Jianle Yang

1,2,3

, Terry Zhang

1,2,3

, Nan Wu

1,2,3

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, Peking Union Medical College and Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, China

2

Key Laboratory of Big Data for Skeletal Deformities, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, China

3

Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Innovation and Application for Skeletal Health Medical Care, Beijing, China

Introduction: This study proposes a novel clinical entity designated as non-vertebral deformity scoliosis (NVDS), characterized by scoliosis independent of vertebral malformations. NVDS demonstrates early-stage radiological homogeneity, thus leading to diagnostic oversimplification as scoliosis alone, potentially neglecting clinically impactful comorbidities. A comprehensive understanding of NVDS-associated comorbidities is crucial for precise diagnosis, treatment decision making, and quality of care assessment. The purpose of this study was to investigates the epidemiological patterns, healthcare burden, and pathophysiological interrelationships of comorbidities related to NVDS. Material and Methods: NVDS was defined as a composite diagnostic category encompassing idiopathic, neuromuscular, and syndromic scoliosis without vertebral malformations, with enrollment restricted to patients aged <50 years. A retrospective analysis was conducted to review the standard demographic information, radiographic data, and clinical records of 1,826 patients with NVDS from our Hospital. A multigroup comparative analysis was performed to evaluate the healthcare burden of the comorbidities. Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses were performed to identify the risk factors for various comorbidities and elucidate the interrelationships among different physiological systems. Results: Comorbidities were identified in 52.8% of patients with NVDS, with predominant involvement of the musculoskeletal (24.42%), cardiovascular (23.99%), and neurological (15.72%) systems. Rib anomalies (11.06%), vertebral number anomalies (8.11%), syringomyelia (5.70%), and neurofibromas (5.20%) were the most frequent comorbidities. Patients with comorbidities demonstrated a larger Cobb angle (p < 0.05) and longer hospitalization duration (p < 0.05) than patients without comorbidities. Female sex (p < 0.01) and large Cobb angles (p < 0.01) were independent risk factors for musculoskeletal and neurological comorbidities in NVDS, whereas reduced BMI (p = 0.002) was a key risk factor for cardiovascular comorbidities. Notably, NVDS patients with musculoskeletal comorbidities had an increased risk of respiratory comorbidities (p < 0.001). Conclusion: Our findings reveal that NVDS represents a complex multi-system disorder rather than an isolated spinal condition, with more than half of the patients exhibiting clinically significant comorbidities. The identified comorbidity predictors, including sex dimorphism, BMI, Cobb angle severity, and pre-existing system involvement, provide a prognostic framework for clinical management. These results underscore the need for a comprehensive multi-system evaluation in patients with NVDS to enable early comorbidity detection and personalized therapeutic interventions.

## ID: AGS26/2948

## P0543: Halo effect of prescribing guidelines for opioid naïve patients translates to opioid non-naïve patients undergoing lumbar decompression: a Michigan Spine Surgery Improvement Collaborative (MSSIC) Study

Anisse Chaker

1

, Geoff Kahn

1

, Michael Melhem

1

, Jian-hui Hu

2

, Kari Jarabek

1

, Heegook Yeo

1

, Jawad Saad

1

, Abdullah Al-Tekreeti

1

, Kenneth Easton

4

, Jad Khalil

3

, Richard Easton

5

, Doris Tong

6

, Miguelangelo Perez-Cruet

7

, Noojan Kazemi

8

, Ilyas Aleem

8

, Muwaffak Abulhak

1

, David Nerenz

2

, Victor Chang

1

1

Henry Ford Hospital - Department of Neurosurgery, Detroit, United States

2

Henry Ford Health - Center for Health Policy & Health Services Research, Detroit, United States

3

Corewell Health William Beaumont University Hospital, Royal Oak, United States

4

University of Michigan Health - West, Wyoming, United States

5

Corewell Health Beaumont Troy Hospital, Troy, United States

6

Michigan State University - Ascension Providence Hospital, Southfield, United States

7

Corewell Health William Beaumont University Hospital - Department of Neurosurgery, Royal Oak, United States

8

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Chronic opioid dependency in spine surgery is an ongoing concern given the ongoing opioid epidemic. We have previously reported on our cohort of opioid-naïve patients, however the literature has shown that patients with opioid use at baseline are at higher risk of less favorable outcomes. Starting in 2022, MSSIC instituted state-wide prescribing guidelines for opioid naïve patients undergoing one- or two-level lumbar decompression surgery (laminectomy, laminotomy, foraminotomy, microdiscectomy) with performance goals of 80% compliance. The goal of this study was to evaluate whether there would be a form of a “halo” effect on prescribing patterns for patients with baseline opioid use, who were not subject to the performance measure. Our hypothesis was that the change in prescribing patterns for the opioid naïve population would also translate to the non-naïve population as well. Material and Methods: The MSSIC database was queried from January 2022 to December 2024 for patients across 29 hospitals and ASCs who underwent elective lumbar decompression surgery and had a pre-operative opioid use history. Patients were grouped based on compliance with the MSSIC morphine milliequivalent (MME) post-operative prescribing cutoff for lumbar decompression: MME ≤ 225 (for a 7-day course). Outcomes analyzed at 90-days and 1-year included continued opioid use, post-operative complications, and patient reported outcomes (PROs) such as pain and functional scores. A multivariate analysis was conducted to control for confounders. Results: 9,240 patients were analyzed in this study. Prior to the study time frame, 59% of non-naïve patients were prescribed ≤ 225 MME, while after 74% patients were within this MME cutoff. Patients who were compliant with the MME cutoff were significantly less likely to have continued opioid use at 90 days and 1-year post-operative (OR 0.78; CI 0.62-0.98, p = 0.016 and OR 0.73; CI 0.56-0.95, p = 0.009, respectively). Those who were compliant with the MME cutoff were also significantly more likely to achieve a minimal clinically important difference (MCID) in back pain at 90 days and 1-year post-operative (OR 1.44; CI 1.08-1.93, p = 0.007 and OR 1.48; CI 1.08-2.04, p = 0.008, respectively). There were no significant differences in ED visits, readmissions, or any complication. Conclusion: We demonstrate that the implementation of a multi-institutional opioid prescribing guidelines for an opioid naïve population has additive benefits for the non-naïve population. We observed a reduction in total opiates prescribed which was associated with decreased long-term opioid use despite patients’ pre-operative opioid use. Notably, this was achieved without deleterious effects realted to inadequate analgesia such as increased adverse events, ED visits, or worsened PROs.

## ID: AGS26/3161

## P0544: Patterns and outcomes of traumatic spinal injuries: a 5-year experience from a level i trauma center in Pakistan

Abdus Salam

1

, Sabah Ullah Din

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Traumatic spinal injuries (TSIs) are a major cause of morbidity and mortality, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where prevention and trauma systems are underdeveloped. Understanding the demographics, mechanisms, anatomical distribution, and outcomes of TSIs is essential to guide policy and clinical practice. Materials and Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted at a Level I trauma center in Pakistan between March 2020 and June 2025. Patients with radiologically confirmed spinal trauma were included. Data collected included age, sex, mechanism of injury, anatomical site, neurological status (ASIA scale), associated injuries, management strategy, ICU admission, length of stay (LOS), and outcomes. Logistic regression was applied to identify independent predictors of mortality. Results: A total of 1,050 patients were identified, representing 18.2% of all trauma admissions. The mean age was 38.9 years, with males comprising 74.1% of cases. Road traffic accidents (52.3%) and falls (35.7%) were the leading causes. Lumbar injuries (33.6%) were most common, followed by cervical (24.8%), thoracic (20.2%), and sacral (6.1%). Neurological deficits were present in 31.4%, particularly in cervical trauma. Nearly half (46.2%) underwent surgery, and 26.7% required ICU care. The mean LOS was 11.4 days. Overall mortality was 13.9%, highest among cervical injuries (17.2%). Independent predictors of mortality included neurological deficit (OR 1.89, p < 0.001), high Injury Severity Score (OR 1.06, p < 0.001), traumatic brain injury (OR 1.55, p = 0.008), and prolonged LOS (OR 1.02, p = 0.004). Conclusion: TSIs accounted for nearly one-fifth of trauma admissions, predominantly from road crashes and falls, with cervical injuries carrying the highest mortality. Preventive strategies, early referral systems, and multidisciplinary trauma care are critical to reducing the burden of spinal trauma in Pakistan.

## ID: AGS26/3254

## P0545: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas: a single-center five-year experience

Ioannis Dravopoulos

1

, Ploutarchos Karydakis

1

, Kyriakos Papakonstantinou

1

, Georgios Georgoulis

1

, Nikolaos Georgakoulias

1

, Nikolaos Paidakakos

1

1

Athens General Hospital ‘G. Gennimatas’, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas (sdAVFs) constitute the most common spinal vascular malformation in adults and a potentially reversible cause of progressive congestive myelopathy when recognized promptly. We summarize our department’s experience in the past five years. Material and Methods: We performed a retrospective review of consecutive sdAVFs managed at a single tertiary neurosurgical center between 2020 and 2025. Variables included demographics, fistula level, surgical technique (laminectomy/hemilaminectomy, clip ligation, use of instrumentation), and immediate and long-term postoperative course. Results: Five patients were treated (median age 65.8 years [IQR 63.8-66.3]; 4 males, 1 female). Lesions were lumbar in three (L2-L3, L2, L5) and thoracic in two (T7, T12). Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) localized sdAVFs in the thoracolumbar/lumbosacral. All underwent microsurgical ligation of the arterialized intradural draining vein via laminectomy/hemilaminectomy with intraoperative Doppler confirmation. Intraoperative ICG was used in 3/5 cases. Early postoperative courses were uneventful with neurological stabilization or improvement; two patients were transferred to inpatient rehabilitation. Functional recovery was achieved in all cases with no relapses to date. Conclusion: Over five years, sdAVFs at our center were managed uniformly with microsurgical ligation, often with selective instrumentation, yielding early clinical stabilization or improvement and no recorded perioperative complications. The anatomical distribution (thoracolumbar predominance), frequent sacral-segment symptoms, and the centrality of prompt, definitive ligation are congruent with contemporary series and reviews, underscoring the need for early recognition and referral to optimize outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3476

## P0546: The Impact of Perioperative Nutrition in Spine Surgery: A Systematic Review

Minjun Park

1

, Nathan McLaughlin

1

, Mayur Patel

1

, Jorge Urquiaga

1

, Mauricio Avila

1

1

Saint Louis University, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Nutrition plays a key role in wound healing, immune function, and recovery from injury. In recent years, there is a growing interest in the role of nutrition and its relationship with spine surgery outcomes. However, the role and guidelines of individual nutrition components and their impact in spinal surgery have not been fully elucidated. Objectives: To review the current literature of the impact of different nutritional components in spinal surgery and suggest a comprehensive nutritional recommendations based on the current available literature. Methods: A PubMed , EBSCO and CINAHL database literature search was conducted from inception to 2025 following the PRISMA guidelines with the following keywords: “nutrition”, “nutritional status”, “nutrition assessment”,“spine”, “surgery” using booleans AND or OR. Results: Forty-seven studies fit our inclusion criteria, including ten randomized controlled trials (RCTs), three prospective studies, and thirty-four retrospective analyses. Retrospective studies consistently showed that preoperative malnutrition is a significant risk factor for adverse outcomes, most commonly postoperative complications (n = 24), and increased length of stay (n = 15). Prospective and randomized trials demonstrated that targeted interventions could improve outcomes. High-protein supplements (e.g., 36 g/day + 1.2 g/kg or a fixed 60 g/day) was associated with reduced pain and disability scores, decreased infection rates, and preservation of paraspinal muscle area. For patients with vitamin D deficiency (< 20 ng/mL), supplementation with 3200 IU daily was found to reduce systemic inflammatory markers and improve early functional outcomes. In patients undergoing major spinal deformity or staged surgeries, compound nutrition formulas and/or postoperative total parenteral nutrition (TPN) helped preserve nutritional status, shorten hospital stays, and reduce the need for albumin transfusions. Conclusion: Based on the available evidence, we recommend perioperative nutritional optimization for patients undergoing spine surgery. Key recommendations include: 1) initiating high-protein supplementation preoperative and continuing for four weeks postoperatively; 2) correcting vitamin D deficiency with 3200 IU daily, beginning four weeks preoperatively and continuing postoperatively; and 3) utilizing tailored compounded nutrition or high-nitrogen TPN for patients undergoing complex reconstructive or staged procedures. Implementing these targeted strategies can help mitigate risks associated with malnutrition and improve surgical outcomes.
