## ID: AGS26/190

## P0547: Preoperative RVAD predicts curve correction in idiopathic early-onset scoliosis treated with traditional growing rods

Youssef Sawiris

1,2

, Wael Koptan

1

, Yasser ElMiligui

1

, Ahmed Sultan

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic, Faculty of Medicine, Cairo University, Cairo, Egypt

2

Al-Haram Specialized Hospital, Giza, Egypt

Introduction: Early-onset scoliosis (EOS) presents significant challenges for spine surgeons, particularly in balancing maximal deformity correction with minimal surgical risk. The rib vertebral angle difference (RVAD) is an established preoperative metric for EOS assessment, but its prognostic value for curve correction after traditional growing rod (TGR) surgery has not been previously defined. This study evaluates the predictive role of preoperative RVAD in idiopathic EOS (IEOS) patients undergoing TGR instrumentation. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was performed on 83 IEOS patients (35 males, 48 females; mean age 6.8 ± 1.06 years) treated with TGRs, biannual lengthening, and quarterly clinical follow-up until definitive fusion. Mean follow-up was 3.12 ± 1.06 years. Recorded parameters included Cobb’s angle, preoperative RVAD, correction percentage, Early Onset Scoliosis Questionnaire 24 (EOSQ-24) scores, and complications. Correlations between preoperative RVAD and correction percentage were analyzed using Pearson’s coefficient and linear regression. Results: Preoperative mean Cobb’s angle was 72.2° ± 14.3, and mean preoperative RVAD was 37.2° ± 18.3. EOSQ-24 scores improved significantly postoperatively (35.0 ± 7.5 to 67.5 ± 5.2; P < 0.001). Preoperative RVAD demonstrated a strong negative correlation with correction percentage (R = -0.821, P = 0.0317), explaining 69.2% of variance (R2 = 0.6919). Complications occurred in 17 patients (20%), including screw breakage (15%) and superficial wound infection (5%). Conclusion: Preoperative RVAD is a strong predictor of postoperative curve correction in IEOS patients treated with TGRs. TGR surgery yields substantial clinical and radiographic improvements with a low complication rate, supporting the inclusion of preoperative RVAD in preoperative planning.

## ID: AGS26/252

## P0548: MRI-based pedicle edema: a sign of segmental instability?

Thomas Ahlin

1

, Charles Sorbanelli

1

, Michael Pheasant

2

, Alex Tang

2

, Tan Chen

3

1

Geisinger Commonwealth School of Medicine, Scranton, United States

2

Geisinger Northeast Orthopaedic Residency, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

3

Geisinger Musculoskeletal institute, Division of Orthopaedic Spine Surgery, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

Introduction: Degenerative spinal diseases, including degenerative disc disease, spondylolisthesis, and spinal stenosis, are prevalent conditions with increasing incidence in the aging adult population. Spinal pedicle edema (PE), a radiological finding seen on T2-weighted MRIs, may serve as a potential imaging biomarker for spinal instability and biomechanical segmental stress, and help guide clinical decision-making particularly in the context of surgical planning. The goals of this study are to evaluate the relationship between PE, long term clinical outcomes following surgery, and well-established markers of segmental instability. Material and Methods: A retrospective chart review was performed for patients undergoing elective 1 or 2 level fusion or decompression surgery for degenerative spondylosis with and without spondylolisthesis. The presence of PE was determined by identifying areas of hyperintense signal within the pedicles. Each MRI was independently reviewed by two investigators with confirmation by a board-certified orthopaedic spine surgeon. Spondylolisthesis was graded based on percent of vertebral slippage: Grade 0 (0%), Grade 1 (0-25%), and Grade 2 (25-50%). The association of spinal PE was evaluated against perioperative PROMIS and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) outcome scores, spondylolisthesis grade, and type of surgical treatment. Descriptive and inferential statistics were performed. Results: A total of 145 (73 PE, 72 no-PE) patients were identified with no differences in distribution of gender, type of surgery, or preoperative outcome scores (p>0.05). Amongst the PE cohort, a similar degree of long-term clinical improvement was observed in patients who underwent isolated decompression versus decompression and fusion (ΔODI: -16.3 vs -17.3, p = 0.875, ΔPROMIS: -9.1 vs -1.7, p = 0.125). A relationship approaching significance was found between PE and facet cysts (p = 0.089), and grade of spondylolisthesis (p = 0.053). No other significant association was found between PE and established findings of spinal instability on MRIs (facet diastasis) and dynamic X-rays (Δ segmental angle and translation). Conclusion: MRI-based pedicle edema is found to be radiographically associated with the presence of lumbar facet cysts and grade of spondylolisthesis approaching significance, but did not in isolation affect clinical outcomes following decompression with and without fusion. Further higher-powered studies are warranted to provide surgically actionable insights on spinal instability.

## ID: AGS26/361

## P0549: Intra & inter-observer accuracy of Cobb’s angle in scoliosis; six weeks apart

Shahzaib Riaz Baloch

1

, Mohammad Sohail Rafi

1

1

Dr. Ziauddin Hospital, Clifton Campus, Ziauddin University, Karachi, Pakistan

Introduction: Cobb’s angle is important for assessing the severity and progression of the curvature, as well as for monitoring and managing spine deformities. The reliability in the determination of Cobb's angle depends on the technical expertise of the observer, as well as on the correct angle measurement and determination of the end vertebra. This study was aimed to find inter and intra observer variations while measuring cobb’s angle on same radiographs 6 weeks apart. Material and Methods: A Reliability and Agreement study of total of 100 radiographs were studied and each observer with a different level of experience measured the same radiographs. Measurements were taken by trained observers at two time points, six weeks apart by five different readers with different years of experience, to check the accuracy in terms of years of experience at 6 weeks’ interval at Dr. Ziauddin Hospital Karachi. Results: The mean differences in Inter-observer Cobb's angle recorded among raters ranged from 2.1° to 13.2° whereas the mean Intra-Observer Cobbs angle ranged from 2.31° to 10.15° when measuring on the same PA radiographs. Conclusion: The reliability in the determination of Cobb's angle depends on the technical expertise of the observer, as well as on the correct angle measurement and determination of the end vertebra. However, even with the same end vertebra used as a reference, Cobb's measurement may vary.
Keywords: Scoliosis, Cobbs Angle, Apex, End Vertebra, Risser sign

## ID: AGS26/390

## P0550: A Structured Ultrasound-Guided Workflow for Level Identification in Lumbar Spine Surgery

Giuseppe Loggia

1

, Ellen Soffin

2

, Fedan Avrumova

2

, Marco Burkhard

2

, Michael Kelly

2

, Michael Singleton

2

, J. Levi Chazen

2

, Darren R. Lebl

2

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, Balgrist Zurich, New York, United States

2

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Despite the routine use of standard fluoroscopic verification, wrong-level spinal surgery continues to be a documented and concerning complication. Fluoroscopy exposes patients and staff to ionizing radiation and requires specialized personnel and infrastructure. Ultrasound (US) has been utilized for spinal level identification (SLI) in obstetric and anesthetic applications but remains underexplored in surgical workflows. Material and Methods: US imaging was performed on a series of fresh-frozen cadavers using a newly developed four-step protocol to identify lumbar intervertebral levels: (1) Midline localization, (2) Sacral surface tracing, (3) S1-Superior articular facet recognition, (4) Sequential interlaminar space enumeration. Five spinal needles were placed under US guidance in each specimen at lumbar levels L1-2 through L5-S1. Fluoroscopic imaging was used to confirm needle placement accuracy. Results: A total of 50 lumbar levels were assessed across ten cadaveric specimens using the four-step US workflow (with 25 procedures performed from the right side and 25 from the left). Fluoroscopic validation of US imaging demonstrated accurate level identification in 49 out of 50 cases, yielding an accuracy rate of 98%. The single misidentification occurred at the L5-S1 level during the third step of the workflow, where the S1 superior articular process was erroneously interpreted as the L5 inferior articular process. No additional misidentifications were observed in the subsequent procedures, and the four-step protocol proved reproducible across specimens. Conclusion: This cadaveric feasibility study establishes a high accuracy of US-guided lumbar level identification. A structured and reproducible workflow for level localization was established, integrating a novel four-step protocol. These findings represent a first step toward a radiation-free alternative for preoperative lumbar level identification in spinal surgery. Further investigations are warranted to validate these results in clinical settings.

## ID: AGS26/473

## P0551: Bertolotti syndrome

Marcos Vaz de Lima

1

, João Paulo Trecco

1

, Robert Meves

1

, Eduardo Achar Filho

1

1

Santa Casa de São Paulo, Spine AI, São paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Lower back disorders are prevalent and a significant reason for emergency care visits. In 2020, 619 million people experienced low back pain, expected to rise to 843 million by 2050. A common cause is the presence of a lumbosacral transitional vertebra (LSTV). Objectives: This study evaluates the prevalence of Bertolotti Syndrome in patients with low back pain in an emergency setting and assesses interobserver reliability of LSTV classifications. Methods: A retrospective analysis of 1023 lumbar spine radiographs from patients presenting with low back pain from 2018 to 2020 was conducted. After exclusions, 469 radiographs were analyzed. Two orthopedists classified LSTVs using Tini and Castellvi’s systems. Statistical analyses included the Kappa agreement index, two-proportion Z test, confidence interval for the mean, and p-value calculations. Results: The prevalence of Bertolotti Syndrome was 62.5% for observer A and 61.6% for observer B. Type I LSTV was the most common, with over 70% of cases, followed by type III at over 15%. The most frequent morphology was bilateral involvement of the transverse process, with more than 50% of cases exhibiting IB morphology according to both classifications. Conclusion: This study found a high incidence of Bertolotti Syndrome (over 60%) in patients with low back pain seeking emergency care, suggesting that LSTV should be more frequently considered in differential diagnoses. Improved recognition of LSTV could lead to better management strategies for low back pain associated with this congenital anomaly. Level of evidence III; Retrospective Cohort Study.

## ID: AGS26/565

## P0552: Validation of an L1PA-based model for predicting 2-year postoperative pelvic tilt after adult spinal deformity surgery

Christopher Diaz

1

, Lane McCoy

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Proper sagittal alignment is critical after adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery, with pelvic tilt (PT) serving as a key parameter for long-term outcomes. The L1 pelvic angle (PA) and T4PA are valuable parameters for assessing sagittal balance. This study evaluates an L1PA-based model for predicting PT at 2 years postoperative in well aligned ASD patients. Material and Methods: Sagittal alignment of ASD patients fused from T1-T4 to the sacrum was assessed. A well-aligned training cohort (WA1) was defined by L1PA = 0.5*(Pelvic Incidence (PI)) - 19 ± 2 and a T4-L1PA mismatch of -3 to +1. We created a regression model to relate immediate postoperative radiographic parameters to 2-year postoperative PT. This model was validated on a separate cohort of aligned ASD patients fused from T5-12 to S1. Finally, we explored if broadening the criteria (WA2) to L1PA = 0.5*PI - 21 ± 5 would impact the model's accuracy. Results: A total of 6 ASD patients met our inclusion criteria for WA1, resulting in the following equation: PT2Y=1.063×L1PA+11.789 (R2=0.61). The model was validated on 21 ASD patients from our validation cohort, where it maintained predictive power with an R2 of 0.59 and a mean squared error of ±5.55. However, relaxing the L1PA alignment criteria to L1PA = 0.5PI - 21±5 resulted in reduced accuracy, with the R2 dropping to 0.44. Conclusion: This study validates an L1PA-based model for predicting PT at 2 years postoperatively in well aligned patients. Model testing revealed that stricter L1PA alignment criteria yielded more reliable PT predictions at 2 years. This relationship serves as a valuable tool for preoperative planning by accurately forecasting PT which could be used to better assess whether patients will achieve optimal sagittal alignment two years after surgery. Future research should explore larger cohorts and refine predictive models to enhance surgical planning and patient-specific alignment goals.

## ID: AGS26/773

## P0553: The cord-to-canal ratio as a radiographic tool in congenital cervical spinal stenosis

Annie Jinks

1

, Robert Ferdon

1

, Chase Walton

1

, Jason Silvestre

1

, Charles Reitman

1

, James Lawrence

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

Introduction: Congenital cervical spinal stenosis (CCS), defined by a reduced sagittal diameter of the cervical spinal canal, is a known risk factor for early onset degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM). Traditional assessment using the Torg-Pavlov ratio (TPR) on radiographs is limited by its focus on osseous anatomy alone. MRI, the gold standard for DCM evaluation, enables assessment of both bony and soft tissue structures and has prompted development of more comprehensive metrics such as the Cord-to-Canal ratio (CCR) and the Spinal Cord Occupation Ratio (SCOR). While multiple advanced classifications now exist, there is no consensus on which MRI-based parameter best correlates with CCS clinical symptoms. This study evaluates the CCR, MRI TPR, and SCOR to determine their interrelationships and their diagnostic utility in identifying symptomatic CCS. Material and Methods: Ninety-seven cervical spine MRIs obtained at a single quaternary care center over a 12-month period, January 2024 - December 2024, were retrospectively reviewed. Measurements were taken at the C5 mid-vertebral level to calculate three MRI-based metrics: CCR (sagittal diameter of the cord/sac), MRI TPR (sagittal dural sac diameter/vertebral body diameter), and the SCOR (axial area of the cord/dural sac). Patient charts were reviewed for myelopathic presentations of signs/symptoms. Sensitivity, specificity, and area under the curve (AUC) were determined for each metric using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis. Pearson correlation coefficients were computed to assess inter-metric relationships. The TPRs were inverted to ensure higher values corresponded with greater stenosis severity. Results: Of the 97 included patients, 56% (n = 54) exhibited myelopathic symptoms. The CCR demonstrated the highest sensitivity (80%) in detecting symptomatic myelopathy, compared to MRI TPR (62%) and SCOR (75%) but had the lowest specificity (44% vs. 76% and 65%, respectively). SCOR achieved the highest AUC (0.75), followed by Torg-Pavlov (0.69) and Cord-to-Canal (0.63). Correlation analysis showed a stronger relationship between CCR and TPR (r = -0.52) than between SCOR and inverted Torg-Pavlov (r = 0.20; p < 0.0001). No significant correlation differences were observed between symptomatic and asymptomatic groups. Conclusion: This study underscores the varying degrees of correlation between commonly used radiological indices for CCS. The CCR demonstrated a moderate inverse correlation with the TPR, suggesting that while these measures are related, they assess distinct aspects of cervical canal stenosis. Conversely, the weak correlation between the Torg-Pavlov Inverted Ratio and SCOR suggests limited predictive value of SCOR in assessing CCS severity. CCR correlates with previously described measures of CCS, supporting its validity as an assessment tool. Given its high sensitivity and low specificity, the CCR may serve as an effective initial screening tool, particularly in clinical settings where early detection and minimizing missed diagnoses are critical.

## ID: AGS26/782

## P0554: Morphometric analysis of lumbar nerve root take-off in the lumbar spine

Andy Hu

1

, Robert Ferdon

2

, Chase Walton

1

, Warren Roth

1

, Michal Williams

1

, Jason Silvestre

1

, Reed Butler

1

, Jamie Wilson

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

2

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

Introduction: Although lateral recess and foraminal stenosis are known causes of radiculopathy, the vertical origin (takeoff height) of lumbar nerve roots has not been quantitatively assessed. A higher nerve root origin from the thecal sac, or root takeoff, may be of clinical relevance, as a more proximal takeoff will consequently result in a greater length of neural tissue traversing the lateral recess. This study aims to characterize variability in lumbar nerve root takeoff height using CT myelography in patients with lumbar spinal pathology. Materials and Methods: Retrospective review of CT myelograms from patients with lumbar pathology with measurements of the vertical origin (root takeoff height) of L4 and L5 nerve roots relative to total interpedicular height, producing a takeoff ratio (h/H). Clinical records were reviewed to categorize symptom distribution as leg-dominant, back-dominant, or mixed. Statistical analyses included inter-rater reliability (κ), intra-rater reliability, group comparisons, correlation testing between takeoff ratio and symptom type, and a priori power analysis. Results: A total of 75 patients were included. Nerve root takeoff height showed substantial interindividual variability at both L3-4 and L4-5 levels. Conclusion: Lumbar nerve root takeoff height demonstrates meaningful variability across patients, representing an anatomical parameter not typically considered in conventional imaging assessments. In this study, we observed that such variability may influence how nerve roots traverse the lateral recess. We speculate that a higher root takeoff could predispose patients to radicular symptoms; however, further investigation is needed to confirm this relationship.

## ID: AGS26/786

## P0555: Utility of the Goutallier classification and the Singapore Sarcopenia Index (SSI) in the assessment of paravertebral muscles as early predictors of sarcopenia.

Bryan Foong

1

, Gabriel Uen Chern Li

1

, Joshua Decruz

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Khoo Teck Puat Hospital, Singapore

Introduction: Image based assessments of muscle quality and ectopic fat infiltration are increasingly recognised as relevant in assessing for sarcopenia. Computed Tomography (CT) is useful in quantifying muscle mass. The Singapore Sarcopenia Index (SSI) was recently proposed as an objective, quantitative assessment of muscle density by measuring Hounsfield Units (HU) on CT scans. However, CT based measures lack assessment of muscle fatty infiltration. The Goutallier Classification has been used to evaluate paravertebral muscle ectopic fat infiltration in sarcopenic adults. We investigate the relationship between the SSI and the Goutalier Classification and evaluate their utility as predictors of sarcopenia. Material and Methods: We group patients into 2 groups based on age (Group A: < 65 vs Group B: ≥ 65). CT and MRI lumbar spine scans were analysed retrospectively. HU of the psoas, erector spinae, multifidus and subcutaneous tissue were measured at the level of L3 The SSI was calculated for each muscle respectively. The Goutallier classification was utilised to evaluate the amount of ectopic fat infiltration of the same muscle groups on MRI scan. Results: 34 patients were assessed as part of this pilot study. The SSI was significantly different between groups A and B for the erector spinae and multifidus (p=0.021; p=0.033) but not the psoas. The Goutallier classification was significantly different between groups A and B for all muscles. Age had a significant positive correlation with Goutallier classification for all muscle groups (p < 0.0001). There was a significant correlation between the SSI and the Goutallier classification for the erector spinae and multifidus (r = -0.59; p = 0.0002 and r = -0.583; p = 0.0003 respectively) but not the psoas (r = -0.289; p = 0.098). Conclusion: The SSI is a reproducible and consistent tool in assessing muscle quality. This study has shown that it can be used in conjuncture with the Goutallier classification to provide more detailed assessment of the paravertebral muscles. This has the potential to streamline the early detection of sarcopenia and influence the timing of treatment.

## ID: AGS26/3270

## P0557 Reliability of Intraoperative pelvic inlet view to assess bicortical S1 pedicle screw placement

Nesa Milan1, David Gendelberg1, Sama Zazai1, Mark Xu1, Hao-Hua Wu2, Ashraf El Naga1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, United States,

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of California, Irvine, Irvine, United States

Introduction: Bicortical S1 pedicle screw fixation is believed to offer biomechanical advantage, especially in the setting of low sacral bone density. However, screws that are too long can put neurovascular structures at risk including the L5 nerve root.6,7 The lateral sacral view is unreliable at determining the degree of anterior S1 pedicle screw perforation given the anterior recession of the sacral ala along which the L5 nerve root runs. The pelvic inlet view, however, is frequently used in pelvic trauma to evaluate the anterior border of the upper sacral segment in the settings of iliosacral and transiliac-transsacral screw fixation. In the study, we seek to assess the reliability of the pelvic inlet view to assess for the degree of anterior S1 pedicle screw penetration. Material and Methods: A retrospective radiographic review was performed of all patients who underwent S1 pedicle screw fixation in which pelvic inlet and lateral sacral fluoroscopic views were saved and either an intraoperative O-Arm or immediate postoperative CT scan was available as a reference standard. Fluoroscopic inlet views were independently evaluated by three fellowship-trained spine surgeons. For the pelvic inlet view, each observer classified screw position as: no bicortical perforation, <1 screw diameter perforation, or >1 screw diameter perforation. On the lateral, observers classified whether zero, one or two screws were bicrotical. Inter- and intra-rater reliability were calculated using the weighted kappa coefficient, in R (v 4.4.2). Observer assessments were compared with the true degree of perforation determined by axial CT imaging to calculate sensitivity and specificity. Results: Seventeen patients with 35 screws (1 revision surgery) were assessed by three spine fellowship-trained surgeons. Pelvic inlet intra-rater reliability was excellent for all observers (Observer 1: κ = 0.90, 95% CI 0.68-0.97; Observer 2: κ = 0.89, 95% CI 0.69-0.96; Observer 3: κ = 0.95, 95% CI 0.86-1.00). Pairwise inter-rater reliability was also excellent (Observers 1 vs 2: κ = 0.86, 95% CI 0.59-0.97; Observers 1 vs 3: κ = 0.91, 95% CI 0.73-0.98; Observers 2 vs 3: κ = 0.88, 95% CI 0.64-0.97). Overall inter-rater agreement across all three observers (Light's κ) was 0.84 (95% CI 0.58-0.93). Compared with CT/O-Arm, pelvic inlet demonstrated pooled accuracy across observers of 0.72 (95% CI 0.63-0.81) with sensitivity 0.67 and specificity 0.92. For lateral views, pooled accuracy was 0.68, sensitivity was 0.68, and specificity was 0.50. Conclusion: The pelvic inlet view demonstrated excellent inter- and intra-rater reliability for assessing the degree of bicortical S1 pedicle screw engagement and favorable sensitivity and specificity as compared to the pelvic lateral. The pelvic inlet view may serve as a more reliable alternative to assessing bicortical S1 pedicle purchase as compared to the sacral lateral, which has traditionally underestimated bicortical perforation. 

## ID: AGS26/866

## P0558: BMD in lumbar spine: a tool of underdiagnosis? Comparison of Lumbar Spine Hounsfield Units with dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry in diagnosing osteoporosis

Gabriel Uen Chern Li

1

, Bryan Foong

1

, Joshua Decruz

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Khoo Teck Puat Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is the gold standard for osteoporosis diagnosis, yet it fails to capture crucial regional variations in trabecular bone quality within the vertebral body, a common site for osteoporotic fractures. Degenerative changes such as osteophytes in the facet joints can artificially inflate Bone Mineral Density (BMD) values, masking underlying bone fragility. This study evaluates the diagnostic sensitivity of vertebral body Hounsfield Units (HU) derived from computed tomography (CT) against DXR for osteoporosis, addressing the issue of false negatives in BMD testing. Material and Methods: We compared BMD T-scores from DXA and individual vertebral body HU values obtained from existing CT scans for 82 individual lumbar vertebrae. Regional HU for facet joints and spinous processes were evaluated as well. Vertebral body HU of 100 and below was considered to be osteoporotic, based on the existing literature. Results: The mean HU of the vertebral bodies were significantly lower than the HU of facet joints and spinous process (p < 0.001). Our data indicate that vertebral HU measurements offer superior sensitivity (97.6%) for detecting localized bone density deficits compared to global BMD scores (18.6%). Degenerative changes, as represented by facetal and spinous process average HU, was higher in the group with false negative BMD at 269 vs 204 in the group with true negative BMD (p = 0.03). Conclusion: Since vertebral HU assessment is a more sensitive tool that can reduce false negatives, we suggest a revamp of the DXA-based BMD to be done in the lateral view instead of anterior-posterior (AP). This would omit the interference from degenerative changes of the posterior elements. The reliance on current DXA-based BMD in the AP view for osteoporosis diagnosis may result in missed diagnoses, particularly in patients with spinal degenerative disease.

## ID: AGS26/909

## P0559: The impact of initial kyphosis and paravertebral muscle atrophy in thoracolumbar kyphosis progression

Kosuke Sato

1

, Hiroshi Kumagai

1

, Masashi Yamazaki

1

1

Ichihara Hospital, Tsukuba, Ibaraki, Japan

Introduction: Osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF) are a significant cause of morbidity, often leading to progressive spinal deformity despite conservative treatment. The development of progressive kyphosis, particularly at the thoracolumbar junction, can result in persistent pain, reduced pulmonary function, and impaired physical mobility, severely impacting a patient's quality of life. Identifying early imaging markers that can predict this progression is crucial for guiding clinical decisions and implementing timely interventions to prevent long-term disability. Objective: This study aimed to identify and evaluate early imaging predictors of progressive thoracolumbar kyphosis (TLK) in patients with osteoporotic fractures of the T11 or T12 vertebrae who underwent conservative management. Material and Methods: This retrospective study reviewed a total of 343 patients diagnosed with vertebral fracture at our hospital between May 2014 and August 2023. From this cohort, we included 39 patients with T11 or T12 OVF who underwent conservative treatment and had a 2-year radiographic follow-up. Patients with initial TLK ≥ 40∘, non-union, or surgical intervention were excluded. We measured initial TLK, local kyphosis (LK), L1 Hounsfield Unit (HU), and T10 paravertebral muscle cross-sectional area (CSA) and Goutallier classification (GC) on MRI. Patients were divided into a kyphosis group (K-group, 2-year TLK ≥ 40∘) and a non-kyphosis group (NK-group, 2-year TLK <40∘) for comparative analysis. Results: The K-group consisted of 8 patients and the NK-group had 31. There were no significant differences in age, BMI, or L1 HU. The K-group had significantly larger initial LK (p = 0.001) and TLK (p = 0.001) compared to the NK-group. Although initial vertebral mobility was not significantly different (p = 0.13), the K-group showed a significantly smaller paravertebral muscle CSA (p = 0.02). In patients with initial LK mobility less than 10∘, a smaller paravertebral muscle CSA (p = 0.047) and more severe fatty degeneration (p = 0.04) were associated with greater TLK progression. Conclusion: In osteoporotic vertebral fractures of the thoracolumbar junction, a larger initial kyphosis is a key risk factor for progression. Furthermore, limited vertebral mobility combined with muscle atrophy and severe fatty degeneration in the paravertebral muscles may also be a significant predictor of future kyphosis.

## ID: AGS26/972

## P0560: A similar PI can contribute to totally different patterns of lumbar lordosis in asympotmatic population

Siyu Zhou

1,2,3

, Yi Zhao

1,2,3

, Hongbin He

4

, Tianchang Wang

4

, Zhuoran Sun

1,2,3

, Weishi Li

1,2,3

1

Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

2

Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Beijing, China

3

Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

4

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: With the development of sagittal alignment restoration, GAP score has been proposed to overcome the disadvantage of classical Schwab's classification. Despite GAP score and its modified versions bring the conception of sagittal balance restoration from absolute numerical values to pelvic incidence (PI)-based proportional method, this score system mainly relies on the linear regression relationships between PI and other parameters derived from the asymptomatic population. However, the predictive effect of regression model between PI and other parameters is relatively low in Asian population, and this may be related to the phenomenon that a similar PI can refer to totally different lumbar lordosis (LL) and sacral slope (SS) in the asymptomatic population, which significantly influence the predictive effect of PI on other parameters. To better understand the deep mechanism of sagittal balance, this phenomenon needs to be further clarified. Material and Methods: The present study recruited 380 asymptomatic Chinese volunteers aged between 18 and 45 years. Sagittal parameters, including PI, pelvic tilt(PT), SS ,LL and segmental lordosis of the vertebrae and intervertebral discs from L1 to S1, were measured on full-spine X-rays. The volunteers were stratified into three groups according to PI ranges of 33.7°-41.9°(group 1), 42.0°-50.2°(group 2), and 50.3°-58.5°(group 3). Within each group, the sagittal parameters were compared between the maximum and minimum LL determined by the mean LL ± 0.5 standard deviation. Results: Among these volunteers, the average age was 26.4±7.0 years, with a mean PI of 46.1° ±8.3°. There were 103, 143, and 79 volunteers in group 1 to 3, respectively. After screening for extreme LL subgroups and controlling PI within the three groups, the extreme LL subgroups consisted of 56, 86, and 46 volunteers, respectively. From group 1 to 3, the mean LL of the maximum subgroups were 55.2° ± 3.4°, 61.3° ± 4.3°, and 62.5° ± 4.9°, and the mean SS were 35.0° ± 2.9°, 39.9° ± 3.3°, and 44.0° ± 4.0°, respectively. The corresponding minimum value groups had mean LL of 36.8° ± 4.5°, 41.2° ± 5.2°, and 46.7° ± 4.1°, and mean SS of 27.4° ± 3.8°, 31.5° ± 4.1°, and 36.4° ± 3.8°. From group 1 to 3, the differences in the total Cobb angle of the lumbar vertebrae between the maximum and corresponding minimum LL subgroups were 5.6° (6.0°-0.4°), 5.4° (10.2°-4.8°), and 6.2° (15.3°-9.1°), respectively. The differences in the total intervertebral Cobb angle (from L1/2 to the L5/S1) were 12.7° (49.1°-36.4°), 14.7° (51.1°-36.4°), and 9.6° (47.2°-37.6°), respectively. Conclusion: In group 1 and group 2, the difference in LL between the maximum and minimum subgroups mainly originates from the intervertebral disc spaces. However, with the increase of PI, the influence of vertebral bodies increases in group 3. Within the three groups, the maximum and minimum value groups exhibit a relationship of mean ΔLL- mean ΔSS≈ 10°,indicating that over-extension of lumbar curvature could be an important reason for maximum LL, which could play am important role in the prevalence of lumbar degeneration.

## ID: AGS26/1096

## P0561: How reliable are our intraoperative X-rays? Predicting post-fusion sagittal alignment

Francesco Caiazzo

1

, Juan Bago

1

, Daniel Alveal-Mellado

1

, Lucas Capo

1

, Judith Salat-Batlle

1

, Paula Lavezzolo

1

1

Instituto Quirúrgico Spanò, Barcelona, Spain

Introduction: Restoring sagittal balance and lumbopelvic alignment effectively is essential in treating lumbosacral degenerative disorders with instrumented fusion. The L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) has shown promise as a postoperative spinopelvic alignment target, demonstrating an association with health-related quality-of-life measures. While surgeons commonly rely on intraoperative X-rays to assess alignment quality, the precision of these measurements and their predictive value for postoperative standing alignment remain underexplored. This study aimed to analyze the correlation, predictive value, and agreement between intraoperative and postoperative X-ray measurements for lumbar lordosis (L1S1) and lumbopelvic alignment (L1PA) in patients undergoing lumbar spine fusion and instrumentation. Material and Methods: Forty-nine patients (69% female, mean age 43.6 years) undergoing lumbosacral fusion for degenerative conditions were included. Inclusion criteria were age 18-65 years and absence of thoracic/thoracolumbar deformity. Intraoperative C-arm fluoroscopy images, taken post-instrumentation from L1 to the femoral heads using the NuVasive Pulse Navigation platform, facilitated a single extended image. Pelvic incidence (PI), lumbar lordosis (L1S1), segmental lordosis (L4-S1), and L1PA angle (L1 centroid to hip axis to midpoint of S1 endplate) were recorded intraoperatively and on full-spine sagittal standing postoperative X-rays. Statistical analysis included mean comparisons of intraoperative and postoperative parameters, correlation and linear regression for predictive value, and Bland-Altman limits of agreement. Results: Intra- and postoperative L1S1 (56.6° vs 54.7°, p = 0.1) and L4S1 (41.9° vs 40.0°, p = 0.1) did not differ. Postoperative L1-S1 was related to intraoperative L1S1 (ρ = 0.70, p < .001), intraoperative L4S1 (ρ = 0.59, p < 0.001), PI (ρ = 0.52, p < .001), but no correlation was found with intraoperative L1PA. In a stepwise multiple linear regression analysis to predict postoperative L1S1, only intraoperative L1S1 was included in the model (β: 0.7, r2: 0.6, RMSE 7.7). Bland-Altman limits of agreement were -16.5° to 20.4°. Postoperative L1PA was statistically different from intraoperative L1PA (3.4° vs 6.0°, p=.002). Postoperative L1PA correlated with intraoperative L1PA (ρ: 0.66, p < .001) and PI (ρ: 0.54, p < .001), but not with intraoperative L1S1 nor intraoperative L4S1. In a stepwise multiple regression to predict postoperative L1PA, intraoperative L1PA (β: 0.7, p < .001) and intraoperative L1S1 (β: 0.1, p = .01) were included in the model with an R2: 0.59 and RMSE: 3.4. Bland-Altman limits of agreement were -9.8° to 5.0°. Conclusion: While intraoperative radiographic measurements can estimate postoperative L1-S1 and L1-PA angles, their utility is limited. Intraoperative variables only account for 60% of the variance in postoperative outcomes, and the wide limits of agreement preclude interchangeable use of intraoperative and postoperative data.

## ID: AGS26/1112

## P0562: The utility of MRI for spinal clearance in blunt trauma: a retrospective cohort study of 539 blunt trauma patients in a level one trauma center in Singapore

Andre Loh

1

, Cayden Leyu

1

, Adam Cheng

1

, Jun Hao Tan

2

, Chloe Chan

2

, Raymen Chandrasagran

2

, Joan Li

2

, James Hallinan

3

, Jiong Hao Tan

2

1

National University of Singapore, Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, Singapore, Singapore

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery

3

Department of Diagnostic Imaging, National University Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: The majority of spinal injuries occur secondary to blunt trauma and patients with significant blunt trauma must be assumed to have an unstable spinal injury until complete radiological and clinical assessment. MRI scans are highly sensitive but can be logistically difficult to perform in ICU patients and may prolong duration of spinal immobilization unnecessarily. In this study we investigated the efficacy of MRI scans in changing patient management in blunt trauma patients and the clinical and CT findings which would indicate an MRI spine. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective study of patients with significant blunt trauma who had MRI of the spine performed between 2018 to 2023 in the National University Hospital, Singapore. Indications for an MRI spine in our center include the presence of spinal fractures on CT scan, neurological deficits, obtunded patient (unable to cooperate with physical examination), tenderness/pain over the spine on physical examination and underlying ankylosing conditions of the spine. Significant blunt trauma is defined as per the Canadian C spine Rule criteria. Fracture morphology was classified based on the AO spine fracture classifications. Change in patient management was defined as MRI scan leading to decision to operate on patient or change from conservative to operative management. We defined a stable fracture as AO type A0 cervical or thoracolumbar fracture and other fractures as potentially unstable. Univariate and Multivariate Analysis were performed. Number of days in which patients were kept on spinal nursing while awaiting MRI was also recorded. Results: In total 539 patients, 135 (25%) female and 404 (75%) males, were identified who had MRI spine performed for spinal clearance. Of these MRI scans performed, 234 (43.6%) led to change in patient management. On multivariate analysis, the presence of a potentially unstable fracture (p < 0.01) and the presence of a neurological deficit (p < 0.01) were found to be significant predictors of an MRI scan being indicted and leading to a change in patient management. Of note when using the presence of a potentially unstable fracture and/or neurological deficit as a criteria for performing an MRI, 234/419 (55.9%) of scans led to a change in patient management, while in patients who did not meet this criteria 0/110 (0.05) % of MRI scans led to a change in management. In patients who had MRI scans which did not change management, a mean of 2 (range 0-14) days was spent undergoing spinal nursing while awaiting MRI. Conclusion: In blunt trauma patients with no clear indication for MRI, an additional MRI scan may lead to unnecessary prolongation of spinal nursing with no additional benefit to the patient. Further study to clearly delineate the indications for an MRI as well as the additional costs and medical complications associated with un-indicated MRI scans are warranted.

## ID: AGS26/1171

## P0563: Coronary artery, thoracic aorta and cardiac valve calcifications in current and former smokers with diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis

Netanja Harlianto

1

, Esther Pompe

1

, Wouter Foppen

1

, Jorrit-Jan Verlaan

1

, Pim de Jong

1

, Firdaus Mohamed Hoesein

1

1

UMC Utrecht, Utrecht, The Netherlands

Introduction: Diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) is a systemic condition characterized by new bony bridges around ligaments, tendons, and joint capsules, in particular of the spine. DISH is strongly associated with obesity, diabetes mellitus, and ischemic stroke. It is hypothesized that diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) and cardiovascular events are causally related via vascular calcifications, but data are inconclusive. We aimed to assess the association between DISH and calcifications of the coronaries, thoracic aorta, aortic valve, and mitral annulus/valve. Material and Methods: Our study population is derived from the Dutch Belgian Lung cancer screening trial and included a total of 3514 male former and current smokers. Calcification volumes of each area of interest were obtained using in house developed automatic validated software from the chest computed tomography scans. Two observers evaluated DISH according to Resnick criteria. Relations between DISH and calcification volumes were evaluated with multivariate linear and logistic regression models. Results: A total of 241 subjects (6.7%) had DISH. Subjects with DISH had higher coronary (402 mm3 vs. 173 mm3), aorta (1158 mm3 vs. 486 mm3) calcification volumes, and a higher proportion of aortic (39.5% vs. 22.3%) and mitral valve/annulus calcifications (5.8% vs 2.8%). Aorta calcifications (OR:1.55;95%CI:1.09-2.20) and aortic valve calcifications (OR:1.52;95%CI:1.13-2.02) presence were associated with DISH in multivariate analyses. DISH was associated with more thoracic aorta (β:0.13; 95%CI:0.04, 0.26), and aortic valve calcification volumes per 500 mm3 (β:0.03; 95%CI:0.004, 0.06), but not coronary artery or mitral valve calcification volumes. Conclusion: In a population of male current and former smokers, the presence of DISH was associated with calcification volumes of the aorta and the aortic valve, but not with calcifications of the coronary arteries or mitral valve. These findings support the hypothesis that DISH and cardiovascular calcifications may share a common biological pathway.

## ID: AGS26/1282

## P0564: Metabolite and imaging biomarkers as predictors of postoperative recovery in cervical myelopathy

Ajoy P Shetty

1

, Karthik Ramachandran

1

, S. Rajasekaran

1

1

Ganga Medical Centre and Hospital, Coimbatore, India

Background: Cervical myelopathy presents a heterogeneous recovery profile following surgical intervention, with limited preoperative tools for prognostication. This study investigates the clinical, imaging, and magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)-derived metabolite parameters associated with differential recovery outcomes in a stratified cohort. Methods: This is a prospective cohort study in which patients with cervical myelopathy who underwent surgical management were followed for 2 years. Patients were categorised into poor and good recovery groups based on postoperative mJOA recovery rate > 50% and < 50% respectively. Demographic, clinical, and imaging variables were compared, with a focus on metabolite ratios obtained via MRS. Statistical correlations and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analyses were used to assess predictive accuracy. Results: The study included 69 patients, divided into poor (n = 34) and good (n = 35) recovery groups. Demographic factors such as age and gender showed no significant differences between groups. However, comorbidities-especially diabetes and hypertension-were more common in the poor recovery group (64.7% vs. 34.3%, p = 0.012). Preoperative mJOA scores were significantly lower in the poor recovery group (10.91 ± 1.64 vs. 14.31 ± 1.51, p = 0.001), indicating greater neurological impairment before surgery. Grade 3 stenosis was also more frequent in the poor recovery group (82.4% vs. 48.6%, p = 0.003). MRS revealed elevated Cho/NAA, Cr/NAA, and MIn/NAA ratios in the poor recovery group, all of which showed strong inverse correlations with recovery rate (Cho/NAA: r = -0.522, p < 0.001). Conversely, NAA/Cr (r = 0.279, p = 0.020) and FA (r = 0.239, p = 0.048) correlated positively with recovery, while ADC values were negatively associated (r = -0.241, p = 0.042). These findings suggest that neuronal integrity and white matter preservation are favourable prognostic indicators, whereas glial activation and diffusion abnormalities reflect irreversible damage. ROC analysis demonstrated that Cho/NAA (AUC = 0.759), Cr/NAA (AUC = 0.750), and MIn/NAA (AUC = 0.686) had good discriminatory power in predicting recovery outcomes. Other metabolites such as glutathione, glutamate, glycine, and lactate showed no significant associations, indicating limited utility in this context. Conclusion: Metabolite ratios derived from MRS-particularly those involving NAA-serve as robust biomarkers for predicting postoperative recovery in cervical myelopathy. Elevated Cho/NAA, Cr/NAA, and MIn/NAA ratios are indicative of poor outcomes, likely reflecting glial proliferation and neuronal loss. These findings support the integration of metabolite profiling into preoperative assessment protocols, complementing clinical and imaging parameters to enhance prognostic accuracy.

## ID: AGS26/1289

## P0565: Optimizing diffusion tensor imaging and tractography for enhanced visualization and clinical assessment of cervical spinal cord pathologies

Luke Pearson

1

, Emilio Supsupin

1

, Alejandro Serrano

1

, Christopher Louviere

1

, Vashisht Sekar

1

, Aboubakr Amer

1

, Ulas Cikla

1

, Mayur Virarkar

1

, Kazim Gumus

1

, Dunbar Alcindor

1

, Kourosh Tavanaiepour

1

, Daryoush Tavanaiepour

1

1

University of Florida Jacksonville, Jacksonville, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord injuries (SCIs), affecting 250,000-500,000 people globally and 17,000 annually in the U.S., result from motor vehicle collisions (38%), falls (30%), violence (13%), sports injuries (9%), and medical errors (5%). Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is the gold standard for assessing spinal cord pathologies, providing detailed soft tissue visualization. However, conventional MRI lacks directional information on white matter tracts, limiting insights into neural fiber disruptions. Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) measures water molecule diffusion to map axonal anisotropy via fractional anisotropy (FA) and mean diffusivity (MD), enabling tractography to visualize nerve fiber orientation. Despite its use in brain imaging, DTI is underutilized in spinal cord assessment due to acquisition and processing challenges. This study evaluates an optimized DTI protocol for cervical spine MRI in four pathology cases (cord contusion, metastasis, myelopathy, multiple sclerosis) and six controls to assess its ability to reveal fiber disruptions for enhanced diagnosis, surgical planning, and follow-up evaluation. Material and Methods: This retrospective, IRB-approved study integrated DTI into cervical spine MRI on a 3T scanner using spin-echo echo-planar imaging (TR 9,200 ms, TE 82 ms, 37 slices, 3 mm thickness, 128×128 matrix, b-values 0 and 1000 s/mm2 across six directions, 2-minute scan). Six controls with normal MRIs and four pathology cases were selected from PACS: an 82-year-old male with C3-C4 contusion, a 62-year-old female with C2-C3 metastasis from breast cancer, a 51-year-old male with C5-C6 myelopathy from disc extrusion, and a 35-year-old female with multiple sclerosis (MS) flare showing multifocal demyelination. Images were anonymized, processed in DSI Studio (Chen May 17, 2024) with distortion correction, and segmented to the brainstem. Regions of interest (ROIs) were set at C2-C3, C5-C6, and C7-T1 for controls, and lesion-level plus cranial/caudal for pathologies. Fiber tracking used FA threshold 0.01914, 10-1000 mm length, with spurious tracts filtered. FA and MD were extracted, with Z-scores comparing pathology metrics to control baselines. Results: Tractography revealed fiber disruptions not visible on standard MRI. Controls showed uniform cranio-caudal tracts (FA 0.45-0.52, MD 0.75-0.85 × 10-3 mm2/s). Contusion displayed posterior fiber reorientation at C3-C4 (low FA, Z-score -1.8; normal MD). Metastasis showed compressed fibers at C2-C3 (low FA, Z-score -2.1; high MD, Z-score +2.3). Myelopathy indicated poor C5-C6 connectivity (high MD, Z-score +1.9; normal FA). MS showed low lesion FA (Z-score -1.5). Reduced FA and elevated MD correlated with lesion severity, though six-direction acquisition introduced minor artifacts. Conclusion: DTI tractography enhances spinal cord lesion assessment by visualizing fiber disruptions and quantifying FA/MD changes, supporting diagnosis, surgical planning, and follow-up in a 2-minute, non-invasive scan. Limitations include small sample size, six-direction acquisition reducing precision, and eddy current artifacts. Future optimizations involve increasing directions (≥ 17), adding reverse phase encoding, and adopting multi-fiber resolution to improve accuracy for routine clinical use.

## ID: AGS26/1364

## P0566: Radiotherapy-induced basilar invagination: a case report and literature review

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Daniel Zambrano Lizcano

1

, Carlos Pacichana

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Pedro Vieira Jiménez

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Basilar invagination (BI) is a rare craniovertebral junction anomaly characterized by abnormal prolapse of the odontoid process through the foramen magnum. While congenital causes predominate, BI can also develop secondary to inflammatory, degenerative, or post-radiotherapy changes. Case Presentation: A 64-year-old male with a history of radiotherapy for squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue developed progressive cervical pain and restricted motion. Imaging revealed odontoid process protrusion above Chamberlain’s line, consistent with BI. The patient underwent occipitocervical stabilization, achieving symptom resolution. Discussion: This case underscores the potential for delayed craniovertebral instability following radiotherapy. The pathophysiology of radiation-induced BI remains poorly characterized and demands clinical awareness. Early imaging surveillance in post-radiotherapy patients with cervical symptoms is critical for timely diagnosis and intervention. Conclusion: Basilar invagination secondary to radiotherapy is a rare but serious complication that requires early recognition to prevent neurological compromise. This case highlights the need for vigilance in head and neck cancer survivors and contributes to the understanding of post-radiation craniovertebral junction pathology.

## P0567: Comparison of sagittal aligment metrics

Igor Barreira

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Igor Arthur Costa

1

, Amabile Galeti

1

, Leandro Resende

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Bianca Soares

1

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Understanding a patient’s sagittal alignment is essential for surgical planning and aligning expectations with patients. This study aimed to assess the reproducibility of several sagittal-alignment measurements and their applicability in a Brazilian outpatient setting. Material and Methods: Two raters independently measured sagittal vertical axis (SVA), spinopelvic sagittal angle (SSA), global tilt (GT), T1-spinopelvic inclination (T1-SPI), and occiput-to-C7 distance in horizontal alignment (ODHA). Reproducibility was analyzed with intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs); p-values < 0.05 were considered significant. Results: Nine patients were evaluated. All measurement strategies showed excellent reproducibility (ICC > 0.9) except T1-SPI, which demonstrated low reproducibility (ICC ≈ 0.4). In addition, SVA could not be measured in one patient and ODHA could not be measured in another. Conclusion: The proposed measurements for assessing sagittal malalignment showed excellent reproducibility, except for T1-SPI, and a very low rate of non-applicability (two cases).

## ID: AGS26/1460

## P0568: Major vessel positions between prone and lateral lumbar interbody fusion a magnetic resonance study

Igor Barreira

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Igor Arthur Costa

1

, Amabile Galeti

1

, Leandro Resende

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Bianca Soares

1

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Anatomical studies suggest that the aorta sits closer to the anterior vertebral body than the inferior vena cava, with vessel proximity increasing at more caudal levels. Whether patient repositioning from standard right-lateral decubitus to prone affects these relationships,and hence the vascular risk profile of single-position prone transpsoas (PTP) lumbar interbody fusion,remains unclear. This study compared the anterior-posterior (“vertical”) and left-right (“horizontal”) positions of the aorta and vena cava at L2-L3 and L3-L4 on magnetic-resonance imaging (MRI) obtained in lateral versus prone positions. Material and Methods: In this single-centre retrospective cohort, 25 consecutive adults undergoing clinically indicated lumbar MRI were scanned both in right-lateral decubitus (hips flexed) and prone (hips extended). Axial T2-weighted slices were acquired on a 3-T scanner. Two blinded raters measured (1) the perpendicular distance from the anterior vertebral cortex to the vessel centre (vertical) and (2) the transverse distance from the mid-sagittal line to the vessel centre (horizontal). Distances left or posterior to the reference lines were assigned negative values. Measurements were averaged, and trimmed-mean differences between positions were tested with a bootstrap Yuen-Welch t-test (trim = 10%); α = 0.05. Results: Horizontal aortic displacement increased significantly in the prone position at both disc levels (mean ± SD: -4.8 ± 5.4 mm lateral vs -9.9 ± 2.9 mm prone at L2-L3; -2.8 ± 5.9 mm lateral vs -7.9 ± 2.7 mm prone at L3-L4; both p < 0.01), reflecting a medial shift toward the mid-line. No significant vertical shifts were detected for the aorta, and neither horizontal nor vertical distances for the vena cava changed meaningfully between positions (all p ≥ 0.2). Effect sizes were small (|d| ≤ 0.8), and 95% confidence intervals excluded clinically relevant forward migration. Conclusion: Prone positioning does not drive the major lumbar vessels anteriorly relative to lateral decubitus. The minor medial aortic migration observed is unlikely to increase vascular injury risk during PTP approaches. These findings support the anatomical safety equivalence of single-position prone transpsoas lumbar interbody fusion to traditional lateral LLIF with respect to major-vessel proximity.

## ID: AGS26/1531

## P0569: Reliability of classical and emerging imaging modalities for fusion assessment after anterior cervical discectomy and fusion: a systematic review

Enrique González Gallardo

1

, Luis Guzmán Pinelo

1

, Justyna Ekert

2

, Harshvardhan Iyer

3

, Kingsley O. Abode-Iyamah

3

, Ian A. Buchanan

3

, Rodrigo Navarro- Ramirez

4

, Alfredo Quinones Hinojosa

3

, Juan Del Castillo-Calcáneo

5

, Guy Gilbert Broc - Haro

5

1

Department of Neurosurgery and Spine Surgery, “Elvia Carrillo Puerto” Regional Hospital., Department of Neurosurgery, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA., Mérida, Mexico

2

Department of Neuroradiology and Neurosurgery, Mayo Clinic Florida., Jacksonville, United States

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Mayo Clinic Florida., Jacksonville, United States

4

Department of Neurosurgery, Cleveland Clinic, Florida., Department of Neurosurgery, Mayo Clinic, Jacksonville, Florida, USA., Fort Lauderdale, United States

5

Department of Neurosurgery and Spine Surgery, “Lic. Adolfo López Mateos” Regional Hospital, ISSSTE., Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Cervical Disc Degenerative Disease (CDDD) is a prevalent spinal disorder, frequently treated with Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF). Although this procedure has shown favorable clinical outcomes, a mayor postoperative complication remains the development of pseudoarthrosis, which may mimic the original clinical presentation and occasionally require revision surgery. Acute radiographic assessment of fusion status is therefore essential. However, no universal imaging criteria currently exist, and reported results vary depending on the modality employed. Material and Methods: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. Eligible studies included adult patients undergoing ACDF with a minimum follow-up of 12 months and postoperative radiographic evaluation of fusion status. Imaging modalities assessed included static radiographs, dynamic radiographs, dynamic flexion-extension radiographs, computed tomography (CT), functional CT radiostereometric analysis (RSA), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and advanced quantitative motion analysis. Primary outcomes included diagnostic accuracy (sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, and clinical outcomes such as revision rates. Results: Fifteen studies were included. Plain static radiographs consistently overestimated fusion rates compared to dynamic imaging and intraoperative exploration. Flexion-extension radiographs improved diagnostic performance, particularly with quantitative motion analysis (sensitivity up to 77%, specificity 100%), though results varied depending on motion thresholds. CT scans demonstrated the strongest correlation with intraoperative exploration (k = 0.81) and the highest interobserver reliability, yet several studies showed CT may overestimate fusion in early postoperative stages, with up to 23% of “fused” levels still demonstrating motion. Functional CT detected a higher rate of nonunion than radiographs, correlating with increased early postoperative neck pain. RSA provided highly accurate motion detection but is not practical for routine clinical use. Retrospective series demonstrated that although radiographs evidence of pseudoarthrosis is frequently reported, only surgically explored cases allow definitive confirmation, reinforcing the limitations of imaging alone in guiding clinical decisions. Conclusion: Current evidence highlights the absence of standardized radiographic criteria for assessing cervical fusion following ACDF. CT remains the most reliable noninvasive modality, though it may overestimate early fusion, whereas dynamic radiographs provide excellent negative predictive value to exclude clinically significant pseudoarthrosis. Functional CT and motion analysis techniques show promise but are limited by radiation exposure availability. Importantly, many radiographic pseudarthroses remain clinically silent, emphasizing the need to integrate imaging findings with patient reported/outcomes. Despite attempts to evaluate evidence is noy sufficiently robust, warranting further research to establish reliable and universally accepted imaging criteria.

## ID: AGS26/2323

## P0570: Correlation of advanced MRI parameters with postoperative neurological outcomes in spinal tumors

majdouline barkache

1

, affes ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is essential for the diagnosis and surgical planning of spinal tumors. Beyond conventional sequences, advanced MRI parameters such as diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) and perfusion metrics may provide insights into tumor biology and spinal cord integrity. This study aimed to evaluate the correlation of advanced MRI parameters with postoperative neurological outcomes in patients undergoing spinal tumor surgery. Materials and Methods: We prospectively analyzed patients with intradural or intramedullary spinal tumors operated at our institution between 2020 and 2023. Preoperative MRI included DTI (fractional anisotropy [FA], apparent diffusion coefficient [ADC]) and dynamic susceptibility contrast perfusion. Neurological function was assessed using the modified McCormick scale preoperatively and at 6-month follow-up. Correlations between imaging parameters and neurological outcomes were determined using multivariate regression analysis. Results: Thirty-six patients (mean age 46.2 years, 53% female) were included. Lower preoperative FA values in the peritumoral spinal cord were significantly associated with poorer neurological recovery (p = 0.01). Elevated ADC values correlated with greater postoperative motor deficits (p = 0.03). Higher tumor perfusion indices were linked to increased intraoperative blood loss but not to long-term functional outcomes. Multivariate analysis identified preoperative FA as the strongest independent predictor of postoperative neurological improvement (OR 2.8, 95% CI 1.3-6.1). Conclusion: Advanced MRI parameters, particularly DTI-derived FA, show significant correlation with postoperative neurological outcomes in spinal tumor surgery. Incorporating these imaging biomarkers into preoperative evaluation may enhance risk stratification and guide surgical decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/2401

## P0571: Thoracic deformity and lung function in scoliosis: idiopathic vs. congenital curves

Abdus Salam

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Scoliosis alters thoracic cage geometry and may impair pulmonary mechanics. Both adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) and congenital scoliosis (CS) predispose patients to restrictive ventilatory defects, yet direct comparisons between these groups are limited. Materials and Methods: This cross-sectional study was conducted at the Orthopedic & Spine Center, Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, from January to December 2022. A total of 40 patients (20 AIS, 20 CS) were included after ethical approval. Demographic data (age, sex, BMI), radiological parameters (Cobb’s angle, curve location, thoracic kyphosis), and pulmonary function tests (FVC, FEV1, FEV1/FVC) were collected. Associations between curve magnitude and pulmonary indices were analyzed. Results: AIS patients demonstrated greater respiratory compromise. Mean FVC was significantly lower in AIS (61.5 ± 11.8%) compared to CS (76.9 ± 19.6%, p < 0.05). Mean FEV1 was also reduced in AIS (63.7 ± 12.9%) versus CS (79.8 ± 18.7%, p < 0.05). The FEV1/FVC ratio remained preserved (> 70%), consistent with restrictive impairment. Cobb’s angle negatively correlated with FVC (r = -0.39). Thoracic curves were linked to greater reductions in lung volumes than thoracolumbar curves, while BMI showed no significant influence. Conclusion: Adolescents with scoliosis, particularly those with AIS, exhibit significant restrictive pulmonary changes. Curve severity and thoracic involvement are major predictors of impairment. Routine pulmonary function testing and early orthopedic intervention are recommended to minimize long-term respiratory morbidity.

## ID: AGS26/2445

## P0572: The prevalence of Schmorl's nodes in osteoporotic vs normal patients: a Middle Eastern population study

Mohamed Othman Mohamed Shafiq

1

, K Venugopal Menon

2

1
Medical City of Military and Security Services, Muscat, Oman, 
2
Spire Hospitals, India, India
Introduction: This study aims to identify the prevalence of Schmorls nodes (SNs) in a cohort of Omani nationals, and also to determine any relation between osteoporosis and Schmorl's nodes. Material and Methods: This retrospective observational study was carried out on Omani nationals. A total of 1,348 DEXA scan patients were included. Among these, 545 patients had complete X-rays and MRI scans that helped ascertain SN status. The X-rays and sagittal, coronal, and axial T2-weighted MRI images were used to identify the presence and exact location of Schmorl's nodes, by one orthopaedic trainee and confirmed by the senior author. The association of each parameter with the presence of SN was analysed using the independent-samples T-test and one-way ANOVA. Results: Prevalence of SN in this population sample appeared to be 41.1% and only 19.1% were of normal bone density. Close to 80% were either Osteopenia or frankly osteoporotic. Vast majority (87.1%) occurred in the lumbar spine and were central in location and mostly solitary occurring in L5. Age of patients differed significantly by their sex type (t = 3.33, p < 0.001). Male patients (Mean = 63) were older in age than female patients. The association between Schmorl's node status and bone status was significant (χ2 = 97.93, p ≤ 0.001). Conclusion: The prevalence of SN in this sample of Omanis was 41.1%, most frequently observed in older men in the lumbar spine. It shows a strong association with osteoporosis/osteopenia (88.4%) and often presents as solitary, central lesions.

## ID: AGS26/2500

## P0573: Endplate vBMD versus EBQ for predicting cage subsidence after LLIF: a comparative study

Hanming Bian

1

, Qiang Yang

1

, Chao Chen

1

, Dong Zhao

1

, Gang Liu

1

1

Capital Medical University Affiliated Beijing Friendship Hospital, Tianjin Hospital, Tianjin, China

Introduction: Although cage subsidence following intervertebral fusion surgery is closely related to bone mineral density in the vertebral endplate region, comparative studies on the efficacy of vBMD and VBQ score-based assessment methods for predicting postoperative cage subsidence remain insufficient. Therefore, this study aims to compare the predictive efficacy of quantitative computed tomography (QCT)-based endplate volumetric bone mineral density (EP-vBMD) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-based endplate vertebral bone quality score (EBQ) for cage subsidence (CS) after lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF). Material and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted on patients underwent single level LLIF in conjunction with pedicle screw fixation (PSF) in our institution between January 2019 to April 2023. We measured the vBMD based on the preoperative CT using the Phantom-less QCT software. Measurement of the VBQ score was based on preoperative MRI. CS was defined as a decrease of more than 2mm in the midpoint height of the intervertebral space. The ROC curve of EP-vBMD and EBQ for predicting CS was drawn, and the predictive efficacy of the two methods was compared by Delong test. Clinical outcomes, including VAS-B, VAS-L, and ODI scores, were assessed preoperatively, postoperatively, and at 1-year follow-up. Results: A total of 97 patients who underwent LLIF were included in this study, including 31 patients with CS and 66 patients without CS. No significant differences were observed between the two groups in VAS-B, VAS-L, or ODI at preoperative, postoperative, and 1-year follow-up (all p>0.05). The EP-vBMD of the CS group was lower than that of the non-subsidence group (NCS), and EBQ was higher than that of the NCS group. The area under the ROC curve of EP-vBMD for predicting CS was larger than that of global and segmental vBMD. The area under the ROC curve of EBQ for predicting CS was larger than that of global and segmental VBQ, and the AUC of EP-vBMD was larger than that of EBQ. The combined prediction model of EP-vBMD and EBQ had the largest AUC value (0.899), but it was not significantly different from EP-vBMD (p = 0.547). Conclusion: The regional endplate BMD assessment based on QCT and MRI can effectively predict cage subsidence after LLIF, and it has better predictive efficiency than the global or surgical segmental vetebrae BMD measurement. EP-vBMD is superior to EBQ in predicting CS. The prediction efficiency of EP-vBMD combined with EBQ was better than EBQ, but not better than EB-vBMD.

## ID: AGS26/2517

## P0574: Selection of proximal fusion level in osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture (OVCF) with kyphosis: the guildence of Hounsfield Unit

Junyu Li

1

, Yiqiao Zhang

2

, Xueshi Tian

2

, Jiahao Zhang

1

, Hanwen Zhang

2

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Yan Zeng

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: The selection of the upper instrumented vertebra (UIV) in chronic Osteoporotic Vertebral Compression Fracture (OVCF) has been proven to be related to postoperative complications. For the first time, we defined the upper maximal vertebra (UMV) as the vertebra above the compressed section that had a higher Hounsfield Unit (HU) value than two vertebrae proximal to the fracture level. The upper sagittal reverse vertebra (USRV) was defined as the first vertebra that presented opposite HU value distribution from the anterior to posterior part of the vertebra compared to proximal vertebrae, counting from the compressed vertebra. In accordance with previous studies, we believe that the UMV and USRV might play an important role in the biomechanical stability of the spine. Therefore, this retrospective study aimed to verify whether locating UIV on UMV or USRV can decrease the risk of adjacent segment degeneration (ASD) and proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK). Material and Methods: This clinical research included 82 chronic OVCF patients (18 males and 64 females) with a mean age of 62.99 ± 8.29 years and mean follow-up of 48.13 ± 20.22 months. Whole spine CT were performed for each patient. The patients were divided into groups according to whether their UIV was below the UMV or USRV. The incidence of ASD and PJK was evaluated in each subgroup. Results: The average HU value of all patients was 94.98 ± 22.76. All sagittal parameters improved significantly after operation and at follow-up. For UMV, the UIV of 41chronic OVCF patients was located on or above the UMV, while that of 41 patients was not. There was a significant difference in PJK between the two groups (12.20% vs 46.34%; p < 0.001), whereas the difference in ASD was not significant (4.88% vs 14.63%; p = 0.137). 68 patients (82.93%) were identified to have USRV. The UIV of 38 patients was located on or above the USRV while that of 30 patients was not. There were significant differences in both PJK and ASD between the two groups (PJK: 7.89% vs 63.33%; p < 0.001; ASD: 2.63% vs 23.33%; p = 0.007). Conclusion: HU values should be considered in the selection of UIV. Locating UIV on UMV might decrease the incidence of PJK and ASD, and taking USRV into the fusion level might reduce the occurrence of ASD.
