## P0575: A three-dimensional morphological assessment for evaluating pre- and post-surgery in cervical laminoplasty

Chih-Ju Chang

1,2

, Jing-Jing Fang

3

1

Cathay General Hospital, Department of Medicine, School of Medicine, Fu Jen Catholic University, Taipei, Taiwan

2

Department of Medicine, School of Medicine, Fu Jen Catholic University, New Taipei City, Taiwan

3

Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan, Taiwan

Introduction: The efficacy of medical treatments and the changes in radiologic imaging before and after treatment have consistently remained pivotal factors. This is particularly critical for surgical procedures, where precise evaluation of disparities pre and post-surgery or the accuracy of implantation is paramount. Based on three-dimensional morphological interests, we provide an automatic quantification evaluation method that delivers an evident base for assessing the outcomes of a widely employed surgical technique, cervical laminoplasty. Material and Methods: The sample study included patients who underwent cervical laminoplasty for cervical spondylotic myelopathy/ossification of the longitudinal ligament. We present a superimposition method that facilitates a unique and precise assessment between pre and post-surgery. The degree of expansion was evaluated by the canal volume increase and canal expansion rate after surgery. Results: There were 31 patients with 112 vertebral segments measured. The target cervical’s pre and post-operative canal areas were 122.63 ± 30.34 and 196.50 ± 37.10 mm2, respectively (p < 0.001). The average cervical canal expansion rate was 64.42%. The expansion effect of C5 cervical laminoplasty was the maximum (71.01%), and the canal volume of other segments expanded by approximately 60%. The functional outcomes demonstrated significant improvements in symptoms. Conclusion: The quantification evaluation method can be utilized for any morphology changes before and after laminoplasty, as it does not lead to errors or variations from different inspection machines or human factors. The automatic method delivers an evident base for assessing the outcomes of a widely employed surgical technique.

## ID: AGS26/3104

## P0576: Spinal level localisation techniques in spine surgery: a literature review and UK regional practice survey

Pranab Kairy

1

, Riaz Mohammed

1

, Jamie Ocallaghan

1

1

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

Introduction: Wrong-level spinal surgery is a significant patient-safety concern and carries substantial medicolegal implications for surgeons, hospitals, and the healthcare system as a whole. Spinal level localisation (level-check) is routinely performed peri-operatively by surgeons to ensure this is avoided. While a review of the literature describes various localisation techniques, the extent to which these are applied in routine practice is less clear. This study aims to review current localisation techniques, evaluate supporting evidence, and compare these findings with a South-West regional survey of spinal surgeons in the United Kingdom. Material and Methods: A review of the literature was conducted to identify studies detailing techniques for spinal-level localisation, categorising them into:
• Traditional methods (e.g., fluoroscopic counting),
• Preoperative marking (e.g., fiducial markers),
• Advanced intraoperative imaging (e.g., navigation),
• Emerging modalities (e.g., AI-based algorithms, ultrasound, augmented reality).
Focus was placed on accuracy, practicality, and consensus guidelines. Additionally, a concurrent regional survey among spinal surgeons in South West UK examined spinal-level localisation practices for primary lumbar surgery, including prevalence and methodology of intraoperative spinal level verification protocols utilising fluoroscopy across three critical surgical phases: pre-incision level marking, pre-decompression verification, and post-decompression confirmation. We also surveyed awareness of national and international guidance and the presence of local Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) or policies. Results:
Fluoroscopy remains the most widely used method globally but is susceptible to error in challenging cases (e.g., anatomical variations such as transitional vertebrae, difficult body habitus such as obesity, and thoracic levels), with reported accuracy of 75-80%. Surgical practice varies on the frequency and method of fluoroscopic verification. Consensus guidelines (Spinal Deformity Study Group; North American Spine Society SMaX Initiative) recommend multiple verification steps and independent radiographic review. The Cleveland Clinic protocol further proposes intraoperative independent radiologist verification.
Preoperative gold fiducial markers demonstrate superior accuracy, achieving near-perfect reliability, especially in high-risk thoracic cases.
3D imaging and navigation significantly improve accuracy over 2D fluoroscopy for complex and multilevel cases.
AI-driven image registration and real-time ultrasound are promising emerging modalities but are not yet widely adopted or validated outside specialist centres.
South West UK Regional Survey (n = 16) revealed: • 69% of respondents perform two fluoroscopic level checks per case. • Pre-incision marking is predominantly performed using a needle inserted through skin and subcutaneous tissues rather than a metal skin marker. • Level confirmation immediately before decompression most often uses a Watson Cheyne dissector on bone (75%). • Post-decompression checks are not routinely performed by half of respondents. • 15 out of 16 surgeons reported having no formal local SOP for level checking. • Awareness of national and international guidelines was low among respondents. Conclusion: While the literature strongly favours advanced, technology-driven localisation for optimal accuracy especially in complex cases, widespread implementation is limited by cost and resource constraints. Routine practice in the majority of centres still relies on multiple fluoroscopic checks, and our survey demonstrates wide variation in practices amongst spinal surgeons. Developing and implementing standardised SOPs based on consensus guidelines can improve localisation consistency and patient safety without necessitating universal adoption of high-cost modalities.

## ID: AGS26/3203

## P0577: Aneurysmal bone cyst of the spine: about four cases and review of the literature

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Omar Hattab

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Aneurysmal bone cysts (ABC) are benign, tumor-like bone lesions characterized by blood-filled channels expanding the osseous architecture. They account for approximately 1.5% of spinal bone tumors and occur mainly in young patients. We report four cases of spinal ABCs treated surgically in our department and provide a review of the literature. Material and Methods: Between 2009 and 2024, four patients (2 males, 2 females) underwent surgery for spinal ABCs. Ages were 16, 18, 26, and 52 years. Clinical, radiological, and surgical data were retrospectively analyzed. Results: The presenting symptoms were gait disturbance in two patients (one of whom presented with paraplegia) and lumbar radiculopathy in two others. The tumors were located in the dorsal spine (2 cases) and the lumbar spine (2 cases). Involvement was limited to the posterior elements in two patients, while intra-canal extension and vertebral body invasion were observed in the other two. Complete tumor excision was achieved in two cases, while the remaining two underwent decompressive laminectomy limited to the site of compression. Postoperatively, neurological symptoms improved in all patients. One patient experienced a large local recurrence requiring a second surgery with instrumented stabilization. Conclusion: Spinal ABCs are rare, benign lesions that can cause severe neurological impairment. Complete surgical excision remains the gold standard, associated with low recurrence rates and favorable outcomes. Instrumented stabilization may be required in selected cases to address postoperative instability. Our series supports the importance of radical resection whenever feasible, while highlighting the risk of recurrence in incomplete excisions.

## ID: AGS26/3242

## P0578: A rare case of spinal dural AVF presenting as Foix-Alajouanine syndrome

Kyriakos Papakonstantinou

1

, Ioannis Dravopoulos

1

, Nikolaos Paidakakos

1

, Georgios Georgoulis

1

1

Athens General Hospital ‘G. Gennimatas’, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas (sdAVFs) are an uncommon cause of venous hypertensive myelopathy (Foix-Alajouanine syndrome). Diagnosis relies on MRI and digital subtraction angiography (DSA); timely fistula occlusion improves neurological outcomes. Material and Methods: A 61-year-old man presented with 24 hours of low-back pain, rapidly progressive symmetric paraparesis, and urinary retention. Physical examination showed distal-predominant weakness, hyporeflexia with absent Achilles reflexes, and a T12 sensory level. CT was significant for spinal degenerative disease; however, MRI suggested vascular malformation. Selective DSA demonstrated a Type I sdAVF supplied by the left iliolumbar artery. Results: Microsurgical ligation via L4-L5 laminectomy with clip ligation of the arterialized draining vein at L5-S1 achieved Doppler-confirmed occlusion. Postoperatively, hip flexion and knee extension improved to 3/5 and ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion to 2/5, enabling assisted sitting and transfer to inpatient rehabilitation. Conclusion: sdAVF should be suspected in acute-subacute paraparesis with sphincter dysfunction despite degenerative imaging. Prompt DSA and definitive ligation can reverse venous congestive myelopathy and facilitate functional recovery.

## P0579: Rare sporadic cavernomatosis involving brain, spine, and radicular lesions: from misdiagnosis to surgical management

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Nessrine Nessib

1

, Insaf Dkhil

2

, Soumaya Jelassi

2

, Amine Ben Lakhel

2

, Sonia Neji

2

, Sadok Ben Amor

3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida Tunis, Tunisia, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

3

Clinique Le Bardo, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Cavernomas are rare cerebrovascular malformations, typically occurring as sporadic solitary lesions, most commonly in the cerebral hemispheric white matter, but also in infratentorial or spinal locations. Multiple lesions affecting different levels of the central nervous system are typically associated with familial forms, making sporadic cases with widespread involvement exceptionally rare. Material and Methods: A 66-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with acute-onset gait disturbances over two months, gradually improving. Examination revealed paraparesis. Initial spinal MRI revealed a right anterolateral intramedullary lesion at T6-T7 with multiple nodular lesions along the cauda equina roots from L1 to L5, leading to a preliminary diagnosis of intramedullary ependymoma with spinal metastases. Results: Subsequent multimodal MRI of the brain and spine revealed multiple supratentorial and infratentorial cavernomas, a solitary intramedullary spinal lesion, and radicular involvement, establishing the diagnosis of multiple cavernomatosis. The patient underwent complete surgical resection of the spinal lesion, resulting in significant improvement of motor deficits. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of cavernoma. Conclusion: Sporadic multiple cavernomatosis involving both brain and spinal cord is exceedingly rare and may mimic metastatic or neoplastic disease, as illustrated by this case. MRI is essential for detecting both cerebral and spinal lesions. Surgical management should be reserved for symptomatic lesions to optimize neurological outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3341

## P0580: The accuracy and reliability of ChatGPT in identifying and classifying L5-S1 spondylolisthesis

Justin Chung

1

, Rowen Lin

1

, Evan Dunn

2

, Grace Kim

3

, Kevin Mo

2

, William Fang

2

, Shanthan Challa

2

, Daniel Lee

2

1

Touro University Nevada, College of Medicine, Henderson, United States

2

Valley Hospital Medical Center, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

3

Western University of Health Science, Pomona, United States

Introduction: Chat Generative Pre-trained Transformer (ChatGPT), released in the fall of 2022, made large language models accessible worldwide. Since then, ChatGPT’s capabilities have expanded to areas including imaging and analysis across multiple various models, including the advanced reasoning o-models. The aim of this study was to determine whether ChatGPT and its o-models, across three different models, would be able to identify and classify single-level spondylolisthesis cases when presented with imaging. Material and Methods: This study utilized 30 images classified under spondylolisthesis from the VinDr-SpineXR database. These images were uploaded into ChatGPT 4o, o4 mini, and o4 mini high. Radiographic measurements of spondylolisthesis were used to grade severity by Meyerding classification. Three questions were asked to each model, all in temporary mode with memory reset after each question was prompted: 1) Can you identify the pathology in this image? 2) Can you identify where the spondylolisthesis is in this image? 3) Can you grade the level of L5-S1 spondylolisthesis in the image? Results: When given these 30 radiographs without regards to grade of spondylolisthesis, GPT 4o performed the best in correctly identifying the images at the highest rate of 30% and correct Meyerding Grading Scale at 53.3%. GPT o4-mini, however, outperformed GPT 4o in identifying the level of spondylolisthesis at 96.7% ± 18% vs 80.0% ± 41% (p = 0.045). With only Grade I spondylolisthesis, GPT 4o performed the best in initially identifying (Question 1) the image at 35.7%. When only considering Grade II spondylolisthesis, all models performed similarly when initially identifying the images (12.5% - 25%) (Question 1). GPT o4-mini correctly identified the level of spondylolisthesis for all images, while GPT 4o and o4-mini-high had a success rate of 81.3% (Question 2). ChatGPT 4o (35.7% ± 50%) was significantly outperformed when compared to GPT o4-mini in classifying Grade I spondylolisthesis (92.9% ± 27%, p < 0.001) and GPT o4-mini-high (92.9% ± 27%, p < 0.001). However, GPT 4o (68.8% ± 48%) significantly outperformed GPT o4-mini (6.3% ± 25%, p < 0.001) and GPT o4-mini-high (12.5% ± 34%, p < 0.001) in Grade II spondylolisthesis. Conclusion: In conclusion, the findings from this study are heterogeneous without any conclusive evidence to determine a superior model. Despite GPT 4o outperforming GPT o4-mini and GPT o4-mini high in the classification of Meyerding Grade II spondylolisthesis, GPT 4o was outperformed by the latter two models in the classification of Meyerding Grade I spondylolisthesis. Of note, there was a large increase in accuracy from 10%-30% to 80%-96.7% from question 1 to question 2, possibly due to learning that spondylolisthesis was in the image. Further studies should be performed looking into higher grades of spondylolisthesis, allowing the GPT models to access memory, and other spinal pathologies such as burst and compression fractures.

## ID: AGS26/3368

## P0581: A systematic review of the prevalence of degenerative thoracic spinal pathology on magnetic resonance imaging in healthy individuals

Eve Lowton

1

, Amir Rafati-Fard

1

, Eleanor White

1

, Muzammil Arif Din Abdul Jabbar

1

, Raunak Khanduja

1

, Benjamin Davies

1

, Mark Kotter

1

, Oliver Mowforth

1

1

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom

Introduction: Degenerative thoracic myelopathy (DTM) encompasses a spectrum of degenerative spinal pathologies that result in symptomatic thoracic spinal cord compression. Whilst the widespread availability of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is beneficial for diagnosis, clinical history and examination remain as important as ever in localising neurological problems. This is particularly essential in the distinction between symptomatic pathology and the increasing burden of incidental findings that result from large volumes of imaging. The prevalence and spectrum of degenerative thoracic spinal pathology in healthy individuals is currently largely uncharacterised. The aim of this systematic review was therefore to synthesise available evidence on degenerative thoracic spinal pathologies on MRI in healthy individuals and to improve understanding of the natural history of DTM. Materals and Methods: A systematic review was registered with PROSPERO (CRD42023484442) and performed following PRISMA guidelines. MEDLINE and Embase were searched from inception to March 2025. All studies investigating degenerative thoracic spinal pathologies on MRI in healthy adults were eligible for inclusion. Duplicate title and abstract screening, data extraction and risk of bias assessments were conducted. Results: Of 3574 records screened, 11 studies satisfied eligibility criteria for inclusion in the final synthesis. There were seven cross-sectional studies, three case-control studies and one cohort study. A total of 3805 individuals were included: 40.3% (1533/3805) were male, with a mean age was 58.2 years. The most prevalent degenerative pathologies identified on MRI were multilevel disc degeneration (two studies; mean prevalence of 54.2%; range 50%-55%), single level disc degeneration (four studies; mean prevalence of 29.7%; range 7%-72.5%), ossification of the ligamentum flavum (two studies; mean prevalence of 7.2%; range 2.1%-11.9%), inflammatory end-plate changes (two studies; mean prevalence of 4.5%; range 3%-20%), and disc herniation (three studies; mean prevalence of 2.5%; range 0.8%-40%). In individuals with multilevel disc pathology the affected discs were most frequently adjacent. Overall, disc degeneration increased with age and findings were more frequently seen in the lower than the upper thoracic spine. Conclusion: Degenerative disc disease is the most prevalent form of degenerative thoracic spinal pathology in healthy individuals. It increases with age and occurs most frequently in the lower thoracic spine. However, data on degenerative spinal pathology in healthy individuals is currently sparse, limiting understanding of the natural history of symptomatic disease. There was significant heterogeneity between studies and a lack of standardised terminology. Future work should include large-scale cross-sectional MRI studies of healthy individuals across wider populations and should seek to promote the use of standardised terminology by researchers and medical professionals.

## P0582: Symptomatic spinal subdural hygroma emerging decades after pediatric hydrocephalus treatment: a case report and review of the litterature

Roua Latrach

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Elouni Emna

1

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal subdural hygroma is a rare condition characterized by cerebrospinal fluid accumulation in the spinal subdural space. Its pathophysiology, natural history, and optimal management remain poorly defined. Material and Methods: A 44-year-old female, operated at age six for presumed hydrocephalus, remained asymptomatic until one year ago, when she developed progressive quadriparesis, dorsal spinal pain, and bladder dysfunction. MRI revealed a dorsal subdural hygroma with a calcified membrane causing spinal cord compression. Results: The patient was initially managed conservatively, but due to worsening pain and progressive quadriparesis, surgical resection was indicated. Conclusion: Spinal subdural hygroma is a rare, poorly understood entity. In this case, a delayed postoperative complication of childhood ventriculoperitoneal shunting is the most likely etiology. Early recognition and timely, individualized surgical intervention are essential to prevent permanent neurological deficits.

## P0583: MRI-guided femoral nerve localization: interobserver reliability study for preoperative LLIF planning

Kyle Dann

1

, Alfred-John Bayaton

2

, Ashish Patel

1

1

The Spine Center at Duly Health and Care, Naperville IL, United States

2

Kettering Health Grandview Hospital Orthopedic Surgery Residency Program, Dayton OH, United States

Introduction: Pre-operative planning for an L4-5 lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) relies heavily on MRI to characterize disc pathology, delineate the severity of stenosis, map regional vasculature, and define lumbar plexus anatomy. At our institution, femoral nerve localization is achieved by tracing the L2, L3, and L4 ventral rami on axial T2-weighted images, following each root from cranial to caudal until they converge within the psoas. This study aims to quantify the interobserver reliability of this MRI-based nerve-tracing method. Material and Methods: Fifteen patients undergoing LLIF at L4-5 were randomly selected from a prospective spine registry. Preoperative lumbar MRIs were retrospectively analyzed using Picture Archiving and Communication System (PACS). Four observers: a fellowship trained spine surgeon, neuroradiologist, PGY-3 resident orthopedic surgery resident, and postdoctoral clinical research fellow independently traced the L2-4 ventral rami on T2-weighted axial images. Reference lines were established on the most caudal axial slice intersecting the L4-5 disc: a vertical line normalized midline orientation, and a perpendicular horizontal line served as the zero-reference for anteroposterior measurements. Nerve tracing followed L2-4 fascicles from their foramina into the psoas. Interobserver reliability was calculated using intraclass correlation coefficients. Results: Interobserver reliability at L4-5 was consistently good. The anteroposterior disc-space width yielded an intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) of 0.86 (95% CI 0.82-0.97). Localization of the left femoral nerve/L2-3 bundle showed a comparable ICC of 0.85 (95 % CI 0.75-0.96), while the right-sided measurement produced an ICC of 0.83 (95% CI 0.69-0.94). According to conventional thresholds (poor < 0.50, moderate 0.50-0.75, good 0.75-0.90, excellent > 0.90), all three values fall within the good-reliability category. Conclusion: Tracing the L2-4 ventral rami on axial T2-weighted MRI provides a reproducible method for localizing the femoral nerve within the psoas at L4-5. Interobserver reliability was consistently good across four observers. This technique enables consistent identification of plexus anatomy and estimation of the femoral nerve’s anterior trajectory, supporting its use in preoperative planning for LLIF.

## ID: AGS26/3374

## P0584: Accuracy of ChatGPT 4o, o4-mini, and o4-mini-high in identifying and quantifying lumbar spine compression fractures

Justin Chung

1

, Rowen Lin

1

, Evan Dunn

2

, Grace Kim

3

, Kevin Mo

2

, William Fang

2

, Shanthan Challa

2

, Daniel Lee

2

1

Touro University Nevada, College of Medicine, Henderson, United States

2

Valley Hospital Medical Center, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

3

Western University of Health Science, Pomona, United States

Introduction: Chat Generative Pre-trained Transformer (ChatGPT) is a large language model that has expeditiously developed upon its abilities since its release in 2022. OpenAI expanded upon its line of generative pre-trained transformers with the release of complex-reasoning o-models in 2024. Newer models have been refined in their ability to assess images, including within the realm of medicine and radiological sciences. The aim of this study was to evaluate ChatGPT and OpenAI’s o-models' ability to identify and quantify compression fractures in the lumbar spine via radiograph. Material and Methods: This study utilized 24 radiographs classified under vertebral collapse from the VinDr-SpineXR database. Using the temporary chat option to avoid any machine learning throughout the study, these images were directly input into ChatGPT 4o, o4-mini, and o4-mini-high. Three questions were asked to each model, with memory reset before each inquiry: 1) “Can you find the pathology in this image?” 2) “At which segment in the lumbar spine is there a compression fracture?” 3) “Can you identify the percentage of compression in the affected vertebra?” Responses were recorded as either yes or no, depending on the accuracy of the response. Results: Overall, models ChatGPT 4o, o4-mini, and o4-mini-high performed at similar levels, correctly identifying lumbar vertebral compression fractures in 42%, 46%, and 58% of proposed images, respectively. Once prompted that there was a compression fracture, ChatGPT 4o significantly outperformed model o4-mini-high at identifying which level was affected 75% ± 44% vs 58% ± 51% (p = 0.02). Once prompted for the location of the compression fracture and comparing the percentage of compression, o4-mini-high demonstrated the highest accuracy at L1 (10/17, 59%), whereas models 4o and o4-mini displayed higher accuracy at level L2 relative to o4-mini-high (67% vs 67% vs 50%). Conclusion: In conclusion, despite having slight observable differences between the models in identifying and classifying vertebral compression fractures, there was no conclusive evidence as to the superiority of the ChatGPT model after the level of L1. While GPT 4o outperformed when prompted to identify the segment in which there was a compression fracture at the level of L1, further studies should be conducted comparing other accessible large language models, as well as the performance of ChatGPT when trained with radiographic data while accessing memory.

## P0585: Surgical management of a giant spinal aneurysmal bone cyst: a case report and literature review

Elouni Emna

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Hopital Fattouma Bourguiba, Monastir, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Aneurysmal bone cysts (ABCs) are benign, blood-filled lesions that typically arise in the metaphysis of long bones. Spinal involvement is exceedingly rare, and most cases are either untreated or managed conservatively. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 35-year-old patient presenting with refractory back pain due to a giant, locally aggressive ABC of the L1 vertebra. Previously reported spinal ABC cases were also reviewed. Results: Imaging revealed an osteolytic, expansile lesion involving the posterior arches of L1 and L2, with multiple fluid-fluid levels. The lesion extended into the spinal canal, compressing the right-sided nerve roots, and anteriorly into the right renal compartment. The patient underwent two sclerotherapy sessions to minimize operative bleeding, followed by a posterior lumbar approach for extensive excision of the posterior portion of the lesion and pedicle screw fixation. The anterior portion of the tumor was inaccessible. Histopathology confirmed the diagnosis of ABC. Postoperatively, the patient experienced complete resolution of pain and neurological deficits. Conclusion: In cases of spinal ABC causing neurological involvement or analgesic dependence, decompressive surgery is indicated to improve prognosis and quality of life. Preoperative sclerotherapy is recommended to reduce intraoperative blood loss, and spinal stabilization should be considered when instability is present.

## P0586: Autonomous, CT-only cervical stenosis triage via interpretable APD and cross-modal dual-transfer learning: a pilot study

Bryson Hewins

1

, Zoe Rudloff

2

1

Naval Medical Center San Diego, San Diego, United States

2

Uniformed Services University of the Health Sciences, Bethesda, United States

Introduction: Cervical canal stenosis is a frequent cause of radiculopathy and myelopathy and a critical concern in trauma evaluation. Although MRI is preferred for soft-tissue and cord assessment, CT is the most accessible modality in acute and resource-limited settings. CT morphometrics such as anteroposterior canal diameter (APD) (< 10 mm = severe; 10-13 mm = moderate) and the Torg-Pavlov ratio remain widely accepted, interpretable thresholds. Yet, no autonomous pipeline exists that ties CT-only analysis to these deterministic metrics, and existing AI approaches often require MRI labels or operate as opaque classifiers. We sought to develop a CT-only, fully autonomous pipeline aligned to APD thresholds and to evaluate a cross-modal, cross-region dual-transfer (MRI to CT, lumbar to cervical) learning strategy. Material and Methods: We used RSNA-2022 Cervical CT (public vertebral segmentations; no stenosis labels). The pipeline automatically localized disc levels C4/5-C6/7, established a midline corridor on the axial disc plane, and measured APD from posterior vertebral-body cortex to anterior laminar cortex. A deterministic silver standard labeled each level as moderate/severe if APD < 13 mm (severe < 10 mm). For the classifier, a ConvNeXt-Tiny backbone (initialized from RSNA-2024 Lumbar MRI when available, otherwise ImageNet) was frozen and a small head trained on CT patches to predict {moderate/severe vs none/mild}. A brief last-stage unfreeze provided a light fine-tune. Evaluation on a held-out subset (n = 32 levels) reported ROC-AUC, average precision (AP), Youden-tuned threshold performance, and Brier score. We generated publication-quality full-slice axial figures with midline APD overlays. Robustness of APD was tested to HU threshold changes (220 to 280 HU). Results: Against the silver standard (APD < 13 mm), the classifier achieved AUC = 0.801 (bootstrap 95% CI 0.571-0.968) and AP = 0.809 (95% CI 0.572-0.962). At the tuned operating point (t* = 0.794), sensitivity = 0.636, specificity = 1.000, accuracy = 0.875; Brier = 0.264. Deterministic APD was robust to HU-threshold variation with median |ΔAPD| = 0.00 mm and maximum 0.38 mm. Representative axial figures show correct level localization and midline-centric APD overlays on full slices. Conclusion: An autonomous, CT-only, APD-aligned pipeline can triage cervical canal stenosis with transparent, threshold-based outputs. Incorporating cross-modal dual-transfer learning provides a robust feasibility baseline for trauma and MRI-limited environments. External validation against expert labels and integration of the Torg-Pavlov ratio are planned.

## ID: AGS26/38

## P0587: Primary cervical spine osteomyelitis and spondylodiscitis due to cutibacterium acnes: a literature review and implications for clinical practice

Riaz Mohammed

1

, Pranav Shah

2

1

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

2

Namaha Specialised Healthcare Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Cutibacterium acnes (C. acnes) is an anaerobic Gram-positive bacterium gaining notoriety for spinal osteomyelitis and spondylodiscitis. Exiting evidence suggested that this microbe shows a prevalence rate ranging from 13% to 44% for colonising intervertebral discs. Most C. acnes spinal infections reported in literature occur following invasive interventions, with presence of bioimplants a significant risk factor. There is however paucity of evidence of primary spondylodiscitis or osteomyelitis from P. acnes. Given that this cutaneous commensal is more common in upper torso and head-neck region, we conducted a literature review of primary cervical spine osteomyelitis and spondylodiscitis from C. acnes. The objectives were to understand risk factors, clinical presentation, disease identification and management for the benefit of spinal surgeons. Methods: PubMed database was searched for all articles till date in English language dealing with human population for cervical spine primary infections associated with C. acnes anaerobe. All reports of such infection after any invasive intervention on the spine were excluded. Full text of relevant articles was then read to identify additional publications on this condition. Results: Literature search revealed only few case series and case reports totalling 12 cases of non-surgery related primary cervical spine infection from C. acnes suggesting an extremely rare condition. All cervical spine levels including atlanto-axial spine, and all age groups including adolescent patient were reported. Unlike post-operative C. acnes spinal infections, primary infections are more likely in immunocompromised patients. Delay in diagnosis is common due to atypical indolent clinical presentation, non-specific imaging features and absence of significant elevation of biochemical markers of inflammation/infection. More severe bony destruction is a possibility due to these factors; with reports in literature of cervical infection associated with synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, and osteitis (SAPHO syndrome) described. Cutibacterium is reported to cause intervertebral disc cell apoptosis of nucleus pulposus, and hence lesser associated tissue inflammation. Paravertebral and epidural inflammation are not typically seen on imaging. Enrichment broth extended-period (14 days) cultures and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests are necessary to confirm the diagnosis. Anti-microbial therapy is required for longer period (8-12 weeks), and surgical intervention is often (75%) necessary to address spinal pathology due to the sequelae of infection. Conclusion: Cutibacterium acnes is often under-recognised and surgeons must be vigilant due to its potential as a differential cause of spinal infections. Unlike other aerobic pathogens commonly associated with vertebral osteomyelitis, patients with C. acnes may manifest atypical clinical presentation and investigations. Due to the rarity of spontaneous-onset C. acnes spondylodiscitis, further large prospective multi-centre studies may give more evidence of epidemiology, optimal diagnosis and management measures.

## ID: AGS26/65

## P0588: The SIE score: a diagnostic tool for infective endocarditis in patients with spondylodiscitis

Artem Ilgeldiev

1

, Victoria Yushenko

1

1

Neurosurgery, Germany

Introduction: Concomitant spondylodiscitis and infective endocarditis (IE) can complicate diagnosis and delay treatment. The SIE Score offers a structured diagnostic framework to estimate IE probability in spinal infection cases. Material and Methods: In a retrospective study of 97 patients (8 with IE), clinical, laboratory, and radiologic data were evaluated. A scoring system with major (2 points) and minor (1 point) criteria was developed based on statistically significant predictors. Diagnostic performance was assessed via ROC analysis. Results: The SIE Score is composed of the following statistically significant criteria: Major criteria (2 points each): - Radiological evidence of spinal infection (p < 0.001), - Cardiac history (p = 0.005), - Persistent CRP elevation post-op (> 10 days) (p = 0.018), - Renal insufficiency (creatinine > 1.5 mg/dL) (p = 0.035). Minor criteria (1 point each): - Positive blood cultures, - Multisegmental involvement, - Thoracic localization. A cut-off score ≥ 6 demonstrated 83% sensitivity and 90% specificity (AUC 0.87). Conclusion: The SIE Score enhances early detection of IE in spondylodiscitis patients and complements the Duke criteria. It is especially useful when cardiological diagnostics are delayed or limited.
Ethics Committee Approval: 2021-702-f-S

## P0589: Outbreak of enterobacter kobei infections linked with a collagen-based adhesion barrier following lumbar disc surgery: an epidemiological and molecular study

Merve Turker Isik

1

, Yasemin Cezaroglu

1

, Fatih Tomakin

2

, Murat Buyuktepe

3

1

Unye State Hospital, Ordu, Turkey

2

Samsun University, Samsun, Turkey

3

Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Postoperative infections following spine surgeries are typically caused by skin flora or hospital-acquired pathogens. We report a novel outbreak of Enterobacter kobei infections linked to a collagen-based adhesion barrier. The goal of this study was to identify the source of increased surgical site infections in lumbar disc herniation (LDH) surgeries and to describe the outbreak investigation and containment measures implemented. Material and Methods: A multidisciplinary outbreak team conducted epidemiological, environmental, and microbiological investigations at a secondary care hospital in Turkey. Molecular typing using Pulsed-Field Gel Electrophoresis (PFGE) was employed to assess the clonal relationships of the isolates. Results: Between May 19 and June 9, 2023, 10 out of 12 patients who underwent LDH surgery developed surgical site infections. Environmental cultures tested negative. Enterobacter cloacae was isolated from wound samples, blood, and collagen-based adhesion barrier samples. Isolates sent to the National Public Health Laboratory were identified as Enterobacter kobei. PFGE analysis revealed two distinct but clonally related groups. The outbreak was contained following a nationwide recall of the adhesion barrier. Conclusion: This incident marks the first reported outbreak of Enterobacter kobei associated with a surgical adhesion barrier. This highlights the need for vigilant infection control and careful monitoring of non-sterilized surgical materials to prevent similar events in the future.

## ID: AGS26/197

## P0590: Impact of comorbidities, demographic factors and antibiotic use on survival outcomes in patients with hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis: a cox proportional Hazards model analysis

Devender Singh

1

, Eeric Truumees

1

, John Stokes

1

, Matthew Geck

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: Hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis presents significant challenges in clinical management due to its complex pathology and variable patient outcomes. This study aims to assess the impact of comorbidities, demographics, and the length of antibiotic usage on survival rates in patients with hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis. We hypothesize that the presence of comorbidities, Body Mass Index (BMI)and extended antibiotic treatment positively correlate with improved survival outcomes in these patients, whereas male gender may be associated with increased mortality. Material and Methods: Data from patients treated for hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis were analyzed using a Cox Proportional Hazards Model. BMI, sex, antibiotic usage post-operation, and the presence of comorbidities were collected. Survival curves were generated using Kaplan-Meier estimates, and differences between groups were assessed with log-rank tests. The study further examined survival impact by gender and BMI through stratified and multivariable analyses. The model's assumptions were verified using time-dependent covariate tests, and the fit was evaluated using concordance index and partial AIC. Results: The study included 88 patients, with a median follow-up of 60 days. Of these, 6 events (deaths) were observed. The survival analysis revealed significant survival differences based on the length of post-operative antibiotic use and the presence of comorbidities. The cohort’s survival probability was robust, with a median survival time reported as infinite, signaling a high long-term survival rate. By day 30, the survival probability stood at approximately 79.7%, and this figure remained consistent through day 60, indicating stability in patient survival post the initial treatment month. The Cox model adjusted for age and sex showed that male patients had a higher risk of mortality (HR: 18.58; 95% CI: 0.74, 467.84; p = 0.08), while longer antibiotic usage was associated with reduced mortality (HR: 0.92; p = 0.03) suggesting an 8% reduction in risk per unit increase in treatment duration. The model's concordance index was 0.85, indicating a good predictive ability. BMI presented a protective trend, albeit non-significantly (HR = 0.92, p = 0.29), suggesting a potential but uncertain protective effect. Conclusion: The study confirms the critical role of post-operative antibiotic treatment duration and the presence of comorbidities in influencing survival outcomes in patients with hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis. Effective management of these factors within the first 30 days is crucial for improving patient survival. While BMI showed a non-significant protective trend, the pronounced risk associated with male gender highlights a need for targeted interventions during the critical initial treatment phase. Tailored management strategies that extend beyond standard protocols and consider individual patient profiles - particularly antibiotic duration, gender, and BMI - are crucial in optimizing treatment outcomes in hematogenous spinal osteomyelitis. This approach not only addresses immediate survival but also improves long-term patient prognosis.

## ID: AGS26/254

## P0591: Changes in the microbial spectrum of pyogenic spondylodiscitis in Germany: a nationwide analysis from 2005 to 2023

Simon Amberger

1

, Frank Hanses

2

, Siegmund Lang

3

, Nils-Ole Schmidt

1

, Sebastian Siller

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

2

Department of Hospital Infection Control, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

3

Department of Trauma Surgery, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

Introduction: Pyogenic spondylodiscitis is a rare but potentially life-threatening spinal infection. Understanding national trends in pathogen distribution and resistance patterns is essential for optimizing diagnosis, therapy, and infection control strategies. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective analysis of nationwide hospital discharge data in Germany from 2005 to 2023. The study included all inpatient cases with a primary diagnosis of ICD-10 codes M46.2*, M46.3*, or M46.4*, in combination with secondary codes (A49, B95-B98, U80-U83) indicating identified pathogens classified as follows: 1) Staphylococcus aureus, 2) Other/unspecified Staphylococci, 3) Streptococci, 4) Escherichia coli and other Enterobacterales, 5) Pseudomonas and other non-fermenters, 6) Other gram-positive pathogens, 7) Others. The data were sourced from the DRG statistics, comprising all fully hospitalized cases reimbursed via diagnosis-related groups. Results: A total of 150,598 patients were identified. Annual case numbers of pyogenic spondylodiscitis with a documented pathogen increased steadily from 4,393 in 2005 to 9,923 in 2023, corresponding to a mean annual growth rate of 4.47% (range: 1.16-12.41%). Incidence increased from 6.67 to 14.62 per 100,000 inhabitants, with an average annual increase of 4.6%. Mortality rose from 3.4% to 6.9%, with an average annual increase of 4.3%. Over the 19-year period, the relative proportion of Staphylococcus aureus declined from 31.4% to 16.0% (mean annual decline rate: 3.6%, range: 0.7-12.5%), while proportions of Streptococci (from 9.2% to 14.4%; mean annual rate of increase: 2.91%, range 2.7-25%), Other/unspecified Staphylococci (from 20.5% to 24.2%; mean annual rate of increase: 1.1%, range 0-10.1%), and Other gram-positive pathogens (from 1.2% to 4.4%; mean annual rate of increase 8.8%, range 5.4-58.3%) increased. Consequently, whereas S. aureus was the most prevalent pathogen in 2005, Other/unspecified Staphylococci predominated in 2023. Analysis of resistance-associated pathogens (ICD U80* and U81*) revealed a marked decrease in MRSA prevalence from 7.3% to 1.6% (mean annual decline rate: 6.9%, range 2.2-28.8%), but an increase in multidrug-resistant gram-negative pathogens (i.e. 3MRGN/4MRGN and ESBL-producing Escherichia coli) from 0.5% to 2.4% (mean annual rate of increase: 16.4%, range 10.3-100%) and vancomycin-resistant Enterococci (VRE) from 0.6% to 1.8%d (mean annual increase 24.8%, range 12.5-250%). Conclusion: In patients hospitalized with pyogenic spondylodiscitis, the relative Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA prevalence declined markedly over time, while the relative prevalence of Other/unspecified Staphylococci and multidrug-resistant gram-negative pathogens and VRE increased in the past two decades in Germany. These trends reflect a shift in resistance patterns that complicate empirical therapy and underline the need for ongoing microbiological surveillance.

## ID: AGS26/467

## P0592: CT-guided percutaneous needle-biopsy sampling in suspected spondylodiscitis: microbiological yield, consequent antimicrobial treatment and relationship with outcome

Hazem Elsheikh

1

, Mohammed Shamseldin

1

, Ralf Puls

1

1

Helios Clinics Erfurt, Erfurt, Germany

Introduction: Spondylodiscitis is an infection affecting the vertebral endplates and intervertebral discs, potentially causing severe neurological deficits, debilitating pain, or death. It may extend to paravertebral, epidural, and posterior spinal elements. Elevated inflammatory markers such as erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) often support suspicion of infection. Imaging-particularly contrast-enhanced MRI, which has high sensitivity and specificity-plays a key role in diagnosis, though histological and microbiological sampling remains the gold standard. Pathogen identification enables targeted antimicrobial therapy, potentially avoiding surgery. However, recent studies have questioned the diagnostic yield of image-guided percutaneous biopsies, even with strong clinical suspicion. Materials and Methods: Eighteen patients with suspected spondylodiscitis underwent CT-guided percutaneous biopsy. Pre-biopsy MRI scans were evaluated for disc, vertebral, epidural, and paravertebral inflammatory changes. CT images were assessed for endplate erosion and disc height loss. Blood cultures were collected before biopsy. Microbiological analysis was performed on all biopsy samples, and histological analysis in three cases. Positive biopsy and blood culture rates were calculated, and correlation between the two-particularly pathogen concordance-was analyzed. The presence of other infections was noted. Inflammatory markers were tracked before and after antimicrobial therapy. Following treatment, the number of patients requiring surgery was recorded, and MRI features were evaluated for their predictive value in determining surgical need. Results: Biopsy sampling was standardized, with each patient providing three specimens (∼2 cm each) via needle biopsy.
• Biopsy cultures were positive in 9 of 18 patients (50%), with 5 (55.5%) yielding gram-positive Staphylococcus species.
• Blood cultures were positive in 11 of 18 patients (61.1%), of which only 3 (27.3%) showed concordant pathogens with biopsy results.
• Among positive blood cultures, 9 patients (81.8%) had Staphylococcus species; 8 of these (72.7%) had additional concurrent infections.
• Seventeen patients received antimicrobial therapy, leading to marked reductions in inflammatory markers. Despite treatment, 6 patients (33.3%) required surgical intervention, half of whom had paravertebral infiltration on pre-treatment MRI. Conclusion: Spondylodiscitis, though rare, is increasingly recognized and carries significant clinical risk. Diagnosis relies on clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings. While blood cultures are frequently used, they may be unreliable due to low concordance with biopsy results and frequent concurrent infections. In our study, only 27.3% of positive blood cultures matched biopsy organisms. Despite a moderate microbiological yield, CT-guided percutaneous biopsy remains preferable to open biopsy due to its safety and cost-effectiveness. Some patients had concurrent infections, and withholding antibiotics prior to biopsy-typically recommended to improve culture yield-was not feasible in all cases. Biopsies performed during ongoing antibiotic therapy may have lowered diagnostic yield. The impact of suspending antibiotics or increasing tissue volume for sampling remains uncertain.

## ID: AGS26/589

## P0593: Cervical epidural abscess: a systematic review

Mark Ehioghae

1

, Kevin Yoon

1

, Jonathan Japa

1

, Addisu Mesfin

2

1

Medstar Washington Hospital Center, Medstar Orthopaedic Institute, Washington, United States

2

Medstar Washington Hospital Center, Georgetown University School of Medicine, Medstar Orthopaedic Institute, Washington, United States

Introduction: Cervical spinal epidural abscess (CSEA) is rare but potentially devastating, accounting for 15-20% of all spinal epidural abscesses. Compared with thoracic or lumbar locations, CSEA is associated with a narrower therapeutic window and greater risk of rapid neurological decline. While surgical intervention is the predominant management strategy reported in the literature, the optimal approach, timing, and outcomes remain incompletely defined. Material and Methods: We systematically searched PubMed and Embase (January 1995-June 2025) using terms related to cervical spinal epidural abscess and surgical management. Retrospective or prospective cohort studies and case series involving adults with surgically treated CSEA were included. Single case reports, pediatric cohorts, non-English studies, and spondylodiscitis without epidural extension were excluded. Fourteen studies encompassing 298 patients met inclusion. Data on demographics, comorbidities, microbiology, surgical techniques, timing, outcomes, and complications were extracted and synthesized descriptively. Results: The weighted mean age was 56.7 years, with 66.2% male. Common comorbidities included diabetes (23%), intravenous drug use (26%), chronic kidney disease/hemodialysis (38%), and immunosuppression/malignancy (11%). The most frequent symptoms were neck pain (85%) and neurological deficit (79%). Abscesses were ventral in 67%, dorsal in 22%, and circumferential in 12%. Staphylococcus aureus was the predominant organism (MSSA 31%, MRSA 17%), followed by streptococci (8%), gram-negative bacilli (5%), and Brucella (3%); cultures were negative in 4%. Anterior decompression and fusion was the most common surgical approach, with posterior or combined procedures used selectively. Across studies reporting timing, 52 patients underwent urgent decompression ≤48 h, 4 within 48-72 h, and 18 >72 h (often after failed medical therapy). Neurological improvement was observed in most cohorts, with complete recovery rates ranging from 50-93%. Complications were infrequent: surgical-site infection (∼1-2%), hardware failure (∼1%), postoperative kyphosis (∼3%), and dysphagia (2 cases). Mortality was ∼1.7% (5/298), largely in older or comorbid patients. Conclusion: CSEA remains a surgical disease, with anterior decompression and fusion most frequently performed. Early surgery (≤ 48 h) is associated with favorable neurological outcomes, while delayed intervention carries higher risk of persistent deficits. Compared with thoracic and lumbar SEA, cervical cases appear to have slightly lower mortality but demand prompt recognition and urgent treatment due to rapid neurologic deterioration. Larger multicenter studies are needed to better define timing thresholds and refine surgical decision-making, particularly in elderly or medically complex patients.

## ID: AGS26/666

## P0594: Beyond the hunch: navigating the landscape of post TB - Kyphosis

Md. Shah Alam

1

, Md. Ziaul Hasan

2

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedic Rehabilitation, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Kyphosis is one of the most common complications of spinal tuberculosis. Patients: are never satisfied with the residual kyphosis even If the disease is cured or arrested. Patients with hunchback live secluded lives both mentally & socially. To achieve correction & prevention surgery is desirable. Nonetheless, satisfactory surgical correction is very difficult & dangerous as well. Material and Methods: This prospective case series was conducted in a tertiary level hospital & in private hospital from January 2003 to July 2025. Most of the patients operated posteriorly to achieve correction. Correction of deformity was easier in wet TB than the dry TB. Global reconstruction was done using single posterior approach. Results: This study comprises 107 cases (M = 40, F = 67), with an average age of 17 years. Mean kyphosis angle was 85+9° preoperatively which came down to 13‡7° finally. There was no major complication. None of the patients develop or worsened neurologically after surgery. Finally patient achieved satisfactory cosmetic appearance. Conclusion: Prevention of deformity should be the primary aim. Long standing severe kyphosis produces painful costopelvic impingement, reduced vital capacity, lumbar canal stenosis & late onset paraplegia. With improved surgical technique & rigid spinal instrumentation involving three column - posterior approach is gold standard for prevention & correction of kyphosis

## ID: AGS26/744

## P0595: Spinal hydatidosis in Southern Chile: clinical experience from an endemic region

Paloma Escalante

1,2

, Hector Escalante

1,2

, Jaime Leñero Navarrete

2,3

1

Universidad de La Frontera, Hospital Dr. Hernán Henríquez Aravena, Temuco, Chile

2

Hospital Dr. Hernán Henríquez Aravena, Temuco, Chile

3

Universidad de La Frontera, Temuco, Chile

Introduction: Hydatidosis, a parasitic infection caused by Echinococcus granulosusis, is endemic in regions with abundant livestock. Due to its presence in the entrails of farm animals it is often consumed by farm dogs which then in turn facilitates transmission to humans. This parasite can affect virtually any organ in humans, though it is predominantly found in the liver and lungs. Although the most frequent osseous manifestation is located in the spine, there has been limited documentation of this in clinical settings. Southern Chile is known for having a high epidemiology level of hydatidosis making it ideal for this study. Even though the spine is frequently infected there is a noticeable lack of publications regarding hydatidosis in this area. The objective of this study is to describe a series of vertebral hydatidosis cases in order to characterize the clinical manifestations, findings in imaging, and outcomes of surgical intervention in southern Chile. Material and Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted with patients that had histological confirmation of vertebral hydatidosis. These patients were treated at the Neurosurgery Unit of Dr. Hernán Henríquez Aravena Hospital in the city of Temuco, Chile. The information collected included demographic data (age and sex), affected vertebral level, clinical presentation, magnetic resonance imaging findings, surgical technique employed, and pharmacological treatment. Results: Four patients were identified 2 of which were female with a mean age 67 years. It was found that thoracolumbar involvement was present in every patient. The initial symptom in 50% of cases was axial pain, while neurological deficit was present in all patients. Computed tomography revealed cystic lesions with spinal canal invasion and bone erosion, whereas magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated multilocular lesions, hypointense on T1, hyperintense on T2, with peripheral enhancement. All patients underwent decompressive laminectomy with excision of multiple hydatid cysts, but two required spinal instrumentation. All patients received perioperative albendazole. Definitive analysis of recurrence and functional outcomes is ongoing, with one patient showing complete remission after 13 years of follow-up since the initial surgery. Conclusion: Although rare worldwide, vertebral hydatidosis remains a therapeutic challenge in contemporary neurosurgical practice. The systematic collection of cases over thirteen years allowed the establishment of a nationally relevant series in Chile. This allowed the refinement of diagnostic and therapeutic protocols, and the contribution of evidence that complements the limited international literature already present. The experiences presented here extends beyond the local context and represents a meaningful contribution to the global scientific community, providing clinical and surgical insights into a rare condition whose accumulated knowledge is essential for improving early diagnosis, complex surgical management, and recurrence prevention.

## ID: AGS26/807

## P0596: Composite diagnostic approach in spinal tuberculosis

Mario Benvenutti-Regato

1,2

, Misael Salazar-Alejo

1,2

, Ana Ballesteros-Suarez

1,2

, Hector Martinez

1,2

, Michel Martínez-Reséndez

1,2

, Jose Figueroa-Sanchez

1,2

1

Tecnologico de Monterrey, Monterrey, Mexico

2

Centro Médico Zambrano-Hellion, San Pedro Garza García, Mexico

Introduction: Spinal tuberculosis (STB) accounts for ∼50% of skeletal TB and 10-15% of extrapulmonary TB cases. Diagnosis remains challenging due to nonspecific symptoms, imaging findings that overlap with pyogenic infections, and the paucibacillary nature of extrapulmonary TB that limits diagnostic yields from smear, culture, and even PCR. Best practice requires a composite diagnostic approach integrating clinical context, imaging, histopathology, mycobacterial culture, and molecular assays, as no single negative test can reliably exclude STB. Moreover, Interferon-Gamma Release Assays (IGRAs) provide supportive evidence of M. tuberculosis infection but are not site-specific and should be interpreted alongside lesion-based testing. When clinical suspicion remains high, negative nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) or culture results should not rule out disease, and management should be guided by the totality of clinical-radiologic findings and, when necessary, a therapeutic trial. Material and Methods: A 72-year-old male with prior lumbar spinal fusion performed 10 years earlier presented with a two-month history of progressive lumbar pain and bilateral lower extremity weakness. Physical examination revealed intermittent paresthesias and 4/5 motor strength bilaterally in L3-S1 distributions. Lumbar spine MRI demonstrated spondylodiscitis at L3-L4 with associated posterior epidural empyema. The patient underwent surgical debridement including hardware removal, L3-L4 laminectomy extension with bilateral foraminotomy, and partial L3-L4 discectomy. Tissue biopsies were obtained from both L3 and L4 vertebral bodies for microbiological analysis. The infectious diseases team initiated empirical treatment for pyogenic spondylodiscitis pending culture results. Initial laboratory studies including Gram stain, KOH preparation, Ziehl-Neelsen staining, and bacterial cultures were negative. Interestingly, tuberculosis PCR from tissue samples was also negative, despite a positive QuantiFERON assay. Based on clinical presentation, imaging findings, and positive QuantiFERON results, tuberculous spondylodiscitis was diagnosed and anti-tuberculous therapy was initiated. Results: The patient was discharged on postoperative day 4 for outpatient management. At the 2-week follow-up, he reported persistent but improved back pain, likely related to spinal instability following hardware removal, and mild residual radicular symptoms from the surgical procedure. Despite these expected postoperative symptoms, the patient demonstrated overall clinical improvement with resolution of neurological deficits. Anti-tuberculous therapy was continued with planned reassessment for spinal reconstruction after completion of at least 3 months of antimicrobial treatment. Conclusion: This case illustrates the value of composite diagnostic strategies for STB. Because extrapulmonary TB is characteristically paucibacillary and tissue sampling may be suboptimal, negative NAAT, smear, or culture results cannot reliably exclude STB. Evidence-based practice integrates clinical and radiological features with histopathology, mycobacterial culture, and molecular assays, wherein positive NAAT/culture supports confirmation, but negative results require cautious interpretation. Emerging evidence indicates that combined approaches enhance diagnostic sensitivity and reduce time-to-diagnosis compared to culture alone. In our patient, the convergence of characteristic MRI findings, clinical context, positive IGRA, and objective clinical improvement with anti-tuberculous therapy substantiated the diagnosis of STB. Clinicians should therefore prioritize composite diagnostic standards and timely treatment initiation over deferring therapy while pursuing definitive single-test confirmation in this disease context.

## ID: AGS26/810

## P0597: Posterior approach with preservation of posterior spinal structures for vertebral subtotal resection in thoracolumbar tuberculosis with spinal cord injury: a retrospective clinical study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Boxing Wang

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Surgical management of thoracolumbar tuberculosis with spinal cord injury remains challenging due to the risks of spinal instability and inadequate decompression. Anterior and combined approaches are associated with significant morbidity, while traditional posterior methods often compromise posterior spinal integrity. This study evaluates the clinical efficacy of a modified posterior approach that preserves posterior spinal structures while enabling thorough decompression and reconstruction. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 21 patients (13 male, 8 female; mean age 49.7 years) with thoracolumbar tuberculosis and spinal cord injury treated between 2013 and 2019. All patients underwent vertebral subtotal resection and anterior decompression via a posterior approach while preserving the posterior ligamentous complex, laminae, and facet joints. Preoperative and postoperative spinal canal dimensions (anteroposterior diameter and cross-sectional area), ASIA impairment scale, and JOA scores were compared. Statistical analysis was performed using paired t-tests with SPSS 17.0. Results: All patients were followed for an average of 2.4 years (range: 2-4 years). Significant improvement was observed in spinal canal dimensions: the anteroposterior diameter ratio increased from 75.37 ± 4.16% to 98.45 ± 1.55% (p < 0.001), and the cross-sectional area ratio improved from 77.26 ± 2.93% to 98.67 ± 0.48% (p < 0.001). JOA scores improved from 6.01 ± 4.32 preoperatively to 10.2 ± 0.77 at 6 months postoperatively (p < 0.001). All 21 patients improved to ASIA grade E by 6 months. One case of pleural rupture leading to pneumothorax was successfully managed with drainage, and one case of delayed wound healing resolved with dressing changes. Conclusion: The posterior approach with preservation of posterior spinal structures allows for effective decompression, debridement, and reconstruction in thoracolumbar tuberculosis with spinal cord injury, while maintaining spinal stability. This technique significantly improves neurological outcomes and reduces complications, offering a viable alternative to conventional anterior or combined approaches.
Keywords: Spinal tuberculosis, posterior approach, vertebral subtotal resection, spinal cord injury, decompression, posterior structure preservation, ASIA score, JOA score

## ID: AGS26/969

## P0598: The use of 18F FDG PET - Computed tomography in the diagnosis and management of spinal implant infection

Muhammad Haruna

1

, Luke Marsh

2

, Marcus De Matas

3

1

Mersey and West Lancashire Teaching Hospitals, Liverpool, United Kingdom

2

The Walton Centre, Liverpool, United Kingdom

3

The Walton Centre NHS Trust, Liverpool, United Kingdom

Introduction: Spinal implant infection can be a cause of post-operative back pain in the presence of a well healed wound. 2-deoxy-2-[18F]fluoro-D-glucose positron emission tomography (18F FDG PET) (PET CT) has been shown to be useful in diagnosing spinal implant infection. If results are indeterminate however, a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) with gadolinium (GAD) can be useful. Material and Methods: Retrospective case series of patients who underwent PET CT to investigate for spinal implant infection between 1/1/2020 - 15/9/2024. Imaging reports, clinic letters, biochemistry and case notes were reviewed. All PET CT scans were reported by a Consultant Radiologist and patients clinically reviewed by a Spinal Consultant. Results: Total of 150 PET CT scans were performed. 117 patients had scans demonstrating no active infection. 16 patients had confirmed infection on PET CT, for which 6 patients underwent surgery; 3 wound washout, debridement and implant retention, 2 implant exchange and 1 implant removal. The rest received long term antibiotics.17 patients had non-conclusive PET CT. Of these, 16 had a further MRI with Gadolinium. 3 patients had confirmed infection on MRI with GAD, for which 1 patient underwent surgery for washout, debridement and implant retention. The rest received long term antibiotics. All patients that underwent surgery also received antibiotics and there were no cases of missed spinal implant infection when using PET CT combined with MRI. Conclusion: PET CT is useful imaging modality for suspected spinal implant infection. In cases where it is inconclusive then MRI with GAD is a useful next line investigation.

## ID: AGS26/1004

## P0599: Carbon fiber-reinforced polyetheretherketone implants versus titanium implants for the surgical management of cervical spondylodiscitis

Magomed Lepshokov

1

, Joachim Oertel

1

1

Medical School of the Saarland University, Homburg, Germany

Introduction: cervical spondylodiscitis associated with high mortality. Through evaluate new technologies we have nowdasy different materials to use in spine stabilization. The study performed to evaluate and compare the clinical, radiological outcomes, savety and diagnostic imaging quality of Titanium Plates with PEEK or Titanium Cages (Ti-PEEK) versus Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Polyetheretherketone (CFR-PEEK) Plates and Cages in the surgical management of cervical spondylodiscitis. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted by patients who underwent anterior cervical debridement and stabilization from 2013 to 2024. Patient records, pre- and postoperative imaging, repeat surgeries and complications were retrospectively anlyzed. Results: Thirty patients met the inclusion criteria: 15 patients were operated with Ti-PEEK and 15 patients with CFR-PEEK impalnats. The mean age of the patients was 64.5 ± 10.8 years. The most primary symptom was neck pain in 28 patients (93.3%), focal neurological symptoms observed in 14 patients (43.3%). Preoperative assessment classified the severity of the condition as ASA II in 16 patients (53.3%), ASA III in 9 patients (30%) and ASA IV in 5 patients (16.7%). There was no significant differences between groups (p > 0.05). In most cases (24/30) Spondylodiscitis was observed in one segment, a prevertebral abscess was detected in 8/30 cases (27%) and epidural empyema in 24/30 patients (80%). Preoperative CT scans revealed vertebral destruction in 4/30 patients (13.3%) and endplate erosion in 10/30 patients (33.3%). No difference between gropus (p > 0.05). The mean duration from admission to surgery was 1.63 ± 1.94 days. Surgical interventions using Plate and Cage (ACDF + CP) were performed on 27 patients, while 3 patients underwent ACCF + CP. The plate was used in all cases to achieve the stability. The most frequently identified pathogen was Staphylococcus aureus. Three reoperations were performed on the operated segment or in the region of the previous surgical approach, all in the CFR-PEEK group. Patients remained in the clinic until their condition stabilized. After treatment, 10 patients were discharged to outpatient care, 19 were transferred for further therapy to local hospitals or rehabilitation clinics, and one patient had an exitus letalis. The average hospital stay was 15.3 ± 20 days, with the longest stay being 120 days due to the adjustment of antibiotic therapy and a second surgery. Follow-up after discharge was available for 19 patients. Overall, radiological assessments after the operation with cervical spine CT were available for 18 patients (60%), and cervical spine MRI for 21 patients (70%). In T1 modes with contrast enhancement in the Ti-PEEK group the informational value was low in the prevertebral are, disc space, and ventral subarachnoid space due to artifacts from the titanium plate. Assessment of the spinal cord was also impossible in one patient. The dorsal subarachnoid space and posterior structures of the cervical spine were sufficiently well-represented for analysis. The CFR-PEEK group achieved the maximum score in each zone, demonstrating high informational value in T1-weighted MRI with contrast enhancement. The T2-weighted MRI in the Ti-PEEK group was not informative in the prevertebral and disc space areas. However, the examination of the ventral subarachnoid space was just as informative as in the CFR-PEEK group. Examination of all other areas in both groups was possible with high diagnostic value. In the follow-up, cervical spine MRI was available for 17 patients (56.67%). The average timing for MRI was 76.12 ± 48.56 days. Cervical spine CT was available in follow-up for 11 patients (36.67%), and another 8 patients (26.67%) had X-ray examinations. Both implant groups showed similar clinical outcomes, in Ti-PEEK 7/8 and CFR-PEEK 10/11 achieving an Odom Score of 1 and 2 at the first follow-up (p = 0.715). Conclusion: While clinical outcomes were comparable, CFR-PEEK implants yielded higher MRI informational value, enhancing postoperative diagnostics and follow-up in cervical spondylodiscitis patients. The presence of reoperations in the CFR-PEEK group highlights the need for careful monitoring in this patient population.

## ID: AGS26/1015

## P0600: Initial bone destruction status and quality of fusion after surgical repair in inaugural spinal tuberculosis

Amin Henine

1

1

Orthopedic A Department, University Center Hospital of Douera, Medical School University of Blida 1 Algeria, Algiers, Algeria

Introduction: Early tuberculous spondylodiscitis sometimes causes significant bone damage from the outset, which can lead to serious complications such as instability, paralysis, and deformity. Medication alone is not always enough to guarantee a cure. The aim of this study is to assess the healing based on the extent of the initial lesion, within the context of surgery. Materials and Methods: A prospective study was conducted on a series of 21 patients. There were 19 women and 2 men. The average age was 42 years (19-67 years). There were 2 cervical locations, 8 thoracic, 7 thoracolumbar and 4 lumbar. There were 14 neurological cases including 7 complicated by a gibbosity, 2 non-deficient kyphosis and 5 unstable spines with risk of worsening. Instrumentation and arthrodesis were systematic and all were previously under specific medical treatment administered after anatomopathological confirmation of the affection. A complete radiological assessment was the rule for all patients. Each had standard radiographs, telemetry, a CT scan and an MRI. The evaluation focused on the number of destroyed vertebrae, the affected column in the anteroposterior plane, the extent of the vertebral body damage, the signs of spine at risk as described by Rajasekaran, as well as the existence of contiguous and skip lesions. Time and rate of fusion according to Bridwell grade were calculated. Results: The mean follow-up was 32.7 months ± 19.7 (7 to 72 months). 52.4% of patients, or 11 cases, had 2 or 3 destroyed vertebrae, and 42.9%, or 9 cases, had more than 3. In 57.1% of the cases, or 12 patients, there was mainly combined anterior and middle column involvement. More than half of the vertebral body was destroyed in 13 patients, or 61.9% of cases. 15 cases (71.4%) had two radiographic criteria of destabilization, and 5 cases (23.8%) had three. Lesions were contiguous in 20 patients (95.2%). They were also found non-contiguous in 8 cases (38.1%). Consolidation was complete for 18 patients (85.7%). The mean fusion time was 10.1 months ± 2.9 (5 months -15 months). 66.7% of patients, or 14 cases, fused between 6 months and one year. Conclusion: Involvement of multiple vertebrae was common, as were initially destructive forms. Instability was often evident and significant. CT and MRI were crucial for diagnosis and treatment strategy. Healing was generally the rule in our patients thanks to surgery and anti-tuberculosis drugs. Indeed, the time and rate of fusion were in accordance with what is reported in the literature.

## ID: AGS26/1018

## P0601: Sagittal tilt in T9 and T1 pre and postoperatively in acute Pott's disease

Amin Henine

1

1

Orthopedic A Department, University Center Hospital of Douera, Medical School University of Blida 1 Algeria, Algiers, Algeria

Introduction: The deformity observed in early spinal tuberculosis is sometimes severe from the outset. It can thus alter the harmony of the spine profile. This can manifest clinically as an anteflexion of the trunk or even a forward bending of the head and neck. The objective of this study was to examine the sagittal inclination angle in T9 and T1 before and after internal fixation at the beginning of the affection. Materials and Methods: This was a prospective study involving 21 patients. They were distributed as follows: 19 women and 2 men. The mean age was 42 years (range: 19 to 67 years). According to location, there were 2 cervical, 8 thoracic, 7 thoracolumbar, and 4 lumbar. All underwent instrumentation with fusion after having prior histological proof of the lesion and initiating appropriate medical treatment. The analysis was based on telemetry of the entire spine, in lateral and frontal views. Sagittal tilt was studied, particularly at T9 and T1. Results: The mean follow-up was 32.7 months ± 19.7 (7 to 72 months). The mean T9 tilt angle preoperatively was 12.4° ± 4.8 (-4°-20°), 12.4° ± 3.1 (6°-18°) postoperatively, and 12.9° ± 2.9 (6°-18°) at 6 months and at the final follow-up. The mean T1 tilt angle preoperatively was 4.1° ± 6.0 (-14°-10°), 4.5° ± 4.5 (-6°-10°) postoperatively, and 4.3° ± 3.8 (-6°-10°) at 6 months and at the final follow-up. Conclusion: The normal sagittal tilt of T9 is 10°+/-3. The vast majority maintained a value between 10° and 20°, or 95.2% of patients (20 cases). Normal T1 sagittal tilt is between 0° and 10°. It was initially beyond this value for 19.0% of patients, or 4 cases, and ultimately normal for 85.7% of patients, or 18 cases. Finally, instrumented arthrodesis effectively maintains an acceptable sagittal tilt in T9 and T1 in active tuberculous spondylitis.

## ID: AGS26/1028

## P0602: Assessment of functional change using the Oswestry Disability Index in patients with hematogenous spondylodiscitis undergoing spinal fusion by minimally invasive surgery

Fernando Marulanda

1

, Carlos Alberto Carmona Lorduy

1

, Carlos Pacichana

2

, Gina Velásquez Solano

3

1

Universidad de Cartagena, Cartagena, Colombia

2

Universidad del Rosario, Bogotá, Colombia

3

Universidad de los Andes, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Currently, spondylodiscitis has an annual incidence of between 0.5 and 2.5 cases per 100,000 inhabitants, with older adults (> 55 years) being the patients most at risk due to their multiple comorbidities such as diabetes, obesity, urinary tract infections, among others. In Colombia, a reported incidence of 1.07 per 100,000 inhabitants has been reported, a value that is within the global figures; however, there is no literature reporting the functional outcomes of the population with this pathology, especially in the Caribbean region. Therefore, the objective of this study is to characterize the population with hematogenous spondylodiscitis in our service, as well as to evaluate the functional change after surgical treatment with minimally invasive surgery and identify risk factors and complications. Material and Methods: A retrospective, cross-sectional, descriptive study was conducted, analyzing patients over 18 years of age, with a confirmed diagnosis of hematogenous spondylodiscitis by symptomatic and imaging criteria, who were treated with spinal arthrodesis by means of MIS at the Hospital Universitario del Caribe in Cartagena, Colombia, between January 1, 2021, and May 31, 2025, and who had their functionality evaluated preoperatively and postoperatively with the Oswestry Disability Index. Results: As preliminary results, a total of 18 patients who presented hematogenous spondylodiscitis between the years 2021 to 2025 were identified at our institution, all patients had a score < 12 points in the Spinal Infection Treatment Evaluation (SITE score), of which 11% died (N = 2), and 11% are hospitalized for a long time. Within the functional results, preoperatively the patients had scores > 40 indicating severe to extreme functional limitation, mainly due to pain and walking limitations. Postoperatively, significant changes were observed in the score, achieving figures < 20 indicating minimal or moderate functional limitation. Conclusion: It is highlighted that patients with hematogenous spondylodiscitis who received comprehensive treatment with antibiotic therapy and spinal fusion through MIS, showed an improvement in functionality evaluated by ODI, this highlights the importance of considering these minimally invasive techniques for the management of this pathology, in order to reduce complications associated with the surgical procedure and the improvement of both clinical and functional outcomes such as pain and walking ability.

## P0603: Early outcome of decompression and fixation for complicated Pott’s disease Sudan experience

Mohamed Hussain Zyad

1

, Mohamed Kabashi Abdalla

1

, Musaab Mohamed Abdalla Hamed

1

, Mohamed Babeker Kheder

1

1

Surgery Department,
Karary University, Omdurman, Sudan

Background: The spine is a common location for osseous tuberculosis, accounting for 50%-60% of cases. Spinal TB is challenging to diagnose and frequently presented at an advanced stage. Delays in determining the diagnosis and course of treatment lead to problems such as spinal cord compression and deformity. Patients and Methods: Observational, hospital-based study, it was conducted during the period from January 2021- May 2022. In many hospitals in Khartoum state, included fifty-two patients underwent surgical decompression for Pott’s disease; with more than 6 months follow up, patients assessment performed using ASIA score. Data analysis was performed by SPSS. (v. 26th). Results: Out of 52 patients, male 28 (53.8%), and female were 24 (46.2%). the mean patient age was 52.13 ± 13.0 years. Within range (30-75 years old). The Half of patients 26 (50%) were completely return to daily life activity, while 19 (36.5%) had some function lost, and 7 (13.5%) unable to return.
