Conclusion: Surgical options involving decompression of spine TB followed by stabilization are utilized in most patients with neurological deficits. It is very effective, and the results are good.

## ID: AGS26/1147

## P0604: Impact of enhanced infection prevention protocols on postoperative infection rates in full endoscopic lumbar discectomy: a 10-year single-centre experience

Sasidharan M D S

1

, Phani Kiran Surapuraju

1

1

Gleneagles HealthCity, Chennai, India

Introduction: Full endoscopic lumbar discectomy is a well-established minimally invasive technique for managing lumbar disc herniation, offering advantages such as reduced hospital stay and faster recovery. Nevertheless, postoperative infection remains a critical concern, particularly during the early learning curve. Over the last two years, we implemented targeted infection prevention measures-continuous irrigation pump, single-use RF probe in every case, and Vancomycin added to irrigation fluid-to further minimize infection risk. This study evaluates their impact on infection rates. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively analysed 699 adult patients who underwent full endoscopic lumbar discectomy between 2014 and 2025 at a single centre. The cohort was divided into two groups: 578 cases (2014-2023) operated without the enhanced protocol and 121 consecutive cases (2023-2025) performed after its introduction. Postoperative infection was defined by clinical signs (fever, wound discharge), radiological evidence, or culture-positive spondylodiscitis requiring intervention, assessed within 30 and 90 days. Results: The infection rate in the initial cohort was 1.38%, predominantly due to spondylodiscitis contributing to readmissions. Following implementation of the enhanced protocol, no infections were observed in the recent 121 cases. No spondylodiscitis, wound infection, or other infection-related complications occurred during this period. Conclusion: Enhanced infection prevention measures in full endoscopic lumbar discectomy-continuous irrigation pump, single-use RF probe, and Vancomycin in irrigation fluid-effectively reduced postoperative infection rates to zero. These findings underscore the importance of rigorous infection control protocols and suggest that their routine incorporation can significantly improve patient safety and surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1152

## P0605: Surgical site infection after lumbar spine surgery: an umbrella review

Tom Linstrom

1

, Charlie Faulks

2

, Gregory Malham

3

, Dean Biddau

4

1

The University of Melbourne, Melbourne, Australia

2

The Alfred, Melbourne, Australia

3

Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Melbourne, Australia

4

Queensland University of Technology, Spine Surgery Research Foundation, Brisbane, Australia

Introduction: Surgical site infection (SSI) is a serious complication following lumbar spine surgery, with reported rates ranging from 0.2% to 16.1%. Multiple patient, clinical, and surgical factors contribute to infection risk, yet findings across systematic reviews remain fragmented. The aim of this review is to consolidate evidence from published systematic reviews and meta-analyses to clarify SSI incidence and risk factors across lumbar spine procedures. Material and Methods: This umbrella review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Overviews of Reviews (PRIOR) framework and was prospectively registered with PROSPERO (CRD42024602543). A comprehensive search of PubMed, MEDLINE, CINAHL, SPORTDiscus, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science was performed from database inception to December 2024. Search strategies combined terms relating to lumbar spine surgery, surgical site infection, and systematic review/meta-analysis. Eligibility criteria included systematic reviews and meta-analyses that reported SSI incidence, risk factors, or preventive strategies in patients undergoing lumbar spine surgery. Non-systematic narrative reviews, single primary studies, and reviews without extractable SSI data were excluded. Two independent reviewers screened titles, abstracts, and full texts, with discrepancies resolved by consensus. Data extracted included study design, number of primary studies, patient population, surgical procedures (fusion vs. decompression; open vs. minimally invasive), SSI definitions, reported incidence, risk factors, and prophylactic measures. Methodological quality of included reviews was appraised using AMSTAR 2. A narrative synthesis was performed, with quantitative pooling of SSI incidence where possible. Subgroup analyses explored variation by surgical approach, patient characteristics, and use of prophylactic interventions. Results: Fifty-nine systematic reviews were included, representing 648 primary studies and 1,248,463 patients. The pooled cumulative incidence of SSI across all lumbar spine procedures was 3.56%. Rates varied substantially by surgical technique and patient subgroup. Fusion procedures consistently demonstrated the highest risk of infection compared to decompression-only procedures. Minimally invasive approaches, despite smaller incisions, were paradoxically associated with a greater risk of SSI than open procedures, likely reflecting longer operative times, limited exposure, and technical challenges. Patient-related risk factors such as diabetes, obesity, smoking, and advanced age were repeatedly associated with elevated SSI incidence across reviews. Intraoperative strategies showed marked effect sizes. Intrawound vancomycin reduced SSI incidence from 7.17% to 1.66%, demonstrating strong prophylactic benefit. The role of systemic antibiotics was more variable, with evidence supporting standard perioperative use but limited clarity regarding extended prophylaxis. Use of drains, length of hospital stay, and revision surgery were also linked with increased infection risk. Conclusion: Fusions and minimally invasive approaches are associated with increased SSI risk, though prophylactic intrawound vancomycin provides substantial protective benefit. Heterogeneity and limited reporting of surgical characteristics constrain subgroup analyses, underscoring the need for higher-quality, procedure-specific data.

## ID: AGS26/1157

## P0606: The impact of location on de novo spondylodiscitis: regions matter but are secondary to comorbidities

Julius Gerstmeyer

1,2,3

, Anna Gorbacheva

4

, Clifford Pierre

1,4

, Mark Kraemer

1,4

, Colin Gold

1

, Cameron Hogsett

1

, Nicholas Minissale

1

, periklis godolias

5

, Tobias Schulte

2

, Thomas A. Schildhauer

3

, Amir Abdul-Jabbar

1,4

, Rod Oskouian

1,4

, Jens Chapman

1,4

1

Swedish Neuroscience Institute, Seattle, United States

2

Department of Orthopedics and Trauma Surgery, St. Josef Hospital Bochum, Bochum, Germany

3

Department of General and Trauma Surgery, BG University Hospital Bergmannsheil, Ruhr University Bochum, Bochum, Germany

4

Seattle Science Foundation, Seattle, United States

5

Department of Orthopedics and Trauma Surgery, St. Josef Hospital Essen-Werden, Essen, Germany

Introduction: Primary spondylodiscitis (SD) cases surging in incidence globally remain a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge for physicians. The effect of lesion location on outcomes remains unclear. This study aims to assess the 90-day all-cause readmission rate in patients suffering from spondylodiscitis in different regions of the spine, with a secondary objective of comparing in-hospital mortality rates. Material and Methods: Utilizing the 2020 Nationwide Readmissions Database (NRD), USA, adult patients (> 18 years) were selected by diagnosis with ICD-10 codes for primary spondylodiscitis. Patients were categorized by localization into eight groups, excluding multifocal patients. Comparative analysis and logistic regressions were performed. Results: Among 5547 patients, lumbar SD was most prevalent, followed by thoracic and lumbo-sacral regions. Cervical SD had the lowest readmission rate (31.3%) and lower odds versus lumbar SD (adjusted OR = 0.73; p = 0.007). Other regions showed no significant differences. In-hospital mortality varied by location. The sacral region, renal failure, and advanced age were the strongest mortality predictors. Conclusion: While the incidence of spondylodiscitis varies by location on the spine, we found no significant differences in readmission rates across regions. However, there were substantial differences in in-hospital mortality rates. Comorbidities, particularly renal failure and advanced age, appear to outweigh spinal localization as risk factors for mortality and readmission.

## ID: AGS26/1353

## P0607: Spondylodiscitis in child: case report

Andre Castilho

1

, Joao Melgaco

1

, Nayara Santos

1

, Pedro Torres

1

, Anna Luiza Amancio

1

1

Hospital Unimed Belo Horizonte, Belo Horizonte, Brazil

Introduction: Discit, an inflammation of the intervertebral disc space, is a pathology that can lead to disc erosion. Knowledge of its manifestation in the pediatric age group remains limited, largely due to the low incidence of documented cases. The most widely accepted etiology is bacterial, particularly in cases of pediatric spondylodiscitis. The mean age for diagnosis in children is between two and eight years. Although any segment of the vertebral column can be affected, the lumbar region is the most frequently involved, accounting for the majority of cases, approximately 75% of patients, with a higher prevalence in children under five years of age. Material and Methods: A 4-year-old female patient, with a previously healthy medical history, presented with sudden lower back pain, frequent falls, and difficulty walking. The symptoms, which included diffuse fever peaks, began 30 days prior. The child was initially diagnosed with viral myositis and treated with analgesics and antipyretics, with no improvement. Seven days after the onset of symptoms, she lost the ability to walk and had a 5% weight loss, which led to a new medical evaluation.Initial imaging studies revealed a decreased intervertebral disc space between L1 and L2. A computed tomography (CT) scan confirmed these findings, also showing lytic lesions in the L2 vertebral body and a rupture of the adjacent bone cortex. Laboratory tests upon admission showed a C-reactive protein level of 22.8 mg/L, suggesting a significant inflammatory process. Based on the clinical presentation and radiological findings, a diagnostic hypothesis of acute spondylodiscitis was raised. Results: Based on the patient's age and the common bacterial etiology in young children (Kingella kingae), empirical intravenous antibiotic therapy with Cefazolin was initiated. After two weeks, the patient was discharged from the hospital, showing significant clinical and laboratory improvement, which obviated the need for a biopsy. Ambulation was restored within 72 hours with the use of an orthopedic brace (OTLS), and painful complaints were completely resolved within seven days of treatment initiation. Outpatient follow-up consisted of oral Cefadroxil therapy for an additional four weeks, with continued use of the brace during ambulation and rest. In subsequent follow-up, the patient remained asymptomatic, with no recurrence of symptoms or complications. Conclusion: This case report highlights the importance of early diagnosis and prompt treatment of spondylodiscitis in the pediatric population. It emphasizes the need for a high degree of clinical suspicion, particularly when children present with atypical lumbar pain that is refractory to initial symptomatic treatment. The timely intervention allowed for a complete recovery without sequelae, reinforcing the positive impact of an assertive diagnostic and therapeutic approach.
References
1. Fucs PMB, Meves R, Yamada HH (2012) Spinal infections in children: a review. International Orthopaedics (SICOT) 36:387-395
2. Fernandez M, Carrol CL, Baker CJ (2000) Discitis and vertebral osteomyelitis in children: an 18-year review. Pediatrics 105 (6):1299-1304
3. Chandrasenan J, Klezl Z, Bommireddy R, Calthorpe D (2011) Spondylodiscitis in children: a retrospective series. J Bone Joint Surg Br 93(8):1122-1125
4. Brown R, Hussain M, McHugh K, Novelli V, Jones D (2001) Discitis in young children. J Bone Joint Surg Br 83(1):106-111
5. Crawford AH, Kucharzyk DW, Ruda R, Smitherman HC Jr (1991) Diskitis in children. Clin Orthop Relat Res 266:70-79
6. Waizy H, Heckel M, Seller K, Schroten H, Wild A (2007) Remodeling of the spine in spondylodiscitis of children at the age of 3 years or younger. Arch Orthop Trauma Surg 127(6):403-407

## ID: AGS26/1408

## P0608: Necrotizing orchiepididymitis with testicular rupture following catheter-associated UTI and spondylodiscitis: a rare urological emergency

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, luis vásquez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Carlos Pacichana

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

, María Constanza Bedoya

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are common hospital-acquired complications that can progress to severe systemic infections when not promptly recognized and treated. We describe a rare case of a complicated UTI that progressed to bacterial pyelonephritis, hematogenous spondylodiscitis, and ultimately necrotizing orchiepididymitis with testicular rupture. Case Description: A 58-year-old man with a recent history of lumbar decompression and arthrodesis developed a catheter-associated UTI, with E. coli and P. aeruginosa isolated. Despite initial antibiotic treatment, he subsequently presented with worsening lumbar pain, neurological deficits, and scrotal swelling. Imaging confirmed spondylodiscitis with epidural abscesses and left-sided testicular necrosis. He underwent emergency orchiectomy, followed by staged anterior L5 corpectomy and posterior instrumentation. Multidrug-resistant E. coli was isolated from both spinal and scrotal specimens. Targeted intravenous antibiotic therapy, debridement, and wound management effectively controlled the infection. At one-year follow-up, the patient remained afebrile with neurological improvement and no recurrence. Conclusion: This case highlights the potential for catheter-associated UTIs to evolve into life-threatening systemic infections with multi-organ involvement. The unique combination of spondylodiscitis and necrotizing orchiepididymitis highlights the need for high clinical suspicion, rapid diagnostic workup, and multidisciplinary management in high-risk postoperative patients. Preventive strategies and early intervention are critical in minimizing morbidity and long-term sequelae.

## ID: AGS26/1440

## P0609: Novel antimicrobial strategy in the treatment of spondylodiscitis

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

, Lucia Vanegas

1

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

1

, Maria Barragán

2

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Universidad del Rosario, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Bioactive Glass (BAG) has high utility in orthopedic surgery due to its pharmacological activity, which promotes tissue regeneration and bone fusion. Furthermore, it optimizes systemic antibiotic treatment by improving pharmacokinetic parameters and exhibiting strong bacteriostatic activity at the application site. This case report presents a patient with spondylodiscitis that required surgical treatment with discectomy and placement of BAG in the intervertebral space. Case presentation: A 71-year-old male patient with a history of a psoas abscess, from which a beta-lactamase-producing Staphylococcus capitis was isolated, and received antibiotic treatment. He subsequently presented with spondylodiscitis at the T11-T12 level, requiring surgery. During the procedure, a discectomy, irrigation, BAG placement in the intervertebral space, and spinal fusion from T8 to L3 were performed. Following surgery and intravenous antibiotic therapy, the patient's symptoms improved, and the infection resolved without recurrence at the 6-month follow-up. Discussion: The most common and well-established treatment for spondylodiscitis is posterior spinal fusion and arthrodesis, and anterior corpectomy for patients with vertebral body involvement, instability, epidural abscesses, and conjunction with intravenous antibiotics. This is a complex, invasive, and highly challenging surgical approach. BAG has demonstrated a high bone fusion rate, as well as bacteriostatic activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. Therefore, this new proposed technique’s goal isto avoid such extensive surgery, aiming to eliminate the infection and stimulate localized bone fusion at the affected segment. Conclusion: The use of BAG in the intervertebral space is recommended for patients with spondylodiscitis, given the high bacteriostatic capacity of the glass and its bone-fusing properties. Therefore, this new technique is proposed to reduce intraoperative time, thus avoiding the need for such extensive surgical procedures, while still effectively eliminating the infection and stimulating localized bone fusion in the affected segment.

## ID: AGS26/1450

## P0610: Percutaneous multilevel drainage: a less invasive treatment of massive spinal tuberculosis

Rodrigo Donoso

1

, David Segovia

1

, marcos ganga

2

, Fernando Lira

1

, Joaquin Bernis

1

, Giovanni Canessa

1

, Jaime Gonzalez

1

1

Hospital San José, Clínica RedSalud Vitacura, Santiago, Chile

2

Universidad de Chile, Clínica Santa María, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Tuberculosis (TB) is a disease that can be debilitating without specific treatment. Developing countries have successfully implemented vaccination campaigns, leading to eradication. However, isolated cases continue to exist. Chile is a developing country, with rising TB rates despite government healthcare efforts. Spinal TB should always be considered as secondary dissemination, even though its rare presentation. Material and Methods: Case report and literature review. A 21 year-old-woman, without comorbidities known, evaluated during COVID-19 pandemic. She manifested severe low and middle back pain for about 6 months, occasional fever, malaise and unexpected weight-loss. X-Rays, CT and MRI were performed, with multiple prevertebral and vertebral body abscesses from T6 to L2. CT biopsy was performed, resulting positive for M. tuberculosis. It was decided by the Infectology specialists to complete 12 months of antibiotic treatment. However, the patient did not improve, presenting a kyphotic thoracic deformity and neural intermittent claudication. Hence, we decided to perform a percutaneous multi pedicular drainage treatment, evacuating about 2 litres of caseum abscess from all affected levels. The patient performed well after the treatment, with minimum pain, capable of daily life activities. Six months post operative X-rays showed marked reduction of abscesses and fusion of some of the segments involved. Also, she was treated by Infectology with a secondary line antibiotic therapy for another 9 months after the surgery. After doing a literature review, we did not find any publication showing a similar treatment technique, therefore we thought of documenting the case. Results: The technique described resulted to be a safe, reproducible and useful technique for this kind of unusual case. We did not find published scientific literature related to this subject. However, there is vastly described performing open surgical techniques to drain these types of massive abscesses. This percutaneous technique can offer the advantages of avoiding a posterior open approach, giving the chance of a quicker recovery and less risk of postoperative infection. Conclusion: Kyphosis in the context of spinal TB presents significant challenges for the spine surgeon, given the potential for generating significant imbalances in the context of osteoporotic bones and secondary contamination. Antibiotic treatment can be reviewed as adjuvant and preventive treatments. This case is of interest due to its rarity in our country, in addition to describing the percutaneous drainage technique, the context of the patient and the severity of her spinal damage is unusual.

## ID: AGS26/1549

## P0611: Microbiological profile of surgical site infections in spine surgery: a retrospective analysis at an orthopedic hospital in Mexico

Cinthya Alejandra Varas-Aguilar

1

, Alexis Manuel Mazariegos-Perez

1

, Hector Ismael De Haro-Segura

1

, Daniel Torres-Fragoso

2

, Alfredo Javier Moheno-Gallardo

1

1

Highly Specialized Medical Unit of Traumatology, Orthopedics, and Rehabilitation “Dr. Victorio De La Fuente Narváez”, Mexico City, Mexico

2

Hospital Angeles Lindavista, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Surgical site infections (SSIs) in orthopedic surgery represent a significant public health concern due to their economic impact on healthcare systems and the psychosocial burden on patients. Postoperative spinal infections are particularly devastating, as they can result in chronic pain, repeated surgical interventions, prolonged hospital stays, and increased morbidity and mortality. Therefore, timely diagnosis and accurate identification of causative organisms are essential to establish appropriate treatment and reduce complications. Material and Methods: We conducted a descriptive, retrospective, cross-sectional study of patients diagnosed with SSIs at the Orthopedic Hospital in Mexico City between January 1, 2022, and December 31, 2023. A targeted search was performed using ICD-10 codes to identify patients with spinal SSIs. Descriptive statistics were applied, including frequencies, measures of central tendency, and standard deviations for quantitative variables. Results: A total of 277 patients with nosocomial infections were identified. The patients mean age was 60.02 years, and female patients represented 50.1% (n = 139) of the sample. Among them, 246 patients (89.16%) were diagnosed with SSIs, and of these, 48 cases (19.51%) involved spinal surgery. The most prevalent surgical procedure associated with infection was lumbar fusion (12.19%), followed by lumbar decompression/discectomy (7.31%). For etiological diagnosis, 6.5% of cases (n=16) underwent biopsy culture, 8.9% (n = 22) swab culture, while in 4.06% (n = 10) the type of culture was not reported. The most frequently isolated pathogen was Escherichia coli (16.32%, n = 8), followed by Enterococcus spp. (16.32%, n = 8), Staphylococcus epidermidis (10.41%, n = 5), and less commonly Staphylococcus aureus, Enterobacter cloacae, and Klebsiella spp. (4.16% each, n = 2). Negative results accounted for 14.58%, and the same proportion was reported as inadequate samples (n = 7). In three cases (6.25%), beta-lactamase-resistant organisms were detected. Conclusion: Spinal SSIs accounted for 19.51% of all SSIs reported at our institution, with lumbar fusion being the procedure most frequently associated with infection. The most commonly isolated pathogen was E. coli, a finding that contrasts with the international literature, where Staphylococcus aureus is generally reported as the predominant organism. This discrepancy may reflect local epidemiological differences that should be considered when selecting prophylactic antibiotics and empirical therapeutic strategies. The high proportion of negative or inadequate cultures (12.5%) highlights the need to optimize sampling techniques, transport, and laboratory processing to reduce false negatives and improve diagnostic accuracy. The detection of beta-lactamase-resistant organisms emphasizes the importance of continuous microbiological surveillance and the implementation of antibiotic stewardship programs to mitigate resistance. Overall, spinal SSIs represent a clinically and epidemiologically significant problem, associated with prolonged hospitalization, increased healthcare costs, and a substantial impact on patient quality of life. These findings underscore the urgent need for multicenter prospective studies to validate our results and contribute to the development of evidence-based preventive strategies and management protocols tailored to the local epidemiological profile.

## ID: AGS26/1598

## P0612: Surgical treatment strategy for chronic infectious spondylitis: cohort analysys with a minimum 3 years follow-up

Denis Naumov

1

, Arkadiy Vishnevsky

1

, Srgey Tkach

1

1

Saint-Petersburg State Phthisiopulmonology Research Institute, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Chronic infectious spondylitis of the cervicothoracic spine represents an etiologically heterogeneous group of diseases characterized by vertebral destruction within the C6-T3 region. There is no common strategy for the surgical treatment of this cohort of patients. Material and Methods: The cohort included 21 patients treated between 2018 and 2022. Considering the etiology, long-term outcomes were analyzed in 14 patients with chronic nonspecific spondylitis and 7 patients with tuberculous spondylitis. Clinical, radiological, and surgical parameters were assessed. Follow-up results were evaluated at list 3 years. Results: Chronic infectious spondylitis in the cohort was classified according to E. Pola (2017) as types B.3 (n = 10), C.2 (n = 4), C.3 (n = 1), and C.4 (n = 6). The mean age at the time of surgery was 48 years and 3 months (range 20-71). The groups were comparable in gender, age, and degree of local sagittal balance impairment. The etiology of chronic spondylitis influenced the extent of the destructive process (p = 0.009) and the severity of back pain syndrome (p = 0.028). Health-related quality-of-life analysis revealed a greater degree of social maladaptation in tuberculous spondylitis group according to the NDI (p = 0.018) and SF-12 (p = 0.002). Indications for various techniques of cervicothoracic reconstruction, including isolated ventral, isolated dorsal, and combined approaches, were determined. Conclusion: The etiology of spondylitis affects the clinical course of the disease and the incidence of postoperative complications. The results of our study allowed for the development of several tactical tools for planning surgical reconstruction, including an algorithm for selecting the surgical approach and determining the need for manubriotomy. Follow-up results of the proposed surgical algorithm confirm its effectiveness and potential for further application.

## P0613: TB spine in paediatric population; decision making, choice of treatment and surgical approaches

Mohd Arman Muhamad Nor

1

, Nadiah Ahmad Fuad

1

, Fadzlishah Johanabas Rosli

1

, Azmi Alias

1

1

Hospital Kuala Lumpur, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

Introduction: Spinal TB in children is relatively uncommon which are roughly 4-5% of pediatric TB cases. Surgical intervention is often required, with 40-50% of pediatric spinal TB cases needing corrective surgery. However the small size of paediatric patient make corrective surgery with instrumentation very challenging. Timely diagnosis is crucial to prevent severe outcomes like kyphotic deformity or neurological deficits. Material and Methods: We have 2 cases with same diagnosis but treated differently in view of different age and body size. The first case is a 3 years old boy which initially presented with right lower limb weakness and abnormal gait since May 2024, progressively worsening to paraparesis over 2 months. Serial investigation done and was diagnosed with disseminated TB with tuberculous spondylodiscitis with bony destruction T3- T6, pulmonary TB and TB tuberculoma. We proceeded with T4 corpectomy, interbody cage placement, and T1-T7 Posterior fixation via posterolateral approach. The second case is 9 months old baby girl, born preterm via SVD at 33weeks 5days presented with bilateral lower limb weakness for 2 months. Mother noticed that child unable to roll as usual and unable to stand with support. Prior to this event, she had history of chicken pox. MRI done noted multiple hypodense areas seen over the right paravertebrae region, extending into vertebrae body, suggestive of paraspinal abscess or bacterial spondylitis with adjacent lung collapse which consistent with TB Spine. This patient was treated with anti-TB medications and spine brace. Conclusion: For very small baby with spine deformity it is very challenging to do spine correction surgery not just because the baby size but no spine instrument can fit the small vertebra. In the second case where the baby is only 9 months old, we unable to do corrective surgery thus we fit a thoracolumbar brace to her to stabilize the spine. In the first case with the 3 years old boy, we managed to do corrective surgery by modifying cervical cage and screws to fit the thoracic level.

## ID: AGS26/1671

## P0614: Spondylodiscitis after vertebroplasty caused by Streptococcus sanguinis - Case report

Tomi Kunej

1

, Robert Bali

1

, Damjana Kunej

1

1

University Medical Centre Maribor, Maribor, Slovenia

Introduction: In our case report we present a case of spondylodiscitis after vertebroplasty of L1 caused by S. sanguinis, complicated by sepsis, endocarditis and status epilepticus. Materials and Methods: 70-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department after a fall from standing height with pain in the lumbar spine. Conventional X-rays showed no obvious fracture in the thoracolumbar spine, and she was discharged with instructions and a prescription for analgesics. In the following days she presented to the ED with persistent back pain and elevated inflammatory markers, for which no obvious source of infection was discovered, she received Amoxicillin/clavulanic acid after consultation with infectious disease physicians. Follow-up X-rays showed a stable fracture of L1, and she was referred for an MRI, which confirmed the L1 fracture without other obvious pathology. Due to increasing back pain vertebroplasty of L1 was recommended and performed 3 weeks after the initial injury. At first follow-up, 3 weeks after the procedure the patient reported persistent pain, localized at L4/L5, for which she received analgesics and dexamethasone. 5 weeks after the vertebroplasty the patient presented again to the ED with severe back pain, no neurological deficits, hemodynamically unstable, with highly elevated inflammatory markers, urgent MRI showed spondylodiscitis at L1/L2 with a small abscess in the left psoas and the patient was admitted to the ICU for further treatment. Vancomycin and ceftriaxone were prescribed empirically after blood cultures were taken. Streptococcus sanguinis was isolated from the blood cultures and cefepime was prescribed as per the antibiogram. Follow-up MRI showed reduction of the abscess with persistent spondylodiscitis in L1/L2. CT of the lumbar spine showed destruction of L1 around the vertebral cement with signs of instability, therefore posterior stabilization was indicated. During the ICU hospitalization the patient suffered endocarditis of the mitral valve, status epilepticus caused by administration of Cefepime and nosocomial pneumonia. Due to the complication of status epilepticus cefepime was exchanged for ceftriaxone. These conditions responded well to medical therapy and 27 days after being admitted the patient could be operated on. Percutaneous posterior fixation from TH11 to L3 was performed, after the procedure the patient was transferred to the trauma department and rehabilitation was initiated. The patient was neurologically intact and ambulatory on the 3rd post op day. Results: The patient responded well to antibiotic therapy and after stabilization of the lumbar spine was able to ambulate independently with minimum back pain. Conclusion: Spondylodiscitis after vertebroplasty is a rare complication, which in our case was complicated by sepsis, endocarditis, pneumonia and status epilepticus which was treated successfully with antibiotics, intensive care and surgical stabilization of the unstable lumbar spine.

## P0615: Clinical and microbiological characteristics of pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis (changes from 2006 to 2022)

Alexander Bazarov

1

, Aleksandra Tsvetkova

1

, Aleksei Farion

2

1

Tyumen State Medical University, Tyumen, Russia, Tyumen Regional Hospital No.2, Tyumen, Russian Federation

2

Surgut Trauma Hospital, Tyumen State Medical University, Surgut, Russian Federation

Introduction: Since the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, a significant increase in purulent-inflammatory diseases, including vertebral osteomyelitis, has been noted. Mass inpatient treatment of the population, invasive treatment and diagnostic procedures, as well as the use of antibiotics, and steroids all lead to a change in the microbiological profile of vertebral osteomyelitis. The ratio of gram positive and gram-negative pathogens, acute and chronic forms and severity of the disease changes. Statistical analysis has been performed. Material and Methods: During the period from 2006 to 2022, 421 patients with pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis were treated in the Regional Clinical Hospital No. 2, Tyumen, Russia: 2006-2019 - 264, 2020-2022 - 157 patients. There is a significant increase in the incidence rate, which amounted to 1.27 per 100 000 people per year in 2010 and 3.69 in 2022, with acute osteomyelitis predominating. Microbiological examination was performed on 295 patients (70.0%). The assessed factors were: gender, age, type of lesion according to the E. Pola 2017 classification, form of the disease, presence of complications, and number of microbiological samples and their result. Results: The number of female patients significantly increased from 26.7% to 37.6% (p = 0.017) and the age of patients of both genders from 48.9 ± 15.0 to 56.5 ± 15.3 (p < 0.001). Type A lesions decreased from 62 (23.5%) to 9 (5.7%) p < 0.001; type B from 124 (47.0%) to 60 (38.2%) p = 0.078; type C increased from 71 (26.9%) to 73 (46.5%) p < 0.001; posterior column lesions - type “0” - from 7 (2.6%) to 15 (9.6%) p = 0.002. There has been an increase in sepsis from 23 (8.7%) to 31 (19.7%) p < 0.001; and in neurological complications from 54 (20.5%) to 49 (31.2%) p = 0.014. Hospital mortality rose from 16 (6.1%) to 26 (16.6%) p = 0.001. A positive result of microbiological examination from the lesion focus was obtained in 129 and 90 (48.9% and 57.3%), blood cultures [out of 27 and 23 (10.2% and 14.6%), respectively, in the time periods. An increase in positive results was noted in the lesion focus for Klebsiella pneumoniae p = 0.016; Proteus mirabilis p = 0.002; Enterobacter cloacae p = 0.007; Enterobacter aerogens p = 0.019; MRSA p = 0.064. In general, an increase in gram-negative pathogens was noted from 3.8 to 9.6% p = 0.015; polymicrobial lesions from 3.0% to 9.6% p = 0.004. In blood cultures, there is an increase in polymicrobial lesions to 2.5% p = 0.01; an increase in Staphylococcus Aureus p = 0.034; Klebsiella pneumoniae p = 0.029; Escherichia Coli p = 0.066. Conclusion: With the onset of the pandemic, the clinical characteristics of the vertebral osteomyelitis changed significantly with an increase in acute forms by 1.7 times, sepsis by 2.3 times and epidural abscess by 2.1 times. A significant increase in posterior column lesions (p = 0.002) and type C lesions (p < 0.001) was also noted. There is a significant increase in gram-negative pathogens from the focus of inflammation (p = 0.015) and blood cultures (p = 0.029), as well as from polymicrobial lesions from the focus (p = 0.004).

## ID: AGS26/1808

## P0616: Emphysematous spondylitis, a rare complication of vertebral osteomyelitis

Alexander Bazarov

1

, Aleksandra Tsvetkova

2

, Nikolai Orishich

2

1

Tyumen State Medical University, Tyumen, Russia, Tyumen Regional Hospital No.2, Tyumen, Russian Federation

2

Clinical Hospital No. 71 of the Federal Medical and Biological Agency of Russia, Ozersk, Russian Federation

Introduction: Emphysematous spondylitis is an extremely rare complication of vertebral osteomyelitis. Its characteristic feature is the formation of gas bubbles in the bones, paravertebral tissues and spinal canal. Often, publications note a combination of diabetes mellitus, emphysematous pyelonephritis and emphysematous spondylitis. Typical pathogens are Klebsiella pneumoniae, Escherichia coli, Proteus and Pseudomonas spp., Enterobacter aerogenes. Most publications are presented as descriptions of isolated cases. In case of late diagnosis and lack of timely surgical treatment, the risk of death is extremely high. Material and Methods: During the period 2019-2025, 189 patients with hematogenous vertebral osteomyelitis were treated at the Tyumen Regional Clinical Hospital No. 2 and the Clinical Hospital No. 71 of the Federal Medical and Biological Agency of the Chelyabinsk Region, Russia, 15 of whom had emphysematous spondylitis. Conservative treatment was received by 4 patients, and 11 underwent surgery. The study assessed descriptive statistics, severity of the condition, main symptoms, concomitant pathology, microbiological test results, and treatment outcome, including in-hospital mortality. Results: The ratio of men and women was 1:2, the predominance of women is not observed in other types of vertebral osteomyelitis, the average age was 63 ± 13 years. The average time of diagnosis was 0.7 [0.25; 1.00] months, and was two times less, compared to the main group without emphysematous spondylitis which was 1.5 [0.75; 3.00] months. Sepsis was diagnosed in 11 (68.7%), epidural abscess in 10 (62.5%). The cervical spine was affected in 1 (13.3%) case, thoracic in 4 (26.7%), lumbar in 9 (60.0%) cases. Acute osteomyelitis was predominant and amounted to 81.3%. Concomitant pathology includes: diabetes mellitus in 9 (60.0%) cases, coronary heart disease in 10 (66.7%), pyelonephritis - 5 (33.3%), and obesity - 4 (26.7%). Previous purulent diseases were detected in 3 (20.0%) cases. 12 patients noted back or neck pain (80.0%), 5 - fever (33.3%). There were monovertebral and monosegmental lesions in 9 (60.0%) cases, and polysegmental and multilevel in 6 (40.0%). Staphylococcus Aureus - 6, MRSA -1, Klebsiella pneumoniae - 5, Escherichia Coli - 4, Proteus mirabilis - 1, were isolated from the focus of lesion and blood. 10 patients were operated on, all underwent drainage and debridement due to the severity of the condition. 6 (40.0%) patients died within 7 days after admission. Hospital mortality was 11 (73.3%). Conclusion: Emphysematous spondylitis as a form of vertebral osteomyelitis is characterized by the most severe condition, with the formation of sepsis and fatal outcomes for most patients. In most cases, it is associated with gram-negative pathogens. Surgical treatment is ineffective due to the severity of the condition on the day of admission to the hospital. Initial antibacterial therapy regimens should be targeted at gram-negative and resistant pathogens.

## ID: AGS26/2001

## P0617: Minimal invasive percutaneous fixation with biopsy without debridement for the treatment of pyogenic spondylodiscitis

Hamdan Abdelrahman

1,2

, Seyed-Emadaldin Sadat

1

, Ahmed Shawky Abdelgawaad

1,2

, Ali Ezzati

1

1

Helios Clinic, Erfurt, Germany

2

Asiut University Hospitals, Asiut, Egypt

Introduction: Spinal infection (SI) is a challenging disease that requires immediate diagnosis and treatment. It can be treated non-surgically with antibiotics and Immobilization with an external orthosis for several weeks to months. If surgical intervention is required, a combined anterior and posterior approach is usually performed. We report here on our experience with the use of minimally invasive percutaneous transpedicular fixation (PTPF) combined with biopsy for microbiology and histopathology without debridement for the treatment in patients with SI. Material and Methods: We reviewed data of 54 patients, between 2014 and 2022, treated for hematogenous SI in the thoracolumbar spine without extensive bone destruction or significant neurological deficit, with biopsy and PTPF. Demographic, operative, and perioperative data were collected and analysed. Results: The study included 24 women and 30 men with mean age of 73.6 years (47-90). Renal insufficiency found in 32 and DM in 29 cases. 10 cases were class II ASA-Score und 44 were class III&IV. The lumbar spine was affected in 16 patients, 19 thoracic, 25 thoracolumbar, cervical in 4 and 6 patients had multifocal SI. All patients were operated minimally invasive and one patient hybrid dorsal Approach. In 52 patients, ≥ 3 segments were fixed. Mean operative time of 70 minutes. Mean blood loss was 300 ml. Intraop. biopsy for microbiological and histopathological examination in 52 patients; organism could be isolated in 45 cases. There were no intraop. surgical complications, one patient died two days postop. due to caradiac infarction. 35 patients had been reoperated for shortening of the fixation (mainly lumbar) after autofusion of the infected segments; occured in 47 patients within 6 months. Preop. VAS of 7/10 reduced to 2 at the last FU. 18 cases had ASIA-D, 15 improved postop. to ASIA-E. Preop. CRP and WBC were 118 and 12.7 reduced to 23.5 and 8.8 at the last FU respectively. Coagulase negative organisms were the commonest isolated organism. Polymicrobial SI in 3 patients. Two patients with specific infections. Antibiotic were given according to culture and sensitivity in culture positive cases. Antibiotic combination therapy is all of cases. Conclusion: Long-term Immobilization is critical, especially in elderly patients because of severe comorbidities. We present a minimally invasive surgical procedure to avoid immobilization and to overcome the disadvantages of ventrodorsal procedure in patients without significant neurological deficit or extensive bone destruction. The internal immobilization and antibiotic therapy without touching the infected tissue is sufficient to completely resolve the spondylodiscitis. The outcomes of this minimally invasive treatment in such multimorbid patients are promising.

## ID: AGS26/2050

## P0618: Postoperative infection in sacroiliac joint arthrodesis and association with the timing of intra-articular corticosteroid injection: a scoping review

Elouise Baker

1

, Riaz Mohammed

1

1

Gloucestershire Royal Hospital, Gloucester, United Kingdom

Introduction: The definitive surgical management of sacroiliac joint (SIJ) pain and dysfunction is arthrodesis, and is usually performed after trial of conservative treatment that includes intra-articular corticosteroid injections (CSI) in the SIJ which has diagnostic and therapeutic purposes. In many instances these steroid injections are performed multiple times, before the final decision is made to offer arthrodesis. Whilst literature reviews have enumerated the safe time interval between steroid injections and subsequent cervical or lumbar spinal surgery, to our knowledge no such evidence exists for SIJ fusion. The literature around timing of CSI prior to lumbar surgery, although varied, suggests a duration of < 1 month increases the risk of infection significantly but ideally should be > 3 months. This study aims to review the available literature for optimal timing of CSI before SIJ fusion for risk reduction postoperatively and symptom control. Material and Methods: A systematic review was conducted using the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic review and Meta-Analyses) guidelines of PubMed, ScienceDirect, National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), and Cochrane databases using the search strategy of “infection AND fusion AND sacroiliac AND injection”. Prospective or retrospective clinical studies depicting either open or minimal-invasive surgery (MIS)/percutaneous SIJ arthrodesis, fusion for all clinical indications including degenerative, inflammatory, instability in adult human population were included. Case reports, review articles, only non-surgical treatment studies and SIJ fusion for trauma were excluded. Results: A total of 1674 results were screened with 34 having their abstracts read for relevance, of which none were found to be looking at the complication rates post SIJ fusion in relation to the proximity of the last CSI to the surgical date. However, three studies looking at surgical management compared to conservative management of SIJ dysfunction showed 64-94% of participants had used CSI prior to SIJ fusion. Conclusion: Our scoping review failed to identify any evidence directly looking at how the timing of CSI may affect postoperative outcome in SIJ fusion however rates of infection ranged from 0.1% to 4%. As well, the studies with more participants previously having used CSI had higher rates of infection postoperatively, regardless of the time of follow-up. Given the particularly frequent utilisation of CSI in SIJ pain and dysfunction, there is a strong need for further research into this area. Similar to existent evidence in other spinal surgery, we believe that CSIs should be limited and where possible avoided in the 3 months preceding SIJ fusion.

## ID: AGS26/2416

## P0619: Percutaneous transforaminal endoscopic surgery (PTES) combined with anterolateral mini-incision surgery for the treatment of thoracic or lumbar tuberculosis with neurologic compression

Yutong Gu

1,2

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Zhongshan Hospital Fudan University, Shanghai, China

2

Shanghai Southwest Spine Surgery Center, Shanghai, China

Introduction: The surgical strategies of thoracic or lumbar tuberculosis (TB) mainly include anterior, anterior combined posterior, and posterior approach for debridement, bone graft fusion and internal fixation. These surgeries may lead to significant morbidity because of extensive dissection and massive blood loss. The purpose of this study is to evaluate the feasibility, efficacy and safety of percutaneous transforaminal endoscopic surgery (PTES) combined with anterolateral mini-incision debridement and short-segment fusion for the treatment of thoracic or lumbar TB with neurologic compression. Material and Methods: 18 patients of single segment thoracic or lumbar TB with neurologic compression were included. PTES under local anesthesia was performed to remove the abscess and necrotic tissue compressing the neurologic elements for neurologic decompression in the prone position. Then, the abscess, infected and necrotic tissue were completely removed through anterolateral mini-access under direct vision in the lateral position. Through the same approach the short-segment fusion using 3D printed titanium cage or artificial vertebral body was performed, and the screws and rod fixation were undertaken with the pedicle screws inserted into involved vertebrae. The transthoracic approach was used for T8-L1, transdiaphragmatic approach for T11-L1, retroperitoneal approach for L1-S1. Operative duration, blood loss, incision length and hospital stay were recorded. Pre- and postoperative visual analog scale (VAS) pain scores of back and leg, Oswestry disability index (ODI), ASIA (American Spinal Injury Association) scale, complications and images were also recorded. Results: There were operative duration of 63 (52-75) min for PTES and 176 (98-210) min for anterolateral mini-incision surgery, blood loss of 290 (40-460) ml, incision length of 9.5 ± 2.3 mm for PTES and 54 ± 12 mm for anterolateral mini-incision surgery, and hospital stay of 6 (4-9) days. VAS scores significantly dropped postoperatively (p < 0.001) and ODI significantly decreased 2 years after surgery (p < 0.001). At the 3-month follow-up, all of 9 patients improved from ASIA scale D to E. All of 18 patients reached ASIA scale E at the 2-year follow-up. Fusion has been achieved in 18 cases. No internal fixation failure and other complication were found. Conclusion: PTES combined with anterolateral mini-incision debridement and short-segment fusion is a feasible, efficient and safe method in treating single segment thoracic or lumbar TB with neurologic compression. It shows advantages of less surgical trauma and faster postoperative recovery.
Key words: Thoracic or lumbar tuberculosis; Minimally invasive spine surgery; Transforaminal endoscopic surgery; Anterolateral mini-incision; Debridement and fusion

## ID: AGS26/2450

## P0620: No difference in surgical site infection rates between cefazolin and alternative antibiotics following spine surgery

Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, William Karakash

1

, Nicole Hang

1

, Jadesola Olurin

1

, Christian Au

1

, Rahki Banerjee

1

, Elizabeth Lechtholz-Zey

1

, Ji Chin

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Surgical site infections (SSIs) occur at a rate of 0.7-10% following spinal surgery with deep infections reported at 1-2% despite preoperative antibiotic prophylaxis. Cefazolin is the most commonly used antibiotic for surgical prophylaxis due to broad coverage against skin flora, however superiority of one antibiotic over others is debated. The current North American Spine Society (NASS) 2013 guidelines on antibiotic prophylaxis in spine surgery state the superiority of one antibiotic has not been clearly demonstrated, while recent studies reported prophylactic vancomycin had a higher risk of infection. This study aims to compare rates of SSIs, deep infections, and superficial wound infections between prophylactic cefazolin and other antibiotics following spine surgery. Material and Methods: All patients who underwent spine surgery at a single academic university hospital between 2015 and 2021 with at least 3 month follow-up were identified from electronic medical records. Patients were excluded if they had indications of trauma or malignancy, evidence of infection prior to admission, prior incision and drainage of an abscess, prior posterior instrumentation within 1 year, or prior surgery with absence of posterior instrumentation within 3 months. Patients who received a different antibiotic postoperatively were excluded. Patients were grouped based on preoperative antibiotic (cefazolin vs alternative). Primary outcomes were rates of SSIs, either superficial or deep, which necessitated irrigation and debridement in the operating room. Potential confounding covariables were measured including age, gender, comorbidities, operative time, and topical antibiotic or drain use. Chi-squared tests were performed for infection rates between groups given cefazolin vs alternative antibiotic. Associations between demographic, comorbidity, operative time, and topical antibiotic or drain use covariables were assessed through multivariable logistic regression. Results: 3055 patients with mean follow-up time of 699 days were identified. Of those, 2603 (85.2%) patients received preoperative cefazolin, while 452 (14.8%) patients received an alternate antibiotic. Compared to patients who received an alternate antibiotic, patients who received cefazolin did not experience different rates of SSIs (2.19% vs 1.77%), superficial wound infections (1.31% vs 0.88%), or deep wound infections (0.96% vs 0.88%) (all p > 0.05). With this sample size, the study had 80% power to find a minimal detectable effect size of 1.76% for SSIs, 1.35% for superficial infection, and 1.18% for deep infection. Of the alternate antibiotic group, 234 patients received vancomycin and 213 received clindamycin. Multivariate logistic regression demonstrated that operative time was associated with rates of SSIs (OR: 1.004 [1.002-1.006], p = 0.003). Conclusion: In this large, single-institution study, pooled rates of SSIs, superficial wound infections, or deep wound infections were not found to be significantly different between preoperative cefazolin and alternative antibiotics. These findings align with current NASS guidelines denying the superiority of any one antibiotic in spine surgery. The only independent risk factor for SSIs identified was operative time, consistent with prior literature linking longer procedures to higher infection risk. The study suggests there may not be a difference between cefazolin and alternative antibiotics when used prophylactically for spine surgery. Additionally, results suggest that efforts in reducing operative time may reduce SSI rates.

## ID: AGS26/2474

## P0621: Clinical outcomes and surgical technique of full-endoscopic debridement and drainage in thoracolumbar infectious spondylodiscitis: a prospective series

Siddharth Yadav

1

1

Consultant Spine Surgeon, Prayagraj, India

Introduction: Thoracolumbar infectious spondylodiscitis presents significant therapeutic challenges due to spinal instability and neurological risks. Tuberculosis is the predominant pathogen in endemic regions, alongside pyogenic organisms. Full-endoscopic debridement and drainage (FEDD) offers a minimally invasive surgical alternative to conventional open procedures. Material and Methods: A prospective series of 50 adult patients with thoracolumbar infectious spondylodiscitis refractory to medical therapy or with abscess formation underwent FEDD. Diagnosis was confirmed through clinical, laboratory, imaging, and microbiological data. Surgical technique included transforaminal endoscopic debridement, irrigation, abscess drainage, and adjunctive posterior percutaneous stabilization when indicated. Outcomes were assessed via pain (VAS), disability (ODI), infection status, radiographic fusion, and complications over a 24-month follow-up. Results: Infection resolution occurred in 86% of cases, with tuberculosis identified in 56% and pyogenic bacteria in the remainder. Recurrence requiring reoperation occurred in 14%. Mean VAS improved from 8.72 to 2.52 and ODI from 73.19 to 22.34. Operative metrics included mean surgery duration of 97.6 minutes, blood loss of 136.8 ml, and hospital stay of 9.7 days. Complications were infrequent, with transient neurological symptoms and minor infections predominant. Radiographic fusion was documented as complete or partial in 64% of patients. Conclusion: FEDD is a safe, effective minimally invasive technique for thoracolumbar infectious spondylodiscitis, achieving high infection control rates and functional recovery, particularly in tuberculosis-predominant populations. This approach minimizes surgical morbidity and facilitates rapid rehabilitation, supporting its incorporation into standard management protocols for complex spinal infections.

## ID: AGS26/2506

## P0622: Whole-body PET/CT reveals systemic dissemination in spinal tuberculosis: findings from the Spinal TB X cohort

Julian Scherer

1,2,3

, Sandra Mukasa

1

, Karen Wolmarans

1

, Fareda Jakoet-Bassier

1

, Ashleigh Taylor

1

, Reto Guler

1

, Tessa Kotze

1

, Taeksung Song

1

, Prof Rob Dunn

1

, Maritz Laubscher

1

, Hans Christoph Pape

2

, Michael Held

1

, Friedrich Thienemann

1,4

1

University of Cape Town, Cape Town, South Africa

2

University Hospital of Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

3

Center for Musculoskeletal Surgery, Charite - University Medicine, Berlin, Germany

4

University of Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

Introduction: Spinal tuberculosis (STB) is a severe form of extrapulmonary tuberculosis associated with diagnostic delay and substantial morbidity. The extent of disease - isolated versus disseminated STB - remains poorly defined. We applied whole-body 18F-FDG-PET/CT to characterise the clinical and radiographic phenotype of STB. Material and Methods: The Spinal TB X cohort (NCT05610098) is a prospective study of HIV-infected and uninfected patients with suspected STB in Cape Town, South Africa. We analysed the first ten patients with microbiologically confirmed STB. Demographic, clinical, microbiological, histological, and PET/CT data were collected. Isolated STB was defined as spinal involvement only, while disseminated STB was defined as additional organ involvement confirmed by microbiology and/or PET/CT. The study was approved by the Human Research Ethics Committee of the University of Cape Town (HREC 243/2022). Written informed consent was obtained from all participants. Results: Ten patients (median age 44 years [IQR 26], 80% male, 30% HIV-positive) were included. A total of 14 spinal lesions involving 44 vertebrae were detected; psoas abscesses were present in six patients. Mean SUVmax and SUVmean of spinal lesions were 15.2 (SD 4.6) and 6.9 (SD 1.5), respectively; mean total lesion glycolysis (TLG) was 363.2 SUVbw*mL (SD 341.9). Based on microbiology alone, 40% had disseminated STB; combining microbiology with PET/CT findings, 60% had disseminated disease. Conclusion: Whole-body PET/CT revealed a high frequency of disseminated disease, underscoring the systemic nature of TB even when clinical presentation is spinal. Isolated STB may represent a distinct phenotype. PET/CT shows promise for assessing disease burden and warrants further evaluation.

## ID: AGS26/2570

## P0623: Diabetes mellitus as a risk factor for infection after a degenerative posterior lumbar fusion: multivariate analysis of 356 cases

Jorge Nuñez

1

, Anna Taberner

1

, Juan Diego Montenegro

1

, Victoria San Gil

1

, Berta Escudero

1

1

Hospital Universitari MútuaTerrassa, COT & CARE Institute SL, Terrassa, Spain

Introduction: Lumbar spinal fusion is a widely used technique in the treatment of conditions such as lumbar stenosis and degenerative spondylolisthesis. However, acute postoperative infection remains a significant complication. The aim of this study was to identify risk factors associated with a higher incidence of acute infection following posterior lumbar fusion for degenerative pathology. Material and Methods: We conducted an observational, retrospective, and analytical study at a single hospital center. Patients over 18 years of age who underwent instrumented lumbar fusion for lumbar stenosis or degenerative spondylolisthesis between 2017 and 2022 were included. Demographic, clinical, and surgical variables were analyzed. Demographic variables included age, sex, and body mass index (BMI). Clinical variables included history of prior surgery (including microdiscectomy, foraminotomy, and previous spinal surgery without instrumentation), diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic corticosteroid use, smoking, and ASA (American Society of Anesthesiologists) classification. Surgical variables analyzed included indication for surgery (lumbar stenosis vs. degenerative spondylolisthesis), type of procedure (posterior arthrodesis with foraminotomy, posterior arthrodesis with laminectomy, circumferential arthrodesis with foraminotomy, circumferential arthrodesis with laminectomy), number of operated levels, surgical duration, postoperative transfusion, and use of postoperative drains. A descriptive statistical analysis of all variables was performed. Subsequently, a bivariate analysis and a multivariate logistic regression model were conducted to identify independent risk factors for acute postoperative infection. Results: A total of 356 patients were analyzed, with a mean age of 60.4 years (SD 12.4); 55.6% were male. Lumbar stenosis was the surgical indication in 73.0% of cases. The overall incidence of acute postoperative infection was 5.9% (21 cases). The most frequently isolated microorganism was Staphylococcus epidermidis (38%), followed by Staphylococcus aureus (34%) and polymicrobial infections (34%). In the bivariate analysis, the following factors were significantly associated with a higher risk of infection: diabetes mellitus (p = 0.010), hypertension (p = 0.042), chronic corticosteroid use (p = 0.001), and surgical duration greater than 3 hours (p = 0.028). BMI ≥ 30 showed a non-significant trend (p = 0.078). In the multivariate logistic regression model, only diabetes mellitus remained an independent and significant risk factor for acute postoperative infection (OR: 3.36; 95% CI: 1.22-9.31; p = 0.019). Although corticosteroid use (OR: 2.95; p = 0.233) and surgical duration greater than 3 hours (OR: 2.37; p = 0.092) showed trends, they did not reach statistical significance in this analysis. Conclusion: Diabetes mellitus was identified as the only independent risk factor for acute postoperative infection following instrumented lumbar arthrodesis for degenerative pathology, with a threefold increased risk. These findings reinforce the importance of a thorough preoperative surgical risk assessment, as well as clear and specific patient counseling in diabetic individuals within the context of informed consent, particularly regarding the increased risk of infection.

## ID: AGS26/2577

## P0624: Unlocking the predictive value of preoperative blood parameters in acute post-spinal surgery infection

Jorge Nuñez

1,2

, Anna Taberner

1

, Alex Guilera

1

, Juan Diego Montenegro

1

, Victoria San Gil

1

, Berta Escudero

1

1

Hospital Universitari MútuaTerrassa, COT & CARE Institute SL, Terrassa, Spain

2

Clinica Teknon, Barcelona, Spain

Introduction: Acute postoperative infection is one of the most serious complications in instrumented spine surgery. Hematological parameters have been recognized as useful prognostic markers in patients, including those with postoperative infections. The aim of this study was to assess the potential of these parameters in predicting acute postoperative infection after instrumented lumbar surgery. In addition, we evaluated their diagnostic accuracy according to patient sex. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of patients who consecutively underwent instrumented lumbar surgery between 2015 and 2023. Demographic data and preoperative hematological parameters, as well as derived ratios, were collected for analysis. The hematological parameters included white blood cell count (WBC), neutrophil count, lymphocyte count, monocyte count, platelet count, hemoglobin (Hb), hematocrit (Hct), and red blood cell count (RBC). Derived ratios included the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR), and platelet-to-volume ratio (PVR). The study population was stratified by sex. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves were generated to determine the area under the curve (AUC) and optimal cutoff thresholds using Youden’s J statistic. Sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value (PPV), and negative predictive value (NPV) were calculated, and statistical significance was assessed. Results: A total of 557 patients were analyzed, of whom 30 (5.4%) developed acute postoperative infections. All hematological parameter values were higher in the infected group. When stratified by sex, statistically significant differences were observed in neutrophils (p = 0.005), PVR (p = 0.006), and PLR (p < 0.001). To rule out infection, the parameters with the highest AUC values were MLR (AUC = 0.65) and neutrophil count (AUC = 0.64). In sex-specific analyses, neutrophil count showed the highest AUC in women (0.66), while MLR reached the highest AUC in men (0.69). Sensitivity and specificity varied among parameters and between sexes, but high NPVs were consistently observed, indicating their usefulness in excluding infection. However, no parameter achieved an AUC > 0.7, suggesting only moderate predictive performance. Conclusion: Neutrophil count and MLR were the most consistent predictors in both sexes; however, they demonstrated only moderate predictive value for acute postoperative infection following instrumented lumbar surgery, with slight sex-related variations. While high NPVs suggest their utility for excluding infection, their overall diagnostic accuracy remains limited.

## ID: AGS26/2605

## P0625: Microbiology and surgical outcome of native spinal infections: a retrospective study of 85 cases

Istvan Klemencsics

1

, Benjamin Hajnal

1

, Peter E. Eltes

1

, Dezsö Jeszenszky

1

1

National Center for Spinal Disorders, Budapest, Hungary

Introduction: Incidence of spinal infections shows an increasing trend worldwide due to the increase of older, multimorbid or immunocompromised patients. Infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacterial pathogens often become therapy-resistant and require long-term antibiotic therapy and multiple surgeries. Material and Methods: A total of 85 patients (68% male - 32% female; average age: 65 y) with cervical/thoracic/lumbar spinal infections who underwent surgical treatment were included between January 2014 and June 2025. The organisms detected by this procedure were investigated, and microbiological cultures and surgical outcomes were retrospectively analyzed. Results: Cultures of specimens obtained by surgical procedures were positive in 72 patients (84.7%). The most frequent site of spinal infection was the lumbar spine (58%), followed by the thoracic spine (37%) and the cervical tract (4%). 87% of the patients underwent decompression and stabilization. Reoperation was necessary in 29% of cases after primary surgical treatment due to progression of septic condition. 3 patients died in the early postoperative period after surgical treatment. 48% of the bacteria were Gram-positive pathogens. Methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) was the most frequently isolated strain in the cohort (28%). MDR was confirmed in 10 cases (N% = 11.76%), in 6 cases Gram-negative bacteria (4 Extended-spectrum beta-lactamases species, 2 Multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii), and in 4 cases Gram-positive bacteria Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Conclusion: In our study cohort, we observed the dominance of Gram-positive bacteria and the emergence of multidrug-resistant bacteria, as well as an increase in their proportion during the study period. Spinal infections, especially caused by MDR bacteria, are often difficult to treat. Further investigations are needed to understand the risk factors for MDR infections for early and appropriate therapeutic intervention.

## ID: AGS26/2614

## P0626: Multidisciplinary management model of espondilodiscitis treatment (TEAM-espondilodiscitis)

Julio González Vásquez

1,2

, Francisco Zamora

1,2

, Pablo Holmgren

1,2

, Felipe Valencia

1,2

, Maria Gabriela Sánchez

1,2

1

Universidad de Santiago de Chile, Santiago, Chile

2

Hospital Barros Luco Trudeau, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Pyogenic spondylodiscitis is a rare but increasingly prevalent spinal infection associated with high morbidity, prolonged hospital stays, and substantial healthcare costs. Diagnosis is often delayed due to non-specific symptoms and the frequent absence of fever, which increases the risk of chronic pain, vertebral instability, and neurological deficits. Multidisciplinary care has been proposed to improve early recognition, optimize treatment, and reduce complications. Material and Methods: A multidisciplinary management model-TEAM-Espondilodiscitis-was developed to standardize the diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up of patients with spontaneous infectious spondylodiscitis. The protocol integrates internal medicine, infectious diseases, spine neurosurgery, radiology, microbiology, rehabilitation, and specialized nursing. Key elements include: (1) early clinical and imaging evaluation in the emergency department; (2) prompt initiation of targeted intravenous antibiotics with transition to oral therapy when appropriate; (3) surgical decision-making guided by the Spinal Instability Spondylodiscitis Score (SISS) and Pola classification; and (4) structured outpatient follow-up with prioritized clinic visits. Results: Implementation of this coordinated model is supported by contemporary literature demonstrating significant benefits of multidisciplinary infection teams. Reported outcomes include earlier diagnosis, improved adherence to evidence-based antibiotic regimens, reduced hospital length of stay, lower rates of re-operation and relapse, and decreased overall mortality compared with conventional single-specialist management. Conclusion: A structured multidisciplinary approach is essential for optimal care of pyogenic spondylodiscitis. The TEAM-Espondilodiscitis protocol aligns with current international guidelines, promotes antibiotic stewardship, and incorporates emerging strategies such as telemedicine and artificial intelligence for early detection and risk stratification, ultimately improving patient outcomes and resource utilization.

## ID: AGS26/2627

## P0627: Microbiological resistance in surgical site infections in pediatric patients undergoing scoliosis correction at a specialized center

Nicolás Augusto Acosta Iriarte

1

, Juan José Coba Lozano

1

, Valentina Porras Rusler

1

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

1

, Nathalia Alejandre Montaño Argote

1

, Andrés Libos Zabala

1

, Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

1

Instituto Roosevelt, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Scoliosis is a three-dimensional deformity of the spine and one of the most common spinal disorders in children. In severe cases, surgical treatment is necessary, but this carries significant risks, with surgical site infections (SSIs) being one of the most challenging complications. These infections can be classified as early (≤ 90 days postoperatively) and late (> 90 days), and they affect morbidity and treatment costs. The most common microorganisms vary depending on the type of infection, with Staphylococcus aureus and Gram-negative bacilli in early infections, and Cutibacterium acnes and coagulase-negative staphylococci in late infections. The incidence of SSIs varies according to the etiology of scoliosis, being higher in cases of neuromuscular scoliosis. The management of these infections is complicated by antimicrobial resistance, and the literature on the microbiological profile in these patients is limited. Material and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted at a national referral center between 2017 and 2023, reviewing the clinical records and culture results of 24 pediatric patients who underwent scoliosis correction surgery. Patients with a confirmed diagnosis of SSIs through tissue or secretion cultures were included. Definitions for early and late infections were established according to the Colombian National Institute of Health and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC). Sociodemographic, surgical, and infectious data were collected and analyzed using the REDCap® platform, RStudio® and Excel® software. Analyses included a Kaplan-Meier survival curve to estimate the time between surgery and SSIs diagnosis. Results: An analysis of 24 patients with a median surgical time of 279 minutes and 10.5 levels operated on revealed that the median time to diagnose a surgical site infection (SSI) was 50 days, although most infections occurred within the first 30 days. Intraoperative prophylactic antibiotics were administered to most patients. Of the 28 cultures analyzed, microorganisms were isolated in 91.67% of cases, with Staphylococcus aureus being the dominant pathogen, accounting for 50% of all isolates. A significant finding was that 21.4% of the S. aureus isolates were methicillin-resistant (MRSA). The analysis also showed other worrying resistance profiles, including extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) resistance in Proteus mirabilis and Escherichia coli, and complete methicillin resistance (MRSE) in all Staphylococcus epidermidis cases. This highlights the high prevalence of S. aureus and the significant challenge posed by its antimicrobial resistance, which complicates treatment. Conclusion: The results showed significant heterogeneity in microbial etiology, with a predominance of Staphylococcus aureus in late infections, especially MSSA strains, and a high incidence of polymicrobial infections. This highlights the need for an individualized diagnostic approach. The infection rate varied according to the etiology of scoliosis, being higher in neuromuscular cases, which is consistent with the literature. Unlike other studies, no late infections due to Cutibacterium acnes were identified, underscoring the importance of adjusting antibiotic protocols to the local context. Limitations of the study include the small sample size and the lack of socioeconomic variables that could influence the occurrence of SSI. In conclusion, surgical site infections have a significant clinical and economic impact, especially in cases involving spinal instrumentation. Microbial diversity and antimicrobial resistance highlight the need to improve preventive and diagnostic strategies to optimize long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2849

## P0628: Pathologic and microbiologic evidence of multifactorial failure in cervical total disc replacements

Fedan Avrumova

1

, Philip Paschal

1

, Alexandra Satanovsky

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Darren R. Lebl

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Cervical total disc replacement (cTDR) has evolved over the years with newer models incorporating materials such as PEEK on ceramic, titanium plasma-spray coatings, and zirconia-toughened alumina ceramic cores. These designs were developed to preserve physiologic motion, minimize metal wear, and reduce postoperative imaging artifacts. Despite these advancements, failures of cTDRs have been reported. In three surgical cases, device failures were observed, with biomechanical dysfunction, abnormal motion, and associated infections identified. Material and Methods: Six cTDRs from three surgical cases, treated by two surgeons at a single institution, were retrieved during revision procedures converting cTDRs to anterior cervical discectomy and fusions (ACDFs). Surrounding soft tissue from the failed cTDR sites was also collected. All explanted cTDRs underwent microbiological analysis using swab sampling and culture, followed by microscopic examination. Pathology reports were generated, and biomechanical wear and material degradation were assessed. Results: Within the six cTDRs recorded across three surgical cases, all explants received both microbiology and pathology reports. In the first case, 5 of 9 swabs (55.6%) were positive for infection, including 2 of 5 (40%) that grew Staphylococcus epidermidis resistant to antibiotics and 3 of 5 (60%) that grew rare Staphylococcus epidermidis; pathology showed that soft tissue was collected and explant debris was identified on it. In the second case, 10 of 20 swabs (50%) were positive, with broth-only Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes identified in 9 of 10 (90%) and broth-only Gram-variable rods in 1 of 10 (10%); pathology revealed soft tissue changes with metallic, polyethylene, and ceramic particulate debris. In the third case, 5 of 8 swabs (62.5%) were positive, including broth-only Cutibacterium (Propionibacterium) acnes in 3 of 8 (37.5%), broth-only Gram-positive rods in 1 of 8 (12.5%), and broth-only Staphylococcus epidermidis in 1 of 8 (12.5%); pathology reported soft tissue alterations with hardware debris fragments. All explants underwent pathology evaluation and were subsequently referred for biomechanics or hardware review. Conclusion: Cervical total disc replacement failures in three surgical cases demonstrated both infectious involvement and soft tissue debris from metallic, polyethylene, ceramic, and hardware sources. When compared to traditional cTDRs, the findings were not consistent with prior retrievals; instead, they revealed unique hydrophobic behavior and peeling phenomena specific to this design. Further studies will be required to clarify these mechanisms, improve patient outcomes, and inform future device design.

## ID: AGS26/2919

## P0629: Hybrid minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion for non-tubercular spondylodiscitis. Role of compartmentalization in infection control

Laksh Agrawal

1

, DR Mihir Bapat

1

, Bharat Patel

1

, Amandeep Gujral

1

, Ashutosh Tripathi

1

, Rushikesh Jadhav

1

, Akshaj Sharma

1

1

Nanavati Max Super Speciality Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Conventional open debridement with transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) remains the gold standard for lumbar spondylodiscitis (SD) but is associated with problems of extensive soft-tissue dissection, creation of dead space, biofilm formation, implant loosening, tissue necrosis and recurrent infections. Although minimally invasive (MI) procedures reduce tissue trauma and accelerate rehabilitation, their exact role in addressing infection-related concerns remains poorly defined. We adopted a hybrid technique-Contained Minimally Invasive TLIF (Contained MI-TLIF)-which integrates midline microscopic transforaminal decompression with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation through separate intramuscular compartments. The aim was to evaluate whether this strategy compartmentalizes infection, preserves implant safety, and improves outcomes in non-tubercular SD. Material and Methods: Fifty-two patients (29 males, 23 females) with biopsy-proven non-tubercular SD underwent midline microscopic TLIF with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation under fluoroscopy. Pain and disability were assessed pre- and postoperatively using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Functional outcome was graded by Kirkaldy-Willis criteria, neurological recovery by ASIA scale, and fusion by Bridwell criteria. Infection control was monitored through serial C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), leukocyte counts, and MRI. Results: Fixation was performed at the diseased level in 34 patients and adjacent levels in 18. VAS and ODI demonstrated significant improvement postoperatively. Functional outcomes were excellent/good in 90% of patients. Radiological fusion was achieved in 98% (Grade 1-2). CRP and ESR normalized within a mean of 8 weeks, correlating with clinical improvement. Complications included two cases of superficial midline wound necrosis and one delayed screw-tract infection secondary to bronchopneumonia at 8 months. Importantly, the infection remained localized to the screw tract without spreading to the interbody fusion site, confirming effective compartmentalization. One patient required revision for implant failure which was a significantly low percentage. Conclusion: The hybrid Contained MI-TLIF technique is a safe and effective option for managing non-tubercular SD. By placing implants in a separate, uninfected myofascial compartment, it limits the risks of biofilm formation, implant loosening, tissue necrosis and recurrent infection, while providing reliable fusion, pain relief, and early rehabilitation. Compartmentalization appears to be a promising strategy for infection control in spinal stabilization.

## ID: AGS26/2921

## P0630: Neurologic deficits in infectious spondylodiscitis: medium-term treatment results

Ahmed Adel Bezzaouia

1

, Meddeb Mehdi

1

, Nader Dekhil

1

, Hassan Hachicha

1

, Khalil Habboubi

1

, Mondher Mestiri

1

, Hassen Makhlouf

1

1

Mohamed Kassab Institute of Orthopedics, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Infectious spondylodiscitis in adults can have significant negative consequences, particularly when neurological deficits are present. This sometimes makes surgery essential, or even the only option for preserving the patient's function and quality of life. The aim of our study is to report on our experience in the medical and surgical management of infectious spondylodiscitis with deficits, with medium- and long-term results. Material and Methods: This was a retrospective, single-center, descriptive study covering a 22-year period from January 2010 to December 2022, collecting data on patients who underwent surgery for infectious spondylodiscitis with neurological deficits.
