For each patient, we extracted epidemiological data, clinical, radiological, and biological characteristics, and details of their medical and surgical management. Results: We identified 35 patients who underwent surgery for infectious spondylodiscitis with a mean age of 63 years and tuberculosis as the cause in 52% of cases. Motor disorders were present in 74% of cases and bladder-sphincter disorders in 15% of patients. Spinal release-synthesis was used in 70% of patients, with complete neurological recovery in 67% of cases and neurological deterioration in 12%. Complications included three cases of pressure sores and two cases of sepsis on osteosynthesis material. Conclusion: This work highlights the importance of well-coordinated therapeutic strategies to optimize the management of this complex pathology and improve long-term outcomes for patients with infectious spondylodiscitis, whose therapeutic success depends not only on the type of surgery performed, but also on early assessment and adequate management of risk factors, including the type and location of the infection, the appropriate use of medical treatment, and the speed of intervention.

## ID: AGS26/2926

## P0631: Persistent oropharyngeal-cervical fistula following multimodal treatment of clival chordoma: a rare and challenging late complication in the upper cervical spine

Thomas Repantis

1

, Ioannis Papaioannou

1

, Kaiti Kyriakou

1

, Lexi Dejager

1

, Andreas Baikousis

1

1

General Hospital “Agios Andreas”, Patras, Greece

Introduction: Chordomas are rare malignant tumors originating from notochordal remnants, most frequently involving the skull base and sacrococcygeal spine. Despite advances in surgical techniques and adjuvant radiotherapy, local recurrence and late radiation-related complications remain significant challenges. We present a rare case of a persistent oropharyngeal-cervical fistula with direct communication between the oropharyngeal cavity and the upper cervical spine following multimodal treatment for clival chordoma. Material - Methods: A 40-year-old woman with no significant comorbidities was initially diagnosed with clival chordoma in 2008 and underwent subtotal tumor resection followed by adjuvant proton beam radiotherapy. In 2015, a local recurrence involving the C1-C2 vertebrae was treated with repeat subtotal resection and CyberKnife radiotherapy. In December 2021, the patient presented with a pharyngo-cervical infection after needle aspiration of the upper oropharynx, which was complicated by a pathological fracture of the anterior arch of C1. Cross-sectional imaging revealed a fistulous tract between the oropharyngeal cavity and the lateral mass of C1-C2, most likely due to radiation-induced osteoradionecrosis combined with secondary infection. Results: Initial management included targeted antibiotic therapy and the PENTOCLO protocol (pentoxifylline, tocopherol, clodronate) to address osteoradionecrosis. In April 2022, occipitocervical fusion was performed to stabilize the pathological fracture and prevent further deformity or neurological compromise. Despite these interventions, the fistula persisted. The most recent follow-up in September 2025 showed that the patient remains neurologically intact and fully functional, participating in daily and even strenuous activities. The most recent CT scan demonstrated a large communication between the oropharyngeal cavity and the pre- and paraspinal spaces on the left side, with a cavity showing a wide opening into the oropharynx and extending laterally beyond the midline at the level of C2. Compared with a CT scan performed two years earlier, the cavity demonstrated progressive expansion and a significantly wider communication channel. A multidisciplinary team proposed surgical closure of the defect using a free flap in collaboration between plastic and neurosurgical teams. However, the patient declined surgical intervention due to concerns about potential complications and continues to be monitored closely with regular clinical and laboratory follow-up. PET-CT revealed no evidence of tumor recurrence. Conclusion: This case illustrates an exceptionally rare and complex late complication after multimodal management of chordoma involving the upper cervical spine. Radiation-induced osteoradionecrosis, pathological fracture, and subsequent fistula formation represent significant therapeutic challenges due to compromised tissue vascularity, chronic contamination from the oropharyngeal flora, and proximity to vital neurovascular structures. Optimal management requires a multidisciplinary approach, combining spinal stabilization, meticulous infection control, and individualized reconstructive strategies to prevent catastrophic outcomes and preserve neurological function.

## ID: AGS26/2927

## P0632: Spinal tuberculosis at different anatomical levels: surgical outcomes of five patients

Fatma Hediye Erzurumlu

1

, Betul Yaman

1

, Inan Uzunoglu

1

, Goktug Ulku

1

, Samet Dınc

1

1

Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Spinal tuberculosis (TB) remains a significant clinical problem in both developing and developed countries, with an increasing incidence in recent years. The disease can affect any level of the spine, leading to neurological deficits, spinal deformities, and abscess formation. The purpose of this case series is to present the surgical and medical outcomes of spinal TB cases with different localizations, ranging from the craniovertebral junction to the lumbar region. Preoperative MRI: contrast-enhanced T1-weighted sagittal, T1-weighted, and contrast-enhanced T1-weighted axial sequences.

 

Materials and Methods: Between 2023 and 2024, five patients diagnosed with spinal TB and treated surgically at Ankara Etlik City Hospital were retrospectively evaluated. Demographic characteristics, levels of involvement, surgical approaches, pathological findings, and postoperative clinical courses were analyzed. All patients received postoperative antituberculous pharmacotherapy in accordance with standard treatment protocols. Postoperative MRI: T1-weighted sagittal, T1-weighted axial sequences. Postoperative lateral and AP graphy.

 

 

Results: The series included five patients aged 22-60 years, consisting of four females and one male. The distribution of spinal involvement was as follows: craniovertebral (n = 1), lower cervical (n = 1), thoracic (n = 1), and lumbar (n = 2).
• The craniovertebral case underwent posterior stabilization due to tetraparesis.
• In the lower cervical case, instability following tracheostomy was surgically corrected.
• The thoracic case presented with a Pott’s abscess at T12, treated with multilevel stabilization and corpectomy.
• Two lumbar cases (L3-iliac and L4-5 levels) underwent abscess drainage and stabilization.
• Histopathological analysis revealed necrotizing granulomatous inflammation, and cultures confirmed Mycobacterium tuberculosis. All patients demonstrated postoperative neurological improvement, with no major surgical complications observed. Conclusion: Spinal TB can present with heterogeneous clinical manifestations depending on the level of involvement. Surgical stabilization and abscess drainage, when indicated, play a crucial role in improving neurological outcomes and preventing deformity. Early surgical intervention not only facilitates histopathological confirmation but also enhances the effectiveness of subsequent antituberculous therapy. Our series highlights the importance of timely combined surgical and medical management in achieving favorable outcomes for patients with spinal TB.

## ID: AGS26/3008

## P0632.1: Effectiveness of the therapy strategy in patients with negative cultures in a tertiary reference center

Juan Carlos Verde

1

, Amado González

1

, Marcos Felipe Contreras

1

, Iris Jacqueline Sotelo

1

1

Centro Médico ISSEMyM Ecatepec, Estado de México, Mexico

Introduction: Over the past two decades, a significant increase in the incidence of spondylodiscitis has been documented in Europe, attributable to the aging population, a higher prevalence of chronic diseases, and immunodeficiency states. Native pyogenic spondylodiscitis is a serious spinal infection, capable of causing disabling pain, neurological deficits, vertebral deformities, and systemic complications. Identifying the causative agent is essential for instituting targeted antibiotic therapy; however, between 37% and 50% of patients remain culture-negative despite available diagnostic methods. Establishing optimal antimicrobial management, its duration, and the criteria for selecting patients for conservative or surgical management are currently challenging. In this context, evaluating the effectiveness of different therapeutic strategies in patients with culture-negative native pyogenic spondylodiscitis is a priority. Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of the therapeutic strategy implemented in patients with culture-negative native pyogenic spondylodiscitis at our tertiary care center in terms of clinical outcomes and complication reduction. Materials and Methods: An observational, longitudinal, and retrospective study was conducted. It included 108 patients diagnosed with culture-negative native pyogenic spondylodiscitis. Results: A total of 108 patients diagnosed with native pyogenic spondylodiscitis and negative cultures were analyzed. All received an initial regimen of intravenous third-generation cephalosporin and vancomycin for two weeks, followed by oral treatment with either a fluoroquinolone or linezolid for an additional four weeks. The overall therapeutic success rate, defined as absence of recurrence or persistence of infection at the end of follow-up, was 87% (n = 94; 95% CI: 80.1-92.4). Furthermore, 60% (n = 65; 95% CI: 50.2-69.1) of patients presented some degree of neurological deficit at the time of diagnosis. None of these patients were treated with surgery; all received conservative management, with significant clinical improvement documented during follow-up (p < 0.05). Subgroup analysis showed no statistically significant differences in success rates between patients treated with fluoroquinolone versus linezolid in the oral phase (p = 0.41). Furthermore, no association was identified between the initial presence of neurological deficit and infection recurrence (p = 0.27). These findings suggest that the antibiotic regimen used offers high clinical effectiveness in patients with negative cultures, and that conservative management may be a safe alternative in the presence of neurological deficit, avoiding the need for surgery in most cases. Conclusion: Culture-negative native pyogenic spondylodiscitis continues to represent a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. Our results show that a structured empirical antibiotic regimen achieved 87% clinical success, even in the absence of microbiological identification. Furthermore, conservative management in patients with neurological deficits was safe and effective, avoiding surgery in most cases. These findings support the need for prospective studies that define standardized therapeutic regimens and criteria.

## ID: AGS26/3201

## P0633: Is back pain a red-flag sign in hemodialysis patients?

Ismail Bozkurt

1

, Huseyin Ozmat

2

, Ulkun Unsal

3

1

Yuksek Ihtisas University, Faculty of Medicine, Department of Neurosurgery, Medical Park Ankara Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

2

Mamak State Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

3

Manisa City Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Manisa, Turkey

Introduction: Infections are frequent complications in patients undergoing hemodialysis (HD). Patients with a catheter face a 2-3-fold higher risk of hospitalization due to infection and mortality compared to an arteriovenous fistula or graft. The primary causative pathogens are gram-positive bacteria, with Staph. Aureus and coagulase-negative staphylococci.1 Recognized issues associated with catheters include bacteremia, infections at the exit site or tunnel, and frequent clotting episodes that necessitate intervention. Effectively managing infected HD catheters is a complex task, with limited evidence-based guidelines available. Nevertheless, persistent bacteremia and the occurrence of metastatic complications, such as epidural abscess, osteomyelitis, discitis, and endocarditis, continue to pose significant challenges for these patients.2 Spinal infections are infrequent but severe consequences of catheter infections, necessitating surgical intervention and frequently resulting in paralysis or death. Such complications arise from a bacteremic episode during a prolonged period to salvage the infected catheter. The duration between the diagnosis of bacteremia and the onset of spinal infection remains unclear. In this study we present our experience with 3 chronic HD patients who presented with spondylodiscitis. Material and Methods: All patients were receiving hemodialysis 3 times a week through a tunneled catheter. (Table 1)
 Results: HD patients are at a risk factor for vertebral infections through antegrade bacteremia. Repeated vascular puncturing, long-term catheter or the contamination of dialysis system are known risk factors. Spondylodiscitis in HD patients have a poorer prognosis with a longer hospital stay, higher 1-year mortality and 2.2 fold increase in in-hospital mortality.3 HD patients tend to have additional co-morbidities further complicating the surgery and post-operative rehabilitation. Thus it is important to quickly recognize, diagnose and treat spinal infections in HD patients. Fever may not always be a presenting symptom in spinal infections of HD patients.3 However, as is the case in our three patients, back pain and elevated CRP levels are almost always present. 2 cases that were operated on had negative bacterial cultures and this may be due to prolonged antibiotic use before recognizing the spinal infection. Cases that are overseen may present with neurological findings. These may also go unrecognized as HD patients are more prone to have little or no activity. Pain is also a frequent complaint of HD patients thus back pain may also be overseen as a non-specific finding. Previous studies have reported a diagnosis period of 3 to 6 months from the onset of back pain.4 Our patients had a similar delayed diagnosis period even after the neurological deficits had developed. Although surgical treatment in these patients increase the neurological recovery only slightly, it allows for the stabilization of the spine and decreases pain thus granting the patient to sit upright. Presence of neurological deficit and identification of MRSA are causes of increased mortality whereas surgical treatment has an OR of 0.37 suggesting a role in reducing mortality rates.4
Conclusion: Hemodialysis patients are prone to a variety of infections including various forms of spinal infections. This risk increases with the use of catheters. Pain is common amongst HD patients and new pain complaints such as back pain can be seen as a non-specific symptom. This prolonged period of diagnosis may lead to irreversible neurological deficits. Thus the primary aim of the surgery becomes to reduce pain, prevent instability, limit the spread of the infection and obtain tissue culture. These three cases have shown us that back pain in HD patients may be a red-flag sign that warrants further evaluation and investigation. In particular, patients with elevated CRP levels along with back pain, it is of vital importance to screen for any spinal pathologies.

References

1. Miller LM, Clark E, Dipchand C, Hiremath S, Kappel J, Kiaii M, Lok C, Luscombe R, Moist L, Oliver M, MacRae J; Canadian Society of Nephrology Vascular Access Work Group. Hemodialysis Tunneled Catheter-Related Infections. Can J Kidney Health Dis. 2016 Sep 27;3:2054358116669129. doi: 10.1177/2054358116669129.
2. Philipneri M, Al-Aly Z, Amin K, Gellens ME, Bastani B. Routine replacement of tunneled, cuffed, hemodialysis catheters eliminates paraspinal/vertebral infections in patients with catheter-associated bacteremia. Am J Nephrol. 2003 Jul-Aug;23(4):202-7. doi: 10.1159/000071479.
3. Kuo G, Sun WC, Lu YA, Chen CY, Kao HK, Lin Y Jr, Chen YC, Hung CC, Tian YC, Hsu HH. Chronic dialysis patients with infectious spondylodiscitis have poorer outcomes than non-dialysis populations. Ther Clin Risk Manag. 2018 Feb 13;14:257-263. doi: 10.2147/TCRM.S153546.
4. Madhavan K, Chieng LO, Armstrong VL, Wang MY. Spondylodiscitis in end-stage renal disease: a systematic review. J Neurosurg Spine. 2019 Feb 15:1-9. doi: 10.3171/2018.9.SPINE18824.

## ID: AGS26/3209

## P0634: Early onset osteoporosis after the COVID-19 pandemic

Ismail Bozkurt

1

, Huseyin Ozmat

2

, Ulkun Unsal

3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Yuksek Ihtisas University, Faculty of Medicine, Medical Park Ankara Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Mamak State Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Manisa City Hospital, Manisa, Turkey

Introduction: To explore the potential association between SARS-CoV-2 infection and early-onset osteoporosis, and to assess the role of postpartum physiology in predisposing young women to vertebral fragility fractures. The systemic impact of SARS-CoV-2 has extended beyond respiratory pathology, with recent literature suggesting deleterious effects on bone metabolism. Mechanisms include inflammatory activation (notably NLRP3 inflammasome), hypocalcaemia, vitamin D deficiency, and mechanical unloading. Postpartum osteoporosis, while rare, may be underrecognized during periods of physiological stress such as the COVID-19 pandemic. Increasingly, spine surgeons are encountering vertebral compression fractures in young women without traumatic antecedents. Material and Methods: A case series of five postpartum women (ages 27-34) presenting with acute vertebral fractures during the COVID-19 pandemic was evaluated. All patients underwent spinal MRI or CT, bone densitometry (DEXA), and laboratory assessment. Relevant clinical data-including COVID-19 infection history, vitamin D levels, and T-scores-were analyzed. Findings were contextualized with recent literature on pandemic-related bone health changes. Results: All patients were actively breastfeeding; four had a confirmed history of COVID-19 infection (ranging from one to three episodes). Vertebral involvement included thoracic, lumbar, and sacral regions. Imaging revealed classic features of fragility fractures, without preceding trauma. DEXA scans demonstrated T-scores between -1.0 and -3.8. Three patients were vitamin D deficient (< 20 ng/mL). Conservative management, including cessation of nursing, cholecalciferol supplementation, and in some cases bisphosphonates, led to symptom resolution. These observations align with emerging data on decreased bone mineral density in young adults during the pandemic. Conclusion: These findings underscore the need for heightened clinical awareness among neurosurgeons and spine specialists when evaluating postpartum women with atraumatic back pain. Early identification and multidisciplinary management can prevent misdiagnosis and avert unnecessary surgical intervention.

## ID: AGS26/3210

## P0635: Haematogenous spondylodiscitis in patients with liver cirrhosis: case series of 36 patients

Hamdan Abdelrahman

1

, Mootaz Shousha

2

, Heinrich Boehm

2

1

Asiut University Hospitals, Asiut, Egypt

2

Zentral Klinik, Bad Berka, Germany

Introduction: The incidence of haematogenous spondylodiscitis is increasing. This may due to a combined effect between an increase in susceptible populations and an improved accuracy in diagnosis. Patients with liver cirrhosis are well known to be prone to infections. Susceptibility to infection in cirrhosis is related to impaired defense mechanisms due to impaired reticuloendothelial system. These are a source of considerable morbidity and often mortality, and hence need prompt and aggressive treatment. Treatment of SI in those patients remains a challenge. Material and Methods: Retrospective analysis of database (2005-2015). In the series of 600 patients, 36 patients (6%) had liver cirrhosis; of them 14 patients had decompensated liver cell failure (26 males and 10 females; mean age of 60.67 ± 10.119 years). According to Child-Pugh score; six patients were class A, 9 were B and 21 were C. We have collected the demographic, clinical, radiological and laboratory data as well as the outcomes of management with mean follow up of 25.4 months. Results: 29 patients (81%) had ASA- Score class IV and 7 had Class III. The most common comorbidities were cardiac in 17 patients, diabetes in 19, renal insufficiency in 16, other sites of infection in 9 and peripheral vascular insufficiency in 11 cases. The pre-surgical interval varied around 33.5 ± 25.1 days (5-150), neurological deficit impaired 24 patients (67%), fever in 15 (an organism could be isolated from blood culture in 10 of them). Nine patients presented with septic manifestations. The lumbar spine was the most common region affecting 25 patients (69%). Non-contiguous infection was found in 9 patients (25%), epidural abscess in 24 and psoas abscess in 12 individuals. The preop. CRP and ESR were elevated in all cases (ø 106 ± 83.22 & 94.9 ± 38.69 respectively) and WBC was pathological in 13 individuals (36%). The commonest isolated organisms were Staph. aureus in 17 patients (MRSA in 4 of them). Ventrodorsal approach was necessary in 28 patients, 30 had minimal invasive surgery and biopsy only was done for microbiological examination in 2 cases. Two patients were re-operated due to recurrent infection (5%). Two patients died with septic shock, 2 with liver cell failure and one with cardiac infarction (in-hospital mortality of 14%). The neurological improvement rate by at least one ASIA grade was 82%. The preop. mean VAS was 8 ± 0.92 and reduced to 2.7 ± 1.28 postop. The postop. antibiotic therapy had to be taken more than 8 weeks in 22 patients. In 15 patients a combination of antibiotics was necessary. Conclusion: Spondylodiscitis in cirrhotic patients is often associated with neurological deficit (67%) as well as high mortality rate especially in patients with decompensated liver cirrhosis. Conservative treatment by antibiotics mostly does not reach the target and external immobilization is often impractical. Because of the poor general status, the indication for surgery should be carefully considered. Surgical intervention for SI in such patients can be performed with acceptable outcomes. Minimal invasive surgeries could be favorable in such cases.

## ID: AGS26/3353

## P0636: Evaluating the safety of prophylactic antibiotic-loaded calcium sulphate beads versus topical antibiotic powder in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis posterior correction surgery

Priyanshu Saha

1

, Aliénor Warr-Esser

1

, David Gower

1

, Febisayo Sidiku

1

, Zion Hwang

1

, Salvatore Russo

2

, Jason Bernard

1

, Timothy Bishop

1

, Darren Lui

1

1

St George's University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Department of Complex Spine Surgery, London, United Kingdom

2

Imperial College Healthcare NHS Trust, London, United Kingdom

Introduction: The objectives of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) correction surgery are to prevent deformity progression, spine balance, maintain neurological function, preserve spinal motion, and achieve cosmesis. Spine Surgery Infection (SSI) management is important in noninfected and infected patients. The use of antibiotic-loaded calcium sulphate beads was investigated in posterior scoliosis fusion surgery. Material and Methods: Retrospective review of all posterior approach surgical patients for scoliosis patients between 2015 and 2023 at St George’s University Hospitals NHS Trust, London, United Kingdom. Patient demographics; surgery approach and type; vertebral levels for posterior instrumentation; prophylactic antibiotic regime; washout/debridement surgery; length of postoperative stay; and postoperative infection rate were recorded. Results: 171 scoliosis posterior spinal fusion patients (126 Female, 45 Male), with an average age of 15.34 years. Patients that underwent posterior only approach was 157 and combined approach was 14. The average number of levels for posterior instrumentation was 12.22 (lowest:2, highest:22). 162 of the surgeries were primary and 9 revisions. There were 10 unintended return to theatres due to complications. 3 postoperative infections occurred that required debridement surgeries, of which all had received topical antibiotics intraoperatively. There were no postoperative surgical site infections in the antibiotic-loaded calcium sulphate beads. Conclusion: The use of antibiotic-loaded calcium sulphate beads for scoliosis correction surgery is a safe method of preventing SSI. Beads deliver local, dose-dependent, antibiotic activity even against “resistant” infections. Beads have been shown in other studies to deliver excellent biofilm reduction, slow continuous elution, and dead space reduction making it an excellent option for non-infected or infected posterior spinal surgery cases. It would prove beneficial for delayed SSI as well that intraoperative antibiotics would not provide.

## ID: AGS26/3395

## P0637: Culture positivity during primary lumbar fusion: risk factors and association with long-term surgical outcomes

Philip Paschal

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

, Tucker Callanan

1

, Gregory Paschal

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Federico Girardi

1

, Darren R. Lebl

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Occult bacteria have been implicated in pseudoarthrosis and implant loosening, yet their prevalence and clinical significance in primary spine surgery remain incompletely understood. This prospective cohort study assessed the prevalence of intraoperative culture positivity during primary lumbar fusion, identified associated risk factors, and evaluated its relationship with long-term surgical outcomes. Material and Methods: Fifty-four consecutive patients undergoing primary posterior lumbar fusion with pedicle screw instrumentation were prospectively enrolled. Inclusion criteria were age 18-80 years with no prior lumbar hardware, trauma, tumor, or infection. All patients received prophylactic antibiotics and antiseptic skin preparation. Intraoperative cultures were obtained from the superficial skin, dermal wound edge, hypodermis, and vertebral pedicle prior to instrumentation. Control swabs from the scalpel, suction, and tap were obtained before incision. Cultures were incubated for 14 days under aerobic and anaerobic conditions. Patients were followed a minimum of four years postoperatively to assess secondary surgical interventions. Results: Out of the 54 patients, 18 (33.3%) had at least one positive culture, averaging 3.1 positive samples per case. Rates of positivity were 13.0% at the superficial skin, 16.7% at the dermis, 13.0% at the hypodermis, and 19.0% at the vertebral pedicle. No correlation was found between vertebral body positivity and skin positivity. No control swabs (suction and scalpel) were positive. Cutibacterium acnes was the most common isolate, present in 83.3% of positive cases. Male sex (p = 0.007) and the use of povidone-iodine preparation (p = 0.017) were significantly associated with positive cultures. At four years, reoperation was required in 27.8% of culture-positive patients compared with 13.9% of culture-negative patients. Conclusion: Occult bacterial colonization was identified in one third of patients undergoing primary lumbar fusion, most frequently at the vertebral pedicle. Culture positivity was associated with sex, type of skin preparation, and higher rates of reoperation by four years. While causality cannot be established in this cohort, these findings suggest that deep-seated bacterial colonization may represent an underrecognized factor influencing long-term outcomes after spine surgery. Larger studies are warranted to clarify the clinical significance and to inform potential strategies for risk mitigation.

## Medical economics

## ID: AGS26/97

## P0638: Evaluating patient readiness for spine surgery: a cross-sectional study

Lindsay Orosz

1

, Rita Roy

1

, Thomas Schuler

1

1

The National Spine Health Foundation, Reston, United States

Introduction: Spine conditions affect millions, and spine surgery is increasing. Surgical innovation and recovery modernization have improved surgical outcomes, but the preoperative phase remains comparatively underdeveloped. A standardized protocol to prepare patients mentally and physiologically does not exist. The result may be a lack of surgery readiness, which can affect patient outcomes, experiences, and satisfaction. This study aimed to evaluate patient readiness for spine surgery by assessing satisfaction with surgery preparation. Material and Methods: This is a prospective observational cross-sectional study. Participants were drawn from a random sample of adult spine surgery patients, completed an anonymous survey, and were grouped as satisfied or unsatisfied with preparation. Additional variables included demographics, surgical details, satisfaction with surgery, and preparation details. Statistical analyses evaluated group differences, problem prevalence, and associations. Results: Of 300 participants, the median age was 66 years, 63% females, 67% lumbar surgery, and 52% had at least one prior spine surgery. Most cases were inpatient (86%) and elective (87%). There was no significant difference (p = 0.305) in the prevalence of those satisfied (43%) or unsatisfied (49%) with their preparation. The difference between those satisfied (42%) and unsatisfied (53%) with their spine surgery trended towards being significant (p = 0.05). Surgery satisfaction was strongly associated with preparation satisfaction, with participants being 28.5 times more likely to report preparation satisfaction when surgical satisfaction was present (p < 0.001). Surgical education, elective surgeries, understanding pain management, and surgery satisfaction were strong predictors of preparation satisfaction. Having a greater number of items (6-11 vs. 0-5) included in the preparation process was associated with preparation satisfaction (p < 0.001). Conclusion: This study detected vulnerability in the spine surgery preparation process. There was no significant difference between those satisfied and unsatisfied with their preparation, leaving room for improvement. A strong association between satisfaction with surgery and with preparation was detected. These results support a public health initiative to develop a standardized readiness guide. An evidence-based standardization of the preparation process has the potential to improve both surgical outcomes and patient satisfaction.

## ID: AGS26/539

## P0639: Demographic trends among residents applying for orthopedic spine surgery fellowships

Josh Callaway

1

, Rahul Bhale

1

, Hania Shahzad

1

, Zachary Lum

1

, Hai Le

1

1

UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento, United States

Introduction: Spine surgery remains one of the least diverse subspecialties within orthopedic surgery in terms of gender, race, and ethnicity. This study aimed to identify demographic trends and evaluate factors influencing match outcomes in spine surgery fellowships between 2022 and 2024. Material and Methods: Data on applicants to spine surgery fellowships from 2022-2024 were extracted from the San Francisco (SF) Match database. Sociodemographic variables such as gender, race, and ethnicity were recorded. Low sample size groups (< 5 applicants over 3 years) were either excluded as predictors-specifically Native American/Alaskan and Native Hawaiian applicants-or combined with similar groups, such as Black Non-Hispanic and Black Hispanic applicants. Univariate logistic regression was used to assess relationships, variance inflation factor (VIF) scores were calculated to check for multicollinearity, and a multivariate logistic regression model was developed to compare applicants who matched with those who did not. Results: Of the 593 applicants, 536 were male (90.39%), 51 were female (8.60%), and 6 did not disclose (1.01%). From 2022-2024, the percentage of female applicants increased from 7.07% to 10.29% and male applicants decreased from 92.93% to 87.75%. The percentage of applicants who chose not to disclose also rose from 0% to 1.96%. The largest ethnic growth was seen in Multi-Racial Non-Hispanics (1.09% to 5.88%) and Multi-Racial Hispanics (0.54% to 2.45%). The percentage of US Allopathic applicants slightly declined from 62.50% to 57.35%, while US Osteopathic and foreign medical graduate (FMG) applicants saw modest increases. Univariate analysis revealed significant correlations between match outcomes and gender, race/ethnicity, and medical school type (p < 0.05). The number of years since graduation and the number of applications to non-spine fellowships showed a negative predictive correlation (p < 0.001). After removing multicollinear features, multivariate logistic regression identified gender, White/Caucasian status, FMG status, total applications, and applications to AAHKS and pediatric fellowships as significant predictors of spine fellowship acceptance. Females were more likely to match (OR = 3.17, p = 0.0036), being White increased the odds (OR = 2.08, p = 0.0376), and FMGs had a lower chance of matching (OR = 0.02, p < 0.0001). Applying to more AAHKS (OR = 0.92, p = 0.00003) or pediatric (OR = 0.88, p = 0.0067) fellowships decreased the odds of matching, with minimal effect from other fellowship types. Conclusion: This study identified several factors that correlate with spine surgery fellowship match outcomes, including gender, race/ethnicity, FMG status, and the number of applications to other fellowships. While there has been some progress, with increased female and multi-racial applicants, structural barriers may still remain. Strategies for recruiting the best talent in the spine surgery fellowship match process, including mentorship programs and targeted outreach, are crucial for achieving greater representation. Finally, applicants should be mindful of the competitive nature of the match, particularly the role that trying to be competitive across multiple fellowship types can play in their success.

## ID: AGS26/612

## P0640: Utility of routine pathological examination of disc specimens after lumbar spine surgery: a retrospective chart review

Hanna von Riegen

1

, Peter Ahorukomeye

2

, Jerry Yang

1

, Mario Keko

1

, Sapan Gandhi

1

, Jason Pittman

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Harvard Medical School in Boston, MA, Boston, United States

2

Harvard Combined Orthopedic Residency Program, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA, Boston, United States

Introduction: Routine histopathological examination of disc specimens after lumbar spine surgery remains common but controversial. Although it may detect unexpected diagnoses, studies suggest limited clinical benefit and questionable cost-effectiveness. U.S. guidelines do not mandate routine pathology in discectomy cases, and institutional practices vary.The objective of this study is to evaluate the clinical utility and economic impact of routine pathological analysis of disc specimens in lumbar spine surgery. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective review of adult patients who underwent lumbar discectomy, microdiscectomy, or interbody fusion between January 1, 2020, and May 21, 2024, at a high-volume academic center. Inclusion required intraoperative disc specimen collection; patients with prior malignancy, surgical site infection, thoracic disc herniation, or facet cyst were excluded. CPT codes (e.g., 63030, 22630) were used for case identification. Data included demographics, pathology results, clinical-pathological concordance, treatment changes, and procedure-level cost averages from fiscal year 2023/2024. Results: A total of 192 patients were included (median age 49.0; 43.2% female; 79.2% White). Most (97.4%) underwent lumbar laminotomy for lumbar spondylosis (98.4%). All pathology reports confirmed herniated disc tissue; no unexpected diagnoses or treatment changes were noted. Routine histology cost approximately $18.67 per case. While per-patient costs were low, the cumulative financial impact could be significant in high-volume centers. Specialized pathology (e.g., immunohistochemistry) was rarely performed. Conclusion: Routine histopathological analysis of disc specimens in lumbar spine surgery did not yield diagnostic or therapeutic benefit in this cohort. Given its limited value and accumulating cost, a selective approach - reserving pathology for atypical or suspicious cases - may be more appropriate.

## ID: AGS26/791

## P0641: Decreasing medicare payments for anterior cervical discectomy and fusion from 2013 to 2023: an analysis of over 10,000 spine surgeons

Jason Silvestre

1

, Robert Ferdon

2

, Anthony Minerva

1

, John Moore

1

, Charles Reitman

1

, James Lawrence

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

2

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

Introduction: Despite its clinical utility and consistent procedural volume, Medicare reimbursement for anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) has undergone policy-driven changes over the past decade. This study investigated longitudinal Medicare reimbursement trends for ACDF among orthopaedic and neurological spine surgeons from 2013 to 2023. Materials and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed using publicly available Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) databases. Surgeons who billed ≥ 10 ACDF procedures (CPT 22551) per year were included. Provider demographics, procedural volumes, average Medicare standardized amount (AMSA), inflation-adjusted AMSA, and average annual procedural income (API) were analyzed. Comparisons were made by surgical subspecialty by year. Results: A total of 10,311 spine surgeons performed 187,062 ACDF procedures between 2013 and 2023. Neurosurgeons comprised 65% of providers and procedures, while orthopaedic surgeons comprised 35%. Average annual case volumes were similar across specialties. Between 2013 and 2023, inflation-adjusted AMSA declined 23% and API declined 27%, despite stable procedural volumes. These declines were consistent across neurosurgeons (-23% AMSA, -27% API) and orthopaedic surgeons (-22% AMSA, -27% API). The number of neurosurgeons performing ACDF decreased over the study period (17% decrease), while the number of orthopaedic surgeons increased (31% increase). Both specialties saw a reduction in average procedures per provider. Conclusion: Medicare reimbursement for ACDF declined significantly over the past decade across both orthopaedic and neurosurgical specialties. These findings raise concerns about the financial sustainability of providing ACDF services in the Medicare population and underscore the need for policy strategies that protect access to surgical care for an aging population.

## ID: AGS26/843

## P0642: 1-2 level lumbar decompression surgery is highly cost effective at 1- and 2-year follow-up

Aaron Buckland

1

, Luca Cordeiro

1

1

Spine and Scoliosis Research Associates Australia, Windsor, Australia

Introduction: 1-2 Level lumbar decompression is widely performed to relieve symptoms of spinal stenosis or disc herniation. With healthcare expenditures rising due to aging populations, technological advancements and growing demands, cost-effectiveness evaluations are essential for optimizing resource allocation and ensuring healthcare sustainability. This study assesses the cost-effectiveness of 1-2 level lumbar decompression in the Australian healthcare system. Material and Methods: A prospective adult registry cohort study was queried for patients undergoing 1-2 level decompression surgery by a single surgeon. Costs analyzed included hospital fees, medical professional fees (NoGAP) disposables, inpatient rehabilitation and revision surgeries. Patient outcomes, assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and EQ5D-3L, enabled cost per QALY calculation with discounting adjusted for health decline and annual inflation at 3%. Cost-effectiveness was determined using the World Health Organization threshold of Cost/QALY below 1×GDP per capita. Results: A total of 167 patients were included with 1-year and 89 patients with 2-year follow-up (mean age 59 yrs, 35% female). On average 1.2 levels were decompressed, the length of stay was 1.2 days, and 2% were discharged to rehabilitation. Revision surgery was required in 6.6% of patients by 2-years, including same-level decompression (2.4%), same-level fusion (2.4%), and adjacent-level surgery (1.8%). The mean total cost was $12,685.66 per patient, driven mainly by hospital costs ($6,844; 53%), followed by Medical Professional Fees ($3,530; 27%), Revision Surgery ($1,737; 13%), disposables ($674; 5%), and inpatient Rehabilitation ($163; 1%). ODI improved from Baseline 41.5, to 15.2 at 1-year and15.5 at 2-years. EQ5D Index increased from Baseline 0.585, to 0.821 at 1-year and 0.804 at 2-years. Cost/QALY was $47,113.61 at 1-year, and $22,167.90 at 2-years, well below the highly cost-effective threshold of $97,131.34. Conclusions: One and 2-level lumbar decompression surgery demonstrates significant improvement in patient reported outcomes at high cost-effectiveness at 1- and 2-year follow-up.

## ID: AGS26/850

## P0643: Single position circumferential fusion demonstrates clinical efficacy and high-cost effectiveness at 2 year follow-up

Luca Cordeiro

1

, Aaron Buckland

1

1

Spine and Scoliosis Research Associates Australia, Windsor, Australia

Introduction: Single-position lumbar fusion surgery (SPS), performed prone or lateral decubitus, aims to streamline workflows, minimize complications, and maintain high fusion rate and alignment restoration. With rising healthcare costs due to aging populations and technological advancements, evaluating cost-effectiveness is essential. This study evaluates the economic and clinical outcomes of SPS, focusing on direct costs, patient-reported outcomes, and Cost/QALY analysis. Material and Methods: A prospective, consecutive registry cohort of patients undergoing 1-2 level SPS for degenerative conditions using anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) and/or lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) with posterior instrumentation and minimum 1-year follow-up. Perioperative metrics and complications were recorded, including reoperations. Patient reported outcomes included ODI and EQ5D-3L, facilitating QALY calculations. Costs included hospital costs, consumables, implants/biologics, rehabilitation, professional fees and revision surgery costs using ‘no-gap’ insurance schedules. Cost/QALY was calculated, with discounting (3%p. a) accounting for ageing and inflation. High cost-effectiveness defined by the WHO guidelines as Cost/QALY < 1xGDP per capita. Results: 83 patients (mean age 65 years; 63% female) were included. Mean 1.3 levels fused, LOS 2.29 days and rehab disposition 3.4%. Baseline ODI 39, improved to 17 at 1-year and 15 at 2-years. EQ5D index improved from baseline 0.559 to 0.775 at 1-year, and 0.760 at 2-years with revision fusion in 1.2% (adjacent segment disease), and 3.6% requiring further decompression. Average cost for 1-2 level SPS was $39,599AUD. Cost/QALY with discounting at 1-year was $182,608, and at 2-years was 72,269. By 2-years, SPS had crossed below the highly cost-effective threshold of $97,131AUD. The cost drivers per patient were implants and biologics (17,094; 47%), Inpatient Hospital costs ($12,228; 30%), Professional Fees ($7,633; 19%), Revision Surgery ($1,113; 3%) and Rehab ($493; 1%). Conclusion: SPS demonstrates substantial QoL improvements and high cost-effectiveness by 2-year postoperatively. Costs were driven predominantly by implant and biologics.

## ID: AGS26/1115

## P0644: Cost-effectiveness of epidural platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection versus open lumbar discectomy in two tertiary care centers of Bangladesh

Shahidul Islam

1,2

, Md. Hasan

3

, Ferdous Banu

4

, Merina Tanzil

2

, Fatima Sultana

2

, Tahmina Akhter

2

, Tonima Sabikun

2

, Warde Jannat

2

, TM Jafor

2

1

Ad-din Women's Medical College, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Barak PRP Medical Centre, Dhaka, Bangladesh

3

Holy Family Red Crescent Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

4

Dhaka Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Disc herniation is a leading cause of disability and economic loss worldwide. Surgery is effective but expensive; whereas epidural platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection has shown potential as a minimally invasive, cost-saving alternative. This study aims to compare the cost-effectiveness of epidural PRP injection with open lumbar discectomy in patients with lumbar intervertebral disc herniation. Materials and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted at two tertiary hospitals. Patients undergoing either epidural PRP injection or open lumbar discectomy were included. Direct costs (procedure, hospital stay, medications), indirect costs (workday loss), and clinical outcomes such as Visual Analog Scale (VAS) were analyzed. Incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) was calculated. Results: 100 patients were evaluated (PRP: n = 50, Surgery: n = 50). The mean treatment cost was significantly lower in the PRP group (USD 120) compared with the Surgery group (USD 635). Average workday loss were 3 days for PRP and 30 days for open surgery. At 12-month follow-up, VAS reduction was measured (PRP 80% vs. surgery 95%). PRP demonstrated a favorable ICER, indicating cost savings without significantly affecting outcomes. Conclusion: Epidural PRP injection provides near similar clinical improvement with much less cost and hospital stay (OPD based), quicker return to work compared to open lumbar discectomy. PRP offers a cost-effective alternative that may reduce the economic burden of intervertebral disc prolapse.

## P0645: Causes of cancellation in elective spinal surgery: frequency analysis and associated predictors

Karen Weissmann

1,2

, Gianella Benvenuto

3,4

, Marcelo Costa

1,5

, Carlos Huaiquilaf

1,5

1

Clinica MEDS, Patagonia Spine Foundation, Santiago, Chile

2

Patagonis Spine Foundation, Santiago, Chile

3

Universidad de Santiago de Chile, Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Santiago, Chile

4

Hospital Exequiel Gonzalez Cortes, Santiago, Chile

5

Redsalud, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Spinal surgery represents one of the surgical specialties with the greatest organizational complexity and demand for technical and human resources. The cancellation of elective procedures constitutes an adverse event that compromises institutional efficiency and the patient’s experience. Materials and Methods: We conducted a retrospective observational study based on an institutional database that included 421 patients with a medical indication for spinal surgery who ultimately did not undergo the procedure. Each patient was asked why the surgery was not performed. Organizational and demographic variables were analyzed. Multinomial logistic regression was used to determine predictive variables. The level of statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: The three most frequent reasons for cancellation were: “Cause not reported,” “Personal reasons,” and “Economic reasons.” Independent predictive factors were hospital center (p = 0.0006), the physician who indicated the surgery (p = 0.000), use of supplies (p = 0.0094), and operating room hours (p = 0.0148). In the multivariate analysis, the variables with the greatest predictive weight for cancellation were: treating physician (relative importance = 0.47; p < 0.01), hospital center (0.06; p = 0.03), type of surgery (0.02; p = 0.08), and diagnosis (0.00; p = 0.56). The overall accuracy of the model was 45.4%. Conclusion: The causes of cancellation in elective spinal surgery show significant association with institutional factors. Identifying these patterns allows the development of targeted strategies to reduce preventable causes and optimize the efficiency of the surgical system.

## ID: AGS26/1393

## P0647: Quality-adjusted life years in spine surgery

Justin Zheng

1

, William Karakash

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Genevieve Fraipont

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Spinal disorders such as degenerative disc disease, spinal stenosis, and disc herniation are major causes of chronic pain and disability worldwide. Surgical intervention often improves quality-adjusted life years (QALY) compared to conservative care, a key measure in cost-utility analysis. With rising spine surgery rates in the aging U.S. population, evaluating safety, economic value, and patient-reported outcomes is essential. While prior studies examined single levels, regions, or pathologies, variability in QALY across procedures and levels remains unclear. This study analyzed 1-year improvements in QALYs generated compared to baseline after single and multi-level cervical and lumbar fusions and decompressions. Material and Methods: Patients who underwent single- or multi-level cervical or lumbar decompression or fusion surgeries, with completed preoperative and one-year follow-up PROMIS-GH questionnaires at a single academic center were included. Patients with prior spine surgery, additional surgeries within one year or surgical indications for malignancy, trauma, and infection were excluded. QALYs were calculated by mapping PROMIS-GH to EQ-5D-3L values and using a validated formula to determine the net gain in health-related quality of life after 1 year postoperative. Baseline was defined as QALYs generated in the preoperative health-state over 1 year. One-sided paired t-tests assessed the significance of improvements in QALYs generated. One-way ANOVA and post hoc Tukey’s tests assessed differences between surgical groups. Linear regression was used to evaluate associations between baseline health states and postoperative improvements. Results: 218 subjects were included in the final analysis, with cervical decompressions (n = 4) excluded due to insufficient sample size. All surgical groups demonstrated significant 1-year improvements in QALYs generated. ANOVA revealed significant overall differences in improvement depending on type of surgery (p = 0.035). The largest gains were seen in 1-level lumbar fusion (QALY: 0.05 ± 0.04) and 1-level lumbar decompression (QALY: 0.05 ± 0.06), while the smallest were in multi-level cervical fusion (QALY: 0.02 ± 0.04) and multi-level lumbar decompression (QALY: 0.02 ± 0.05) (all p < 0.05). 1-level cervical fusion (QALY: 0.03 ± 0.05, p = 0.016) and multi-level lumbar fusion (QALY: 0.04 ± 0.05, p < 0.001) also generated more QALYs compared to non-operative management. Regression analysis demonstrated a significant negative association between baseline health status and postoperative improvements (R = -0.338, p < 0.001). Conclusion: QALYs generated were significant across all spine surgeries, with the greatest gains in 1-level lumbar fusion and decompression. Multi-level procedures showed smaller gains and higher negative outcomes compared to single-level counterparts. Lower baseline health states were associated with greater improvements, underscoring the influence of preoperative condition. Economically, if the 0.05 QALYs gained in single-level lumbar decompression were sustained for 5 years (0.25 QALYs total), cost per QALY = $88,708, referencing the Spine Patient Outcomes Research Trial’s costs. This is well within commonly cited U.S. willingness-to-pay ranges ($50,000-$150,000/QALY) despite not accounting for further improvements in health state beyond 1 year. These findings inform evidence-based clinical decision-making and provide valuable insights into the comparative effectiveness of spine surgeries in cost-utility analyses.

## ID: AGS26/1403

## P0647.1: Portal Message Utilization in Orthopedic Spine Surgery: Patterns, Predictors, and Financial Implications

Sydney Rucker

1

, Michal Williams

1

, Robert Ferdon

1

, Jason Silvestre

1

, John Glaser

1

, Charles Reitman

1

, James Lawrence

1

, Robert Ravinsky

1

1

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

Introduction: Electronic patient portals (EPPs) aim to enhance engagement and access to healthcare by facilitating communication between patients and providers. While extensively studied in primary care, their role in orthopedic spine surgery remains underexplored. This study aimed to quantify portal message volume, identify demographic predictors of high utilization, and estimate the potential financial and administrative burden portal messaging may impose on orthopedic spine practices. Material and Methods: In this retrospective review of patient portal messaging data from a single-center orthopedic spine department, we analyzed portal messages sent via Epic MyChart by 833 new patients seen between April 2, 2024 and October 31, 2024. Messages were categorized by timeframe and encounter type. Patients were stratified into low- (Group 1) and high-utilizers (Group 2), with high utilization defined as ≥2 standard deviations above the mean number of portal messages. Independent t-test and chi-squared analyses demographic differences between groups; including age, sex, race, marital status, provider, and insurance payor. Billable encounters were identified per Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) guidelines and assigned costs by Evaluation and Management (E/M) service level. A Monte Carlo Simulation was used to estimate lost revenue from unbilled patient-physician interactions (100,000 iterations, R Studio v4.3.3). Results: A total of 548 messages were sent (mean 0.658 per patient; SD 1.66; range 0-18). The most frequent encounter types included appointment scheduling (97) and imaging related concerns (94), followed by other post-operative concerns (79), pain management (70), and paperwork or other third party (59). High utilization (≥4 messages, Group 2) was observed in 43/833 patients (5.2%), with a mean of 5.19 messages per patient (range 3-18). Patient age at initial encounter was not a significant predictor of portal message use (p = 0.276). Race was the only significant predictor of high portal utilization (χ² = 6.172, p = 0.046), with White/Caucasian patients overrepresented in Group 2 (86.0% vs 70.3%), while Black/African American patients were underrepresented (7.0% vs 23.0%). Only 25/548 messages sent (4.6%) met CMS criteria for billable services. Messages distribution by E/M service levels was: Level II = 8%, Level III = 72%, Level IV = 20%. The simulation estimated a 95% confidence interval for total unbilled charges between $4,677 and $10,548. Conclusion: Portal message utilization among patients within the orthopedic spine department significantly varied by race, suggesting potential disparities in access or engagement. Although only a small proportion of encounters qualified as billable, this study highlights potential gaps in patient-physician communication and the growing administrative burden of post-encounter portal messaging. Further investigation is needed to elucidate breakdowns in communication, optimize portal use, and ensure equitable access to digital orthopedic care.

## ID: AGS26/1730

## P0648: The influence of marketing mix factors on the interest in undergoing surgery for patients with back pain

Dimas Rahman Setiawan

1

, Nurminingsih Nurminingsih

2

, Yuli Prapancha Satar

2

1

Sigma Brain and Spine Center, Jakarta, Indonesia

2

Universitas Respati Indonesia, Jakarta, Indonesia

Introduction: As a hospital specializing in spine treatment, our institution requires information to increase public interest in undergoing spinal surgery among patients with back pain and to develop effective and efficient marketing strategies that will enhance program growth and competitiveness compared to other hospitals. Material and Methods: This study employed a quantitative approach with a cross-sectional design. Study population consisted of 112 patients, and the sampling technique used was purposive sampling. Data were collected using a structured questionnaire consisting of closed-ended questions with predefined response scales. Data analysis was carried out using univariate, bivariate, and multivariate statistical methods. Results: The results showed a significant relationship between patient interest in undergoing surgery and the marketing mix elements of product (p = 0.036), price (p = 0.000), promotion (p = 0.005), people (p = 0.000), and process (p = 0.034). Multivariate analysis indicated that the most dominant factors were the marketing mix elements of people (p = 0.043) and price (p = 0.018). Conclusion: The marketing mix elements of product, price, promotion, people, and process significantly influence the interest of patients with back pain in undergoing surgery at our hospital. Among these, people and price are the most dominant factors.

## P0649: The Spinal Invasiveness Score (SIS) as a tool to improve surgical waitlist management

Gurjovan Sahi

University of Toronto Temerty Faculty of Medicine, Toronto, Canada

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, Department of Surgery, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

Division of Spine Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

4

Division of Spine Surgery, Toronto Western Hospital, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

Introduction: Spine care delivery worldwide faces significant challenges due to prolonged patient wait times. Conventional waitlist management methods that rely solely on patient count fail to capture case complexity, often leading to inequities-patients requiring complex procedures such as scoliosis correction typically wait longer than those scheduled for less invasive operations like discectomies. However, the Spinal Invasiveness Score (SIS) offers an alternative framework for waitlist organization that has the potential to address these shortcomings. SIS is a validated system that assigns points based on six procedural parameters: anterior decompression, anterior fusion, anterior instrumentation, posterior decompression, posterior fusion, and posterior instrumentation. By quantifying surgical complexity, SIS-based waitlists allow stratification by procedure category and enable more accurate comparisons of surgical demand and resource needs. This study explores the perceptions of spine surgeons and healthcare administrators on the benefits, limitations, and potential barriers to SIS adoption in routine clinical practice. Material and Methods: A qualitative descriptive study was conducted surveying spine surgeons and hospital administrators across Canada. Participants reviewed a case study comparing the current waitlist model with the SIS-based approach and were surveyed on their preferred method for wait time calculation and asked for insights on the potential advantages and barriers of adopting SIS. Responses were analyzed using thematic analysis to identify recurring themes around SIS’s perceived benefits, limitations, and barriers to adoption. Results: Among 21 respondents, 17 (81%) favored the SIS approach over the current system. This group included 9 of 11 surgeons and 8 of 10 administrators, showing robust support across disciplines. Respondents identified several benefits of the SIS approach:
• Enhanced Prioritization: The SIS model's ability to quantify procedural complexity allows for the subcategorization of waitlists (e.g., multi-level vs. single-level surgeries), facilitating more precise case prioritization.
• Improved Case Triaging: The SIS system enables more efficient triaging of surgeries based on their complexity, allowing healthcare providers to allocate resources more effectively.
• Better Waitlist Management: By distinguishing between different types of procedures (e.g., instrumented vs. non-instrumented), the SIS approach supports optimal scheduling and resource distribution. However, barriers identified included difficulty for the health system to capture SIS score for each surgery, cost of implementation and SIS not capturing full complexity of cases such as elevated BMI or revision status. Conclusion: The Spinal Invasiveness Score demonstrates strong potential as an equitable, standardized tool for surgical waitlist management that can be adapted to diverse global healthcare systems. By quantifying invasiveness, SIS provides a more accurate reflection of surgical demand than simple patient counts, supporting better prioritization and resource utilization. Although challenges remain regarding data integration and capturing case complexity beyond invasiveness, early experiences suggest that SIS facilitates tailored scheduling solutions, such as allocating longer operating sessions for complex cases. With thoughtful implementation, SIS could play an important role in addressing global challenges of surgical access and efficiency.

## ID: AGS26/2379

## P0650: Uninformed compliance or informed consent: what patients think about animal-based products in spinal surgery

Remil Ignatius

1

, Riaz Mohammed

1

1

Gloucestershire Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Gloucester, United Kingdom

Introduction: Animal-based products (ABP) are routinely utilized in standard spinal procedures. Patients ordinarly are uninformed of ABP used in their treatment, whilst most surgeons lack adequate knowledge to deliver comprehensive ABP information. Montgomery principles state that patients must receive sufficient information regarding their treatment, including material origins and ethical considerations. Increasingly in a multicultural society, a cohort of patients will necessitate an enhanced understanding of ABP used in their treatment, prior to providing informed consent. This study assesses patient understanding of ABP used in spinal surgery. Method: We conducted a prospective survey of 68 patients attending a spinal elective outpatient clinic. Median age 61 yrs (27-91years) of whom 17 patients were women, formed the cohort. A standardised questionnaire was distributed to assess patient awareness, acceptance, and preferences regarding ABP. The responses were documented and analysed. Results: 82% of patients were unaware of ABP utilisation in spinal surgery. 75% considered that all patients should be informed about ABP utilisation and receive reasonable background information prior to providing their informed consent. 26% reported a preference to be informed of ABP utilisation for an emergency spinal operation. 5% reported that they would not give consent for ABP utilisation for an emergency spinal operation. The most frequently cited reason influencing patients' decisions regarding animal-based products is ethical concerns. Horse-based ABP were deemed least favourable ABP. Conclusion: This study identified that most patients are uninformed of ABP routinely used in their spinal surgery. Despite varied responses to ethical, culture, religious and dietery considerations, most patients would accept the use of ABP necessarily involved in either an elective or emergency spinal operation. A strong preference for full disclosure regarding ABP utilisation confirms the requirement for its inclusion as part of fully informed consent.

## ID: AGS26/2447

## P0651: Robot-assisted anterior lumbar interbody fusion does not increase costs compared to computer-navigated procedures: a single-institution study

Jordan Gasho

1

, Genevieve Fraipont

1

, Edward Shontz

1

, William Karakash

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Robotic systems have been increasingly adopted in spine surgery to improve surgical precision and outcomes. Prior reports suggest that robotics reduce radiation exposure, lower surgical complications, and improve pedicle screw placement accuracy. While these studies suggest clinical benefits, the financial implications remain unclear, particularly in anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) with posterior spinal fusion (PSF). Current health policies prioritize high-quality care at low cost. With the growing adoption of robotic techniques, hospitals must evaluate the economic difference between robotic and computer-navigated ALIF. While some studies indicate that robotic systems can be cost-effective, others suggest that robot-assisted spine surgeries have a higher intraoperative cost. This study examines the cost-effectiveness of robotic-assisted versus computer-navigated ALIF with PSF. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on patients who underwent single-level L5-S1 ALIF with same-day PSF at a single academic institution from March 2018 to May 2025. Exclusion criteria included staged procedure, deformity surgery, multi-level fusion, and other interbody techniques. Patients were stratified by technique of pedicle screw placement into one of two groups: computer-navigated ALIF and robot-assisted (RA) ALIF. All RA-ALIFs were performed using the Mazor Robotic guidance system. Cost data pertaining to the procedure, including total cost, operating room (OR) cost, and other costs, were obtained from financial services. The medical record was reviewed for demographic data and clinical variables, including estimated blood loss (EBL), length of stay (LOS), and operative time. Descriptive statistics included Wilcoxon rank-sum test for continuous variables and chi-squared test for categorical variables. Multivariable logistic regression was used to evaluate cost across treatment groups. Results: A total of 75 patients met inclusion criteria, including 23 (30.7%) computer-navigated ALIFs and 52 (69.3%) RA-ALIFs. The mean age of the patient population was 61.0 ± 13.4 years and 54.5 ± 13.6 years for the computer-navigated and RA-ALIF cohorts, respectively. Thirty-seven (49.3%) of the patients were male, and 38 (50.7%) were female. Mean total costs were $44,525 ± 19,245 for computer-navigated and $42,994 ± 11,700 for RA-ALIF (all p > 0.05). Operative costs were $16,958 ± 14,775 and $17,977 ± 11,938, respectively, while other costs were $27,567 ± 16,900 and $25,017 ± 11,215 (all p > 0.05). EBL (126.59 vs 120.77 mL) and operative time (5.10 vs 5.28 hours) were similar across groups (p > 0.05). LOS was significantly shorter following RA-ALIF (4.30 vs 3.17 days, p = 0.01). Multivariable regression adjusting for age, BMI, sex, diabetes, and hypertension revealed no statistically significant difference in total cost, operative cost, or other costs between robot-assisted and computer-navigated ALIF (all p > 0.05). Conclusion: Robot-assisted ALIFs were not associated with higher total cost, OR cost, or other costs. These findings offer valuable insight into the economic viability of RA ALIF, emphasizing its cost-effectiveness. By clarifying the financial impact, this study can inform clinical practice and hospital policy, supporting the adoption of robotic techniques.

## ID: AGS26/2521

## P0652: Measuring value in surgery: a systematic review of economic indices across specialties

Kenneth Nguyen

1

, Emaad Lala

2,3

, Kento Yamanouchi

4,5

, Sydney Pattison

2,6

, Aiyush Bansal

2

, Abhinav Balu

7

, Philip Louie

2

1

Virginia Mason Medical Center, Tulane University, Seattle, United States

2

Virginia Mason Medical Center, Seattle, United States

3

Virginia Mason Medical Center, University of Washington, Seattle, United States

4

Virginia Mason Medical Center, Keio University, Seattle, United States

5

Keio University, Tokyo, Japan

6

University of Washington, Seattle, United States

7

Northwestern University, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Rising healthcare costs and increasing procedural complexity have accelerated the shift toward value-based care in surgery. Central to this shift is the ability to measure the relative value of interventions, balancing patient outcomes with economic impact. While numerous indices and models have been proposed to evaluate surgical value, there is little consensus on which measures are most appropriate, and variability across studies limits cross-disciplinary comparisons. To date, no systematic review has comprehensively assessed how value indices are being applied across surgical specialties. The purpose of this study is to identify economic value indices, characterize their components, and evaluate their application. Materials and Methods: This systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. PubMed, Embase, and Google Scholar were searched in July 2025 for studies published between 2015-2025 using combinations of “surgery,” “value-based care,” “cost-effectiveness,” and “value index.” Eligible studies were peer-reviewed, English-language primary research applying a quantitative value index, model, or formula to evaluate surgical care across any subspecialty. Exclusions included case reports, reviews, pharmacologic, or nonoperative interventions. Two reviewers independently screened studies, with arbitration by a third. Extracted data included surgical specialty, type of index, components measured (e.g., costs, QALYs, complications), study design, and key findings. Results: A total of 342 articles published between 2015 and 2025 met the inclusion criteria. Our systematic review identified 11 different indices used to measure economic value in surgery across various specialties. Frequently applied indices included the Incremental Cost Effectiveness Ratio (ICER), used in 189 studies (55.3%), and the Time-Driven Activity-Based Costing (TDABC) in 93 studies (27.2%). Other commonly used indices were the Cost-Specific Metrics (such as cost per length of stay or reoperation), applied in 92 studies (26.9%), the Return on Investment (ROI) in 23 studies (6.7%), and the Net Monetary Benefit (NMB) in 27 studies (7.9%). Additional indices included the Activity-Based Costing (17 studies, 5.0%), the Incremental Cost Utility Ratio (ICUR) in 8 studies (2.3%), as well as the Operative Value Index (OVI) in 6 studies (1.8%). Least commonly observed indices were the Net Health Benefit (NHB) and Net Present Value, each in 3 studies (0.9%), the Average Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (ACER) in 3 studies (0.9%), and the Procedure Value Index (PVI) in 3 studies (0.9%). Highly represented surgical specialties include general, orthopedic, neurosurgery, cardiothoracic, and vascular surgery. Conclusion: To our knowledge, this is the first systematic review to evaluate the indices and tools used to measure surgical value across specialties. We found that patient-level cost-effectiveness metrics, most notably ICER, remain the dominant approach. However, a growing number of studies employ broader economic and operational frameworks such as NMB, ROI, and TDABC, reflecting increased recognition of financial efficiency and resource utilization as critical components of value. This heterogeneity highlights the need for a more standardized framework to guide how surgical value is assessed across specialties.

## ID: AGS26/2536

## P0653: “The value proposition:” what do healthcare stakeholders actually value when it comes to enabling technology and innovation?

Grace Gannon

1,2

, Kenneth Nguyen

1

, Aiyush Bansal

1

, Rafael Garcia de Oliveira

1

, Philip Louie

1

1

Virginia Mason Medical Center, Seattle, United States

2

University of Washington, Seattle, United States

Introduction: Advancements in spine technology are essential for improving patient outcomes, but conflict often arises due to differing priorities held by stakeholders. There is a gap in understanding how diverse value priorities influence technology adoption and how to measure value in this field. This study aims to develop a stakeholder-specific value index to better assess spine technologies, guide informed decision-making and optimize resources. Materials and Methods: A modified Delphi study was conducted to identify key evaluation factors for adopting spine technologies, involving adults with 3+ years’ experience across spine surgery, hospital administration, medical technology, and insurance. Participants were recruited via purposive and snowball sampling, aiming for at least 50 diverse participants. The study included a survey with initial open-ended responses, followed by Likert-scale ratings to reach consensus (≥ 50% agreement) across value metrics like operational costs, patient satisfaction, and technological advancement. Data was collected through REDCap, analyzed thematically and statistically, resulting in stakeholder-specific lists of the top 10 evaluation factors and insights into technology scores across these criteria. Results: Out of 30 invited participants, 24 completed a 145-question survey. Consensus was defined as ≥ 50% agreement per question. Overall, the greatest disagreement appeared in Value Ratings & Barriers (36%) and Clinical Impact (44%), with notable gaps around reoperation rates, surgical expectations, and cost-effectiveness. Surgeons and medtech professionals showed the highest levels of non-consensus-up to 45%-particularly in Technology Features, Operational Efficiency, and Value Ratings. In contrast, hospital administrators demonstrated strong alignment across nearly all categories, reaching up to 100% consensus in Financial & Value-Based Metrics and over 90% in Operational Efficiency and Patient Outcomes. These differences highlight varying priorities across stakeholder groups, especially regarding usability, institutional support, and long-term value. Data from insurance/payer representatives was insufficient for analysis due to low participation. Conclusion: The impetus for this work was to identify the priority factors each stakeholder group considers when evaluating and adopting enabling technologies (ET). However, the data revealed that even within individual stakeholder groups, there is limited consensus on key ET-related factors. Results from the study highlighted substantial disparities in stakeholder predilections-particularly around value ratings, clinical impact, and technology features. These findings underscore a broader issue: while major decisions around ET are common in health systems, the early stages of stakeholder engagement are often combative rather than collaborative. This lack of alignment begins to uncover the deeper challenges health systems face when negotiating and implementing new technologies. To address this, the next survey will focus on areas where consensus is lacking, aiming to clarify or reconcile stakeholder value differences. Ultimately, this will support the development of a stakeholder-specific value index for spine technologies-providing a framework for more productive initial discussions and helping align adoption decisions with diverse perspectives to refine resource use.

## ID: AGS26/3299

## P0655: Why business tools are essential for managing a spine program

Aazad Abbas

1

, Gurjovan Sahi

2

, Nischal Ghimire

3

, Ali Eren

3

, Michael Johnson

3

, Jay Toor

3

1

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, Department of Surgery, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

2

Temerty Faculty of Medicine, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

3

Division of Spine Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

Introduction: Spine care delivery globally, and in particular Canada, is in a state of crisis and clinician advocacy to hospital leadership and government is essential to successfully implement positive change. While clinical research and quality improvement work are both well-established domains familiar to surgeons, business techniques such as granular financial quantification and operational modeling are not commonly used but can play a crucial role in advocacy efforts. This study describes 2 instances that business tools were used from October 2023 to October 2024 to successfully advocate for major progress in increasing the capacity of a provincial spine program. Material and Methods: The first example of the use of a business technique was granular financial quantification to compare costs of an existing program to export spine surgeries to the USA against the costs of building capacity within the province instead - via capital investment to expand the spine program within the province. The second use case was quantification of the existing waitlist via an operational model in order to accurately ascertain the exact OR resources required to address a surgical backlog. Results: The initiative led to the successful cancellation of surgeries being sent to the United States, yielding net savings of $30 949 per case and $4.1M per annum. The capital procurement for the secondary site expansion ($2.2M) along with an operating budget ($959 000) was approved due to the demonstration of a return on investment (ROI) of 27%. The precise quantification of the waitlist demonstrated a 4.3-year waitlist for spine surgery, on track to reach 5.7 years by 2027. This data provided a strong case for additional OR time, which is being incrementally added, after a fifteen-year status quo. Conclusion: Advocacy for additional resources was unsuccessful until the introduction of business tools resulting in accurate quantification (in this case, net profit/ROI values and precise wait times). We hope these techniques become more widely implemented nationally as our experience highlights the potential benefits of strategic data-driven decision-making.
