## P0656: Lateral access minimally invasive spine surgery in adult spinal deformity

Santosh Kumar Bashyal

1

1

Nepal International Health Services (NIHS), CAMS (Center for American Doctor Specialist), Kathmandu, Nepal

Introduction: Oblique lumbar interbody fusion (OLIF) and percutaneous posterior approach for screw fixation (PPS) are the latest minimally invasive treatments for spinal deformity in adult patients (ASD). Adult spinal deformity is a condition that causes sagittal and coronal imbalance, debilitating pain, neurological impairment, and/or functional loss. The approach to treating and managing ASD needs to concentrate on restoring age-specific harmonious alignment, comorbidities, and risk factors of everyone for the prevention of devastating complications and improvement of quality of life. The core concept of this minimally invasive spinal surgery technique (MISS) is to gain adequate strong fusion via the insertion of a cage. Some of the important points to consider are: 1) the placement of an accurate reference frame is necessary to avoid an otherwise risk of cage or PPS misplacement, 2) the exposure to radiation is significantly decreased for the operating surgeons and their staff, and 3) the navigating O-arm-guided PPS is accurate. The latest novel techniques used for ASD correction include oblique lumbar interbody fusion at the L5-S1 level (OLIF51) to consider success in creating lumbar lordosis and 360° fusion. The application of CT-MRI combined fusion image clarification purposes of lumbosacral vascular anatomy using 3D non-enhanced MRI and CT medical image fusion techniques. The objective of this retrospective study is to discuss key points that will help in the strategic planning of lateral access in ASD surgery. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 54 patients who had undergone OLIF with PPS for ASD from October 2019 to January 2022 (average 71.5 ± 6.2 years old, 4 males and 50 females) with a mean follow-up period of 29.2 months. Clinical outcomes are expressed by values including the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for back pain. The imagistic assessment was also performed preoperatively and at 12 and 24 months postoperatively. For OLIF51, CT-MRI fusion images were obtained before surgery. Results: Postoperative ODI and VAS were 30.5 ± 18.9% and 31.2 ± 6.9 mm, respectively. The average operating time and blood loss during the surgical exposure were 490.9 ± 85.4 min and 1195.2 ± 653.8 ml. Preoperative SVA, PI-LL, and PT were 96.5 ± 55.9 mm, 39.3 ± 22.1°, and 34.5 ± 11.0°, respectively. Postoperatively, SVA and PT became normal (24.1 ± 39.0 mm, 17.1 ± 10.3°) and PI-LL was ideal (2.4 ± 12.6°). Postoperative ODI and VAS were 30.5 ± 18.9% and 31.2 ± 6.9 mm. For OLIF51, the results revealed a gain in L5-S1 lordosis and intervertebral disc height of 9.4° and 4.2 mm, respectively. The complications consisted of PJK in 21 cases (38.9%), rod breakage in 5 cases (9.3%), and deep or superficial wound infection in 2 cases (3.7%). Conclusion: Clinical and radiological results of OLIF and PPS for ASD correction were excellent. The radiographic outcomes showed that OLIF51 created good L5-S1 lordosis and L5-S1 disc height. CT-MRI fusion images were useful for evaluating the vascular anatomy of OLIF51. The C-arm-free technique is a safe and effective technique that reduces radiation exposure.

## ID: AGS26/102

## P0657: Stable low-grade degenerative spondylolisthesis does not adversely affect clinical outcome of full-endoscopic decompression in patients with spinal stenosis

Yi-Hao Liang

1

, Maria Auron

2

, Yi-hyun Roh

3

, Rohit Kavishkar

4

, Dimas Rahman Setiawan

5

, Maria Pedraza

3

, Win Boonsirikamchai

6

, Jin-Sung Kim

3

1

Minimally Invasive Spine Center, Orthopaedic Hospital of Guangdong Provincial Hospital of Chinese Medicine, Guangzhou University of Chinese Medicine, Guangzhou, China

2

Department of Orthopedics, Spine Surgery Unit, Hadassah Hebrew University Medical Center, Jerusalem, Israel

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Seoul St. Mary’s Hospital, The Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, South Korea

4

Asha Nursing Home, Pune, India

5

Medistra Hospital, Jakarta, Indonesia

6

Department of Orthopedics, Bhumibol Adulyadej Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: The optimal surgical management of lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) in the presence of stable low-grade degenerative spondylolisthesis remains a subject of ongoing debate. This study aimed to evaluate the clinical outcomes and radiological stability of full-endoscopic unilateral laminotomy with bilateral decompression (Full-Endoscopic ULBD) in this patient population. Specifically, we sought to determine whether this minimally invasive approach compromises spinal stability or leads to progression of spondylolisthesis over time. Material and Methods: A total of 36 patients diagnosed with LSS and grade I degenerative spondylolisthesis who underwent Full-endoscopic ULBD at the Minimally Invasive Spine Center between February 2019 and December 2023 were retrospectively reviewed. All patients completed at least 12 months of follow-up, with a mean final follow-up duration of over 1 year. Clinical outcomes were assessed preoperatively, at 12 months, and at the final follow-up using the visual analog scale for back pain (VAS back), visual analog scale for leg pain (VAS leg), and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Radiological evaluations included canal cross-sectional area (CSA), lumbar lordosis (LL), segmental lumbar lordosis (SLL), sagittal vertical axis (SVA), slip percentage (Slip%), pelvic tilt (PT), and other sagittal alignment parameters. At the 12-month follow-up, mean scores were as follows: VAS back, 1.71 ± 2.04; VAS leg, 2.17 ± 2.19; and ODI, 14.82 ± 12.83. At final follow-up, the respective values were: VAS back, 1.92 ± 2.03; VAS leg, 2.26 ± 2.25; and ODI, 14.40 ± 13.35. Results: All clinical outcome measures demonstrated statistically significant improvement from baseline at both 12 months and final follow-up (p < 0.001 for all comparisons). Pain scores and functional disability were markedly reduced, indicating sustained clinical benefit of the procedure. Notably, the canal cross-sectional area (CSA) significantly increased at final follow-up (from 51.49 ± 28.41 mm2 preoperatively to 90.30 ± 50.60 mm2, p = 0.001), suggesting effective and lasting neural decompression. While lumbar lordosis and segmental lumbar lordosis angles did not exhibit statistically significant changes (p > 0.1), the slip percentage remained stable throughout the postoperative period (preoperative: 12.40 ± 5.34%; final follow-up: 11.99 ± 4.62%, p = 0.724), indicating no progression of vertebral slippage. Additionally, pelvic tilt improved significantly at the 12-month mark (from 20.37 ± 7.92° to 12.77 ± 8.47°, p < 0.001), although this change was not maintained at the final follow-up. Conclusion: Full-endoscopic ULBD is a safe and effective surgical option for patients with lumbar spinal stenosis and stable grade I degenerative spondylolisthesis. The procedure achieves significant and sustained improvements in pain and functional outcomes without inducing spinal instability or progression of spondylolisthesis. Future prospective studies with larger cohorts and extended follow-up are warranted to further substantiate these findings.
Keywords: Lumbar Spinal Stenosis, Degenerative Spondylolisthesis, Endoscopic Spine Surgery, Unilateral Laminotomy and Bilateral Decompression, Minimally Invasive Surgical Procedures, Clinical Outcomes, Spinal Stability.

## ID: AGS26/110

## P0658: Hybrid endoscopic thoracic discectomy using robotic arm and navigation for highly migrated calcified disc herniation: case and tech note

Yi-Hao Liang

1

, Jin-Sung Kim

2

1

Minimally Invasive Spine Center, Orthopaedic Hospital of Guangdong Provincial Hospital of Chinese Medicine, Guangzhou University of Chinese Medicine, Guangzhou, China., Guangzhou, China

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Seoul St. Mary’s Hospital, The Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: We discuss a case of T5/T6 highly migrated calcified disc herniation on the right side that was successfully treated by hybrid endoscopic thoracic discectomy robotic arm and navigation. Material and Methods: With the development of techniques, endoscopic spine surgery has emerged as a reliable treatment for thoracic myelopathy. This approach offers high-resolution, off-axis visualization of the surgical field. The field is poised to advance further as endoscopic instruments are refined, becoming less invasive and more precise through the integration of navigation and robot-assisted systems. Here we provide a step-by-step guide for performing the hybrid endoscopic thoracic discectomy using navigation and robotic arm for addressing high migrated calcified disc herniation. Results: A 62-year-old woman presented to us with chief complaints of Both legs’ weakness. She had difficulty standing and walking after squatting due to weakness in her legs and her ODI score was 66. On examination her both side knee extension and ankle dorsiflexion were grade 4 without dysesthesia. The imaging examination confirmed the diagnosis of thoracic myelopathy caused by a highly migrated calcified disc herniation at T5-6 level. The patient underwent an endoscopic thoracic discectomy using robotic arm and navigation for addressing highly migrated calcified disc herniation, resulting in an excellent outcome. Conclusion: The continuous development of navigation and robotic systems in endoscopic thoracic surgery enhanced accuracy in surgical incisions and instrument placement, as well as improved efficiency in locating pathology and achieving precise decompression. Endoscopic thoracic discectomy combines full-endoscopy and UBE techniques to leverage the benefits of both approaches, including the cross-viewing of full-endoscopy cannula and the use of larger Kerrison rongeurs under UBE.
Keywords: endoscopic spine surgery, thoracic disc herniation, migrated calcified disc herniation, navigation, robotic arm assist.

## ID: AGS26/178

## P0659: Endoscopic resolution of radiculopathy after minimally invasive TLIF: a case report with intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring

Alicia Collado Gosalvez

1

, Rafael Luque

1,2

, Tomasz Rumin

3

, Jose Luis Perez Gonzalez

4

, Eduardo Gómez

1

, Susana Martín Albarrán

1

, Ignacio Dominguez

1

1

Hospital Clínico San Carlos, Madrid, Spain

2

Clínica Santa Elena, Madrid, Spain

3

Hospital Infanta Elena, Valdemoro, Spain

4
Clinico San Carlos Hospital, Madrid, Spain,
Introduction: Persistent or recurrent radicular pain after instrumented lumbar fusion such as TLIF represents a significant clinical challenge. There is still very little literature available regarding this topic. Causes include epidural fibrosis, osteophyte formation, or foraminal stenosis. Revision with open surgery carries risks of bleeding, infection and neural injury, particularly in patients with postoperative scarring. Endoscopic transforaminal foraminotomy with osteophytectomy, guided by intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IOM), may provide an ultra-minimally invasive alternative in selected cases. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 44-year-old male patient who presented with progressive lumbar pain with right L5 radiculopathy for 2 years. The pain was moderately severe (VAS 7), requiring daily analgesics and preventing him from performing work activities. The patient has previous history of open L4 laminectomy performed several years ago in another hospital, followed by right-sided minimally invasive L4-L5 TLIF at our institution (October 2021) which initially improved his symptoms. On physical examination there is no neurological impediment. Our patient had scars from previous surgeries along the middle line (from laminectomy) and in both sides (Wiltse approach from TLIF). RMI and a CT showed right L4-L5 foraminal and lateral recess stenosis due to an L5 facet osteophyte and fibrosis. No hardware complications were detected. A uniportal right-sided endoscopic transforaminal approach in L4-L5 under general anesthesia and continuous IOM was performed. Severe foraminal fibrosis was debrided. The compressive osteophyte was removed with foraminotomy and motorized instruments until free mobilization of the L5 root was confirmed (“floating sign”). Isolated neurotonic discharges and decreased MEP amplitudes in the right gastrocnemius were observed initially, but normalized after decompression. Transient MEP changes occurred during cannula placement and resolved after repositioning. Sacral reflex study (H-reflex) showed right absent baseline responses, which recovered and normalized by the end of surgery. Representative intraoperative neurophysiological waveforms and endoscopic images are displayed in the poster figures. Results: The patient experienced immediate postoperative improvement in right leg sensory symptoms and was discharged within less than 24 hours. At follow-up, he reported significant pain relief and progressive return to normal daily activities. Postoperative CT confirmed removal of the osteophyte. No neurological complications were observed. Conclusion: Foraminal fibrosis and osteophytes are recognized causes of radiculopathy after TLIF. Endoscopic decompression provides an effective and safe approach in patients with important fibrosis and previous surgeries. This technique minimizes tissue disruption and avoids manipulation of existing hardware. The use of IOM was a key differential feature in this case, ensuring root safety and guiding decompression in real time. Intraoperative changes correlated with clinical outcomes. This case supports endoscopic foraminal decompression as a valuable option in selected post-fusion radiculopathy patients, thought further studies are needed to assess long-term effectiveness and reproducibility. Careful patient selection and a thorough understanding of the potential benefits and risks of the procedure are crucial for optimizing outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/225

## P0660: Indications for lumbar disc herniation surgery in patients without neurological deficits: a critical review of red flags and the role of pulsed radiofrequency combined with physiotherapy

Dmytro Ishchenko

1

, Krzysztof Szalecki

1

, Paweł Nauman

1

1

Neuroorthopedic Unit, Orthopedic and Trauma Department, Mazowiecki Regional Hospital św. Jana Pawła II, Siedlce, Poland

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation (LDH) is a common cause of radicular low back pain, with most patients presenting with no motor deficits. Current guidelines recommend initial conservative management, including analgesics, physiotherapy, and patient education for neurologically intact individuals. Surgery is typically reserved for cases with “red flag” signs - cauda equina syndrome or rapidly progressive neurological impairment. The indications for discectomy in patients without deficits remain controversial. This review critically examines the current evidence on operative decision-making in such cases, focusing on the predictive value of red flags and the role of pulsed radiofrequency (PRF) therapy combined with rehabilitation. Material and Methods: A literature review was conducted using PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Library, covering publications from 2000 to 2025. Search terms included “lumbar disc herniation”, “surgery indications”, “red flags”, “pulsed radiofrequency”, “physiotherapy” and “conservative management.” Eligible studies included adult patients with symptomatic LDH without neurological deficits. Emphasis was placed on comparative studies of surgical versus conservative treatment, interventional procedures such as PRF, and guidelines addressing red-flag frameworks. Additional focus was given to the literature on the influence of socioeconomic factors and intervention timing. Results: Urgent surgery is mandatory in patients with neurological deficits. Conservative management remains the first-line treatment for neurologically intact patients, with most patients experiencing symptom resolution within 6-8 weeks. However, delaying surgery in patients with persistent pain beyond this interval can negatively affect socioeconomic outcomes, including prolonged sick leave, reduced work productivity, and academic disruption. Early microdiscectomy offers faster pain relief and quicker return to function, although long-term results often match those of conservative treatment within one year. Evidence suggests that relying solely on red flags to guide surgical timing is inadequate, as it fails to address patient-specific contexts such as significant occupational or educational consequences from delayed recovery. PRF, particularly combined with physiotherapy, has shown promise in reducing radicular pain and in delaying or avoiding surgery. Physiotherapy, including core stabilization, manual therapy, and targeted exercises, remains the cornerstone of the treatment pathway. While PRF may extend remission periods and improve function, its variable efficacy requires careful follow-up. For patients whose symptoms persist despite optimal conservative and minimally invasive measures, early surgical intervention should be considered, even in the absence of neurological deficits. Predictive tools such as the Microdiscectomy for Lumbar Disc Herniation Scoring System may help identify patients most likely to require surgery. Factors, such as prolonged symptom duration, advanced disc degeneration, and nerve root compression, are associated with higher rates of conservative treatment failure. Conclusion: Surgical management of LDH in patients without neurological deficits requires an individualized multifactorial approach. Conservative treatment should be the initial strategy; however, exclusive reliance on red flags is insufficient for guiding elective surgery. Early surgery may be justified in cases where conservative care, including PRF and physiotherapy, fails to achieve sustained relief or when socioeconomic factors significantly affect quality of life. Treatment decisions should balance clinical severity, the risk of neurological deterioration, and patient-specific socioeconomic considerations. Future guidelines should incorporate both clinical and socioeconomic predictors to optimize the timing and selection of surgical interventions.

## ID: AGS26/226

## P0661: Restoring sagittal balance through a biportal endoscopic fusion combined with bilateral osteotomy

Diana Chávez

1

, Alberto Perez

1

1

Hospitales Angeles, Mexico, Mexico

Introduction: Endoscopic fusion is a minimally invasive spine surgery technique accepted as an alternative for the treatment of lumbar spine disorders. Biportal endoscopic decompression can be applied to almost all cases of lumbar stenosis and has become a safe technique for minimally invasive spine surgery. In cases of spinal instability a fusion is needed and also biportal endoscopic is an option. In the past two decades, high-level evidence has shown that sagittal balance correction parameters impact quality of life scales and future revision prognostics. The main objective of this study is the description of the biportal endoscopic technique for sagittal balance correction performing a interbody fusion and bilateral osteotomy. Material and Methods: With the use of the Unintech Endosurg Standard System and multimodal intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring with electromyography stimulation by the same spine neurosurgeon and neurophysiologist, Preplanning is conducted to select the correct cage size and determine the level of osteotomy. Two skin incisions of approximately 2cm are made, followed by dissection with dilators. The endoscope is introduced, and dissection over the ipsilateral facet joint is performed. We then perform a partial facetectomy of inferior articular process and a total facetectomy of the superior articular process. We locate the inferior lamina and perform over-the-top decompression, as well as a contralateral facetectomy through the same approach. Next, we continue dissection over Kambin’s triangle, perform a discectomy and introduce the titanium 3D banana-shaped cage. Finally, percutaneous screws are inserted compressing to help improve lumbar lordosis achieved with the previous osteotomy. Intraoperative X-Ray and 6 weeks and 3 months control to ensure spinal fusion and follow-up for spinal balance correction.The database includes demographic data, hospital length of stay, clinical scales, and functional follow-up (EVA, ODI) of patients treated at a single spine center. Results: Three patients underwent surgery for a total of five biportal fusions with osteotomy levels. The average age of the patients was 34 years, with two being female. The average hospital stay was 1.5 days. Before surgery, 100% of the patients used some form of neuromodulator, and only one required it for three weeks after the procedure. Sagittal alignment parameters showed improvements in lumbar lordosis and segmental lordosis. Mismatch and pelvic tilt were within normal values. Fusion outcomes were assessed at six weeks and three months, with CT reaching 200 UH. Clinical scales indicated that VAS pain and functional scores improved. No complications were observed. Conclusion: The biportal endoscopic technique is safe for performing interbody fusion and bilateral osteotomy with single-side access. It is a feasible method for correcting sagittal parameters, with a lower rate of complications related to access. Overall, these findings suggest that biportal fusions can be a viable option for patients seeking relief from spinal conditions while minimizing recovery time and medication dependency. Future studies could expand on these results to enhance understanding and optimize patient care in similar cases.

## ID: AGS26/234

## P0662: Comparative study between open mini-incision TLIF (4.0 cm) vs hybrid endoscopic TLIF; Surgical and early clinical results report

Woraphot Wichan

1

, Thippatai Chaichompoo

1

1

North East Spine & Joint Center, Udonthani, Thailand

Introduction: Surgeons choose minimally invasive spine surgery due to its positive outcomes and speedy recovery for patients. Many surgeons from different institutes say uniportal and biportal endoscopic TLIF surgery are equally effective as micro or open surgery. Compared to open TLIF surgery with a 4.0 cm mini-incision, TLIF endoscopic surgery requires sophisticated surgical skills and expensive endoscopic equipment. Which method is best? This study compared the pros and cons of each method. Material and Methods: The retrospective analysis comprised 63 patients who had single-level open and hybrid endoscopic TLIF surgery between February 1 and September 30, 2023. Based on surgical approaches, 34 patients were in the open TLIF group and 29 were in the hybrid endoscopic group. The study excluded cancer and infection patients and included symptomatic and unstable lumbar spine spondylolisthesis patients. In addition to gender, age, underlying disease, body mass index (BMI), and smoking status, we will collect data on symptoms before and after surgery, such as leg and back pain, on the first day after surgery, the day of discharge, and 3 months after surgery. Evaluation of patient satisfaction and ODI data before and three months after surgery. Surgery length, blood loss, complications, pain medication, and postoperative hospitalization were recorded. Results: A total of 63 patients were enrolled. Mean ages, genders, BMI, intraoperative blood loss, painkiller consumption, and hospital stay were not significantly different across groups (p > 0.05). The mean operational time was substantially shorter in the open TLIF group compared to the hybrid endoscopic group (p < 0.001). The Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) of leg discomfort did not differ between groups on the first day after surgery, discharge, or 3 months following surgery (p > 0.05). On the first day following surgery, the open TLIF group had considerably lower back pain VAS scores compared to the hybrid endoscopic group (p < 0.05). However, VAS values for back pain were similar between groups on discharge and 3 months following surgery. (p > 0.05). Both groups improved their ODI scores, although there was no significant difference. (p > 0.05). Open and hybrid endoscopic TLIF groups reported 91.8% and 87.6% satisfaction rates, respectively. No significant difference existed across groups. (p > 0.05). The hybrid endoscopic TLIF group had 3 postoperative neurapraxia instances that cleared within 3 weeks. Additionally, one patient needed pedicular screw revision. The open TLIF group had 4 neurapraxia cases, which recovered within 4 weeks. Conclusion: Hybrid endoscopic TLIF surgery does not outperform open TLIF surgery in reducing leg and back pain on the first day, discharge, and 3 months after surgery, while improving quality of life, blood loss, postoperative hospitalization, and painkiller usage. However, hybrid endoscopic TLIF surgery involves more expensive materials, takes longer, and exposes patients to more fluoroscopic radiation.

## ID: AGS26/260

## P0663: Return to work after transforaminal endoscopic lumbar surgery in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Anthony Baumann

1

, Omkar Anaspure

2

, Aryan Anaspure

3

, Mark Villers

4

, Ethan Cottrill

5

, Melissa Erickson

5

, M. Burhan Janjua

6

, Muhammad Abd-El-Barr

7

, Jacob Hoffmann

1

, Peter Passias

5

1

Cleveland Clinic Akron General, Akron, United States

2

Perelman School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, United States

3

Rutgers University, New Brunswick, United States

4

Northeast Ohio Medical College, Rootstown, United States

5

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Duke University, Durham, United States

6

Department of Neurosurgery, University of Oklahoma, Norman, United States

7

Department of Neurosurgery, Duke University, Durham, United States

Introduction: TELS is a minimally invasive spine surgery technique that has been examined extensively in the literature for a wide variety of outcomes; however, no synthesized study exists on RTW, limiting clarity of the data. The purpose of this study is to examine return to work (RTW) outcomes in adult patients after transforaminal endoscopic lumbar surgery (TELS) to enhance education and manage patient expectations. Material and Methods: A pre-registered systematic review and meta-analysis (CRD420251064802) was conducted that searched four databases through June 1st, 2025. Inclusion criteria was articles that examine RTW outcomes in adult patients after TELS. Outcomes include time to RTW and percent RTW. A random effects meta-analysis using mean difference (MD) was also conducted for RTW time for TELS versus open microdiscectomy. Results: Seventeen observational articles were included out of 141 articles retrieved. Patients (n = 2,228; 57.3% male) who underwent TELS had a pooled average age of 46.5 (6.7) years with a total complication rate of 11.7% and a reoperation rate of 3.8%. Pooled average time to RTW after TELS was 43.0 (45.5) days (range: 5.6-125.1 days) with 90.3% (range: 75.4-100.0%) of patients experiencing RTW. Adult patients who underwent TELS (n = 381) had a clinically and statistically significant faster RTW as compared to patients who underwent open microdiscectomy (n = 395) via meta-analysis (MD = -30.9 days; p < 0.001). Conclusion: In the largest study population to date, patients who underwent TELS had favorable RTW outcomes, with a pooled average time to RTW of approximately six weeks with approximately 90% of patients also experiencing RTW. Patients who underwent TELS had a clinically and statistically significant faster time to RTW by approximately four weeks as compared to patients who underwent OMD; however, this outcome should be taken with caution given the small sample size and low data certainty. Future controlled studies continuing to examine TELS in isolation or compared to OMD should include various outcomes related to RTW, such as time to RTW, percent RTW, as well as type of postoperative RTW.

## ID: AGS26/279

## P0664: Comparative effectiveness of percutaneous discectomy and conservative management in symptomatic disc herniation: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Jose Otavio Donadeli Tome

1

, Guilherme Pajanoti

2

, Fernando Bezerra

3

, Rafaella Santanna

4

, Luciano Rodrigues

5

1

ABC Medical School, Santo André, Brazil

2

Hospital Israelita Albert Einstein, São Paulo, Brazil

3

Anhembi Morumbi University, São José dos Campos, Brazil

4

Federal University of Santa Maria, Santa Maria, Brazil

5

Paulista School of Medicine, Federal University of São Paulo (UNIFESP), São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Percutaneous discectomy (PD) is a minimally invasive technique designed to relieve radicular pain while minimizing tissue disruption. Although it has been used in clinical practice for decades, the evidence comparing PD with conservative treatment (CT) in patients with symptomatic lumbar and cervical disc herniation remains limited and heterogeneous. This study aimed to assess the efficacy and safety of PD compared with CT in this patient population. Material and Methods: A systematic search of PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane CENTRAL through July 2025 identified randomized controlled trials and observational studies comparing PD with CT in patients without spinal stenosis. Pain outcomes (VAS, NRS) and functional outcomes (ODI) were pooled using random-effects meta-analysis. Sensitivity analyses were conducted to address heterogeneity, including subgroup analyses of RCTs, lumbar-only interventions, and comparisons excluding epidural steroid injections (ESI). Risk of bias was evaluated using ROB 2 and ROBINS-I. Results: Nine studies (5 RCTs, 4 observational; 876 patients) were included. PD significantly reduced pain compared with CT (MD -1.64; 95% CI -2.17 to -1.10; p < 0.001), with stronger effects when ESI studies were excluded (MD -1.94; 95% CI -2.85 to -1.60; p < 0.001). Functional improvement favored PD but was not statistically significant (MD -3.67; 95% CI -10.40 to 3.06; p = 0.28). Subgroup analyses confirmed consistent pain reduction across RCTs and lumbar-only studies. Risk of bias was low to moderate; heterogeneity was largely attributable to study design and comparator variations. Conclusion: PD provides superior pain relief compared to conservative management for disc herniation, though functional benefits remain unclear. These findings support PD as a minimally invasive option for selected patients, particularly after failure of conventional therapy or when surgery carries increased risk. Further high-quality RCTs are needed to clarify long-term outcomes and its comparative role against ESI and open discectomy.

## ID: AGS26/304

## P0665: The efficacy of intraosseous basivertebral nerve ablation: retrospective, single-arm, cohort study

Justin Seungjae Hyun

1

, Nick Jain

1

1

DISC Sports and Spine Center, Newport Beach, United States

Introduction: The innervation of vertebral endplates by the basivertebral nerve makes it a prime target for intraosseous basivertebral nerve ablation (BVNA) in treating vertebrogenic low back pain associated with type 1 or type 2 Modic changes. This study represents the clinical outcomes of 103 patients with significant differences between pre-op, 3-month, and 12-month intervals in a community practice setting. BVNA has emerged as a promising minimally invasive option for patients who have not responded to conservative management, including physical therapy, medications, and injections. This study aims to provide real-world evidence supporting its safety and efficacy outside of controlled clinical trials. Material and Methods: Intraosseous BVNAs were performed in 103 patients at a single site using the Intracept procedure (Relievant Medsystems, Inc.). A retrospective, open-label, single-arm analysis was conducted on 103 patients who underwent intraosseous BVNA, with clinical assessments including Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores, and responder rates at baseline, 3 months, and 12 months post-procedure. Patient demographics, comorbidities, and prior interventions were also recorded to characterize the study population and ensure appropriate patient selection. Results: Patients demonstrated statistically significant reduction of VAS and ODI scores at 3 months and 12 months postoperatively compared to baseline. VAS scores decreased by 3.6875 (95% CI: [2.4028, 4.9722]) at 3 months and 3.0120 (95% CI: [1.6360, 4.3640]) at 12 months from baseline. Similarly, ODI scores declined by 17.84 (95% CI: [8.4214, 27.2586]) at 3 months, with sustained improvement of 12.76 (95% CI: [0.6624, 24.8576]) at 12 months. Observations showed no significant difference between 3 and 12 months (p = 0.330 for VAS, p = 0.424 for ODI), suggesting durable clinical benefit. Subgroup analyses indicate consistent benefits across different age groups and baseline disability levels. Conclusion: Intraosseous BVNA demonstrates clinically significant improvements in chronic low back pain with durable benefits up to 12 months post-procedure. BVNA appears to be a minimally invasive treatment with positive outcomes in real-world practice, offering a potential option for patients who have not responded to conservative therapies. Further prospective studies with longer follow-up are warranted to confirm the long-term efficacy and safety of BVNA across diverse patient populations.

## ID: AGS26/315

## P0666: Comparison of open, tubular, and bi-portal endoscopic decompression for lumbar spine degenerative disease – A 2-year follow-up study

Vikaesh Moorthy

1

, Sumanth Madhusudan Prabhakar

1

, Joshua Decruz

2

, Kelvin Lor

2

, Eugene Yang

2

, Boon Chuan Pang

2

, Mu Liang Ang

1

1

Woodlands Health, Singapore, Singapore

2

Khoo Teck Puat Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: In recent times, minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS) has rapidly expanded to include tubular and endoscopic techniques designed to preserve paraspinal muscle integrity, minimize soft tissue damage and blood loss, and enhance postoperative recovery in patients undergoing surgery for lumbar spine degenerative disease. However, the optimal surgical treatment for lumbar degenerative disease remains controversial. We aim to review the clinical and patient-reported outcomes of lumbar spine surgeries across open discectomy/decompression (OD), tubular microdiscectomy/decompression (TM), and biportal endoscopic discectomy/decompression (BE) over a 2-year follow-up period. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed for 129 patients who had undergone decompression surgeries for lumbar spine degenerative disease at a tertiary institution from January 2021 to February 2023, with 2-year follow-up. We evaluated demographics and perioperative factors including surgical duration, postoperative length of stay, incidence of complications including dural tears, wound complications, recurrence of symptoms and VAS leg and back pain scores at intervals of 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year and 2 years postoperatively. Results: 129 patients were included, with a mean age of 54.3, 52.9, and 52.9 years in those who underwent OD (n = 28), TM (n = 38) and BE (n = 63) respectively. Half of the OD surgeries (50%) were performed for single-level decompression, while 44.7% of TM and 39.7% of BE were performed for discectomies. The BE group had significantly shorter postoperative length of stay compared to the TM and OD groups (3.0 vs 4.2 vs 5.8 respectively, p = 0.003). There were no significant differences between the groups for other perioperative factors including surgical time, incidence of dural tears or surgical complications. In terms of patient-reported VAS leg and back pain scores, all 3 groups showed an improvement in outcomes from preoperative to immediate postoperative assessment. However, there were no significant differences in VAS back or leg pain scores between the groups at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year or 2 years postoperatively. Conclusion: There was no significant difference in clinical outcomes, including pain control and complication rates, between the 3 different surgical techniques used to address lumbar degenerative disease. BE spine surgery had the shortest post-operative length of stay, potentially due to faster recovery as a result of less soft tissue and muscle trauma. Overall, compared to other procedures, BE is a safe, effective, and viable surgical procedure for treating lumbar spinal degenerative disease, and may offer potential advantages compared to currently established surgical techniques, as familiarity with the technique increases.

## ID: AGS26/388

## P0667: Midline-sparing separation surgery for spinal metastases: a comparative study against the conventional open approach

Aaron Gelinne

1

, Daniel Faraj

2

, Miles McAllister

2

, Allie Harbert

1

, Zoe Robinow

1

, Satvir Saggi

1

, michael galgano

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, United States

2

School of Medicine, University of North Carolina, Chapel Hill, United States

Introduction: In patients with disseminated cancer, the vertebral column is the predominant site of bony metastasis. Separation surgery is a well-established technique in which the surgeon creates a several-millimeter gap between the tumor and spinal cord, providing sufficient space for subsequent spinal stereotactic radiosurgery. Conventionally, separation surgery has been performed through a bilateral open approach with or without fusion. While effective, disrupting the midline structures can lengthen post-operative recovery and increase the likelihood of hardware failure due to alteration of spinal biomechanics. We present a novel midline-sparing technique utilizing a paramedian approach that spares midline bony structures, contralateral paraspinal muscles, and facet joints to preserve spinal stability and report comparison with the standard approach. Materials and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort review of patients who underwent separation surgery for thoracolumbar spinal metastases at a single institution by a single surgeon from 2022-2025. Patients with confirmed metastatic disease with epidural involvement were included and stratified into standard (bilateral open approach with or without fusion) and midline-sparing (MSp) groups based on surgical approach. Continuous variables were compared using the Mann-Whitney U test. Significance was defined as a p < 0.05. Results: In total, 38 patients were included: 7 underwent separation surgery via MSp and 31 via standard approach. Median age, BMI, and ASA scores were not different between groups (all p > 0.05). Median operative time did not differ between MSp (228min [IQR 95]) and standard approaches (238min [IQR 98]; p=0.81). Median intraoperative blood loss was lower in MSp cases, but this difference was not statistically significant (100mL [IQR 250] for MSp vs 250mL [IQR 225] for standard; p = 0.13). Length of stay did not differ between approaches (median 6 days [IQR 7] after MSp vs 7.5 days [IQR 10] after standard; p = 0.58), nor did visual-analog pain scores at discharge (median 1 [IQR 3] after MSp vs 3 [IQR 7] after standard; p = 0.39). Conclusion: This study showed that operative characteristics including blood loss and length of surgery did not significantly differ between MSp and standard approaches. Further studies with long term follow-up are needed to evaluate patient reported outcomes and differences in hardware failure.

The case highlights the clinical challenge of persistent pain and the need for innovative surgical solutions. ## P0669: Thoracoscopic technology in treating giant thoracic disc herniation: a retrospective study based on 280 patients

Kunfeng Song

1

, Daniel Rosenthal

2

Hochtaunus-Kliniken, Bad Homburg, Germany

Introduction: Thoracic disc herniation (TDH) is a rare but complex condition to treat. Its pathological features and surgical challenges make traditional treatment methods highly invasive. In contrast, thoracoscopic technology, with its minimally invasive and precise nature, has emerged as an ideal choice. Material and Methods: This study retrospectively analyzed 280 cases of giant thoracic disc herniation (GTHD) treated with thoracoscopic surgery from 2003 to 2023. The study focused on the outcomes in patients with calcified lesions and assessed long-term efficacy using follow-up data, including neurological improvement, pain relief, and recurrence rates. Its effectiveness is particularly pronounced in calcified lesions, providing a reliable foundation for clinical practice.

## P0670: Optimizing epidural pressure in endoscopic spine surgery

Marcos Vaz de Lima

1

, Antonio Roth Vargas

, Enrico Ioriatti

, Marco Moscatelli

, Priscila Fernandes

, Martin Komp

, Sebastian Ruetten

6

Saint Anna Hospital, Herne, Germany

Introduction: High epidural pressure during the endoscopic spinal procedure can lead to disastrous consequences for the patient, particularly in cases of dural injury. In order to avoid these complications, an efficient outflow system was created to maintain the work area pressure at acceptable levels without compromising the quality of the image obtained by the endoscope and enabling an effective surgery. Methods: The two types of cannulas available on the market were compared, the conventional and the oval with the optimized ouflow evaluated by the COMSOL Multiphysics software, used in simulating real-world physics for creating an anatomically accurate model of the human spine. ## ID: AGS26/479

## P0671: Morphometric analysis of the lumbar interlaminar window and its surgical implications

Marcos Vaz de Lima

1

, Eduardo Achar Filho

1

, Robert Meves

1

, João Paulo Trecco

1

1

Santa Casa de São Paulo, Spine AI, São paulo, Brazil

Full-endoscopic lumbar spine surgery has gained popularity due to advantages over open surgery, including reduced tissue trauma, less postoperative pain, faster rehabilitation, and lower complication rates. The transforaminal endoscopic discectomy was the first-generation technique, but it encounters challenges at certain levels (e.g. L5-S1) and in migrated disc herniations due to anatomical constraints such as high iliac crest or narrow foramen. The interlaminar endoscopic approach, first described by Ruetten et al. (2007), expanded the reach of endoscopy to these problematic cases by accessing the disc through the interlaminar space. This interlaminar approach enabled effective endoscopic discectomy at L5-S1 and later was applied to higher lumbar levels and even spinal canal stenosis. However, to accommodate the endoscope at upper levels (L3-L4, L4-L5), a smaller bony window often necessitates partial laminotomy or drilling of the inner facet to create a sufficient working channel. In practice, the feasibility and ease of an interlaminar approach depend on the size of the interlaminar “window” (the bony space between adjacent laminae) available on preoperative imaging. Despite its surgical importance, the normal dimensions of the lumbar interlaminar window have not been well characterized in the literature. Prior morphometric studies of the lumbar spine have focused on canal diameters and interpedicular distances rather than the interlaminar space. A recent study by Sakçı et al. (2018) specifically examined the radiologic anatomy of the lumbar interlaminar window and highlighted considerations for endoscopic and microscopic disc surgery, underscoring the relevance of this anatomical parameter to surgical planning. In order to build on this knowledge, we performed a morphometric analysis of the lumbar interlaminar window in a large patient cohort. In this study, we measured both the vertical and horizontal dimensions of the interlaminar window at levels L3-L4, L4-L5, and L5-S1 on anteroposterior (AP) radiographs. Our objectives were to define normative ranges of interlaminar window size, to assess how these dimensions vary with spinal level, sex, and age, and to correlate them with implications for interlaminar endoscopic lumbar procedures. We hypothesized that lower lumbar levels (especially L5-S1) would have larger interlaminar windows than upper levels, and that smaller window size (e.g. at L3-L4 or in certain patient subsets) would correlate with the need for more extensive bony work during endoscopic access. By quantifying these anatomical patterns, we aim to improve preoperative planning for interlaminar endoscopic discectomy and decompression.

## ID: AGS26/530

## P0672: Radiological parameters after LLIF for adjacent-level disease treatment

Igor Barreira

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Posterior spinal arthrodesis is a standard surgical treatment for degenerative lumbar diseases, but it can lead to Adjacent-level disease (ASD)-a symptomatic condition caused by biomechanical overload on spinal segments neighboring the fusion. Traditionally, treating ASD required extending the original fusion, a revision surgery associated with high morbidity, increased blood loss, and prolonged hospital stays. As an alternative, minimally invasive techniques like lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) have emerged. LLIF offers a less traumatic approach that achieves high fusion rates, restores sagittal parameters through indirect decompression, and enhances stability, making it a viable option for both the treatment and prevention of ASD. Objective: To analyze the radiological outcomes of using the LLIF technique to treat adjacent-level disease. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing LLIF surgery for treatment of adjacent level disease, with preoperative radiography and postoperative radiography at 3 months to 1-year of follow-up, who had completed the informed consent form were included. Patients whose radiographs did not allow the measurements proposed in the study to be performed were excluded. The following outcomes were analyzed: pelvic incidence, pelvic version, sacral tilt, lumbar lordosis, and adjacent-level segmental lordosis on preoperative and postoperative radiographs. Results: 37 patients were included in the study (16 women and 21 men) were included in this study. The most frequently addressed levels were L3L4 (20) and L2L3 (14). Patients had an average of 4.9° (±4.2°) segmental lordosis gain at the addressed level, whereas they had an average of 6.7° (±7.3°) lumbar lordosis correction (p < 0.05). Conclusion: The use of the LLIF technique for the treatment of adjacent-level disease can significantly improve several spinopelvic parameters and segmental lordosis.

## ID: AGS26/555

## P0673: Minimally invasive oblique lateral approach for lumbar corpectomy

Carlos Fernández Carballal

1

, José Manuel Garbizu Vidorreta

1

, Teresa Panaderos Useros

1

, Angela Moreno

1

, Roberto Garcia Leal

1

1

HGU Gregorio Marañón, Madrid, Spain

Introduction: The oblique lateral approach, also known as the pre-psoas approach, using minimally invasive techniques, is employed in degenerative disc disease as an alternative to lateral (LLIF) or posterior approaches. However, there is limited evidence regarding the applicability of this approach for performing lumbar corpectomies of various etiologies. Material and Methods: Patients who underwent lumbar corpectomy via an minimally invasive anterior-to-psoas approach in pour institution were selected. Data were collected regarding the type of pathology, perioperative complications, and whether posterior fixation was used for supplementation. Results: Between 2018 and 2025, 14 patients underwent lumbar corpectomy using an oblique lateral approach. The etiology was traumatic in 6 patients, metastatic in 5, and infectious (spondylodiscitis) in the remaining 3 patients. In the latter group, corpectomies were performed at two vertebral levels (L1-L2, L2-L3, and L3-L4), while in cases of fractures and metastases, a single-level corpectomy was performed (5 patients at L3, 4 patients at L2, and 2 patients at L4). In all cases, the vertebral body was replaced with an expandable cage, and posterior fixation was added percutaneously in 12 patients and via an open approach in 2 patients. One patient with pulmonary carcinoma metastasis died postoperatively due to complications from the primary disease. Four patients experienced complications in the immediate postoperative period, and two required reoperation (one for screw malposition and another for deep infection in a patient with prior spondylodiscitis). Conclusion: The oblique lateral approach enables lumbar corpectomy using a minimally invasive technique and provides better access to the spinal canal compared to traditional lateral approaches, while minimizing psoas muscle retraction and reducing the risk of injury to the lumbar plexus.

## ID: AGS26/639

## P0674: Has thoracoscopic spine surgery been replaced a comparative study with transforaminal endoscopic discectomy

Kunfeng Song

1

, Daniel Rosenthal

2

, Honggang Zhou

1

, Mingkui Shen

1

, Hejun Yang

1

1

Third People's Hospital of Henan Province, Zhengzhou, China

2

Hochtaunus-Kliniken, Bad Homburg, Germany

Introduction: To compare the clinical efficacy of thoracoscopic discectomy and transforaminal endoscopic spine surgery (TFES) in the treatment of giant thoracic disc herniation (GTHD) with ventral spinal cord compression, and to clarify the application scope and clinical positioning of the two surgical methods. Material and Methods: A total of 86 patients with GTHD were retrospectively enrolled and divided into two groups according to the surgical method, with 43 cases in each group: one group underwent thoracoscopic decompression, and the other group received TFES via intercostal-foraminal access. The therapeutic effects of the two surgical methods were systematically compared by evaluating neurological function improvement (using Frankel classification and mJOA score), Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) score, surgical access success rate, and recurrence rate at 2-year postoperative follow-up. Meanwhile, perioperative indicators such as intraoperative blood loss, postoperative hospital stay, and complication rate were recorded in both groups. Results: In the treatment of central calcified lesions, thoracoscopic surgery achieved significantly better decompression effect, with a lesion removal rate of 94%, which was significantly higher than 62% in the TFES group (χ2 = 12.87, p < 0.001). Although the TFES group had a shorter average operation time [(85.2 ± 16.3) min vs. (112.5 ± 21.7) min in the thoracoscopic group, t = 6.73, p < 0.001], the incidence of postoperative residual compression was higher (27.9% vs. 4.7% in the thoracoscopic group, p < 0.01). The 2-year postoperative follow-up results showed that the long-term recurrence rate in the TFES group was 11.6% (5/43), which was significantly higher than 2.3% (1/43) in the thoracoscopic group (p < 0.05). In addition, there were no significant differences in intraoperative blood loss, postoperative hospital stay, and complication rate between the two groups (p > 0.05). Conclusion: Although transforaminal endoscopic spine surgery (TFES) has the advantage of minimal invasiveness, thoracoscopic surgery remains irreplaceable for central or calcified giant thoracic disc herniation with ventral spinal cord compression-it performs better in terms of thoroughness of lesion resection and reducing the risk of long-term recurrence. Therefore, with the continuous development of minimally invasive spinal surgery techniques, thoracoscopic spine surgery still has important clinical value in patients with specific indications and has not been completely replaced by transforaminal endoscopic surgery.

## ID: AGS26/640

## P0675: Short-term outcomes of percutaneous anterior thoracoscopic surgery for thoracic disc herniation

Kunfeng Song

1

, Daniel Rosenthal

2

, Hejun Yang

1

, Mingkui Shen

1

, Haijun Ma

1

1

Third People's Hospital of Henan Province, Zhengzhou, China

2

Hochtaunus-Kliniken, Bad Homburg, Germany

Introduction: Thoracic disc herniation is characterized by complex anatomical structures within the thoracic cavity, making traditional open surgery associated with large trauma and slow postoperative recovery. As a new minimally invasive technique, percutaneous anterior thoracoscopic surgery can achieve precise anterior decompression through small incisions. However, systematic data on its short-term efficacy and safety remain limited, requiring further verification of its clinical application value. Methods: Patients with thoracic disc herniation who underwent percutaneous anterior thoracoscopic surgery at our hospital from January 2021 to December 2022 were retrospectively enrolled. Baseline data (age, gender, lesion segment, herniation type) of patients were collected. The Visual Analog Scale (VAS) was used to assess the improvement of thoracolumbar pain before surgery and at 1 week, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months after surgery; the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) was applied to evaluate the recovery of patients' daily activity ability after surgery. Preoperative and postoperative thoracic MRI/CT imaging examinations at the last follow-up were used to assess the thoroughness of disc decompression (herniated material resection rate). Meanwhile, operation time, intraoperative blood loss, postoperative hospital stay, and the occurrence of complications (infection, nerve injury, pleural effusion, etc.) were recorded. Paired t-tests were used to compare the differences in VAS and ODI scores between preoperative and postoperative time points. Results: A total of 30 patients were included, including 18 males and 12 females, aged 35-65 years with an average of (48.6 ± 8.3) years. Lesion segments were distributed at T6-T12, including 17 cases of central herniation, 13 cases of paracentral herniation, and 9 cases of calcified herniation. All patients successfully completed the surgery without conversion to open surgery. The average operation time was (95.2 ± 15.7) minutes, the average intraoperative blood loss was (42.5 ± 13.6) mL, and the average postoperative hospital stay was (4.8 ± 1.2) days. During the 12-month postoperative follow-up, the VAS score of patients decreased continuously from the preoperative average of (8.2 ± 1.1) points to (2.3 ± 0.9) points at 12 months after surgery, with statistically significant differences between each time point and preoperative values (all p < 0.01). The ODI index improved from the preoperative average of (60.4 ± 12.3)% to (20.5 ± 7.8)% at 12 months after surgery, with an improvement rate of 66.1%, and the difference was statistically significant (p < 0.01). Imaging evaluation showed that the resection rate of herniated disc tissue was over 90% in all patients, and the ventral spinal cord compression was completely relieved. No serious complications such as incisional infection or permanent neurological deficit occurred after surgery; only 2 patients developed a small amount of pleural effusion, which was cured by conservative treatment. Conclusion: Percutaneous anterior thoracoscopic surgery is a minimally invasive, safe, and effective approach for the treatment of thoracic disc herniation. It can significantly relieve thoracolumbar pain and improve daily activity function in the short term with a low complication rate, and its short-term efficacy and safety are worthy of recognition.

## ID: AGS26/664

## P0676: Endoscopic spine surgery in lumber spinal canal stenosis: bridging the gap with modern world of spinal surgery

Md. Shah Alam

1

, Md. Ziaul Hasan

2

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedic Rehabilitation, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Lumbar spinal canal stenosis (LSCS) is a leading cause of pain and disability in the aging population. Conventional open decompression, though effective, is associated with extensive tissue disruption, blood loss, prolonged hospital stay, and delayed rehabilitation. With advances in optics, instrumentation, and surgical techniques, endoscopic spine surgery has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative, aiming to reduce morbidity while ensuring adequate decompression. Material and Methods: A narrative review of recent literature and clinical outcomes was conducted focusing on endoscopic decompression techniques for central and lateral recess stenosis. Parameters analyzed included operative time, blood loss, hospital stay, complication rates, and functional outcomes such as Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for pain and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Results: Endoscopic spine surgery demonstrates significant advantages over conventional approaches, including reduced soft tissue trauma, minimal blood loss, shorter hospitalization, and faster postoperative recovery. Functional outcomes measured by VAS and ODI show superior improvements to open techniques, with a lower incidence of perioperative complications. Furthermore, technological advances have expanded indications, enabling safe and effective treatment of complex stenotic pathologies. Conclusion: Endoscopic spine surgery represents a paradigm shift in the treatment of LSCS, combining efficacy of decompression with the benefits of minimally invasive surgery. By aligning surgical practice with the global movement toward patient-centered, less invasive care, it effectively bridges the gap between traditional spine surgery and the modern world of spinal innovation.

## ID: AGS26/729

## P0677: Long-term radiological results of anterior cervical foraminotomy and their clinical correlation

Dmitry Pechiborshch

1

, Vitaly Stepanenko

1

, Yuri Shulev

1

, Maya Voronova

1

, Ekaterina Karelskaya

1

1

City Multidisciplinary Hospital №2, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Anterior cervical foraminotomy (ACF) was introduced by Hae-Dong Jho in 1996 as an alternative to ACDF in order to preserve the intervertebral disc (IVD) and mobility of the operated segment. The purpose of our work is to measure the IVD height and mobility of the operated segment in patients with cervical degenerative compressive monoradiculopathy after ACF in the long-term postoperative period. Material and Methods: The study comprises 50 patients (44±9 years, 28-72 years) after ACF with a long-term postoperative period (3 months-6 years, average period 22 months). The height of the operated and adjacent IVD was measured by the interbody distance and by Farfan method on CT before and after surgery. The mobility of the operated and adjacent segments was measured by the interspinous distance on functional radiographs. Results: The height (interbody distance according to CT) of the adjacent above, operated and adjacent below IVD was 4.6 ± 0.9 mm, 4.6 ± 0.9 mm and 5.1 ± 0.9 mm before surgery and 4.4 ± 0.9 mm, 2.8 ± 1.0 mm and 4.8 ± 0.9 mm after surgery, respectively. The mobility of the operated segment after ACF was 3.7 ± 2.4 mm (according to the interspinous distance on functional radiographs). The mobility of the adjacent above and adjacent below segments was 7.1 ± 3.8 mm and 6.8 ± 2.4 mm, respectively. Mobility of the operated segment ≥ 3 mm was detected in 38 (76%) patients (convincing mobility), less than 3 mm was determined in 12 (24%) patients (questionable mobility). Preserved disc (IVD height according to the Farfan method > 0) and convincing mobility (≥ 3 mm) altogether were detected in 25 (50%) patients. In 4 (8%) patients, the disc was preserved, but the segmental mobility was questionable. Disc collapse (IVD height according to the Farfan method=0) with questionable mobility of the operated segment was detected in 8 (16%) patients. In the remaining 13 (26%) cases disc collapse was accompanied by convincing mobility of the operated segment. According to the Odom scale, excellent results were obtained in 36 patients (72.5%), good - in 9 (17.5%), poor - in 5 (10%). Moderate and significant neck pain after ACF were noted in 22% and 10% of patients, respectively. A significant decrease in the IVD height of the operated segment correlates with the appearance or increase of neck pain after ACF. No correlation was found between changes in the height of the IVD and the mobility of the operated segment with clinical results according to Odom. Conclusion: In 84% of cases after ACF the IVD and/or mobility of the operated segment were preserved. A significant decrease in the IVD height of the operated segment correlates with the appearance or increase of neck pain after ACF.

## ID: AGS26/759

## P0678: Efficacy of low-dose Escherichia coli-derived recombinant human bone morphogenetic protein-2 in minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion

Tae Hoon Kang

1

, Jeongwoon Han

1

, Geumho Lee

1

, Byungjun Kang

1

1

SMG-SNU Boramae Medical Center, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (misTLIF) offers advantages over open procedures, including reduced tissue disruption and faster recovery. However, the limited availability of autologous bone grafts remains a major drawback. Recombinant human bone morphogenetic protein-2 derived from Escherichia coli (E.BMP-2) has emerged as a scalable osteoinductive substitute. Although widely studied in open fusion techniques, its efficacy and safety in the context of misTLIF have not yet been investigated. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the efficacy and safety of low-dose E.BMP-2 with hydroxyapatite (HA) carriers in the context of misTLIF. Material and Methods: This retrospective case-control study included 70 patients (85 fusion levels) who underwent one- or two-level misTLIF for degenerative lumbar conditions between 2016 and 2024, with a minimum follow-up of one year. Patients were divided into two groups: the E.BMP-2 group (34 patients, 39 levels), who received standard bone grafts supplemented with 1 mg of E.BMP-2 on hydroxyapatite (HA) carriers, and the control group (36 patients, 46 levels), who received standard bone grafts only. Fusion status was evaluated using dynamic radiographs and CT scans. Solid fusion on radiographs was defined as segmental motion of less than 3°, and on CT as continuous trabecular bridging across the intervertebral space. Clinical outcomes were assessed with the visual analog scale (VAS) for back and leg pain, the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and the physical and mental component summaries of the Short Form-36 (SF-36). Perioperative and delayed complications, including fever, elevated inflammatory markers, seroma, heterotopic ossification, and cage subsidence, were reviewed using imaging studies and medical records. Results: The E.BMP-2 group showed significantly higher fusion rates (89.7% vs. 63%, p = 0.005) without increased complications. Clinical improvements were similar between groups. No cases of seroma, heterotopic ossification, or reoperation were observed in the E.BMP-2 group. The cage subsidence rate was significantly lower in the E.BMP-2 group at 6 and 12 months. A standardized application protocol using 1 mg of E.BMP-2 per patient, along with a containment technique, likely contributed to these favorable outcomes. Conclusion: Low-dose E.BMP-2 appears to be a safe and effective osteoinductive option for misTLIF, particularly where autologous bone is limited. Proper carrier selection, dosing, and technique are key to optimizing outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/761

## P0679: Learning curve for full-endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion in experienced uniportal endoscopic surgeon

Gun Keorochana

1

, Pheeraphon Yiamphiphatthana

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, Ramathibodi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Adoption of Full-endoscopic uniportal transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (FE-TLIF) is challenged by a steep learning curve. This study aimed to evaluate the learning curve, perioperative outcomes, and complications of conventional FE-TLIF performed under fluoroscopic guidance with a non-expandable cage by an experienced uniportal endoscopic surgeon. Material and Methods: An ambidirectional cohort study was conducted on 29 consecutive patients who underwent FE-TLIF at a single center between January 2023 and February 2025. Operative time was used as the primary metric for learning curve analysis, assessed using the cumulative sum (CUSUM) method and polynomial regression. Perioperative outcomes and complications were compared between the early and late phases, defined by the learning point derived from the CUSUM analysis. Results: The learning curve stabilized at approximately 12 cases, as identified by the peak of the fitted polynomial regression curve. The model showed a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.52, indicating moderate explanatory power. Operative time showed a non-significant reduction between the early (237.5 ± 33.9 min) and late phases (216.1 ± 28.4 min; p = 0.0885). Postoperative pain on day 2 was significantly lower in the late phase (1.88 ± 1.80) than in the early phase (3.67 ± 2.02; p = 0.0225). Complications were more frequent in the early phase but not significant (p = 0.3970). Conclusion: Proficiency in conventional FE-TLIF using fluoroscopic guidance and a non-expandable cage can be achieved after approximately 12 cases in an experienced expert in uniportal endoscopic surgery. Although the regression model demonstrated moderate fit (R2 = 0.52), the findings provide practical insights for surgeons adopting this technique, emphasizing the importance of procedural familiarization to reduce complications and improve outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/806

## P0680: Risk factors for intraoperative bone cement leakage in percutaneous kyphoplasty: a prospective dual-center non-randomized study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Bone cement leakage is a common and potentially serious complication of percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP). While previous studies have identified several risk factors, evidence remains limited regarding their consistent impact across different hospital settings, especially in county-level versus urban centers. This study aims to prospectively identify significant risk factors for cement leakage during PKP in patients with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCF). Material and Methods: A non-randomized prospective cohort study was conducted across two centers: one county-level hospital and one municipal hospital in Chongqing, China. A total of 200 patients (100 per center) undergoing PKP for OVCF were enrolled. Preoperative parameters included bone mineral density (T-score ≤ -2.5) and degree of vertebral collapse (Genant grade ≥ II). Intraoperative data encompassed cement volume, injection time, and needle approach. Cement leakage was evaluated within 24 hours postoperatively using CT and classified according to Zou et al. (2021). Multivariate logistic regression was performed using R software (Bibliometrix package) to identify independent risk factors, including age >70 years, posterior wall breach, cement volume > 5 mL, and operative level. Results: Cement leakage occurred in 33 patients (16.5%), with distribution across leakage types as follows: paravertebral (8.0%), intravenous (4.5%), intradiscal (3.0%), and spinal canal (1.0%). Regression analysis identified three independent risk factors: posterior wall breach (adjusted OR = 4.21, 95% CI: 2.05-8.63, p < 0.001), cement volume > 5 mL (aOR = 3.24, 95% CI: 1.52-6.89, p = 0.002), and age >70 years (aOR = 2.15, 95% CI: 1.08-4.30, p = 0.029). No significant difference in leakage rates was observed between the two centers (p = 0.442). Conclusion: Posterior wall breach, cement volume >5 mL, and advanced age are significant predictors of cement leakage during PKP. These findings support preoperative risk stratification and tailored surgical planning to mitigate leakage complications. The consistency of risk factors across hospital levels underscores their broad clinical relevance.
Keywords: Percutaneous kyphoplasty, bone cement leakage, risk factors, osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture, dual-center study, posterior wall breach

## ID: AGS26/823

## P0681: Endoscopic spine surgery: navigating the neural architecture

Sarwar Jahan

1

, Md. Shah Alam

2

, Md.Mahamudul Hasan

3

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopedic Rehabilitation (NITOR), Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

3

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopedic rehabilitation (NITOR), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery (ESS) represents a paradigm shift in the management of spinal pathologies, transitioning from traditional open exposures to ultra-minimally invasive, targeted interventions. This review explores the core principles and advanced techniques of ESS, emphasizing the criticality of navigating the intricate neural architecture of the spine-including the spinal cord, nerve roots, and thecal sac-with unparalleled precision. By utilizing specialized optics and instruments through small-diameter portals. Promising outcomes such as reduced tissue trauma decreased blood loss, shorter hospital stay & faster recovery have been driven its adoption. Material and Methods: This prospective observational study was carried out at the Department of Orthopedic & Spine Surgery at Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh, between May 2025 to August 20205. A total of 30 patients who underwent full endoscopic spine surgery for various degenerative lumbar pathologies were included. Inclusion Criteria:
• Age between 20 and 70 years.
• Patients with single or multiple level lumbar disc herniation, spinal stenosis, foraminal stenosis, or recurrent disc herniation confirmed by MRI/CT.
• Persistent radicular pain, back pain, or neurogenic claudication not relieved by ≥6 weeks of conservative management.
• Fit for surgery under general anesthesia.
• Surgery was done by posterior Stenoscopic Lumbar Decompression (PSLD) & UBE.
• Clinical outcomes was assessed by VAS, ODI, MacNab criteria.
Results: ESS demonstrates high success rates (>85-90%) for contained and non-migrated lumbar disc herniations, with excellent relief of radicular pain. The complications were observed as dural tear, nerve root irritation, heamatoma & discitis, but the percentage of complications was very minimum (less than 2%). Clinical outcome was assessed by ODI (62.3 to 18.5) & VAS (7.4 to 1.9). The patient satisfaction was assessed by Macnab criteria (excellent 56.5% & good 32.4%). Conclusion: ESS embodies a sophisticated, patient-centric approach that prioritizes the integrity of the neural architecture, resulting in reduced blood loss, diminished postoperative pain, and accelerated recovery. As technology and techniques continue to evolve, ESS is poised to expand its role in restoring spinal health with maximal safety and efficacy.

## ID: AGS26/829

## P0682: Opioid use following sacroiliac joint fusion in opioid-naïve patients

Regan Carlson

1

, Kari Odland

1

, Bishop Anyiwe

1

, David Polly

1

1

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

Introduction: Sacroiliac joint (SIJ) pain is a common cause of chronic lower back pain, affecting a significant portion of the population and contributing to disability. In opioid-naïve patients, the appropriate quantity and duration of opioids are critical to balancing effective pain relief with minimizing risks. However, research on opioid prescribing practices following SIJ fusion, particularly in opioid-naïve patients, remains limited. This study aims to evaluate narcotic prescription practices post-discharge in SIJ fusion patients. Methods: This retrospective chart review examined opioid-naïve SIJ fusion patients seen between 2018 and 2023. Eligible patients were adults who underwent MIS SIJ fusion and were prescribed opioids at post-op. Exclusions included those with sporadic or chronic opioid use, or cases involving revision surgeries, fractures, tumors, infections, or trauma. Demographic data (age, sex, BMI), opioid type, dosage, and morphine milligram equivalent (MME) were collected. Patients were grouped based on the procedure (SIJ fusion only vs. SIJ fusion with concomitant lumbar fusion). All common opioid formulations were included. Results: A total of 116 patients were included in this study. Thirty-eight patients (32.8%) were male, 78 were female (67.2%). The overall mean number of oxycodone 5mg tablets prescribed at post-op was 30.0 (±13.2) and associated overall mean MME at post-op was 233.9 (±109.9). There was no difference between number of oxycodone 5mg tablets (p = 0.95) and associated MME (p = 0.40) in patients with concomitant lumbar fusion and those without. Conclusions: This study describes the general trends in opioid prescribing after minimally invasive SIJ fusion. Prescribing patterns in opioid naïve patients did not find a greater number of oxycodone 5mg tablets or associated MMEs between patients with concomitant lumbar fusion and those without.
