## P0683: Two-year outcomes after SI joint fusion using porous threaded implants at a tertiary referral center

Ryan Moen

1

, Kari Odland

1

, Jonathan N. Sembrano

1

, Christopher Martin

1

, David Polly

1

1

University of Minnesota, Minneapolis, United States

Purpose: The sacroiliac joint (SIJ) contributes to 15% to 30% of all chronic low back pain (LBP) with an even higher contribution (35-43%) after lumbar fusion. Surgical fusion of the SIJ has emerged as the intervention of choice to eliminate the motion thought to cause inflammation and SI joint pain. Herein, we report outcomes from primary SIJ fusions using a porous threaded 3D-printed implant (PTI, iFuse TORQ) with 2 year outcomes in a subset of this cohort. Patients and Methods: A retrospective chart review at 1 academic medical institution was performed. Medical records of patients ≥ 18 years old who underwent primary SIJ fusion with PTI were reviewed. Data collection included patient demographics, mechanical failures, and postoperative outcomes. Results: 64 patients (mean age 54, 72% women) underwent SI joint fusion, with 7 undergoing simultaneous bilateral treatment. Surgical time averaged 42 minutes for unilateral cases and 88 minutes for bilateral cases. There were no perioperative adverse events, no PTI malpositions or breakages. 23 patients had 24-month outcomes. At 24 months, pain improvement averaged 1.6 points (p = .0021) and ODI improved 11.6 points (p = .0082). Revision surgery occurred in 7 cases (symptomatic screw lucency, n = 6 and clinical deterioration without lucency, n = 1). Conclusion: In our real world evidence case series, patients undergoing primary SIJ fusion with PTI demonstrated clinically reasonable improvement in pain and disability due to pain. The rate of surgical revisions was low.

## ID: AGS26/839

## P0684: When approach matters: tubular but not endoscopic complications prolong hospitalization

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Thomas Karadimas

1

, Maya Toothman

1

, Bryan Clampitt

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Patrick Kim

1

1

University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS) has shortened hospital stays compared to open decompression for lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS), reflecting quicker recovery and reduced costs. Yet within MISS, the two leading approaches, tubular microscopy and full endoscopy, remain under debate when it comes to how they influence hospitalization. Length of stay (LOS) is not only a marker of surgical efficiency but also a practical indicator of patient recovery. This study evaluated whether LOS differs between tubular and endoscopic decompression and explored perioperative factors that might prolong hospitalization. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 180 patients treated for LSS with minimally invasive decompression between June 2021 and April 2024 (tubular: n = 101; endoscopic: n = 79). Admission-to-discharge and surgery-to-discharge times were recorded, along with demographic findings, perioperative complications, and smoking status. LOS between the groups were compared using two-sample t-tests. Within-group analyses of LOS and modifiers were conducted using Pearson correlations. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: Mean LOS was not significantly different between groups for either admission-to-discharge (t = -1.489, p = 0.138) or surgery-to-discharge intervals (t = -1.404, p = 0.163). However, complication status strongly influenced outcomes in the tubular group: intraoperative complications (ρ = 0.352, p < 0.001) and postoperative complications (ρ = 0.245, p = 0.013) correlated with longer stays, particularly durotomy (ρ = 0.352, p < 0.001) and infection (ρ = 0.212, p = 0.033). In contrast, LOS after endoscopic decompression was not significantly affected by complication status. Smoking status showed no impact on LOS in either cohort. Conclusion: While overall hospital stays were similar between endoscopic and tubular decompressions, recovery after tubular surgery was more vulnerable to setbacks from complications. Endoscopic LOS appeared stable regardless of complication status, suggesting a more predictable hospitalization course. These findings highlight the importance of approach selection not only for surgical outcomes but also for recovery reliability and resource utilization.

## ID: AGS26/899

## P0685: Evaluation of the feasibility of 2-level lumbar canal decompression using unilateral biportal endoscopy

Mauricio Hansen

1

, Matthieu Vassal

1

, Alexandre Dhenin

1

, Guillaume Lonjon

1

1

Clinique Saint Jean Sud de France, Montpellier, France

Introduction: Multi-level surgery for lumbar stenosis is common and generally treated by open laminectomy. The endoscopic technique, increasingly used in spine surgery, is indicated for single-level surgeries, but because of the longer operative time, rarely for 2 and almost never for more than 2 levels. However, the advantages of endoscopy make this tedious procedure legitimate. Unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE), which appears to allow rapid 1-level decompression, allows for consideration on 2 levels. This study aims to assess the feasibility of this procedure. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective observational single-center study. We included all patients who underwent double-level UBE decompression between April 2022 (start date of the UBE technique by the center’s surgeons) and December 2024. Demographic, operative and clinical data (ODI, lumbar and radicular pain: L-VAS and R-VAS) were collected. Results: 20 women and 15 men, mean age 67, underwent double-level lumbar decompression surgery since March 2023. 17 patients were operated on levels L3L5, 10 on levels L4S1, 7 on levels L2L4, and 1 patient was operated on levels L2L3 and L4L5. The average operative time was 109 min for the entire procedure, and the average length of hospital stay was 1.7 days; all patients were mobilized on the first postoperative day. The following complications were observed: 5 dural tears (14.3%, ie 7.1% of operated levels), 2 hematomas (5.7% of patients) including 1 requiring revision surgery, and 1 patient experienced postoperative visual impairment, which resolved within 3 months. Mean preoperative data were: ODI 43.3, L-VAS 6.8 and R-VAS 6.9/10. At 3-months postoperative, they were improved respectively by 15.2, 2.3 and 2.7 points; 87.5% of patients were satisfied or very satisfied. At 1-year, ODI improved by 28.8 points, L-VAS by 2.2 and R-VAS by 1.5. Conclusion: This monocentric case-series demonstrates the feasibility of 2-level UBE decompression with good clinical results. These results need to be confirmed by larger-scale studies.

## ID: AGS26/919

## P0686: Clinical & radiological outcome of minimally invasive decompression and interbody fusion in dorsalspondylodiscitis - 2 years follow up

Prashant Baid

1

1

Department of Spine Surgery, Apollo Multispeciality Hospitals, Kolkata, India

Introduction: Open Spinal fusions and decompression are a common treatment for dorsal spondylodiscitis with / without end plate defects and / or neurodeficit and back pain caused by pyogenic, tubercular and parasitic aetiology. Majority of this subset of patients are elderly and have many associated co-morbidities (DM, CKD, immunocompromised, myeloma, lymphoma etc). We introduce a novel MIS technique that allow thorough disc debridement and places an interbody graft or a cage similar to the MIS procedure as done in lumbar spine through a lumbar analogue Kambin’s triangle in dorsal spine and allow decompression through a tubular retractor system (Medtronic METRx II system). Material and Methods: This is a prospective observational study of 25 patients who had undergone this procedure. All the patients were assessed clinically &radiologically. Assessment of all patients were done using VAS score, ODI score, ASIA score and Radiological assessment was done using BRIDWELL score. Results: 25 patients underwent MIS interbody fusion and decompression for spondylodiscitis in dorsal spine with various associated medical comorbidities. There were significant improvement (p < 0.05) in ODI and VAS (Back) scores in the post operative periods. In addition there was significant improvements in ASIA scores of the patients who had neurodeficits. The Bridwell grading for spinal fusion shows grade 4 fusion in all the cases at the end of two years follow up. Conclusion: MIS decompression and interbody fusion in dorsal spine is a novel and new technique with a very effective clinical and radiological outcome in this this subsets of patients.

## ID: AGS26/927

## P0687: Comprehensive analysis of the relationship between preoperative morphological and radiological characteristics of lumbar segments and long-term clinical outcomes of lumbar total arthroplasty and transforaminal interbody fusion in railway workers

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2,3

, Andrei Kalinin

1

, Yurii Pestryakov

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, Marat Aliyev

5

, Ermek Dyussembekov

6

, K. Daniel Riew

7,8

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Neurosurgery, Kazakhstan-Russian Medical University, Almaty, Kazakhstan, Almaty, Kazakhstan

6

Department of Neurosurgery, Asfendiyarov Kazakh National Medical University, Almaty, Kazakhstan, Almaty, Kazakhstan

7

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, USA, New York, United States

8

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, USA, New York, United States

Introduction: In-depth study of degeneration processes at the level of microstructural changes in the supporting elements of the spine is necessary for an objective assessment of the severity of the degree of degeneration and the choice of the volume of surgical intervention to optimize long-term clinical outcomes in railway workers. The aim of the study was to evaluate the relationship between preoperative morphological and radiological characteristics of lumbar segments and remote clinical outcomes of surgical treatment of railway workers with degenerative diseases of the spine using lumbar total disk replacement (TDR) and transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) to objectify the indications for their use. Material and Methods: The results of surgical interventions of 223 railway workers were retrospectively analyzed. In the period 2015-2018, TDR (n = 64) and TLIF (n = 159) were performed for single-segment degenerative disease at the lumbar level. Univariate logistic regression analysis was used to identify and assess the influence of various risk factors on the development of unsatisfactory clinical outcomes of each surgical intervention. Results: It has been established that the influence on the formation of a unsatisfactory long-term outcome of lumbar TDR are: age > 40 years, BMI > 28 kg/m2, smoking, posterior osteophytes, dynamic sagittal angle over 100, a decrease in the height of the interbody space > ½ of the overlying one, IVD degeneration according to Pfirrmann C. > III, FJ degeneration according to Fujiwara A. > II, ADC < 1200 mm2/sec, facet angle > 600, tropism of the FJ, the cell density of the nucleus pulposus < 1500 cells/mm3, the presence of collagen types X and XI. For TLIF - BMI > 28 kg/m2, smoking, work category by the level of energy consumption III, dynamic linear translation < 4 mm, facet angle < 600. Conclusion: The obtained results contribute to the systematization of patients taking into account the severity of preoperative degenerative changes in the lumbar segments and provide an optimal choice of surgical strategy taking into account the prediction of the follow-up postoperative result. Evaluation of the prognostic value of microstructural degenerative transformations in the tissues of the spinal cord has the potential for the development of unified surgical approaches aimed at selecting a surgical technology taking into account grouped morphological and radiological signs. A unified choice of surgical technology based on grouping patients taking into account a comprehensive analysis of preoperative morphological and radiological signs is aimed at effectively eliminating existing clinical symptoms in the long-term postoperative period in railway workers with single-level lumbar degenerative disease.

## ID: AGS26/943

## P0688: Open versus minimally invasive TLIF for L5-S1 isthmic spondylolisthesis: a retrospective single-center study

Vadim Byvaltsev

1,2,3

, Andrei Kalinin

1,2

, Yurii Pestryakov

1

, Ravshan Yuldashev

4

, Andrei Shcherbatykh

1

, K. Daniel Riew

5,6

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical University, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Railway Clinical Hospital, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

3

Department of Traumatology, Orthopedic and Neurosurgery, Irkutsk State Medical Academy of Postgraduate Education, Irkutsk, Russia, Irkutsk, Russian Federation

4

Department of Spine and Spinal Cord Pathology, Republican Specialized Scientific and Practical Medical Center for Neurosurgery of the Ministry of Health of the Republic of Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan, Tashkent, Uzbekistan

5

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Columbia University, New York, USA, New York, United States

6

Department of Neurological Surgery, Weill Cornell Medical School, New York, USA, New York, United States

Introduction: Currently, interbody fusion is the primary method for achieving arthrodesis across the isthmic spondylolisthesis L5. The results of treating such patients using minimally invasive technologies remain insufficiently reported. The objectives of this study was to conduct a comparative analysis of the postoperative results of patients with isthmic spondylolisthesis L5 by using minimally invasive and open posterior reconstruction with two-level transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion. Material and Methods: The retrospective study included 77 patients with isthmic spondylolisthesis L5 operated on from January 2015 to December 2020. Two groups of patients were identified: in the first (Open group, n = 39), a phased two-level open rigid stabilization performed from the midline approach was performed; in the second (MIS group, n = 38), transcutaneous transpedicular fixation was performed, then laminectomy and two-level transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion were performed through a central mini-approach. Perioperative parameters and clinical data, including the level of pain syndrome by VAS and functional status by ODI, were assessed before surgery, at discharge, 12, 24 months and last follow-up after surgery. According to the MRI performed before the operation and in the long-term postoperative period, the area of ​​the multifidus muscle was calculated. The minimum follow-up period was 24 months, the maximum was 85 months, and the median was 42 months for patients in both groups. Results: Comparison of the results in the MIS group revealed significantly better indicators of the duration of the intervention (p = 0.003), blood loss (p < 0.01), mobilization time (p = 0.02), long term clinical parameters of severity of pain in the lumbar spine (p < 0.001) and functional status in ODI (p < 0.001). The duration of X-ray exposure was comparable in both groups (p = 0.06). The number of perioperative complications in the Open group was 25%, in the MIS group - 7% (p = 0.02). An estimate of the area of the multifidus muscle on MRI of the lumbar spine before surgery and on average after 30 months established muscle atrophy of more than 50% in the Open group and no more than 20% in the MIS group (p < 0.01). Conclusion: Minimally invasive posterior reconstruction with two-level transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion in patients with isthmic spondylolisthesis L5 can significantly reduce damage to the multifidus muscle and the risks of adverse perioperative effects, as well as significantly improve the long-term clinical results of patients compared to the two-level open rigid stabilization performed from the midline approach.

## ID: AGS26/983

## P0689: Ten-step technique for navigated tubular transforaminal and extraforaminal lumbar interbody fusion

Ahmet Kartal

1

, Minaam Farooq

1

, Mousa Hamad

1

, Rachel Bratescu

1

, Chibuikem Ikwuegbuenyi

1

, Noah Willett

1

, Blake Boadi

1

, Jessica Berger

1

, Alan Hernandez-Hernandez

2

, Pedro Leonardo Villanueva-Solórzano

2

, Roger Härtl

1

1

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at New York Presbyterian Hospital, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

2

National Institute of Neurology and Neurosurgery, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (mTLIF) is an effective option for degenerative lumbar disease refractory to nonoperative care that requires fusion with or without decompression. We present a ten-step, fully 3D-navigated tubular workflow for mTLIF and its extraforaminal variant - minimally invasive extraforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (mELIF) - integrating expandable interbody cages and single-step navigated pedicle screws to enhance safety, efficiency, and reproducibility. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed our experience with mTLIF and mELIF procedures, based on a total of 350 cases, to develop step-by-step surgical techniques and workflows. These techniques were supplemented by illustrations and operative videos demonstrating the key steps. Results: Our overview documents the key technical details for the reliable and reproducible performance of mTLIF and mELIF. Across 350 patients, complications occurred exclusively in the mTLIF cohort (2.1%), including CSF leak, wound infection, and hardware complications, with no neurological complications reported. Median hospital stay was shorter for mELIF (24 hours) compared to mTLIF (48 hours). Median follow-up time was 2.0 years. Conclusion: We present a comprehensive ten-step workflow for navigated mTLIF and mELIF. This workflow integrates three key features: (1) total 3D navigation, eliminating radiation exposure for the surgical team while minimizing radiation to the patient, (2) the use of expandable cages to enhance segmental lordosis; and (3) single-step navigated pedicle screws designed to streamline the surgical workflow. By providing clear and standardized workflows, we aim to support the education and training of surgeons, enabling consistent and safe outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/988

## P0690: Impact of diabetes mellitus on minimally invasive spine surgery outcome: a systematic review

Mohsen Rostami

1

, Daven Mejica

1

, Joshua Vignolles-Jeung

1

, Sawyer Bauer

1

, Katherine Baker

1

, Anjali Pradhan

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Patrick Kim

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Diabetes mellitus (DM) is a common comorbidity known to increase the risk of complications after spinal surgery. Minimally invasive spine surgery (MIS) has been shown to reduce tissue trauma and perioperative morbidity; however, its safety and effectiveness in diabetic patients remain uncertain. In this study we evaluated the impact of diabetes mellitus on postoperative complications and outcomes following minimally invasive lumbar spine procedures. Material and Methods: This systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines and registered in PROSPERO. A comprehensive literature search was performed across PubMed, Scopus, Embase, and Web of Science through December 2024. Studies included if they evaluated adult diabetic patients undergoing MIS lumbar discectomy, interbody fusion, or decompression. Methodological quality was assessed using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale. Results: Sixteen studies were included: eleven focused on interbody fusion or discectomy (n = 2,041), and five on decompression-only procedures (n = 1,256). The majority of studies reported similar rates of surgical complications between diabetic and non-diabetic patients, though some identified increased risks for medical complications, prolonged hospitalization, or pseudarthrosis in diabetics. Functional outcomes, including pain scores and disability indices, were generally comparable across groups in long-term follow-up. However, diabetic patients occasionally reported lower postoperative quality of life scores. Due to heterogeneity in study design and reporting, a meta-analysis was not performed. Conclusion: Despite some variability in perioperative risks, MIS appears to offer safe and effective outcomes for diabetic patients. The long-term benefits are largely similar to those observed in non-diabetic populations. Further research comparing MIS and open surgery in diabetics is warranted to guide surgical decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/1002

## P0692: Simulation training in spinal endoscopic surgery: a systematic review of current status and future directions

Gilberto Perez

1

, Abdulwhab Alotaibi

1,2

, Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Omid yousefi

1

, Muhammad Abd-El-Barr

3

, Saman Shabani

1

, Mohamad Bakhaidar

1,2

1

Medical College of Wisconin, Milwaukee, United States

2

Ottawa University, Ottawa, Canada

3

Duke University Medical Center, Durham, United States

Introduction: This systematic review aims to comprehensively evaluate the existing literature on endoscopic spine surgery (ESS) simulation. By assessing current designs, validation processes, and educational impacts in training of ESS simulators, our study aims to identifies strengths and limitations, highlights critical gaps, and proposes approaches to enhance simulator effectiveness. Material and Methods: A systematic search identified 2,130 studies, with 10 meeting the inclusion criteria. The included studies comprised descriptive studies, randomized controlled trials, cohort studies, and a cross-sectional study. Data were extracted on simulator models, validation approaches, training strategies, and educational outcomes. Results: Eleven simulator models were evaluated: animal (18%), mixed reality (9%), self-made (55%), synthetic (9%), and cadaveric (9%). Only six studies validated their simulators, revealing variable face, content, and construct validity. Notably, the mixed reality simulator showed the most promising outcomes. Multimodal teaching strategies combining didactic instruction and hands-on practice led to improvements in learner satisfaction, confidence, and technical performance. However, studies exhibited moderate to high risk of bias, with an average MERSQI score of 9.8/18. Conclusion: Diverse ESS simulation models demonstrate potential to enhance surgical training. This review identified moderate methodological quality and variability in study design, sampling, and validity across existing programs. Nevertheless, consistent improvements in technical performance and learner satisfaction were observed with multimodal training approaches. Standardized, validated simulators and structured curricula are essential to improve surgical proficiency, optimize educational outcomes, and reduce variability in practice.

## ID: AGS26/1022

## P0693: Percutaneous spine stabilization: benefits and limitations

Jakub Jezek

1

, Jiri Skala-Rosenbaum

1

, Martin Kapounek

1

, Jan Svec

1

1

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, University Hospital Kralovske Vinohrady and Third Medical Faculty of Charles University, Prague, Czech Republic

Introduction: Percutaneous spine stabilization has become increasingly used in recent years for both traumatic and degenerative disorders. Its main advantages include reduced invasiveness, less surgical trauma, and faster recovery compared with open techniques. However, certain limitations and potential pitfalls remain less frequently discussed. This study aimed to evaluate our own patient cohort treated with this technique, focusing on short-term outcomes and perioperative complications. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 47 patients who underwent percutaneous spine stabilization at our institution between 2020 and 2024: 39 for traumatic injury, 4 for tumors, and 3 for spondylodiscitis. Outcomes assessed included operative time, postoperative pain (VAS), perioperative blood changes, clinical status, radiological findings (X-ray and/or CT), and complications. Minimum follow-up was 6 months. Results: Analysis revealed considerable variability in operative parameters and clinical results depending on diagnosis and surgical extent. Expected benefits-particularly reduced blood loss and faster recovery-were observed, but several less frequently addressed drawbacks of the percutaneous approach also emerged. Conclusion: Percutaneous spine stabilization is an effective and minimally invasive technique in appropriately selected cases. Its application requires careful risk assessment and sufficient surgical experience across both traumatic and non-traumatic conditions.

## ID: AGS26/1048

## P0694: Precision without navigation: C-arm guided MISS for thoracolumbar fractures-redefining recovery and compliance

Mahamudul Hasan

1

, Shah Alam

1

, Sarwar Jahan

1

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopaedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Minimally Invasive Spine Surgery (MISS) has revolutionized the management of thoracolumbar fractures by reducing surgical trauma and enhancing recovery. While advanced navigation systems like O-arm and robotic guidance offer precision, their cost and accessibility remain barriers in many clinical settings. This study evaluates the efficacy of C-arm fluoroscopy-guided MISS in thoracolumbar fractures, focusing on recovery metrics, hospital stay, intraoperative blood loss, and patient compliance. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on patients undergoing percutaneous pedicle screw fixation for thoracolumbar fractures using only C-arm fluoroscopy. No intraoperative navigation or robotic assistance was employed. Clinical parameters including operative time, estimated blood loss, hospital stay duration, Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) scores, and postoperative compliance were recorded. Screw placement accuracy was assessed via postoperative CT imaging. Results: C-arm guided MISS demonstrated:
• Reduced blood loss (mean: 50 mL) compared to traditional open techniques.
• Shorter hospital stay (average: 2 days), facilitating early mobilization.
• High patient compliance, attributed to minimal postoperative pain and smaller incisions.
• Acceptable screw placement accuracy (Grade A+B: 90.4%) without navigation support.
• No significant perioperative complications were observed.
Conclusion: MISS using only C-arm fluoroscopy offers a viable, cost-effective alternative to navigation-assisted techniques in thoracolumbar fracture management. It ensures favorable outcomes in terms of recovery, safety, and patient satisfaction, especially in resource-constrained environments. This approach empowers spine surgeons to deliver high-quality care without reliance on expensive technology, making it globally scalable.

## ID: AGS26/1105

## P0695: Assessment of blood loss during kyphoplasty for neoplastic vertebral fractures: impact of PET/CT-defined lesion activity in multiple myeloma

Bedirhan Albayrak

1

, Yunus Emre Yıldırım

2

, Ercan Bayar

3

, Furkan Erdoğan

4

, Hüseyin Sina Coşkun

4

, Nevzat Dabak

4

1

Samsun Training and Research Hospital, Samsun, Turkey

2

Sinop Boyabat State Hospital, Sinop, Turkey

3

Kastamonu Tosya State Hospital, Kastamonu, Turkey

4

Ondokuz Mayıs University, Samsun, Turkey

Introduction: PBK is a widely used intervention in the management of painful vertebral compression fractures (VCFs) in MM. While PET/CT imaging plays a central role in disease monitoring, its potential to predict intraoperative complications such as bleeding has not been established. We aim to investigate whether 18F-FDG PET/CT-defined lesion activity predicts intraoperative bleeding risk during percutaneous balloon kyphoplasty (PBK) in patients with multiple myeloma (MM), and to explore the correlation between radiological restoration and clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: This study evaluated 26 patients with neoplasia (35 vertebral levels) treated with PBK between June 2021 and January 2024. Data included demographics, radiological parameters (anterior and mid-vertebral height, local kyphotic angle), functional outcomes (VAS, ODI), and intraoperative bleeding volumes. PET/CT was used to determine lesion activity in MM patients. Logistic regression analysis (Firth's method) was used to assess predictors of high bleeding (> 100 mL). Statistical comparisons were made based on fracture etiology, spinal level, and age group. Results: Among 20 MM patients, five had PET/CT-active lesions; four of these experienced bleeding ≥ 100 mL. 6 patients had other neoplasia. Mean bleeding was 100 mL in MM and 13 mL in non-MM cases. Firth’s logistic regression analysis identified PET/CT-defined lesion activity as a significant predictor of high bleeding risk (OR: 4.81; 95% CI: 1.14-26.2; p < 0.05). Radiological restoration (AVHR, MVHR, LKA) showed no correlation with clinical improvement (VAS/ODI). Cement leakage occurred in 5 cases, with no neurological complications. Conclusion: Metabolic activity on PET/CT is a strong and independent predictor of increased intraoperative bleeding risk in MM patients undergoing PBK. While PBK offers effective radiological correction, clinical outcomes are not directly associated with anatomical restoration. Preoperative PET/CT may aid in risk stratification and surgical planning for this patient group.

## ID: AGS26/1128

## P0696: A modified technique of minimally invasive cortical bone trajectory screws with U-rod for treatment of spondylolysis

Lincoln Liu

1,2,3

, Hung-Chang Chang

2,3

, Cheng-Neng Chen

2,3

, He-Shu Ku

2,3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Tamsui, Taiwan

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Mackay Memorial Hospital, Taitung, Taiwan

3

Department of Medicine, Mackay Medical University, New Taipei City, Taiwan

Introduction: Spondylolysis is a common cause of low back pain especially in young athletic population. It involves a defect, either unilateral or bilateral, of the pars interarticularis. Numerous techniques have been developed for the surgical treatment of spondylolysis, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. This article aims to describe a minimal invasive novel technique using cortical bone trajectory screws with a U-rod to stabilize the spondylolysis. Material and Methods: Adult patients who presented between July 2020 and August 2024 with low back pain due to spondylolysis was included in this study. Imaging studies of the lumbar spine was performed to confirm the spondylolysis. All patients were confirmed to be treated conservatively for at least twelve months prior to surgical intervention. A 2.5 cm midline incision was made over the level of the spondylolysis. Under fluoroscopic guidance, two screws were inserted into the level of the spondylolysis in the cortical bone trajectory and the screws were connected with a U-shaped bent rod. Results: Four adult patients (1 male, 3 females) were included. The mean age was 38.5 ± 16.1 years. Average intraoperative blood loss was 6 ± 3 ml. Mean postoperative hospital stay was 3 ± 0.5 days. Both postoperative Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores showed significant improvement. Lumbar spine CT scans performed one year post-operation confirmed fusion of the pars defect in 7 of the 8 defects. Conclusion: We described a modified novel, minimally invasive technique for the treatment of spondylolysis. This approach offers the advantage of being minimally invasive, leading to less blood loss, short hospital stays, and fast recovery. It also requires less technical expertise than traditional pars screw placement and utilizes readily available instrumentations. Patients reported improved pain scores and imaging confirmed successful fusion of the pars defect.

## ID: AGS26/1133

## P0697: Minimally invasive treatment of lumbar degenerative spondylolisthesis using a minimally invasive approach with iCT and surgical navigation

Nikolay Konovalov

1

, S.V.Kaprovoy

1

, S.V.Ivanov

1

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation., Moscow, Russian Federation

Background: Most surgeons adhere to the “gold” standard in the treatment of spondylolisthesis - a traditional median approach with wide laminectomy and fusion of the segment, which is accompanied by profound blood loss, soft tissue and muscles injury and persistent postoperative pain. Given the fact that most patients with spondylolisthesis have severe concomitant pathology due to age, the issue of minimizing risk of surgical treatment in this category of patients is quite acute. Methods: 224 patients (74 men and 150 women) were included in the study with 2 groups - patients who underwent surgical treatment using a traditional method (104 patients) and patients treated with a minimally invasive paravertebral approach (120 patients). In both groups, the level of intervention was determined using intraoperative CT (Siemens Somatom Definition Edge) and surgery was performed under navigation guidance (StealthStation S8). Both groups were, analyzed for blood loss, pain length of stay, surgical time and other parameters. Results: After thorough analysis of both groups, patients treated with a minimally invasive approach reported better outcomes for blood loss, postoperative pain, length of stay, surgical time and other parameters. Use of preoperative planning, intraoperative CT and navigation increased safety and ensured precise planning of surgical approach, estimation of performed decompression with high accuracy of screw and interbody implant placement. Conclusions: Minimally invasive spondylolisthesis surgery can reduce pain in the area of surgical intervention; reduce the time of surgery, the amount of blood loss and the time of hospitalization. The use of iCT and surgical navigation allows screws to be inserted into the vertebral bodies under stronger convergence, and this in turn eliminates the risk of fusion system failure.

## P0698: Current concepts of treatment of spinal cord vascular pathology: surgical, radiosurgical and endovascular. Experience of Burdenko centre of neurosurgery

Nikolay Konovalov

1

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation., Moscow, Russian Federation

Objectives: Describe the current concepts and our clinical experience of surgical treatment of various vascular pathology of the spinal cord. Background: Vascular pathology of the spinal cord are a relatively rare and overlooked group of lesions that can cause substantial neurologic morbidity. It comprises various pathologies, including dural arteriovenous fistulae, arteriovenous malformation (AVM), cavernous malformation (CM), hemangioblastomas, infarction and genetic abnormalities. Clinical presentation of vascular pathologies is variable, as the localization within the spinal cord correlate with different symptoms and requires prompt recognition and treatment. Treatment may involve surgery, endovascular embolization, radiation therapy or combination treatments. Methods: Authors report the current treatment options and their clinical experience on surgical, radiosurgical and endovascular treatment of various spinal cord vascular pathology. Results: Presentation contains our data on surgical, radiosurgical and endovascular treatment of various spinal cord vascular pathology, our tips and tricks for treating patients and an analysis of our patients series. Conclusions: Diagnosis and treatment of vascular pathologies of the spinal cord remains a difficult task even for the modern neurosurgeon. Surgery continues to be the mainstay treatment method, although radiosurgical and endovascular treatment methods show more and more promising results. Our analysis of current available literature and our vast experience help delineate clinical hallmarks necessary for successful treatment. It is worth noting, that most treatment strategies are reliant on a multidisciplinary approach.

## ID: AGS26/1158

## P0699: Modified hybrid MISS TLIF: what we do?

Md. Shah Alam

1

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: MIS-TLIF is a common surgical procedure to treat lumbar spondylolisthesis. However, we introduced and practice our own method of doing the surgery. We have invented our own Modified Hybrid MIS-TLIF and documented the long-term results of Our own surgical strategy. Material and Methods: Eighty-four patients who had undergone instrumented Modified Hybrid MIS-TLIF between July 2021 and June 2025 were retrospectively reviewed. Here we used MIS Screw system with a midline mini open Incision to put the cage for fusion. The visual analog scale, Oswestry Disability Index, patient satisfaction rate, and the patient’s return-to-work status, effect on back muscle were used to assess clinical and functional outcomes. Radiologic follow-up were carried out in patients to check for adjacent segmental degeneration (ASD). The plain radiographs, computed tomography were used in all patients in last follow-up period. Results: The mean visual analog scale scores for back and leg pain decreased from 5.9 and 7.1 to 3.5 and 3.7, respectively, in the DS group (n = 49) and from 6.7 and 6.8 to 1.8 and 2.0, respectively, in the IS group (n = 35) (p < 0.001). The mean Oswestry Disability Index scores improved from 60.7% to 23.5% in the DS group and from 63.9% to 19% in the IS group (p < 0.001). Patient satisfaction rate was 80% and 81% in the DS and IS groups, respectively. Paraspinal Muscle Cross sectional area increased by 6.7% postoperatively. Evidence of fusion was observed radiologically in 47 (96%) and 35 (100%) of the patients in the IS and the DS group, respectively, giving an overall fusion rate of 97.7% (82 out of 84). The final ASD rate, observed using radiography, was 68.4% (33 out of 49) in the DS and 40% (14 out of 35) in the IS group. However, 15.8% (7 out of 49) in the DS and 3% (1 in 35) in the IS group had symptoms associated with ASD. Conclusion: The long-term clinical and radiologic outcomes after instrumented Modified Hybrid MIS-TLIF in patients with unstable single-level spine are favorable.

## ID: AGS26/1188

## P0700: Less pain, faster recovery: endoscopic surgery for lumbar spinal stenosis

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Samantha Schimmel

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Bryan Clampitt

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Patrick Kim

1

1

University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) is a prevalent condition, particularly in older adults, causing pain, mobility impairment, and significant societal and healthcare burdens. Advances in surgical techniques have shifted from open approaches to minimally invasive methods, including tubular and endoscopic techniques. This study compares the outcomes of tubular and endoscopic approaches to LSS to determine the potential superiority of one technique. Material and Methods: A single-center, retrospective study analyzed 181 patients undergoing tubular or endoscopic LSS surgery. In-hospital and outpatient data were collected on different demographic and outcome parameters, including complication rates, recurrence, and pain intensity measured using the visual analogue scale (VAS). Statistical analysis was performed using multivariate analyses and parametric and non-parametric mean comparisons. Results: No significant differences in demographics were observed. Most outcomes, including average postoperative pain, complication rates, and recurrence, were comparable between groups. Analysis showed postoperative maximum pain to be significantly lower in the endoscopic group (4.3 ± 3.6) compared to the tubular group (7.1 ± 3.3, p < 0.001*). This difference was most pronounced in single-level procedures (tubular: 8.0 vs. endoscopic: 3.5, W = 686, p = 0.0004). The reduction in maximum pain compared to preoperative levels was also significantly greater in the endoscopic group (-3.8 vs. -0.9, p = 0.02*). Regression analyses further supported these findings. In univariate analysis, endoscopic surgery was significantly associated with lower postoperative maximum pain (β = -2.95, p < 0.001) and greater pain reduction (β = -2.85, p = 0.021). After adjustment for potential confounders such as complications, preoperative pain, and demographics, the multivariate model revealed an even stronger independent effect of endoscopic surgery on reducing postoperative maximum pain (β = -4.05, p = 0.012, 95% CI: -7.15 to -0.95) and on change in maximum pain (β = -4.05, p = 0.012, 95% CI: -7.15 to -0.95). Other factors, including intraoperative complications, BMI, inpatient treatment, and smoking status, did not significantly affect postoperative pain outcomes. Conclusion: In the evolving landscape of spinal surgery, our study reveals that while tubular and endoscopic techniques offer comparable outcomes for lumbar spinal stenosis, the endoscopic approach stands out by significantly reducing postoperative maximum pain.

## ID: AGS26/1198

## P0701: Infection-free spine surgery: endoscopic advantage over tubular decompression

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Chloe Chose

1

, Bryan Clampitt

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Maya Toothman

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Patrick Kim

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Minimally invasive spine surgery has reshaped lumbar decompression, replacing large exposures with tubular and endoscopic techniques. Both aim to minimize muscle trauma, speed recovery, and reduce complications. Yet whether one approach offers a distinctly advantageous safety profile remains debated. Our study directly compared complication rates of tubular and endoscopic decompression for lumbar spinal stenosis. Material and Methods: We reviewed 180 consecutive patients treated between 2021 and 2024 (endoscopic: n = 79; tubular: n = 101) in our tertiary care center. Patients’ charts were analyzed for intra- and postoperative complications: incidental durotomy, infection, surgical site hematoma, new-onset dysesthesia, recurrence of stenosis, and reoperation rate. Group comparisons were performed using Chi-square and Fisher’s exact tests. Results: There were no differences in mean age (49.6 vs. 52.6 years, p = 0.233), gender distribution (54 [53.5%] vs. 42 [53.2%] male, p = 0.968), body mass index (29.6 vs. 31.2, p = 0.101), and Charlson Comorbidity Index (2 vs. 2, p = 0.207) between the tubular and endoscopic groups. No infections occurred in the endoscopic group, compared to 5% in the tubular group (p < 0.05). Comparing other complications between the groups, no significant associations were found: durotomy (7.7% vs. 9.9%, p = 0.36), hematoma (1.0% vs. 2.0%, p = 0.37), dysesthesia (2.0% vs. 3.0%, p = 0.59), recurrence (15.0% vs. 11.9%, p = 0.51), and reoperation (5.0% vs. 5.9%, p = 0.80). Conclusion: Overall, both tubular and endoscopic decompression proved to be safe, with low complication rates and favorable risk profiles. The absence of infections in the endoscopic cohort was noteworthy and may point toward a relative advantage in reducing infectious morbidity. Other complications, however, were comparable between the groups. Taken together, these findings suggest that the endoscopic approach might offer added value in specific domains of perioperative safety. Larger multicenter studies are needed to validate these trends and refine patient selection.

## ID: AGS26/1201

## P0702: Adjacent disc vacuum phenomenon as a risk factor for adjacent vertebral fracture after balloon kyphoplasty

Kento Okamoto

1

, Ryota Kimura

2

, Naohisa Miyakoshi

2

1

Yuri General Hospital, Yurihonjo, Japan

2

Akita University Graduate School of Medicine, Akita, Japan

Introduction: Balloon kyphoplasty (BKP) is widely performed for osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF) to achieve pain relief and restoration of vertebral height. However, subsequent adjacent vertebral fracture (AVF) remains a major clinical concern after BKP. While several predictors for AVF have been reported, the potential impact of vacuum phenomenon (VP) in the adjacent intervertebral disc has not been clarified. This study aimed to investigate whether the presence of adjacent disc VP increases the risk of AVF after BKP. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed consecutive patients who underwent BKP for OVF between April 2019 and September 2024. Inclusion criteria were single-level OVF between T11 and L2 and a minimum follow-up of three months with radiographic evaluation. Patients with a history of prior spinal surgery, multiple-level BKP, or concomitant decompression/fixation were excluded. VP was assessed on preoperative sagittal computed tomography (CT) scans and classified into VP-positive (VP+) and VP-negative (VP-) groups. Clinical and radiological parameters including age, sex, time from injury to surgery, bone mineral density (lumbar spine and femoral neck T-scores), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) risk patterns (confined high-intensity signal and diffuse low-intensity signal), intravertebral cleft, endplate or posterior wall injury, and presence of pre-existing vertebral fractures were collected. The primary outcome was the occurrence of AVF at final follow-up, diagnosed by plain radiographs. Group comparisons were performed using chi-square or Fisher’s exact test for categorical variables and t-test or Mann-Whitney U test for continuous variables. Logistic regression analysis was used to identify independent risk factors for AVF. Results: A total of 124 patients were included (mean age, 76.2 ± 8.1 years; 54 males, 70 females). VP was observed in 48 patients (38.7%), with cranial in 19, caudal in 14, and both in 15. Patients in the VP+ group also had a longer injury-to-surgery interval [32.5 days (range, 5-300) vs 17.0 days (2-120), p = 0.004], a higher prevalence of endplate injury (47.9% vs 28.9%, p = 0.037), and a greater frequency of intravertebral cleft (75.0% vs 40.8%, p = 0.001). During follow-up, AVF occurred in 21 patients (16.9%). When AVF(+) and AVF(-) groups were compared, patients in the AVF(+) group were more likely to have VP (71.4% vs 32.0%, p = 0.001) and had a longer injury-to-surgery interval [40.0 days (range, 6-150) vs 17.0 days (2-300), p = 0.002]. Multivariable logistic regression analysis was performed with VP and injury-to-surgery interval as independent variables. In this model, the presence of VP remained independently associated with AVF (odds ratio, 4.97; 95% confidence interval, 1.71-14.5; p = 0.02), whereas injury-to-surgery interval was not (p = 0.63). Conclusion: The presence of VP in the adjacent intervertebral disc was identified as an independent risk factor for adjacent vertebral fracture following BKP. These findings highlight the importance of preoperative CT assessment of disc VP in risk stratification for patients undergoing BKP. Surgeons should be aware that patients with adjacent disc VP may require closer follow-up or preventive strategies to mitigate the risk of AVF.

## ID: AGS26/1224

## P0703: Comparative analysis of MIS-TLIF and lumbar decompression outcomes for LDH with and without degenerative spondylolisthesis: a cohort study with minimum 12-month follow-up

Victor Chen

1

, Stone Sima

1

, Owen Zhou

1

, Osama Khalil

1,2

, Lauren Barber

1,3

, Charmian Stewart

1

, Alisha Sial

1,2,4

, Vinay Kulkarni

2

, Ashish Diwan

1,2,4

1

Spine Labs, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, St George and Sutherland Clinical Campuses, School of Clinical Medicine, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

2

Spine Service, Department of Orthopaedics, St George and Sutherland Clinical School, University of New South Wales, New South Wales, Australia, Sydney, Australia

3

Department of Orthopaedics, Virginia Commonwealth University, Virginia, United States

4

Spinal Unit, Discipline of Orthopaedic Surgery, School of Medicine, The University of Adelaide & The Royal Adelaide Hospital, Adelaide, Australia

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation is a common cause of radiculopathy and functional impairment, with surgical options including decompression alone or decompression combined with fusion. Minimally invasive fusion techniques, such as Transforaminal Lumbar Interbody Fusion (MIS-TLIF), have been developed to provide biomechanical stability while reducing muscle dissection, blood loss, and postoperative pain compared with traditional open fusion. Although decompression alone is effective for isolated disc herniations, its role in the management of degenerative spondylolisthesis (DS) and disc herniations remains controversial due to concerns regarding segmental instability and higher reoperation risk. There is currently a paucity of literature investigating decompression versus minimally invasive fusion techniques regarding pain relief and long-term functional outcomes. The present study aims to compare patient-reported outcomes between MIS-TLIF and decompression in patients with lumbar disc herniations with and without DS. Methods: This study was a retrospective cohort analysis of a prospectively collected database of adult patients with lumbar disc herniations, with or without degenerative spondylolisthesis greater than grade 1 confirmed on radiography. Patients underwent 1-4 level surgery with either MIS-TLIF or decompression between 2020 and 2024. Clinical outcomes were assessed using the visual analogue scale (VAS) for back pain and leg pain, and the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), recorded pre-operatively and at 3, 6, and 12 months post-operatively. Results: A total of 212 patients (51% male) were included, and the mean age was 61 ± 15 years. Half of the cohort (50%) were diagnosed with degenerative spondylolisthesis (DS), 42% underwent MIS-TLIF, 58% underwent decompression, and 75% were primary surgeries. Females had a significantly higher rate of DS compared to males (χ2 = 12.8, p < 0.001), and patients with DS were older than those without DS (64 ± 14 vs 57 ± 16 years, p < 0.001). Treatment strategy varied by diagnosis: patients with lumbar disc herniation without DS were twice as likely to undergo decompression, whereas those with DS were twice as likely to undergo MIS-TLIF (χ2 = 6.4, p = 0.011). Pairwise comparisons of estimated marginal means, adjusted for baseline levels, were performed for back pain VAS, leg pain VAS, and ODI. Among patients without DS, TLIF was associated with significantly higher back pain scores compared to decompression at 12 months (mean difference 1.65, p = 0.007). In contrast, among patients with DS, decompression was associated with higher back pain scores as compared to TLIF at 12 months (mean difference 1.50, p = 0.005). For leg pain, DS patients treated with decompression demonstrated worsening scores between 3 and 12 months (mean difference 1.03, p = 0.018). Regarding disability, decompression in patients with DS had significantly worse ODI scores at 12 months compared to non-DS patients (mean difference 6.19, p = 0.047). In patients without DS, TLIF was associated with increased disability scores at 3 months (mean difference 9.8, p = 0.008), 6 months (mean difference 7.4, p = 0.041), and 12 months (mean difference 12.1, p = 0.002). Conclusion: In patients without DS, decompression provided superior outcomes, with lower back pain and disability scores compared with TLIF. In contrast, in patients with DS, TLIF resulted in improved back pain at 12 months and stable leg pain, whereas decompression was associated with progressive worsening of pain and disability despite similar short-term results. These findings suggest that decompression is the preferred surgical approach for patients with lumbar disc herniations only, while MIS-TLIF may be considered the treatment of choice in cases of lumbar disc herniations with DS.

## ID: AGS26/1275

## P0704: Prediction of new vertebral compression fractures after vertebroplasty based on radiomorphometric parameters

Nikita Molodiuk

1

1

Sytenko Institute of Spine and Joint Pathology National Academy of Medical Sciences of Ukraine, Kharkiv, Ukraine

Introduction: New vertebral compression fractures (NVCF) following percutaneous vertebroplasty (PVP) remain a clinical challenge. This study aimed to identify radiographic morphometric parameters associated with NVCF risk in a Ukrainian cohort and develop a simple predictive model for clinical application. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study (2023-2025) was conducted at the Institute of Spine and Joint Pathology, Ukraine, involving 27 patients (24 females, 3 males; mean age 71.1 ± 1.3 years) with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures treated with PVP. Morphometric parameters of 109 vertebrae were assessed on digital radiographs (frontal/sagittal, RadiAnt DICOM Viewer, precision 0.1 mm/0.1°): anterior (Ha), middle (Hm), posterior (Hp) heights, relative compression (%), wedge index, local kyphotic angle, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, and scoliosis (Cobb angles). Statistical analysis (Statistica 13) included Shapiro-Wilk normality tests, Mann-Whitney comparisons (p < 0.05), and multivariate discriminant analysis (backward stepwise). Results: NVCF occurred in 40.7% of cases (45 fractures in 11 patients). Significant differences between NVCF and non-NVCF groups were observed for Ha compression (20.69 ± 1.16 mm vs. 23.89 ± 0.78 mm, p = 0.0338), Hm (17.84 ± 1 mm vs. 21.31 ± 0.61 mm, p = 0.0021), Hp (26.97 ± 0.81 mm vs. 29.61 ± 0.51 mm, p = 0.0073), lumbar lordosis (18.00 ± 1.74° vs. 38.28 ± 1.46°, p = 0.01), and scoliosis (7.53 ± 0.56° vs. 5.90 ± 0.48°, p = 0.022). The discriminant model, based on Hm (canonical correlation -0.863) and relative Ha compression (0.139), achieved Wilks’ Λ = 0.8 (χ2 = 15.77, p = 0.000376), classification accuracy of 76.3% (sensitivity 75.9%, specificity 76.9%), and AUC=0.763. Simplified function: F = 6.17 - 0.28×Hm - 0.035×Ha (%); F > 0 indicates NVCF risk. Other parameters were excluded due to low discriminatory power or collinearity. Conclusion: This two-parameter model, using Hm and relative Ha compression, offers moderate predictive accuracy for NVCF post-PVP. Its simplicity suits resource-limited Ukrainian clinics. External validation and inclusion of confounders (e.g., BMD, therapy) are required for broader adoption.
Keywords: Percutaneous vertebroplasty, new vertebral compression fractures, radiographic morphometry, discriminant analysis, osteoporosis.

## ID: AGS26/1420

## P0705: Bertolotti syndrome, tubular resection of megapophysis in a young patient - ​​Case report

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Luna Lizarazo Rojas

2

, Valentina Serrato Cristancho

2

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

, Jose Leonardo Guerrero Cardozo

1

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Universidad de Los Andes, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Bertolotti’s syndrome, first described in 1917, is characterized by a lumbosacral transitional vertebra with pseudoarticulation between the transverse process of L5 and the sacrum or iliac crest. It remains an underdiagnosed cause of chronic low back pain in young adults. Surgical management is considered when conservative treatment fails. Case presentation: 23-year-old female with a 5-year history of chronic low back pain radiating to the right lower limb, refractory to conservative management including radiofrequency denervation. Imaging confirmed a right L5 mega-transverse process with pseudoarticulation to the sacrum. After multidisciplinary evaluation, the patient underwent a minimally invasive resection of the transverse process, performed through a 3.5 cm paramedian incision using a tubular retractor under fluoroscopic guidance. The pseudoarticulation was resected with high-speed burr and Kerrison clamp under fluoroscopic vision and intraoperative CT, followed by hemostasis and layered closure. Results: The procedure was uneventful, with an estimated blood loss of 300 cc. Postoperative pain was controlled with multimodal analgesia. At follow-up visits (1, 3, and 6 months), the patient reported significant improvement in radicular pain and functional capacity, with only mild localized discomfort at the surgical site. Radiographic controls demonstrated expected postoperative changes without complications. Conclusion: Bertolotti’s syndrome should be considered in young patients with chronic refractory low back pain. Surgical resection of the transverse process though a minimally invasive approach is a safe and effective therapeutic option to resect the pseudoarticulation, achieving meaningful pain relief and functional improvement in selected cases mainly in young patients.
