## P0706: Lateral surgery for thoracic disc herniation

Igor Barreira

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Igor Arthur Costa

1

, Amabile Galeti

1

, Leandro Resende

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Bianca Soares

1

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Thoracic disc herniations are among the most complex degenerative pathologies of the spine, due to the difficulty of access to the region, the presence of several important structures in the adjacencies of the access routes, and due to the potential complications that can occur during the surgical treatment. Thus the aim of this study was to analyze the clinical outcomes of using the minimally invasive technique by a lateral approach for the treatment of herniated discs of the thoracic spine. Material and Methods: The study included patients undergoing surgical treatment of thoracic disc herniation using a minimally invasive technique, who had completed the ICF. Patients who did not have complete data about the surgery and patients who had not undergone surgery for thoracic herniation treatment through a minimally invasive lateral approach were excluded from the study. The following parameters were analyzed: demographic data, surgical data, and clinical outcomes. Results: 13 patients were included in the study, 6 women and 7 men. The most approached levels were T11-T12, with an average of 358 minutes of blood loss and 175 minutes of surgery. Conclusion: The use of the minimally invasive technique by lateral approach for the management of thoracic disc herniation showed good postoperative evolution and low complication rate.

## ID: AGS26/1471

## P0707: Full-endoscopic management of bone graft migration following lumbar interbody fusion presenting with foraminal stenosis in a multi-morbid patient

Panlop Tirawanish

3

, Siravich Suvithayasiri

2

, Luksika Wisitthipadkeekul

3

, Monchai Ruangchainikom

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Department of Orthopedics, Chulabhorn Hospital, Chulabhorn Royal Academy, Bone and Joint Excellence Center, Thonburi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

3

Division of Orthopedic, Golden Jubilee Medical Center, Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Nakorn Pathom, Thailand

Introduction: Transforaminal endoscopic spine surgery is a minimally invasive technique that accesses the spinal foramen to decompress the spinal nerve roots. Unlike conventional posterior-based surgery, it offers several advantages, including reduced muscle and soft tissue disruption, feasibility under local anesthesia, and faster postoperative recovery.1,2 Revision surgery, however, remains challenging due to altered anatomy and scar tissue formation. Despite these difficulties, endoscopic decompression has been associated with a low incidence of complications, even in elderly patients with complex degenerative disease, adjacent segment pathology, or failed back surgery syndrome. In such scenarios, it is frequently regarded as a “rescue” procedure. This report describes a rare case of postoperative bone graft migration following biportal endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (UBE-TLIF), managed successfully with full-endoscopic revision surgery in a high-risk patient. Material and Methods: A 56-year-old woman of Thai nationality who has systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), hypertension, and polyarthritis was admitted with a two-month history of severe back pain accompanied by right-sided sciatica. Her visual analog scale (VAS) pain score was 8/10. Neurological examination revealed sensory deficits in the right L4-L5 dermatomes, with preserved motor function. An MRI demonstrated significant central canal stenosis at L3-L5, recess stenosis on both sides, and grade 1 spondylolisthesis at L4-L5. She underwent UBE-TLIF from L3-L5, using local bone graft alone at L3-4 and local bone graft with a PEEK cage at L4-5. Postoperatively, her pain and neurological symptoms improved significantly. On postoperative day 2, however, she developed acute dyspnea and hypoxemia. Pulmonary embolism secondary to inferior vena cava thrombosis was confirmed, and anticoagulation with enoxaparin was initiated along, followed by prophylactic vena cava filter placement. The next day, she developed worsening right leg pain, numbness, and progressive weakness. An examination revealed right quadriceps strength of grade I and tibialis anterior strength of grade III. Results: The procedure was completed uneventfully with complete removal of the displaced bone fragments. Postoperative imaging confirmed full decompression. The patient’s visual analog scale score improved from 8 to 2, and quadriceps strength improved from grade I to IV. She was ambulatory with assistance by day 3 and regained independent walking at 6 months, with only mild residual numbness. No recurrent neurological or systemic complications were observed. Conclusion: Bone graft migration after endoscopic lumbar fusion, though rare, may cause severe neurological compromise. Full-endoscopic transforaminal decompression under local anesthesia offers a reliable and beneficial revision strategy in high-risk patients. Early recognition and timely intervention are crucial for optimizing outcomes and minimizing systemic complications.

## ID: AGS26/1486

## P0708: Endoscopic spine surgery: constraints and success in the management of lumbar spine pathology

Sarwar Jahan

1

, Shah Shah Alam

2

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopedic Rehabilitation (NITOR), Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital (BSOH), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery (ESS) has emerged as a transformative, minimally invasive surgical (MIS) technique for treating lumbar spine pathologies, including disc herniations, spinal stenosis, and spondylodiscitis. Promising outcomes such as reduced tissue trauma, decreased blood loss, shorter hospital stays, and faster recovery have driven its adoption. However, its technical execution presents unique constraints that can impact surgical success. This objective of the study is to evaluate the clinical success rates and patient outcomes of fully endoscopic techniques for lumbar pathology and to delineate the significant learning curve, technical constraints, and patient selection criteria that are critical for achieving optimal results. Material and Methods: This prospective observational study was carried out at the Department of Orthopedic & Spine Surgery at Bangladesh Spine & Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh, between May 2025 to August 20205. A total of 30 patients who underwent full endoscopic spine surgery for various degenerative lumbar pathologies were included. Inclusion Criteria:
• Age between 20 and 70 years.
• Patients with single or multiple level lumbar disc herniation, spinal stenosis, foraminal stenosis, or recurrent disc herniation confirmed by MRI/CT.
• Persistent radicular pain, back pain, or neurogenic claudication not relieved by ≥6 weeks of conservative management.
• Fit for surgery under general anesthesia.
• Surgery was done by posterior Stenoscopic Lumbar Decompression (PSLD) & UBE.
• Clinical outcomes was assessed by VAS, ODI, MacNab criteria.
Results: ESS demonstrates high success rates (> 85-90%) for contained and non-migrated lumbar disc herniations, with excellent relief of radicular pain. The complications were observed as dural tear, nerve root irritation, heamatoma & discitis, but the percentage of complications was very minimum (less than 2%). Clinical outcome was assessed by ODI (62.3 to 18.5) & VAS (7.4 to 1.9). The patient satisfaction was assessed by Macnab criteria (excellent 56.5% & good 32.4%). Conclusion: Endoscopic spine surgery is a highly successful and patient-centric option for a defined subset of lumbar spine disorders. While it offers substantial advantages over traditional open and other MIS techniques, its efficacy is constrained by a demanding technical learning process and specific anatomical challenges. Future success in expanding its indications hinges on continued technological innovation, structured surgical training, and the development of standardized protocols to overcome its current limitations.

## ID: AGS26/1588

## P0709: Early clinical outcomes of percutaneous stenoscopic lumbar decompression: a retrospective analysis of the first 8 cases

Mohammed Sameer Vk

1

1

NIMS Hospital,Wandoor, Shanti Hospital,Omasswery, Careland Hospital Nilambur, Manjeri, India

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery has become a minimally invasive alternative to open procedures, aiming to reduce pain, disability, and recovery time. Evidence from peripheral centers, however, remains limited. Objective: To evaluate clinical outcomes of percutaneous stenoscopic lumbar decompression (PSLD) in patients with lumbar degenerative pathology, with emphasis on pain and functional improvement. Material and Methods: A retrospective observational study was conducted on 8 patients who underwent PSLD. Pain was assessed using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), and functional disability using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), both preoperatively and at follow-up. Statistical analysis was performed using paired t-tests. Results: The mean preoperative VAS score was 7.75 ± 1.44, which significantly decreased to 2.12 ± 1.73 postoperatively (t = 8.44, p < 0.001), representing a 72.6% reduction in pain. The mean ODI score improved from 57.75 ± 4.23 to 7.12 ± 2.95 (t = 24.46, p < 0.001), corresponding to an 87.7% functional improvement. The mean follow-up duration was approximately 4-6 months (range 99-172 days). No major perioperative complications were observed, and all patients achieved early mobilization. Conclusion: PSLD is a safe and effective minimally invasive technique for lumbar degenerative conditions, providing significant pain relief and functional recovery, even during the initial learning curve. Larger prospective studies with longer follow-up are warranted to validate these promising results.

## ID: AGS26/1589

## P0710: Multi-level posterior endoscopic cervical decompression for cervical radiculopathy: case series

Supreeth Bharadwaj

1,2

, Gayani Petersingham

1,3

, Wentian Li

1,3

, Prashanth Rao

1,3,4

1

Brain and Spine surgery, Sydney, Australia

2

Western Sydney University, Sydney, Australia

3

UNSW, Sydney, Australia

4

Norwest Private Hospital, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: Cervical radiculopathy remains a debilitating condition caused by compression or irritation of cervical nerve roots, leading to pain, functional impairment and reduced quality of life. While most patients respond to conservative management, surgical intervention is indicated for persistent or severe symptoms. Traditional open or microscopic cervical discectomy and foraminotomy achieves effective decompression but is associated with soft tissue disruption, prolonged recovery, and increased post-operative pain. Endoscopic cervical decompression has therefore emerged as a collection of novel surgical techniques aiming to address the limitations associated with open or microscopic surgeries. Despite growing interest, evidence on the outcomes and complications of endoscopic techniques remains limited, particularly for multi-level cervical decompression. Multi-level endoscopic decompression allows for an ultra-minimally invasive alternative for multiple segment pathology. Hence, this case series reports the clinical outcomes and complications associated with multi-level posterior endoscopic cervical decompression for cervical radiculopathy. Through addressing a critical gap in the literature, this study aims to inform surgical decision-making in the management of cervical radiculopathy. Material and Methods: This retrospective single-centre case series included 12 patients undergoing multi-level posterior endoscopic cervical discectomy and foraminotomy for cervical radiculopathy between 2023 and 2025. Inclusion criteria included adult patients with symptomatic cervical radiculopathy refractory to conservative management who underwent multi-level posterior endoscopic cervical decompression. Exclusion criteria included prior cervical surgery, myeloradiculopathy and radiculopathy caused by other pathologies including tumour, infection and trauma. Pre-operative and post-operative patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) were assessed using validated questionnaires, including Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) for arm, neck and back pain, the Neck Disability Index (NDI), and EQ-5D-5L for quality of life. Incidence of complications was also recorded. Outcomes were measured at routine post-operative follow up appointments and paired t-test were utilised to evaluate changes in scores and the efficacy of the intervention. Results: The case series included 12 patients (7 males, 5 females; mean age 55.8 ± 11.9) and found an improvement in all validated questionnaires post-operatively compared to pre-operatively. There was statistically significant outcomes in pain, functional capacity and quality of life in the following PROMs: VAS neck (Δmean = -3, p < 0.003), VAS back (Δmean = -3.083, p < 0.009), NDI (Δmean = -10.5, p < 0.0005) and the EQ-5D-5L subcategories ‘Activities’ (Δmean = -1.083, p < 0.003), ‘Pain’ (Δmean = -1.25, p < 0.0009) and ‘Quality of Life’ (Δmean = 22, p < 0.001). However, improvements in VAS arm (Δmean = -1.67, p < 0.24) and EQ-5D-5L subcategories ‘Mobility’ (Δmean = -0.083, p < 0.75), ‘Self-care’ (Δmean = -0.25, p < 0.27) and ‘Anxiety’ (Δmean = -0.083, p < 0.75) were not statistically significant. Conclusion: Evidence on multi-level posterior endoscopic cervical decompression remains limited, as only a small proportion of patients require multi-level surgery. In this series, several validated outcome measures showed statistically significant improvements, suggesting promising results of this surgical intervention for patients with cervical radiculopathy. However, more robust, multi-centre studies with larger patient cohorts are required to better define safety and efficacy of multi-level posterior endoscopic decompression.

## ID: AGS26/1606

## P0711: Minimally-invasive reconstructive spinal surgery for infectious spondylitis: 3 years follow-up analysis

Denis Naumov

1

, Dmitry Lepakov

1

, Arkadiy Vishnevsky

1

1

Saint-Petersburg State Phthisiopulmonology Research Institute, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: The choice of treatment strategy for infectious spondylitis remains debatable. Currently, up to 50-65% of patients receive conservative treatment. The role and place of minimally invasive surgical technologies in patients with infectious spondylitis is defined mainly in the context of debridement procedures; however, the potential for performing minimally invasive spinal reconstructions has not been fully explored. Material and Methods: The cohort study 81 patients, aged from 24 to 79 years (M = 51.2 ± 6.4 years), who underwent MIS spinal reconstructions of lumbar and lumbo-sacral spine due to spondylitis. All cases operated by three column reconstructions with anterior column fusion by tubular (n = 62) or video-assisted (n = 19) techniques followed by transcutaneous screw fixation. Follow-up period was evaluated at list 12 months. Analyzed parameters included: ASIA, VAS, ODI, Charlson Comorbidity Index, ASA, SF-36. Risk of complications was assessed using the SITE score (Pluemer J., 2021). Intraoperative blood loss (ml) and operation time (min) was recorded. Orthopedic and infectious complications were evaluated according to the Clavien-Dindo classification (2004). Hospitalization duration (days) was also taken into account. Results: Neurological improvement was observed postoperatively in all groups. In group I, blood loss amounted to 150 ± 19 ml, whereas in group II 200 ± 56 ml. The severity of postoperative pain was 4.95 ± 0.56 and 2.8 ± 0.7 in the groups. Length of hospital stay was 9.1 ± 1.4 days in group I and 11.4 ± 3.9 in group II. No infectious complications were detected in group I; group II had 10 (13.3%) IIIB complications according Clavien-Dindo. Postoperative quality of life assessed 1 month after surgery using the SF-36 questionnaire was 88.5 ± 3.61 in group I and 72.86 ± 3.47 in group II. The ODI index at 1 year postoperatively was 25.48% ± 12.92, 31.41% ± 10.91 in group II respectively. Conclusion: The use of minimally invasive technologies for the treatment of infectious spondylitis of the lumbar spine reduced complications rate and decrease intraoperative blood loss, operation time and length of hospital stay up to 60% versus traditional surgical technologies.

## ID: AGS26/1705

## P0712: Biportal endoscopic assisted cervical lateral mass screw fixation: surgical technique and clinical outcome

Songwut Sirivitmaitree

1

1

Bandung Crown Prince Hospital, Udon, Thailand

Introduction: Cervical lateral mass screw fixation is a widely used posterior spinal stabilization technique for treating various cervical spine conditions. There are many advantage such as strong biomechanic, low risk of spinal cord injury and suitable for multilevel fixation but the disadvantage were extensive muscle stripping, large incision, persistence post-operative neck pain, wound dehiscence. The biportal endoscopic spine surgery, originally developed for lumbar spine, has been adapted to cervical spine procedures to minimize surgical morbidity. So biportal endoscopic assisted cervical lateral mass screw fixation is an advance minimally invasive technique that combine biportal endoscopic with traditional cervical lateral mass screw fixation. This approach aims to enhance visualization, reduce soft tissue damage, decrease blood lost while maintaining the stability and efficacy of cervical lateral mass screw fixation. Material and Methods: This study included 17 patients with multilevel of cervical spondylotic myelopathy who underwent biportal endoscopic-assisted lateral mass screw fixation between march 2023 and September 2024. A novel multi portal approach was used to facilitate bilateral screw placement with minimal muscle disruption. Clinical outcomes were assessed using Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for neck pain, Neck Disability Index (NDI), length of hospital stay, analgesic consumption, blood lost, complication and radiographic fusion rates at 6 months. Results: All patients underwent successful bilateral fixation with no intraoperative neurovascular complications. the average VAS of neck pain day one after operation was 2.3 and NDI scores improved significantly postoperatively (p < 0.01). Length of hospital stay3.5 days, all of the patient no need intravenous analgesic and no wound complication. All of cases radiographic fusion was confirmed at 6 months. No cases of screw malposition or hardware failure were observed. Conclusions: Biportal endoscopic assisted cervical lateral mass screw fixation is promising minimal invasive technique. this approach may lead better postoperative outcome, include reduce pain, short hospital stay and no wound complication. however further studies are necessary to establish its long-term efficacy, safety profile and optimal indication.

## ID: AGS26/1714

## P0713: Uniportal versus biportal endoscopic lumbar discectomy and decompression: comparative outcomes and technical pearls

Samuel Sing Li Ong

1

, Terry Teo Hong Lee

1

, Andy Yeo Kuei Siong

1

, Dalun Leong

1

, Dinesh Shree Kumar

1

, Zhihong Chew

1

1

Changi General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: In today's ever-evolving landscape of spine surgery, endoscopic spine surgery has quickly gained traction amongst both surgeons as well as patients. Uniportal and biportal techniques have emerged as viable if not better alternatives than conventional or minimally invasive approaches for discectomy and decompression. However, the relative effectiveness of these two techniques remains debatable. This retrospective study aims to compare the outcomes of uniportal versus biportal endoscopic techniques in lumbar discectomy and decompression. Material and Methods: Conducted in a tertiary integrated spine centre, a total of 31 patients who underwent endoscopic lumbar spine surgery were included across a 13-month period. 11 patients had uniportal surgery (4 discectomies and 7 decompressions) while 20 patients had biportal surgery (12 discectomies and 8 decompressions). Only one and two-level surgeries were included in this study. In total, 38 levels were performed. The main outcome measures included were operative duration, length of hospital stay, and post-operative complications. Statistical analysis was then performed to determine any significant differences between the two approaches. Results: Our findings suggest that the duration of surgery per level for biportal discectomy (101.54 ± 28.15 minutes) is significantly shorter compared to uniportal discectomy (123.75 ± 2.5 minutes) (p < 0.05). On the contrary for decompression surgery, the duration of surgery is shorter using uniportal compared to biportal technique but this was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). There was also no significant difference in the length of hospital stay between the uniportal and biportal groups (p > 0.05). All patients reported improvement in symptoms. However, 2 complications were recorded. 1 had a recurrence of back pain after 6 months post-biportal decompression while another had a dura tear during uniportal decompression which was sealed and the patient was discharged well. Conclusion: Both endoscopic uniportal and biportal techniques can be effectively utilised for lumbar discectomy and decompression procedures with good outcomes. Both are versatile techniques with their own pros and cons.

## ID: AGS26/1721

## P0714: Percutaneous versus open short-segment pedicle screw fixation for thoracolumbar burst fractures: clinical and serial radiographic 2-year follow-up

Chih-Hsuan Wung

1

, Hou-Tsung Chen

1,2

1
Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Kaohsiung Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Kaohsiung, Taiwan,

2

Cheng-Shiu University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan

Introduction: To investigate the clinical and serial radiographic outcomes over a two-year follow-up period between percutaneous pedicle screw fixation (PPSF) and open pedicle screw fixation (OPSF) in the treatment of thoracolumbar burst fractures (TLBF). Material and Methods: Patients with TLBF accepted either PPSF or OPSF were included. Available preoperative and postoperative plain radiographs and computed tomography (CT) scans of the thoracolumbar spine within 2 years follow up were required. Clinical outcomes including operative time, blood loss and postoperative hospital stay were assessed. Radiographic outcomes including Cobb angle and lumbar lordosis angle (LLA) were evaluated. Results: Fifty-four patients were enrolled. PPSF group has 29 patients (mean age, 51.76 ± 14.30), and OPSF group has 25 patients (mean age, 49.16 ± 13.31). The mean fixation vertebrae were 3.3 ± 0.2. The volume of intraoperative blood loss was significantly lower in the PPSF group (61.9 ± 34.8 mL) compared to the OPSF group (262.7 ± 205.1 mL) (p < 0.001). There were no significant differences in operation time (p = 0.114) and postoperative hospital stay (p = 0.447) between the two groups. There was no significant difference of preoperative Cobb angle between the two groups (PPSF group: 20.7° ± 9.3°; OPSF group: 27.6° ± 6.4°) (p = 0.238). Both groups demonstrated a significant reduction in Cobb angle over the 2-year period following surgery. The Cobb angle was significantly lower in the PPSF group (6.7° ± 3.5°) compared to the OPSF group (9.1° ± 6.4°) at two years postoperatively (p = 0.049). There were no significant differences in the serial postoperative LLAs and LLA reduction between the groups within the 2-year postoperative period. Conclusion: PPSF is recommended as the preferred treatment for TLBF compared to OPSF due to its superior clinical and radiographic outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1731

## P0715: Biportal endoscopic posterior cervical foraminotomy (PCF) versus posterior cervical inclinatory foraminotomy (PCIF)

Songwut Sirivitmaitree

1

1

Bandung Crown Prince Hospital, Udon, Thailand

Introduction: Cervical radiculopathy is commonly treated with posterior cervical foraminotomy (PCF), which decompresses the nerve root while preserving motion segments. Traditional PCF, however, may involve significant facet joint resection, potentially leading to postoperative pain and instability. Biportal endoscopic PCF has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative, offering enhanced visualization and reduced tissue trauma. More recently, posterior cervical inclinatory foraminotomy (PCIF) has been introduced to minimize facet resection by using an oblique drilling trajectory. This study compares the clinical and radiologic outcomes of biportal endoscopic PCF and PCIF, focusing on decompression efficacy, facet preservation, and postoperative recovery. Material and Methods: Patients with unilateral cervical foraminal stenosis who underwent either biportal endoscopic PCF or PCIF were retrospectively reviewed. Clinical outcomes were assessed using Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for neck and arm pain and Neck Disability Index (NDI). Radiologic parameters included facet resection rate, and preservation of cervical alignment. Operative time, hospital stay, and complications were also compared. Results: Both groups showed significant improvement in VAS and NDI scores postoperatively (p < 0.01). PCIF demonstrated superior facet preservation, with a lower facet resection rate (mean 32.2% vs. 47.6%, p < 0.001). PCIF had shorter operative time (mean 60.9 ± 2.6 min vs. 65.7 ± 7.4 min, p = 0.03) and comparable decompression efficacy. No major complications were reported. Conclusions: PCIF offers better facet preservation and shorter operative time, while PCF provides efficient decompression. Both techniques are effective for treating cervical radiculopathy, and selection should be tailored to patient anatomy and surgical goals.

## ID: AGS26/2007

## P0716: Interspinous ligament preservation in midline interspinous fixation: five-year comparative outcomes

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Sukanya Chebrolu

1

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

8

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: The posterior ligament complex contributes to lumbar stability and resists flexion. Interspinous fixation can be performed through a midline approach, but surgeons frequently excise the interspinous ligament to facilitate device seating. The clinical value of preserving the interspinous ligament has not been well defined across longer follow up. This study compared early and five-year outcomes for patients who underwent midline interspinous fixation with preservation of the interspinous ligament versus excision. Materials and Methods: A retrospective cohort included consecutive patients who underwent single level lumbar fusion with midline interspinous fixation. Group 1 underwent implantation with preservation of the interspinous ligament. Group 2 underwent implantation after ligament removal. The primary endpoint was change in Visual Analogue Scale pain scores at two weeks and five years. Secondary endpoints were computed tomography confirmed fusion rate, spinous process fracture, implant migration, and time to functional recovery. Comparisons used tests for continuous variables and chi square or Fisher exact tests for categorical outcomes, with significance at p < 0.05. Results: Eighty-six patients (n = 86) met inclusion criteria (Group 1 n = 42; Group 2 n = 44). Baseline pain severity did not differ. At two weeks, preservation produced lower pain than removal (3.2 versus 4.8, p < 0.001), and this difference persisted at five years (2.4 versus 4.1, p < 0.001). Fusion was more frequent with preservation (92.8% versus 79.5%, p = 0.048). Spinous process fracture occurred in 0% versus 9.1% (p = 0.038). Implant migration occurred in 0% versus 6.8% (p = 0.11). Median time to functional recovery was shorter with preservation (4.2 weeks versus 6.8 weeks, p < 0.001). No device failures were recorded. Conclusion: Preserving the interspinous ligament during midline interspinous fixation was associated with superior short-term comfort, higher fusion rate, fewer spinous process fractures, and faster functional recovery through five years. These findings support preservation of the ligament when technically feasible and provide a rationale for midline techniques that respect the posterior tension band.

## ID: AGS26/2031

## P0717: Enhanced accuracy and safety in MIS TLIF using navigation, intraoperative CT, and robotic guidance

Senol Jadik

1

1

Kuwait Hospital, Kuwait City, Kuwait

Introduction: To evaluate the feasibility, accuracy, and clinical outcomes of minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS TLIF) surgeries performed with navigation, intraoperative CT scanning, and robotic arm guidance. Material and Methods: A prospective study was conducted on 101 patients who underwent MIS TLIF between 2023 and 2025. The mean age was 59.4 years (SD ±14.7). Surgical levels included 56 single-level, 25 two-level, 7 three-level, and 2 four-level lumbar fixations. Pedicle screw placement was guided by intraoperative CT-based navigation and robotic arm assistance, without the use of fluoroscopy. Data were collected on screw accuracy, neurological outcomes, revision surgeries, radiation exposure, surgical time, and surgeon workflow. Results: No screw malpositioning was observed across all cases, including multilevel fixations. No postoperative neurological deficits related to screw placement occurred, and no revision surgeries were required. Radiation exposure was significantly reduced, with no fluoroscopy necessary during screw insertion. Overall surgical time decreased compared to conventional techniques. After an initial learning curve of 5-10 cases, setup and handling became straightforward and integrated smoothly into routine workflow. The use of navigation and robotic assistance allowed the surgeon to remain focused on neural decompression, improving surgical efficiency and safety. Conclusion: MIS TLIF with intraoperative CT navigation and robotic arm guidance is a highly accurate, safe, and efficient technique, even in multilevel fixations. This approach minimizes radiation exposure, eliminates fluoroscopy, reduces operative time, and enhances surgeon confidence and focus. The technology is feasible for routine clinical use and contributes to improved surgical safety and outcomes.

## P0718: Full-endoscopic transforaminal thoracic partial pediculectomy for palliative decompression of radicular pain in spinal metastasis: a technical note

Siravich Suvithayasiri

1

, Warayos Trathitephun

1

, Akarawit Asawasaksakul

2

, Jin-Sung Kim

3

1

Chulabhorn Hospital, Chulabhorn Royal Academy, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Ramkhamhaeng Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

3

Seoul St. Mary's Hospital, College of Medicine, the Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Severe radicular pain from thoracic spinal metastases in oncologic patients presents a significant treatment challenge. While minimally invasive surgery (MISS) reduces morbidity, reports on purely endoscopic techniques for palliative decompression in this population are scarce. We present a novel, full-endoscopic transforaminal technique with partial pediculectomy to decompress a thoracic nerve root compromised by a metastatic tumor. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 72-year-old female with colon cancer and a metastatic lesion causing severe, debilitating right-sided T9 radiculopathy. The patient’s Spine Instability Neoplastic Score (SINS) indicated spinal stability. The procedure involved a full-endoscopic, transforaminal approach at the T9-T10 level. Foraminotomy was achieved via partial resection of the T9-T10 superior articular process and the superior portion of the T10 pedicle to decompress the T9 nerve root. Operative data and health-related quality of life (HRQoL) scores, including the Pain Numeric Rating Scale (NRS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), were documented. Results: The total operative time was 115 minutes with an estimated blood loss of only 20 ml. The patient was ambulatory with assistance on the first postoperative day. A significant clinical improvement was observed, with the NRS score decreasing from 10 to 2 and the ODI score decreasing from 100 to 20. The patient was discharged two days post-surgery and proceeded with radiotherapy seven days later. No surgical complications were reported. Conclusion: Full-endoscopic thoracic foraminotomy with partial pediculectomy is a feasible and effective minimally invasive technique for palliating radicular pain in carefully selected patients with spinal metastases. This approach provides rapid pain relief and functional recovery, enabling patients to promptly resume essential oncologic treatments like radiotherapy.

## ID: AGS26/2060

## P0719: The use of endoscopic spine surgery for spinal metastasis: a scoping review of the current literature

Siravich Suvithayasiri

1

, Win Boonsirikamchai

2

, Piya Chavalparit

3

, Jin-Sung Kim

4

1

Thonburi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Bhumibol Adulyadej Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

3

Vajira Hospital, Navamindradhiraj University, Bangkok, Thailand

4

Seoul St. Mary's Hospital, College of Medicine, the Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Spinal metastases are an increasing clinical challenge, often requiring surgical intervention for pain relief, neurological preservation, and mechanical stabilization. Endoscopic spine surgery (ESS) has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative to traditional open procedures, potentially offering reduced morbidity and faster recovery. However, its application for the specific challenges of metastatic disease, such as tumor vascularity and distorted anatomy, is not well-defined. The extent and nature of the published evidence on this topic are unknown. The objective of this study was to conduct a scoping review to systematically map the existing literature on ESS for spinal metastases, summarize reported techniques and outcomes, and identify critical knowledge gaps. Material and Methods: A scoping review was performed in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. A comprehensive literature search of PubMed, Scopus, Embase, and the Cochrane Library was conducted for all relevant publications up to September 2025. Inclusion criteria were studies of any design reporting on the use of uniportal or biportal ESS for the treatment of spinal metastases in human subjects. Exclusion criteria included narrative reviews, non-English language publications, and studies describing endoscopic-assisted open surgery or techniques other than true full-endoscopic procedures. Two reviewers independently screened titles, abstracts, and full texts for eligibility. Data regarding study characteristics, patient demographics, tumor types, surgical techniques, and reported advantages and challenges were extracted and charted. Results: The search identified 189 records, of which 13 studies met the final inclusion criteria. The evidence base consisted predominantly of case reports and small case series, representing Level IV or V evidence, with a total of 46 patients reported. No randomized controlled trials or direct comparative studies were identified. Both uniportal and biportal ESS techniques were described for neural decompression and separation surgery in the cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine. The most common primary tumor histologies reported were lung, colon, and prostate cancer. Frequently reported advantages (“pearls”) included reduced blood loss, shorter hospital stays, minimal soft tissue trauma, and faster recovery enabling timely initiation of adjuvant radiotherapy. Key reported challenges (“pitfalls”) included difficulties with hemostasis, especially in hypervascular tumors, limited maneuverability for extensive bone resection, and a steep learning curve frequently cited by authors as a significant barrier to adoption. Conclusion: ESS is a feasible and emerging minimally invasive surgical option for carefully selected patients with spinal metastases. However, this scoping review confirms that the current body of literature is of low evidentiary quality, lacks comparative data, and is insufficient to establish definitive indications or assess superiority over other surgical techniques. The findings highlight a critical need for multicenter, prospective cohort studies or registries to better define patient selection criteria, evaluate oncological and functional outcomes, and establish evidence-based protocols for the safe and effective application of ESS in the multidisciplinary management of spinal metastases.

## ID: AGS26/2130

## P0720: Who gets sent where? Cardiovascular burden in endoscopy, tumors in tubular

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Maya Toothman

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Jay I. Kumar

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Patrick Kim

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Surgical approach in lumbar spinal stenosis is often discussed in terms of technique and outcomes, yet referral patterns may shape which patients end up in each group. If certain comorbidities are disproportionately represented in one approach, outcome differences may reflect case mix rather than purely technical factors. This study examined baseline characteristics of patients referred for tubular versus endoscopic decompression at a single institution where one surgeon exclusively performs endoscopy and all others use tubular techniques. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 180 patients who underwent minimally invasive decompression for lumbar spinal stenosis between June 2021 and April 2024. Patient demographics, cardiovascular risk factors, systemic comorbidities, ASA score, and Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI) were extracted from electronic health records. Comparisons between tubular and endoscopic cohorts were performed using Chi-square, Fisher’s exact, and t-tests. Results: Patients referred for endoscopic decompression more often carried higher comorbidity burdens, including active smoking (19% vs. 6.3%, p = 0.014), hypertension (63.3% vs. 45.5%, p = 0.018), coronary artery disease (15.2% vs. 5.0%, p = 0.020), congestive heart failure (6.3% vs. 0%, p = 0.010), and peripheral vascular disease (6.3% vs. 0%, p = 0.010). Median ASA scores were higher in the endoscopic cohort (3 [IQR 2-4] vs. 2 [IQR 1-3], p = 0.017). No significant differences were observed in age, sex distribution, BMI, chronic lung disease, or CCI. Conclusion: At our center, endoscopic referrals tended to involve patients with greater cardiovascular comorbidity and higher ASA scores. These patterns suggest that surgeon preference and referral bias likely influenced case allocation. When interpreting outcome comparisons between tubular and endoscopic decompression, baseline differences in referral populations must be considered to avoid over-attributing findings to surgical technique alone.

## ID: AGS26/2158

## P0721: When recovery lingers: factors linked to extended opioid use following tubular and endoscopic decompression

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Samantha Schimmel

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Yosef Nafii

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Patrick Kim

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS), including tubular and endoscopic techniques, is increasingly used for the treatment of lumbar spinal stenosis due to its advantages of reduced tissue trauma and faster recovery. However, a subset of patients continues to require opioids beyond one month after surgery, a phenomenon historically more common in traditional open procedures. Prolonged opioid use is associated with poorer outcomes, increased costs, and heightened dependence. This study aimed to identify perioperative and clinical factors associated with extended opioid use following tubular and endoscopic lumbar decompression. Material and Methods: A retrospective review of 180 patients who underwent MIS decompression for lumbar stenosis between June 2021 and April 2024 was performed (tubular: n = 101; endoscopic: n = 79). Data were extracted from electronic health records, including demographic findings, hospital course, prior surgical history, perioperative complications, and anatomic features of pathology. Pearson correlation analyses were used to identify variables associated with opioid use persisting longer than one month. Results: Across both techniques, illicit drug use (tubular: rho = 0.378, p = 0.002; endoscopic: rho = 0.262, p = 0.021) and longer hospital stays (tubular: rho = 0.300, p = 0.013; endoscopic: rho = 0.403, p < 0.001) were consistent predictors of extended opioid dependence. Distinct patterns emerged between techniques. In the tubular group, postoperative infection (rho = 0.249, p = 0.040) and prior spine surgery (rho = 0.295, p = 0.015) were strongly correlated with prolonged use. In contrast, within the endoscopic cohort, anatomical factors predominated, with far lateral discs (rho = 0.268, p = 0.018) and specific disc levels (L1-L2: rho = 0.268, p = 0.018; L3-L4: rho = 0.224, p = 0.049) linked to persistent opioid needs. Conclusion: Prolonged postoperative opioid use after MISS is multifactorial. While illicit drug use and longer hospitalizations were universal predictors, tubular cases were more vulnerable to infection- and history-related risk, whereas endoscopic outcomes were shaped more by anatomical variables. These findings may help optimize patient selection, improve preoperative counseling, and guide perioperative strategies aimed at reducing long-term opioid dependence.

## ID: AGS26/2175

## P0722: The infinity decompression: a stepwise biportal endoscopic technique for over-the-top lumbar decompression

Diogo Rodrigues

1

, Sara Sequeira

2

, Raquel Cunha

3

, Alexandre Castro

3

, João Alves

3

, Ricardo Frada

3

, Artur Teixeira

3

, Eduardo Pinto

3

1

ULS Santo António, ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Porto, Portugal

2

ULS Lisboa Ocidental, Faculdade de Medicina da Universidade de Lisboa, Lisboa, Portugal

3

ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Over-the-top (OTT) decompression has been widely adopted as a minimally invasive approach for central and bilateral lumbar stenosis. The application of unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) offers enhanced visualization, wider working angles, and safer bone and soft-tissue resection compared to tubular or uniportal endoscopic techniques. The objective is to describe a novel, systematic UBE-based OTT decompression technique, named “The Infinity Decompression”, due to its continuous 13-step sequence resembling the infinity symbol. Material and Methods: The Infinity Decompression was developed as a standardized workflow integrating ipsilateral and contralateral bony recession with progressive ligamentum flavum release. A total of 13 steps were defined, from initial ipsilateral laminotomy to final contralateral flavum release. Key technical pearls include early identification of ipsilateral landmarks, stepwise bone resection tailored to ligamentum flavum insertions, preventive coagulation before flavectomy, and “in one piece” hemi-flavum removal. Results: The systematic sequence facilitates reproducible decompression, minimizes the risk of neural or dural injury, and optimizes the working corridor for contralateral access. Preventive measures such as distal-to-proximal undercutting of the base of the spinous process, controlled facetectomy boundaries, and Kerrison-assisted direct cuts enhance safety. In foraminal stenosis, extended contralateral cranial lamina resection allows improved access to the emerging nerve root. Conclusion: The Infinity Decompression is a safe, effective, and reproducible UBE approach for central and bilateral lumbar stenosis. By combining logical sequencing with technical refinements, it improves reproducibility, shortens the learning curve, and preserves spinal stability. This method may represent a new paradigm in endoscopic lumbar decompression.

## ID: AGS26/2333

## P0723: Full-endoscopic posterior cervical discectomy for upward, downward, and recurrent cervical disc herniations: a technical case series

Katsuhiko Ishibashi

1

, Ryuichi Watanabe

1

, Kento Takebayashi

2

, Ryoji Tominaga

1

, Yasushi Inomata

1

, Kazuyoshi Yanagisawa

1

, Hisashi Koga

2

, Hirohiko Inanami

3

, Hiroki Iwai

3

1

Iwai Orthopaedic Hospital, Tokyo, Japan

2

Iwai Orthopaedic Hospital, Iwai FESS Clinic, Tokyo, Japan

3

Iwai Orthopaedic Hospital, Iwai FESS Clinic, Inanami Spine and Joint Hospital, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Cervical disc herniation with cranial or caudal migration poses technical challenges for posterior endoscopic discectomy. In addition, recurrent disc herniation after previous decompression is often managed with anterior fusion surgery due to concerns regarding adhesions and neural injury. However, anterior approaches carry risks such as dysphagia or adjacent segment disease. We report three cases of migrated or recurrent cervical disc herniation successfully treated by full-endoscopic posterior cervical discectomy (PECD), including technical modifications such as partial pediculotomy or hemilaminotomy. Material and Methods: While PECD is routinely performed for typical cervical disc herniation cases at our institution, these three cases were particularly challenging due to disc migration or recurrence. We retrospectively reviewed three consecutive cases treated with full-endoscopic posterior cervical discectomy at our institution between 2022 and 2025. The cases included: 1) upward-migrated C6/7 disc herniation, 2) downward-migrated C5/6 disc herniation, and 3) recurrent C6/7 disc herniation two years after prior foraminoplasty. All procedures were performed under general anesthesia using a posterior interlaminar approach. Partial pediculotomy or hemilaminotomy was added as necessary to access the migrated or recurrent fragments. Clinical outcomes were assessed by VAS (visual analog scale) for arm pain pre- and postoperatively. MRI and CT were used to confirm decompression. Results: All three patients experienced significant improvement in radicular arm pain immediately postoperatively. In the recurrent case, intraoperative findings revealed adhesions and a migrated fragment located caudal to the previous decompression site. A safe corridor was achieved via partial pediculotomy without dural violation. No intraoperative or postoperative complications were observed. At final follow-up (3 months), all patients-maintained symptom relief without recurrence. Imaging confirmed adequate decompression in all cases. Conclusion: Full-endoscopic posterior cervical discectomy is feasible and effective for challenging cases of upward- or downward-migrated, as well as recurrent, cervical disc herniation. With appropriate bony modification, this technique can safely access displaced fragments while avoiding anterior approach-related complications. However, advanced endoscopic skills and careful patient selection are required. Further case accumulation and outcome analysis are needed to validate this approach.

## P0724: The role of UBE in emergency spine surgery: effective treatment of severe neurological deficits

Diogo Rodrigues

1

, Sara Sequeira

2

, Raquel Cunha

3

, Alexandre Castro

3

, João Alves

3

, Ricardo Frada

3

, Artur Teixeira

3

, Eduardo Pinto

3

1

ULS Santo António, ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Porto, Portugal

2

ULS Lisboa Ocidental, Faculdade de Medicina da Universidade de Lisboa, Lisboa, Portugal

3

ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Neurological deficits affecting the cervical and lumbar spine can lead to significant impairments, such as drop foot or cervicobrachialgia, which severely impact quality of life. These conditions, often caused by nerve compression, present with motor deficits and pain requiring urgent intervention to prevent disability. This report highlights the emergency treatment of two patients using the Unilateral Biportal Endoscopy (UBE) technique. Material and Methods: A 56-year-old woman presented with right-sided sciatica, foot and toe weakness (MS grade 0), and numbness. MRI showed lumbar canal narrowing at L4/5. She underwent a 75-minute unilateral biportal endoscopic decompression. A 48-year-old man presented with cervicobrachialgia, decreased arm abduction (MS grade 2/5), and paresthesias. MRI revealed a large C4/5 foraminal disc herniation. He underwent emergency foraminoplasty and herniation removal. Results: Both patients experienced rapid symptom relief post-surgery. The first patient’s foot movement improved to FM grade 4+ within 6 weeks, with full recovery by 3 months. The second patient’s arm strength improved to FM grade 4+/5 at 6 months. Follow-up imaging confirmed effective decompression in both cases. Conclusion: These cases demonstrate the feasibility of biportal endoscopy in emergency settings, offering rapid symptom relief and functional recovery. The minimally invasive approach is effective for addressing severe nerve-related deficits, even in urgent scenarios, with excellent outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2345

## P0725: UBE-TLIF: minimally invasive fusion with enhanced visualization

Diogo Rodrigues

1

, Sara Sequeira

2

, Raquel Cunha

3

, Alexandre Castro

3

, João Alves

3

, Ricardo Frada

3

, Artur Teixeira

3

, Eduardo Pinto

3

1

ULS Santo António, ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Porto, Portugal

2

ULS Lisboa Ocidental, Faculdade de Medicina da Universidade de Lisboa, Lisboa, Portugal

3

ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) often presents with foraminal stenosis and/or spondylolisthesis, leading to nerve compression, back pain, and radicular symptoms. When conservative management fails, surgical intervention such as transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) is indicated. Unilateral Biportal Endoscopy (UBE)-assisted TLIF is an emerging minimally invasive technique that enhances visualization and reduces tissue damage while achieving effective decompression and fusion. Material and Methods: The UBE-TLIF procedure for LSS at L4/5 consisted of the following steps: 1. Preparation: Patients were positioned prone under general anesthesia, with operative and imaging monitors in direct view. 2. Incisions: Two 10 mm longitudinal incisions were made 25 mm from the symptomatic pedicles. 3. Bone Removal: The base of the lower facet was marked with the drill, and the capsule and interfacetary ligament were detached using an osteotome. The lower facet was broken into three wedges to facilitate removal through the portal. The base of the upper facet was then marked with the drill, followed by vertical osteotomy to complete the process. 4. Discectomy and Cage Placement: Excision of the ligamentum flavum en bloc to ensure complete decompression. The intervertebral disc was then excised, and the endplates were carefully prepared under direct endoscopic visualization to avoid damage and ensure proper cage integration. A TLIF cage was inserted without damaging neural structures, as the dura and exit root were protected with retractors. 5. Neural Decompression: The ligamentum flavum was resected, and epidural vessels coagulated. Continuous saline irrigation improved visibility and reduced neural injury risks. Contralateral decompression followed ipsilateral decompression in bilateral cases. 6. Stabilization: Bilateral percutaneous pedicle screws were placed to stabilize the segment and promote fusion. Results: The UBE-TLIF procedure demonstrated effective decompression and stable fusion. Postoperative assessments confirmed proper cage placement and fusion. This technique underscores the importance of meticulous preparation and advanced visualization tools to achieve optimal outcomes with minimal complications. Conclusion: UBE-TLIF is a safe, effective, and minimally invasive option for treating LSS with foraminal stenosis or spondylolisthesis. Its potential for rapid recovery and reduced complications underscores its value in modern spinal surgery. Further studies are warranted to establish its broader clinical efficacy.

## P0726: Exploring the efficacy of minimally invasive spine fixation in thoracolumbar spinal injuries

Md. Ziaul Hasan

1

, Md. Shah Alam

1

, S M Zubaer Hasan

2

, SK Muhammad Rahman

1

, Sefat-E-Rabbi Eva

3

1

National Institute of Traumatology and Orthopedic Rehabilitation, NITOR, Dhaka, Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital. BSOH, Dhaka, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Dhaka Dental College, Dhaka, Dhaka, Bangladesh

3

Sir Salimullah Medical College, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Traumatic thoracolumbar fractures are common, and surgical fixation is a well-established treatment option, with the aim to achieve spinal stability and preserve neurological function. Pedicle screw fixation using a minimally invasive spine (MIS) surgical approach has emerged as an alternative approach for the treatment of thoracolumbar fractures. The aim of this study is to collect data regarding epidemiology, management, and outcomes of patients treated with MIS pedicle screw fixation for traumatic thoracolumbar fractures in our neurosurgical department. Material and Methods: This was a retrospective cohort study including all patients who underwent MIS fixation from July 2021 to June 2024. Results: A total of 38 patients were included, 23 males and 15 females; the mean age was 38 years. The majority of injuries were from falls. In 12 cases, the fracture involved a thoracic vertebra and in 10 cases a lumbar vertebra and 16 cases thoracolumbar. 40% (n = 15) of the patients presented with a neurological deficit on admission and 75% (n = 11) of those showed postoperative improvement in their neurology. The average length of hospital stay was 6 days. MIS fixation achieved a satisfactory regional sagittal angle (RSA) postoperatively in all patients. The vast majority of patients had no or mild postoperative pain and achieved a good functional outcome. Conclusion: MIS fixation is a safe surgical option with comparable outcomes to open surgery and a potential reduction in perioperative morbidity. MIS surgery achieves a rapid and significant improvement in pain score, functional outcome, Frankel Grade, and RSA.

## ID: AGS26/2426

## P0727: Challenges and evolving strategies of full-endoscopic foraminotomy in the lower lumbar spine: a narrative review

Jong Hun Seo

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, College of Medicine, Chosun University, Gwangju, South Korea

Introduction: Lumbar foraminal stenosis (LFS) is a common degenerative condition that frequently involves the L4-5 and L5-S1 segment. Traditional surgical treatments have included microscopic foraminotomy and interbody fusion. With the advancement of endoscopic techniques, full-endoscopic foraminotomy has emerged as a minimally invasive option. However, in the lower lumbar spine, particularly at L5-S1, anatomical barriers such as a high iliac crest, large transverse process, and sacral ala often complicate the surgical approach. Material and Methods: We reviewed multiple studies on full-endoscopic foraminotomy, including our institutional research, to evaluate the technical evolution, clinical outcomes, and complications associated with various approaches. Results: The transforaminal approach, based on accessing the foraminal space through Kambin’s triangle and performing minimal partial resection of the superior articular process tip, has been widely used. Nevertheless, because the procedure is performed within a narrow working space, a relatively high incidence of postoperative dysesthesia has been reported. Furthermore, in lower lumbar segments such as L5-S1, anatomical barriers often make the initial working sheath landing challenging. Several advanced transforaminal techniques have been introduced to overcome these limitations, enabling decompression despite anatomical obstacles; however, these methods are associated with longer operative times and greater technical difficulty. Recently, the interlaminar contralateral approach and the paraspinal approach using a large endoscopic system have been described. The interlaminar contralateral approach, in particular, has demonstrated favorable clinical outcomes with a low complication rate. Conclusion: Relying solely on the transforaminal approach may not be optimal for all patients. Instead, tailoring the surgical strategy by incorporating the interlaminar contralateral or paraspinal approach, depending on the anatomical features of each case, can provide more effective and safer decompression for lumbar foraminal stenosis, especially at the L5-S1 level.

## ID: AGS26/2490

## P0729: Bibliometric analysis of endoscopic and microendoscopic spine surgery publications in spine

Hannah Shelby

1

, Sarah Bergren

1

, Natalie Kistler

1

, Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery is an evolving subset of minimally invasive spine surgery that has been increasing in popularity. Advances in specialized instrumentation and high-resolution imaging have extended its use beyond treatment of lumbar discectomy to encompass spinal stenosis, instability, thoracic and cervical myelopathy, infections, and intradural tumors. Despite its growing popularity, bibliometric trends within high-impact spine journals remain underexplored. This study sought to evaluate patterns of research productivity, citation impact, and geographic leadership in endoscopic and microendoscopic spine surgery publications in Spine from journal inception to 2025. Material and Methods: The Web of Science Core Collection was searched for full-endoscopic and microendoscopic spine surgery articles published in Spine between 1976 and 2024 using keywords. Citation analysis identified top authors (first, last, and any position) and top cited articles. Relative Citation Index (RCI) was calculated as citations divided by total publications per author. Study characteristics were reviewed independently by two investigators, looking at article type, level of evidence (LoE), topic, and country of origin. Results: Forty-four articles were identified, all published between 1994 and 2025. The most frequently cited authors in any position were predominantly affiliated with institutions in South Korea (40%) and Germany (40%). Author S.H. Lee was ranked highest among the top 20 authors in any position. Authors in any position featured the highest average citation count (662.6, range 277-1193) and RCI (184.6, range 13.97-723). The top 20 cited articles of all time averaged 222.5 citations, most commonly being case series (40%) and randomized controlled trials (25%), with LoE IV being most common (50%). Lumbar discectomy was the most frequent research focus (45%). In the last decade (2014-2024), top cited studies averaged 61.8 citations, with cohort studies (30%) and randomized controlled trials (20%) dominating the data. Among these works, China (40%) and South Korea (30%) contributed the largest share of recent high-impact studies. Similarly, a large portion (40%) of the top articles of all time were also associated with South Korean institutions. Conclusion: This bibliometric analysis reveals the top authors in endoscopic spine surgery offering a better understanding of the current literature that exists in the field. The work particularly underscores a growing international focus on minimally invasive spinal techniques and the need for continued high-quality comparative studies.

## ID: AGS26/2498

## P0730: A transfacet approach to the lumbar nerve root canal

Ivan Tarev

1

, Asen Cekov

1

, Marin Guentchev

1

, Anastas Kanev

1

, Christo Tzekov

1

1

Acibadem City Clinic Tokuda Hospital, Sofia, Bulgaria

Introduction: Intraforaminal and extraforaminal lumbar nerve root compressions are common causes of radiculopathy, most often resulting from disc herniation or stenosis. Conventional surgical approaches, such as posterior midline hemilaminectomy/interlaminotomy with facetectomy or the Wiltse paraspinal approach, may risk postoperative instability or require extensive tissue dissection. To address these limitations, we describe a novel posterior-anterior transfacet approach that enables safe and effective decompression while preserving facet joint integrity. Material and Methods: For a period of 2 years, 11 patients with intraforaminal or extraforaminal disc herniations underwent transfacet foraminotomy. Preoperative imaging included MRI in all cases and CT in selected patients. Outcome measures included the North American Spine Society Patient Satisfaction Index (PSI), reoperation rates, and radiographic and biomechanical analyses. Postoperative CT and finite element modeling were employed to assess facet joint integrity and stress distribution. Results: The median operative time was 47 minutes with minimal intraoperative blood loss. All patients experienced immediate postoperative pain relief, and 9 were pain-free at discharge. At dismissal, 10 patients reported PSI = 1, and one patient PSI = 3. At last follow-up (median 26 months), 10 patients maintained PSI scores of 1 or 2. Two patients required reoperation due to recurrent herniation. Postoperative CT imaging revealed spontaneous reduction of the drilled facet defect volume by 50-75% within one year. Finite element analysis demonstrated only a transient 9.2% increase in facet stress postoperatively, normalizing at 12 months. Conclusion: The posterior-anterior transfacet approach provides a safe, efficient, and stability-preserving alternative for decompression of intraforaminal and extraforaminal lumbar disc herniations. This technique achieves rapid symptom relief, demonstrates spontaneous bony remodeling, and avoids long-term compromise of facet joint strength. Larger prospective studies are warranted to confirm these promising outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2510

## P0731: Comparison of minimally invasive spinal surgery with stereotactic radiation with open surgical techniques: outcomes in epidural compression, complications and survival

Andrew Quinn

1

, Leilani White

1

, Sabrina Crooks

1

, Haley Pang

1

, Keanu Chee

1

, Mahesh Karandikar

1

, Anand Hemmady

1

, Christoph Hofstetter

1

, Andrew Ko

1

, Fangyi Zhang

1

, Simon Lo

1

, Benjamin Grannan

1

, Anubhav Amin

1

1

University of Washington, Seattle, United States

Introduction: Metastatic spine disease causes significant neurologic morbidity, and neurosurgical intervention can be technically challenging in achieving sufficient decompression and stabilization to enable oncologic therapy. Both open and minimally invasive surgical approaches are viable for treating spinal metastases. Compared to open approaches, recent evidence suggests minimally invasive spine (MIS) surgery reduces complications, shortens hospital stays, and improves survival. This study evaluates whether MIS surgery provides equivalent decompression to open surgery while lowering complication rates for surgical treatment of spinal metastases. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who underwent open versus MIS surgery for metastatic spine disease at two institutions from 2018-2023. Spinal cord compression was assessed pre- and postoperatively using the Epidural Spinal Cord Compression (ESCC) scale on magnetic resonance imaging. Group differences were analyzed with Fisher’s exact and Welch’s t-test (p < .05). One-year survival was estimated with Kaplan-Meier curves. Results: One-hundred and fourteen patients were included in this study. Sixty-six (58%) were male. The median age was 61 (32-86). Eighty-nine (78.1%) underwent open surgery while 25 (21.9%) underwent MIS surgery. In the open group, preoperative ESCC scores showed 6 (6.7%) no/low compression, 14 (15.7%) with dural compression, and 69 (77.5%) with cord compression; postoperatively, 41 (46.1%) and no/low compression, 21 (23.6%) had dural compression, 14 (15.7%) had cord compression, and 13 (14.6%) were lost to follow-up. In the MIS surgery group, preoperative ESCC scores showed 9 (36%) with no/low compression, 10 (40%) with dural compression, and 6 (24%) with cord compression; postoperatively, 19 (76%) had no/low compression, 4 (16%) had dural compression, and 2 (8%) had cord compression. MIS surgery was associated with reduced blood loss (155.8 vs. 525.1mL, p < .01) and lower complication rates (MIS = 16%, open = 39.3%; p = .03). One-year survival was 57% (95% CI 47.3-68.7%) after open surgery and 78% (95% CI 56.1-91.9%) after MIS surgery. Average length of stay was 7.0d for MIS surgery patients, and 19.3d for open surgery patients; this different was not statistically significant. Conclusion: This study demonstrates that MIS surgery for treatment of metastatic spine disease is associated with lower blood loss and lower complications while achieving similar rates of surgical decompression in comparison to open surgery. While there was no statistically significant difference in length of stay between groups, the trend towards shorter stays with MIS surgery suggests a potential benefit for patient recovery and resource utilization. Future studies could employ a similar analysis in a larger sample size as to minimize sampling biases. Nonetheless, MIS surgery for metastatic spine disease represents a less invasive alternative for adequately managing metastatic spine disease and warrants further prospective analysis to determine whether MIS approach confers a survival benefit in comparison to open approaches.

## ID: AGS26/2540

## P0732: Unilateral biportal endoscopic decompression and discectomy for multilevel lumbar disc prolapse in an adolescent: a case report and technical note

Selvin Vijayan

1

, Krish Sridhar

1

1

Kauvery Hospital, Chennai, India

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation is uncommon in the pediatric population, accounting for < 5% of surgically treated cases. Multilevel disease with severe canal stenosis is even rarer, and surgical management must balance effective decompression with preservation of spinal stability and growth potential. Endoscopic approaches, particularly unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE), are increasingly recognized as minimally invasive alternatives with favorable outcomes. Materials and Methods: A 15-year-old male presented with 2 months of progressive low back pain radiating to the right lower limb, aggravated by ambulation. Neurological examination revealed right L5 weakness (3/5). MRI demonstrated L3-4 and L4-5 disc prolapse with severe canal stenosis, showing interval progression compared to prior imaging. After failure of conservative therapy, UBE decompression and discectomy were performed-right-sided at L3-4 and left-sided at L4-5-using a two-portal endoscopic technique under general anesthesia. Results: The procedure achieved adequate decompression with minimal bony resection. Postoperatively, the patient experienced significant relief of radicular pain and improved neurological function (right L5 motor power improved from 3/5 preoperatively to 4/5 at discharge). Early mobilization was initiated on postoperative day one, and independent ambulation was achieved by day two. No perioperative complications occurred. At discharge, the patient was neurologically stable, ambulant with minimal support, and tolerating oral diet. Conclusion: UBE decompression is a safe and effective motion-preserving option for managing multilevel lumbar disc prolapse in adolescents. This case highlights its advantages of superior visualization, minimal soft tissue trauma, and early recovery. Given the rarity of pediatric lumbar stenosis requiring surgery, this report contributes to the limited literature supporting UBE as a valuable minimally invasive technique in complex and age-sensitive cases.

## P0733: Managing lumbar adjacent segment disease: a description of minimally invasive techniques and algorithm

Arvind G Kulkarni

1

1

Mumbai Spine Scoliosis & Disc Replacement Center, Bombay Hospital, Breach Candy Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: To manage symptomatic lumbar adjacent segment disease effectively using MIS TLIF techniques. Material and Methods: This study presents a tailored, step-by-step guide for managing lumbar adjacent segment disease (ASD) using minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS TLIF) techniques in patients with prior spinal fusion surgeries, whether open or minimally invasive. Four distinct surgical approaches are outlined, each selected based on the time elapsed since the primary surgery, the level of ASD involvement, and the fusion status of the previously operated segment. The study includes patients who underwent surgical treatment for lumbar ASD between January 2008 and December 2023. Results: Eight cases were operated with the four different techniques. The study age group was from 40 to 80 years (Mean age: 68.5 years). None of them had to be converted to open surgery. Patients operated using the described techniques reported significant clinical improvements, with the mean Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) decreasing from 58.47 ± 6.07 to 22.58 ± 3.299, and the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores improving from 7.076 ± 1.04 to 1.9 ± 0.77 of the pre-operative symptoms. Conclusion: Drawing on their extensive clinical experience, the authors offer valuable insights into innovative minimally invasive posterior techniques for managing lumbar adjacent segment disease.

## ID: AGS26/2564

## P0734: Technical note: a novel posterior oblique lateral (POL) approach in full endoscopic spine surgery-an alternative to the transforaminal approach

Pornpavit Sriphirom

1

, Akkapong Nitising

1

, Luckchai Phonwijit

1

, Bunpot Sitthinamsuwan

1

, Songrit Vuttipongkul

1

1

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Surgery, Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery for various lumbar pathologies continues to evolve. While the transforaminal route through Kambin’s triangle remains a cornerstone, its application is often limited by anatomical variations such as disc height loss, spondylolisthesis, nerve root anomalies, or a high iliac crest. Therefore, we developed the Posterior Oblique Lateral (POL) approach, a novel endoscopic entry, to overcome these anatomical constraints. Material and Methods: This technical note describes the POL approach, which begins with docking on the transverse process along a posterior-oblique trajectory, followed by stepwise identification of the superior articular process, facet capsule, and pars before advancing to Kambin’s triangle under direct visualization. The technique creates a wider working angle and minimizes early contact with neural structures. We demonstrate its use in three patients with foraminal stenosis, spondylolytic spondylolisthesis requiring fusion, and far-out syndrome. Results: The POL approach provided a safe working corridor in all cases, including a critically narrowed Kambin’s triangle situation. It offers safe decompression of exiting and traversing roots, facilitated fusion with facetectomy and cage placement, and allowed extraforaminal decompression. All patients experienced symptomatic improvement and recovered without perioperative complications. Conclusion: In anatomically constrained cases, the POL approach may serve as a safe and versatile endoscopic alternative by providing a reproducible working corridor that could complement the standard transforaminal approach. It may also lower the risk of exiting nerve root injury and can be performed safely without neuromonitoring under general anesthesia, supporting its potential for routine clinical use.

## P0735: Endoscopic treatment of extradural schwannoma of the l5 nerve root: case report with 2-year follow-up and literature review

Cristian Ricardo Correa Valencia

1

, Camila Salazar

2

, Diego Contreras

3

, Matias Nahuelpan

2

, Rodolfo Lopez

2

1

Clinica Las Condes, Santiago, Chile

2

Hospital Hernan Henriquez Aravena, Temuco, Chile

3

Complejo Asistencial de Padre las Casas, Temuco, Chile

Introduction: Schwannomas are benign tumors arising from Schwann cells of the peripheral nervous system, typically involving the nerve root in either its intra- or extradural portion. Resection of these tumors represents a surgical challenge due to the risk of postoperative neurological deficit. Although endoscopic techniques offer the advantages of minimally invasive surgery, fewer than five cases have been reported to date. In this case, we used the Full Endoscopic Plus approach (uniportal with an accessory portal) for tumor resection. This is the first reported case with the longest follow-up. Material and Methods: A 38-year-old male presented with severe right L5 radicular pain (VAS: 8/10), paresthesia in the L5 dermatome, and motor weakness (M4) with a 24-month history of symptoms. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed an extradural schwannoma of the right L5 root. Following surgical planning, tumor resection was performed through an L4-L5 portal, where an L5 hemilaminectomy allowed proximal root exposure, combined with a caudal accessory portal for medial dissection of the L5-S1 foramen with identification of the tumor-nerve root interface. Resection was achieved until negative motor responses were obtained with intraoperative monitoring. Postoperative imaging confirmed complete removal of the lesion. Results: The patient was discharged within 24 hours, without motor deficit, and reported significant pain reduction (radicular VAS: 2/10). At the 24-month follow-up, the patient remains asymptomatic (radicular VAS: 0/10), with preserved neurological function (M5), no recurrence on MRI, and no de novo instability. Conclusion: This case demonstrates that the Full Endoscopic Plus technique allows radical resection of extradural L5 schwannomas with functional preservation, providing sustained mid-term results. Its success relies on: Optimal portal planning, strict intraoperative neuromonitoring, microanatomical dissection of the tumor-nerve interface. The maximum tumor size suitable for this approach remains to be defined.

## ID: AGS26/2626

## P0736: Outcome of minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion with tubular microscopic decompression for lumbar degenerative disorders

Atif Ali

1

, Shehzad Javed

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital Lahore, Lahore, Pakistan

Background: Minimally Invasive Transforaminal Lumbar Interbody Fusion (MIS-TLIF) combined with bilateral decompression is increasingly recognized as a reliable surgical option for lumbar degenerative disorders. This technique offers the advantages of reduced tissue disruption, lower perioperative morbidity, and faster recovery compared with conventional open approaches. The present study evaluates clinical and perioperative outcomes of MIS-TLIF in patients treated at a tertiary care teaching hospital. Methods: Fifty patients who had Tubular Microscopic Decompression and MIS-TLIF over two years were the subject of a retrospective analysis. Perioperative parameters like operative duration, intraoperative blood loss, length of hospital stay, and complication rates were among the outcome measures, as were pain reduction measured by the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and functional improvement measured by the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Preoperative and postoperative scores were compared to evaluate the effectiveness of surgery. Results: After surgery, patients showed a noticeable and statistically significant improvement in their functional status and level of pain. Significant pain relief and functional recovery were demonstrated by the mean ODI scores improving by 30 points (p < 0.01) and the mean VAS scores decreasing by 4.5 points (p < 0.01) at the 6-month follow-up. The procedure's minimally invasive nature was highlighted by the positive perioperative results, which included an average intraoperative blood loss of 150 mL and a mean hospital stay of 3 days. With a 4% incidence, complications were rare and mostly minor wound-related problems that went away with conservative treatment. Conclusions: For lumbar degenerative disorders, MIS-TLIF with bilateral decompression is a safe and successful surgical approach that minimizes blood loss and hospital stay while providing significant improvements in pain management and functional outcomes. This technique has great potential due to its low complication profile and quick recovery, especially when customized to the clinical and anatomical needs of each patient.
