## P0737: Extra-foraminal disc herniations: technique & outcomes of transforaminal tubular discectomy using a 14-mm tubular retractor

Arvind G. Kulkarni

1

1

Mumbai Spine Scoliosis & Disc Replacement Center, Bombay Hospital, Breach Candy Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: While Trans-Foraminal (TF) approach with an open technique as well as endoscopic approach have been popular, TF microscopic discectomy using a 14-mm diameter tubular retractor is scarcely known. The objective of this study is to report our experience of TF discectomy with a 14mm tubular retractor system in a consecutive case series of 20 patients with extra-foraminal disc herniations. Material and Methods: A consecutive group of 20 patients with extra-foraminal lumbar disc herniations that had failed a trial of conservative treatment, were operated using a far lateral TF approach with a 14 mm tubular retractor system from March 2018 to January 2024. Patient demographics, operative time, blood loss and complications were documented. Pre-operative and post-operative VAS scores for back and leg pain at 6 weeks, 6 months, and 1year were documented. Results: A 14mm tube (METRx system, Medtronic Sofamor Danek, Memphis, TN) was successfully utilized in all patients. The mean operative time was 30 ± 10.22 minutes while intra-operative blood loss was 30 ± 15 ml. The levels affected in descending order were L3-L4 (8), L4-L5 (5), L5-S1 (4) and L2-L3 (3). All patients were mobilized out of bed on the same day post-surgery. The VAS score for leg pain improved from 7.65 ± 0.77 to 2.0 ± 0.72 post-operatively while the post-operative VAS score for back pain was 1.23 ± 0.83. Only one patient with a L5-S1 extra-foraminal herniation presented with a recurrence and he was managed conservatively. Conclusion: The 14-mm tubular TF discectomy offers a safe and effective technique, providing sustained pain relief and functional improvement while minimizing tissue trauma and preserving integrity.

## P0738: Manejo del síndrome facetario lumbar mediante crioablación percutánea en un centro médico de tercer nivel

Marcos Felipe Contreras Zuñiga

1

, A. González Moga

1

, M, Fuentes Rivera

1

, J.C. Verde Becerra

1

, H. Santos Benítez

1

Centro Médico ISSEMyM Ecatepec, Mexico, Mexico

Introduction: Lumbar facet syndrome is a frequent etiology of chronic low back pain, with an estimated prevalence ranging from 15% to 31% and a lifetime incidence reported up to 52% in certain series (1). Percutaneous cryoablation constitutes a minimally invasive technique that may provide temporary pain relief with a low rate of complications (2). The procedure induces axonotmesis by freezing at -60 °C, while preserving the nerve’s connective structures and allowing subsequent regeneration (3). Objective: To evaluate the effectiveness of percutaneous cryoablation in patients with lumbar facet syndrome treated at Centro Médico ISSEMyM Ecatepec between February 2024 and February 2025. Material and Methods: Observational, prospective study including 15 patients diagnosed with lumbar facet syndrome. The sample was selected by convenience and was non-probabilistic. Results: In the immediate postoperative period, 100% of patients reported pain improvement. Of the ten patients with severe functional limitation (Oswestry Disability Index), four reported no functional limitation, six reported minimal limitation, and five reported moderate limitation at the first postoperative month. Conclusion: Percutaneous cryoablation is an effective and safe option for the treatment of lumbar facet syndrome in patients who do not respond to conservative management. Despite the small sample size, the results are promising and highlight the need for studies with larger populations and longer follow-up.

## P0739: Combined percutaneous and mini-open decompression and fixation for pars defect using a multiaxial facet screw system: technique and case series

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Sukanya Chebrolu

1

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

1

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Pars interarticularis defects can progress to mechanical back pain and spondylolisthesis. Open fusion remains a standard option but carries morbidity from muscle detachment and wider exposure. A combined mini open decompression and percutaneous fixation strategy may achieve stabilization with less soft tissue disruption. This report describes the technique using a multiaxial facet screw system and presents clinical and radiographic outcomes. Materials and Methods: A retrospective review identified four consecutive patients with imaging confirmed pars defects and persistent symptoms after nonoperative management. All underwent targeted neural decompression through a limited midline incision followed by percutaneous placement of multiaxial facet screws across the pars with direct grafting. Two patients received additional interbody fusion and pedicle screw fixation to augment stability, and one patient received a single screw due to bone quality. Pain was measured with Visual Analogue Scale at baseline and at follow up. Fusion was assessed with serial radiographs and computed tomography. Adverse events and reoperations were recorded. Results: The cohort included three males and one female (n =4) with a mean age of thirty-six years and a mean body mass index of 30.2. No intraoperative complications occurred. Mean Visual Analogue Scale improved from 6.5 preoperatively to 3.0 at six months (p = 0.032), with further improvement at one year. Fusion was confirmed in all cases. No implant failures or pseudarthrosis were observed. One patient sustained trauma at one year and required revision. Another experienced trauma without the need for additional surgery. Follow up imaging showed progressive healing and maintained stability. Conclusion: Combined mini open decompression and percutaneous multiaxial facet screw fixation was feasible and achieved pain reduction and fusion in all cases in this small series. The technique may offer a lower morbidity alternative to open fusion for selected patients with pars defects and warrants evaluation in larger cohorts with longer follow up.

## ID: AGS26/2697

## P0740: Surgical outcomes and complications for patients undergoing vertebral body tethering with a 5-8 year follow-up

Rachelle Imbeault

1

, Jesse Shen

2

, Marie Beauséjour

3

, Marjolaine Roy-Beaudry

1

, Isabelle Turgeon

1

, Soraya Barchi

1

, Stefan Parent

1

1

CHU Sainte-Justine, Montreal, Canada

2

CHUM, Montreal, Canada

3

Université de Sherbrooke, Longueuil, Canada

Introduction: VBT aims to gradually correct scoliosis, using patients’ growth, while preserving spine motion. We report outcomes and surgical complications at a minimum of 5 years (range 5-8 years) post VBT in skeletally immature patients. Our hypothesis is that vertebral body tethering (VBT) has acceptable complication rates while maintaining satisfactory curve correction at a minimum follow-up of 5 years. Tether breakages, depending on the timing of their occurrence, do not exert a significant impact on the postoperative curve progression. Material and Methods: In this prospective single center cohort study we reviewed 74 idiopathic scoliosis patients who underwent VBT, with a minimum of five-year follow-up. Patients had preoperative, first erect, one-year, two-year and five-year X-rays. Postoperative data at each of these visits were analyzed. Interval increases of ≥ 5° in coronal curve angle from the smallest postoperative measure prompted segmental interscrew angle measurements to detect tether breakage, suspected with a ≥ 5° increase. Results: All 74 patients were skeletally immature, mean age at surgery is 11.8 ± 1.3 years and the mean follow-up time is 63.4 ± 8.4 months. VBT was performed on an average of 7.4 vertebral levels. Instrumented Cobb angle was 48.7° ± 9.2° preop, 17.1° ± 12.3° at the 2-year postop and 25.7° ± 14.0° at the 5-year postop. Unplanned Return to the Operating Room (UPROR) occurred in 12 patients (16,2%). Four patients needed either partial or complete release of the tether due to overcorrection, 2 patients needed revision of VBT due to curve progression, 4 patients required posterior spinal fusion (PSF) due to curve progression. One patient needed revision of VBT followed by PSF. Two patients developed a small dural tear recognized postoperatively and treated with a blood patch. Forty-nine patients (66%) had a suspected broken tether at the last follow up. The mean time of the 1st tether breakage is 38.1 ± 15 months. Without UPROR, 66% (49/74 pts) had a curve < 40° at a minimum of 5 years post-op after a single surgical event. Conclusion: In our cohort 66% (49 patients) had a suspected radiological tether break after 5 years and 7% (5 patients) required PSF. VBT offers a significant correction in the coronal and transverse planes postoperatively with a reoperation rate of 16.2%.

## P0741: In-vitro investigation and the effect of endoscopes on spinal epidural pressure changes with a dynamic irrigation pump

Metehan Öztürk

Istanbul University, Faculty of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

5

RIWOspine GmbH, Knittlingen, Germany

Introduction: Over the past few decades, full-endoscopic spinal surgery has become an increasingly utilized technique with expanding clinical indications. With its growing prevalence, unique complications have been reported that may be related to elevated epidural pressure, including headaches, neck pain, seizures, intracranial hemorrhage, and retinal hemorrhage. During full-endoscopic procedures, continuous irrigation with pressurized isotonic saline is employed to maintain a clear surgical field. However, this irrigation may induce pressure changes in the epidural space. The present study aimed to measure these potential pressure alterations and to investigate the influence of different endoscope designs and irrigation modes of dynamic irrigation pumps on pressure changes at various spinal epidural levels. Material and Methods: Two cryopreserved cadavers were used in this experimental study. Measurement needles were inserted into predetermined epidural sites along the spine. Continuous irrigation was performed under three different pump settings using two endoscopes with distinct designs. The pressure conditions examined included Arthro mode-low pressure, Arthro mode-medium pressure, and Spine mode. Pressure values were measured with PeakTech P 5150 differential pressure meters and simultaneously transferred to a laptop via USB connection. All data were exported to Microsoft Excel for detailed analysis. Results: When comparing the three irrigation settings, the lowest pressure levels were observed under Spine mode, ranging between 37-42 mmHg, which were distinctly lower than those recorded under Arthro mode settings. Certain surgical maneuvers-such as introducing an instrument through the working channel or temporarily blocking the endoscope outflow-were found to cause rapid epidural pressure fluctuations across different spinal levels. In addition, comparisons between the two endoscopes revealed that Model X consistently demonstrated lower pressure levels than Model Y under Arthro mode-low and medium pressure settings. In Spine mode, both endoscopes produced similar pressure patterns, though Model X showed slightly higher values than Model Y. These variations are likely attributable to differences in endoscope design and the mechanisms of the irrigation pump. Conclusion: Epidural pressure alterations induced by continuous irrigation during full-endoscopic spinal surgery may contribute to potentially serious complications. Surgical maneuvers, endoscope design, and irrigation pump settings play a pivotal role in determining the magnitude of these pressure changes. Awareness of these contributing factors and appropriate preventive measures are essential to ensuring patient safety and minimizing the risk of pressure-related adverse events during full-endoscopic procedures.

## ID: AGS26/2733

## P0742: Unilateral biportal endoscopic resection of intradural extramedullary tumors: case series and systematic review

Rodolfo Guerrero-Perez

1

, Daniel Oswaldo Davila
, Adrian Sánchez-Gómez

2

1

Hospital San José Celaya, Guanajuato, Mexico

2

ISSSTE, Hospital Regional 1ro de Octubre, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Endoscopic spinal surgery has transformed the management of spinal disorders by providing minimally invasive alternatives with reduced morbidity compared to open approaches. Among these, unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) has emerged as a versatile technique, combining the visualization benefits of endoscopy with the surgical freedom of microsurgery. Originally developed for degenerative lumbar conditions, UBE allows independent movement of the endoscope and instruments through two portals, minimizing tissue trauma and optimizing outcomes. While its application in lumbar decompression and fusion is well established, the use of UBE for intradural extramedullary tumors, such as meningiomas, has not been reported. This study presents the first clinical series of intradural extramedullary tumor resections using UBE and a systematic review of the literature. Material and Methods: We conducted a case series of three patients undergoing UBE-assisted resection of intradural extramedullary tumors. Patient selection was based on clinical and radiological findings consistent with spinal cord compression symptoms. The surgical technique employed a unilateral biportal posterior approach with continuous saline irrigation, as described in standard UBE protocols. Postoperative outcomes were assessed clinically and radiologically. In parallel, a systematic review was performed in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. Searches were conducted in PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science using combinations of the terms: “unilateral biportal endoscopy,” “UBE,” “spinal tumor,” “intradural extramedullary,” and “meningioma.” Studies were included if they reported clinical application of UBE for tumor resections. No restrictions were applied regarding year or language of publication. Results: Case 1: A patient with a history of neurofibromatosis presented with left C7 paresis (4/5), cervicalgia, and C7-predominant paresthesias. MRI revealed a cervical intradural extramedullary lesion with dural tail, suggestive of meningioma. UBE-assisted resection was performed without complications, and the patient was discharged after 24 hours with improved neurological function. Case 2: A thoracic lesion at T2-T3 presented with ataxia, distal paresthesias from T4 caudally, paresis (L2-S1, 4-/5), and dysesthesia evolving over three months. UBE resection was uneventful, with immediate postoperative relief of dysesthesia and progressive motor recovery within 72 hours. Case 3: A 1 × 1 cm thoracic lesion at T10, radiologically consistent with meningioma, was associated with girdle-like paresthesias. UBE-assisted removal achieved complete resection with symptomatic improvement postoperatively. Systematic Review: Literature search identified no prior reports of cervical or thoracic intradural extramedullary tumor resections performed with UBE. Existing studies focused predominantly on degenerative conditions, stenosis, and lumbar pathology. Our findings therefore represent the first reported application of UBE for this indication. Conclusions: This report constitutes the first documented series of intradural extramedullary tumor resections using UBE. The favorable outcomes observed-including rapid recovery, minimal complications, and short hospitalization-highlight the potential of UBE as a minimally invasive alternative for select spinal tumors. By replicating the surgical freedom of open microsurgery while minimizing tissue trauma, UBE may redefine management strategies for these lesions. Our cases establish proof-of-concept and lay the groundwork for further clinical studies, potentially expanding the role of UBE beyond degenerative conditions into oncological applications. These findings represent a milestone in minimally invasive spinal oncology and suggest that UBE could become a valuable addition to the neurosurgical armamentarium.

## ID: AGS26/2744

## P0743: Uniportal versus biportal endoscopic surgery for lumbar pathologies: an umbrella review

Rafael Lindi Sugino

1

, Jason Pittman

2

, Samuel Cho

3

, Carl Johanssen

4

, Weby Mizael

5

, Jullyene Pokorny

6

, Gabriel Pokorny

6

1

Einstein Hospital Israelita, São Paulo, Brazil

2

Harvard, Massachusetts, United States

3

Mt. Sinai, New York, United States

4

Oslo university, Oslo, Norway

5

Weby Mizael, São Paulo, Brazil

6

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Endoscopic lumbar surgery can be performed through either a single working portal (uniportal) or two separate portals (biportal), yet it remains unclear which approach offers superior operative efficiency and safety. Conflicting findings across recent systematic reviews warrant an umbrella review to consolidate the highest-level evidence and guide surgical decision-making. Methods: We performed an umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses comparing unilateral biportal with uniportal full-endoscopic lumbar surgery in adults with spinal stenosis or disc herniation. MEDLINE, Embase, Web of Science, Scopus, the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, Epistemonikos and LILACS were searched from inception to 1 September 2025 without language or publication-status limits; PROSPERO and reference lists were also screened. Two reviewers independently selected studies, extracted pooled effect estimates for operative time, intra-operative blood loss and overall complications, and assessed quality with AMSTAR-2. Study overlap was quantified with the Corrected Covered Area. Log-transformed pooled effects were synthesised using a Hartung-Knapp random-effects model, providing 95% prediction intervals, I2 and small-study bias tests (Egger or Peters). Analyses were repeated after de-duplicating primary studies, and certainty of evidence was graded with GRADE. Results: Six eligible meta-analyses contributed 763 biportal and 654 uniportal cases (five on lumbar spinal stenosis, one on disc herniation). For stenosis, two high-quality reviews showed a mean operative-time saving of 10-16 minutes with biportal surgery, whereas a smaller review found no difference. In disc-herniation surgery, biportal techniques were 17 minutes slower on average. Differences in blood loss were clinically trivial, ranging from a 3 mL reduction to a 10 mL increase. No review demonstrated a statistically robust difference in overall complications (pooled odds-ratio range 0.60-1.38; representative estimate 0.68, 95 % CI 0.45-1.01). Heterogeneity was low to moderate (I2 ≤ 18 %), and evidence certainty was graded moderate due to inconsistency in operative-time findings. Conclusion: Current evidence, from six meta-analyses pooling more than 1 400 cases, suggests that biportal endoscopic surgery offers a modest operative-time advantage over uniportal techniques for lumbar spinal stenosis, but this benefit is not consistent across pathologies and disappears in disc-herniation cohorts. Differences in intra-operative blood loss are clinically negligible, and complication rates do not differ meaningfully between approaches. Overall certainty is moderate, limited chiefly by inconsistency in time outcomes. Well-designed primary trials with uniform outcome reporting are still needed to clarify whether the small efficiency gains of biportal surgery translate into faster recovery, reduced costs or improved long-term function.

## ID: AGS26/2748

## P0744: Better alignment, higher risks: open TLIF compared to minimally invasive TLIF

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Gersham Rainone

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Jay I. Kumar

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, University of South Florida, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) is widely used to treat degenerative lumbar spine disorders. Minimally invasive (MI) techniques were introduced to reduce perioperative morbidity, but their impact on radiographic correction compared to open TLIF remains debated. This study compared perioperative safety, radiographic outcomes, and long-term complications of single level MI versus open TLIF. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 62 patients who underwent single level TLIF between 2021 and 2024 at a tertiary academic medical center (32 open and 30 MI). Baseline demographics and surgical indications were comparable, although spondylolisthesis was more frequent in the MI group (86.7 percent versus 56.2 percent, p=0.008). Clinical, radiographic, and perioperative data including operative time, estimated blood loss (EBL), hospital stay, complications, rates of adjacent segment disease (ASD), and extent of sagittal alignment correction were analyzed with parametric and nonparametric tests. Results: MI TLIF demonstrated significant reductions in operative time (138.9 versus 186.2 minutes, p = 0.001), EBL (51.5 versus 185.1 mL, p < 0.001), and total length of stay (4.1 versus 7.7 days, p = 0.036). The rate of drain placement was also lower in the MI group (0 percent versus 75 percent, p < 0.001). Intraoperative durotomy occurred more frequently with open TLIF (18.8% versus 0%, p = 0.013). At follow up, ASD was significantly more common in the open group (50.0 percent versus 23.3 percent, p = 0.03). Radiographically, open TLIF achieved greater correction of both global lordosis (4.9 degrees versus 0.7 degrees, p = 0.047) and segmental lordosis (4.9 degrees versus 2.5 degrees, p = 0.045). Other complication rates including pseudarthrosis, infection, and reoperation were low and comparable across groups. Functional outcomes and symptom improvement were also similar across groups. Conclusion: MI TLIF offers selective advantages in reducing operative morbidity, shortening hospitalizations, and lowering rates of ASD while maintaining comparable functional outcomes. Open TLIF provides superior restoration of sagittal alignment and may be more favorable when deformity correction is the primary goal. Surgical approach should be tailored to the patient’s pathology and clinical objectives.

## ID: AGS26/2749

## P0745: Endoscopic treatment of Chiari type-1: systematic review and metanalysis

Rafael Lindi Sugino

1

, Ellano Pontes

2

, Rayan Santos

2

, Dante Picciotti

3

, Weby Mizael

4

, Samuel Cho

5

, Jullyene Pokorny

6

, Gabriel Pokorny

6

1

Einstein Hospital Israelita, São Paulo, Brazil

2

Universidade Federal do Maranhão, Maranhão, Brazil

3

Centro de Estudos do Servicos de Urologia da FMABC, Santo André, Brazil

4

Weby Mizael, São Paulo, Brazil

5

Mt. Sinai, New York, United States

6

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Conventional posterior-fossa decompression with wide suboccipital craniectomy and duraplasty is effective for Chiari malformation type I but is associated with notable morbidity, including cerebrospinal-fluid (CSF) leak, pseudomeningocele and prolonged recovery. Advances in visualization technology have enabled endoscope-assisted and fully endoscopic decompressive techniques that aim to minimize tissue disruption, reduce blood loss and shorten operative time while preserving clinical efficacy. However, published experience remains fragmented and small-scale. A systematic synthesis of peri-operative outcomes is therefore warranted to clarify the current safety profile and technical feasibility of these minimally invasive approaches. Methods: A systematic review was undertaken in accordance with PRISMA-2020 guidelines. MEDLINE (via PubMed), Embase, Web of Science, Scopus and the Cochrane CENTRAL database were searched from inception to 23 September 2025 using the keywords “Chiari I”, “posterior fossa decompression”, “endoscope-assisted”, “fully endoscopic” and related synonyms. Reference lists of included papers were screened manually. Eligible studies were English-language clinical reports (any design) that described endoscope-assisted or fully endoscopic posterior-fossa decompression for symptomatic Chiari I and detailed at least one peri-operative outcome of interest. When multiple publications reported overlapping cohorts, the study with the larger patient number was retained. Two independent reviewers screened titles/abstracts, evaluated full texts, and extracted data on: author, year, sample size, operative time, estimated blood loss, number and nature of complications, need for duraplasty, study design, and type of endoscopic approach. Disagreements were resolved by consensus. Because of small heterogeneous samples, quantitative pooling was limited to simple weighted means for continuous variables and aggregate counts for dichotomous outcomes; no formal meta-analysis or between-group statistical testing was attempted. Risk of bias was appraised with the NIH Study-Quality tools for case series and the JBI checklist for case reports. Results: Four studies met the eligibility criteria, encompassing 41 patients who underwent minimally invasive posterior-fossa decompression for Chiari I. Thirty-eight procedures (93%) used an endoscope-assisted technique and three (7%) a fully endoscopic approach. As for Operative metrics, the weighted mean operative time was 117 min (range of study means 101-192 min). Endoscope-assisted cases averaged 110 min, whereas the two fully endoscopic reports required a mean of 192 min. Intra-operative blood loss, documented for the endoscope-assisted cohorts only, yielded a weighted mean of 56 mL (study means 30-72 mL); both fully endoscopic papers described negligible or unquantified loss. When analyzing Complications, A total of 7 procedure-related events (17%) were reported, all within the largest endoscope-assisted series and confined to dural tears (n = 4) or inadequate decompressions requiring secondary intervention (n = 3). No neurological deficits, infections, or mortality occurred. The fully endoscopic cases were complication-free. As for the use of Duraplasty, Routine duraplasty was avoided in 40 of 41 patients (98%); one illustrative fully endoscopic case incorporated a planned patch graft, while grafting in the endoscope-assisted series was restricted to repair of inadvertent dural tears. Conclusion: Current evidence, driven by a single 23-patient cohort, indicates that endoscope-assisted decompression achieves short operative times, minimal blood loss, and low morbidity, although minor dural complications persist. Fully endoscopic techniques appear feasible and similarly blood-sparing but are based on only three reported cases and demand longer operating times; larger prospective series are required to validate these preliminary findings.

## ID: AGS26/2784

## P0746: Modified transfacet approach in full endoscopic lumbar interbody fusion: a one-year outcome report

Khananut Jaruwanneechai

1

, Abhirat Suebsing

1

, Akarawit Asawasaksakul

2

, Warayos Trathitephun

3

, Boonserm Pakdeenit

4

, Siravich Suvithayasiri

5 6

1

Department of Orthopedics, Warinchamrab Hospital, Ubon Ratchathani, Thailand

2

Spine Clinic, Department of Orthopedics, Ramkhamhaeng Hospital,, Bangkok, Thailand

3

Department of Orthopedics, Chulabhorn Hospital, Chulabhorn Royal Academy, Bangkok, Thailand

4

Orthopedic Center, Bangkok Hospital Pattaya, Pattaya, Thailand

5

Bone and Joint Excellence Center, Thonburi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

6

Department of Orthopedics, Chulabhorn Hospital, Chulabhorn Royal Academy, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Full-endoscopic lumbar interbody fusion (FE-LIF) represents a significant evolution in minimally invasive spine surgery, aiming to reduce approach-related morbidity. Our novel technique, the modified transfacet approach facilitates direct decompression and cage insertion under continuous endoscopic visualization. However, there remains a need for robust data on its mid-to-long term efficacy. This study aimed to evaluate the one-year clinical and radiological outcomes of patients underwent single-level FE-LIF using this specific technique for degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study included 41 patients who underwent single-level FE-LIF at a single institution between January 2023 and June 2024. Key demographic and perioperative data, including operative time, estimated blood loss (EBL), and length of stay (LOS), were collected. Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for back and leg pain and the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) at preoperative, 1-day, 1-month, 6-month, and 12-month follow-ups. The primary radiological outcome, fusion status, was evaluated at 12 months using CT scans and graded according to the Bridwell classification, with grades I and II considered solid fusion. Results: The cohort had a mean age of 61.9 years and a mean BMI of 26.2 kg/m2. The mean operative time was 143.9 ± 12.0 minutes, with an average EBL of 91.3 ± 22.6 mL and a mean LOS of 3.1 ± 0.8 days. Significant improvements were observed across all clinical metrics (p < 0.001). VAS back pain decreased from 7.78 ± 0.65 preoperatively to 1.07 ± 0.57 at one year, while VAS leg pain decreased from 7.80 ± 0.84 to 0.85 ± 0.53. Functional outcomes showed marked improvement, with the ODI score decreasing from 67.61 ± 2.87% to 10.29 ± 2.08% at the final follow-up. At one year, the CT-confirmed fusion rate (Bridwell Grade I or II) was 95.1% (39 of 41 patients). Conclusion: In this cohort, FE-LIF via the modified transfacet approach provided significant and sustained improvements in pain and functional disability at one year. Consistent with a high radiographic fusion rate and favorable perioperative metrics, we can conclude that the technique is safe and effective for alternative minimal invasive surgery in selected patients with degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis.

## ID: AGS26/2788

## P0747: Would you recommend your spine surgery? Postoperative satisfaction versus reality

Hiroyuki Nakarai

1

, So Kato

1

, Yasushi Oshima

1

1

The University of Tokyo Hospital, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Patient satisfaction is a key driver of surgical recommendation, yet little is known about which spine surgeries patients actually recommend, or whether satisfaction always translates into recommendation. This study aimed to identify patient and surgical factors associated with recommending surgery to others, as well as discrepancies between satisfaction and recommendation. Material and Methods: We conducted a multicenter retrospective cohort study using registry data from 13 tertiary referral hospitals (2017-2021). Adults (> 18 years) who underwent surgery for degenerative or deformity conditions were included; revision, trauma, and tumor cases were excluded. Demographics, surgical details, and EuroQOL-5D (EQ-5D) scores were collected. Procedures were grouped into eight categories: cervical deformity (CD), thoracolumbar deformity (TLD), posterior cervical decompression (PCD), anterior cervical fusion (ACF), posterior cervical fusion (PCF), thoracolumbar disc herniation surgery (TLDH), posterior thoracolumbar decompression (PTLD), and posterior thoracolumbar fusion (PTLF). Combined anteroposterior procedures were categorized as posterior. Satisfaction and recommendation were assessed at 1 year postoperatively using 7-point Likert scales. A discrepancy between satisfaction and recommendation (DSR) was defined as a recommendation score ≥2 points lower than satisfaction. Logistic regression analysis was performed to identify risk factors for DSR, adjusting for age, sex, BMI, smoking history, surgical procedure, interbody fusion, osteotomy, use of endoscopy, and baseline EQ-5D. Results: A total of 7,415 patients (mean age 65 ± 14 years, 60% male) were included: CD (n = 16), TLD (n = 175), PCD (n = 1031), ACF (n = 288), PCF (n = 236), TLDH (n = 1326), PTLD (n = 2576), PTLF (n = 1767). Endoscopy was used in 2689 (36%) cases. Overall, 82% reported satisfaction (≥ “Somewhat satisfied”), and 77% would recommend their surgery (≥ “Somewhat recommend”). Recommendation rates varied by procedure: CD (88%), TLD (59%), PCD (70%), ACF (76%), PCF (69%), TLDH (88%), PTLD (78%), PTLF (74%). DSR rates were: CD (13%), TLD (15%), PCD (7%), ACF (8%), PCF (9%), TLDH (7%), PTLF (11%). Female patients had higher odds of DSR (aOR = 1.7, p < .001), while endoscopic surgery was associated with lower odds (aOR = 0.52, p < .001). Conclusion: Recommendation rates differed substantially across procedures, with TLD and PCF lowest. Female patients were more likely to experience DSR, indicating that reported satisfaction does not always align with willingness to recommend. Endoscopic surgery was linked to reduced DSR. Enhancing patient counseling and minimizing surgical invasiveness may improve patient perception and reduce satisfaction-recommendation discrepancies.

## ID: AGS26/2860

## P0748: Endoscopic versus open lumbar decompression: a propensity-matched national cohort study of 50,000 patients with 90-day follow-up

Harlene Kaur

1

, Rahul Kumar

1

, Rohit Srinivas

2

, Dana Hazem

3

, Swapna Vaja

4

, Nathan Lee

4

1

University of Massachusetts Chan Medical School, Worcester, United States

2

University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, United States

3

Geisel School of Medicine at Dartmouth, Hanover, United States

4

Midwest Orthopaedics at RUSH, Rush University Medical Center, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Endoscopic lumbar decompression has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative to conventional open decompression for degenerative lumbar disease. While prior studies suggest potential benefits in perioperative recovery, large-scale evidence comparing safety outcomes between endoscopic and open approaches remains limited. This study compared 90-day complication rates, reoperation, and healthcare utilization between endoscopic and conventional lumbar decompression using a real-world national database. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective cohort study using the TriNetX Research Network, which aggregates de-identified electronic health records from 105 U.S. healthcare organizations. Adults undergoing lumbar decompression were identified using procedure codes for percutaneous endoscopic lumbar decompression and discectomy (endoscopic cohort) or conventional open decompression (non-endoscopic cohort). Patients with fracture, infection, tumor, deformity, or concurrent alternative decompression codes were excluded. The TriNetX platform applies a 20-year look-back for index events; no patients were excluded by this criterion. Outcomes were assessed from day 1 through day 90 following the index procedure. Primary outcomes were incidental durotomy, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak or pseudomeningocele, and unplanned reoperation. Secondary outcomes included emergency department revisit, inpatient readmission, length of stay, discharge disposition, meningitis, wound disruption, venous thromboembolism, and myocardial infarction. Superficial and deep surgical site infections were not analyzed due to export limitations. Propensity score matching was performed 1:1 using age, sex, race/ethnicity, comorbidities, and surgical indication as covariates. Measures of association are reported as risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals; significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: After matching, 25,242 patients were included in each cohort. Endoscopic decompression demonstrated significantly lower rates of incidental durotomy (0.3% vs 0.5%, RR 0.55, p < 0.001), CSF leak or pseudomeningocele (0.2% vs 0.5%, RR 0.45, p < 0.001), and unplanned reoperation (0.1% vs 1.8%, RR 0.06, p < 0.001) compared with open decompression. Healthcare utilization was slightly higher with endoscopic decompression (7.2% vs 6.8%, RR 1.07, p = 0.041). Meningitis was also more frequent in the endoscopic cohort (0.4% vs 0.3%, RR 1.44, p = 0.018). No significant differences were observed in wound disruption (0.7% vs 0.8%, p = 0.269), deep vein thrombosis (0.8% vs 0.7%, p = 0.193), or myocardial infarction (0.4% vs 0.2%, p = 0.356). Conclusion: In this large matched cohort with 90-day follow-up, endoscopic lumbar decompression was associated with statistically lower rates of incidental durotomy, CSF leak, and unplanned reoperation compared with conventional open decompression. However, the absolute differences were small (e.g., durotomy 0.3% vs 0.5%) and unlikely to be clinically meaningful. Endoscopic cases showed slightly higher healthcare utilization and meningitis, but no increases in systemic complications were observed. Overall, these findings support the safety of endoscopic approaches, though their advantages over open decompression appear modest in magnitude.

## P0749: Clinical and radiological outcome of endoscopic interlaminar decompression for Grade 1 stable degenerative Spondylolisthesis

Karunakaran S

1

, Vijayaraghavan G

1

, Balaji Bashyam

1

1

MGM Healthcare, Chennai, India

Introduction: Traditional Decompression laminectomy for grade 1 degenerative spodylolisthesis can cause postoperative instability due to loss of posterior ligamentum complex. Decompression and fusion is a more morbid procedure resulting in loss of motion segments. Endoscopic interlaminar decompression can preserve the posterior stabilising structures as well the motion segment. This study aims to analyse the clinical and radiological outcome after interlaminar endoscopic decompression for grade 1 degenerative spondylolisthesis. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively studied 26 patients of grade 1 degenerative spodylolisthesis with mean age of 63.3 years ,18 females and 8 males. Listhesis was at L45 in 22 patients, L5S1 in 3 patients and L34 in 1 patient. The average follow up was 1.7 years. All patients w Had pre and post operative VAS and ODI scores. Radiological parameters include slip grade, slip angle, slip percentage, disc height and disc index were measured in lateral radiographs. Results: All patients had significant improvement in VAS and ODI scores postoperatively at immediate ,6 months and 1 year follow up. There was no significant difference in the radiological parameters, grade of slip, slip angle, percentage of slip, disc height and disc index. No patients required fusion till the end of follow up. Conclusion: Interlaminar endoscopic decompression is a safe and effective procedure for grade 1 lumbar spondylolisthesis.

## P0750: Endoscopic versus open lumbar discectomy: a two-year prospective randomized trial

Kacper Domisiewicz

1

, Klaudia Kozlowska

2

, Dariusz Latka

3

, Pawel Kita

1

, Kajetan Latka

1

1

The St. Hedwig's Regional Specialist Hospital, Wodociagowa 4, Poland

2

Department of Biomedical Engineering, Faculty of Fundamental Problems of Technology, Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, Wroclaw, Poland

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Institute of Medical Sciences, University of Opole, Opole, Poland

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation is a leading cause of back and radicular pain, often requiring surgical management when conservative treatment fails. Traditional open discectomy remains the gold standard, yet minimally invasive endoscopic approaches are increasingly employed. The study hypothesis was that endoscopic discectomy provides faster recovery and reduced tissue disruption, albeit with a potentially higher reoperation rate compared with conventional open discectomy. Materials and Methods: This prospective randomized clinical trial enrolled 200 patients with single-level lumbar disc herniation. Patients were assigned to conventional open discectomy (n = 100) or endoscopic discectomy (n = 100). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the lumbar spine was performed preoperatively, immediately postoperatively, and at 1, 3, 6, 12, 18, and 24 months. Clinical outcomes were assessed at each follow-up visit using the visual analogue scale (VAS) for back and leg pain, the Core Outcome Measures Index (COMI), and the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Results: The endoscopic group demonstrated a higher reoperation rate compared with the open group. However, patients undergoing endoscopic discectomy returned to work earlier. Functional outcomes, as measured by COMI and ODI, were comparable between both groups throughout the two-year follow-up. Radiological evaluation revealed fewer postoperative changes in the spinal canal, paraspinal soft tissues, and subcutaneous layers in the endoscopic cohort, and smaller surgical scars were confirmed clinically. Conclusion: Endoscopic discectomy is associated with faster recovery and reduced soft tissue disruption but carries a higher risk of reoperation compared with conventional open discectomy. These findings support the role of endoscopic techniques as a viable alternative to open surgery, particularly in patients prioritizing early return to function.

## ID: AGS26/2925

## P0751: Unilateral biportal endoscopy: reframing its classification within full-endoscopic spine surgery

Vítor Moura Gonçalves

1,2

1
Department of Neurosurgery, Lusíadas Lisboa Hospital, Lisbon, Portugal, 
2
Faculty of Medicine, University of Porto, Porto, Portugal
Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery (ESS) has evolved considerably in recent years. The AOSpine consensus paper standardized nomenclature for working-channel endoscopic procedures, classifying unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) as “endoscopy-assisted.” However, this classification may not fully reflect current evidence and surgical practice, where UBE demonstrates characteristics consistent with full-endoscopic spine surgery (FESS). Material and Methods: We critically reviewed the AOSpine consensus definitions, the etymological and conceptual foundations of “endoscopy,” and recent literature on UBE. Comparative analysis with uniportal working-channel techniques and analogous multiportal endoscopic procedures (arthroscopy, transnasal endoscopy) was performed to assess whether UBE fulfills the defining criteria of FESS. Results: UBE provides direct, continuous, and exclusive endoscopic visualization within a fluid-irrigated environment, using small incisions and high-resolution optics. This fulfills the essential criteria of FESS, distinguishing it from endoscopy-assisted or hybrid approaches that rely on complementary visualization modalities. Clinical studies demonstrate favorable outcomes, ergonomic benefits, cost-efficiency, and a shorter learning curve. Comparable multiportal procedures in other fields are universally recognized as full-endoscopic, reinforcing the conceptual validity of UBE as FESS. Conclusion: UBE fulfills the fundamental requirements of full-endoscopic spine surgery. Reclassifying UBE as FESS would enhance semantic precision, scientific communication, and clinical adoption, aligning terminology with evolving surgical practice and supporting evidence-based research in ESS.

## P0752: Two-year radiological fusion outcomes following biportal endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion using banana-shaped interbody cages

Ja-Yeong Yoon

1

, Sang-bum Kim

1

, Dong-Hwan Kim

1

, Hwan-Jung Kim

2

, Chang-Hwa Hong

3

, Min-gu Jang

4

, Daehee Choi

1

1

Chungnam National University Sejong Hospital, Sejong, South Korea

2

Dajeon Hankook Hosptial, Daejeon, South Korea

3

Soonchunhyang University Cheonan Hospital, Cheonan, South Korea

4

Konyang University College of Medicine, Daejeon, South Korea

Introduction: Biportal endoscopic transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (BESS-TLIF) is an emerging minimally invasive technique. This study aimed to evaluate the two-year radiological fusion outcomes of single-level BESS-TLIF using a specific banana-shaped, porous titanium interbody cage. Material and Methods: This retrospective study reviewed 51 patients who underwent the specified procedure. The primary endpoint was the radiological fusion rate, assessed by computed tomography (CT) over 24 months using a three-grade system. Factors influencing fusion, particularly bone graft composition (demineralized bone matrix [DBM] only vs. DBM with I-FACTOR), were also analyzed. Results: The final complete fusion rate at two years was 96.1%. Bony fusion occurred predominantly in the posterior and intracage regions. The only significant factor influencing fusion was the bone graft material. The 'DBM with I-FACTOR' group achieved complete fusion significantly faster than the 'DBM only' group (log-rank test, p < 0.001), with a higher final fusion rate (100% vs. 83.3%, p = 0.045). Conclusion: Single-level BESS-TLIF using a banana-shaped, porous titanium cage provides favorable two-year radiological fusion rates. The selective addition of I-FACTOR as an oste-oinductive supplement can significantly accelerate the time to achieve solid arthrodesis.

## ID: AGS26/2956

## P0753: Lumbar UBE program outcomes: case-mix and early outcomes from a consecutive single-team series

João Magalhães Morais

1

, João Alves

1

, Eduardo Pinto

1

, Ricardo Frada

1

, Artur Teixeira

1

1

ULS Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) is a minimally invasive technique for lumbar degenerative disease that uses two small portals and standard endoscopic instruments to achieve targeted discectomy and decompression with limited muscle trauma. Its growing adoption reflects reproducible visualization, preservation of paraspinal musculature, and the potential for fast mobilization and short hospital stay. Material and Methods: Retrospective audit of consecutive lumbar UBE cases performed by the same surgical team from May 2024 to February 2025. Descriptives are presented as median for continuous variables and proportions for categorical variables. Primary endpoints at ≥ 6-month follow-up were validated patient-reported outcomes (VAS back/leg, modified Macnab) and reoperation at the index level. Secondary endpoints included operative time, estimated blood loss (EBL), 90-day adverse events (dural tear, infection, new neurologic deficit, readmission), conversion to open surgery, time to independent ambulation without aids, length of stay, and early VAS at 3 months. Results: Ninety-one patients were analyzed (median age 58.0 years; 51.6% men). The primary diagnosis was lumbar disc herniation in 56.0% (51/91) and lumbar stenosis in 44.0% (40/91). Among disc herniations, protrusions predominated (92.2%, 47/51), with extrusions 5.9% (3/51) and multiple herniations 2.0% (1/51), corresponding to 51.6%, 3.3%, and 1.1% of the total cohort, respectively. Among stenosis cases, central stenosis comprised 67.5% (27/40), foraminal 30.0% (12/40), and mixed 2.5% (1/40)-i.e., 29.7%, 13.2%, and 1.1% of the overall sample. Early postoperative outcomes were favorable: conversion to open surgery 0%, index-level reoperation 0%, EBL < 30 mL, median modified Macnab 1.2 (1 = excellent), mean VAS at 3 months 2.0, mean time to independent ambulation 14 hours, and 100% discharge ≤ 24 hours. Conclusion: This consecutive UBE series was dominated by protrusive herniations and central stenosis and demonstrated rapid recovery with no conversions, excellent early patient-reported status, and universal 24-hour discharge. Taken together with contemporary evidence, these real-world results support UBE as a safe, efficient option that achieves clinical outcomes comparable to open/microscopic surgery while facilitating early mobilization and streamlined perioperative care.

## ID: AGS26/2963

## P0754: Lumbar corpectomy and fusion through minimal invasive oblique approach for osteoporotic burst fracture under 3D navigation

Shiu-Jau Chen

1

1

MacKay Memorial Hospital, Mackay Medical University, Taipei, Taiwan

Introduction: Compression fractures of the lumbar spine are common conditions in the elderly population. After conservative treatment proves ineffective, some patients may undergo minimally invasive surgery using vertebral augmentation with bone cement. However, in cases where vertebral fragmentation is severe, surgical removal of the fractured vertebra and reconstruction with a mobile artificial vertebra may be necessary. We assess the safety and accuracy of navigation-assisted oblique lumbar surgery, potential challenges, and overall benefits of the procedure. We also aim to evaluate whether combined anterior and posterior fusion can reduce the number of fused segments. Material and Methods: From 2022 to 2024, four patients with L3 vertebral burst fractures underwent navigational-assisted oblique lumbar surgery for vertebra removal and artificial implantation in a hybrid operating room. Simultaneously, patients underwent posterior fusion of the adjacent segments, with two cases utilizing bone cement-enhanced pedicle screws. Under general anesthesia, patients were positioned laterally. Employing a lateral approach that minimizes disruption to the abdominal and lumbar muscles, we, guided by navigation, removed the fragmented L3 vertebra and implanted a titanium expandable artificial vertebra (see Figures). Follow-up lumbar X-ray imaging was conducted at seven days, one month, three months, and six months post-surgery to assess implant stability and bone fusion. Prognosis was evaluated using back pain indices and the ODI functional scale. Results: Two patients experienced significant improvement in both pain Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), along with a notable enhancement in lumbar lordosis angle. However, for the remaining two patients, one exhibited no change, while the other experienced a deterioration in symptoms. The unsatisfactory outcomes were attributed to cage subsidence and screw loosening, prompting revision surgery for one patient due to intractable pain. Conclusion: Lumbar corpectomy performed through an oblique approach under 3D navigation is a secure procedure that contributes to reduced radiation exposure. Nevertheless, cage subsidence remains a prevalent issue, particularly among osteoporotic patients, potentially resulting in surgical failure. Based on our cases, we recommend the use of cement-augmented screws for posterior fixation to address this challenge and enhance the overall efficacy of the procedure.

## ID: AGS26/3031

## P0755: Unilateral parapedicular kyphoplasty technique: a single-center series of 85 cases

Volkan Oğlin

1

, Ferhat Harman

2

, Süleyman Akkaya

3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Yeditepe University, İstanbul, Turkey

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Marmara University, İstanbul, Turkey

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Van Educaiton and Research Hospital,Van, Turkey

Introduction: Kyphoplasty and vertebroplasty are standard minimally invasive options for vertebral compression fractures. The conventional transpedicular approach is effective but technically challenging in patients with narrow or anatomically difficult pedicles. It also requires bilateral pedicle entry to achieve symmetrical cement filling, which increases invasiveness and carries a risk of medial pedicle perforation with cement leakage into the spinal canal. The parapedicular approach has been proposed as an alternative that allows unilateral access to the vertebral body while still providing bilateral cement distribution. This study aimed to assess the feasibility, safety, and outcomes of unilateral parapedicular kyphoplasty through cadaveric dissections and a clinical series. Materials and Methods: Four formalin-fixed cadavers were dissected to evaluate the parapedicular entry trajectory. After anatomical preparation, instruments were advanced via the parapedicular route under fluoroscopic guidance, and positioning was confirmed. For the clinical part, we retrospectively reviewed 85 patients treated with unilateral parapedicular kyphoplasty between 2021 and 2024 at a tertiary center. The cohort included 15 patients with metastatic lesions and 70 with osteoporotic fractures. All procedures were performed under sedation with patients prone. The entry point was located about 6 cm lateral to the midline at the level of the superior endplate. A Jamshidi needle was advanced at a 45° angle toward the pedicle-vertebral junction, allowing access to the vertebral midline. A working cannula was inserted, biopsy specimens obtained, and polymethylmethacrylate cement injected under fluoroscopy. Cement volumes were 3-6 ml in thoracic and 3-8 ml in lumbar vertebrae. Results: Cadaveric dissections confirmed safe access to the vertebral body midline without pedicle violation. In the clinical series, cement distribution was adequate and bilateral in all patients despite unilateral entry. Postoperative visual analog scale (VAS) scores showed significant improvement, reflecting effective pain control. Only one complication occurred: a pneumothorax, which was managed with chest tube insertion, and the patient was discharged on day three. All other patients were discharged on postoperative day one without incident. No symptomatic cement leakage, neurological deficits, or vascular complications were observed. Both metastatic and osteoporotic subgroups benefited from the technique, with favorable outcomes and rapid recovery. Conclusion: Unilateral parapedicular kyphoplasty provides a safe and effective alternative to the traditional transpedicular approach. It reduces the risk of medial pedicle perforation and cement leakage while enabling bilateral cement filling through a single entry. The technique avoids the need for bilateral pedicle access, thereby simplifying the procedure and minimizing morbidity. Furthermore, it is applicable to thoracic vertebrae (except T1-T3, where scapular anatomy may limit access) as well as lumbar levels. This single-center series demonstrates that unilateral parapedicular kyphoplasty is feasible, reproducible, and clinically effective. Larger prospective studies with longer follow-up are warranted to confirm its role in the management of vertebral compression fractures.
Keywords: kyphoplasty, vertebroplasty, parapedicular approach, osteoporosis, vertebral compression fracture

## ID: AGS26/3033

## P0756: Navigating the learning curve in uniportal endoscopic spinal surgery: overcoming complications and strategies for succes

Prashanth Rao

1,2,3

, Wentian Li

1,4

, Gayani Petersingham

1,4

1

Brain and Spine Surgery, Sydney, Australia

2

Norwest Private Hospital, Sydney, Australia

3

Brain and Spine Surgery, UNSW, Sydney, Australia

4

UNSW, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: Uniportal endoscopic spinal surgery offers a minimally invasive approach with potential for enhanced patient outcomes. However, the technical complexity introduces a significant learning curve that can initially affect outcomes. This study evaluates a single surgeon’s transition to endoscopic spine surgery, focusing on outcome optimisation over time. Methods: A retrospective review was conducted of 400 consecutive uniportal endoscopic spine surgeries performed by a single surgeon from October 2019 to May 2025. The data analysed included demographics, procedural details, clinical outcomes including Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire (RMDQ), and EuroQol five-dimension questionnaire (EQ-5D) and complication rates. Patients were stratified into groups of 100 cases to evaluate trends across the learning curve. Results: Of the 400 patients the average age was 53.6 ± 17 years, ratio of male to female was 176:224 and BMI 30 ± 7 kg/m2. Operative time, estimated blood loss and mean hospital length of stay all decreased from the first 100 cases to the final 100 cases. The overall complication rate declined from 15% to 3%, with only one intraoperative complication in the last 100 cases. Interlaminar cases initially had higher complication rates, but these improved significantly over time. Transforaminal cases showed fewer complications throughout. Patient reported outcomes exhibited marked improvement: ODI from 48.2 to 18.7, RMDQ from 14.1 to 5.3, and EQ-5D index scores rose from 0.52 to 0.83. Conclusion: This study demonstrates uniportal endoscopic spinal surgery can be safely and effectively integrated into clinical practice despite a steep learning curve. Improvements in efficiency, complication rates, and patient outcomes underscore the importance of mentorship, iterative technique refinement, and consistent outcome tracking in successful adoption

## ID: AGS26/3099

## P0757: Cardiac safety of antepsoas oblique lumbosacral interbody fusion: incidence and risk factors for perioperative abnormal electrocardiograms in minimally invasive spine surgery

Rehan Khan

1

, Nader El-Hajj

2

, Robert Azario

3

, Daman Dhunna

1

, Rohith Ryali

1

, Matthew Kim

3

, Sofia Khan

4

, Brian Tao

1

, Tony Tannoury

3

, Chadi Tannoury

3

1

Chobanian & Avedisian School of Medicine, Boston University, Boston, United States

2

University of Colorado Anschutz School of Medicine, Aurora, United States

3

Boston Medical Center, Boston, United States

4

State University of New York Downstate Medical Center, Brooklyn, United States

Introduction: Cardiac complications are serious perioperative complications in spine surgery. The minimally invasive antepsoas (MIS-ATP) approach to lumbar fusions involves limited neurovascular mobilization, yet the evidence regarding its associated cardiovascular risks remain unknown. Therefore, the objective of this study is to assess the incidence and risk factors for abnormal perioperative Electrocardiogram (ECG) after MIS-ATP fusion. Material and Methods: A retrospective review of 454 patients who underwent MIS-ATP fusions at a single academic medical center between 2007 and 2020 was conducted. Demographics, comorbidities, preoperative echocardiography (ECHO) data, postoperative ECG findings were collected. Preoperative visible L1-L4 aortoiliac calcification was assessed by three graders. Analysis was performed via t-tests, ANOVA, Chi-square, and Fisher's exact tests. Multivariate logistic regression was conducted to identify independent predictors of abnormal ECG. Results: Common cormorbidities included: preoperative vascular calcification (49.7%), hypertension (HTN) (59.5%), and diabetes (24.4%). Postoperative ECG abnormalities were present in 83 patients (18.3%), most frequently: repolarization changes (5.7%), conduction/bradyarrhythmias (3.7%), and atrial arrhythmias (2.6%). Regression analysis identified advanced age (OR 1.07), HTN (OR 2.10), ECHO abnormalities (OR 2.02), DVT/PE history (OR 7.18), and staged > 3-level procedures as risk factors (OR 3.23) (p < 0.05 for all). Preoperative beta-blocker, statin, and anticoagulant use were protective (OR 0.44, p = 0.020). Conclusion: MIS-ATP was associated with a relatively low rate of perioperative ECG abnormalities despite high prevalence of vascular calcification. Careful risk stratification and perioperative optimization can help maintain MIS-ATP’s strong safety profile in higher-risk populations.

## P0758: Clinical and radiological outcomes of unilateral biportal endoscopic TLIF with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation in grade 1 isthmic spondylolisthesis: a 1-year follow-up study of 16 patients

Yusuf Bayram

1

, Enes Karataş

2

, M. Hamza Budulgan

3

1

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Uskudar Unıversity, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Istanbul Medeniyet University, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Uskudar Unıversity, Faculty of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

Background: Isthmic spondylolisthesis at the lumbosacral junction frequently requires surgical stabilization. While open transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) remains effective, it is associated with extensive soft tissue trauma, blood loss, and prolonged recovery. Unilateral biportal endoscopic (UBE) TLIF combined with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative that may reduce perioperative morbidity while maintaining fusion reliability. Methods: We retrospectively evaluated 16 patients with Grade 1 isthmic spondylolisthesis at L5-S1 who underwent UBE-TLIF with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation between January 2022 and May 2025. A standardized unilateral facetectomy, predominantly via the left-sided approach, was performed in all cases. Operative parameters, perioperative hematologic changes, complications, and radiological outcomes were assessed. Clinical outcomes were measured using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for leg pain and the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) preoperatively and at 3, 6, and 12 months. Results: The mean age was 43 years (range, 28-64). All patients underwent single-level fusion at L5-S1. The mean operative time was 180 minutes, with an average blood loss of 100-200 mL. Hematocrit decreased by 4-5%, and hemoglobin reduction did not exceed 1 g/dL, reflecting the limited hematologic impact of the procedure. All patients were mobilized on the first postoperative day, and the mean length of hospital stay was 2.4 days. Preoperative VAS improved from 7.2 ± 1.0 to 1.6 ± 0.8 at 12 months (p < 0.001), and ODI improved from 64% to 20% (p < 0.001). At 1-year follow-up, no cases of implant loosening, retropulsion, or postoperative instability were observed. Radiographic evaluation demonstrated solid fusion in 14 patients (87.5%), while 2 patients (12.5%) showed incomplete anterior fusion without symptoms or need for revision. No neurological complications occurred. Conclusion: UBE-TLIF with percutaneous pedicle screw fixation, performed with a unilateral facetectomy approach, is a safe and effective minimally invasive technique for Grade 1 isthmic spondylolisthesis at L5-S1. At 1-year follow-up, the procedure provided rapid mobilization, short hospitalization, excellent clinical improvement, stable instrumentation, and high fusion rates, with minimal blood loss and negligible complications. These findings highlight the reliability and safety of endoscopic fusion in appropriately selected patients.

 

## ID: AGS26/3109

## P0759: Intraoperative changes in modified root coronal angle following biportal endoscopic lumbar decompressive foraminotomy and discectomy for lumbar foraminal stenosis

Min-Seok Kang

1

1

Konkuk University Medical Center, Konkuk University School of Medicine, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: The standard treatment for lumbar foraminal stenosis (LFS) is paraspinal decompressive foraminotomy (PDF); however, approximately 20% of patients experience unsatisfactory outcomes. Several prognostic factors have been identified, including the lumbosacral junction, reduced posterior disc height, altered foraminal morphology, and a narrow coronal root angle (CRA). CRA is radiologic parameter defined as the obtuse angle between the exiting and traversing nerve roots on MR or CT coronal images. Previous studies have reported that CRA changes following removal of ventral pathologies. This study aimed to investigate intraoperative changes in the CRA during biportal endoscopic PDF (BE-PDF) and to evaluate whether these changes are related to clinically improvement. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included 92 patients who underwent BE-PDF for moderate to severe LFS. Intraoperatively, the modified coronal root angle (mCRA)-defined as the acute angle between the inferior margin of the exiting nerve root and the upper endplate of the caudal vertebra-was measured immediately after flavectomy (mCRA1) and again after removal of the ventral pathologies (mCRA2). Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) preoperatively and at 1 year postoperatively. Patients were stratified into two groups based on whether the ODI improved by ≥13 points compared with the preoperative value, according to the minimal clinically important difference (MCID). Radiographic parameters, including preoperative lumbar lordotic angle (LLA), disc lordotic angle (DLA), and posterior disc height (PDH), were also measured. The relationships between mCRA and other radiographic parameters were analyzed for statistical significance between the two groups. Results: A total of 92 patients were included in the final analysis. The mean ODI score improved from 59.21 preoperatively to 25.79 at 1 year postoperatively. Patients were categorized into Group A (n = 74), who achieved the MCID, and Group B (n = 18), who did not. The mean mCRA1 was 28.25° in Group A and 30.17° in Group B, with no statistically difference. However, the mean mCRA2 was 32° in Group A and 38.03° in Group B, demonstrating a statistically significant difference (p = 0.01). Furthermore, the intraoperative change in mCRA (mCRA1-mCRA2) was 3.75° in Group A and 7.86° in Group B, which was also significant (p < 0.001). Radiological parameters, including LLA, DLA, and PDH, are comparable between the two groups (p > 0.05). Conclusion: Our results indicate that intraoperative changes of mCRA are significantly related to clinical outcomes, implying its potential utility as an adjunctive marker of decompression adequacy.

## P0760: Full endoscopic cervical discectomy achieves favorable outcomes in acute sequestered disc herniations: a retrospective series of 18 patients

Yusuf Bayram

1

, Enes Karataş

2

, M. Hamza Budulgan

3

1

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Uskudar Unıversity, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Istanbul Medeniyet University, Istanbul, Turkey

3

Uskudar Unıversity, Faculty of Medicine, Istanbul, Turkey

Background: Acute sequestered cervical disc herniations frequently cause disabling radiculopathy or myelopathy. Although anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) is the conventional treatment, fusion-related complications have led to interest in motion-preserving techniques. Full endoscopic cervical discectomy (FECD) is a minimally invasive alternative that may provide comparable efficacy while preserving cervical mobility. Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 18 patients who underwent FECD for acute sequestered cervical disc herniations between January 2022 and June 2024. All patients presented with acute neurological deficits and MRI-proven sequestration. Exclusion criteria were trauma, infection, and neoplastic lesions. All procedures were performed under general anesthesia using a full endoscopic interlaminar system (Richard Wolf GmbH, Knittlingen, Germany). Demographics, affected levels, laterality, operative time, complications, and clinical outcomes were evaluated. Outcomes included Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for arm pain and Neck Disability Index (NDI), assessed preoperatively and at 1, 3, and 6 months postoperatively. Results: The mean age was 37 years (range, 24-52). The most frequently affected level was C6-7 (n = 9), followed by C5-6 (n = 6) and C3-4 (n = 3). Twelve patients (66.7%) had left-sided herniations. The mean operative time was 62 minutes (range, 45-85). Preoperative VAS for arm pain averaged 7.6 ± 1.2, improving to 1.4 ± 0.8 at 6 months (p < 0.001). Mean NDI improved from 68% preoperatively to 18% at 6 months (p < 0.001). Radicular pain completely resolved in all patients, and no recurrent herniations were observed during follow-up. No neurological or systemic complications occurred, and postoperative cervical stability was preserved.

 

 

Conclusion: FECD is a safe and effective motion-preserving technique for acute sequestered cervical disc herniations. In this 18-patient series, the procedure achieved complete resolution of radicular pain, significant functional recovery, and excellent early outcomes without recurrence or major complications. FECD may be considered a strong alternative to fusion surgery, particularly in young and active patients.

## ID: AGS26/3205

## P0761: Radiographic and clinical findings of expansion cages in transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF): a comparative monocentric study

Giacomo Cammarata

1

, Francesco Certo

1

, Angelo Basile

1

, Carmelo Vitaliti

1

, Giuseppe Barbagallo

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Universitary Hospital G. Rodolico-San Marco“, Catania, Italy

Introduction: Minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) has become a widely accepted surgical option for the treatment of lumbar instability, degenerative spondylolisthesis, and recurrent disc herniation. Expandable cages are specifically designed to restore disc height and optimize sagittal alignment while reducing tissue trauma. Despite their increasing use, there is still debate about whether different cage designs or surgical guidance approaches influence outcomes. The present study investigates the clinical, radiological, and proportional alignment results of mini-open TLIF with expandable cages in a consecutive single-center cohort. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 333 patients treated with mini-open TLIF and expandable cages at our institution January 2021 and January 2025. Demographic data, comorbidities, surgical details, and intraoperative parameters were recorded. Clinical assessment included the Numerical Rating Scale (NRS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and SF-36, collected preoperatively, at discharge, and at the last available follow-up. Radiological evaluation was performed with standing X-rays, MRI, and CT scans at baseline, immediately after surgery, and during follow-up. Parameters included sagittal alignment (SS), lumbar lordosis (LL), and evidence of cage subsidence. The Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) score was calculated postoperatively in patients with complete radiological data. Adverse events and complications were systematically documented. Statistical analyses included paired t-tests for repeated measures and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests where appropriate, with significance set at p < 0.05. Results: Clinical outcomes demonstrated marked and statistically significant improvements. Mean NRS decreased from 8.3 ± 1.9 preoperatively to 5.2 ± 3.0 postoperatively and 4.1 ± 3.4 at follow-up (paired t-test, p < 0.001). ODI improved from 0.5 ± 0.2 at baseline to 0.3 ± 0.2 postoperatively (p < 0.001), reflecting substantial functional recovery. SF-36 scores confirmed a consistent improvement in all domains, with a mean value of 0.6 ± 0.3 at follow-up. Radiological analysis showed stable sagittal correction over time. At follow-up, mean SS was 40.4 ± 11.5°, and LL 52.6 ± 12.7°. Cage subsidence was negligible, and no significant differences were observed among the various cage designs. Comparative subgroup analysis did not reveal any clear superiority of a specific cage type regarding clinical scores, radiological correction, or subsidence rates. The GAP score was calculated in 62 patients with complete datasets. When proportions were applied to the entire cohort, approximately 260 patients (78%) were classified as proportioned (GAP 0-2), and 73 patients (22%) as moderately disproportioned (GAP 3-6). Importantly, no cases of severe disproportionality (≥ 7) were detected. This distribution indicates that the majority of patients achieved sagittal alignment proportional to their pelvic parameters. Conclusion: Mini-open TLIF with expandable cages offers significant pain relief, functional recovery, and stable correction of sagittal parameters, with negligible cage subsidence across designs. Differences among cage configurations did not significantly influence outcomes. GAP score analysis confirmed that most patients reached proportional sagittal alignment, supporting the reliability and safety of expandable cages in minimally invasive lumbar fusion. These findings suggest that the procedure ensures both effective clinical improvement and restoration of proportional spinopelvic balance, making expandable cages a valuable option in the management of lumbar instability.

## ID: AGS26/3217

## P0762: Comparative analysis of OLIF versus OLLIF in degenerative disc disease of lumbar spine

Kannan Karthik Kailash

1,2

1

Sri Ramachandra Higher Institute for Research For Research and Education, Indian Institute of Technology Madras India, Chennai, India

2

SRIHER Chennai India, IIT Madras India, Chennai, India

Background: Minimally invasive lumbar interbody fusion techniques, such as Oblique Lumbar Interbody Fusion (OLIF) and Transkambin fusion (TKF), have gained prominence for treating lumbar degenerative diseases. This study aims to compare their functional and radiological outcomes over one year. Methods: A total of 40 patients diagnosed with lumbar degenerative disease were enrolled, with 20 undergoing OLIF and 20 undergoing TKF. Functional outcomes were assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for back and leg pain preoperatively and at one-year follow-up. Radiological outcomes, including disc height restoration, segmental lordosis, and fusion rates, were evaluated through X-ray and CT scans. Perioperative parameters such as operative time, estimated blood loss, and hospital stay were also analyzed. Results: Both OLIF and TKF groups demonstrated significant improvement in ODI and VAS scores at one year (p < 0.05). However, the OLIF group showed superior disc height restoration (p = 0.03) and segmental lordosis improvement (p = 0.02). The fusion rate at one year was comparable between the two groups (OLIF: 90%, TKF: 85%, p = 0.4). The TKF group had a significantly shorter operative time (p = 0.01), reduced intraoperative blood loss (p = 0.02), and a shorter hospital stay (p = 0.03). Complication rates were low in both groups, with transient nerve irritation occurring in two OLIF patients and one TKF patient. Conclusion: Both OLIF and TKF provide effective pain relief and functional improvement at one year. OLIF offers superior radiological outcomes, particularly in restoring disc height and segmental alignment, making it a preferred choice in cases requiring better sagittal realignment. TKF, on the other hand, demonstrates advantages in surgical efficiency, reduced operative time, blood loss, and hospitalization. These findings suggest that the selection between OLIF and TKF should be based on individual patient characteristics, surgical goals, and surgeon expertise.

## ID: AGS26/3225

## P0763: Transmass and paramass approaches for rare cervical lesions with tailored stabilization: a three-case technical report

Yunus Emre Batman

1

, Ferdi Afşin

1

, Mehmet Said Çil

1

, Metehan Eseoğlu

1

, Bahadır Topal

1

, İlhan Yilmaz

1

1

Basaksehir Cam and Sakura City Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Bow-Hunter syndrome (BHS) is a rare form of vertebrobasilar insufficiency (VBI) caused by mechanical compression of the vertebral artery, most often triggered by head rotation. While the majority of cases occur at the craniovertebral junction, postoperative subaxial variants are exceedingly uncommon. We present a patient who developed a novel BHS-like syndrome characterized by cervical deformity and vertical vascular compression following posterior cervical surgery involving facet cage placement and decompression. Case Description: A 50-year-old male with ankylosing spondylitis underwent posterior cervical decompression with bilateral facet cage placement at C4–6 in another hospital for cervical stenosis and spondylolisthesis. Due to persistent symptoms, a revision procedure was performed, during which the right-sided cages were removed. Two weeks later, the patient began experiencing recurrent vertigo and syncopal episodes exclusively when transitioning to a sitting position. On admission, neurological examination revealed tetraparesis (upper extremities 2/5, lower extremities 3/5). CT angiography demonstrated coronal imbalance and listhesis with a dominant right vertebral artery loop at C4–5. Symptom relief was observed with the application of a large Philadelphia cervical collar providing partial traction, further supporting the diagnosis of posture-dependent vertebrobasilar insufficiency. Results: The patient subsequently underwent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) from C4 to C7, with PEEK cages restoring alignment and decompressing the vertebral artery. Postoperatively, all syncopal and vertigo episodes resolved completely. Follow-up CT angiography demonstrated correction of the coronal deformity and elimination of vertebral artery loop compression, while MRI confirmed resolution of foraminal narrowing. Conclusion: This case illustrates a rare postoperative variant of Bow-Hunter syndrome in which vertebral artery compression was induced by cervical deformity following posterior cervical surgery. Unlike classical BHS, symptoms were triggered not by head rotation but by vertical loading during postural change (sitting). Recognition of this unusual mechanism is critical, as timely anterior decompression and fusion achieved both radiographic correction and complete clinical recovery. Surgeons should maintain a high index of suspicion for vertebrobasilar insufficiency in patients—particularly those with underlying rheumatologic disease—who present with unexplained vertigo or syncope after posterior cervical procedures.

## ID: AGS26/3230

## P0764: Two-year outcomes of unilateral biportal endoscopic spine surgery for lumbar disc herniation: a prospective cohort study

Abdus Salam

1

1

Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Lumbar disc herniation (LDH) is a leading cause of radiculopathy and disability. Unilateral Biportal Endoscopy (UBE) has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative to open discectomy, with promising outcomes. However, long-term data from low- and middle-income countries are limited. This study evaluates two-year outcomes of UBE in a tertiary spine center in Pakistan. Material and Methods: A prospective cohort of 100 patients aged 20-60 years with single-level LDH unresponsive to conservative treatment underwent UBE between January 2023 and December 2024. Patients with prior surgery, instability, trauma, or infection were excluded. Outcomes were assessed using the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Visual Analog Scale (VAS), and Modified Macnab criteria at regular intervals up to 2 years. Results: The cohort was 65% male, with a mean age of 41.2 years. Mean ODI improved from 58.6 to 18.4 (p < 0.001). VAS scores for leg and back pain improved from 7.8 and 6.2 to 2.1 and 1.9, respectively (p < 0.001). According to Macnab criteria, 80% had excellent, 12% good, 5% fair, and 3% poor outcomes. Mean hospital stay was 2 days; most resumed work within 6-8 weeks. Complications were minimal and managed conservatively. Conclusion: UBE is a safe, effective, and minimally invasive option for LDH, offering excellent functional recovery and high patient satisfaction, with outcomes aligning with global standards.
