## P0765: Learning curve of biportal endoscopic spine surgery

Bousrih Anis

1

, yousef sbai

1

, Ahmed Ben Hnia

1

, HIchem Abid

1

, Ramzi Bouzidi

1

, Mouadh Nefiss

1

1

Mongi Slim Hospital, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: The benefits of unilateral biportal endoscopy (UBE) for lumbar disc herniation surgery have been demonstrated in numerous studies. However, the main challenge in adopting this innovative technique is the learning curve. The aim of this study was to evaluate the outcomes of a series of lumbar disc herniation cases operated on during our learning phase. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study between October 2023 and March 2025, including patients with lumbar disc herniation treated by UBE. Outcome measures to assess the learning curve were: operative time, Visual Analog Scale (VAS) scores for leg and back pain, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), time to symptom resolution and return to work, and perioperative complications. Results: Our series included 40 patients who underwent biportal endoscopic surgery for lumbar disc herniation, with a mean age of 47.9 years. The L4-L5 level was involved in 64% of cases and L5-S1 in 30%. Two patients had combined L4-L5 and L5-S1 herniations. The herniation was median or paramedian in 33 cases and foraminal or extraforaminal in 7. The approach was interlaminar in 28 cases (15 left, 13 right) and paraspinal in 12. The mean operative time was 140 minutes. Conversion to open surgery was required in 3 cases. Analysis revealed a learning curve cutoff at 22 cases: mean operative time in the early phase (≤22 cases) was 160 minutes compared to 110 minutes in the late phase (subsequent 18 cases). Clinically, VAS leg and back pain scores improved from 7 to 2, and mean ODI decreased from 83% to 32.8%. Complete symptom resolution occurred immediately postoperatively in 16 patients, within 45 days in 9, within 3 months in 5, and within 6 months in 7. Three patients had persistent postoperative symptoms; one underwent repeat endoscopic decompression. Return to work occurred within 3 months. Only one complication was recorded: a dural tear, repaired by open surgery. Conclusion: Biportal endoscopic spine surgery is a rapidly expanding minimally invasive technique with growing indications. Its reduced tissue disruption supports its integration into the routine practice of orthopedic spine surgeons.

## ID: AGS26/3352

## P0766: Minimal invasive and transkambin approach for lumber fusion - Empowering next generation neurosurgeon without mandatory fellowship

Farha Anjum

1

, Nikolay Peev

2

, Umar Farooq

3

1

Sozial Stiffung Bamberg, Bamberg, Germany

2

Belfast Health, World Spinal Column Society, Belfast, United Kingdom

3

Omar Hospital, Lahore, Pakistan

Introduction: Minimally invasive and endoscopic approaches have transformed spinal surgery by reducing muscle trauma, blood loss, and postoperative pain compared with traditional open methods. However, their adoption is often limited by a steep learning curve, raising the question of whether formal subspecialty fellowship training is essential for young neurosurgeons. Methods: This review synthesizes evidence from systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and expert consensus, evaluating operative time, blood loss, hospital stay, fusion rates, pain relief, functional outcomes, and complication profiles. Special attention is given to the learning curve, technical considerations, and the integration of fundamental neurosurgical skills that may enable safe initiation of minimally invasive spinal techniques without prolonged fellowship training. Results: Minimally invasive approaches are now well established in clinical practice, demonstrating outcomes comparable to open surgery but with reduced blood loss, complications, hospital stays, and faster recovery. Although operative times are often longer during the early stages of adoption, this reflects the technical demands of the procedures rather than inherent limitations. With increasing surgical experience, operative times typically shorten and may become comparable to, or even less than, conventional open techniques. A critical determinant of success lies in understanding that every complex surgical procedure is composed of smaller, reproducible technical steps. Surgeons who are already proficient in elements such as nerve decompression, discectomy, or interbody cage placement can leverage these existing skills to accelerate their transition to minimally invasive techniques. By approaching the procedure as a modular sequence of familiar maneuvers, the learning curve is effectively flattened, risk is reduced, and patient safety is maintained. Conclusion: Adopting a stepwise integration strategy in implementation of Minimally invasive techniques in the practice - initially applying parts or steps of the minimally invasive techniques in less complex cases and progressively advancing to more challenging scenarios-has been shown to enhance confidence and outcomes while reducing complication rates. Such structured progression highlights that formal fellowship training, while beneficial, may not always be mandatory if appropriate foundational neurosurgical expertise is present and applied in a step wise fashion while implementing new surgical techniques in the clinical practice.

## ID: AGS26/3365

## P0767: Minimally invasive versus open sacroiliac joint fusion: complications and reoperation rates in a national database analysis

Pirooz Fereydouni

1

, Arthur Cowman

1

, Makenzie Tomihiro

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Ryan Hoang

1

, Alekos Theologis

2

, Yu-po Lee

1

1

The University of California, Irvine, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Orange, United States

2

The University of California, San Francisco, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, San Francisco, United States

Introduction: Minimally invasive sacroiliac joint fusion (MMI) is increasingly used to reduce perioperative morbidity compared with open techniques. Using a national claims database, we compared patient characteristics and outcomes between MMI and open sacroiliac fusion. Materials and Methods: We performed a retrospective cohort study using the PearlDiver Mariner database, identifying adults who underwent SI fusion via CPT 27279 or 27280. Baseline demographics and comorbidities were compared, and complications were assessed within 90 days and up to 10 years postoperatively. Two-sided p-values were used for comparisons. Results: We identified 19,600 patients in the MMI cohort and 10,085 in the Open cohort. Patients in the MMI cohort were slightly older on average (59.57 ± 13.16 vs 58.30 ± 13.12 years, p < 0.001) and had a higher proportion of females (69.9% vs 67.9%, p < 0.001). Several comorbidities were more prevalent in the open cohort, including congestive heart failure (17.1% vs 13.4%, p < 0.001), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (33.3% vs 31.3%, p < 0.001), coronary artery disease (33.1% vs 31.5%, p = 0.007), and hypertension (78.1% vs 76.9%, p = 0.017), while obesity was more common in the minimally invasive cohort (54.0% vs 50.6%, p < 0.001). Within 90 days, the minimally invasive cohort had significantly lower rates of acute kidney injury (1.2% vs 2.5%, p < 0.001), deep vein thrombosis (0.6% vs 1.8%, p < 0.001), hematoma (0.2% vs 0.7%, p < 0.001), pneumonia (1.0% vs 2.1%, p < 0.001), pulmonary embolism (0.1% vs 0.5%, p < 0.001), blood transfusion (0.3% vs 1.5%, p < 0.001), and urinary tract infection (3.4% vs 5.6%, p < 0.001), while cardiac arrest was rare and similar between groups (0.1% vs 0.2%, p = 0.052). At 1 year, reoperation was lower in the minimally invasive cohort (0.2% vs 0.4%, p = 0.005) and wound infection was also lower (1.0% vs 1.9%, p < 0.001). Beyond 1 year, reoperations were rare and not significantly different between cohorts (p ≈ 0.055), whereas late wound infection at 5-10 years remained lower after minimally invasive fusion (0.1% vs 0.5%, p < 0.001). Conclusion: In this large national cohort, MMI SI fusion was associated with lower early complication rates and lower 1-year reoperation and wound infection compared with open SI fusion, despite small differences in baseline characteristics. These findings support the potential safety advantages of minimally invasive SI fusion while underscoring the need to interpret results in the context of observational data and possible confounding.

## ID: AGS26/3388

## P0768: Clinical and radiological outcomes of oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion

Amir Sharif

1,2

, Hamid Reza Abbasi

1

1

Inspired Spine, Burnsville, United States

2

Sana Regio Klinikum Pinneberg, Pinneberg, Germany

Introduction: Oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion (OLLIF) is a novel operation for fusions of the lumbar spine from T12-S1. In OLLIF, the disk is approached from an oblique lateral angle guided by electrophysiological monitoring and biplanar fluoroscopy; the disk space is accessed through Kambin's triangle. Material and Methods: We present perioperative, clinical, patient-reported and radiological outcomes from a series of 303 OLLIF procedures on 568 levels performed by the same surgeon. Results: For a single-level OLLIF, mean surgery time was 56.6 ± 37.7 minutes, with a blood loss of 42.2 ± 31.1 mL, fluoroscopy time of 198.8 ± 87.2 seconds and a hospital stay of 2.2 ± 1.7 days. At the one-year follow-up, 10-point pain scale scores improved from 8.6 ± 1.3 to 4.1 ± 3.0 (p < 0.001). Total Oswestry disability index score improved from 56.6% ± 15.3% to 38.6% ± 21.4% (p < 0.001). At the one-year follow-up, 15 (5%) patients had mild nerve root irritation defined as sensory symptoms and motor weakness better than 4/5. Only one patient had neuropraxia due to weakness (3/5). There was one case (0.3%) of superficial wound infection and one case of bleeding into the psoas major. Reoperation within one year was performed for 14 (4.7%) patients. Interbody fusion was achieved in 98.7% of levels. Conclusion: While OLLIF has previously been described, this study is the first to present clinical, patient-reported, and radiological outcomes of OLLIF. Review of the literature shows that OLLIF produces perioperative outcomes, complication rates, and fusion rates that compare favorably with similar procedures. We establish that OLLIF is a safe, efficient and efficacious procedure for fusions of the lumbar spine.

## ID: AGS26/3420

## P0769: Quantifying radiation exposure in minimally invasive spinal surgery: a single-surgeon study of minimally invasive surgery-oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-OLLIF) with double C-Arm technique

Amir Sharif

1,2

, Hamid Reza Abbasi

1

1

Inspired Spine, Burnsville, United States

2

Sana Regio Klinikum Pinneberg, Pinneberg, Germany

Introduction: In the advancement of spinal surgery, minimally invasive techniques such as the oblique lateral lumbar interbody fusion (OLLIF) have emerged, offering improved clinical outcomes and the use of dual C-Arm intraoperative imaging. Despite the benefits, the radiation exposure to surgeons and staff from this dual-source imaging has not been thoroughly examined. This study aims to quantify the radiation exposure received by surgeons during OLLIF procedures, filling a gap in current research that primarily focuses on single-source imaging. Material and Methods: Over 12 months, radiation doses were measured across 121 surgeries at four locations. Data analysis included average radiation exposure per surgery and variations across surgical sites. Results: The findings showed an average radiation emission of 198.78 mGy per OLLIF surgery, with the surgeon receiving approximately 0.06 mSv per surgery. Cumulative doses for the surgeon were below the safety thresholds set by European and American standards. The significance of these findings lies in their contribution to the understanding of occupational safety in spinal surgery. Conclusion: The results indicate that with proper protective measures, such as lead aprons, the radiation exposure from the OLLIF technique is manageable within occupational limits. This study highlights the importance of monitoring radiation exposure and may influence the development of new guidelines and protective strategies in the field. Future research could expand the cohort of surgeons to validate these findings, develop new protective strategies, and explore radiation exposure in more complex multi-level fusions.

## P0770: Perioperative outcomes of minimally invasive sacroilliac joint fusion using hollow screws through a lateral approach: a single surgeon retrospective cohort study

Amir Sharif

1,2

, Hamid Reza Abbasi

1

1
Inspired Spine, Burnsville, United States, 
2
Sana Regio Klinikum Pinneberg, Pinneberg, Germany
Introduction: Sacroiliac joint (SIJ) pain is a common cause of lower back pain and a significant source of disability in the United States. There is no consensus on the best surgical treatment for SIJ pain that is not responsive to conservative therapy. Minimally invasive fusion of the SIJ using hollow fenestrated screws from a lateral trajectory is a newer technique for SIJ fusion. Material and Methods: This study presents perioperative and patient-reported outcomes amongst 62 patients who underwent SIJ fixation with hollow fenestrated screws. Results: We find that mean disability on the Oswestry disability index improved from 52.2% to 34.9% at one-year post-op. Mean operative time was 34±9 minutes and blood loss was 22 ± 35ml. Only six patients required overnight hospitalization. There were two cases of complications requiring operative intervention. Conclusion: We conclude that SIJ fixation using hollow fenestrated screws is a safe and effective procedure for the fixation of the SIJ. Further investigation is warranted to determine the best surgical treatment for SIJ pain.

## ID: AGS26/3481

## P0771: Minimally invasive SI joint fusion improves pain and disability with low reoperation rates: a meta-analysis of randomized control trials and prospective cohort studies

Alexander Baur

1

, Austin Nguyen

2

, Sree Vemu

2

, B Christoph Meyer

2

, Mark Prasarn

2

1

Center for Research, Liberty University, Lynchburg, United States

2

Houston Methodist Hospital, Houston, United States

Introduction: Low back pain is a leading cause of disability worldwide, and the sacroiliac joint (SIJ) is implicated as a primary pain generator in up to 25% of cases. However, diagnosis of SIJ-mediated pain is difficult due to overlapping symptomatology with lumbar spine and hip pathology, and is often one of exclusion confirmed by diagnostic injections. Minimally invasive SIJ fusion has emerged as a treatment option for patients with confirmed SIJ dysfunction who fail nonoperative care, yet variability remains regarding reported effectiveness and reoperation rates. Objective: To conduct a meta-analysis of high-level prospective evidence studies evaluating changes in Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), SI joint pain scores, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) outcomes, and adverse events (SAE) requiring an additional operation. Methods: A systematic review and meta-analysis were performed according to PRISMA guidelines. Eleven studies met inclusion criteria based on study design, quality of evidence, and reporting of patient-reported outcomes. Demographic data, baseline characteristics, and outcomes were extracted. Meta-analyses were conducted to calculate pooled estimates of VAS and ODI improvements, and pooled reoperation rates. Results: Eleven studies reporting on 1,181 sacroiliac joint fusions were included, with a mean follow-up of 27.8 months. Meta-analysis showed a VAS improvement of 45.5 points (95% CI: 38.6-52.3) and an ODI improvement of 23.3 points (95% CI: 21.0-25.7), both exceeding established MCID thresholds (p < 0.001). The meta-analysis of reoperation rates for implant-related serious adverse events was 3% (95% CI: 2%-4%). Conclusion: Minimally invasive SIJ fusion yields large, clinically meaningful, and reproducible improvements in pain and disability with a low reoperation rate if performed in the appropriate patient population. Outcomes are most favorable when patient selection is rigorous and SIJ dysfunction is accurately identified as the primary pain generator, underscoring the importance of standardized diagnostic pathways in optimizing surgical benefit.

## ID: AGS26/16

## P0772: Navigated robotic spine surgery: a one-year prospective multicenter analysis of complications and revision rates in 688 thoracolumbar fusions

Lindsay Orosz

1

, Jeffrey Gum

2

, Ehsan Jazini

3

, Yusuf Rafiqzad

3

, Rita Roy

1

, Colin Haines

3

, Greg Poulter

4

, Ronald A. Lehman

5

, Christopher Gooed

3

1

The National Spine Health Foundation, Reston, United States

2

Norton Leatherman Spine Center, Louisville, United States

3

Virginia Spine Institute, Reston, United States

4

OrthoIndy, Indianapolis, United States

5

Columbia University Medical Center, New York, United States

Introduction: The merging of computerized preoperative planning, robotic guidance, and real-time 3-dimensional navigation into one advanced enabling technology has the potential to optimize screw placement and improve surgical outcomes. In the Prospective Robotic-Guided Registry of Spine Surgery (PRoGRSS), outcomes were tracked for cases using an integrated robotic-assisted navigation system for spine surgery. The purpose of this study is to determine surgical complication and revision surgery rates at 12 months following thoracolumbar surgery using this platform. Material and Methods: In this prospective multicenter cohort, PRoGRSS includes adults undergoing navigated robotic thoracolumbar surgery from 2020-2024. Cases were enrolled by 6 surgeons at 4 centers spanning 3 US regions. Each surgeon’s experience using navigation and robotics was advanced. Surgical complications and revision surgeries were collected to 12 months and reported as proportions. Results: Of 688 surgeries, the median age was 60 years and 50% were females. There were 5,078 executed screws using robotic guidance with navigation confirmation. Half of cases were staged or single day, with 16% revision cases. Screw placement was split evenly between percutaneous and open approaches, with 35% pelvic fixation, 51% decompressions, 78% pedicle and 22% cortical screws. Intraoperatively, 97% had no adverse events. None of the 3% intraoperative adverse events (2.3% durotomy, 0.5% vascular injury, 0.3% loss of motor signals, and 0.1% small pneumothorax) were robot related. Cumulatively, 9% had surgical complications (0.2% were robot related) and 3% underwent a revision surgery up to 12 months. Conclusion: Experienced users of an integrated navigation and robotic spine platform achieved low complication and revision surgery rates for thoracolumbar spine surgery. Our real-world data found 3% intraoperative adverse events (0% robot related), 9% postoperative surgical complications (0.2% robot related), and 3% revision surgeries up to 12 months. By improving implant accuracy and spinal alignment with computerized planning and navigated robotic guidance, thoracolumbar fusion patients have the potential to achieve successful outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/17

## P0773: One-year fusion rates following navigated robotic-guided thoracolumbar spine surgery

Lindsay Orosz

1

, Jeffrey Gum

2

, Ehsan Jazini

3

, Yusuf Rafiqzad

3

, Rita Roy

1

, Colin Haines

3

, Greg Poulter

4

, Ronald A. Lehman

5

, Christopher Gooed

3

1

The National Spine Health Foundation, Reston, United States

2

Norton Leatherman Spine Center, Louisville, United States

3

Virginia Spine Institute, Reston, United States

4

OrthoIndy, Indianapolis, United States

5

Columbia University Medical Center, New York, United States

Introduction: The merging of computerized preoperative planning, robotic guidance, and real-time 3-dimensional navigation into one advanced enabling technology has the potential to optimize implant placement, spinal alignment, and improve surgical outcomes in thoracolumbar fusions. In the Prospective Robotic-Guided Registry of Spine Surgery (PRoGRSS), outcomes were tracked for cases using an integrated robotic-assisted navigation system for spine surgery. The purpose of this study is to determine radiographic fusion rates at 12 months following thoracolumbar surgery using this platform. Material and Methods: In this prospective multicenter cohort study, PRoGRSS includes adults undergoing navigated robotic thoracolumbar surgery from 2020-2024. Cases were prospectively enrolled by 6 surgeons at 4 centers spanning 3 US regions. Each surgeon’s prior experience using navigation and robotics was advanced. Radiographs were collected at 12 months as part of a pre-specified plan for analysis. Two of the sites collected dynamic radiographs at 12 months and fusion was analyzed by a third party using validated motion detection software. Fusion was defined as < 2° of angular motion. Results: Of a subgroup of 143 cases from 2 sites with 1 year follow up, 692 screws (83% pedicle, 17% cortical) were implanted using navigated robotic guidance. The posterior approach was 77% percutaneous and 23% were open with a midline incision. Of these, 118 cases with dynamic radiographs were available for fusion analysis at 12 months, including 234 individual levels of fusion. In this cohort, the average age was 54 years, 53% were females, 13% were nicotine users, 55% were obese, 95% had a CCI < 2, and 44% had a primary diagnosis of spondylolisthesis. Of 234 levels, 98% had < 2° of angular motion and were considered to be fused, whereas 2% were not fused at 12 months. Conclusion: Experienced users of an integrated navigation and robotic spine platform achieve high fusion rates 1 year following thoracolumbar spine surgery. Although achieving fusion depends on several factors, improving implant accuracy and spinal alignment with computerized planning and navigated robotic guidance may improve the success of spinal fusion and contribute to higher fusion rates.

## ID: AGS26/64

## P0774: Enhancing the success rate of percutaneous pedicle fixation: geometric analysis of needle angles and lengths with the introduction of a flat planning grid system

Artem Ilgeldiev

1

, Victoria Yushenko

1

1

Neurosurgery, Germany

Introduction: Precise needle placement is a critical requirement in percutaneous pedicle screw fixation, directly influencing screw trajectory, bone purchase, and overall surgical safety. However, achieving accurate placement can be particularly challenging for trainees and early-career surgeons, especially in anatomically complex cases. Misjudgment of insertion angles or trajectory length may result in suboptimal fixation or cortical breach. To address these challenges, this study explores the geometric relationship between needle length and insertion angle and introduces a novel, radiographically compatible flat planning grid system aimed at enhancing intraoperative accuracy and standardization. Material and Methods: A trigonometric model was developed to simulate horizontal reach based on three commonly used needle lengths: 80 mm, 120 mm, and 140 mm. Calculations were made across a full angular spectrum ranging from 5° to 85°, generating a geometric dataset that illustrates how angle and length combinations influence the needle’s horizontal advancement. Based on these calculations, a two-dimensional flat grid system was designed for overlay integration into standard fluoroscopic X-ray views. The grid system provides reference lines corresponding to known angle-length combinations, allowing surgeons to visually estimate and adjust trajectory in real-time during percutaneous screw placement. Results: Key findings from the geometric simulation and planning system development include:
• Horizontal Reach: Longer needles (particularly the 140 mm type) substantially increase horizontal reach at shallow angles. For example, at 30°, a 140 mm needle covers a reach of approximately 121 mm compared to 69 mm for an 80 mm needle-an advantage in patients with large soft tissue envelopes or high BMI.
• Angle Optimization: Entry angles less than 45° showed the most predictable and linear horizontal behavior, making them ideal for safe and reproducible needle insertion. These angles also allow better tactile control and reduce the risk of cortical breach, particularly useful in teaching scenarios.
• Visual Planning Tool: The developed flat grid planning system offers simple, real-time intraoperative guidance using known anatomical landmarks and the needle tip as reference. This method reduces variability in initial needle positioning, enhances reproducibility, and may shorten the learning curve for surgical trainees. Conclusion: This study demonstrates the practical value of combining geometric modeling with radiographic visualization to optimize percutaneous pedicle screw placement. The proposed flat planning grid is a low-cost, high-impact tool that enables precise estimation of insertion depth and angle, tailored to the chosen needle length. It supports both surgical accuracy and intraoperative confidence, especially in educational or minimally invasive spine surgery settings. Future validation in cadaveric models and clinical workflows may confirm its benefit in reducing procedural errors and improving training outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/118

## P0775: Robot-assisted positioning for percutaneous endoscopic interlaminar discectomy

Fei Xu

1

, Weishi Li

1

1

Orthopaedic Department, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: During percutaneous endoscopic Interlaminar discectomy (PEID),a range of technologies including medical robotics, visual navigation, and spatial registration have been proposed to expand the application scope and success rate of minimally invasive surgery.The use of robotic technology in surgery is conducive to improving accuracy and reducing risk. This study aims to introduces a precise and efficient targeting method tailored for robot-assisted positioning under C-arm fluoroscopy inPEID. Material and Methods: This study conducted a retrospective analysis of 107 patients with LDH who underwent surgical treatment at our hospital from February 2023 to February 2024(average age: 43.3 ± 13.3 years;61 males and 43 females). The method entails constructing a specialized end-effector capable of simultaneous fluoroscopy calibration and robot-to-image-space registration. The average time of surgical procedure and average number of fluoroscopy exposures were collected. Preoperative and postoperative last follow-up data were collected, including assessment scores of the Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS).Paired t-tests were employed to compare differences in each clinical outcome between the preoperative and follow-up time. Results: These techniques effectively reduce both radiation exposure and operation time. Clinical data reveals that the average time for robot-assisted positioning stands at 2.5 ± 0.7 minutes, with anteroposterior and lateral radiographs demonstrating accuracies of 2.4 ± 2.8 mm and 3.1 ± 3.7 mm, respectively, during robot-assisted positioning. Postoperative VAS scores for back pain and leg pain were significantly lower than preoperative scores (1.2 ± 1.8 vs. 4.1 ± 2.3, 0.9 ± 2.0 vs. 5.9 ± 1.8; p < 0.05, p < 0.05, respectively). Postoperative JOA scores were significantly higher than preoperative scores (26.1 ± 3.5 vs. 14.5 ± 4.9; p < 0.05). Conclusion: Through the evaluation of the system in robot-assisted positioning during PEID, this study substantiates the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method in clinical applications.

## ID: AGS26/152

## P0776: Accuracy of navigated pedicle screw placement using robotic-assisted compared to hand-guided screw technique: a prospective monocentric study

Tommaso Araceli

1

, Nils-Ole Schmidt

1

, Sebastian Siller

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

Introduction: Posterior stabilization with pedicle screw-rod system +/- intervertebral cage for the treatment of degenerative spondylolisthesis (DS) has firmly established itself as a reference technique. Navigated pedicle screw placement (NPSP) represents the standard-of-care, and besides the hand-guided (HG) procedure, this can also be provided by robotic-assistance (RA). This study aims to compare the precision of these two techniques. Material and Methods: We prospectively included all patients undergoing surgical treatment for DS Meyerding Grade I from February-October 2024. Patients were dichotomized into those undergoing NPSP (LoopX®, BrainLab) with HG or RA (Cirq® Robotics, BrainLab) technique. We fused and metrically compared pre- and postsurgical CT images in a navigation software in a blinded fashion. Screw placement accuracy was measured by comparison of the implanted screw position with the planned trajectory by deviation in mm at the entry point in the craniocaudal (DEcc) and mediolateral plane (DEml), deviation at the screw tip in the craniocaudal (DTcc) and mediolateral plane (DTml), and angular deviation of the pedicle screw axis in ° (AD) as well as by Gertzbein Robbins-System (GRS) and statistically analyzed. Results: We analyzed 111 screws in 28 patients (10 women, 18 men, w/m ratio 0.56, median age 61.5, range 19-78 years). 14 patients were treated with the RA technique placing 53 screws, and 14 with the HG technique placing 58 screws overall. No differences were observed in age (p = 0.22), gender (p = 0.13), BMI (p = 0.97), and ASA score (p = 0.88). Between implanted and planned pedicle screws, mean DEml in the RA group was 2.6±1.4mm and 2.1±1.2mm in the HG group, mean DTml in the RA group was 2.7 ± 1.5 mm and 2.6 ± 1.6 mm in the HG group, mean DEcc in the RA group was 2.5 ± 1.1 mm and 2.2 ± 1.1 mm in the HG group, mean DTcc in the RA group was 2.4 ± 1.3 mm and 2.1 ± 1.0 mm in the HG group, and mean AD in the RA group was 3.5 ± 2.2° and 3.4 ± 2.1° in the HG group. Altogether, there was a trend for a slightly higher screw placement inaccuracy in the RA group, with statistical significance only for the mean DEml (p = 0.037). However, considering the GRS classification, in RA group 52 (96%) were Grade A and 2 (4%) Grade B and in HG group 54 (93%) were Grade A and 4 (7%) Grade B with no statistical difference between groups (p = 0.46) meaning an adequate screw positioning in all cases. Conclusion: Accuracy in pedicle screw placement significantly differs only regarding DEml in favor of the HG cohort. However, according to GRS all screws were placed adequately, and no intraoperative revision was required.

## ID: AGS26/232

## P0777: Comparison of navigated expandable vertebral cage with conventional expandable vertebral cage for minimally invasive lumbar/thoracolumbar corpectomy

Masato Tanaka

1

1

Okayama Rosai Hospital, Okayama City, Japan

Introduction: The thoracolumbar burst fracture is one of the most common spinal injuries. If the patient has severe symptoms, corpectomy is indicated. Currently, minimally invasive corpectomy with a navigated expandable vertebral cage is available thanks to spinal surgical technology. The aim of this study is to retrospectively compare clinical and radiographic outcomes of conventional and navigational minimally invasive corpectomy techniques. Material and Methods: We retrospectively evaluated 21 patients who underwent thoracolumbar minimally invasive corpectomy between October 2016 and January 2021. Eleven patients had a navigated expandable cage (group N) and 10 patients had a conventional expandable cage (group C). Mean follow-up period was 31.9 months for group N and 34.7 months for group C, ranging from 12 to 42 months in both groups. Clinical and radiographic outcomes are assessed using values including visual analogue scale (VAS) for back pain and Oswestry disability index (ODI). This data was collected preoperatively and at 6, 12, and 24 months postoperatively. Results: Surgical time and intraoperative blood loss of both groups were not significantly different (234 min vs. 267 min, 656 mL vs. 786 mL). Changes in VAS and ODI were similar in both groups. However, lateral cage mal-position ratio in group N was lower than that of group C (relative risk 1.64, Odds ratio 4.5) and postoperative cage sinking was significantly lower in group N (p = 0.033). Conclusion: Clinical outcomes are not significantly different, but radiographic outcomes of lateral cage mal-position and postoperative cage sinking were significantly lower in the navigation group.

## ID: AGS26/321

## P0778: Comparison of the accuracy between TINAVI robot-assisted and free-hand pedicle screw placement

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: Currently there is no studies comparing the accuracy between TINAVI (China's self-developed robot) robot-assisted pedicle screw placement and free-hand pedicle screw placement in spinal surgery. The aim of this study is to compare the accuracy of screw placement between the two surgical procedures in the treatment of adolescent lumbar spondylolysis. Material and Methods: The clinical data of 65 adolescents with lumbar spondylolysis who underwent surgery in Xi’an Honghui Hospital from January 2021 to January 2023 were analyzed retrospectively, including 20 males and 45 females with an age of 23.8 ±5.3. The distribution of spondylolysis included 2 cases of L3, 13 cases of L4, 50 cases of L5. According to the Meyerding classification of spondylolisthesis degree, there were 52 cases of grade I, 5 cases of grade II and 8 cases without spondylolisthesis. All patients were treated with double segmental pedicle screw reduction and autogenous iliac bone graft. According to different screw placement methods, they were divided into TINAVI orthopaedic robot-assisted screw placement group (robot group, n = 32) and free-hand screw placement group (free-hand group, n = 33). The satisfactory rate of pedicle screw placement and cortical penetration rate were calculated according to Neo standard, and the superior articular process invasion rate of screw was calculated by Babu standard. Results: There was no significant difference in preoperative general data between the two groups (p > 0.05). All patients completed the operation successfully and no serious operative complications occurred. A total of 128 screws were implanted in the robot group, and the rate of the screw placement satisfaction, cortical penetration and articular process invasion were 98.2%, 3.1% and 2.3%, respectively, while 132 screws were implanted in the free-hand group. The rate of screw placement satisfaction, cortical penetration and articular process invasion were 90.9%, 9.1% and 7.6% respectively, and there were significant differences between the two groups (p < 0.05). Conclusion: Compared with free-hand screw placement, TINAVI orthopaedic robot-assisted screw placement can improve the accuracy of pedicle screw placement in adolescents with lumbar spondylolysis.

## ID: AGS26/336

## P0779: Intraoperative 3D imaging with MRI fusion-based navigation: a true GPS in spine surgery

Shubham Kadam

1

, Ayush Sharma

1

1

Dr Babasaheb Ambedkar Memorial Hospital, Mumbai, India

Introduction: Spine surgery requires precise planning and execution to ensure optimal outcomes. This study explores the use of intraoperative 3D imaging with MRI fusion-based navigation (F-Navigation) to enhance accuracy and safety in various spine procedures. Material and Methods: To evaluate the accuracy and safety of F-Navigation in spine surgery, including cervical laminectomy, posterior endoscopic cervical foraminotomy (PECF), spinal tumor excision, and infective thoracolumbar spondylodiscitis. A prospective review of 102 patients undergoing spine surgery with F-Navigation was conducted. A fusion software integrated intraoperative 3D images with preoperative MRI using predetermined protocols. Results: F-Navigation demonstrated high accuracy in identifying both bony and soft tissue anatomy. The system enabled precise localization of pathology in 98% of cases (100/102). F-Navigation facilitated: - Precise decompression of the spinal cord in cervical laminectomy, - Accurate targeting of the C junction in posterior endoscopic foraminotomy, - Precise trajectory into the disc space for biopsy and debridement in spondylodiscitis, - Complete resection of tumor margins while preserving critical neural structures. Conclusion: Intraoperative 3D imaging with MRI fusion-based navigation (F-Navigation) is a valuable tool in spine surgery, enhancing accuracy, safety, and clinical outcomes. Further studies are necessary to evaluate long-term outcomes, compare F-Navigation with other navigation systems, and investigate cost-effectiveness.

## ID: AGS26/378

## P0780: Proximal facet joint violations in augmented reality-guided versus freehand pedicle screw placement

Giuseppe Loggia

1

, Aron Alakmeh

1

, Franziska Altorfer

1

, Fedan Avrumova

1

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Mazda Farshad

2

, Darren R. Lebl

1

1
Hospital for Special Surgery, Balgrist Zurich, New York, United States, 
2
Balgrist, Zurich, Switzerland
Introduction: Augmented reality (AR)-guided navigation has been shown to improve pedicle screw placement accuracy. While proximal facet joint violations (FJVs) is a known risk factor for adjacent segment degeneration, no study to date has directly compared FJV rates between AR-guided and conventional freehand (FH) pedicle screw placement. This study compares the incidence and severity of proximal FJVs between AR-guided and conventional FH pedicle screw placement. The secondary objective was to evaluate potential associations between FJVs and patient-specific or surgical factors. Material and Methods: Adult patients who underwent lumbar or sacral pedicle screw placement using either AR-guided or conventional FH techniques were included. Proximal FJVs were evaluated on postoperative computed tomography (CT) and classified using the modified Park and Shah systems. Demographic and surgical variables were recorded, and generalized linear mixed models were used to analyze associations with FJV occurrence. Results: A total of 412 proximal facet joints in 206 patients were analyzed, with 103 patients in each group. The incidence of proximal FJVs was significantly lower in the AR group (7.8%, n = 15) compared to the FH group (17.0%, n = 36) (p < 0.001). In both cohorts, FJVs occurred mostly at the L5 level and were predominantly unilateral. Among all violations, most of these findings were classified as modified Park grade 1 (n = 29, 7.0%) or grade 2 (n = 21, 5.1%). One grade 3 violation (0.2%) was observed in the FH group. Baseline demographic and operative characteristics were comparable between groups. Conclusion: These findings demonstrate that AR-assisted pedicle screw placement was associated with significantly fewer proximal FJVs compared to the FH technique. The highest violation rate was observed at the L5 level in both groups. Further studies are warranted to confirm these findings across diverse populations and surgical settings.

## ID: AGS26/379

## P0781: The impact of surgeon experience and technique optimization in robotic-assisted sacropelvic screw placement

Giuseppe Loggia

1

, Fedan Avrumova

1

, Marco Burkhard

1

, Michael Kelly

1

, Franziska Altorfer

1

, Joseph Babrowicz III

1

, Allison Lowe

1

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Ek Tsoon Tan

1

, J. Levi Chazen

1

, Darren R. Lebl

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, Balgrist Zurich, New York, United States

Introduction: Robotic-assisted (RA) sacropelvic screw placement (SPSP) uses computed tomography (CT) for planning and registration but exposes patients to ionizing radiation. Three-dimensional (3D) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provides a radiation-free alternative. Given the technical demands of SPSP, surgeon experience may influence efficiency and accuracy. This study evaluates MRI-guided RA SPSP in human cadavers, compares a non-fellowship-trained (NFTS) and a fellowship-trained spine surgeon (FTS), and examines the effect of technique refinements. Material and Methods: Human specimen underwent preoperative 3D MRI of the sacropelvic region. Bilateral S1 pedicle, S1-alar-iliac (AI), and S2AI screws were placed under RA guidance by a NFTS and a FTS. Procedural timing and screw accuracy were recorded and evaluated on post-operative CT using the Gertzbein-Robbins scale (GRS). Results: All 48 planned screws were placed in eight cadaveric specimens without workflow disruptions. Median S1 pedicle screw insertion times were identical for the NFTS and FTS at 2.7 min. S1AI/S2AI screw insertion times were longer for the NFTS (6.8 min, IQR 4.9-7.7) than for the FTS (4.3 min, IQR 3.3-5.9; p = 0.014). Accuracy was high, with 95.8% of screws graded as GRS Grade A. Two GRS Grade E breaches, both due to skiving, occurred early in the NFTS series. Technique refinements, including routine tapping and reduced insertion speed, were introduced after early cases. Operative times declined over successive sessions for both surgeons. Conclusion: 3D MRI-guided RA SPSP achieved submillimetric accuracy and consistent procedural performance across the two different experience levels. Structured workflows and technique refinements improved procedural efficiency and support MRI-based navigation as a radiation-free alternative for complex sacropelvic fixation.

## ID: AGS26/452

## P0782: Analysis of the influence of surgical experience on accuracy of pedicle screw placement: a comparative study using fluoroscopy and 3D-navigation

Jula Gierse

1

, Joel Stöter

1

, Benno Bullert

1

, Paul Grützner

1

, Sven Vetter

1

1

BG Unfallklinik Ludwigshafen, Ludwigshafen, Germany

Introduction: Dorsal instrumentation using a fixateur interne is a frequently applied method for surgical stabilization of thoracic spine instabilities. However, precise placement of pedicle screws proves to be technically demanding. Inaccurate screw placement poses the risk of injury to neurovascular structures. Therefore, a high level of accuracy should be aimed for, which can be increased by using navigated procedures. The influence of surgical experience is another important factor to be considered. The following study compares fluoroscopy with a 3D-navigated procedure on accuracy of thoracic pedicle screw placement regarding the influence of surgical experience. Material and Methods: Four fresh frozen human specimens were dorsally instrumented with pedicle screws from T6-T12. Two specimens were instrumented by an experienced spine surgeon, while the other two were instrumented by a young resident. A 3D-navigation system was used unilaterally, and fluoroscopy was used contralaterally. The same C-arm cone beam CT was used for both procedures. Screw accuracy was evaluated according to the Gertzbein and Robbins classification (Grades A-E). Screws graded A and B (perforations < 2 mm) were considered accurate. Additionally, screws located in the costovertebral joint with a screw-pedicle ratio > 1.0 were considered as accurate (Grade CV). The time required per screw was compared across the systems and surgeons. Results: A total of 56 thoracic pedicle screws were placed. Each surgeon placed 28 screws. The experienced surgeon misplaced one screw achieving an overall accuracy of 96.43%. The accuracy for the experienced surgeon with fluoroscopy was 92.86% and with navigation 100% (p > 0.99). The resident surgeon misplaced three screws, resulting in an overall accuracy of 89.23%. The accuracy for the resident with fluoroscopy was 78.57% and 100% with navigation (p = 0.22). No statistical significance was found in the comparison of the surgeons for navigation (p > 0.99) and fluoroscopy (p = 0.596). The average time per screw needed for the experienced surgeon was 1.78±0.14 minutes using navigation and 2.35 ± 0.32 minutes using fluoroscopy (p = 0.031*). For the resident, the time per screw needed was 3.11 ± 0.2 minutes using navigation and 5.36 ± 0.51 minutes using fluoroscopy (p < 0.001*). Statistical analysis showed significant differences between the surgeons regarding time demand per screw with navigation (p < 0.001*) and fluoroscopy (p < 0.001*). Conclusion: This study indicates that navigation and experience influence the placement of thoracic pedicle screws. The overall high accuracy supports the safety of navigated procedures. Especially the resident surgeon achieved higher accuracy using navigation which indicates to be a helpful technique for this demanding procedure.

## ID: AGS26/695

## P0783: Screw fusion as correction of atlantoaxial and craniocervical instability using O-arm system versus C-arm procedure - A single review of 270 patients

Nikolay Mirchev

1

1

University of Marburg - Campus Fulda, Spine Center, Fulda, Germany

Introduction: A prospective analysis of the surgical treatment and results of 270 patients with benign and malignant craniocervical, atlantoaxial lesions operated during 12 years period (2011-2022) at our department was performed. The aim of the analysis was to assess, the fusion rate and the clinical outcome in patients after correction of craniocervical and atlantoaxial instability, comparing O-Arm System procedures versus C-arm procedure. Material and Methods: We analyzed 175 patients with traumatic lesions, 21 patients with inflammatory lesions, and 74 patients with craniocervical tumors. We performed 312 operations. Because of craniocervical/cervical instability we made dorsal or/and ventral fusion in all of 270 patients. In order to improve screw placement accuracy, we performed intraoperative O-Arm in 238 cases (88.14%). In 32 cases (11.86%) we used C-arm. Results: We analysed the significant operative outcome factors: the operative duration was 2h. in O-Arm operations and 4h in X-ray procedures and the blood loss was 220 ml in O-Arm and 500ml in C-Arm procedures. The mean screw length (prognostic fusion rate factor) in C1/C2/C3 vertebras was 30 mm in O- Arm procedures and 24 mm in C-Arm procedures. The most common operative complications were: CSF leak - in 2 cases, postoperative infection - in 8 cases, screw misplacement - in 3 C-arm procedures, and 2 cases of early operative mortality. The 24-monts follow-up of 230 patients showed good recovery in 74% of patients, moderate disabling-14% of patients, severe disabling -5% of patients, vegetative state - 3% of patients, death -4% of patients with malignant lesions or in bad general condition. Conclusion: Using of O-Arm increase significant operative screw placement accuracy, and preserve intraoperative nerve and vertebral artery injury. The fusion rate in O-arm procedures is 88% and 71% in C-Arm procedures. The clinical outcome in O-arm procedures is better than C-Arm procedures. The complication rate in O-arm procedures was significantly reduced (6,5%) comparing to C-arm procedures (14%). Recently because of the improvement of neuroimaging techniques, surgical techniques and neurointensive care the results of treatment of these lesions are recently better.

## ID: AGS26/714

## P0784: Expanding the role of navigation in minimally invasive spine surgery: case series beyond screw placement

Joaquim Cruz Teixeira

1

, João Levy Melancia

1

, José Hipólito Reis

1

, Diogo Belo

1

1

Hospital CUF Tejo, Lisboa, Portugal

Introduction: Navigation is a tool with proven benefits in spinal surgery. Strong evidence supports its superior accuracy in pedicle screw placement compared to free-hand techniques. However, novel applications of spinal navigation can also enhance minimally invasive procedures beyond instrumentation, improving surgical precision while reducing muscle trauma and blood loss. Material and Methods: We present five cases where spinal navigation was applied to minimally invasive approaches for diverse spinal pathologies. Navigation was used to refine surgical trajectories, minimize approach-related morbidity, and ensure precise targeting of lesions.
Results: • Case 1: A 68-year-old female with prior L4-S1 fusion and recurrent radiculopathy due to foraminal stenosis underwent tubular extraforaminal foraminoplasty.
• Case 2: A 37-year-old male with right T10 radiculopathy caused by a T9-T10 thoracic disc herniation underwent tubular transpedicular microdiscectomy.
• Case 3: A 19-year-old female with left T10 radiculopathy due to a T9-T10 osteoid osteoma underwent en bloc resection via posterior tubular approach.
• Case 4: A 47-year-old male with a dorsal osteoid osteoma underwent complete resection through a navigation-guided minimally invasive approach.
• Case 5: A 50-year-old female with a D9-D10 thoracic disc herniation causing spinal cord compression underwent removal via a tubular minimally invasive approach. All patients experienced symptomatic improvement, with no perioperative complications. Conclusion: Spinal navigation is a powerful tool beyond screw placement. It expands the scope of minimally invasive surgery by enabling accurate and safe approaches across a spectrum of spinal disorders, from degenerative stenosis to thoracic disc herniations and rare oncologic lesions. Broader adoption of navigation in complex minimally invasive procedures may further reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

## P0785: Intraoperative spiral computed tomography and modern navigation system. Role and application possibilities in spine surgery

Evgeny Brinyuk

1

, N.D. Shmelev

1

, N.A. Konovalov

1

, S.V. Kaprovoy

1

, R.A. Onoprienko

1

, B.A. Zakirov

1

, Yu.M. Poluektov

1

, S.V. Ivanov

1

, L.R. Gabrielyan

1

, M.O. Kudymets

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation., Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: Priority during interventions on the spine and spinal cord is patient safety. For this purpose, various intraoperative imaging systems are used. The purpose of the work is to evaluate the effectiveness of using an intraoperative spiral computed tomograph combined with a navigation system, and to describe the features of intraoperative CT visualization and navigation in the surgical treatment of spinal diseases. Material and Methods: In the period from September 2017 to June 2025 more than 4,500 surgical interventions on spinal cord injury, degenerative diseases of the spine, tumors of the spine and spinal cord using intraoperative CT and a navigation system were performed. Results: The use of intraoperative CT increases the effectiveness of surgical intervention, allowing to assess the volume of decompression, extent of tumor resection during surgery, increases the accuracy of implant placement, thereby minimizing the risk of intraoperative complications, increasing patient safety and reducing treatment costs. The use of intraoperative CT and a navigation system allows transcutaneous surgical procedures to be performed in hard-to-reach areas, increasing the accuracy of instrument placement, improving the quality of surgical treatment and patient safety. Conclusion: An undeniable advantage of using intraoperative CT with a navigation system is the ability to control the radicality of removal of bone tumors and tumors with an ossified component “in real time”, as well as the possibility of the most targeted placement of interbody implants and transpedicular screws. This technology significantly simplifies surgical treatment in conditions of complex anatomical landmarks (thin root of the vertebral arch, scoliotic or post-traumatic spinal deformity, osteopenia) and the use of two-dimensional images does not allow adequate visualization of the area of ​​surgical interest, reducing operating time and minimizing radiation exposure to the surgical team and the patient.

## ID: AGS26/1058

## P0786: Novel use of a reference frame of navigation attached to percutaneous pedicle screws: accuracy and radiation profile with 3D C-arm navigation

Nodoka Manabe

1

, Sho Ishiwata

2

, Yohei Kakuta

3

1

East Maebashi Orthopaedic Hospital, Maebashi City, Japan

2

East Maebashi Orthopaedic Hospital, Maebashi, Japan

3

Kiryu Kosei Hospital, Kiryu, Japan

Background: Intraoperative 3D C-arm imaging combined with navigation has improved percutaneous pedicle screw (PPS) placement. Conventionally, reference frames (RF) are fixed to spinous processes or the pelvis, but their stability can be limited. We explored a novel technique of attaching the RF directly to the inserted PPS. Materials & Methods: We analyzed 140 patients who underwent LLIF for lumbar degenerative scoliosis, spondylolisthesis, or spinal stenosis (68 males, mean age 70±10 years, BMI 25±5). Patients were allocated to: (1) C-arm group (fluoroscopy only) and (2) 3D-Navi group (3D C-arm with navigation). Within the 3D-Navi group, outcomes were compared between cases with and without PPS-mounted RF. Evaluated parameters included operative time, blood loss, fluoroscopy time, and perioperative transfusion. Radiation indices (Air Kerma area product, cumulative Air Kerma, number of CT-like scans) and screw placement accuracy (postoperative CT) were assessed. Results: The 3D-Navi group demonstrated significantly reduced fluoroscopy time compared with the C-arm group, with further reductions in the PPS-RF subgroup. Although Air Kerma indices did not differ significantly, the PPS-RF subgroup required fewer and more consistent CT-like scans. PPS insertion accuracy was higher in the 3D-Navi group. Conclusion: Attaching the RF to PPS provides stable fixation independent of spinous or pelvic bone quality, is applicable when multiple adjacent screws are inserted, and adapts better to alignment changes. This technique may enhance PPS accuracy while contributing to reduced occupational radiation exposure.
