## P0787: The use of spinal navigation technologies for spinal fusion in LMICs: a mixed methods analysis from the Tanzania experience

Magalie Cadieux

1

, Romani R. Sabas

2

, Chibuikem Ikwuegbuenyi

3

, Tim Bui

1

, Mariam Mzingi

2

, Boaz Yonah

2

, Marta Garvayo

3

, Sengua Koipapi

2

, Salim Msuya

2

, Rebeka Majige

2

, Julie Woodfield

2

, Zarina Shabhay

2

, Rujvee Patel

1

, Halinder Mangat

3

, Hamisi Shabani

2

, Roger Härtl

3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Washington University School of Medicine in, St. Louis, United States

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania

3

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at New York Presbyterian Hospital, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

Introduction: In high-income countries, spinal navigation systems have been associated with reduced radiation exposure, improved screw placement accuracy and time, fewer wound complications, shorter patient hospital stays, and higher patient satisfaction in the immediate postoperative period. Introducing this technology to low and middle-income countries (LMICs) could replicate these benefits but may have unique challenges. We aimed to understand the experience of neurosurgeons after the introduction of the Brainlab Kick 2.0 Navigation System for spinal instrumentation in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania and to analyze the learning curve of the surgeons with the utilization of the device. Material and Methods: This is an exploratory sequential mixed methods study. Semi-structured face-to-face interviews were conducted with neurosurgeons and trainees at Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute (MOI) in Tanzania during December 2023. Study participation was voluntary. Neurosurgeons were asked to reflect on their experience with the introduction and utilization of Brainlab spine navigation at the institution. Data analysis was performed using an inductive approach. All transcripts were coded using Dedoose v9.0, and specific categories were then formulated. The learning curve was then evaluated by collecting the duration of screw placement and stage of implementation for every Brainlab case from April 2023-December 2024. Results: Eleven neurosurgeons and trainees with between 1 to 20 years of experience were interviewed. Each interview lasted on average thirteen minutes. Three key themes were found: 1) Know-how description with spinal neuronavigation system, 2) Recognition of spinal navigation advantages, 3) Overcoming challenges with healthcare technology. Over an 18-month period of Brainlab navigation use, 56 cases were attempted, with 48 (86%) successful cases. Success rates improved over time (p = 0.048). The average screw placement time was 65 minutes (SD: 25), and the mean operative time was 213 minutes (SD: 53). No major screw misplacement issues were reported. Screw placement time significantly decreased during Phase III (takeoff phase) of the learning curve (p = 0.033). Conclusion: In general, neurosurgeons at MOI had mixed feelings with their experience using the spinal navigation system mainly due to challenges encountered and lack of knowledgeable personnel to help the team. However, the recognized advantage of such technology is a motivation to keep improving their experience. Based on screw placement duration, there was also an improvement in successful technology use over time.

## ID: AGS26/2496

## P0788: Navigation-assisted full-endoscopic spine surgery for complex lumbar pathologies: a preliminary report and case series

Phanurut kaewpornmongkol

1

, Siravich Suvithayasiri

2,3

, Somyot Wunnasinthop

1,3

, Monchai Ruangchainikom

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Department of Orthopedics, Chulabhorn Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

3

Bone and Joint Excellent Center, Thonburi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Full-endoscopic spine surgery (FESS) has become an increasingly accepted minimally invasive alternative to conventional open or mini-open approaches. Its advantages include reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, lower complication rates, and faster functional recovery. Despite these benefits, FESS can be technically demanding, particularly in revision cases, multilevel disease, or when pathology involves deep or anatomically complex regions such as the foraminal or extraforaminal zones. In these situations, precise localization and orientation are crucial for safe and effective decompression. Traditionally, intraoperative fluoroscopy has been used to assist with localization; however, fluoroscopy is limited by two-dimensional imaging, cumulative radiation exposure, and potential inaccuracies in complex cases. Intraoperative CT-based navigation has emerged as a valuable adjunct, providing real-time, three-dimensional (3D) volumetric imaging with high accuracy, reduced radiation to the surgical team, and the ability to update intraoperative images without significantly prolonging operative time. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the feasibility, safety, and outcomes of integrating real-time 3D navigation into FESS for various complex lumbar pathologies, including revision surgeries and foraminal disc herniations. Material and Methods: Five patients with lumbar spine disease were retrospectively reviewed after undergoing FESS assisted by real-time 3D navigation between 2024 to 2025. Navigation data were registered intraoperatively and used to determine trajectory, orientation, and target localization. Surgical procedures included transforaminal endoscopic lumbar decompression (TE-LRD), bilateral revision interlaminar endoscopic lateral recess decompression (IE-LRD) at L5-S1, and minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS-TLIF) at L4-5 combined with lumbar endoscopic unilateral laminotomy for bilateral decompression (LE-ULBD) at L5-S1. Primary outcomes included feasibility of navigation integration, operative time, and intraoperative blood loss. Secondary outcomes included complication rates, postoperative pain (VAS), functional improvement (Oswestry Disability Index, ODI), health-related quality of life (EQ-5D-5L), and subjective patient satisfaction. Representative cases were supplemented with intraoperative images and surgical videos to demonstrate technical workflow and key advantages of navigation-assisted FESS. Results: All five patients underwent successful decompression and/or fusion with significant improvement in clinical symptoms. The cohort included challenging cases, such as revision surgery for recurrent stenosis and foraminal disc herniation at L5-S1. Navigation consistently provided accurate delineation of anatomical landmarks and facilitated safe access to pathological targets, particularly in narrow or scarred surgical corridors. The mean operative time was 207 ± 103.6 minutes, and the mean estimated blood loss was 69 ± 82.5 mL. No intraoperative or postoperative complications were observed. At follow-up, the mean EQ-5D-5L score was 0.95, with one subjective patient satisfaction 90%. Symptom resolution and functional recovery were comparable to conventional endoscopic procedures, but navigation offered added precision and confidence in technically demanding scenarios. Conclusion: Real-time 3D navigation is a valuable adjunct for FESS, particularly in complex lumbar pathologies where anatomical orientation is challenging. The technique enables accurate intraoperative guidance, enhances surgical precision, and supports safe and effective decompression without increasing operative risk or significantly prolonging surgical time. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility and safety of integrating navigation into endoscopic spine surgery. While limited by small sample size, this study provides preliminary evidence that navigation-assisted FESS can achieve excellent clinical outcomes while reducing technical uncertainty in difficult cases. Larger prospective studies are warranted to further validate these results and establish standardized protocols for its application in advanced endoscopic spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2675

## P0789: Surgical ingenuity that outsmarts navigation errors: stepwise and marker-guided switch techniques for cervical screws

Yu Yamamoto

1

, Ryuya Maejima

1

, Motonori Ishii

2

, Masahito Hara

1

, Masakazu Takayasu

2

1
Aichi Medical University, Nagakute, Japan, 
2
Inazawa Municipal Hospital, Inazawa, Japan
Introduction: Intraoperative navigation has revolutionized spine surgery, yet its Achilles’ heel remains: navigation images cannot be corrected in real-time during screw insertion. Even small deviations may lead to suboptimal or dangerous trajectories. We introduce two novel, Surgical Ingenuity techniques - the Stepwise method and the Marker-Guided Switch Navigation method-that make real - time correction possible and thereby enhance accuracy in cervical screw fixation. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing posterior cervical fixation with pedicle screws, paravertebral foramen screws (PVFS), and lateral mass screws (LMS) were included. Stepwise method: Screws were intentionally under-inserted by ∼2 mm, verified with intraoperative O-arm CT, and then advanced step by step to the ideal cortical position, minimizing navigation error. Marker-Guided Switch Navigation: Metallic fiducials were placed on mobile lamina/spinous processes and registered. During drilling, tapping, and insertion, a navigated instrument alternated between the fiducial and the planned trajectory, enabling real-time detection and immediate correction of navigation drift. Results: Stepwise was applied in the majority of cases, and Marker-Guided Switch Navigation in a smaller subset (22 screws). In total, over 100 screws across both techniques were successfully placed with high accuracy and without neurovascular complications or need for revision surgery. Conclusion: These techniques directly address the critical weakness of navigation-lack of real-time correction during screw insertion. Stepwise and Marker-Guided Switch Navigation provide practical, skill-driven innovations that enhance accuracy and safety across all cervical screw types.

## ID: AGS26/2923

## P0790: The role of intraoperative navigation in the management of distal junctional failure in adult spinal deformity surgery

Calogero Velluto

1

, Laura Scaramuzzo

1

, Maria Ilaria Borruto

1

, Gianmarco Vavalle

1

, Luca Proietti

1

1

Fondazione Policlinico A. Gemelli IRCCS, Rome, Italy

Introduction: Distal junctional failure (DJF) is a relevant complication after long thoracolumbar fusions for adult spinal deformity (ASD), with reported prevalence up to 20%. Sacroiliac junction involvement often leads to S1 screw loosening or breakage, necessitating revision. Iliac fixation provides additional distal stability but requires precise screw placement, which may be technically demanding. Intraoperative navigation has been introduced to enhance accuracy and optimize biomechanical outcomes. Material and Methods: A retrospective review was conducted on 22 patients who underwent revision surgery for DJF between 2020 and 2023. The mean age was 66.9 years (range 57-77), with a female predominance (15/22, 68%). All patients had prior instrumented thoracolumbar fusion for degenerative ASD. DJF was confirmed by standing full-spine radiographs, CT, and MRI. In all cases, iliac fixation was performed with intraoperative navigation, which was also applied for the revision or replacement of S1 screws. Radiographic assessment included lumbar lordosis, PI-LL mismatch, pelvic tilt, sagittal vertical axis, and global sagittal alignment parameters, with focus on the mismatch between L1 pelvic angle (L1PA) and T4 pelvic angle (T4PA). Primary outcomes were screw accuracy and implant survival; secondary outcomes included pain (VAS), spinopelvic alignment, and complication rates. Results: Navigation enabled accurate iliac screw placement in all patients. At 24-month follow-up, iliac screw loosening occurred in only one patient (4.5%). All revised S1 screws remained radiographically stable. Lumbar lordosis improved from 34.5° ± 9.2 to 46.8° ± 8.7 (p < 0.01). PI-LL mismatch decreased from 18.2° ± 6.5 to 7.6° ± 5.1 (p < 0.01). Sagittal vertical axis improved from 62 mm ± 21 to 28 mm ± 15 (p < 0.01). The L1PA-T4PA mismatch was significantly reduced, from a mean of 9.8° ± 4.7 preoperatively to 3.9° ± 2.8 at 12 months (p < 0.01), indicating a more balanced sagittal alignment. VAS scores decreased from 6.7 ± 1.4 preoperatively to 3.0 ± 1.1 at 12 months (p < 0.01). No major intraoperative complications (neurovascular injuries, iliac fractures) were reported. The overall reoperation rate at 12 months was 4.5% (1/22). Conclusion: Intraoperative navigation for DJF revision with mandatory iliac fixation ensured accurate screw placement, improved construct stability, and enhanced clinical outcomes. The significant correction of spinopelvic parameters and the reduction of the L1PA-T4PA mismatch highlight the role of navigation in restoring global sagittal balance. This strategy appears to lower the risk of secondary failure and reoperation, supporting its value as a reliable option in revision ASD surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2972

## P0791: Contribution of 3D imaging in the surgical planning of a T4 thoracic spine fracture

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hospital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Introduction: Upper thoracic spine fractures are rare but surgically demanding due to the narrow pedicles, difficult exposure, and proximity to vital structures. Accurate preoperative planning is essential to ensure safe fixation and optimal alignment. Three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction enhances fracture assessment and guides implant strategy compared to conventional imaging. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 36-year-old male with a high-energy trauma resulting in a T4 burst fracture (AO Spine B2). Preoperative CT and 3D reconstruction were performed. The 3D model provided detailed visualization of vertebral comminution, pedicle morphology, and canal dimensions, allowing precise planning of screw trajectories and construct length. The patient underwent posterior fixation from T2 to T6 using pedicle screws. Results: 3D imaging facilitated accurate identification of screw entry points and trajectories, reducing intraoperative uncertainty and operative time. Surgery was uneventful, with restoration of sagittal alignment and stable fixation. At follow-up, radiographs confirmed maintained construct stability without loss of correction or instrumentation-related complications. Conclusion: 3D imaging is a valuable adjunct in the preoperative planning of complex thoracic spine fractures. By improving visualization of fracture morphology and optimizing implant placement, it enhances surgical accuracy and safety. Routine integration of 3D reconstruction may improve outcomes in challenging levels such as T4.

## ID: AGS26/2973

## P0792: Role of 3D imaging in surgical planning for thoracic scoliosis

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hospital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Introduction: Scoliosis surgery requires precise understanding of vertebral rotation, pedicle morphology, and sagittal alignment. Conventional radiographs and 2D CT may underestimate three-dimensional deformities, increasing the risk of screw malposition. Three-dimensional (3D) reconstruction enhances visualization and supports tailored surgical planning. Material and Methods: A 14-year-old female with progressive idiopathic thoracic scoliosis (apex T7-T9) underwent preoperative evaluation with standing radiographs, CT, and 3D reconstruction. The 3D model was used to assess vertebral rotation, pedicle size, and sagittal alignment. Posterior spinal fusion from T5 to T10 with segmental pedicle screw fixation was planned using the 3D analysis to determine optimal screw trajectories and fusion levels. Discussion: 3D imaging revealed pedicle asymmetry and rotational deformity at the apex that were underestimated on 2D CT. Preoperative planning based on 3D reconstruction allowed accurate screw selection and trajectory, reducing intraoperative uncertainty and fluoroscopy time. Surgery was performed without complications. Postoperative imaging demonstrated correction of the main curve from 42° to 18° and restoration of sagittal alignment. At 12-month follow-up, correction was maintained with solid fusion and no neurological or mechanical complications. Conclusion: 3D reconstruction is a valuable adjunct in scoliosis surgery, providing detailed assessment of vertebral morphology and pedicle anatomy. It enhances surgical accuracy, facilitates safe deformity correction, and should be integrated into preoperative planning even in non-severe scoliosis cases.

## ID: AGS26/2974

## P0793: real-time augmented reality in perioperative for low-cost navigation

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hospital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Introduction: The cervical region's anatomy is intricate due to the proximity of vital organs to the osteo-ligamentous structures of the cervical spine. This complexity renders any surgical approach delicate, with significant risks of vascular and neurological complications. Preoperative planning and a comprehensive understanding of the patient's specific anatomy are crucial for ensuring successful surgical outcomes. We have adopted a new technique: pedicle screw navigation using augmented reality. Materials and Methods: The patient, diagnosed with cervicarthrosis at levels C3-C7 accompanied by cervical myelopathy and neurological signs, was indicated for a posterior arthrodesis with pedicle screws and decompression. Preoperative planning involved performing a cervical spine CT scan followed by 3D reconstruction using specialized software. This produced a 3D model of each vertebra, reflecting the patient's unique anatomy. Subsequently, we prepared the screw trajectories on the reconstruction. A detailed examination of the entire reconstruction allowed for a comprehensive 3D model of the cervical spine with the intended construct. During the surgical procedure, the surgeon utilized augmented reality glasses to visualize the cervical spine reconstruction in 3D. This enabled real-time observation of screw trajectories, cuts, and the construct without manual manipulation. Evaluation was based on operative time, accuracy of screw placement relative to preoperative planning, and radiological assessment (CT scan). Results: The surgical procedure lasted 150 minutes. The placement of screws was facilitated by viewing the 3D model, which minimized manual adjustments and reliance on image intensification. Postoperative outcomes were uncomplicated: no neurological complications, resolution of paresthesias, and significant reduction in cervicobrachial neuralgias. The postoperative CT scan confirmed accurate screw positioning. Discussion: Augmented reality navigation achieved satisfactory results, comparable to those predicted during preoperative planning, with high precision in screw trajectories. The 3D reconstruction of the spine reduced operative time and enabled real-time visualization of predefined screw paths, facilitating easier manipulation of the construct. Conclusion: The integration of new technologies such as augmented reality and artificial intelligence represents the future of surgery. These innovations provide excellent results with minimal risk and render surgical procedures more reproducible.

## ID: AGS26/2976

## P0794: A low-cost 3D-printed guide for pedicle screw navigation in the lower cervical spine

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hospital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Introduction: Posterior cervical spine osteosynthesis is increasingly popular for providing stable arthrodesis along with decompression and release. Currently, pedicle screw placement is the standard practice for achieving spine stability. However, the complexity of this anatomical region and the presence of vital structures pose significant challenges. Cervical pedicles are anatomically smaller compared to thoracolumbar pedicles, making the surgical procedure more delicate and prolonged with a notable risk of postoperative complications. To address this issue, we have adopted a novel approach: 3D reconstruction and 3D-printed guides. Materials and Methods: This preliminary retrospective study involved two cases of cervical spondylosis from C3 to C7, with signs of myelopathy on imaging. The surgical indication was posterior spinal fusion with pedicle screws and decompression. Surgical planning began with cervical spine CT imaging followed by 3D reconstruction to understand the patient's anatomy and pre-plan screw trajectories. Custom 3D-printed guides were then created for each vertebra based on the pre-established screw paths. Evaluation metrics included operative time, bleeding, postoperative complications (particularly neurological), and screw positioning accuracy compared to preoperative planning using radiological control (CT). Results: The average operative time was 160 minutes. Image intensifier use was required only twice. No blood transfusions were necessary postoperatively. Postoperative outcomes were uncomplicated: no neurological issues, resolution of paresthesias, and a significant reduction in cervical radiculopathy. Postoperative CT scans demonstrated screw placement accuracy of 90% (with screws deviating by 4° transversely and 5° frontally). The average postoperative hospital stay was 4 days. Discussion: Pedicle screw placement in the cervical spine is challenging due to anatomical complexity and the small size of the pedicles. Precise screw positioning is crucial for achieving solid arthrodesis and avoiding complications. This technique has demonstrated the feasibility of achieving intra-pedicular screw placements that closely match preoperative plans, thereby mitigating potential complications such as neurological damage to the spinal cord and roots or vascular injury to the vertebral artery. The procedure duration was reduced with lower risk of infection and minimal reliance on image intensification. Conclusion: The combination of 3D reconstruction and intraoperative augmented reality navigation has yielded excellent results with precise anatomical accuracy, minimizing complications. This approach facilitates the surgical procedure, making it more reproducible and manageable.

## ID: AGS26/3113

## P0795: Intraoperative navigation improves pedicle screw accuracy and lowers staff radiation at the cost of early time overhead: a three-year thoracolumbar cohort (2022-2024)

Nikola Bojic

1

1

University clinical Center of Republic of Srpska, Banja Luka, Bosnia and Herzegovina

Introduction: Thoracolumbar fractures are the most common localization of spinal trauma. Accurate pedicle screw placement is essential for biomechanical stability and the prevention of neurological injury. Conventional fluoroscopic guidance has limitations in accuracy and exposes the surgical team to ionizing radiation. Intraoperative navigation (O-arm/3D CT) enables higher accuracy of screw placement and significantly reduces staff radiation exposure, although it may prolong operative time. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 284 patients operated between 2022 and 2024 for degenerative conditions and thoracolumbar trauma. In 2022, 86 patients were treated, in 2023 a total of 92, and in 2024 a total of 106 patients. Patients were divided into two groups based on intraoperative guidance: conventional fluoroscopy and navigation. The primary outcome was pedicle screw accuracy on postoperative CT scans using the Gertzbein-Robbins classification. Secondary outcomes included perioperative complications, revision rates, operative time, and radiation exposure for both staff and patients. Results: The proportion of procedures performed with navigation increased from 30% in 2022 to 70% in 2024. Navigation demonstrated superior accuracy of screw placement (97.5% accurately placed screws, 142/146) compared to fluoroscopy (91.0%, 126/138; p < 0.01). Revision for malposition was required in 1.4% of the navigation group versus 4.3% of the fluoroscopy group. Operative time was longer in the navigation group by an average of 15 minutes in 2022, but this difference decreased to 5 minutes in 2023, and by 2024 operative time was nearly equivalent between groups. Staff radiation exposure was negligible in the navigation group, while patient radiation dose was higher compared to fluoroscopy. Conclusion: Intraoperative navigation significantly improves pedicle screw accuracy, reduces revision rates, and almost eliminates radiation exposure for surgical staff. Although initially associated with longer operative time, the learning curve effect led to near parity with fluoroscopy by the third year. Despite higher patient radiation doses, navigation demonstrates clear clinical benefits and may represent a new standard of care in thoracolumbar trauma surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3260

## P0796: Accuracy of pedicle screw placement: navigation versus free-hand technique - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Omar Hattab

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Pedicle screw placement is a cornerstone of posterior spinal fixation. Malposition may lead to neurological injury, vascular complications, and construct failure. Navigation-assisted techniques have been developed to improve placement accuracy compared to conventional free-hand methods, particularly in anatomically challenging regions. Material and Methods: A systematic review of PubMed and Google Scholar was conducted using the keywords: “pedicle screw,” “navigation,” “free-hand,” “accuracy,” “posterior spinal fixation,” and “complications.” Studies comparing navigated versus free-hand pedicle screw placement in cervical, thoracic, and lumbar spine were included. Data on placement accuracy, cortical breaches, neurovascular complications, operative time, and clinical outcomes were extracted and analyzed.
Results: • Navigated pedicle screw placement consistently shows higher accuracy across all spinal levels, especially in complex anatomical regions.
• Navigation significantly reduces cortical breaches and neurovascular injury.
• Free-hand placement remains effective in experienced hands but shows higher variability and increased risk in cases of altered anatomy or deformity.
• Operative time may be slightly longer with navigation; however, reduced revision rates and improved safety justify its use.
• Preoperative CT planning and intraoperative imaging further enhance placement precision and complement navigation techniques. Conclusion: Navigation-assisted pedicle screw placement provides superior accuracy and safety compared to free-hand techniques, particularly in anatomically complex or deformed spines. Integration of navigation and imaging guidance is recommended to optimize outcomes in posterior spinal fixation.
