## P0797: The spine - The holistic way to the universe

Alexandrina Nikova

1

, Eustathios Vlachakis

2

, Athena Papachristou

1

, Savvas Lipiridis

1

, Stefanos Korfias

3

, Theodossios Birbilis

4

1

Asclipio Voulas Hospital, Athens, Greece

2

417 Army Equity Fund Hospital, Athens, Greece

3

Evaggelismos Hospital, Athens, Greece

4

Democritus university of Thrace, Alexandroupolis, Greece

Introduction: The spine is a subject of interest since Ancient Times, dating back to Egypt, Greece and probably even older ages. The “Father” of spine is believed to be Hippocrates, who not only accurately described the anatomy of the spinal cord but also mentioned for the first time the spinal alignments - scoliosis, lordosis, kyphosis. The Hippocratic times, however, were also related to the holistic approach to the diseases, taking into consideration the dietary, environment and Gods. His medications were based on natural components, very similar to the current homeopathic medicine. On the other hand, in spirituality the spine is linked to the foundation of life, and link between conscious and unconscious. The present study has the aim to emphasize the relation of the holistic approach to the spinal diseases and the currently available data on the changes of white matter tracts and behavior. Material and Methods: The study is conducted based on literature search on world engines such as Medline, Cochrane and Wiley for studies reporting white matter changes in patients with spinal disease and their behavioral changes. Additionally, data on spiritual medicine was retrieved for additional information. Results: Several studies describe brain and spinal white matter changes, related to the level of spinal disease/injury and duration of disease. As known, white matter tracts are related to cognition and behavior. In this term, meditation and spiritual practices are related to functional and structural changes, assessed with MRI imaging, as well as with pain and anxiety relief. Additionally, the metabolomics established high quality studies on the synergy of diet and nutrition in spinal diseases Finally the homeopathic medicine is used in variety of clinical trials showing beneficial effects of homeopathy over placebo for pain relief, but no data were available of white matter structures. Conclusion: Even though Hippocratic Era has passed, variety of fields such as spirituality, spine surgery, alternative medicine, divided into categories, approach the same issue. Neither one, however, is able to treat the spinal disease/injury alone. Therefore, we believe that the approach to the spinal diseases should be holistic, as should be the care for each patient.

## ID: AGS26/210

## P0798: Spinal involvement in chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis

Veronika Petukhova

1

, Alexander Mushkin

1

, Alexey Maletin

1

, Mikhail Kostik

2

1

Pediatric Orthopedics and Surgery Department, Saint-Petersburg Research Institute of Phthisiopulmonology, Saint-Petersburg, Russia, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

2

Hospital Pediatrics Department, Saint-Petersburg State Pediatric Medical University, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis (CRMO) is a rare autoinflammatory bone disorder that frequently affects the spine in pediatric patients, causing persistent pain, structural deformities, and potential neurological complications. Vertebral involvement is reported in 28-81% of cases, most commonly in the thoracic spine. Diagnosis is challenging due to nonspecific symptoms and similarities with infectious spondylodiscitis or malignancies. Whole-body MRI is the most informative non-invasive method for early detection and follow-up, while histopathological evaluation is occasionally required to exclude infection or tumor. Material and Methods: This abstract is based on a synthesis of published literature on pediatric spinal CRMO and a 10-year retrospective clinical observation of children with confirmed CRMO and vertebral involvement. Data from international studies on prevalence, clinical features, imaging, and treatment were integrated with our long-term single-center experience, focusing on treatment strategies and surgical indications. Results: Literature data show that NSAIDs are effective in approximately 60-70% of patients, bisphosphonates in up to 90.9%, and TNF-α inhibitors in about 66.7%. Our own 10-year clinical experience confirms that conservative management is effective in the vast majority of cases, with surgery required only for severe complications such as progressive deformity, spinal instability, or neurological compromise. Earlier treatment strategies, based on general principles for chronic osteomyelitis, often led to unnecessary surgical interventions without improved outcomes. The integration of imaging-based monitoring with stepwise pharmacological therapy reduced surgical morbidity and improved long-term functional results. Conclusion: Pediatric spinal CRMO requires early recognition, whole-body MRI evaluation, and a tailored conservative treatment strategy. Surgical intervention should be reserved for well-defined complications, representing a paradigm shift from earlier aggressive surgical approaches. This approach aligns with the non-infectious, inflammatory nature of the disease and minimizes unnecessary operative risks.

## ID: AGS26/431

## P0799: Can long-term corrective cast and brace treatment avoid growth-friendly surgery in early-onset scoliosis? - Indications and limitations

Ryo Sugawara

1

, Hideaki Watanabe

2

, Naoya Taki

2

, Saki Onuma

2

, Ichiro Kikkawa

3

, Katsushi Takeshita

4

1

Japan Community Healthcare Organization Sendai Hospital, Jichi Medical University, Sendai, Japan

2

Jichi Children's Medical Center Tochigi, Shimotsuke, Japan

3

Nasu Central Hospital, Otawara, Japan

4

Jichi Medical University, Shimotsuke, Japan

Introduction: The optimal management of early-onset scoliosis (EOS) remains controversial, particularly regarding the balance between growth-friendly surgery (GFS) and the risks of multiple surgical interventions. At our institution, we aim to avoid GFS in patients with EOS by extending alternatively repetitive cast and brace treatment (ARCBT) into the school-age years whenever possible, completing treatment with a single final fusion (FF). This study retrospectively assessed the feasibility, efficacy, and limitations of this strategy. Material and Methods: We reviewed 24 EOS patients who underwent at least two cycles of ARCBT and were followed until triradiate cartilage closure, which was used as a threshold for considering FF. Data collected included age at presentation, number of casts, Cobb angle progression, treatment status at triradiate closure, and final outcomes. Results: The cohort included 7 idiopathic scoliosis (IS), 7 syndromic scoliosis (SS), and 10 neuromuscular scoliosis (NMS) cases (10 males, 14 females). Mean age at initial presentation was 4.8 ± 2.3 years. Casting began at 5.6 ± 2.4 years, with initial Cobb angle improving from 47.2 ± 9.6° to 24.7 ± 6.9° (47.4% correction). Patients underwent an average of 7.2 casts during treatment. Mean follow-up was 6.8 ± 2.4 years to triradiate closure and 11.8 ± 4.2 years to final follow-up. Four patients required surgery before triradiate closure due to curve progression: GFS in 2 SS and early FF in 1 SS and 1 NMS. Their mean Cobb angle improved from 98.0 ± 14.6° to 49.0 ± 5.1°, although progression occurred in both GFS cases. Among 20 patients who reached triradiate closure without surgery, mean Cobb angles were IS: 53.7 ± 16.6°, SS: 77.8 ± 28.8°, and NMS: 66.9 ± 22.1°, with no statistically significant differences among groups. FF was later performed in 10 patients (IS: 5, SS: 2, NMS: 3), achieving correction from 77.4 ± 11.8° to 35.4 ± 15.3°, which was maintained at final follow-up. The remaining 10 patients (IS: 2, SS: 2, NMS: 6) remained non-surgical, with final Cobb angle of 70.4 ± 32.2°. In 5 patients with curves > 70°, surgery was not performed due to severe comorbid progression (n = 3) or parental refusal (n = 2). Conclusion: All IS patients avoided GFS through extended ARCBT. Some SS and NMS patients also reached FF without GFS; however, others may have had suboptimal outcomes due to delayed surgical intervention. Extending ARCBT into school age appears to be a feasible strategy for selected EOS patients, but careful patient selection and timely surgical decision-making are essential to optimize long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/853

## P0801: Impact of a standardized preoperative protocol for anxiety management and prone positioning in PKP patients: a prospective intervention study on surgical efficiency and patient satisfaction

wenjie wu

1

, Qian Cheng

1

, Li Huo

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: PKP patients are typically elderly individuals experiencing severe pain and anxiety. Suboptimal preoperative preparation can prolong operative time, increase intraoperative risks, and negatively impact the patient experience. This study evaluates the effect of a nurse-led, standardized preoperative protocol on improving these metrics. Material and Methods: From January to June 2023, 120 patients scheduled for PKP were consecutively enrolled and assigned to an intervention group (n = 60) or a usual care group (n = 60). The usual care group received routine preoperative nursing. The intervention group additionally received a standardized protocol administered by an experienced spine nurse: 1) Structured interview and anxiety mitigation using the Self-Rating Anxiety Scale (SAS) followed by targeted psychological support; 2) Preoperative prone position acclimatization training on a hospital bed to gradually increase tolerance. Time for intraoperative positioning, instances of intraoperative vital sign fluctuation, total operative time, and patient satisfaction scores (0-10) at 24 hours postoperatively were recorded and compared. Results: Preoperative SAS scores were significantly lower in the intervention group (45.3 ± 6.7 vs. 55.8 ± 8.4, p < 0.01). Compared to the usual care group, the intervention group required less time for positioning in the operating room (5.2 ± 1.1 vs. 8.7 ± 2.3 min, p < 0.01), exhibited fewer episodes of vital sign fluctuation (1.2 ± 0.5 vs. 2.8 ± 1.1, p < 0.01), and showed a trend towards shorter operative time (29.5 ± 4.8 vs. 31.9 ± 5.6 min, p = 0.06). Patient satisfaction was significantly higher in the intervention group (8.9 ± 0.7 vs. 7.1 ± 1.3, p < 0.01). Conclusion: A nurse-led standardized preoperative protocol, by effectively reducing patient anxiety and facilitating positional acclimatization, significantly optimizes surgical workflow, enhances intraoperative safety, and greatly improves patient satisfaction. This model highlights the critical value of nursing intervention in perioperative PKP care and is readily transferable to hospitals at various levels.

## ID: AGS26/947

## P0802: Rehabilitation strategies following posterior cervical decompression for cervical spondylotic myelopathy: a narrative review and framework for clinical practice

Eeric Truumees

1

, Kartik Akkihal

1

, Devender Singh

1

, Matthew Geck

1

, John Stokes

1

1

Ascension Texas Spine and Scoliosis, Austin, United States

Introduction: While posterior surgical approaches, including laminectomy and laminoplasty, stop the progression of CSM, many patients continue to experience persistent neurological deficits and diminished physical function. Postoperative rehabilitation is increasingly recognized as crucial for optimizing outcomes but core elements like timing and best-practice protocols remain poorly defined. The aim of the study was to synthesize evidence surrounding rehabilitation interventions after posterior cervical decompression for cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM) and to identify key priorities for the development of standardized perioperative programs. Material and Methods: Comprehensive review of PubMed, Ovid, Embase, and Web of Science was performed for therapeutic English-language studies from 1990 to August 4, 2025, evaluating rehabilitation after posterior cervical decompression for CSM. Results: 29 studies were included. Consistent participation in multidisciplinary programs, including physical and occupational therapy, gait/balance work, and progressive strengthening, was associated with improved function, performance, and patient satisfaction. Particularly, early initiation was linked to larger neurological and functional gains. Hand dexterity and low-intensity aerobic activity offered benefit during the collar phase. Extended collar use was not associated with superior outcomes and may risk muscle atrophy. Adjunctive options such as functional electrical stimulation and SEM gloves demonstrated potential as complements to standard therapy, but literature remains preliminary. Conclusion: A staged, multidisciplinary, and patient-centered rehabilitation approach, initiated early and integrating mobility, hand dexterity, and balance training, is supported by current evidence and may optimize postoperative recovery in CSM. High-quality randomized trials are needed to confirm optimal timing, sequence, and use of emergent technologies in this population.

## ID: AGS26/1110

## P0803: The effects of brace treatment on sagittal pelvic parameters in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis patients with Lenke type 5 curves and Risser stage 4-5

Hüseyin Sina Coşkun

1

, Altan Aksu

2

, Bedirhan Albayrak

2

, Abdurrahman Murat Yıldırım

3

, Kayra Barut

4

, Furkan Erdoğan

1

, Recep Dinçer

5

, Özcan Kaya

6

1

Ondokuz Mayıs University, Samsun, Turkey

2

Samsun Training and Research Hospital, Samsun, Turkey

3

Bafra State Hospital, Samsun, Turkey

4

İzmir EMOT Hospital, İzmir, Turkey

5

Süleyman Demirel University, Isparta, Turkey

6

Biruni University, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: It is well-established that Risser stage 4 marks the near completion of spinal growth, whereas Risser stage 5 indicates full skeletal maturity. Some studies have reported the application of bracing at these later stages, primarily for cosmetic concerns, though improvements in angular parameters have also been documented. This study aimed to evaluate the impact of brace treatment on sagittal spinopelvic alignment in adolescents with Lenke type 5 idiopathic scoliosis and Risser stage 4-5 skeletal maturity. We sought to assess whether bracing, despite late skeletal maturity, could induce meaningful structural changes in spinopelvic parameters and the Cobb angle. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 81 patients diagnosed with Lenke type 5 scoliosis. Patients were grouped into brace-treated (n = 34) and non-braced (n = 47) cohorts. Radiographic evaluations of pelvic and spinal coronal, sagittal parameters - including Main thoracic (MT) curve, Thoracolumbar (TL) curve, pelvic incidence (PI), pelvic tilt (PT), sacral slope (SS), lumbar lordosis (LL), and thoracic kyphosis (TK) - were performed at baseline, 6 months, and 24 months. Changes were analyzed using the Wilcoxon and Mann-Whitney U tests. Results: In the braced group, the mean reduction in the main thoracic (MT) curve during the first 6 months was 3.03°, followed by a slight increase of 0.38° between months 6 and 24. The overall net reduction in MT over 24 months was 2.65°, which was statistically significant (p = 0.0001). For the thoracolumbar (TL) curve, a decrease of 4.15° was observed in the first 6 months, followed by an increase of 1.09° between 6 and 24 months. The net 24-month change was a 3.06° reduction, which was also statistically significant (p = 0.0059). In the braced group, significant improvements were observed in sagittal parameters PT (+1.82°, p = 0.0022), LL (-8.5°, p = 0.0003), and TK (-4.94°, p = 0.0020) over 24 months. SS decreased initially but returned to baseline, showing no long-term significance. PI remained stable. Correlation analysis revealed strong positive associations between LL and both PI (r = 0.58, p < 0.001) and PT (r = 0.50, p < 0.001). Conclusion: Brace treatment may lead to favorable modifications in coronal, sagittal and related pelvic alignment, even in skeletally mature patients. These findings support bracing use in even late Risser stage AIS cases to improve overall postural balance and pelvic orientation.

## ID: AGS26/1260

## P0804: Efficacy of Schroth exercises in management of adolescent idiopathic scoliosis within Asia

Hui Zhen Grace Chua

1

, June Mei Tse Quek

1

, Youheng Ou Yang

1

, Chay You Ang

1

, Lei Jiang

1

, Karine Cheng Ning Sia

1

, Yong Hao Pua

1

, Jun Xiang Ng

1

, Reuben Soh

1

1

Singapore General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: The Schroth method in the management of Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis (AIS) consists of sensorimotor, postural and breathing exercises aimed at recalibration of normal postural alignment, static/dynamic postural control, and spinal stability. It involves exercises customised to the patient’s spinal curve, and can be prescribed on its own or to complement bracing regimes. This study was conducted to investigate the efficacy of Schroth exercises in the absence of bracing in management of AIS within a school screening programme. Materials and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted on prospectively collected database of AIS patients seen at a single tertiary hospital in Singapore from 2022 to 2025. Inclusion criteria were patients diagnosed with AIS, aged between 10 to 18 years old, and participated in supervised Schroth exercises with the physiotherapists with no other ongoing or prior interventions such as bracing or surgery. Only patients completing a minimum of four sessions of Schroth exercises, typically monthly, were included. Reasons for exclusion were most commonly not achieving the minimum frequency or duration of Schroth exercises, with others being concurrent bracing or a lack of follow-up data. A control group involving AIS patients doing normal exercises, wearing a brace or having no intervention was also analysed for comparison. Cobb angle and Angle of Trunk Rotation (ATR) were measured in both groups, while SRS-22 (Scoliosis Research Society-22 patient questionnaire) scores were collected from the Schroth group. Results: A total of 50 AIS patients were included in the study (70.0% females, mean age 13.60 ± 1.42 years, BMI 18.27 ± 3.66, Risser 2.6 ± 1.2). They attended 5.5 ± 0.5 sessions of Schroth exercises across 6.2 ± 4.2 months. Improvements in thoracic ATR (p = 0.028, CI [0.09, 1.57], Cohen’s d = 0.32) and lumbar ATR (p = 0.042, CI [0.03, 1.53], Cohen’s d = 0.30) were statistically significant, but change in Cobb angle was not significant (p = 0.396, CI [-1.41, 0.57], Cohen’s d = 0.12). Improvement in quality of life was also represented by a significant improvement in SRS-22 scores (p = 0.001, CI [-0.23, -0.06], Cohen’s d = 0.65). Analysis of patients who defaulted on physiotherapy showed that school commitments and examinations played a major role in compliance to the sessions. Conclusions: Schroth exercises are effective in improving ATR and SRS-22 scores, while effective in maintaining Cobb angle. The improvement in ATR and SRS-22 scores provides evidence that Schroth exercises should be considered in patients with slightly more mature spines, in addition to standardised treatment. Qualitative studies can also be carried out amongst those excluded from the study to investigate their reasons for not attending supervised Schroth exercise sessions on a regular basis.

## ID: AGS26/1411

## P0805: Postoperative physical therapy utilization for cervical disc replacement: an analysis of practice patterns, predictors, and comparison to anterior cervical discectomy and fusion

William Karakash

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Justin Lantz

2

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

2

Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Cervical disc replacement (CDR) is increasingly used as an alternative to anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) for managing cervical degenerative disease. It provides potential benefit, particularly in younger patients with 1-2 level disease and good bone quality, with its motion-preserving properties. While postoperative physical therapy (PT) is commonly employed following ACDF and has been linked to improved patient-reported outcomes, PT utilization patterns after CDF remain poorly understood due to limited research and absence of established guidelines. This study aimed to identify patterns and predictors of postoperative PT utilization after CDR and compare to its usage following ACDF. Material and Methods: Patients who underwent one- to two-level CDR from 2010 to April 2023 were identified in the PearlDiver national database via CPT codes. Patients with trauma, malignancy, infection, or revisions within one year were excluded. PT utilization was assessed between 1 week to 1 year postoperatively. Multivariable logistic regression was performed to identify independent predictors of PT utilization following CDR, controlling for age, gender, Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI). Patients who underwent one- to two-level ACDF acted as a control group. Chi-squared and independent T-tests compared proportion of patients who attended PT, time to first PT visit, and number of total PT visits between CDR and ACDF cohorts. Results: Among 33,558 patients who underwent one- to two-level CDR, 7,115 (21.2%) patients utilized PT in the first year postoperatively. Patients who engaged in PT were older (47.34 vs 47.06 years), had more comorbidities (ECI 3.48 vs 3.0), more likely female (41.4% vs 53.8%), and had higher mean family income ($85,187 vs $82,686) (all p < 0.05). The strongest predictors of PT utilization included diagnosis of radiculopathy (OR: 1.64), myelopathy (OR: 1.51), fibromyalgia (1.50), mean family income > $75,000 (OR: 1.44), education status > 75%ile (OR: 1.43), and Midwest region (OR: 1.39) (all p < 0.001). Factors associated with decreased PT utilization included Medicare insurance (OR: 0.65), age > 70+ (OR: 0.73), Southeast region (OR: 0.80), male gender (OR: 0.85), education status < 25%ile (OR: 0.88), and outpatient surgery (OR: 0.89). When compared to ACDF, a similar number of CDR patients utilized postoperative PT (21.2% vs 21.7%), but they initiated PT earlier (75.2 vs 90.6 days) and attended less PT visits (10.14 vs 10.93 visits) (all p < 0.05). Conclusion: Less than one-quarter of patients engaged in postoperative physical therapy usage following one- to two-level CDR. These patients tend to have a higher comorbidity index and higher socioeconomic status. Preoperative diagnoses, comorbidities, education status, and insurance type predicted PT utilization. Compared to ACDF patients, CDR patients initiated PT earlier and required fewer visits, despite utilizing PT at a similar rate. Identifying predictors of PT use and differences compared to ACDF highlights the absence of standardized rehabilitation protocols. These findings emphasize the need for evidence-based guidelines to optimize recovery and reduce variability in postoperative care.

## ID: AGS26/1423

## P0806: Patterns of physical therapy utilization following microdiscectomy

Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Natalie Kistler

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Justin Lantz

2

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

2

Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Lumbar microdiscectomy (MD) is a minimally invasive surgery for symptomatic disc herniation refractory to conservative management. Physical therapy (PT) after MD may aid recovery by initiating mobilization and strengthening, but optimal use and application of protocols remain unclear. This study investigates the frequency, timing, and predictors of PT utilization on a national, multicenter level following MD. Material and Methods: The PearlDiver Mariner national database was used to identify patients undergoing MD from 2010-2022 using CPT codes. Patients were stratified using CPT codes for postoperative PT interventions specifically for ICD-9/10 diagnosis codes related to lumbar disc degeneration. Patients under 18, with malignancy, infection, trauma, rheumatoid arthritis, revisions within one year, or less than 1 year follow-up were excluded. PT utilization was assessed between 7-365 days postoperatively. Patient demographics, comorbidities, time to PT, and preoperative PT utilization were collected. Statistical analyses included ANOVA, chi-squared tests, univariate and multivariate logistic regression to identify significant predictors of PT use. Results: Among 372,376 patients in this national cohort, 89,970 (24.2%) received postoperative PT following MD surgery. Patients utilizing PT were slightly younger (52.8 ± 14.6 years), disproportionately female (49.9%), had higher comorbidity scores (ECI 3.31 ± 2.92), and greater mean family income ($77,937 ± $21,722). Timing of PT initiation varied: 38% started PT within 6 weeks postoperatively, 68% by 12 weeks, 79% by 18 weeks, 84% by 24 weeks, and 100% by one year, with commercial insurance enabling the shortest delay (84.4 days) compared to Medicaid (100.6 days) and Medicare (97.6 days) (p < 0.05). Postoperative PT users had markedly higher rates of preoperative PT use when compared to patients who did not undergo postoperative PT (25.8% vs. 8.6%); notably, preoperative PT emerged as the strongest predictor of postoperative PT use (OR: 3.73) (both p < 0.05). Other independent predictors included inpatient surgical status (OR: 1.19), advanced education (OR: 1.28), and comorbidities including obesity (OR: 1.35), osteoarthritis (OR: 1.43), fibromyalgia (OR: 1.49), and depression (OR: 1.29) (all p < 0.05). Geographic region was significant: patients in the Midwest (OR: 1.42) and Northeast (OR: 1.22) were more likely to receive PT, while those from the South (OR: 0.68) and West (OR: 0.92), or with Medicare (OR: 0.72), were less likely (all p < 0.05). Surgical complexity also played a role, as multi-level procedures increased PT utilization (OR: 1.07), whereas single-level surgery was associated with lower PT use (OR: 0.94). Additionally, patients experiencing medical (OR: 1.24) or surgical complications (OR: 1.32) were more likely to engage in PT (all p < 0.05). Conclusion: Postoperative PT after MD is underutilized, with only 24% of patients engaging in therapy. Utilization varies by age, comorbidities, insurance, region, levels involved, and postop complications, with preoperative PT strongly predicting postoperative use. These findings highlight disparities and the need for standardized, equitable rehabilitation guidelines to optimize recovery. This study highlights significant disparities in postoperative PT utilization following MD, influenced by demographic, socioeconomic, and clinical factors. Understanding these predictors can guide the development of standardized, equitable rehabilitation protocols to optimize patient recovery and resource allocation.

## ID: AGS26/1487

## P0807: The effects of postoperative activity restrictions on outcomes after spine surgery: a systematic review

Kevin Mathew

1

, Camille Flynn

1

, William Karakash

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Justin Lantz

2

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

2

Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, CA, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Postoperative activity restrictions are commonly prescribed after spine surgery. However, it is unclear whether postoperative restrictions improve clinical or surgical outcomes after spine surgery. The primary aim of this study was to investigate the effects of postoperative activity restrictions in spine surgery on patient-reported outcomes. Material and Methods: This review included studies discussing degenerative spine surgery patients with postoperative activity restrictions, defined as limitations on specific movements such as bending or twisting, or activities such as driving or weightlifting. Electronic searches were conducted using PubMed, CENTRAL, Embase, and Web of Science databases in November 2024. The Revised Cochrane Risk-of-Bias tool for randomized trials (RoB 2) and Risk Of Bias In Non-randomized Studies - of Interventions, Version 2 (ROBINS-I V2) tools were used to evaluate included studies for risk of bias. Pain, function/disability, and data on recurrence (reherniation), adverse events and complications were collected from each study. Meta-analysis was initially planned but was deemed unfeasible due to the limited number and high heterogeneity of included studies. The Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development, and Evaluation (GRADE) approach was used to determine the certainty of the body of evidence. Results: 3,723 studies were screened. Four studies met the inclusion criteria: 1 randomized controlled trial and 2 prospective single-arm cohort studies discussing open lumbar discectomy, and 1 prospective non-randomized two-group cohort study discussing percutaneous endoscopic lumbar discectomy (PELD). Notably, there are no studies discussing restrictions after cervical or thoracic spinal surgery. No significant differences were found between restrictive and liberal postoperative activity restrictions, and the certainty of the findings were low for all included outcome measures. Conclusion: Limited evidence exists regarding the effectiveness of postoperative activity restrictions on outcomes after lumbar spine surgery and there is no literature discussing postoperative activity restrictions after cervical or thoracic spine surgery. The certainty of evidence makes it difficult to provide a definitive conclusion for or against the use of postoperative activity restrictions in spine surgery. Robust randomized controlled trials are needed to determine the impact of various types of activity restriction protocols across several types of spinal surgery. Future studies could provide the basis for the creation of standardized, evidence-based postoperative activity restriction protocols to ensure optimal patient outcomes after spine surgery. Clinical recommendations for postoperative activity restrictions after spine surgery are primarily based on individual surgeon preference, and the evidence surrounding their use is extremely limited. The continued use of non-evidence-based activity restriction protocols has significant medical, legal and financial implications on practice.

## ID: AGS26/1701

## P0808: Spontaneous spinal subdural hematoma: a case series of an uncommon cause of paraplegia

Maria Eleni Sakellaridou

1

, Vasiliki Georgilaki

1

, Tiffany Georgiou

1

, Eftichia Simoglaki

1

, Georgios Georgountzos

1

, Panagiotis Papadopoulos-Manolarakis

1

, Moshos Fratzoglou

1

, Theofilos Paleologos

1

1

Neurosurgical Department, General Hospital of Nikea-Piraeus, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Spinal subdural hematoma (SSDH) is a rare pathology with significant morbidity and mortality. It can be traumatic, iatrogenic or spontaneous and is believed to account for 4-6,5% of all spinal hematomas. The aim of this study is to showcase this rare cause of severe neurological deficit and to highlight the need of correct diagnosis. Cases Presentation: The first patient was 67-year-old male patient with no history coagulopathy or trauma. He presented with acute back pain during rest time and subsequent paraparesis (3/5 at all the muscle groups of the lower extremities, ASIA D) and sensory deficit below the T10 level. The thoracic spine MRI showcased a SSDH at the T8 level with mild spinal cord compression. Conservative treatment was selected. The patient underwent intensive physiotherapy with improvement of his symptoms. The second case was that of an 87-year-old woman with a history of clopidogrel intake due to coronary disease. She presented with acute back pain during swimming and subsequent paraplegia and sensory deficit below T6 level (ASIA A). The thoracic spine MRI revealed a large SSDH from T4 to T9 with spinal cord compression. Conservative treatment was selected with no improvement of the patient’s clinical status. The third patient was a 74-year-old female patient that was hospitalized due to chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and was under therapeutic anticoagulant medication due to pulmonary embolism. She presented with acute lumbar pain followed by paraplegia and sensory deficit below T2 level (ASIA A). The MRI of the cervical, thoracic and lumbar spine showed a large SSDH from C7 to T7 with spinal cord compression. An acute myocardial infarction was suspected, and she was submitted in the ICU where she was intubated after 24 hours. Conservative treatment was selected for the SSDH. Discussion: The spontaneous SSDH can occur due to coagulating disorders or anticoagulant medications, underlying vascular malformations, neoplasms and brittle vessel walls. In many cases the initial imaging examinations were unable to identify an underlying cause. The SSDH causes spinal cord compression resulting to severe motor, sensory and autonomic dysfunctions. Conclusion: The SSDH can result to high morbidity and mortality, and it is of grave importance the prompt diagnosis and the selection of the correct treatment.

## ID: AGS26/1712

## P0809: Patterns of physical therapy utilization following anterior lumbar interbody fusion

Natalie Kistler

1

, William Karakash

1

, Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Chimere Ezuma

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Zorica Buser

2

, Justin Lantz

3

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

2

Langone Health, New York, United States

3

Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Postoperative physical therapy (PT) can improve pain and functionality after lumbar fusion without increasing complication rates, however, its role following anterior lumbar fusion (ALIF) remains understudied. This study aimed to identify trends and predictors of postoperative PT utilization following ALIF and characterize the frequency, timing, and types of PT services utilized. Materials and Methods: In this retrospective cohort study, the PearlDiver Mariner database was used to analyze patients undergoing ALIF from 2010-2022. Patients were identified using International Classification of Disease 9th (ICD-9) and 10th (ICD-10) editions and Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) codes. Exclusion criteria included less than one year follow-up, posterior instrumentation > 5 levels, prior ALIF, revision surgery within one year, and surgical indications for neoplasm, infection, revision, or trauma. PT utilization was assessed between 7-365 days postoperatively. Patient demographics, medical comorbidities, and operative characteristics were compared between study cohorts. The secondary objectives were to characterize the frequency, timing, and types of PT services delivered. Statistical analyses included t-tests, chi-squared tests, and multivariable logistic regression to identify predictors of PT use. Results: Among 112,808 patients undergoing ALIF, 28,718 (25.5%) utilized PT within the first year. PT users were younger, had higher Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI) scores, and higher income levels (p < 0.001). Multivariable analysis identified positive predictors of PT usage, including commercial insurance (OR: 1.52), orthopedic referral (OR: 1.26), higher income (OR: 1.07), previous decompressions (OR: 1.08), and postoperative medical (1.21) or surgical (1.10) 90 day-complications (p < 0.001 for all). Of the patients who utilized PT following ALIF, the average number of visits was 12.25. the average number of postoperative days before patients’ first PT visit was 102.98 ± 84.24 days. Patients with Medicare took the longest to initiate their first PT session (113.72 ± 90.40 days, p < 0.001) whereas those with commercial insurance initiated postoperative PT the quickest (100.36 ± 82.86 days, p < 0.001). Timing of PT initiation varied: 23.6% within 6 weeks, 31.8% between 6-12 weeks, 17.9% between 12-18 weeks, and 26.6% after 18 weeks. The most frequently billed interventions also had the highest average visits per patient, the highest being CPT-97110 for therapeutic exercises (87.3% and 11.1 visits). Conclusion: PT utilization was low after ALIF and practice patterns after ALIF varied significantly and was influenced by insurance type, comorbidities, income level, and surgical complexity. In total, these results characterize a lack of evidence-based clinical guidelines for managing ALIF patients postoperatively, specifically recommendations outlining which patients are suitable for PT and the appropriate timing of intervention. These findings underscore the understudied nature of PT and its contribution to postoperative outcomes following lumbar fusion procedures while cautioning providers to consider socioeconomic disparities in access to rehabilitation when developing future guidelines. The present study emphasizes the need for the development of evidence-based rehabilitation protocols to improve outcomes after ALIF. We invite these efforts in the hope that care standardization can result in reduction of existing disparities and overall improve outcomes for all patients.

## ID: AGS26/1992

## P0810: Extensive spinal dural arteriovenous fistula from the craniocervical junction to the lumbar region in a young adult: a rare case report

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

, Mohamed Amine Hadj Taieb

2

1
Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, 
2
Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia
Introduction: Spinal dural arteriovenous fistulas (SDAVFs) are rare vascular malformations, representing ∼3% of all spinal cord vascular lesions. Typically seen in older males and located in the thoracolumbar region, SDAVFs result in venous congestion and progressive myelopathy. We present a rare case of extensive SDAVF, spanning from the craniocervical junction to the lumbar spine, in a young adult. This poster aims to report an exceptional case of diffuse SDAVF in a 32-year-old male with atypical symptoms and extensive radiologic involvement, but no overt myelopathy. Material and Methods: A 32-year-old man presented with two months of lower back and neck pain, bilateral leg numbness, and intermittent claudication. Neurological examination showed 4/5 monoparesis of the right lower limb, hyperreflexia, and bilateral Babinski signs. MRI and MRA revealed tortuous perimedullary vessels extending from the craniocervical junction to the lumbar spine, without T2 cord signal changes. Spinal angiography identified a dural fistula fed by the lateral sacral arteries, with extensive perimedullary venous drainage. The artery of Adamkiewicz showed delayed filling without venous outflow. Results: The diagnosis of extensive SDAVF was confirmed, with radiological findings disproportionate to the relatively mild clinical symptoms. This atypical presentation, especially in a young adult without myelopathy, underscores diagnostic challenges. Treatment options, including endovascular embolization or surgical disconnection, were discussed based on angioarchitecture and extent. Conclusion: SDAVF should be considered even in young patients with subtle neurological deficits. MRI plays a pivotal role in early suspicion, but spinal angiography remains diagnostic. This case is notable for the rare combination of young age, widespread venous involvement, and minimal clinical impact-highlighting the importance of early recognition and tailored management in such presentations.

## ID: AGS26/2453

## P0812: Postoperative physical therapy utilization for posterior cervical fusion: an analysis of practice patterns in the United States

Kevin Mathew

1

, Amara Emeh

1

, William Karakash

1

, Henry Avetisian

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, George James

2

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

, Justin Lantz

2

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

2

Division of Biokinesiology and Physical Therapy, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Posterior cervical fusion (PCF) is a common spinal procedure used to treat conditions such as spondylosis, spinal stenosis, and cervical degenerative disc disorders involving myelopathy or radiculopathy. PCF accounted for 14.0% of all cervical spine surgeries in the United States between 2010 and 2022. However, the role of postoperative physical therapy (PT) following this procedure remains largely unexplored. The primary objective of this study was to describe postoperative PT utilization patterns and to analyze potential predictors of PT utilization after PCF. Material and Methods: This study identified patients who underwent PCF between January 2010 and April 2023 in the United States using the PearlDiver Mariner Database (PearlDiver Technologies, Colorado Springs, CO, USA). The database was queried for Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) codes and International Classification of Disease 9th (ICD-9) and 10th (ICD-10) editions codes that corresponded to a diagnosis of degenerative cervical radiculopathy and/or cervical myelopathy and a PCF procedure (CPT-22600 and CPT-22614). The mean time to PT was also assessed, stratified by preoperative diagnosis. Predictive factors of postop PT usage following PCF were evaluated with univariate and multivariable logistic regression analyses. Results: A total of 46,078 patients (21,027 female, 25,051 male) who underwent one- to five-level PCF) for cervical radiculopathy and/or myelopathy were identified. Of these, 10,130 (21.98%) received postoperative physical therapy within one year. The mean time to PT was 93.97 ± 75.36 days. On multivariable logistic regression, demographic factors associated with increased PT utilization included commercial insurance status (OR: 1.52 [1.45-1.60], p < 0.001), residence in the Midwest (OR: 1.37 [1.30-1.44], p < 0.001) or Northeast (OR: 1.21 [1.15-1.28], p < 0.001) , and annual income >$75,000 (OR: 1.09; [1.04-1.14], p < 0.001). Medicare insurance (OR: 0.58 [0.55-0.62], p < 0.001) was linked to decreased PT utilization, as were age over 70 years (OR: 0.76 [0.72-0.80], p < 0.001), low education level (OR: 0.78 [0.74-0.83], p < 0.001), and residence in the Southeast (OR: 0.69 [0.66-0.72], p < 0.001) or West (OR: 0.88 [0.83-0.94], p < 0.001). Inpatient surgery setting (OR: 1.31 [1.20-1.43], p < 0.01), multilevel fusion (OR: 1.39 [1.31-1.48], p < 0.001), and mixed radiculopathy/myelopathy diagnosis (OR: 1.39 [1.33-1.45], p < 0.001) also increased PT utilization. Conclusion: PT utilization after PCF remains low. Insurance status, age, and education status were predictors for PT utilization, highlighting the importance of considering financial and socioeconomic barriers when creating postoperative treatment plans. Younger patients with commercial insurance, high income, and advanced education are the most likely to attend postoperative PT, while older patients with Medicare, low income, and basic education are least likely to attend postoperative PT.

## ID: AGS26/2508

## P0813: Nonoperative therapy of sacral chordomas

Sean Bae

1

, Mark Ehioghae

2

, Ala Alshomali

1

, Aaron Phung

1

, Linus Lee

3

, Justin Hyde

1

, Jamie Lee

1

, Jonathan Japa

2

, Kevin Yoon

2

, Addisu Mesfin

4

1

Georgetown University School of Medicine, Washington, DC, United States

2

MedStar Health Research Institute, Washington, DC, United States

3

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, MedStar Georgetown University Hospital, Washington, DC, United States

4

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, MedStar Washington Hospital Center, Washington, DC, United States

Introduction: Chordomas are rare, malignant tumors arising from notochordal remnants. Roughly half of chordomas occur in the sacrum, and patients most often present with sacral pain as the primary complaint. Although surgical resection is the accepted standard treatment, some patients forgo surgery due to the high risk of surgical complications. There is an increasing need for nonoperative or palliative treatment options for patients with unresectable tumors. Currently, little is known regarding the long-term efficacy of nonoperative management of sacral chordomas, and no standard of care has been established for these cases. High-dose fractionated particle radiotherapy has emerged as a promising alternative for surgical resection in cases of unresectable sacral chordomas. Material and Methods: A systematic search of PubMed was conducted to identify studies published between 1998 and 2023 reporting nonoperative management of sacral chordomas. Duplicate records were removed. The search strategy included the keywords: “nonoperative treatment,” “sacral chordomas,” “radiotherapy,” and “functional outcomes.” Studies that included patients undergoing surgical intervention or hybrid therapies combining surgery and radiation were excluded. Eligible studies included case reports and case series that provided treatment details and clinical outcomes. Data from four additional case studies of nonoperatively managed sacral chordomas were collected and compared with the literature to provide a comprehensive overview of current nonoperative management strategies. Results: The literature review included breakdowns by age, sex, spinal segment, treatment modality, and outcome. Among 52 patients across 11 studies, we identified a mean age of 62.8 years with a clear predominance within men. Of patients who were managed nonoperatively for their sacral chordomas, the incidence in males occurred at a 2.7 times higher rate than females (73.1% vs 26.9%, respectively). The most common symptoms associated with the tumor were sacral pain, lower limb dysfunction, and bowel or bladder involvement, pointing to the proximity to critical neurovascular structures. Anatomically, the S2 region was the most commonly involved vertebral level (25%), followed by S1 and sacrococcygeal region (15.4% each). The mainstay method of diagnosis was biopsy, using MRI and CT for further corroboration. Out of 52 patients, 30 (57.7%) died of sacral chordoma, 5 (9.6%) died of secondary complications, 7 (13.5%) were alive with disease, and 30 (57.7%) were alive with no evidence of disease at an average follow-up duration of 35.7 months. CIRT was the most common treatment modality (50%), followed by cryoablation (17.3%) and proton/photon beam radiotherapy. Conclusion: Nonoperative management of sacral chordomas shows promising outcomes for patients who are not candidates for surgery. Our literature review and case series support nonoperative treatment of sacral chordomas as a valid alternative to surgery offering lower morbidity for some patients. Future studies should prioritize long-term follow-up and standardize data collection including associated symptoms, resolution of symptoms, and comorbidities. As the need for nonsurgical treatment modalities for sacral chordomas increases globally, prospective studies will be imperative in establishing effective treatment. Although recurrence remains a risk, nonoperative management may offer meaningful functional preservation and local disease control.

## ID: AGS26/2526

## P0814: 3D radiological and clinical prognostic factors for failure and improvement of bracing for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS): a comparative analysis

Nikos Karavidas

1

1

Schroth Scoliosis & Spine Clinic, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Brace treatment for Adolescent Idiopathic Scoliosis (AIS) is indicated for curves above 25ο during growth. The rate of success and failure varies in the published literature, and some factors have been highlighted as positive or negative to the treatment outcome. To date, no study compared the characteristics of failures and improvements from the same initial sample. Our objective was to separately analyze radiological and clinical prognostic factors for Cobb angle difference more than 5ο in progressive (PROG) and improved (IMPR) groups of patients and detect potential associations. Material and Methods: Comparative Experimental Study. 113 patients (73 IMPR and 40 PROG) with progression or improvement from our prospective database, were selected for analysis. Αll patients that received brace treatment and practiced scoliosis specific exercises (SSE) with the following criteria were included: Cobb angle > 25ο, Risser sign 0-2 with at least 12 months of treatment and one follow-up x-ray without brace. We evaluated initial Cobb angles (Cobb-IN), in-brace correction (IBC) and final Cobb angles (Cobb-FI) for thoracic (TH) and lumbar (LU) curves. The factors that we analyzed were Lenke curve type, apex location, type of progressed and improved curve, brace and SSE compliance (self-reported), supervised sessions/year, Pelvic Incidence (PI), Pelvic Tilt (PT), Sacral Slope (SS), Roussouly type, thoracic kyphosis (TK), lumbar lordosis (LL), family history, Risser sign, Triradiate Cartilage (TRC) ossification, Body Mass Index (BMI), Angle Trunk Rotation (ATR) for thoracic and lumbar curves. Chi-square, Fisher’s exact and Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical analysis. Results: IBC (41% IMPR - 22.9% PROG, p < 0.001), Cobb-FI TH(25.7ο IMPR - 50.1ο PROG, p < 0.001), Cobb-FI LU (21.9ο IMPR - 49.26ο PROG, p < 0.001), Brace compliance (78.1% high, 19.2% moderate, 2.7% poor for IMPR - 32.5% high, 32.5% moderate, 35% poor for PROG, p < 0.001), SSE compliance (53.4% high, 32.9% moderate, 13.7% poor for IMPR - 15% high, 40% moderate, 45% poor for PROG, p < 0.001), curve type (single 35.6%, double 64.4% IMPR - single 15%, double 85% PROG, p = 0.028), Lenke types (1C 4.1%, 3C 2.7%, 5C 20.6% IMPR - 1C 12.5%, 3C 12.5%, 5C 0% PROG, p = 0.008), Roussouly types (p = 0.038), Risser (p = 0.035), TRC (open 46.5% IMPR - open 72.5% PROG, p = 0.01), menarche (no 56.2% IMPR - 77.5% PROG), BMI (17.7 IMPR - 19.2 PROG, p = 0.043) and sessions/year (26.1 IMPR - 15.4 PROG) were significantly different between groups. Cobb-IN TH (35.8ο IMPR - 37.3ο PROG, p = 0.594), PI (50.6ο IMPR - 47.3ο PROG, p = 0.151), PT (10.6ο IMPR - 8.5ο PROG, p = 0.088), SS (40ο IMPR - 37.8ο PROG, p = 0.274) and family history (57.5% IMPR - 50% PROG, p = 0.554) were not significantly different between groups. Conclusion: Higher IBC and compliance with brace and SSE (also more supervised sessions), later growth stage, single curve types and lower BMI were associated with better outcomes. Initial Cobb angles, pelvic parameters and family history did not significantly affect the result. Our study showed that many prognostic factors are associated with treatment outcome and should be carefully monitored for improving prognosis.

## ID: AGS26/2541

## P0815: Effects of GLP-1 receptor agonist medications on spinal fusion surgery outcomes: systematic review

Khaled Elsousi

1

, Mohamed Emara

1

, Ahmad Alilo

1

, Yasir Al-Humairi

2

, Muhammed Ali Najah

1

, Abdulla Sherzad

1

, Khaled Almansoori

2

1

College of Medicine, University of Sharjah, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates

2

Trauma and Orthopedics Surgery, Rashid Hospital, Dubai, United Arab Emirates

Introduction: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists (GLPRAs) are a novel class of antidiabetic drugs. Such drugs help achieve adequate glycaemic control and aids in weight loss. Recent basic science literature that GLPRAs have osteoclast and immune modifying effects, which spiked interest on their effect on arthroplasty, and arthrodesis. However, its effect on spinal fusion remains unclear. To systematically review the current literature on the clinical and radiological outcomes of the safety and efficacy profile of GLPRAs on patients undergoing spinal fusion. We also aim to report these outcomes in different patient populations such as non-diabetics. Material and Methods: A comprehensive literature search was conducted among four databases including PubMed, Scopus, Embase, and Web of Science (WOS). Keywords that included but were not limited to “GLP-1”, “arthrodesis”, and “spinal fusion”. Results: Twelve retrospective studies met our inclusion criteria. Most studies synthesized data from the global health network TriNetX. Most studies focused on lumbar fusions, where cervical vertebras were less commonly studied. Overall five out of six papers reporting pseudarthrosis rates showed favourable outcomes in GLPRAs users where only one study showed inferior results. In both obese and non-obese patients with diabetes subgroups, GLPRA therapy was linked to fewer infections, readmissions, and revision surgeries. However, among obese patients without diabetes, prolonged GLPRAs use > 9 months before and after surgery, was associated with higher rates of urinary tract infection, acute kidney injury, and additional lumbar fusion procedures. Conclusion: Current evidence suggests that GLPRA use among specific patient populations may improve fusion-related outcomes during lumbar spine procedures. However, prolonged use may cause considerable side effects. Future prospective and randomised trials are needed to confirm these findings and help guide practice.

## ID: AGS26/2563

## P0816: Adolescent upper thoracic kyphosis: a 24-month prospective cohort study on the effects of antikyphotic exercises and screen-time reduction

Yiğit Önaloğlu

1

, Furkan Akbaş

2

, Mehmet Ali Talmac

1

1

Başakşehir Çam and Sakura City Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Istanbul Provincial Directorate of Health, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Upper thoracic kyphosis (C7-T5) in adolescents has been increasingly recognized, often linked to prolonged smartphone and computer use. This study aimed to prospectively evaluate the clinical and radiographic outcomes of screen-time reduction and a standardized antikyphotic exercise program in patients stratified by sagittal balance. Materials and Methods: A total of 110 adolescents (10-18 years, postmenarcheal, Risser 3-5) were enrolled; 100 completed 24 months of follow-up. Inclusion required a C7-T5 angle ≥25° on standing lateral radiographs. Patients with congenital anomalies, neuromuscular disorders, kyphoscoliosis, prior spine surgery, trauma, or connective tissue disease were excluded. No neurological deficits were detected on examination. Daily screen time (phone/computer) was assessed using validated questionnaires. All participants were advised to reduce screen time by 50%, verified at one-month intervals. Sagittal balance was classified by C7-S1 sagittal vertical axis (SVA): ≤ 40 mm defined the balanced group (n = 60), while > 40 mm defined the imbalance group (n=40). The imbalance group received a standardized program of antikyphotic foam roller exercises, performed at home twice weekly for 1 hour. Families confirmed adherence with monthly checklists; mean compliance was 90%. Clinical and radiographic evaluations were performed every six months. Improvement was defined as ≥ 5° reduction in C7-T5 angle, deterioration as > 5° increase, and changes within ±4° were considered stable. Results: Baseline C7-T5 kyphosis averaged 31.2 ± 3.4° in the balanced group and 31.6 ± 3.1° in the imbalance group (p=0.49). Cervical lordosis (C2-C7) and thoracic kyphosis (T5-T12) were similar between groups (p > 0.40). Mean daily screen time decreased from 6.5 ± 1.0 to 3.3 ±0.9 h in the imbalance group and from 6.2±1.1 to 3.0 ± 0.8 h in the balanced group, with no significant difference in reduction (p = 0.68). In the balanced group, mild improvement or stable alignment was observed across all patients, with no deterioration. In the imbalance group, 12-month outcomes showed 70% improvement, 20% stable, and 10% deterioration. For deteriorating cases, exercise duration was doubled (2 hours, twice weekly); by 24 months, these patients achieved stability. Final distribution was 70% improved and 30% stable. VAS scores were significantly higher at baseline in the imbalance group (p < 0.05) but improved significantly at follow-up (p < 0.01), narrowing the gap with the balanced group. Conclusion: Reducing screen time was beneficial for all adolescents with upper thoracic kyphosis, while the addition of antikyphotic exercises provided greater benefit in patients with sagittal imbalance. Increasing exercise intensity stabilized initially worsening cases. These findings suggest that lifestyle modification combined with a structured exercise program can limit progression and potentially reduce the need for surgical intervention in skeletally mature adolescents.

## ID: AGS26/2585

## P0817: Enhancing motor function in pediatric cerebral palsy through transcutaneous spinal cord stimulation: systematic review of current evidence

Omar Alomari

1

, Halit Alioglu

2

1

Hamidiye International School of Medicine, University of HealthSciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Dr. Lutfi Kirdar Kartal Training and Research Hospital, University of HealthSciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Cerebral palsy (CP) is the most common motor disorder in childhood, leading to spasticity, muscle weakness, impaired coordination, and functional limitations. Conventional interventions including physical therapy, medications, and surgery often have limited efficacy or adverse effects. Transcutaneous spinal cord stimulation (tSCS) is a non-invasive neuromodulatory technique that may enhance sensorimotor integration and voluntary movement in pediatric CP populations. This systematic review aimed to synthesize current evidence on the effects of tSCS on motor function, spasticity, postural control, and neuromuscular coordination in children with CP. Material and Methods: A comprehensive search of PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science was conducted up to July 2025. Eligible studies included observational and interventional trials assessing spinal neuromodulation in pediatric CP populations. Data extraction and methodological quality assessment were independently performed using NIH tools for observational studies and Cochrane RoB 2 for randomized trials. Results: Seven studies (128 participants, ages 2-16 years, GMFCS I-V) were included. tSCS was delivered via various devices, targeting thoracolumbar and cervical spinal segments, often combined with task-specific training. Across studies, tSCS consistently improved gross motor function (GMFM-88 gains exceeding minimal clinically important differences), enhanced postural control and voluntary movement, and reduced spasticity (MAS/Tardieu scores). Functional improvements were observed across GMFCS levels, including transitions from non-ambulatory to independent walking in some participants. Neuromuscular coordination improved with decreased co-contraction indices, and interventions were well tolerated with only minor transient adverse events reported. Limitations included small sample sizes, heterogeneous protocols, and short follow-up periods. Conclusion: tSCS is a promising, safe, and feasible adjunct to conventional rehabilitation for children with CP, demonstrating clinically meaningful improvements in motor function, postural control, and spasticity. Future high-quality, controlled studies are needed to standardize protocols and confirm long-term efficacy.

## ID: AGS26/2848

## P0818: Intravenous acetaminophen reduces opioid use and improves outcomes in single- and multi-level lumbar fusion

Gregory Paschal

1

, Fedan Avrumova

1

, Philip Paschal

1

, Sydney Warwick

1

, Deven Bhatnagar

1

, Ellen Soffin

1

, Joseph Nguyen

1

, Andrew Sama

1

, Frank Cammisa

1

, Darren R. Lebl

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

Introduction: Opioid analgesics are a common choice for the management of post-operative pain subsequent to spinal surgery. Opioid use is associated with an array of side effects, often contributing to increased morbidity and extended hospital stay. Spine surgery is the largest medical contributor to opioid abuse, followed by orthopaedics overall. Combined, spine and orthopaedics account for over one fourth of all cases of opioid abuse stemming from prescription to previously opioid-naïve patients. In pursuit of diminishing opioid consumption, alternatives such as intravenous (IV) acetaminophen have demonstrated considerable potential. An assessment of patient outcomes with the perioperative administration of IV versus oral (PO) acetaminophen may demonstrate the value of such medications in the reduction of opioid usage in lumbar spine surgery. The aim of this study is to prospectively evaluate clinical outcomes for patients undergoing lumbar fusion with the administration of either IV or oral acetaminophen perioperatively, with supplemental opioids as needed. We hypothesize that the use of IV acetaminophen will provide significant pain management, thereby reducing hospital stay, opioid consumption, and comorbidity, and that it will do so more effectively than oral acetaminophen. Material and Methods: Under IRB approval, consented patients were randomized prior to surgery into either IV or PO acetaminophen groups. Patients underwent lumbar circumferential fusion with anterior interbody fusion and posterior pedicle screw fixation at one or two levels. Preoperative baseline surveys including demographics, VR-12, Visual Analog Score (VAS) Pain, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and medication use were collected. The first dose of the assigned acetaminophen was administered within 3 hours prior to surgery. Postoperatively, 7 additional doses were given every 6 hours, with access to supplemental opioids as needed. Morphine equivalent opioid consumption was calculated daily until discharge, 6 weeks, and 6 months post-operatively. VAS scores were recorded daily until discharge, 6 weeks, and 6 months post-operatively. ODI was recorded at 6 weeks and 6 months post-operatively. Statistical analysis was performed comparing outcomes in each group. Results: Eighty-three patients (36 male, 47 female) were prospectively randomized and enrolled in the study. Demographics between groups were similar, however the IV group was statistically older (60 ± 8.85 IV, 55.3 ± 6.58 PO, p = 0.024). The length of stay was the same between groups. Daily analgesic morphine equivalent consumption was statistically significantly lower for the IV group than the PO group upon completion of the study doses (67.9 ± 53.47 IV, 94.3 ± 52.99 PO, p < 0.04) and remained statistically different through discharge (23.5±32.01 IV, 65.7±63.33 PO, p < 0.0005). VAS Back pain and VAS Leg pain scores were similar for both groups prior to discharge. ODI decreased similarly by Month 6. Conclusion: Although both groups (IV vs PO) reported similar clinical outcomes with regard to pain and disability, the IV acetaminophen group was able to achieve this reduction with statistically significantly less opioid usage. Although patients in the IV group were statistically older, they took significantly less MME to produce similar pain management results. Our findings suggest that IV acetaminophen is a safe and effective alternative to current opioid-based postoperative analgesic regimens and should be considered.

## ID: AGS26/2952

## P0819: A rare presentation of Bertolotti’s syndrome mimicking radicular sciatica: a case report

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

, Elouni Emna

2

1
Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, 
2
Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia
Introduction: Bertolotti’s syndrome is an underrecognized cause of chronic low back and radicular pain, resulting from lumbosacral transitional vertebrae (LSTV). It may be misdiagnosed as discogenic sciatica due to overlapping clinical presentations. We aim to present a case of Bertolotti’s syndrome mimicking L5 radiculopathy caused by a neo articulation between a hypertrophic L5 transverse process and the sacral ala. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 43-year-old woman with a six-year history of inflammatory left-sided L5 radiculopathy associated with hyperalgesia and radicular claudication, limiting ambulation to 300 meters. Neurological examination confirmed an L5 radicular syndrome. Initial imaging in 2017 showed a left L4-L5 disc herniation. A follow-up MRI in 2021 revealed a hypertrophic left L5 transverse process forming a pseudo. articulation with the sacral ala and iliac wing, associated with inflammatory and degenerative changes. Additional findings included disc bulging at L3-L4 and L4-L5 without significant canal stenosis. Results: The patient’s radiculopathy was attributed to both mechanical compression and local inflammatory changes from the neoarticulation. The atypical evolution and imaging prompted reconsideration of the initial diagnosis, leading to the identification of Bertolotti’s syndrome as the primary etiology. Management options were discussed, including surgical resection of the neoarticulation, but conservative treatment was maintained due to symptom stability. Conclusion: Bertolotti’s syndrome should be considered in chronic or atypical radicular presentations, especially when imaging reveals transitional vertebrae or neoarticulations. Early recognition allows for tailored management and avoids misdiagnosis of discogenic disease. Targeted imaging and a high index of suspicion are essential for accurate diagnosis.

## ID: AGS26/2964

## P0820: Safety and utilization trade-offs of spinal cord stimulation in postlaminectomy syndrome: a large matched-cohort analysis of revision surgery, systemic complications, and mortality

Muaz Wahid

1

, Zuhair Zaidi

1

, Salah Aoun

1

1

UT Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) is commonly used for post-laminectomy syndrome, but comparative safety and utilization outcomes beyond analgesia are unclear. We conducted a large matched evaluation of SCS versus non-SCS management, quantifying perioperative and 1-year benefits and trade-offs across surgical, systemic, and utilization endpoints, including absolute risk differences. Material and Methods: In a retrospective, multi-institutional matched-cohort analysis, adults with post-laminectomy syndrome were assigned to SCS or non-SCS cohorts. Propensity score matching across demographic and clinical variables yielded balanced groups of ∼22,000-26,000 patients per arm (varying by outcome). Prespecified endpoints were assessed at 90 days and 1 year: revision laminectomy, device-related reoperation, infection, pneumonia, deep-vein thrombosis (DVT), stroke, ulceration, depression/anxiety, emergency department (ED) visits, intensive care admission, opioid abuse, myocardial infarction (MI), acute kidney injury (AKI), counseling, and death. Effects were reported as odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) and p-values. Results: At 90 days, SCS was associated with lower odds of revision laminectomy (0.26% vs 1.36%; OR 0.19, 95% CI 0.14-0.25; p < 0.0001), ulceration (0.08% vs 0.24%; OR 0.32, 0.19-0.53; p < 0.0001), death (0.19% vs 0.38%; OR 0.51, 0.36-0.72; p < 0.0001), pneumonia (0.46% vs 0.62%; OR 0.74, 0.58-0.95; p = 0.018), stroke (0.19% vs 0.34%; OR 0.57, 0.40-0.81; p = 0.0014), and dural tear (0.07% vs 0.13%; OR 0.56, 0.32-0.98; p = 0.039); higher odds occurred for device-related reoperation (2.27% vs 0.19%; OR 12.24, 9.14-16.40; p < 0.0001), infection (1.08% vs 0.38%; OR 2.86, 2.26-3.61; p < 0.0001), depression (3.18% vs 2.31%; OR 1.38, 1.21-1.56; p < 0.0001), and ED visits (8.18% vs 7.44%; OR 1.10, 1.04-1.17; p = 0.0019); AKI, MI, DVT, suicide, antidepressant use, and ICU admission were not different. At 1 year, SCS remained associated with fewer revision laminectomies (1.02% vs 2.50%; OR 0.40, 0.34-0.47; p < 0.0001), lower ulcer risk (0.37% vs 0.73%; OR 0.51, 0.40-0.65; p < 0.0001), and lower mortality (0.93% vs 1.28%; OR 0.72, 0.61-0.85; p = 0.0001); benefits also appeared for pneumonia (1.58% vs 2.02%; OR 0.78, 0.68-0.89; p = 0.0003), DVT (0.90% vs 1.10%; OR 0.81, 0.68-0.97; p = 0.023), counseling (0.69% vs 0.96%; OR 0.72, 0.59-0.87; p = 0.0008), and dural tear (22 vs 41 events; OR 0.54, 0.32-0.90; p = 0.017). Trade-offs included markedly higher device-related reoperation (6.66% vs 0.31%; OR 22.69, 18.14-28.38; p < 0.0001) and higher stroke at 1 year (2.05% vs 0.78%; OR 2.68, 2.23-3.16; p < 0.0001). Infection at 1 year was not significantly different (0.19% vs 0.27%; p = 0.062). Conclusion: In real-world matched cohorts of post-laminectomy syndrome, SCS is associated with fewer revision decompressions, lower ulceration, lower pneumonia and DVT, and lower mortality, balanced against substantially higher device-related reoperations and a persistent 1-year stroke signal. The novel contribution is a large, decompression-specific analysis that delineates domain-specific benefits (reduced revision surgery and select systemic complications) alongside system-level trade-offs (device revisions and late stroke), to guide shared decision-making, inform peri-implant infection bundles, psychological screening, and proactive vascular-risk management, and to motivate prospective validation of the stroke finding with granular vascular covariates.

## ID: AGS26/3059

## P0821: Deep learning-based method to predict long-term outcome of Bracing in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Guilin Chen

1,2,3

, Jie Wang

1,2,3

, Jianguo Zhang

1,2,4

, Nan Wu

1,2,3

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Peking Union Medical College Hospital, Peking Union Medical College and Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, People’s Republic of China, Bejing, China

2

Beijing Key Laboratory of Big Data Innovation and Application for Skeletal Health Medical Care, Beijing, People’s Republic of China, Bejing, China

3

Key Laboratory of Big Data for Skeletal Deformities, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, People’s Republic of China, Beijing, China

4

Key Laboratory of Big Data for Skeletal Deformities, Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences, Beijing, People’s Republic of China, Bejing, China

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is a three-dimensional spinal deformity commonly managed with bracing to prevent curve progression and avoid surgery. However, individual responses to brace treatment vary markedly, influenced by factors such as skeletal maturity, initial curve magnitude, and compliance. The long-term effectiveness of bracing is crucial because the risk of progression begins to increase when the curve exceeds 30 degrees after skeletal maturity has been achieved. Existing prognostic models typically use non-AI methods or focus on short-term, binary outcomes, limiting their clinical utility for long-term, personalized decision-making. Material and Methods: We retrospectively collected data from 294 AIS patients treated at two tertiary centers. Standing full-spine radiographs were obtained at baseline (pre-brace) and immediately post-brace, along with clinical variables (age, sex, and Risser stage). A deep learning regression model was developed to predict Cobb angles at a minimal one-year follow-up. Through integration of spatiotemporal features and longitudinal feature fusion, it effectively captured patterns of disease progression. Rule-based post-processing informed by clinical insight further enhanced predictive accuracy. Performance was assessed via stratified three-fold cross-validation and external validation on another held-out cohort. Evaluation metrics included mean absolute error (MAE), root mean square error (RMSE), and coefficient of determination (R2). Model interpretability was explored using SHAP and Grad-CAM. Results: A total of 294 patients who met the inclusion criteria were recruited from two independent centers. T Cobb angles were similar between groups (24.86 ± 8.17° in cohort 2 vs. 26.51 ± 10.01° in cohort 1; p = 0.141), while TL/L angles were significantly higher in the cohort 2 (26.48 ± 7.17° vs. 23.78 ± 7.63°; p = 0.029, Mann-Whitney U test). Three-fold cross-validation was applied. The mean absolute error (MAE) of the algorithm using V0, V1, and clinical information as inputs were 4.06 for the thoracic curve (R2 = 0.811) and 3.75 for the thoracolumbar/lumbar curve, respectively. Such superiority was demonstrated by comparison with existing advanced DL architectures. Classic convolutional neural networks (CNNs), such as DenseNet, ResNet, and EfficientNet, achieved MAEs ranging from 5.2° to 6.1°, with corresponding R2 values between 0.59 and 0.73. Our model got the best performance. SHAP analysis identified pre-brace Cobb angles as the top predictor, while Grad-CAM highlighted the model’s focus on the application of the brace forces and the iliac crest. Conclusion: The deep learning-based algorithm developed in this study demonstrated strong predictive performance for thoracic and lumbar curve angles in patients with AIS undergoing bracing. By integrating radiographic and clinical data, the model offers a promising tool to support personalized treatment planning. Future research should validate its applicability across diverse populations and clinical settings and more data should be recruited.

## ID: AGS26/3297

## P0822: the role of acetazolamide in reducing morbidity after intraoperative CSF leaks in spine surgery: a retrospective cohort study

Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Jay I. Kumar

1

, Cesar Carballo

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

, Adolfo Viloria-Hidalgo

1

1

University of South Florida, Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak is a common risk of spinal surgery. Our study investigated the effectiveness of acetazolamide (ACZ) in preventing complications and changing the management of patients with intraoperative durotomies. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed all spinal surgeries performed at our tertiary care center from 2023-2024 (N = 955). Of these, we identified patients who presented with an intraoperative durotomy (N = 94) and compared outcomes of patients who were treated postoperatively with ACZ 250 mg TID (N = 17) to those who were not (N = 77). Endpoints included wound-related complications, postoperative length of stay, readmission and reoperation rates, and flat bedrest requirements. Results: Wound dehiscence was observed in 0% of ACZ-treated patients vs 3.8% of non-ACZ-treated patients (p = 0.411; Figure 1). The readmission rate was 0% for ACZ-treated patients vs 2.5% for the non- ACZ-treated group (p = 0.505); the reoperation rate was 0% vs 1.3% (p = 0.625), respectively. 6% of the ACZ-treated group was placed on flat bedrest postoperatively vs 20.3% of the control group (p = 0.154). Among patients who did not undergo primary durotomy repair, those treated with ACZ spent an average of 4 fewer days in the hospital postoperatively compared to patients not treated with ACZ (8.11 vs 12.03 days, respectively; p = 0.149). Conclusion: Among patients presenting with intraoperative durotomy during spine surgery, those treated with ACZ experienced a lower rate of wound-related complications, flat bedrest placement, readmission, and reoperation, as well as shorter postoperative LOS. The latter was especially true for patients in which a primary durotomy repair was not achievable. Even though these findings were not statistically significant due to small sample size, our study shows that ACZ could serve as an effective adjunct in the treatment of intraoperative CSF leaks, particularly in patients with complicated durotomy not amenable to primary repair.

 

## ID: AGS26/3450

## P0823: Novel objective-subjective pain assessment score results in decreased opioid prescription after elective spine surgery: a prospective pilot study

Dia Halalmeh

1

, Yusuf Ansari

2

, Arwa Alabide

3

, Ashra Mirza

4

, Hazem Eltahawy

5

1

University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics, Iowa City, United States

2

Temple University, Philadelphia, United States

3

University of Kufa, Kufa, Iraq

4

Enhance Center for Interventional Spine and Sports, Livonia, United States

5

Central Michigan University Medical School, Saginaw, United States

Introduction: Opioid dependence after spine surgery is a major contributor to the opioid epidemic in the United States. Current pain assessment tools are largely subjective, linking patient satisfaction to reported pain and encouraging liberal opioid prescribing. Incorporating objective criteria into pain evaluation may improve prescribing practices while maintaining patient satisfaction. Methods: The OBSUB scale, a 10-point tool combining subjective pain scores (points 1-5) with objective signs (points 6-9: autonomic activation, avoidance behaviors, postural guarding, and distraction) plus a point for pain exaggeration, was implemented in 44 patients over 3 months. Postoperative analgesics were prescribed progressively according to the WHO analgesic ladder, with emphasis on non-opioid alternatives and staff education. Mean daily Morphine Milligram Equivalents (MME) were recorded for four intervals: 30 days preoperatively, and 0-30, 31-60, and 61-90 days postoperatively. Results: Compared to institutional pre-intervention averages, mean daily MME decreased by 53.2% in the first postoperative month (p = .30), 32.2% in the second month (p = .0040), and 91.8% in the third month (p = .0085). At 90 days, 76% of patients were no longer prescribed opioids compared to 48% prior to intervention. Despite more restrictive prescribing, patient satisfaction increased by 27% based on Hospital Consumer Assessment of Healthcare Providers and Systems (HCAPHS) scores. Conclusions: The OBSUB scale, integrating objective with subjective pain measures, effectively reduced postoperative opioid prescriptions without compromising patient satisfaction. This approach offers a promising strategy to mitigate opioid dependence after spine surgery and warrants validation in larger multicenter trials.
