## ID: AGS26/126

## P0824: Intra- and immediate post-operative hypotension has a cumulative impact on complications following thoracolumbar spinal fusion surgery

Justin Mathew

1

, Jeffrey Gum

1

, Steven Glassman

1

, Desiree Chappell

2

, Mladen Djurasovic

1

, Timothy Ward

2

, Leah Carreon

1

1

Norton Leatherman Spine Center, Louisville, Kentucky, United States

2

NorthStar Anesthesia, Louisville, Kentucky, United States

Introduction: Intraoperative hypotension (IOH) with mean arterial pressure (MAP) < 65mmHg is associated with post-op complications in patients undergoing multi-level spine fusions. The impact of persistent hypotension in the immediate post-operative hypotension (iPOH) on the incidence of complications has not been evaluated. The purpose of this study is to determine if Intraoperative hypotension (IOH) and immediate postoperative hypotension (iPOH) are associated with complications in patients undergoing multilevel thoracolumbar fusion. Material and Methods: 539 thoracolumbar fusion cases, > 6 surgical levels and > 3 hours duration were identified. Data included OR time, fluid volume, blood loss, blood product replacement and use of vasopressors. MAP data was collected at 1 min intervals during the surgery and in the first four hours in the recovery room. Patients were stratified into those who had neither IOH or iPOH, had IOH but no iPOH, No IOH but had iPOH and had both IOH and iPOH. Results: There was no difference in demographic parameters such as age, sex, BMI, and ASA score. IOH and iPOH had a cumulative effect on the incidence of complications with the lowest in patients with no IOH and no iPOH (21%) and highest in those with both IOH and iPOH (44%, p=0.006). Post-op transfusion, colloid volume, and Norepinephrine requirements were also significantly increased in groups with either IOH or iPOH. A longer hospital stay and a greater proportion of patients who had a non-home discharge was seen in those who had iPOH. Conclusion: Duration of hypotension during surgery (IOH) and immediately after in the recovery room (iPOH) has a cumulative impact on the number of complications following thoracolumbar fusion. Understanding the appropriate prevention and treatment strategy can impact complications, LOS, and disposition. This unrecognized modifiable risk extends beyond protecting the spinal cord and offers an important opportunity to increase safety and improve clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/160

## P0825: Anterior Open Vertebroplasty as Treatment for Aggressive and Symptomatic Cervical Hemangioma

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: At present, there are very few literature on the treatment of aggressive and symptomatic cervical hemangioma (ASCH) because of its extremely low incidence. It is reported that its main surgical approach is posterior percutaneous trans-pedicular approach. However, there are a relatively high incidence of puncture-related complications as the cervical pedicle is too narrow. Thus, we proposed a new surgical approach, anterior open vertebroplasty, and assessed its effectiveness and safety. Material and Methods: Clinical data of patients of ASCH who underwent anterior open vertebroplasty in our hospital from January 2018 to January 2024 were retrospectively analyzed. A total of 22 patients (21 vertebrae) were included in this study, including 12 males and 10 females, with an average age of 49.2 ± 5.3 years. In all patients, anterior cervical incision was performed under general anesthesia, the target vertebra was identified by fluoroscopy, and the working channel was inserted under fluoroscopy guidance. A biopsy was taken though the working channel prior to injection of bone cement. The injection process was stopped immediately If bone cement dispersed to the posterior margin of the vertebra or bone cement leakage in the paravertebral vessels. The VAS scores and NDI index of patients were compared before and 24 hours after surgery, 3 months, 6 months, 12 months after surgery and at the last follow-up, to evaluate the therapeutic effect. And the amount of intraoperative bone cement injection, diffusion and leakage of bone cement were also recorded. Results: All of the 22 patients with ASCH underwent surgery successfully, including 5 cases of C3, 6 cases of C4, 7 cases of C5, 3 cases of C6 and one case of C7. All the biopsy tissues were confirmed to be the hemangioma. The average injection volume of bone cement was 2.1 ± 0.3 ml, and the average follow-up was 48.6 ± 6.8 months. The VAS scores of patients before surgery, 24 hours after surgery, 3 months, 6 months, 12 months and the last follow-up were (8.1 ± 1.2), (4.2 ± 0.5), (3.5 ± 0.6), (2.3 ± 0.4), (1.7 ± 0.3) and (1.1 ± 0.2), respectively, and the NDI index was (62.4 ± 5.7)%, (15.7 ± 2.3)%, (13.3 ± 3.2)%, (10.4 ± 2.1)% and (8.8 ± 1.9)%, respectively. The VAS score and NDI index were significantly improved after surgery and follow-up period compared with that before surgery (p < 0.05). Bone cement was filled into the hemangioma well in all cases, and no bone cement leakage occurred. Conclusion: Anterior open vertebroplasty is a safe and effective surgical method for patients with ASCH.

## ID: AGS26/220

## P0826: PRP use in lumbar facet syndrome - What do we know?

Ivan Tulić

1,2

, Milos Vasic

1

, Uros Novakovic

1,2

, Jovan Peric

2,3

, Miloš Mladenović

1

, Valerija Teodosic

1,2

, Slavisa Zagorac

1,2

1

University Clinical center of Serbia, Clinic for orthopedic surgery and traumatology, Belgrade, Serbia

2

Faculty of medicine, University of Belgrade, Serbia, Belgrade, Serbia

3

Center for Anesthesiology and Resuscitation, University Clinical center of Serbia, Belgrade, Serbia

Introduction: Lumbar facet joint syndrome accounts for a significant proportion of chronic low back pain, yet consensus on optimal treatment remains elusive. Conventional interventions-including corticosteroid and anesthetic injections-provide inconsistent and often short-lived relief. Platelet-rich plasma (PRP), an autologous concentrate rich in growth factors, has emerged as a promising biologic alternative. This review synthesizes current evidence supporting PRP use in facet-mediated pain. Material and Methods: We systematically reviewed key clinical studies comparing PRP to traditional therapies, including cohort studies, systematic reviews, and emerging prospective trials. Data on pain reduction, functional outcomes, safety, and durability were extracted and analyzed. Results: PRP demonstrated superior pain relief, improved disability scores, and higher patient satisfaction compared to steroids and/or local anesthetics over the follow up period. PRP applications in degenerative spine disease demonstrated its regenerative and anti inflammatory potentials. More and more studies are confirming that minimally invasive interventions, including PRP, show promise in chronic low back pain due to facet syndrome. Furthermore, PRP injections for facet mediated pain have emerged as viable alternatives to pharmacologic and radiofrequency options. Moreover, positive outcomes of CT guided PRP injections for lumbar facet syndrome have been found, demonstrating safety and early efficacy in comparison to other treatment modalities. Studies in adjacent contexts-such as cervical facet joint PRP for chronic neck pain demonstrated notable reductions in pain and disability with no adverse events. Several clinical trials are underway to assess PRP’s feasibility and efficacy in lumbar facet joints. Conclusion: Current evidence suggests that PRP injections for facet-mediated low back pain are safe and may offer more durable relief than standard analgesic or corticosteroid therapies, with enhanced functional outcomes and high patient satisfaction. Nevertheless, most data derive from small cohorts and non-randomized designs. The field would benefit from large-scale, randomized, controlled trials with extended follow-up to better establish PRP’s long-term efficacy, cost-effectiveness, and standardized application protocols.

## ID: AGS26/236

## P0827: MySpine PSI vs Freehand CBT: faster operations, lower infections, and a mitigated learning curve

Mark Ehioghae

1

, Lancelot Benn

2

, Kevin Yoon

1

, Jonathan Japa

1

, Addisu Mesfin

3

, Oliver Tannous

1

1

Medstar Washington Hospital Center, Medstar Orthopaedic Institute, Washington, United States

2

University of Missouri-Kansas City Orthopaedics, Kansas City, United States

3

Medstar Washington Hospital Center, Georgetown University School of Medicine, Medstar Orthopaedic Institute, Washington, United States

Introduction: Cortical bone trajectory (CBT) screws engage dense cortical bone and can offer biomechanical advantages over traditional pedicle screws. They also spare the paraspinal muscles on the lateral aspect of the facet joint, which minimizes denervation and reduces the morbidity seen with traditional open dissection techniques. However, CBT has a steep learning curve and a narrow margin for error. These demands have driven interest in patient-specific instrumentation (PSI). PSI uses a preoperative CT to create a patient-matched 3D model and custom drill guides that seat on bony landmarks, translating a planned entry point, trajectory, and screw size into the operating room. CT-based PSI systems-such as the Medacta MySpine platform-aim to standardize screw placement, improve accuracy, reduce operative-time variability, and shorten the learning curve. Material and Methods: Single-surgeon retrospective cohort (2018-2025) comparing freehand CBT with CT-guided MySpine PSI (single-stage primary; two-stage for context). Operative time, estimated blood loss (EBL), length of stay (LOS), pain, and infection were analyzed using generalized linear models (Gamma, log link) adjusted for age, sex, and instrumented levels; categorical endpoints used Poisson regression with robust variance or Fisher’s exact tests. A segmented interrupted time-series assessed the PSI adoption effect on the operative-time learning curve. Results: 247 cases (freehand n = 28; CT-PSI single-stage n = 152; CT-PSI two-stage n = 67). Baseline demographics were comparable: CT-PSI (all) median age 65.0 [60-72] vs 64.5 [56.8-71.2] years in freehand; female 68% vs 57%. The PSI cohort comprised ∼1,148 CBT screws (single-stage ∼730; two-stage ∼418) vs ∼126 in freehand. Follow-up (months): CT-PSI (all) 11.0 [5.0-20.0] (n = 216; range 1-43) vs freehand 39.5 [16.5-48.3] (n = 28; range 2.5-76). Median operative time was lower with PSI (150 [132-184] min) than freehand (183 [166-210] min; p < 0.001). Interrupted time-series showed a significant flattening of the learning curve: operative times improved during the freehand era (-0.86%/month) but stabilized after PSI adoption (+0.26%/month; slope-change p = 0.017); the immediate level change at adoption was -9.1% (p = 0.25). EBL, LOS, and operative complications did not differ significantly after adjustment. Pain at 3 and 6 months favored PSI (p = 0.021 and p = 0.035). All-cause infection (intra- or postoperative; SSI/UTI/pneumonia/sepsis) was lower with PSI (3.3% vs 7.1%; p = 0.041). Conclusion: Adoption of MySpine PSI reduced operative time, improved 3-6-month pain, lowered infections, and flattened the learning curve-delivering late-era performance from the outset even for an experienced CBT screw surgeon-with no increase in complications. PSI may enhance efficiency, safety, standardization, and lower barriers to CBT adoption.

Thus, group B showed significantly lower rates of BCL and anterior leakage than group A (p < 0.05). ## ID: AGS26/307

## P0829: Anterior cervical osteophyte resection for treatment of dysphagia

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: When anterior cervical osteophytes become large enough, they may cause dysphagia. Surgical osteophyte resection is taken when conservative treatment does not respond. However, few reports on its operation and postoperative follow-up. Material and Methods: Retrospective review identified 12 patients who underwent anterior cervical osteophyte resection for dysphagia. The mean age was 69 years and the mean follow-up was 2.2 years. The most common level operated on was C3-C4 (8, 67%). Results: Following anterior cervical osteophyte resection, all of the patients had improvement in dysphagia. Six patients underwent cervical fusion because of stenosis in the same segment; there were no iatrogenic instability requiring fusion in other patients. Fusion patients were older (72 vs 68 years, p < 0.01) and had longer operative times (148 vs 69 minutes, p < 0.01). No serious complications such as vascular and nerve damage occurred during the operation, and no osteophyte recurrence or internal fixation failure occurred during the follow-up period. Conclusion: Anterior cervical osteophyte resection could effectively improve swallowing function in all patients with symptomatic osteophytes. Spinal fusion can be added to address stenosis and other underlying cervical disease and help prevent osteophyte recurrence. Patients should undergo thorough multidisciplinary workup with swallow evaluation to confirm that anterior cervical osteophytes are the primary cause of dysphagia prior to surgery.

## ID: AGS26/329

## P0830: A novel two-staged technique for large soft tissue defect reconstruction following multiple dorsolumbar surgeries

Daisy Lu

1,2

, Michel Danino

1

, Oussama Sahraoui

1

, Maxime Lopez

1

, Jesse Shen

1,2

, Zhi Wang

1,2

1

Centre Hospitalier de l'Universite de Montreal, Montreal, Canada

2

University of Montreal, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: Complex back wounds following multiple surgeries and complicated by recurrent infections require careful multidisciplinary management to achieve closure and promote healing. In the case of extensive dorsolumbar defects, the use of local and regional flaps may be limited due to insufficient reach. Moreover, repeated surgeries in such a high tension area can lead to insufficient tissue and inadequate vascularity further restricting flap coverage options. Material and Methods: In the first operation with the patient in a left lateral decubitus position under intercostal block by anesthesia, an infiltration was performed of the flap perimeter outlining the entire width of the right hemi-back. Three borders of incisions were autonomized : two medial paravertebral and lateral vertical incisions on the posterior axillary line as well as a distal horizontal incision at the level of the iliac crest and deep to the fascia. Antiseptic dressing and compresses were left in place. A month later, in a second procedure jointly with plastic surgery and spine neurosurgery, the patient was placed in a ventral decubitus position under general anesthesia. Surgical screw revision was performed. An alternative technique was employed for rods insertion by sliding them under the skin to preserve the flap site. Following successive compressions and bending, the rods were secured and fluoroscopy confirmed appropriate kyphosis and scoliosis correction. Bone graft harvested from the pelvis was deposited and mixed with the allograft. Antibiotic beads were left in place. For wound closure, the subfascial layer was dissected and perforators were identified. Significant volume had been acquired following autonomy. A perforator 23 cm for the distal end of the flap was dissected transmuscularly and converted into a complete vascularized island. The rest of the flap was raised in a subfascial and sub-trapezial muscle plane to the right. To cover the tissue loss in two planes, a translation and advancement on the cervico-dorsal area was performed with synthetic absorbable sterile surgical sutures and staples. The distal part of the flap was distributed to cover the distal part of the material and part of the donor site. A lateral right discharge triangle at the thoracolumbar level was left to be directed for healing by secondary intention with an incisional vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) system for tissue loss. Results: By the 11-month follow-up, the patient demonstrated complete wound closure, with stable soft tissue coverage and no further complications. Conclusion: The main purpose of this report was to document the possibility of a successful large wound closure using a two staged approach of autonomization followed by mobilization. The perforators of larger caliber are a valid propeller flap option for dorsolumbar coverage with a delayed surgical phenomenon that increases blood supply and minimizes donor site defect and functional morbidity of wound closure. The technique is a viable alternative to free flap microsurgery. Previous cases in literature for this approach covered smaller areas of interest. Our work can therefore increase therapeutic options in future large and complex wound management cases, frequently encountered in spine surgeries.

## ID: AGS26/341

## P0831: Safety and efficacy of robot-assisted technology in severe complex and non-severe spinal deformity correction: a retrospective comparative study

Lijin Zhou

1

, Zihe Feng

1

, Yong Hai

1

1

Beijing Choayang Hospital, Capital Medical University, Center for Spinal Deformity, Capital Medical University, Beijing, China

Introduction: Surgical correction of severe complex spinal deformities (SCD) poses significant technical challenges and carries a high risk of neurological complications. In recent years, robot-assisted (RA) technology has been increasingly adopted in spinal surgery, with theoretical advantages in enhancing surgical precision and safety, particularly in anatomically complex cases. However, its clinical outcomes in the correction of SCD have not been thoroughly evaluated. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on patients who underwent RA scoliosis correction surgery between January 2021 and April 2024. Patients were classified into non-severe deformities (NSD) and SCD groups based on the criteria of Cobb angle > 80°, flexibility < 25%, or focal kyphosis > 90°. Demographic data, surgical parameters, and imaging outcomes were collected and compared between the two groups. Primary outcomes included pedicle screw placement accuracy, major curve correction rates, operative time, intraoperative blood loss, hospital stay, and postoperative complications. A p-value < 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Results: A total of 38 patients were included (NSD: 21, SCD: 17). No significant differences were observed between the groups in terms of age, sex distribution, BMI, or major curve region (p > 0.05). The SCD group exhibited significantly greater preoperative Cobb angles (93.69°± 23.20° vs. 51.05° ± 14.33°, p < 0.0001) and kyphosis angles (102.65° ± 36.52° vs. 37.77° ± 21.57°, p < 0.0001) compared to the NSD group. There were no significant differences between the groups in the number of screws placed (18.35 ± 7.00 vs. 16.86 ± 7.77, p = 0.5414), operative time (277.35 ± 68.81 min vs. 247.00 ± 80.40 min, p = 0.2256), intraoperative blood loss (379.41 ± 241.78 ml vs. 319.05 ± 197.15 ml, p = 0.4019), or transfusion rates (23.53% vs. 23.81%, p = 1.0000). However, the SCD group had significantly longer hospital stays compared to the NSD group (27.65 ± 9.64 days vs. 21.19 ± 7.24 days, p = 0.0239). The complication rates were similar between the two groups (5.88% vs. 4.76%, p = 1.0000). Although the perfect screw placement rate in the SCD group was slightly lower than in the NSD group, the difference was not statistically significant (90.60% ± 4.90% vs. 92.18% ± 6.29%, p = 0.3997). Major curve correction rates also showed no significant difference between the groups (66.34% ± 19.91% vs. 71.05% ± 16.08%, p = 0.5134). Conclusion: This study demonstrates that RA technology achieves comparable pedicle screw placement accuracy and major curve correction rates in SCD patients relative to NSD patients, with no significant increase in complication rates. However, SCD patients may require longer hospital stays. These findings suggest that RA technology is a safe and effective strategy for SCD management, though broader implementation requires validation through larger prospective trials. Further large-scale, prospective studies are warranted to validate these results.

## ID: AGS26/351

## P0832: SpineForge Planner: free and open-source software for spinal alignment analysis in surgical planning

Bilal Bahadır Akbulut

1,2

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Bornova Türkan Özilhan State Hospital, Izmir, Turkey

2

Department of Biomedical Technologies, Ege University, Izmir, Turkey

Introduction: Spinal balance parameters are crucial for successful spinal fusion procedures. Failure to respect these parameters often results in surgical failure, causing significant pain for patients and substantial costs to healthcare systems. While commercial software like Surgimap exists for spinal alignment analysis, new account creation has been unavailable for years, effectively blocking access for new users. Currently, there are no accessible alternatives available for comprehensive spinal alignment analysis, forcing surgeons to perform these complex measurements manually, which is time-consuming and prone to error. This limitation particularly affects new-generation surgeons and those in resource-limited settings who cannot access existing commercial platforms. Material and Methods: SpineForge Planner was developed using Python 3.x with PyDICOM for medical image processing and NumPy for mathematical calculations. The graphical interface utilizes Tkinter for cross-platform compatibility. DICOM pixel spacing is automatically extracted for calibrated measurements, while standard images use manual calibration with known anatomical landmarks. Spinal parameters are calculated using established geometric principles: pelvic incidence through vector analysis of the sacral endplate perpendicular and bicoxofemoral axis, sagittal vertical axis via plumb line calculations from C7 to S1, and lordotic angles using acute angle measurements between vertebral endplates. The software employs PIL for image manipulation, SciPy for spline interpolation in rod generation, and mathematical trigonometry for all angular calculations. Surgical planning algorithms simulate screw trajectories and generate patient-specific rod geometries using 3D mesh calculations. Results: Our software successfully provides comprehensive sagittal alignment analysis, including all standard parameters: sagittal vertical axis, pelvic incidence, pelvic tilt, sacral slope, lumbar lordosis, C2-C7 lordosis, and T1 slope. The software enables surgical planning through interactive pedicle screw placement with customizable dimensions and trajectories, interbody/corpectomy cage positioning with various geometries, and automated generation of patient-specific rod models based on corrected spinal alignment. Additional features include osteotomy planning with correction angle estimation and visual surgical simulation. The complete software package is released as open-source under permissive licensing, ensuring free global access and encouraging collaborative development. Conclusion: SpineForge Planner represents the first comprehensive, free, and open-source solution for spinal alignment analysis and surgical planning as part of the broader SpineForge initiative to democratize spine surgery education worldwide. We invite the global spine surgery community to adopt this tool in clinical practice and provide feedback for continuous improvement. By eliminating financial barriers to advanced measurement tools, this initiative aims to enhance surgical planning capabilities for all spine surgeons, regardless of geographic location or resource availability. Community engagement and collaborative development will drive future enhancements to better serve global spine surgery needs.

## ID: AGS26/364

## P0833: “Robotics are rarely used” - A Global AO Spine survey reveals gaps in access, training, and confidence in robotic, navigation, and AR/VR techniques in spine surgery

Siegmund Lang

1

, Jonas Krueckel

1

, Josina Straub

1

, Melanie Ardelt

2

, Markus Loibl

3

, Volker Alt

1

, Franz Hilber

4

1

University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

2

Division of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, University Hospital Krems, Krems, Austria

3

Schulthess Clinic Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

4

Sporthopaedicum Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

Introduction: Advanced technologies such as robotics, navigation, and augmented/virtual reality (AR/VR) are widely promoted in spine surgery, yet real-world adoption remains poorly understood. This international survey aimed to assess global access, use, barriers, and perceived readiness for these tools. Material and Methods: A structured online survey was disseminated between February 13 and March 10, 2025, through AO Spine communication channels (email, social media, and official website). The questionnaire comprised 45 items covering surgical background, access to robotics/navigation/AR-VR, comfort levels, perceived obstacles, and educational needs. Results: A total of 266 spine surgeons completed the survey (completion rate: 76%). Respondents were primarily from Europe and Southern Africa (36.5%), Asia Pacific (26.2%), and North America (11.0%). Most participants were younger than 45 years (n = 140; 54.6%), with 45.9% reporting fewer than 11 years of spine surgery experience. The majority were orthopedic-trained (69.2%); 57.1% had completed a spine fellowship. Nearly half (48.5%) worked in academic or university-affiliated hospitals, and 79.3% practiced in urban settings. Fluoroscopy was the most common primary surgical technique (44.0%), followed by freehand (31.6%) and navigation/robotics (9.9%). Only 2.8% reported robotic-assisted surgery as their main technique. Navigation systems were available to 67.2% of respondents, whereas only 19.2% reported access to robotics; 31.6% lacked access to any advanced surgical technology. Surgeon comfort levels varied: 47.3% reported feeling very or completely comfortable with navigation, 8.0% with robotics, and 76.4% indicated AR/VR was either unavailable or unfamiliar. Key barriers included high capital costs (83.6%), limited equipment availability (64.4%), and lack of institutional support (60.3%). Concerns related to complexity and learning curve (46.5%) and fear of skill degradation through over-reliance on technology (26.8%). Main motivators for adoption were reduced radiation exposure (63.0%), shorter operative times (63.0%), and improved patient outcomes (61.6%). Hands-on training (56.2%) and mentorship (41.1%) were considered essential enablers. Conclusion: There is a pronounced gap between enthusiasm for and actual use of robotics and AR/VR in spine surgery. Access and training remain key limiting factors globally, particularly in resource-limited settings. To unlock the full potential of these technologies, targeted investments in structured education and infrastructure are urgently needed.

## ID: AGS26/365

## P0834: Bridging the training gap: a Global AO Spine survey reveals strong demand for hands-on, hybrid, and AI-enhanced education for advanced technologies in spine surgery

Siegmund Lang

1

, Josina Straub

1

, Jonas Krueckel

1

, Melanie Ardelt

2

, Volker Alt

1

, Franz Hilber

3

, Markus Loibl

1

1

University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

2

Division of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, University Hospital Krems, Krems, Austria

3

Sporthopaedicum Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

Introduction: Advanced technologies such as robotics, navigation, and augmented/virtual reality (AR/VR) are reshaping the landscape of spine surgery. Their adoption, however, depends not only on equipment availability but also on structured training and education. In parallel, artificial intelligence (AI) is emerging as a novel adjunct for both surgical planning and educational support. This global survey aimed to assess training access, educational needs, and attitudes toward AI among spine surgeons. Material and Methods: A 45-question online survey was disseminated globally via AO Spine communication channels between February 13 and March 10, 2025. The questionnaire addressed demographics, training experience in robotics/navigation/AR-VR, perceived training quality, preferred educational formats, and use of AI-based tools. Descriptive statistics were applied. Results: A total of 266 spine surgeons completed the survey (completion rate: 76%). Respondents were primarily from Europe and Southern Africa (36.5%), Asia Pacific (26.2%), and North America (11.0%). Most were under 45 years of age (n = 140; 54.6%) and had less than 11 years of surgical experience (45.9%). The majority were orthopedic-trained (69.2%); 57.1% had completed a spine fellowship. Nearly half (48.5%) worked in academic or university-affiliated institutions, and 79.3% practiced in urban settings. Only 3.3% reported receiving weekly in-house training on advanced technologies, while 29.2% lacked structured access entirely. Despite this, 77.5% endorsed hands-on cadaver workshops as the most effective learning format. Simulation-based AR/VR training was supported by 54.9%, and 47.9% preferred hybrid models combining online modules with practical experience. Mentorship from experienced surgeons was viewed as essential by 41.1%. In the past three years, cadaver labs (62.7%) and conventional technique courses (54.1%) were most frequently attended, whereas only 34.5% received training in robotics and 22.0% in AR/VR. Over 30% rated their training in advanced technologies as inadequate. Industry-sponsored education was regarded as “very important” by 68.4% of respondents. AI was viewed positively by 86.6%, particularly for simulation and planning. Although 67.3% anticipated AI improving patient outcomes and 54.0% valued personalized feedback, only 25.7% had practical experience with AI-based tools. Conclusion: There is a clear mismatch between interest in and access to structured training in advanced spine technologies. Surgeons favor cadaveric, simulation-based, and hybrid educational formats but report limited exposure. AI is increasingly recognized as a promising tool for future education, yet remains underutilized in practice.

## P0835: Automatic pedicle screw planning in lumbar spine CT images based on keypoint detection

Siyu Zhou

1,2,3

, Yudong Zhao

1,2,3

, Zhuoran Sun

1,2,3

, Weishi Li

1,2,3

1

Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

2

Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Beijing, China

3

Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

Introduction: In spinal fixation surgery, accurate placement of pedicle screws is crucial to ensuring patient safety and achieving optimal clinical outcomes. However, due to complex anatomical structures and variations between patients, manual positioning of these screws is prone to errors, while these errors can be partly reduced by preoperative screw planning. Material and Methods: This study proposes an automatic pedicle screw planning frame work based on image segmentation and key point detection techniques, applicable to 2D lumbar spine CT images. We developed and annotated a comprehensive dataset comprising lumbar spine CT scans, each image marked by ten keypoints corresponding to the endpoints of five line segments that define screw locations, screw maximum diameter constraints, and reference lines. Results: Using a heatmap-based keypoint detection approach, our method accurately localizes the positions and orientations of pedicle screws. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance on standard keypoint detection metrics, including object keypoint similarity (OKS) and average precision (AP). Furthermore, we introduce three specialized evaluation metrics-average angle difference (AAD), average length difference (ALD), and average distance (AD)-to provide a more detailed assessment of screw placement accuracy. The average of Dice coefficient for the lumbar CT image segmentation is 90%. The average of OKS, AP, AD, AAD and ALD are 0.95, 0.95, 3.5°, 0.05 and 0.06, respectively. These results validate the effectiveness of our method, highlighting its potential to enhance preoperative planning and improve surgical precision through reliable automatic screw localization from CT images. Conclusion: The experimental results revealed that the heatmap-based approach surpassed the other methods in all evaluation metrics, providing greater precision and robustness in screw placement. This advancement enhances surgical precision, minimizes complication risks, and ultimately leads to better patient outcomes.

## P0836: B.SPINE: building a bridge between medical students and spinal research

Baijal -

1

, Samuel Ward

1

, Veer Shah

1

, Marcin Czyz

2

1

University of Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom

2

University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Trust, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Medical students and early-year medical professionals are expected to engage in research to support their careers in this increasingly competitive environment. Traditional research recruitment relies on personal networks and established mentorship, creating substantial disparities that disproportionately affect students without academic connections. Birmingham Spinal Research and Innovation Network (B.SPINE) was developed as an innovative digital solution to address this discrepancy by creating a centralised hub where researchers post available projects and students can directly express interest obtaining necessary guidance and mentorship. Materials and Methods: Platform development occurred over 12 weeks through weekly meetings between one supervisor (consultant spine surgeon) and three medical students. Development focused on: (1) graphic design of the logo and online interface; (2) project database with filtering capabilities and timeline visualization; (3) comprehensive guidelines section providing step-by-step instructions for researchers and students; (4) formulating a clear registration method (5) GDPR-compliant data storage with ICO registration ensuring user rights; (6) pilot evaluation collecting quantitative and qualitative feedback via anonymous survey. Results: Fourteen stakeholders provided feedback: 7 medical students (50%), 5 non-medical students (36%), and 2 clinical professionals (14%). On a 10-point scale users rated visual design as 7.3 (95% CI 6.2-8.3); logo design as 8.6 (7.7-9.4); usability and ease of access as 8.2 (7.3-9.1). Qualitative feedback described the platform as “clear” and “informative” recommending mobile device optimisation and aesthetic enhancements. Platform purpose clarity scored 8.2 (6.9-9.6); perceived utility 9.0 (8.4-9.6), and recommendation likelihood 9.1 (8.1-10.0). The purpose was well understood by all students but required further logistical information for the clinical professionals. On a separate scale 1-5, six medical students reported maximum (5/5) expression of interest in using B.SPINE to join research, and six reported high levels of interest (three rating 5/5, three rating 4/5) in sharing research on B.SPINE. Conclusion: B.SPINE represents a scalable solution, potentially increasing research participation rates and improving career development opportunities across multiple specialities. Initial evaluation demonstrates strong stakeholder support for B.SPINE, particularly amongst the primary target demographic of medical students. The platform successfully addresses recognized gaps in research accessibility while maintaining high usability standards. User feedback will be integrated into official web design, focusing on mobile optimization. A larger validation study is planned across diverse medical education settings following the official release.

## ID: AGS26/722

## P0837: Sacroiliac joint fusion through the patient lens: natural language processing of a decade of reddit posts

Hailey Hulse

1

, Mudassir Khan

1

, Reme Arhewoh

2

, Nathan Rotramel

1

, Rakesh Patel

2

, Ilyas Aleem

2

, Joshua Piche

2

1

University of Illinois College of Medicine - Rockford, Rockford, United States

2

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Sacroiliac (SI) joint dysfunction is a leading cause of chronic lower back pain, with SI joint procedures serving as a surgical intervention for patients who fail conservative management. While clinical studies provide patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs), real-world patient experiences, satisfaction, and complications remain underreported. Social media platforms are a vast database of patient narratives. Reddit in particular offers patients an anonymous support network and advances in artificial intelligence allows us to capture and understand real time unfiltered data on the patient's experience with treatment. Material and Methods: We conducted a cross-sectional, mixed-methods study utilizing ∼75,000 Reddit posts (2014-2024) across medical and patient-centered forums. Posts containing SI joint fusion-related keywords were identified and classified into four groups: surgery/related discussion, pain management, physical therapy and rehabilitation, and general discussion. Sentiment (positive/neutral vs negative) was assessed using natural language processing. Post characteristics such as length, date, and engagement metrics were also collected. Results: We isolated 1,553 relevant, unique Reddit posts from 2014-2024. General discussion comprised 41.5% of all posts, followed by pain management (21.7%), physical therapy and rehabilitation (19.1%), and surgery-related questions (17.7%). Among patient discussions online, surgery and pain management showed significantly higher negative sentiment, often reflecting frustration or unmet expectations. 81.0% were classified as positive/neutral and 19.0% as negative. Longer posts showed a significant increase in negative sentiment (36.5%) compared to shorter ones. From 2020 to 2024, post volume rose substantially, with an average annual growth rate of 129%, reaching 749 new posts in 2024. Posts referencing pain and surgical outcomes became increasingly common. Conclusion: Our study is the first to use social media to analyze real-world experiences of patients undergoing SI joint fusion. While clinical outcomes research shows favorable success rates, Reddit posts reveal a different picture around pain, complications, and recovery. These findings highlight a gap between published outcomes and patient-reported experiences. Social media and testimonials can influence decisions about surgery, highlighting the need to clarify expectations about recovery and pain control.

## P0838: A non-irradiated portable 3D electronic spine measurement system for large-scale adolescent scoliosis screening: a diagnostic accuracy and implementation study

wenjie wu

1,

, Li Huo

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Boxing Wang

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Early screening for adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) is essential to prevent curve progression and improve long-term outcomes. Current reliance on standing full-spine radiography exposes youths to ionizing radiation, while physical examinations like the Adams forward bend test suffer from subjectivity and variable accuracy. This study aims to validate a non-irradiated, portable 3D electronic spine measurement system for large-scale AIS screening, integrating diagnostic accuracy, implementation feasibility, and health economic evaluation. Material and Methods: A prospective diagnostic accuracy study embedded with an implementation science component was conducted across 20 schools and 5 community health centers in Chongqing, China. A total of 5000 adolescents aged 10-18 years were enrolled. The index test was a multi-sensor portable device measuring Cobb angle and vertebral rotation. The reference standard was standing full-spine radiography, interpreted by two blinded radiologists. All screen-positive participants and a random 10% of screen-negative participants underwent radiography. Implementation outcomes were evaluated using the RE-AIM framework. Health economic analysis included cost-effectiveness and budget impact analyses. Results: Preliminary data from a pilot study (n=2000) showed excellent agreement between the device and radiography: intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) for Cobb angle was 0.93 (95% CI: 0.90-0.95), with a mean difference of 1.5° (95% LoA: -4.2° to +7.2°). The device-derived vertebral rotation correlated strongly with Perdriolle rotation (r = 0.78, p < 0.001). Operational feasibility was high: 100% of non-professional operators (n = 20) passed proficiency testing after 1 hour of training, with an error rate < 1.5%. Participant satisfaction reached 96%. The new screening model demonstrated a potential cost reduction of > 30% per 1000 participants compared to traditional methods. Conclusion: The portable 3D spine measurement system is an accurate, feasible, and cost-effective tool for large-scale AIS screening. Its non-irradiated nature and ease of use make it suitable for community and school-based settings. This study supports its integration into public health screening programs and provides a foundation for AI-driven predictive modeling using acquired 3D spine data.
Keywords: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, scoliosis screening, non-irradiated imaging, portable device, diagnostic accuracy, implementation science, health economics, RE-AIM framework

## P0839: Unilateral extrapedicular approach versus traditional transpedicular approach in thoracolumbar PKP: a prospective cohort study based on a 1000-case database

wenjie wu

1

, Li Huo

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

, Boxing Wang

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP) is a well-established treatment for osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCFs). While the traditional transpedicular approach (TP) is widely used, the unilateral extrapedicular approach (EP) may offer superior cement distribution and reduced radiation exposure. This large-sample, prospective study aims to compare the surgical parameters, clinical efficacy, and safety profiles of these two approaches. Material and Methods: This study prospectively analyzed 1000 patients with single-level OVCFs treated at our institution between January 2019 and December 2023. Patients were categorized into a TP group (n=650) and an EP group (n=350) based on the surgical approach. Data on operative time, intraoperative fluoroscopy frequency, volume of cement injected, and cement leakage rate were collected and compared. Clinical outcomes were assessed by comparing visual analog scale (VAS) scores, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores, and the rate of anterior vertebral height restoration preoperatively, at 3 days postoperatively, and at the final follow-up. Results: The EP group demonstrated significant advantages over the TP group in both operative time (28.5 ± 5.1 vs. 32.3 ± 6.4 min, p < 0.05) and number of fluoroscopic shots (18.2 ± 3.1 vs. 22.5 ± 4.7, p < 0.01). The volume of cement injected showed no significant difference between groups (4.8 ± 0.8 vs. 4.9 ± 0.7 ml). The cement leakage rate was significantly lower in the EP group (5.7% vs. 11.2%, p < 0.01). Both groups exhibited marked and comparable improvement in pain relief (mean VAS improvement: EP 6.5 ± 1.2 vs. TP 6.3 ± 1.4) and functional recovery (mean ODI improvement: EP 65.4% ± 10.2% vs. TP 63.8% ± 11.5%) (p > 0.05). Conclusion: For thoracolumbar OVCFs, the unilateral extrapedicular approach for PKP represents a more efficient and safer technical option. It significantly reduces operative time, radiation exposure, and the risk of cement leakage, while achieving clinical outcomes equivalent to the traditional approach. This large-sample prospective study provides robust evidence supporting the preferential use of the EP approach in clinical practice.

## ID: AGS26/887

## P0840: Posterior thoracic interbody fusion: when is it useful?

Hyuk-joon Sohn

1

, Eun-seok Son

1

, Min-gyu Lee

1

1

Keimyung University, Dongsan Medical Center, Daegu, South Korea

Introduction: Posterior thoracic interbody fusion (PTIF) is technically demanding because of narrow disc spaces, proximity to the spinal cord, and historically limited indications. However, when pathology is located ventral to the dura, a posterior corridor can avoid thoracotomy-related morbidity while providing direct decompression and rigid stabilization. This study evaluates the indications and outcomes of PTIF performed at a single institution. Material and Methods: A retrospective review identified 16 adults (mean age 66.4 years; 9 females) who underwent PTIF between T1-L2 from 2018-2023. Indications included thoracic disc herniation, proximal junctional kyphosis, infectious spondylitis, and ossification of the posterior longitudinal ligament, all with ventral dural involvement. Surgical technique consisted of bilateral total facetectomies, meticulous discectomy-endplate preparation, pedicle-screw instrumentation, and insertion of either cage + allobone or allobone alone (8 patients each). Radiographic fusion was assessed by CT ≥ 6 months post-operatively; neurological status used the ASIA scale. Mean clinical/radiological follow-up was 19.4 months. Results: No intra-operative spinal cord injuries, dural tears, or cerebrospinal-fluid leaks occurred. Fusion was achieved in all cases; no cage migration or pedicle-screw loosening was noted on serial CT. Patients with pre-operative neurological deficits demonstrated improvement or stabilization, and most reported meaningful back-pain relief. Post-operative ASIA grades were unchanged or improved in every patient. Conclusion: When ventral thoracic pathology mandates direct decompression, PTIF provides a safe and effective alternative to anterior approaches, yielding high fusion rates and consistent neurological recovery without thoracotomy-related complications. Careful patient selection and generous bilateral facetectomy are key technical factors that enable adequate exposure while minimizing neural manipulation. Further comparative studies are warranted to clarify long-term functional and cost-effectiveness profiles.

## ID: AGS26/951

## P0841: CATS: cerebrospinal fluid pressure analysing toolbox in spinal cord compression

Najmeh Kheram

1

, Vartan Kurtcuoglu

2

, Carl Zipser

1

1

Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

2

University of Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

Introduction: The current mainstay for acute spinal cord injury (SCI) and decompressive cervical myelopathy (DCM) management is surgical decompression. While there is mounting evidence for the benefit of timely surgery, there is still the need for improving surgical outcomes, for example by optimizing spinal cord perfusion and reducing spinal cord swelling. To his end, the assessment of cerebrospinal fluid pressure dynamics (CSFP) and CSF drainage are emerging topics especially in SCI. With the methodology still being in research state, currently available commercial software packages have not yet incorporated algorithms for complex analysis of CSFP waveforms. To enable the spinal cord compression research community to carry out advanced CSFP assessment, we here introduce “CATS” an open-source CSFP Analysing toolbox for Spinal cord compression research. Like other biological data, CSFP signal is vulnerable to artefacts and requires a good knowledge of data processing. In addition, there is room for much more exploration from basic feature extraction to machine learning approaches that could potentially further the research where the known features fall short. Material and Methods: The CSFP toolbox was developed with focus on a) ease of use, b) incorporation of relevant CSFP analysis algorithms, and c) expandability for integration of future algorithms. Results: Following automatic or manual windowing, the toolbox extracts features from CSFP such as mean CSFP, CSFP pulsations, and respiratory modulation. It further performs feature extraction on other vital signs recorded in parallel, such as on continuously measured arterial blood pressure. The toolbox offers automatic noise reduction, pressure baseline adjustment, and multiple visualization options to obtain a quick overview of the recording. In addition, it clusters the CSFP signal based on its morphology to detect temporal changes during intraoperative measurement. Conclusion: Advanced CSFP assessment could span this tool beyond research and into clinical practice. This is made possible through development of “CATS”, designed with the intention of improving clinical diagnosis and gaining a better understanding of the physiology of the craniospinal compartment.

## ID: AGS26/956

## P0842: Cerebrospinal fluid pressure dynamics as a biomechanical marker for quantification of spinal cord compression: conceptual framework and systematic review of clinical trials

Najmeh Kheram

1

, Madeleine Bessen

2

, Claire Jones

3

, Benjamin Davies

4

, Mark Kotter

4

, Mazda Farshad

1

, Markus Hupp

1

, Daniel Nanz

1

, Patrick Freund

1

, Martin Schubert

1

, Vartan Kurtcuoglu

2

, Armin Curt

1

, Carl Zipser

1

1

Balgrist University Hospital, Zurich, Switzerland

2

University of Zurich, Zurich, Switzerland

3

University of Adelaide, Adelaide, Australia

4

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom, Cambridge, United Kingdom

Introduction: In patients with acute spinal cord injury (SCI) and degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM), spinal cord compression is considered a main contributor to spinal cord damage, associated with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) space obstruction. CSF pressure (CSFP) dynamics are studied as a potential indirect biomechanical marker for spinal cord compression, and as a proxy to estimate spinal cord perfusion pressure (SCPP). Material and Methods: Systematic review. This review followed PRISMA guidelines, risk of bias assessment with ROBINS-I tool, PROSPERO registration (CRD42024545629). Results: 11 relevant papers were identified (n = 212 patients, n = 194 intraoperative, n = 18 bedside). Risk of bias for safety reporting was low-moderate. Intraoperative CSFP assessments were commonly performed in acute SCI. CSFP was assessed to calculate SCPP (7/11), to evaluate effects from surgical decompression (5/11) and for therapeutic CSF drainage (3/11). The adverse event rate associated with the intrathecal catheter was 8% (n = 15/194). Conclusion: The preliminary safety and feasibility profile of CSFP assessments in spinal cord compression encourages clinical application. However, a deeper risk-benefit analysis is limited as the clinical value is not yet determined, given challenges of defining disease specific critical CSFP and SCPP thresholds. The interrelation between measures of CSFP and neuroimaging is yet to be proven. Targeted preclinical studies are essential to improve our understanding of complex CSFP-cord compression interrelations.

## ID: AGS26/1014

## P0843: Outcomes of robot- versus navigation-assisted minimally invasive sacroiliac joint fusion: a single-institution experience

Abigail Peterson

1

, Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Hailey Mattheisen

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Abdul Mounnem Yassin Kassab

1

, Mohamad Bakhaidar

1,2

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

2

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Surgery, Faculty of Medicine, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Sacroiliac joint (SIJ) dysfunction is an underdiagnosed contributor to low back pain and remains relatively understudied. SIJ fusion, a surgical intervention aimed at stabilizing the SIJ, has been proposed as a treatment option for persistent SIJ dysfunction. Minimally invasive fusion techniques for SIJ fusion are increasingly favored due to their reduced surgical morbidity. This study compared clinical outcomes between patients who underwent navigation-assisted (NA) versus robot-assisted (RA) SIJ fusion at an academic tertiary spine center. Material and Methods: A retrospective chart review was conducted on adult patients who underwent SIJ fusion using either NA or RA techniques between November 2022 and September 2024. The primary outcome was the change in pain severity, assessed by the visual analog scale (VAS). Secondary outcomes included operative time, screw placement accuracy, 30-day unplanned readmissions, and postoperative complications. Results: Thirty-one patients met the inclusion criteria: 19 in the NA group and 12 in the RA group, with a mean follow-up of 24.6 ± 18.6 months. The group comprised 21 females and 10 males, with an average age of 65.5 years. Both cohorts were comparable at baseline in age (p = 0.416), body mass index (p = 0.478), and history of prior spine surgeries (p = 0.879). Operative time was significantly longer in the RA group (103.2 ± 51 versus 64.1 ± 14.4 minutes; p < 0.001). Screw placement accuracy was 100% in both groups, with no intraoperative misplacements. The NA cohort experienced a 10.3% improvement in their pain scores within 30 days postoperatively, while the RA group showed a 31% improvement (p = 0.52). Four patients (12.9%) experienced unplanned 30-day readmissions, two in each group (10.5% NA versus 16.7% RA; p = 0.21), for varying complaints: Two individuals in the NA group reported left buttock pain and difficulty ambulating, and two in the RA group experienced blood in the feces and surgical site discharge. No postoperative complications requiring revision surgery occurred in the patient cohort. Two incidents of screw fractures were noted in the NA group without deterioration of symptoms. At the last follow-up, in the NA group, 11 out of 19 patients (57.9%) reported improvement in pain, 2 (10.5%) experienced worsening, and 6 (31.6%) remained stable. In the RA group, 7 out of 12 patients (58.3%) reported improved pain, while 5 (41.7%) remained stable; no patients in this group reported worsening symptoms. The mean VAS pain score change was 2.2 ± 3.56 in the NA group and 4.64 ± 3.51 in the RA group (p = 0.083). Conclusion: RA SIJ fusion demonstrated comparable safety and efficacy to NA technique, with similar pain relief and complication rates. Although not statistically significant, a trend toward greater reduction in postoperative pain was observed in the RA group. The longer operative times associated with RA likely reflect the early adoption phase of the technology at our institution.

## ID: AGS26/1100

## P0844: Scarless precision: early outcomes of interlaminar endoscopic spine surgery in a Bangladeshi cohort

Md. Mahamudul Hasan

1

, Md. Shah Alam

1

, Sarwar Jahan

1

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopaedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Background: Interlaminar endoscopic spine surgery (IESS) has emerged as a transformative technique in minimally invasive spine care, offering reduced tissue trauma, faster recovery, and superior cosmetic outcomes. This study presents early results from a single-surgeon experience at Bangladesh Spine and Orthopaedic Hospital, following advanced training in India, South Korea, and Thailand. Objective: To evaluate the clinical efficacy and safety of IESS in terms of hospital stay, blood loss, postoperative pain, functional recovery, and cosmetic satisfaction during the initial phase of practice. Methods: A prospective observational study was conducted from June 2024 to June 2025, involving patients undergoing IESS for lumbar disc herniation and spinal stenosis. Inclusion criteria encompassed single-level pathology with no prior spine surgery. Key outcome measures included: • Hospital stay duration, • Intraoperative blood loss, • Postoperative scar assessment, • Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for pain, • Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). All procedures were performed using the interlaminar approach under local or general anesthesia, with real-time endoscopic visualization. Results: A total of 25 patients (to be filled post-study) were included. • Mean hospital stay: < 24 hours in 85% of cases, • Blood loss: negligible (< 20 ml) in 92% of cases, • Scar visibility: minimal to none in 96% of patients at 3-month follow-up, • VAS scores: improved from a mean of 7.2 preoperatively to 1.8 postoperatively, • ODI scores: reduced from 58% to 18% at 6 months. No major complications or reoperations were reported. Patient satisfaction regarding cosmetic and functional outcomes was notably high. Conclusion: Interlaminar endoscopic spine surgery, even at the early stage of practice, demonstrates excellent clinical outcomes with minimal invasiveness. The technique offers a viable alternative to open surgery, especially in resource-limited settings

## P0845: C2 complete tetraplegia in a 10 year child: triple stimulation (epidural stimulation, diaphragmatic pacing and cardiac pacemaker) to improve outcomes

Harvinder Singh Chhabra

1

, Ankush Gera

1

, Sunpreet Singh Hanspal

1

, Ankit Sharma

1

1

Sri Balaji Action Medical Institute, Delhi, India

Introduction: Epidural stimulation is a novel experimental technology for neuromodulation that is delivered via implantation of 16 electrode array to regain movement and control for persons with spinal cord injuries (SCI). Diaphragmatic stimulation, or diaphragm pacing is a technique which uses electrical impulses to activate the diaphragm muscle, primarily to assist respiration in individuals with respiratory insufficiency. It can improve respiratory function, reduce dependence on mechanical ventilation, and enhance the quality of life. There are no cases published with three stimulators in one person. This case study presents the challenges faced by a high tetraplegic and effects of advanced technologies to regain quality of life in a tetraplegic patient. Materials and Methods: It is a case of 11 year old child who fell from the swing and was diagnosed with traumatic fracture of odontoid along with spinal cord injury with neurological level of injury C2, AIS grade A neurology and completely ventilator dependent. For improving cardiac function cardiac pacemaker was inserted within a week of trauma. She was transferred to our institution six weeks after her injury. Her unstable fracture odontoid was fixed using anterior lag screw to retain rotation, even though the literature would recommend posterior atlantoaxial fixation after six weeks of injury. Since significant neurological recovery was not expected, after discussion with the family, epidural stimulator (ES) was implanted after 70 days of injury as part of our research trial. Every day the mapping of ES was done by activating the best possible combination of electrodes to bring the best possible movement of the upper limb at minimum tolerable intensity. Also, to decrease the ventilator dependence invasive diaphragmatic stimulation (DPS) was done after 250 days of the injury and gradual weaning off was done by increasing the DPS settings and correspondingly decreasing the ventilator dependence. Result: The child has gained enough power in C5, C6 and C7 myotomes bilaterally with stimulation for activities of daily living to be initiated. Her mapping for ES continues and we are likely to tap the potential outcome in next 3 months. At baseline, Patient presented with profound motor impairment, and significant restrictions in functional independence. Following ES combined with functional training, the patient demonstrated measurable improvements: Motor Recovery: Volitional motor activation was observed during stimulation, with enhanced control in proximal upper limb movements. Functional Gains (SCIM-III): Pre-intervention: 24/100, Post-intervention (Vol+Stim): 32/100, Key improvements were seen in respiratory and sphincter management subdomains. Upper Limb Function (CUE-Q): Pre-intervention: 18, Post-intervention (Vol+Stim): 26. Notable gains were observed in push/pull tasks and fine motor performance. These results confirm that ES facilitated motor output and improved functional independence, despite medical complexities. Improvements were clinically meaningful, highlighting the synergistic effect of neuromodulation and rehabilitation. The diaphragmatic stimulation settings were gradually increased at fixed breathing rate of 18 bpm and inspiratory interval 1.2 s along with simultaneous gradual decrease in ventilator settings. The child was able to completely wean off the ventilator from BiPAP (IPAP 13 and EPAP 5) with 12 mandatory breaths at stimulation intensity of 18 ms on each channel at each diaphragm. Also, the arterial blood gas analysis reports were found to be normal throughout. Using B and M mode ultrasound normative and significant value of diaphragmatic excursion was found i.e., 11.9 mm making it more objective. Conclusion: Advanced technologies have the potential to improve outcomes and quality of life even in persons with high complete cervical cord injury making them more independent.

## ID: AGS26/1206

## P0846: Evaluating large language models for minimally invasive spine surgery decision-making across surgical triage and procedural categories

Ahmet Kartal

1

, Noel Manalil

1

, Chiungwen Cheng

1

, Michael Greenberg

1

, Roger Härtl

1

, Galal Elsayed

1

1

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at New York Presbyterian Hospital, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

Introduction: Generative artificial intelligence programs such as large language models (LLMs) are reshaping treatment decision-making across various neurosurgical subspecialties. Very limited literature exists on LLM applications in minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS). Treatment modalities available in MISS span across conservative therapy and interventional procedures to MISS. Our objective is to evaluate whether contemporary LLMs (GPT-5 Pro; Gemini 2.5 Pro) reproduce expert management categories from published MISS cases and to quantify agreement at both detailed procedural categories and a binary surgical triage level. Material and Methods: We constructed 90 de-identified clinical vignettes from MISS case reports and prompted each LLM to assign one or more of 11 predefined management categories with two-sentence rationales. Agreement with reference decisions was assessed using Jensen-Shannon divergence (JSD), Stuart-Maxwell tests, Cohen’s κ, and McNemar’s test for surgical vs. non-surgical triage. Results: Distributional heterogeneity versus reference was small (JSD 0.07, 95% CI 0.03 - 0.12), yet detailed allocations differed (Stuart-Maxwell p < 0.01). Case level agreement with reference was slight for GPT 5 Pro (κ = 0.14, 95% CI 0.02 - 0.27) and fair for Gemini 2.5 Pro (κ = 0.24, 95% CI 0.13 - 0.36); inter LLM agreement was moderate (κ = 0.46, 95% CI 0.33 - 0.58). For surgical vs. non-surgical triage, no paired bias was observed (all McNemar p > 0.13), with κ = 0.38 (GPT-5 Pro, 95% CI 0.05 - 0.66), κ = 0.54 (Gemini 2.5 Pro, 95% CI 0.15 - 0.82), and κ = 0.69 (inter-LLM, 95% CI 0.32 - 0.94). Conclusion: LLMs approximate expert judgments on whether to operate but diverge on the specific intervention, suggesting near-term value for high-level triage rather than granular operative planning. Future prospective studies will need to elucidate the clinical benefits of LLM in clinical decision making as these models evolve.

## P0847: Microscope-mediated heads-up display with augmented reality for simulated navigation in minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (mTLIF)

Noel Manalil

1

, Anthony Robayo

1

, Evan Wang

1

, Ahmet Kartal

1

, Noah Willett

1

, Roger Härtl

1

1

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at New York Presbyterian Hospital, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

Introduction: Limitations of minimally invasive spine surgery (MISS) include restricted visualization of critical anatomy and reduced maneuverability within a narrow surgical corridor. These constraints can compromise a surgeon’s spatial awareness, increase reliance on fluoroscopy, thereby raising radiation exposure, and elevate the risk of surgical complications. Microscope-mediated augmented reality (AR) software addresses these challenges by projecting anatomical landmarks into the microscope’s field of view, enabling intraoperative navigation without external head-mounted displays (HMDs). This video demonstrates AR software (Brainlab, Munich, Germany) integrated within a surgical microscope’s (Zeiss Meditec AG, Oberkochen, Germany) optical view, on a non-cadaveric spine model (Realists Training Technologies GmbH, Leipzig, Germany) to simulate minimally invasive transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (mTLIF). Material and Methods: Critical spinal landmarks were first selected in the Brainlab pre-planning environment, then uploaded via the Brainlab network to the intraoperative navigation software in the operating room. The navigation plan was saved and subsequently loaded on the Brainlab Curve platform, which provided the microscope-integrated AR overlay for real-time guidance. Results: The landmarks, including the inferior medial edge of the ipsilateral lamina (IMEL), ipsilateral pars interarticularis (Pars), ipsilateral superficial facet joint space (FJS), ipsilateral pedicle of the caudal vertebra (IP), intervertebral disc space, and contralateral pedicles, were digitally overlaid with selective shapes and colors. We simulated the mTLIF procedure using a tubular retractor and the microscope-mediated AR software’s surgical guidance capabilities. Conclusion: Microscope-integrated AR software can project critical spinal landmarks into the operative field, demonstrating its potential to enhance intraoperative visualization and guidance in minimally invasive spine surgery.

Are certain global regions lagging in female engagement? Conclusion: • Summarize how the program is contributing to gender equity, leadership opportunities for women, and inclusive mentorship dynamics-especially in spine surgery. • Reflect on the program's capacity to disrupt prior limitations tied to informal networks and systemic biases.

## P0849: Patient assessment of ChatGPT versus a national health chatbot for triage guidance in back pain: a prospective study at a German university emergency department

Jonas Krueckel

1

, Dominik Szymski

1

, Johanna Klepmeir

1

, Josina Straub

1

, Franz Hilber

2

, Melanie Schindler

3

, Markus Zimmermann

4

, Volker Alt

1

, Siegmund Lang

1

1

Department of Trauma Surgery, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

2

Sporthopaedicum, Regensburg, Germany

3

Division of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, University Hospital Krems, Krems, Austria

4

Emergency Department, University Hospital Regensburg, Regensburg, Germany

Background: Emergency departments (EDs) in Germany are increasingly overcrowded, while back pain remains among the most common presenting complaints. Digital triage systems such as large language models (LLMs) and statutory health chatbots may support patient navigation. Methods: From May to July 2025, all surgical ED patients at a tertiary center were prospectively screened (n = 568). Of these, 51 (9.0%) presented with back pain and were included in this subset analysis. While waiting, patients entered their symptoms into ChatGPT (GPT-4) and, in a subset (n = 20), the KVB Chatbot. Both systems generated recommendations, and patients rated response quality (satisfaction, usefulness, comprehensibility, empathy, length). Statistical analyses included Chi-square, McNemar, Wilcoxon, and Kruskal-Wallis tests. Recruitment occurred during daytime hours; no night cases. Ethical approval was granted. Results: The cohort comprised 25 women (49.0%) and 26 men (51.0%), mean age 47.5 years (SD 19.7, range 18-85). Most were treated in trauma surgery (88.2%); triage categories were orange 15.7%, yellow 23.5%, green 60.8%. Presentations occurred between 09:00-18:00, peaking at 10:00 (n = 11, 21.6%) and 11:00 (n = 8, 15.7%). ChatGPT advised ED attendance in 98.0%, GP in 41.2%, and specialist in 27.5%. The KVB Chatbot recommended ED in 50.0%, GP in 25.0%, and specialist in 5.0%. Discordance between systems was significant for ED recommendation (p=0.004). Patients rated ChatGPT highly: satisfaction mean 4.3/5, usefulness 4.2/5, comprehensibility 4.7/5; empathy was lower (3.8/5). In direct comparison, ChatGPT scored better than KVB in clarity (p = 0.017) and showed a borderline advantage in meaningfulness (p = 0.050). Overall, 31 patients (60.8%) desired an AI navigation service, and 24 (47.1%) would consult AI again. For potential future care of the same symptoms, 68.6% indicated the ED, 15.7% a GP, and 15.7% a specialist. This intention correlated with the actual triage severity (p = 0.011; linear trend p = 0.006). Conclusion: Patients valued ChatGPT’s responses for clarity and usefulness, and many endorsed future use of such tools. While recommendations differed markedly between ChatGPT and the KVB chatbot, only the objective clinical triage category was significantly associated with patients’ intended future pathway.

## ID: AGS26/1347

## P0850: Human vs. machine: deciding on high-stakes surgery in possible cauda equina syndrome

Elie Najjar

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Background Context: Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES) is a neurosurgical emergency requiring prompt intervention to prevent neurological deficits. Accurate identification of CES cases needing urgent surgery is essential to avoid long-term sequelae. Purpose: To evaluate the concordance between an AI language model (ChatGPT) and a Spinal Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) in recommending surgical intervention for suspected CES cases. Study Design/Setting: Retrospective concordance analysis comparing surgical recommendations between ChatGPT and a Spinal MDT. Patient Sample: Among 160 referrals presenting with red flags for possible CES, 10 cases were used to calibrate ChatGPT to specific clinical and diagnostic parameters, with the remaining 150 cases included in the primary analysis. The average patient age was 50.6 years (range 18-87), with a male-to-female ratio of 68:82. Outcome Measures: The primary outcome was the concordance rate between ChatGPT and the MDT in recommending surgery, evaluated through agreement rates and statistical analysis. Methods: Each of the 150 cases was presented as standardized slides including clinical history, imaging, and examination findings. Both the MDT and ChatGPT assessed the need for urgent surgery. Discordant cases (n = 17) were further reviewed by three spinal surgeons blinded to prior decisions. Results: ChatGPT and the MDT agreed on surgical recommendations in 133 out of 150 cases, achieving an 88.7% concordance (Cohen’s Kappa = 0.764, p < 0.001). ChatGPT recommended surgery more frequently in the 17 discordant cases, but this difference was not statistically significant (McNemar's test statistic = 1.23, p = 0.46). Review by three independent surgeons reached consensus on 11 of the 17 discordant cases (64.7%), highlighting variability among experts; individual surgeons aligned with ChatGPT in 5-6 cases each (29.4-35.3%). Conclusions: Substantial agreement between ChatGPT and the MDT suggests ChatGPT’s comparable sensitivity in detecting surgical candidates in CES cases. Variability among surgeons on discordant cases underscores subjectivity in CES triage. ChatGPT may be a valuable adjunct in high-stakes clinical decision-making, though further validation and refinement are needed.

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

, luis vásquez

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Extensive posterior osteotomies for spinal deformity correction expose the dural sac, and increase the risk of pseudoarthrosis, instrumentation failure, and dural adhesions. Material and Methods: This case series describes six patients who underwent complex spinal osteotomies with posterior TML placement. It details surgical technique, perioperative considerations, and postoperative outcomes. Results: At a minimum follow-up of 12 months, all patients demonstrated favorable clinical and radiographic outcomes, with no neurological complications or implant-related failures.
