Titanium mesh lamina is a viable adjunct in high-grade spinal osteotomies, offering neuroprotection and structural support. Further studies are needed to validate its long-term efficacy.

## ID: AGS26/1494

## P0852: Spinal cord stimulation for chronic pain secondary to post COVID large fiber neuropathy

Jorge Morales- del Angel

1

, Armando Díaz-Martínez

2

1
ISSSTELEON, ISSSTE Regional, Monterrey, Mexico, 
2
Hospital Universitario Dr. José E. González, Monterrey, Mexico
Introduction: Neuropathic pain is a recognized complication of COVID-19, with both small and large fiber neuropathies reported. Large fiber neuropathy can lead to significant disability, often presenting with sensory deficits and severe, treatment-resistant pain. Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) has emerged as a potential therapeutic option for refractory neuropathic pain. We report the case of a patient with post-COVID large fiber neuropathy who demonstrated significant pain relief following SCS implantation. Material and Methods: A 54-year-old male with a history of severe COVID-19 infection presented with persistent neuropathic pain in the upper and lower extremities. The pain, described as burning and stabbing, was refractory to multiple pharmacological interventions, including anticonvulsants, opioids, and tricyclic antidepressants. Neurological examination revealed diminished vibratory sensation and proprioception, consistent with large fiber involvement. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies confirmed a post-COVID large fiber neuropathy. The patient’s pain was classified as refractory, significantly impairing his daily activities and quality of life. Given the chronic and intractable nature of his symptoms, a trial of spinal cord stimulation was proposed. Two electrodes were implanted: a 5x6x5 lead at the thoracic T9-T10 level and a 2x8 lead at cervical C5-C7 level. Results: Post-COVID neuropathies, though increasingly recognized, remain a challenging entity with limited treatment options. Large fiber neuropathy, in particular, lacks established guidelines for intervention. Spinal cord stimulation has been successfully used in other neuropathic pain syndromes, such as diabetic neuropathy and post-surgical pain syndromes, and appears to be a promising option in post-COVID cases. The mechanism of SCS in neuropathic pain relief includes modulation of dorsal column pathways, inhibition of central sensitization, and restoration of normal pain processing. In this case, the dual-lead approach targeting both cervical and thoracic regions provided comprehensive pain relief, highlighting the importance of tailored neuromodulation strategies. Following the permanent implantation, the patient reported a substantial reduction in pain scores, improved functional status, and decreased reliance on analgesic medications. Conclusion: This case demonstrates the potential of spinal cord stimulation as an effective treatment for chronic pain secondary to post-COVID large fiber neuropathy. Further studies are needed to establish the role of neuromodulation in this emerging patient population.

## ID: AGS26/1717

## P0853: Longitudinal analysis of PROMIS physical function and pain scores following SI joint fusion: a two-year follow-up study

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Krupa Patel

1

, Kirsten Mansfield

1

, Aayush Unadkat

1

, Elias Schwartz

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: The demand for sacroiliac (SI) joint surgery has increased due to accumulating evidence supporting its efficacy. However, the outcomes following SI joint fusion are not yet fully understood. The Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System (PROMIS) offers standardized assessments of physical function (PF) and pain, making it a valuable tool for evaluating recovery trajectories after SI joint fusion. Material and Methods: This study retrospectively analyzed data from 140 consecutive patients undergoing SI joint fusion surgery at a tertiary care center. PROMIS PF and Pain scores (T-scores, mean = 50, SD = 10) were collected preoperatively and at designated postoperative intervals: 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, 1 year, and 2 years. Higher PF scores represent better physical function, while higher pain scores indicate greater pain severity. Patient satisfaction at the final follow-up and demographic data (age, sex, BMI, and comorbidities) were also evaluated. Follow-up beyond one year was limited as patients typically concluded care after recovery. Results: The study cohort consisted of 75 females (53.6%) and 65 males (46.4%), with a mean age of 55.8 years (range 27-83). Most patients were White (92.1%). PROMIS PF improved from baseline (35.0) to 1 year (38.2), with initial decline at 2 weeks (30.8) followed by steady improvement. PROMIS Pain showed improvement from baseline (66.7) to 6 months (62.3) and 1 year (63.7). Prior lumbar fusion predicted worse outcomes at 1 year (PF: 36.6 vs 39.6, p = 0.052; Pain: 61.3 vs 65.3, p = 0.049). Higher preoperative PF predicted better outcomes at 6 months (PF: 43.3 vs 33.3, p = 0.046) and 1 year (PF: 41.9 vs 34.8, p = 0.046). Lower preoperative pain predicted improved function at 6 weeks (PF: 38.9 vs 34.6, p = 0.051) and 6 months (PF: 47.3 vs 34.5, p = 0.048). The overall satisfaction rate was 79.1%, with no significant differences across demographic or comorbidity subgroups, though osteoporosis showed a trend toward lower satisfaction (65.6% vs 83.2%, p = 0.100). Conclusion: SI joint fusion demonstrates meaningful improvements in PROMIS Physical Function and Pain scores through 1 year. Recovery was evident and maintained as early as 6 weeks out from surgery. Prior lumbar fusion and preoperative functional status are significant predictors of outcomes. While overall satisfaction is high, the presence of osteoporosis may influence satisfaction rates. These findings may help inform patient selection and set appropriate expectations for postoperative recovery trajectories.

 

## ID: AGS26/1736

## P0854: Reimagining surgical consent in the AI era: a quality assessment of informed consent generator for spine surgery

Ali Kaplan

1

, Begüm Aslantaş Kaplan

1

, İlker Solmaz

2

1

Tokat Erbaa State Hospital, Tokat, Turkey

2

Private Practice, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Traditional informed consent forms in spine surgery often utilize standardized, generic language that fails to address individual patient characteristics and educational backgrounds. This practice can lead to inadequate patient comprehension and ncreases medico-legal risks for surgeons by potentially invalidating the consent itself. The advent of Large Language Models (LLMs) presents an opportunity to address these challenges. Context-engineering, is a methodology of structuring an LLM's operational environment with a specific role, workflow, and a curated knowledge base to ensure reliable and contextually appropriate outputs. This study aimed to develop and evaluate a context-engineered LLM for creating personalized, evidence-based consent forms and to quantitatively compare their quality against standard national templates. Material and Methods: We employed Google's NotebookLM, a state-of-the-art LLM with built-in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) capabilities, which allow dynamic retrieval of high-quality literature. The model was supplied with a curated knowledge base including AO Spine and NASS clinical practice guidelines, and systematic reviews and meta-analyses published within the last five years relevant to the selected pathologies. A detailed meta-prompt was engineered to instruct the model on its role, workflow, and the principles of generating high-quality consent forms. Five common spine surgery procedures were selected: unilateral biportal endoscopic lumbar discectomy, open cervical microdiscectomy, posterior instrumented fusion for lumbar spinal stenosis, posterior fossa decompression for Chiari malformation, and excision of foramen magnum meningioma. For each procedure, AI-generated consents were created for three educational levels (elementary, high school, university) and varying age groups, resulting in 45 personalized consent documents These were compared against corresponding National Neurosurgical Society standard consent forms using three evaluation metrics: readability assessment (Flesch Reading Ease and Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level), informed consent quality criteria developed by Spatz et al., and a novel personalization scoring system assessing patient-specific and procedure-specific content integration. Results: AI-generated forms demonstrated significantly superior performance across all metrics (p < 0.001). Regarding readability, AI forms had substantially higher Flesch Reading Ease scores (mean 75.3 vs. 45.2) and dynamically aligned Flesch-Kincaid Grade Levels to each persona. In contrast, standard forms consistently required a college-level education (mean grade 14.8). On the Spatz et al. quality checklist (max score: 20), AI forms achieved near-perfect scores (mean 19.2), while standard forms scored significantly lower (mean 14.5). The most significant disparity was observed in the Personalization Score (max score: 20). AI forms effectively integrated patient-specific data, achieving a mean score of 19.5, whereas standard forms scored poorly in comparison (mean 11.5). Conclusion: Context-engineered LLM systems can produce highly personalized, readable, and comprehensive informed consent documents that significantly outperform traditional standardized forms across multiple quality metrics. The integration of evidence-based medical knowledge through RAG architecture ensures clinical accuracy while maintaining patient-specific relevance. By enhancing patient comprehension and creating a more robust, personalized document, this approach offers a powerful tool to foster shared decision-making and strengthen the medicolegal integrity of the informed consent process in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1755

## P0855: Cervical spine diagnostics with generative AI: a comparison of OpenEvidence 2.0 and ChatGPT-4o

Fedan Avrumova

1

, Michael Cesar

1

, Giuseppe Loggia

1

, Klemenz Strebel

1

, Rafael Castro

1

, Jiaqi Zhu

1

, Celeste Abjornson

1

, Christopher M. Bono

2

, Darren R. Lebl

1

1

Hospital for Special Surgery, New York, United States

2

Harvard Medical School, Boston, United States

Introduction: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly applied in spine care, but concerns remain regarding accuracy, sourcing, and hallucinated references. ChatGPT has shown limitations in citation validity and guideline alignment, whereas OpenEvidence (OE) is designed to prioritize verified, evidence-based content. This study represents the first comparison of OE and ChatGPT using cervical spine clinical guidelines (CSCGs). Material and Methods: A cross-sectional comparative analysis was performed using 110 evidence-based clinical questions derived from 10 CSCGs authored by six academic societies. Responses were generated by OE (v2.0) and ChatGPT-4o, with a subset repeated using standardized prompts to assess prompt-engineering effects. Primary outcomes were guideline alignment score and citation validity. Secondary outcomes included source distribution, publication year, susceptibility to hallucinations, and additional prompting. Results: OE produced 999 citations, all verified as accurate, compared with 393 from ChatGPT, of which only 184 (46.8%) met accuracy criteria (p < 0.001). In analyses limited to a subset of CSCGs, OE achieved a significantly higher mean guideline alignment score compared with ChatGPT (4.6 ± 0.8 vs 4.1 ± 0.7; p = 0.03). ChatGPT generated 88 partially hallucinated citations (22.4%), most commonly due to incorrect hyperlinks (34.3%), author names (27%), or publication years (24%). Compared with ChatGPT, OE cited a greater proportion of peer-reviewed articles (79.8% vs 60.1%) and fewer non-journal sources (p < 0.001), with citations that were, on average, more recent (2018 ± 5.6 vs 2012 ± 7.4; p < .001). In the prompt-engineering analysis (n = 824 citations), OE contributed a larger proportion of citations (63.7% vs 36.3%; p < 0.001), maintained higher validity (89.5% vs 71.4%; p < 0.001), and demonstrated lower rates of partial (4.0% vs 18.6%) and full hallucinations (6.5% vs 10.0%). OE’s initial 100% accuracy declined to 89.5% when outputs were reformatted through ChatGPT-4o, suggesting cross-model contextual contamination. Conclusion: OE demonstrated superior CSCG concordance, citation validity, and sourcing quality compared with ChatGPT, with substantially lower susceptibility to hallucinations. As AI platforms evolve, physician oversight remains essential to ensure AI outputs are applied accurately, safely, and in alignment with evidence-based standards, reinforcing their role as stewards in integrating AI into patient care.

## P0856: Comparative analysis of vertebral augmentation in osteoporotic compression fractures: vertebroplasty versus kyphoplasty versus stenting: our experience

Kannan Karthik Kailash

1

1

Sri Ramachandra Higher Institute for Research For Research and Education, Indian Institute of Technology Madras India, Chennai, India

Introduction: Osteoporosis is a most common metabolic bone disease commonly associated with aging. Fractures in spine is complication of the on going metabolic pathology. It’s management includes two aspects i.e. treatment of Ongoing osteoporosis and treatment of fracture. Fracture management in osteoporosis is dubious, it ranges from conservative to Percutaneous vertebral augmentation to Instrumented Stabilisation. Augmentation Procedures are now popular option for management of Osteoporotic Compression fractures, but dilemma lies in choosing the most appropriate procedure. Aim: To analysis the factor affecting the selection of Augmentation procedure for Osteoporotic compression fractures. Materials and Method: Study type retrospective analysis. Duration of study August 2022 to May 2024. Place of Study: Sri Ramachandra Institute Of Higher Education and Research, Porur, Chennai. Inclusion criteria: Symptomatic Osteoporotic Compression Fractures after at least 2 weeks of Conservative treatment. Exclusion criteria: Non Symptomatic fractures, Fractures with Neurological deficit. Functional Parameters observed: VAS and ODI. Radiological parameters observed: segmental kyphosis, Focal kyphosis, Anterior Vertebral Body Height and Posterior Vertebral Body Height. Results: 120 patients (73 females and 47 male) under vertebral augmentation procedures out of which 57 had Vertebroplasty, 23 had Kyphoplasty and 40 had implantable device (vertebral body stent). None of the patients had revisions. All had equal functional improvement in immediate post op i.e. VAS Score of 4. Implantable device had excellent radiology improvements i.e. mean Height restoration of 31.7%, mean fracture kyphosis improved by 40.9%, mean segmental kyphosis angle improved by 62.69%. followed by Kyphoplasty with mean height restoration of 15.6%, mean fracture kyphosis improved by 22.4%, mean segmental kyphosis angle improved by 38.3%. Vertebroplasty group mean height restoration of 2%, mean fracture kyphosis improved by 5%, mean segmental kyphosis angle improved by 6%. At 6 months follow implantable device group had mean improvement in ODI by 90% while Kyphoplasty group had 76% Vertebroplasty group had 74%. Radiological implant device group had similar radiological parameters, where as loss of correction was observed in the Kyphoplasty group mean height restoration reduced by 91%, fractures kyphosis decrease by mean of 93%, segmental kyphosis decrease by mean of 94%. Vertebroplasty group had lost all the correction gain. At 12 months follow implantable device group had mean improvement in ODI by 92% while Kyphoplasty group had 81% Vertebroplasty group had 80%. Radiologically implant device group had 5% loss of fracture kyphosis and 7% loss of segmental kyphosis. where as at 12 months follow up both Kyphoplasty group and Vertebroplasty at similar radiology parameters as compare to their preoperative measurements. Discussion: Vertebral body augmentation procedure are effective for OVCF type 2,3 and some times in type 4. Most of the procedures in short term gives similar pain relief and are comparable. But radiological parameters varies from procedure to procedures which in turns impact the complication rate as well as long term functional and radiological outcomes. Factors to consider before choosing most appropriate procedures depend upon patient (BMD, Risk factors for Embolism, Other comorbidities, functional demands) and fracture specific like duration of fracture , type of fracture, Kummell lesion , angular motion, end plate defect. MRI findings also plays a crucial role in prognostic the outcome of the procedures. In our series we found Implantable device type augmentation appropriate choice in patients with additional comorbidities and additional fractures features in X-rays and MRI. Where as Vertebroplasty and Kyphoplasty had similar outcome,

## P0857: Artificial intelligence and machine learning based predictive analytics in cervical myelopathy

Sathish Muthu

1,2

, Siddeshwar Siddamanickam

1,3

1

Department of Spine Surgery, Orthopaedic Research Group, Coimbatore, India

2

Department of Orthopaedics, Government Medical College, Karur, India

3

New Cross Hospital, Wolverhampton, United Kingdom

Introduction: Recent advancements in Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) offer transformative potential in the field of predictive analytics for neurological disorders, including cervical myelopathy. We aim to evaluate the current landscape of AI and ML applications in predictive analytics for cervical myelopathy, focusing on diagnostic accuracy, patient stratification, surgical decision-making, and outcome prediction. Materials & Methods: A systematic search was conducted across PubMed, Embase, Scopus, and Web of Science using keywords related to cervical myelopathy and AI/ML. Studies were selected based on relevance to AI-driven predictive models in diagnosis, prognosis, and management of cervical myelopathy. Data extraction included algorithm types, validation methods, clinical applicability, and performance metrics. Qualitative synthesis was performed to identify thematic trends and methodological gaps. Results: Out of 169 records, 41 studies met inclusion criteria. AI/ML models demonstrated high accuracy in gait analysis, hand motion tracking, and image-based diagnostics, with some achieving >90% sensitivity and specificity. ML algorithms effectively stratified patients into phenotypic clusters and predicted surgical outcomes, including postoperative complications and opioid use. Radiomics and deep learning enhanced imaging interpretation, while ensemble models improved prognostic reliability. Despite promising results, limitations included data heterogeneity, retrospective design, and lack of standardised validation protocols. Conclusions: AI and ML are reshaping cervical myelopathy management by enabling precise diagnostics, personalised treatment planning, and predictive modelling. Integration of multimodal data and explainable AI frameworks is essential for clinical adoption. Future research should prioritise prospective validation, ethical safeguards, and interdisciplinary collaboration to bridge the gap between technological innovation and patient-centred care.

## P0858: Artificial intelligence and cervical sagittal balance

Paulo Cibrão Coutinho

1

, Tiago Soveral Pereira

1

, Pedro Varanda

1

1

Braga University hospital, Braga, Portugal

Introduction: Cervical Sagittal Balance (CSB) is a fundamental cornerstone of spinal biomechanics and surgical outcomes. Artificial intelligence (AI) has recently emerged as a promising tool to enhance the assessment of spine alignment, clinical decision-making with personalized treatment and support outcome prediction. This systematic review aims to provide a comprehensive evaluation of AI’s impact on CSB. Material and Methods: A systematic review with thorough literature search was conducted on PubMed, covering publications from January 2020 to December 2025. evaluating the application of AI in cervical sagittal balance. Eligible studies included those reporting image measurement, predictive analytics for surgical outcomes or integration of AI into clinical decision-making and prognostic factors. Data were synthesized regarding methodological approaches, supervised and self-supervised learning, large language models, accuracy, reproducibility, clinical applicability and limitations. Results: AI-based image recognition demonstrated high accuracy and consistency for the evaluation of sagittal parameters such as T1 slope, cervical lordosis and C2-C7 Sagittal Vertical Axis (cSVA). Machine learning models showed potential in predicting Patient-reported Outcomes, postoperative alignment restoration and risk predicting models. Deep learning algorithms applied to three-dimensional and dynamic imaging provided novel insights into cervical kinematics. However, heterogeneity in datasets and lack of external validation may restrict a broader generalization. There is an increasing trend towards sustainable growth strategies for AI-health care solutions. Conclusion: AI applications in cervical sagittal balance represent a rapidly advancing field with significant potential to improve personalized surgical spine care. Future research should focus on improving dataset diversity, Person-Generated Health Data and integration into decision-support workflows before widespread application in clinical practice.

## P0859: Awake spine surgery - Our experience

Kiriprasad Ramachandran

1

, Kannan Karthik Kailash

1

, Sudhir Ganesan

1

1

SRI Ramachandra Institute of Higher Education and Reserach, Chennai, India

Introduction: Awake spine surgery is an emerging technique that offers several advantages over traditional methods, such as reduced anesthesia risks, shorter recovery times, and improved postoperative outcomes. Materials and Methods: Type of study : Observational. Place of study : Tertiary care Hospital in South India. Duration of Study : 2 yrs. Inclusion criteria : • Surgery involving single level, • Surgery involving minimal invasive approach, Exclusion criteria : • Surgery involving more than one level, • Surgery with unpredictable duration, • Surgery involving expandable cages and osteobiologics, • Patient with high risk of respiratory compromise, Obstructive sleep apnea , Pre existing anxiety or depression, Intracranial Hypertension. Results: In our series, 50 patients (23 male, 27 female) were included. Twenty seven patients underwent Transforaminal endoscopic discectomy under local anesthesia, with one requiring conversion to general anesthesia. The average surgery duration was 45 minutes, with minimal blood loss and patients were mobilized six hours post-surgery. Significant improvements were observed in the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) in 2 yrs of follow up. Twenty patients underwent Microlumbar discectomy under erector spinae block, with an average surgery duration of 30 minutes and minimal blood loss and were mobilized after six hours. These patients also showed significant improvements in ODI and VAS scores. Three patients underwent minimally invasive Transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (MIS TLIF) under regional anesthesia, with an average surgery duration of 115 minutes, 100 ml blood loss and were mobilized after six hours. Significant improvements in VAS and ODI scores were observed. No neurological complications or procedure related complications were observed. No patient in our series required revision surgery. Conclusion: Awake spine surgery, encompassing transforaminal endoscopic discectomy, microlumbar discectomy, and MIS TLIF, demonstrates promising results in terms of surgical efficiency, minimal blood loss, and rapid postoperative recovery. Our experience with 50 patients suggests that this approach is a viable alternative to traditional spine surgery methods, offering significant benefits in terms of patient outcomes and overall satisfaction. Further studies with larger patient populations and longer follow-up periods are warranted to validate these findings and establish awake spine surgery as a standard practice in spinal surgery.

## P0860: Degenerative cervical myelopathy progression monitoring: empowering patients through the use of mobile applications

Adrian Ifrim

1

, Miki Shikanai

2

, Alexandru Budu

3

1

Jesus College, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom

2

University of Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom

3

Queen Elizabeth Hospital, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Degenerative Cervical Myelopathy (DCM) is a chronic condition caused by compression of the cervical spinal cord, resulting in variable loss of motor and sensory function. Identifying the point when decompressive surgery is indicated is time sensitive. Therefore, monitoring disease progression within the community becomes crucial. Mobile applications (apps) are powerful tools to fulfil this role, as they are convenient, cost-effective, and objective. This also makes them suited to collect clinical data for research. We designed and developed MyMyeloPath to address these aspects. Material and Methods: Our app was designed to collect data stored locally and securely on the patient’s phone. Development used the .NET MAUI platform so that the app is available for both Android and iOS operating systems. The interface displays three main pages: (i) a Questionnaire page, allowing measurement of disease progression via the patient-derived Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (p-mJOA) scale; (ii) a built-in Pedometer to reflect gait, and (iii) a Minigame, a real-time task for assessing dexterity. Results: The app provides an all-in-one platform for DCM patients to self-assess their disease progression. It also serves as an educational resource- either directly, or through sign-posting to relevant literature via a built-in PDF viewer. The simplicity of the interface aims to maximise user retention and consistency. A notification-based reminder system, and an implementation of a daily to-do list also serve this purpose. Graphical representations provide an effective way for patients to visualise their data. Conclusion: Here we developed an app that aims to monitor DCM disease progression. This user-friendly tool will empower patients to take an active role in monitoring their condition. The next step is its imminent release within a representative cohort of DCM patients, in order to assess its utility and gather relevant clinical data.

## P0861: Effects of glucagon-like peptide-1 agonists on clinical outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery

Brendan Colleran

1

, Allison Kelliher

2

, Tan Chen

3

1

Geisinger Commonwealth School of Medicine, Scranton, United States

2

Geisinger Northeast Orthopaedic Residency, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

3

Division of Orthopaedic Spine Surgery, Geisinger Musculoskeletal Institute, Wilkes-Barre/Scranton, United States

Introduction: Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are increasingly prescribed for type 2 diabetes and obesity, providing glycemic control, weight loss, and potential anti-inflammatory effects. The impact of preoperative GLP-1 use on spine surgery recovery and long-term clinical outcomes remain unclear. Given the rising prevalence of GLP-1 prescriptions, understanding their effect on surgical outcomes has become increasingly relevant. The goal of this study is to evaluate the relationship between preoperative GLP-1 use, recovery trajectory, and long-term clinical outcomes following lumbar fusion surgery. Material and Methods: A single-center retrospective cohort study was performed of 298 adults who underwent elective lumbar fusion for degenerative pathology between 2019 and 2024. GLP-1 exposure was defined as an active prescription within 3 months preoperatively. Group differences in age and sex were assessed using independent t-tests and chi-square analyses. Functional outcomes were assessed using PROMIS and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores at baseline, 6 months, and 12 months. Group differences in improvement were evaluated with independent samples t-tests. Descriptive and inferential statistics were performed. Results: Thirty-five patients (11.7%) were prescribed GLP-1 therapy within 3 months of surgery. GLP-1 and non-GLP-1 groups were similar in age (56.5 vs 57.4 years, p = 0.714) and sex distribution (77.1% vs 58.2% female, p = 0.077). A more rapid ODI improvement was observed in GLP-1 users (ΔODI: -18.9) at 6 months post-operatively than non-users (ΔODI: -8.5) [p = 0.002]. Both cohorts demonstrated similar long-term improvement at 1 year post-operatively (ΔODI -17.14 vs -19.80, p = 0.526). PROMIS improvement at 12 months was greater in GLP-1 users (ΔPROMIS: 8.34) than non-users (ΔPROMIS: 4.71, p = 0.025). No significant differences were observed at 6 months (ΔPROMIS: 3.95 vs 4.85, p = 0.549). Conclusion: Preoperative GLP-1 use within 3 months of elective lumbar fusion was associated with a more rapid ODI improvement at 6 months and improved patient-reported quality of life at 12 months postoperatively. These preliminary findings suggest a beneficial role of GLP-1 therapy in perioperative spinal recovery, potentially mediated by metabolic and anti-inflammatory effects.

## ID: AGS26/2392

## P0862: Evaluation of receiporcal changes after prone positioning: systematic review and metanalysis

Igor Barreira

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Igor Arthur Costa

1

, Amabile Galeti

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Leandro Resende

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Bianca Soares

1

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Lateral lumbar interbody fusion (LLIF) has emerged as an effective technique for treating degenerative spinal conditions, scoliosis, and other deformities. It offers several advantages over posterior approaches, including muscle preservation and direct access to the disc space. Surgical technique innovations-particularly patient positioning-have aimed to optimize clinical and radiographic outcomes. However, a comprehensive analysis comparing traditional lateral decubitus LLIF and the more recent prone transpsoas technique (PTP) is still lacking. This systematic review and meta-analysis aims to bridge this gap, analyzing morphological, neurological, and functional outcomes associated with prone positioning in LLIF. The findings may influence global spine surgery practice by providing evidence-based insights into whether prone positioning offers superior clinical efficacy and safety. Material and Methods: Following PRISMA-2020, we systematically searched MEDLINE, Embase, Scopus and CENTRAL from inception to 10 September 2025 for English-language cadaveric or volunteer studies that imaged the lumbar spine in both prone and lateral decubitus positions. Two reviewers independently screened titles/abstracts, examined full texts and extracted mean segmental lordosis, psoas and plexus coordinates, retro-psoas corridor width, and visceral or vascular displacement; conflicts were resolved by consensus. Owing to heterogeneous measurement techniques, results were synthesised narratively given the discrepancy between adopted methodologies for similar measurements between articles. Results: Seven positional-imaging studies (169 volunteer or cadaveric specimens) compared lumbar anatomy in prone versus lateral decubitus. As for the lumbar plexus position, three investigations (n = 72) showed that prone positioning moves the femoral nerve ∼ 8-10 % further posterior along the vertebral-body width at L4-L5, enlarging the safe transpsoas window; no shift was observed at other levels. As for changes in the Psoas muscle morphology, Four MRI cohorts (n = 91) reported a consistent posterior migration of the psoas, with the most detailed series quantifying a 4.1 ± 3.4 mm shift (p < 0.01). Furthermore, when analyzing the working corridor, one study documented a 16.2 mm increase in skin-to-disc distance when prone (134.9 ± 21.4 mm vs 118.7 ± 19.3 mm; p < 0.001); another found corridor dimensions equal or wider than lateral at L4-L5. Viscera and vessels, one study showed prone shifted the colon 20.8 mm posteriorly and one that prone translated the kidney 7.0 ± 5.8 mm anteriorly; vascular displacement was minimal (≤ 2.3 mm). Finally, two studies demonstrated that the prone positioning technique led to significantly increase in lumbar lordosis, and one further showed significant increase in L4S1 and L4L1 lordosis. Conclusion: Prone positioning consistently posteriorises the psoas-plexus complex, possibly enlarging the psoas working corridor, and provide moderate to higher increase in global and regional lordosis while causing only minor, clinically negligible shifts in major vessels and abdominal viscera. These anatomical advantages support the safety and technical feasibility of single-position prone lateral interbody fusion. Larger, standardised imaging studies are needed to confirm level-specific effects and determine whether the spatial gains observed translate into improved surgical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2393

## P0863: Comparison of surgical and radiological outcomes between prone transpsoas and lateral LLIF - Systematic review and metanalysis

Igor Barreira

1

, Rafael Moriguchi

1

, Rodrigo Amaral

1

, Luiz Pimenta

1

, Fernando Marcelino

1

, Igor Arthur Costa

1

, Amabile Galeti

1

, Leandro Resende

1

, Gabriel Pokorny

1

, Jullyene Pokorny

1

, Bianca Soares

1

Instituto de Patologia da Coluna, São Paulo, Brazil

Introduction: Prone transpsoas / prone lateral lumbar interbody fusion (PTP-LLIF) has gained popularity because it allows single-position surgery, yet its peri-operative benefits over traditional lateral-decubitus LLIF remain debated. Material and Methods: We updated a systematic review and meta-analysis, removing the Hiyama 2019 comparison study. Eleven prone-only cohorts (n = 245) and three direct comparative studies (n_prone = 84; n_lateral = 132) were pooled. Single-arm proportions were synthesized with a logistic-normal random-effects model; mean differences (MD) for continuous outcomes used inverse-variance random-effects weighting. Results: Complications. The pooled proportion of patients experiencing ≥ 1 complication after prone LLIF was 18 % (95 % CI 9 %-33 %). Across three comparative studies, prone positioning showed a non-significant 45 % reduction in odds of complications versus lateral (OR = 0.55, 95 % CI 0.28-1.07). Of the complications transient psoas-related symptoms dominated, hip-flexor pain/weakness or thigh dysesthesia accounted for 268 events (84 % of all complications; 39 % of patients). However, serious events were infrequent: vascular injuries = 6 (0.9 %), cage subsidence/failure = 5 (0.7 %), and persistent neurologic deficits = 5 (0.7 %). “Other” medical or wound issues totaled 33 episodes (4.9 %). As for the surgical time. Prone cohorts averaged 212 min (95 % CI 162-262). In head-to-head studies, prone surgery was 49 min faster than lateral (MD = -49.6 min, 95 % CI -84.0 to -15.2; I2≈0 %). As for the mean estimated blood loss (EBL), the single-arm mean for the prone cases cohort was 159 mL (95 % CI 122-195). While the pooled MD against Lateral decubitus (+30 mL, 95 % CI -26 to 86) did not differ significantly favored any position. Conclusion: This systematic review with early evidence studies about prone transpsoas surgery, suggest that the technique is capable of providing faster surgeries with similar blood loss and complications rates compared to traditional LLIF approaches. Although early evidence favours the single-position prone approach for operative efficiency, heterogeneity in reporting and limited comparative data warrant further high-quality, prospective studies capturing standardized safety and radiographic outcomes.

## P0864: Use of AI for implant identification, feature detection, and classification in spine surgery: a scoping review

Harjind Kahlon

1

, Navid Ghaderi

1

, Gurjovan Sahi

1

, Ali Eren

2

, Nischal Ghimire

3

, Jaskaran Singh

2

, Jay Toor

2

, Aazad Abbas

4

1

Temerty Faculty of Medicine, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

2

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

3

Department of Surgery, University of Manitoba, Winnipeg, Canada

4

Division of Orthopaedic Surgery, University of Toronto, Toronto, Canada

Introduction: Traditional clinician-based identification of spinal implants on radiographs and CT scans can be time-consuming and erroneous, which poses risks during critical periods such as postoperative surveillance and planning of revision procedures. Artificial intelligence (AI) technology has demonstrated significant potential in automating this process to enhance implant detection accuracy, but the exact extent and performance of such applications in spine surgery are not clear. This review aims to map the current literature on the use of AI for spinal implant identification, feature detection, and classification tasks. Material and Methods: A systematic search of PubMed, EMBASE, and MEDLINE was conducted from inception to March 13, 2025 to identify studies pertaining to application of AI models to spinal implant identification and detection tasks. Data pertaining to implant and imaging type, model details, and performance metrics were abstracted. Results were tabulated and presented in a descriptive summary format. Results: A total of 12 studies published between 2019 and 2025 were included in this review. The imaging modality consisted of radiographs in 9 studies and CT scans in 3. Detected implants include pedicle screws (n = 5), anterior cervical discectomy and fusion plates (n = 2), growing rods (n = 1), and unspecified implants (n = 4). Six studies focused primarily on implant detection while the other six focused on manufacturer identification. Convolutional neural networks (n=9) were the predominant architectures used, with support-vector machines (n = 2) and simple computer-vision (n = 1) also being used. Commonly reported performance metrics included accuracy, sensitivity/specificity, precision, F1 scores, and AUC, although reporting was quite heterogeneous across studies. Reported implant and manufacturer detection accuracies ranged from 86.6%-99.8% and 61.0%-100%, respectively. Compared to clinicians, deep learning (DL) models were also more accurate (79%-100% vs 44%-82%) and faster in detection/labelling tasks. Conclusion: AI models are highly accurate and efficient in implant identification and classification tasks. Studies are currently limited by heterogeneous methods and small datasets, necessitating future research to fully elucidate the role this technology can play in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2469

## P0865: A decade of novel technological innovations in spine surgery: bibliometric and literature review

Reza Akbar Bastian

1

1

An-Nisa General Hospital, Tangerang, Indonesia

Introduction: In the last decade, spine surgery has been strongly influenced by new technologies such as robotics, navigation, endoscopy, artificial intelligence, extended reality, and three-dimensional printing. Research in these fields has grown quickly, but a clear picture of overall trends and clinical impact is still limited. Material and Methods: We carried out a bibliometric analysis of studies indexed in Scopus between January 2015 and August 2025. Publication growth, citation patterns, and the contributions of leading countries, institutions, and authors were examined. Co-authorship and keyword networks were mapped with VOSviewer, and collaboration indices and thematic trends were analyzed in R Studio. We also reviewed the most frequently cited papers to highlight clinical use, reported benefits, and existing limitations of these technologies. Results: A total of 856 publications from 179 journals were identified, showing an average annual growth rate of 23.2%. The field is highly collaborative, with 3,593 contributing authors and almost six co-authors per paper, though only about 22% of studies involved international partners. The United States, China, and South Korea produced the largest share of work. Robotics accounted for about 30% of the literature, followed by minimally invasive and endoscopic techniques (25%) and navigation systems (20%). These approaches were consistently linked with improvements in accuracy, safety, and recovery. Artificial intelligence, 3D printing, and extended reality appeared less often but have shown steady growth in recent years. Persistent challenges include cost, technical demands, and unequal global access. Conclusion: Technological innovation has become central to the progress of spine surgery. Robotics, navigation, and minimally invasive techniques dominate the literature, while newer areas such as artificial intelligence and 3D printing are beginning to gain momentum. Making these advances more affordable, practical, and widely accessible will depend on stronger collaboration, broader training, and attention to cost-effectiveness.
Keywords: spine surgery, technological innovations, robotics, endoscopy, navigation, artificial intelligence, 3D printing, extended reality

## ID: AGS26/2470

## P0866: Interlaminar full endoscopy for exiting root compression and lateral recess stenosis: surgical technique and clinical outcomes

Siddharth Yadav

1

1

Consultant Spine Surgeon, Prayagraj, India

Introduction: Lumbar exiting nerve root compression and lateral recess stenosis are frequent causes of radiculopathy and disability. Conventional open decompressive surgery may lead to significant soft tissue injury and prolonged recovery. Full endoscopic interlaminar decompression has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative, but comprehensive outcome data in targeted patient groups remain limited. Material and Methods: This prospective study enrolled 45 patients (mean age 53 ± 12 years) with MRI-confirmed lumbar exiting root compression or lateral recess stenosis unresponsive to ≥ 8 weeks of conservative treatment. All patients underwent full endoscopic interlaminar decompression via a standardized surgical technique. Preoperative and postoperative outcomes were assessed using Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for leg pain and Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), as well as complication rates and patient satisfaction (Modified Macnab). Data were analyzed with paired t-tests and effect sizes (Cohen’s d). Results: Postoperative VAS significantly improved from 7.8 ± 1.2 to 2.1 ± 1.0 (p < 0.001, d=1.8), and ODI from 52 ± 10 to 18 ± 9 (p < 0.001, d = 1.6). Mean operative time was 45 ± 10 minutes; hospital stay averaged 1.5 ± 0.7 days. One patient (2.2%) experienced a dural tear without persistent neurological deficit. No postoperative motor deficits were observed. Patient satisfaction was rated excellent or good in 87% of cases. Conclusion: Full endoscopic interlaminar decompression is a safe and efficacious minimally invasive option for lumbar exiting root compression and lateral recess stenosis. This technique achieves robust symptom relief, low complication rates, and rapid patient recovery, supporting its role in modern spine surgery.

## P0868: Modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy for osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCF): a retrospective study of 104 patients

Junyu Li

1

, Jiahao Zhang

1

, Siming Xian

1

, Yihao Liu

2

, Hanwen Zhang

2

, ZHUO RAN SUN

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Yan Zeng

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCF) caused by osteoporosis is a common clinical fracture type. There are many surgical treatment options for OVCF, but there is a lack of comparison among different options. Therefore, we counted a total of 104 cases of OVCF operations with different surgical plans, followed up the patients, and compared the surgical outcome indications before, after and during the follow-up. Material and Methods: 104 patients who underwent posterior osteotomy (Modified PSO, SPO, PSO, VCR) and kyphosis correction surgery at our hospital between April 2006 and August 2021 with a minimum follow-up period of 24 months were included. All cases were injuries induced by a fall incurred while standing or lifting heavy objects without high-energy trauma. The mean CT value was 71 HU, which was below 110 HU, indicating severe osteoporosis. The indications for surgery included gait disturbance due to severe pain with pseudarthrosis, increased kyphotic angle, and progressive neurological symptoms. Pre- and postoperative CL, TLK, TK, PrTK, TKmax, GK, LL, PI, SS, PT, SVA, TPA, were investigated radiologically. Additionally, We evaluated estimated blood loss, surgical time and perioperative symptom. Results: The results show, after operation, TLK (37.32 ± 10.61° vs. 11.01 ± 8.06°, p < 0.001), TK (35.42 ± 17.64° vs. 25.62 ± 12.24°, p < 0.001), TKmax (49.71 ± 16.32° vs. 24.12 ± 13.34°, p < 0.001), SVA (44.91 ± 48.67 vs. 23.52 ± 30.21, p = 0.013), CL (20.23 ± 13.21° vs. 11.45 ± 9.85°, p = 0.024) and TPA (27.44 ± 12.76° vs. 13.91 ± 9.24°, p = 0.009) were improved significantly in modified pedicle subtraction osteotomy (mPSO) after operation. During follow-up, TLK (37.32 ± 10.61° vs. 13.88 ± 10.02°, p < 0.001) and TKmax (49.71 ± 16.32° vs. 24.1 2± 13.34°, p < 0.001) were improved significantly in modified PSO group. In addition, estimated blood loss (790.0 ± 552.2 ml vs. 987.0 ± 638.5 ml, p = 0.038), time of operation (244.1 ± 63.0 min vs. 292.4 ± 87.6 min, p = 0.025) were favorable in Modified PSO group compared to control group. Conclusion: To conclude, mPSO could acquire a favorable degree of kyphosis correction as well as fewer follow-up complications. Compared with other surgical methods, it also has the advantages of less surgical trauma and shorter operation time. It can be an effective solution for the treatment of OVCF.

## P0869: A proof-of-concept study with NVDX3, an osteogenic implant of human allogenic origin, in the treatment of low grade degenerative lumbar spondylolisthesis by interbody fusion in adults

Anas Dyab

1,2

, Mohammad Abu Kaff

3

, Stefanos Apallas

4

1

Medizentrum Limperstberg, Luxembourg, Luxembourg

2

Salutem Hospital, Heidelberg, Germany

3

King Hussein medical Center, Amman, Jordan

4

Chrisitan Doppler Clinic Salzburg, Paracelsus Private Medical University, Salzburg, Salzburg, Austria

Introduction: Advances in biomaterials offer new opportunities to improve outcomes in spinal fusion procedures by enhancing bone healing and stability. This proof-of-concept study investigates the application of NVDX3, a bioactive, osteoconductive stem cells material, in Transforaminal Lumbar Interbody Fusion (TLIF) to promote faster and more robust fusion. TLIF is a widely used technique for treating degenerative lumbar disc disease, but achieving consistent and timely fusion remains a clinical challenge, particularly in patients with risk factors such as osteoporosis, smoking, or diabetes. Methods: Five patients, all presenting with symptomatic lumbar disc degeneration and varying degrees of spinal instability, underwent TLIF procedures augmented with NVDX3. The material was applied during the interbody fusion process to the surgical site between the vertebral endplates, with the goal of enhancing osteogenesis and promoting faster fusion. Clinical outcomes will be assessed at regular intervals over 24 months using a combination of physical examinations, Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) scores, and Visual Analog Scale (VAS) for pain. Radiographic fusion will be evaluated using X-rays and CT scans at 3, 6, and 12 months postoperatively to monitor the progression of bone growth and stability of the implants. Results: showed that all five patients demonstrated early signs of bone formation at the 3-month follow-up. The radiographic evidence confirmed continuous bone growth across the interbody space, with no signs of pseudoarthrosis or hardware loosening. Clinical outcomes were also favorable, with significant improvements in pain relief and functional recovery.
