## P0870: Robot or basic navigation? Operative and clinical outcomes of pedicle screws (ROBOCOP): a prospective observational study in posterior thoraco-lumbar fixation

Simone Grannò

1,2

, Francesca Brigatti

1

, Cyrus Latifi

1

, Leonardo Anselmi

1

, Burçe Göksu

1

, Aria Nouri

1

, Gianpaolo Jannelli

1

, Dionysios Tsinas

2

, Nicolas Lauper

2

, Dennis E. Dominguez

2

, Karl Schaller

1

, Enrico Tessitore

1

& Granit Molliqaj

1

1

Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences, Geneva University Hospitals, Geneva, Switzerland

2

Spine Team, Division of Orthopedic Surgery and Musculoskeletal Trauma Care, Geneva University, Hospitals and University of Geneva’s Faculty of Medicine, Geneva, Switzerland

Introduction: Pedicle screw fixation remains a cornerstone of spinal stabilization, with accuracy directly impacting safety and outcomes. Traditional freehand techniques have been largely supplemented by navigation and, more recently, robotic systems. While recent literature has predominantly compared robot-assisted navigation (RAN) with fluoroscopy or freehand techniques, no robust prospective comparison with standard navigation (NAV) has yet been published. The ROBOCOP study represents the first project from French-speaking Switzerland to systematically compare RAN and NAV in pedicle screw placement, aiming to generate new evidence in a high-volume academic spine surgery center. Material and Methods: ROBOCOP is a single-center, prospective observational study at Geneva University Hospitals. All patients undergoing elective or emergent posterior spinal fixation with either RAN (ExcelsiusGPS, Globus) or NAV (BrainLab/O-ARM, Medtronic) are being consecutively enrolled. The primary outcome assessed is screw accuracy, by Gertzbein-Robbins classification for position within the pedicle and Shah classification for facet joint violation. The secondary outcome assessed is time, sub-quantified as absolute time-per-screw, technical planning time as well as overall operative time. Tertiary outcomes include intraoperative radiation exposure, blood loss, length of stay, complications, and patient-reported outcomes (VAS, ODI, EQ-5D-3L, McCormick, McNab). Sample size calculations indicate a minimum of 217 patients (868 screws) to detect a 5% difference in malposition rates with 80% power. However, we plan to exceed this substantially, targeting over 1000 screws to enhance subgroup and multivariate analyses. Results: Patient enrollment and data collection began in February 2025, and are actively underway. An interim analysis at 12 months will be ready for presentation before the Global Spine Congress 2026. These data will provide early comparative insights into accuracy, safety, and patient-centered outcomes of RAN versus NAV pedicle screw placement. Conclusion: ROBOCOP will deliver high-quality prospective evidence on navigated versus robotic spinal instrumentation, with the potential to refine surgical strategy and improve patient-centered outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2633

## P0871: Reliability of surgical planning in Surgimap®: comparison between planned and used screws in the lumbar spine

Alberto Perez_Contreras

1

, Andrea Rojas Barco

1

1

Hospital Angeles Pedregal, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Preoperative planning in spinal surgery is a key element for optimizing clinical outcomes, reducing surgical variability, and anticipating patient-specific anatomical challenges. In recent years, this process has advanced notably thanks to the incorporation of technologies such as artificial intelligence, robotic surgery, and computer-assisted navigation systems. Within this context, Surgimap® has established itself as a reference digital tool for radiographic analysis, surgical simulation, and implant planning. Being accessible and non-invasive, it represents a valuable option, especially in settings without access to navigation or robotics. Surgimap®, developed by Nemaris Inc. in 2008, is a free software that enables visualization and planning of surgical cases, including standardized parameter measurements, simulation of osteotomies, and implant placement, thus allowing estimation of postoperative results. Several studies have validated the utility of Surgimap® in predicting postoperative sagittal alignment. Langella et al. (2017) demonstrated that this software can accurately predict sagittal correction when planned with a target PT (Pelvic Tilt) of 20°. Despite its increasing use, concordance between digitally planned implants and those actually used in surgery has not been thoroughly evaluated. Therefore, this study aims to contribute to that analysis by evaluating the real reliability of surgical planning performed with Surgimap®. Material and Methods: • Study type: Observational, ambispective. • Population: Patients undergoing thoracolumbar spine surgery with preoperative planning in Surgimap®. • Inclusion criteria: Patients with complete planning in Surgimap® (including measurements of transpedicular screws and interbody spacers). • Exclusion criteria: Patients in whom transpedicular screws were not simulated, or measurements were unavailable. • Measurement: Linear measurement in Surgimap® vs. implant placed intraoperatively. Screw placement was performed under neuronavigation or fluoroscopy. If the preoperative planned screw was considered too long or too short, intraoperative adjustment was made. Results: A total of 5 cases were included: 3 (60%) with Adult Spinal Deformity (ASD) and 2 (40%) with Lumbar Degenerative Disease (DLD), with 41 instrumented levels. In preoperative planning, one screw per side was considered without distinction between left and right. Differences between planned and placed screws were calculated. Changes in screw measurements occurred in 3 patients: in the first case, 8 screws were replaced with shorter ones; in the second, 18 screws were replaced with longer and wider ones; and in the last case, 2 screws were replaced with shorter and thinner ones. In 22 out of 41 levels (53.66%), the screw length matched exactly. In 17 levels (41.46%), shorter screws were used. In only 2 levels (4.88%), longer screws were used. The mean difference between planned and placed screws was -2.56 mm. These findings suggest a tendency to intraoperatively shorten screws, influenced by visual perception with navigation or fluoroscopy images. Comparison between ASD (n = 33) vs. DLD (n=8) showed greater fidelity in DLD patients, with 75% exact matches vs. 48% in ASD patients. Conclusion: Surgimap® is an effective and accessible preoperative planning tool that accurately simulates transpedicular screw placement. This study shows discrepancies between planned and placed screws may have clinical consequences, including complications such as PJK. Greater reliance on digital planning should be promoted to optimize outcomes and reduce complications.

## ID: AGS26/2712

## P0872: AP-DADNet: an anatomical prior-driven framework for 3D spinal cord segmentation and shape quantification in CT with metal artifacts

Junyu Li

1

, Hanlin Xia

2

, Hanwen Zhang

3

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Duanduan Chen

2

, Miao Yu

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

School of Life Science, Beijing Institute of Technology, Key Laboratory of Convergence Medical Engineering System and Healthcare Technology, &e Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing, China

3

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: Spinal deformity, including scoliosis and kyphosis, is a common disease that may seriously affect the quality of life of patients. Surgical treatment is the key method to improve the symptoms of these diseases, and the evaluation of the effect of surgical treatment of spinal deformity urgently requires an accurate spinal cord morphology quantification method. This study proposes an anatomical prior-driven spinal cord segmentation and quantification framework to break through the bottleneck of segmentation and fracture in the postoperative metal artifact area of traditional methods, and provide a reliable three-dimensional morphological basis for surgical parameter calculation. Material and Methods: This study proposes an anatomical prior-driven deformable attention dynamic convolution network AP-DADNet (Anatomical Prior-Driven Deformable Attention and Dynamic Convolution Network). First, based on the prior segmentation results of the vertebrae, direction-sensitive spinal cord candidate regions are generated. Anatomically conditioned dynamic convolution is introduced in the UNet encoder, and the convolution kernel parameters are dynamically adjusted using the vertebral position to enhance the feature discrimination of the metal artifact area. A deformable attention module is added at the jump connection to adaptively align multi-scale features guided by vertebral coordinates. Finally, BiConvLSTM is used to model the long-range continuity of the segmentation results along the spinal axis to reduce the fracture phenomenon. After extracting the spinal cord of patients before and after surgery, the center line was fitted using B-spline, and the curvature-torsion quantification was used to evaluate the surgical correction effect. Results: The validation on a dataset of 240 preoperative and postoperative CT images of 120 patients with spinal deformity showed that the Dice coefficient of the evaluation index of AP-DADNet on the test set reached 0.88, which was 12.7% higher than that of the baseline U-Net, significantly improving the fracture and false positive phenomenon of spinal cord segmentation. The changes in curvature and torsion calculated based on the spinal cord center line fitted by B-spline can accurately reflect the differences in spinal morphology before and after surgery, proving the effectiveness of surgical treatment of spinal deformity. Conclusion: This study achieved high-precision three-dimensional reconstruction of the spinal cord in CT images with metal artifacts in patients with spinal deformity for the first time, and its anatomically driven dynamic fusion mechanism provides a new paradigm for medical image segmentation. The spinal morphological changes caused by surgery were quantified by differential geometry parameters, providing explainable decision support for the formulation of personalized surgical plans. In the future, biomechanical simulation will be integrated to build a surgical effect prediction system.

## ID: AGS26/2777

## P0873: AI-Assisted Cervical Correction Predictability Index in Ankylosing Spondylitis with Severe Global Kyphosis

Ahmed Elsaghir

1,2

, Hesham El Saghir

3,4

, Yara Elsaghir

5

1

Helios Klinikum Erfurt, Erfurt, Germany

2

Health and Medical University Erfurt, Erfurt, Germany

3

Alexandria University, Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Spine Surgery Unit, Alexandria, Egypt

4

Spine Surgery Department, Zentralklinik, Bad Berka, Germany

5

Department of Radiology, Alexandria Faculty of Medicine, Alexandria, Egypt

Introduction: Severe global kyphosis (SGK) is a characteristic deformity in ankylosing spondylitis (AS). The hunched posture often prevents acquisition of whole-spine radiographs on a single film, whereas medical photography allows only the chin-brow vertical angle (CBVA). The need for additional cervical osteotomy following thoracolumbar correction remains difficult to predict. This study introduces a novel AI-assisted parameter, the Cervical Correction Predictability Index (CCPI), to assess the necessity for cervical osteotomy in AS patients with SGK. Methods: A prospective study was conducted on 25 patients (18 males, 7 females) with SGK.• Data acquisition: AP and lateral photographs and whole-spine radiographs were obtained with patients standing and knees fully extended. • AI-assisted processing: Lateral photographs and radiographs were digitally extracted (iPhone software) and montaged in 1:1 ratio using MacBook software. • Measurements: o Kyphotic Tilt Angle (KTA): angle between the vertical axis and a line drawn from T1 to the most inferior kyphotic vertebra. o CBVA: standard chin-brow vertical angle. o CCPI: calculated as. All patients underwent thoracolumbar kyphosis correction. Results: • 22 patients (88%) with CCPI < 20° were satisfied postoperatively and did not require cervical osteotomy. • 1 patient with CCPI = 32° required cervicothoracic osteotomy. • 2 patients with CCPI = 25° and 28° accepted their residual head position without additional surgery. Conclusion: The Cervical Correction Predictability Index (CCPI) is a simple, reliable, and reproducible AI-assisted parameter. It allows prediction of the need for cervical osteotomy in AS patients with severe global kyphosis and may help refine surgical planning.

## ID: AGS26/2970

## P0874: The orthomate application in spine surgery: evaluation of knowledge and preparation among residents and instrumentalists

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hopital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Introduction: Effective preparation for spine surgery requires mastery of complex procedures and instrumentation. The Orthomate application provides digital access to structured surgical information prior to interventions. This study evaluates its impact on knowledge acquisition and readiness among residents and surgical instrumentalists in spine procedures. Materials and Methods: A prospective observational study was conducted involving 30 participants (12 residents, 18 instrumentalists) in spine surgeries. Questionnaires were completed 30 minutes before each procedure. Usability was assessed using the System Usability Scale (SUS), while perceived usefulness and preparation adequacy were evaluated using descriptive and comparative statistical analyses. Results: Orthomate significantly enhanced participants’ preparation for spine surgery. SUS scores averaged 77 for residents and 68 for instrumentalists. A majority of residents (66%) and instrumentalists (46%) rated the application as “very useful.” Most participants (70%) considered the 30-minute preoperative access sufficient, though 35% suggested earlier access could be beneficial. A significant positive correlation was found between usability and perceived usefulness (p < 0.001). The perception of preparation adequacy varied significantly between residents and instrumentalists. Discussion: The application facilitates rapid assimilation of procedural steps and instrumentation details, enhancing confidence and readiness in spine surgery. Participants suggested the addition of multimedia content and extended preoperative access to further improve effectiveness. Conclusion: Orthomate is a valuable tool for spine surgery education, improving both knowledge and operative preparation. This study supports the integration of digital applications in surgical training programs for spine procedures.

## ID: AGS26/3006

## P0875: Intrabody cage implantation via lateral approach for Kummell disease

Hyungchang Lee

1

, Dong-Ju Yun

1

, Hyeomg-Seok Oh

1

, Dae Jin Kim

1

, Byeong-Wook Hwang

1

, Sang-Jin Lee

1

, Sang-Ho Lee

2

1

Busan Wooridul Spine Hospital, Busan, South Korea

2

Wooridul Spine Hospital, Seoul, South Korea

Introduction: Kummell disease can be managed with various treatment options. Recently, intrabody cage implantation has emerged as a novel approach. In this study, we introduce a technique of implanting an interbody cage into the vertebral body via an anterolateral approach. Material and Methods: Between 2022 and May 2025, six patients with Kummell disease underwent anterolateral intrabody cage implantation. The mean age was 75.8 years, and the cohort included three men and three women. The affected vertebral levels were L1 in four cases, L2 in one case, and L3 in one case. A 3D-printed OLIF cage was used in four patients. In one patient, a single TLIF PEEK cage was implanted, and in another patient, two TLIF PEEK cages were implanted. Surgical Technique: With the patient in the lateral decubitus position, the vertebral body was exposed using an approach similar to the OLIF technique. Under C-arm fluoroscopic guidance, a chisel or electric burr was used to create an entry window at the lateral wall of the vertebral body. The intravertebral cavity was then prepared using a shaver or burr. After confirming the space with a cage trial, the definitive cage was inserted. Results: The mean preoperative VAS score was 8.3, which improved to 2.8 at the final follow-up (mean follow-up, 22.2 months). Radiologically, no additional vertebral body collapse was observed during the follow-up period. Cage position was satisfactory in all cases. Conclusion: Lateral intrabody cage implantation represents a novel and effective surgical option for Kummell disease. This technique provided marked pain relief and durable radiological stability at mid-term follow-up, suggesting its potential as an alternative to conventional treatments. However, the small number of cases limits generalization, and further studies with larger cohorts are warranted.

## ID: AGS26/3120

## P0876: Three-dimensional radiological predictors of brace treatment outcomes in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis

Rayan Affes

1

, Philippe Voyer-Nguyen

1

, Wen Bo Chen

1

, Justin Dufresne

1

, Marie Beauséjour

1,2

, Samuel Kadoury

3

, Marjolaine Roy-Beaudry

1

, Stefan Parent

1

1

CHU Sainte-Justine, Montreal, Canada

2

Université de Sherbrooke, Longueuil, Canada

3

Ecole Polytechnique de Montréal, Montreal, Canada

Introduction: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS) affects 2-3% of adolescents and represents the most common spinal deformity in this age group. Bracing remains the gold standard nonoperative treatment for moderate progressive curves, with randomized trials demonstrating a success rate of approximately 70%. However, treatment outcomes are highly variable, and a substantial proportion of patients continue to experience curve progression requiring surgery. Current prognostic factors including initial Cobb angle, skeletal maturity, growth velocity, curve flexibility, and in-brace correction provide only partial predictive value. Moreover, these factors rely predominantly on two-dimensional measures and do not fully capture the three-dimensional (3D) nature of the deformity. Recent developments in low-dose EOS stereoradiography and 3D reconstruction techniques have enabled detailed morphologic assessment of spinal and pelvic parameters. Despite increasing recognition of the 3D complexity of AIS, validated models predicting brace treatment success are still lacking. This study aimed to identify baseline 3D radiological parameters associated with a favorable brace response in AIS patients. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 112 AIS patients treated with a rigid brace at CHU Sainte-Justine between 2013 and 2021, followed from skeletal immaturity to maturity. Over 300 radiological parameters derived from EOS 3D reconstructions were extracted. Principal component analysis and clinical relevance screening reduced this number to 70 variables. After eliminating multicollinearity, candidate predictors were entered into a multivariate logistic regression with backward selection. Treatment success was defined as absence of surgery and curve progression < 5°. Data were standardized and internally validated with bootstrapping and penalized methods (LASSO/elastic net). Predictive performance was assessed by AUC ROC, Nagelkerke R2, Brier score, and calibration. Results: The final model retained five independent predictors: thoracolumbar Cobb angle (3D frontal plane), spinal length, coronal imbalance, pelvic obliquity, and T8-T9 disc wedging. Apparent AUC was 0.77, with a Nagelkerke R2 of 0.28 and a Brier score of 0.20. Bootstrap validation confirmed satisfactory internal robustness. Penalized regression approaches supported the stability of the first four predictors, while disc wedging appeared less consistent. Conclusion: Five 3D radiological parameters were associated with successful brace treatment in AIS. These results highlight the prognostic value of 3D spinal and pelvic morphology. However, key clinical determinants such as brace adherence, treatment duration, age, and skeletal maturity remain to be integrated into the models. Future work will focus on combining radiological and clinical predictors to refine risk stratification and develop more powerful predictive tools, potentially enhanced by machine learning approaches.

## ID: AGS26/3168

## P0877: Using ultrasonic osteotomes to reduce post-operative complications in lumbar stenosis surgery: a retrospective study about 74 patients

Jean Lefebvre

1

1

Clinique La Sagesse, Rennes, France

Introduction: Lumbar canal stenosis is a common surgical condition with potentially serious complications. Ultrasonic micro-vibration osteotomy saws are innovative new tools for performing laminectomies and lumbar canal recalibrations, but have been little studied to date, although the results so far are promising, particularly when compared to drilling techniques.
The aim of this study was to analyse whether these new tools reduced post-operative complications compared to the techniques commonly used by surgeons. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective before-and-after study on 74 consecutive patients who underwent surgery for lumbar canal stenosis following the acquisition of an ultrasonic micro-vibration osteotomy device (Piezosurgery, Mectron SPA, Carasco, Italy). Twenty-eight patients operated on in 2022 were included in the ‘Piezo’ group and 46 patients operated on in 2023 were included in the control group. The two groups of patients were comparable preoperatively in terms of age, gender, ASA score and number of levels operated on. Patients in the Piezo group underwent laminectomy performed exclusively with the ultrasonic osteotome; patients in the control group underwent laminectomy performed with Gouge forceps, Kerrisson-Smith forceps and/or bone scissors, depending on the surgeon's preference. The primary endpoints were the overall rate of post-operative complications, including dural tears, haemorrhagic complications (including post-operative anaemia) and neurological complications, infections and the reoperation rate. The secondary endpoints were operating time, the rate of return home and length of stay. Results: Our results showed that the use of an ultrasonic osteotome was associated with a significant reduction in the overall complication rate (21% vs. 76%, p = 0.009), particularly the rate of post-operative anaemia (3.6% vs. 8.7%, p = 0.02). There were no differences between the two groups in terms of other complications, including the rate of dural tears. In the Piezo group, the length of stay was shorter (1.6 days vs. 2.1 days, p = 0.02), as was the duration of surgery (51.3 min vs. 70.5 min, p = 0.004). Conclusion: Our results show a significant association between the use of an ultrasonic micro-vibration osteotomy micro saw and a reduction in post-operative complications, particularly anaemia, as well as a reduction in operating time and length of stay for patients. Randomised controlled trials are therefore necessary and justified to confirm these results.

## ID: AGS26/3350

## P0878: Trends and insights in prone lateral approach for spine surgery: a bibliometric analysis

Mohamad Bakhaidar

1,2

, Meriem boukhiam

3

, Abdulwhab Alotaibi

2

, Omid yousefi

2

, Ataollah Shahbandi

2

, Gilberto Perez

2

, Saman Shabani

2

, Muhammad Abd-El-Barr

3

1

Ottawa University, Ottawa, Canada

2

Medical college of Wisconin, Milwaukee, United States

3

Duke University Medical Center, Durham, United States

Introduction: The prone lateral approach, also known as prone transpsoas (PTP), represents an important advancement in minimally invasive spine surgery. It enables simultaneous access to anterior and posterior spinal elements from a single patient position. Although the technique has gained considerable traction within the spine surgery community, the literature remains dispersed across multiple subspecialty journals without comprehensive synthesis. This study aims to analyze publication trends on this emerging technique, providing an overview of current research and identifying gaps in the literature. Material and Methods: We conducted a systematic search using relevant keywords across multiple databases from inception to September 2025. We included all peer-reviewed publications on the PTP approach in spine surgery. Articles were then extracted from Scopus database for bibliometric data. The data were analyzed using the bibliometrics package in R and Publish or Perish (POP) software. Key metrics included annual publications, citation trends, countries and institutions of origin, and publishing journals. Results: A total of 104 peer-reviewed articles were published between 2014 and 2025, accumulating 1,008 citations with an h-index of 19. Publication volume demonstrated strong growth, with 23 articles published in 2025 alone (through August). The United States accounted for the majority of research output (93 articles, 89.4%). World Neurosurgery and Operative Neurosurgery were the most productive journals (13 articles each, 12.5%). Conclusion: This bibliometric analysis demonstrates the rapid growth and increasing recognition of the prone lateral approach in spine surgery. The research landscape is characterized by strong U.S. leadership, diverse journal representation, and evolving clinical applications. These findings highlight the successful transition of this approach from an experimental technique to an established clinical practice with significant impact on minimally invasive spine surgery.

 

 

## ID: AGS26/3447

## P0879: Robot assisted spinal surgery: a systematic review

Hugo Arturo González Martínez

1

, Jesús Alberto Pérez Contreras

1

1

Hospital Angeles Health System, Mexico, Mexico

Introduction: Although robotic-assisted spinal surgery has demonstrated improved accuracy in the placement of implants, there remains debate regarding its impact on functional outcomes and complication rates. This study sought to comprehensively evaluate the advantages of robotic-assisted spinal surgery compared with conventional techniques. Material and Methods: Observational, analytical and descriptive study based on a systematic review of the literature indexed in PubMed. Inclusion criteria: English-language articles published within the last 10 years. Objective: To evaluate the clinical, functional, and technical impact of robot-assisted surgery in spinal instrumentation procedures. Results: Robot-assisted spinal surgery is an evolving field aiming to improve precision and safety. The technique has shown value but also presents specific challenges and limitations. Systems and pedicle screw placement accuracy (reported): - Mazor X and Mazor SpineAssist/Renaissance: accuracy reported up to 100% (Gertzbein-Robbins Grade A). - ExcelsiusGPS: accuracy ≈ 99% (Grade A or B). - TiRobot: accuracy 98.9% with deviation < 2 mm. Reported benefits: - Reduced operative time (in selected series). - Lower intraoperative blood loss. - Reduced exposure to intraoperative radiation. - Fewer postoperative complications in some reports. - Reduced postoperative pain. - Shorter length of hospital stay. Limitations and challenges: - Cost-effectiveness concerns. - Steep learning curve and system adoption issues. - Reported complications (observed in some series): - Postoperative anemia. - Need for revision surgery. - Surgical-site infection. - Increased postoperative opioid consumption in certain cohorts. Applications / Indications (reported uses): - Pedicle screw placement. - Burst fractures. - Spinal deformity correction. - Vertebral metastases. Conclusion: Robotic assistance in spinal surgery has improved accuracy and safety-especially for pedicle screw placement-and is associated in multiple series with reductions in intraoperative blood loss and radiation exposure. Challenges remain regarding cost, learning curve, and certain complications. Continued technological development and integration with tools such as artificial intelligence are likely to broaden the clinical impact of robotic systems in spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/3457

## P0880: Comparing objective surgical outcome measures versus patient reported outcomes with the use of wearable technology in lumbar spine surgery: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Noojan Kazemi

1

, Natalie Slosar

1

, Paul Carne

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) are the gold standard for evaluating recovery after lumbar spine surgery, but they remain subjective and may incompletely capture functional change. Wearable technologies, such as accelerometers and activity monitors, provide objective, continuous measures of mobility that may complement PROMs. However, their validity and correlation with traditional outcomes remain uncertain. This study systematically reviews the use of wearable technology for measuring postoperative outcomes in lumbar spine surgery and quantifies correlations between wearable-derived metrics and PROMs. Material and Methods: A systematic review was conducted according to PRISMA guidelines. PubMed/MEDLINE, Embase/Elsevier, and CINAHL were searched through August 2025, yielding 10,831 records. Eligible studies included patients undergoing lumbar surgery for degenerative conditions, reported PROMs and wearable-derived gait data, and provided correlation coefficients. For quantitative synthesis, studies were included if they reported Pearson correlations of change in wearable-derived mobility metrics (e.g., step count, gait velocity) with change in PROMs from baseline to postoperative timepoints. One effect per study per domain (disability, pain, health-related quality of life [HRQL]) was extracted at the first postoperative timepoint ≥6 weeks. Correlations were Fisher z-transformed, pooled under random-effects models, and back-transformed for presentation. Heterogeneity was summarized with I2, and significance tested on the Fisher-z scale. Results: Fifteen studies met inclusion criteria, of which eight contributed to the meta-analysis. Pooled change-change correlations demonstrated strong association with disability (r = 0.646, 95% CI 0.475-0.770, I2 = 51%, p < 0.0001), moderate association with pain (r = 0.459, 95% CI 0.235-0.637, I2 = 0%, p = 0.0002), and weaker association with HRQL (r = 0.357, 95% CI 0.066-0.592, I2 = 29.4%, p = 0.0174). Gait velocity and composite indices showed stronger correlations with PROM changes than daily step counts, which were less consistent. Conclusion: Wearable-derived mobility metrics significantly correlate with PROM changes after lumbar spine surgery, with strongest associations for disability. These findings support wearables as complementary tools for outcome assessment. Standardization of metrics, timing, and reporting is needed to enable clinical integration and define clinically meaningful thresholds.

## ID: AGS26/3466

## P0881: The role of XLIF in removing failed interbody implants: a systematic review of the literature

Yusuf Ansari

1

, Arwa Alabide

2

, Shresht Sitaram

1

, Dia Halalmeh

3

1

Temple University, Philadelphia, United States

2

University of Kufa, Kufa, Iraq

3

University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics, Iowa City, United States

Introduction: This review examines the novel application of the Extreme Lateral Interbody Fusion (XLIF) approach for the removal of foreign bodies in the spine, including subsided cages, failed hardware, and other implants. It aims to evaluate the efficacy, safety, and technical considerations of XLIF in this context and compare it to traditional anterior and posterior surgical approaches. Methods: A comprehensive literature search in accordance with PRISMA was conducted using Google Scholar, PubMed/MEDLINE, and the Cochrane Library. Search terms included “extreme lateral interbody fusion,” “lateral lumbar interbody fusion,” “cage retrieval,” “revision surgery,” and “foreign body removal.” Articles were selected based on relevance to XLIF use in foreign body removal and included case reports, clinical trials, and observational studies published in English before April 7, 2025. Results: Seven documented cases met inclusion criteria. The XLIF approach demonstrated advantages such as reduced operative time, blood loss, and shorter hospital stays. It allowed safe access around scar tissue and critical neurovascular structures. The technique was successfully used to remove migrated or failed implants in the lumbar spine with minimal complications, most of which were transient. The approach also enabled the insertion of larger interbody cages, contributing to improved spinal stability and fusion outcomes. Conclusion: The XLIF approach is a promising alternative for foreign body removal in complex spinal revision surgeries. However, potential complications, such as transient nerve injury, underscore the need for careful patient selection and surgical expertise. Further studies are needed to validate its broader clinical application.

## P0882: Hirayama disease: treated with cervical epidural PRP injection - A case report of muscle wasting reversal and functional improvement

Shahidul Islam

1,2

, Md. Hasan

3

, Ferdous Banu

4

, Merina Tanzil

1

, Saleh-Al-Nayem Chy.

1

, Tahmina Akhter

1

, Warde Jannat

1

, TM Jafor

1

, Shukanya Samia

1

1

Barak PRP Medical Centre, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Ad-din Women's Medical College, Dhaka, Bangladesh

3

Holy Family Red Crescent Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

4

Dhaka Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Hirayama disease is a rare, self-limiting cervical myelopathy affecting mostly young individuals; characterized by progressive and asymmetric wasting of distal upper limb muscles. Conventional management is usually conservative. Surgical intervention might be needed in progressive cases. No medication has shown proven efficacy in reversing muscle wasting so far. Materials and Methods: We report the case of a 19-year-old female presenting with progressive weakness and atrophy of her right hand and forearm over one year. Neurological examination and cervical spine MRI with flexion confirmed the diagnosis of Hirayama disease. After failure to improve with standard conservative therapy, the patient was treated with autologous platelet-rich plasma (PRP) injection in cervical spine. The patient demonstrated significant functional improvement and visible reversal of muscle wasting. Results: To our knowledge, this is the first reported case of cervical epidural PRP injection showing regenerative potential in Hirayama disease. PRP contains growth factors which might promote neuron and microvasculature repair and regeneration. Conclusion: Autologous PRP injection may represent a novel and promising treatment for selected cases of Hirayama disease, especially where muscle wasting persists or progresses. Larger studies are needed to validate its safety, efficacy, and mechanism of action.

## ID: AGS26/2685

## P0883: Bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix in single-level standalone ACDF: a retrospective comparative study

Kingsley Chin

1

, Erik Spayde

2

, William M. Costigan

3

, Sandra Thompson

4

, Mark Jones

5

, Ajay Yeddu

6

, Rajmani Krishnan

7

, Vito Lore

1

, Hope Estevez

1

, Suraj Yeddu

1

, Sajni Yeddu

1

, Shreyas Krishnan

8

, Jason A. Seale

1

, Chukwunonso Ilogu

1

, Jacob Ryu

9

1

LESS Society, LESS Clinic, Fort Lauderdale, United States

2

St. Charles Spine Institute, Thousand Oaks, CA, United States

3

Congress Orthopaedic Associates, Pasadena, United States

4

The Pain Center, Boise, Idaho, United States

5

Pain Medicine of the Sounth, Knoxville, United States

6

Desert Interventional Spine Consultants, Gilbert, United States

7

Island Interventional Pain Management, Commack, NY, United States

8

LESS Society, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, United States

9

Midwestern University Chicago - College of Osteopathic Medicine, Chicago, United States

Introduction: Demineralized bone matrix is commonly used as a graft material in anterior cervical discectomy and fusion, especially with standalone polyetheretherketone interbody cages. Its clinical performance can vary because of donor variability and limited intrinsic osteogenic cells. Bioactive glass releases ions that stimulate osteogenesis and may improve early graft integration. Clinical evidence comparing bioactive glass combined with demineralized bone matrix to demineralized bone matrix alone in standalone constructs is limited. This study evaluated whether adding bioactive glass is associated with earlier radiographic fusion and faster improvement in pain and disability after single-level fusion. Materials and Methods: We performed a retrospective comparative study of consecutive adults who underwent single-level fusion with a standalone polyetheretherketone interbody cage at a single ambulatory center. Patients received either bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix as the interbody graft or demineralized bone matrix alone. Minimum follow up was two years. Primary outcome was radiographic fusion at prespecified intervals, assessed on dynamic radiographs by two fellowship trained spine surgeons with discrepancies resolved by consensus. Secondary outcomes were Visual Analogue Scale scores for neck and arm pain and Neck Disability Index. Baseline demographics, perioperative variables, complications, and reoperations were recorded. Group comparisons were descriptive; no imputation was used. Results: Eighty one patients (n = 81) met inclusion criteria: forty four received bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix and thirty seven received demineralized bone matrix alone. Baseline characteristics were comparable between groups. Radiographic review suggested earlier fusion in the bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix group, with most patients showing arthrodesis by six months, whereas fusion in the demineralized bone matrix only group was more often observed at or after one year. By final follow up, complete fusion was observed in all patients without pseudarthrosis. Patients who received bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix reported faster improvement in neck pain at six and twelve months, with parallel gains in disability. At two years, both groups demonstrated substantial and sustained improvement. There were no graft related complications, device failures, or index level revisions in either group. One patient in the bioactive glass plus demineralized bone matrix cohort underwent adjacent level surgery during follow up. All procedures were performed in an ambulatory setting without unplanned admissions. Conclusion: In this retrospective cohort, adding bioactive glass to demineralized bone matrix in single-level standalone fusion was associated with earlier radiographic fusion and faster symptom relief compared with demineralized bone matrix alone. Both strategies achieved complete fusion by final follow up with low complication rates. Bioactive glass combined with demineralized bone matrix may be a useful adjunct when autograft is not used. Prospective studies with standardized imaging and randomized allocation are needed to confirm these observations and to define cost effectiveness.

## ID: AGS26/3405

## P0884: Systematic review of predictive patient risk factors in SCS explantation

Grace Kim

1

, Evan Dunn

2

, Justin Chung

3

, Rowen Lin

3

, Kevin Mo

2

, William Fang

2

, Shanthan Challa

2

, Daniel Lee

2

1

Western University of Health Science, Pomona, United States

2

Valley Hospital Medical Center, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Las Vegas, United States

3

Touro University Nevada, College of Medicine, Henderson, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) is a widely used intervention for chronic neuropathic pain, but a significant proportion of patients undergo device explantation due to complications or suboptimal outcomes. Identifying preoperative predictors of explantation, both modifiable and non-modifiable, is crucial for improving patient selection and long-term therapy success. This systematic review evaluates the association between preoperative risk factors and the rates of SCS explantation. Material and Methods: This review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. A comprehensive search of PubMed and Google Scholar in June 2025. Eligible studies included comparative cohorts of adult SCS recipients that reported explantation outcomes in relation to diagnosis, lead type, preoperative opioid use, smoking history, age, or sex. Two independent reviewers screened and extracted data, resolving disagreements by consensus. Results were synthesized descriptively. Results: 15 retrospective cohort studies comprising 86,071 patients who underwent SCS implantation met inclusion criteria. Among these, 5,932 patients (6.9%) underwent device explantation during follow-up periods ranging from 6 months to 9 years. Study sizes varied from small single-center cohorts to large database studies exceeding 55,000 patients, with most (n = 11) reporting intermediate follow-up durations of 12-24 months. 6 studies found a higher proportion of paddle leads in the explant group. Of the 6 studies that reported explant indications for both neuritis and back pain, 4 found a higher proportion of explants due to neuritis. Preoperative opioid use was reported in 7 studies, with 5 demonstrating higher opioid use among patients who underwent explantation. 7 out of 11 studies that reported sex-specific data found a higher proportion of females in the explant group. Age-related findings were mixed across 14 studies, with 7 reporting older age and 6 reporting younger age in explanted patients. Smoking was reported in 5 studies, with 3 showing higher use among those who underwent explantation. Conclusion: This review identifies several potential predictors associated with SCS explantation, including paddle lead use, neuritis diagnosis, preoperative opioid use, female sex, and smoking. Age-related trends remain inconclusive. These findings may inform patient selection and perioperative counseling to optimize long-term outcomes following SCS implantation.

## P0885: Surgical safety in degenerative spine disease under aspirin therapy: a retrospective study

Victoria Yushenko

1

, Artem Ilgeldiev

1

1

Neurosurgery, Germany

Introduction: Managing patients on antiplatelet therapy during spinal surgery is controversial due to bleeding concerns. This study aimed to assess the safety of continuing low-dose aspirin in patients undergoing spine surgery for degenerative conditions. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on 43 patients undergoing lumbar spine surgery (disc herniation or stenosis) between 2015 and 2022. All patients remained on 100 mg aspirin daily. Surgical outcomes, intraoperative blood loss, and postoperative complications were reviewed. Results: No major bleeding events or reoperations occurred. Median blood loss was 50 mL, with no transfusions required. No thromboembolic complications were observed. These findings support the safety of continuing low-dose aspirin during spine surgery in patients with cardiovascular risk. Conclusion: Low-dose aspirin can be safely continued in degenerative spine surgery without increased bleeding risk, offering dual benefit by preserving cardiovascular protection.
Approved by the Ethics Committee of Westfalen-Lippe, reference number 2025-025-f-S

## ID: AGS26/81

## P0886: Pneumoencefalus: rare complication after lumbar spine surgery

Joan Toribio

1

, Laura Perelló

1

1

Hospital Universitari Son Llatzer, Palma de Mallorca, Spain

Introduction: Surgery. It is a rare complication after epidural anesthesia and we have not found any bibliographic reference to the presence of pneumoencephalus after lumbar surgery. The following clinical case of pneumocephalus after lumbar spine surgery is presented. Material and Methods: 46-year-old woman with symptoms of low back pain and left L4 radiculopathy with a diagnosis of left foraminal disc herniation L4-L5 and degenerative disc disease at that level. L4-L5 transforaminal circumferential arthrodesis, pedicle instrumentation with interbody implant were performed. On the third post-operative day, the patient began inflammatory pain in the lumbar region and in the iliac crest, with suppuration through the drainage hole corresponding to the crest; surgical debridement were performed. Firstly, after taking intraoperative culture samples, the iliac crest is debrided, and subsequently the lumbar area is instrumented, the bilateral posterolateral and right interlaminar graft is partially removed, and cleaning is carried out with a mechanical pulsatile irrigation device. In the immediate post-operative period, the patient reported severe pain in the posterior region of the cervical spine. After 12 hours of evolution, the patient reported photophobia and severe frontal pain. A cranial CT scan was performed, which showed the presence of a large pneumoencephalus in the frontal area. Results: The patient’s evolution is favorable with a good appearance of the surgical wound, no germ was isolated, so it was decided to extend oral antibiotic treatment for 3 months (Ciprofloxacin 750 mg/12 hours and Rifampicin 600 mg/12 hours). The pneumocephalus after successive control CT scans is completely reabsorbed after 3 weeks. After 6 weeks, a lumbar MRI was performed in which a small pseudomeningocele was observed, most likely an unnoticed dura mater lesion and the excessive pressure of the pulsatile irrigation that caused the introduction of air into the intradural space. After one year of evolution, the patient did not report lumbar or radicular pain, radiologically no postero-lateral bone mass was observed, although there was at the interbody level. CT, with sagittal and 3D reconstruction one year later, shows images of interbody fusion mass, without signs of lysis in pedicle screws Figs. 12; Radiologically there are no signs of osteolysis in the pedicle screws, all of this, together with the disappearance of the lumbar pain, suggests the existence of a solid interbody arthrodesis. Conclusion: We do not recommend the use of pulsatile irrigation in spinal debridements, given the possible existence of unnoticed dural lesions through which air driven by the excessive pressure of pulsatile irrigation can penetrate.

## ID: AGS26/119

## P0887: Relationship between the postoperative variations of paraspinal muscles and adjacent-segment degeneration in patients with degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis after posterior instrumented lumbar fusion

Fei Xu

1

, Weishi Li

1

1

Orthopaedic Department, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: This study aimed to quantify pre- and postoperative paraspinal muscular variation following posterior lumbar interbody fusion (PLIF) in patients with degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis (DLSS) and measure the association of this variation with adjacent-segment degeneration (ASD). Material and Methods: Data from 149 patients who underwent L4-S1 PLIF for DLSS were collected. Patients were divided into radiologically confirmed ASD and control groups according to follow-up radiological findings. MRI was performed before surgery and at the last follow-up. Muscular parameters including the relative cross-sectional area (rCSA), relative functional cross-sectional area (rFCSA), relative total cross-sectional area (rTCSA), and fatty infiltration (FI) of the multifidus (MF), erector spinae (ES), and psoas major (PM) muscles were measured on preoperative and follow-up L2-S1 MR images. Logistic regression was used to investigate risk factors for ASD. Results: The rate of radiological ASD was 42.3% at the final follow-up (mean 25.71 ± 8.35 months). At surgical levels, the rFCSA and rTCSA of the MF and ES muscles decreased. The FI of the MF from L2-3 to L5-S1 and ES muscles at L5-S1 significantly increased after surgery, while the rFCSA and rTCSA of the PM muscle increased and its FI decreased. At adjacent levels, the rFCSA and rTCSA of the MF muscle and rTCSA of the ES muscle decreased and the FI of the MF muscle increased postoperatively (p < 0.05), but the rFCSA and rTCSA of the PM muscle increased and its FI decreased (p < 0.05). The FIs of the MF, ES, and PM muscles at adjacent levels significantly differed between the ASD and control groups. Logistic regression analysis indicated that higher BMI (p = 0.002) and FI of the PM muscle at adjacent levels (p = 0.025) were significant risk factors for ASD. Conclusion: The functional area decreased in the MF and ES muscles and increased in the PM muscle after L4-S1 PLIF. A compensatory postoperative decrease in FI of the PM muscle at the adjacent level was a protective factor for ASD in DLSS patients after PLIF.

## ID: AGS26/120

## P0888: Correlation between lordosis tilt and lordosis distribution index and occurrence of proximal junctional kyphosis following surgery for adult degenerative scoliosis

Fei Xu

1

, Weishi Li

1

1

Orthopaedic Department, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: This study was aimed to evaluate the effect of postoperative reciprocal progression of lordosis tilt (LT) and lordosis distribution index (LDI) and occurrence of proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) following surgery for adult degenerative scoliosis (ADS). Material and Methods: 122 consecutive patients with ADS treated with correction of deformity followed-up for a minimum of 2 years. Spinopelvic parameters were measured at pre-operation post-operation and the latest follow-up. Japanese Orthopaedic Association score (JOA), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), visual analog scale (VAS), were measured at the latest follow-up. Associations between LT, LDI and PJK were analyzed using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analyses. Results: The prevalence of PJK in the present study was 24.6%. Outcomes of patients with PJK were significantly worse than that in patients without PJK. Postoperative reciprocal progression in LT and LDI with lumbar lordosis (LL) restorative surgery was found. Preoperative risk factors of PJK were older age, larger LT and larger Cobb angle of the curves. Postoperative risk factors for PJK included postoperative LT and postoperative Cobb angle of the curves, which were smaller than that in preoperative time. We found a strong correlation between postoperative LT and Cobb angle of the curves resulting in PJK. And the patients with LT larger than 8° were at higher risk of getting PJK. Conclusion: LT was a spinopelvic parameter of analyzing the relationship between the relative positions of spine and pelvis, which indicates the inclines of the total lordosis. LT could be used to predict the occurrence of PJK in patients undergoing surgery for ADS. Appropriate postoperative LT is crucial to prevent progression of PJK.

## ID: AGS26/272

## P0889: Fatty infiltration in the multifidus predicts screw-loosening following short-segment decompression and fusion: proof of why we should protect and rehabilitate the paraspinal muscles

Murat Ekşi

1

, Emel Özcan-Ekşi

2

1
İstinye University, Istanbul, Turkey, 
2
Private Practice, Istanbul, Turkey
Introduction: Screw-loosening is a common instrumentation-related complication following fusion. Patients who present with pain and neurological symptoms due to screw-loosening require revision. It has been reported that fat-infiltrated and/or atrophied paraspinal muscles were associated with low back pain, disability, radiculopathy, and instrumentation-related failures. However, there is limited and conflicting knowledge regarding the association of paraspinal muscles with screw-loosening. In the present study, we aimed to identify whether fatty infiltration in the paraspinal muscles was associated with screw-loosening. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of the clinical and radiological data of the patients who underwent short-segment decompression and fusion for lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) at a tertiary spine clinic between 2013 and 2023. Goutallier’s classification system was used for grading fatty infiltration in the paraspinal muscles. Results: Patients with screw-loosening had fattier multifidus at the upper lumbar spine (particularly L2-L3, cephalad to the upper instrumented level of L3-L4) compared to those without screw-loosening. In univariate analysis fatty multifidus at L2-L3 level, elder age, and male sex had ORs of 1.509 (p = 0.008), 1.116 (p = 0.001) and 4.702 (p = 0.004), respectively. In multivariate analysis fatty multifidus at L2-L3 level, elder age and male sex had ORs 1.428 (p = 0.043), 1.109 (p = 0.003), and 5.911 (p = 0.004), respectively. Conclusion: Fatty infiltration in the multifidus muscle (particularly in the one at the cranial end of the fusion mass) is predictive for screw-loosening following short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion for LSS. Preserving multifidus in subjects is essential to prevent future long-term complications of spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/273

## P0890: Higher Charlson Comorbidity Index Score correlates with higher rate of pseudoarthrosis following short-segment lumbar fusion surgery

Murat Ekşi

1

, Emel Özcan-Ekşi

2

1

İstinye University, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Private Practice, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: In the present study, we aimed to analyze whether the Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI) score was predictive for pseudoarthrosis in patients who underwent with short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion for degenerative lumbar spine disorders (DSD). Material and Methods: Consecutive patients who underwent short-segment lumbar decompression and fusion for DSD between 2013 and 2023 in a tertiary spine clinic were included into the study. Patients’ charts were reviewed for patients’ comorbidities, smoking status, and the CCI score was calculated accordingly. Subcutaneous fat index (SFI) was used for the anthropometric evaluation of the participants. Results: Comorbidities/smoking had no significant effect on the pseudoarthrosis rate, when analyzed separately. However, subjects with pseudoarthrosis at their final follow-up visits had significantly higher CCI scores at the pre-operative era compared to the subjects without pseudoarthrosis (p = 0.022). Besides, in patients with pseudoarthrosis and those without pseudoarthrosis 48.1% and 75.3% were overweight according to SFI, respectively (p = 0.003). Multivariable regression analysis depicted ORs of 1.256 (p = 0.018) for the CCI score and 0.269 (p = 0.003) for overweight. The ROC analysis revealed a cut-off value of 2.5 points for the CCI score. Conclusion: A combined effect of comorbidities might yield higher pseudoarthrosis rates following short-segment decompression and fusion for DSD. Each one-point increment in CCI score and SFI score yielded a 26% increment and a 73% decrement in risk for pseudoarthrosis in those subjects. A cut-off value of 2.5 points for the CCI score could distinguish the subjects who might have pseudoarthrosis following short-segment surgery for DSD.

## ID: AGS26/289

## P0891: Preoperative anemia severity predicts complications following anterior cervical spine surgery

Michael Kim

1

, Melissa Romoff

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Preoperative anemia is a well-established risk factor for adverse surgical outcomes, with several studies in arthroplasty demonstrating a strong exposure-response relationship between anemia of increasing severity and risk of complications. These findings have led to the integration of anemia screening and optimization protocols into perioperative pathways for total hip and knee arthroplasty. However, despite similar concerns around blood loss, transfusion, and recovery in spine surgery, anemia severity has not been systematically studied in this population. In particular, anterior cervical spine procedures pose unique physiologic demands and may be especially sensitive to hematologic compromise. Understanding how the degree of preoperative anemia influences both short-term complications and longer-term structural outcomes is essential for tailoring perioperative care and implementing patient counseling. This study evaluates the relationship between anemia severity and postoperative outcomes in a large cohort of patients undergoing cervical spine surgery, with 90-day and 1-year follow-up. Material and Methods: Using the TriNetX Research Network, adult patients who underwent anterior cervical spine procedures were identified and stratified into four groups based on preoperative anemia severity: severe (< 8 g/dL), moderate (8-9.9 g/dL), and mild (10-11.9 g/dL), and no anemia. Propensity score matching (1:1) was performed using the Charlson Comorbidity Index to control for baseline medical complexity across cohorts. Postoperative outcomes were assessed at two time points: 90-day complications (surgical site infection, transfusion, pneumonia, postoperative anemia, and venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE)) and 1-year outcomes (pseudoarthrosis and reoperation). Risk ratios (RR), 95% confidence intervals (CI), and p-values were calculated for each comparison. Results: A total of 34,391 patients were included in the analysis. Anemia of any severity was associated with significantly elevated rates of 90-day complications compared to non-anemia patients. Patients with severe anemia had the highest relative risk for transfusion (RR: 7.11, p < 0.0001), DVT/PE (RR: 3.70, p < 0.00001), and pneumonia (RR: 2.74, p < 0.0001). Moderate and mild anemia also demonstrated consistent and significant associations with adverse events, including elevated risk for infection, transfusion, and postoperative anemia. At 1 year, patients with severe and moderate anemia had markedly higher rates of reoperation (RR: 1.54 and 1.93, respectively). Pseudoarthrosis rates were not significantly different across groups. Conclusion: Preoperative anemia, particularly hemoglobin < 10 g/dL, is an independent predictor of both early postoperative complications and 1-year reoperation. The stepwise increase in risk with worsening anemia underscores the need for anemia stratification in preoperative risk models. While anemia is not always modifiable, especially in the setting of chronic disease or malignancy, its presence should prompt heightened perioperative vigilance and transparent discussion with patients regarding surgical risk. Future prospective studies are warranted to determine whether preoperative correction of anemia can reduce complication rates and improve long-term outcomes in cervical spine patients.

## ID: AGS26/313

## P0892: Late implant removal of steffe plate system without the original instrumentation set in the pedicle screw era: a note on the technical nuances and workarounds

Sreejith Chandran Menon

1

1

Meitra Hospital, Kozhikode, India

Introduction: Steffee plate system, introduced in 1982, was once a standard instrumentation method for spinal fixation and was widely used till modern pedicle screws were introduced. Occasionally, older patients present back for revision surgeries or with late infection of the existing Steffee implants and in many parts of the world, the original instrumentation sets and implants are no longer available. Younger generation of spine surgeons, who are trained in the pedicle screw era are often not exposed to the system ,and may face difficulties with the removal of the same. Material and Methods: We discuss the case reports of 2 diabetic patients with history of Steffee plate fixation for trauma spine about 15 years ago, presented to us with delayed infection of implants necessitating implant exit. Due to the unavailability of original instrumentation, implant exit was done with difficulty using general orthopaedic set and limb reconstruction system (LRS) instrumentation, keeping a metal cutting burr as backup. The parts of the system were reviewed, with emphasis to the relation between top-nuts and the screw. Results: The notable findings intra op were the presence of fibrous discoloured scar around the plates, and the cold welding of top-nuts to the conical tapered screw or plate. Even though infected screws were loose and were expected to be removed easily, the top-nuts and the whole screw were rotating as a single system around the plate, preventing unscrewing. In one case, the screw was found cut flush with the top nut, providing no place to hold a counter-torque. The hexagonal T wrench was not engaging in the top-nut due to the blunting of nut corners, and a polygonal ring wrench from the orthopaedic LRS set had to be used. A pointed nose plier/vise-grip was inevitable to loosen the nuts where standard vise-grip failed to grab tightly. Counter torque during nut removal was given with a spanner, or by holding the plate with another pointed plier. Since none of the tricks worked in 2 screws in one case, the entire plate-screw construct was levered up as a whole using an osteotome, which was possible due to the infected loose screws, risking pedicle breakage. As both of our cases had documented spine fusion in pre op CT scan and no instability, revision of instrumentation was not done, with no pedicle fracture noted intra op clinically and patients showed no worsening of deformity in follow ups. Conclusion: Late removal of implants are sought with difficulty when the original instrumentation is no longer available, and may result in an unsuccessful surgery. The general orthopaedic set with 2 or more pointed nose pliers, hexagonal T wrench, spanners of all sizes, wide osteotome, pointed vise-grips and a polygonal ring wrench in LRS set were the minimum requirements used in our cases; with a metal cutting burr and hollow mills arranged as backup. Special care must be taken to avoid iatrogenic fractures and pedicle breakages in cases where a re-instrumentation is planned. These cases should be well planned ;possible difficult scenarios described should be kept in mind and may require a backup plan when being done in a resource limited setting.

## ID: AGS26/366

## P0893: Preventive measures against postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) in spine surgery

Yushi Hoshino

1,2

, Shu Sakai

1

, Ai Sakai

1

, Yusei Miyanaga

1

, Ichiro Okano

2

, Yusuke Oshita

2

, Yoshinori Imaizumi

1

, Yoshifumi Kudo

2

1

Deptartment of Orthopedic Surgery, Asahi University Hospital, Gifu, Japan

2

Deptartment of Orthopedic Surgery, Showa Medical University, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) following spinal surgery is reported to occur in approximately 30% of cases. Like postoperative pain, PONV is unpleasant, contributes to reduced patient satisfaction, and can also hinder rehabilitation. This study evaluated the effectiveness of PONV prevention measures implemented during spinal surgery. Material and Methods: A total of 335 spinal surgeries performed between January 2020 and August 2022 were retrospectively reviewed. Male: 187, Female: 148, mean age: 66.3 years. Surgical sites: Cervical spine: 30 cases, Thoracic spine: 25 cases, Lumbar spine: 280 cases. PONV risk assessment (Apfel score: female, non-smoker, motion sickness/history of PONV, postoperative opioid use) was used to guide preventive measures. Preventive measures included: ① Droperidol bolus within IV-PCA, ② Intravenous Dexamethazone during surgery, ③ Female patients: Intravenous Metoclopramide immediately postoperatively. PONV occurrence was evaluated in these cases. Results: PONV: 19 cases (5.7%). Nausea only: 12 cases; vomiting present: 7 cases. Gender: Male 5 cases, Female 15 cases; PONV occurrence was significantly higher in females. No significant differences were observed by surgical site. Additionally, only one female patient required supplemental antiemetic medication. Discussion: In our previous report, PONV occurred in 31.4% of spinal surgery patients. This time, using the Apfel score as a reference, we focused prevention on postoperative opioid use and female patients. As a result, PONV incidence decreased significantly. Furthermore, among PONV cases, only one patient actually required additional antiemetic medication. Therefore, we consider this prevention method also reduces the severity of PONV. Since PONV itself occurs only within the first few postoperative days, it has not been actively prevented in Japan. However, given the trend toward shorter hospital stays, once PONV occurs, it contributes to delayed rehabilitation and reduced patient satisfaction, making proactive measures essential. Because PONV onset involves multiple factors, prevention with a single agent is difficult. Therefore, multifaceted preventive strategies are necessary, requiring collaboration not only within the orthopedic department but also with anesthesiology and other departments. Conclusion: As a result of implementing targeted PONV prevention measures, particularly for postoperative opioid users and female patients, PONV was observed in only 5.7% of cases.

## ID: AGS26/406

## P0894: Preoperative anemia severity predicts complications following lumbar decompression

Melissa Romoff

1

, Michael Kim

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Preoperative anemia is a well-established risk factor for adverse surgical outcomes, with several studies in joint arthroplasty demonstrating a stepwise increase in complication risk as anemia severity worsens. These findings have led to the integration of anemia screening and optimization protocols into perioperative pathways for total hip and knee arthroplasty. However, despite similar concerns around blood loss, transfusion, and recovery in spine surgery, anemia severity has not been systematically studied in this population. In particular, lumbar decompression procedures have unique physiologic demands and may be especially sensitive to hematologic compromise. Understanding how the degree of postoperative anemia influences both short-term complications and longer-term structural outcomes is essential for tailoring perioperative care and implementing patient counseling. This study evaluates the relationship between anemia severity and postoperative outcomes in a large cohort of patients undergoing lumbar decompression, with 90-day and 1-year follow-up. Material and Methods: Using the TriNetX Research Network, adult patients who underwent lumbar decompression procedures were identified and stratified into four groups based on preoperative anemia severity: severe (< 8g/dL), moderate (8-9.9g/dL), and mild (10-11.9g/dL), and no anemia. Propensity score matching (1:1) was performed using the Charlson Comorbidity Index to control for baseline medical complexity across cohorts. Postoperative outcomes were assessed at two time points: 90-day complications (surgical site infection, transfusion, pneumonia, postoperative anemia, and venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE)) and 1-year outcomes (pseudoarthrosis and reoperation). Risk ratios (RR), 95% confidence intervals (CI), and p-values were calculated for each comparison. Results: Among over 109,000 patients undergoing lumbar decompression, anemia severity was associated with significantly elevated rates of 90-day complications and 1-year outcomes. Patients with severe anemia had the highest relative risks across nearly all metrics, including transfusion (RR: 6.91, p < 0.0001), postoperative anemia (RR: 3.72, p < 0.0001), lumbar fracture (RR: 3.22, p < 0.0001). Pseudoarthrosis (RR: 1.94, p < 0.0001), and reoperation (RR: 1.73, p < 0.0001) were also significantly more common. Patients with moderate anemia demonstrated similar trends, with increased risk of transfusion (RR: 5.22), postoperative anemia (RR: 2.78), lumbar fracture (RR: 2.59), pseudoarthrosis (RR: 1.76), and reoperation (RR: 1.50), all p < 0.0001. Even mild anemia was associated with higher complications rates. These findings demonstrate a clear stepwise relationship between anemia severity and adverse postoperative outcomes following lumbar decompression. Conclusion: Preoperative anemia, particularly when moderate to severe, is an independent predictor of both early postoperative complications and 1-year reoperation. The stepwise increase in risk with worsening anemia underscores the need for anemia stratification in preoperative risk models. While anemia is not always modifiable, especially in the setting of chronic disease or malignancy, its presence should prompt heightened perioperative vigilance and transparent discussion with patients regarding surgical risk. Future prospective studies are warranted to determine whether preoperative correction of anemia can reduce complication rates and improve long-term outcomes in patients undergoing lumbar decompression.

## ID: AGS26/424

## P0895: Extended oral antibiotic prophylaxis after anterior cervical spinal fusion is associated with increased infection and sepsis rates

Melissa Romoff

1

, Michael Kim

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Extended oral antibiotic prophylaxis, commonly employed in hip and knee arthroplasty beyond the standard 24-hour perioperative period1, has gained popularity as a preventive measure2. However, recent large-scale matched cohort studies have reported no reduction in prosthetic joint infection rates and, in some cases, an increase in complications. Despite limited evidence, the practice remains widespread, particularly among high-risk patients3,4. In spine surgery, the efficacy of extended oral antibiotics in reducing postoperative infection, sepsis, or revision rates has not been evaluated in large, multi-institutional studies. Given the rising concerns over antibiotic resistance and microbiome disruption, this study investigates the clinical utility of extended prophylaxis following posterior cervical spinal surgery. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was performed using data from the TriNetX United States Collaborative Network. Adult patients (≥ 18 years) who underwent posterior cervical spinal surgery were identified via CPT codes. All patients were assumed to have received standard intravenous antibiotic prophylaxis within 24 hours postoperatively. Cohorts were defined by the presence or absence of extended oral antibiotic prescriptions administered between 24 hours and 7 days following surgery. Propensity score matching (1:1) was used to control for age, sex, BMI, smoking status, and relevant comorbidities (e.g., renal failure, diabetes mellitus). The primary outcome was 90-day postoperative spinal surgical site infection. Secondary outcomes included reoperation and sepsis. Outcomes were analyzed using Kaplan-Meier survival estimates, hazard ratios, and cumulative incidence statistics. Results: A total of 9,472 patients who received extended oral antibiotics following posterior cervical spinal fusion were propensity score matched 1:1 to 9,472 patients who did not. The 90-day incidence of surgical site infection was statistically higher in the extended antibiotic cohort compared to controls (20.612% vs. 18.232%; RR: 1.131, p ≤ 0.0001). Reoperation rates were also elevated in the antibiotic group (3.324% vs. 2.832%, RR: 1.174, p = 0.0424). Sepsis occurred in 3.206% of patients receiving extended antibiotics versus 2.449% of controls (RR 1.309, p < 0.0011). Wound infections were significantly more frequent in the extended antibiotic cohort (4.307% vs. 2.97%; RR 1.471, p < 0.0001). Conclusion: Extended oral antibiotic prophylaxis following posterior spinal fusion was associated with significantly higher rates of surgical site infection, sepsis, wound infection, and reoperation compared to standard perioperative prophylaxis alone. These findings, while accounting for potential selection bias, challenge the presumed protective benefit of extended antibiotic use in this context. In light of the increased complication rates and the broader implications for antimicrobial resistance and microbiome disruption, the routine use of extended prophylaxis warrants critical reevaluation.

## ID: AGS26/472

## P0896: Cervical spinal cord injury elevates risk of psychiatric morbidity in matched cohort analysis

Michael Kim

1

, Melissa Romoff

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a life-altering condition associated with disproportionately high rates of depression and anxiety, with prevalence estimated to be two to three times greater than in the general population. These psychiatric comorbidities negatively affect rehabilitation engagement and functional outcomes. However, few studies have isolated the psychological impact of SCI from that of trauma more broadly. This study aimed to compare the incidence of new-onset mood and anxiety disorders in patients with cervical SCI versus matched trauma-exposed controls with long bone or pelvic fractures. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted using the TriNetX United States Collaborative Network. Adult patients (≥1 8 years) with a new diagnosis of cervical SCI were identified and matched 1:1 to patients with pelvic or long bone fractures. Patients with prior diagnoses of mood or anxiety disorders or prior psychiatric medication use were excluded. The primary outcomes were new diagnoses of mood or anxiety disorders and new prescriptions for antidepressants or anxiolytics between 14 and 90 days post-injury. Propensity score matching controlled for age, sex, race, comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, obesity), and substance use. Psychiatric outcomes were assessed using Kaplan-Meier analysis, hazard ratios, risk ratios, and odds ratios. Results: A total of 36,492 patients with cervical SCI were matched to 36,492 orthopedic trauma controls. New-onset mood or anxiety disorders occurred more frequently in the SCI cohort (0.85%) compared to controls (0.29%; RR 2.90, 95% CI: 2.33-3.61, p < 0.0001). Cervical SCI was also associated with significantly increased initiation of psychiatric medications, including SSRIs (RR 5.11), SNRIs (RR 4.17), atypical antidepressants (RR 4.51), tricyclic antidepressants (RR 7.38), and anxiolytics (RR 4.62; all p < 0.0001). SCI patients were over eight times more likely to be referred for psychiatric services (RR 8.07, 95% CI: 4.63-14.06). Conclusion: Cervical SCI is associated with a markedly increased risk of new-onset mood and anxiety disorders, psychiatric medication initiation, and mental health service utilization compared to matched high-energy orthopedic trauma patients. These findings highlight a distinct psychiatric burden following SCI and underscore the need for early mental health screening and integrated psychiatric care in this population.

## ID: AGS26/482

## P0897: Identifying predictors of postoperative hemoglobin recovery patterns following TLIF

Melissa Romoff

1

, Michael Kim

1

, Eric Lin

2

, Emily Mills

1

, Mark Spoonamore

2

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

2

University of Southern California, Los Angeles, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Hemoglobin (Hgb) trends following transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) are commonly used to estimate blood loss, guide transfusion protocols, and monitor recovery. Prior studies have largely focused on identifying the nadir - the absolute lowest postoperative Hgb value - as a proxy for blood loss or clinical concern. However, this single time point may not accurately represent the full clinical trajectory. The concept of postoperative Hgb “plateau” has not been well defined or systematically analyzed in the TLIF population. In this study, we propose a novel framework to classify patients based on Hgb stabilization patterns and use regression-based approaches, including LASSO, to identify clinical predictors of cohort membership. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed adult patients undergoing TLIF with postoperative Hgb measurements through postoperative day (POD) 3-5. Patients were assigned to one of four cohorts based on daily Hgb changes: Constant (minimal variation, daily change < 0.5 g/dL). Decline (ongoing decrease without stabilization), Late (nadir on or after POD4 without plateau), or Plateau (initial drop followed by stabilization, defined as < 0.5 g/dL change over ≥ 2 consecutive days). Transfused patients were excluded from hemoglobin visualization trends. Clinical and perioperative variables -including age, sex, BMI, estimated blood loss (EBL), drain output, number of TLIF levels, preoperative Hgb, platelet count, and transfusion status- were analyzed using multinomial logistic regression. To reduce model complexity and validator predictor robustness, LASSO-penalized regression was performed on a subset with complete data. Results: A total of 57 patients met inclusion criteria. Hemoglobin nadirs occurred most commonly on POD3 (n = 17, 30%), followed by POD4 (n = 16, 28%), POD2 (n = 11, 19%), POD5 (n = 9, 16%). POD1 (n = 4, 7%). Based on daily hemoglobin patterns, patients were classified into four cohorts reflecting distinct postoperative trends: Constant (n =7), Decline (n = 10), Late (n = 20), Plateau (n = 20). Of the 57 patients, 30 (53%) experienced a > 0.5g/dL hemoglobin decline between POD1 and POD2. Fewer patients showed continued decline on subsequent days: 12 (21%) from POD2 to POD3, 11 (19%) from POD3 to POD4, and 5 (9%) from POD4 to POD5. Average Hgb change interval was -0.72 g/dL (POD1-2), -0.11 (POD2-3), -0.12 (POD3-4), and +0.04 (POD4-5). Traditional multinomial regression revealed that the Decline cohort was biologically distinct. Compared to the Late cohort, Decline patients were more likely to be male (p = 0.048) and have lower preoperative platelet counts (p = 0.017). In the LASSO model, five variables were retained: gender, EBL, preoperative Hgb, platelet count, and TLIF levels. Within this model, Decline patients showed significantly higher intraoperative blood loss (p = 0.031), lower platelet counts (p = 0.006), were more likely to be male (p = 0.049), and showed a trend toward lower preoperative Hgb (p = 0.063). Despite the smaller sample size, the Lasso model achieved a 58.3% classification accuracy supporting the robustness of selected predictors.
