Conclusion: Hemoglobin commonly continues to decline after POD1 in TLIF patients, with over half of the cohort experiencing a > 0.5 g/dL drop between POD1 and POD2. This trajectory is often misinterpreted as ongoing blood loss, prompting unnecessary diagnostic workups. Recognizing this expected pattern may reduce unwarranted interventions during routine postoperative care.

## ID: AGS26/483

## P0898: Postoperative NSAID use and lumbar fusion outcomes: timing-dependent risks by surgical approach

Michael Kim

1

, Melissa Romoff

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Postoperative pain control is essential after lumbar fusion, but reliance on opioids raises concerns about prolonged use and dependency. In response, alternative, opioid-sparing strategies increasingly incorporate NSAIDs for their analgesic and anti-inflammatory benefits. However, NSAID use in spine surgery remains controversial due to potential inhibition of bone healing and increased risk of nonunion. While prior studies have linked NSAIDs to pseudoarthrosis, most have evaluated NSAID exposure as a binary variable, without accounting for timing - a factor that may significantly affect outcomes. As NSAIDs become more common in postoperative protocols, clarifying how timing influences risk is critical. This study investigates the association between NSAID use at three postoperative intervals (0-30, 30-60, and 60-90 days) and complication risk following TLIF/PLIF and ALIF procedures, to better understand the temporal impact of analgesic timing on surgical outcomes. Material and Methods: Using the TriNetX Research Network, adult patients undergoing TLIF/PLIF or ALIF were identified and stratified based on NSAID use during one of three isolate postoperative intervals: 0-30 days, 30-60 days, or 60-90 days. NSAID users were compared to matched non-users within each time frame. Outcomes included 90-day complications (postoperative anemia, transfusion, surgical site infection [SSI], pneumonia, venous thromboembolism [DVT/PE]) and 1-year complications (reoperation, pseudoarthrosis, and lumbar fracture). Risk ratios (RR), 95% confidence intervals (CI), and p-values were calculated for each outcome. Results: Of 11,022 TLIF/PLIF patients, NSAID use within 0-30 days was associated with significantly lower rates of postoperative anemia (RR: 0.774, p < 0.0001), transfusion (RR: 0.85, p = 0.0022), and DVT/PE (RR: 0.797, p = 0.0057),however it conferred a significant increase in pseudoarthrosis (RR: 1.11, p < 0.0001). In contrast, delayed NSAID use (30-90 days) was associated with a significant increase in reoperation risk (RR: 1.8, p = 0.0147) and lumbar fracture (RR:1.667, p = 0.0416) in the 60-90 day group, despite continued reduction in transfusion and anemia. Of 9,048 ALIF patients, NSAID use in the early postoperative period (0-30 days) was associated with a significantly lower risk of pseudoarthrosis (RR: 0.906, p = 0.0138) and reduced reoperation rates (RR: 0.785, p < 0.0001). In contrast, NSAID use between 30-90 days was linked to decreased transfusion and reoperation, but showed a trend toward increased pseudoarthrosis. Conclusion: NSAID use demonstrates a complex, timing-dependent pattern of risk. In TLIF/PLIF, early NSAID use (0-30 days) was linked to reduced rates of anemia, transfusion, and thromboembolic events, but at the cost of a modest increase in pseudoarthrosis risk. Delayed use (30-90 days) further lowered hematologic complications but was associated with increased rates of reoperation and lumbar fracture, In contrast, early NSAID use after ALIF was associated with lower rates of pseudoarthrosis and reoperation, while delayed use was linked to reduced transfusion and reoperation without a clear impact on fusion outcomes. These findings underscore the importance of both surgical approach and NSAID timing in postoperative management. Prospective studies are warranted to clarify causality and guide the development of procedure-specific, time-targeted NSAID guidelines.

## ID: AGS26/560

## P0899: Epidural hematoma following thoracic vertebroplasty in multiple myeloma patients: two case reports and literature review

Ricardo Navarro-Navarro

1,2

, Tito Fernadez-Varela

1

, Brian Melchiorsen

1

, Laura Pastor-Martin

1

, Jose Augusto Lorenzo-Rivero

1

, Arturo Montesdeoca-Ara

1

, Alba López-Martel

3

1

Hospital Universitario de Gran Canaria Dr Negrin, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

2

University of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria (ULPGC), Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

3

Fundación Canaria Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria de Canarias, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

Introduction: Epidural hematoma (EH) after vertebroplasty is a rare but potentially serious complication (0.2-1.2%), usually related to vascular injury during pedicular puncture. Early recognition via MRI or CT is critical when neurological deterioration occurs. We report two cases in multiple myeloma (MM) patients. Material and Methods: Case 1: A 67-year-old female with newly diagnosed MM presented with T4-T8 vertebral fractures and renal insufficiency, without neurological deficit. Conservative treatment failed, and T5, T7, and T8 vertebroplasties were performed. Persistent thoracic pain prompted MRI, revealing an T4-T7 epidural hematoma without cord compression. Case 2: A 71-year-old female with MM relapse, renal failure, thrombocytopenia, and multiple dorsal fractures underwent T1-T3 cemented arthrodesis and T8 and T12 vertebroplasty. Postoperatively, she developed acute ASIA C deficit. MRI showed T7-L5 extensive epidural hematoma with intramedullary signal changes. Results: Case 1: Surgery was not indicated. One month later, she developed ASIA C deficit; follow-up MRI showed resolution of the hematoma but spinal cord signal changes. She did not regain mobility and died two months later due to MM progression. Case 2: Urgent decompression at D8 and D12 provided local relief but no clinical improvement. She died two weeks later due to pulmonary and renal complications. Conclusion: Vertebroplasty is generally safe and effective for MM-related fracture pain, even in immunocompromised patients. Hematoma risk is elevated due to coagulation abnormalities in MM. Literature shows most thoracic spine vertebroplasty EHs recover neurologically after decompressive laminectomy; key predictors are hematoma size, motor impairment severity, and rapid decompression. In our cases, advanced MM and poor general condition likely contributed to unfavorable outcomes. These reports highlight the importance of vigilant post-vertebroplasty monitoring in MM patients.

## ID: AGS26/564

## P0900: Evaluating the role of C2PA in predicting proximal junctional failure

Lane McCoy

1

, Christopher Diaz

1

, Neset Tang

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: C2 Pelvic Angle (PA) has been proposed as a predictor of proximal junctional failure (PJF), with prior work suggesting a C2PA > 20° may be linked to worse outcomes. This study assesses whether immediate postoperative C2PA - alone or alongside other alignment measures such as L1PA, T4PA, and Proximal Junctional Angle (PJA) - is associated with PJF development following adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. Material and Methods: 730 ASD patients who underwent fusion of ≥7 levels with pelvic instrumentation were included. Patients were then divided into two groups based on the presence or absence of PJF, defined as symptomatic proximal junctional kyphosis requiring revision. Immediate postoperative radiographic parameters-C2PA, PJA, L1PA, and T4PA - were recorded. The L1PA formula (L1PA = 0.5*(Pelvic Incidence) - 19 ±2) was used to assess alignment. Various C2PA thresholds (≥ 10°, ≥ 15°, ≥ 20°, ≥ 25°, ≥ 30°, ≥ 35°, ≥ 40°) were tested for association with PJF. The immediate postop radiographic parameters were also assessed for a relationship with the development of PJF. Results: The mean age was 49.1 years, with a follow-up of 20.5 months. PJF developed in 5.75% of patients. Immediate postoperative C2PA, including values >20° and in combination with L1PA alignment, was not significantly associated with PJF (p = 0.71, p = 0.95). Likewise, no C2PA threshold was linked to increased PJF risk (all p > 0.1). However, immediate postoperative PJA was significantly higher in patients who developed PJF (mean 11.17°) compared to those who did not (mean 6.13°, p < 0.001). Conclusion: Immediate postoperative C2PA did not correlate with PJF in this cohort, even when evaluated across multiple cutoff points or combined with alignment metrics like L1PA or T4PA. In contrast, elevated immediate postoperative PJA was significantly associated with PJF development. These findings suggest PJA may provide more relevant predictive insights than C2PA in evaluating PJF risk.

## ID: AGS26/587

## P0901: Management of subarachnoid-pleural fistula after spinal deformity surgery using non-invasive positive pressure ventilation: a case report

Rosa M Egea-Gámez

1

, Alfonso González-Menocal

2

, Rafael González-Díaz

1

1
Hospital Infantil Universitario Niño Jesús, Madrid, Spain, 
2
Hospital Universitario Infanta Elena, Madrid, Spain
Introduction: Subarachnoid-pleural fistula (SPF), an abnormal communication between the subarachnoid and pleural spaces, is an uncommon complication after spinal surgery, with an incidence of 1.4%. It predominantly presents as pleural effusion (≈74%), since the pressure gradient between the pleural cavity and the subarachnoid space favors the accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) in the pleural space. Detection of β2-transferrin in the fluid provides a highly sensitive diagnostic tool (≈85.7%). This study reports an illustrative clinical case highlighting the importance of accurate diagnosis and effective management of this complication, focusing on the role of non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV). Material and Methods: A 14-year-old adolescent with neuromuscular kyphoscoliosis (global hyperkyphosis and scoliosis of 73°) underwent surgical correction of the deformity with instrumented fusion from T2 to the ilium, without intraoperative complications. On postoperative day 6, the patient developed a right pleural effusion without evident signs of infection. Thoracentesis confirmed a transudate; a chest tube was placed, β2-transferrin testing of the pleural fluid was positive, and spinal CT revealed minimal intracanal invasion of a T8 pedicle screw, establishing the diagnosis of SPF. Results: The initial course was unsatisfactory, with continuous chest tube drainage averaging 300 ml/day and persistence of a 2-3 cm effusion on radiological follow-up. In the absence of improvement, acetazolamide and NIPPV were initiated. This strategic approach led to progressive reduction of the effusion, reaching minimal values of 3 mm after 4 days and complete resolution after 9 days. NIPPV was discontinued on day 10 without recurrence of the effusion. Conclusion: SPF should be considered in the differential diagnosis of pleural effusion after spinal surgery. Continuous application of NIPPV for 10 days proved to be an effective strategy, achieving fistula closure without the need for surgical reintervention. This case underscores the importance of comprehensive postoperative management in spinal surgery and highlights the relevance of NIPPV as a valuable tool in the treatment of this complication.

## ID: AGS26/603

## P0902: Meat mincer effect during cemented spondylodesis that leads to cement leakage migration to the inferior vena cava

Marc Prod'Homme

1

, Alexis Paoli

2

, Arun Thangavelu

3

, Blaise-Dominique Zambaz

1

, Sébastien Déglise

2

1

Clinic CIC, Clarens, Switzerland

2

University Hospital of Lausanne (CHUV), Lausanne, Switzerland

3

EUROSPINE Center of Excellence, Brig, Switzerland

Introduction: Cement leakages during percutaneous cementoplasty are well-known complications. Accounting for 10 to 30% of cases, they may be treated conservatively most of the time. Only some posterior leakages may require emergency decompression to free from neural structure compression. Vascular leakages are frequent, but usually their extension is limited by the control of cement pressure. The aim of this study was to describe a rare case of cement leakage migration to the inferior vena cava. Material and Methods: An 82-year-old woman, with osteoporosis and previous L3/4/5 decompression and mild left L5 paresis, was followed by lumbar MRI because of a new onset of lumbar pain after carrying a mild weighted device at home. MRI found an osteoporotic fracture OF4 of L3 with 25% of vertebral height loss, as well as recurrence of severe L4/5 stenosis by a disc herniation. Dynamic lumbar X-rays showed unstable spondylolisthesis at L4-5 level. A L3 kyphoplasty was scheduled with redo of decompression and cemented L4/5 spondylodesis. At the end of the spondylodesis, an image compatible with cement was observed at the L1-2 level under fluoroscopy. L3 kyphoplasty was performed and posterior decompression with dural suture for dural tear. A postoperative CT-scan confirmed cement migration within the inferior vena cava. The patient was transferred to the university hospital center at the vascular surgery department to remove the cement by anterior spra-umbilical laparotomy and vena cava approach. Results: Fragment of cement was removed in one piece without the need for vascular plasty. Intraoperative findings showed a structure looking like minced meat, with pieces stuck together. Postoperatively, the patient was treated with analgesics and curative heparin. After 2 days at the middle care unit, she was able to come back to the vascular surgery department. The follow-up was uneventful. Mechanism of cement leakage and its migration was considered as a high pressure on cement during L5 pedicle screw insertion, leading to cement leakage through the vertebral venous system and lumbar veins, with connection to the iliac veins and thus the inferior vena cava. Conclusion: This rare complication illustrated the need to carefully manage cemented lumbar spondylodesis and to avoid putting pedicle screws after cement injection. Further studies are needed to assess the behavior of the cement in vivo and the pressure around the pedicle screws during insertion.

## ID: AGS26/659

## P0903: White cord syndrome: systematic review and case series of 22 cases

Humaid Al Farii

1

1

Sultan Qaboos University, Muscat, Oman

Background: White Cord Syndrome (WCS) is a rare but serious complication that can occur after spinal decompression surgery. This review looks at 22 cases-20 from the published literature and 2 from Sultan Qaboos Hospital in Oman-to better understand patient characteristics, imaging findings, surgical practices, and outcomes. Our findings suggest that early detection and intervention may lead to better neurological recovery. Most patients had partial recovery, and common comorbidities included hypertension and diabetes. Methods: Fifty reported cases of WCS were initially identified through online databases. After excluding duplicates and unrelated cases, 20 published reports were analyzed alongside two additional local cases. We collected data on demographics, comorbidities, MRI results, surgical methods, use of intraoperative monitoring, timing of neurological deficits, and recovery outcomes. The data were summarized using descriptive statistics and trend analysis. Results: The average patient age was 55.3 years, and most were male. Hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation were the most frequent comorbidities. Most patients had severe cervical stenosis or disc herniation, often with T2 hyperintense signals on preoperative MRI. Posterior decompression and fusion were the most common surgical techniques, and intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM) was used in over 90% of cases. Despite surgery, many patients developed new neurological deficits-typically tetraplegia or quadriparesis-within 48 hours. Outcomes showed: 60% had partial recovery, 20% had full recovery and 20% had no recovery. Longer recovery periods were associated with more severe initial injuries. Although the use of IONM correlated with somewhat better outcomes, the small sample size limited statistical certainty. Conclusion: White Cord Syndrome is a rare but critical complication that can dramatically affect postoperative recovery. Close attention to preoperative imaging, comorbidity management, and early postoperative care may help improve outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/667

## P0904: Propensity-matched analysis of anticoagulation in lumbar spine surgery: increased complications without VTE benefit in decompressions, equivocal in fusions

Reme Arhewoh

1

, Elias Schwartz

1

, Jack Eichman

1

, Aayush Unadkat

1

, Abdel Diab

1

, Joshua Piche

1

, Rakesh Patel

1

, Ilyas Aleem

1

1

University of Michigan, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: The use of anticoagulation in spine surgery remains debated due to competing risks of thromboembolic events versus surgical complications. Anatomical location and need for instrumentation introduce distinct risk profiles often overlooked when spine surgery is considered as a whole. Prior studies have been limited by small sample sizes, retrospective designs, and unclear anticoagulation indications. Prescribing differences between orthopedic and neurosurgery services at our institution, combined with prospectively collected outcomes, created a natural experiment to address this question. Material and Methods: A multicenter prospectively collected database was queried for all patients treated at a tertiary care institution from 2013–2023. Chart review confirmed adherence to service-specific prescribing patterns. Patients with prior DVT or hypercoagulable conditions were excluded. Propensity score matching balanced baseline characteristics. Composite outcomes for overall and neurologic complications were created. T-tests and Chi-square tests were used for continuous and categorical variables, respectively. Binary logistic regression with Firth correction was employed for rare events. Results: The matched cohort included 1,722 patients (861 per group), with a mean age of 63 years, 89% White, and 58% male. Overall complication rates were similar between anticoagulated (AC) and non-anticoagulated (NAC) patients (33.4% vs. 30.4%, p = 0.18). DVT (1.2% vs. 0.7%, p = 0.315), PE (0% vs. 0.3%, p = 0.083), and hematoma requiring reoperation (0.2% vs. 0.9%, p = 0.057) were low and nonsignificant. However, AC patients had higher rates of wound dehiscence (2.1% vs. 0.6%, p = 0.006), CSF leaks (4.8% vs. 1.9%, p = 0.001), neurogenic bowel/bladder dysfunction (1.0% vs. 0.2%, p = 0.034), urinary retention (6.9% vs. 3.6%, p = 0.002), and longer hospital stays (3.90 vs. 1.94 days, p < 0.001). Discharge to skilled nursing facilities was more frequent in AC patients (12.1% vs. 6.6%, p < 0.001). Return to OR within 90 days was higher in the NAC group (5.5% vs. 3.5%, p = 0.047). In the lumbar decompression subgroup (n = 883), AC patients had significantly higher rates of any complication (32.7% vs. 22.3%, p < 0.001), neurologic complications (9.9% vs. 4.1%, p < 0.001) and new weakness (9.3% vs. 3.6%, p < 0.001), Lumbar fusion patients showed no significant differences in complication rates, though return to OR remained higher in NAC (p = 0.039). Logistic regression identified time to discharge (OR = 1.22, p < 0.001) and prior spine surgery (OR = 1.73, p < 0.001) as consistent predictors of overall and neurologic complications. Coronary artery disease (OR = 1.59, p = 0.013) increased risk; Asian race was protective (OR = 0.113, p = 0.039). Within the decompression subgroup, anticoagulant use independently predicted neurologic complications (OR = 2.57, p = 0.026. Conclusion: Anticoagulation in lumbar spine surgery was associated with increased complications across multiple domains without reducing thromboembolic events. Risk was concentrated in lumbar decompression patients, while fusion patients showed no significant differences. These findings underscore the need to tailor anticoagulation strategies to individual patients and procedural factors, and raise questions about routine anticoagulation use in elective lumbar procedures.

## ID: AGS26/711

## P0905: Management of wound necrosis and dehiscence after spinal surgery: Collaboration between orthopaedic and plastic surgery

Ahmet Hamdi Olçar

1

, Serdar Tuncer

2

1

Sancaktepe Prof.Dr.Ilhan Varank Training and Research Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Istanbul Bilim University, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Wound complications after spinal surgery can have serious consequences, therefore early and aggressive management is necessary. However, it is difficult to construct a surgical plan due to the wide range of wound problems. We undertook a retrospective chart review of patients who underwent reconstruction of the back after spinal surgery. The goals of the study were to determine the patients managed with primary repair versus those who required flap reconstruction, and based on these results to develop an algorithm for the management of wound necrosis and dehiscence after spinal operations. Material and Methods: We collected information on patient demographics, the index spine operation, location and size of the necrosis or skin defect, comorbidities, types of wound problems, reconstructive methods, complications after repair, and follow up time. A reconstructive algorithm, based on a two layer (deep and superficial) repair was constructed. Results: Thirty two patients with mean age 38 (4-80) were reconstructed. Most of the wounds (n = 18) were located in the thoracic area, followed by the lumbar (12), and cervical (n = 2). The index spinal operations were performed for scoliosis or other spinal deformities (n = 9), disc disease (n= 6), spinal stenosis (n = 10), vertebral fracture (n = 5), metastasis of prostate cancer (n = 1), and neural tube defect (n = 1). Necrosis or defect sizes ranged between 2x5 cm and 30x8 cm. The wounds were closed in a single operation in all patients. Overall there were 8 muscle flaps (five paraspinous muscular, two latissimus dorsi muscular, and one trapezius musculocutaneous), 5 fasciocutaneous flaps, and 20 skin only primary closures. One patient underwent implant removal 6 months later due to exposure of the prosthesis at another site. Conclusion: An algorithmic approach is useful to address the different requirements in post spinal surgery wound complications. Patients with an intact muscular layer and relatively minor skin losses can be salvaged with primary skin only repair, which is simple and aesthetically the most advantageous. Larger defects confined to the skin layer can be closed with local or regional skin flaps. Patients with exposed implants, active CSF leak, and history of radiotherapy require reconstruction of both the muscle and skin layers.

## ID: AGS26/763

## P0906: A postoperative variant of Bow-Hunter syndrome: dynamic subaxial vertebral artery compression following posterior cervical surgery

Yunus Emre Batman

1

, Ferdi Afşin

1

, Mehmet Said Çil

1

, Aydın Talat Baydar

1

, Bahadır Topal

1

, İlhan Yilmaz

1

1

Basaksehir Cam and Sakura City Hospital, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Bow-Hunter syndrome (BHS) is a rare form of vertebrobasilar insufficiency (VBI) caused by mechanical compression of the vertebral artery, most often triggered by head rotation [1-4]. While the majority of cases occur at the craniovertebral junction, postoperative subaxial variants are exceedingly uncommon [5]. We present a patient who developed a novel BHS-like syndrome characterized by cervical deformity and vertical vascular compression following posterior cervical surgery involving facet cage placement and decompression. Case Description: A 50-year-old male with ankylosing spondylitis underwent posterior cervical decompression with bilateral facet cage placement at C4-6 in another hospital for cervical stenosis and spondylolisthesis. Due to persistent symptoms, a revision procedure was performed, during which the right-sided cages were removed (Figure 1). Two weeks later, the patient began experiencing recurrent vertigo and syncopal episodes exclusively when transitioning to a sitting position. On admission, neurological examination revealed tetraparesis (upper extremities 2/5, lower extremities 3/5). CT angiography demonstrated coronal imbalance and listhesis with a dominant right vertebral artery loop at C4-5 (Figure 2-3). Symptom relief was observed with the application of a large Philadelphia cervical collar providing partial traction, further supporting the diagnosis of posture-dependent vertebrobasilar insufficiency. Results: The patient subsequently underwent anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) from C4 to C7, with PEEK cages restoring alignment and decompressing the vertebral artery. Postoperatively, all syncopal and vertigo episodes resolved completely. Follow-up CT angiography demonstrated correction of the coronal deformity and elimination of vertebral artery loop compression, while MRI confirmed resolution of foraminal narrowing (Figure 4-5-6). Conclusion: This case illustrates a rare postoperative variant of Bow-Hunter syndrome in which vertebral artery compression was induced by cervical deformity following posterior cervical surgery. Unlike classical BHS, symptoms were triggered not by head rotation but by vertical loading during postural change (sitting). Recognition of this unusual mechanism is critical, as timely anterior decompression and fusion achieved both radiographic correction and complete clinical recovery. Surgeons should maintain a high index of suspicion for vertebrobasilar insufficiency in patients-particularly those with underlying rheumatologic disease-who present with unexplained vertigo or syncope after posterior cervical procedures.
References
1. Spetzler RF, Hadley MN, Martin NA, Hopkins LN, Carter LP, Budny J. Vertebrobasilar insufficiency. Part 1: Microsurgical treatment of extracranial vertebrobasilar disease. J Neurosurg. May 1987;66(5):648-61. doi:10.3171/jns.1987.66.5.0648
2. Sorensen BF. Bow hunter's stroke. Neurosurgery. May-Jun 1978;2(3):259-61. doi:10.1227/00006123-197805000-00013
3. Nagashima C. Surgical treatment of vertebral artery insufficiency caused by cervical spondylosis. J Neurosurg. May 1970;32(5):512-21. doi:10.3171/jns.1970.32.5.0512
4. Bakay L, Leslie EV. Surgical treatment of vertebral artery insufficiency caused by cervical spondylosis. J Neurosurg. Dec 1965;23(6):596-602. doi:10.3171/jns.1965.23.6.0596
5. Shimizu T, Waga S, Kojima T, Niwa S. Decompression of the vertebral artery for bow-hunter's stroke. Case report. J Neurosurg. Jul 1988;69(1):127-31. doi:10.3171/jns.1988.69.1.0127

## ID: AGS26/778

## P0907: Increased risk of venous thromboembolism following posterior versus anterior cervical spine fusion: a propensity-matched cohort study

Robert Ferdon

1

, Jason Silvestre

2

, Warren Roth

2

, Charles Reitman

2

, James Lawrence

2

, Robert Ravinsky

2

1

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

2

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

Introduction: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) remains a potentially dangerous postoperative complication in spine surgery, with limited data comparing procedure-specific risk across surgical approaches. This study aimed to compare VTE incidence following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) versus posterior cervical decompression and fusion (PCDF), using a large, multicenter database. Materials and Methods: A retrospective, multicenter cohort study was conducted using the TriNetX database. Adult patients undergoing single- or multi-level ACDF or PCDF between 2004 and 2024 were identified using Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) codes. Patients with prior VTE, thrombophilia, or prior cervical spine surgery were excluded. Propensity score matching (1:1) was performed based on demographics, comorbidities, and anticoagulation use. Primary outcomes included 30- and 90-day incidence of VTE events (pulmonary embolism, upper and lower extremity deep venous thrombosis [DVT]) and postoperative bleeding complications. Differences between PCDF and ACDF were reported as odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs). Results: A total of 4,381 patients were included in each single-level cohort, and 15,577 patients in each multi-level cohort. Compared to single-level anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), single-level posterior cervical decompression and fusion (PCDF) was associated with significantly increased odds of venous thromboembolism (VTE) at both 30 days (OR: 1.96, 95% CI: 1.28-2.94; p = 0.001) and 90 days (OR: 2.05, 95% CI: 1.43-2.94; p < 0.001). Similarly, multi-level PCDF was associated with higher odds of VTE compared to multi-level ACDF at 30 days (OR: 3.83, 95% CI: 3.13-4.76; p < 0.001) and 90 days (OR: 3.97, 95% CI: 2.94-4.76; p < 0.001). No significant differences in postoperative bleeding complications were observed between groups. Conclusion: PCDF was associated with increased VTE complications with no differences in postoperative bleeding events when compared to ACDF. These findings underscore the need to reconsider existing VTE prophylaxis strategies for posterior cervical surgery. Incorporating surgical approach into risk stratification algorithms may help optimize outcomes by balancing thrombotic and bleeding risks. Further research is needed to refine chemoprophylaxis protocols and assess the clinical impact of VTE events in this patient population.

## ID: AGS26/801

## P0908: Bedside sarcopenia screening in resource-limited settings: a pragmatic tool for reducing spinal surgery complications

Wenjie Wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Sarcopenia screening in low-resource settings remains unvalidated. This study demonstrates that bedside functional measures achieve accuracy comparable to MRI. To validate a low-cost sarcopenia screening tool and quantify its prognostic value for postoperative complications. Material and Methods: Retrospective cohort study of 650 patients undergoing spinal surgery (2022.1-2023.12) at a county Chinese hospital. Interventions: Bedside screening (grip strength + gait speed) vs MRI-based assessment. Results: Sarcopenia increased risks of adjacent fractures (16.7% vs 5.2%; adjusted odds ratio (aOR) = 3.21), implant failure (11.4% vs 3.1%; aOR = 2.89), and deep infection (5.3% vs 1.0%; aOR = 4.12). Bedside screening (grip strength + gait speed) achieved AUC = 0.79, comparable to MRI. Conclusions: This first surgical cohort validation demonstrates that bedside screening reduces costs by 60% while matching MRI accuracy, providing a cost-effective and equitable strategy for LMICs.

## ID: AGS26/832

## P0909: Incidence of pseudarthrosis in osteoporotic patients after posterior lumbar arthrodesis from 2014-2024 at the centers for diagnosis and advanced medicine and medical conferences and telemedicine (CEDIMAT), Dominican Republic

Aurelis Caridad

1

, Sonya Mendez Castellanos

1

, Luis Bonilla

2

1

Universidad Iberoamericana de Santo Domingo, Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic

2

Centro de Diagnostico de Medicina Avanzada Telemedicina, Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic

Introduction: Pseudarthrosis is a significant complication following posterior lumbar arthrodesis, with a reported incidence of approximately 9.8%. Osteoporosis is a known risk factor for mechanical complications after spinal fusion, yet its impact and the predictive utility of diagnostic tools like Hounsfield Units (HU) from computed tomography (CT) scans remain understudied in the Dominican Republic. This study aims to determine the incidence of pseudarthrosis in osteoporotic patients after lumbar arthrodesis at a single center and to identify predominant risk factors, including the predictive value of Hounsfield Units compared to DEXA T-scores. Material and Methods: A retrospective, analytical cohort study will be conducted. Medical records of patients who underwent posterior lumbar arthrodesis at CEDIMAT between 2014 and 2024 will be reviewed. Inclusion criteria comprised patients with pre-surgical CT scans and bone densitometry (DEXA), while those with tumors, previous fractures, trauma, or prior lumbar surgery were excluded. Variables analyzed included demographic data, comorbidities (smoking, diabetes, hypertension), Surgical techniques, osteoporosis diagnosis including Treatment and its duration, and radiological parameters (HU values and DEXA T-scores). Data collection was performed using a standardized form. Statistical analysis included descriptive statistics, Chi-square tests, ANOVA, Pearson's correlation, and binary logistic regression to identify predictors of pseudarthrosis. Results: Data collection is ongoing and the results will be available for presentation at the congress. Conclusion: This study is poised to significantly improve medical practice by translating its findings into direct, actionable clinical protocols, particularly within the Dominican Republic and similar healthcare settings. Primarily, it addresses a critical local gap by providing the first high-quality evidence on the incidence and specific risk factors for pseudarthrosis following lumbar fusion in an osteoporotic population, thereby empowering surgeons at CEDIMAT and nationally to develop standardized guidelines mandating pre-operative bone health assessment for at-risk patients. A key anticipated outcome is the validation of Hounsfield Units (HU) from routine CT scans as a superior, cost-effective predictor of pseudarthrosis compared to formal DEXA scans; this would offer an accessible diagnostic tool that requires no additional cost or radiation, greatly enhancing risk stratification, especially in resource-conscious environments. By enabling the identification of high-risk patients beforehand, the study directly supports pre-operative optimization through osteoporosis treatment, allows for better-informed surgical planning and patient counseling, and ultimately aims to reduce the need for costly and complex revision surgeries. Consequently, this research promises to elevate the standard of care, improve surgical outcomes, reduce healthcare costs, and improve patient safety and quality of life by moving from a reactive to a proactive, preventive model in spinal surgery.
Keywords: Pseudarthrosis, Osteoporosis, Spinal Fusion, Lumbar Vertebrae, Hounsfield Units, Risk Factors, Dominican Republic.

## ID: AGS26/854

## P0910: Risk factor analysis for cement leakage during PKP and validation of a comprehensive prevention strategy in a county hospital setting: a real-world study of 1000 procedures

Wenjie Wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Junxiong Cao

1

, Li Huo

1

, Boxing Wang

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Cement leakage is the most frequent complication of PKP, potentially leading to serious consequences such as neurological deficits or pulmonary embolism. Most existing risk models are derived from tertiary care centers, and their applicability in resource-conscious county hospitals remains unverified. This study aims to identify independent risk factors relevant to our setting and validate the effectiveness of a comprehensive prevention protocol. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed our database of 1000 PKP procedures performed between 2018 and 2023. Patients were stratified into leakage and non-leakage groups based on intraoperative imaging. Potential risk factors were evaluated, including patient demographics (age, sex, BMI), fracture characteristics (level, posterior wall integrity, chronicity), and surgical factors (approach, cement viscosity, injection timing). Multivariate logistic regression identified independent risk factors. Based on these findings, a comprehensive prevention strategy-emphasizing refined preoperative assessment, precise intraoperative control of cement viscosity and timing, and a standardized real-time fluoroscopy monitoring protocol-was implemented in mid-2022. Leakage rates were prospectively compared before and after implementation. Results: The overall cement leakage rate was 8.5% (85/1000). Multivariate analysis identified breach of the posterior vertebral wall (OR = 4.21, p < 0.001), fracture level (thoracolumbar junction vs. lumbar, OR = 2.15, p < 0.05), and low-viscosity cement usage (OR = 3.78, p < 0.001) as independent risk factors. Following the implementation of the strategy, the leakage rate significantly decreased from 10.8% to 5.1% (p < 0.01). Conclusion: Breach of the posterior wall, thoracolumbar junction fractures, and use of low-viscosity cement are independent risk factors for cement leakage during PKP. A tailored, comprehensive prevention strategy based on these factors can significantly mitigate leakage risk in a county hospital setting. This protocol demonstrates high clinical practicality and transferability.

## ID: AGS26/961

## P0911: Minimally invasive tubular decompression versus traditional open surgery for lumbar spinal stenosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis

Ahmet Kartal

1

, Noel Manalil

1

, Mousa Hamad

1

, Shahier Paracha

1

, Anthony Robayo

1

, Evan Wang

1

, Ibrahim Hussain

1

, Roger Härtl

1

1

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at New York Presbyterian Hospital, Weill Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

Introduction: Lumbar spinal stenosis (LSS) is often treated with surgical decompression to relieve symptoms and improve quality of life. While open laminectomy is effective, it requires soft-tissue disruption and prolongs recovery. Minimally invasive (MI) tubular decompression aims to reduce tissue trauma, shorten hospital stay, and lower complications. Our objective is to compare clinical outcomes, perioperative characteristics, and complication rates of MI tubular decompression and open laminectomy for symptomatic LSS. Material and Methods: Following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines, we conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis by searching PubMed, Embase, and Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) for studies from 2015 to April 2025 comparing MI tubular-assisted and open decompression for LSS. Primary outcomes included complications, perioperative parameters, and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs). Results: From 2,853 screened articles, nine studies comprising ten cohorts were included. MI tubular surgery significantly reduced overall complications (odds ratio [OR] 0.42, 95% confidence interval [CI] [0.21; 0.86], p = 0.0165), surgical site infections (OR 0.28, 95% CI [0.11; 0.69], p = 0.0055), estimated blood loss (EBL; mean difference [MD] -46.65 mL, 95% CI [-80.89; -12.41], p = 0.0076), and hospital length of stay (LOS; MD -1.39 days, 95% CI [-1.99; -0.79], p = 0.0001). No significant differences were found in reoperation rates, surgical time, dural tears, or improvements in PROMs, with the exception of back pain assessed by the Visual Analog Scale (VAS-B). While the random-effects model showed no significant change in VAS-B scores, the common-effect model revealed a significant reduction (MD = -0.66, 95% CI [-0.88; -0.45], p < 0.0001). Conclusion: MI tubular surgery showed significantly reduced overall complication rates, rates of surgical site infections, EBL, and hospital LOS compared to open surgery. Additionally, results indicate a favorable trend in reducing VAS-B scores. These findings strongly support MI tubular-assisted decompression as the preferred surgical approach versus open decompression for LSS.

## ID: AGS26/1070

## P0912: Postoperative chyle-related complications in lumbar spine surgery: a systematic review of risk factors, management approaches, and clinical outcomes

Mohamad Bakhaidar

1

, Abdulwhab Alotaibi

2

, Gilberto Perez

2

, Ataollah Shahbandi

2

, Omid yousefi

2

, Randall Treffy

2

, Saman Shabani

2

1

Ottawa University, Ottawa, Canada

2

Medical College of Wisconin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Postoperative chyle-related complications in lumbar spine surgery are rare and pose significant diagnostic and management challenges. Delayed diagnosis can lead to severe complications, including nutritional deficiencies, infection, and prolonged recovery. This systematic review aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of all reported cases of chyle-related complications in lumbar spine surgery, to better understand the demographics, clinical presentations, management options, and outcomes associated with this rare complication. Additionally, we propose a diagnostic and management algorithms based on the evidence generated from this review and the relevant literature. Material and Methods: We conducted a systematic search using PubMed, Embase, and Scopus to identify articles on postoperative chyle-related complications in lumbar spine surgery. Case reports, series, and observational studies were included. We excluded studies involving thoracic duct injuries, abnormal lymphatic system conditions, and those with insufficient clinical data. Two reviewers independently screened the articles for inclusion. Data was extracted on demographics, surgical details, clinical presentation, management, and outcomes. Results: We included 25 studies with a total of 55 patients. The mean age was 60.1 years, with 58.2% being female. Spinal deformity was the most common surgical indication (51.9%), followed by degenerative spine disease (25%). The majority of patients (95.3%) underwent an anterior lumbar spine approach. The most common chyle-related complication was isolated intra-abdominal lymphocele (90.9%). Abdominal swelling (52.7%) and pain (45.4%) were the most common presenting symptoms, with a median onset of 14 days postoperatively. Computed tomography was the most frequently used diagnostic tool (83.6%). Conservative treatment with observation or percutaneous drainage and dietary modification was started in (83.6%), with a success rate of (81.7%). Regardless of the initial treatment, most patients (93%) experienced complete resolution, with a few recurrences requiring further intervention. Conclusion: Postoperative chyle-related complications following lumbar spine surgery are rare, but early diagnosis and intervention are crucial for achieving favorable outcomes. We believe that this systematic review and algorithms summarize the existing literature and provides an up-to-date understanding of the presentation, management, and outcome of this condition.

## ID: AGS26/1104

## P0913: Usefulness of spinal anesthesia in spine surgery for elderly patients

Shu Sakai

1

, Yushi Hoshino

1

, Satoshi Yoshiyama

1

, Ai Sakai

1

, Yusei Miyanaga

1

, Yoshinori Imaizumi

1

, Yoshifumi Kudo

2

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Asahi University Hospital, Gifu, Japan

2

Department of Orthopedic Surgery Showa Medical University, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Spinal surgery in patients for whom general anesthesia is difficult, such as the elderly or those with compromised cardiopulmonary function, poses significant challenges in perioperative management. We evaluated the efficacy and safety of performing spinal surgery using spinal anesthesia in elderly patients deemed high-risk for general anesthesia. Subjects: Eight cases undergoing spinal surgery at our institution between October 2023 and July 2025, deemed high-risk for general anesthesia and thus performed under spinal anesthesia. Ages ranged from 74 to 90 years (mean 86.0 years); 3 males and 5 females. Underlying conditions included cardiac disease (4 cases), respiratory disease (2 cases), and neurological disease (2 cases). Surgeries included: L4/5 posterior decompression (2 cases), L1 BKP (3 cases), L3 BKP (1 case), L5 BKP (1 case). All patients were ASA-PS Class III. Methods: Spinal anesthesia was administered by an anesthesiologist using 0.5% isobaric bupivacaine. Dexmedetomidine was used as a sedative adjunct during surgery as needed. The presence of intraoperative and perioperative complications was evaluated. Results: None of the 8 patients experienced unstable respiratory or circulatory status during surgery, and no cases required conversion to general anesthesia. No nausea, vomiting, or delirium was observed. Furthermore, no serious complications occurred. Discussion: Spinal anesthesia preserves spontaneous breathing and maintains stable hemodynamics, allowing safe implementation even in patients difficult to anesthetize under general anesthesia. While spinal anesthesia has been reported to reduce perioperative complications compared to general anesthesia in elderly patients with proximal femoral fractures, similar benefits are likely achievable in spinal surgery for short-duration, localized procedures. However, spinal anesthesia has limited applicability for lengthy fixation procedures or multi-level spinal surgeries. Challenges remain regarding adjustment of the anesthetic block and unexpected prolongation of surgery time, necessitating careful case selection. Although the number of cases in this study was limited, it suggests spinal anesthesia may be a viable option for patients with general anesthesia contraindications. Conclusion: Spinal anesthesia may be a viable option for spinal surgery in elderly patients and those with contraindications to general anesthesia. Future studies with larger case numbers are needed to further clarify its indications and limitations.

## ID: AGS26/1167

## P0914: Post-op complications after thoracic and lumbar spine long-fursion surgery in children: what does Clavien-Dindo classification show?

Alexander Mushkin

1,2

, Veronika Petukhova

1

, Nikita Khusainov

1

, Andrei Pershin

1

, Alexey Maletin

1

, Kuklina Svetlana

1

1

Saint-Petersburg Research Institute of Phthisiopulmonology, Saint-Petersburg, Russia, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

2

Pavlov First Saint Petersburg State Medical University, Saint-Petersburg, Russia, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Correction of spinal deformities in children with a long (4+ instrumented spinal segments) fusion associates with a risk of perioperative complications. However, in rutine practice, some events are systematically not registered and analyzed. The Clavien-Dindo classification (СDC) provides a standardized framework for grading surgical complications. The aim of this study was to assess perioperative and early postoperative complications after long spinal fusion in pediatric patients and to identify risk factors for severe complications. Material and Methods: A retrospective single-center cohort study included 136 children (1-17 years, mean 11.7 ± 4.6) consecutively operated between 2020 and 2023 on thoracic and lumbar spine deformities - idiopathic (50), congenital (61), syndromic (5) , neuromuscular (14) scoliosis, and Scheuermann’s kyphosis(6), - with open posterior fixation instrumentation on a 4+ motion segments. Complications were classified according to Clavien-Dindo. Statistical analysis evaluated associations between patient/surgical factors and complications grade IIIB or higher.Patients with short-fusion (ex. hemivertebra exision) were exluded. All complication were registed from medical charts by independent reseacher whom not participate in the treatment of patients. Results: Overall, 55 complications/events were documented (40.4%). The majority were grade I-II (41; 30.1%), not affecting treatment tactics. Complications of grade III-IV occurred in 13 cases (9.6%), including 10 grade IIIB (stability failures, early surgical site infections, neurological deterioration), and 1 grade IVA (massive intraoperative blood loss with hemodynamic instability). One patient (0.7%) died from pulmonary embolism (grade V). Risk analysis revealed that insurtion of 18+ pedicle screws significantly correlated with prolonged surgery, increased intraoperative blood loss, and higher risk of grade IIIB-IVA complications (p < 0.05). Etiology of deformity, sex, age, and underweight were not associated with severe peri- and early post-op complications. Conclusion: Clavien-Dindo classification in pediatric spinal deformity surgery with a long fusion provides a comprehensive and reproducible assessment of complications. Most complications were minor (grades I-II), nearly 10% required active surgical or intensive care management (grades IIIb - V according CDC). The number of anchor elements (>18) was the most important predictor of severe complications correlated with continious surgery and blood loos.

## ID: AGS26/1196

## P0915: External anal sphincter monitoring in spine surgery: enhanced amplitude with coccygeal reference and comparative effects of needle versus seal electrodes

Yoshikazu Yanagisawa

1

1

Department of Orthopedic surgery, Fukuoka Mirai Hospital, Fukuoka city, Japan

Introduction: Intraoperative monitoring of the external anal sphincter (EAS) helps detect sacral nerve injury during spine surgery, but conventional placement of two needle electrodes within the sphincter often produces small or absent responses. We investigated whether using the coccyx as the reference electrode increases signal amplitude and compared needle versus adhesive coccygeal electrodes. Material and Methods: First, we reviewed 28 spine surgeries monitored with both the traditional double-needle method and a single-needle EAS recording using a coccygeal adhesive (seal) reference. Recordability and peak-to-peak amplitudes were compared. Next, ten consecutive patients underwent transcranial Br-MsEP monitoring with the recording needle in the EAS and the reference electrode either a needle inserted into the coccyx or an adhesive seal. We analysed peak-to-peak amplitude, onset latency and waveform morphology. Results: In the retrospective series, recordable EAS responses were obtained in 23/28 cases (82%) with the coccygeal seal reference but only 9/28 cases (32%) with the double-needle method, and mean amplitude increased from 54.6 µV to 84.0 µV. In the prospective trial, mean amplitudes were 119.0 µV with the coccygeal needle reference and 105.8 µV with the seal reference; this difference was not significant (p = 0.36). Latencies and phase were identical, and only two patients showed minor waveform differences. No postoperative bladder or rectal dysfunction occurred. Conclusion: A single-needle EAS recording with a coccygeal reference yields more reliable and larger Br-MsEPs than the traditional double-needle method. The type of coccygeal reference (needle vs adhesive) does not significantly affect amplitude, although the needle tends to produce slightly larger signals. We recommend starting with an adhesive coccygeal reference for safety and cost reasons and switching to a needle reference only when amplitudes are inadequate.

## ID: AGS26/1202

## P0916: Recent COVID-19 infection increases risk of 90-day complications following single and multiple level lumbar spinal fusion: a propensity-matched analysis

Kyle Stump

1

, Henry Morar

1

, Dhara Patel

2

, Nikita Iyer

1

, Anupama Jayachandran

1

, Theresa Pazionis

2

1

Lewis Katz School of Medicine at Temple University, Philadelphia, United States

2

Temple University Hospital, Department of Orthopaedics and Sports Medicine, Philadelphia, United States

Introduction: The impact of recent COVID-19 infection on postoperative complications, specifically venous thromboembolism (VTE), following elective orthopedic procedures is increasingly recognized. However, it remains unclear whether postoperative VTE risk differs in patients with recent preoperative COVID-19 undergoing single versus multi-level lumbar fusion. This study aimed to evaluate 90-day postoperative VTE risk following single and multi-level lumbar fusion in patients with a recent COVID-19 diagnosis. Material and Methods: Using the TriNetX global electronic health record database, we identified adults 18 years and older who underwent single or multi-level lumbar arthrodesis between February 1, 2020, and February 1, 2025. Patients with a COVID-19 diagnosis within 30 days prior to surgery were compared to controls without recent infection. Propensity matching accounted for demographics and comorbidities. Primary outcomes included VTE, deep and superficial surgical site infection (SSI), emergency department (ED) visits, readmission (after postoperative day five), wound dehiscence, pneumonia, acute respiratory failure, acute respiratory distress syndrome, intubation, myocardial infarction, pericarditis, acute renal failure, sepsis, and stroke. Outcomes were assessed at 90 days using risk ratios (RR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI). Significance was defined as p < 0.05. Results: There were 334 matched pairs in the single-level fusion analysis. The study group was 51.2% female and 45.2% male, with mean age 61.6 ± 12.9 years. Recent COVID-19 infection within 30 days of single-level fusion was associated with increased ED utilization (RR 1.621, 95% CI 1.047 - 2.510). No differences were observed for the remaining complications at 90 days. In the multi-level fusion analysis, 379 matched pairs were included. The study group was 43.3% female and 54.6% male, mean age 62.2 ± 14.8 years. Patients with recent COVID-19 were at increased risk of pneumonia (RR 3.300, 95% CI 1.650 - 6.599), acute respiratory failure (RR 3.400, 95% CI 1.704 - 6.782), acute renal failure (RR 2.364, 95% CI 1.185 - 4.715), and readmission (RR 1.436, 95% CI 1.179 - 1.750). No other significant differences were found. Conclusion: VTE risk did not differ between COVID-19 positive and negative patients in either group. However, recent infection was associated with increased ED use after single-level fusion and elevated respiratory, renal, and readmission risks following multi-level fusion. These findings suggest additional antithrombotic prophylaxis is not warranted, though vigilance for pulmonary and renal complications is advised.

## ID: AGS26/1242

## P0917: Risk factors of surgical site infection after posterior fixation of thoracolumbar junction fractures

Mouldi Lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Yassine Aloui

1

, Hamza Daly

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar junction fractures are among the most frequent spinal injuries requiring surgical management. Despite improvements in surgical techniques and perioperative care, postoperative infection remains one of the most feared complications, as it may compromise fixation stability, prolong hospitalization, and impair functional recovery. Reported infection rates after thoracolumbar fracture surgery vary between 2% and 10%. Identifying risk factors is therefore essential to guide perioperative prevention strategies. The objective of this study is to determine the risk factors for surgical site infection after posterior instrumentation of thoracolumbar junction fracture. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of 120 patients operated on for thoracolumbar junction fractures in our Department of Orthopedic Surgery between January 2010 and January 2020. All patients were treated by posterior fusion assembly using short, long, or mixed assembly. Infections were classified as early (< 30 days) or late (≥ 30 days). Demographic variables, comorbidities, fracture patterns, surgical characteristics, and perioperative complications were analyzed. Statistical analysis included calculation of odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI). Results: Seven cases of postoperative infection were recorded (incidence 5.8%), including 4 early infections. We found that diabetes was a significant independent risk factor (OR = 15.15; 95% CI: 2.7-84.89; p = 0.02). In addition, postoperative urinary tract infection was also strongly associated with surgical site infection (OR = 20.4; 95% CI: 3.38-123.51; p = 0.04). Other variables such as age > 60 years, sex, obesity/overweight, fracture characteristics, and the type of fixation (short, long, or mixed constructs) had not a statistically significant impact on the risk of infection. Furthermore, no associations were found between the neurological deficit, pedicular fractures, associated chest injury, laminectomy, or grafting and the risk factors for surgical site infection. Conclusion: Although relatively infrequent, postoperative infections after thoracolumbar fracture surgery carry considerable morbidity. Our findings highlight diabetes and urinary tract infection as major risk factors. These results emphasize the importance of thorough preoperative assessment, optimization of glycemic control, and systematic screening and treatment of urinary tract infections before surgery. Preventive measures targeting these modifiable factors may contribute to reducing postoperative infectious complications and improving surgical outcomes in thoracolumbar fractures.

## ID: AGS26/1250

## P0918: Lumbar artery pseudoaneurysm after transpedicular fixation of thoracolumbar tuberculous spondylitis: case report

Adhi Prayoga Ida Bagus

1,2

, Aries Hidayat

1,2

, Primadenny Ariesa Airlangga

1,2

, Lukas Widhiyanto

1,2

1

Department of Orthopaedic and Traumatology, Dr. Soetomo General Academic Hospital, Surabaya, Indonesia, Surabaya, Indonesia

2

Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology, Faculty of Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Surabaya, Indonesia, Surabaya, Indonesia

Introduction: Injury of the lumbar arteries after the use of transpedicular screws in spinal tuberculosis fixation is a relatively rare complication. In general, vascular complications of spinal instrumentation incidence have a range from 0.01% to 0,22 % with reported high morbidity and mortality. Lumbar arteries are small branches of vessel arising from abdominal aorta, iliac lumbar artery, or medial artery, which has a diameter of up to 2.6-2.9 mm. Due to its proximity from the main artery, it can cause a potentially fatal haemorrhage if not evaluated and treated properly. Most of cases have a rapid progression of disease with hemodynamic shock. We hereby report a patient with a retroperitoneal hematomas secondary to a pseudoaneurysm of lumbar artery iatrogenic injury due to pedicle screw fixation. Material and Methods: A 19-year-old female with moderate right flank to abdominal pain was admitted to our department. He had already undergone Th12/L1 and L3/L4 transpedicular fixation using the free-hand technique at previous hospital. The symptoms are also accompanied by slowly progressing bulging mass on her right side of the abdomen. Initial clinical evaluation upon arrival to our department as follows: heart rate was 140 beats per minute with systolic / diastolic blood pressure 85/60 mmHg. The physical examination revealed abdominal pain with bloating, low back pain, with no sign of neurological deficit of lower extremities. Laboratory finding shows low level of Haemoglobin (6.5 g/dl). Results: After the patient was considered stable following resuscitation, we conduct a serial radiographic survey to determine the cause of hypovolemic shock. An initial urgent MRI with contrast of the abdomen shows a large volume retroperitoneal accumulation of fluid that have a mixed intensity of abscess and haemorrhage segment. Further investigation with CT angiography reveals lumbar artery pseudoaneurysm just posterior to right renal artery. The patient was referred to the interventional-radiology department where arteriography was performed and followed by embolization of the pseudoaneurysm. Due to large volume of retroperitoneal abscess and haemorrhage (1500 cc), we perform clot and abscess evacuation through right lateral lumbotomy, and a large amount of consolidated clot was found at retroperitoneal space. After several days, the patient’s symptoms and general performance improved significantly. She started walking by herself with walker and was discharged. Conclusion: Lumbar artery pseudoaneurysm followed by retroperitoneal haemorrhage is a relatively rare complication faced in spinal instrumentation. Diagnosis can be difficult for many reasons by its nonspecific and delayed presentation of symptoms and signs, that renders unawareness of this condition. A hemodynamically unstable patient with a large retroperitoneal hematoma and no obvious intraabdominal injury should undergo urgent arteriography. Simultaneous embolization should be considered to halt the disease progression.

## ID: AGS26/1264

## P0919: Polyethylene glycol (PEG) coated collagen pad (Hemopatch) as a dural sealant, with or without primary sutures in uniportal endoscopic spine surgery: early experiences and a technical note

Gun Keorochana

1

, Pilan Jaipanya

2

1

Department of Orthopedics, Ramathibodi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Chakri Naruebodindra Medical Institute, Samut Prakan, Thailand

Introduction: Dural tears during spinal surgery are a significant challenge, especially with the increasing use of endoscopic techniques, which are prone to such injuries due to their small working channels. Hemopatch, composed of a protein-reactive polyethylene glycol (PEG) monomer with a bovine-derived collagen backing, is designed for dural repair. When applied, its active PEG binds to tissue proteins, promoting rapid hemostasis and forming a barrier to prevent cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage. This study shares our experience using Hemopatch for endoscopic spinal dural repairs and reviews the relevant literature. Material and Methods: We describe a series of patients in whom Hemopatch was used intra-operatively. The first 2 of these patients, hemopatch was used as a primary repair agent while in 2 others, it was used as an add-on pad after the primary suture under full endoscope. We also describe the surgical technique of patch placement and primary dural repair under endoscopic system. Results: Four patients with an average age of 64.8 ± 33 years underwent full endoscopic lumbar decompression or discectomy for lumbar degenerative spine issues. The dural tear was successfully closed in all cases. Hemopatch was used in all cases, and in 2 cases, primary suturing with prolene was performed before applying the dural sealant. A Valsalva maneuver was performed, showing no leakage after repair. Immediately after surgery, there were no symptoms related to dural leakage. Only one patient experienced mild dizziness without headache on postoperative day 1, which subsided the next day, allowing discharge on postoperative day 2. All patients were followed up for more than 6 months, and there were no complications related to cerebrospinal fluid leakage, infection, wound dehiscence, wound swelling, or postdural puncture headaches. Conclusion: Dural injury is a significant and challenging issue in endoscopic spine surgery. In this case series, all dural defects were ef-fectively closed using the described technique, and no subsequent complications occurred. The use of Hemopatch, either with or without primary repair, could be a successful alternative sealant for dural tears in full endoscopic spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1407

## P0920: Negative pressure wound therapy in spine surgery: enhancing healing, reducing infections, and navigating the challenges

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Valentina Ortiz

1,2

, Alejandro Hermida Pineda

2

, Luis Carlos Morales Saenz

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Universidad de La Sabana, Chia, Colombia

Introduction: Surgical site infections (SSIs) in spinal surgery pose a major challenge, incidence remains high despite standard preventive measures. Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) has gained attention as a strategy to reduce infection rates, enhance wound healing, and minimize the need for reoperations. Material and Methods: A narrative review of the current literature was conducted, focusing on clinical applications, outcomes, and limitations of NPWT in spinal surgery. Key studies evaluating infection rates, wound healing parameters, and complication profiles were analyzed. Results: Recent studies indicate that prophylactic NPWT in spinal fusion surgery may reduce SSIs by up to 40%. Additional benefits include improved wound stability, reduced dehiscence rates, and enhanced tissue perfusion. However, evidence remains mixed regarding cost-effectiveness and optimal duration of therapy. Reported complications include skin blistering, increased drainage, and, in rare cases, bleeding or retained dressing fragments. Conclusion: NPWT is a promising adjunct in spine surgery, particularly for high-risk patients undergoing extensive posterior instrumentation or revision procedures. While evidence supports its role in reducing SSIs and improving wound healing, cost considerations and patient selection criteria require further investigation to optimize clinical application.

## ID: AGS26/1413

## P0921: Ovarian vein thrombosis a complication in spine surgey: case report and literature reveiw

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Carlos Pacichana

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Daniel Zambrano Lizcano

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Ovarian vein thrombosis (OVT) is a rare vascular complication, usually associated with postpartum period or gynecological conditions. Its occurrence, as a postoperative event in spinal surgeries, is rare and represents a diagnostic challenge due to its nonspecific clinical presentation. Case presentation: We report the case of a 28-year-old woman with a history of juvenile idiopathic scoliosis who underwent two-stage thoracolumbar fusion. Three weeks after the second surgical procedure, the patient presented with abdominal pain and non-palpable mass sensation in the left abdomen. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed bilateral pelvic varices and a filling defect in the right gonadal vein, consistent with OVT. Anticoagulant therapy with enoxaparin was initiated, followed by clinical monitoring with anticoagulation, with favorable clinical outcome and no complications. Discussion: In this case, risk factors for thrombosis were identified, suchas: postoperative immobility, opioid analgesia, and venous compression due to prolonged surgical positioning. The absence of gynecological and obstetric factors highlights the need to include OVT in the differential diagnosis of postoperative abdominal pain in young women. CT angiography was crucial for timely diagnosis and the prevention of major complications such as pulmonary thromboembolism. Conclusion: OVT can present itself as a rare postoperative complication in spinal surgery. Early recognition allows for effective treatment and prevention of adverse outcomes. This case highlights the importance of a comprehensive assessment of postoperative abdominal pain.

## ID: AGS26/1443

## P0922: Diabetic neuropathy and perioperative complications in anterior cervical discectomy and fusion

Lane McCoy

1

, Travis Kotzur

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Diabetic neuropathy (DN), a common complication of longstanding diabetes mellitus (DM), has been linked to adverse musculoskeletal outcomes in prior literature. However, its role in perioperative risk following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) remains unclear. This study aims to evaluate DN as an independent risk factor for perioperative complications in ACDF and to compare outcomes among patients with DN, DM without neuropathy, and those without DM. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on primary ACDF patients identified from the TriNetX database. DN and DM were identified by ICD-10 diagnostic codes. The primary outcomes were postoperative infection, postoperative spinal implant infection (PSII), mechanical complication, pseudoarthrosis, revision rates, deep venous thrombosis and pulmonary embolism rates, sepsis, wound disruption, and readmission. Propensity score matching was used to control for demographics and Charlson Comorbidity Index between cohorts. Between DM and DN patients, hemoglobin A1c was also matched to control for severity of diabetes. Multivariate regression compared primary outcome rates among patients with DN, DM without neuropathy, and non-diabetic controls. Risk ratios (RR) were calculated for individual variables. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. Results: A total of 30,210 patients, aged 40-90 years, who underwent primary ACDF were identified. After successful matching, there were three comparative cohorts with a population of 18,922 in DM vs no DM, 5,616 in DN vs DM, and 5,672 in DN vs no DM. DN patients had significantly increased risk of postoperative infection (RR 1.43; CI 1.05-1.95; p = 0.024), PSII (RR 1.47; CI 1.09-1.97; p = 0.011), revision (RR 1.23; CI 1.04-1.45; p = 0.016), sepsis (RR 1.92; CI 1.55-2.37; p < 0.001), wound disruption (RR 1.95; CI 1.33-2.87; p < 0.001), and readmission (RR 1.48; CI 1.33-1.65; p < 0.001) compared to patients without DM. DM patients had significantly increased risk of postoperative infection (RR 1.22; CI 1.01-1.48; p = 0.038), sepsis (RR 1.26; CI 1.1-1.44; p < 0.001), and readmission (RR 1.18; CI 1.11-1.25; p < 0.001) compared to patients without DM. Patients with DN had significantly increased risk of mechanical complication (RR 1.43; CI 1.11-1.84; p = 0.006), revision (RR 1.31; CI 1.12-1.55; p = 0.001), sepsis (RR 1.36; CI 1.14-1.62; p < 0.001), and readmission (RR 1.32; CI 1.19-1.47; p < 0.001) compared to patients with DM. Conclusion: DN is associated with a significantly increased risk of both surgical and medical complications following ACDF surgery, surpassing the risks observed in patients with DM alone. Compared to non-DM patients, those with DN showed markedly elevated rates of postoperative infection, PSII, revision, sepsis, wound disruption, and readmission. Even among diabetic patients, the presence of neuropathy significantly increased the risk of mechanical complication, revision, sepsis, and readmission. These findings underscore the importance of distinguishing DN from DM alone in preoperative risk assessment and highlight the need for tailored perioperative management strategies in this population.

## ID: AGS26/1445

## P0923: A comparison of opioid-inclusive anesthesia and opioid-sparing anesthesia in complication development post-anterior cervical discectomy and fusion

Lane McCoy

1

, Travis Kotzur

1

, Brian Neuman

1,2

1

Washington University School of Medicine in St. Louis, St. Louis, United States

2

Barnes Jewish Hospital, St. Louis, United States

Introduction: Opioid-inclusive anesthesia (OIA) has been a cornerstone of intraoperative management in spine surgery, but mounting evidence links perioperative opioids to immunosuppressive effects and histamine-mediated vasodilation that can precipitate intraoperative hypotension. This has prompted a shift toward opioid-sparing anesthesia (OSA). While prior spine studies suggest improved outcomes with OSA, its impact on perioperative complications in anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) remains underexplored. This study compares complication rates between ACDF patients receiving OSA versus OIA to assess whether OSA is associated with a measurable reduction in perioperative risk. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on primary ACDF patients identified from the TriNetX database. Patients were divided into groups based on if they received OSA or OIA. Anesthesia type (OSA or OIA) was identified from intraoperative anesthesia records. The primary outcomes were postoperative infection, postoperative spinal implant infection (PSII), rates of revision, deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism rates, sepsis, wound disruption, readmission, transfusion rates, and ileus. Propensity score matching was used to control for age, sex, BMI, in addition to other comorbidities and surgical conditions, ensuring similar cohorts. Multivariate regression compared primary outcome rates between the groups. Risk ratios (RR) were calculated for individual variables. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. Results: A total of 94,785 patients, aged 40-90 years, who underwent primary ACDF surgery were included. After successful matching, we had 27,820 patients divided equally between OIA and OSA groups. Patients who received OSA during surgery had significantly decreased risk of revision (RR 0.88; CI 0.80-0.97; p = 0.012), pulmonary embolism (RR 0.67; CI 0.52-0.85; p < 0.001), sepsis (RR 0.71; CI 0.58-0.86; p < 0.001), readmission (RR 0.55; CI 0.52-0.58; p < 0.001), transfusion (RR 0.53; CI 0.42-0.67; p < 0.001), and ileus (RR 0.68; CI 0.53-0.88; p = 0.004) following ACDF. Other primary outcomes - including postoperative infection, PSII, wound disruption, and DVT - failed to show any difference in risk between OSA and OIA cohorts. Conclusion: OSA was associated with significantly lower risks of several serious perioperative medical and surgical complications, including revision surgery, pulmonary embolism, sepsis, readmission, need for transfusion, and postoperative ileus. Compared with OIA, OSA was associated with a 30-50% relative risk reduction for these complications. The findings suggest that OIA may pose a significant increase in perioperative complications throughout ACDF surgery, supporting the consideration of OSA as a preferred anesthetic approach to enhance postoperative outcomes while mitigating opioid-related risks.

## ID: AGS26/1498

## P0924: Comparative risk of spinal epidural hematoma following laminoplasty and laminectomy with fusion in patients with degenerative cervical myelopathy

Henry Avetisian

1

, Marco Napolitano

1

, Mirbahador Athari

1

, William Karakash

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Dil Patel

1

, Chimere Ezuma

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Degenerative cervical myelopathy (DCM) is the leading cause of spinal cord dysfunction in adults, typically due to cervical spondylosis or ossified posterior longitudinal ligament (OPLL). Surgical decompression is indicated for patients with mild to severe DCM, with laminoplasty (LP) and laminectomy with fusion (LF) being two commonly employed posterior approaches. Although symptomatic epidural hematoma (EDH) is rare, it remains a potentially catastrophic complication that can result in paralysis or death. This study aims to compare the incidence, risk, and timing of EDH within 7 days following LP vs LF in patients with DCM. Material and Methods: The PearlDiver national database was queried to identify patients with DCM who underwent LP or LF from 2010 to 2023. Patients with concomitant anterior cervical surgery were excluded. EDH occurring within 7 days postoperatively was identified using ICD coding. Secondary outcomes included hospital readmission, time to EDH presentation, and time from symptom onset to reoperation. Chi-squared testing was used to compare incidence between groups, and multivariable logistic regression was used to assess risk, adjusting for age, gender, Elixhauser Comorbidity Index (ECI), and presence of OPLL. Results: Among 104,170 included patients, 6,477 (6.2%) underwent LP and 97,693 (93.8%) underwent LF. The LP cohort was slightly older (61.81 vs 61.35, p < 0.001), had a lower mean ECI (7.03 vs 7.69, p < 0.001), lower proportion of females (37.33% vs 42.36%, p < 0.001), and a higher prevalence of OPLL (3.57% vs 2.15%, p < 0.001). The incidence of EDH was significantly lower in the LP group (0.29% vs 0.73%, p < 0.001), and multivariate regression confirmed a reduced risk of EDH with LP (aOR: 0.41, p < 0.001). Among patients who developed EDH, most cases occurred after discharge and required hospital readmission-68.4% in the LP group vs 81.3% in the LF group. The mean time to EDH presentation was 4.63 ± 2.03 days for LP and 3.91 ± 2.07 days for LF. The time from symptom onset to reoperation was 3.40 ± 2.22 days for LP and 3.13 ± 2.47 days for LF. Conclusion: Laminoplasty is associated with a significantly lower incidence and risk of postoperative epidural hematoma compared to laminectomy with fusion in patients with DCM. Given that most hematomas presented after discharge, strategies such as extended inpatient monitoring and perioperative risk mitigation-including delayed anticoagulation and use of surgical drains-should be considered for high-risk patients to minimize delays in diagnosis and optimize outcomes. While there was a significant difference in relative rates of EDH between treatment modalities, absolute risk only decreased by 0.44% in the LP group, owing to the relatively rare nature of this complication. In patients who may be deemed equipoise for LP or LF, laminoplasty may be favorable in those patients at higher risk for epidural hematoma including patients with underlying coagulopathy or patients who may require early postoperative anticoagulation due to cardiac comorbidities or venous thromboembolism risk factors.

## ID: AGS26/1521

## P0925: Perioperative complications following common spine procedures in patients with myasthenia gravis: a nationwide claims analysis

Mirbahador Athari

1

, Daniel Rusu

1

, Aidan Lindgren

1

, Maya Abu-Zahra

1

, Marc Abdou

1

, Jeffrey C. Wang

1

, Raymond Hah

1

, Kiran Alluri

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Keck School of Medicine of the University of Southern California, Los Angeles, United States

Introduction: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune neuromuscular disorder characterized by fluctuating weakness and fatigability. While perioperative complications in MG have been described in general surgery and arthroplasty, outcomes after spine surgery remain poorly understood. Given the prevalence of spine surgery in older, comorbid patients, it is important to define the complication profile in MG. This study evaluated perioperative complications in MG compared with non-MG patients undergoing common spine procedures and compared anterior versus posterior cervical approaches. Materials and Methods: We performed a retrospective cohort study using the PearlDiver Mariner database (2010-2022). Adult patients undergoing one-level lumbar decompression, one-level lumbar fusion, one- to two-level anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), and one- to two-level posterior cervical laminectomy with or without fusion were identified using CPT, ICD-9-PCS, and ICD-10-PCS codes. MG patients were identified by ICD-9/10 diagnosis codes prior to surgery. Controls were matched 1:3 based on age, sex, and comorbidity index. Outcomes included inpatient complications, 90-day readmissions and reoperations, and MG-specific complications such as perioperative myasthenic crisis. Univariate analysis was used to calculate odds ratios (OR).
