Results: After matching, MG cohorts included 306 lumbar fusions, 1,248 lumbar decompressions, 471 ACDF, and 116 posterior cervical procedures compared with appropriately matched controls. Baseline demographics were well balanced with standardized mean differences < 0.01, although MG patients demonstrated slightly higher comorbidity indices. For lumbar fusion, MG patients had higher rates of sepsis (3.9% vs < 1%, OR 4.12, p = 0.001). In the lumbar decompression cohort, MG patients experienced increased respiratory failure (4.8% vs 2.3%, OR 2.17), surgical site infections (SSIs, 2.6% vs 1.5%, OR 1.73), and readmissions (9.7% vs 6.3%, OR 1.60) (all p < 0.05). For ACDF, MG patients had higher likelihood of anemia (10.2% vs 6.6%, OR 1.61), respiratory failure (4.9% vs 2.9%, OR 1.72), and readmissions (7.2% vs 4.6%, OR 1.61) (all p < 0.05). Posterior cervical procedures had higher 90-day anemia (25.0% vs 15.8%, OR 1.78, p = 0.027). When directly comparing ACDF with posterior cervical laminectomy and fusion, posterior procedures were associated with higher rates of respiratory failure (12.1% vs 4.0%), anemia (21.6% vs 10.2%), and acute kidney injury (all p < 0.05). At 90 days, posterior patients continued to have more respiratory failure (12.1% vs 4.9%), anemia (25.0% vs 12.3%), acute kidney injury, and SSIs (all p < 0.05). Rates of readmission and myasthenic crisis were similar by approach. Conclusion: MG patients undergoing spine surgery face significantly higher risks of perioperative complications, including respiratory failure, sepsis, anemia, and readmission, with perioperative myasthenic crisis occurring exclusively in this population. Sub-analysis of cervical procedures showed that posterior approaches were associated with greater risks of respiratory, hematologic, and infectious complications compared with ACDF. These findings underscore the importance of tailored perioperative planning, vigilant monitoring, and careful surgical approach selection in MG patients to optimize safety.

## ID: AGS26/1524

## P0926: Sacroiliac joint fusion for treating lower back pain in patients with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: case series

Hasan Jamali

1,2

, Andrew Berg

1

, Gayani Petersingham

1,2

, Prashanth Rao

1,2,3

1

Brain and Spine surgery, Sydney, Australia

2

UNSW, Sydney, Australia

3

Norwest Private Hospital, Sydney, Australia

Introduction: Sacroiliac joint (SIJ) fusion is a surgical option for individuals with SIJ dysfunction who have not experienced symptomatic improvement through non-surgical treatments. This approach may also be beneficial for patients with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS) who are more prone to acquiring SIJ dysfunction. The purpose of this study is to compile existing literature on patient outcomes following SIJ fusion for the treatment of lower back pain (LBP) secondary to SIJ dysfunction in EDS and to present a retrospective case series of this intervention using validated measures of pain, disability, and quality of life. Material and Methods: A search was conducted across three databases in April 2025. Studies from the past 10 years were included if they provided data on SIJ fusion for the treatment of LBP in EDS patients. Additionally, a retrospective case series looking at 17 SIJ fusion cases performed by one neurosurgeon across 10 EDS patients with a concomitant diagnosis of SIJ dysfunction was performed, analysing surgeries performed between July 2019 and December 202 4. The data analysed looked at demographics, procedural details, clinical outcomes, and complication rates. Results: Only one study, a case series, met the inclusion criteria for our literature review. Although this study measured outcomes related to pain, disability, and quality of life, these results were reported using a ‘Satisfaction score’ rather than validated questionnaires. The study did note nerve impingement due to implant malpositioning in one patient, and a lack of pain relief in another. Our case series assessed these outcomes using validated scales and found an improvement in all of them postoperatively compared to baseline. There were statistically significant improvements in pain, disability, and quality of life post-SIJ fusion according to the following PROMs: VAS back (Δmean = -2.643, p < 0.001), VAS leg, (Δmean = -1.929, p < 0.001), RMDQ (Δmean = -2.667, p < 0.05), and the EQ-5D-5L subcategories ‘Mobility’ (Δmean = -0.8462, p < 0.05) and ‘Activities’ (Δmean = -0.6923, p < 0.05). However, improvements in pain and disability according to ODI (Δmean = -3.647%, p = 0.4630), EQ-5D Overall (Δmean = +8.000, p = 0.3340), and the EQ-5D-5L subcategories ‘Self-care’ (Δmean = -0.4615, p = 0.1390), ‘Pain’ (Δmean = -0.6154, p = 0.0876) and ‘Depression’ (Δmean = -0.2308, p = 0.5845) were not statistically significant. Conclusion: Although evidence regarding the effectiveness of SIJ fusion for alleviating LBP in patients with EDS is limited, it may prove to be a beneficial treatment option. The results of our case series and the study included from the literature review show promising results for the benefits of this intervention in this subpopulation. A trial registry specific to EDS should be established to explore this further.

## ID: AGS26/1577

## P0927: Anterior wall perforation and cranially directed trajectory of pedicle screws are the risk factors for upper instrumented vertebral fracture after spinal fixation in patients with osteoporotic vertebral fracture

Kazumichi Yagura

1

, Syunsaku Nakata

2

, Yoshitaka Aonuma

2

, Masaki Seike

2

, Yutaro Okamura

2

, Takumi Nishio

2

, Tomoya Asakura

2

, Shu Takahashi

2

, Masaya Sekimizu

1

, Kazuyuki Segami

2

, Yusuke Oshita

3

, Toshiyuki Shirahata

1

, Koji Kanzaki

2

, Yoshifmi Kudo

4

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Showa Medical University Koto Toyosu Hospital, 5-1-38 Toyosu, Koto-ku, Tokyo, Japan

2

Showa Medical University Fujigaoka Hospital, 1-30 Fujigaoka, Aoba-ku, Yokohama-shi, Kanagawa, Japan

3

Showa Medical University Northern Yokohama Hospital, 35-1 Chigasakichuou, Tsuzuki-ku, Yokohama-shi, Kanagawa, Japan

4

Showa Medical University School of Medicine, 1-5-8 Hatanodai, Shinagawa-ku, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: Spinal fixation in patients with osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF) is widely employed to reduce instability and facilitate early mobilization. However, upper instrumented vertebral fractures (UIVF) sometimes occur postoperatively, frequently necessitating revision surgery with cephalad extension of fixation due to pedicle screw loosening or cutout. This study aimed to elucidate the risk factors associated with UIVF following spinal fixation in OVF patients. Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study included 49 patients who underwent spinal fixation for OVF between 2019 and 2025. Patients were divided into UIVF group (n=9, 18%) and non-UIVF group (n=40, 82%). Comparative analyses were conducted for demographic characteristics, preoperative vertebral height and angle, Hounsfield unit (HU) values of the UIV on CT, pedicle screw diameter and length at the UIV, occupancy rate of pedicle screw (ORPS)-defined as the ratio of pedicle screw length to the anteroposterior vertebral body diameter at the UIV, UIV pedicle screw-vertebral endplate angle (UIV-PVA) (positive values indicating cranially directed screws, negative values indicating caudally directed screws), and the presence of anterior wall perforation. Results: The mean patient age was 78 years (range, 55-92), with a mean follow-up of 23 months (range, 3-50). No significant differences were observed between groups in sex, body mass index, surgical time, estimated blood loss, length of hospital stay, preoperative vertebral height and angle, or CT-derived HU values. Although the degree of correction of the instrumented vertebral angle from preoperative to immediate postoperative measurements did not differ between groups, the UIVF group demonstrated a significant loss of correction at final follow-up (p < 0.001). Compared with the non-UIVF group, the UIVF group exhibited significantly longer pedicle screws (49 mm vs. 45 mm, p=0.027), higher ORPS (94% vs. 88%, p = 0.01), and more frequent anterior wall perforation (78% vs. 38%, p < 0.001). Furthermore, the UIVF group demonstrated significantly greater UIV-PVA (-3.8° vs. -9.7°, p = 0.022). Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis identified a UIV-PVA cutoff value of -0.5°. Conclusion: These findings indicate that anterior wall perforation of the UIV due to long pedicle screw insertion and cranially directed screw trajectory are significant risk factors of UIVF following spinal fixation in patients with OVF. Meticulous intraoperative attention to screw length and upward angulation may mitigate the risk of postoperative UIVF.

## ID: AGS26/1599

## P0928: Cement leakage in cement-augmented pedicle screw instrumentation: a clinical analysis of 230 screws

Nobuaki Hattori

1

, Akihiko Hiyama

1

, Satoshi Nomura

1

, Hiroyuki Kato

1

, Masato Sato

1

, Masahiko Watanabe

1

, Daisuke Sakai

1

1

Tokai University School of Medicine, Department of Orthopedic Surgery, 143 Shimokasuya, Isehara, Kanagawa, Japan

Introduction: Cement-augmented pedicle screws (CAPS) are useful devices for enhancing implant fixation in patients with osteoporosis. However, cement leakage is a frequent complication. This study investigated cement leakage associated with CAPS currently available in Japan, focusing on the systems available from Depuy Synthes and Medtronic. Material and Methods: Between 2023 and 2025, a total of 46 patients underwent spinal instrumentation with CAPS at our institution (mean age, 76.2 years; 21 males, 25 females; 154 screws by Depuy Synthes and 76 screws by Medtronic). Postoperative CT was used to evaluate the presence of cement leakage. The investigated parameters included patient demographics, overall leakage rates, laterality (right vs left), and spinal level (thoracic vs lumbar). Statistical analyses were performed using the chi-square test and the significance level was set at 0.05. Results: There were no significant differences in age (76.1 ± 6.9 years in the Depuy Synthes group vs 76.5 ± 7.5 years in the Medtronic group, p = 0.65) or sex (p = 0.30) between the two groups. The overall cement leakage rate was 29.1% (95% CI 23.2-35.0). Device-specific leakage rates were 24.0% (95% CI 17.3-30.7) in the Depuy Synthes group and 39.5% (95% CI 28.5-50.5) in the Medtronic group. Leakage rates according to laterality were higher on the right side in both groups: Depuy Synthes group (right 29.9% [95% CI 19.8-40.0] vs left 18.2% [95% CI 9.5-26.9]) and Medtronic group (right 47.4% [95% CI 31.6-63.2] vs left 31.6% [95% CI 16.7-46.5]), although these differences were not statistically significant. When analyzed in total, right-sided screws showed a significantly higher leakage rate than left-sided screws (35.7% [95% CI 26.9-44.4] vs 22.6% [95% CI 15.0-30.3]; OR 1.90, 95% CI 1.06-3.39, p = 0.042). Leakage rates according to spinal region were significantly higher in the thoracic spine (35.7% [95% CI 26.8-44.6]) than in the lumbar spine (22.8% [95% CI 15.3-30.5]; OR 1.87, 95% CI 1.05-3.34, p = 0.04). Conclusion: Cement leakage in CAPS was predominantly observed in the thoracic spine and on the right side. These findings suggest that spinal level and screw trajectory should be carefully considered during preoperative planning and intraoperative procedures to minimize the risk.

## ID: AGS26/1633

## P0929: Pulmonary embolism after cement augmentation

Renate Krassnig

1

, Hohenberger Gloria Maria

2

1

Department of Orthopedics and Trauma Surgery, Medical Universisty Graz, Graz, Austria

2

LKH Weiz, Weiz, Austria

Introduction: Loosening and pullout of pedicle screws is a common problem in spine surgery. For this reason, pedicle screws are increasingly used in combination with cement augmentation, especially in osteoporotic bone. A feared complication is cement leakage into the venous system with subsequent pulmonary cement embolism. Evidence-based recommendations for dealing with these complications are rare. Material and Methods: In a 59-year-old patient with a burst fracture of the first lumbar vertebra, cement leakage into the inferior vena cava over a distance of 4 cm occurred during a spondylodesis with cement-augmented pedicle screws. After a literature review and a subsequent interdisciplinary discussion with a trauma surgeon, a vascular surgeon, an angiologist and an interventional radiologist, the most suitable procedure with the lowest possible patient risk was determined. Results: Cases of cement leakage are very rarely reported in the literature. Most cases are asymptomatic and the most common complication is pulmonary cement embolism. Treatment recommendations include observation, temporary oral anticoagulation, percutaneous interventional removal, and open procedures. In our case, due to the thromboembolic risk and the relatively young patient, we decided to attempt percutaneous cement removal by an interventional radiologist with a vascular surgeon and a cardiac surgeon on standby. After collecting the cement line with a snare, the cement became abnormal and migrated into the right atrium. From the right atrium, most of it could be secured piecemeal with the snare. However, a small portion caused peripheral pulmonary cement embolism. Within the first three months postoperatively, the patient remained asymptomatic on oral anticoagulation with Phenprocoumon. Conclusion: Recommendations for the treatment of cement leakage in augmented pedicle screws are only available on the basis of observations in small series and case reports. Prospective clinical studies are not expected in the near future. An interdisciplinary approach is essential for a satisfactory result.

## ID: AGS26/1759

## P0930: Vascular injury following two-level anterior lumbar interbody fusion: a large database analysis

Jose Anzueto

1

, Rahul Jayaram

1

, Nihar Duddu

1

, Kashif Qureshi

1

, Sujai Arakali

1

, Fabrizio Darby

1

, Khaled Alok

1

, Carlos Goulart

1

, Mihir Gupta

1

1

Yale School of Medicine, New Haven, United States

Introduction: Anterior lumbar interbody fusion (ALIF) is utilized for the management of degenerative lumbar pathology. It carries risk of vascular injury due to the anterior approach. To focus on cases with the greatest vascular exposure, we examined specifically two-level ALIF, where mobilization of the iliac vessels increases technical complexity. Reported rates vary widely in the literature, and large-scale, population-level data are limited. We aimed to characterize the incidence, risk factors, and outcomes associated with vascular injury following two-level ALIF using a large national, multi-insurance, administrative claims database. Material and Methods: We queried the PearlDiver Mariner database (2013-2022) for patients undergoing two-level ALIF, identified using CPT codes 22558 and 22585. Patients with arterial and venous injuries were identified using ICD-9/10 diagnosis codes. Demographics, comorbidities, and perioperative factors were collected, including age, sex, payer type, region, Charlson comorbidity indices, and social determinants of health. The primary outcomes of interest included intraoperative arterial injury, intraoperative venous injury, and all intraoperative vascular injuries, including undefined vascular injuries. Multivariable regression was planned to identify independent risk factors for vascular injury. Results: A total of 21,475 patients undergoing two-level ALIF were identified. The incidence of all intraoperative vascular injury was 0.44%, with 0.05% arterial and 0.35% venous. On multivariable analysis, regional differences were the only independent predictor of intraoperative vascular injury. Significant associations were observed for the Northeast (OR 0.05, 95% CI 0.003-0.23, p = 0.003) and the Midwest (OR 0.41, 95% CI 0.21-0.77, p = 0.0067). All other clinical factors, including age, sex, comorbidity indices, and payer status, were non-significant. Conclusion: Vascular injury following two-level ALIF occurs in approximately 0.44% of cases. Regional differences were the only independent predictor, suggesting that nonclinical factors may play a role in outcomes. These findings indicate that regional variation exists and warrant further study to better understand the factors underlying these differences.

## ID: AGS26/1770

## P0931: Cerebrospinal fluid leaks after intradural spinal tumor surgery: prevention and management

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage is a common complication after intradural spinal tumor surgery. It may lead to pseudomeningocele, wound infection, meningitis, and delayed recovery. Identifying preventive strategies and effective management is essential to reduce morbidity. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 40 patients who underwent intradural spinal tumor resection over a 5-year period. Surgical techniques for dural closure, use of sealants, and postoperative drainage were analyzed. Patients with postoperative CSF leakage were evaluated for clinical presentation, management, and outcomes. Results: CSF leakage occurred in 8 patients. Risk factors included large dural openings, tumors adherent to the pia, and revision surgery. Preventive measures included watertight dural suturing with fine non-absorbable material, reinforcement with autologous fat or fascia grafts, and selective use of fibrin sealants. Management varied: 4 patients were treated successfully with bed rest and pressure dressing, 3 required lumbar drainage, and 1 underwent surgical re-exploration with dural repair. No patient developed meningitis. At final follow-up, all patients achieved favorable neurological outcomes. Conclusion: CSF leaks remain a significant challenge after intradural spinal tumor surgery. Meticulous watertight dural closure, reinforcement with grafts or sealants, and judicious use of drains are key preventive measures. Early recognition and tailored management-ranging from conservative therapy to surgical re-exploration-can prevent serious complications and ensure good clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1834

## P0932: Paradoxical malnutrition in obese patients undergoing anterior cervical discectomy and fusion: a propensity-matched analysis of postoperative outcomes

Sean Kelleher

1

, Kyle Stump

1

, Prasad Kanuparthi

2

, Bong-Soo Kim

2

1

Lewis Katz School of Medicine at Temple University, Philadelphia, United States

2

Temple University Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Philadelphia, United States

Introduction: Obesity, as defined by body mass index (BMI) > 30 kg/m2, and malnutrition are well-known risk factors for postoperative complications following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF). Historically, these two nutritional states have been considered mutually exclusive; however, an emerging body of evidence supports the theory of paradoxical malnutrition, in which patients exhibit both an elevated BMI and a deficiency in either macro- or micronutrients. These patients may be underrecognized in spine surgery populations where BMI alone is used for risk stratification. The purpose of this study is to investigate postoperative outcomes following single-level anterior cervical discectomy and fusion in obese patients with protein-calorie malnutrition. Material and Methods: This study utilized the TriNetX Research Network, a global deidentified electronic medical record database, to identify adults > 18 years who underwent ACDF between January 1, 2015, and January 1, 2025. The study group included patients BMI > 30 kg/m2 diagnosed with protein-calorie malnutrition (ICD-10-CM E43 or E44) within 90 days before ACDF, compared to a control group comprised of patients BMI > 30 kg/m2 without protein-calorie malnutrition. Cohorts were 1:1 propensity-matched based on baseline demographic characteristics and comorbidities. The caliper for successful matching was set at a standard difference (std diff) < 0.1. Primary outcomes were evaluated within 90 days following the index procedure utilizing risk ratios (RR) and 95% confidence intervals (CI). P-value < 0.05 denotes statistical significance. Results: A total of 174 matched pairs were analyzed. The cohorts were balanced across all covariates included in the propensity score except for mean BMI at index; mean BMI was 33.3 ± 6.6 kg/m2 in the paradoxical malnutrition group compared with 35.7 ± 6.4 kg/m2 in the control group (std diff 0.371). Within 90 days postoperatively, paradoxical malnutrition was associated with increased risk of venous thromboembolism (RR 2.727, 95% CI 1.412-5.267; p = 0.002), pneumonia (RR 3.000, 95% CI 1.514-5.946; p = 0.001), acute respiratory failure (RR 5.300, 95% CI 2.788-10.074; p < 0.001), acute renal failure (RR 3.700, 95% CI 1.901-7.203; p < 0.001), sepsis (RR 3.800, 95% CI 1.956-7.383; p < 0.001), unplanned intubation (RR 2.300, 95% CI 1.128-4.688; p = 0.017), dysphagia (RR 2.565, 95% CI 1.662-3.959; p < 0.001), and all-cause readmission (RR 4.654, 95% CI 3.220-6.726; p < 0.001). Conclusion: Paradoxical malnutrition is associated with a significantly increased risk of postoperative complications following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion compared to matched obese controls. These findings highlight that reliance on BMI alone is insufficient for preoperative risk stratification. A more nuanced approach to preoperative clearance that accounts for both BMI and nutritional status is warranted. Early identification of paradoxical malnutrition would enable targeted nutritional optimization, with the potential to reduce preventable morbidity and resource use. Prospective studies are warranted to validate nutritional assessment tools for patients with obesity.

## ID: AGS26/2143

## P0933: Sacral fracture as a rare complication of circumferential lumbosacral fusion with ALIF, OLIF, and pedicular fixation in a young patient

Ratko Yurac

1

, Joaquin Zuniga Soria

1,2

, Andrea Marre

2

, Nicolas Bifani

2

, Juan Jose Zamorano

1

, Bartolomé Marré

1

, Guisela Quinteros

1

1

Clínica Alemana de Santiago, Santiago, Chile

2

Facultad de Medicina Universidad del Desarrollo-Clínica Alemana, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: Sacral fractures following circumferential lumbosacral arthrodesis are rare but clinically significant. Risk factors include pre-existing spondylolysis, poor bone quality, and insufficient pelvic support, all of which predispose to structural failure at S1 in complex constructs. Objective: To describe the surgical management and clinical course of a patient who developed a sacral fracture after L4-S1 circumferential fusion, highlighting biomechanical considerations and risk factors. Case Report: A 55-year-old male presented with irritative and deficit lumbosciatica due to lytic spondylolisthesis and L5-S1 foraminal stenosis. He underwent a three-stage circumferential fusion: ALIF at L5-S1, OLIF at L4-L5, and posterior pedicular fixation from L4 to S1. Postoperatively, he was diagnosed with interstitial lung disease requiring prolonged high-dose corticosteroids. The initial outcome was favorable; however, two months later, progressive paresthetic lumbosciatica developed. CT imaging revealed a fracture of the superior endplate of the S1 dome, cage subsidence, and partial recurrence of listhesis. Revision surgery via a posterior approach included foraminal decompression, S1 screw replacement, and resection of a free bone fragment, leading to progressive symptom relief, functional recovery, and bone consolidation. Discussion and Conclusion: This case highlights a rare complication after a circumferential construct generally considered biomechanically stable, most likely precipitated by corticosteroid-induced bone fragility. The outcome emphasizes the critical role of bone quality and construct design in preventing such failures, particularly in patients with systemic risk factors. Revision surgery successfully restored stability and improved symptoms. Careful preoperative evaluation of bone density and systemic conditions is essential when planning lumbosacral fusions.

## P0934: Preoperative antidepressants are associated with increased risk of opioid use and overall complications following anterior and posterior cervical fusion: A nationwide propensity-matched cohort study

Muhammad Waheed

1

, Sazid Hasan

2

, Mohamed Fawaz

3

, Abdel Rahman Diab

3

, Zina Smadi

4

, Rahul Vaidya

1

, Ahmad Nassr

5

, Ilyas Aleem

6

1

Detroit Medical Center/Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

2

University of Toledo Medical Center, Toledo, United States

3

University of Michigan Medical School, Ann Arbor, United States

4

The University of Jordan, School of Medicine, Amman, Jordan

5

Mayo Clinic, Rochester, United States

6

University of Michigan Health System, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Antidepressant use has been linked to adverse perioperative outcomes across surgical specialties. However, its impact on postoperative opioid utilization and complications following cervical fusion remains unclear. This study investigated the association between preoperative antidepressant use and postoperative outcomes after anterior cervical fusion (ACF) and posterior cervical fusion (PCF). Material and Methods: We conducted parallel retrospective cohort analyses utilizing TriNetX for patients undergoing primary ACF and PCF between December 31, 2006, and December 31, 2022. Patients aged ≥ 18 years old with ≥ 2-year follow-up were stratified by 90 days preoperative use of antidepressants. Cohorts were 1:1 propensity matched for demographics and comorbidities resulting in 4,575 ACF and 3,497 PCF pairs. Medical outcomes, opioid abuse, healthcare utilization, mechanical and surgical outcomes were assessed through risk ratios (RR), risk differences, p-values, and Kaplan-Meier analysis. Results: Antidepressant use was associated with increased new opioid prescriptions from 2 weeks to 2 years (ACF RR 1.090-1.130, p < 0.001; PCF RR 1.060-1.078, p < 0.001), higher hospital readmissions (RR 1.484, p < 0.001), prolonged inpatient stays (RR 1.329, p < 0.001), and ED visits from 6 weeks to 2 years (ACF RR 1.150-1.184, p < 0.001; PCF RR 1.092-1.144, p ≤ 0.034). Postoperative infections were elevated at 2 years (ACF RR 1.679-2.060, p ≤ 0.008; PCF RR 1.375-1.677, p ≤ 0.020). Opioid abuse was higher at 2 years (ACF RR 2.8000, p = 0.003; PCF RR 2.667, p = 0.001), and pulmonary embolism increased in ACF at 2 years (RR 1.633, p = 0.032). Reoperations were elevated at 1 and 2 years (RR 1.238-1.371, p ≤ 0.045), with reduced 2-year reoperation-free survival in ACF (p = 0.001) and PCF (p = 0.045). Conclusion: Preoperative antidepressant use is associated with significantly increased risks of postoperative opioid utilization and both medical and surgical complications, including sepsis, infections, adjacent segment disease, and reoperations, up to two years after primary anterior or posterior cervical fusion. These findings suggest that antidepressant use may serve as an important marker of perioperative risk and should be considered when counseling patients and optimizing care before cervical fusion.

## ID: AGS26/2241

## P0935: Can ChatGPT prevent wrong level spine surgery?

Sohum Desai

1

, Aadit Desai

1

1

University of Texas Rio Grande Valley School of Medicine, Edinburg, United States

Introduction: Wrong level spine surgery is considered a preventable surgical error. It can lead to adverse patient outcomes, litigation, and a compromised professional reputation. Artificial intelligence assets such as Open AI’s ChatGPT could help as the latest release supports imaging, performs tasks at human-like levels, and is widely available. Furthermore image analysis has been seen as a strength of AI in spinal surgery. We sought to study the accuracy of ChatGPT in identifying spinal level of intraoperative markers placed during spine surgery on fluoroscopic imaging. Material and Methods: A prospectively maintained neurosurgical database was queried for degenerative spine cases between 2024 to 2025. Intraoperative fluoroscopic images were obtained from the electronic medical record, anonymized, and used in the body of a prompt asking ChatGPT to identify the spinal level where the marker was located. This confirmed against the reported level of the marker by the radiologist and the operative note of the surgeon. Results: A total of 75 degenerative spine cases were reviewed with 37.3 % being cervical, 6.6% thoracic , and 56% lumbar. There were no cases of transitional anatomy. ChatGPT agreed with a board certified neurosurgeon in just 28% of cases. Conclusion: No, the current iteration of ChatGPT does not reliably identify correct levels of spine surgery. Responses are generated based on the knowledge and patterns trained on (with a cutoff in 2024), not from accumulating experience with individual users or cases.

## P0936: Impact of hypothyroidism on short-term and long-term outcomes following anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF): a nationwide propensity-matched cohort study

Muhammad Waheed

1

, Abdul-Lateef Shafau

1

, Abdurrahman Ehsan

2

, Ahmad Hasan

1

, Abdel Rahman Diab

3

, Omar Diab

4

, Rahul Vaidya

1

, Ahmad Nassr

5

, Ilyas Aleem

6

1

Detroit Medical Center/Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

2

Wayne State University, Detroit, United States

3

University of Michigan Medical School, Ann Arbor, United States

4

University of Texas Medical Branch, Galveston, United States

5

Mayo Clinic, Rochester, United States

6

University of Michigan Health System, Ann Arbor, United States

Introduction: Hypothyroidism has been implicated in adverse perioperative outcomes across several surgical specialties, including lumbar spine surgery. However, its influence on anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) remains insufficiently characterized. This study aimed to determine the association between preoperative hypothyroidism and postoperative medical and mechanical complications following ACDF. Material and Methods: A retrospective propensity-matched cohort analysis was conducted using the TriNetX database, encompassing patients who underwent primary ACDF between 2010 and 2025 across 54 U.S. academic centers. Patients with a diagnosis of hypothyroidism (n = 3,246; ICD-10-CM code E03.9) were matched 1:1 to euthyroid controls based on demographic and clinical covariates. Outcomes of interest included 90-day medical complications and 2-year mechanical complications. Results: Among 3,239 matched pairs at 90-day follow-up, patients with hypothyroidism experienced significantly higher rates of pneumonia (1.9% vs. 1.0%; p = 0.004; relative risk [RR], 1.85; 95% confidence interval [CI], 1.21-2.82) and hematologic complications (1.5% vs. 0.8%; p = 0.008; RR, 1.85; 95% CI, 1.16-2.95). At 2-year follow-up (3,085 matched pairs), hypothyroidism was independently associated with an increased incidence of adjacent segment disease (1.3% vs. 0.7%; p = 0.023; RR, 1.94; 95% CI, 1.08-3.48). Conclusion: Preoperative hypothyroidism is associated with elevated risks of both short-term and long-term complications after ACDF, particularly pneumonia, hematologic events, and adjacent segment disease. These findings underscore the importance of preoperative optimization of thyroid function as a potentially modifiable risk factor to improve outcomes in this surgical population.

## ID: AGS26/2268

## P0937: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs are associated with higher reoperation rates after anterior cervical discectomy and fusion: a nationwide cohort analysis

Muhammad Waheed

1

, Malik Al-Jamal

1

, Ashar Ahmed

2

, Abdel Rahman Diab

3

, Omar Diab

4

, Ali Elaydi

5

, Rahul Vaidya

1

1

Detroit Medical Center/Wayne State University School of Medicine, Detroit, United States

2

Wayne State University, Detroit, United States

3

University of Michigan Medical School, Ann Arbor, United States

4

University of Texas Medical Branch, Galveston, United States

5

Norton Leatherman Spine, Louisville, United States

Introduction: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is an inflammatory autoimmune disease that compromises joint integrity, often necessitating surgical interventions such as anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF). The association of Disease-Modifying Anti-Rheumatic Drugs (DMARDs) on ACDF outcomes remains unclear. This study evaluates the impact of DMARD therapy on short- and long-term postoperative complications including reoperation rates in RA patients undergoing ACDF. Material and Methods: We retrospectively queried the TriNetX database (2010-2023) to identify adults (18-90 years) undergoing primary single- or two-level ACDF. Patients with trauma, malignancies, or revision fusion were excluded. Patents with RA were subcategorized based on DMARD use within 1 year prior to their surgery. All patients were matched 1:1 via propensity score matching (508 DMARD users, 508 non-users). Postoperative outcomes included medical and mechanical complications at 90 days and 2-year follow-up. Kaplan-Meier analysis assessed reoperation-free survival. Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: RA patients on DMARDs experienced significantly higher rates of readmission (4.3% vs 2.0%; p = .031) and reoperation (4.1% vs 2.0%; p = .045) within 90 days postoperatively, compared to matched controls. At 2-year follow-up, reoperation rates remained elevated in the DMARD cohort (8.3% vs 4.1%; p = .006). Kaplan-Meier analysis demonstrated a significantly reduced reoperation-free survival among DMARD users (p = .0083). Conclusion: DMARD use in RA patients undergoing ACDF is linked to higher short- and long-term reoperation rates and reduced reoperation-free survival. This indicates that while DMARDs may provide symptomatic relief to patients with RA, they may compromise surgical durability. Such risks and benefits should be considered by clinicians when managing RA patients requiring ACDF.

## ID: AGS26/2479

## P0938: Three-stage ALIF revision surgery for failed TLIF cages

Francesco Caiazzo

1

, Daniel Alveal-Mellado

1

, Lucas Capo

1

, Paula Lavezzolo

1

, Judith Salat-Batlle

1

, Juan Bago

1

1

Instituto Quirúrgico Spanò, Barcelona, Spain

Introduction: Revision of transforaminal lumbar interbody fusion (TLIF) surgeries presents substantial surgical challenges due to dense scar tissue and pre-existing instrumentation. While anterior approaches offer superior visualization for cage retrieval, existing posterior constructs often impede the adequate intervertebral distraction essential for comprehensive cage removal and deformity correction. Material and Methods: We describe a three-stage surgical strategy (back-front-back) developed to overcome these limitations. We retrospectively reviewed 11 consecutive patients who underwent this revision surgery for indications including pseudarthrosis, cage subsidence, or sagittal malalignment to evaluate the procedure's safety and its efficacy in restoring sagittal alignment. Results: The three-stage procedure was technically successful in all 11 patients. The mean surgical time was 258.2 ± 51.7 minutes, and the mean blood loss was 492.7 ± 391.3 ml, metrics comparable to those reported for standard two-stage revisions. Postoperative imaging confirmed satisfactory cage placement and led to significant improvements in key sagittal alignment parameters, including lumbar lordosis. Conclusion: Our preliminary results suggest that this three-stage approach for TLIF revision safely addresses the challenges posed by existing posterior instrumentation. The technique effectively facilitates cage removal and restoration of sagittal alignment with acceptable operative times, offering a promising strategy for complex revision cases.

## ID: AGS26/2487

## P0939: Characterization of variables associated with alterations in electrophysiological monitoring in pediatric patients undergoing scoliosis correction surgery: a comparative study

Juan José Coba Lozano

1

, Mauricio Montoya Cañón

2

, Maria Fernanda Echeverry Martínez

3

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

4

, Mario Herrera Méndez

1

, Juan Manuel Sierra

1

, Ricardo León Restrepo Vallejo

1

, David Jimenez

2

1

Instituto Roosevelt, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Pontificia Universidad Javeriana de Cali, Bogotá, Colombia

3

Universidad del Rosario, Bogotá, Colombia

4

Universidad de la Sabana, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (IONM) is crucial in scoliosis correction surgeries, as it allows for the early detection of iatrogenic spinal cord injuries, facilitating timely intervention to prevent permanent neurological sequelae. IONM has been essential, providing real-time information on changes in the neurophysiological tracing of structures at risk, with high sensitivity and specificity. Although complications in spine surgery correction are rare (1-1.6%), in severe cases, complications can increase to 13-31%, which has promoted the routine use of IONM. Factors such as a high Cobb angle, intraoperative bleeding, instrumented levels, spinal cord morphology, and surgical time are also correlated with an increased risk of neurological injury. This study seeks to identify the variables associated with increased neurological risk in patients with spinal deformities, using a population from a national referral center in spine surgery. Material and Methods: A comparative analytical study will be conducted to evaluate the demographic, clinical, and intraoperative characteristics of pediatric patients undergoing scoliosis correction surgery between 2015 and 2025 at a national referral center. Patients with alterations in intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring (decrease in somatosensory evoked potential (SSEP) amplitude > 50%, increase in SSEP latency > 10%, or complete loss of Transcranial motor evoked potentials (TcMEP) signals) will be compared with those without alterations. Information will be collected from medical records and monitoring records, and the data will be analyzed using logistic regression in RStudio to identify the variables associated with the alterations. Patients with incomplete data or who underwent surgery at other institutions will be excluded. The data will be entered and protected using the REDCap platform, and analyses will be performed to compare the two groups and determine the risk variables. Results: The results of this study are expected to demonstrate a direct correlation between specific demographic, clinical, and intraoperative variables and an increased risk of intraoperative neurological alterations during scoliosis correction surgery. Factors such as the severity of the deformity, the degree of associated kyphosis, the etiology, and the patient's nutritional status are expected to be key predictors of an altered response in evoked potentials. With a large patient cohort, this analysis will provide a solid evidence base for refining and optimizing safety protocols, contributing to the reduction of neurological complications. Furthermore, the findings will enable the creation of risk models that will guide advance decision-making both in preoperative planning and throughout the surgical procedure. Conclusion: The characterization of demographic, clinical, and intraoperative variables in scoliosis surgery is essential for understanding and mitigating the risk of intraoperative neurological injuries. By identifying the risk factors associated with alterations in neurophysiological monitoring, this study will provide a scientific basis for refining clinical management protocols. The results are expected to enable more accurate patient stratification and optimize intraoperative decision-making, contributing to safety and improved clinical outcomes in scoliosis surgery. This work will contribute to the scientific literature by providing specific evidence from a national reference center, strengthening the scientific community in this area.

## ID: AGS26/2514

## P0940: Novel radiological predictors for proximal junctional kyphosis in osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture (OVCF) with kyphosis following posterior corrective surgery

Junyu Li

1

, Yinghong Ma

2

, Baitao Liu

2

, Junjie Ma

2

, Hanwen Zhang

2

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Yan Zeng

1

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: This study aimed to identify the effect of some novel risk factors associated with L1 vertebrae and parameters closely related to the sagittal alignment for the occurrence of proximal junctional kyphosis (PJK) following surgery for patients with osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures (OVCF) kyphosis. Material and Methods: 105 OVCF patients undergoing posterior corrective surgery between January 2008 and June 2021 with a minimum 2-year follow-up were included. These patients were divided into PJK and non-PJK groups. Spinopelvic parameters, including thoracolumbar slope (TLS) and the L1 plumb line (L1PL) were measured preoperatively, postoperatively, and at follow-up. Multivariate logistic analysis was performed on various risk factors and Global Alignment and Proportion (GAP) scores. Associations between novel parameters and PJK were analyzed using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis. Results: PJK was identified in 21.9% of patients. The mean age and follow-up were 64.87+8.96 years and 36.84+8.29 months, respectively. There were no statistically significant differences (p > 0.05) in confounding factors such as baseline demographic characteristics and preoperative/immediate postoperative PI-LL mismatch between the PJK group and the non-PJK group. Multiple comparisons showed that the proportion of PJK in the severely disproportioned group (the group with the highest GAP scores) and that of the other two groups with lower GAP scores were statistically different (P < 0.001). Regarding sagittal parameters, potential risk factors for PJK included preoperative TK (p = 0.002), preoperative TLS (p = 0.037), postoperative TLS (p = 0.003), and postoperative L1PL (p = 0.001). Among these, postoperative TLS (≥ 14.69°) and L1PL (≤ -1.91 cm) were identified as independent risk factors. Conclusion: TLS and L1PL can be used to predict the occurrence of PJK in patients undergoing surgery for OVCF and are crucial for preventing the progression of PJK. Achieving a proportionate GAP Score postoperatively seems to be a viable option as higher GAP scores were associated with higher rates of PJK.

## ID: AGS26/2516

## P0941: Clinical and biomechanical analysis of postoperative rod fracture in spinal deformity

Junyu Li

1

, Jinhua Wang

2

, Yihao Liu

3

, Xiang Zhang

1

, Yilin Lu

1

, Hanwen Zhang

3

, Zhuo Ran Sun

1

, Yongqiang Wang

1

, Yan Zeng

1

, Weishi Li

1

, Lin Zeng

1

, Miao Yu

1

, Dingshun She

2

1

Department of Orthopaedics, Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing Key Laboratory of Spinal Disease Research, Engineering Research Center of Bone and Joint Precision Medicine, Beijing, China

2

School of Engineering and Technology, China University of Geosciences, Beijing, China

3

Peking University Health Science Center, Beijing, China

Introduction: At present, the phenomenon of postoperative rod fracture has become a hot topic of current research. It not only brings physical and psychological burden to patients, but also brings economic burden to patients. However, there is no consensus on how to design the connecting rod according to the characteristics of the patient and how to determine the most suitable curvature of the connecting rod. Therefore, we hope to explore how to avoid rod fracture from the aspects of sagittal parameter, rod design and rod curvature through this study. Material and Methods: The rod fracture group had a mean age of 52.76 ± 13.16 years, with 9 females and 8 males among them. While the non-rod fracture group had a mean age of 60.54 ± 11.46 years, with 55 females and 30 males among them. We compared postoperative Cobb angle, SVA, CL, LL, TK, PT, PI, SS and the average curvature of the fixed rod between rod-fracture group and the control gruop. In finite element simulation analysis, TC4 titanium alloy is chosen as the modeling material, and a titanium alloy rod model is established, and finite element modeling is carried out by ABAQUS, with the model parameters of 5.5mm in diameter and 360mm in length. The rod was pre-bent to different curvatures to analyze the corresponding residual stresses, and to investigate the effect of different plastic deformation on the residual stresses caused by different pre-bending degrees of the titanium alloy rod. Results: We found that for sagittal parameters, greater postoperative TK (26.13 ± 13.04 vs. 36.14 ± 18.96, P=0.012), SVA (18.13±45.63 vs. 49.41±54.93, P=0.018), and LL (8.12±39.91 vs. 45.21±16.06, P<0.001) were risk factors for rod fracture, as reflected in the mean curvature of the fixed rods (1.94±0.69 vs. 3.91±1.49, P<0.001). At the same time, we find that the residual stresses in the fixed rods increase as the curvature increases, and the maximum residual stresses are distributed in the center of the rods. In the cross-section, as the curvature increases, the maximum residual stress distribution point gradually moves from the surface of the rod to the center of the rod, which indicates that the rod fracture occurrence sites are usually located at the surface and the center of the bar. Finally, the increase in curvature and residual stress leads to a smaller factor of safety for the rod and a decrease in its ability to withstand stress, resulting in a higher incidence of rod fracture. Conclusion: All in all, the occurrence of rod fracture is closely related to the postoperative sagittal parameters, which can be reflected in the average curvature of the fixed rod implanted in the spine. At the same time, biomechanical finite element analysis confirms this. Based on this result it has prospective implications for the specific design of spinal fixation rods and the elimination of residual stresses in the fixation rods to minimize the occurrence of rod fracture.

## ID: AGS26/2694

## P0942: Placement of a new screw from the next to the broken screw in revision spinal surgery; case series

Halil Ibrahim Süner

1

, Abdullah Revaha Yildirim

2

, Baran Babayigit

2

, Atakan Gok

2

, Emre Durdag

1

, Ozgür Kardes

1

, Soner Civi

1

, Kadir Tufan

1

1

Baskent University School of Medicine Department of Neurosurgery, Adana Dr. Turgut Noyan Application and Research Center, Adana, Turkey

2

Baskent University School of Medicine Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: As is well known, cases requiring revision spinal surgery present the most challenges for spine surgeons due to the risks involved and the challenges involved in spinal reconstruction and patient rehabilitation. Screw loosening, fractured instruments, adjacent segment disease, and surgical site infection are the primary conditions requiring revision. We aimed to present patients who underwent revision spinal surgery and, upon discovery of a broken screw during surgery, we placed a new transpeduncular screw adjacent to the broken screw and in the same peduncle. Material and Methods: We reviewed 15 patients who underwent revision spinal surgery at our clinic between 2021 and 2024. Upon discovery of a broken screw during surgery, we placed a new transpeduncular screw adjacent to the fractured screw that had been retained within the vertebra. Results: Thirteen patients with long-term follow-up were included in the study. The mean follow-up period was 20.3 months. Eleven were female and two were male. The mean age of the patients was 62.46 years (37-77 years). The presenting complaint in all patients was low back pain, followed by pain in both legs (n = 10). Nearly half of the patients had neurological claudication (n = 6). The most common level of broken screws was the L5 vertebra (n = 10). New transpeduncular screws were placed in both L5 peduncles of five patients (38.46%), adjacent to the broken screws. All patients underwent revision surgery. According to early postoperative imaging, one transpedicular screw was revised in each of two patients. All patients had better VAS, ODI, and JOA scores in the early postoperative period compared to the preoperative period. Conclusion: Revision instrumentation is one of the most challenging operations in spinal surgery due to the risks involved and the difficulty of the approach. Especially in cases where the broken screw must be left in the vertebra, the ability to use the same vertebra for reoperation is an advantage for both the patient and the surgeon. This eliminates the need to skip segments when instrumenting and contributes to the new spinal restoration. Because this approach is, to our knowledge, rarely encountered in the literature, we believe our case series will contribute to spinal surgery.

## P0943: Risk factors for hardware failure following posterior fixation of thoracolumbar junction fractures

Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Hedi Atig

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Ismail Karray

1

, Jihed Bechikh

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar junction fractures are frequent due to the biomechanical vulnerability of this transitional zone between the rigid thoracic spine and mobile lumbar spine. Posterior fixation is considered the gold standard for stabilization. However, implant failure remains a significant complication that compromises correction and long-term outcomes. The aim of our study is to identify the predictive risk factors for hardware failure following posterior fixation of thoracolumbar fractures. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of 120 patients who underwent posterior fixation for thoracolumbar junction fractures between January 2010 and January 2020. In our series, we noted nine cases of hardware failure. Several potential risk factors were analyzed, including demographic parameters, comorbidities, neurological status, and fracture-related characteristics. Surgical parameters and complications were included. Results: Several risk factors for hardware failure were identified: Advanced age (≥ 60 years) was a significant factor (p = 0.02, OR = 0.19, CI [0.04-0.80]). Diabetes was strongly associated with failure (p = 0.07, OR = 7.83, CI: 1.88-32.51), while obesity/overweight showed a statistical trend (p = 0.09). Short-segment fixation carried a high risk (p = 0.00, OR = 23.75, CI: 4.47-125.99), and nonunion was the most powerful predictor (p < 0.001, OR = 22.8, CI: 9.67-53.72). However, long fusion assembly (p = 0.04, OR = 0.04, CI: 0.04-1.09) and mixed fixation had protective effects. Furthermore, gender, neurological deficit, vertebral level, fracture types B and C, luxation, sepsis, and laminectomy did not reach statistical significance. Conclusion: In our series, the risk factors were advanced age, diabetes, obesity, short-segment fixation, and nonunion. Long-segment and mixed fusion assembly demonstrated protective roles. Careful surgical planning based on the patient’s comorbidities and the morphology of the fracture is essential to reduce the risk of implant failure.

## ID: AGS26/2747

## P0946: Analyze of the risk factors for nonunion after posterior fixation of thoracolumbar junction fractures

mouldi lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Hedi Atig

1

, Jihed Bechikh

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Ismail Karray

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar junction fractures are the most common traumatic spinal injuries due to the biomechanical vulnerability of this transition zone. Posterior fixation is widely considered the gold standard for stabilization. However, nonunion remains a severe complication that compromises spinal fusion, increases the risk of implant failure, and negatively impacts long-term functional outcomes. The aim of this study is to identify the predictive risk factors for nonunion after posterior surgical treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of 120 patients treated surgically for thoracolumbar fractures by posterior instrumentation between January 2010 and January 2020. In our series, we observed four cases of nonunion. Several demographic, clinical, radiological, and surgical parameters were analyzed, including age, gender, diabetes, obesity/overweight, neurological status, fractured vertebra (T12 or L1), fracture-related features (pedicular fracture, articular fracture, luxation, fracture type A2/A3), as well as fixation strategies (short-segment, long-segment, or mixed fixation), bone grafting, and postoperative complications such as sepsis and implant failure. Statistical analysis was performed using odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) and p-values. Results: Regarding the demographic parameters, we found a several risk factors for nonunion: Advanced age (≥ 60 years) was significantly associated with nonunion (p = 0.076). Female gender showed a higher risk (p = 0.021). Diabetes was a strong predictor (p = 0.001), as well as overweight/obesity (p = 0.032). Among surgical parameters, long-segment fixation was protective (p = 0.04, OR = 0.53, CI: 0.05-5.3), whereas short-segment fixation significantly increased the risk (p = 0.01). Sepsis was also correlated with nonunion (p = 0.18, OR = 7.3, CI: 0.64-83.6). Other factors such as neurological deficit, fractured vertebra (T12 vs. L1), fracture pedicle, luxation, fracture type (A2, A3), mixed fixation, bone grafting, and infection did not reach statistical significance. Conclusion: After posterior fixation for thoracolumbar junction fractures, nonunion is a rare and serious complications. In our series, the main risk factors were advanced age, female gender, diabetes, obesity, short-segment fixation, and sepsis. Conversely, long-segment fixation demonstrated a protective role. Identifying patients at risk of nonunion is crucial for optimizing surgical strategy, selecting adequate fixation length in order to reduce the incidence of this serious complication.

## ID: AGS26/2803

## P0947: Suction-induced intradural nerve root kinking following lumbar decompression

Bashar Altunbi

1

, Oludare Ashaolu

1

1

Swansea bay University Health Board, Port Talbot, United Kingdom

Introduction: Postoperative radiculopathy after lumbar decompression is typically attributed to hematoma, residual stenosis, or nerve injury. Suction drain-induced intradural nerve root kinking is a rarely reported mechanical cause. We present a unique case with direct imaging correlation and rapid symptom resolution. Material and Methods: A 67-year-old male underwent standard L4/5 interlaminar decompression with bilateral undercutting facetectomies. A subfascial closed-suction drain was placed. Within 18 hours, the patient developed severe bilateral radiculopathy. MRI revealed posterior deviation of intradural nerve roots toward the drain site. Results: Suspecting mechanical distortion from negative pressure, the suction was immediately disengaged. Repeat MRI showed restoration of normal nerve root configuration. Symptoms resolved rapidly, and the patient was discharged without recurrence. Conclusion: Suction-induced intradural nerve root kinking is a rare but reversible complication. Surgeons should consider drain-related mechanical effects in the differential diagnosis of early postoperative radiculopathy.

## P0948: Unexpected challenges in endoscopic spine surgery: systematic insights and thoracic case study

Kacper Domisiewicz

1

, Kajetan Latka

1

1

The St. Hedwig's Regional Specialist Hospital, Wodociagowa 4, Poland

Introduction: Endoscopic spine surgery has emerged as a minimally invasive alternative to open techniques, offering reduced tissue trauma and faster recovery. Nevertheless, this approach is not free from complications, which range from dural tears and nerve injuries to rare, unexpected systemic events. Materials and Methods A systematic review of recent literature was performed focusing on complications of spinal endoscopy. Additionally, we report a case of a 71-year-old female admitted with thoracic radiculopathy and left lower limb paresis due to a T6/7 disc herniation, who underwent endoscopic discectomy. Results The review demonstrates a generally low incidence of complications, most of which were minor, although re-operations and infection-related issues were described. In our case, while the herniation was completely removed and neurological symptoms improved, the patient developed acute dyspnea on the second postoperative day. Imaging revealed a right-sided pleural effusion, and thoracocentesis drained 2000 mL of clear fluid. The patient recovered well and returned to the neurosurgical ward in good general condition. Conclusion Although spinal endoscopy is associated with fewer complications than traditional open surgery, unexpected systemic complications can occur. Thoracic cases in particular demand careful perioperative monitoring. Awareness of both common and rare complications is crucial to ensure optimal patient outcomes and safety.

## ID: AGS26/3135

## P0949: A systematic review on predictors for outcomes in patients undergoing spinal surgery above 80 years of age

Sai Bhargava Reddy Maddireddy

1

, Adrian Gardner

1

, Morgan Jones

1

, Jwalant Mehta

1

, Egidio J da Silva

1

1

The Royal Orthopaedic Hospital, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: The global population aged 80 and above is rapidly increasing and is projected to triple between 2020 and 2050, reaching approximately 426 million individuals worldwide. This demographic shift is accompanied by increased incidence of degenerative spinal conditions, particularly lumbar spinal stenosis, degenerative spondylolisthesis, and osteoporotic vertebral fractures. Despite advances in surgical techniques and perioperative care, the elderly remain at increased risk for postoperative complications, mortality, and delayed recovery. However, chronological age alone may be an inadequate predictor of perioperative outcomes. Objectives: This review aims to systematically evaluate which preoperative factors, including frailty, other comorbidities, cognitive status, and surgical complexity, predict postoperative outcomes including complications, mortality, functional and neurological recovery, and cognitive decline. Methods: Our review was conducted according to the guidelines put by PRISMA for systematic review and the protocol was registered in PROSPERO (ID: 1186596). A search of PubMed Central, Cochrane, and Google Scholar from 2015 to 7/5/25 was performed. All titles, abstracts, and full text articles were screened by two independent reviewers at all stages. The Newcastle Ottawa Scale (NOS) was used to assess the quality of the studies, and narrative synthesis was performed as, due to the heterogeneity of the outcomes data, a meta-analysis was not possible. Results: Ten observational studies met the criteria, with sample sizes ranging from 36 to 7880. The most consistently reported and statistically supported predictors were frailty (via modified Frailty Index, mFI), ASA classification, and surgical complexity as demonstrated by spinal instrumentation. Within the individual cohorts, chronological age alone was not a significant determinant of outcomes when adjusted for frailty and comorbidity burden. Kweh et al. found that patients with mFI ≥3 had a major complication rate of 29.2% compared to 13.0% for low-frailty patients (mFI <3), and Jang et al. reported that mFI ≥0.27 significantly predicted 3-month mortality (p=0.024). ECOG-CCI was a strong predictor for severe complications (OR 1.6, p=0.004), and instrumented fusion showed a 15.7% delirium rate (p=0.017). Liao et al. noted improvements in ODI and VAS pain scores (ODI: 61.8 → 30.0; p<0.001), and Kuo et al. found no significant difference in 2-year mortality between octogenarians and younger patients after adjusting for comorbidities. The certainty of the current evidence is low because of heterogeneous reporting and limited follow-up among studies. Conclusions: Frailty (mFI) consistently outperformed age and ASA class in predicting complications and early mortality. The age threshold of ≥85 did not exhibit an independent association with unfavourable outcomes, thereby highlighting the importance of prioritizing functional and physiological health over mere chronological age. While ASA is a useful screening tool for rapid risk identification, our review suggests it should alone not be the deciding factor for operative planning in octogenarians. These findings should be cautiously interpreted, however, given the GRADE Low Certainty of this evidence, until validation through a large prospective trial is conducted in this population.

## ID: AGS26/3193

## P0950: Non-operative management of late-onset cerebrospinal fluid leak following full-endoscopic spine surgery with epidural blood patch: a technical note, case report, and scoping review

Jackapol Kamolpak

1

, Warayos Trathitephun

1

, Siravich Suvithayasiri

2

1

Department of Orthopedic Surgery, Chulabhorn Hospital, Chulabhorn Royal Academy, Bangkok, Thailand

2

Bone and Joint Excellence Center, Thonburi Hospital, Bangkok, Thailand

Introduction: Incidental durotomy is an acknowledged complication of endoscopic spine surgery, but late-onset cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaks presenting weeks after the index procedure are exceedingly rare and pose a significant management challenge. Standard treatment often involves conservative measures or revision surgery, the latter of which carries inherent risks of infection, neurological injury, and complications arising from dissecting through scar tissue. The epidural blood patch (EPB), which long-time considered a cornerstone in managing post-dural puncture headaches, is not well-established as a definitive treatment in this specific delayed following a spinal endoscopy procedure. This work reports a unique case of a late-onset CSF leak with significant symptomatology successfully treated with a targeted EPB and presents a scoping review of current literature on this non-operative technique. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 44-year-old female who underwent a successful lumbar endoscopic unilateral laminotomy for bilateral decompression (LE-ULBD) at L4/5. The patient’s postoperative course and the technical details of the EPB procedure are described. Additionally, a scoping review of the PubMed and Scopus databases was conducted to identify all published reports on the use of epidural blood patch for the treatment of CSF leaks that occur as a delayed complication of endoscopic spinal surgery. Results: The patient was discharged on the first postoperative day with complete resolution of her initial radicular pain. Three weeks later, she developed severe, debilitating postural headaches without diplopia, and nausea, with an MRI confirming a large pseudomeningocele at the surgical site, diagnostic of a late-onset dural tear. After a brief period of conservative management failed to provide relief, a targeted epidural blood patch with 15 cc of autologous blood was performed. The patient experienced immediate and complete resolution of her symptoms. At subsequent follow-ups, she remained asymptomatic with no recurrence, thereby avoiding the need for revision surgery. Our scoping review of the literature revealed a paucity of data, underscoring the novelty of this management approach for this specific complication. Conclusion: In our experience, a targeted epidural blood patch is a feasible, safe, and highly effective non-operative treatment for late-onset CSF leakage following full-endoscopic spine surgery. This technique provides an option of definitive solution that circumvents the significant morbidity associated with revision surgery. We assert that the epidural blood patch could be considered a primary and valuable tool in the management algorithm for this rare but challenging complication.

## ID: AGS26/3283

## P0951: Clinical sequelae of rod fracture: functional and opioid outcomes in adult spinal deformity

Schahin Salmanian

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Kiana Yeganeh

1

, Ryan Screven

1

, Jay I. Kumar

1

, Mohsen Rostami

1

, Mark S. Greenberg

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

University of South Florida, Department of Neurosurgery, Brain, and Spine, Tampa, United States

Introduction: Rod fracture (RF) is one of the most frequent mechanical complications of adult spinal deformity (ASD) surgery. While its predictors are increasingly well understood, less is known about its clinical impact on patient-reported outcomes and postoperative recovery. This study evaluated pain, opioid use, and functional disability in patients with and without RF. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 140 ASD patients who underwent long-segment posterior spinal fusion (≥7 levels) between 2015 and 2023 at a single academic center. RF was confirmed radiographically during follow-up. Postoperative outcomes assessed included average and maximum pain scores (VAS), opioid use rate and duration, and functional disability measured by the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI). Statistical comparisons were conducted using Spearman’s correlation. Results: RF occurred in 35.7% of patients at a median of 761 days postoperatively. Pain levels were similar between groups, as median average pain was 5.0 in both RF and non-RF patients (p = 0.940), and median maximum pain was also comparable (9.0 vs. 9.0, p = 0.944). The rate of opioid use did not differ significantly (94% RF vs. 89.7% non-RF, p = 0.390). However, RF patients required significantly longer opioid therapy (36.7 ± 25.7 vs. 26.6 ± 25.5 days, rho = 0.210, p = 0.016). Functional outcomes were also impaired. ODI scores were higher in RF patients (median 18 vs. 14, rho = 0.258, p = 0.003), indicating greater postoperative disability. Conclusion: Rod fracture did not translate into increased pain or higher opioid initiation rates but was associated with prolonged opioid dependence and worse functional disability. These findings underscore the clinical burden of RF, highlighting its role in extended recovery and reduced quality of life despite unchanged pain scores. Preventing RF through surgical and biomechanical optimization may therefore yield substantial benefits not only in construct longevity but also in reducing postoperative opioid exposure and improving functional outcomes.

## P0952: A rare complication of a broken spinal needle during lumbar puncture: a case report

Hamza Daly

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

, Chaima Debabi

2

, Bacem Trabelsi

2

, Ben Ahmed Sameh

2

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, Mohamed Hassine

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

2

Department of anesthesiology and Intensive Care, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Lumbar puncture (LP) is a routine diagnostic and therapeutic procedure widely used in neurology, intensive care, and anesthesiology. Most complications are benign, such as post-dural puncture headache or local back pain. Needle breakage is an extremely rare but alarming complication that may result in neurological injury or foreign body migration. We report a rare case of spinal needle fracture during lumbar puncture, which required surgical extraction. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 54-year-old male who was admitted to the intensive care unit with prolonged fever and clinical suspicion of meningitis. A lumbar puncture was attempted for diagnostic purposes. During the procedure, accidental breakage of the spinal needle occurred. Lumbar spinal ultrasound confirmed the presence of a retained metallic fragment at the lumbar level. Results: The patient was immediately transferred to the orthopedic operating room. Surgical extraction was performed via a posterior lumbar approach, which allowed complete retrieval of the broken needle under direct visualization. The procedure was uneventful. Postoperative recovery was favorable, with no neurological deficit or secondary complication. Conclusion: Needle fracture during lumbar puncture is exceedingly rare, with only a few cases reported in the literature. Potential risk factors included the use of low-quality or fragile needles, the excessive force or repeated attempts at puncture and an inappropriate technique of insertion. Complications can be serious, including migration of the fragment, neurological injury, or infection.

## ID: AGS26/3386

## P0953: Effect of pseudarthrosis after ACDF on development of adjacent segment degeneration: a minimum of 5 years follow-up

Jae Jun Yang

1

, Jun-Young Choi

1

1

Dongguk University Ilsan Hospital, Goyang, South Korea

Introduction: Pseudarthrosis after anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) is failure of fusion and featured by the postoperative segmental motion at the index level. Ironically, the segmental motion is spotlighted as the advantage of cervical disc replacement (CDR) for prevention of adjacent segment degeneration (ASD). It was hypothesized that the segmental motion of pseudarthrosis may have protective effect against ASD just same as that of CDR. The objective of this study is to elucidate the effect of pseudarthrosis after ACDF on surgical outcomes and development of ASD. Material and Methods: Retrospective cohort studies were performed. Fusion status and ASD were evaluated at 2 years and minimum 5 year final follow-up. The patients were allocated based on the fusion status at 2 years. Radiological and clinical outcomes were compared between the fusion group and the pseudarthrosis group. Results: One-hundred four patients were enrolled for 2 year short-term evaluation. There was no significant difference in radiological and clinical outcomes between two groups. The ASD rates were 36.9% and 7.7% in the fusion group and the pseudarthrosis group, respectively (p < 0.001). 92 patients were enrolled for minimum 5year long-term evaluation. Radiological and clinical outcomes demonstrated no significant difference between two groups. The ASD rates were 55.6% and 15.8% in the fusion group and the pseudarthrosis group, respectively (p<0.001). Multivariate logistic regression analysis demonstrated that fusion at 2 years, single level surgeries, and larger postoperative T1 slope were significantly correlated with development of ASD. Conclusion: Pseudarthrosis caused significantly less ASD compared to fusion both in the short-term and long-term follow-up after ACDF. The segmental motion of pseudarthrosis after ACDF may have a preventive effect on ASD.

## ID: AGS26/3486

## P0954: Lumbar posterior instrumentation complicated by postoperative cerebrospinal fluid leak, PRES and multidrug-resistant infection: a rare case

Yunus Emre Yilmaz

1

1

Ankara Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) is a rare but potentially reversible neurological condition typically associated with hypertension, immunosuppressive therapy, renal dysfunction, or systemic infections. PRES following spinal surgery is exceedingly uncommon, with only isolated case reports available in the literature. Postoperative cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage and infection have been suggested as possible triggers. We report an unusual case of PRES after revision lumbar surgery, complicated by CSF leakage, John Cunningham virus (JC virus) positivity and multidrug-resistant Acinetobacter infection. Material and Methods: Clinical data from a 58-year-old female patient who underwent posterior instrumentation for recurrent lumbar disc herniation were retrospectively reviewed. Information was obtained from electronic medical records, operative reports, neuroimaging studies, microbiology results, and laboratory parameters. Brain and spinal MRI, CSF and blood cultures, and urine PCR for JC virus were analyzed. The management course, including dural repair, wound revision, and prolonged intravenous antibiotic therapy under multidisciplinary care, was documented. Results / Case Presentation: A 58-year-old female underwent posterior instrumentation for recurrent lumbar disc herniation. Two weeks after discharge, she presented with acute left hemiplegia and cognitive impairment. Brain MRI demonstrated radiological features consistent with PRES. CSF culture was negative, although JC virus was detected in urine. During hospitalization, the patient developed a wound CSF leak, requiring dural repair and wound revision. Wound culture grew Acinetobacter baumannii and prolonged targeted antibiotic therapy (vancomycin, meropenem, colistin, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) was administered. Despite severe neurological deterioration, subsequent MRI revealed regression of PRES-related lesions, while CRP levels decreased from 140 mg/L to 5 mg/L. After a total of 10 weeks of multidisciplinary inpatient management, the patient was transferred to rehabilitation without active wound leakage. Conclusion: PRES after spinal surgery is extremely rare, with only sporadic reports describing its occurrence in the postoperative setting. Potential mechanisms include altered cerebrovascular autoregulation due to CSF leakage, infection-related inflammatory response and hemodynamic instability. The coexistence of PRES, JC virus detection and multidrug-resistant infection in our patient underscores the multifactorial pathogenesis and highlights diagnostic challenges. This case represents an exceptionally rare scenario of PRES following spinal surgery, emphasizing the importance of considering PRES in the differential diagnosis of acute neurological decline in the postoperative period. Early recognition and multidisciplinary management are essential for improved outcomes.

## Trauma – cervical

## ID: AGS26/22

## P0955: Modified halter traction for severe post-traumatic cervical kyphosis: a novel preoperative technique

Dustin Kress

1

, Don Moore

1

1

University of Missouri, Columbia, United States

Introduction: Cervical fractures in diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) patients are highly unstable, with 73.5% experiencing complications. Progressive cervical kyphosis often requires extensive osteotomies with significant morbidity. Traditional halo traction has limitations in elderly patients with comorbidities. We present a modified halter traction technique for preoperative correction of severe post-traumatic cervical kyphosis. Material and Methods: A 78-year-old male with DISH sustained a C5-C6 disc space fracture from a ground-level fall. Poor collar compliance led to progressive kyphosis from 24 degrees to 35 degrees at four weeks with chin-on-chest deformity. Modified halter traction was implemented five days preoperatively using mandible/occipital straps with foam padding, a 35-degree patient incline, and neck support. Traction started at 2 pounds and gradually increased to 10 pounds. Daily radiographs monitored correction. During breaks, the patient tolerated 3-4 hour sessions with Miami J collar. Staged surgeries included C5-6 anterior discectomy/fusion and posterior C3-T2 instrumentation/fusion. Results: Serial radiographs demonstrated progressive correction over five days, transforming 36 degrees of kyphosis to 17 degrees of lordosis - a total correction of 53 degrees. This eliminated chin-on-chest deformity and achieved near-neutral alignment. Preoperative correction avoided extensive cervical osteotomies. Surgical blood loss was minimal (25cc anterior, 200cc posterior). The technique was well-tolerated and manageable by nursing staff and family, with periodic breaks for hygiene and meals. Conclusion: Modified halter traction provides an effective alternative to halo traction for severe post-traumatic cervical kyphosis in DISH patients. Advantages include superior patient tolerance, removable application, and reduced surgical complexity by avoiding extensive osteotomies, blood loss, and operative time. The technique is ideal for elderly patients unsuitable for halo traction. Prospective studies are needed to validate efficacy and establish selection criteria.

## ID: AGS26/129

## P0956: Outcomes of anterior cervical discectomy and fusion with cage and plate construct in patients with traumatic cervical spine injury

Jahan Aslami

1,2

, Brianne Giuffrida

1,2

, Shahed Elhamdani

2

, Navya Dandu

2

, Alex Yu

2

, Daniel Altman

1,2

1

Drexel University College of Medicine, Pittsburgh, United States

2

Allegheny Health Network, Pittsburgh, United States

Introduction: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF) has become a standard treatment of degenerative cervical spine disease due to its favorable complication profile and high rates of fusion. Utilizing this approach for traumatic cervical spine injuries, however, has been debated in the literature. Anterior, posterior, or combined approaches can be selected based on fracture type and location. In traumatic cervical instability, ACDF is generally supplemented with posterior instrumentation for additional stability and to mitigate the risk of hardware failure. This can increase the risk of complications, add operative time/exposure, and may necessitate additional levels of fusion. A novel technique utilizing the ACDF with a hybrid construct including a cage and plate may provide sufficient stability while negating complications associated with posterior approaches. This case series assesses the reliability of ACDF with a cage plate construct (CPC) in cases of cervical spine trauma. Material and Methods: Patients at a single institution who underwent ACDF with CPC after cervical spine trauma were retrospectively reviewed. The primary outcome was hardware failure and return to the operating room. Patient demographics, co-morbidities, mechanism/type of injury, operative procedure and average follow up duration were also recorded. Descriptive statistics were used to represent the data. Results: Fourteen patients (6 males and 8 females) met inclusion criteria, and the procedure was performed by 2 surgeons over a span of 4 years. Average age was 52 ± 20.8 years with an average operative time of 134 ± 74 minutes. Seven patients were injured from motor vehicle collisions while 7 sustained falls. Eight patients had vertebral fractures, 3 had traumatic spondylolisthesis, 2 had disc herniations and 1 patient had a combination of spondylolisthesis and a disc herniation. Eleven patients underwent single level fusion while 3 underwent 2 level fusion. Eight out of 14 patients had a fusion at the C6/7 level. Average follow up was 6.5 +/- 4.3 months. Two patients were immediately lost to follow up and were excluded from the average. Two other patients died from unrelated medical conditions before 6 months of follow up.
