Zero out of 14 patients suffered from hardware failure, peri-operative complications or returned to the operating room for additional spinal procedures. Conclusion: ACDF in the context of traumatic spinal injury is often supplemented with posterior instrumentation to ensure a stable construct. This combined approach increases the risk of perioperative complications due to increased operative time and multiple incision sites. Importantly, posterior operations may require more extensive levels of fusion which ultimately reduces range of motion while ACDF with CPC only involves the injured level. The results of our limited case series provide evidence for the safety and efficacy of ACDF with CPC without the need for posterior instrumentation. All patients in our study had stable constructs in the immediate post-operative period and sustained no peri-operative complications. With longer follow up, rates of successful fusion can also be determined. Future studies could better characterize the utility of ACDF with CPC in the context of specific injury patterns.

## ID: AGS26/132

## P0957: Complex type 3 odontoid fracture with C2 body comminution presenting as a rare post-traumatic anterior atlanto-axial subluxtaion: a brief description of management

Uday Raman

1

, Nikhil Kadanthode

1

, Sreejith Chandran Menon

1

, Midhun P. C

1

1

Meitra Hospital, Kozhikode, India

Introduction: Odontoid fractures of the second cervical vertebra (C2) are categorized into three types (Anderson-D’Alonzo system) with type III extending into the body of the axis. A type 3 fracture can be termed as complex when comminution involves more than 50% of the lateral mass or additional fracture lines extend into the vertebral body or pars. These fractures are usually the result of high energy trauma often causing significant instability and neurological issues.The usual C1-C2 subluxation pattern is of the dens being displaced posteriorly causing spinal cord compression . Our case is unique where the C1-C2 is anteriorly subluxated with the C1-dens complex translating anteriorly as a whole. This case report discusses the management of a 50-year-old female with a complex Type III odontoid fracture with severe C2 body comminution and C1-C2 anterior subluxation. Material and Methods: A 50-year female presented to the emergency department with alleged history of road traffic accident with injury to the neck . Her initial evaluation was done according to ATLS protocol and a collar was applied till full clinicoradiological diagnosis was done. Cervical spine tenderness was noted and neck movements were restricted .Her neurological examination was unremarkable with no neurological deficits post injury .A CT polytrauma protocol was done for her which revealed a complex type 3 odontoid fracture with severe C2 body comminution and anterior translation of the C1-Dens complex with C1-C2 anterior subluxation. MRI was also done which confirmed the diagnosis with no spinal cord compression or any protruding disc fragment .As there was no neurological deficit but an unstable C1-C2 injury and hence it was decided to go ahead with C1-C3 posterior fixation to achieve reduction and stabilisation.With the help of mayfeild clamp the patient was positioned prone and then through a midline incision from the base of occiput to the C4 the posterior elements were exposed and step by step fixation was done starting with C1 lateral mass screws bilaterally , then C2 bilateral pars screws and C3 lateral mass screws. Then with appropriate reduction maneuvres the C1-dens complex was brought posteriorly to reduce the C1-C2 subluxation and then the rods were fixed to the screws to achieve C1-C3 fixation.All the above steps were carefully monitored with intraoperative neuromonitoring. Results: Post operatively a hard cervical collar was applied. She was made to stand and walk on the next day itself. No post operative neurological deficits were noted. She was started on incentive spirometry and was allowed to do daily light activities . No major complications after the surgery were noted and she was discharged on post operative day 2. She was told to continue with the collar for at least 6 weeks. Follow-up radiographs showed no signs of displacement and showed signs of union in the C2 body. Conclusion: This case highlights that the current classification systems may not fully capture rare but clinically important variants and thus reinforcing the need for improvements in the classification system and allow for flexible surgical planning and incorporation of C3 fixation when C2 anatomy is severely disrupted.

## ID: AGS26/227

## P0958: Atypical cause of cardiac arrest: traumatic instability at the craniocervical junction

Kenny Hagar

1

, Zaccaria Richard

1

, Jan Wulf

1

, Adrian Cavalcanti Kußmaul

1

, Wolfgang Böcker

1

, Julia Schneller

1

1

LMU München, MUM - Muskuloskelettale Universitätsmedizin München, Trauma and Orthopedic Surgery, Munich, Germany

Introduction: Managing cardiac arrest in the context of polytrauma represents a highly complex, interdisciplinary emergency scenario. Traumatic cardiac arrests presenting with pulseless electrical activity (PEA) are associated with a poor prognosis. Diagnostic efforts typically focus on the well-known reversible causes (the 4 Hs and HITS). However, injuries of the upper cervical spine are rarely considered as differential diagnoses in the acute phase. Prehospital diagnosis is further hampered by the lack of imaging modalities, making early detection of potentially unstable dislocations difficult. Case Presentation: A 31-year-old forestry worker was struck on the back of the head and thoracoabdominal region by a falling log approximately 30 cm in diameter while felling a tree. His left lower limb was trapped. Upon arrival of the emergency physician, the patient was unconscious and exhibited agonal breathing. He was intubated for airway protection, and cardiopulmonary resuscitation was initiated due to a PEA rhythm. He was airlifted to our emergency department. On arrival in the trauma bay, the patient was intubated, ventilated, had a GCS score of 3, and continued to show PEA on ECG. Following ATLS-protocol, a whole-body CT scan was performed, which revealed both atlanto-occipital and atlanto-axial dislocations. Additional findings included multiple thoracic, abdominal, and extremity injuries, without evidence of intracranial hemorrhage. Due to the severe craniocervical instability, a halo fixator was applied immediately on the CT table, resulting in morphological realignment of the dislocation. Subsequently, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) was achieved-after a total resuscitation time of 115 minutes. However, due to rapidly progressive multi-organ failure, further surgical intervention was not feasible. The patient died shortly thereafter in the emergency department. Discussion: This case illustrates the rare but clinically significant scenario of traumatic cardiac arrest caused by a high cervical spine injury. Damage to cardiorespiratory centers in the brainstem can lead to neurogenic circulatory collapse presenting as PEA. In unconscious patients, classical clinical signs (e.g., neurological deficits) may be absent, increasing the risk of delayed diagnosis. In this case, whole-body CT enabled timely identification of the injury, and immediate halo fixation likely contributed causally to achieving ROSC-despite an extremely prolonged low-flow time. This suggests that mechanical stabilization in high cervical dislocations may represent a potentially life-saving intervention in exceptional circumstances. Conclusion: Injuries at the craniocervical junction should be considered in the differential diagnosis of traumatic cardiac arrest. Early imaging in the trauma bay and prompt application of a halo fixator can be crucial. This case reveals a critical gap in current trauma care and emphasizes the need to expand clinical algorithms to include this specific injury pattern.

## ID: AGS26/491

## P0959: Predictors of return to work following cervicothoracic spinal cord injury

Josh Callaway

1

, Hania Shahzad

1

, Audrey Zhao

1

, Brian Vasquez

1

, Muhammad Padela

1

, Wyatt Vander Voort

1

, Rolando Roberto

1

, Safdar Khan

1

, Yashar Javidan

1

, Eric Klinerberg

1

, Allan Martin

1

, Hai Le

1

1

UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento, United States

Introduction: Following spinal cord injury (SCI), approximately 65% of individuals do not return to paid employment, which can significantly impact quality of life and long-term independence. Being able to identify early which patients are more likely to return to work (RTW) allows clinicians to provide targeted support. However, accurate prediction remains challenging due to heterogeneity in injury severity, socio-demographics, and clinical course. By using the National Spinal Cord Injury Model Systems Database, this study seeks to identify predictors of RTW after a cervicothoracic SCI at one year post-injury through a tiered logistic regression modeling approach. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis of de-identified data from the National Spinal Cord Injury Model Systems Database was performed. Inclusion criteria required patients (1) sustained a traumatic cervical or thoracic SCI, (2) had documented neurological level of injury at index admission, and (3) were a part of the competitive labor market pre-injury (n = 6043). RTW was defined as resuming any form of employment, including full-time, part-time, self-employed, supported work, or military service, after injury. Individuals with missing data essential to model construction were excluded. Logistic regression was used to identify predictors of RTW, incorporating demographic, injury, clinical, discharge, and socioeconomic variables. Model performance was evaluated using 5-fold stratified cross-validation with AUC-ROC, accuracy, and F1-score as performance metrics. Results: Multivariate analysis consistently identified ASIA Motor Index Score at discharge and rehab admission as a significant predictor of RTW (OR 1.01-1.02, p < 0.001). Discharge motor level, neurologic level, and ASIA Impairment Scale Grade were significant predictors of RTW. Among socioeconomic indicators, job type, marital status, and family income were found to contribute to predictive performance. Individuals in skilled professional occupations, service/manual labor jobs, and skilled labor positions had significantly lower odds of returning to work (Model 3 ORs 0.07-0.56, p < 0.001). Although sex, race, and intermittent catheter use were significant in Models 1 and 2, they were not retained in the other multivariate models after adjustment for more granular clinical and socioeconomic variables, suggesting their effects may be mediated by these factors. Models using only broad demographic and neurologic variables predicted RTW poorly (accuracy 58-60%, AUC-ROC 0.61-0.62 across models 1-2). Incorporating more granular clinical and socioeconomic variables improved performance substantially (77-81% accuracy, AUC-ROC 0.82-0.88 across models 3-7). Conclusion: This study highlights several key predictors of RTW after cervicothoracic SCI, including neurological function (ASIA Motor Index Score, motor and neurologic level, ASIA Impairment Scale), specific job types, trauma etiology, bladder management methods, marital status, and family income. Incorporating these factors allows for a more holistic approach to post-injury care. While no prior models exist specifically for cervicothoracic SCI, the best-performing model (AUC-ROC 0.88, F1 0.83) outperforms or matches most published prognostic models for RTW and related outcomes in other injury populations, including orthopedic trauma, low-back surgery, and traumatic brain injury. A free, interactive online tool implementing these predictors is available at https://rtw-sci.streamlit.app/. Future studies should focus on validating these predictors prospectively.

## ID: AGS26/657

## P0961: Surgical versus conservative management in ASIA D central cord syndrome: a retrospective study of functional outcomes and impact of frailty on mildly symptomatic central cord syndrome patients

Linda Lim Huiling

1

, A Aravin Kumar

1

, Zhihong Chew

3

, Andy Yeo Kuei Siong

3

, Terry Teo Hong Lee

3

, Ling Ji Min

1,2

, Lester Lee

1,2

, Dinesh Shree Kumar

3

, Zhiquan Damian Lee

1,2

1

Department of Neurosurgery, National Neuroscience Institute, Singapore

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Changi General Hospital, Singapore

3

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Changi General Hospital, Singapore

Introduction: Central cord syndrome (CCS) is the most common type of incomplete spinal cord injury (SCI), and is associated with advanced age, cervical spondylosis, and low-velocity injuries in the elderly. This demographic frequently presents with comorbidities and frailty, raising questions about the benefits of surgery, particularly in ASIA D CCS patients with relatively mild symptoms. Early surgery (within 24 hours) is increasingly supported for many SCI; however, its role in CCS remains contentious. Some centres advocate early surgery for ASIA D CCS, while others favour conservative management unless deterioration occurs. The optimal management of ASIA D CCS remains a subject of debate. This study aims to provide a direct comparison of functional outcomes between surgical intervention and conservative management in patients with ASIA D CCS, addressing a critical gap in existing evidence and guiding clinical decision-making. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted at 2 Singapore hospitals from January 2015 to December 2023. CCS was defined as a traumatic injury resulting in a cervical neurological level of injury and greater weakness in the upper limbs compared to the lower limbs, evidenced by a lower average bilateral motor score for the C5-T1 segments below the neurological level of injury, as compared to the L2-S1 segments. Results: A total of 54 patients were included: 28 in the conservative group (mean age 67.7 years) and 26 in the surgical group (mean age 61.5 years). Surgical timing was 65.4% after 72 hours, 26.9% within 24-72 hours, and 7.7% within 6-24 hours. There were no statistically significant differences between surgical and conservative groups in ASIA scores at 1, 3, 6, or 12 months (p > 0.05), FIM change from admission to discharge (p = 0.162), or one-year mortality (p = 1.0). Subgroup analyses showed no significant differences in outcomes based on timing of surgery or injury impact (88.9% low-impact, 11.1% high-impact; p = 0.778). While BMI categories showed significant differences in ASIA 1-month scores (p < 0.001) and age categories in ASIA 3-month scores (p = 0.021), direct comparisons between both groups within these categories revealed no significant differences. The Modified Frailty Index showed a trend towards significance (p = 0.060), but analysis within frailty categories found no significant differences between both groups in the low frailty category, with insufficient data for the high frailty category. Discussion: In this study, most patients in the surgical group underwent intervention > 72 hours after injury. Our results showed that functional outcomes did not differ significantly between patients treated surgically and those managed conservatively. However, the small sample size limited our ability to determine whether factors such as frailty influenced outcomes between the two groups. Despite this, the findings suggest that high BMI and frailty may negatively impact recovery in both groups. Further research is needed to investigate the optimal management strategy for ASIA D CCS patients. Conclusion: This study found no significant difference in functional outcomes between surgically and conservatively managed ASIA D CCS patients. While limited by sample size, the findings highlight the potential negative impact of high BMI and frailty on recovery in both groups.

## ID: AGS26/758

## P0962: Individualised fracture specific tricortical autograft for cervical flexion teardrop fractures

Stjepan Ivandić

1

, Jure Pavešić

1

, Mišo Krstičević

1

, Stipe Corluka

1,2,3

1

Spinal Surgery Division, Department of Traumatology, University Hospital Center Sestre, Milosrdnice, Zagreb, Croatia

2

St Catherine Specialty Hospital, Zagreb, Croatia

3

Department of Anatomy and Physiology, University of Applied Health Sciences, Zagreb, Croatia

Introduction: Cervical flexion teardrop fractures are hyperflexion injuries in which the anteroinferior part of the vertebral body is fractured. They commonly occur at the C5 level due to a stress increaser and can result in a localized kyphotic angle and deformity. They are highly unstable and warrant surgical treatment. Although an anterior approach is most commonly used, the surgical technique depends on the size and morphology of the fracture fragment. In cases involving a large fragment, corpectomy is the preferred standard of treatment, as ACDF with a PEEK or titanium cage risks hardware migration due to endplate compromise. In this technical report, we describe a new “puzzle” technique in which ACDF is performed with a custom individualized tricortical iliac graft that is custom-fitted and individualized according to the specific morphology and shape of the fracture of the patient. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients with flexion teardrop fractures treated at our institution using the puzzle technique. Inclusion criteria were the diagnosis of flexion teardrop fracture involving a large anterior vertebral body fragment and treatment with a patient-specific tricortical iliac crest autograft. Demographic data, surgical technique, radiographic results, complications and patient-reported results were analyzed. Results: Two patients with cervical flexion teardrop fractures were treated using the puzzle technique. In both cases, the fractured fragment was removed, and a custom tricortical iliac crest autograft was shaped to match the vertebral defect and the space of the intervertebral disc, followed by anterior plating, ensuring a stable construct. Postoperative imaging confirmed an adequate graft position without subsidence or hardware failure. Both patients reported good neck function with reduced VAS pain scores. Conclusion: The puzzle technique enables an adequate reconstruction of the vertebral defect and disc space. It is an excellent alternative to corpectomy, as it preserves bone and an additional healthy segment. Early outcomes in two patients demonstrate encouraging results without hardware failure or migration.

## ID: AGS26/1234

## P0963: Predictive factors for adjacent segment degenerative changes following anterior surgical treatment of rotational injuries of the lower cervical spine

Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Dhiaeddine Baccouch

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Mohamed Hassine

1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Disc degeneration is a physiological aging process. The disc above or below a spinal fixation appears to be exposed to accelerated degeneration. Adjacent segment degeneration (ASD) is a potential long-term complication after cervical spine surgery. Identifying risk factors is essential to optimize patient follow-up and prevent late complications. The objective of our work is to determine the predictive factors of adjacent segment degeneration after treatment of rotational injuries of the lower cervical spine. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective study including 38 patients treated surgically via an anterior approach for lower cervical rotational spine injuries between 2015 and 2022. According to the UCLA grading system at the last follow-up, patients were divided into two groups: • Group A: patients without significant degeneration (UCLA < 3, n = 34). • Group B: patients with advanced degeneration (UCLA grade 3 or 4, n = 4). The following parameters were analyzed and compared between the two groups: age, gender, BMI, smoking, diabetes, posterior wall involvement, intracanal fragment, and Argenson classification. Results: Our series included 38 patients with a mean age of 41.86years. The sex ratio was 8.5. The body mass index (BMI) was 25.28 kg/m2 with extremes of 20.47 kg/m2 and 28.72 kg/m2. The most commonly affected level was C5-C6 (52.6% of cases). The average oh anthelisthesis was 4.29 mm. The mean of Cobb angle was 17.74°. According to the Argenson classification, the unilateral facet fracture was the most frequent (47.4% of cases). The average follow-up was 15.82 months. ASD was reported in 10.52% of cases. In a univariate study, the predictive factors for ASD were: the older age (p = 0.018; OR = 1.346; CI [1.005-1.850]), diabetes (p = 0.09; OR = 31; CI [2.409-398.879]) and posterior wall involvement (p = 0.047; OR = 16; CI [1.415-180.896]). However, we didn’t find a significant relationship between gender (p = 0.628; OR = 0.882; CI [0.780-0.998]), BMI (p = 0.627; OR = 2.26; CI [0.223-25.140]), smoking (p = 0.52; OR = 1.61; CI [0.20-12.9]), and the anatomical characteristics of the fracture and the risk evolution for ASD. In a multivariate study, the only factor predicting osteoarthritic degeneration was age (p = 0.01). Conclusion: The causative factors of adjacent-segment disease are controversial. Disc degeneration is a normal manifestation of the ageing process. Nevertheless, disc disease is more prevalent at levels adjacent to interbody fusion than in the normal population, suggesting accelerated disc degeneration due to increased loading of the adjacent levels. Furthermore, lesions that are missed during the pre-operative work-up may play a role, as the available investigations do not always have high negative predictive values

## ID: AGS26/1324

## P0964: Does age influence the clinical and radiological outcomes of surgical treatment via the anterior approach for lower cervical rotational spine fractures?

Raboudi Hamza

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Dhiaeddine Baccouch

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, Yassine Aloui

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Rotational injuries of the lower cervical spine are rare but potentially debilitating. Age-related anatomical and physiological differences can influence healing and postoperative outcomes. It is essential to understand the impact of age on clinical and radiological parameters in order to optimize management strategies. The aim of our study is to evaluate the influence of patient age on functional and radiological outcomes following anterior surgical treatment of lower cervical rotational spine injuries. Material and Methods: It is a retrospective study including 38 patients treated surgically via an anterior approach for lower cervical spine rotational injuries from 2015 to 2022. Patients were divided into two age groups: • Group A included patients under 50 years, • Group B included patients older than 50 years. Functional outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS), Neck Disability Index (NDI), and cervical spine mobility. Radiological outcomes were evaluated by the Bridwell fusion score and the different radiological measurements at the last follow up. Results: Group A included 23 patients. Their average age was 31.96 years (range from 18 to 58), with male predominance (82.6%). The mean BMI was 24.56 kg/m2 . Two patients are diabetic. Unilateral facet fracture was noted in 10 cases (43.5%). The mean of follow up was 41.13 months. Group B included 15 patients. Their average age was 61.20 years (range from 52 to 76). All patients are male. The mean BMI was 26.39 kg/m2 . Diabetes was noted in 26.7% of cases. According to Argenson classification, unilateral facet fracture was noted in 5 cases (33.3%), unilateral facet dislocation in 5 cases and fracture-separation of the articular mass in 5 cases. The average of follow-up was 40.36 months. Patients of group A have better range of motion of rotation and inclination (Right rotation (64.26° vs 57.07°, p = 0.03), (Left rotation (64.13° vs 52.40°, p = 0.04), (Right inclination (35.87° vs 24.47°, p = 0.001), (Left inclination (35.87° vs 24.40°, p = 0.001). However, we didn’t found any difference in flexion (p = 0.83), extension (p = 0.13) and NDI score (p = 0.19). VAS scores were lower in group A (2 vs 2.8, p = 0.042), indicating reduced postoperative pain. Regarding the radiological results, the Bridwell fusion score was significantly better in the younger group (1.39 vs 1.87, p = 0.03). when compare group A to group B, we noticed a statistically significant difference in Cobb angle (4.7° vs 10°; p = 0.001) and segmental rotation angle (5.47° vs 9.06; p = 0.02). We couldn’t find any statistically significant difference in others parameters: Anthelesthesis (1.13 mm vs 1.1 8mm; p = 0.09), Cobb angle C2-C7 (24.17° vs 27.6°; p = 0.31) and absolute rotation angle (7.39° vs 6.8°; p = 0.76). Conclusion: Patients under 50 years of age exhibit superior functional and radiological outcomes following anterior surgical treatment of lower cervical rotational injuries. These findings suggest age as a relevant prognostic factor.

## ID: AGS26/1329

## P0965: Effect of Body Mass Index on postoperative outcomes following anterior surgical management of lower cervical rotational spine injuries

Raboudi Hamza

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Dhiaeddine Baccouch

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Mohamed Hassine

1

, Abdenasser Ben Salem

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Body mass index (BMI) is often considered a potential risk factor for poor postoperative outcomes in spine surgery. However, its actual influence on clinical and radiological results remains debated, particularly in cases of cervical spine trauma. The aim of our study is to assess the impact of BMI on functional, clinical, and radiological outcomes following anterior surgical treatment of rotational injuries of the lower cervical spine. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study including 38 patients surgically treated via an anterior approach for rotational fractures of the lower cervical spine between January 2015 and December 2022. Patients were divided into two groups based on BMI: Group A: Normal weight (BMI < 25), Group B: Overweight/obese (BMI ≥ 25). Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analog Scale (VAS), Neck Disability Index (NDI), and cervical spine mobility parameters. Radiological outcomes were evaluated using the Bridwell fusion score and the different radiological measurements at the last follow up. Results: Group A included 16 patients. Their average age was 33.5 years, with male predominance (81.3%). The mean BMI was 31.51 kg/m2. Two patients are diabetic. Unilateral facet fracture and unilateral facet dislocation were noted in 6 cases each. The mean of follow up was 41.13 months. Group B included 15 patients. Their average age was 50.77 years with male predominance (95.5%). The mean BMI was 26.57 kg/m2 . Diabetes was noted in 13.6% of cases. According to Argenson classification, unilateral facet fracture was the most reported (40.9% of cases). The average of follow-up was 40 months. No statistically significant differences were observed between the two groups across all parameters: • VAS (2.19 vs 2.41, p = 0.56), • NDI (10.88 vs 11.59, p = 0.78), • Cervical flexion, extension, rotation, and lateral inclination (all p > 0.2), • Bridwell fusion score (1.56 vs 1.59, p = 0.90). Regarding the radiological results, we didn’t found a statistically significant difference for all radiological measurements: Anthelesthesis (p = 0.07), Cobb angle (p = 0.85), absolute rotation angle (p = 0.36) and segmental rotation angle (p = 0.9). Conclusion: In patients undergoing anterior surgical management of lower cervical rotational spine injuries, BMI does not appear to significantly influence functional recovery, pain levels, or fusion quality. These findings support that overweight and obese patients can achieve comparable outcomes to those with normal BMI in this specific surgical context.

## ID: AGS26/1363

## P0966: Influence of fracture pattern on functional and radiological outcomes after anterior surgical management of lower cervical rotational injuries

Dhiaeddine Baccouch

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Hamza Daly

1

, Yassine Aloui

1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Rotational injuries of the lower cervical spine may present in various anatomical patterns, including unilateral facet dislocation (UFD), fracture-separation of the articular mass (FSAM), and unilateral facet fracture (UFF). Identifying the prognostic value of these fracture types is essential for surgical planning and outcome prediction. The aim of our study is to compare clinical and radiological outcomes following anterior surgical treatment based on fracture type: UFD, FSAM, and UFF. Material and Methods: It is a retrospective study including 38 patients who underwent anterior cervical spine surgery between 2015 and 2022 for rotational lower cervical spine injuries. Patients were categorized into three groups: • Group A: included 13 patients who had operated for Unilateral Facet Dislocation, • Group B: included 10 patients who had operated for Fracture-Separation of the Articular Mass, • Group C: included 15 patients who had operated for Unilateral Facet Fracture. Functional and clinical results were evaluated by the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Neck Disability Index (NDI) and cervical range of motion (flexion, extension, rotation, inclination). Radiological outcomes were evaluated using the Bridwell fusion score and the different radiological measurements at the last follow up. Results: The mean age was 42.54 years in group A, 41.3 years in group B and 45.80 years in group C. We noted a male predominance in all group. The mean follow-up was 15.82 months. Patients of group C have significantly better cervical mobility and functional outcomes compared to patients in group A: Right rotation (65.47° vs 56.77°, p = 0.03) and left rotation (65° vs 54.31°, p = 0.02). Furthermore, functional and clinical outcomes and spinal mobility are better at the last follow-up in patients of group B compared to patients in group A. The difference is not statistically significant. Spinal extension (47.6 vs 38.4 p = 0.03) and NDI score (8.13 vs 15.9 p = 0.008) were better in patients in group C compared with patient in group B, with a statistically significant difference. Regarding radiological results, we found a statistically significant difference between patient in group A and B only for the regional Cobb angle (8.63 vs 4.22 p = 0.02). Furthermore, we did not find a significant difference between the various radiological parameters between patients in group A and C except for the regional Cobb angle (8.63 vs 5.05 p = 0.05). No statistically significant difference was found between patients in groups B and C with regard to the various radiological parameters at the last follow-up. Conclusion: Unilateral facet fractures (UFF) are associated with significantly better functional recovery and cervical mobility compared to other fracture types following anterior surgical treatment. Fracture morphology appears to be an important prognostic factor and may assist in tailoring postoperative management.

## ID: AGS26/1388

## P0967: Urgent surgery in subaxial cervical spinal cord injury

Rafael Luque

1

, Alicia Collado Gosalvez

1

, Angela Carrascosa

1

, Jose Luis Perez Gonzalez

1

, Eduardo Gómez

1

, Ignacio Dominguez

1

1

Clínico San Carlos Hospital, Madrid, Spain

To evaluate the clinical characteristics, treatment, and functional evolution of patients with subaxial cervical spinal cord injury treated in our hospital over the course of one year. Prognostic factors, the type of injury according to the ASIA classification, the therapeutic approaches used, and short- and medium-term functional outcomes are analyzed. Material and Methods: A prospective and observational study was conducted in patients with traumatic subaxial cervical spinal cord injury admitted to the Department of Trauma Surgery of our hospital in 2024, with a minimum follow-up of 6 months. Patients with a confirmed diagnosis through clinical evaluation and imaging studies (X-ray, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging) were included. Epidemiological data, mechanism of injury, neurological level, and ASIA classification at admission and discharge were recorded. The treatment performed, length of hospital stay, and associated complications were documented. Functional evolution was assessed using the ASIA Scale and Frankel scale during the preoperative follow-up, at hospital discharge, and during follow-up at the Reference Center for spinal cord injury. Results : Five patients were included, with a median age of 43 years (IQR 23-74); 60% were women. The injury mechanism was high-energy traffic accidents in 80% of cases. Radiologically, 60% presented a type C lesion according to the AOSpine classification, 20% type B3, and 20% type B2. The Allen and Ferguson classification in the series showed a predominance of extension-distraction mechanism (40%). The ASIA scale at admission showed 1 case of grade A (20%), 2 cases of grade B (40%), and 2 cases of grade C (40%). In all patients, urgent anterior fixation (cage and plated screws) was performed within the first 24 hours (< 12 h in 80% of cases) and posterior fixation (lateral mass or pedicle screws) was performed either urgently (20%) or in a second stage (80%), with a median of 10 days. Sixty percent of the patients showed neurological improvement at discharge. Conclusions: Acute spinal cord injury is a devastating and highly disabling condition. There is evidence in the literature that early decompression surgery within 24 hours after spinal cord injury is associated with neurological improvement, especially in incomplete injuries, with potential recovery of up to 2 ASIA levels.

## ID: AGS26/1560

## P0968: New algorithm for managing cranio-cervical dissociation: narrative review and case series

Juan José Zamorano

1

, José Gengler Prieto

1

, Esteban Peña

1

, Ignacio Cirillo

1

, Marcos Gimbernat

1

, Ignacio Farías

1

, Carlos Tapia

1

, sebastian blanco gruebler

1

1

Hospital del Trabajador, Santiago, Chile

Introduction: The occipitocervical junction represents a critical region for craniospinal stability, supported by osteoligamentous structures whose disruption may immediately endanger the patient’s life. Occipitocervical dissociation (OCD) is a rare injury resulting from high-energy trauma, often overlooked during the initial evaluation and associated with severe neurological and vascular complications. Despite advances in imaging, diagnosis remains a clinical challenge, underscoring the need for standardized diagnostic and therapeutic strategies. This study reviews the literature and the cases managed at our center, proposing a comprehensive management algorithm based on clinical and imaging findings. Material and Methods: A narrative review was conducted in PubMed, Cochrane, and Google Scholar, including experimental, observational, and case reports published between 1990 and 2024. Mechanisms of injury, imaging parameters, classifications, and therapeutic modalities were analyzed. In parallel, a retrospective review of nine surgically treated patients with OCD at our institution was performed, recording clinical characteristics, imaging findings, surgical techniques employed, and clinical outcomes at six months. Based on this synthesis, a diagnostic-therapeutic algorithm was developed. Results: The literature search identified 213 titles, of which 28 relevant articles were selected. In our institutional series, the patients had a mean age of 28.8 years, predominantly male (7/9), with high-energy trauma mechanisms, mainly traffic accidents. All presented associated injuries, including traumatic brain injury and vascular damage in two cases. The surgical approach involved posterior instrumented fixation and occipitocervical arthrodesis, achieving radiological consolidation in all patients at six months. The proposed algorithm begins with computed tomography to assess Harris lines and the C0-C1 interval. A positive finding indicates OCD and mandates surgical management after CT-angiography. In negative cases, secondary findings such as occipital condyle avulsion or pre-/retro-odontoid hematoma are evaluated. If present, magnetic resonance imaging is indicated. Findings of C0-C1 synovitis, tectorial membrane disruption, posterior ligamentous complex injury, or soft-tissue hematoma/edema support surgical management, whereas their absence favors conservative orthopedic treatment and follow-up. This stepwise approach integrates clinical and radiological parameters, guiding safe and reproducible therapeutic decisions. Conclusion: Although infrequent, occipitocervical dissociation constitutes a life-threatening emergency in which prognosis depends on early diagnosis and prompt surgical treatment. The combination of validated imaging parameters within a structured algorithm enhances diagnostic accuracy and guides therapeutic decisions, minimizing the risk of neurological deterioration and favoring bone consolidation. Our clinical experience supports the application of a standardized diagnostic-therapeutic algorithm as an effective tool to optimize the management of these complex injuries in contemporary spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1567

## P0969: Anterior fixation in type ii odontoid fractures and a rare three-part fracture of the axis

Berkay Ayhan

1

1

Ankara Education and Research Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: Type II odontoid fractures are common upper cervical injuries that often require surgery to ensure union and stability. Anterior odontoid screw fixation provides direct stabilization while preserving the C1-2 motion segment and normal atlantoaxial rotation. In contrast, posterior C1-2 fusion, although effective for stability, can eliminate ∼50% of cervical rotation. We present a single-center retrospective series of 14 patients (Jan 2015-May 2024) with Type II odontoid fractures treated by anterior screw fixation, reporting clinical and radiological outcomes, complication rates and cervical rotational range of motion. The series includes a unique case of a “three-part fracture of the axis” (combined Type II odontoid + Hangman fracture), a rare injury (∼1% incidence) successfully managed in a single session with combined anterior and posterior fixation, while preserving cervical rotational motion. Material and Methods: Fourteen patients with Type II odontoid fractures were retrospectively reviewed. Clinical outcomes (neurologic status, complications, range of motion) and fracture union on imaging were assessed at final follow-up. All patients underwent anterior odontoid screw osteosynthesis. In one patient with a combined odontoid + Hangman’s fracture, a supplemental posterior C2 pedicle screw fixation with C2-3 wiring was performed in the same session. Postoperative cervical rotational range of motion was assessed clinically in degrees at a minimum of 6 months follow-up, once fracture healing was confirmed. Any perioperative complications were documented. Results: All patients achieved fracture union. All were neurologically intact at final follow-up, and most reported excellent neck function with minimal pain. Mean postoperative total cervical rotation was 158 ± 11°, indicating near-normal C1-2 mobility and reflecting ∼80-90% preservation of atlantoaxial rotation as reported in the literature. Complications were minimal: no neurovascular injuries occurred, and one patient had transient dysphagia that resolved spontaneously. In the three-part fracture case, the combined anterior-posterior approach achieved solid fusion and similarly preserved cervical rotation. This demonstrates that a one-stage motion-preserving fixation can successfully stabilize even complex axis fractures. Conclusion: Anterior odontoid screw fixation in this series yielded a high fusion rate and favorable outcomes while preserving cervical rotation. Even in a rare complex three-part fracture of the axis, a motion-preserving surgical strategy was feasible and led to good functional recovery. Therefore, when not contraindicated, anterior fixation should be considered the preferred approach for Type II odontoid fractures due to its clear advantage in maintaining atlantoaxial motion.

## ID: AGS26/1604

## P0970: Efficacy, safety and reliability of surgery by single anterior approach in neglected, lately presented sub-axial cervical spine injury

Shah Alam

1

1

Bangladesh Spine and Orthopedic Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: The optimal method for the management of neglected traumatic fracture dislocation of the subaxial cervical spine has not been well established. Management of a patient who presents late, more than three weeks after the initial injury, is challenging and differs from that of the patient who is seen early. 3 established surgical approach have been practiced in cervical spine injuries: 1. Anterior, 2. posterior, and 3. combined Anterior - posterior. Due to complex presentation of injury morphology and locked facet in late and neglected cases, most of the surgeon practice Combined approach in surgical management of such cases. Our country is resource constrained and here cost effectiveness plays a big role in decision-making. So, we need a method which is cost-effective, can be done with fewer implants and is safe and effective. In this case series, we performed a single Approach to correct dislocation and decompression of spinal cord and outcome was measured scientifically. Material and Methods: It is a prospective interventional study done in Our tertiary care centers. Study period: 10 years. Sample size 65. Cases operated at least 21 days after injury were included. Surgery was done by single anterior approach (ACDF). Using Stand-alone cage or Tricortical bone graft and anterior cervical plates and screws. Outcome analysis done by ASIA grading, VAS, and ODI scoring. Bridwell fusion grading was used to assess interbody fusion. Minimum follow-up period was 6 months. Results: Total sample size was 195. Age range was 21-55 (44.53 ± 12.5). 86.7% male (n = 168). The most common mechanism of injury fell from height 46.7% (n = 90); the most commonly injured level was C 5/6, 60% (n = 117). Average preoperative delay was 25 (±4) days. 66% (n = 126) patients had 1 grade ASIA impairment score (AIS) improvement, 20% (n = 39) had 2 AIS grade improvement, and 13% (n = 15) patients had no improvement of the postoperative ASIA score. Postoperative VAS and Macnab scores improved significantly. 80% (n = 156) patients achieved grade 1 fusion according to Bridwell fusion grading. 2 Patient had post operative dysphagia 1 patient had neck pain, No patient had any post-op neurological deterioration. Conclusion: Late and neglected cases of cervical spine injury can be managed by only anterior surgery with significantly good neurological and functional outcomes. Specially in a resource-constrained and poor patient-loaded country like us, this method is effective to reduce the cost of surgery and post-surgical morbidity of combined anterior and posterior surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1739

## P0971: Epidemiology of traumatic cervical spine fracture at tertiary level trauma center in Nepal

Gyanendra Shah

1

, Prajwala Yogi

2

, Saroj G C

3

, Bishal Pradhan

4

, Chunauti Bahik

5

, Aliska Niroula

6

1

Department of Orthopaedics, National Trauma Center, Kathmandu, Nepal

2

Department of Research and Development, National Academy of Medical Sciences, Bir Hospital, Kathmandu, Nepal

3

Kakani Primary Health Center, Nuwakot, Kakani, Nepal

4

Nepalese Army Institute of Health Sciences, Kathmandu, Nepal

5
Patan Academy of Health Sciences, Lalitpur, Nepal, 
6
Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Sheffield, United Kingdom
Introduction: The cervical spine usually protects the spinal cord; thus, injury to the cervical spine may lead to paralysis, damage, and is considered a medical emergency condition that needs an appropriate response to prevent further injury. Traumatic cervical spine fracture outcome is different based on poor road conditions and challenging geographical terrain, road traffic accidents, and falls in developing countries. Along with placing a heavy socioeconomic load on families and nations, it also contributes to high rates of mortality and morbidity. Timely diagnosis and management of the patients can affect the overall outcome. Similarly, there is a shift in the trend of presentation of traumatic cervical spine fractures in young individuals to the late 40s population in Western countries, which might be changed in developing countries. However, the epidemiology of cervical spine fractures is less well documented. The availability of these data can aid in building protocols for reducing and managing disease burden. The primary objective of our study is to evaluate the epidemiology of traumatic cervical spine fractures in patients presenting to a tertiary-level trauma hospital in Nepal. Material and Methods: The data for this descriptive cross-sectional study were gathered retrospectively from the National Trauma Centre's records of patients who visited the trauma center between December 2019 and December 2024, a span of five years. All cases of traumatic cervical spine fractures have been included in this study. The records with incomplete or missing data were excluded from the study. Demographics, the type and extent of the injury, the admission outcome, and the involvement of neurology are all covered. Results: We have registered 727 patients with traumatic cervical spine fractures among the patients presented. The mean age of the patient was 47.18 years, with the majority falling within the age group 40-59 years, 325 (44.70%) patients. The male and female comprise 555 (76.43%) and 172 (23.65%), respectively. The most common mechanism of injury was falling injury 467 (64.23%), followed by road traffic accidents 80 (11%). The most frequently affected cervical level was C5-C6. Among the patients admitted to the trauma center, patients who recovered were 532 (73.17%), and deaths were 40 (5.52%). Of the total patients, 106 (14.66%) were admitted to the ICU. The average length of hospital stays for the patients admitted in ICU was 20.26 days. Conclusion: Patients with cervical traumatic damage are more likely to be male, and most cases involve people between the ages of 40 and 60. Fall injuries are the most frequent source of injuries, which is in line with results from other research. After suffering catastrophic cervical spine fractures, almost two-third of patients healed. These findings highlight the need for preventive strategies, improved trauma care protocols, and early intervention to reduce morbidity and mortality in resource limited settings.

## ID: AGS26/1829

## P0972: Enhancing accuracy and confidence in cervical pedicle screw insertion: a case series on intraoperative Doppler use

Sun Geon Yoon

1

1

Ulsan University Hospital, Ulsan, South Korea

Introduction: In cases of traumatic cervical spine injury requiring surgical stabilization, cervical pedicle screw (CPS) fixation can be considered as a method to achieve strong fixation. CPSs are known to provide approximately three to four times greater pull-out strength compared to lateral mass screws; however, due to technical difficulty and the risk of vertebral artery or spinal cord injury, their use has been limited. Intraoperative computed tomography-based navigation facilitates CPS insertion, but such equipment remains available only in a limited number of institutions due to its high cost. Hong et al. previously reported that the use of intraoperative Doppler ultrasonography could improve the accuracy of CPS insertion. In this study, we sought to evaluate whether the use of intraoperative Doppler during posterior fixation in patients with traumatic cervical spine injury influences operative time, estimated blood loss, accuracy of pedicle screw placement, and insertion angle. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients who underwent surgery for cervical spine trauma at our institution between January 2019 and August 2025. A total of 196 patients were initially identified. Patients who underwent occipitocervical fusion, combined anterior-posterior fixation, extended fixation below T3, laminoplasty, or anterior cervical procedures alone were excluded. Fifty-nine patients remained for analysis. Demographic and surgical variables including age, sex, number of fused levels, number of laminectomy levels, operative time, estimated blood loss (EBL), screw insertion angle, and pedicle screw accuracy according to the Gertzbein-Robbins classification - Breadow modification for the cervical spine were analyzed to evaluate the impact of intraoperative Doppler use. Results: After covariate adjustment for age, sex, fused levels, and laminectomy levels, intraoperative Doppler use was associated with a significantly shorter operative time (β = -87.01 minutes; 95% CI, -125.17 to -48.86; p < 0.001) and reduced log-transformed EBL (β = -0.78; 95% CI, -1.34 to -0.22; p = 0.006). While raw-scale EBL showed only a borderline reduction (β = -516.17 mL; 95% CI, -1050.85 to 18.50; p = 0.059), Doppler monitoring markedly increased the odds of accurate screw placement (OR = 20.28; 95% CI, 6.10-67.44; p < 0.001). Analysis of screw insertion angles demonstrated a trend toward more favorable trajectories in the Doppler group (β = 3.82 degrees; 95% CI, -0.07 to 7.72; p = 0.054), although this did not reach statistical significance. Conclusion: Intraoperative Doppler monitoring significantly improved the safety and efficiency of cervical pedicle screw insertion in traumatic cervical spine surgery. Its use was associated with shorter operative times, reduced blood loss, and markedly higher accuracy of screw placement, with a trend toward more favorable insertion angles. Given its relative simplicity and accessibility compared with intraoperative navigation systems, Doppler monitoring may serve as a valuable adjunct to enhance the safety and precision of CPS fixation in cervical spine trauma.

## ID: AGS26/2493

## P0973: Mortality following surgical management of traumatic sub-axial cervical spine injuries in a South African tertiary centre

Khangelani Sondovana

1

, Alberto Puddu

1

, Piet Mare

1

1

University of KwaZulu Natal, Grey's Hospital, Pietermaritzburg, South Africa

Introduction: Traumatic sub-axial cervical spine injuries frequently result in spinal cord injury and significant mortality and morbidity. These outcomes are particularly severe in low- and middle-income countries (LMIC), where access to healthcare resources is limited and local survival data are scarce. This study aims to evaluate the mortality of adult patients undergoing surgical intervention for traumatic sub-axial cervical spine injuries in a South African tertiary centre. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective observational study of adult patients who underwent surgery for sub-axial cervical spine injuries between 2018 and 2022. Mortality data were collected using the South African Medical Research Council death registry. Primary outcomes were 30-day mortality and medium-term mortality. Multivariate logistic regression was performed to determine factors independently associated with 30-day mortality, and Cox proportional hazard analysis assessed medium-term survival predictors. Results: Eighty-six patients were included. The medium-term mortality rate was 19.8% (17/86) at a mean follow-up of 4 years and 6 months (SD 14 months). The 30-day mortality rate was 12% (10/86). Univariate logistic regression identified spinal shock, ASIA grade on arrival, Injury Severity Score (ISS), and lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI) as significant factors associated with 30-day mortality. In a multivariate logistic regression analysis, the ASIA grade was the only independent factor associated with 30-day mortality (OR 0.34, 95%CI 0.12-0.995, p = 0.049). Similarly, Cox proportional hazard analysis showed that ASIA grade was the sole independent factor associated with medium-term mortality (OR 0.38, 95%CI 0.19-0.73, p = 0.004). Respiratory failure was the cause of death in all but one case. Among patients with complete spinal cord injury (ASIA A), 30-day survival was 60% (12/20), and medium-term survival was 35% (7/20). Conclusion: The ASIA neurological injury grade was the most significant factor associated with survival in patients undergoing surgery for traumatic cervical spine injuries. A high mortality rate was observed among patients with complete spinal cord injury in both the short- and medium-term. Nonetheless, a significant number of patients with complete neurological injury survive the first year post-injury, underscoring the need for ongoing multidisciplinary support to optimize their quality of life.
Keywords: Sub-axial cervical spine injury, spinal cord injury, ASIA grade, mortality, South Africa

## ID: AGS26/2543

## P0974: Posterior cervical fixation preserving semispinalis cervicis muscle

Kaoru Eguchi

1

1

Nagoya Medical Center, Nagoya University, Nagoya, Japan

Introduction: C2 hangman fracture is a cervical spine fracture that mainly occurs due to trauma involving hyperextension. Depending on the extent of the fracture, anterior or posterior cervical fixation surgery may be necessary. The semispinalis cervicis muscle is one of the strong posterior cervical support tissues, and there are reports of cervical laminoplasty and C2 pedicle screws that preserve this area. We report a case performed by posterior cervical fixation surgery, preserving the semispinalis cervicis muscle, for C1-3 fixation in a patient with a C2 hangman fracture. Material and Methods: The patient is a 30-year-old female. She sustained injuries when the car overturned in a traffic accident while driving. She was transported to our hospital. Cervical CT scan revealed a C2 hangman's fracture (Effendi type I). After hospitalization and observation, she was fitted with a rigid cervical collar and discharged. Conservative treatment was continued. Two months post-injury, non-union of the fracture site and worsening cervical alignment, along with severe neck pain, were observed. C1-3 posterior fixation surgery under general anesthesia was performed using navigation guidance. The semispinalis cervicis muscle attached to C2 was preserved, and an oblique screw insertion through the lateral side of the semispinalis cervicis at C3, targeting the C2-3 joint area and C3 pedicle screw entry point, was safely performed. Postoperative progress was favorable, and she was discharged home a week post-surgery. Postoperative CT scan showed that the C3 pedicle screws were well inserted from the lateral side of the semispinalis cervicis, and no signs of muscle atrophy or other abnormalities were observed one year post-surgery. Bonny fusion at the fracture site was confirmed, so implant removal surgery was performed. Post-surgery, she did not state significant neck pain, her head rotation improved, and even months later, the semispinalis cervicis was well preserved. Results: There are some reports of inserting C2 pedicle screws and performing cervical laminoplasty while preserving the semispinalis cervicis muscle, but there are few reports regarding pedicle screw insertion at C3-4. In this case, we were able to achieve good accuracy by attaching a reference arm to the C2 spinous process and performing navigation. Postoperative CT scans showed that the muscles were well preserved, and muscle preservation was still achieved after hardware removal. The surgical method used in this case, which preserves the semispinalis cervicis, can be applied to posterior cervical fixation procedures, including occipito-cervical (O-C) fixation for degenerative diseases or trauma at the craniovertebral junction. However, it is suggested that the semispinalis cervicis muscle extends more laterally at higher levels because making surgical exposure and rod fixation would be challenging for lower cervical levels such as C5-7. Additionally, while attaching the reference arm to the C2 spinous process allowed for good navigation accuracy in this case, future challenges include how to implement navigation when high instability is present at the C2 spinous process. Conclusion: We experienced a case in which posterior C1-3 fixation was performed for a C2 hangman’s fracture while preserving the semispinalis cervicis muscle. Further accumulation of cases is needed to determine the extent to which posterior fixation with preservation of the semispinalis cervicis can be applied to trauma and degenerative diseases.

## ID: AGS26/2603

## P0975: Risk factors of fracture instability and non-union of type II/III odontoid fracture

Doohee Han

1

, baejae Kim

1

, Joohyung Shin

1

, Jong Hyeok Park

1

, Jae-Tack Hong

2

1

Bundang Jesaeng General Hospital, Daejin Medical Center, Seongnam-si, Korea, Rep. of South

2

Eun Pyeong St. Mary's Hospital, The Catholic University of Korea, Seoul, Korea, Rep. of South

Introduction: Odontoid fracture has been known to the second most common fracture of cervical spine. We preferentially give patients with Type II and III odontoid fracture conservative treatment using cervical brace for 6 weeks if possible. After conservative treatments, sound union was not found at CT images. Instead, we used the term of fracture stability was defined as no motion at fracture site is observed on dynamic flexion and extension cervical lateral radiograph. We analyzed the fracture stability of Type II and III odontoid fracture after conservative treatment and union rate of Type II and III odontoid fracture after one year. Material and Methods: Medical records and radiologic data were retrospectively reviewed on patients with Type II and III odontoid fracture at double center from January 2006 to December 2023. Total 54 patients visited hospitals for Type II or III odontoid fractures. 7 patients undergone posterior C1-C2 fusion emergently were excluded because they showed the injury of transverse atlas ligament and neurologic deficit. 8 patients undergone surgical treatment instantly were excluded because they delayed to diagnose odontoid fracture more than 3 months. One patients diagnosed the fracture induced by infection and one patients diagnosed pathologic fracture were excluded. Finally, 37 patients included in this study. Age, sex, body mass density (BMD), Anderson and D’Alonzo classification were evaluated for patients’ risk factors. Sagittal tilt (ST), Sagittal gap (SG), Gap of fracture line (GF), and coronal tilt (CoT) were measured for radiologic risk factors. Results: The mean age was 61.1 years, and 70% of patients were men. Mean follow-up was 25.2 months. Among 37 patients, 11 patients had osteoporosis, and 9 patients was osteopenia. 17 patients had normal BMD. Anderson and D’Alonzo type III was accounted for 59%, Type II was 41%. The fracture stability of Type II/III odontoid fracture was 30% during 6 weeks. The union rate of Type II/III odontoid fracture was 78.4% during one year. Conclusion: Osteoporosis is the most important risk factor for fracture instability and non-union over one year. Either sagittal tilt > 11° or gap >2 mm as radiologic measurements are relative to fracture instability during conservative treatment. No radiologic measurements are not considering as risk factor for non-union of odontoid fracture over one year. Age is a single important risk factor for non-union over one year.

## ID: AGS26/2717

## P0976: Para-vertebral foramen screws in acute cervical trauma: a single-center series highlighting safety and early stability without navigation

Takayuki Awaya

1

, Kenji Uda

1

, Takashi Abe

1

, Kazuki Ishii

1

, Hinako Nagamatsu

1

, Tomotaka Iwakoshi

1

, Osamu Suzuki

1

, Yusuke Nishimura

2

1
Nagoya Ekisaikai Hospital, Nagoya, Japan, 
2
Nagoya University Hospital, Nagoya, Japan
Introduction: Cervical pedicle screws (CPS) provide strong fixation but carry high risk of neurovascular injury, while lateral mass screws (LMS) are safer but mechanically weaker. Para-vertebral foramen screws (PVFS) are designed to combine the advantages of both techniques. Although their biomechanical superiority over LMS has been suggested, clinical evidence in acute cervical trauma remains limited. We aimed to evaluate the safety and early stability of PVFS in the acute trauma setting. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 28 consecutive patients who underwent posterior cervical fixation with PVFS for acute cervical trauma. Twenty patients (121 screws) were available for ≥12 months of follow-up (mean 18 months). Surgical strategy included posterior-only fixation or posterior fixation with additional anterior reconstruction. Outcomes included radiographic screw loosening, symptomatic complications, and need for reoperation. Results: All PVFS were inserted without navigation using fluoroscopy alone. No symptomatic complications, vertebral artery injuries, or reoperations were observed. Radiographic loosening occurred in ∼5% of screws, all within 3 months and only in the posterior-only group; all cases remained asymptomatic. In contrast, no loosening was observed in the anterior add-on group. Compared with historical data on LMS, which demonstrate loosening rates of 10-15%, PVFS provided greater initial stability while maintaining excellent safety even in high-energy trauma. Conclusion: PVFS can be safely and reproducibly inserted without navigation and provide stronger initial stability than LMS. These features make PVFS highly compatible with the acute trauma setting, offering a safe and effective alternative for posterior cervical fixation.

## ID: AGS26/3130

## P0977: Non-operative management of a cervical penetrating spinal cord injury presenting as Brown-Séquard syndrome: a case report

Artur Bastos Rocha

1

, Leonardo Fraga Lima

1

, Eduardo Varjão Vieira

1

, Camila Moura Sousa

1

, Wagner de Azevedo Herculano

1

, Thiago de Oliveira Carneiro

1

, João Pedro Cruz de Souza Monteiro

1

, Ruan Pablo Carmo dos Santos

1

, Iogo Henrique de Oliveira Araújo

1

, Antonio Gutemberg de Souza Martins

1

, Pedro Henrique Velasco Pondé de Sena

1

, Lucas Chaves Lelis

1

, Jaquisson Guimarães Filho

1

, Márcio César de Mello Brandão

1

Hospital Geral Clériston Andrade, Feira de Santana-BA, Brasil

Introduction: Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury (TSCI) caused by penetrating trauma, such as stab wounds, is a less common etiology but the main traumatic cause of Brown-Séquard Syndrome (BSS). BSS is characterized by incomplete hemisection of the spinal cord, resulting in ipsilateral motor paralysis and proprioception loss, along with contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation. It is important to emphasize that BSS has the best ambulatory recovery prognosis among all incomplete spinal cord injuries. This case aims to discuss the successful non-operative management of a cervical zone II penetrating injury, in which initial imaging findings suggested complete cord transection, but clinical progression was consistent with BSS. Material and Methods: A 36-year-old male patient was admitted following an anterior right paramedian cervical stab wound (Zone II). On admission, he presented with severe flaccid tetraparesis. CT angiography excluded major vascular injury, and computed tomography showed no bony instability. Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) revealed a linear, transfixing T2/STIR hyperintensity at C5-C6, with ligamentous discontinuity, suggesting transverse cord transection. Given hemodynamic stability, absence of compressive retained fragments, and preserved biomechanical stability of the spine, a non-operative approach was chosen, including broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy and intensive supportive care. Results: Conservative treatment led to asymmetric and progressive neurological improvement that began early, highlighting the superiority of clinical evaluation over initial imaging findings. During outpatient follow-up, the patient evolved into a pattern consistent with BSS, presenting with disproportionate and asymmetric tetraparesis, significant motor recovery in the left hemibody (C5-C7 5/5), and contralateral sensory improvement on the right side. Lower limb examination revealed motor strength up to 3/5 for hip and knee flexion, indicating movement against gravity. Clinically, the patient achieved resolution of post-traumatic neurogenic bladder and regained sphincter control. Conclusion: The success of the non-operative management is validated by the recovery of bladder control and motor function, reinforcing that diagnosis and prognosis in incomplete injuries should be guided by serial neurological examinations and the spinal cord’s intrinsic recovery potential. The main lesson lies in exercising caution when interpreting MRI in penetrating injuries and emphasizing a conservative approach, which ensures the best functional outcomes in well-selected patients.

References

1. Yildirim AE, Divanlioglu D, Cetinalp NE, Dalgic A. Brown-Séquard syndrome associated with a spinal cord injury caused by a retained screwdriver: a case report. Surg Neurol Int. 2022;13:223.
2. De la Cruz R, Nogueira-Neto E, Rivas JJ, Martinez-Ramos A. Brown-Séquard syndrome without vascular injury associated with Horner's syndrome after a stab injury to the neck. Spinal Cord Ser Cases. 2015;1:15007.
3. Segar DJ, Caruso JP, Chang YF, Sundararajan S, Miele VJ. Traumatic Brown-Séquard syndrome: modern reminder of a neurological injury. Spinal Cord Ser Cases. 2020;6:61.
4. Bono CM, Heary RF. Management of Penetrating Injuries to the Spine. In: Vaccaro AR, ed. Spine Trauma. Philadelphia: Clinical Gate; 2015.
5. Van Toorn R, Basson J, Fieggen G. Imaging findings of penetrating spinal cord injuries secondary to stab wounds on magnetic resonance imaging in a tertiary trauma unit, South Africa. S Afr J Radiol. 2019;23(1):1674.

## ID: AGS26/3149

## P0978: Gunshot wound with projectile lodged at C1-C2 level submitted to bullet removal without neurological deficits: case report

Artur Bastos Rocha

1

, Leonardo Fraga Lima

1

, Eduardo Varjão Vieira

1

, Camila Moura Sousa

1

, Wagner de Azevedo Herculano

1

, Thiago de Oliveira Carneiro

1

, João Pedro Cruz de Souza Monteiro

1

, Ruan Pablo Carmo dos Santos

1

, Iogo Henrique de Oliveira Araújo

1

, Antonio Gutemberg de Souza Martins

1

, Pedro Henrique Velasco Pondé de Sena

1

, Lucas Chaves Lelis

1

, Jaquisson Guimarães Filho

1

, Márcio César de Mello Brandão

1

1

Hospital Geral Clériston Andrade, Feira de Santana-BA, Brasil

Introduction: Gunshot wounds (GSWs) are a major public health issue, most often affecting young men, and account for 14.3% of spinal cord injuries. When involving the spine and spinal cord, they frequently result in severe neurological deficits, with most patients classified as ASIA (American Spinal Injury Association Impairment Scale) grade A. Complete tetraplegia occurs in about 12.4%, while minimal or no deficits are observed in only 0.6%. High cervical injuries are the least common (25.6%) but represent the most severe presentations. This report highlights the rarity of the lesion site and bullet location, with the patient classified as ASIA-E and demonstrating excellent postoperative recovery. Material and Methods: A case report of a 29-year-old male sustained a gunshot wound to the cervical region and right upper limb (RUL). On admission, he was conscious (Glasgow 15), hemodynamically stable, with isochoric and photoreactive pupils. Wearing a cervical collar, he presented monoparesis and hypoesthesia in the RUL, with preserved motor and sensory function in other limbs. Initial radiography revealed a retained projectile in the occipital region, with an entry wound in posterior cervical zone III. Non-contrast cervical computed tomography showed metallic artifacts at the occipital bone with an adjacent fracture near the foramen magnum. A projectile was identified within the spinal canal at C1-C2, associated with a comminuted and misaligned fracture of the posterior arch of C1, along with a second projectile in posterior soft tissues near the left posterior arch of C1. Cervical CT angiography confirmed these findings and ruled out injury to major cervical vessels. Neurological status was graded as ASIA-E. Results: The patient underwent elective posterior C1-C2 arthrodesis with removal of retained projectiles, without intraoperative complications. Postoperatively, he remained in a cervical collar, with adequate pain control. Neurological evaluation revealed ulnar claw deformity of the right hand, consistent with ulnar nerve motor deficit. Postoperative cervical CT confirmed removal of the intracanalar projectile at C1. The patient remained stable, underwent active kinesiotherapy, ambulation, and sitting, with satisfactory postoperative recovery. Conclusion: High cervical GSWs are rare and usually severe; however, this case demonstrates a favorable outcome following bullet removal and C1-C2 arthrodesis in a patient without major neurological deficits. This report emphasizes the importance of individualized evaluation and timely surgical management to optimize prognosis.

References

1. National Spinal Cord Injury Statistical Center. Spinal cord injury: facts and figures at a glance. Birmingham, AL: University of Alabama; 2021 [cited 2022 Jul]. Available from: “https://medicine.umich.edu/sites/default/files/content/downloads/NSCISCSCIFactsandFigures 2021.pdf”
2. Ryu B, Kim SB, Choi MK, Kim KD. Radiculopathy as delayed presentations of retained spinal bullet. J Korean Neurosurg Soc. 2015;58:393-396. doi: 10.3340/jkns.2015.58.4.393. PMID: 26587197.
3. Morrow KD, Podet AG, Spinelli CP, Lasseigne LM, Crutcher CL, Wilson JD, Tender GC, DiGiorgio AM. A case series of penetrating spinal trauma: comparisons to blunt trauma, surgical indications, and outcomes. Neurosurg Focus. 2019 Mar 1;46(3):E4. doi: 10.3171/2018.12.FOCUS18577. PMID: 30835674.
4. Santangelo G, Wathen C, Pieters T, George DD, Worley L, Macaluso D, Dagli MM, Catanzaro S, Gu B, Welch WC, Petrov D, Vates GE, Ozturk AK, Schuster J, Stone JJ. Multi-institutional review of characteristics and management of gunshot wounds to the spine. Spine J. 2024 Sep;24(9):1553-1560. doi: 10.1016/j.spinee.2024.04.032. PMID: 38740190.
5. Bono CM, Heary RF. Gunshot wounds to the spine. Spine J. 2004 Mar-Apr;4(2):230-40. doi: 10.1016/S1529-9430(0300178-5). PMID: 15016402.
