## ID: AGS26/3156

## P0979: Acute traumatic sub-axial central cord syndrome: can delaying surgery causes disaster?

Sharif Jonayed

1

1

National Institute of Traumatology & Orthopaedic Rehabilitation(NITOR), Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Traumatic central cord syndrome (TCCS) is an incomplete spinal cord injury defined by greater weakness in upper versus lower extremities, variable sensory loss, and variable bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction. The optimal timing of surgery for TCCS remains controversial. The purpose of this study was to evaluate whether timing of surgery for TCCS predicts neurological outcomes, length of stay, and complications. Material and Methods: Forty-two patients with TCCS without fracture or dislocation were identified and divided into two groups described as two groups i.e. early surgery and delayed surgery group. Clinical outcomes including ASIA Motor Score (AMS), Japanese Orthopaedic Association (JOA) score upon admission and follow-up, change in AMS, and JOA recovery rate were analyzed. Logistic regression analysis was performed to show the correlation between timing of surgery and clinical outcomes. Results: All patients received a minimum of 1-year follow-up and showed significant neurological recovery at the final follow-up. No statistical differences in final AMS and JOA scores were observed between the two groups. There is insufficient evidence that lengths of hospital or intensive care unit stay differ between patients who undergo early versus delayed surgery. Furthermore, there is insufficient evidence that timing between injury and surgery predicts mortality rates or serious or minor adverse events. Conclusion: Surgery for TCCS ,24 hours after injury appears safe and effective. Although there is insufficient evidence to provide a clear recommendation for Early surgery (24 hours), it is preferable to operate during the first hospital admission and within 2 weeks after injury in selected patients.

## ID: AGS26/3257

## P0980: Safety and outcomes of Goel-Harms dorsal stabilization in sitting position for dens fractures without navigation

Senol Elmas

1

, Adelina Fjerza

1

, Veit Rohde

1

, Tammam Abboud

1

, Bilal Younes

1

1

University Medical Center Göttingen, Göttingen, Germany

Introduction: Fractures of odontoid account for about 10-15% of all cervical spine fractures. Type II fractures are unstable and carry a morbidity and mortality rate of roughly 6%. Numerous techniques exist for C1/C2 stabilization, but the goal of this paper is to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of a modified Goel-Harms procedure performed freehand under lateral fluoroscopy in the sitting position. Material and Methods: This retrospective study describes 161 consecutive patients between 2014 and 2023 who underwent C1-C2 fixation using a modified Goel-Harms technique in the sitting position, without navigation. Postoperative CT scans confirmed screw positioning. Screw placement was graded according to Gertzbein and Robbins. Surgery-related complications were recorded. Bony fusion was evaluated at 3, 6, and 12 months. Results: A total of 161 patients (72 men [44.7%], 89 women [55.3%]; mean age 77 years) underwent surgery with 644 screws placed. Seven patients with a persistent foramen ovale were operated on in the prone position and were therefore excluded. In 53 cases (32.9%), extension to C3 was performed due to associated pars or pedicle fractures. 624 screws (96.8%) were Grade A and B, 16 (2.4%) Grade C, and 4 (0.6%) Grade D and E. Only these four screws required revision for misplacement. Dorsal decompression with a C1 laminectomy was performed in 21 patients (13%), and 6 (3.7%) underwent revision for wound healing disorder. Four C2 screws breached the vertebral artery canal but did not compress the artery (0,6%). Two patients experienced vertebral artery injuries with total occlusion on CTA due to screw misplacement (0,3%), however without resulting neurological deficits and therefore the screws were not revised. Average surgical duration was 165.8 minutes (median blood loss 120 ml). None of the patients experienced an intraoperative air embolism or developed new neurological deficits. Direct after surgery, 116 patients (72.0%) reported neck pain and 10 (6.2%) radicular pain. The pain rate and intensity improved over time, but we did not have a reported pain scale to quantify the changes. Fusion rates reached 29.8% at 3 months and 62.1% at 6 months, though only 27 patients (16.8%) returned for a one-year follow-up. Conclusion: The modified Goel-Harms C1-C2 dorsal fixation performed in the sitting position under lateral fluoroscopy without navigation is a safe and straightforward technique with low complication rates. It should be considered a viable option in the surgical treatment of C1-C2 fractures. The surgeon's experience plays a critical role in the success of this procedure.

## ID: AGS26/3258

## P0981: Cervical spine instability in high-energy trauma: biomechanical considerations for posterior fixation - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: High-energy cervical spine trauma can result in complex instability patterns, including fractures, dislocations, and ligamentous injuries. Posterior fixation is often necessary to restore stability, but careful consideration of biomechanical principles is critical to optimize construct strength and minimize complications. Material and Methods: A systematic review of PubMed and Google Scholar was conducted using the keywords: “cervical spine trauma,” “posterior fixation,” “biomechanics,” “instability,” and “pedicle screw.” Included studies encompassed cadaveric, in vitro, finite element, and clinical analyses evaluating cervical spine stability, fixation techniques, and construct performance in traumatic settings. Results: • High-energy trauma frequently produces multilevel ligamentous and osseous injuries, increasing motion and stress at affected segments. • Pedicle screws provide superior stability compared to lateral mass screws, particularly under rotational and lateral bending loads. • Short-segment fixation may be inadequate in certain fracture patterns, whereas long-segment constructs improve overall stability but reduce segmental mobility. • Accurate screw placement, trajectory planning, and vertebral morphology considerations are essential to prevent neurovascular injury and construct failure. • Intraoperative navigation and imaging significantly enhance placement accuracy and safety. Conclusion: Cervical spine instability after high-energy trauma poses considerable biomechanical challenges. Posterior fixation strategies must be individualized based on injury pattern, vertebral morphology, and load distribution to optimize stability while minimizing complications. Further clinical and biomechanical studies are needed to refine fixation techniques and improve outcomes in this high-risk population.

## ID: AGS26/3267

## P0982: Role of emergency physicians in initial triage of high-risk cervical spine trauma: clinical-imaging concordance - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

1
Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, 
2
Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia
Introduction: Cervical spine injuries in high-energy trauma present a significant risk for morbidity and mortality. Emergency physicians often rely on clinical decision rules such as NEXUS and the Canadian C-Spine Rule to triage patients and determine the need for imaging. Understanding the concordance between these clinical assessments and radiological findings is crucial to optimize patient safety and resource utilization. Material and Methods: A systematic review of PubMed and Google Scholar was conducted using the keywords: “cervical spine trauma,” “emergency triage,” “NEXUS,” “Canadian C-Spine Rule,” “clinical decision rules,” and “imaging concordance.” Studies reporting sensitivity, specificity, and agreement between initial clinical evaluation by emergency physicians and radiological findings (CT or MRI) were included. Data on study design, patient population, and injury patterns were extracted. Results: • Clinical decision rules (NEXUS and Canadian C-Spine Rule) demonstrated high sensitivity (typically > 95%) for detecting clinically significant cervical spine injuries. • Specificity varied widely (25-60%), reflecting over-triage in low-risk populations. • Discordances were most often due to subtle ligamentous or soft tissue injuries detectable only on MRI. • Studies emphasized that structured clinical assessment improves early triage, reduces unnecessary imaging, and enhances patient safety when combined with judicious use of advanced imaging. Conclusion: Emergency physician-led triage using validated clinical rules shows high sensitivity but limited specificity for high-risk cervical spine trauma. Imaging remains essential for detecting subtle injuries. Integration of structured clinical assessment with selective imaging ensures optimal balance between patient safety and resource utilization.

## ID: AGS26/3287

## P0983: Treatment of cervical spine injuries caused by air weapons - Case report

Miloš Mladenović

1

, Milos Vasic

1

, Uros Novakovic

1

, Ivan Tulić

1

, Valerija Teodosic

1

, Slavisa Zagorac

1

1

University Clinical Center of Serbia, Belgrade, Serbia

Introduction: Injuries caused by air rifles and other air weapons are rare but potentially life-threatening. Most incidents occur in children and young adolescents, with the head and neck region being the most frequently affected. Material and Methods: We present a case of a 20-year-old male patient with a cervical spine injury caused by an air rifle. The patient was treated at the Department of Spine Surgery, University Clinical Center of Serbia (UKCS), and followed for three years on an outpatient basis. Results: The patient was admitted to the emergency department after sustaining a self-inflicted air rifle injury to the neck. Initially examined by an otorhinolaryngologist at another institution, he was referred to our center the following day for further evaluation. On admission, he was conscious, oriented, and communicative, with stable vital signs (blood pressure within normal limits, oxygen saturation 100% on room air, afebrile). He reported no dysphagia, dyspnea, or voice changes. Neurological examination showed GCS 15/15 and ASIA E. A small entry wound (1.5 cm) was observed on the right side of the neck, without hematoma, swelling, subcutaneous emphysema, or exit wound. A palpable thrill was present, but no pulsating masses were noted. CT of the cervical spine revealed a bullet lodged in the C4/C5 intervertebral space without fractures, dislocations, or spinal cord compression. The patient was admitted for monitoring and antibiotic prophylaxis. Given the absence of vertebral fracture, spinal cord involvement, or neurological deficit, a non-operative approach was chosen. During a three-year follow-up, the patient reported no significant complaints. Serial radiographs and one follow-up CT confirmed the stable position of the foreign body. The patient continues to attend regular follow-up visits, showing no complications. Conclusion: Although cervical spine injuries from air rifles are extremely rare, they require careful evaluation due to the potential for life-threatening complications. In selected cases without vertebral instability or neurological compromise, conservative treatment combined with long-term follow-up may be an effective management strategy. Ongoing surveillance, as in this case, is essential to ensure patient safety and monitor for delayed complications.

## ID: AGS26/3338

## P0985: Clinical outcomes of lateral mass screw for traumatic subaxial facet dislocation

Bilalzaib Zaib

1

1

Ghurki Trust and Teaching Hospital Lahore, Lahore, Pakistan

Objective: This study aimed to analyze the functional and neurological outcome of patients diagnosed with subaxial cervical spine bilateral facet dislocation managed by standard posterior midline approach and lateral mass screw fixation by Margerl technique. Material and Methods: Methods: We retrospectively evaluated 22 patients with traumatic cervical spine injuries who presented at the Orthopaedics and Spine Centre Ghurki Trust Teaching Hospital, Lahore from March 2020 to October 2023. Patients included in this study who has subaxial cervical spine bilateral facet dislocation managed by standard posterior midline approach and lateral mass screw fixation by Magerl technique. Functional outcomes was assessed by neck disability index and ASIA impairment scale at last follow up. Preoperative and post operative neurological status was evaluated with ASIA impairment scale. All the data were analyzed using SPSS version 23. Results: Results: Total 22 patients participated in the study of which 72.73% were male and 27.27% female. Mean age was 39 ± 17.02 (13-70) years. Regarding level of dislocation, most common level was C5-6 which was involved in 12 patients, followed by C3-4 and C4-5 which was involved in five patients each. Pre-operatively 50% of the patients had intact neurology (ASIA E), followed by ASIA D which made up of the 27.27% patients, ASIA C 9.09%, ASIA B patients (4.55%), ASIA A 9.09% illustrating the severity of the neurological dysfunction. Postoperatively no neurological recovery was observed in ASIA A or ASIA B patients, while all ASIA C and D patients showed complete recovery. Mean Neck Disability Index (NDI) was 21 ± 19.44. Conclusion: Conclusion: After a good reduction, lateral mass screws fixation are a safe and reliable approach for cervical fixation that not only stabilizes the cervical spine but also leads to a patient’s excellent functional recovery.
Keywords: Cervical Spine, Facet Dislocation, Lateral Mass Screw, Neck Disability IndeX

## P0986: Analysis of mortality following cervical spinal cord injury. Operation as added risk

Kabir Abubakar

1

, Kawu Ahidjo

1

, Aminu Nuraddeen

1

1

National Orthopaedic Hospital, Kano, Nigeria

Introduction: We observed a relatively higher mortality in our patients with Cervical Spine Injury treated with operative stabilization. This is what prompted us to make a comparison with the non-operated cohort in terms of mortality. Material and Methods: We examined our records over a 12month period for the total number of SCI cases we received, the total number that died within the period and whether the death occurred before or after surgery. Results: Most of the deaths occurred in patients with cervical spinal cord Injury (CSCI). There was no significant difference in the population groups except that one group had surgery while the other did not. Over ninety percent of the patients had Complete SCI (ASIA A) Interestingly, we had an equal number of mortality in the two groups for CSCI, but in terms of percentage, mortality in the operated group was 24% while it was only 11.3%. This is of great significance. Conclusion: The mortality in the operated cohort of traumatic CSCI is significantly higher that the mortality in the non-operated group. We need to individualize convincing indications for surgery.

## P0987: The urgent need of ICU guidelines for the management of cervical SCI

Alexandrina Nikova

1

, Eustathios Vlachakis

2

, Theodossios Birbilis

3

, Stefanos Korfias

4

1

Asclipio Voulas Hospital, Athens, Greece

2

417 Army Equity Fund Hospital, athens, Greece

3

Democritus University of Thrace, Alexandroupolis, Greece

4

Evaggelismos Hospital, Athens, Greece

Introduction: Spinal cord injury (SCI), in general, is a cause of deterioration, mortality and severe disability. Upper cervical spine injury is not only cause of quadriplegia but also it might be a associated with increased mortality. On the othat hand, lower spinal cord injury could be a major cause of quadriplegia but also for paraplegia. Patients suffering from lower incomplete cervical SCI could eventually have better quality of life compared to those suffering from complete lower cervical SCI and upper cervical SCI. As expected many of those patients are admitted to the intensive case unit (ICU), accompanied by other injuries. However, some of those patients have SCI without any additional injuries and their ICU stay is prolonged because of lacking recommendation how to treat these patients. Therefore, the present study has the aim to summirize the issues related to the ICU management of SCI patients and make an appeal for the spine community to organize and recommend thereupetic approaches for patients with cervical spinal cord injury in the ICU. Material and Methods: The literature was investiageted on world engines such as MEDLINE, Cochrane, Wiley,clinicaltrials.gov for articles on cervical SCI and ICU management for the duration between 2000 and 2025. Results: The data on the cervical spinal cord injury despite of the many attempts remains vogue on the subject of ICU management. There are less than 10 clinical trials without any sufficient conclusions and even lower number of meta-analyses on the subject. The issues discussed are regarding the ventilation and tracheostomy of the patients with cervical SCI, hyper-hypothermia, dysreflexia and timing/approach of surgery. Conclusion: Despite of the increased knowledge on SCI and how to prevent secondary SCI, little is known on the subject of the cervical SCI and ICU. The latter is a major key for the management of SCI patients not only because of the severity of these injuries but also for their outcome. More studies are needed in more organized and global attempt to provide better care for the patients.

## ID: AGS26/179

## P0988: Spine trauma resource priorities in Sub-Saharan Africa: a Delphi approach

Charlotte Wahle

1

, East Africa Spine Trauma Network

1

1

University of California, San Francisco, Institute for Global Orthopaedics and Traumatology, San Francisco, CA, United States

Introduction: Optimally caring for spine trauma patients requires significant resource availability and highly specialized training. In LMICs where there may be more barriers to resources and training, spine trauma represents a major source of disability and public health burden. The aim of this study was to evaluate the highest priority resource needs for spine surgeons in sub-Saharan Africa in order to provide safe, high-quality spine trauma care. Material and Methods: This study utilized a Delphi methodology with a series of three electronic surveys. Participants were identified through the Continental Association of African Neurosurgical Societies (CAANS) College of Surgeons East, Central, and Southern Africa (COSECSA) networks. Response collection lasted from August 2024 to May 2025. The initial survey was an open ended collection of all spine trauma resource priorities in the region. For each subsequent round of surveys, participants rated topics on a Likert scale from 1 (most important) to 9 (least important). Topics that reached consensus were defined as those ranked 1, 2 or 3 (highly important) by ≥ 70% of the sampled group. Results: Initial invitations were electronically sent to 75 potential participants across the CAANS and COSECSA networks. Of those 75 potential participants, a total of 36 participants agreed to participate and responded to the first round of the survey [Table 1]. Thirty-four participants (94.4%) completed the second round of the survey. The mean score on a likert-scale from 1 to 9 was calculated with 1 being most important and 9 being least important. The results for each resource are displayed in Table 2. Thirty-five (97.2%) completed the final round of the survey. Overall, fifty-one items reached consensus and were included in the scale, meaning they received >70% agreement of one, two or three on the likert scale indicating a high degree of importance [Table 2]. The most highly rated items included braces/c-collars (97.1%), training/education of emergency room physicians on spine trauma management (97.1%), spine-trained surgeons (97.1%), and access to spine implants - including anterior cervical systems (97.1%) and posterior instrumentation (97.1%). Many of the items that did not reach consensus and were seen as less important included those which are highly specialized spine care items and/or require additional training, including microscopes (54.3%), endoscopes (37.1%), navigation (31.4%) and specialized personnel such as neuro-anesthesiologists (68.6%) or neuro-radiologist (57.1%). Conclusion: We performed a comprehensive survey of thirty-six spine trauma surgery experts in sub-Saharan Africa using a Delphi approach to gain consensus as to the highest priority resource needs for spine trauma care. The most highly rated items primarily pertained to pre-operative and intraoperative stabilization of the spine itself, with lower priority given to highly specialized spine care items. Given the high burden of disease and resource needs in these regions, efforts should be focused on providing only the most essential needs before considering more specialized tools and technologies.

## P0989: Impact of race/ethnicity on recovery and socioeconomic changes after spinal cord injury: a multicenter analysis

Josh Callaway

1

, Rahul Bhale

1

, Hania Shahzad

1

, Allan Martin

1

, Wyatt Vander Voort

1

, Yashar Javidan

1

, Eric Klinerberg

1

, Rolando Roberto

1

, Hai Le

1

1

UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord injury (SCI) results in profound motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction with lasting health and socioeconomic consequences. Despite advances in surgical and rehabilitative care, disparities in access, management, and recovery remain underexplored. Prior studies suggest that racial and ethnic minority patients experience higher odds of postoperative complications, delayed diagnosis, and differences in surgical management compared with White patients. This study aimed to investigate racial and ethnic differences in SCI presentation, management, and outcomes at discharge and one year post-injury, focusing on neurologic recovery and socioeconomic measures. Material and Methods: We performed a retrospective cohort analysis using the National Spinal Cord Injury Model Systems (SCIMS) database, the largest longitudinal U.S. SCI registry. Adults aged 18-65 years who sustained traumatic SCI between 2010 and 2020 with complete baseline hospitalization (Form I) and 1-year follow-up (Form II) records were included. Demographic, clinical, and socioeconomic variables were extracted, including race/ethnicity, mechanism of injury, insurance, family income, occupational status, and American Spinal Injury Association (ASIA) classification. Patients were stratified into White and Other Race, Non-White cohorts. Group comparisons employed chi-square and ANOVA tests. Logistic regression models, both univariate and multivariate, were constructed to assess associations between race/ethnicity and outcomes. Results: A total of 3,382 patients were analyzed (mean age 39.7 ± 14.4 years; BMI 26.9 ± 6.5). Non-White patients were younger, more often injured through violent mechanisms, had lower income, and were more frequently covered by Medicaid compared with White patients, who were more often injured in vehicular accidents and had higher private insurance coverage (63.9% vs. 40.3%). White patients were more likely to undergo spine surgery (89.1% vs. 70.9%, p < 0.01). At discharge, neurologic impairment distributions differed slightly, but ASIA Motor Scores were comparable. At one year, rehospitalization rates did not differ significantly (34.1% vs. 34.8%, p = 0.75). However, disparities persisted in insurance coverage, income, and occupational status, with Non-White patients more frequently unemployed or in lower-wage jobs (p < 0.01). Among 1,232 patients with complete ASIA follow-up data, no significant racial differences were observed in impairment categories or changes from baseline. Univariate models suggested that Non-White patients had lower odds of rehospitalization and ASIA improvement, and higher odds of changes in occupation and income. After adjustment for socioeconomic and clinical covariates, associations with neurologic outcomes and rehospitalization were no longer significant, whereas disparities in occupational change (OR 1.75, 95% CI 1.42-2.17, p < 0.01) and insurance status (OR 1.47, 95% CI 1.23-1.76, p < 0.01) remained robust. Conclusion: In this large multicenter cohort, race/ethnicity was not independently associated with neurologic recovery or rehospitalization after SCI once clinical and socioeconomic factors were considered. However, significant disparities in post-injury insurance coverage and occupational outcomes persisted, underscoring the need for targeted policies and interventions to address socioeconomic inequities in SCI recovery.

## ID: AGS26/797

## P0990: Anterior pelvis external fixation and prone distal femoral traction. A novel intraoperative technique for complex spinopelvic injuries

Richard Storey

1,2

, Peggy Miller

1

, Frank Lyons

1,2

, John Duddy

1

, Kevin McSorley

1

1

Mater Misericordiae University Hospital, University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

2

University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

Introduction: Spinopelvic trauma represents a complex range of injuries known for their surgical challenges. Unstable, combined, spinopelvic injury patterns can demand multiplanar fragment control as well as, strong, accurate reduction vectors maintained during prone positioning. Skeletal traction and external fixation are well known in spine and trauma surgery for their safety and simplicity as well as powerful deformity correction and fracture reduction. Whilst routine in supine surgery, practical application, particularly of both techniques simultaneously in prone surgery is not well described. Material and Methods: Two illustrative cases are described following informed consent. Case 1. A 37-year-old male with an AO 61C1.3 (vertical shear, Denis III) fracture following a car vs cyclist accident. His pattern consisted of complex segmental pubic rami fractures and his left hemipelvis was 5 cm rostral to his right. Initial surgical attempt consisted of unilateral distal femoral skeletal traction and anterior superior acetabular external fixation prior to proning. In the prone position without femoral traction, haemodynamic instability resulted in postponement of surgery and immediate CT angiogram demonstrated fracture position loss from earlier supine reduction. On second attempt, distal femoral traction and pelvic binder in addition to the external fixator were used during proning and 20 kg distal femoral skeletal traction maintained through the bed intraoperatively allowing uncomplicated L5 to S2 alar-iliac (AI) posterior fixation and transiliac-transsacral. The anterior external fixator provided powerful counter traction against the bed bolster through the uninjured hemipelvis. Airway, chest, upper and lower limbs were checked regularly throughout the case. Case 2. A 67-year old female with osteoporosis, AO 61C3.3 fracture and distal femoral fracture following fall from a height. Bilateral lower limb skeletal traction was attempted for reduction of bilateral, posteriorly displaced sacral fractures. A previous fixed distal femoral fracture meant bilateral proximal tibial traction skeletal traction was used. An anterior external fixator was not used. The bilateral proximal tibial traction resulted in a more dorsal force vector (up from the bed) compared to the distal femoral traction point in the prone position. Results: In case one, safe prone positioning and maintenance of intraoperative traction was achieved. Midline posterior approach could be completed and minimal further direct fracture maneuvers required for anatomic reduction and posterior fixation using navigated L5 - S2 AI fixation and navigated transiliac-transsacal screw fixation. In case two. Bilateral distal proximal tibial traction without anterior external fixation counter traction resulted in the patient losing position on the bed and was abandoned for standard open reduction techniques. Conclusion: Technique utility of simultaneous anterior external fixation and skeletal femoral traction has been demonstrated in a case with good bone quality and unilateral vertical hemipelvis displacement and should be considered in these fracture patterns requiring a primarily posterior approach. Anterior external fixation provided excellent counter traction and in combination with distal femoral traction resulted in anatomic reduction and an excellent clinical outcome. Caution is advised in cases with bilateral sacral injuries, osteoporosis or polytrauma limiting safe distal femoral traction.

## ID: AGS26/935

## P0991: Mental health problems in spinal cord injury patients - An umbrella systematic review

Sathish Muthu

1,2

, Vibhu Krishnan Viswanathan

1,3

1

Department of Spine Surgery, Orthopaedic Research Group, Coimbatore, India

2

Department of Orthopaedics, Government Medical College, Karur, India

3

Department of Spine Surgery, Devadoss Multispeciality Hospital, Madurai, India

Introduction: Mental illness is a significant comorbidity in individuals with spinal cord injury (SCI), yet the evidence base is fragmented across multiple systematic reviews. This overview aims to synthesize findings from existing reviews to clarify prevalence estimates, associated factors, and methodological quality across mental health domains in SCI populations. Materials & Methods: We systematically identified and synthesized systematic reviews reporting on mental health disorders in SCI populations. Eligible reviews included adults or children with traumatic or non-traumatic SCI, assessed for conditions such as depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), substance use disorders, cognitive impairment, and related psychological outcomes. Data on study characteristics, populations, outcomes, and key findings were extracted. Methodological quality was appraised using AMSTAR 2, and primary study overlap was assessed. Narrative synthesis was performed without meta-analysis. Results: Included reviews spanned publication years 2002-2025 and addressed depression (n = 10), anxiety (n = 4), post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) (n = 4), substance use disorders (SUD) including alcohol and opioids (n = 5), and cognitive impairment (n = 1). Depression prevalence ranged from 17% to 52% depending on the assessment tool, while anxiety symptoms affected up to 27% of individuals, with PTSD rates varying from 14% to 44%. Substance use was prevalent post-injury, with alcohol misuse affecting up to 51% and opioid use reaching 39% in pooled estimates. Methodological quality was rated high in 5 reviews, moderate in 10, and low in 4. The mean CCA across reviews was 8.1%, indicating moderate overlap. Heterogeneity in outcome measures and populations limited direct comparisons but revealed consistent associations with pain, low quality of life, and poor social support. Conclusions: Mental illness is highly prevalent in SCI populations, with depression and substance use disorders most frequently studied. Despite methodological variability, consistent patterns of psychological vulnerability emerge across reviews. This overview highlights the need for harmonized outcome reporting and targeted interventions, especially in underrepresented subgroups such as pediatric and low-resource settings.

## ID: AGS26/1103

## P0992: Posttraumatic spinal deformity in patients after spine trauma with decreased health and function outcomes

Erin E.A. De Gendt

1

, Sander Muijs

1

, Lorin Benneker

2

, F. Cumhur Oner

1

1

University Utrecht, Utrecht, The Netherlands

2

Sonnenhofspital, Bern, Switzerland

Introduction: Disability, pain, and decreased function in daily life are very important concerns for patients after a thoracolumbar spine trauma. Recently a new term Posttraumatic Spinal Deformity (PSD) was introduced defined as: condition where trauma to the spine results in deformity in any plane and results in pain and impaired function with or without a neurological deficit. In this study we analyzed radiographic and patient specific factors in a group of patients after a trauma to the spine with significant and lasting impairment and their conformity to the new definition. Material and Methods: A patient cohort with impaired function (Oswestry Disability Index > 40%) (ODI) was selected from an existing database of patients after long-term follow-up after spine trauma (>12 months). Patient factors were analyzed such as: age, fracture region, type and treatment, comorbidities and work status. Radiographic measurements and Patient Reported Outcome Measurements were analyzed. Descriptive statistics and Spearman’s rho for correlation were used. Results: In total, 31 patients were included with a median age of 52 years (IQR 33). A B-type fracture in the thoracic spine was mostly involved. Fulltime employment dropped from 62% to 26%. Strong correlation was seen between the total scores of the ODI and the AO Spine Patient Reported Outcome Spine Trauma (PROST). But a very small correlation was seen between the pain dimensions of the ODI and the AO Spine PROST. Conclusion: This patient cohort did fit within the newly developed definition of Posttraumatic Spinal Deformity. The amount of measured deformity was variable and did not correlate to an impaired function at long term follow-up.

## ID: AGS26/1299

## P0993: Open reduction of L5/S1 traumatic spondyloptosis with “double lever technique”

Geok Hwee Teo

1

, Mohamad Fauzlie Yusof

1

, Ahmad Aziman Arifin

1

, Muhamad Zharif Nor Asikin

1

1

Melaka General Hospital, Melaka, Malaysia

Introduction: Spondyloptosis is a severe spinal injury characterized by complete dislocation of upper vertebra over the lower vertebra. It is also referred to as grade V spondylolisthesis (Meyerding Classification). Spondyloptosis of L5/S1 is often associated with neurological deficits and loss of bowel and bladder control. Traumatic L5/S1 spondyloptosis requires high energy trauma and is extremely rare, particularly in children. We report a case of traumatic L5/S1 spondyloptosis in a 13-year-old boy and emphasize on the reduction technique. Case presentation: A 13-year-old boy was involved in a high-speed motor vehicle accident. He was uncertain about the mechanism of injury due to retrograde amnesia. Neurological examination revealed bilateral L4 & L5 deficits, more severe on the right side, with muscle power graded 0/5 but sphincter function was preserved. Radiographs and CT scans showed L5/S1 spondyloptosis with bilateral pedicle fractures from L2 to L5, resulting in dissociation of posterior spinal columns. MRI demonstrated crowding of cauda equina nerve roots and disruption of posterior ligamentous complex from L2 to S1. The patient was initially resuscitated due to a serious intraabdominal injury with a shattered spleen. He underwent an emergency laparotomy with splenectomy. After stabilizing for one week, he was brought to operating theater again for spine surgery with open reduction via posterior surgery and instrumentation from T12 to the pelvis. Results: Intraoperatively, the entire posterior elements from L2 to L5 were floating due to bilateral pedicle fractures and severe disruption of posterior ligamentous complex. Decompression was performed first by removing the detached posterior elements at L4 and L5. The thecal sac was found to be intact. We insert two Schanz pins into L5 vertebral body and another two in posterior iliac bone in the same location and direction as intended pedicle screws. The Schanz pins were then connected to external fixator bars at L5 and S1 each. A Bristow was carefully introduced via an imaginary Kambin’s triangle in L5/S1 as the first lever while a Hohmann retractor was applied as the second lever using S1 bar as a fulcrum to lift the L5 bar. This maneuver eased and allowed controlled reduction while minimizing risk of neural injury. We name the reduction technique as “double lever technique”. The Schanz pins were subsequently replaced with pedicle screws and the posterior instrumentation was completed in the usual manner. Conclusion: Traumatic L5/S1 spondyloptosis in children is extremely rare but disastrous. Reduction is often difficult and carries a high risk of iatrogenic neural injury. The described “double lever technique” provides a controlled and safe reduction method while minimizing unwanted complications.

## ID: AGS26/1625

## P0994: Spinopelvic fixation in traumatic instability: case report and clinical decision-making algorithm

Javier Matta

1

, Alejandra Suarez

1

, David Florez

1

, Victor Arrieta

1

, Diana Medina

2

, Juan Pablo Melo

3

1

Hospital Militar Central, bogota, Colombia

2

Universidad Autonoma de Bucaramanga, Bogota, Colombia

3

Universidad Militar Nueva Granada, Bogota, Colombia

Introduction: Spinopelvic instability represents a significant therapeutic challenge, typically resulting from high-energy trauma such as motor vehicle collisions or falls from substantial heights. These injuries are associated with high morbidity and mortality, with reported mortality rates approaching 50% and neurological deficits occurring in 30% to 100% of cases. Various surgical stabilization techniques are available; however, the selection of the optimal approach is multifactorial. This report presents a clinical case of traumatic spinopelvic dissociation managed with anterior pelvic ring fixation and posterior triangular stabilization. The case underscores the limited existing literature on this pathology and includes a comprehensive review of current treatment strategies. Furthermore, a management algorithm is proposed to facilitate standardized and reproducible surgical decision-making in similar clinical contexts. Material and Methods: This case report describes a patient with an unstable C1 fracture classified according to the AO/OTA system, associated with a Denis II fracture of the right sacral ala, right sacroiliac joint dissociation, involvement of the superior articular facet of S1, ipsilateral iliopubic and ischiopubic rami fractures, and residual pubic symphysis diastasis. The patient was followed for two years with functional outcome assessment. For the literature review, a systematic search was performed in PubMed and Ovid using the MeSH term “Triangular osteosynthesis”, identifying 527 articles. After exclusion of duplicates and non-relevant studies, 41 articles were included in the final analysis. Results: At the time of case resolution, the patient’s postoperative course was uneventful, without requiring intensive care unit admission, and early rehabilitation was initiated. At the 2-year follow-up, the patient reported a Numerical Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) score of 3/10 and an Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) of 4%, with no associated sensory or motor deficits. The patient successfully resumed full daily activities. Based on the literature review, we propose an algorithm for the diagnostic evaluation of spinopelvic injuries, as well as a structured approach to fixation sequencing and surgical management options. Conclusion: Pelvic trauma and spinopelvic instability constitute a significant diagnostic and therapeutic challenge. In our experience, the systematization of pelvic trauma management through the development of standardized care protocols and diagnostic-therapeutic algorithms has been essential for optimizing clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/1660

## P0995: Efficacy of sacral-alar-iliac screw fixation (SAIFIX) for pelvic ring fractures - Nine Case Series

Yuki Nagaoka

1

, Ryota Kimura

1

, Yuichi Ono

1

, Daisuke Kudo

1

, Yuji Kasukawa

1

, Michio Hongo

1

, Naohisa Miyakoshi

1

1

Akita University Graduate School of Medicine Department of Orthopedics Surgery, Akita, Japan

Introduction: Pelvic ring fractures are often caused by high-energy trauma or bone fragility. In particular, in cases of high-energy trauma, it is well known that the mortality rate is high due to severe hemorrhage and the coexistence of multiple injuries. Furthermore, with the progression of population aging, the incidence of fragility fractures of the pelvis (FFP) is expected to increase, along with the demand for sacral-alar-iliac screw fixation (SAIFIX). Posterior fixation using SAIFIX is minimally invasive while providing strong fixation. In cases with concomitant burst fractures, skip fusion can minimize range-of-motion loss and preserve postoperative function. We report nine cases of pelvic ring fractures treated with SAIFIX. Material and Methods: The subjects were nine cases of pelvic ring fractures (2 males and 7 females) treated with SAIFIX at our hospital between February 2021 and March 2025. The mean age was 63 years (range, 23-83 years). The injury mechanisms included five cases of high-energy trauma and four cases of FFP. The mean waiting period until surgery was 14 days, and three patients received temporary external fixation in the acute phase. We evaluated bone union, postoperative ambulatory ability, and the need for additional surgery. Results: No surgical complications were observed. Bone union was confirmed in all eight cases that could be followed for more than six months, and all nine patients were able to achieve independent ambulation. No cases required additional surgery, nor was any progression of fracture displacement observed. In addition, three patients had concomitant thoracolumbar burst fractures; however, skip fusion was performed, allowing preservation of lumbosacral mobility. Conclusion: SAIFIX is minimally invasive yet provides strong fixation, offering perioperative advantages such as early mobilization and reduced risk of infection. In cases of pelvic ring fractures complicated by spinal injuries, even in high-energy trauma cases that often require long fusion with conventional techniques, skip fusion can be selected, potentially minimizing postoperative functional impairment. Further studies are needed to evaluate long-term outcomes and to compare SAIFIX with other surgical methods.

## ID: AGS26/1664

## P0996: Prehospital spinal immobilization in trauma patients with sensorimotor deficits: a systematic review

Felix Corr

1

, René Hartensuer

2

, Jan Wnent

3

, Dina Wiersbicki

4

, Nader Hejrati

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery & Interdisciplinary Spine Center, HOCH Health Ostschweiz, Cantonal Hospital of St. Gallen, St. Gallen, Switzerland

2

Department of Orthopedics and Trauma Surgery, Klinikum Achaffenburg, Aschaffenburg, Germany

3

3 Department of Emergency Medicine, University Hospital Schleswig-Holstein, Kiel, Germany

4

Department of Orthopedics and Trauma Surgery, University Hospital Leipzig, Leipzig, Germany

Introduction: Spinal immobilization is widely used in prehospital trauma care to prevent secondary spinal cord injury, although evidence is limited. Reported harms include airway compromise, raised intracranial pressure, transport delays, and pressure injuries. International guidelines generally cite sensorimotor deficit as the only absolute indication, yet clinical evidence remains inconsistent. This review specifically addressed the question of whether adult patients with blunt or penetrating spinal trauma and concomitant sensorimotor deficits benefit from prehospital immobilization with respect to neurological outcomes and survival. Material and Methods: We conducted a PRISMA-compliant systematic review (PROSPERO: CRD420251035627) of observational studies comparing prehospital spinal immobilization with no or selective immobilization in adult trauma. Databases (Pubmed, Cochrane Library, BASE, Google Scholar) were searched from inception to August 2025. Studies were eligible if they included adults (≥ 16 years) with traumatic injury, specified immobilization status, and reported neurological or mortality outcomes. Primary outcomes were neurological deterioration and all-cause mortality. Risk of bias was assessed using ROBINS-I. Results: Eighteen studies published between 1989 and 2024, encompassing over 72,000 adult trauma patients across North America, Europe, the Middle East, and Asia, met inclusion criteria. The majority were retrospective cohort studies (n = 16), with only two prospective investigations. Methodological heterogeneity, confounded by indication, and retrospective designs resulted in a high overall risk of bias and very limited confidence in the available evidence. Trauma mechanisms included both blunt (62.5%) and penetrating (37.5%) injuries, with immobilization strategies ranging from cervical collars to rigid backboards, vacuum mattresses, and full spinal motion restriction. Evidence specific to patients with sensorimotor deficits was sparse. The largest prospective cohort reported neurological deterioration in 59% of non-immobilized patients (n = 19), while a large retrospective (N = 454) registry paradoxically found higher odds of neurological disability among immobilized patients (OR 2.03, 95% CI 1.03-3.99). Smaller retrospective series described deterioration in both groups, with isolated cases in non-immobilized patients as well as in immobilized patients. Across broader trauma populations, immobilization did not consistently reduce neurological decline, mortality, or spinal cord injury confirmation. Conclusion: Marked heterogeneity and methodological limitations preclude quantitative synthesis of available studies, leaving no robust effect estimate for neurological or survival benefit. The only consistent recommendation remains immobilization in patients presenting with sensorimotor deficits, as endorsed uniformly across international guidelines. In unselected trauma populations, the balance of evidence indicates no clear benefit and potential harm, underscoring the need for selective, risk-adapted protocols. Until high-quality, mechanism-stratified trials are available, guideline-based immobilization in the presence of neurological deficits remains the most defensible standard of care.

## ID: AGS26/1809

## P0997: When trauma and chemistry collide: successful management of an open spinal fracture with burn injury

César Correia

1

, Patrícia Cunha

2

, Rui Matos

2

, João Lucas

2

, João Soeima

2

, Inês Henriques

2

, José Simães

2

, Vítor Caetano

2

1

Unidade Local de Saúde de Braga, Braga, Portugal

2

Unidade Local de Saúde do Alto Ave, Guimarães, Portugal

Introduction: Open spinal fractures represent a rare but formidable orthopedic challenge due to the complex anatomy involved and the significant risk of complications. These injuries typically result from high-energy trauma, such as motor vehicle collisions or falls from height, and are frequently associated with extensive soft-tissue and neurovascular damage. Materials and Methods: We report the case of a 75-year-old farmer admitted with a severe dorsolumbar burn following the explosion of a backpack sprayer (containing a mixture of hydrofluoric and phosphoric acid) that he was carrying while cycling. On admission, the patient exhibited no pelvic instability or motor deficits but presented with an extensive third-degree dorsolumbar burn exposing gluteal musculature, accompanied by localized pain and mild paresthesia in both feet. The Poison Control Center was contacted, and, given the risk of deep tissue progression and systemic hypocalcemia or hypomagnesemia, topical calcium gluconate or carbonate gel was promptly administered. Radiographic evaluation revealed an acute L3 fracture (AO: B2(A3)) associated with right transverse process fractures of L2-L3 and left transverse process fractures of L1-L4, without neurological deficits. Conservative management was initiated with bed rest, log-roll transfers, and block mobilization. Due to the extent of the burn injury, the patient was transferred to a dedicated Burn and Trauma Unit, where urgent plastic surgical debridement was performed. Three weeks later, he underwent reintervention for lumbar seroma drainage, followed by continued wound care. One month later, with pain well controlled and paresthesia resolved, the patient underwent open posterior transpedicular fixation from L1 to L4. Results: The early postoperative course was favorable, with effective pain control, absence of infection, and preserved neurovascular function. The patient began ambulation with a Jewett brace shortly thereafter. At 4 weeks, wounds were fully healed, no inflammatory signs were noted, and radiographs demonstrated ongoing fracture consolidation, allowing initiation of physiotherapy. By 3 months, he remained pain-free, neurologically intact, and ambulated independently without support. Radiographs confirmed consolidation. At 5 months postoperatively, the patient had returned to his baseline functional status, performing all pre-injury activities-including gardening tasks-without restriction. Clinical evaluation demonstrated complete wound healing, absence of local or systemic complications, and full, symmetric muscle strength. Conclusion: Open spinal fractures are serious injuries requiring urgent, multidisciplinary management focused on patient stabilization, hemorrhage control, infection prevention, and timely surgical intervention. Definitive surgical fixation is often necessary not only to restore spinal stability but also to facilitate soft tissue healing and optimize functional recovery. Prevention of infectious and neurological complications is paramount, as is minimizing the deleterious impact of prolonged immobilization. Early initiation of rehabilitation is essential for restoring function, ensuring long-term recovery, and preserving quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/2293

## P0998: The role of sacro-iliac joint fusion in pelvic trauma: personal experience

Leo Massari

1

, Dario Lo Re

1

, Achille Saracco

1

, Gaetano Caruso

1

1

University of Ferrara, Ferrara, Italy

Introduction: Pelvic ring fractures are uncommon injuries with a prevalence of 20-37/100,000 in the general population and still represent a leading cause of death and disability in patients involved in high-energy trauma.1,2,3 Sacroiliac joint rupture, which includes both sacrum fracture or sacroiliac joint dislocation and crescent iliac wing fracture involving the sacroiliac joint, is among the factors associated with a worse functional outcome of this type of injury.4,5 Posterior fixation of the disrupted sacroiliac joint can be achieved with iliosacral screws, anterior sacroiliac plating, transiliac bars, or transiliac plating.6,7,8 The aim of this study was to verify the clinical outcome of patients with iliosacral fracture surgically treated with screws and titanium triangular implants and its correlation with the various types of traumatic mechanism. Methods: A retrospective analysis of patients with sacroiliac joint disruptions and treated with stabilization and fusion using screws and titanium triangular implants, from January 2020 to March 2024 was conducted and clinical outcomes were evaluated. For each patient, the type of traumatic mechanism was evaluated with the consequent type of pelvic fracture classified according to the classifications of Tile, Young-Burgess and the AO, the time to hospital stay, any further pathologies or traumatic injuries, the time of surgery, clinical outcome with SF-12, Majeed, New Score Pelvic System scores and number of positive tests for sacroiliac joint dysfunction. Results: We treated and recruited 18 patients (16 M, 2 F) who had sustained sacroiliac joint fracture/dislocation due to a pelvic ring fracture. The median age of patients was 67 (44-79). The average time to hospital stay was 32,5 days. The laterality of the sacroiliac lesion was right 8 and left 10. The mean time for the surgical procedure was less than 3 hours. The mean follow-up time was 1 year. The respective mean scores of Majeed score, PCS and MCS-12 (SF-12) and New Pelvis Score System were: 77.5, 41.275, 55.395. On physical examination, patients tested positive on average for only 1 provocative test for sacroiliac dysfunction. Conclusions: Based on clinical outcomes and results, the combination of screws and triangular implants for SI Joint Stabilization and Fusion has proven to be an effective and safe tool for reduction and stabilization of SI joint injuries in patients with complex pelvic fractures. This type of instrument proves to be effective not only in dysfunction or pain of the sacroiliac joint but also in traumatic injuries of the pelvis.

References

1. Meccariello L, Razzano C, De Dominicis C, Herrera-Molpeceres JA, Liuzza F, Erasmo R, Rocca G, Bisaccia M, Pagliarulo E, Cirfeda P, Gómez Garrido D, Rollo G. A new prognostic pelvic injury outcome score. Med Glas (Zenica). 2021 Feb 1;18(1):299-308.
2. Papakostidis C, Kanakaris NK, Kontakis G, Giannoudis PV. Pelvic ring disruptions: treatment modalities and analysis of outcomes. Int Orthop. 2009 Apr;33(2):329-38.
3. Gansslen A, Pohlemann T, Paul C et al (1996) Epidemiology of pelvic ring injuries. Injury 27(Suppl 1):S-A13-S-A20
4. Hoffmann MF, Jones CB, Sietsema DL. Persistent impairment after surgically treated lateral compression pelvic injury. Clin Orthop Relat Res. 2012 Aug;470(8):2161-72.
5. Dujardin FH, Hossenbaccus M, Duparc F, Biga N, Thomine JM. Long-term functional prognosis of posterior injuries in highenergy pelvic disruption. J Orthop Trauma. 1998;12:145-150; discussion 150-151.
6. Matta JM, Saucedo T. Internal fixation of pelvic ring fractures. Clin Orthop Relat Res. 1989 May;242:83-97

## ID: AGS26/2375

## P0999: Disc prolapse after lumbar fracture: clinical relevance, management, and recent evidence with case illustrations

Mohammad Elhalabi

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery and Spine Surgery Evangelisches Klinikum Niederrhein, Dinslaken/Bethesda Duisburg, Germany, Dinslaken, Germany

Introduction: Lumbar spine fractures may involve adjacent intervertebral discs, leading to acute disc prolapse or progressive degeneration. This association is under-recognized but has important implications for neurological outcome, spinal stability, and surgical decision-making. Material and Methods: A narrative review of the literature (2020-2025) was performed focusing on traumatic disc prolapse in the setting of lumbar fractures. Key aspects analyzed included incidence, imaging, pathophysiology, and management strategies. Additionally, two illustrative clinical cases will be presented during the talk to demonstrate practical challenges and solutions. Results: Traumatic disc prolapse is increasingly recognized as a predictor of post-traumatic instability and deformity. MRI remains the gold standard for diagnosis, while CT is limited for soft tissue assessment. Management options range from conservative observation and CT-guided infiltration to vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty, decompression, and fusion. Recent evidence emphasizes that disc integrity strongly influences long-term outcomes. The forthcoming case illustrations highlight both minimally invasive management (vertebroplasty with infiltration) and surgical decompression with stabilization in progressive neurological deficit. Conclusion: Disc prolapse following lumbar fracture is a clinically significant condition that requires early recognition and tailored management. Understanding the contribution of disc injury to spinal instability helps guide optimal treatment. Case-based discussion will underline the spectrum of conservative, minimally invasive, and surgical strategies in real-world practice.
Keywords: lumbar fracture, disc prolapse, instability, vertebroplasty, spine surgery.

## ID: AGS26/2467

## P1000: High-energy gunshot wound to the lumbosacral spine (L4-5) with cauda equina syndrome: a case report

Angel Asdrubal Rebollar Mendoza

1

, Pedro Edmundo Aviles Jiménez

2

, Adriana Elizabeth Romero Figueroa

1

, Josias Eduardo Cañedo Corral

2

1

Neurológical and Trauma Surgery Department; Regional Military Hospital, Mazatlán, Sin., Mexico

2

Military Trauma Center, México City, Mexico

Introduction: Gunshot injuries to the lumbosacral spine are uncommon but may result in severe neurological deficits, including cauda equina syndrome. Management is challenging and requires prompt, multidisciplinary intervention. Material and methods (Case Presentation): A 28-year-old male was admitted by air ambulance to a military trauma center after sustaining a high-energy gunshot wound to the lumbosacral region. Upon arrival, he was evaluated following the atls protocol, and subsequently underwent laparotomy, which revealed no major organ damage. He presented with complete motor and sensory loss below L2 and complete cauda equina syndrome, classified as ASIA A. Imaging revealed a comminuted-burst fracture at L5 with bullet fragments in the spinal canal and associated soft tissue damage. He underwent damage control surgery, which included: 1.- Posterior Decompressive Laminectomy. 2.- Instrumentation with polyaxial screws L4-S1. 3.- A dual graft technique employing thoracolumbar fascial tissue and synthetic substitute. 4.- Rotational flap of the thoracolumbar deep musculature (mutifidus, interspinalis, erector spinae, iliocostalis lumborum) for soft tissue coverage. Results: Postoperatively, there was no cerebrospinal fluid leakage. Neurological recovery progressed from complete to partial cauda equina syndrome, with persistent loss of anal sphincter function, partial urinary sphincter control, and partial recovery of hip flexion and knee extension. At 4-month follow-up, the patient was able to walk with crutches. Conclusion: High-energy gunshot injuries to the lumbosacral spine with cauda equina syndrome require prompt damage control surgery and multidisciplinary management. At 4 months, follow-up remains a short period for meaningful neurological recovery, highlighting the need for continued close surveillance and long-term rehabilitation.

## ID: AGS26/3284

## P1001: Complications associated with prolonged immobilization in the emergency setting prior to neurosurgical management - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

1
Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, 
2
Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia
Introduction: Patients with acute spinal trauma frequently require immobilization in the emergency department prior to definitive neurosurgical care. Prolonged immobilization may lead to secondary complications, including pressure ulcers, compartment syndrome, and respiratory compromise. Understanding these risks is essential to optimize patient safety. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed and Google Scholar with the keywords: “spinal trauma,” “emergency immobilization,” “prolonged immobilization,” “pressure ulcers,” “compartment syndrome,” and “respiratory complications.” Studies reporting incidence, risk factors, and clinical outcomes of complications related to prolonged immobilization were included. Results: • Pressure ulcers were reported in 5-20% of patients, particularly in the elderly and those with limited mobility or comorbidities. • Compartment syndromes and peripheral nerve injuries occurred in cases of rigid immobilization devices combined with delayed surgical intervention. • Respiratory complications, including hypoventilation and pneumonia, were more frequent in patients with thoracic or high cervical immobilization, especially when ventilatory support was delayed. • Early mobilization, frequent position changes, adequate padding, and rapid transfer to definitive care were consistently associated with reduced complication rates. Conclusion: Prolonged immobilization in the emergency setting carries significant risk for pressure injuries, compartment syndromes, and respiratory complications. Protocols emphasizing early transfer to neurosurgical care, careful positioning, and regular monitoring are critical to minimize secondary morbidity. Further prospective studies are needed to quantify optimal immobilization durations and preventive strategies.

## ID: AGS26/3302

## P1002: Analgesia protocols in emergency management of spinal trauma: literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Pain management is a cornerstone of emergency care in patients with spinal trauma. Effective analgesia improves patient comfort, facilitates neurological assessment, and may reduce secondary complications. Various strategies - including systemic opioids, NSAIDs, and locoregional blocks-are employed, but their relative efficacy and impact on neurological evaluation remain debated. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed and Google Scholar with the keywords: “spinal trauma,” “emergency analgesia,” “opioids,” “NSAIDs,” “regional blocks,” and “neurological assessment.” Studies reporting analgesic protocols, pain control outcomes, effects on neurological evaluation, and adverse events were included. Results: • Opioids remain the mainstay for acute severe pain, providing rapid analgesia but requiring monitoring for sedation that may mask neurological deficits. • NSAIDs offer moderate pain relief, with minimal impact on neurological assessment, but their use may be limited by renal or bleeding risk. • Locoregional techniques, such as paravertebral or epidural blocks, provide effective segmental analgesia and reduce systemic opioid requirements; careful selection is needed to avoid interfering with neurological exams. • Multimodal protocols combining opioids, NSAIDs, and regional techniques improve patient comfort, reduce opioid consumption, and facilitate accurate neurological assessment. • Early and structured analgesic management is associated with improved patient cooperation, quicker imaging, and safer mobilization. Conclusion: Tailored analgesia protocols in emergency spinal trauma enhance patient comfort and allow reliable neurological evaluation. Multimodal strategies integrating systemic and locoregional techniques are recommended, with attention to safety and monitoring. Further studies are needed to optimize protocols and balance pain control with accurate neurological assessment.

## Trauma - thoracolumbar

## ID: AGS26/50

## P1003: Long term results after combination of cryoablation and balloon kyphoplasty for osteoporotic thoracolumbar spine fractures

Valery Stavrev

1

, Vladimir Stavrev

1

, Petko Ganev

1

, Hristo Petrichev

1

, Spas Radev

1

, Valentin Petrov

1

1

Medical University Plovdiv Bulgaria, Plovdiv, Bulgaria

Introduction: Both cryoablation and balloon kyphoplasty are well known techniques for management of painful osteoporotic fractures of the thoracolumbar spine. We evaluate or long term results after combination of these two techniques for a period of 7 years. Material and Methods: We analyze our results after percutaneous balloon kyphoplasty and cryoablation in a group of 67 adult patients with painful osteoporotic fractures in the thoracolumbar region of the spine. All patients were admitted and treated at the Department of Orthopedics and Traumatology of the Medical University - Plovdiv. The procedure was performed under general anesthesia, with patients positioned face down on a radiolucent operating table. In balloon kyphoplasty, we used the “Guardian” system - South Korea. In cryoablation, we used a cryoablator of the type: “Metrum Cryoflex”, Warsaw - Poland. After removing the cannulas for balloon kyphoplasty, we used the same openings for the insertion of the cryoprobe to the medial branch of the ramus dorsalis of the spinal root. When incising the skin, we used the smallest scalpel tip, and for this reason, there was no need for a skin suturing. Results: We analyzed our results using the visual analog scale to assess the postoperative reduction of pain in all our patients who underwent this combined procedure. The follow-up examinations were conducted immediately after the procedure, on the 3rd, 7th day, one month, 3 months, and 6 months after the surgery. We did not manage to record any fractures of the bodies of the adjacent vertebrae to the cement-augmented vertebrae 6 months after balloon kyphoplasty. The average pain reduction after this combined technique was more than 50%, as measured by VAS. Two of the patients - one man and one woman had minimal response to this procedure, which necessitated open decompression, instrumentation, and fusion immediately after the attempt for minimally invasive treatment. Conclusion: Both balloon kyphoplasty and cryoablation are effective percutaneous minimally invasive techniques used for pain management therapy in adult patients. Until now, we have not found studies on the combined use of balloon kyphoplasty and cryoablation in the same patient. The pain relief effect was confirmed by the visual analog scale and was maintained for more than 6 months. For these reasons, we recommend this combined technique for adult patients with painful osteoporotic vertebral fractures who are contraindicated or refuse open surgery.

## ID: AGS26/80

## P1004: Lumbar spine stress fracture adjacent to instrumentation in ankylosing spondylitis patient. Case report

Laura Perelló

1

, Joan Toribio

2

1

Hospital Universitari Son Llatzer, Palma de Mallorca, Spain

2

Hospital Universitari Son Llàtzer, Palma de Mallorca, Spain

Introduction: This report describes sequentially vertebral stress fracture in an ankylosing spondylitis patient with pre-existing multilevel spine instrumentation. Material and Methods: 65-year-old presented to the emergency department with an injury to the neck after a fall riding a bycicle in November 2021. Plain radiograph evaluation revealed a dis-placed fracture at C6 and displaced fracture at the L1-L2 disc space, the patient was neurologically intact. The patient underwent postural reduction and open reduction via an internal fixation of both fractures. The cervical spine injury was addressed first, anterior plating and structural iliac graft from C5-C7 and 1 week after posterior multilevel pedicular screw and rod construct from T11-L4. After the second surgery the patient developed a nosocomial pneumonia (methicillin-resistant Stafilo-coccus Aureus) , renal failure and ADRS which requires intensive care for 5 months. Follow-up radiological control showed a complete bone healing and he was dis-charged to physiotherapy treatment for 3 months with complete functional recovery for daily activities and walking leisure. Results: Two years later, in October 2023, he started with back pain without previous trauma, we identified in lateral Rx a L5 stress transverse fracture in subacute stage. CT scan demonstrated a lysis in the vertebral body, representing material fatigue failure of bone. Long spinal instrumentation with screws to the ilium connected to the older implant allowed a good fixation in this osteoporotic bone scenario. Currently patient needed again 3 weeks of intensive care and antibioticoterapy. Bone healing considered at 6 months after surgery, again with complete functional recovery. Conclusion: According to Ho et al, recurrent stress fractures in patients with ankylosing spondylitis were noted to occur below the level of previous anterior iatro-genically fused spine segments. However, the application of instrumentation in the patient with ankylosing spondylitis may decrease the risk of refracture at this level but may further contribute to the rigidity of the involved spine segment, and it may assume the powerful moment arm in traumatic or non traumatic episodes, with stress forces targeting the spine segments at the weakest point. However, increased stress forces were redirected to the cephalaad and caudal levels to the instrumen-tation. In our patient the stress forces were redirected to the caudal level to the instrumentation with L5 stress fracture. Thus, in patient with ankylosing spondylitis with previous internal instrumentation who has even minimal trauma or without trauma with back pain the clinician should be suspect of the possibility of stress frac-ture at cephalad or caudal levels to the instrumentation.

## ID: AGS26/147

## P1005: Lifesaving embolization of a massive lumbar artery bleeding after a burst L2 vertebral fracture

Ioannis Papaioannou

1

, Georgia Pantazidou

1

, Thomas Repantis

1

, Andreas Baikousis

1

1

General Hospital of Patras, Patras, Greece

Introduction: Lumbar artery injury after comminuted lumbar fracture due to high-energy trauma especially after a suicide attempt is an infrequent injury. Lumbar artery bleeding might represent a life-threatening event, given the consequent retroperitoneal hemorrhages, which can provoke neurological complications. Our case highlights the value of embolization as the gold standard for these bleedings as a life-saving intervention. Material and Methods: A 35-year-old male patient was admitted to our department after a suicide attempt. The patient suffered from schizophrenia and the medication was interrupted. On admission, the Glasgow Coma Score was excellent without any head trauma, although he had tachycardia (120 pulses/minute) sufficient blood pressure of 110/65 mmHg, and 95% oxygen saturation. Communication due to his medical history was difficult, although the patient was alert. He complained about feet and back pain. He was neurologically intact and full body computed tomography angiography revealed active bleeding of the right inferior lumbar segmental arteries. The patient gradually became hemodynamically unstable, with severe tachycardia, hypotension, and anuria. Furthermore, the CT scan and radiograms disclosed a burst L2 vertebral fracture, bilateral un-displaced open type 3A calcaneal fracture, and sacroiliac dislocation. Results: The patient was supported via blood transfusion, controlled fluid intake, and tranexamic acid administration until sufficient urinary excretion and correction of vital signs and acidosis. Due to the inability to further stabilize the patient, embolization of the segmental vertebral arteries was decided, and the patient was transferred to the nearby interventional radiologist department. A successful embolization via coil was performed and the patient was totally stabilized hemodynamically. The next day the patient underwent posterior Th12- L4 instrumentation accompanied with decompression of the unstable L2 burst fracture. We also stabilize the sacroiliac dislocation via iliac screw insertion and rod application. The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged five days after surgery. The calcaneal fractures were treated conservatively, and we monitored the patient closely due to the open fractures. During the 6-month follow up the L2 vertebral fracture, the calcaneal fractures, and the wounds were completely healed. CRP and ESR levels remained within normal limits throughout the monitoring. The patient was totally mobilized three months after surgery. Conclusion: Lumbar fractures can lead to retroperitoneal hemorrhages, which represent a potentially life-threatening event associated with neurological complications. In our case, the L2 comminuted fracture caused the rupture of the right lumbar arteries. Lumbar artery bleeding can occur as a complication of other conditions like renal mass biopsies, spinal procedures, or trauma as in our case. Lumbar artery bleeding is a rare condition, and no guidelines exist for the management of this life-threatening entity. According to the existing literature, endovascular intervention constitutes the treatment of choice. In several cases, collateral circulation led to multiple embolization with a bilateral approach. In our case, the approach was unilateral due to the sufficient embolization of the bleeding arteries and the stable hemodynamic condition of the patient. Clinicians should be aware of this life-threatening traumatic event and should not hesitate to urgently embolize the patient.

## ID: AGS26/204

## P1006: Usage of the transpedicular screw fixation method in the treatment of pediatric patients with injuries of the thoracic and lumbar spine

Sergei Zalepugin

1

1

Republican Research Center of Traumatology and Orthopedics, Minsk, Belarus

Introduction: The incidence of spinal injuries in patients under 18 years of age has increased significantly in recent years, which represents a significant economic, social and medical problem. The most common method of surgical stabilization of spinal fractures in pediatric patients is transpedicular posterior spinal fusion, which is widely used by spinal neurosurgeons in adult patients. Material and Methods: From 2019 to 2024, 35 children with injuries of the thoracic and lumbar spine underwent surgical treatment using the transpedicular screw fixation method. Among the injured, girls prevailed (21 cases, 60%). The age of the victims ranged from 9 to 17 years. The main causes of damage were: catatrauma (19 cases), road accident (5 cases), sports injury (6 cases), other reasons - 5 cases. In 5 cases, suicidal attempts occurred. Co-injury was observed in most cases (20 patients, or 57%), which is natural for high-energy injury. Vertebral-spinal injury with neurological disorders was observed in 13 patients, the disorders ranged from mild inferior (4 children) to moderate/severe paraparesis (5 patients) and inferior paraplegia (4 children). 6 children had pelvic organ dysfunction in the form of urinary and fecal retention or incontinence. All thirty-five patients, within a period of 1 to 57 days after the injury, underwent a number of surgical interventions from the posterior surgical access using a screw fixation method (posterior decompression + spinal fusion). In 12 cases it was necessary to perform the second stage of surgical treatment - anterior decompression of the spinal cord or its roots. Verticalization of patients was carried out within 1 to 5 days after surgery.
Results: In all patients the nearest, up to 1 year, results were evaluated. In children operated in 2019-2021, the results were studied in terms of 3 to 5 years. The procedures used, clinical results and the quality of the fixative installation were assessed. All patients managed to achieve positive results. The use of internal fixation made it possible to carry out early verticalization of children, eliminate pain syndrome and achieve a regression of neurological disorders in most patients (especially in cases when the operation was performed early after injury - from 1 to 3 days,). Within the first month, the ability to self-care was fully restored. Bone fusion was observed within 6-12 months after surgery. There were no complications after surgery. The analysis of postoperative radiographs, CT and MRI images revealed the correct standing of the screws in all cases. Conclusion: The posterior spinal fusion using the new method of screw fixation in pediatric patients allows to achieve durable stabilization of damage, begin early rehabilitation of patients and reduce the duration of hospital treatment by 2-3 times. Thus, we recommend the use of a transpedicular fixator in children as a reliable, really technically feasible method for restoring spinal stability with a low risk of intra- and postoperative complications.

## ID: AGS26/327

## P1007: How should we manage spine fractures in ankylosing spondylitis

Nagy Darwish

1

1

Ulster Independent clinic, Belfast, United Kingdom

Introduction: How should we manage spine fractures in ankylosing spondylitis? A retrospective cohort study on Northern Irelands experience. Fractures are 4 times more common in ankylosed spine, commonly occur in cervical region, caused by minor trauma. Management challenges include fracture instability, multiple co-morbid population, high risk of neurological damage and difficult to position in theatre. Our aim is to find out what interventions do we undertake here? And does intervention have an impact on neurological outcome. Material and Methods: Retrospective analysis of all admission to RVH between 01/01/2000 and 31/12/2021 diagnosed with Ankylosing spondylitis fractures, data was obtained from fracture outcome Research Database (FORD) and inpatient fracture database. Fracture level, neurological function, time to surgery, treatment options and complication has been recorded and analyzed. Results: 33 patients included in this study, ages 34-94, 1 female and 32 males, 12% C2-C6, 38% C6-T4, 50% T5-L4. 90.9% treated surgically, 9.1% treated conservatively. 43% underwent surgery within 24 hours, 30% 24-48 hours, 17% 48-72 hours, 10% more than 72 hours. 70% posterior approach and fixation, 27% both anterior and posterior approach, 3% anterior fixation only. 67%neurological function remained the same, 19% improved, 14% worsen function post operatively. Complications: 3 deteriorations in neurological function, all operatively managed, 1 metal work failure, 4 wound infections, 3 mortalities at 30 days. Conclusion: Highly unstable fractures with high-risk neurological compromise. Patient intraoperative positioning is key, urgent stabilization is recommended. Posterior stabilization alone provides adequate fixation in most patients, but both should be considered in the correct patient.

## ID: AGS26/426

## P1008: Lumbar spinal cord injury elevates risk of psychiatric morbidity in matched cohort analysis

Melissa Romoff

1

, Michael Kim

1

, Emily Tse

1

, Courtney Spitzer

1

, Andy Ton

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Abhinav Sharma

1

, Justin Chan

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Don Park

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

Univeristy of California, Irvine, Orange, United States

Introduction: Spinal cord injury (SCI) is a life-altering condition associated with disproportionately high rates of depression and anxiety, with prevalence estimated to be two to three times greater than in the general population. These psychiatric comorbidities negatively affect rehabilitation engagement and functional outcomes However, few studies have isolated the psychological impact of SCI from that of trauma more broadly. This study aimed to compare the incidence of new-onset mood and anxiety disorders in patients with lumbar SCI versus matched trauma-exposed controls with long bone or pelvic fractures. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort study was conducted using the TriNetX United States Collaborative Network. Adult patients (≥ 18 years) with a new diagnosis of lumbar SCI were identified and matched 1:1 to patients with pelvic or long bone fractures. Patients with prior diagnoses of mood or anxiety disorders or prior psychiatric medication use were excluded. The primary outcomes were new diagnoses of mood or anxiety disorders and new prescriptions for antidepressants or anxiolytics between 14 and 90 days post-injury. Propensity score matching is controlled for age, sex, race, comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, obesity), and substance use. Psychiatric outcomes were assessed using Kaplan-Meier analysis, hazard ratios, risk ratios, and odds ratios. Results: A total of 39,992 patients with lumbar SCI were matched to 29,992 orthopedic trauma controls. New-onset mood or anxiety disorders occurred more frequently in the SCI cohort (1.44%) compared to controls (0.38%; RR 3.789, p < 0.0001).
