Lumbar SCI was also associated with significantly increased initiation of psychiatric medications, including SSRIs (RR 3.859), SNRIs (RR 4.831), atypical antidepressants (RR 4.006), tricyclic antidepressants (RR 8.227), and anxiolytics (RR 3.474; all p < 0.0001). SCI patients were over six times more likely to be referred for psychiatric services (RR 6.467, p < 0.0001). Conclusion: Lumbar SCI is associated with a markedly increased risk of new-onset mood and anxiety disorders, psychiatric medication initiation, and mental health service utilization compared to matched high-energy orthopedic trauma patients. These findings highlight a distinct psychiatric burden following SCI and underscore the need for early mental health screening and integrated psychiatric care in this population.

## ID: AGS26/536

## P1009: Thoracolumbar spine trauma outcomes: impact of ankylosing spondylitis and diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis

Josh Callaway

1

, Hania Shahzad

1

, Rahul Bhale

1

, Stevin Lu

1

, Westin Mace

1

, Kelsey Hideshima

1

, Devan Higginbotham

1

, Yashar Javidan

1

, Safdar Khan

1

, Wyatt Vander Voort

1

, Rolando Roberto

1

, Hai Le

1

1

UC Davis Medical Center, Sacramento, United States

Introduction: Thoracolumbar trauma in patients with ankylosing spondylitis (AS) and diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis (DISH) poses unique challenges due to spinal rigidity and altered biomechanics. This study compared demographics, surgical management, and outcomes of surgically treated thoracolumbar trauma patients with AS, DISH, and controls, hypothesizing distinct injury patterns, surgical needs, and outcomes in AS and DISH. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 189 patients undergoing surgery for thoracolumbar trauma at a level 1 trauma center (2015-2024): AS (n = 20), DISH (n = 32), and controls (n = 137). Data included demographics (age, sex, comorbidities, ASA), clinical factors (mechanism, ASIA scale, ISS), and surgical variables (approach, fusion levels, complications). Outcomes included neurological changes, hospital/ICU stay, complications, mortality, and readmission. Descriptive statistics and regression models were used; significance was set at p < 0.05. Results: AS (72 ± 13.6 years) and DISH (74 ± 11.5 years) patients were older than controls (45 ± 22.3 years, p < 0.0001) and more often male (AS 90%, DISH 75%, controls 64%). Type 2 diabetes was more prevalent in AS (45%) and DISH (41%) versus controls (10%, p ≤ 0.003). Osteoporosis was highest in DISH (34%) compared to AS (15%) and controls (6%, p < 0.001). Low-energy falls accounted for most injuries in AS (55%) and DISH (56%) versus 25% in controls (p = 0.001). Fusion constructs were longer in AS (5.8 ± 1.6 levels) and DISH (5.1±2.2) than controls (3.8 ± 2.3, p < 0.0002). Thoracic fusion predominated in AS (50%) and DISH (66%) (p = 0.0002). AS patients were significantly more likely to undergo open surgery (OR 8.09, p = 0.026). Clinical outcomes also diverged. ICU admission was more frequent in AS (55%) and DISH (47%) compared to controls (31%, p = 0.02). In-hospital mortality was highest in AS (15%) versus DISH (6%) and controls (3%, p = 0.037), and AS patients were nearly 800 times more likely to die in-hospital (p = 0.044). Postoperative complications, including infection and hardware failure, occurred more often in AS (25%) and DISH (22%) than controls (12%), though not always reaching statistical significance. A greater proportion of AS (50%) and DISH (47%) patients required skilled nursing or rehabilitation placement compared with controls (45%, p = 0.004). Conclusion: This study demonstrates how AS and DISH patients sustaining thoracolumbar trauma are demographically distinct, with advanced age, male predominance, and higher comorbidity burden. Their rigid, osteoporotic spines predispose them to unstable fractures from low-energy falls, unlike the high-energy mechanisms typical in controls. Surgically, they require longer constructs and more thoracic fusions, and AS patients often necessitate open approaches due to fracture complexity. Clinically, AS patients are particularly vulnerable, with markedly higher ICU use, complication rates, and in-hospital mortality. Both AS and DISH patients face significant functional decline, reflected in higher rates of post-acute rehabilitation needs. These findings underscore the importance of early recognition, aggressive pre-operative optimization, individualized surgical strategies, and comprehensive post-discharge care pathways. Improved risk stratification and multidisciplinary management may help mitigate poor outcomes in this fragile and high-risk population.

## ID: AGS26/591

## P1010: Characterization of thoracolumbar spine fractures caused by nautical vehicles in Cartagena, Colombia: multicenter study between the years 2020 and 2024

Luis Maldonado

1

, Carlos Alberto Carmona Lorduy

1

, Carlos Pacichana

2

, Gina Velásquez Solano

3

1

Universidad de Cartagena, Cartagena, Colombia

2

Universidad del Rosario, Bogotá, Colombia

3

Universidad de los Andes, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar spine fractures are widely described in the literature, highlighting their mechanism of injury associated with high-energy impact. These injuries have been extensively studied in traffic accidents, falls from heights, sports injuries, and war injuries. However, there is no comprehensive report on these types of injuries related to the use of nautical vehicles. Furthermore, there are no clear reports on the frequency, type, and consequences of these injuries in our setting. Therefore, the objective of this study is to identify the prevalence and characterize thoracolumbar fractures due to the use of nautical vehicles in two hospitals in Cartagena, Colombia. Material and Methods: A cross-sectional, descriptive, and retrospective study was conducted, evaluating patients over 18 years of age who were admitted to the Medihelp Clinic and the Bocagrande Hospital in Cartagena, Colombia, between January 1, 2020, and December 31, 2024, with a diagnosis of thoracolumbar spine fractures while using nautical vehicles. The study included a description of sociodemographic, clinical, and imaging factors, as well as the mechanism of trauma, complications, and functional outcomes. Results: Preliminary results were obtained from a total of 53 patients with thoracolumbar fractures during the study period, with an average of 12.5 patients per year. Most of the cases were foreigners (tourists), with young adults (35 to 40 years) being the most affected age group, and women were the predominant sex. Axial compression was identified as the primary mechanism of trauma. Conclusion: The characterization of these types of fractures associated with the use of these vehicles provides guidance on the risk factors and complications to which these patients are exposed. It also highlights the importance of having medical teams trained to manage these types of injuries early, especially in coastal and tourist cities, as well as the development of preventive and safety programs by local authorities.

## ID: AGS26/689

## P1011: Mortality risk factors in patients with osteoporotic vertebral fractures treated conservatively: a regional hospital cohort study

Hirotaka Ogi

1

, Hiroki Ushirozako

1

, Yukihiro Matsuyama

1

1

Hamamatsu University Schol of Medicine, Hamamatu, Japan

Introduction: With population aging, the number of patients with osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF) has been increasing. However, evidence on mortality risk factors following conservative treatment remains scarce. OVF, like hip fractures, is a serious condition directly linked to survival in the elderly. This study aimed to identify risk factors for all-cause mortality in OVF patients treated conservatively at Morimachi Hospital, the only acute-care hospital in the region. Material and Methods: We retrospectively analyzed 613 OVF patients who underwent conservative treatment at our hospital between 2016 and 2024. All-cause mortality (in-hospital and post-discharge) was set as the primary outcome. Clinical variables included age, sex, height, weight, body mass index (BMI), prognostic nutritional index (PNI), and comorbidities. Survival and mortality groups were compared using univariate analyses (t-tests or chi-square tests). Variables with p < 0.10 were entered into multivariate logistic regression, and odds ratios (ORs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were calculated to identify independent predictors. Results: During the observation period, there were 125 deaths (20.4%). Compared with survivors, the mortality group was older, had lower BMI and PNI, and had higher proportions of male patients and collagen diseases. Multivariate analysis identified the following independent predictors of mortality: age (OR 1.082, 95% CI 1.048-1.117, p < 0.001), male sex (OR 1.898, 95% CI 1.237-2.912, p = 0.003), low BMI (OR 0.845, 95% CI 0.792-0.902, p < 0.001), low PNI (OR 0.894, 95% CI 0.859-0.930, p < 0.001), and collagen disease (OR 3.050, 95% CI 1.364-6.820, p = 0.007). ROC analysis revealed cutoff values of PNI 36 (sensitivity 42%, specificity 84%, AUC 0.66) and BMI 20 (sensitivity 80%, specificity 46%, AUC 0.66). Conclusion: In 613 OVF patients treated conservatively, advanced age, male sex, low BMI, low PNI, and collagen disease were independent risk factors for mortality. PNI, which can be easily calculated from routine blood tests, is a practical prognostic marker, and PNI < 36 may serve as a threshold for identifying high-risk patients. A unique strength of this study is that, being conducted in the region’s sole acute-care hospital, nearly complete follow-up of all deaths was achieved, overcoming the common limitation of missing mortality data. These findings underscore the importance of nutritional assessment, lifestyle guidance, and targeted intervention for high-risk patients, and provide new evidence for prognostic management in OVF care.

## ID: AGS26/735

## P1012: Complex management of bilateral L5 pedicle fractures in a young adult: from pseudomeningocele to circumferential fusion

João Levy Melancia

1

, Joaquim Cruz Teixeira

1

, Nelson Silva

1

, José Hipólito Reis

1

, Diogo Belo

1

1

Hospital CUF Tejo, Lisboa, Portugal

Introduction: Bilateral pedicle fractures of L5 are exceedingly rare, particularly in young patients. Their management can be further complicated by concomitant traumatic injuries and postoperative sequelae. We present a unique case highlighting multiple unusual aspects, including delayed fixation due to abdominal trauma, pseudomeningocele formation, instrumentation failure, and the need for a combined circumferential approach. Material and Methods: A 23-year-old male sustained a high-energy motor vehicle accident resulting in bilateral L5 pedicle fractures. Surgery was initially postponed due to concurrent sigmoid rupture requiring urgent laparotomy and colostomy. During follow-up, MRI revealed a large lumbar pseudomeningocele. He underwent posterior L4-S1 fixation with neuronavigation, L5 laminectomy, pseudomeningocele drainage, and dural repair. Postoperatively, neurological function improved, although complicated by urinary tract infection and partial wound dehiscence, treated conservatively. At 24 years of age, he developed worsening back pain and radiculopathy. Imaging demonstrated progressive instrumentation failure, with bilateral S1 screw fractures. A revision surgery was performed in three sequential stages within the same operative session: (1) posterior removal and replacement of fractured screws; (2) anterior transperitoneal L5-S1 discectomy and interbody fusion with cage (Synfix, DePuy Synthes) and bone morphogenetic protein; (3) final posterior fixation with rods between L5 and S1. The postoperative course was uneventful, with significant pain relief and neurological stability. Results: This case illustrates several uncommon features: bilateral L5 pedicle fractures, development of a large pseudomeningocele, contraindication to initial anterior fusion due to abdominal trauma, and subsequent bilateral screw fractures. The feasibility of combining anterior and posterior approaches sequentially within the same operative session ultimately enabled durable circumferential stabilization. Conclusion: Complex L5 pedicle fractures in young patients may require individualized, multi-step surgical strategies. When complicated by pseudomeningocele and hardware failure, circumferential fusion performed in a single operative session can provide definitive stabilization and favorable clinical outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/800

## P1013: Association of a FRAX-guided clinical pathway with reduced refracture risk in osteoporotic vertebral fractures: a retrospective cohort study in LMICs

wenjie wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Osteoporotic vertebral fractures in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) are associated with high refracture rates (68% at 3 years) and limited access to dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA), necessitating cost-effective alternatives to guideline-recommended care. To evaluate whether a FRAX-guided perioperative pathway reduces refracture risk in patients undergoing percutaneous vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty (PVP/PKP). Material and Methods: This retrospective cohort study compared 400 patients managed under a FRAX-guided pathway (January 2022-January 2024) with 400 historical controls (January 2019-December 2021) at a single center in county China. After applying inclusion/exclusion criteria and propensity score matching (1:1 ratio, caliper = 0.1), 200 matched pairs (400 patients) were included in the final analysis. Inclusion criteria: age ≥ 50 years, radiologically confirmed osteoporotic fractures, and complete 12-month follow-up. A 4-phase pathway: (1) preoperative FRAX-based risk stratification (cutoff ≥ 15%), (2) intraoperative cement volume restriction (≤ 4.0 mL/vertebra), (3) algorithm-guided antiresorptive therapy, and (4) SMS-based adherence monitoring. Results: Primary outcome: 12-month radiographic refracture incidence. Secondary outcomes: bone health metrics (DXA T-score, FRAX reduction), functional recovery (Oswestry Disability Index), and cost-effectiveness. The FRAX-guided group (n = 200 matched pairs) had a 52% lower 12-month refracture incidence (12.7% vs. 26.1%; RR = 0.48, 95% CI 0.29-0.81) and a 49% reduction in cement leakage (9.2% vs. 18.3%). Functional improvement (ODI Δ = 10.8) and cost-effectiveness (ICER = $1,980/QALY) were significant. Conclusions: A FRAX-guided perioperative pathway significantly reduced refracture rates and costs in a resource-limited setting, providing a scalable, DXA-independent model for LMICs. This study supports task-shifting and low-tech strategies to improve osteoporosis care where guidelines are currently impractical.
Keywords: Osteoporotic vertebral fracture; FRAX tool; LMICs; Refracture prevention; Cost-effectiveness; Task-shifting

## ID: AGS26/803

## P1014: mFRAX-CL: a resource-efficient preoperative risk score for osteoporotic fractures in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs)

wenjie wu

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

, Li Huo

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Osteoporotic fractures (OPFs) in low-resource settings lack accessible risk stratification tools. A simplified, cost-effective tool is urgently needed to guide anti-osteoporosis interventions and reduce refracture rates. To develop and validate the mFRAX-CL score, a preoperative risk score for OPF patients without bone mineral density (BMD) testing. Material and Methods: This retrospective diagnostic accuracy study followed TRIPOD guidelines and analyzed data from 800 patients undergoing percutaneous vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures at a district hospital in Chongqing, China (January 2020-December 2022). The 2020-2022 cohort (n = 720) was used for model development, while the 2023 cohort (n = 80) served as a temporal validation set. Exposure: mFRAX-CL score (height loss ≥ 3 cm, prior fractures, age, sex, BMI, smoking). Results: Among 720 included patients (mean [SD] age, 68.4 [8.2] years; 562 [78%] female), 152 (21.1%) developed refractures. Primary outcome was 1-year postoperative refracture (vertebral/non-vertebral). Model performance was assessed via area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), sensitivity, calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow test), and decision curve analysis (DCA). The mFRAX-CL score showed significantly higher discrimination than FRAX without BMD (AUC = 0.87 vs. 0.71; ΔAUC = 0.16, p < .001). Sensitivity and specificity were 91% and 76% at a threshold of 8 points. Calibration showed minimal deviation (slope = 0.95, intercept = -0.05; Hosmer-Lemeshow p = .21). DCA indicated net clinical benefit at threshold probabilities of 10% to 35%. After adjusting for confounding via propensity score weighting, high-risk patients (score ≥ 8) who received zoledronic acid had a significantly lower refracture rate than those who did not (12% vs 35%; adjusted odds ratio = 0.30; 95% CI, 0.15-0.60; p < .001). Screening cost was $1 per case (incremental cost-effectiveness ratio = $120 per quality-adjusted life-year). Conclusions and Relevance: The mFRAX-CL score, which omits BMD, demonstrated superior discrimination and cost-effectiveness, offering a scalable tool for LMICs to reduce refracture-related morbidity and mortality. The observed association with zoledronic acid benefit warrants confirmation in a randomized trial.

## ID: AGS26/805

## P1015: Feasibility and safety of a 23-hour discharge protocol after percutaneous kyphoplasty in county-level hospitals: a dual-cohort study

Wenjie Wu

1

, Li Huo

1

, Xiaojing Si

1

, Chuanqi Zou

1

1

The People's Hospital of Banan District, Chongqing, China

Introduction: Short-stay protocols following percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP) are increasingly adopted in high-resource settings, yet their feasibility and safety in county-level hospitals remain underexplored. This study evaluates a 23-hour discharge pathway for PKP patients in a Chinese rural healthcare context. Material and Methods: A dual-cohort design was employed: • Retrospective cohort: 600 eligible PKP cases were selected from a database of 1000 procedures (2019-2024) across Chongqing county hospitals. Patients were stratified into short-stay (< 24 hours) and conventional-stay (> 48 hours) groups. • Prospective validation cohort: 100 consecutive patients will be enrolled from October 2025 using a structured 23-hour discharge criteria (cement volume < 5 mL, no cement leakage, VAS improvement > 50%, independent ambulation). Data collected included cement volume, leakage rate (classified per Nieuwenhuijse), preoperative ODI/VAS, complications, 30-day readmission, 90-day refracture rate, hospitalization costs, EQ-5D satisfaction, and family caregiver competency (10-item scale). Economic analysis incorporated both direct medical costs and indirect costs (caregiver lost wages, transportation). Statistical analyses included propensity score matching (1:1, caliper 0.2) for retrospective data and sample size estimation (n = 100, 80% power, α = 0.05) for prospective comparison. Results: Preliminary retrospective analysis (n = 600) showed: • Cement leakage rate: 8.3% (Type B: 5.2%, Type C: 2.1%, Type D: 1.0%). • Median VAS improvement: 5 points (IQR: 4-6). • 30-day readmission rate: 2.5% in short-stay vs. 3.8% in conventional-stay (p = 0.28). • Refracture rate at 90 days: 1.7% vs. 2.1% (p = 0.65). Prospective outcomes will be reported upon completion. Conclusion: A 23-hour discharge protocol after PKP is feasible and safe in county-level hospitals, with no significant increase in short-term complications or readmissions. The protocol supports resource-efficient care without compromising patient outcomes, suitable for broader implementation in grassroots healthcare settings.
Keywords: Percutaneous kyphoplasty, short-stay surgery, discharge protocol, county hospital, cement leakage, health economics

## ID: AGS26/957

## P1016: Is indirect decompression & stabilaization enough for patients MIS fixation for thoraco-lumbar burst fracture with incomplete neurology, 2 years follow up

Baid Prashant

1

1

Department of Spine Surgery, Apollo Multispecialty Hospitals, Kolkata, India

Introduction: Open spinal fixation and posterior decompression remains the corner stone for treating thoraco-lumbar burst fracture with neuro deficit. Many surgeons have also tried anterior approach to direct decompress and reconstruct the spine with equally good or better results. Although both approaches have given good results in terms of stabilization and neural decompression but these approaches causes severe morbidity in peri and post op periods. Wound dehiscence, infection, blood loss , prolong hospitalizations are some of them. We introduce a MIS technique that allow stable fixation of these fracture using MIS pedicle screw fixation spanning the fracture and indirectly decompressing the neural elements by rod distraction technique causing the fracture to reduce. A vertebroplasty / Kyphoplasty is done at the fractures level for anterior support and preventing collapse of the vertebra. Material and Methods: This is a prospective observational study of 32 patients who had undergone this procedure. All the patients were assessed clinically & radiologically. Patient with TL burst fracture with motor power in lower limb ≥ 3 and without bowel and bladder involvement were chosen in the study. Assessment of all patients were done using VAS score, wedging angle of the vertebra and MRC grading of the lower limb muscles. Results: 32 patients underwent MIS pedicular fixation with/ without fenestrated screw with vertebroplasty / kyphoplasty at the fractured vertebral level after indirect decompression at the compression level and after restoring significant height of fractured vertebra using rod distraction technique and or positioning of the patient (extension). There were significant improvement (p < 0.05) `VAS scores in the immediate and late post operative periods. In addition there was significant improvements in motor power of lower limb muscles (MRC grade) in of the patients who had neuro deficits in immediate and early post operative period which was maintained till late follow up (2 years). 30 out of 32 patients were self ambulatory by first week post op without any assistance and rest 2 were ambulatory by 3 week of surgery. Conclusion: MIS pedicle screw stabilization with vertebroplasty / kyphoplasty after indirect decompression using rod distraction technique is safe and rewarding procedure and avoid morbidity of decompression laminectomy in these subset of patient with power in lower limbs not less than MRC grading 3 and without bowel/ bladder involvement

## ID: AGS26/966

## P1017: Spinal chronic subdural hematoma: a case report

Arun Thangavelu

1

, Marc Prod'homme

2

, Jean-Yves Fournier

3

, Ferdinand Krappel

1,4

, Samuel Schmid

1

, Wolfgang Perrig

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, EUROSPINE Center of Excellence, Brig, Switzerland

2

Clinic CIC, Clarens, Switzerland

3

Department of Neurosurgery, Hospital Sion, Brig, Switzerland

4

Wirbelsäulenzentrum - Regenerative Medizin-Schmertherapie, Brig, Switzerland

Introduction: Spinal hematomas are rare conditions that can cause spinal cord compression, radicular pain, and irreversible neurological deficits. Spinal subdural hematomas (spinal SDH) represent 4.1% of all spinal hematomas. Risk factors include coagulopathies, blood dyscrasias, lumbar puncture, spinal surgery, trauma, and vascular malformations. Spinal SDH can be classified as traumatic, iatrogenic, or spontaneous. Traumatic spinal SDH, first described in 1826, has only 58 documented cases and accounts for 9.7% of all spinal SDH incidents. Material and Methods: Case presentation: An 86-year-old male undergoing anticoagulation therapy for atrial fibrillation experienced severe pain after a spinal sprain while cycling on 20/08/2024. An MRI of the lumbar spine to T11 showed no abnormalities. The pain worsened, and on 02/01/2025, the patient developed progressive paresis of the left leg and sensory loss below T10 on the left side. Clinical Findings: The patient showed fluctuating proximal left-sided leg weakness (M2-3), sensory deficit below T10 on the left side, pronounced ataxia, inability to walk and urinary and fecal retention. Investigations: Emergency MRI revealed an intradural extramedullary mass at T11 level, emphasizing the left side. A thoracic spine MRI identified an intradural extramedullary hematoma. Intervention: The patient underwent microsurgical laminectomy at T10 and T11 levels, evacuation of subarachnoid extramedullary hematoma, and septal resection with left-side neurolysis. Results: The patient was evaluated at 6 weeks and 6 months postoperatively with resumed anticoagulation therapy. At 6 months, patient was pain-free, showed only minimal atactic gait with walking sticks. Neurological evaluation showed mild left hip flexor weakness (M4-M5), with restored tactile sensitivity. Patellar and Achilles reflexes were symmetrically reduced, with negative Babinski sign. The left knee-heel test remained dysmetric but improved. Bowel function improved, while micturation remained limited with Suprapubic catheter. Conclusion: Spinal chronic subdural hematoma are extremely rare and can manifest with rapid neurological symptoms, with MRI being the diagnostic tool of choice. Anticoagulated Patients with trauma history and progressive back pain should be assessed for spinal SDH. For small lesions without cord compression, conservative management is appropriate. However, spinal SDH above conus medullaris with neurological deficits requires urgent surgery due to ischemic necrosis risk.

## ID: AGS26/1030

## P1018: Aseptic vertebral necrosis in thoracolumbar trauma

Oleksii Nekhlopochyn

1

, Vadim Verbov

1

, Ievgen Cheshuk

1

, Milan Vorodi

1

1

Romodanov Neurosurgery Institute, Kyiv, Ukraine

Introduction: Instability of traumatic spinal injuries is a major indication for urgent stabilization surgery to prevent neurological decline and progressive deformity. In military and resource-limited conditions, however, staged evacuation often delays surgery. This has revealed rare but clinically important complications, among them post-traumatic aseptic vertebral body necrosis (AVN), which differs from Kümmell’s disease and requires further analysis. The aim was to describe and analyze clinical cases of aseptic vertebral necrosis following unstable thoracolumbar injuries. Materials and Methods: A retrospective study was conducted at the Romodanov Neurosurgery Institute (Kyiv, Ukraine) from 2021 to 2024, supplemented by teleconsultations from Kyiv hospitals. Inclusion required unstable thoracolumbar injury without stabilization in the first two weeks. Exclusion criteria were penetrating trauma, septic spondylitis/discitis, or prior spinal injury/surgery. Clinical data included demographics, trauma mechanism, neurological status (ASIA), AO Spine TLSICS classification, TLICS score, and radiological findings. Results: Nineteen patients met the inclusion criteria. Among them, six (31.6%) developed post-traumatic aseptic necrosis of the vertebral body. The median age was 40 years (range 18-61), and two-thirds of patients were male. Mechanisms of trauma included falls from height, ground-level falls, and traffic accidents, with the thoracolumbar junction being the most frequent site of injury. According to AO Spine classification, the majority of injuries were type B2 or type C. Neurological status on admission ranged widely: most patients presented with complete deficits (ASIA A), some with partial deficits (ASIA B), and others without neurological impairment (ASIA E). In patients with AVN, imaging demonstrated progressive lysis of the anterior vertebral body, in some cases extending to the adjacent endplates, and in severe cases resulting in near-total destruction of the vertebra. Importantly, laboratory markers such as white blood cell count and C-reactive protein remained within normal limits, and intraoperative cultures were consistently negative, confirming the aseptic nature of the process. The presence of chronic septic foci was not associated with the development of necrosis. All patients with verified necrosis underwent stabilization surgery, which included decompression, corpectomy with cage implantation, and posterior pedicle screw fixation. In every case, surgical intervention arrested further bone lysis. Follow-up CT scans obtained two to six months after surgery confirmed the integrity of the implants and demonstrated no further progression of destructive changes. Conclusions: Post-traumatic aseptic vertebral necrosis is a rare but significant complication of delayed stabilization in unstable thoracolumbar injuries. The process is non-infectious and most likely ischemic in origin, aggravated by pathological mobility of the injured segment. Early surgical stabilization should be regarded not only as a preventive measure against neurological deterioration but also as essential for avoiding destructive changes of the vertebral body. These findings underline the importance of timely stabilization, especially in wartime or delayed-care scenarios.

## ID: AGS26/1041

## P1019: Casting or bracing? Long-term clinical and radiological outcomes of two conservative modalities in thoracolumbar vertebral fractures

Alim Baymurat

1

, Halil Gok

2

1

Gazi University Medical Faculty, Department of Orthopaedics and Traumatology, Ankara, Turkey

2

Ankara Etlik City Hospital, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: To evaluate and compare the long-term clinical and radiological outcomes of hyperextension casting versus Jewett bracing in the conservative management of thoracolumbar vertebral compression fractures. Material and Methods: Thirty-five patients with stable compression fractures between T10 and L2 treated from 2002 to 2012 were retrospectively reviewed (Brace: n = 18, Cast: n = 17). Outcomes included Visual Analog Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), kyphotic angle (Cobb method), bone mineral density (BMD) index, and hospital stay. Median values were used; Mann–Whitney U and Chi-square tests were applied. Results: There were no significant differences in age, sex, or follow-up duration (p > 0.05). However, the brace group had significantly higher BMD Index (27.3 [26.8–28.1] vs. 26.3 [25.3–26.9]; p = 0.005*) and shorter hospital stay (1.0 [1.0–1.0] vs. 2.0 [2.0–2.0] days; p < 0.001*). Functional outcomes (ODI, VAS) were better in the brace group (p < 0.001*), while kyphotic progression was more limited in the cast group (p = 0.042). Conclusion: While the Jewett brace may offer an advantage in terms of early mobilization and functional recovery, hyperextension casting appears to be more effective in limiting kyphotic progression over time.

## ID: AGS26/1114

## P1020: Spinal injuries in professional football players: a media-based study

Lara Silva

1

, Pedro Diniz

2,3

, Ricardo Rodrigues-Pinto

1,4

1

Instituto de Ciências Biomédicas Abel Salazar, Porto, Portugal

2

Orthopedics Department, CHU Brugmann, Brussels, Belgium

3

Department of Bioengineering and iBB, Institute for Bioengineering and Biosciences, Instituto Superior Técnico, University of Lisbon, Lisbon, Portugal

4

Orthopedics Department, Centro Hospitalar Universitário de Santo António, Porto, Portugal

Introduction: Football is the most popular sport in the world and is associated with a wide range of injuries. Although lower limb injuries are the most prevalent, spine injuries can be highly debilitating in professional athletes, leading to prolonged recovery periods and chronic pain. The lumbar spine is particularly relevant, as it is the most frequent site of complaints among elite athletes, typically presenting as lower back pain. Our aim is to analyze the most common spinal injuries associated with lower back pain in professional football players, examining their distribution by playing position, tier, and world region, as well as assessing their impact in terms of time lost due to injury, injury recurrence over a two-year period, and match participation time before and after the spinal injury. Material and Methods: Average match participation time 2 seasons before the injury (Year -2), 1 season preceding the injury (Year -1), season of the injury (Year 0) and 1 season after injury (Year 1), time lost due to injury and injury recurrence, along with players’ characteristics, leagues, tiers, teams, and playing position data were collected from Transfermarkt.com through a customized web scraping framework. Additional data on spine injuries were manually gathered from reliable online sources. The statistical analysis was performed using IBM SPSS Statistics with statistical significance set at p < 0.05. Results: Among 407 professional football players, herniated disc was the most reported specific spinal injury (14.7%) followed by lumbar fracture (13.5%). Injury distribution differed significantly by severity levels (p < 0.001), tiers (p < 0.001), and world regions (p = 0.007), although the last findings may reflect sample bias due to overrepresentation of the first tier and European players. No significant association was found with playing position. Severe injuries accounted for the highest proportion (44.2%), mainly herniated discs. Injury recurrence occurred in 9.3% of players, most commonly in disc herniation (11.7%), without significant associations. Match participation declined between the season preceding injury and season of the injury and remained reduced one season post-injury. In the season preceding the injury, significant differences were observed between injury types and the match participation (p = 0.04), with players with lumbar fractures (52.5 minutes) showing the highest average. Conclusion: This study provides a comprehensive analysis of spinal injuries in professional football players using media-based data. Herniated discs and lumbar fractures were the most reported injuries, with severity levels differing significantly across injury types. Match participation declined between the season preceding injury and season of the injury and remained reduced one season post-injury. These findings underscore the impact of spinal injuries on recovery and match participation. Our study also highlights the value of media-based data as a complementary tool for injury surveillance in sports injury, while acknowledging of limitations related to diagnostic precision and possible sample biases.

## ID: AGS26/1215

## P1021: Mechanical instability after civilian thoracolumbar gunshot injuries: a systematic review to support classification development

2,3

, Ignacio Cirillo

4,5,6

, Juan P. Cabrera

7

, Otávio Vitório Alvarenga Pereira

8

, Emiliano Vialle

8

, Charles Carazzo

9

, Michael Dittmar

10

, Alfredo Guiroy

11

, Ratko Yurac

12

, Gregory Schroeder

13

1

Centro Médico Integral Fitz Roy, Sanatorio Guemes, Hospital General de Agudos Dr. Teodoro Alvarez, Buenos Aires, Argentina

2

Sanatorio Guemes, Buenos Aires, Argentina

3

Hospital General de Agudos Dr. Teodoro Álvarez, Buenos Aires, Argentina

4

Hospital del Trabajador, Clínica Las Condes, Universidad del Desarrollo, Santiago, Chile

5

Clínica Las Condes, Santiago, Chile

6

Universidad Andrés Bello, Santiago, Chile

7

Department of Neurosurgery, Hospital Clínico Regional de Concepción, Concepción, Chile, Concepcion, Chile

8

Spinal Surgery Group, Department of Orthopedics, Cajuru Hospital, Catholic University of Parana, Curitiba, Brazil, Cajuru, Brazil

9

Neurosurgery, University of Passo Fundo, São Vicente de Paulo Hospital - Passo Fundo - RS, Brazil, Passo Fundo, Brazil

10

Centro Médico Puerta de Hierro. Zapopan, México., Zapopan, Mexico

11

Neurosurgery. Elite Spine Health and Wellness Center (Florida, USA, Florida, United States

12

Spine Unit, Department of Traumatology, Clínica Alemana, Santiago, Chile, Santiago, Chile

13

Rothman Institute, Philladelphia, United States

Introduction: Although spinal gunshot wounds are often viewed as inherently stable, a clear consensus on the definition or classification of unstable fracture patterns is lacking. We aim to identify risk factors for mechanical instability in thoracolumbar gunshot wounds to develop a new classification system. Material and Methods: A systematic review following PRISMA 2020 guidelines was conducted across multiple databases from January 1, 1990, to September 12, 2024 (PROSPERO: CRD42023458804). Results: Of 2,400 records, 33 studies (3,195 patients) met inclusion criteria, mostly retrospective and single-center with varying definitions of instability. Direct comparison was limited due to clinical heterogeneity. Only 12 studies defined mechanical instability, mainly using the Denis three-column theory. Instability was reported in less than 10% of cases, except for one higher-rate outlier. The main markers included vertebral body comminution and potential ligamentous injury. Management was mainly conservative, with a low fixation rate (1.5-9.7%). Imaging relied on CT and static X-rays, with some studies advocating for dynamic radiographs. Conclusion: There is a lack of standardized definitions for mechanical instability in civilian thoracolumbar gunshot injuries. Four recurrent unstable patterns were detected, with vertebral body comminution as the most consistent marker. The need for a standardized classification system and further prospective studies is evident.

## ID: AGS26/1246

## P1022: Does age influence the clinical and radiological outcomes of surgical treatment for thoracolumbar junction fractures?

mouldi lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Abdenasser Ben Salem

1,1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, Yassine Aloui

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar junction fractures represent a frequent cause of spinal trauma. Surgical management aims to restore spinal alignment, ensure stability, and improve functional and clinical results. However, age-related differences in bone quality, bone healing and comorbidities may significantly affect outcomes. Understanding the impact of age on both functional and radiological parameters is crucial for optimizing surgical strategies. The aim of this study was to evaluate the influence of patient age on postoperative results after posterior surgical fixation of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Material and Methods: It is a retrospective study including 120 patients surgically treated for thoracolumbar fractures between January 2010 and January 2020. All patients underwent posterior pedicle screw fixation, with variable assembly (long, mixed and short). Our populations are divided into two age groups: Group A: patients younger than 60 years and Group B: patients aged 60 years and older. Functional outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and Dallas Pain Questionnaire. Radiological outcomes were evaluated by measuring regional kyphosis (RK), vertebral kyphosis (VK), traumatic regional angulation (TRA), and Beck index at postoperative and the last follow-up. The evaluation of the results was done by calculating the loss of correction of radiological parameters between the postoperative radiographs and the last follow-up radiographs as well as at final follow-up. Complications including infection, hardware failure, nonunion, and material removal were also analyzed. Results: Group A included 72 patients with a mean follow-up of 58.7 months, while Group B included 48 patients with a mean follow-up of 55.1 months. Functional outcomes were significantly better in younger patients: mean VAS (1.82 vs 3.37, p = 0.03), ODI (17.2 vs 34.77, p < 0.001), and Dallas score (10.1 vs 24.68, p < 0.001). Radiological analysis showed a greater loss of correction in elderly patients for CR (2.14° vs 0.75°, p = 0.03), VK (1.89° vs 0.62°, p = 0.001), and ART (2.17° vs 0.77°, p = 0.03). Postoperative kyphotic angles were also significantly higher in Group B (RK 14.17° vs 11.71°, p = 0.02; CV 15.17° vs 11.74°, p = 0.03). However, we didn’t find a statistically significant difference in Beck index (p = 0.31). Complications were more frequent in elderly patients, with higher rates of hardware failure (16.7% vs 4.2%, p = 0.06) and nonunion (8.3% vs 0%, p = 0.04). Material removal was more common in younger patients (34.7% vs 19.4%, p = 0.01). However, no significant differences were noted in surgical site infection (5.6% vs 8.3%, p = 0.06). Conclusion: Age has a significant impact on functional and radiological outcomes after surgical treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Patients under 60 years of age achieved superior pain relief, functional recovery, and maintenance of correction, while older patients had higher rates of loss of correction, hardware failure, and nonunion. This may be explained by the significant mechanical stresses exerted on the hinge between a less mobile thoracic spine and a mobile lumbar spine, as well as osteoporosis. These results confirm the role of age as a prognostic factor to be taken into account when planning surgical management.

## ID: AGS26/1253

## P1023: Impact of Body Mass Index on functional clinical and radiological outcomes after posterior surgical treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures

Mouldi Lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Mohamed Hassine

1

, Hamza Daly

1

, Abdenasser Ben Salem

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Obesity is an increasingly common health issue worldwide and is associated with higher perioperative risks in spine surgery. Its impact on functional recovery, radiological correction, and complication rates following thoracolumbar fracture surgery remains controversial. The aim of this study was to assess the influence of body mass index (BMI) on postoperative outcomes after posterior fixation of thoracolumbar junction fractures. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study including 120 patients operated on our department for thoracolumbar junction fractures between January 2010 and January 2020. All patients underwent posterior fusion assembly. Patients were divided into two groups according to BMI: • Group A: normal weight/overweight (mean BMI 25.8 kg/m2). • Group B: obese (mean BMI 32.7 kg/m2). Clinical outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), and the Dallas Pain Questionnaire (Dallas score). Radiological parameters included vertebral kyphosis (VK), regional kyphosis (RK), traumatic regional angulation (TRA), and Beck index, evaluated at last follow-up. Complications such as infection, hardware failure, nonunion, and implant removal were also recorded. Results: Group A included 66 patients with a mean follow-up of 59.5 months, while Group B included 34 patients with a mean follow-up of 49 months. Functional outcomes were poorer in obese patients: mean VAS (2.97 vs 2.06, p = 0.03) and Dallas score (20.29 vs 12.12, p = 0.001). No significant difference was found for ODI (30.76 vs 19.09, p = 0.14). Radiological analysis showed slightly higher postoperative kyphotic angles in obese patients (RK 14.39° vs 11.65°, p = 0.05), while differences in VK, TRA, and Beck index were not statistically significant. Infection occurred more frequently in obese patients (4.8% vs 2.5%, p = 0.08), while rates of hardware failure (8.8% vs 7.6%), nonunion (5.9% vs 3%), and implant removal (29.4% vs 28.8%) were comparable between groups. Conclusion: Obesity has a negative impact in clinical and radiological outcomes after thoracolumbar junction fracture surgery, with higher pain levels, worse Dallas scores, and greater postoperative kyphosis. Although complication rates did not increase significantly, the upward trend in infection rates suggests the need for optimized perioperative management in obese patients. These findings highlight the importance of BMI as a relevant prognostic factor to be considered in surgical planning and post-operative care.

## ID: AGS26/1312

## P1024: Risk factor analysis for predicting vertebral body re-collapse after posterior instrumented fusion of thoracolumbar junction burst fractures

Mouldi Lamouchi

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, Raboudi Hamza

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Guermazi Yahia

1

, Yassine Aloui

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: The thoracolumbar junction (T12-L1) represents a transitional zone between the rigid thoracic spine and the mobile lumbar spine, making it particularly vulnerable to fractures. Among these, burst fractures are the most common and often require posterior instrumented fusion. While initial correction is usually obtained, postoperative re-collapse of the vertebral body remains a major complication that can compromise both functional and radiological outcomes. The aim of our study is to evaluate risk factors and predictors for postoperative re-collapse in thoracolumbar burst fractures. Material and Methods: It is a retrospective study including 61 patients who underwent posterior instrumented fusion for thoracolumbar junction burst fractures between 2010 and 2023. The surgical procedure consisted in short, long or combined segment fusion. The population was divided into two groups: well maintained (G1) and re-collapse (G2) group. Patients were assigned to the re-collapse group if their vertebral body height loss was greater than 20% at any follow-up compared with immediate postoperative results. Results: The mean age was 49.38 years (range from 24 to 74 years), with male predominance (57.4% of cases). The mean BMI was 28.65 kg/m2. Diabetes was reported in 19.7% of cases. Fracture locations were T12 in 29 cases (39.3%) and L1 in 36 cases (59%). Short fixation was performed in 11 patients (18%), mixed fixation in 21 (34.4%), and long fixation in 29 (47.5%). Laminectomy was performed in 3 cases (4.9%). The mean follow-up was 41 months. Re-collapse occurred in 18 of 61 patients (29.5%). We didn’t find any significant difference between the two groups for age, gender, BMI, diabetes, neurological deficit, posterior wall recession, intra-canal fragment and pedicle and articular fractures. Multivariate logistic regression analysis identified three independent risk factors short segment fixation (p = 0.0001; Ratio odds = 20; 95% CI [3.67-108.84]), surgical site infection (p = 0.3; Ratio odds = 3.47; 95% CI [2.32-5.18]) and nonunion (p = 0.006; Ratio odds = 4; 95% CI [2.54-6.29]). The long segment fixation is a protector factor for re-collapse (p = 0.008; Ratio odds = 0.19; 95% CI [0.05-0.69]). Laminectomy and bone grafting were not risk factors for re-collapse. Conclusion: The occurrence of re-collapse remains possible but unpredictable no matter the surgical technique used. The type of segment fixation is a crucial parameter to consider. Careful surgical planning and long-term follow-up are recommended, particularly in patients treated with short fixation at the thoracolumbar junction.

## ID: AGS26/1373

## P1025: Thoracolumbar fractures. arthrodesis or not?

Carlos Mariano Nirino

1

1

Hospital Santamarina, Buenos Aires, Argentina

Introduction: The aim of this paper is to describe the results obtained in 91 patients, all between 18 and 70 years, operated in the last 6 years with thoracolumbar vertebral fractures post traumatic. In all cases we used the same technique, which consists of fixing two levels above and two levels below the fractured vertebrae including it in the osteosynthesis system getting full or partial reduction of the fracture without arthrodesis. After the fracture consolidation we proceed to the removal of the implant between 6 to 10 months so all patients recover the capacity of flexo extension of their thoracolumbar spine. Most of the fractures are produced by falls from their own height either standing or seated, secondly traffic accidents and finally by osteoporosis, which is a low energy mechanism that is not included in this work. Material and Methods: 91 patients were evaluated, 58 men and 33 women. The complaint was for post traumatic back or low back pain by falling from their own height standing or sitting, in the 64% of cases, with no more than 48 hours of evolution that did not get better with rest and got worse with mobilization. The rest of the cases were traffic accidents. All patients were studied by MRI and X-rays. All fractures were treated by the same surgeon within the first 72 hours of making the diagnosis. Fractures were divided as follows: T9 (1), T10 (7), T11 (20), T12 (26), L1 (21), L2 (12), L3 (2) and L4 (2). 77% of cases were type A fractures according to the AO classification, and the remaining 23% were type B, which were associated with rupture of the posterior wall and invasion of the spinal canal. Only 3 fractures had neurological and motor compromise C, according to Frenkel classification. The rest of the fractures were Frenkel E. Results: All patients returned to their usual activities. It took them 42 days on average to return to work and from 6 to 9 months to their sports activities with the exception of the 3 Frenkel C. As postoperative complications patients had scar hygromas that were cured with puncture and compressive bandage. Everyone referred complete or partial improvement of their symptoms. In 1 case we had a postoperative complication which was the rupture of one of the lower transpedicular screws of the system at 3 months; but when we removed the implant the pain disappeared without complications. Conclusion: This method is usually used in our department to treat thoracolumbar fractures. In our experience this type of fractures are one of the most common, it is a severe pathology that should be diagnosed and treated early. We always consider surgical treatment (temporary fixation without arthrodesis) because patients are able to move quickly without getting risks of more complex injuries during the process of consolidating. Patients had no important complications and in all cases there was a complete remission of symptoms and return to normal activity. They were followed for around two years after surgery.

## ID: AGS26/1801

## P1027: Nail gun-induced penetrating thoracic spinal cord injury: case report and surgical approach

Leonard Sang Xian Leong

1

, Eu Gene Teo

2

, Ping An Teh

1

1

Hospital Sungai Buloh, Selangor, Malaysia

2

Hospital Umum Sarawak, Sarawak, Malaysia

Introduction: Non-missile penetrating spinal cord injuries (NMPSCI) are rare, accounting for less than 1.5% of penetrating spinal injuries. Surgical management is technically demanding, as improper handling may exacerbate neurological deficits. A tailored operative strategy is essential to ensure safe extraction of the foreign body and preservation of cord function. Material and Methods: We report a 23-year-old male who sustained an accidental nail gun injury to the thoracic spine at work. He presented with left lower limb monoparesis (MRC grade 3) and sensory loss below T6, with intact sphincter function. CT revealed a metallic nail traversing the posterior thoracic region with its tip embedded in the spinal cord at T5. Emergency surgery was performed with the following key steps: • Precise intraoperative localization using fluoroscopy. • Microsurgical nail extraction under direct vision to minimize secondary cord injury. • Nail removal prior to laminectomy to prevent cord vibration during bone work. • Adequate laminectomy for decompression in case of cord edema and for optimal dural exposure. • Watertight dural repair to prevent CSF leakage. • Intraoperative ultrasonography to inspect the cord, avoiding unnecessary durotomy or myelotomy. • Continuous neurophysiological monitoring to safeguard neural integrity. Results: Postoperatively, neurological function was preserved without deterioration. Motor power improved to MRC grade 4, though residual sensory deficit persisted below T6. MRI showed minimal cord edema, no hematoma, and no residual compression. The patient mobilized with assistance and was discharged well one week later. Conclusion: Successful management of NMPSCI requires a structured surgical strategy. Critical principles include controlled foreign body extraction, adequate exposure, meticulous dural repair, and the adjunctive use of ultrasonography and neuromonitoring. Learning point: A stepwise microsurgical approach with careful intraoperative decision-making allows safe removal of intramedullary foreign bodies while maximizing neurological recovery.

## ID: AGS26/2292

## P1028: What is the most accurate and reliable method for measuring interspinous widening in CT?

Mohamed Ali

1

1

Prince Mohamed Ben AbdelAziz Hospital, Riyadh second Cluster, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

Introduction: Interspinous widening (ISW) showed varying accuracy in detecting posterior ligamentous complex (PLC) injury on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) due to the lack of standardized methods of measurement. The aim of this study is to compare the accuracy of various methods to measure ISW on computed tomography (CT) in detecting PLC injuries on for thoracolumbar fractures (TLFs). Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 215 consecutive patients with acute TLFs (T10-L2). Three reviewers evaluated MRI and CT images. We massed 4 ISW measurements:1) supraspinous distance (SSD): distance between the most posterior aspect of the two spinous processes; 2) midspinous distance (MSD): between the midpoints of the spinous processes; 3) interlaminar distance (ILD): between the most anterior aspect of lamina; 4) interspinous angle (ISA): the angle between the 2 spinous processes. For SSD, MSD, and ILD, four different measurements were calculated according to the reference, either the average of upper and lower adjacent levels or whether a ratio or absolute value was used. We compared the diagnostic accuracy of various methods of ISW measurement in detecting black stripe discontinuity on MRI using the area under the receiver operator curve (AUC) and interobserver reliability using interclass correlation (ICC). Results: All ISW methods showed low accuracy as denoted by AUC < 0.70 (range 0.51-0.65). At the optimum threshold, there was a trend for higher specificity and lower sensitivity. The ICC was poor for 2 measurements, moderate for 5 measurements, and good for 5 measurements. Conclusion: This study shows the limited accuracy of ISW methods in detecting PLC injury on MRI. However, ILD showed a trend toward better accuracy; there was no statistically significant difference between all methods. The study indicates that ISW, especially when isolated, should be interpreted with caution as an indicator for PLC injury.

## ID: AGS26/2314

## P1029: First reported posterolateral lumbar fusion using trabecular allograft from a bone bank in Chile: clinical feasibility and public health impact

Pablo Urtubia Pérez

1,2

1

Hospital Regional de Copiapó, Servicio de Salud Atacama, Copiapó, Chile

2

Universidad de Atacama, Escuela de Medicina, Copiapó, Chile

Introduction: Autologous iliac crest graft remains the standard for lumbar fusion but is associated with donor-site morbidity, increased operative time, and limited availability. Biologic allografts from accredited bone banks offers a cost-effective and safe alternative, particularly in healthcare systems where resources are constrained. In Chile, bone banks are rare, and no previous cases of posterolateral lumbar fusion using trabecular allograft have been documented. We present the first reported Chilean case, emphasizing not only clinical feasibility but also the broader public-health implications of this practice in a public hospital setting. Material and Methods: A 20-year-old male patient with an unstable lumbar fracture at L2 underwent posterior instrumentation and posterolateral fusion at Hospital Regional de Copiapó. Trabecular bone allograft, processed and provided by the institutional tissue bank, was placed bilaterally in the posterolateral gutters. The patient was followed clinically and radiographically at 3 and 6 months. Outcomes included pain relief (VAS), functional recovery (ODI), and radiological fusion assessed by CT and Bridwell criteria. A comparative cost analysis with conventional strategies (autologous iliac crest harvest and synthetic substitutes) was performed using institutional procurement data. Results: Surgery was uneventful, with reduced operative time due to avoidance of iliac crest harvesting. The patient experienced significant improvement in pain (VAS reduced from 5 to 0) and disability (ODI improved from 26% to 4%) at 6 months. CT confirmed progressive trabecular incorporation and solid posterolateral fusion by 6 months. No graft-related complications, infections, or hardware failures were observed. Cost analysis revealed a reduction of approximately 30% in implant-related expenses compared to synthetic substitutes, while eliminating donor-site morbidity associated with autograft harvest. Conclusion: This case represents the first reported posterolateral lumbar fusion using trabecular allograft from a bone bank in Chile. Beyond the successful clinical outcome, the use of biologic allografts in a public hospital provides a safe, effective, and economically advantageous strategy. This approach has the potential to improve access to spinal fusion surgery, reduce health-system costs, and serve as a model for other low- and middle-income settings.

## ID: AGS26/2566

## P1030: Relationship between MRI poor prognostic factors and serum cytokine levels in osteoporotic vertebral fractures

Yasuhiro Nakajima

1

, Yasukazu Hijikata

2

, Ayako Motomura

1

, Takashi Tsujiuhi

1

, Yukio Seki

1

1

Daido Hospital, Nagoya, Japan

2

Department of Healthcare Epidemiology, Graduate School of Medicine and Public Health, Kyoto University, Kyoto, Japan

Introduction: Osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF) commonly affect the elderly and carry a poor prognosis. Although publications like the New England Journal of Medicine do not include percutaneous vertebroplasty (BKP) in standard treatment, it may improve the quality of life (QOL) for OVF patients. However, identifying prognostic factors for OVF remains challenging. In a previous study, we demonstrated that elevated IL-6 levels in bone marrow fluid correlated with poor prognosis in OVF patients requiring emergency hospitalization due to mobility impairment. This study aims to confirm the correlation between IL-6 concentrations in bone marrow fluid and serum in OVF patients, clarify that serum IL-6 levels can predict poor prognosis in OVF, and identify suitable candidates for early BKP. Material and Methods: Several studies have identified two MRI-based poor prognostic factors for OVF. This study analyzed the correlation between serum and intraoperatively obtained bone marrow IL-6 concentrations in forty OVF patients undergoing BKP. We also examined the relationship between MRI findings and serum IL-6 concentrations. Results: In OVF patients undergoing BKP, serum and bone marrow IL-6 concentrations correlated. The MRI poor prognosis group also showed significantly higher serum IL-6 concentration. Conclusion: We previously reported a correlation between MRI poor prognostic factors and bone marrow IL-6 concentration. This study further demonstrated a correlation between serum IL-6 concentration and bone marrow IL-6 concentration. Serum IL-6 is easier to collect and less expensive to test. The clinical significance of using serum IL-6 concentration to predict poor OVF prognosis is substantial. Based on serum IL-6 levels, this study may suggest effective treatment strategies for OVF, such as recommending early BKP for considered to have a poor prognosis.

## ID: AGS26/2597

## P1031: Risk factors of new vertebral fracture after vertebroplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture

Joohyung Shin

1

, baejae Kim

1

, Doohee Han

1

, Jong Hyeok Park

1

1

Bundang Jesaeng General Hospital, Daejin Medical Center, Seongnam-si, South Korea

Introduction: To determine the natural course of new vertebral fractures after vertebroplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture and to analyze risk factors for understanding the incidence of adjacent vertebral fracture and remote vertebral fracture. Material and Methods: The study subjects included 190 patients who received vertebroplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture. Age, body mass index, bone density, cement leakage, location of fracture, sagittal imbalance, segmental kyphosis were checked to detect risk factor for new vertebral fracture after vertebroplasty. During the follow-up, any new developed vertebral fractures were identified. We analyzed the time of occurrence differed between adjacent vertebral fracture and remote vertebral fracture by calculating survival analysis and each risk factors separately. Results: New vertebral fractures occurred in 44 patients (23%) after vertebroplasty. Adjacent vertebral fracture occurred 19 patients (10%), and remote vertebral fracture occurred 25 patients (13%). The onset time of adjacent vertebral fracture was about 6.2 ± 1.8 months after vertebroplasty, showing a significant difference from that of remote vertebral fracture which was 15.2 ± 1.8 months (p < 0.001). In multivariate analysis, risk factors of adjacent vertebral fracture included osteoporosis, vertebroplasty in thoracolumbar junction, sagittal imbalance, segmental kyphosis (p < 0.005). Risk factors of remote vertebral fracture included osteoporosis, sagittal imbalance (p < 0.005). Conclusion: After vertebroplasty for osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture, a significant difference in the meantime of occurrence between adjacent vertebral fracture and remote vertebral fracture was found. Osteoporosis and sagittal imbalance were important for new vertebral fracture after vertebroplasty, even in both adjacent and remote vertebral fracture.

## P1032: Pedicle morphological changes in thoracolumbar vertebral fractures: implications for safe intermediate screw insertion

Takahiro Hirano

1

, Ichiro Okano

1

, Koki Tsuchiya

1

, Yusuke Dodo

1

, Joji Ogawa

1

, Yoshifmi Kudo

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Showa Medical University School of Medicine, Tokyo, Japan

Introduction: The use of intermediate screws at the fracture level has been increasingly adopted in thoracolumbar fracture fixation. However, canal violation or screw malposition may occur due to pedicle morphological changes. This study investigated pedicle morphological changes, including pedicle angle and lateralization, in thoracolumbar fractures. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed patients with thoracolumbar fractures (including AO typeA, B1 and B2) treated in our institutions between 2017 and 2024. Radiographic measurements, including pedicle angle, lateralization, were obtained on CT. Pedicle lateral deviation and the distance from the pedicle center to the lateral vertebral wall were assessed on plain radiographs. In addition, patient demographics (age, sex, and mechanism of injury categorized as high- or low-energy trauma) and Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity Score (TLICS) and Load Sharing Classification (LSC) were included. Multivariable regression analysis was performed to identify predictors of pedicle morphological changes. Results: A total of 105 patients were included with a mean age of 55 years. Fractured vertebrae demonstrated significantly smaller pedicle angles and greater lateralization compared with adjacent levels. Across all fractures, the mean pedicle angle change was 1.7° and the mean lateralization was 0.7 mm. These changes were most pronounced in type A4 fractures. Multivariable analysis identified high-energy trauma and greater comminution as predictors of pedicle morphological changes. Conclusion: In thoracolumbar burst fractures, particularly AO A4 fractures and high-energy injuries, pedicle morphological changes were pronounced. Surgeons should carefully evaluate pedicle morphology on preoperative CT to reduce the risk of canal violation during intermediate screw insertion.

## P1033: Lumbopelvic fixation for spinopelvic dissociation and complex sacral and pelvic ring injuries at a level 1 trauma center

Sultan Baz

1

, Arthur Cowman

1

, Brandon Lehman

1

, Hana Hanks

1

, Ryan Hoang

1

, Ryan Le

1

, Vivian Chen

1

, Emily Mills

1

, Jason Horowitz

1

, Alexander Nazareth

1

, Alan Santiago

1

, Hao-Hua Wu

1

, Yu-po Lee

1

, Don Park

1

, Nitin Bhatia

1

, Sohaib Hashmi

1

1

University of California, Irvine, School of Medicine, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Orange, United States

Introduction: Spinopelvic dissociation, unstable sacrum fractures, and complex sacral and pelvic ring injuries are commonly a result of high-energy trauma, often associated with polytraumatized patients. Treatment options include open reduction internal fixation (ORIF), percutaneous fixation, and lumbopelvic fixation. Lumbopelvic fixation creates a stable construct allowing for immediate postoperative mobilization. We hypothesize that the use of single or dual S1/S2 Alar-Iliac (SAI) screws is a durable option in the setting of unstable sacral fractures and complex pelvic ring injuries. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed records from a single Level 1 Trauma center for all patients treated with lumbopelvic fixation for spinopelvic dissociation or sacral/pelvic ring injuries from September 2021 to November 2023. We collected clinical data, including patient demographics, intraoperative complications, surgical site infection (SSI), and postoperative complications. Results: 18 patients were included, with a mean follow-up of 313 days (range 64-459). Diagnoses included 9 spinopelvic dissociations, 5 Young and Burgess anteroposterior compression III injuries, 3 Denis Zone 3 vertical shear sacrum fractures, and 1 Young and Burgess lateral compression II nonunion. All patients were allowed immediate weight bearing as tolerated. Average preoperative sacral kyphosis was 29.1 degrees, and postoperative sacral kyphosis was 18.8 degrees. Preoperative sacral shortening was 8.6 mm, and postoperative shortening was 4.6 mm. There were a total of 53 SAI screws placed. 6 patients underwent anterior pelvic ring ORIF and 1 underwent acetabulum ORIF. Perioperative complications included 4 SSIs requiring return to the operating room and 1 early failure of fixation requiring revision. Long-term complications included 1 bilateral rod fracture and 3 painful hardware requiring implant removal. There were no neurologic complications. Conclusion: Lumbopelvic fixation is an effective technique for stabilizing complex spinopelvic, sacrum, and pelvic injuries. Depending on the fracture pattern, it can be used in isolation or in combination with various fracture fixation techniques. LP fixation allows improvement of segmental sacral kyphosis and stable construct allowing immediate weight-bearing.

## P1034: Beyond survival: functional status and health related quality of life following traumatic spinal injury in Tanzania

Chellandra Samuels

1

, Mariam Mzingi

, Romani R. Sabas

, Elochukwu Ibekwe

, Chibuikem Ikwuegbuenyi

, Halinder Mangat

, Hamisi Shabani

, David Shearer

, Ashraf El Naga

, Roger Härtl

, David Gendelberg

, African Spine Trauma Network, SIGN Spine

4

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania

3

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital, Weill-Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

4

SIGN Fracture Care International, Richland, Washington, United States

Introduction: Traumatic spinal injury (TSI) is a major cause of disability and mortality worldwide, occurring more frequently in low- and middle- income countries (LMICs). TSI impacts a patient's functional status and health quality, including both physical and psychological domains of life. Understanding how spine trauma and its management impact patients’ quality of life is essential to provision of optimal spine care. This study aims to assess spine trauma patients’ functional outcomes over time utilizing standardized measures of impairment and health related quality of life (HRQOL). Material and Methods: All spine trauma patients presenting to a single tertiary referral center in Tanzania between July 2024 and August 2025 were included in this analysis. Demographic information, injury characteristics, and HRQOL measures were recorded at 2-, 6-, 13-, 26-, and 52- weeks following spine trauma. AO PROST questionnaire responses were used as a measure of patient reported HRQOL. Mean PROST scores were calculated, then stratified by ASIA grade at each timepoint. Results: A total of 149 patients were included in this study. At time of analysis, 81.5% of eligible patients reached 2-week follow-up, and nearly 75% of eligible patients completed 6 month follow-up evaluation. A decline in overall PROST scores was observed at the 2-week follow-up (41.2 ± 34.2), followed by improvement (54.3 ± 31.6) at 6 months. The greatest increase in PROST scores occurred between the 6-week and 3-month follow-up points. Across all time points, patients with ASIA grade E had the highest average PROST scores, followed by ASIA grade D. By contrast, ASIA grade A patients consistently had the lowest PROST scores. Average PROST scores improved across all ASIA grade groups between 2 weeks and 6 months. Conclusion: In this study, conducted at a hospital located in Tanzania, follow-up rates have consistently exceeded 70%, demonstrating strong engagement from enrolled patients. The overall high follow-up rate demonstrates the feasibility of collecting data relevant to spine trauma in an LMIC in Africa. Overall, AO Spine PROST scores show an improving average trend following trauma, and higher grades of spinal injury correlate to better long-term patient-reported functional outcomes. Despite these findings aligning with those of studies conducted in high income countries, LMICs have insufficient capacity to provide optimal spinal trauma care for all their patients. This study offers unique insights via employing standardized
