## P1035: Mid-term outcomes of vertebral body stenting for osteoporotic vertebral fractures and the validity of MRI-based indications

Kenji Uda

1

, Takayuki Awaya

1

, Tomotaka Iwakoshi

1

, Hinako Nagamatsu

1

, Kazuki Ishii

1

, Takashi Abe

1

, Osamu Suzuki

1

, Yoshitaka Nagashima

2

, Yusuke Nishimura

2

, Ryuta Saito

2

1

Nagoya Ekisaikai Hospital, Nagoya, Japan

2

Nagoya University Hospital, Nagoya, Japan

Introduction: Vertebral body stenting (VBS) has emerged as a novel device for osteoporotic vertebral fractures (OVF), comparable to balloon kyphoplasty (BKP), and was approved for clinical use under the Japanese national health insurance system in 2021. At our institution, VBS has been performed since June 2021, with 118 cases (118 vertebrae) treated by December 2023. This study evaluated one-year clinical outcomes and compared the efficacy of VBS between patients with and without poor prognostic MRI findings, which are associated with non-union. Material and Methods: Among 118 cases, 87 patients were retrospectively analyzed after excluding those who required additional fixation due to severe vertebral damage or in whom stent deployment was unsuccessful. Clinical outcomes included numerical rating scale (NRS) for pain, perioperative complications, adjacent vertebral fractures, and progression to non-union or delayed vertebral collapse. Radiological parameters included anterior vertebral height (AVH), middle vertebral height (MVH), posterior vertebral height (PVH), and vertebral angle (VA), measured at four time points: preoperatively, postoperative day 1 (POD1), postoperative month 6 (POM6), and POM12. Furthermore, differences in AVH, MVH, PVH, and VA were compared between patients with and without poor prognostic MRI findings, defined as high-intensity or diffuse low-intensity changes on T2-weighted imaging. Results: The mean age and operative time were 79.2 years and 27.2 minutes, respectively. NRS scores significantly improved from 6.2 preoperatively to 2.8 postoperatively. Cement leakage occurred in 16% of patients but was asymptomatic in all cases. No non-union was observed; delayed vertebral collapse occurred in 3 cases, all asymptomatic. Radiological improvements in AVH, MVH, PVH, and VA were significant at POD1 and were maintained through POM12 compared with preoperative values. Patients with poor prognostic MRI findings demonstrated significantly greater improvements in AVH, MVH, and VA at both POD1 and POM12. Conclusion: VBS was effective in maintaining vertebral height and preventing progression to non-union or delayed vertebral collapse during the observation period. Moreover, patients with poor prognostic MRI findings benefited more from VBS, suggesting the validity of MRI-based treatment selection.

## ID: AGS26/2911

## P1036: Enhancing the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification System to Guide Surgical Decision-Making for Burst Fractures

Anoop Chinthala

1

, Barnabas Obeng-Gyasi

2

, Jonathan Arnold

1

, William Snyder

1

, Trenton Line

1

, Felix Pabon-Rodriguez

1

, Taliyah Griffin

1

, Kailah Young

3

, Ethan Brown

4

, Alexei Christodoulides

1

, Aladine Elsamadicy

4

, Dan Schneider

5

, Gordon Mao

1

1

Indiana University School of Medicine, Indianapolis, United States

2

Allegheny Health Network, Pittsburgh, United States

3

University of Tennessee Health Science Center, Memphis, United States

4

Zucker School of Medicine at Hofstra, Long Island Jewish Medical Center and North Shore University Hospital, Northwell Health, Manhasset, United States

5

The Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center, Columbus, United States

Introduction: The Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity Score (TLICS) is widely used to guide management of thoracolumbar spine trauma. However, its applicability to burst fractures remains debated, particularly given the uniform assignment of 2 points for morphology regardless of fracture complexity. The objective of this study is to evaluate the diagnostic performance of TLICS in predicting surgical management for thoracolumbar burst fractures and to determine whether incorporating radiographic modifiers into the morphology domain improves predictive accuracy. Material and Methods: A retrospective analysis was performed on patients with confirmed burst fractures treated from 2018 to 2023 using a statewide institutional database linked to a Level 1 Trauma Center. Patients were divided into surgical and non-surgical cohorts. Original TLICS scores were calculated retrospectively. Modified TLICS (mTLICS) scores were generated by assigning additional morphology points for radiographic thresholds: AP canal diameter < 9.7 mm, retropulsion ≥ 5 mm, anterior vertebral height loss ≥ 8.2%, and kyphotic angle ≥ 15°. Diagnostic performance was assessed using ROC analysis and comparison of sensitivity and specificity at a surgical threshold of > 4 points. Results: A total of 185 patients with thoracolumbar burst fractures were included in the final analysis. Of this sample, 65 patients (35.1%) underwent surgical intervention, while 120 patients (64.9%) were managed non-operatively. Among surgically treated patients, 68% had a TLICS score ≤ 4, suggesting underestimation of surgical need. The original TLICS score demonstrated limited discriminative ability (AUC = 0.654). At a threshold of > 4, specificity was 90.8%, sensitivity was 2.3%, with a PPV of 65.6% and NPV of 71.2%. The best-performing mTLICS model was mTLICS-7, which incorporates all four radiographic modifiers. This modified score assigned 2 points for AP canal narrowing, 1 point each for retropulsion, vertebral height loss, and kyphotic angle. ROC analysis of mTLICS-7 yielded an AUC of 0.828 with sensitivity of 83.1%, specificity of 70.0%, PPV of 63.3%, and NPV of 87.4%, representing marked improvement over the unmodified scoring system. Conclusion: The TLICS scoring system demonstrates limited sensitivity in identifying surgical candidates among patients with thoracolumbar burst fractures, in part due to fixed morphology scores that fail to reflect the heterogeneity of fracture severity. While TLICS remains a valuable decision-support tool, our findings highlight the importance of refining its morphology component. Incorporating radiographic modifiers such as canal compromise, retropulsion, anterior vertebral height loss, and kyphotic angle can significantly enhance its predictive accuracy.

## ID: AGS26/2931

## P1037: Comparative outcomes of percutaneous and conventional open pedicle screw fixation for thoracolumbar burst junction fractures without neurologic deficit

Raboudi Hamza

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Hedi Atig

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Ismail Karray

1

, Jihed Bechikh

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology department. Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar Burst fractures requiring surgical intervention can be treated with either open or percutaneous stabilization, each with some distinct risks and benefits. There is insufficient evidence available to support one approach as superior. The aim of our study is to compare the functional clinical and radiological outcomes of percutaneous versus conventional open posterior fixation for thoracolumbar burst junction fractures. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study of 61 patients who underwent posterior fixation for thoracolumbar burst fractures. Patients were divided into two groups according to the surgical approach: • Group A included 35 patients treated using the conventional open posterior fixations. • Group B included 26 patients treated using the percutaneous posterior fixation. Functional and clinical outcomes were assessed using Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Oswestry and Dallas scores and return-to-work time. The evaluation of results was done by calculating the variation of radiological parameters between pre-operative radiographs, postoperative radiographs and last follow-up radiographs. The gain is the preoperative value minus the postoperative value. The loss of correction is the value at the last follow-up minus the postoperative value. Results: In group A, the mean age 49.89 years. The mean BMI was 28.71 kg/m2. We noted a male predominance (26 males and 31 females). The most frequent fracture vertebra was L1 (37 cases). The mean follow-up was 53.82 months. In group B, the mean age was 46.88 years with female predominance (10 males and 16 females). The mean BMI was 26.42 kg/m2. L1 vertebra was the most frequent (18 cases). The mean follow-up was 49.04 months. Percutaneous fixation was associated with better clinical outcomes, including earlier verticalization (p = 0.007), shorter return-to-work time (p = 0.03), lower postoperative VAS (1.58 vs 2.42 p = 0.003), and improved Oswestry (13.08 vs 23.07) and Dallas scores (8.26 vs 15.45) (p = 0.002 and p = 0.001, respectively). Regarding radiological parameters, the gain in regional kyphosis was 10.09° for group A and 8.04° for group B (p = 0.3). The gain in vertebral kyphosis was 8.29° and 8.85° for group A and B respectively (p = 0.22). At the last follow-up of group A, the loss in regional kyphosis (RK) was 1.64°, the loss in vertebral kyphosis (VK) was 1.18 and the loss of traumatic regional angulation (TRA) was 1.64. At the last follow-up of group B, the loss in RK was 0.38°, the loss in VK was 0.42° and the TRA loss was 0.46. At this stage, there was a significant difference between the two groups for VK (p = 0.009) and TRA (p = 0.01). Regarding the complications, conventional open pedicle screw fixation was linked to higher complication rates, including hardware failure (p = 0.011), while other complications such as nonunion (p = 0.44) and sepsis (p = 0.32) did not reach statistical significance. Conclusion: Percutaneous posterior fixation for thoracolumbar burst fractures provides superior clinical recovery and better radiological outcomes compared with open fixation, without increasing complication rates. These findings support the use of percutaneous techniques to optimize patient recovery, kyphosis correction, and maintenance of spinal alignment.

## ID: AGS26/2968

## P1038: A one-year report on the current burden of spine trauma at a tertiary referral hospital in Tanzania

Chellandra Samuels

1

, Mariam Mzingi

2

, Elochukwu Ibekwe

3

, Chibuikem Ikwuegbuenyi

3

, Romani R. Sabas

2,3

, Sheryne Zeitoun

1

, Halinder Mangat

3

, Hamisi Shabani

2

, David Shearer

1

, Ashraf El Naga

1

, Roger Härtl

3

, David Gendelberg

1

, African Spine Trauma Network, SIGN Spine

4

1

Institute of Global Orthopaedics and Traumatology, USCF, San Francisco, CA, United States

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania

3

Department of Neurological Surgery, Och Spine at NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital, Weill-Cornell Medicine, New York, United States

4

SIGN Fracture Care International, Richland, Washington, United States

Introduction: Traumatic spinal injury is a significant cause of high morbidity and mortality worldwide. A major spine trauma care gap is the lack of data, specifically in lower- and middle-income countries (LMICs). The number of trauma registries are limited, secondary to the costs associated implementing and managing the database. Understanding the current state of spine trauma in LMICs will provide valuable information, guiding future interventions. This study aims to describe spine trauma patient demographic, injury, and management characteristics amongst the cohort. Material and Methods: This study is a prospective observational cohort study enrolling adult patients with spine trauma at a tertiary referral hospital in Tanzania since July 2024. At enrollment, patients’ demographics, socioeconomic details, injury characteristics, hospitalization data, and management details were entered into REDCap. Patients are followed up at the following time points : 2 weeks, 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months and 12 months. Results: Between July 2024 - August 2025, 150 spine trauma patients have been enrolled. The average age of enrolled spine trauma patients was 38.05 years (SD = 13.20). Majority of patients were male (129, 86.0%). Most patients were uninsured (121, 80.7%) and working (125, 90.0%) at time of injury. Prevalent mechanisms of injury for enrolled patients include motor vehicle accident (71, 47.3%) and fall (46, 30.7%). Neurologic deficits were present for 111 (75.0%) patients. Thoracolumbar levels (95, 71.4%), were the most common injury level. Out of the 149 enrolled patients, 118 patients were determined to be surgical candidates, and of these, 105 surgeries were performed. Delays to surgery were experienced in 77 cases (72.6%), most commonly caused by inability to pay. Nonsurgical management included orthosis (15,48.4%), immobilization (3, 9.7%), and pain management (25, 80.6%). Instrumentation was performed in 103 (98.1%) surgeries, and of these, 63 (61. 2%) cases utilized donated instruments. Most commonly instrumented levels were thoracolumbar levels, specifically, T11 (38, 36.9%), T12 (47, 45.6%), L1 (49, 47.6%), L2 (52, 50.5%), L3 (27, 26.2%). Of the entire cohort, 110, 98, 70, and 6 patients have been evaluated for 6 week, 3-, 6-, and 12- month follow-up, respectively. Follow-up rate at 6 months is 74%. Conclusion: Data collection of spine trauma patients at this medical center in Tanzania has provided detailed information on patient demographic, injury, and management characteristics. Data collection is needed for greater understanding of spine trauma in lower resourced settings and development of interventions aimed at providing optimal spine trauma care.

## ID: AGS26/3020

## P1039: Pneumorrhachis following a subscapular stab wound: a case report and literature review

Elouni Emna

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Yassine Chaker

3

, Roua Latrach

1

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Hopital Fattouma Bourguiba, Monastir, Monastir, Tunisia

3

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Pneumorrhachis is a condition characterized by the presence of air in the spinal canal. It is most often discovered incidentally, associated with a variety of underlying conditions. However, chest trauma associated with pneumorrhachis is a rare occurrence. The mechanism by which free air becomes trapped in the spinal canal following chest injury remains unclear. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 17-year-old adolescent who was the victim of a stab wound with a left suprascapular injury. Upon admission, imaging revealed a massive left pneumothorax, extensive pneumomediastinum, and diffuse pneumorrhachis. Despite the severity of the injuries, the patient exhibited no neurological deficits. Results: The management included chest drainage for the pneumothorax, along with close clinical and radiological monitoring for the pneumorrhachis. The patient's condition improved progressively, with gradual resolution of the pneumorrachis. Conclusion: Pneumorrhachis is often found incidentally, associated with a variety of underlying conditions, particularly in cases of traumatic pneumothorax and skull base fractures. Pneumorrhachis is typically managed conservatively and should be carefully considered in the presence of neurological deficits.

## ID: AGS26/3047

## P1040: Treatment of high energy traumatic thoracolumbar burst fractures with armed kyphoplasty

Dominik Baschera

1,2

, Yannick Straub

3

, Leo Kronberger

4

, Gregory Jost

3,5

, Daniel Coluccia

1,2

, Ulf Schneider

2

, Manuel Moser

6

, Edin Nevzati

2,3,7

1

Stadtspital Zürich Triemli, Zürich, Switzerland

2

Lucerne Cantonal Hospital, Luzern, Switzerland

3

University of Basel, Faculty of Medicine, Basel, Switzerland

4

Die Orthopäden -Kompetenzzentrum für Orthogeriatrie, Graz, Austria

5

Spine Surgery, Spitalzentrum Biel, Biel, Switzerland

6

Department of Neurosurgery, Cantonal Hospital Graubuenden, Chur, Switzerland

7

Department of Neurosurgery, University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus School of Medicine, Aurora CO, United States

Introduction: Management of complete (AO A4) and incomplete (AO A3) high energy traumatic thoraco-lumbar burst fractures is still controversial. The aim of this study was to compare intravertebral fracture reduction using armed SpineJack® Kyphoplasty (SJ) and compare it with conventional minimal invasive posterior percutaneous instrumentation (PPI) techniques. Material and Methods: Retrospective single centre analysis of surgically treated traumatic thoraco-lumbar burst fractures (AO A3 and A4) with SJ, PPI, or a combination of both (SJ & PPI) from January 2018 to March 2022. The primary endpoint was the 30-day morbidity. Secondary endpoints were operation time, length of hospital stay, reoperation rates and postoperative radiographic fracture reduction. Results: A total of 64 patients were included in the study. 14 patients were treated with SJ, 40 patients received PPI and 10 patients received a combination of both (SJ & PPI). 26 were suffering from a polytrauma (ISS ≥ 15). A total of 16 (25%) patients had complications in the 30 days post index surgery: The complications were mild in 9 (4 patients grade 1a, 5 patients 1b) and moderate (2b) in 7 patients; there was no difference between treatment groups. Rehospitalization within 30 days after surgery was necessary in two patients, one in the PPI and one in the SJ & PPI group. The mean surgery time in the SJ group was 48 ± 51minutes, 139 ± 62 minutes in the PPI group, and 135 ± 75 minutes in the SJ & PPI group (p < 0.05). There were no significant differences for radiographic parameters between groups. Conclusion: SJ is a viable and safe treatment option for traumatic incomplete or complete burst fractures, either as a standalone procedure or in combination with short dorsal instrumentation. It has similar radiographic fracture reduction capabilities when compared with dorsal instrumentation techniques, but has the advantage of shorter operative times especially when used as a standalone treatment device.

## ID: AGS26/3079

## P1041: A rare case of open thoracolumbar fracture: a case report

Shyful Nizam Sumari

1

, Sook Kwan Chan

1

, Choong Hoon Foo

1

1

Hospital Queen Elizabeth, Sabah., Kota Kinabalu, Malaysia

Introduction: Open spinal fractures are rare and often associated with high-energy trauma, severe wound contamination, and neurological deficits. They pose significant management challenges due to the risk of infection, dural involvement, and spinal instability. Early surgical intervention and appropriate antimicrobial therapy are essential to optimize outcomes. We present a case of an open thoracolumbar fracture with severe contamination successfully managed with early debridement, stabilization, and prolonged follow-up. Case presentation: A 24-year-old male estate worker sustained an occupational injury when his safety harness became entangled in a conveyor machine, pulling him against the machinery. He developed a deep laceration over the lumbar spine with exposed muscle and spinous processes. On examination, the wound was grossly contaminated with sand and metallic particles. Neurological assessment revealed incomplete cord injury (ASIA B). He was hemodynamically stable. Imaging findings on CT noted L1 chance fracture and L2 comminuted posterior element fracture. MRI showed L1 chance fracture with widened L1/L2 bilateral neuroforamina due to facet joint dissociation. Visualization of L2-L3 was limited due to metallic foreign body artifact. In the emergency department, intravenous cefuroxime and metronidazole were administered, and the wound was irrigated with normal saline. On day 2 post-trauma, the patient underwent wound debridement, posterior spinal instrumentation and fusion from T11 to L4, and L1-L2 laminectomy. Prophylactic gentamicin was given in addition to cefuroxime and metronidazole prior to incision. Intraoperatively, extensive contamination with sand and metallic debris was noted, extending down to the dura. The dura was exposed but intact without cerebrospinal fluid leakage. Debridement of superficial and deep muscle was performed. Estimated blood loss was 1 Liter, requiring transfusion of 2 pints of packed red blood cells. Antibiotics were continued for 5 days postoperatively. The patient was discharged in good condition with no wound complications. At 1-year follow-up, the wound had healed completely. Neurological recovery was limited but improved from grade 0 to grade 1 power in both lower limbs, with intact sensation. Discussion: Open thoracolumbar fractures are extremely rare and most often result from high-energy trauma. They are associated with a high risk of infection, neurological deterioration, and poor functional outcomes. The management principles include immediate antibiotic therapy, meticulous debridement, and spinal stabilization. In this case, the presence of gross contamination with soil and metallic foreign bodies raised significant concern for deep infection. However, timely debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and rigid stabilization prevented septic complications. Neurological recovery remained limited, reflecting the severity of the initial spinal cord injury. This case supports previous reports emphasizing early multidisciplinary management in achieving satisfactory wound healing and long-term spinal stability despite severe contamination. Conclusion: Open thoracolumbar fractures are rare but serious injuries requiring urgent multidisciplinary management. Early broad-spectrum antibiotics, thorough surgical debridement, and spinal stabilization are crucial to reduce complications. Although neurological recovery may be limited, satisfactory wound healing and stable spinal reconstruction can be achieved, as demonstrated in this case.

## ID: AGS26/3108

## P1042: Cement-augmented minimally invasive fixation for a U-shaped sacral insufficiency fracture: a case report

Rafael Silva

1

, Artur Teixeira

1

, Ricardo Frada

1

, Eduardo Pinto

1

, Manuel Godinho

1

, João Alves

1

, João Magalhães Morais

1

1

Unidade Local de Saúde Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Sacral insufficiency fractures are an increasingly recognized cause of severe low back and pelvic pain in elderly patients, most often related to osteoporosis. These injuries are frequently underdiagnosed due to their non-specific presentation and the limited sensitivity of plain radiographs. While many sacral insufficiency fractures are stable and can be managed conservatively, the U-shaped configuration is a severe and biomechanical unstable subtype, resulting in spinopelvic dissociation and a risk of progressive kyphosis. In these cases, surgical fixation is often required to restore stability, relieve pain, and allow early mobilization. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 60 year old female patient with osteoporosis who was admited with acute severe low back and radicular pain, gait inability, and functional impairment. CT imaging demonstrated a U-shaped sacral insufficiency fracture involving both sacral alae and the S1 body. The patient underwent minimally invasive lumbopelvic percutaneous fixation with fenestrated pedicle screws at L4-L5 and iliac screws, augmented with polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) cement. Results: The procedure was uneventful, with no intraoperative complications. Postoperatively, the patient experienced significant pain relief, was mobilized with partial weight-bearing, and discharged the following day. At one-month follow-up, she reported marked functional improvement, with radiographs confirming stable fixation and no loss of alignment. Conclusion: This case highlights the importance of recognizing U-shaped sacral insufficiency fractures as an unstable entity requiring surgical management. Cement-augmented minimally invasive lumbopelvic fixation proved to be a safe and effective technique, providing immediate stabilization, rapid pain relief, and early mobilization in an osteoporotic patient with spinopelvic dissociation.

## ID: AGS26/3139

## P1043: Impact of loss of correction on functional and clinical outcomes after posterior surgical treatment of thoracolumbar junction fractures

Raboudi Hamza

1

, Jlidi Mohamed

1

, mouldi lamouchi

1

, Karim Mallek

1

, Ismail Karray

1

, Sami Mbarki

1

, Hamza Daly

1

, Hedi Atig

1

, Jihed Bechikh

1

, Walid Bouaicha

1

, Selim Daas

1

1

Orthopedics and Traumatology Department, Hospital of Mohamed Taher Maamouri, Nabeul, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar fractures, a prevalent type of spinal fracture, typically occur at the thoracolumbar junction. This region, where thoracic kyphosis transitions into lumbar lordosis, has complex anatomical structures and experiences significant biomechanical stress. Loss of vertebral alignment after posterior stabilization of these injuries may compromise long-term functional recovery. This study aimed to evaluate the impact of loss of correction on functional and clinical outcomes in patients treated surgically. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective study including 60 patients treated for thoracolumbar junction fractures with posterior fixation. The loss of correction was defined as a ≥ 20% decrease in vertebral height, Cobb angle, or vertebral kyphosis from the immediate postoperative measurement Patients were divided into two groups: Group A included 18 patients with significant loss of correction and Group B included 42 patients without significant loss. Functional outcomes were assessed using the Visual Analogue Scale (VAS), Oswestry Disability Index (ODI), Dallas Score, return to work, work reassignment, and duration of work absence. Results: The mean age of Group A was 49.78 years and 49.33 years for Group B. The mean BMI was 28.75 in Group A and 28.60 in Group B. Group A included 8 women and 10 men, whereas Group B included 17 women and 25 men. Diabetes was present in 5 patients in Group A and 7 in Group B. Initial neurological deficit was observed in 1 patient in Group A and 3 patients in Group B. The mean follow-up was 61 months for Group A and 50.21 months for Group B. Among the 60 patients included, 18 patients experienced significant loss of correction (Group A), while 42 patients maintained vertebral alignment (Group B). Functional evaluation showed that patients with loss of correction had slightly higher pain scores on the Visual Analogue Scale (mean 2.78 vs 2.32; p = 0.33) and Oswestry Disability Index scores (mean 26.72 vs 22.59; p = 0.40), although these differences were not statistically significant. However, the Dallas Score was significantly higher in Group A compared to Group B (21.94 vs 13.31; p = 0.007), indicating worse functional outcomes in patients with loss of correction. Return to work rates were similar between groups, with 8 patients resuming work in Group A and 19 in Group B (p = 0.79), while work reassignment occurred in 3 patients in Group A and 6 patients in Group B (p = 0.79). The average duration of work absence was also comparable (150 days in Group A vs 158.75 days in Group B; p = 0.62). Conclusion: Postoperative loss of vertebral correction in thoracolumbar junction fractures is associated with poorer functional outcomes as measured by the Dallas Score. The factors responsible for this loss of correction should be investigated in future studies to better prevent this complication and improve long-term recovery.

## ID: AGS26/3232

## P1044: Isolated unstable L5 burst fractures presenting as cauda equina syndrome

Prabodh Kantiwal

1

1

All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Jodhpur, India

Introduction: Burst fractures of the fifth lumbar vertebra (L5) are rare injuries that usually result from high-energy axial compressive loads. Their distinct anatomy and biomechanical features set them apart from other lumbar spine injuries. To our knowledge, the treatment approaches for L5 burst fractures have not been comprehensively outlined. Reported treatment options include prolonged immobilisation and surgical stabilisation, most commonly involving an instrumented fusion from L4 to the sacrum. Material and Methods: We present two cases of L5 Burst fractures with cauda equina syndrome. The patient sustained injury due to a fall from a height. On admission, the evaluation of radiographs and neurological status was done. The measurements of the angle between the L4 lower and the S1 upper-end plates, the angle between the upper and lower-end plates of L5 and the height of the anterior and posterior rims of the L5 vertebral body were done. CT scans were assessed for a relative narrowing of the spinal canal. The fractures of L5 were stabilised with a short-segment L4-S1 fixation in combination with canal and posterolateral fusion (PLF) decompression and transpedicular bone grafting. Results: Radiographs, neurological findings and subjective complaints were assessed at 3, 6, and 12 months of follow-up. Both patients were satisfied with the improved symptoms. It was recommended that they discontinue medication and TLSO wearing after six weeks. Conclusion: The posterior instrumented spinal fusion of L5 alone is sufficient for treating most L5 burst fractures. Despite worsening spinal alignment parameters, the functional outcomes are good at the recent follow-up. Often, good clinical outcomes are in contradiction with radiological findings.

## ID: AGS26/3236

## P1045: Multi-level contiguous isolated lumbar spinous process fractures with intact neurology in a young adult

Prabodh Kantiwal

1

1

All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Jodhpur, India

Introduction: Contiguous multiple spinous process fractures localized to the lumbar vertebra are rare. We report a case of multilevel (L1-L4) continuous lumbar spinous process fractures, with the entire injury confined to the posterior column only. It was an isolated posterior column injury involving the complete lumbar spine. Material and Methods: A 24-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a lower back injury due to a fall from 10 feet height. There were no significant fractures visible on X-rays of the cervical and thoracic spine, but posterior element injuries to the lumbar spine X-rays. CT scan of the whole spine was done as further workup, which revealed the following injuries: spinous process fractures on L1-L4, L5-S1 facet joint fracture on the right side, and transverse process fracture on the L1-L5 right side. The patient was treated conservatively. He was advised bed rest till the pain subsided, then mobilised with a thoracolumbosacral corset (TLSO) for four weeks. Results: The patient was satisfied with the improved symptoms. He was recommended to discontinue medication and TLSO wearing after six weeks. Conclusion: In the literature, continuous lumbar spinous process fractures without any injury to the anterior and middle column are extremely rare; all can be treated conservatively with good outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3239

## P1046: Remodeling of thoracolumbar junction fractures: balloon kyphoplasty alone or with posterior percutaneous spinal instrumentation?

Betul Yaman

1

, Samet Dinç

1

, Burak Sener

1

, Atakan Besnek

1

, Ahmet Yucel

1

, gokhan Gurkan

2

, baran taşkala

3

, Inan Uzunoglu

1

1

Ministry of Health Etlik City Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

2

Medicalpoint International Izmir Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Izmir, Turkey

3

Istanbul Basaksehir Cam and Sakura State Hospital, Department of Neurosurgery, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Traumatic spine fractures commonly occur at the thoracolumbar junction (T10-L2), most often at Th12-L1. Balloon kyphoplasty is an effective minimally invasive option for pain relief and vertebral height restoration, though its role in cases with spinal canal compromise remains debated. Various surgical strategies, including kyphoplasty alone or in combination with posterior percutaneous spinal instrumentation (PPSI), have been proposed to improve outcomes in these fractures. Material and Methods: Between October 2022 and March 2025, patients with thoracolumbar fractures who underwent balloon kyphoplasty (Group A, n = 31) or kyphoplasty combined with PPSI (Group B, n = 29) were retrospectively reviewed. All patients were neurologically intact (Frankel grade E). Inclusion criteria were traumatic fractures between T10 and L2 treated within 60 days of injury; pathological fractures due to malignancy were excluded. Demographic and radiological data were collected, including age, sex, fracture level, and fracture type based on the AO classification. Measurements comprised anterior (AVB) and posterior vertebral body (PVB) heights, spinal canal area, and local kyphotic angle, assessed preoperatively and postoperatively, with kyphotic angle also evaluated at 6-month-follow-up. All procedures were performed via a posterior approach. In Group B, pedicle screw-related implants allowed short-segment. The mean cement volume was 4.5-6 cc, and balloon expansion volume 15-20 cc. Results: A total of 60 patients were analyzed (Group A: 31, Group B: 29). Group A patients were older (71.1 vs. 59.4 years; p = 0.003) and predominantly female (24/31 vs. 15/29; p = 0.037). Fractures were most frequent at L1 (n = 32), followed by T12 (n = 17), L2 (n = 8), T11 (n = 2), and T10 (n = 1). By AO classification, A1 fractures were the most common in both groups (∼45%). Group B showed more A2 fractures (31.0%), whereas Group A had more A3 (35.5%) and A4 (12.9%) fractures. Preoperative PVB height showed a significant difference between the two groups (p = 0.002), and this difference remained significant postoperatively (p = 0.008). The mean preoperative spinal canal area was significantly larger in Group B (279.2 mm2) compared with Group A (204.9 mm2; p < 0.001). In the overall cohort, AVB and PVB heights improved significantly postoperatively compared with preoperative values (anterior p < 0.001; posterior p = 0.018). The local kyphotic angle also improved after surgery (p < 0.001), decreasing from 12.8° to 10.1° in Group B and from 12.4° to 9.4° in Group A. However, the degree of kyphotic angle correction did not differ significantly between the groups (p = 0.715). Kyphotic angle correction was significant from preoperative to postoperative 6-month-follow-up (p < 0.001), but not between postoperative and 6-month-follow-up measurements (p = 0.117). In the subgroup of L1 fractures, a trend toward significance was observed for age differences between Group A and Group B (p = 0.052).For L1 fractures, repeated measures ANOVA revealed a significant increase in spinal canal area over time(preoperative-vs.-postoperative; p = 0.004),while no significant interaction between time and treatment type was observed (p=0.252),indicating comparable improvements in both groups. Conclusion: Both balloon kyphoplasty and kyphoplasty with PPSI improved radiological outcomes in thoracolumbar fractures. While stabilization was linked to greater spinal canal area, overall improvements were similar, suggesting both methods are effective, with added stability from the combined approach in selected cases.

## ID: AGS26/3240

## P1047: Percutaneous transpedicular fixation for thoracolumbar extension-distraction fractures in patients with ankylosing spinal disorders

Hamdan Abdelrahman

1

, Seyed-Emadaldin Sadat

2

, Ahmed Shawky Abdelgawaad

2

, Ali Ezzati

2

1

Asiut University Hospitals, Asiut, Egypt

2

Helios Clinic Erfurt- Germany, Asiut University Hospitals- Egypt, Erfurt, Germany

Introduction: Typical traumatic thoracolumbar (TL) fracture in patients with ankylosing spinal disorders (ASD) is a hyperextension injury involving all three spinal columns, which is associated with unfavorable outcomes. Although a consensus on the management of these highly unstable injuries is missing, minimally invasive surgery (MIS) has been progressively accepted as a treatment option, since it is related to lower morbidity and mortality rates. This study aimed to evaluate clinical and radiological outcomes after percutaneous instrumentation for hyperextension TL fractures in patients with ASD at a single institution. Material and Methods: retrospective analysis of prospectively collected data from 2013 to 2022 from Level I trauma center, 88 cases of thoracic and lumbar spinal fractures in patients with ASD treated with a PTPF. Back pain was assessed at preoperative, postoperative, and final follow-up visits using the visual analog scale (VAS). Patient-reported outcomes via the Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) were obtained to assess disability and mobility during follow-up. Union rate and implant failure. Intra- and postoperative complications were recorded. Results: A total of 29 patients with AS, 41 with DISH, 18 with advanced spondylosis. The mean patient age was 71.2 ± 6.8 years. The VAS score for back pain significantly improved over the follow-up period (from 8.4 ± 1.1 to 2.8 ± 0.8, p < 0.001). At the last follow-up, all patients had minor disability (mean ODI score 23.4 ± 5.1, p = 0.002). The Cobb angle (5.2° ± 2.9° preoperatively to 4.4° ± 3.3° at follow-up) and SI (7.9° ± 4.2° to 8.8° ± 5.1°) were maintained at follow-up, showing no loss of segmental kyphosis. Bone union was observed in all patients. The overall complication rate was 10%, while the reoperation rate for implant failure was 4%. Conclusion: Percutaneous instrumentation for traumatic hyperextension TL fractures in ASD demonstrated good clinical and radiological outcomes, along with a high bone union level and low reoperation rate. Accordingly, MIS reduced the complication rate in the management of these injuries of the ankylosed spine.

## ID: AGS26/3272

## P1048: Initial management of thoracolumbar fractures in the emergency setting: impact of transfer delay on neurological outcome - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Thoracolumbar fractures, particularly those associated with neurological deficits, require timely assessment and management to optimize outcomes. Delays between emergency department presentation, imaging, and neurosurgical transfer may influence neurological recovery. This review examines the relationship between early management and functional outcomes. Material and Methods: A systematic literature search of PubMed and Google Scholar was performed using the keywords: “thoracolumbar fracture,” “emergency management,” “transfer delay,” “neurological outcome,” and “spinal trauma.” Studies reporting timing of initial assessment, imaging, transfer to definitive care, and subsequent neurological recovery were included. Data regarding study design, patient population, fracture type, and reported outcomes were extracted. Results: • Early imaging and rapid transfer to specialized spinal units are consistently associated with improved neurological outcomes. • Delays in assessment or transfer, particularly >12-24 hours, correlate with increased rates of persistent motor or sensory deficits. • Factors contributing to delays include initial triage, access to advanced imaging (CT/MRI), and inter-hospital transfers. • Protocolized trauma pathways, including early recognition and expedited transfer, reduce delay and may improve functional recovery. Conclusion: Timely initial management of thoracolumbar fractures, including rapid imaging and prompt neurosurgical referral, is critical for optimizing neurological outcomes. Standardized emergency protocols and early coordination with spinal units are recommended to minimize delays. Further prospective studies are warranted to quantify the impact of specific time thresholds on long-term recovery.

## ID: AGS26/3293

## P1049: Non-surgical management of stable thoracolumbar fractures in the emergency setting: outcomes and comparison with delayed surgical intervention - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Stable thoracolumbar fractures are common in trauma patients and may be managed conservatively in the emergency setting. Non-surgical management, including bracing, analgesia, and activity modification, aims to prevent neurological deterioration while promoting early mobilization. The effectiveness and outcomes compared with delayed surgical intervention remain under discussion. Material and Methods: A systematic review of PubMed and Google Scholar was performed using keywords: “thoracolumbar fracture,” “stable fracture,” “non-surgical management,” “orthopedic treatment,” “brace,” and “conservative treatment.” Studies reporting outcomes of conservative treatment, complications, and comparisons with secondary surgical intervention were included. Results: • Conservative treatment with rigid or dynamic bracing, analgesia, and early mobilization resulted in satisfactory pain control and maintenance of spinal alignment in most patients. • Neurological deterioration was rare when appropriate fracture stability criteria were applied. • Comparative studies indicate that outcomes of delayed surgery following failed conservative management were generally favorable, though initial conservative management did not increase complication rates. • Patient compliance, fracture morphology, and regular follow-up imaging were key factors influencing treatment success. • Early mobilization and structured outpatient follow-up reduced the incidence of complications such as chronic pain and postural deformities. Conclusion: Non-surgical management is effective for stable thoracolumbar fractures in the emergency setting, providing good functional and radiological outcomes. Delayed surgical intervention remains a viable option in cases of pain persistence, deformity progression, or neurological compromise. Careful patient selection and close follow-up are essential to optimize outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3301

## P1050: Comparative outcomes of short-segment versus long-segment fixation in thoracolumbar burst fractures

Nikola Bojic

1

1

University Clinical Center of Republic of Srpska, Banja Luka, Bosnia and Herzegovina

Introduction: Whether short-segment (SS) or long-segment (LS) posterior fixation yields superior outcomes in thoracolumbar burst fractures remains debated. Prior meta-analyses and contemporary series suggest SS preserves motion segments and reduces perioperative burden, while LS may better maintain sagittal correction and lower implant failure in select patterns (osteoporosis, comminution). This study compares radiological and clinical outcomes of SS versus LS fixation in AO type A3/A4 fractures and contextualizes findings against recent evidence. Material and Methods: A retrospective cohort of 157 consecutive patients with thoracolumbar burst fractures (AO A3/A4) operated between 2020-2024 was analyzed. Group A received SS fixation (one level above/below), Group B LS fixation (two above/two below). Outcomes included kyphosis correction (Cobb angle) and loss of correction at last follow-up (≥12 months), neurological recovery (Frankel), functional scores (VAS, ODI), perioperative metrics, and complications (implant failure, reoperation). Subgroup exploration considered bone quality and fracture comminution. Results: Both strategies achieved comparable immediate deformity correction. At 12 months, loss of correction averaged 4-6° in SS vs 1-2° in LS, aligning with pooled literature showing greater durability of alignment after LS in selected cases. Implant failure occurred more often after SS (∼7%) than LS (∼3%), consistent with reports identifying higher SS failure in osteoporotic or highly comminuted patterns and the mitigating role of fracture-level screws/bone grafting. Neurological recovery (Frankel) and patient-reported outcomes (VAS, ODI) were similar between groups, while SS demonstrated shorter operative time and lower blood loss. Conclusion: In AO A3/A4 thoracolumbar burst fractures, SS and LS are both effective. SS confers perioperative advantages and segment preservation but shows greater risk of correction loss/implant failure in specific morphologies; LS provides superior radiographic durability where bone quality is poor or comminution is substantial. Findings mirror contemporary meta-analyses and series supporting individualized selection (fracture pattern, bone quality) and the potential value of adjuncts (fracture-level pedicle screws, transpedicular grafting) to enhance SS stability.

## ID: AGS26/3322

## P1051: Pediatric chronic wound caused by a penetrating foreign body

Talel Kamoun

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Elouni Emna

1

, Sofiene Bouali

1

, hatem daoud

1

, Khouloud Bensalem

2

, Houssem Hdhili

1

, Alaa Belhadj

1

1

Institute National de Neurologie Mongi-Ben Hamida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Hospital Habib Bourguiba Sfax, Sfax, Tunisia

Introduction: Chronic wounds are wounds that do not heal in the expected timeframe usually required for healing, thus remaining in the inflammatory stage for too long. They often become a suitable habitat for germs, and infections set in further complicating treatment. It is a health problem often present in old patients with multiple chronic pathologies such as diabetes. In this case report, the presence of such wound in a young patient was found out to be secondary to a penetrating foreign object (plant needle) embedded deep within the wound. Case Report: We discuss the case of a 9-year-old patient, who consulted our emergency room with complaints of a fever and a purulent content leaking from a 1 cm wound at the lumbar region. The patient’s family reported that the wound has been resistant to all treatment and wouldn’t heal despite multiple consultations to the emergency room. Upon examination, the patient exhibited severe pain at the mobilisation of the lumbar spine, he had no motor deficit. He however had an inflammatory mass at the level of the wound, with pus leaking through the wound. Further imaging revealed a purulent collection. The patient was operated on via a posterior approach, per-operatively, a 4 cm plant-needle was discovered that has perforated the dura. No CSF leak was present upon removal. Discussion: Chronic wounds in the paediatric population represent a rare occurrence that should always raise a red flag, especially in a post-traumatic context. In such scenarios, persistent inflammation and failure to respond to conventional treatment should prompt further investigation, including surgical exploration, even when imaging findings are inconclusive. Early identification and removal of the offending agent are critical to resolving infection, preventing complications, and facilitating complete wound healing. Conclusion: This case underscores the importance of considering retained foreign bodies as a potential etiology in pediatric patients presenting with chronic, non-healing wounds, particularly in the absence of underlying systemic.

## ID: AGS26/3339

## P1052: Thoracic duct injury in spine surgery: a systematic review of presentation, management, and clinical outcomes

Mohamad Bakhaidar

1

, Abdulwhab Alotaibi

2

, Gilberto Perez

2

, Ataollah Shahbandi

2

, Omid yousefi

2

, Saman Shabani

2

1

Ottawa University, Ottawa, Canada

2

Medical college of Wisconin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Thoracic duct injury (TDI) is a rare yet a serious complication in spine surgery that requires prompt identification and management. TDI can occur in the setting of spinal trauma or as an iatrogenic damage during spine surgery. Given the rarity of this complication and the paucity of literature on the subject, we conducted a systematic review to summarize all reported cases of TDI related to spine surgery. We aim to provide an up-to-date understanding of the presentation, diagnosis, management, and outcomes of this condition. Material and Methods: We conducted a systematic search to identify articles related to TDI in spine surgery. We included all observational reports that discussed spine surgery-related TDI. Two independent reviewers screened the articles for inclusion. Data were extracted on patient demographics, surgical details, clinical presentation, management strategies, and outcomes. Results: We included 64 studies, involving a total of 70 patients with a mean age of 39 years. Two major groups were included for analysis, TDI secondary to spine trauma (28 patients, 40%) and TDI secondary to an iatrogenic injury (42 patients, 60%). In the traumatic group, injuries were mostly at the thoracolumbar level, with dislocation/translation fractures being the most common type (31%). In the iatrogenic group, the primary surgical indication was spinal deformity (54%) followed by degenerative spine disease (24%). Anterior cervical approaches were the cause of the TDI in 8 patients (19.05%). Patients presented with a median of 3 days after the initial etiological event. Chylothorax was the most frequent clinical diagnosis in both groups (70%). The most common therapeutic intervention was percutaneous drainage 80%, and most of the patients had complete resolution (96%). Two patients died due to complications. Conclusion: This review offers an updated overview of this challenging complication and help to guide the diagnosis and management of this condition.

## ID: AGS26/3342

## P1053: Stability of the lumbar spine in traumatic paraplegia following fracture dislocation

Özge Selahi

1

1

Kırıkkale Yüksek İhtisas Hastanesi, Kırıkkale, Turkey

Introduction: More than half of all spinal trauma cases involve a thoracolumbar fracture, making it the most frequent spinal injury. However, thoracolumbar fracture-dislocations are rare and account for less than 3% of these injuries. A combination of shear, rotation, and flexion-extension forces can cause trauma to both static and dynamic stabilizing elements, including the vertebral bodies, discs, facets, and ligaments, resulting in vertebral fracture-dislocations that disrupt the entire spinal column. Subsequent paraplegia is more common in cases of thoracolumbar spine dislocation. Here, we report a case of complete fracture-dislocation of L2 through L1 vertebrae with paraplegia. Material and Methods: A 43-year-old man presented with acute back pain secondary to a fall. He was immediately transferred to the emergency department, complaining of severe back pain and having complete paraplegia and anesthesia of his lower extremities. The physical examination at admission revealed stable vital signs and several small abrasions on the lower back. Bilateral lower extremity strength was 1/5 with complaints of urinary and fecal incontinence. Radiographs and computed tomography (CT) showed fracture dislocation at the L1-L2 level, more than 50% anterolisthesis at L1-L2 with anterior wedging of L2. and an initial magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed complete ligamentous disruption through the disc space and disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex (PLC) with subluxation of the vertebral bodies, classified as AO L1/2 type C, L2 type A1. The patient underwent emergent open decompression at L1-2 and pedicle screw fixation at T12-L4. The displaced segment was carefully reduced using rod distraction, and a cross-connector was applied. In addition, decortication of the posterior elements and autologous bone grafting were performed before drainage and wound closure. The patient was placed in a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO). The TLSO was continued for about 3 months. At the end of the 3-month follow-up, the patient was able to mobilize without support, but urinary and fecal incontinence still continued. Results: Since the spine is unstable as a result of fracture-dislocation and insufficiency of the 3 columns, it requires surgical intervention that includes decompression of the neurological elements, reduction of abnormal alignment, and stabilization with spinal fusion. Surgical treatment for individuals with spinal cord injuries is now recognized as an initial component of the intricate rehabilitation process for these patients. The most important results after the procedure were the beneficial psychological effect of early mobilisation and the adjustment to a new way of living with their disability. Conclusion: Traumatic thoracolumbar fractures (TLF) are common injuries that occur after trauma. Burst fractures compromise the most commonly encountered TLF. Thoracolumbar spine fracture-dislocation, which usually occurs as a result of high-energy trauma, is frequently associated with spinal cord injury, and severe spinal fractures leading to dislocation can lead to complete neurological dysfunction. Patients with fracture-dislocation spinal injuries and spinal cord transections experienced the most severe outcomes, whereas individuals with wedge compression fractures and cord edema had more favorable results. In order to provide a stable environment for neurological and musculoskeletal repair, the spine should be stabilized as soon as possible.

## ID: AGS26/3355

## P1054: Radiological and functional outcomes of thoracolumbar burst fractures screw fixation: open vs percutaneous pedicle instrumentation

Bousrih Anis

1

, Ahmed Ben Hnia

1

, HIchem Abid

1

, yousef sbai

1

, Ramzi Bouzidi

1

, Mouadh Nefiss

1

1

Mongi Slim Hospital, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Burst fractures of the spine, particularly at the thoracolumbar junction, constitute a significant public health issue. The optimal surgical strategy, particularly for Magerl type A3 fractures, remains a matter of ongoing debate. This study aimed to compare the functional and radiological outcomes of percutaneous versus open osteosynthesis for thoracolumbar vertebral fractures. Material and Methods: We conducted a comparative study in the Department of Orthopedic Surgery at Mongi Slim Hospital, involving 32 patients with Magerl type A3 fractures of the thoracolumbar junction (T12-L2) over 7 years from January 1, 2015, to December 31, 2022, with a mean follow-up of 45.75 months. For each group, we evaluated epidemiological parameters, functional outcomes (Visual Analog Scale [VAS], Oswestry Disability Index [ODI] )and radiological parameters (vertebral kyphosis, regional kyphosis, traumatic regional angulation, Load Sharing Score [LSS], Beck’s index). Results: The study population was predominantly male (sex ratio 1.67) with a mean age of 46.2 years. The most common causes of injury were domestic trauma, followed by occupational accidents, with 76% of cases being isolated injuries. Compared with conventional surgery, percutaneous instrumentation was associated with significantly reduced operative time, hospital stay, postoperative recovery, and perioperative blood loss. Functionally, patients treated percutaneously had lower postoperative pain (VAS) and disability (ODI) scores (p < 0.001), as well as faster mobilization and return to prior activities, indicating quicker overall recovery. Radiological outcomes, including vertebral and regional kyphosis and Beck’s index, were similar between the two groups. However, the percutaneous technique resulted in significantly higher exposure to radiation. Conclusion: Percutaneous thoracolumbar osteosynthesis is a safe and effective approach, offering reduced perioperative morbidity, faster functional recovery, and satisfactory radiological results. It is particularly recommended for fractures without neurological deficits or associated ligamentous injuries.

## ID: AGS26/3396

## P1055: Surgical management of spinal fractures in ankylosing spondylitis: a retrospective study of 30 cases

Mohamed Amine Dhifallah

1

, Adnen Ben ammou

1

, Souha Bennour

1

, Mohamed Seddik Akremi

1

, Cherif Kammoun

1

, Mohamed Ben Salah

1

1

Hôpital Charles Nicolle, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal fractures in ankylosing spondylitis are unstable lesions associated with high morbidity and mortality. Surgical management is often required, but outcomes remain debated. This study aimed to evaluate the surgical results of spinal fractures in ankylosing spondylitis patients. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective review of 30 patients treated surgically for spinal fractures between 2015 and 2024. Demographic data, fracture location, neurological status, surgical approach, and postoperative outcomes were analyzed. Results: The mean age was 58 years, with a male predominance (85%). Cervical fractures were most common (60%), followed by thoracolumbar lesions (40%). Neurological deficits were present in 50% of cases. Posterior instrumentation was performed in 80% of patients. At final follow-up, 70% showed neurological improvement, with a complication rate of 20%. Conclusion: Surgical stabilization of spinal fractures in ankylosing spondylitis provides favorable functional outcomes despite high complication rates. Early diagnosis and appropriate surgical strategy are essential to reduce morbidity.

## ID: AGS26/3413

## P1056: Conservative management of unstable thoracolumbar fractures: a case series in medically fragile patients

Himanshu Shekhar

1

, Katie Hoban

1

1

NHS Tayside, University of Dundee, Dundee, United Kingdom

Introduction: Thoracolumbar fractures account for a significant proportion of spinal injuries, with management guided by fracture stability and neurological status. Surgical stabilisation is generally recommended for Type B2/B3 and considered mandatory for Type C injuries due to their high instability. However, surgery may be contraindicated in patients with severe comorbidities or polytrauma. Material and Methods: We report a case series of four patients (mean age 71 years; range 54-89) with unstable thoracolumbar fractures (two Type B, two Type C) managed conservatively. Three patients had ankylosing spondylitis. Non-operative management was chosen in three cases due to medical unsuitability for surgery and in one case by patient preference. Treatment included spinal precautions, gradual postural fracture reduction, mobilisation in a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO), and close radiological monitoring. Results: All patients achieved fracture healing with maintained alignment and no requirement for delayed surgical intervention. TLSO bracing was discontinued after confirmed healing, and no major complications were observed during inpatient care or follow-up. Conclusion: This case series demonstrates that a structured conservative protocol can provide satisfactory outcomes in selected patients with unstable thoracolumbar fractures who are unsuitable for surgery. These findings support the role of individualised, multidisciplinary decision-making, though larger studies are needed to validate this approach.

## ID: AGS26/3445

## P1057: Efficacy, safety, and reliability of a single posterior approach for unstable thoracolumbar burst fractures treated with anterior reconstruction and posterior instrumentation

Asraf Ul Matin

1

1

Comilla Medical College, Cumilla, Bangladesh

Introduction: The optimal management of unstable thoracolumbar burst fractures remains debated. While combined anterior-posterior instrumentation provides robust stability, it is associated with increased morbidity. A single posterior approach that enables anterior column reconstruction and posterior fixation may achieve decompression, deformity correction, and stabilization with reduced surgical burden. Methods: Between January 2017 and June 2025, 27 patients (18 males, 9 females; mean age 39.8 ± 11.9 years, range 16-60) with acute unstable thoracolumbar burst fractures (T11-L4) and neurological deficits were treated using a single posterior approach with anterior reconstruction and posterior instrumentation. Inclusion criteria were McCormack’s load-sharing score ≥ 6 and thoracolumbar injury severity score (TLISS) ≥ 5. Outcomes assessed included neurological recovery (ASIA scale), pain (Visual Analogue Scale, VAS), radiological correction (kyphotic angle, Cobb’s method), and operative parameters (duration, blood loss). Results: The mean operative time was 248 ± 36 minutes (range: 200-320), with an average blood loss of 465 ± 110 mL (range: 320-680). Neurological recovery showed a mean ASIA grade improvement of 1.6 ± 0.5 in patients with incomplete spinal cord injuries, while those with complete injuries remained unchanged at final follow-up. Radiologically, the mean kyphotic angle improved from 24.7° ± 5.9 (range: 17-38) preoperatively to 5.6° ± 2.4 (range: 2-10) postoperatively. Pain scores improved significantly, with VAS decreasing from 6.0 ± 1.1 (range: 4-8) preoperatively to 1.8 ± 0.7 (range: 1-3) at final follow-up. The mean follow-up duration was 44.2 ± 19.1 months (range: 12-96). Conclusion: The single posterior approach is a safe, reliable, and cost-effective surgical option for unstable thoracolumbar burst fractures provides effective decompression, deformity correction, and stabilization in unstable thoracolumbar burst fractures. Compared with combined anterior-posterior surgery, It allows reconstruction of all spinal columns with reduced morbidity and satisfactory neurological, radiological, and functional results.
