## ID: AGS26/56

## P1058: Patient-specific 3D-printed PEEK implants for vertebral body replacement in spinal tumor surgery

Nikita Zaborovskii

1

, Sergei Masevnin

1

, Vladislav Murakhovsky

1

, Dmitry Ptashnikov

2

1

Vreden National Medical Research Center of Traumatology and Orthopedics, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

2

Clinical Hospital of the Russian Academy of Sciences, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Anterior column reconstruction after spinal tumor resection remains a complex challenge. Traditional options - autografts, allografts, and implants from titanium or other materials - have inherent limitations, especially regarding imaging artifacts and biomechanical compatibility. Polyetheretherketone (PEEK) is increasingly favored due to its radiolucency, biocompatibility, and mechanical properties similar to bone. The advent of 3D printing enables the creation of patient-specific PEEK implants (PSI), potentially improving anatomical fit and surgical outcomes. This study evaluates the feasibility, integration, and clinical results of 3D-printed PEEK PSI for vertebral body replacement (VBR) in patients with spinal tumors over a 12-month follow-up. Material and Methods: This retrospective single-center case series included five patients (three males, two females, mean age 51.2 years) who underwent VBR with 3D-printed PEEK PSI following tumor resection between April 2022 and June 2023. Inclusion criteria were thoracic or lumbar spinal tumors (benign, primary malignant, or solitary metastatic), PEEK 3D VBR reconstruction, and follow-up exceeding 12 months. Implants were designed from CT data using BonaPlanner software and manufactured via fused filament fabrication from medical-grade PEEK. Surgical approaches and fixation methods were tailored to each case. Outcomes assessed included radiological evidence of bony fusion (Brantigan-Steffee classification), local angle and segment height stability, and complications. Descriptive statistics were performed using R software. Results: The mean follow-up was 19.2 months (range: 12-28 months). All patients survived the follow-up period; one experienced local recurrence. Three patients received postoperative radiotherapy. Postoperative imaging demonstrated a decrease in local angle without significant progression during follow-up. Segment heights remained stable; no implant subsidence or hardware failure occurred. All patients achieved at least probable radiographic fusion, with three demonstrating solid fusion. No adverse tissue reactions or infection were observed. Conclusion: Patient-specific 3D-printed PEEK implants for vertebral body replacement in spinal tumor surgery are feasible and demonstrate promising clinical outcomes, including stable integration, minimal complications, and clear postoperative imaging. The technology offers significant advantages for surgical planning and postoperative management, particularly in oncological settings requiring radiotherapy. However, customization is resource-intensive, and intraoperative adaptability remains a challenge. Larger, prospective studies are warranted to confirm these results and further define the role of PEEK PSI in complex spinal reconstruction.

## ID: AGS26/94

## P1059: Initial experience with painvision for the evaluation of pain due to spinal cord tumors

Takahiro Tanaka

1

1

Yokohama City University School of Medicine, Yokohama, Japan

Introduction: PainVisionR was introduced in 2007; however, reports on its application in the field of spine surgery are limited. Our department adopted PainVisionR for spinal surgery cases starting in April 2024. Since spinal tumors often present with radicular neuropathic pain, we evaluated the degree of neuropathic pain before and after tumor resection using Pain Vision™ and report our findings. Material and Methods: We analyzed 16 spinal tumor surgeries performed in our department from April 2024 onward (10 schwannomas, 4 meningiomas, 1 choroid plexus papilloma, and 1 Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma), including 8 male and 6 female patients. Neuropathic pain caused by spinal tumors was assessed using both the Visual Analog Scale (VAS) and PainVisionR pain intensity values. Pain improvement rates were calculated based on these measurements. Results: The mean patient age was 54 years. Postoperative pain evaluations were conducted on average 6.8 days after surgery. Surgical treatment significantly improved VAS scores from 45 to 5 (p < 0.001) and PainVisionR pain intensity from 199.9 to 35.3 (p < 0.001). The improvement rates were 70.4% for VAS and 73.6% for PainVisionR, with no significant difference between the two improvement rates (p = 0.84). The correlation between the two evaluation items was moderately strong (r = 0.514, p = 0.042). Surgical resection significantly improved neuropathic pain both subjectively (VAS) and objectively (PainVisionR). No significant difference was observed between the improvement rates measured by the two methods, suggesting that PainVisionR is a useful objective tool for evaluating neuropathic pain associated with spinal tumors. Conclusion: For surgeons, PainVisionR provides a valuable objective measure to assess the efficacy of surgical treatment beyond imaging studies like MRI, by quantifying neuropathic pain. This tool may therefore aid in clinical decision-making and outcome evaluation in spinal tumor surgery.

## ID: AGS26/113

## P1060: Total excision of upper thoracic eden type 3 dumbell tumor by posterior approach alone: a case report

Uday Raman

1

, Nikhil Kadanthode

1

, Sreejith Chandran Menon

1

, Midhun P.C

1

1

Meitra Hospital, Kozhikode, India

Introduction: Schwannomas of the neural elements of the spine account for approximately 25% of all spinal tumors. Most commonly in thoracic spine followed by cervical and lumbar regions. Dumbbell tumor is a conceptual definition where a tumor connects two or more separate regions such as intradural space, epidural space, and locations outside the paravertebral space. Schwannoma sometimes extend into the root through the foramen and then extend into the paravertebral space thus forming a dumbbell tumor. Surgical strategies for treating these tumors is dependent on Eden’s classification. A combined posterior and thoracotomy approach in two stages is the usual surgical option. If the extraforaminal tumor component is large, a combined posterolateral approach which includes a costotranversectomy and thoracotomy is used with a multispecialty team. We would like to present a case of a giant Schwannoma of the upper thoracic spine, where complete resection was achieved with a single stage, posterior surgery only. Material and Methods: 52-Year-old male presented with upper back ache and numbness of both lower limbs of 3 months with unsteadiness of gait. Neurological examination revealed upper motor neuron signs in the lower limb sparing the upper limbs. MRI revealed a Lobulated intraspinal , extradural lesion with left neural foraminal and extraspinal extension (Dumbell shaped appearance) at T5-T6 and CT revealed smooth scalloping and widening of left neural foramen of the T5 vertebra by dumbbell shaped lesion with Intra-thoracic extension posterior to aorta with thinning and erosion of left T5 pedicle, transverse process and lamina of the T5 vertebra .He was diagnosed to have T5-T6 extradural dumbbell tumor (Eden type 3) with Nurick grade II Thoracic compressive myelopathy. He underwent T5-T6 decompression, tumor excision and biopsy, T4-T7 posterior instrumented stabilisation & posterolateral fusion. A single posterior midline incision with Laminectomy of T5 and T6 and Partial laminectomy of T4 and a left sided T5 and T6 Costotransversectomy was done to expose the tumor in its entirety. The tumor was arising from the T5 nerve root which had to be sacrificed to excise the tumor in total measuring 4.0x3.7x2.0 cm. Care was taken to avoid any pleural or aortic injury by using blunt instrumentation to free the tumor margins. Posterior Instrumentation was done from T4-T7 for stability. Haemostatsis was achieved and routine closure was done. Results: Postoperatively left T5 dermatome paraesthesia was seen with no additional major complications. Incentive spirometry was started on post operative day 1, started walking on post operative day 2 with drain removed on the same day. Postoperative chest x ray showed no signs of haemothorax, pneumothorax or atelectasis. He was discharged on postoperative day 3. Histopathology confirmed Schwannoma as diagnosis without malignancy. Followup at 1 and 6 months showed recovery of upper motor neuron signs and gait improvement. No radiological signs of recurrence were seen. Conclusion: Our novel approach of single-stage surgery with laminectomy and costotransversectomy may be a useful approach in excising thoracic dumbbell tumors especially in thoracic spine thus potentially avoiding major thoracic surgery and its complications.

## ID: AGS26/136

## P1061: Epidural lumbar spinal Schwannoma: case report and literature discussion

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia, Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia, Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia, tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Nerve sheath tumors are the most common spinal neoplasms and make up for 40% of extramedullary tumors of the spinal column. Majority of these lesions are purely intradural (50-80%) or associated to an extradural extension (10-20%). Purely extradural schwannomas account for 1-10% of all spinal schwannomas and are more often seen in the cervical region. Lumbar extradural schwannomas account for 2.4-3.2% of all nerve sheath tumors. This report presents the case of a 53 year old patient with chronic back pain and bilateral crural pain. Material and Methods: A 53-year-old man with no medical history complained of back and crural pain associated to progressive paresthesia in both legs for 1 year. Neurological examination was normal except for mild hypoesthesia in both lower limbs, without particular systematization. Among other negative signs, the patient had neither sphincter disorders, nor signs in favor of neurofibromatosis. Computed tomography (Figure 1) showed a left giant lumbar L1-L2tumor associated to a scalloping of the L1-L2 left intervertebral foramen. Magnetic resonance imaging (Figure 2 and 3) disclosed at a voluminous, circular lesion at the level of left L1-L2 foramina. This tumor was hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, with intensive enhancement after Gadolinium injection. On axial sections, the terminal cone was deflected to the opposite side of the lesion. Initially, a scan guided biopsy was performed. Pathological examination concluded to a schwannoma. Results: Nerve sheath tumors are the most common spinal neoplasms, and represent up to 40% of extramedullary tumors of the spinal column. The majority of these lesions are purely intradural (50-80%) or intradural associated to extradural extension (10-20%). Purely extradural schwannomas only account for 1-10% of all spinal schwannomas, and are more often found in the cervical region. Lumbar extradural schwannomas account for 2.4-3.2% of all nerve sheath tumors. In the literature, extradural schwannomas have not been the focus of investigation among the series of spinal nerve sheath tumors, and have been the subject of relatively little study in general. Most of the studies that discusses this dumbbell shaped tumor presented more often as a single case report or small series. Clinical manifestations of lumbar epidural schwannomas are not specific. They are usually shortened to chronic back pain associated to crural pain and limb numbness. In some cases cauda equina syndrome can been seen. MRI is the gold standard in matter of diagnosis for spinal tumors, and schwannomas in all different locations in general (8). Epidural schwannomas are usually hypointense on T1-weigted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and show intense contrast enhancement. The enhancement is probably due to the opening of gap junctions, that freely communicating with a relatively large extracellular space. The aim of surgical treatment of spinal schwannomas is to get a total removal of the tumor, and to optimize functional outcome through conservation of the pathological nerve root. Different strategies for surgical management have been discussed by several authors. Giant dumb-bell tumors are effectively managed by a combined posterior and anterolateral approach or a single stage lateral extracavitary approach. Tumors with a large foraminal and paraspinal component limited to one segmental level can be managed by hemi-laminectomy, unilateral facetectomy and intertransverse approach. In our experience, we reached the tumor through a facetectomy that made it possible to remove the tumor in its totality. Purely paraspinal nerve sheath tumours have been traditionally approached through posterior midline incision with muscle dissection lateral to the facet and tip of transverse. Conclusion: Purely extradural lumbar schwannomas are uncommon. They are rarely described as an individualized entity. Clinical manifestations are not specific and the MRI is the most useful method for diagnosis. Classic surgical approaches are progressively regressing in the benefits of minimal invasive ones, which allow resection of the tumors without compromising spinal balance, and even for giant forms.

## ID: AGS26/143

## P1062: Extraventricular neurocytoma of the medullar conus: an exceptional pathologic diagnosis

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Ines Ferchichi

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Central neurocytoma is currently recognized as a distinct class of central nervous system (CNS) tumors. It is classically defined as a typically benign supratentorial mass located in the lateral ventricles or the third ventricle. Extraventricular localizations are very rare, in particular those in the spinal cord, reported only in sporadic cases. We report a new case of medullar neurocytoma. Material and Methods: We present the case of a patient operated for an intramedullar tumor which was thought to be an ependymoma on imaging. Only pathologic exam redressed the diagnosis by revealing an extraventricular neurocytoma. Results: We report the case of a 12-year-old girl, with no notable pathological history, who presented for the progressive onset of a weakness of the lower limbs since 6 months, associated with a sphincteric dysfunction since 1 year. She also complains of chronic low back pain. Neurological examination revealed flaccid paraplegia, lower extremity areflexia with a right Babinski sign and L1 sensory level. Magnetic resonance imaging showed an intramedullary intradural tumor at the level of lower thoracic spine, with extensive syringohydromyelia. The tumor was in iso-T1 and slightly in hyper-T2. Homogeneous enhancement is observed after injection of Gadolinium. The diagnosis of ependymoma was initially suspected. The decision was to operate the patient. A laminectomy from D10 to D12 was performed, followed by complete resection of a haemorrhagic, friable, grayish intramedullary tumor surrounded by a thin white capsule. Postoperative, the patient developed transient constipation, and no motor improvement was observed until discharge. Pathological exam with immunohistochemistry redressed the diagnosis towards a grade II extraventricular neurocytoma. No adjuvant treatment was undertaken. 5 months after the surgery, the patient could walk with crutches, and the sphincter disorders have regressed. Conclusion: Central medullary neurocytoma is a rare entity with only sporadic cases reported in the literature. Surgical resection is the reference treatment and the diagnosis is based on an immunohistochemical study.

## ID: AGS26/144

## P1063: Primary cervical localization of myxoid liposarcoma: case report and review of the litterature

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Aziz Bedioui

1

, Mohamed Aziz Hermassi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Liposarcoma is the most common soft tissue sarcoma in adults. The myxoid subtype is the second most frequency lesion, accounting for 30% to 40% of cases. Extrapulmonary metastatic locations are often found especially in the skeleton, with a propensity to the spine. However, primary spinal involvement is rare, since only five cases have been identified so far. Hereby, we present a case of primary liposarcoma of the cervical spine. Material and Methods: We report the case of an 80-year-old patient, suffering from arterial hypertension, who presented for the onset of a motor deficit in the 4 limbs since 15 days. Physical examination found a spastic quadriplegia. MRI revealed a spinal cord compression by an extensive epiduritis ranging from C3 to C7, with adjacent intramedullar hyperintensity. Facing this presentation, the diagnosis of infectious spondyliscitis was considered at first. The patient was operated for medullar decompression. Pathological examination concluded to a grade 2 myxoid liposarcoma. Results: Liposarcoma is the most common soft tissue tumor. It accounts for about 20% of all malignant mesenchymal tumors. Five histological types are described: well differentiated, dedifferentiated, myxoid, round cells and pleomorphic. Myxoid liposarcomas are usually found in the extremities, especially the thighs. Unlike other types of liposarcoma which typically metastasize to the lungs, myxoid liposarcomas tend to spread to extrapulmonary sites, such as retroperitoneum, mediastinum, chest wall, peritoneum, and heart. This predilection can be explained by the abundance of adipose tissue at these locations. Complete surgical resection combined to radiotherapy is usually sufficient for local control. Chemotherapy may be indicated in case of metastases. Nevertheless, prognosis remains reserved. Conclusion: Liposarcoma is the most common soft tissue tumor in adults. However, primary spinal involvement is very rare. Despite of its rarity, our case demonstrates that liposarcoma should be discussed as a differential diagnosis of cervical tumors.

## ID: AGS26/264

## P1064: Clinical outcomes after spine surgery for patients with potentially unstable metastatic spinal disease (Spine Instability Neoplastic Score 7-12): a systematic review

Anthony Baumann

1

, Grayson Talakasi

2

, Omkar Anaspure

3

, Blake Stoller

4

, Mathias uhler

5

, Erika Bahler

6

, Ethan Cottrill

7

, Maria LaMontagne

1

, Keegan Conry

1

, Gordon Preston

1

, Jacob Hoffmann

1

1

Cleveland Clinic Akron General, Akron, United States

2

University of Iowa, Iowa City, United States

3

Perelman School of Medicine, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, United States

4

University of Toledo, Toledo, United States

5

University of Akron, Akron, United States

6

Department of Family Medicine, Piedmont Healthcare, Peachtree, United State

7

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Duke University, Durham, United States

Introduction: While the surgical management of metastatic spinal disease has wide consensus for “stable” (Spine Instability Neoplastic Score [SINS] 0-6) and “unstable” (SINS 13-18) disease, there is currently limited consensus regarding spine surgery for patients with “potentially unstable” (SINS 7-12) metastatic disease. However, in the literature, few robust systematic reviews have examined this topic. The purpose of this study is to examine the clinical outcomes for patients with potentially unstable metastatic spinal disease who underwent spine surgery. Material and Methods: This systematic review examined articles from PubMed, CINAHL, MEDLINE, and Web of Science from database inception until December 18th, 2024. Inclusion criteria were studies that examined clinical outcomes in patients with SINS 7-12 who underwent spine surgery. Results: Among 111 articles, 10 observational studies were included. Of 1,546 patients, 942 with SINS 7-12 underwent spine surgery for metastases. Average postoperative survival ranged from 10-14 months, with an 80% six-month survival and 59% one-year survival. One article found a statistically significant survival benefit with surgery over radiation, while two did not. Patients with SINS 10-12 were more likely to receive instrumentation than SINS 7-9. Surgery may be more effective than radiation for patient-reported outcomes. Rates of subsequent spine surgery after non-surgical treatments ranged from 2.3%-25.5%. Risk factors for instrumentation may include osteolytic lesions and > 50% vertebral body collapse. Cutoff scores of SINS 9 or 10 were associated with instrumentation. Conclusion: Average postoperative survival in patients with SINS 7-12 after spine surgery ranged from 10-14 months with an 80% six month survival and a 59-76% one year survival, with a trend towards no difference in postoperative survival in patients treated with spine surgery with or without radiation versus radiation alone. Spine surgery for patients with SINS 7-12 may have benefit for both neurological status and patient reported outcome measures, although benefits are unclear in comparison to radiation alone. Subgroups of spine instability within SINS 7-12 may exist, as patients with SINS 7-9 were less likely to receive instrumentation as compared to patients with SINS 10-12 with SINS 9 or 10 representing a cut off for instrumentation. Rates of subsequent spine surgery in patients with SINS 7-12 after non-surgical treatment failure ranged from 4.0-25.5%, suggesting caution should be exercised when choosing non-surgical treatment options. No individual component of the SINS criteria was a clear risk factor for spine surgery in patients with SINS 7-12. This study represents the first systematic review and largest study examining clinical outcomes after spine surgery for patients with SINS 7-12 with data based on ten moderate quality observational studies. The results and conclusions of this study should be taken with caution, given the high study heterogeneity and very low certainty of evidence. Future randomized studies should examine SINS 7-12 as well as subgroups with SINS 7-12 to further enhance decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/306

## P1065: Different treatment strategies for primary spinal aneurysmal bone cyst: a mean 5-year follow-up study from a single spinal center

Chaoyuan Ge

1

, Wenlong Yang

1

, Zhengwei Xu

1

1

Xi'an Honghui Hospital, Xi'an, China

Introduction: There are few reports on primary spinal aneurysmal bone cysts due to its extremely low incidence. The aim of this study is to investigate the efficacy of different treatment strategies for primary spinal aneurysmal bone cyst. Material and Methods: Patients with primary spinal aneurysmal bone cyst treated between November 2015 and November 2022 in our hospital were retrospectively analyzed. Patients’ basic information, preoperative spinal cord function score, lesion location, treatment strategies, and postoperative remission were collected. Results: A total of 12 patients were included in this study, including 7 males and 5 females aged from 16 to 56 years old. The average follow-up period was 5 years. Among the 12 patients, 8 patients presented with local pain, 4 patients presented with local pain and neurologic symptoms of extremities, of which 3 were classified as ASIC D, 1 was ASIC C. The cyst arose in the cervical spine in one patient, in the thoracic spine in 3 patients and in the lumbar spine in 6 patients. Only spinal posterior column was involved in 2 patients, and both anterior and posterior columns were involved in 8 patients. Seven patients received open surgery, among whom 6 patients had symptom relief and 5 patients had no recurrence in long-term follow- up. Two patients received minimally invasive arterial embolization, and clinical symptoms were significantly relieved, the lesions were reduced and ossified in long-term follow-up. One patient was treated with denosumab, and the symptoms were relieved, the lesions were reduced and ossified in the last follow-up. Conclusion: The tumor size and severity of spinal aneurysmal bone cyst are varied in patients. Personalized operation scheme is helpful to relieve pain and nerve compression, maintain spinal stability, reconstruct bone structure, reduce postoperative recurrence and increase the benefit of patients.

## ID: AGS26/334

## P1066: 3D-printed vertebral body improves reconstruction after C2 tumor resection: a comparative study

Pan-pan HU

1

, Feng Wei

1

, Zhongjun Liu

1

, Xiaoguang Liu

1

1

Peking University Third Hospital, Beijing, China

Introduction: C2 vertebral tumors pose major surgical challenges due to the complex anatomy of the upper cervical spine, proximity to the vertebral artery, and the need for stable reconstruction following resection. Conventional titanium mesh constructs have historically been used for anterior column reconstruction but are associated with hardware complications. The advent of personalized 3D-printed artificial vertebral bodies (AVBs) offers the potential for improved stability and osseointegration. This study compared surgical outcomes between titanium mesh and 3D-printed AVBs after C2 tumor resection. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 31 consecutive patients with C2 vertebral tumors treated between 2009 and 2020 at a single tertiary spine center. All underwent staged posterior fixation and anterior resection via retropharyngeal, transoral, or transmandibular approaches. Patients were divided into two groups according to reconstruction material: titanium mesh (n = 13) or personalized 3D-printed AVB (n = 18). Clinical data, surgical variables, complications, and fixation status were analyzed. The primary endpoint was implant stability during follow-up. Results: The cohort included 14 chordomas, 11 giant cell tumors, and six other histologies. Gross total resection was achieved in 90.3% of patients, and en bloc resection in 9.7%. The overall perioperative complication rate was 45.2%, with mortality of 6.5%. Patients reconstructed with titanium mesh had a higher complication rate than those with AVBs (69.2% vs. 27.8%, p = 0.022). Approach selection influenced outcomes: retropharyngeal procedures had fewer complications compared with transoral or transmandibular approaches (30.0% vs. 72.7%, p = 0.022). During follow-up, hardware failure occurred in 4/13 titanium mesh patients (30.8%) compared with 1/18 AVB patients (5.6%, p = 0.060). Titanium mesh complications included implant migration, loosening, and rod breakage. In contrast, AVBs demonstrated reliable fixation, reduced halo vest dependence (16.7% vs. 76.9%, p = 0.003), and better conformity to anatomical defects. Conclusion: C2 tumor resection remains technically demanding and carries substantial risk, but reconstruction using 3D-printed AVBs provides superior implant stability and fewer approach-related complications compared with titanium mesh. Personalized AVBs not only reduce the incidence of hardware failure but also minimize the need for external immobilization. Our findings support the use of 3D-printed AVBs as a safer and more effective reconstructive option in upper cervical surgery, though further refinement of prosthesis design is warranted.

## P1067: A case series describing operative interventions for spinal metastatic disease undertaken In Oxford, UK, from 2020-2022

Jennifer Paskins

1,2

, Victoria Bradley

1,2

, Euan Stirling

1,3

, Gerard Mawhinney

1,3,4

, Jeremy Reynolds

1,3

, Alex Anderson

2

, Nicolas Beresford-Cleary

1,3

1

OxMINT, Oxford Metastatic Intervention, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

2

Department of Palliative Medicine, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

3

Oxford Spinal Surgery Unit, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

4

Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Oxford, Oxford, United Kingdom

Introduction: Metastatic or secondary bone tumours are known to be associated with skeletal-related events, such as pathological fractures, spinal cord compression, or hypercalcemia. These deposits are often highly painful and cause significant functional impairment and harm to quality of life. Between January 2020 and December 2022 the Oxford Spine Unit undertook operative intervention on 88 cases of metastatic spinal disease. This case series will look to explore the patterns of activity seen in those cases including surgical outcomes, complication rates and mortality. Material and Methods: A retrospective audit of surgical cases was undertaken using the electronic patient recorded based on coding for spinal surgical intervention and metastatic cancer affecting the bony skeleton. Data was extracted by a team of clinicians using a pre-agreed data capture template with key measures drawn from the literature including prognostic scores such as Tomita and Tokuhashi, functional scores including the Spinal Metastatic Staging System and FRANKEL and complication categorisation SAVES. Results: 88 patients underwent operative intervention in the two year period with on average two spinal levels being instrumented. 56% presented with stage 4 disease denoting neurological impairment at the time of assessment and 80% had emergent rather than timely surgery undertaken. The average Tokuhashi score for the cohort was 9.5 and the average Tomita score was 4.8 with only 10% having a narrative prognostication offered by the home oncologist who knew the patient. Frankel scores were mapped from pre to post operatively with 82% having scores of D and E post operatively. 28% of patients had recognised post operative complications with average survival of 12 months and an average hospital stay of 19 days. Conclusion: This data offers an insight into practice at a large regional referral centre for spine pathology in the UK. It suggests that the majority of cases are presenting emergently with established neurology with concomitant impact on outcomes, complications and the healthcare economy.

## P1068: Describing a study searching for the individualized best outcome for patients with metastatic spinal disease

Jennifer Paskins

1,2

, Victoria Bradley

1,2

, Jeremy Reynolds

1,3

, Euan Stirling

1,3

, Gerard Mawhinney

1,3,4

, Alex Anderson

1,2

, Nicolas Beresford-Cleary

1,3

1

OxMINT, Oxford Metastatic Intervention, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

2

Department of Palliative Medicine, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

3

Oxford Spinal Surgery Unit, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

4

Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Oxford, Oxford, United Kingdom

Introduction: Recent work has focused on the concept of a “textbook outcome” for patients with metastatic spinal disease by understanding key influencing factors such as physiology and frailty, prognosis, pathophysiology and risk of neurological impairment as well as the core underpinning belief that surgery is in fact a gold standard treatment for such disease. We will describe a study which has commenced to explore the patient’s own concept of what is a “best” outcome. Extensive research has offered insights into the potential myriad factors that affect patient decision making and experience around such major intervention such as expectations, distress, understanding, thresholds of acceptability and uncertainty. Material and Methods: We are undertaking a feasibility study to start to delineate the concept of a “best outcome”. We aim to recruit 15 patients with metastatic spinal disease who are referred for consideration of surgical or radiological intervention. Inclusion Criteria: adults over the age of 18, metastatic malignancy of any cellular origin, performance status conducive to engagement e.g. greater than or equal to 40% AKPS at referral, able to speak English fluently (can be a non-primary language). Patients enrolled would be interviewed pre and post any intervention (surgery, IR) using a semi-structured interview format derived from the published concepts to explore the individual’s expectations, priorities and values and thresholds of acceptability. Patients will be stratified based on their spinal metastatic staging (I-IV) and the influence of key demographic categories on their decision making will be explicitly explored including the impact of socio-economic factors, health state and place in treatment pathway (e.g. how many lines of treatment remain open to them). Results: We would share our study protocol and early thematic analysis of findings which would ensure they covered the patient’s perspective on the key metrics being explored in the AO KF Tumour Frailty Working Group discrete choice work i.e. complications post intervention and any potential to systemic anti-cancer treatment, ambulatory status and functional status. Conclusion: We hope to be able to demonstrate some early understanding growing out of this work around the essential element of personalisation in spinal tumour surgery when thinking about outcomes.

## P1069: A description of available integrated networks of managing pain from metastatic bone disease in the United Kingdom

Alex Anderson

1,2

, Victoria Bradley

1,2

, Jennifer Paskins

3

, Euan Stirling

1,4

, Gerard Mawhinney

1,4,5

, Jeremy Reynolds

1,4

, Nicolas Beresford-Cleary

1,4

1

OxMINT, Oxford Metastatic Intervention, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

2

Department of Palliative Medicine, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

3

Sobell House, Oxford University Hospitals, Oxford, United Kingdom

4

Oxford Spinal Surgery Unit, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

5

Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Oxford, Oxford, United Kingdom

Introduction: We would estimate based on rising prevalence of cancer in the UK predicted to reach 3.5million in 2025, the number of patients living with bone metastases will reach epidemic proportions. While management pathways of the much rarer primary bone tumours are well established there has not until now there has not been a unified pathway for the review of secondary bone tumour patients in the UK to ensure equitable and appropriate management. We have previously presented the setup of a novel collaborative multi-specialty service to assess and treat all patients with symptomatic metastatic bone disease in Oxford, England. However, there are not equivalent services in other parts of Great Britain. Material and Methods: Using established networks in the Palliative Care training programme within England, Scotland and Wales a questionnaire was disseminated to clinicians working in pain and symptom management to ask them to share their local and regional networks for management of symptomatic metastatic bone disease. This questionnaire was disseminated via email with a first and subsequent reminders, and was hosted online using Microsoft Forms software. The questionnaire asked respondents to describe their links with key relevant specialist colleagues including spinal surgery, orthopaedic surgery, neurosurgery, interventional pain and anaesthetics, clinical oncology and psychological medicine. The questionnaire also looked to establish whether these relationships were formalised in meetings or referral pathways or informal. Results: The results highlight the highly variable nature of such complex networks for management of symptomatic metastatic bone disease around Great Britain with some areas having highly developed and well integrated collaborative fora, and others having limited or no access to key specialities. Over 90% of respondents had a formal or informal relationship with Interventional pain services in contrast to 62% having no relationship at all with a Neurosurgical service. A recuring theme across all relationships with specialist services was an absence of clear inclusion and exclusion criteria for referrals or established referral pathway. Equally, it was evident that even within the same region providers had significant differences in the services they had relationships with. Conclusion: These results suggest that management of metastatic bone disease is likely to be highly variable around England, Scotland and Wales due to networks of differing maturity, formality and integration. Exemplar services can serve to encourage development of such more enhance offers more broadly.

## P1071: The role of the expert patient tutor in teaching medical students about metastatic bone disease

Jennifer Paskins

Introduction: Medical school education is largely provided by a mixture of academics and healthcare professionals offering a wealth of knowledge and experience but a fixed perspective on the healthcare environment. ## P1072: Spinal surgery in multiple myeloma patients: results of 10 years of experience at a tertiary center

Ricardo Navarro-Navarro

, Tito Fernadez-Varela

, Brian Melchiorsen

, Arturo Montesdeoca-Ara

, Alba López-Martel

3

1

Hospital Universitario de Gran Canaria Dr Negrin, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain

3
Fundación Canaria Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria de Canarias, Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain
Introduction and Objectives: Multiple myeloma (MM) is the most common primary spinal malignancy, causing lytic lesions that may result in severe pain, pathological fractures, neurological deficits, or secondary spinal deformity. We present our 10-year experience in surgical management of MM-related vertebral fractures. Materials and Methods: We performed a retrospective analysis of all surgical procedures for MM-related pathological fractures at our unit between January 2015 and September 2024. A total of 127 interventions were included: 12 vertebral biopsies (9%), 17 open arthrodesis ± decompression procedures (13%), and 98 percutaneous vertebral cement augmentation (VCA) procedures: 89 vertebroplasties and 9 kyphoplasties. RVC procedures provided pain relief in 98% of cases, and arthrodesis in 88%. Postoperative complications occurred in 17% of arthrodesis patients and 12% of VCA patients. Complications are summarized in . Conclusions: Management of MM-related vertebral fractures requires a multidisciplinary approach involving hematology and radiation oncology. Percutaneous vertebral cement augmentation is the most frequently performed surgical technique and is highly effective for pain control. In cases of mechanical instability or neurological deficit, open decompression and arthrodesis may be required, with higher complication rates than in non-MM patients, but with significant neurological and quality-of-life benefits.
 

## ID: AGS26/614

## P1073: Posterior-only vs. combined anterior-posterior reconstruction for thoracolumbar spinal metastasis: a propensity-matched comparative cohort analysis

Hanna von Riegen

1

, Naveen Arunachalam Sakthiyendran

2

, Erica Greiner

1

, Peter Ahorukomeye

3

, Ara Nazarian

1

, Jason Pittman

1

, Sapan Gandhi

1

1

Department of Orthopedics, Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Harvard Medical School in Boston, MA, Boston, United States

2

Boston University School of Medicine, Boston, United States

3

Harvard Combined Orthopedic Residency Program, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, USA, Boston, United States

Introduction: As the life expectancy of cancer patients continues to rise, the need for durable spinal reconstruction in metastatic disease is increasing. In particular, reliable anterior column support following posterior corpectomy is gaining relevance. However, it remains unclear whether combining anterior and posterior instrumentation improves short-term outcomes compared to a posterior-only approach. This study compared one-year outcomes of posterior-only versus combined anterior-posterior spinal stabilization in patients undergoing surgery for spinal metastases. Material and Methods: We conducted a retrospective, comparative cohort study using the TriNetX US Collaborative Network. Adult patients undergoing spinal stabilization for thoracolumbar metastases were identified. Cervical procedures were excluded. Cohort 1 included patients who underwent posterior-only stabilization after exclusion of all anterior procedure codes and cage implantations. Cohort 2 included patients who received combined anterior-posterior instrumentation. 1:1 propensity score matching was performed for age, sex, race, body mass index (BMI), and smoking status (ICD-10: Z87.891). Primary outcomes were one-year mortality, revision surgery, and hospital readmission. Results: A total of 4,593 patients were included in the matched analysis (posterior-only: 2,903; anterior-posterior: 1,690). Mortality did not differ significantly between groups (posterior-only: 30.1% vs. anterior-posterior: 27.9%; OR 1.11; 95% CI, 0.97-1.27; p = 0.1179). Revision surgery occurred significantly more often in the anterior-posterior group (9.8% vs. 3.9%; OR 0.371; 95% CI, 0.289-0.475; p < 0.0001). Readmission rates were also significantly higher in the anterior-posterior group (63.9% vs. 60.1%; OR 0.851; 95% CI, 0.752-0.963; p = 0.0108). Conclusion: In patients undergoing surgical treatment for spinal metastases, posterior-only stabilization was associated with lower revision and readmission rates at one year without an increase in mortality. Given the increasing survival of oncology patients and the growing demand for stable anterior column reconstruction, these findings support the consideration of less invasive posterior-only strategies in selected cases. Further studies are warranted to guide surgical decision-making in this complex population.

## ID: AGS26/643

## P1074: Biportal endoscopic resection of thoracic schwannoma: surgical technique and strategies to overcome key challenges

Tran Vu Hoang Duong

1,3

, Pham Anh Tuan

2,3

, Le Tan Bao

1,3

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Xuyen A General Hospital, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Nguyen Tri Phuong Hospital, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

3

Faculty of Medicine, University of Medicine and Pharmacy at Ho Chi Minh City, Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

Biportal endoscopic spine surgery (BESS) has been increasingly adopted for various spinal pathologies; however, its application for intradural extramedullary tumors (IDEMs) remains technically challenging. Key difficulties include maintaining optimal irrigation pressure, ensuring safe tumor dissection, achieving hemostasis, and preventing cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leakage. This study describes the surgical technique and perioperative strategies for BESS resection of thoracic IDEMs through the case of a 59-year-old female presenting with progressive lower limb weakness, gait disturbances, and sphincter dysfunction. MRI revealed a T10-T11 intradural extramedullary schwannoma causing severe spinal cord compression. The tumor was resected using the BESS technique under intraoperative neuromonitoring (IONM), with a focus on optimizing the surgical field, minimizing dural manipulation, and securing watertight closure. Total tumor resection was achieved, with an operative time of 150 minutes and an estimated blood loss of 180 mL. Postoperatively, the patient showed significant neurological improvement, including partial recovery of motor function and bladder control. MRI on postoperative day 5 confirmed complete tumor removal without spinal cord injury or CSF leakage. At the two-month follow-up, she continued to improve, demonstrating better lower limb strength and resolution of preoperative sensory disturbances. This case highlights the feasibility of BESS for thoracic IDEMs, suggesting its potential as a minimally invasive alternative in selected cases. Optimizing endoscopic visualization, refining microsurgical techniques, and developing specialized instrumentation are crucial for improving outcomes. Further studies are necessary to validate the long-term efficacy and safety of this approach.
Key words: biportal endoscopic spine surgery, intradural extramedullary tumors, schwannoma, surgical technique.

## ID: AGS26/645

## P1075: Analysis of different molecular subtypes of female breast cancer with spine metastasis

Tzu Hua Chen

1

, Fon-Yih Tsuang

1

1

National Taiwan University Hospital, Taipei, Taiwan

Introduction: As for prognosis predictive markers for breast cancer, different molecular subtypes might indicate different survival outcomes. However, in most circumstances in Taiwan, there was no different treatment strategies according to the different molecular subtypes of female breast cancer with spine metastases. In our study, we aim to analyze the outcomes of different molecular subtypes for breast cancer with spine metastases and to elucidate the effect of different histologic and molecular subgroups. Material and Methods: We prospectively registered patients who received surgical treatment in National Taiwan University Hospital between January 2008 and December 2024, and reviewed the pathology reports. The overall survival stratified by molecular subtypes of either primary breast cancer or metastatic spine tumor was calculated. We also studied patient demographics, synchronous tumors, brain or visceral metastasis, and treatments. The performance of established models (revised Tokuhashi score and SINS score) was tested. Results: A total of 142 patients surgically treated for breast cancer spine metastases were identified (100% female sex). 14 (10%) patients had different molecular subtypes between breast and spine specimen, and all of them had a trend of malignancy change in spine metastasis. In origin breast molecular subtype, patients with triple-negative molecular subtype had the worst outcomes (log-rank test p = 0.0004); while in spine metastasis molecular subtype, both triple-negative and HER2 enriched subtype implied a lower survival rate (log-rank test p = 0.0119). We also found out that comparing with visceral metastasis, patients with brain metastasis seemed to be related to a worse outcome. Conclusion: Spine metastases risk tools might be more predictive after accounting for molecular subtypes and brain metastasis in patients with breast cancer.

## ID: AGS26/700

## P1076: Uncommon presentation of tenosynovial giant cell tumor at the C1-C2 foramen: case report and minimally invasive resection

Joaquim Cruz Teixeira

1

, João Levy Melancia

1

, José Hipólito Reis

1

, Diogo Belo

1

1

Hospital CUF Tejo, Lisboa, Portugal

Introduction: Tenosynovial giant cell tumors (TGCT) are benign proliferative lesions of synovial tissue, most frequently located in large appendicular joints. Their occurrence in the cervical spine, particularly at the C1-C2 level, is extremely rare. Clinical and radiological overlap with more common lesions, such as schwannomas, often complicates preoperative diagnosis. Material and Methods: A 72-year-old male presented with a 6-month history of right-sided cervical pain radiating to the occipital region, following the C2 distribution. Neurological examination was otherwise unremarkable. Past history included two lumbar discectomies, with no regular medication. Cervical MRI revealed a well-circumscribed right C1-C2 foraminal lesion, both extradural and extraforaminal, measuring 15 × 27 × 16 mm, with mass effect on the foramen. Imaging features suggested a right C2 schwannoma. The patient underwent minimally invasive posterior paravertebral tubular approach. Dissection of the right C2 lamina and inferior border of the right C1 lamina allowed piecemeal removal of a fibrous tumor, closely related to the lateral dura and vertebral artery. Gross total resection was achieved. At one-month follow-up, the patient was pain-free and neurologically intact. Histopathological analysis, despite cautery artifacts, was consistent with tenosynovial giant cell tumor, with no signs of malignancy. Results: TGCT in the cervical spine is exceptionally rare and can mimic more common tumors on imaging. In this case, the lesion was initially suspected to be a schwannoma. Minimally invasive tubular access allowed safe and complete resection, despite proximity to the vertebral artery. Pathology confirmed TGCT, an entity only rarely described in the skull base and cervical spine. Awareness of this rare diagnosis is important, as complete resection is associated with excellent outcomes, while incomplete excision may predispose to recurrence. Conclusion: Tenosynovial giant cell tumors are rare in the cervical spine and may mimic nerve sheath tumors radiologically. Minimally invasive tubular approaches can achieve safe and complete resection with favorable clinical outcomes. TGCT should be considered in the differential diagnosis of cervical foraminal lesions.

## ID: AGS26/716

## P1077: Human versus machine in survival prediction for metastatic spinal cord compression: a retrospective cohort study

Elie Najjar

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Background: Accurate survival prediction in metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC) is critical for guiding treatment decisions, yet remains challenging, particularly for intermediate survival durations. We compared the accuracy of oncologist judgment, surgeon-calculated Tokuhashi scores, and ChatGPT-assisted predictions in estimating survival outcomes in MSCC patients. Methods: This retrospective study included 99 consecutive patients referred to a tertiary spinal oncology center with radiologically confirmed MSCC. Anonymized clinical data were used to calculate surgeon Tokuhashi scores, document oncologist-estimated life expectancy, and generate ChatGPT-assisted survival predictions based on both literature review and Tokuhashi calculation. Predictions were compared against actual survival outcomes (< 6 months, 6-12 months, > 12 months). Machine learning analyses identified key predictors of survival. Results: Overall prediction accuracies were 54% for ChatGPT Tokuhashi-based predictions, 50% for surgeon Tokuhashi scores, 48% for oncologist judgment, and 37% for ChatGPT literature-based estimates. Recall for short survival (< 6 months) was highest with surgeon (71%) and ChatGPT Tokuhashi (69%) methods, whereas intermediate survival (6-12 months) remained difficult to predict across all modalities. Functional status (Karnofsky score) and patient age emerged as the strongest survival predictors across logistic regression, random forest, decision tree, and XGBoost models, surpassing primary tumor type and metastasis burden. Conclusions: Structured prognostic tools and AI-assisted scoring can complement clinical judgment in predicting short-term survival in MSCC. However, intermediate-term survival prediction remains a critical unmet need. Future prognostic strategies should prioritize dynamic functional metrics over static tumor classifications to improve personalized decision-making.

## ID: AGS26/720

## P1078: Development and validation of the NOVA Score for surgical triage in metastatic spinal cord compression

Elie Najjar

1

, Nasir Quraishi

1

1

Queen's Medical Centre, Nottingham University Hospitals, Nottingham, United Kingdom

Study Design: Retrospective diagnostic model development and validation study. Objectives: To develop and validate the NOVA Score, a pragmatic, surgeon-oriented tool for predicting 12-month survival in patients with metastatic spinal cord compression (MSCC), and to compare its performance to established prognostic scores and oncologist estimates. Methods: Two independent cohorts of patients with MSCC referred for surgical consideration were included from a tertiary spine center: a derivation cohort (n = 184) and a validation cohort (n = 100). Feature selection was performed using random forest analysis, multivariable logistic regression, and permutation-based variable importance. The final NOVA Score incorporated three clinical parameters: Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS), primary tumor category, and presence of extraspinal metastases. The score ranges from 0-10, with a threshold of ≥ 7 used to predict > 12-month survival. Predictive performance was assessed using accuracy, F1-score, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC), Cohen’s kappa, and calibration plots. Results: In the validation cohort, the NOVA Score achieved an accuracy of 71.0%, AUC of 0.693, F1-score of 47.3% for > 12-month survival, and Cohen’s kappa of 0.28. Calibration plots showed good agreement in the mid-to-high probability range. Compared to the Revised Tokuhashi Score, OSRI, Modified Bauer Score, and oncologist estimates, NOVA demonstrated superior sensitivity for identifying long-term survivors using only three readily available variables. Conclusions: The NOVA Score is a simple, reproducible tool for early prognostication in MSCC. Its minimal input requirements and balanced performance support its utility for surgical triage in multidisciplinary settings. Further external validation is warranted.

## P1079: The use of a lyoplant spacer to maintain a safe zone in separation surgery for spinal metastases: case series and technical note

Nicolien Kasperts

1

, Netanja Harlianto

1

, Joanne van der Velden

1

, Jasper van Tiel

1

, Jorrit-Jan Verlaan

1

1

UMC Utrecht, Utrecht, The Netherlands

Introduction: Separation surgery is a neo-adjuvant surgical treatment to facilitate safe delivery of ablative radiotherapy dose especially for radioresistant spinal metastases. Effective stereotactic radiation therapy (SBRT) requires a safe zone of 2-3 mm to achieve sufficient coverage of the tumor without increasing the risk of radiation-induced spinal cord toxicity. This safe zone can be maintained by placing a lyoplant as a spacer to separate the tumor area and dura after closure, a technique performed at our institution since 2018. We describe a case series and technical note of our institutional experience with a lyoplant spacer for spinal metastases separation surgery and report (preliminary) clinical outcomes. Material and Methods: Consecutive spinal metastases patients that underwent separation surgery with (off-label use of) a lyoplant spacer between June 2018 and March 2024 were included. Patients underwent SBRT after separation surgery. After separation of tumor from the dura was completed, a lyoplant patch was cut to twice the size of the defect and folded and placed on the dura. The wound was closed normally in layers. Complication rates, lyoplant positioning on post-operative MRI imaging, and local recurrence rates were evaluated. Results: Fifteen patients treated with separation surgery placing a lyoplant were included (median age 66 years, 74% male). At baseline, the majority of patients had no (n = 13) or minor (n = 2) neurological deficits, which resolved for all patients following surgery. No perioperative or postoperative complications were reported related to lyoplant. Postoperative MRI imaging confirmed good positioning of lyoplant, and adequate space between tumor site and spinal cord was maintained for all patients. In the postoperative period, one patient experienced screw loosening, which required a reoperation. SBRT was given to fourteen patients, for which one complication of local edema was reported. Fourteen patients reported no local recurrence during the follow-up period (median 18 months, range: 3-66 months). Local recurrence was observed for one patient after five years. Conclusion: Treatment with lyoplant spacer for separation surgery is suggested to be safe and feasible and has potential benefits that allows for high dosage delivery of postoperative radiation therapy by functioning as a protective barrier between the excised tumor area and the spinal cord. Further evaluation with a larger patient population is required to validate its long-

## P1080: Analysis of the results of surgical treatment of patients with intradural extramedullary tumors using minimally invasive and open methods of treatment

Evgeny Brinyuk

1

, N.A. Konovalov

1

, S.V. Kaprovoy

1

, R.A. Onoprienko

1

, B.A. Zakirov

1

, Yu.M. Poluektov

1

, N.D. Shmelev

1

, S.V. Ivanov

1

, L.R. Gabrielyan

1

, M.O. Kudymets

1

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation, Moscow, Russian Federation

Introduction: Traditionally, the main approach to such tumors is laminectomy. In the surgical treatment of extramedullary neoplasms, minimally invasive approaches have recently begun to be actively used. These techniques have emerged as alternative surgical interventions to reduce complications. A minimally invasive approach for removing a spinal cord tumor means the use of retractors, which can reduce the length of the skin incision and reduce muscle trauma, and hemilaminectomy (which avoids complete resection of the vertebral arches and parts of the facet joints on both sides). Material and Methods: We studied 196 patients who were treated at the Federal State Institution National Medical Research Center for Neurosurgery with intradural extramedullary tumors. Results: Improving the outcomes of surgical treatment of patients with intradural extramedullary tumors of the spinal cord. Conclusion: Minimally invasive approaches are performed using small skin incisions (3-5 cm), using tube retractors, minimal damage to soft tissues, minimal precision bone resection, wide visualization of the subdural space due to lateral durotomy and an oblique angle of attack, the ability to visualize the ventral surface of the spinal canal without traction of the spinal cord and an overall small volume of surgical intervention, which is associated with minimizing early postoperative complications and faster recovery of patients.

## ID: AGS26/902

## P1081: Utility of the lateral base dural tacking method in cord tumor surgery performed using unilateral hemilaminectomy: a comparison of dural window widths

Soobin Im

1

, Je Hoon Jeong

1

, Jeng Hoon Kang

1

1

Soonchuhyang University Bucheon Hospital, Bucheon, South Korea

Objective: Unilateral hemilaminectomy, which is used to remove spinal cord tumors, is simpler than laminoplastic laminotomy and affords certain biomechanical advantages. However, both incomplete tumor removal and inadvertent infliction of spinal cord damage attributable to the narrow surgical corridor remain of concern. When a spinal cord tumor is to be removed, it is important to ensure that the dural window along the surgical corridor is of adequate width. This study aimed to determine that the utility of lateral base dural tacking (LBT) method when cord tumor surgery is performed using a unilateral hemilaminectomy-a comparison of dural window widths with a traditional dural tack-up and a suspending-out (DSO) method with the aid of digital image-analysis software. Methods: Twenty-one consecutive patients who had intradural-extramedullary spinal cord tumors removed using a unilateral hemilaminectomy were included in the study and analyzed retrospectively. We acquired DSO and LBT dural window images using surgical microscopes under identical conditions in consecutive order and then removed the tumors using the LBT method. We used digital image-analysis software to analyze the images quantitatively. The pixel numbers of LBT and DSO window were compared using a paired t test. Results: Twenty-one tumorous lesions were successfully removed without any major problems using a unilateral hemilaminectomy through LBT windows. The mean pixel numbers of the LBT and DSO windows were 126,787 ± 41,938 and 85,940 ± 21,638. The LBT windows were 46% larger than the DSO windows (p < 0.001). Conclusions: We objectively proved that the utility of the LBT method for widening the surgical corridor created during hemilaminectomy.

## ID: AGS26/905

## P1082: Cauda equina neuroendocrine tumour complicated by intra-tumoural hemorrhage in a young male: a case report and literature review

Mark Hao Xuan Yeo

1

, Wen Qiang Lee

1

, Krishan Kumar Sharma

2

, Sathiyamoorthy Selvarajan

3

, Lei Jiang

1

1

Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Singapore General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

2

Department of Neurosurgery, National Neuroscience Institute, Singapore General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

3

Department of Anatomical Pathology, Singapore General Hospital, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Cauda equina neuroendocrine tumors (CE-NET) are a rare group of intradural extramedullary (IDEM) tumours and are hard to differentiate from other forms of IDEM tumors such as schwanommas and ependymomas on imaging. While literature surrounding CE-NET and others IDEM tumors continues to grow, evidence on complications such as intra-tumoral hemorrhage remains rare, with few cases reported in the literature. We present a rare care of intra-tumoral hemorrhage in a CE-NET causing impending conus medullaris syndrome. Material and Methods: The patient, a 35-year-old Caucasian male with no significant past medical history presented with a 1-day duration of atraumatic severe lower back pain with radicular symptoms in the L1 dermatome, precipitated by weightlifting. This was complicated by acute retention of urine, although digital rectal exam revealed an intact anal tone and neurological exam was unremarkable. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) detected an intradural extramedullary mass lesion adjacent to the conus medullaris at the T12-L1 level with heterogenous enhancement, suggestive of intra-tumoral hemorrhage. The exact tumor type was unknown. Consequently, the patient underwent T12/L1 laminectomy and excision of lesion. A literature review was conducted for similar cases of intra-tumoral hemorrhage in IDEM tumors. Results: Histopathological examination of the tumour showed sheets of round, mononuclear cells in vascularized stroma with well-differentiated features. Keratin positivity and non-specific staining supported the diagnosis of neuroendocrine tumour (NET) over paraganglioma. Post-operatively, neuromonitoring demonstrated significantly improved somato-sensory evoked potential (SSEP) from L3-S1, which was found to be diminished pre-operatively. The patient’s lower back pain demonstrated considerable improvement and the patient’s pain was well-controlled with oral analgesia by post-operative day 4. Neurological exam was unremarkable, although patient demonstrated mild left scrotal neuropathic pain, possibly secondary to dissection of the nerve root. The patient was discharged by post-operative day 5. Our literature review found that schwanomma remains the most common tumour histology presenting with intra-tumoural hemorrhage (63%), followed by PGLs (16%) and ependymomas (11%). Clinically, majority of cases present with bilateral lower limb weakness and some cases may present with delayed onset of severe pain due to the indolent nature of the tumour. In terms of management, majority of studies in the literature have demonstrated good outcomes including short length of hospital stay and complete resolution of symptoms. The recurrence rate after gross total resection of CE-NETs remains low even with aggressive tumour characteristics at the start, ranging from 0-7%. Conclusion: Intra-tumoural hemorrhage in a CE-NET tumour is rare, yet can cause debilitating symptoms. Hence, it should be strongly considered in a patient suspected to have IDEM and presenting with acute severe lower back pain. Radiographic evaluation is insufficient to identify the exact tumour type for IDEM tumours, although some features of intra-tumoural hemorrhage can be identified. Histology is often required for the definitive diagnosis of CE-NET. Surgical resection remains the gold standard management for IDEM tumours with quick resolution of symptoms and low recurrence rate.

## ID: AGS26/910

## P1083: Clinical presentation, diagnostic challenges, and surgical outcomes of spinal paragangliomas: a 15-year institutional experience

Halit Alioglu

1

, Mert Yavuz

1

, Omar Alomari

2

, Berkay Bozkurt

1

, Baris Ozoner

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Dr. Lutfi Kirdar Kartal Training and Research Hospital, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

2

Hamidiye International School of Medicine, University of Health Sciences, Istanbul, Turkey, Istanbul, Turkey

Introduction: Spinal paragangliomas are rare neuroendocrine tumors, most commonly arising in the cauda equina and filum terminale region, representing 3-4% of spinal lesions. Despite their benign histology, they present with nonspecific symptoms and radiological overlap with other spinal tumors, making preoperative diagnosis challenging. This study aimed to present a consecutive series of patients with surgically treated spinal paragangliomas, describing their clinical presentation, diagnostic features, treatment strategies, and outcomes. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed all patients who underwent surgery for spinal paragangliomas at our institution between January 2010 and July 2025. Demographic, clinical, radiological, histopathological, and surgical data were extracted from medical records. Outcomes were assessed using recurrence rates and McCormick grades. Gross total resection (GTR) was attempted in all cases. Statistical analysis was descriptive and performed using R. Results: Nine patients (mean age 52.3, 55.6% < 6 months symptom duration) were included. The most common symptom was low back pain (77.8%), followed by leg pain/numbness (22.2%) and gait difficulty (11.1%). Neurological signs were absent in 77.8% of patients, while motor deficits occurred in 22.2%. MRI revealed lumbar involvement in 77.8%, sacral in 11.1%, and cervical/thoracic in 11.1%. Most tumors were intradural extramedullary (88.9%) and ≤ 20 mm in size (55.6%). Histopathology confirmed paraganglioma with universal chromogranin positivity, synaptophysin positivity in 88.9%, S100 positivity in 88.9%, and Ki-67 ≤ 8% in 88.9%. Surgical approaches included hemilaminectomy (44.4%), total laminectomy (44.4%), and hemilaminectomy for cervical/thoracic lesions (11.1%). GTR was achieved in all cases without perioperative complications, and nerve root/filum preservation was universal. Time from diagnosis to surgery was ≤ 1 month in 55.6%. Hospital stay was 3-4 days in 77.8% of patients. Radiologically, all tumors were T1 isointense, with T2 hyperintensity in 77.8%. Contrast enhancement was observed in all, with cystic components in 33.3% and hemorrhagic features in 11.1%. Over a median follow-up ranging from ≤ 6 months to > 5 years, no recurrences were observed. Functionally, 88.9% achieved McCormick grade I, while one patient remained grade V due to severe deficits. Conclusion: Spinal paragangliomas, although rare, should be considered in the differential diagnosis of intradural extramedullary spinal tumors, particularly in the lumbar and sacral regions. MRI features are suggestive but not definitive, necessitating histopathological confirmation. Surgical management with GTR is safe, feasible, and provides excellent outcomes with no recurrences observed during follow-up. Early recognition and complete excision are key to optimizing long-term prognosis.

## P1084: Surgical treatment results of chordoma (3-23 yrs follow up)

Ufuk Aydinli

, Murat Aksakal

2

Acibadem Hospital Bursa, Bursa, Turkey, 
2
Medicabil Hospital, Bursa, Turkey
Introduction: Chordoma is a rare type of cancer that is seen in the skull base and spine. It is diagnosed 1 in million people every year. It is a slow-growing but locally aggressive tumor. Even after surgery recurrence rates are high (40-60%). Because of Complicated location of the tumor, needs extensive experience. Material and Methods: Between 2000- 2023 we operated 21 chordoma case. 4 were female and 17 were male. Age distribution were 10-72 yrs. 2 were at the C1-2 location whereas 3 were at the mobil spine. The rest were at the sacrum. Longest follow up was 20 yrs. Results: Within 21 patients 2 patient died early postoperative period while 6 were died 3 yrs postop-8 yrs postop. All patients had local recurrences 2yrs to 5yrs. time. Most common complication infection and management of surgical wound and instrument failure. Conclusion: Since chordoma is resistant to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, the main treatment is surgical removal of the tumor within the safe zone. Local recurrence is 100% within 10 yrs postoperatively. Then years overall survival is around 40-50%.

## ID: AGS26/954

## P1085: Utility of carbon fiber-reinforced polyetheretherketone implants in spinal oncology: a single-center experience on safety, clinical feasibility, and postoperative care

Ataollah Shahbandi

1

, Abdul Mounnem Yassin Kassab

1

, Kevin Wojcik

1

, Peter Palmer

1

, Pegah Ghamasaee

1

, Saman Shabani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Medical College of Wisconsin, Milwaukee, United States

Introduction: Carbon fiber-reinforced polyetheretherketone (CFRP) implants are gaining attention in spinal oncology due to their radiolucency, which enhances postoperative imaging and facilitates precise radiotherapy planning. Despite these theoretical benefits, real-world data on their clinical integration and impact on postoperative management remain limited. This observational study aimed to evaluate the practical use of CFRP spinal implants in patients with primary and metastatic spinal tumors. The goal was to assess their clinical feasibility, safety, and contribution to imaging-based follow-up and adjuvant therapy planning. Material and Methods: A retrospective chart review was conducted on patients treated with CFRP instrumentation for spinal tumors at a tertiary care center between 2022 and 2025. Collected data included patient demographics, surgical details, postoperative outcomes, and use of adjuvant therapies. Results: Forty-five patients were included, with 19 diagnosed with primary spinal tumors and the remainder with metastatic tumors. The patient cohort included 21 males (46.7%) and 24 females (53.3%), with a mean age of 58.9 ± 15.4 years. The median Spine Instability Neoplastic Score at the spinal index level was 9 (interquartile range [IQR]: 4.5-12). All patients underwent decompression with instrumentation, and cage placement was performed in 17 patients (37.8%). The median span of instrumentation covered five levels (IQR: 3-7). Fully CFRP constructs were used in 17 patients (37.8%), while hybrid constructs were implemented in the remaining 28 patients (62.2%). The mean clinical follow-up was 14.6 ± 13.9 months. At final follow-up, neurological function improved or remained stable in 42 patients (93.3%) compared to their preoperative baseline. The most frequently observed postoperative complications included cerebrospinal fluid leakage and deep vein thrombosis, each occurring in 4 patients (8.9%). Three patients received revision surgeries because of complications, including distal junctional failure, proximal junctional failure, and tumor recurrence, all diagnosed and planned based on MRI findings. Regarding CFRP instrument-related complications, one patient (2.2%) showed screw haloing. No complications involving CFRP rods, plates, or cages were observed. No intraoperative hardware failures or delayed implant-related complications necessitating revision occurred. Postoperative MRI was obtained in 43 patients (95.6%). In 19 cases (44.2%), imaging was triggered by the onset or worsening of symptoms. MRI was used for immediate postoperative assessment in 37 patients (86%), such as evaluating decompression adequacy. Long-term follow-up MRI was conducted in 15 patients (34.9%), while only six patients (13.9%) had MRI specifically for planning and evaluating adjuvant therapy effects. Fifteen patients (33.3%) received adjuvant radiotherapy. MRI was used for treatment planning in 6 cases (40%), while 9 (60%) relied solely on CT. Two patients (4.4%) experienced local recurrence, both of which were detected on MRI, with a median time to recurrence of 9.5 months. Conclusion: CFRP spinal implants provide a safe and efficacious alternative to traditional titanium instruments, offering additional advantages in imaging and radiotherapy planning. Nonetheless, inconsistent adjuvant care practices limit the full realization of these benefits. Standardized protocols and studies involving larger cohorts with extended follow-up are needed to validate their long-term value.

Introduction: Metastatic spinal cord compression is a serious sequela of malignancy that can lead to neurological deficits and significant impairment in quality of life, with surgery being an integral part of its treatment. Objective: Review the survivorship outcomes of patients undergoing surgery for metastatic spinal disease including cord compression. ## ID: AGS26/984

## P1087: Non-syndromic osteochondroma of lumbar spine: a systematic review of management and outcomes

Mohsen Rostami

1

, Dana Saleh

1

, Diego Soto Rubio

1

, Srujan Kopparapu

1

, Anjali Pradhan

1

, Gersham Rainone

1

, Mohamed Emara

1

, Adrian Urdaneta

1

, Vito Evola BS

2

, Patrick Kim

1

, Puya Alikhani

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Brain and Spine, University of South Florida, Morsani College of Medicine, Tampa, United States

2

Dr. Kiran C. Patel College of Osteopathic Medicine, Fort Lauderdale, United States

Introduction: Osteochondromas are the most common benign bone tumors, but rarely arise in the lumbar spine. Due to their infrequency, understanding of their clinical presentation, management, and outcomes is limited, with most available data derived from case reports and small series. This systematic review aims to synthesize the literature on non-syndromic lumbar spine osteochondromas and to present an illustrative institutional case. Material and Methods: A systematic search was conducted in PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Scopus from database inception to April 2024, following PRISMA 2020 guidelines. Studies reporting clinical cases or series of non-syndromic lumbar spine osteochondromas with sufficient details on presentation, tumor characteristics, management, and outcomes were included. The risk of bias was evaluated using the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) tools for case reports and case series. Data extraction encompassed demographics, clinical characteristics, tumor features, operative management, and postoperative outcomes. Results: A total of 39 studies encompassing 56 patients were included. The mean age was 42 years; 59% were male. Most lesions originated from the inferior articular process (43%) and commonly affected L4 and L5. The mean lesion size was 29.7 ± 22.2 mm, with significantly smaller lesions in patients with radiculopathy than those with low back pain or palpable mass (p = 0.00034). Radiculopathy (50%) and low back pain (25%) were the most frequent presentations (p < 0.001). The majority (80%) underwent posterior surgical excision without instrumentation, with en-bloc resection performed in 78.5%. Complete symptomatic improvement was observed in 94% of patients, and recurrence was rare. Conclusion: Lumbar spine osteochondromas most frequently arise from the inferior articular process and often produce radiculopathy due to intracanalicular growth. Surgical excision-especially en-bloc resection-yields excellent outcomes and a low recurrence rate. Conservative treatment may be considered in selected asymptomatic patients. Early recognition and individualized management are essential for optimal outcomes.

## P1088: Patient outcomes and survivorship after surgical treatment of metastatic spinal disease including cord compression presenting with instability or neurological deficit at regional centre

Muhammad Haruna

1

, Archisa Marya

2

, Surya Gandham

2

, Marcus De Matas

2

1

Mersey and west Lancashire Teaching Hospitals, Liverpool, United Kingdom

2

The Walton Centre NHS Trust, Liverpool, United Kingdom

Introduction: Metastatic spinal compression is a significant disease process that can be life threatening. While survival after surgery is dependent on type of cancer and extent of metastates. There is no consensus on life expectancy after surgery. Surgery remains crucial for symptom control and improved quality of life. Review patient reported outcome measures (EQ5D/VAS) and survivorship after surgery for metastatic spinal disease including cord compression. Material and Methods: Retrospective case analysis of prospective data collected for 49 patients treated surgically for metastatic spinal disease including cord compression at a UK regional centre by a single surgeon between June 2018-June 2024. Analysis of online clinical case notes, PACS imaging, and the British Spine Registry. All patients underwent surgery. Primary outcome: survival duration post-surgery; secondary outcome: quality of life (EQ5D/VAS). Data on surgical type, cancer demographics, and complications were recorded.
