## ID: AGS26/1067

## P1089: What is the incidence of symptomatic thromboembolism post-surgical treatment of spinal metastases in an Asian cohort of patients

Adam Cheng

1

, Cayden Leyu

1

, Andre Loh

1

, Nishant Barua

2

, Raymen Chandrasagran

3

, Qian Hui Rachel Lim

3

, Jun Hao Tan

3

, James Hallinan

4

, Naresh Kumar

3

, Jiong Hao Tan

3

1

National University of Singapore, Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, Singapore, Singapore

2

Trinity College Dublin, University of Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

3

National University Hospital, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Singapore, Singapore

4

National University Hospital, Department of Diagnostic Imaging, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: It is estimated that over 10% of cancer patients develop symptomatic spinal metastasis, and with advances in oncological and surgical treatment, more patients are being treated surgically for spinal metastases. The presence of cancer, spinal surgery and loss of mobility are all considered to be risk factors for thromboembolism. The risk of symptomatic thromboembolism (deep venous thrombosis/pulmonary embolism) has not been defined and hence we aim to identify the percentage of patients who develop symptomatic thromboembolism as well as the risk factors for this. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective cohort study of patients treated between 2005 to 2024 at the National University Spine Institute Singapore. Inclusion criteria included patients surgically treated for symptomatic spinal metastases, excluding patients treated with only kyphoplasty/vertebroplasty. Patient clinical, demographic, neurological and oncological data were collected. The primary outcome measured was symptomatic thromboembolism, defined as symptomatic pulmonary embolism (PE) or symptomatic deep venous thromboembolism (DVT) diagnosed within 90 days of surgical treatment. This was diagnosed on CT- pulmonary angiogram and duplex ultrasound. No pre- or post-operative chemoprophylaxis was prescribed. Univariate and Multivariate analysis was performed. Results: In total, 503 patients were included in this study of whom 20 (4%) were diagnosed with symptomatic thromboembolism. Of these patients 16 (3.2%) were female and 4 (0.8%) were male. On univariate analysis, days of SHD/ICU stay, length of stay, female sex, presence of medical complication and higher Bilsky Grade (1C to 3) was associated with significantly higher rates of symptomatic thromboembolism. On multivariate analysis female sex (p = 0.013) and higher Bilsky Grade (1C to 3) (p = 0.003) was significantly associated with symptomatic thromboembolism. The incidence of post-operative hematoma requiring surgical treatment was 9 (1.8%) of patients. There were no cases of mortality secondary to thromboembolism. Conclusion: The incidence of symptomatic thromboembolism in patients post spinal tumor surgery is reported to be between 11-20%, however in our cohort the incidence was 4%. This was despite the absence of a consistent chemoprophylaxis protocol in our Centre. This might be due to the perceived lower incidence of symptomatic thromboembolism in Asian patients when compared to western populations. However, this paper highlights the need to identify patients at increased risk and provide appropriate chemoprophylaxis. These include patients of female sex and with higher grade cord compression Bilsky 1C to 3.

## P1090: Pre-operative angioembolisation is not associated with reduced intra-operative blood loss in surgically treated metastatic epidural spinal cord compression (MESCC)

Cayden Leyu

1

, Adam Cheng

1

, Andre Loh

1

, Nishant Barua

2

, Qian Hui Rachel Lim

3

, Raymen Chandrasagran

3

, James Hallinan

3

, Jun Hao Tan

3

, Naresh Kumar

3

, Jiong Hao Tan

3

1

National University of Singapore, Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University Hospital, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Singapore, Singapore

2

Trinity College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland

3

National University Hospital, Department of Orthopaedic Surgery, Singapore, Singapore

Introduction: Metastatic spine tumor surgery (MSTS) for Metastatic Epidural Spinal Cord Compression (MESCC) can be associated with significant blood loss. Increased blood loss is associated with increased morbidity and mortality. Angioembolization is increasingly employed to reduce perioperative blood loss, however its role is controversial in non-hypervascular tumors. In this study, we aim to investigate the predictive factors of increased blood loss in patients undergoing MSTS for MESCC. Material and Methods: This is a retrospective cohort study of 424 patients treated at the National University Spine Institute, Singapore, between 2005 and 2024. Demographic and oncological data were collected and operative data, including Operative Time, Surgical Approach (Posterior, Anterior, Anterior and Posterior (Combined)), Number of Levels Decompressed and Instrumented, Surgical type (Open vs Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS) vs Hybrid), and Surgical Invasiveness (Decompression vs Separation Surgery vs Corpectomy/ en-bloc excision). Tumors were further classified into Hypervascular (Renal/Thyroid/Liver/Gallbladder) and non-hypervascular tumours. Univariate and multivariate analysis was performed to identify predictive factors for increased blood loss. Results: 424 patients were identified, 208 (49.1%) were male and 216 (50.9%) were female. Mean total blood loss was 775mLs, total blood loss ranged from 250-6400 mLs, mean transfusion was 295 mLs, mean transfusion ranged from 0-3500 mLs. 49 (11.6%) patients had hypervascular tumors and 86 (20.3%) patients underwent pre-operative embolization. 309 (74.1%) underwent Open procedures, 91 (21.8%) MIS procedures and 17 (4.1%) underwent Hybrid procedures. The Posterior approach was the most common with 375 patients (89.7%), followed by Anterior approach with 12 patients (2.9%) and the Anterior and Posterior (Combined) approaches with 28 patients (6.7%). On multivariate analysis, the number of levels instrumented (p = 0.047), female sex (p = 0.021), hypervascular tumor histology (p ≤ 0.01), open surgical approach (p ≤ 0.01) and combined surgical approach (p = 0.019) was found to be associated with significantly higher intra-operative blood loss. On further subgroup analysis, preoperative angioembolization was not associated with significantly higher intra-operative blood loss. Conclusion: Preventing excessive perioperative blood loss can reduce surgical morbidity and mortality. Intra-operative measures such as Minimally Invasive Surgical approaches, avoiding excessive levels of instrumentation and Anterior and Posterior (Combined) approaches where possible are surgical measures that can prevent blood loss. Identifying patients at risk of increased blood loss and adopting a multidisciplinary approach to reducing blood loss in these patients may also play a role. The role of angioembolization is controversial in non-hypervascular tumors, while current literature suggests that it has a role in hypervascular tumours, it did not significantly reduce blood loss in our cohort.

## ID: AGS26/1106

## P1091: Psychological impact of spine sarcoma on wellbeing

Victoria Williamson

1,2

, Gerard Mawhinney

3,4

, Dana Cimatu

5

, Thomas Land

6

, Jeremy Reynolds

4

1

Department of Psychology, University of Bath, Bath, United Kingdom

2

Department of Psychology, Kings College London, London, United Kingdom

3

Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Oxford, Oxford, United Kingdom

4

Department of Spinal Surgery, Oxford University Hospitals NHS Trust, Oxford, United Kingdom

5

Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, Oxford, United Kingdom

6

Department of Neurosurgery, University Hospitals Birmingham, Birmingham, United Kingdom

Introduction: Spinal sarcomas are rare but highly aggressive tumours that often require extensive and complex treatment, including surgery, chemotherapy, and/or radiation. Despite such intensive care, recurrence is common, and treatment frequently results in long-term physical and neurological impairment. The psychological burden of spinal sarcoma is significant, yet remains under-researched, with mental health outcomes often overlooked in favour of physical ones. Preliminary data from AO Spine indicate that patients treated for spinal sarcoma report psychological wellbeing comparable to those with other life-altering illnesses like AIDS or ischemic heart disease. This study sought to address the current gap by exploring the mental health impact of spinal sarcoma and identifying support needs to improve overall patient wellbeing. Material and Methods: We conducted qualitative, semi-structured interviews with 16 patients who received diagnosis and treatment for spine sarcoma. Results: Following treatment, patients reported difficulties in accessing specialised rehabilitation and pain management. Many report that their GPs and physiotherapists lack the necessary training and sarcoma-related expertise to provide adequate support. Barriers to psychological care were frequently reported and social isolation was common. As a result, patients with spine sarcoma reported experiencing reduced quality of life, along with high levels of anxiety and depression. Their family members may also experience significant stress as they navigate the complexities of sarcoma diagnosis and treatment. The life-changing nature of diagnosis/treatment can leave spouses and family members unexpectedly taking on caregiving responsibilities, managing significant financial pressures, and struggling to talk about spine sarcoma with their children. Conclusion: These challenges highlight the need for targeted guidance for patients with spine sarcoma and their families. The findings also highlight the need for improved post-treatment care that addresses the multi-faceted impact that spine sarcoma can have on patient wellbeing. Finally, the findings illustrate the need for upskilling clinical care teams who provide support to patients to reduce barriers to diagnosis, increase knowledge around sarcoma and improve access to holistic care.

## ID: AGS26/1124

## P1092: Spino-pelvic reconstruction combined with vascularised fibular graft in the treatment of Ewing sarcoma involving the iliac wing

Bedirhan Albayrak

1

, Şafak Aydın Şimşek

1

, Tolgahan Cengiz

2

, İsmail Büyükceran

3

, Hüseyin Sina Coşkun

3

, Nevzat Dabak

3

1

Samsun Training and Research Hospital, Samsun, Turkey

2

Kastamonu İnebolu State Hospital, Kastamonu, Turkey

3

Ondokuz Mayıs University, Samsun, Turkey

Introduction: In malignant pelvic tumors, a limb-sparing procedure is a more favorable treatment for the patient than hemipelvectomy, but it challenges the surgeon. These cases present two patients with spinopelvic fixation after tumor resection and reconstruction with vascularised fibular graft (VFG). Patients and Methods: Case-1: A 15-year-old male patient applied to the orthopedics outpatient clinic for about two months with low back pain on the left side. He had muscle weakness in the lower extremities, more prominently on the left. A massive mass in the iliac wing was detected and the biopsy revealed the diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma. Case-2: A 16-year-old female patient with pain in the right pelvic region has been admitted to the outpatient clinic. Imaging studies have revealed lytic lesions in the right iliac wing. The biopsy has been performed and the diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma has been made. Following the neo-adjuvant chemotherapy, iliac wing resection and sacropelvic fixation were planned for both patients. Results: After total resection of the iliac wing, the sacrum and the acetabulum were fixed with two spinal pedicle screws. The reduction was achieved with 2 rods, and bone-to-bone (sacrum-acetabulum) contact was established with a VFG. The patients did not develop any complications in the clinical follow-ups, mobilized with partial weight in the 1st month and full weight in the 3rd month. Conclusion: In pediatric patients, biological reconstruction such as spinopelvic fixations combined with VFG should be considered for limb-salvage and may prevent secondary deformities after resection of the massive bone.

## ID: AGS26/1131

## P1093: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors: the do's and the don'ts

Nikolay Konovalov

1

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation, Moscow, Russian Federation

Objectives: Describe the current data and our experience of surgical treatment of IMSCT's. Background: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors (IMSCTs) are rare, predominantly benign tumors accounting for 20-30% of all spinal cord tumors. Intramedullary tumors can lead to severe neurological deficit in the form of motor and sensory disorders, decrease and loss of conductive function, which leads to a significant decrease in quality of life or even death. Although these tumors are quite thoroughly studied, surgical treatment of these patients remains a difficult, even arduous task. We describe our experience and what tips and tricks we found for ourselves after more than 1000 cases of intramedullary spinal cord tumors treated over the course of 25 years. Methods: Authors report the current recommended treatment options available from literature and their experience and know-how on surgical treatment of every tumor type based on experience of more than 1000 cases. Various uncommon tumour presentations and surgical nuances are extensively described. Results: Presentation contains our data on surgical treatment of more than 1000 cases of intramedullary spinal cord tumors, our tips and tricks for treating patients with various tumor types and a short analysis of our patients series. Conclusions: Surgery for IMSCT continues to be challenging due to the rarity of this disease, the high functional relevance of the spinal cord, and the consequent risk of inducing severe neurological deficits. Taking into account modern surgical Methods, wide knowledge and treatment experience in most cases complete or maximally safe tumor resection can be achieved. Despite advancements in neurosurgical techniques and assistive devices, IMSCT resection still carries a considerable risk for neurological deterioration, reaching as high as 90% in the immediate postoperative period. We report our tips and tricks for treating patients with various tumor types and a short analysis of our patients series.

## ID: AGS26/1132

## P1094: Cavernous malformations of the spinal cord - Surgical treatment and nuances

Nikolay Konovalov

1

1

Federal State Autonomous Institution «N. N. Burdenko National Medical Research Center of Neurosurgery» of the Ministry of Health of the Russian Federation, Moscow, Russian Federation

Objectives: To analyse our experience of surgical treatment of cavernous malformations of the spinal cord and describe the indications and techniques of surgical treatment. Background: Vascular tumors of spinal cord are a rare pathology, which is most often comprised of hemangioblastomas and cavernomas of different localization. These vascular tumors arise sporadically, however hemangioblastomas may be associated with the von Hippel-Lindau disease. The spectrum of clinical manifestations of these pathologies is quiet wide - from asymptomatic carriage to severe neurological deficit that lead to permanent disability or death of the patient. Methods: From 2002 to 2020 at Burdenko Neurosurgery Institute 70 patients with cavernous malformations at different spinal levels were operated. Preoperative assessment consisted of MRI data and thorough neurological examination. Patient status was assessed on before and after surgical treatment and analyzed. Results: Complete microsurgical removal of cavernous malformations of the spinal cord is the only treatment method that prevents recurrent hemorrhages. Indications for surgery are based on the clinical course of the disease and the patient’s condition at the time of examination. In the presence of persistent neurological symptoms, surgery was performed even if their severity was insignificant (2 or more on the McCormick scale). In case of obvious hemorrhage and international macrohematoma, surgery is performed 2-3 weeks after the hemorrhage. With complete regression of symptoms (1 on the McCormick scale), surgery was not indicated and observation is recommended. Conclusions: Surgery of vascular tumors of the spinal cord is a sophisticated and multicomponent task that requires a search for the correct approach to treat the patient, a decision made on the need for embolization of vascular tumor, as well as microsurgical treatment when needed.

## ID: AGS26/1290

## P1095: Intradural extramedullary tumors in the cervical spine: why “benign” does not always mean simple

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Cervical intradural extramedullary (IDEM) tumors, including schwannomas and meningiomas, are typically benign. However, their management is often complex due to the narrow cervical canal, proximity to critical neurovascular structures, and risk of postoperative instability. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 12 patients with cervical IDEM tumors operated over 3 years. Clinical presentation, imaging, surgical approach, complications, and outcomes were analyzed. Results: The series included 7 schwannomas and 5 meningiomas. Gross total resection was achieved in 11 patients (91.6%). Surgical challenges arose mainly from ventral/ventrolateral tumor locations (n = 4) and foraminal extension (n = 3). Intraoperative neuromonitoring helped minimize neurological morbidity. Postoperative complications included cerebrospinal fluid leakage (n = 2) and transient neurological worsening (n = 1). No mortality occurred. At a mean follow-up of 18 months, 10 patients had excellent recovery, while 2 had mild residual deficits. One patient developed cervical kyphosis requiring secondary stabilization. Conclusion: Although histologically benign, cervical IDEM tumors can present significant surgical challenges. Tumor location, dural involvement, and spinal stability must guide resection strategy. Careful preoperative planning, microsurgical technique, and neuromonitoring are essential to optimize outcomes. This study illustrates that “benign” does not always equate to “simple” in spinal oncology.

## ID: AGS26/1292

## P1096: Safety of denosumab use in giant cell tumor of the axial skeleton: a systematic review

Tekla Giliga

1

, Kristof Koch

1

, Ádám Biczó

1

, Benjamin Hajnal

1

, Áron Lazáry

1

, Dezsö Jeszenszky

1

1

National Center for Spinal Disorders, Budapest, Hungary

Introduction: Despite numerous studies evaluating the use of denosumab in the management of giant cell tumor of bone (GCT), particularly in challenging anatomical locations such as the spine and sacrum, its long-term safety profile remains inadequately defined. While denosumab has demonstrated promising efficacy in reducing tumor size and facilitating surgical resection, concerns persist regarding potential adverse effects, recurrence rates, and optimal treatment duration-especially in cases where surgical options are limited or not feasible. The aim of the study was to analyze the available literature data focusing on the reported adverse events related to the administration of denosumab for the management of GCT localized in the axial skeleton. Material and Methods: Following PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews, MEDLINE, EMBASE, Web of Science, the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews were queried for papers that reported on the efficacy and/or safety of the use of denosumab in spinal, sacral or pelvic GCT. Cohort studies and case reports were included in the study if 1) subjects had histologically confirmed GCT, 2) patient(s) got at least one dose of denosumab, 3) follow-up was longer than 12 months. Papers reporting on the safety information were ultimately analyzed, with an emphasis on clinically significant AEs that may be associated with denosumab use. Results: A total of 46 papers met the inclusion criteria (13 cohort studies and 33 case reports involving fewer than five subjects), of which 13 did not report any safety data. The final 33 papers reported data on 366 patients, with a mean age of 29.1 years and a female/male ratio of 1.8. 193 patients (52.7%) in the studied population underwent surgical therapy. Two-thirds of the patients (N = 249, 68%) had GCT in the sacro-pelvic region, while the rest had mobile spine localization of the tumor. Clinically significant denosumab related AEs were reported in 30 patients (8.2%). Most common AE was osteonecrosis of the jaw (13 pts, 3.6%). AEs related to calcium metabolism occurred in 8 cases (2.2%). 6 patients were diagnosed with the malignant transformation of the tumor (1.6%). 2 cases developed pathological fracture and one patient was diagnosed with an apical tooth granuloma. Conclusion: The available evidence indicates that clinically significant adverse events are not uncommon with denosumab treatment for axial skeleton GCT; however, the literature shows inconsistency in reporting its long-term safety profile. Malignant transformation/tumor formation rate is not to be neglected either. Based on our findings, daily Ca and Vitamin D supplementation, frequent dental check-ups and close radiological follow-up (MRI) are recommended. There is an urgent need for large, prospective cohort studies to gain a clearer understanding of the relationship between dosage, treatment duration and the safety and efficacy of denosumab in spinal GCT.

## ID: AGS26/1382

## P1098: Solitary spinal langerhans cell histiocytosis presenting with vertebra plana in a child: a case report and literature review

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Andres Rodriguez Buitrago

1

, Carlos Pacichana

1

, Daniel Zambrano Lizcano

1

, Juan Esteban Alarcón

1

, Pedro Vieira Jiménez

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare pediatric disorder characterized by the clonal proliferation of Langerhans cells, with a male predominance (2.5:1). It may present as a multisystemic disease (Letterer-Siwe), with bone and endocrine involvement (Hand-Schüller-Christian), or as isolated bone lesions (eosinophilic granuloma). Spinal involvement commonly manifests as vertebra plana, typically diagnosed through computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). Case Presentation: We describe a case of a 9-year-old male presenting with localized thoracic pain. Imaging revealed a lytic lesion affecting over 40% of the T5 vertebral body, consistent with vertebra plana. Histological confirmation of LCH was achieved via biopsy and immunohistochemistry (CD1a and CD207 positivity). Given the absence of neurological deficits or instability, the patient was managed conservatively with spinal orthosis and systemic chemotherapy (Vinblastine and Prednisone) per standard protocols. Clinical and radiological follow-up demonstrated resolution without recurrence. Conclusion: LCH should be considered in pediatric patients with lytic spinal lesions. In the absence of neurological compromise or instability, conservative management can yield excellent outcomes. Early recognition and a multidisciplinary approach are key to a favorable prognosis.

## ID: AGS26/1418

## P1099: A paradigm shift in age? Multiple myeloma with orthopedic manifestations in young patients

Fernando Alvarado Gómez

1

, Alejandra Ruiz Cuartas

1

, Valentina Serrato Cristancho

2

, Luna Lizarazo Rojas

2

, Stephania Beltran Bermudez

1

, Carlos Acosta

1

1

Fundacion Santafe de Bogota, Bogotá, Colombia

2

Universidad de Los Andes, Bogotá, Colombia

Introduction: Multiple myeloma is a hematologic malignancy characterized by the clonal proliferation of plasma cells in the bone marrow, with a median age at diagnosis of approximately 68 years. However, an increasing number of cases in younger patients has been observed, raising the need to reconsider this disease as a possible common diagnosis in this age group. Up next, we describe a series of multiple myeloma cases in young patients with spinal involvement and discuss clinical implications for orthopedic and onco-hematologic practice. Material and Methods: We present four clinical cases diagnosed at a referral center: three men and one woman, all presenting with persistent axial pain and associated red flags. The diagnostic work-up included imaging studies (PET-CT, MRI, CT), hematologic evaluation, bone marrow biopsy, and genetic profiling (FISH). Results: All four patients showed multifocal lytic lesions involving the spine, in some cases neurological symptomas were associated. Secretory variants of multiple myeloma (IgA λ, IgG κ) were confirmed, with bone marrow infiltration ranging from 60-90%. Cytogenetic profiles revealed high-risk abnormalities, including 17p deletion, both (4;14) and (14;16) translocations. Despite being outside the typical age range, all patients met SLIM-CRAB criteria for active multiple myeloma. Conclusion: This case series highlights that multiple myeloma may present with severe orthopedic manifestations in patients younger than traditionally expected. Early recognition of clinical signs with prompt diagnostic evaluation is crucial to avoid delays in treatment. Our findings support the need to broaden clinical suspicion of multiple myeloma regardless of age, as epidemiological trends suggest an increasing diagnosis among younger adults.

## P1100: When difficulty arises in diagnosis of spinal tumour (telangiectatic osteosarcoma)

Shahidul Islam

1,2

, Md. Hasan

3

, Ferdous Banu

4

, Merina Tanzil

2

, Fatima Sultana

2

, Saleh-Al-Nayem Chy.

2

, Tonima Sabikun

2

, Shukanya Samia

2

1

Ad-din Women's Medical College, Dhaka, Bangladesh

2

Barak PRP Medical Centre, Dhaka, Bangladesh

3

Holy Family Red Crescent Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

4

Dhaka Medical College Hospital, Dhaka, Bangladesh

Introduction: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma (TOS) of the spine is rare accounting only 0.086% of all primary osteosarcomas. In Bangladesh there is no case report of TOS. We found various difficulties in the way of diagnosis of spinal SOL in a 25 year old young man in Bangladesh. In the first two steps of diagnosis like FNAC and post-operative histopathology reveals ABC (Aneurysmal Bone Cyst). But from recurrent lesions, incisional biopsy reveals a case of TOS (Telangiectatic Osteosarcoma). We aim to present such a rare case like TOS and make awareness about its diagnostic difficulties. Materials and Method: It is an observational study. The study was conducted in Department of Orthopedics and Spine Surgery in Ad-din Women’s Medical College & Hospital during the period of August, 2014 to August, 2019. We did X-RAY, MRI of lumber spine & CT angiogram & biochemical tests. MRI reveals aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC). Then we operated the patient & tissue histology report was in favour of ABC. After 6 month of surgery patient came back with recurrence of symptoms. Repeated X-ray was done revealing radiological feature like sunburst appearance. Tissue biopsy was done again report reveals osteosarcoma (Telangiectatic osteosarcoma of spine). Then the patient was treated by oncologist and got the treatment like chemotherapy & radiotherapy. Patient survived for 5 years. Result: Though we passed through the roads of diagnostic difficulties, at last we reached the correct diagnosis through which patient got the available treatment. Conclusion: The dilemmas associated with the diagnosis of TOS has been emphasized in literature. In particular as ABC is almost indistinguishable from TOS on radiograph. Hence the correct diagnosis rest on a careful histological examination.

## ID: AGS26/1581

## P1101: Thoracoscope-assisted WBB type 3B total en bloc excision of posterior pancoast tumor with spine invasion

Ching Hua Huang

1

, Meng Wei Chung

1

1

China Medical University Hospital, Taichung City, Taiwan

Introduction: A Pancoast tumor is an apical lung carcinoma that invades the chest wall, affecting structures such as the brachial plexus, vertebral bodies, and sympathetic ganglia. Posteriorly located Pancoast tumors pose a significant surgical challenge due to their intimate relationship with the vertebral column, spinal cord, and critical neurovascular structures. This case report details the successful en bloc surgical management of a posterior Pancoast tumor, which involved the T2 spine, highlighting the technical considerations and favorable oncologic outcomes. Case Presentation: We present a 48-year-old male with a history of smoking, who was referred with a persistent right chest wall pain and unilateral anhidrosis. Imaging studies, including CT, MRI and PET-CT, revealed a mass at the right lung apex with invasion into the T2 vertebral bodies and the T2-T3 nerve roots. A CT-guided biopsy confirmed invasive carcinoma with focal squamous differentiation. The patient underwent neoadjuvant chemoradiation, which resulted in a partial response. Following a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the patient was deemed a candidate for curative resection. A two-stage, en bloc resection of the tumor, a portion of the right upper lung lobe, right 2nd, 3rd rib and the T2 vertebral bodies with subsequent spinal reconstruction was performed. In the first stage, thoracoscope was used for portion of the right upper lung lobe excision, esophagus dissection and feeding artery ligation. Then, the patient was put into prone position for removal of the specimen and further reconstruction. Discussion: En bloc spondylectomy for tumors involving the spine is a complex procedure associated with high morbidity. However, for a select group of patients, it offers the only chance for a curative resection with clear margins. The use of the thoracoscopy can provide good visual acuity and relative flexible angle during the operation. The successful outcome in this case underscores the importance of a comprehensive preoperative evaluation, multidisciplinary planning, and a meticulous surgical technique to achieve complete resection and minimize complications. Conclusion: En bloc spondylectomy combined with thoracoscopy can be a safe and effective treatment strategy for posterior Pancoast tumors with vertebral invasion. This approach provides excellent local control and durable survival in carefully selected patients. This case demonstrates that with appropriate surgical expertise, a curative resection is possible for these challenging tumors.

## ID: AGS26/1597

## P1102: Grisel syndrome in adult patient

Rafael Luque

1

, Alicia Collado Gosalvez

1

, Jose Luis Perez Gonzalez

1

, Eduardo Gómez

1

1

Clínico San Carlos Hospital, Madrid, Spain

Introduction: Grisel’s syndrome is an infectious process of the soft tissues that affects bony and ligamentous elements, producing atraumatic instability of C1-C2. There is very little literature on cases in adults. Material and Methods: We present a 64-year-old male patient with a personal history of dilated cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, and cervical spondyloarthropathy. He attends the spine surgery clinic due to headache and neck pain following a box falling on his head. After imaging studies, the condition is labeled as post-traumatic pain, and analgesic treatment and follow-up are prescribed. Two weeks after the trauma, he requires admission to the internal medicine department due to a 4-day history of cough with secretions he is unable to expectorate, odynophagia, and a “nasal” voice progressively worsening, with chills and fever sensation the night before admission. Cervical MRI identifies collections in the retropharyngeal space, inversion of the physiological cervical lordosis, and anterior listhesis of C4 over C5. Medical treatment with antibiotics and analgesics is indicated along with outpatient monitoring. After 6 weeks, due to clinical persistence with neurological worsening (muscle strength right upper limb 4/5, left upper limb 2/4, right lower limb 3/4, left lower limb 2/5; sensory 2/2), admission is arranged to complete the study. Repeat MRI shows increased epidural soft tissue with enhancement within the spinal canal at C1, C2, and C3 levels, poor definition of the joint spaces between lateral masses of C1 and C2, new findings compared to the previous study. Results: Given the clinical severity and imaging findings, surgical intervention is decided. Diagnosis: acute spinal cord compression at C1-C2, suspected septic arthritis; acute on chronic spinal cord compression at C4-C5 with listhesis. Via a posterior midline cervical approach (pus is drained intraoperatively from lateral C1-C2 joints and approximately at C4-C5 level). Under fluoroscopic and neurophysiological control, screws are placed in the lateral masses of C1, pars of C2, lateral masses of C3, C4, C5, C6, and pedicles of T1-all bilaterally. A small occipital plate with five screws is placed. Central decompressive laminectomy is performed from C1 to C6. Granulation-like tissue causing circumferential compression between C1 and C2 is identified and removed; samples are sent for pathology and microbiology. Bilateral rods are then shaped and fixed. The procedure is performed with neurophysiological monitoring without intraoperative complications. The patient shows good postoperative evolution. Due to isolation of MRSA in 4/4 intraoperative cultures from 11/06 and 2/2 blood cultures taken during febrile peak, treatment with daptomycin is maintained and later switched to linezolid. After 4 weeks of hospitalization, the patient is transferred to a rehabilitation center with the following exam: Neurological exam: Free of rigidity or pain in all four limbs; normal tone. Muscle strength upper limbs: Right upper limb: C5 4/5, C6 4+/5, C7 4-/5, C8 4+/5, T1 4/5; Left upper limb: C5 3+/5, C6 4/5, C7 3+/5, C8 3+/5, T1 4/5. Muscle strength lower limbs: Right lower limb: L2 4+/5, L3 4+/5, L4 4+/5, L5 4+/5, S1 5/5; Left lower limb: L2 4/5, L3 4/5, L4 5/5, L5 4+/5, S1 3/5. Light touch sensation: bilateral hypoesthesia at C6-C7-C8, bilateral hypoesthesia from T10 to T12 and in the L5 territory. Trunk control is maintained in long sitting and short standing (with supervision) without assistive devices. Transfers from chair to standing and from supine to sitting are performed autonomously or with minimal assistance from one person. The patient walks with a walker inside the room. Conclusion: Although more typical in children as a complication of an otorhinolaryngological infection presenting as torticollis, treatment is usually conservative. The most frequently found pathogen is Staphylococcus aureus. In our case, the pre-existing degenerative pathology and direct trauma masked the clinical picture and caused the appearance of a spinal cord cuff requiring decompressive surgical treatment for resolution.

## ID: AGS26/1695

## P1103: Craniovertebral intradural meningiomas: analysis of surgical results (retrospective two-center study)

Vitaly Stepanenko

1

, Vladimir Shamanin

1

, Alexander Trashin

1

, Dmitry Pechiborshch

1

, Yuri Shulev

1

, Roman Khalepa

2

, Murodzhon Kosimshoev

2

, Yuliy Kubetskiy

2

, Jamil Rzaev

2

1

City Multidisciplinary Hospital №2, Saint-Petersburg, Russian Federation

2
“Federal Center of Neurosurgery”, the Ministry of Health, Novosibirsk, Novosibirsk, Russian Federation
Introduction: Craniovertebral region (CVR) meningiomas account for 0.3-3.2% of all meningiomas. The purpose of this research is to retrospectively analyze the results of surgical treatment of CVR meningiomas with justification of the choice of approach depending on the localization of the tumor matrix. Material and Methods: 36 patients with CVR meningiomas operated on in the period 2009-2022 years were included in this research. The overall functional status was assessed using the Karnofsky scale. The Frankel scale was used to assess motor conductors. The neurological status was assessed in detail by the function of the cranial nerves (PCMIS - Petroclival Impairment Scale). All patients underwent MRI before surgery, which verified the tumor matrix in the sagittal and axial planes, and calculated the tumor volume. Neurophysiological monitoring was performed intraoperatively: the function of the cranial nerves (CN) was monitored. The radicality of tumor removal was assessed using the D. Simpson scale. Control MRI, CT studies, assessment of the neurological and functional status were performed 2 weeks (upon discharge from the hospital) and 1 year after surgery. D. Paley's classification was used to analyze postoperative complications. Results: In the group of 36 patients, there were 6 men and 30 women. The average age was 45 (29-72) years. Initial dysfunction of the caudal CN was noted in 22.2% of patients. When assessing the functional status, the average score on the Karnofsky scale before the operation was 65.52 (± 6.41) points. In the postoperative period, an improvement in the functional status was noted to 71.71 (± 14.16) points at discharge, after 1 year, recovery to 86.9 (± 5.61) points. Before the operation, conduction disorders were noted in the form of hemiparesis in 10 (27.8%) and tetraparesis in 4 (11.1%) patients. When assessing after 1 year, hemiparesis persisted in 2 (6%) and tetraparesis in 1 (3%) patient. Two weeks after the operation (at discharge), a decrease in the function of the cranial nerves was noted in 20 (55.6%) patients. One year after the operation, mild dysfunction of the caudal group of the cranial nerves persisted in 9 (25%) patients, moderate dysfunction - in 1 (2.8%) patient. According to the localization of the tumor matrix in the sagittal plane, the patients were divided into 3 groups: the lower third of the clivus - 15 (41.7%), the foramen magnum level - 18 (50%), the C1-C2 level - 3 (8.3%). According to the localization of the tumor matrix in the axial plane, two groups were distinguished: anterior (anterolateral) - 27 (75%), posterior (posterolateral) - 9 (25%). The rate of tumor resection (according to the Simpson scale) grade II was achieved in 25 patients (70%), grade III - in 8 (22%), grade IV - in 3 (8%). Conclusion: When the tumor matrix is ​​localized at the level of the lower third of the clivus, the average volume of tumor tissue is the largest and the incidence of complications is higher compared to the location of the matrix at the level of the foramen magnum and C1-C2. For the anterior and anterolateral location of the tumor matrix, the posterolateral approach is optimal, and for the posterior and posterolateral localization of the matrix - the median suboccipital approach is optimal.

## ID: AGS26/1787

## P1104: Clinical profile and short-term outcomes of patients with spine tumors in a tertiary referral centre in Tanzania

Mariam Mohamed Mzingi

1,2

, Frank Oure

3

, Albert Lazaro

1

, Nicephorus Rutabasibwa

1

1

Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, College of Surgeons of East Central and Southern Africa, Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania

2

College of Surgeons of East, Central and Southern Africa, Arusha, Tanzania

3

Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, Dar Es Salaam, Tanzania

Introduction: Spine tumors, both primary and metastatic, contribute significantly to neurological disability and reduced quality of life. Their management is complex worldwide but is particularly challenging in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), where patients often present late and access to advanced imaging, histopathology, and specialized surgical care is limited. Data from sub-Saharan Africa remain scarce, yet are essential to inform context-specific strategies for optimizing care and improving outcomes. Objective: To describe the clinical-demographic characteristics, clinical presentation, tumor profile, management, and short-term outcomes of patients with spine tumors managed at a tertiary referral center in Tanzania. Materials and Methods: We conducted a retrospective review of patients surgically treated for spine tumors at Muhimbili Orthopaedic Institute, Dar es Salaam, between March 2024 and August 2025. Data collected included demographic details, clinical presentation, imaging and referral patterns, tumor location, surgical management, histopathological findings, and short-term outcomes. Descriptive statistics were used for analysis. Results: A total of 51 patients were included, of whom 26 (51%) were male. Health insurance coverage was present in 68.6% of patients. The most common presenting symptoms were back pain (43.1%) and limb weakness (43.1%), with a median symptom duration of 4.5 months before presenting to the referral centre. Only 39.2% of patients had imaging and a definitive diagnosis established at the referring facility, reflecting gaps in peripheral diagnostic capacity. Tumor location was extradural in 47.1%, intradural-extramedullary in 31.4%, and intramedullary in 21.6% of cases, with the most common level involved being the thoracic spine. Metastatic lesions accounted for 15.7% of tumors. Histological confirmation was limited by poor follow-up; however, meningiomas (33.3%) were the most frequently reported pathology among those with results. The median length of hospital stay was 5 days. At discharge, 42 patients (82.3%) had unchanged neurological status compared to admission, 6 patients (11.8%) showed improvement, and 3 patients (5.9%) had deterioration. Conclusion: Patients with spine tumors in Tanzania typically present late, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment. Diagnostic limitations in peripheral hospitals and weak referral systems exacerbate this challenge. Strengthening referral pathways, expanding access to advanced imaging, and ensuring timely histopathological follow-up are critical to improving outcomes. Further prospective studies are needed to evaluate the long-term impact of early referral and multidisciplinary care models on survival and functional recovery in LMIC settings.
Keywords: Spine tumors, referral systems, low-and middle-income countries, Tanzania

## ID: AGS26/1880

## P1105: Intramedullary spinal sarcoidosis presenting as a tumor mimic: diagnostic lessons from a surgical case

Eliezer Villanueva-Castro

1

, Tomas Moncada-Habib

2

, Zachary Porter

1

, Deanna Sasaki-Adams

1

, Hector Soriano-Baron

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, University of Arkansas for Medical Sciences, Little Rock, United States

2

Department of Neurosurgery, Instituto Nacional de Neurología y Neurocirugía, Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Intramedullary spinal sarcoidosis is an exceptionally rare presentation of a systemic granulomatous disease and can closely resemble primary intramedullary tumors on imaging. Misinterpretation may lead to unnecessary aggressive resections. We report a surgically managed case and highlight key diagnostic lessons relevant to spinal oncology practice. Materials and Methods: A 36-year-old male with progressive cervical myelopathy was evaluated. MRI demonstrated an enhancing intramedullary lesion, initially suspected to be neoplastic. To obtain a definitive diagnosis, limited surgical decompression with biopsy was performed. Histopathology revealed non-caseating granulomas consistent with sarcoidosis. A focused literature appraisal was performed to contextualize diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in similar surgically managed cases. Results: Surgery allowed safe tissue sampling without neurological deterioration. The patient recovered well under postoperative corticosteroid therapy, achieving meaningful functional improvement. In the published literature, only a small number of intramedullary sarcoidosis cases have been confirmed histologically following surgical intervention. Most were managed with biopsy or limited decompression, rather than gross total resection, reflecting the infiltrative and ill-defined nature of these lesions. Clinical outcomes were generally favorable when surgery was combined with immunosuppressive therapy, although recurrence and long-term neurological sequelae remain documented. Conclusion: Intramedullary spinal sarcoidosis should remain an essential differential diagnosis in patients with intramedullary lesions, even in the absence of systemic manifestations. Early biopsy is pivotal for diagnostic accuracy and prevents unnecessary tumor resections. Recognition of this rare entity in the surgical decision-making process may improve long-term outcomes and optimize management strategies in spinal oncology.

## ID: AGS26/1902

## P1106: Complete resection fo osteoid osteoma in the cervical spine by totally endoscopic approach

Bruno Castro

1

1

Hospital São João de Deus, Divinopolis, Brazil

Introduction: Our team report a case of a 19 years old woman suffering from neck pain for the last 3 years (VAS 8, ODI 22), causing severe activities limitations, using pain killers 2 to 3 times a day, with CT and MRI showing an sclerosing lesion on the left lateral mass of the C5 vertebra. After failure of conservative surgery, it was proposed a surgical resection of the lesion, using endoscopic surgery, instead of traditional open surgery, which would have to resect the facet joint, and spine ligaments, resulting into instability of the spine, and thus an arthrodesis would had to be performed. Material and Methods: The patient was operated under general surgery, on prone position, with soft pads guarding the face and eyes, and intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring. A 7 mm skin incision was made 1 cm from midline on the left, above the left lateral mass of C5, using intraoperative radioscopy. The 30° uniportal spine endoscope was positioned, and cortical bone of the lateral mass was drilled, until a purplish-white lesion was identified and resected from the spine in a single piece. Thermal ablation was performed into the tumor site, and the skin was closed with simple suture. The procedure took less than 90 minutes. Results: The patient had a full recovery, and was discharged 12h after the surgery with a soft foam neck brace. She returned for examination after 14 days with resolution of the neck pain, and now after more than 1 year of follow-up she is pain free (VAS 0, ODI 0), with no neck limitations regarding to range of movement and activities. Pathological report confirmed it to be an osteoid osteoma. Conclusion: Full endoscopic spine surgery has been successfully used to treat several spine diseases, and its use for oncological purposes, specially related to bone tissue, might be of great advantages, regarding minimally invasiveness, spine stability maintenance, shorter surgery time, shorter hospital stay e faster recovery.

## ID: AGS26/1978

## P1107: Surgical management of intramedullary spinal cord metastases (ISCM): extent of resection outweighed by clinical and oncological factors

Meltem Ivren

1

, Dilber Yalman

1

, Basem Ishak

1,2

, Sebastian Ille

1

, Sandro Krieg

1

, Lenga Pavlina

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Heidelberg University, Im Neuenheimer Feld 400, 69120, Heidelberg, Germany, Heidelberg, Germany

2

Department of Neurosurgery, ATOS Klinik Wiesbaden, Wiesbaden, Germany, Wiesbaden, Germany

Introduction: With the rarity and complexity of intramedullary spinal cord metastases (ISCM), evidence-based guidelines on surgical strategy are rare, especially concerning the ideal extent of resection (EOR). This study evaluates differences in clinical and surgical outcomes as well as short-term mortality among patients undergoing biopsy, subtotal, or gross total resection (GTR). Material and Methods: We retrospectively included 16 patients who underwent surgical treatment for histologically confirmed ISCM between 2015 and 2024. Stratified by EOR (biopsy, subtotal, or total resection), perioperative complications, neurological outcomes, and 90-day mortality were assessed. We also performed a literature review of ISCM surgical case series comprising ≥ 5 patients. Results: Sixteen patients were analyzed (median age 59 years; 56% male), with thoracic lesions representing the most common location (56%). Postoperative complications occurred in 19%, yet no permanent neurological impairment, intraoperative mortality, or transfusion requirements were observed. 90-day mortality was lowest among patients undergoing GTR (13%) vs. subtotal resection (60%) or biopsy alone (33%) (p = 0.015). Multivariate analysis did not demonstrate an independent survival advantage of EOR (p = 0.834), indicating potential bias from patient selection. Across all groups, neurological function was either stable or improved. Conclusion: Surgical resection of ISCM is generally safe and can help symptom relief or stabilization. GTR was associated with lower early mortality rates. However oncological status and neurological deficits seemed more relevant prognostic factors. Hence, surgery should focus on clinical stability and be individualized according to the systemic disease burden and overall clinical status.

## ID: AGS26/1985

## P1108: Sacral metastasis from pulmonary adenocarcinoma mimicking a chordoma: a case report and literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Chordomas are rare tumors commonly arising in the sacrum, often posing diagnostic and therapeutic challenges. Rarely, metastatic lesions from primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma can mimic sacral chordomas both clinically and radiologically. We present a case of sacral metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma initially suspected to be a chordoma. We aim to describe a rare case of sacral metastasis from lung adenocarcinoma mimicking a primary chordoma and to highlight the importance of differential diagnosis in sacral masses. Material and Methods: A 58-year-old man presented with chronic sacral pain and constipation. Neurological examination was unremarkable. MRI revealed a 63x46 mm lobulated sacral mass suggestive of chordoma, infiltrating soft tissues and abutting the posterior rectal wall. CT scan confirmed a lytic sacrococcygeal lesion. Results: The patient underwent surgical resection. Intraoperatively, the tumor appeared friable, hemorrhagic, and right-sided with adjacent bone lysis. Gross total resection was achieved. Histopathological analysis revealed a necrotic, poorly differentiated carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry confirmed a metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with a pulmonary origin. A thoracoabdominal CT scan identified a right lung mass, sacrococcygeal osteolytic metastases, liver nodules, and a right adrenal metastasis. Conclusion: This case emphasizes the importance of considering metastatic disease in the differential diagnosis of sacral tumors. Pulmonary adenocarcinoma, although rare, can present as a sacral mass mimicking chordoma. Multidisciplinary evaluation, including advanced imaging and histopathological assessment, is essential for accurate diagnosis and management.

## ID: AGS26/2006

## P1109: Intradural metastatic melanoma of the spine: staged surgical management and delayed neurological recovery

Malbery Cerda Rodriguez

1

, Edward Walteros

1

, Dario Fuertes

1

1

Clinica Imbanaco, Cali, Colombia

Introduction: Intradural metastatic melanoma is one of the rarest presentations of spinal tumor dissemination, reported almost exclusively in isolated case reports. Overall, intradural spinal metastases account for only 0.8-3.9% of symptomatic spinal metastases, with intradural extramedullary disease estimated at ≤5% in radiologic series. Within this already rare group, melanoma represents an exceptionally uncommon etiology. These lesions are typically associated with rapid neurological decline, poor prognosis, and limited evidence regarding delayed recovery after surgical treatment. Material and Methods: A 56 year old female presented with severe cervical pain and rapidly progressive tetraparesis. A posterior cervicodorsal pigmented cutaneous lesion was identified, without prior melanoma diagnosis. Neurological evaluation revealed an incomplete cervical cord syndrome, classified as ASIA C. MRI demonstrated intradural extramedullary lesions at C6-C7 and T3 with critical cord compression. At admission, the primary tumor was unknown. Surgical decision-making was guided by SINS (14, unstable) and neurological status (ASIA C). A presumptive application of Tokuhashi (score 8) and Tomita (score 6), assuming a high-grade malignancy, supported an aggressive but palliative surgical strategy. These scores were later confirmed retrospectively after histopathology revealed high-grade metastatic melanoma. A staged surgical approach was performed, consisting first of posterior cervical decompression with instrumented fusion from C4-T5, durotomy, and intradural tumor resection at C6-C7 under continuous multimodal neurophysiological monitoring, followed by right anterolateral thoracotomy with T3 corpectomy and tumor excision. Results: Neurological stability was preserved intraoperatively. Histopathology confirmed high-grade metastatic melanoma with moderate proliferative activity Ki67 10%. The patient remained ASIA C preoperatively and immediately after surgery, but at three months follow up she demonstrated a remarkable delayed neurological recovery, improving to ASIA D. Conclusion: This case illustrates that even in intradural melanoma metastasis an entity of extreme rarity and poor prognosis presumptive prognostic scoring (Tokuhashi, Tomita), combined with SINS and ASIA, can guide urgent surgical decisions prior to histological confirmation. Staged decompression with intradural tumor resection under neurophysiological monitoring and stabilization preserved spinal cord integrity and enabled meaningful delayed recovery. These findings emphasize that the absence of immediate postoperative improvement does not preclude significant neurological gains, reinforcing the importance of multidisciplinary strategies in spinal oncology.

## ID: AGS26/2013

## P1110: Photodynamic nail stabilization for pathologic sacral insufficiency fracture treatment and prevention after sacrectomy

Jestin Williams

1

, Youssef Khalafallah

2

, Shalin Patel

1

, Rob North

1

, Zachary Lawson

1

, Laurence Rhines

1

, Maryam Tilton

3

, Farshid Alambeigi

3

, Jeffrey Siewerdsen

1

, Justin Bird

1

1

MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, United States

2

Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, United States

3

University of Texas, Austin, United States

Introduction: Oncologic patients are at risk of sacral insufficiency fractures (SIF) secondary to pathologic lesions, osteoporosis, or surgical treatments such as partial sacrectomy. The photodynamic nail (PDN) is a MIS skeletal stabilization device with the potential to reduce morbidity and enhance MRI compatibility. The objective of this study was to assess the safety and efficacy of sacral PDN stabilization when used for the treatment or prevention of SIFs. Material and Methods: A retrospective chart review of patients who underwent PDN sacral fixation for treatment or prophylaxis of SIF was conducted. Primary outcome measures were fracture stability, fracture prevention, and device-related complications. Secondary outcomes were postoperative pain and ambulatory status. Results: Eight patients were identified (mean age of 57.3 years). Seven patients underwent trans-sacral trans-iliac fixation. There were no implant-related complications. The average time for follow-up was 6.6 months. Average pre- and post-operative visual analog pain scores were 8.4 and 1.4, respectively. Pre-operatively 2 patients were wheelchair dependent, 3 ambulated with walkers, and 3 with no assistive devices. Ambulatory status remained stable or improved in all cases. All fractures were stable at the most recent follow-up. No fractures developed in the 3 patients treated prophylactically after partial sacrectomy. Conclusion: Early clinical results suggest that PDN is a viable option for SIF treatment and prevention after partial sacrectomy. Pain improved after fracture stabilization and no fracture developed in the prophylactic stabilization group. Future studies with larger numbers and long-term outcomes are warranted. Furthermore, on-going investigations into the biomechanical behavior of PDNs placed into compromised sacral bone will be useful in developing patient specific treatment plans.
