## P1111: Spinal tumors in unusual locations: learning from the unexpected

Majdouline Barkache

1

, Affes Ameur

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Neurosurgery Departement Sahloul Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal tumors usually occur in predictable locations. Rarely, they present in atypical sites, mimicking vascular, infectious, or malignant lesions, which complicates diagnosis and surgical planning. Reporting such cases improves clinical awareness and surgical strategies. Materials and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed three patients with spinal tumors in unusual locations, focusing on clinical presentation, imaging, surgical approach, and outcomes. Results: • Case 1: A 27-year-old female with a cervical extradural schwannoma extending into the vertebral artery foramen, initially suspected as a vascular lesion. Complete excision was achieved using microsurgical dissection and intraoperative neuromonitoring. • Case 2: A 40-year-old male with a thoracic intramedullary hemangioblastoma, presenting with rapid paraparesis and no von Hippel-Lindau disease. Surgical resection was performed with careful attention to spinal cord preservation. • Case 3: A 19-year-old female with a lumbar extradural meningioma, a rare histologic variant. Total resection was achieved without compromising spinal stability. Conclusion: Spinal tumors in atypical locations pose diagnostic and surgical challenges. Awareness of unusual presentations, precise imaging interpretation, and adaptable microsurgical strategies are essential to optimize outcomes. Sharing these cases enhances diagnostic accuracy and guides surgical decision-making in complex spinal oncology scenarios.

## ID: AGS26/2171

## P1112: Sacropelvic hydatid disease presenting with cauda equina syndrome: a rare parasitic cause of neurological decline

Mario Colville

1,2

, Rodolfo Lopez Allendes

1,2

, Cristian Correa

2

1

Universidad de la Frontera, Hospital Hernan Henriquez Aravena, Temuco, Chile

2
Dr. Hernan Henriquez Aravena Hospital, Temuco, Chile,
Introduction: Osseous hydatidosis is a rare manifestation of Echinococcus granulosus infection, accounting for less than 4% of all cases. The spine is the most frequently affected skeletal site, with sacropelvic involvement being extremely uncommon. In Chile, the national incidence in 2023 was 3.3 per 100,000, with the Araucanía Region -where this case originates- reporting 8.9 per 100,000 and the highest number of hospital discharges nationally. Sacral hydatidosis often presents insidiously, but can result in devastating neurological sequelae due to cauda equina compression. We report a complex case of sacropelvic hydatidosis with progressive neurological deterioration, highlighting key surgical and diagnostic considerations. Material and Methods: A 28-year-old woman from rural southern Chile (endemic zone) with a history of untreated hydatidosis presented with progressive low back pain, bilateral leg weakness, neurogenic bladder requiring intermittent catheterization, and fecal incontinence. Neurological examination revealed flaccid paraparesis, bilateral foot drop (M0 dorsiflexion), saddle anesthesia, and areflexia. Imaging showed prior lumbopelvic arthrodesis, but due to sacral symptoms, new MRI and CT scans were obtained. These revealed multiloculated cystic lesions eroding the sacrum and presacral space with compression of sacral nerve roots. Results: The imaging findings were consistent with invasive hydatid disease involving sacral segments S1-S3, extending bilaterally (WBB sectors 3-9, layers A-E). The case mimicked other destructive sacral lesions such as chordoma or tuberculous spondylitis. Due to the extent of involvement and prior fixation, resection was limited. Surgical management included prior laminectomy and spinopelvic fixation (S2AI screws). Intraoperatively, 20% hypertonic saline was used as a scolicidal agent for cavity irrigation, following WHO recommendations. Postoperative medical therapy with Albendazole (800 mg/day) was maintained. Despite residual neurologic findings such as foot drop and neurogenic bladder, the patient achieved functional ambulation using a cane and currently maintains an independent lifestyle while caring for her 4-year-old daughter. Conclusion: Sacropelvic hydatidosis, although rare, should be considered in endemic areas in patients with lumbosacral symptoms and atypical imaging findings. High clinical suspicion and early imaging are essential. The use of the WBB surgical classification adapted to the sacrum allows better planning of resections in these destructive cases. Management requires a multidisciplinary team approach. Surgery aims to decompress, stabilize, and reduce cyst burden, while antiparasitic therapy helps mitigate recurrence. This case illustrates that, even in the context of extensive bone and neural involvement, a well-planned surgical and medical strategy can preserve long-term function and quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/2222

## P1113: Occipitocervical fixation for osteolytic metastasis of C2 secondary to lung carcinoma: case report and literature review

Afonso Teles

1

, André Guimarães

1

, Sara Machado Neves

1

, Rui Chaves

1

, Henrique Lascasas

1

, José Raposo

1

, Sofia Neto

1

, Pedro Gomes

1

1

Unidade Local de Saúde de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, Vila Real, Portugal

Introduction: Metastatic involvement of the upper cervical spine is uncommon but poses significant surgical challenges due to the risk of craniocervical instability and neurological compromise. The axis (C2) is a rare metastatic site; however, osteolytic infiltration may lead to cervicobrachialgia, impending pathological fracture, and functional decline. Occipitocervical fixation has been reported as an effective strategy to achieve pain control, prevent instability, and improve quality of life, even in the palliative setting. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 56-year-old male, previously autonomous, diagnosed with pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The patient initially underwent left lower lobectomy with mediastinal lymphadenectomy through a video-assisted thoracoscopic (VATS) approach. During follow-up, an osteolytic metastatic lesion of C2 was identified, associated with cervicobrachialgia and a high risk of instability. A posterior occipitocervical fixation was performed as a prophylactic stabilization procedure with the aim of pain relief and prevention of pathological fracture. Results: The surgery was completed without intraoperative or postoperative complications. The patient reported significant relief of cervicobrachialgia in the immediate postoperative period and was mobilized early. At the most recent follow-up, he remained neurologically and functionally stable. The literature supports occipitocervical fixation as the most frequently reported surgical strategy for C2 metastases, either alone or combined with vertebroplasty. Published series demonstrate consistent pain reduction, improvement in neurological function, and median survival ranging between 12 and 18 months, with low rates of complications. Conclusion: Osteolytic metastases of C2 secondary to lung carcinoma are rare but clinically relevant due to its destabilizing potential. Posterior occipitocervical fixation is a safe and effective surgical option, providing pain control, prevention of pathological fracture, and maintenance of functional autonomy in selected patients. This case highlights the importance of a multidisciplinary approach and supports, in line with current literature, the role of surgical stabilization as a palliative measure with a meaningful impact on quality of life.

## ID: AGS26/2346

## P1114: Current management of giant cell tumor in the spine: case report and literature review

Diego Molina-Botello

1

, Eric Estrada-Estrada

1

1

Centro médico nacional siglo XXI (IMSS), Mexico City, Mexico

Introduction: Giant cell tumor (GCT) of the spine is a primary benign but locally aggressive neoplasm, with the potential to destroy vertebral bodies, cause spinal cord compression, and present frequent recurrences. Surgical treatment is particularly challenging due to adjacent anatomical structures and the need to preserve spinal stability. Resection remains the treatment of choice, usually combined with vertebral reconstruction to restore spinal biomechanics. Material and Methods: A 21-year-old female with a cervicothoracic GCT (C7-T1) underwent staged surgeries. In April 2023, she received a T1 laminectomy with partial tumor resection, followed by posterior instrumentation from C5 to T3 with transmass lateral screws at C4-C6 and pedicle screws at T1-T3. In March 2024, due to tumor progression (66 × 41 × 40 mm) and spinal cord compression, a third surgery was performed through sternotomy and anterior cervicothoracic approach with the oncology team. Corpectomy of C6-T2 was performed using ultrasonic aspirator, with macroscopic tumor resection (8 × 7 × 6 cm) and reconstruction using expandable mesh-plate (60 × 61 mm), three expandable screws (14 mm), and demineralized bone matrix. Results: The patient evolved without immediate complications, with complete motor function recovery and adequate spinal stability after reconstruction. She is currently under clinical and radiological follow-up, receiving adjuvant medical therapy with denosumab. Conclusion: Staged resection with combined posterior and anterior approaches, complemented by vertebral reconstruction, represents an effective surgical strategy for cervicothoracic GCT. This case exemplifies the technical complexity and the need for structured planning to achieve local control and spinal stability. Denosumab may serve as a valuable adjuvant in recurrent or incompletely resected tumors.

## ID: AGS26/2383

## P1115: Epidural cavernous hemangioma: case report

Matias Mondragón

1

1

Instituto Clavel, Barcelona, Spain

Introduction: Epidural cavernous hemangiomas are rare benign vascular malformations of the spine, representing less than 1% of all spinal tumors. They are typically located in the intramedullary or intradural compartments, while the purely epidural form is exceptionally uncommon. Clinical manifestations are often related to progressive spinal cord or nerve root compression, such as radiculopathy, motor weakness, or sensory deficits. Acute presentations are unusual and can lead to diagnostic uncertainty, as imaging findings frequently mimic other vascular malformations or neoplastic processes. We present the case of a 49-year-old woman who presented with acute orthostatic headache, in whom imaging revealed an epidural vascular lesion. The case highlights the challenges in diagnosis and the importance of surgical management. Material and Methods: A 49-year-old female patient presented to the emergency department with acute-onset orthostatic headache, without additional neurological deficits on examination. Laboratory findings were unremarkable. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine demonstrated a well-defined epidural vascular lesion, with radiological characteristics initially suggestive of a lymphangiomatous malformation. After a multidisciplinary discussion, the patient was scheduled for surgical resection through a posterior laminectomy approach. Intraoperatively, a vascular, encapsulated epidural lesion was identified and completely excised. The specimen was submitted for histopathological analysis to confirm the diagnosis. Results: The surgical procedure was uneventful, with minimal intraoperative bleeding despite the vascular nature of the lesion. The mass was completely resected, and no dural involvement was observed. Histopathological analysis revealed dilated vascular channels lined by a single layer of endothelial cells, consistent with cavernous hemangioma. Postoperatively, the patient experienced complete resolution of her headache and had no new neurological deficits. She was discharged in good condition. At follow-up, she remained asymptomatic, with no evidence of recurrence on imaging. Conclusion: Epidural cavernous hemangiomas are extremely rare vascular lesions, often misdiagnosed due to their atypical clinical presentation and nonspecific radiological features. Although the most common presentation is progressive neurological impairment, this case demonstrates that acute symptoms such as orthostatic headache may also occur, further complicating the diagnostic process. Preoperative identification is challenging, as imaging may suggest alternative diagnoses, including lymphangiomatous or neoplastic lesions. Surgical excision is both diagnostic and therapeutic, and complete resection generally leads to excellent outcomes with low recurrence rates. Awareness of this rare entity is essential for clinicians, radiologists, and surgeons, as early recognition and timely surgical intervention can prevent neurological deterioration and allow definitive treatment. This case underscores the importance of including epidural cavernous hemangioma in the differential diagnosis of spinal epidural vascular lesions presenting with atypical symptoms.

## ID: AGS26/2417

## P1116: Comparison of instrumented fusion versus decompression alone in spinal tumor surgery

Mustafa Kavcar

1

, Baran Can Alpergin

1

1

Etlik City Hospital Department of Neurosurgery, Ankara, Turkey

Introduction: The optimal surgical approach for spinal tumors remains debated. While decompression relieves neural compression, fusion may improve stability and long-term outcomes. This study compares decompression alone versus decompression with fusion in spinal tumor surgery. Materials and Methods: A retrospective cohort of 82 patients undergoing resection of spinal tumors (2022-2025) was analyzed. Group A: decompression only (n = 40); Group B: decompression with instrumented fusion (n = 42). Outcomes included postoperative pain (VAS), neurological improvement (ASIA grade), reoperation rate, and complications. Results: Both groups achieved neurological improvement (p < 0.01). Fusion group had superior maintenance of spinal stability with fewer reoperations (5% vs 18%, p = 0.04). Operative time and blood loss were greater in the fusion group (p < 0.01). Complication rates were similar (14% vs 12%, p = 0.76). Conclusion: Fusion provides long-term stability benefits in spinal tumor surgery at the cost of longer surgery. In patients with pre-existing instability, fusion should be strongly considered, while decompression alone may suffice in select cases.

## ID: AGS26/2438

## P1117: Management of intramedullary spinal cord tumors

Shubha Dip Nandi

1

, Selvamuthukumaran K.

1

, Senthilkumar K

1

, Gowtham Kuncha

1

1

Meenakshi Superspeciaity Hospital, Meenakshi Mission Hospital and Research Centre, Madurai, India

Introduction: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors are rare lesions, accounting for approximately 4-10% of all central nervous system (CNS) tumors and nearly 25% of intradural spinal tumors in adults. In pediatric populations, this number is significantly higher, representing nearly 50% of intradural spinal neoplasms. These tumors present unique diagnostic and therapeutic challenges due to their location within the spinal cord parenchyma. The advent of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) has dramatically enhanced early diagnosis and anatomical localization. Despite advances in microsurgical techniques, including intraoperative neurophysiological monitoring and high-definition microscopy, the surgical management of intramedullary spinal cord tumors remains demanding due to risks of neurological deterioration. This study aims to detail our institutional experience in managing such tumors, assess clinical outcomes, and identify prognostic indicators for recovery. Material and Methods: A total of 27 patients who underwent surgery for intramedullary spinal cord tumors between 2018 and 2024 at Meenakshi Mission Hospital, Madurai, were retrospectively reviewed. Patients of all age groups and both genders were included. Data were collected regarding patient demographics, symptomatology, neurological status (using the Modified McCormick scale), tumor location on MRI, histopathological diagnosis, extent of surgical resection, and postoperative course. All surgeries were performed under general anesthesia in the prone position. Standard posterior midline approaches with multilevel laminectomy or laminoplasty were used. Tumors were accessed via midline myelotomy and resected using microsurgical techniques. Gross total resection was attempted in all cases. Postoperative neurological status was recorded, and follow-up was maintained for a minimum of 6 months. Complications, recurrence, and functional recovery were analyzed. Results: Among the 27 patients, 15 were males and 12 females, with a mean age of 29.4 years (range: 4-61 years). The average duration of symptoms prior to diagnosis was 92 days. The most common presenting complaints were motor weakness (all 27 cases), followed by sensory disturbances (18 cases) and bowel/bladder dysfunction (11 cases). Tumor location included cervical (9), cervicodorsal (3), dorsal (10), dorsolumbar (2), and conus/cauda region (3). Preoperative functional assessment revealed that 13 patients were Modified McCormick Grade III, 8 were Grade II, and 6 were Grade IV. Histologically, ependymomas (12 cases) and low-grade astrocytomas (9 cases) were the most common, followed by hemangioblastomas (3), cavernomas (2), and one case of intramedullary metastasis. Gross total resection was achieved in 23 patients (85.2%), and subtotal resection in 4 cases due to infiltrative tumor nature. Postoperative complications included one case of new-onset paraplegia, two cases with transient worsening of motor power, and one wound-related complication. At 6-month follow-up, 20 patients showed stable or improved neurological function, with the mean postoperative Modified McCormick score improving from 3.1 to 2.7. There were no mortalities. Conclusion: Intramedullary spinal cord tumors, though rare, require high clinical suspicion and timely intervention to optimize outcomes. Ependymomas and low-grade astrocytomas form the majority of such lesions. Gross total excision, wherever feasible, should be the surgical goal, especially for well-demarcated tumors such as ependymomas. Preoperative neurological status and tumor histology are key predictors of functional outcome. A multidisciplinary approach with meticulous microsurgical technique and vigilant postoperative care ensures favorable long-term results.

## ID: AGS26/2569

## P1118: Augmentation techniques with silicone in spine oncologic surgery

Stefano Telera

1

, Francesco Crispo

1

, Mario Lecce

1

, Fabrizio Rasile

1

, Edoardo Mazzucchi

1 1

, Laura Raus

1

, Roberto Gazzeri

2

1
IRCCS Istituto Nazionale Tumori Regina Elena, Rome, Italy, 
2
San Giovanni Addolorata Hospital, Rome, Italy
Introduction: Since its introduction, pedicle screw instrumentation in all fields of spine surgery, has afforded stronger biomechanical constructs, higher fusion rates, fewer instrumented levels, better outcomes and lower complication rates, when compared to previous surgical techniques. However, it mainly depends on a robust screw purchase on the pedicle and a solid bone-implant interface which decreases in the presence of osteopenia, infection or cancer diseases, possibly leading to catastrophic implant failure. Cement augmentation of the instrumented vertebrae coupled with standard or fenestrated screws, has been successfully used to improve the bone-implant interface and pullout strength, potentially improving long-term outcomes by reducing the length of the implant and the overall complications. Polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) is the most commonly used pedicle screw augmentation cement, showing several advantages including rapid solidification, biocompatibility, physician familiarity and cost effectiveness. Reported drawbacks which include a high exothermic polymerization temperature, the lack of osteoconductive properties and the possible difficulties during revision surgery prompted the research of new materials. VK100® is a silicon filling material, biocompatible, non-exothermic and with elastic properties more similar to bone, if compared to PMMA. Material and Methods: We report our results using augmentation technique in pedicle screw instrumentation with VK100, for patients affected by spinal metastases. Results: Between 2020 and 2025, a series of 14 patients with vertebral metastases have been operated with spinal fixation implemented with augmentation making use of VK100® silicon filling material of the fixed levels. They were 5 males and 9 females, median age was 61.5 (range 58-74). Histology of the tumors included: lung carcinoma in 6 cases, breast carcinoma in 4, kidney carcinoma in 2, thyroid and uterus carcinoma in one case respectively. Pathologic vertebral levels were L2 (n°5); L3 (n°4); L5 (n°1), D12 (n°2); D11 (n°1); D8 (n°1). Indication for surgery were pain which was present in all patients and/or neurologic deficits and spinal instability. Fixation techniques include open decompression plus percutaneous fixation one-three levels above and below the pathologic levels and augmentation in 10 cases, percutaneous fixation and augmentation in 4 cases. Overall augmentation with silicone was performed in 41 vertebral bodies. There were not technical issues, standard technique (first vertebroplasty and then positioning of the screws) were employed in 11 cases, fenestrated screws in the last three cases without time constraints related to the curing process of the filling material. Antero-posterior and lateral double fluoroscopy or intraoperative CT scan were employed. The mean survival was 12 months ±8 months (5 patients are still alive). All patients showed a significant reduction of pain measured by VAS and Dennis Pain Scale. There were no significant complications related to the procedure: four cases of asymptomatic silicone leakages and one case of asymptomatic pulmonary embolism. One patient developed after surgery a mild paraparesis which improved with corticosteroids. In all cases the patients were able to receive adjuvant systemic therapies or radiotherapy/radiosurgery. Until now we did not observe any case of pull out of the screws or failure of the systems implanted and therefore no conclusions about technical issues of revision surgery can be drawn in oncologic patients. Conclusions: In our experience, silicone VK100® appeared to be a safe and effective filling material for augmentation techniques in spine fixation. Its biomechanic properties, low elastic modulus, long working time, absence of exhortemic reaction may be particular useful in oncologic patients.

## ID: AGS26/2752

## P1119: Spinal ependymoma and glioblastoma: association, degeneration or misdiagnosis

Roua Latrach

1

, Mohamed Amine Hmida

1

, Gallaoui Slim

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Iadh Ksira

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Ependymoma and glioblastoma are two distinct types of central nervous system tumors. While the former arises from ependymal cells lining the ventricles, the latter originates from glial cells. Both tumors most commonly occur in the brain, with spinal cord involvement being rare. To date, no cases of association or malignant transformation between these two tumor types have been reported. In this article, we present the case of a 25-year-old female patient with a history of anemia, who was admitted in December 2024 for intercostal neuralgia associated with motor deficit and bladder dysfunction. Imaging revealed an anteroposterior tumor at the D3-D4 and D4-D5 levels, with additional lesions at C3 and C7. She underwent surgery with incomplete resection of a dorsal intradural extramedullary lesion. Histopathological examination confirmed a Grade 3 anaplastic ependymoma. Before chemotherapy could be initiated, the patient was re-admitted in February 2025 for bilateral C7 neuralgia with worsening motor deficit. MRI demonstrated enlargement of the C3 and C7 tumors, as well as new lesions in the sacral region. She underwent biopsy of a C6-C7 intramedullary lesion, which unexpectedly revealed a Grade IV glioblastoma. MRI performed at the time of surgery showed no cerebral involvement. The patient was subsequently referred for radiotherapy and chemotherapy. Given these histological findings and the absence of similar cases in the literature, we considered the possibilities of misdiagnosis, tumor association, or malignant transformation. However, a definitive conclusion cannot be drawn, and our interpretation remains hypothetical pending the reporting of additional cases in the future.

## ID: AGS26/2789

## P1120: From diagnosis to donation: lived experiences, consent, and support needs in craniospinal oncology research

Gerard Mawhinney

1,2

, Helen Higham

3

, Jeremy Reynolds

4

, Simon Leedham

5

, Olaf Ansorge

1

1

University of Oxford, Nuffield Department of Clinical Neurosciences, Oxford, United Kingdom

2

Oxford University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, Spinal Sarcoma Service, Oxford Spinal Surgery Unit, Oxford, United Kingdom

3

University of Oxford, Nuffield Dept of Clinical Neurosciences, Oxford, United Kingdom

4

Oxford University Hospitals NHS Trust, Spinal Sarcoma Service, Oxford, United Kingdom

5

University of Oxford, Nuffield Dept of Medicine, Oxford, United Kingdom

Introduction: Craniospinal tumours are rare, aggressive cancers that disrupt both neurological function and quality of life. While tissue donation for research is vital for developing new treatments, consent is often sought at the height of emotional distress and clinical uncertainty-moments when patients may feel least prepared to decide. Understanding this journey from diagnosis to donation is essential to make research participation both ethical and effective. This national cross-sectional survey captures the lived experiences of people with primary craniospinal tumours, examining consent timing, communication, and explores the potential use of digital adjuncts as shared decision-making tools within this process. Material and Methods: A national, cross-sectional online survey was co-designed with patient advisors and completed by 50 UK adults diagnosed with primary brain tumours or spinal sarcomas. Recruitment occurred via patient networks, advocacy groups, and across social media platforms. The survey combined closed and open-ended questions exploring diagnosis experiences, emotional responses to tissue donation requests, consent timing and clarity, involvement of clinicians/family, and attitudes toward an NHS-hosted digital dynamic consent portal. Quantitative data were analysed descriptively; qualitative responses underwent thematic analysis. Results: 50 respondents (62% female, 70% White, 36-75y; 32% brain tumour, 68% spinal sarcoma), only 52% recalled being asked to donate tissue; almost a quarter found this stressful, citing poor timing, emotional overload, and lack of context. 62% preferred discussions in specialist appointments, not pre-/day-of surgery. While two-thirds found information clear, over a third had no chance to ask questions. Consent was obtained mainly by clinicians or nurses. Family discussion was uncommon (49%), yet most who hadn’t (79%) wished they could. Digital engagement was near-universal (96%), with 94% favoring a digital consent portal offering tumour-specific education, FAQs, as useful decision-making resources-security concerns were rare (8%). Findings highlight missed communication opportunities and strong appetite for digital consent tools. Conclusion: Patients with craniospinal tumours are eager to contribute to research but seek better timing, clearer communication, and emotional support in tissue donation consent. They strongly support a flexible, personalised dynamic digital consent platform, securely delivered via NHS systems, with tailored information and ongoing engagement. Co-designed with patients, such tools could address missed opportunities, enhance autonomy and experience and embed ethical, person-centred practices-offering a clear roadmap to improve care and accelerate translational craniospinal research.

## ID: AGS26/2811

## P1121: Preoperative embolization of spinal metastatic bone disease and its effectiveness in reducing intraoperative blood loss: case report from a single center study

Arief Purwodito

1

, Muhamad Aulia Rahman

1

, Paul Atmaja

1

, Muhammad Andika Wibisono

1

, Fabianto Santoso

1

1

RISEandSPINE, Jakarta, Indonesia

Introduction: Surgery for spinal metastatic tumors is challenging due to both technical difficulties and the risk of significant intraoperative blood loss. Theoretically, preoperative embolization reduces the tumor’s blood supply, thereby decreasing intraoperative bleeding. This report presents our experience with preoperative embolization for spinal metastatic tumors in the thoracic region. Material and Methods: 12 patients included in this study. We performed three preoperative embolization procedures for tumors involving the thoracic spinal column and nine surgeries without preoperative embolization. Tumor and spinal angioarchitecture were evaluated using MRA and conventional spinal angiography. Our center utilized Interlock and IDC detachable embolization coils (Boston Scientific). The degree of embolization was graded as complete, near-complete, or partial. In this study, we excluded total blood loss reported by anesthesia and included only blood loss measured after tumor exposure. The embolization procedure is performed 1 day prior to surgery. Results: Near-complete embolization was achieved in two patients, while one patient had partial embolization. There is no complication related to embolization procedure. The mean intraoperative blood loss in the preoperative embolization group was 1,445 mL, compared to 2,450 mL in the non-embolization group. Conclusion: Preoperative embolization of spinal tumors can be performed with a high success rate, even in developing countries with limited angiosuite facilities. Compared to surgery without embolization, preoperative embolization significantly reduces intraoperative blood loss in patients with spinal metastatic disease.

## ID: AGS26/2855

## P1122: Spinal metastases of unknown primary: is bone biopsy essential for diagnosis?

Hee-Woong Chung

1

, Han-Dong Lee

1

, Seok-Jun Hong

1

, Nam-Su Chung

1

1

Ajou University School of Medicine, Suwon, South Korea

Introduction: Spinal metastasis is a common occurrence in patients with various cancers and myeloid diseases, and the spine accounts for about 80% of all bone metastases. Delayed diagnosis of primary cancer in patients with spinal metastases of unknown origin (SMUO) adversely affects prognosis and increases the risk of skeletal-related events. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the usefulness of tumor markers, chest and abdominal CT, PET/CT, and bone biopsy for identifying the primary cancer in patients with SMUO. Material and Methods: This retrospective study included patients with SMUO who were treated at our institution between 2018 and 2023. Among a total of 73 patients identified, 64 who underwent electrophoresis, laboratory testing, chest and abdominal CT, PET/CT, and bone biopsy were included in the final analysis. The primary outcome measure was the diagnosis of the primary cancer. We assessed the sensitivity and specificity of tumor markers and electrophoresis to determine their diagnostic value. In addition, the diagnostic yields of CT, PET/CT, and bone biopsy were compared with each other. Results: Among the 64 patients included in the study, the final diagnosis was established in 59 cases. Lung carcinoma was the most common malignancy, followed by hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), multiple myeloma, and prostate carcinoma. Tumor marker levels were elevated in 44 of 64 patients. HCC, multiple myeloma, and prostate carcinoma each demonstrated a marker with high sensitivity and specificity (AFP, M protein, and PSA, respectively), whereas no tumor marker with adequate sensitivity was identified for lung carcinoma. The primary site was detected in 44 patients (68.8%) by chest and abdominal CT and in 46 patients (71.9%) by PET/CT. Bone biopsy established the final diagnosis in 35 patients (54.7%). Conclusion: In SMUO, specific tumor markers such as AFP and PSA, as well as protein electrophoresis, appear to be useful for the final diagnosis. Chest and abdominal CT and PET/CT are valuable for identifying solid tumors, but not hematologic tumors. Bone biopsy showed a lower diagnostic yield for solid tumors compared to CT or PET/CT.

## P1123: Aggressive thoracic vertebral hemangioma: a rare cause of spinal cord compression

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

2

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Roua Latrach

1

, Mohamed Ali Kharrat

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Vertebral hemangiomas are common benign vascular lesions, often discovered incidentally and usually asymptomatic. They account for 10-12% of the general population and represent 2-3% of spinal tumors. In rare instances, these lesions exhibit aggressive behavior, extending beyond the vertebral body and leading to spinal cord compression. Material and Methods: We present the case of a 14-year-old girl with progressively worsening back pain and paraplegia secondary to spinal cord compression. Imaging revealed features consistent with an aggressive thoracic vertebral hemangioma. The patient underwent urgent decompressive laminectomy, and histopathological analysis confirmed the diagnosis. Results: The patient achieved complete neurological recovery. During follow-up, however, she developed progressive thoracic kyphosis, which required a second surgery consisting of corporectomy with osteosynthesis. Conclusion: This case highlights the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges of aggressive vertebral hemangiomas in the pediatric population. Early recognition and timely surgical management are crucial to achieve favorable neurological outcomes and to prevent long-term spinal deformity.

## P1124: Primary intradural extramedullary extraosseous Ewing’s sarcoma of thoracic spine: a case report and literature review

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Roua Latrach

1

, Talel Kamoun

2

, Mohamed Ali Kharrat

2

, Jalel Kallel

2

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Extraosseous Ewing’s sarcoma is a highly malignant mesenchymal tumor that rarely occurs in the spinal canal, and its clinical outcomes remain poorly defined. To date, only a few cases of primary intradural extramedullary Ewing’s sarcoma have been reported, with heterogeneous follow-up results. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 14-year-old girl presenting with Brown-Séquard syndrome due to an intradural extramedullary lesion at level of T7, located anterolaterally on the right, compressing the spinal cord and causing neurological compromise. Results: The patient underwent a T7 hemilaminectomy with gross total resection of the intradural extramedullary tumor. She demonstrated significant neurological improvement postoperatively. Histopathological analysis confirmed the diagnosis of Ewing’s sarcoma, and she was subsequently referred for adjuvant chemotherapy and local radiation therapy. Conclusion: This case illustrates the exceptional occurrence of intradural extramedullary Ewing’s sarcoma in children. Early recognition and prompt surgical intervention are essential for neurological recovery, but long-term outcomes remain uncertain given the rarity of this entity.

## P1125: Isolated posterior epidural plasmacytoma causing spinal cord compression: a case report and review of the litterature

Elouni Emna

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Talel Kamoun

1

, Roua Latrach

1

, Mohamed Ali Kharrat

1

, Jalel Kallel

1

1

Department of Radiology, Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: Isolated extraosseous epidural plasmacytoma, without involvement or collapse of the vertebral bodies, is an extremely rare entity. Only a few cases have been reported in the literature, and their clinical management and outcomes are not well defined. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 70-year-old male who presented to our department with progressive paraparesis. Imaging revealed an isolated posterior epidural mass at the T9-T10 level, causing spinal cord compression. Results: The patient underwent gross total resection of the mass and achieved complete neurological recovery. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of plasmacytoma. Conclusion: This case highlights the rare occurrence of isolated epidural plasmacytomas and emphasizes the importance of early recognition and prompt surgical management. Gross total resection can lead to excellent neurological recovery in these rare lesions.

## P1126: Spinal plasmacytoma: a case series of four operated patients

Affes Ameur

1

, Atef Ben Nsir

2

, Elouni Emna

2

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal plasmacytoma is a rare but serious condition that can occur either as a solitary lesion or as part of multiple myeloma. Its clinical and radiological presentation is often variable, posing diagnostic challenges. We present four cases of spinal plasmacytoma managed surgically in our department. We aim to report a series of four cases of spinal plasmacytoma and highlight the diagnostic and therapeutic challenges involved. Material and Methods: Four patients were operated for spinal plasmacytoma: • Case 1: A 58-year-old woman with progressive mechanical low back pain for two months. MRI showed a pathological L2 fracture; CT confirmed 50% vertebral height loss. • Case 2: A 60-year-old woman with a history of mitral valve disease and asthma, experiencing chronic resistant back pain. MRI showed suspicious L2 signal anomalies and distant lesions. She later developed a compression fracture of L2. • Case 3: A 75-year-old woman in complete remission from multiple myeloma since 2020, presenting with new-onset back pain and urinary symptoms. MRI revealed compression fractures at D7 and L4, with L4-L5 spondylolisthesis. • Case 4: A 58-year-old man with mixed axial pain, costal pain, and spinal stiffness. CT and MRI revealed osteolytic lesions at D9-D10 with vertebral collapse and epiduritis. In all cases, pathological examination confirmed plasmacytoma. Results: All patients underwent surgical decompression and stabilization. Postoperative outcomes were favorable, with significant clinical improvement and uncomplicated recoveries. Conclusion: Spinal plasmacytoma should be considered in the differential diagnosis of vertebral lesions and persistent back pain. Early diagnosis and multidisciplinary management are crucial for optimizing patient outcomes. This series illustrates the role of combined therapies-surgery, radiotherapy, and in selected cases chemotherapy-in effective disease control.

## ID: AGS26/2979

## P1127: Management of C2 hemangiomas with kyphoplasty in the cervical spine: a case report

Aymen Ben Mahmoud

1

, Majdi sghaier

1

, ahmed saadallah

1

, Haithem Kamoun

1

, Majdi Ben Romdhane

1

1

Hopital FSI La Marsa, Faculté de Medecine de Tunis, University Tunis Manar, La Marsa, Tunisia

Cervical spine hemangiomas, although rare, can lead to significant clinical symptoms, including pain and neurological deficits, particularly when they adopt an aggressive course. Conventional management typically involves conservative approaches or invasive surgical intervention, both of which pose challenges, especially in the sensitive cervical region. Kyphoplasty, a minimally invasive procedure, presents a novel alternative for vertebral stabilization and pain relief. This case report discusses a symptomatic C2 vertebral hemangioma successfully managed with kyphoplasty, underscoring its effectiveness and safety in this anatomically complex region. Case Presentation: A 57-year-old female presented with a six-month history of progressively worsening neck pain radiating to the occipital area, accompanied by intermittent upper limb numbness. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) revealed a substantial hemangioma in the C2 vertebra, contributing to cortical thinning and mild spinal canal encroachment. Conservative management had failed to alleviate her symptoms. Kyphoplasty was performed under fluoroscopic guidance, involving the injection of bone cement. The procedure was completed without complications, and the patient reported significant pain relief within 24 hours. At three-month follow-up, imaging confirmed stable cement placement, with no evidence of complications or symptom recurrence. Clinical Discussion: Although traditionally employed for vertebral compression fractures, kyphoplasty is emerging as a viable treatment for symptomatic cervical spine hemangiomas. Its minimally invasive nature offers significant advantages in the cervical region, where conventional surgery poses heightened risks due to the proximity of critical neurovascular structures. In this case, kyphoplasty provided rapid symptomatic relief and stabilization of the C2 vertebra, demonstrating its potential efficacy in managing benign lesions in the cervical spine. However, the procedure necessitates meticulous patient selection and precise technical execution to mitigate risks, such as cement leakage, which could have severe consequences in the cervical spine. Conclusion: This case illustrates the promise of kyphoplasty as a therapeutic option for symptomatic cervical spine hemangiomas, offering substantial pain relief and vertebral stabilization with a reduced risk profile compared to more invasive surgical methods. The successful outcome in this instance suggests that kyphoplasty may become an important tool in the management of benign cervical spine lesions, particularly in challenging anatomical locations like the C2 vertebra. Further studies are warranted to establish best practices and assess long-term outcomes associated with this technique.

## ID: AGS26/3044

## P1128: Aneurysmal bone cyst of spine-multidisciplinary management, outcomes and recurrence rate

Sushruth Raj

1

1

The Spine Foundation, Mumbai, India

Introduction: We report twenty four cases of spinal aneurysmal bone cysts (ABCs) managed surgically at The Spine Foundation, Mumbai. This case series aims to highlight the clinical presentation, imaging and pathological findings, and the role of surgical excision following pre-operative embolization in the management of spinal ABCs and recurrence of disease. Method: A retrospective review of operating room logs from 2004 to 2025 identified twenty four patients with spinal ABCs who underwent surgical treatment at our institution. Data collected included clinical presentation, radiological and pathological features, surgical interventions, postoperative outcomes and recurrence rates. Results: 16 patients were female (66%), with ages ranging from 13 to 25 years (mean age: 17.75 years). Lesion distribution included the thoracic spine in twelve cases (50%), cervical spine in six cases (25%), and lumbar spine in six (25%). Common presenting symptoms were back pain, gait instability, and progressive limb weakness. No patient underwent pre-operative embolization followed by surgical excision and spinal fixation. Postoperative outcomes showed varying degrees of neurological and symptomatic improvement. Recurrence was seen 3 patients (12.5%). Conclusion: Spinal ABCs are benign but potentially aggressive lesions that may cause significant neurological symptoms. They often present with non-specific signs, making diagnosis challenging. Pre-operative embolization is a useful strategy to minimize intraoperative bleeding. Surgical decompression and stabilization remain the mainstay of treatment and can result in notable neurological recovery, even in patients with advanced deficits. Radiation therapy may be considered as an adjunct in select or recurrent cases. ABCs have a high recurrence rate.

## P1129: 20-year single-center experience with a syndromic approach to surgical treatment of tumors and spondylitis of the cervical spine in children

Dmitrii Glukhov

1

, Alexander Mushkin

2,3

1

Saint Petersburg State University Hospital, Saint Petersburg, Russian Federation

2

Saint Petersburg Research Institute of Phthisiopulmonology, Saint Petersburg, Russian Federation

3

Pavlov First Saint Petersburg State Medical University, Saint Petersburg, Russian Federation

Introduction: Objective of the study is to analyze the immediate and long-term outcomes of surgical treatment in children with tumor and infectious destruction of the cervical vertebrae. Material and Methods: The study included data from 94 children. The following parameters were assessed: the effectiveness of perioperative halo fixation, surgical approaches, methods of spinal reconstruction and stabilization, dynamics of pain and neurological disorders, and the pattern of postoperative complications. The data were statistically processed using nonparametric analysis methods. Statistical analysis and visualization of the results were performed using the R programming language. Results: In 12.8% of cases, the halo device was used due to significant deformities and spinal stenosis. Of the 34 resections requiring a combined approach, 30 were performed in a single surgical session. The median duration of the surgical stage was 162.5 minutes, and the median blood loss was 100 ml. Anterior column reconstruction was required in 62.8% of cases, and posterior instrumental fixation was required in 42.6%. Local pain was relieved in all cases. Four patients with neurological impairments showed an improvement in their neurological status by one level or more according to the Frankel scale, two patients showed a deterioration by one level, and nine patients showed no significant change in their neurological status. Complications were observed in 26.6% of cases, with only five patients requiring repeat surgery. Destruction of three or more vertebrae impacts surgical outcome (p = 0.0298). Conclusion: A unified syndromic approach to treating children with cervical vertebral destruction is justified and is associated with a minimal incidence of clinically significant perioperative complications.

## ID: AGS26/3101

## P1130: Primary bone B-cell lymphoma presenting as cauda equina syndrome: a case report

Artur Rocha

1

, Leonardo Fraga

1

, Ruan Santos

1

, João Monteiro

1

, Wagner Herculano

1

, Eduardo Varjao

1

, Camila Moura

1

, Iogo Henrique

1

, Pedro Ponde

1

, Lucas Lelis

1

, Jaquisson Guimarães

1

, Antonio Gutemberg Martins

1

, Kaio Mata

2

, Marcio Brandao

1

1

Hospital Geral Cleriston Andrade, Feira de Santana, Brazil

2

Escola Baiana de Medicina e Saúde Pública, Salvador, Brazil

Introduction: Primary bone lymphoma (PBL) is a rare extranodal form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), accounting for only 3-7% of primary bone tumors and less than 2% of all NHL cases in adults. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), it is characterized by isolated or multiple bone involvement without visceral or nodal disease. Clinical presentation with myeloradicular compression symptoms, such as cauda equina syndrome, is particularly uncommon. This highlights the critical role of neurosurgeons not only in initial management and decompression but also in obtaining biopsy material and ensuring timely referral for onco-hematologic treatment. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 30-year-old man, previously healthy, who presented with a 3-month history of incapacitating low back pain radiating to the left lower limb, followed by progressive gait impairment. Subsequently, he developed urinary and fecal retention, leading to admission to the Neurosurgery Department of Hospital Geral Clériston Andrade. He denied B symptoms, such as fever, night sweats, or weight loss. On physical examination, he exhibited grade IV paresis in the left lower limb, associated with hypoesthesia, hypotonia, and muscle hypotrophy. Muscle strength was grade III and reflexes grade II in both lower limbs, along with saddle hypoesthesia. Laboratory tests were within normal limits. Results: Lumbosacral magnetic resonance imaging revealed an expansive, infiltrative lesion from S1 to S3, invading the left sacral ala and compressing the cauda equina roots. The patient underwent lumbosacral arthrodesis from L4 to S2 with L5-S1 foraminal decompression, resulting in partial improvement of neurological deficits. Bone biopsy from the S1 pedicle showed a round-cell infiltrate with necrosis. The initial immunohistochemical panel was inconclusive, requiring further analysis. The final evaluation confirmed a high-grade B-cell lymphoma with a germinal center pattern. The panel showed positivity for CD10, CD19, CD20, CD45, CD79a, PAX-5, BCL6, and FLI-1, with a Ki-67 proliferation index greater than 90%. Markers such as CD3, CD30, TDT, MUM1, ALK, BCL2, CD138, EBV-LMP, Desmin, ERG, NKX2.2, and Myogenin were negative. Following diagnosis, the patient was promptly referred to an Onco-Hematology service. His young age and the high Ki-67 index suggested a favorable response to systemic chemotherapy with the R-CHOP regimen (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone), combined with radiotherapy. Literature data indicate that PBL demonstrates a better therapeutic response compared to non-osseous lymphomas of similar histology. Conclusion: PBL is an exceptionally rare cause of cauda equina syndrome. Initial neurosurgical management is crucial for both decompression and biopsy, minimizing permanent neurological sequelae. Histopathological evaluation together with immunohistochemistry is essential for establishing a definitive diagnosis and ensuring rapid referral for appropriate onco-hematologic treatment.

References

1. Wang Zining et al. Two cases on primary bone marrow lymphoma. Cancer Pathogenesis and Therapy, v. 2, n. 1, 58-61, 2024.
2. Wang, Hao-Yuan et al. Primary bone marrow lymphoma: A hematological emergency in adults with fever of unknown origin. Cancer Medicine, v. 7, n. 8, 3713-3721, 2018.
3. Zhou, Hai-Yan et al. Primary bone lymphoma: A case report and review of the literature. Oncology Letters, v. 8, n. 4, 1551-1556, 2014.
4. Suresh, Pooja K. et al. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma in blood and bone marrow: a rare presentation of leukemic phase at diagnosis. Hematology, Transfusion and Cell Therapy, v. 42, n. 2, 180-183, 2020.
5. Yohannan, Binoy; Rios, Adan. Primary diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the bone. Journal of Hematology, v. 12, n. 2, 75, 2023.
6. Bindal, Poorva et al. Primary bone lymphoma: a case series and review of literature. Case Reports in Hematology, v. 2020, n. 1, 4254803, 2020.
7. Fonseca HC, et al. Linfoma ósseo primário: relato de caso. Hematology, Transfusion and Cell Therapy, v. 42, 234, 2020.
8. Wang Jia et al. A rare case report of primary bone lymphoma and a brief review of the literature. OncoTargets and therapy, 4923-4928, 2016.

## ID: AGS26/3103

## P1131: Minimally invasive resection of a thoracic spine osteoid osteoma: a case report

Rafael Silva

1

, Artur Teixeira

1

, Diogo Rodrigues

1

, Eduardo Pinto

1

, Ricardo Frada

1

1

Unidade Local de Saúde Entre Douro e Vouga, Santa Maria da Feira, Portugal

Introduction: Osteoid osteoma is a benign osteoblastic tumor and in 10% of the cases occurs in the spine. Thoracic involvement is less frequent than lumbar or cervical, but clinically relevant due to persistent nocturnal pain, risk of spinal deformity, and rare neurological symptoms. The nidus typically arises from posterior elements, and complete excision is required to avoid recurrence. Minimally invasive tubular approaches may reduce morbidity and accelerate recovery. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 29-year-old male with one year of nocturnal dorsal pain without neurological deficits. MRI demonstrated an osteoid osteoma involving the right lamina and inferior articular facet of T8. The patient underwent surgical excision using a minimally invasive tubular retractor system with partial T8 facetectomy. Results: The procedure and hospital stay were uneventful. Wound healing was satisfactory, with no local or systemic complications. Histopathology confirmed an osteogenic lesion consistent with osteoid osteoma. At three-month follow-up, the patient was asymptomatic, with complete pain resolution and full return to daily activities without functional limitations. Conclusion: This case illustrates a rare osteoid osteoma of the thoracic spine and the efficacy of minimally invasive tubular resection. The approach enabled complete nidus excision with minimal soft tissue disruption, leading to rapid recovery and excellent short-term outcome. Minimally invasive surgery should be considered a safe and effective option for posterior element osteoid osteomas of the spine.

## ID: AGS26/3114

## P1132: Pathologic fracture of a lumbar vertebra secondary to metastatic pancreatobiliary adenocarcinoma presenting with paraplegia: case report

Artur Bastos Rocha

1

, Leonardo Fraga Lima

1

, Eduardo Varjão Vieira

1

, Camila Moura Sousa

1

, Wagner de Azevedo Herculano

1

, Thiago de Oliveira Carneiro

1

, João Pedro Cruz de Souza Monteiro

1

, Ruan Pablo Carmo dos Santos

1

, Iogo Henrique de Oliveira Araújo

1

, Antonio Gutemberg de Souza Martins

1

, Pedro Henrique Velasco Pondé de Sena

1

, Lucas Chaves Lelis

1

, Márcio César de Mello Brandão

1

1

Hospital Geral Cleriston Andrade, Feira de Santana, Brazil

Introduction: Vertebral body compression fractures are common in elderly patients. The main etiology is osteoporosis, but metastatic cancer must be included in the differential diagnosis of severe low back pain. In men, the most frequent source of bone metastasis is prostate cancer, while metastases from pancreatic neoplasms are rare and carry a poor prognosis, since disease presentation often relates to invasion of abdominal structures. Osseous metastases are uncommon and, when present, usually occur at advanced stages. They typically manifest as osteolytic lesions, while osteoblastic lesions are extremely rare. Thus, cases such as ours are scarcely reported and stand out due to their rarity. Material and Methods: A 68-year-old male, hypertensive, with prior venous thromboembolism, was admitted with low back pain radiating to the lower limbs, associated with paresthesia and urinary incontinence for two years. He had progressed to paraplegia one year earlier, becoming unable to walk. On admission, he was lucid, oriented, and responsive (Glasgow 15). Lower limbs were atrophic with proximal paraplegia and muscle strength grade 2. He presented with antalgic flexor posture and severe pain aggravated by mobilization. Previous imaging demonstrated a pathologic fracture at L2, with suspected malignant spinal neoplasm. Imaging revealed an expansive lesion with soft tissue and calcified components, leading to destruction and diffuse thinning of the L2 vertebral body, measuring 12.3 × 8.5 × 6.3 cm, highly suspicious for neoplastic involvement. CT showed thoracolumbar scoliosis and lytic lesions in L1. CT angiography suggested pulmonary thromboembolism, and chest CT identified pulmonary nodules. Urine culture isolated multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Results: The patient underwent two major surgeries: posterior fixation from L1 to L4 on the right with L2 Over The Top laminectomy and extradural lesion resection for biopsy, followed by vertebral tumor resection and deformity correction. Histopathology and immunohistochemistry confirmed metastatic pancreatobiliary adenocarcinoma with positive markers MUC-05 AC, EMA, and CK7. He received prolonged antibiotic therapy for Pseudomonas (4 weeks) and full anticoagulation for VTE and PE. A stage 4 sacral pressure ulcer evolved with infection by E. coli and fistula at the lumbar surgical site. One month after surgery, the patient presented marked clinical frailty (Karnofsky 30-40%, ECOG 3) and progressive deterioration. Given the advanced neoplastic disease, the palliative care team was involved. He was discharged for continuation of treatment at home. Conclusion: Vertebral fractures due to metastasis from pancreatobiliary adenocarcinoma are rare and carry a poor prognosis. This case highlights the importance of investigating persistent low back pain with progressive neurological deficit and underscores the need for multidisciplinary management and palliative care in advanced disease.

References

1. Chih YP, Wu WT, Lin CL, Jou HJ, Huang YH, Chen LC, Chou LW. Vertebral compression fracture related to pancreatic cancer with osteoblastic metastasis: a case report and literature review. Medicine (Baltimore). 2016;95(5):e2670. doi:10.1097/MD.0000000000002670. PMID: 26844499.
2. Mao C, Domenico DR, Kim K, Hanson DJ, Howard JM. Observations on the developmental patterns and consequences of exocrine pancreatic adenocarcinoma: findings of 154 autopsies. Arch Surg. 1995;130(2):125-34. doi:10.1001/archsurg.1995.01430020027005. PMID: 7848070.
3. Gregory LS, Choi W, Burke LJ, et al. Breast cancer cells induce osteolytic bone lesions in vivo through suppression of osteoblast activity in mice. PLoS One. 2013; 8(7): e68103. doi:10.1371/journal.pone.0068103. PMID: 23844213.
4. Pneumaticos SG, Savidou C, Korres DS, Chatziioannou SN. Pancreatic cancer's initial presentation: back pain due to osteoblastic bone metastasis. Eur J Cancer Care (Engl). 2010;19(1):137-40. doi:10.1111/j.1365-2354.2007.00920.x. PMID: 19708936.
