## ID: AGS26/3134

## P1133: Balloon kyphoplasty in multiple myeloma-related vertebral compression fractures: a systematic review of clinical outcomes and safety

Azmi Farhadi

1

1

Faculty of Medicine Universitas Airlangga, dr. Soetomo General Hospital Surabaya, Surabaya General Hospital Ministry of Health, Surabaya, Indonesia

Background: Vertebral compression fractures (VCFs) are among the most common and debilitating skeletal complications of multiple myeloma (MM). Balloon kyphoplasty has emerged as a minimally invasive intervention to stabilize vertebrae, restore height, and alleviate pain, yet evidence specific to MM remains fragmented. Objective: To systematically review the clinical outcomes, safety, and broader implications of balloon kyphoplasty in patients with MM-related VCFs. Material and Methods: This review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA 2020 guidelines. PubMed, Cochrane Library, and ScienceDirect were searched from inception to March 2025. Eligible studies included prospective or retrospective cohorts and case series with ≥ 5 MM patients treated with kyphoplasty. Outcomes of interest included pain, function, vertebral height restoration, complications, and additional systemic or psychosocial effects. Results: Of 304 records identified, 10 studies met inclusion criteria, encompassing > 460 patients and several hundred vertebral levels. Across studies, kyphoplasty consistently achieved rapid and durable pain reduction, with sustained improvements in disability indices such as the Oswestry Disability Index, EQ-5D, and Karnofsky Performance Status. Radiographic analyses demonstrated partial vertebral height restoration and correction of kyphosis, though some regression occurred over time. Cement leakage rates ranged from 4-26%, but were almost universally asymptomatic. Beyond symptomatic relief, kyphoplasty enabled earlier resumption of systemic therapy and improved psychosocial outcomes, including reductions in fatigue, anxiety, and depression. Conclusions: Balloon kyphoplasty is a safe and effective intervention for MM-related VCFs, providing not only symptomatic relief but also functional and systemic benefits. Further randomized studies are needed to refine patient selection, optimize timing, and establish its role within comprehensive myeloma management.

## ID: AGS26/3198

## P1134: Mature teratoma of the conus medullaris in an adult: a rare case report

Affes Ameur

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal teratomas are exceedingly rare lesions, particularly in adults. They are most commonly reported in children and adolescents, with the conus medullaris being an exceptional location. We present the case of a 37-year-old woman with a mature spinal teratoma, highlighting the diagnostic challenge and surgical outcome. Material and Methods: A 37-year-old female with a medical history of treated hepatic and pulmonary hydatid cysts presented with chronic low back pain evolving over 3 years, associated with bilateral non-systematized sciatica and neurogenic claudication. During the past year, she developed bladder dysfunction requiring intermittent catheterization and constipation. Neurological examination revealed lumbar tenderness, decreased reflexes on the left, absent reflexes on the right, but no motor or sensory deficit and no saddle anesthesia. MRI revealed a well-defined, oval intradural lesion at L1-L2, oval, hyperintense on T1/T2 suggesting subacute hemorrhage, without contrast enhancement. Differential diagnosis included intradural schwannoma or organized hematoma. Results: The patient underwent L1-L2 laminectomy and microsurgical excision of the lesion. Postoperatively, she remained neurologically intact, with only transient lower limb paresthesia. Histopathological examination confirmed a mature teratoma, composed of adipose tissue, respiratory epithelium, acinar structures, squamous epithelium, and glial tissue with reactive gliosis. No immature elements or malignant transformation were identified. Conclusion: Spinal teratomas of the conus medullaris in adults are rare and may mimic more common intradural lesions on imaging. MRI findings are nonspecific and histopathology remains the gold standard for diagnosis. Complete surgical excision is the treatment of choice and can achieve excellent outcomes with preservation of neurological function. This case underlines the importance of considering teratoma in the differential diagnosis of intradural conus medullaris lesions, even in adult patients.

## ID: AGS26/3206

## P1135: Spinal metastases from an intracranial anaplastic meningioma: a rare case and review of the literature introduction

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Meningiomas are usually benign tumors, but atypical and anaplastic variants may exhibit aggressive behavior, including extracranial metastases. Spinal dissemination is exceedingly rare, representing approximately 0.1% of operated meningiomas. We report a case of multiple spinal metastases from an intracranial anaplastic meningioma, with a review of the literature. Material and Methods: A patient with a history of surgically treated intracranial meningioma was admitted with progressive spinal symptoms. Clinical presentation, radiological findings, and histopathological features were analyzed. Literature data regarding spinal meningioma metastases were reviewed to contextualize this case. Results: Spinal MRI revealed multiple vertebral lesions consistent with metastatic involvement. Histopathological analysis confirmed metastatic deposits originating from the previously diagnosed intracranial anaplastic meningioma. The patient’s clinical course was marked by progressive neurological decline despite multidisciplinary management. Conclusion: Spinal metastases of meningiomas are extremely rare but should be considered in patients with a history of atypical or anaplastic meningiomas presenting with new spinal symptoms. The prognosis of metastatic meningiomas depends largely on the histological subtype, with anaplastic variants carrying a poor outcome. This case underlines the importance of long-term follow-up in patients with high-grade meningiomas and raises awareness of their metastatic potential.

## ID: AGS26/3212

## P1136: Malignant hematologic diseases with vertebral involvement: a retrospective series of 11 cases

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal involvement in hematologic malignancies accounts for 5-13% of tumor-related spinal cord compressions, and may be inaugural. Multiple myeloma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma are the most frequent etiologies. Material and Methods: We retrospectively reviewed 11 patients surgically treated for vertebral involvement of hematologic malignancies between 2017 and 2025. The cohort included 5 men and 6 women, mean age 53 years (range 31-84). All underwent spinal MRI and surgical management consisting of laminectomy with instrumentation (n = 9), corporectomy with fixation (n = 1), or CT-guided biopsy (n = 1). Results: The main presenting symptoms were motor weakness (n = 8), radicular pain (n = 2), and sphincter dysfunction (n = 1). Neurological examination revealed paraparesis in 2 cases and complete paraplegia in 7. MRI showed thoracic involvement in 7 cases and lumbar in 4, with vertebral body lesions in 8, combined vertebral and posterior arch involvement in 3, and epidural extension in 9. Histopathology revealed multiple myeloma (n = 4), plasmacytoma (n = 3), B-cell lymphoma (n = 3), and acute myeloid leukemia (n = 1). Spinal involvement was the first manifestation of hematologic disease in 9 patients. Adjuvant therapy included chemotherapy (n = 9) and radio-chemotherapy (n = 2). At 3 years, survival was 45% (5/11 patients). Conclusion: Vertebral localizations of hematologic malignancies are a rare but serious cause of spinal cord compression. MRI is the gold standard for diagnosis and assessment of extension. Surgical decompression with stabilization, combined with systemic therapy, provides symptom relief and functional improvement. Prognosis remains poor and is mainly determined by early diagnosis and the underlying hematologic disease.

## ID: AGS26/3214

## P1137: Paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration and Modified Frailty Index as predictors of perioperative outcomes in patients treated surgically for spinal metastatic disease

Robert Ferdon

1

, Aidan Gillespie

2

, Michal Williams

2

, Ronit Kulkarni

2

, Jason Silvestre

2

, Robert Ravinsky

2

1

University of South Carolina - School of Medicine, Columbia, United States

2

Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, United States

Introduction: The objective of this study was to identify the role of lumbar paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration, as quantified by the Goutallier classification, and perioperative outcomes in patients undergoing surgical treatment for metastatic spinal disease. Material and Methods: Patients undergoing surgical intervention for metastatic spinal disease were retrospectively identified through a single institution since 2021. Preoperative magnetic resonance imaging at the L4-5 level was used to assign Goutallier grades of paraspinal muscle fatty infiltration. Baseline demographics, comorbidities, and frailty status were collected using the Modified Frailty Index (MFI-5). Outcomes of interest included length of hospital stay, discharge disposition, postoperative complications and adverse events, readmission and reoperation rates within 180 days, and morbidity (death). The relationships between demographics, Goutallier grade, MFI-5 score, and postoperative outcomes were analyzed using Chi-squared analyses, Kruskal-Wallis tests, and multivariable regression. Results: A total of 43 patients met inclusion criteria, 27 males and 16 females with a mean age of 65.7 years and mean Goutallier grade of 2.16. Tumor types varied including prostate (n = 9), lung (n = 7), renal (n = 6), and breast (n = 2). There was no significant association between Goutallier grade and length of stay (p = 0.410), surgical time (p = 0.407), reoperation (p = 0.485), readmission (p = 0.557), or non-home discharge (p = 0.472). Complication rates overall did not differ significantly when deaths were included as complications (p = 0.470). However, analysis of death as a distinct morbidity outcome revealed a significant association with Goutallier grade (p = 0.029), with higher Goutallier scores, particularly grade 4, demonstrating increased mortality risk. Conclusion: Paraspinal fatty infiltration was not associated with differences in perioperative resource utilization or general complication rates. However, higher Goutallier grades were significantly linked to increased mortality following surgery for spinal metastasis. These findings support fatty infiltration as a potential noninvasive imaging biomarker of poor prognosis and mortality risk in this population, providing value for preoperative counseling and individualized risk stratification.

## ID: AGS26/3219

## P1138: Sacral chordomas: a series of four cases

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Omar Hattab

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia

Introduction: Chordomas are rare malignant bone tumors arising from notochordal remnants. They represent the most frequent sacral tumors, predominantly affecting patients over 50 years old. We present a retrospective analysis of four patients treated for sacral chordoma over the past twenty years, focusing on clinical presentation, imaging, surgical management, complications, and postoperative outcomes. Material and Methods: Four male patients aged 52-89 years (mean 70) were included. Clinical, radiological, surgical, and follow-up data were retrospectively analyzed. Results: All patients presented with localized sacral pain; one had bilateral sciatica, and another had a palpable sacral mass. Preoperative bladder dysfunction was present in all patients, with one also showing perineal hypoesthesia. No motor deficits were observed. Preoperative pulmonary metastases were found in the oldest patient. Surgical management included subtotal resection in two patients and partial resection in the other two. Recurrence occurred in all cases, prompting reoperation in three patients. Overall survival ranged from 17 months to 11 years (mean 47 months). Conclusion: Sacral chordomas are rare but aggressive tumors. Maximal surgical resection remains the treatment of choice, reducing recurrence and metastasis risk, but often at the cost of significant neurological sacrifice. Early diagnosis is essential to optimize surgical outcomes and preserve function. Long-term follow-up is mandatory due to the high rate of recurrence.

## ID: AGS26/3228

## P1139: From collapse to comeback: paediatric cervical Ewing’s sarcoma response to chemo-radiotherapy

Prabodh Kantiwal

1

1

All India Institute of Medical Sciences, Jodhpur, India

Introduction: Ewing’s sarcoma is a malignant tumour that most frequently affects children and adolescents. Ewing’s sarcoma involving the cervical spine is exceptionally rare in children. Early clinical presentation may be subtle, often as persistent spinal pain without neurological deficit. Here, a young boy presented with Ewing’s sarcoma of the C4 vertebra with soft tissue invasion and spinal cord compression. Material and Methods: A boy in his first decade presented with gradually worsening neck pain over several weeks. The pain was localised and not associated with recent trauma, fever, or systemic symptoms such as night sweats or weight loss. MRI of the spine revealed collapse of the C4 vertebral body, with hyperintense soft tissue spanning prevertebral and neural foramina regions and accompanying para-spinal extension. No major cord compression was seen, and the remainder of the spine was unremarkable. CT-guided biopsy of the lesion was performed. Histopathological examination revealed uniform small, round, blue cells arranged in sheets with necrosis and occasional vague rosette formations. Immunohistochemistry was positive for vimentin, CD99, NKX2.2, and FLI1, and negative for synaptophysin, chromogranin, WT1, and MyoD-1-confirming Ewing’s sarcoma. Staging workup, including PET-CT, revealed disease localised to the cervical spine without evidence of distant metastases. The child was started on an interval-compressed regimen of chemotherapy comprising vincristine, doxorubicin, and cyclophosphamide, alternating with ifosfamide and etoposide (VAC/IE protocol). Definitive radiotherapy was subsequently delivered to the cervical spine to maximise local control. Results: The patient showed rapid improvement in pain and energy levels within months of commencing therapy. Functionally, he resumed full participation in school and regular childhood activities. Repeat imaging with MRI and PET-CT post-therapy showed resolution of the soft tissue component, persistence of vertebral collapse (vertebra plana), and no evidence of active or metastatic disease. No neurological deficits or late toxicity have been observed over one year of surveillance. Continued monitoring for recurrence, spinal deformity, or long-term sequelae is ongoing. Conclusion: Persistent neck pain in children warrants prompt MRI and staged diagnostic workup to exclude malignancy. Chemotherapy and targeted radiotherapy are the pillars of modern management for spinal Ewing’s sarcoma. Non junctional, stable, neurologically intact paediatric patients should be managed with combined chemo-radiotherapy. Surgery should be reserved for instability or neurological compromise.

## ID: AGS26/3238

## P1140: Primary intradural cervical malignant melanoma: a case report

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Omar Hattab

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia,

Introduction: Primary malignant melanoma of the central nervous system (CNS) is extremely rare, with spinal involvement being even less common. It belongs to the group of CNS melanocytic tumors. We report a case of intradural cervical melanoma causing spinal cord compression. Material and Methods: A 51-year-old male with no significant medical history presented with 6 months of left C6 radicular pain, gait disturbances, bladder dysfunction, and sexual impotence. Neurological examination revealed spastic gait, quadripyramidal syndrome, bilateral Babinski, positive Hoffmann, and a sensory level above C5. MRI of the cervical spine showed a 3.2 cm intradural, extramedullary posterolateral lesion extending from C5 to C7, displacing the spinal cord anteriorly, with heterogeneous T1 signal, superior hyperintense “cap sign,” moderate gadolinium enhancement, and heterogeneous T2 signal. Systemic work-up was negative. Surgical intervention consisted of total resection via posterior approach. Histopathology and immunohistochemistry were used to confirm diagnosis. Results: Postoperatively, radicular pain, gait disturbance, and bladder dysfunction significantly improved. Residual pyramidal signs and sexual dysfunction persisted. Follow-up MRI showed no local recurrence. Systemic evaluation excluded a primary melanoma elsewhere, confirming the diagnosis of primary intradural malignant melanoma. Conclusion: Primary intradural cervical melanoma is rare. MRI facilitates preoperative suspicion, but histopathology is definitive. Surgical resection is the main treatment, often combined with adjuvant radiotherapy. Prognosis can be favorable with regular MRI follow-up to detect local or distant recurrence.

## ID: AGS26/3245

## P1141: Spinal Ewing sarcoma: report of two cases

Affes Ameur

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Omar Hattab

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia,

Introduction: Primary spinal Ewing sarcoma is rare. Management typically involves a combination of surgery and adjuvant radio-chemotherapy. Prognosis remains poor due to aggressive tumor biology and potential neurological compromise. Material and Methods: We report two pediatric cases of primary spinal Ewing sarcoma surgically treated between 2018 and 2025. Clinical presentation, imaging, surgical intervention, and outcomes were analyzed. Results: Case 1: An 11-year-old boy with no prior history presented with acute spinal cord compression over 10 days, showing flaccid paraplegia and hypoesthesia at D10-D12. MRI revealed a right paravertebral mass with intracanalar extension from D10 to L2. He underwent D12-L3 laminectomy with complete tumor resection. Postoperatively, partial neurological improvement was observed. Systemic staging was negative. Despite adjuvant radio-chemotherapy, the patient died 10 months later from medullary aplasia. Case 2: A 5-year-old girl presented with a 2-month history of spinal cord compression, including spastic tetraparesis and sphincter dysfunction. Imaging revealed C2 osteolysis with dysplastic changes. Posterior surgical decompression was performed emergently. Postoperative neurological status remained stable. She died 7 months later from acute respiratory distress due to infection. Both patients demonstrated rapid neurological compromise. Surgery allowed partial or temporary symptom relief, but long-term outcomes were poor despite multimodal therapy. Conclusion: Spinal Ewing sarcoma is a rare but aggressive malignancy in children. Early recognition is critical, particularly in young patients presenting with lytic vertebral lesions and soft tissue involvement. Prognosis remains guarded, even with aggressive surgical and adjuvant treatment.

## ID: AGS26/3290

## P1142: Emergency detection of compressive spinal tumors: differential diagnosis with acute trauma - Literature review

Affes Ameur

1

, Maryem Khayat

2

, Majdouline Barkache

1

, Rihab Ben Fraj

1

, Mahmoud Ben Messaoud

1

, Chiheb Abdelileh

1

, Omar Hattab

1

1

Sahloul Hospital, Sousse, Tunisia, Sousse, Tunisia

2

Fattouma Bourguiba Hospital, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Acute paraparesis or quadriparesis often prompts suspicion of spinal trauma in emergency settings. However, compressive spinal tumors may present with a similar acute neurological picture, posing a diagnostic challenge. Early differentiation is critical to guide appropriate management and avoid delays in treatment. Material and Methods: A systematic literature review was conducted using PubMed and Google Scholar with the keywords: “compressive spinal tumor,” “emergency presentation,” “acute paraparesis,” “differential diagnosis,” and “spinal trauma.” Studies describing cases of acute neurological deficits initially suggestive of trauma but ultimately attributed to neoplastic lesions were included. Data on clinical presentation, imaging findings, diagnostic delays, and outcomes were extracted. Results: Compressive spinal tumors may mimic acute traumatic cord injury, particularly when symptom onset is rapid. MRI is the gold standard for differentiation, revealing expansile lesions, vertebral body destruction, or epidural masses not apparent on standard X-rays. Misdiagnosis as trauma can lead to inappropriate immobilization and delay in definitive neurosurgical or oncological intervention. Early imaging and awareness of red flags (e.g., progressive deficits, history of malignancy, back pain preceding acute weakness) improve diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes. Conclusion: In emergency settings, acute paraparesis may result from compressive spinal tumors rather than trauma. Prompt MRI and clinical vigilance are essential for accurate diagnosis, timely neurosurgical referral, and optimized neurological recovery. Emergency physicians must consider neoplastic causes in the differential diagnosis of sudden spinal cord syndromes.

## ID: AGS26/3298

## P1143: Bifocal spinal cord intramedullary cavernous malformations: case report

Emna Mzoughi

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Ghassen Gader

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Cavernous malformations or cavernoma are hamartomas-like uncommon vascular malformations of the central nervous system characterized by abnormally dilated blood vessels with a thin endothelium without intervening normal nervous tissue. Spinal cord cavernomas are rare entity but has been reported in literature. Material and Methods: Here, we report an extremely rare case of bifocal localization of spinal cord intramedullary cavernous malformations in the cervical and thoracic regions. A 40-year-old Arabic man, who presented with a three month history of acute cervical and thoracic pain with progressive weakness of right lower limb. Neurological examination revealed a spastic monoparesis of his right leg. Spinal cord MRI revealed two cavernous malformations at cervical and thoracic level. The patient underwent a surgical excision of the two lesions and a histopathological examination done by two different pathology laboratories revealed the features of cavernous malformation. Conclusion: Spinal cord intramedullary cavernous malformations although rare have a significant morbidity. It should be diagnosed early on a range of nonspecific clinical and MRI findings and then treated early.

## P1144: Lumbar spinal epidural angiolipoma mimicking a discal herniation

Emna Mzoughi

1

, seif amara

1

, Mouna Rkhami

1

, Mohamed Badri

1

, Kamel Bahri

1

, Ihsen Zammel

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, Trauma and Burns Center, Ben Arous, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal angiolipomas are fairly intrequent benign tumours that are usually located in the epidural space of the thoracic column and represent 0.14% to 1.3% of all spinal tumours. Lumbar angiolipomas are extremely fare, representing only 9.6% of all apinal extradural angiolipomas. Material and Methods: The present report describes a rase case of a lumbar epidural angiolipoma, in a 38 year-old woman presenting with a S days history of paralytic sciatalgia. The clinical examination, found an obesity, and a neurological deficit of the right leg, Th lumbar CI; showed a typical image of a discal herniation at the LA-15 level. Per operative, we are estonished not to find a discal herniation, but a fibrous sticky yellowish hyper vascular tumor in the anterior epidural space, encompassing the dural sheath, and fusing into the foramina of L5. Histological study revealed the tumor as an angiolipoma. Symptoms were relieved after tumor excision, with immediate recovery of the deficit, after one month, there were no sequelae. Conclusion: Although extremely rare, lumbar epidural angiolipoma should be considered in the differential diagnosis of lumbar spinal epidural lesions. MRI is essential for diagnosis. The prognosis after surgical management of this lesion is favorable.

## ID: AGS26/3311

## P1145: A rare convergence: multiple spinal schwannomas and spinal instability in a non-NF2 patient

Talel Kamoun

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Elouni Emna

1

, Sofiene Bouali

1

, hatem daoud

2

, Khouloud Bensalem

2

, Houssem Hdhili

1

, Alaa Belhadj

1

1

Institut National de Neurologie Mongi-Ben Hamida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

hopital Habib Bourguiba Sfax, sfax, Tunisia

Introduction: Schwannomas are a benign encapsulated tumors arising from Schwann cells of the nerve sheath, that can occur anywhere in the central nervous system. They most commonly occur however at the cerebello-pontine angle and the lumbar region. Schwannomas occurring at the conus medullaris and the Cerebello-pontine angle in the same patient however represent a rare association, especially in the absence of predisposing conditions such as type 2 neurofibromatosis. the occurrence of these tumours in patients showing no signs of NF-2 represents a rare occurrence, rarely reported in the literature. One more particularity reported in this case is the 20-year period that separates the symptoms of these two pathologies, thus raising the question on the pathophysiological phenomena at work in the absence of NF-2. Case Report: We report the case of a 60-year-old patient, who has a history of a cystic schwannoma of the right fifth cranial pair who consulted for a left L5 sciatica that has started about one year prior to consultation, and resisted to medical treatment. He had no history of trauma or fever. Upon clinical examination, the patient exhibited severe pain with difficulty walking, he reported a Visual analogue pain of 7/10. He however had no motor deficit, his reflexes were present, and the urogenital sphere examination as well as The cutaneous and ophthalmological examination showed no particularities, no signs of neurofibromatosis notably. His spine Radiographies showed a grade 1 listhesis between the L3-L4 and L4-L5 vertebrae. His spinal MRI showed multiples masses of the conus terminalis and spina equina whose radiologic characteristics were consistent with spinal schwannomas. The patient was operated on via a posterior approach, 12 pedicle screws were placed in the D11 to L4 vertebrae, and the tumors were removed in their entirety. The post operative course was uneventful, and the patient was discharged on day 3. Discussion: Spinal schwannomas are relatively uncommon, accounting for approximately 0.3% of all primary intraspinal tumors. They most frequently occur in the lumbar region and typically originate from the dorsal nerve roots. While multiple spinal schwannomas are well-documented in patients with Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF-2), their presence in individuals without any clinical signs of NF-2 is exceptionally rare and sparsely reported in the literature. In this case report, we present a 60-year-old patient with a prior history of a cystic schwannoma affecting the right fifth cranial nerve. The patient sought medical attention for left-sided L5 sciatica. Imaging studies revealed multiple schwannomas located at the conus medullaris, along with evidence of spinal instability-an additional factor that complicated the surgical approach. The coexistence of multiple spinal schwannomas and spinal instability in a patient with a history of cranial nerve schwannoma, yet no signs of NF-2, represents a highly unusual clinical scenario. This rare constellation of findings highlights the importance of thorough diagnostic evaluation and individualized surgical planning, and it underscores a gap in the current literature that warrants further exploration. Conclusion: This case illustrates a rare presentation of multiple spinal schwannomas in a 60-year-old male patient, that has a history of a schwannoma of the fifth cranial pair. A multidisciplinary approach, including advanced imaging, and mutli-disciplinary surgical management, is crucial for accurate diagnosis and optimal patient outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/3329

## P1146: The hidden lesion - Cervicothoracic intramedullary ependymoma as a diferential diagnosis - A case report

Diogo Mesquita

1

, Ricardo Lage

2

, Diogo Belo

3

1

ULSSM, Lisboa, Portugal

2

Unidade de Saúde Local de Santa Maria, Lisboa, Portugal

3

Cuf Tejo, ULSSM, Lisboa, Portugal

Introduction: A 61-year-old female presented with a one-year history of low back pain radiating to the left leg, accompanied by progressive neurological deficits that started with paresthesia on both legs and feet. Imaging of the lumbar spine revealed a grade I L4-L5 spondylolisthesis in the Meyerding scale, with mild central canal stenosis. Surgical management was pursued due to the severity of symptoms. The patient underwent a L4-L5 laminectomy and posterior fusion. During the procedure there was an incidental dural tear. After surgery the patient showed improvement in pain and neurological symptoms but a few days later a Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak was noticed. The patient remained in conservative treatment suggested by the surgeon, until there was no more evidence of CSF leakage, which spanned for the next four months. A few days after the CSF leak stopped the patient started to progressively worsen again until she initiated to have paraparesis that evolved for the next two months, when she finally came to our consultation for the first time and was evaluated in our center. Materials and Methods: The patient’s surgical and clinical reports were inspected. All the imaging files the patient had as well as the surgical video for the surgery we performed. When the patient was initially evaluated in our center, she presented with signs of progressively worse tetraparesis, with a grade 1 muscle strength on the lower limbs and grade 4 on the upper. A cervical and thoracic magnetic resonance image (MRI) was done and an intramedullary lesion was diagnosed between C7 and T3. A new surgery was proposed and the patient agreed. We performed laminotomy of C6 through T4, myelotomy, complete tumor resection, and laminoplasty. Results: Postoperatively, the patient’s neurological status stabilized, with modest improvement in lower extremity strength by the fifth postoperative day, achieving a grade 2 muscle strength and allowing for discharge. A postoperative MRI showed a completed removal of the tumor with no surgical complications. Conclusions: This case highlights the importance of maintaining a broad differential diagnosis in patients presenting with new neurological deficits after spinal surgery. The unexpected detection of an intramedullary ependymoma underlines the value of comprehensive imaging and clinical vigilance. The initial improvement after the first surgery and its correlation to the CSF leak could’ve suggested other etiologies. Early surgical intervention was crucial in achieving partial neurological recovery and preventing further deterioration.

## ID: AGS26/3361

## P1147: Spinal skeletal metastases with local gelatinous production from primary pancreatic adenocarcinoma presenting with lower limb paraparesis

Sandra Cilevska

1

, Igor Merdzanoski

2

, Vladimir Nanceski

3

, Aleksandra Tanevska

4

, Mario Radevski

5

1

Trauma Surgeon

2

Trauma General and Spine Surgeon, University Clinic of Traumatology, Skopje, Macedonia

3

Orthopedic Surgeon, General Hospital, Orthopedis department, Gostivar, Macedonia,

4

Trauma resident, University clinic of Traumatology, Skopje, Macedonia

5

Medical doctor

Introduction: Pain and neurological symptoms in trauma surgery are often attributed to mechanical injury.Sometimes, that masks pathological processes occurring in the background. Neoplastic cell disseminations in the spine are a rare occurrence, especially in patients with an unknown existing malignant process.In metastatic tumors, the most common are adenocarcinomas, especially from the lungs, breast, and prostate, while those from the pancreas occur much less often. When they appear, their most common localization in the spine is the thoracic and lumbar parts. In this case, we will show how a malignant involvement of the spine was incidentally discovered, which points out the importance of clinical thinking and differential diagnosis. Case presentation: In this case, we present a 62-year-old man that presented to the emergency department with complaints of progressive muscle weakness in the legs, and inability to walk and stand, for the last 3 months, which gradually worsened. He also had upper back pain for 6 months, for which he received anti-inflammatory drugs without improvement. On examination, complete sensation was preserved. Asia scored with an incomplete lesion Gr C. A series of examinations were performed, including CT and MRI of the thoracic spine, Bone scintigrafy, which showed pathological osteolytic fractures of the thoracic vertebral bodies III, IV and V, with compression of the spinal cord at the same level by the soft tissue substrate. An operative intervention was performed with transpedicular fixation ( two levels above and below) and laminectomy, and a ground biopsy that was negative. Lab Tu marker shown Ca19-9 194 idicative for panreatic carcinoma. After the surgery the pain subsided and he started moving independently, with a walking aid and was sent to do physical therapy. Furthermore, in consultation with a gastroenterologist and oncologist, a pancreatic biopsy was performed, which finally confirmed the primary tumor pathologically (Moderately differentiated G2 invasive pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma) and the patient began chemotherapy. 3 months after the surgery, the patient complained of pain in the surgical region, and on examination a large amount of fluctuating content was noted locally, which upon puncture, showed gelatinous mucinous material that continuously increased in the following period, which was resolved by placing a catheter. Conclusion: This case illustrates the importance of not overlooking the rare possibility of metastases. The appearance of pathological fractures of the vertebral bodies, besides other things, can be thought of as secondary metastases. This makes us think about their origin. What makes this case interesting is the serous mucin secretion from the disseminated neoplastic cells at the thoracic spinal level even after paliative surgical fixation and decompresion was made. Unfortunately, we are talking about terminal patients. However, an operational approach can significantly improve the quality of remaining life.
Keywords: Spinal metastases, paraparesis, mucin producion, pancreatic adenocarcinoma

## ID: AGS26/3367

## P1148: Aggressive Vertebral Hemangioma in a Pediatric Patient: A Rare Cause of Acute Spinal Cord Compression”

Talel Kamoun

1

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Elouni Emna

1

, Sofiene Bouali

1

, Hatem Daoud

1

, Alaa Belhadj

1

, Khouloud Bensalem

1

1

Institut National de Neurologie Mongi-Ben Hamida, Tunis, Tunisia

Introduction: While Spinal hemangiomas typically present as benign slow growing asymptomatic lesions, they can rarely present extraosseous expansion and spinal cord compression, thus exhibiting atypical behaviour characteristic of “Aggressive hemangiomas” which are a rare type of hemangiomas which can be responsible of serious comorbidities and functional consequences through compression of the spinal cord. Case Report: We report the case of a 14-year-old male patient, with no medical history, who presented to the emergency room with complaints of weakness and sensitive loss of the legs as well as pain. He had no history of trauma or fever. On neurological examination the patient presented a paraparesis and diminished sensitivity at the level of the D6 vertebra. His patellar and achileen reflexes were increased with bilateral Babinski sign.His MRI showed a spinal mass at the level of the D6 vertebra that was responsible of compression of the spinal cord, whose caracteristics were consistant with an aggressive hemangioma. Emergency decompressive surgery was performed, and a decompressive posterior dorsal laminectomy was performed with significant improvement of the symptomes. However, the patient developed kyphosis post operatively, and so a second stage surgery was performed to remove the tumour and reestablish the sagittal balance. Discussion: Aggressive hemangiomas can exhibit extraosseous extension, cortical bone erosion, and spinal cord compression. These caracteristics were responsible In this case,of acute paraparesis, sensory deficits, and hyperreflexia. The MRI remains the cornerstone for diagnosis, with aggressive hemangiomas typically showing hyperintense signals on T1- and T2-weighted sequences and evidence of epidural extension. Surgical decompression is often the first-line treatment in cases of neurological compromise. In our patient, posterior laminectomy provided immediate relief of symptoms. However, the development of postoperative kyphosis highlights a known complication of extensive laminectomy, especially in the pediatric population where spinal biomechanics are still evolving. The need for a second-stage surgery to remove the tumor and restore sagittal balance underscores the importance of considering spinal stability during initial surgical planning. This case also raises awareness of the rare but serious nature of aggressive hemangiomas in children. While most literature focuses on adult presentations, pediatric cases-though infrequent-require prompt recognition and multidisciplinary management to prevent long-term disability. Conclusion: This case underscores the importance of recognizing aggressive spinal hemangiomas as a potential cause of acute neurological deterioration, even in children. Prompt imaging and surgical decompression are critical to prevent irreversible deficits. Furthermore, careful surgical planning is essential to mitigate postoperative complications such as kyphosis, particularly in the growing spine. Multidisciplinary care and long-term follow-up are key to optimizing outcomes in these complex cases.

## ID: AGS26/3378

## P1149: Agressive spinal haemangioma Enneking Type II: case series and literature review

Ahmed ElGhamry

1

, Filippo Cainelli

2

, Adnan Shaikh

1

, Mohamed A Abdalla

1

, Leslie Bridges

1

, Darren Lui

1

1

St George’s University Hospital, London, United Kingdom

2

St George’s University of London, London, United Kingdom

Introduction: Vertebral hemangiomas are common and typically benign vascular lesions, with a prevalence of 10-12% in the general population and 2-3% of spine tumors. A small group of hemangiomas are categorised as aggressive haemangiomas. Operative haemangioma cases are rare. The salient features leading to operative care are acute presentation, presence of neurological deficit, intractable spinal or radicular pain and spinal instability. Materials and Methods: Our study is a retrospective study of case series of 8 cases of different age and sex. It included data from medical records, radiology and pathology reports. Study and research factors included age, sex, severity and acuity of clinical presentation, presence of neurological deficit, radiological findings, spinal instability, intervention done, post intervention outcome and follow up period. Results: In our series, age varied from 18 to 70 years with mean age of 45 years. Male to female ration was 2:1. One case was diagnosed in pregnant female. A 100% of our cases presented <4 months, three cases presented with acute neurological deterioration within 1 weeks and needed urgent surgical decompression. Seven out of 8 cases had neurological deficit (87.5%). All our cases were managed purely by surgical intervention ( 7 cases underwent spinal decompressive laminectomy, one case had surgical decompression and posterior spinal fixation ) without the need for preoperative embolization. Intraoperative blood loss varied between 400-1300 ml. All cases had improvement of their symptoms and satisfactory clinical outcome with no complications. Conclusion: According to our series, rapid speed of onset, neurological deficit and painful instability are key factors in determining surgical intervention. Surgical decompression laminectomy is an acceptable surgical option to treat aggressive spinal haemangiomas with satisfactory clinical and radiological outcome.

## ID: AGS26/3424

## P1150: Primary thoracic melanocytoma: a case report and review of the litterature

Elouni Emna

1

, Mohamed Amine Hadj Taeib

2

, Khalil Ghedira

1

, Mehdi Darmoul

2

1

Institut National de Neurologie, Mongi Ben Hmida, Tunis, Tunisia

2

Hopital Fattouma Bourguiba, Monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Primary melanocytomas of the central nervous system are exceedingly rare, with the lumbar and thoracic spinal cord being uncommon sites of occurrence. These tumors typically arise at the cranial base, ventral medulla, or cervical spinal cord due to the higher concentration of leptomeningeal melanocytes in these regions. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 42-year-old male who presented with a three-month history of thoracolumbar back pain and progressive gait disturbance. Neurological examination revealed paraparesis and urinary retention. Spinal MRI identified a 31 × 15 × 13 mm intradural extramedullary mass at the D11 level, with evidence of intralesional hemorrhage. Results: The patient underwent subtotal resection of the lesion. Postoperatively, there was no significant recovery of motor function or bladder control. Histopathological analysis confirmed the diagnosis of spinal cord melanocytoma. The patient was discharged and referred for rehabilitation and adjuvant radiation therapy. Conclusion: Spinal melanocytomas are rare entities that pose diagnostic and therapeutic challenges. While surgical resection remains the primary treatment, achieving total removal is often limited by the tumor’s location and adherence to neural structures. Postoperative functional recovery may be incomplete, emphasizing the need for multidisciplinary management, including rehabilitation and radiotherapy, to optimize long-term outcomes.

## ID: AGS26/2330

## P1153: Spinal Cord Metastasis of a Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A RareClinical Presentation

Amine Trifa

1

, Kais Maamri

1

, Emna Mzoughi

1

, Ghorbel Mohamed

1

, Mehdi Darmoul

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Primary central nervous system (CNS) lymphomas are rare in immunocompetent patients. Among them, diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), a subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, is the most frequent and typically involves the brain. Secondary CNS involvement from nodal lymphomas, particularly intramedullary metastases, remains exceptional. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 57-year-old patient with a history of DLBCL treated with chemo- and radiotherapy, achieving complete remission. He presented with severe right-sided lumbosciatalgia, resistant to conventional analgesics and persisting at rest. Spinal MRI revealed a tumoral process of the conus medullaris extending to the cauda equina roots. Histopathological examination confirmed a secondary localization of lymphoma. Results: We report the case of a 57-year-old patient with a history of DLBCL treated with chemo- and radiotherapy, achieving complete remission. He presented with severe right-sided lumbosciatalgia, resistant to conventional analgesics and persisting at rest. Spinal MRI revealed a tumoral process of the conus medullaris extending to the cauda equina roots. Histopathological examination confirmed a secondary localization of lymphoma. Conclusion: Intramedullary metastases are uncommon but may occur in both immunocompetent and immunocompromised patients. In individuals with a history of lymphoma, such lesions should raise strong suspicion, though histological confirmation by surgical biopsy remains essential. Chemotherapy continues to be the mainstay of treatment, while these cases highlight the need for awareness of rare but severe CNS presentations of systemic lymphomas.

## ID: AGS26/2326

## P1152: Osteochondroma of the Cervical Spine: An Exceptional Atlas Localizationin a Child

Amine Trifa

1

, Kais Maamri

1

, Emna Mzoughi

1

, Ahmed Amine Daoued

1

, Mehdi Darmoul

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Osteochondroma typically affects long bones, either as a solitary lesion or in the context of multiple hereditary exostoses. Spinal involvement is rare, accounting for only 1 -4% of all cases, and localization to the atlas is exceptional. Material and Methods: We report the case of a 13-year-old girl with a solitary osteochondroma arising from the posterior arch of the atlas, revealed by a posterior cervical swelling without associated neurological deficits. Preoperative imaging suggested the diagnosis, which was confirmed by histopathological examination following en bloc surgical excision of the lesion. Results: We highlight the radiological features of spinal osteochondromas and the surgical challenges posed by their proximity to critical structures, including the spinal cord and vertebral artery. Although rare, high cervical spine localizations are readily diagnosed with imaging. Surgery remains the treatment of choice, aiming for complete resection. Conclusion: The pathogenesis of osteochondromas remains uncertain. Prognosis is generally favorable after total excision, but potential complications such as neurological compression or, more rarely, sarcomatous transformation, justify surgical management even in asymptomatic cases.

## ID: AGS26/2322

## P1151: Extradural Spinal Metastasis from Nasopharyngeal Carcinoma Presenting with Acute Cord Compression: A Rare Case

Amine Trifa

1

, Kais Maamri

1

, Emna Mzoughi

1

, Ghorbel Mohamed

1

, Mehdi Darmoul

1

1

Department of Neurosurgery, monastir, Tunisia

Introduction: Spinal metastases are a common cause of epidural spinal cord compression,most frequently originating from lung, breast, or prostate cancer. Metastaticspread from nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC). Material and Methods: We present the case of a 50-year-old male with no significant past medical history who was admitted with rapidly progressive motor weakness of the lower limbs, gait disturbance, and sphincter dysfunction. Neurological examination revealed spastic paraparesis with sensory level and signs of cord compression. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine demonstrated an extradural mass causing severe compression of the spinal cord. The patient underwent emergency decompressive laminectomy with complete excision of the lesion. Postoperatively, there was partial neurological improvement. Histopathological examination of the specimen revealed metastatic carcinoma consistent with nasopharyngeal origin. Subsequent investigations confirmed the presence of a nasopharyngeal primary tumor. Results: Subsequent investigations confirmed the presence of a nasopharyngeal primary tumor. Conclusion: Extradural spinal metastasis from NPC is an exceptional occurrence. This case illustrates the importance of considering metastatic disease in patients presenting with acute spinal cord compression and emphasizes the role of urgent surgical decompression followed by oncological management.
